Letters

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/letters/docview/3106331719/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=unknown&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Letters&amp;title=The+Spectator&amp;issn=11899417&amp;date=2024-09-18&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.11&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Spectator&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Homeless busing talk nothing new
Re: Are 'busloads' of homeless travelling to Hamilton?
Sept. 14
During my term as mayor, I was made aware by staff that Toronto was giving one-way bus tickets to its needy residents. Destination: Hamilton and its social service programs. I recall phoning the then mayor, David Miller, to ask about this practice. He wasn't aware of it, but didn't think it was appropriate if it indeed was happening. He promised to look into it with his staff. We never spoke of it again, but our staff also didn't see the practice recurring.
Makes one wonder about the current situation. Rather than being defensive, city staff and local councillors should take the issue seriously.
Larry Di Ianni, Hamilton
Schools have
telephones, too
Re: Public school block
on social-media platforms
set to roll out soon, Sept. 16
I draw attention to a point in this article where "some parents worry about how they will contact their kids in case of an emergency." If I'm not mistaken, the school has a phone number that can be used to convey such messages. It worked when I went to school. You would be called down to the office and they would deliver the information. Worked in the '60s and it should work now.
Catherine Castellan, Hannon
Blame the game not the player
Re: Sparks fly at Hamilton council over lobbying report, Sept. 12
Our city councillors are not rewarded for addressing city-wide needs. Their election, and re-election, depends on how well they meet the wants of the voters in their wards.
So it is no surprise Coun. Matt Francis would do what he can to preserve parking spaces in Stoney Creek. If that means inhibiting the non-profit Indwell, one of the few organizations addressing the affordable housing crisis in our city, so be it.
Until we change our electoral system to councillors-at-large, with term limits, we will not make the needed progress on the challenges facing us.
Jim Sweetman, Dundas
Kids' toys don't have a gender
I laughed heartily at the "Pickles" cartoon in the Saturday Spec (Re: girl fork vs. man fork). Then immediately had a sober thought of reflection: my grandson's parents are taking great care - as are many parents of today - to downplay the idea of things for girls and things for boys, choosing a more gender-neutral approach. I wish my parents could have done this many years ago and not taken away the toy train I so coveted to give to the neighbour boy for his birthday - as trains were not for girls.
Renate Manthei, Hamilton
Collecting transit fares isn't fair
Re: 'Free' transit isn't really free, Sept. 14
A letter writer referring to Margaret Shkimba's recent plea for free public transit complains that free transit is not really "free" and the costs to operate and maintain bus services should be borne by the fares, or user fees, of those who use the service and not taxpayers.
As a taxpayer, my taxes pay to operate and maintain the roads - upon which the writer probably walks, cycles or drives - that are fare-free! Most of us would be appalled at the idea of road user fees or tolls to travel the city, yet Hamilton makes its youngest, oldest and least able citizens pay exactly that.
Jim Young, Burlington
Stores, services need to be closer
Re: Canadian Tire pitching
redevelopment of its downtown Hamilton store, Sept. 14
I can't help but wonder once again why the city and environmental groups want us to have a walkable community, but don't have everyday stores to run their home in the area that they live in.
While I have no issue with the tower plan, why couldn't the first, and/or second floors if required, be used as an updated Canadian Tire store with the housing units above that space? When I think of the area, there aren't readily available grocery, hardware or everyday essential stores around that people can walk to and buy day-to-day needs for their homes or do it yourself projects.
You cannot keep putting people in highrises and expect them to walk/bike or take the bus for miles to purchase their daily needs. In my opinion, that just reinforces the need for a car.
Mary Hickey, Burlington
Salary nothing
to gripe about
Re: Doug Ford's slap
at Jagmeet Singh reveals
his indifference to his own
MPPs, Sept. 14
Martin Regg Cohn opines Ontario MPPs that make a base salary of $116,550 are having a hard time because of inflation.
Well, how do you think the average worker is surviving on minimum wage? According to recent statistics, there has been an increase of 134 per cent of families using food banks and for every dollar earned people are having to spend $1.68 to survive.
The streets of Ontario cities are saturated with tent encampments of the homeless. While many suffer from mental illness or substance abuse, a large portion of those living on the street actually work but can't afford the high cost of rent for apartments.
Both Liberal and Conservative, federal, provincial and municipal politicians share in the blame of the hopelessness that Canadians feel. Multinational corporations make record profit by gouging consumers with unchecked price increases, while the Bank of Canada blames the average working man for inflation and destroys any chance they may have of owning a house with crippling high interest rates.
If MPP backbenchers are having such a hard time living, they should take the advice given to every average worker who grips about their job: quit. There are plenty of people willing to be trained seals and clap for $116,500.
Robert Brant, Hamilton
Big trucks aren't
a big problem
Re: Mad about rogue big rigs? Now, you can report Hamilton truck route cheaters online, Sept. 16
The new reporting system brought in by the city is just an enhanced snitch line. The safety concerns regarding "Kenworths" is way overblown. The real dangers on our own city streets are not caused by big trucks, but by loud, obnoxious small cars and pickups. And remember folks, if you have "it," it was probably delivered by a truck.
John H. Miller, Hamilton
Trudeau gambling with our lives
Re: Trudeau says Ukraine can strike deep into Russia with NATO arms, Putin hints at war, Sept. 14
Justin Trudeau is gambling with our lives when he endorses Ukraine striking deep inside Russia. Everyone knowledgeable about these weapons understands they are U.S., U.K., French and German systems dependent on satellite targeting, complex communication systems and technicians, none of which are under the control of Ukraine, which is merely a NATO proxy state. A Ukrainian soldier is called in only at the last moment to press the "launch" button.
What Trudeau is actually endorsing are direct U.S./NATO attacks on Russia, which the Russian government views as acts of war and against which it promises to retaliate. Thus, the world is only steps away from a nuclear confrontation that could wipe out all of humanity.
Trudeau should dissociate Canada from this latest NATO escalation, stop funding the war and do what he has foolishly refused to do for more than two years: dial up President Vladimir Putin to discover what it would take to stop the killing before the conflict mushrooms into a nuclear Armageddon.
Ken Stone, Hamilton
Coalition To Stop The War

The city, social services and others say there is no evidence to support his claim. Document 16 of 500

CUPE executive Fred Hahn dismisses controversy over 'antisemitic' video he posted as 'old news'

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/cupe-executive-fred-hahn-dismisses-controversy/docview/3106493321/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=CUPE+executive+Fred+Hahn+dismisses+controversy+over+%27antisemitic%27+video+he+posted+as+%27old+news%27&amp;title=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-09-17&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Embattled union leader Fred Hahn says he has not decided whether to seek re-election at CUPE after its national executive board "lost confidence" in him and sought his resignation over a Facebook video post denounced as antisemitic.
"We'll cross those bridges when we come to them," Hahn told reporters Tuesday, noting the next vote for national vice-presidents of Canada's largest public-sector union isn't until spring 2026.
His comments came 10 days after the CUPE hierarchy conceded it could not oust Hahn as an elected national vice-president over concerns the video violated the union's equality policy and accused him of fuelling "conflict" for refusing to resign.
The former social worker and outspoken critic of Israeli attacks on Gaza expressed dismay at lingering controversy over the video, which depicted a Jewish athlete with a Star of David arm tattoo leaping off a diving board at the Paris Olympics and turning into a bomb that explodes.
The Aug. 11 video post - which Hahn took down seven days later at the urging of CUPE national president Mike Hancock - raised concerns that its content conflates Judaism with military strikes by Israel in Gaza that have killed more than 40,000 Palestinians. The Israeli offensive was launched in the wake of the surprise attack by Hamas last Oct. 7 in which some 1,200 people in Israel were killed and more than 200 hostages were taken.
"It's remarkable that while tens of thousands of people have lost their lives, while we have workers on strike trying to just get some fairness ... we want to still talk about what I happened to post on Facebook," he said.
"This thing that happened a couple of weeks ago, that has gotten tons of attention and so much ink spilt, and so much of your time and attention as media people is ... I hope now old news," he replied when asked if he felt the need to apologize for the video.
"People don't participate in the Olympics on Team Christian or Team Muslim or Team Jewish, they participate from their states. I recognize that people saw that video in a way that it was not intended to be seen, and I took it down," he said.
"What I've heard from our members who are Palestinian is a great deal of pain," he added.
Hahn was appearing at a news conference in the legislature's media studio with New Democrat MPPs Terence Kernaghan (London North Centre) and Jamie West (Sudbury), where they urged Premier Doug Ford's government to pass "anti-scab" legislation that would ban employers from using replacement workers during strikes.
Aside from serving as a national vice-president of CUPE - a union that for years has been highly critical of Israel's treatment of Palestinians - Hahn is the elected president of the union's Ontario division.
The Ontario arm's executive board recently passed motions in favour of Hahn continuing as both a national vice-president and as provincial president, while calling for more training and a "greater awareness around the issues of antisemitism and anti-Palestinian racism."
As a result of the video post and the subsequent accusation from CUPE national's executive board that it violated the union's equality policy, Hahn has received both support and criticism from some CUPE locals, members and outside groups depending on their points of view. Hancock, CUPE's national president, said he considered the video "antisemitic. I felt it was inappropriate."
The equality policy states the union "should neither condone nor tolerate behaviour that undermines the dignity or self-esteem of any individual or creates an intimidating, hostile or offensive environment."
Hahn rejected suggestions he has become a lightning rod or a distraction for the union.
"I don't think that's true at all," he said.
CUPE Ontario is the subject of a human rights complaint filed last November alleging systemic discrimination and promotion of antisemitism that isolates Jewish members.
CAPTION: Fred Hahn, Ontario president of the Canadian Union of Public Employees (CUPE), speaks at a news conference in Toronto on July 16, 2018.

Document 17 of 500

Downtown long-term-care home to close as part of asylum lands deal

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/downtown-long-term-care-home-close-as-part-asylum/docview/3105981252/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Downtown+long-term-care+home+to+close+as+part+of+asylum+lands+deal&amp;title=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-09-17&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: A downtown long-term care home will be turned into affordable housing under a sweeping deal that will transform former hospital lands on the West Mountain brow.
Hamilton Continuing Care at 125 Wentworth St. S. will close when two new long-term care facilities are built on former surplus provincial land that used to house Hamilton Psychiatric Hospital (HPH) on Fennell Avenue West.
The 64 beds at the home, owned by private long-term care operator Schlegel Villages, will be moved to become part of the 512 beds the industry giant plans to accommodate at 290 Fennell Ave. W., near West Fifth Street.
The Hamilton Continuing Care facility that borders the Stinson and St. Clair neighbourhoods will be given by Schlegel to non-profit social housing group Indwell to redevelop.
"There is no intention at all to close that home prior to the move," Kristian Partington, spokesperson for Schlegel Villages, said in a statement. "We have donated the building to Indwell and once the move has been finalized, they will then be able to use the asset to offer more supportive housing in the city's core."
The closure of Hamilton Continuing Care and construction of the two new long-term care homes is still years away. Deadlines as far as eight-and-a-half years out are included in the deal that saw the province transfer the Mountain brow property to Schlegel Villages for about $24.3 million on Aug. 29, as reported exclusively by The Spectator.
Under the agreement, Schlegel has to file a site plan application with the city within two years and obtain building permits within four years. Construction must start within four-and-a-half years, with residents moving into the completed long-term care homes within eight and a half years.
However, Partington says Schlegel hopes to move faster to build the two long-term care homes - that will provide a private room to every resident - than what is set out in the deal that has still not been publicly announced.
"There are many steps to complete before we can go ahead in terms of municipal approvals," stated Partington. "But we are eager to get started as soon as possible."
The long-term care homes are the priority for the 28.8-acre property, with other key parts of the plan given more time to develop in the agreement. This includes expansion of Mohawk College across the street from its Fennell campus by 2044.
Land registry documents previously obtained by The Spectator show a transaction late last month that transfers some brow property for $7.3 million from Schlegel to Mohawk.
"Our hope would be to see construction flow within close timing of each other," stated Partington.
The agreement states that the "Beckfield building" on the site must be redeveloped for college use by Sept. 30, 2032. Building permits for the construction of a new building have to obtained by Sept. 30, 2042, and construction must be done by March 31, 2044.
However, Mohawk College expects to develop the property before those dates.
"The college plans are not anticipated to take 20 years to complete," Mohawk spokesperson Bill Steinburg said in a statement. "A timeline for the college's development of the property will be established as the other portions of the property are developed."
The first step for Mohawk will be to incorporate the property into the campus master planning process over the next two years.
"The future purpose of the property needs to be determined based on growth areas of the college and where that demand exceeds the capacity in the current campus footprint," stated Steinburg. "No matter what is developed, it will be focused on educating students and supporting them in their college education."
Mohawk can't fully revive the expansion plans it had when the Progressive Conservative government backed out of a deal in 2020 to sell the entire property to the college.
"The original campus master plan was developed with a larger property in mind," stated Steinburg. "The recently acquired property is a portion of that space, so the new plan will reprioritize the land use through a revised campus master plan."
The future of the property came into question in 2014 when St. Joseph's Healthcare opened the West 5th Campus, which is a modern mental-health hospital that also provides medical outpatient services and diagnostic imaging.
Located at 100 West 5th St., the new hospital replaced the neighbouring HPH that was built in 1875 as the Hamilton Asylum.
The former Liberal government negotiated a deal in 2018 to sell the surplus provincial land to Mohawk College and use the proceeds to build affordable housing downtown.
But Premier Doug Ford's government wanted to see a long-term care home with at least 256 beds built on the property alongside residential development. To fast-track the long-term care home, a controversial minister's zoning order (MZO) was used, allowing Ontario's minister of municipal affairs and housing to override local zoning.
City council unsuccessfully asked for the MZO to be rescinded in 2021.
The land was put on the market in 2022 and the Ministry of Long-Term Care has been telling The Spectator a deal was in the works since February 2023 without providing any detail. The ministry did not respond to The Spectator's request for comment on the recent sale.
However, land registry documents outline a "campus of care" on the property that includes a retirement home, independent-living seniors' apartments and a related "health hub." The agreement leaves open the opportunity to include resident amenities like stores, hair salons, libraries or a community centre within the sprawling development.
The total value of the multifaceted project is not stated - but a $350-million charge was registered on the property with the province at the same time as the sale.
"The new long-term care villages will become part of a broader continuum of care with retirement suites and independent seniors' apartments built in future phases," stated Partington. "These will include the Schlegel Villages signature Main Street and Town Square and a variety of services and experiences residents and neighbours can access every day of the year."
Partington says it will be similar to The Village of Wentworth Heights at 1620 Upper Wentworth St., near Rymal Road East. The village has a long-term care home with 120 beds as well as a number of other residential options for seniors, from independent apartments to assisted living to retirement suites.
"Between The Village of Wentworth Heights and Hamilton Continuing Care, Schlegel Villages has more than 20 years of deep connection to Hamilton, and this project will serve to strengthen those roots significantly," stated Partington. "There are many moving parts here and there are still many details to be officially finalized, but we are thrilled to be taking the next steps."
The deal effectively gives some natural lands on the property to the City of Hamilton for a $2 fee. The city has previously sought ownership or protection of green space and trails along the brow.
The agreement also allows for Indwell to restore Century Manor for affordable housing - the deteriorating Victorian Gothic manor that was part of the original hospital has been closed to the public since 1995.
In addition, Indwell would be given a second lower-city property to develop for affordable housing.
It is part of the community building that Schlegel's president and CEO says he envisions for the property and beyond.
"For seven decades, our family has been in the service of older adults in seniors housing," James Schlegel said in a statement. "We have always focused on community building and through the wonderful partnerships we have with Indwell and Mohawk College, we'll see an amazing community come to life in a revitalized corner of Hamilton's proud heritage."
Joanna Frketich is a reporter at The Hamilton Spectator. jfrketich@thespec.com
CAPTION: The province transferred surplus land at 290 Fennell Ave. W. to Schlegel Villages for about $24.3 million on Aug. 29.Hamilton Continuing Care at 125 Wentworth St. S. will close when two new long-term care facilities are built on the Mountain brow.

Document 18 of 500

Sept. 17: Ford is putting our progress at risk, Indwell deserves only praise and other letters to the editor

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/sept-17-ford-is-putting-our-progress-at-risk/docview/3105981200/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Sept.+17%3A+Ford+is+putting+our+progress+at+risk%2C+Indwell+deserves+only+praise+and+other+letters+to+the+editor&amp;title=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-09-17&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Ford puts our progress at risk
Re: Alarming ambulance shortages come to an end in Hamilton. How did it happen?, Sept. 11
What wonderful news in the Spec that there's been such a dramatic decrease in Code Zeroes for ambulances and a reduction in ambulance response times. Bravo to everyone that had a hand in making this happen!
Unfortunately, with the announcement from the Doug Ford government of the closing of supervised consumption sites (SCS), my guess is calls for ambulance services will dramatically increase. Will the province be ponying up extra cash and staff to manage the expected increase?
Imagine how much more taxpayers will spend on ambulance services (or die waiting for services) than what would be spent on maintaining SCS!
Matthew Freeland, Hamilton
I can't put trust in our premier
I do remember Premier Doug Ford promising to put alcohol into corner stores. I do not remember him promising to spend, at the minimum, a quarter of a billion dollars to move the start time up one year.
I do not remember Ford promising to reduce health-care funding and work to privatize it as quickly as he can.
Another promise I do not remember hearing was to reduce funding for education. There is much talk about foreign students taking up badly needed residences. The main reason we have so many foreign students is the underfunding of universities and colleges in Ontario. The universities and colleges made up the lack of funding by recruiting foreign students who pay at least twice as much as a local student.
Ford did promise to keep the basic income program going until its trial period ended. He cut that program almost immediately.
Ford did promise the general public that he would never open the Greenbelt up for development. He also promised developers he would open the Greenbelt for development and they would reap billions in profits. He tried to open it up, but public opinion made him stop, for the present time, any development in the Greenbelt. I have no doubt he will find a way/excuse to let the developers have their way.
I, for one, do not trust Premier Ford. He has made and broken so many promises that it is hard to remember whether what he is doing is a promise or a reversal of one of his promises.
Bill Sullivan, Hamilton
What exactly is the problem?
Re: Questioning kaffiyehs in schools, Sept. 13
Public education and politics have always existed together, often uneasily, and cannot be separated without doing a grave disservice to each. Education without any political implications is impossible and politics without much education generally tends away from democracy.
I am left wondering how wearing a kaffiyeh, which represents the struggle for a Palestinian state, is unfair and disrespectful to certain students. Are there students out there who believe that the ideas of self-determination, equity, freedom and state sovereignty are unfair and disrespectful to them? Do they not seek to determine their own selves and destinies, to ensure that justice is done to themselves and, even more, to others, and to be free, which is, as theologian James Cone said, not to do what you want to but to become what you should be? Also, what precisely is triggering about the struggle for statehood?
The wearing of a kaffiyeh is not indoctrination, but simply the bringing of the whole student into conversation with their self and others, the initial, crucial step toward a robust education. The board should be commended for attempting to create an environment that is indeed fair to and respectful of all cultures and identities; such is the nature of public, not private, education.
Joshua Weresch, Hamilton
Sounds like sour grapes
Re: Sparks fly at Hamilton council over lobbying report, Sept. 12
Seriously, Coun. Matt Francis? Attempting to throw shade on the mayor, Graham Cubitt, Invizij Architects and even Indwell, one of only a few non-profits who build affordable homes in our city, over a technical oversight? Sounds like sour grapes and tantrums to me.
David Lloyd, Hamilton
Indwell deserves only praise
I was stunned to see veiled attacks on Indwell, its development director and architect, by Coun. Francis and the lobbying commissioner.
Indwell should receive nothing but praise for its persistent effort and successes in building deeply affordable non-profit homes to get the most needy of our city off the streets.
Of course it needs to frequently deal with city staff - how else will it overcome the many roadblocks in financing, building and renovating the many homes it has delivered across the city?
For the lobbying commissioner to accuse them of "benefiting financially" is a pretzel twist of logic, to say the least. They are demonstrably non-profit. And clearly the Stoney Creek councillor is only concerned about lobbying in this case as an excuse to overturn the decision to build more affordable units in place of parking spaces.
Dave Carson, Dundas
Allegations are misdirected
Whenever I feel depressed about the inability of the city of Hamilton to make any appreciable progress on solving our homelessness crisis, I think about Hamilton is Home to cheer me up. This is an alliance of seven non-profit community housing providers with a goal of building 3,000 new affordable apartments in the next three years to attempt to end homelessness in Hamilton. One of the seven agencies in this alliance is Indwell. They are a Hamilton-based charity that has been helping with homelessness for vulnerable people for 50 years. I am not associated with either one of these agencies; I wish I were, because I think they are at the heart of all that is good in our community.
I was mystified, then, to read in the Spectator that there has been an investigation into Indwell's relationship with the mayor's office. Indwell has responded on its website; check it out for reassurance that these allegations are misdirected. Not only does the city seem unable to solve the homelessness crisis, they seem to be challenging an agency that comes to them with solutions. For my part, when I need to feel good about Hamilton being my home, I will continue to reflect on the work being done by Indwell and Hamilton is Home.
Pat Dickinson, Hamilton
We shouldn't have to tolerate this
Those of us who have chosen to live here in the city of Hamilton understand that there are trade-offs, and sometimes it means putting up with enhanced nuisances such as noise, light and air pollutants. However, it should not mean that we have to tolerate the kind of disorder and delinquency in our public realm and in our private lives that we are experiencing of late. One cannot help but feel sometimes that property crime, vandalism, debris and other expressions of contempt make many of us feel whether there's value in remaining in the city and its neighbourhoods. We've reached the point of experiencing empathy burn out.
Ross Prince, Hamilton
Trump clearly unfit to lead
We will all recall that Donald Trump was elected U.S. president on the basis of a promise to "drain the swamp." Ironically, he has created a swamp of his own, consisting of ever more outrageous lies and conspiracy theories that have sucked his election campaign into a black hole. Migrants eating cats and dogs in Ohio! If there was ever any doubt that this man is unfit for the office of the president, this certainly seals it.
Ralph D'Angelo, Hamilton
Ford is not 'getting it done'
Re: Not much to see at Ford's beer utopia, Sept. 13
With beer now in corner stores and spending a fortune of taxpayers' money to do it, including another fortune in advertising, Doug Ford is boasting he keeps his promises and is "getting it done." Everyone knows that affordability is the main issue facing people these days. So, why is Ford concentrating on booze rather than the enormous affordability problems facing the people of Ontario?
Let's look at Ford's record on being "for the people."
Shortly after coming into office in 2018, Ford abruptly cancelled Ontario's basic income pilot program, only one year into the program, making it impossible to get any data about improving quality of life, including health and employment outcomes, food security and financial well-being. Ford's decision left 4,000 participants out in the cold. It was the same when Ford cancelled sick days. You just can't have the idle poor sitting at home on the couch. Which begs the question: what about the idle rich?
Then Ford guts legal aid, harming the most vulnerable and marginalized people in the province. Revenue shortfalls Ford created himself with tax cuts to the wealthy shows Ford to be penny-wise and dollar-foolish. Ford put people's health and safety at risk, cancelling low-cost wastewater testing for disease. During the pandemic, 4,000 of our most vulnerable people died, mostly in private long-term-care facilities. Then Ford turns around and gives these same-private operators 30-year licences and hundreds more beds while at the same time passing Bill 218, which shielded private owners of long-term-care facilities from being sued for negligence. On Aug. 29, Ford announced "the largest competitive energy procurement" of private power in Ontario's history, while constantly repeating "affordability." Guess what will happen to hydro rates? Affordable? Yeah, right!
Time and again, Ford shows he is very much like Trump and only cares about his rich friends and himself.
Paul Kahnert, Markham
CAPTION: It was recently announced that Hamilton's ambulance availability has been greatly improved. But will this progress be lost once supervised drug consumption sites are closed, a reader wonders.

Document 19 of 500

Byelection results don't just signal the Liberals will lose the next election. They signal a wipeout

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/byelection-results-dont-just-signal-liberals-will/docview/3105980618/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Byelection+results+don%27t+just+signal+the+Liberals+will+lose+the+next+election.+They+signal+a+wipeout&amp;title=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-09-17&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: As of early Tuesday morning, with all polls reporting but at least one recount possible, the NDP has won Winnipeg's Elmwood-Transcona. That long-time NDP seat will stay orange, despite a strong challenge from the Conservatives. Meanwhile, in Montreal's LaSalle-Émard-Verdun, the Bloc has seemingly squeaked out a tiny win over (in something of a surprise) a close Liberal challenge and a more distant NDP candidate in third. While the closer-than-expected finish might serve as something of a balm for Liberals, the top-line result is grim. This is yet another once (mostly) safe seat lost by Justin Trudeau.
The Winnipeg riding staying with the NDP was foretold, and will probably be little remarked upon (the only particularly interesting story there was the growth in the Conservative vote since 2021 - the party is clearly poised to push hard against less-secure NDP holdings come the next election). The Liberals losing LaSalle, though, is a defeat akin to their loss of Toronto-St. Paul's in June. That earlier loss seemed to genuinely shock many Liberals; the defeat in LaSalle, in contrast, had been gently telegraphed as probable by the party for the last two weeks. Whether the sudden shock or the long wait was more painful is up to them to decide for themselves.
Indeed, the only Liberal who had recently expressed any confidence in the party's chances in LaSalle was, in fact, the Liberal leader.Star columnist Althia Raj opened her recent column at the end of the Liberal caucus retreat by noting that the PM was confidently boasting that his party would hold both seats, leaving reporters in Nanaimo baffled by his confident claims of imminent victory.
Another byelection loss forces Justin Trudeau's Liberals to face a hard reality
No such victories occurred. But hey, leaders lead, right? No one should have expected Trudeau to solemnly step up to a microphone and bum out all his party's volunteers by sighing heavily and saying, "Man, we're screwed. Have you seen these polls?" So the PM can be forgiven a bit of rhetorical flourish as he sought to pump up his team.
But only to a point, because past that point, projecting confidence in the face of imminent catastrophe starts to look, sound and feel less like a steady hand on the wheel and more like having slipped on a banana peel through a portal into an alternate reality. And wondering what plane of existence the boss is currently inhabiting is not something that'll leave the troops all fired up and confident, either.
Here in this reality, the situation facing the Liberals is increasingly clear and awfully bleak. All three of the recent byelections have broadly confirmed what opinion polls have been telling us, with only fairly mild variation, since last summer. The Conservatives took St. Paul's, in the heart of Liberal Toronto. Even though the CPC lost in Elmwood-Transcona, they increased their vote share by almost 16 per cent or so, in line with what polls had suggested. And in LaSalle, we got more proof, as if any was needed, that the Liberal polling collapse is real - they're down 16 per cent there. For a party that won the last two general elections on the back of "vote efficiency" - winning a lot of ridings by tiny margins - there just aren't that many places where a 16-point loss can be absorbed.
These byelections, and basically every poll since last summer, are not simply signalling that the Liberals will lose the next election. They're signalling that they'll get mostly wiped out, erasing the rebuilding accomplished by Justin Trudeau over the last decade. Many have written off the Liberal Party of Canada before, and I won't do that again here. But gosh. How many other races like St. Paul's, Elmwood-Transcona and LaSalle can they lose, and by these margins, before the party becomes non-functional? What does the collapse of the Ontario and Quebec Liberal - the federal farm teams - mean for any kind of attempted comeback after that kind of drubbing?
We're told the PM plans to "unleash" himself. That the party thinks that the cure for their woes is more Trudeau. Well, all right then! Hopefully, for their sake, it works soon. The party doesn't have much more time, if it has any at all, for soothing delusions. A massive defeat looms. And strangely, the question isn't whether they can avoid it, but whether they'll even try.
CAPTION: Bloc Quebecois candidate Louis-Philippe Sauve at the party's byelection night party for the riding of LaSalle-Emard-Verdun on Tuesday, Sept.17 in Montreal.

Subject: Elections

Location: Montreal Quebec Canada; Canada

Document 20 of 500

Watchdog probes cost of stocking stores early: Critics say tab could be 3 or 4 times Ford's estimated $225M

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/watchdog-probes-cost-stocking-stores-early/docview/3105628799/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Watchdog+probes+cost+of+stocking+stores+early%3A+Critics+say+tab+could+be+3+or+4+times+Ford%27s+estimated+%24225M&amp;title=The+Spectator&amp;issn=11899417&amp;date=2024-09-17&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.10&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Spectator&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Ontario's fiscal watchdog is investigating the cost of Premier Doug Ford's deal to put beer and wine in convenience stores more than a year early to see if it will give taxpayers a hangover.
The probe by the Financial Accountability Office (FAO) comes at the request of Ontario's Liberals, which have dubbed the alcohol policy change announced last spring "a billion-dollar boozedoggle."
Sales of wine, beer, cider and ready-to-drink cocktails began in about 4,000 convenience stores Sept. 5. Liberal Leader Bonnie Crombie charged Monday that the Progressive Conservative government is "spending potentially more than $1 billion on booze, rather than hiring the nurses, doctors and health-care workers that we need" at a time when 2.5 million Ontarians do not have a family physician.
Ford has maintained the cost of an early end of the "master framework agreement" with the Beer Store - which was to run until the end of next year - is $225 million.
But critics say the tab could be three or four times higher because of an additional $375 million in rebates to the Beer Store, owned by Labatt, Molson and Sleeman, in addition to the cost of a 10 per cent discount for retailers that will buy the beer, wine and other alcoholic beverages from the LCBO. That could cost the provincial liquor monopoly $150 million a year.
Broader alcohol sales will begin at big box stores by the end of October.
Ford shrugged off the FAO probe Monday at a campaign-style stop in Windsor, where he pledged over $9 million to expand a training centre for electricians and autoworkers as Ontario builds more electric vehicle battery plants.
"I think it's a great deal," he told reporters, citing greater convenience to consumers who can also buy wine and beer in many supermarkets in addition to Beer Stores and LCBO outlets.
Ford also stumbled over the timing of the next election, referring to it taking place this year when the next scheduled provincewide vote is not until June 2026.
"I'm sorry, 2024 was a slip," said the premier, who has ruled out an early election this fall but has repeatedly left the door open to calling one next spring.
In a letter to Liberal MPP Stephanie Bowman (Don Valley West), her party's finance critic, the FAO said it will "estimate the financial costs and benefits of accelerated expansion ... and compare these fiscal impacts to a scenario where the province expands alcohol sales" as per the previous plan starting in 2026.
A report from the watchdog agency is expected early next year, providing its investigators receive "timely information from the province."
There is a risk that information considered "commercially sensitive" could impact the results, the agency said.
That means the report could be available if Ford were to call an early election. If Ford were to send voters to the polls early next year it would happen ahead of an expected October 2025 federal vote, that polls suggest could put a cost-cutting Conservative Leader Pierre Poilievre in the prime minister's office.
There are also concerns the RCMP investigation of the Ford government's $8.28-billion Greenbelt land swap scandal could be released right in the regular election cycle.
CAPTION: Alcohol sales began in about 4,000 convenience stores Sept. 5. Liberal Leader Bonnie Crombie said the Ford government is "spending potentially more than$1 billion on booze, rather than hiring the nurses, doctors and health-care workers" we need.Steve RussellToronto Starfile photo

Full text: Ford puts our progress at risk
Re: Alarming ambulance shortages come to an end in Hamilton. How did it happen?, Sept. 11
What wonderful news in the Spec that there's been such a dramatic decrease in Code Zeroes for ambulances and a reduction in ambulance response times. Bravo to everyone that had a hand in making this happen!
Unfortunately, with the announcement from the Doug Ford government of the closing of supervised consumption sites (SCS), my guess is calls for ambulance services will dramatically increase. Will the province be ponying up extra cash and staff to manage the expected increase?
Imagine how much more taxpayers will spend on ambulance services (or die waiting for services) than what would be spent on maintaining SCS!
Matthew Freeland, Hamilton

Re: Sparks fly at Hamilton council over lobbying report, Sept. 12
I was stunned to see veiled attacks on Indwell, its development director and architect, by Coun. Francis and the lobbying commissioner.

Sounds like
sour grapes
Seriously, Coun. Matt Francis?
Attempting to throw shade on the mayor, Graham Cubitt, Invizij Architects and even Indwell, one of only a few non-profits who build affordable homes in our city, over a technical oversight? Sounds like sour grapes and tantrums to me.
David Lloyd, Hamilton
We shouldn't have to tolerate this
Those of us who have chosen to live here in the city of Hamilton understand that there are trade-offs, and sometimes it means putting up with nuisances such as noise, light and air pollutants. However, it should not mean that we have to tolerate the kind of disorder and delinquency in our public realm and in our private lives that we are experiencing of late. What exactly
is the problem?
Re: Questioning kaffiyehs
in schools, Sept. 13
Public education and politics have always existed together, often uneasily, and cannot be separated without doing a grave disservice to each. Education without any political implications is impossible and politics without much education generally tends away from democracy.
I am left wondering how wearing a kaffiyeh, which represents the struggle for a Palestinian state, is unfair and disrespectful to certain students. Are there students who believe the ideas of self-determination, equity, freedom and state sovereignty are unfair and disrespectful to them? Do they not seek to determine their own selves and destinies, to ensure justice is done to themselves and, even more, to others, and to be free, which is, as theologian James Cone said, not to do what you want to but to become what you should be? Also, what precisely is triggering about the struggle for statehood?
The wearing of a kaffiyeh is not indoctrination, but simply the bringing of the whole student into conversation with their self and others, the initial, crucial step toward a robust education. The board should be commended for attempting to create an environment that is fair to and respectful of all cultures and identities; such is the nature of public, not private, education.
Joshua Weresch, Hamilton

Document 22 of 500

Governments are failing climate test

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/governments-are-failing-climate-test/docview/3105628727/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=unknown&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Governments+are+failing+climate+test&amp;title=The+Spectator&amp;issn=11899417&amp;date=2024-09-17&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.8&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Spectator&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: As the world grapples with the escalating threat of climate change, it's shocking to note our governments seem to be getting their priorities backwards.
Instead of focusing on the future and the well-being of the planet, they're mired in short-term thinking and misguided investments.
Rather than investing in renewable energy solutions like windmills and heat pumps and vital public services like health care, our governments are squandering billions on weapons and white-elephant projects like carbon capture and storage.
The Intergovernmental Panel on Climate Change (IPCC) has issued this warning and a challenge.
The warning: The pace and scale of climate action are insufficient to tackle climate change.
The challenge: Cut global greenhouse gas emissions by nearly half by 2030. To do this, we need political commitment, inclusive governance, international co-operation and a sharing of diverse knowledge.
It has stated the choices and actions implemented in the next six years will have impacts now and will reverberate for hundreds, even thousands, of years.
It said we need a rapid and systematic transition away from fossil fuels to limit global warming to 1.5 C above pre-industrial levels.
This transition away from fossil fuels has been defined by the IPCC as a comprehensive energy system transformation, involving rapidly increasing renewable energy sources, phasing out the continued extraction, production and combustion of fossils fuels, improving energy efficiency, and electrifying transportation, industry and buildings.
Although the IPCC includes carbon capture and storage (CCS) for residual emissions from things like cement and steel production and power plants, as part of the transition away from fossil fuels, it is a solution we should not put our hopes on.
CCS is hindered by complex engineering, high costs, a lack of storage facilities, plus it requires energy to operate, which can reduce the overall efficiency of power plants and industrial processes.
Relying on widespread carbon capture deployment, even if it could work, would take too long and be largely responsible for delaying decisive action now to accelerate the urgently needed energy transition to renewable energies.
Considering this, why then on May 7 did Canada's Energy and Natural Resources Minister Jonathan Wilkinson defend carbon capture and storage technology as both effective and affordable when its long track record of failure and its promise is so poor?
It's deeply concerning our government's enthusiasm for carbon capture technology and the continued use of natural gas to heat homes coincides with the oil and gas industry's vigorous lobbying efforts to promote them.
The revolving door between industry executives and government regulators raises serious concerns about the influence of fossil fuel interests on our climate policy.
It appears the government is more interested in protecting the profits of polluters than in protecting the planet.
In Canada, pivotal elections are looming that will determine the political landscape for the remainder of the decade. In those elections, does the guidance of the IPPC and many wise world scientists matter?
Saying yes, and then ignoring that guidance, as Premier Doug Ford did when he overturned the decision by the Ontario Energy Board to continue his love affair with Enbridge Gas, produces a crisis of priorities.
It also undermines public trust, fosters confusion, sows division, amplifies cynicism, threatens the future of the planet and reduces hope for humanity.
We need to see a radical shift now. On Dec. 31, we will have only 60 months remaining until the year 2030.
That is why our looming elections are so important. To have a chance to cut the world's carbon emissions in half, our governments need to address their crisis of priorities now. At this time, the most important thing we citizens can do to help fight climate change is vote for who will.
Robert Hicks is a member
of Seniors for Climate
Action Now (SCAN!). seniorsforclimateactionnow.org
CAPTION:

CREDIT: Robert Hicks Robert Hicks is a member of Seniors for Climate Action Now (SCAN!). seniorsforclimateactionnow.org;

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/downtown-ltc-home-close-as-part-asylum-lands-deal/docview/3105628675/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Downtown+LTC+home+to+close+as+part+of+asylum+lands+deal%3A+Hamilton+Continuing+Care+beds+will+move+to+Mountain+brow&amp;title=The+Spectator&amp;issn=11899417&amp;date=2024-09-17&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.1&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Spectator&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: A downtown long-term-care home will be turned into affordable housing under a sweeping deal that will transform former hospital lands on the west Mountain brow.
Hamilton Continuing Care at 125 Wentworth St. S. will close when two new long-term-care facilities are built on former surplus provincial land that used to house Hamilton Psychiatric Hospital (HPH) on Fennell Avenue West.
The 64 beds at the home, owned by private long-term-care operator Schlegel Villages, will be moved to become part of the 512 beds the industry giant plans to accommodate at 290 Fennell Ave. W., near West Fifth Street.
The Hamilton Continuing Care facility that borders the Stinson and St. Clair neighbourhoods will be given by Schlegelto non-profit social housing group Indwell to redevelop.
"There is no intention at all to close that home prior to the move," Kristian Partington, spokesperson for Schlegel Villages, said in a statement. "We have donated the building to Indwell and once the move has been finalized, they will then be able to use the asset to offer more supportive housing in the city's core."
The closure of Hamilton Continuing Care and construction of the two new long-term-care homes is still years away. Deadlines as far as eight-and-a-half years out are included in the deal that saw the province transfer the Mountain brow property to Schlegel Villages for about $24.3 million on Aug. 29, as reported exclusively by The Spectator.
Under the agreement, Schlegel has to file a site plan application with the city within two years and obtain building permits within four years. Construction must start within four-and-a-half years, with residents moving into the completed long-term care homes within eight and a half years.
However, Partington says Schlegel hopes to move faster to build the two long-term care homes - that will provide a private room to every resident - than what is set out in the deal that has still not been publicly announced.
"There are many steps to complete before we can go ahead in terms of municipal approvals," stated Partington. "But we are eager to get started as soon as possible."
The long-term care homes are the priority for the 28.8-acre property, with other key parts of the plan given more time to develop in the agreement. This includes expansion of Mohawk College across the street from its Fennell campus by 2044.
Land registry documents previously obtained by The Spectator show a transaction late last month that transfers some brow property for $7.3 million from Schlegel to Mohawk.
"Our hope would be to see construction flow within close timing of each other," stated Partington.
The agreement states that the "Beckfield building" on the site must be redeveloped for college use by Sept. 30, 2032. Building permits for the construction of a new building have to obtained by Sept. 30, 2042, and construction must be done by March 31, 2044.
However, Mohawk College expects to develop the property before those dates.
"The college plans are not anticipated to take 20 years to complete," Mohawk spokesperson Bill Steinburg said in a statement. "A timeline for the college's development of the property will be established as the other portions of the property are developed."
The first step for Mohawk will be to incorporate the property into the campus master planning process over the next two years.
"The future purpose of the property needs to be determined based on growth areas of the college and where that demand exceeds the capacity in the current campus footprint," stated Steinburg. "No matter what is developed, it will be focused on educating students and supporting them in their college education."
Mohawk can't fully revive the expansion plans it had when the Progressive Conservative government backed out of a deal in 2020 to sell the entire property to the college.
"The original campus master plan was developed with a larger property in mind," stated Steinburg. "The recently acquired property is a portion of that space, so the new plan will reprioritize the land use through a revised campus master plan."
The future of the property came into question in 2014 when St. Joseph's Healthcare opened the West 5th Campus, which is a modern mental-health hospital that also provides medical outpatient services and diagnostic imaging.
Located at 100 West 5th St., the new hospital replaced the neighbouring HPH that was built in 1875 as the Hamilton Asylum.
The former Liberal government negotiated a deal in 2018 to sell the surplus provincial land to Mohawk College and use the proceeds to build affordable housing downtown.
But Premier Doug Ford's government wanted to see a long-term care home with at least 256 beds built on the property alongside residential development. To fast-track the long-term care home, a controversial minister's zoning order (MZO) was used, allowing Ontario's minister of municipal affairs and housing to override local zoning.
City council unsuccessfully asked for the MZO to be rescinded in 2021.
The land was put on the market in 2022 and the Ministry of Long-Term Care has been telling The Spectator a deal was in the works since February 2023 without providing any detail. The ministry did not respond to The Spectator's request for comment on the recent sale.
However, land registry documents outline a "campus of care" on the property that includes a retirement home, independent-living seniors' apartments and a related "health hub." The agreement leaves open the opportunity to include resident amenities like stores, hair salons, libraries or a community centre within the sprawling development.
The total value of the multifaceted project is not stated - but a $350-million charge was registered on the property with the province at the same time as the sale.
"The new long-term care villages will become part of a broader continuum of care with retirement suites and independent seniors' apartments built in future phases," stated Partington. "These will include the Schlegel Villages signature Main Street and Town Square and a variety of services and experiences residents and neighbours can access every day of the year."
Partington says it will be similar to The Village of Wentworth Heights at 1620 Upper Wentworth St., near Rymal Road East. The village has a long-term care home with 120 beds as well as a number of other residential options for seniors, from independent apartments to assisted living to retirement suites.
"Between The Village of Wentworth Heights and Hamilton Continuing Care, Schlegel Villages has more than 20 years of deep connection to Hamilton, and this project will serve to strengthen those roots significantly," stated Partington. "There are many moving parts here and there are still many details to be officially finalized, but we are thrilled to be taking the next steps."
The deal effectively gives some natural lands on the property to the City of Hamilton for a $2 fee. The city has previously sought ownership or protection of green space and trails along the brow.
The agreement also allows for Indwell to restore Century Manor for affordable housing - the deteriorating Victorian Gothic manor that was part of the original hospital has been closed to the public since 1995.
In addition, Indwell would be given a second lower-city property to develop for affordable housing.
It is part of the community building that Schlegel's president and CEO says he envisions for the property and beyond.
"For seven decades, our family has been in the service of older adults in seniors housing," James Schlegel said in a statement.
"We have always focused on community building and through the wonderful partnerships we have with Indwell and Mohawk College, we'll see an amazing community come to life in a revitalized corner of Hamilton's proud heritage."
Joanna Frketich is a reporter at The Hamilton Spectator. jfrketich@thespec.com
CAPTION: Hamilton Continuing Care at 125 Wentworth St. S. will close when two new long-term-care facilities are built on the Mountain brow, off Fennell Avenue and West Fifth Street.The Hamilton Spectator file photo

CREDIT: Joanna Frketich Reporter Joanna Frketich is a reporter at The Hamilton Spectator. jfrketich@thespec.com

Document 24 of 500

Ontario's fiscal watchdog probes cost of early move to put beer, wine in convenience stores

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/ontarios-fiscal-watchdog-probes-cost-early-move/docview/3106195692/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Ontario%27s+fiscal+watchdog+probes+cost+of+early+move+to+put+beer%2C+wine+in+convenience+stores&amp;title=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-09-16&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Ontario's fiscal watchdog is investigating the cost of Premier Doug Ford's deal to put beer and wine in convenience stores more than a year early to see if it will give taxpayers a hangover.
The probe by the Financial Accountability Office (FAO) comes at the request of Ontario's Liberals, which have dubbed the alcohol policy change announced last spring "a billion-dollar boozedoggle."
Sales of wine, beer, cider and ready-to-drink cocktails began in about 4,000 convenience stores Sept. 5.
Liberal Leader Bonnie Crombie charged Monday that the Progressive Conservative government is "spending potentially more than $1 billion on booze, rather than hiring the nurses, doctors and health-care workers that we need" at a time when 2.5 million Ontarians do not have a family physician.
Ford has maintained the cost of an early end of the "master framework agreement" with the Beer Store - which was to run until the end of next year - is $225 million.
But critics say the tab could be three or four times higher because of an additional $375 million in rebates to the Beer Store, owned by Labatt, Molson and Sleeman, in addition to the cost of a 10 per cent discount for retailers that will buy the beer, wine and other alcoholic beverages from the LCBO. That could cost the provincial liquor monopoly $150 million a year.
Broader alcohol sales will begin at big box stores by the end of October.
Ford shrugged off the FAO probe Monday at a campaign-style stop in Windsor, where he pledged over $9 million to expand a training centre for electricians and autoworkers as Ontario builds more electric vehicle battery plants.
"I think it's a great deal," he told reporters, citing greater convenience to consumers who can also buy wine and beer in many supermarkets in addition to Beer Stores and LCBO outlets.
Ford also stumbled over the timing of the next election, referring to it taking place this year when the next scheduled provincewide vote is not until June 2026.
"I'm sorry, 2024 was a slip," said the premier, who has ruled out an early election this fall but has repeatedly left the door open to calling one next spring.
In a letter to Liberal MPP Stephanie Bowman (Don Valley West), her party's finance critic, the FAO said it will "estimate the financial costs and benefits of accelerated expansion ... and compare these fiscal impacts to a scenario where the province expands alcohol sales" as per the previous plan starting in 2026.
A report from the watchdog agency is expected early next year, providing its investigators receive "timely information from the province." There is a risk that information considered "commercially sensitive" could impact the results, the agency said.
That means the report could be available if Ford - who enjoys a solid lead in the latest Abacus public opinion poll - were to call an early election. If Ford were to send voters to the polls early next year it would happen ahead of an expected October 2025 federal vote, that polls suggest could put a cost-cutting Conservative Leader Pierre Poilievre in the prime minister's office. There are also concerns the RCMP investigation of the Ford government's $8.28-billion Greenbelt land swap scandal could be released right in the regular election cycle.
Ford has hailed the expansion of alcohol sales to convenience stores as a long-awaited fulfilment of a 2018 election promise.
CAPTION: Alcohol sales began in about 4,000 convenience stores Sept. 5.

Document 25 of 500

Justin Trudeau and Pierre Poilievre spar as Parliament returns and one party leader says an election is not far off

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/justin-trudeau-pierre-poilievre-spar-as/docview/3106195671/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Justin+Trudeau+and+Pierre+Poilievre+spar+as+Parliament+returns+and+one+party+leader+says+an+election+is+not+far+off&amp;title=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-09-16&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: OTTAWA-One of the first things many MPs saw when they returned to Parliament Hill on Monday was a large, flapping "F--k Trudeau" flag.
As if they needed a reminder about the mood in Canadian politics.
The flag, one of several, was raised at the familiar anti-establishment demonstration that is often stationed on the sidewalk in front of West Block, where parliamentarians pass on their way to the House of Commons. But the regular protest was larger Monday, totalling perhaps about two dozen people, and boosted by the presence of those who drove to the capital from places like Toronto and Chatham, Ont.
Some sported hats that read "Defund the Politicians" and denounced the Liberal government's online harms legislation. Others demanded to know the names of MPs alleged to have cooperated with foreign powers, with one hollering "Arrest the traitors!" through a megaphone. Still more waved placards with slogans like "No Immigration" and "Free Coutts" - the latter a reference to the so-called "Freedom Convoy" blockade in Alberta that saw two men recently sentenced to more than six years in jail for mischief and possession of firearms.
It was a sure sign that The Show was back in town - this time with the added drama of two significant byelections and a newly vulnerable Liberal minority government that could fall within the coming weeks.
"We are playing chicken with four cars," Bloc Québécois Leader Yves-François Blanchet said, referring to the four main parties in the House of Commons.
"Eventually one will hit another one. There will be a wreckage," he added. "So I'm not certain that this session will last a very long time."
That game of "chicken" is now entirely afoot, as each party tries to position itself most favourably for an election that could come soon but still might not happen for several months, depending on the renewed political jostling in this minority Parliament.
Monday, during question period, Conservative Leader Pierre Poilievre kicked things off by asking Trudeau to call an election. The Conservatives have dominated national polls for more than a year, with projections indicating a Tory majority would be almost certain if an election were held today.
"Why not let Canadians choose a common sense Conservative government?" Poilievre said.
Trudeau responded by stating his government remains focused on working in the current Parliament to pursue policies like more affordable child care, a national school food program, and dental coverage for lower-income Canadians. He also alleged Poilievre is pushing for an election because "he just cares about himself and his own political interest."
But the fate of the Trudeau government, if it tries to hang on without calling an election, lies with the other two main opposition parties: the Bloc and the NDP.
Both have signalled a desire to keep the session going, but each has also said they are ready for a campaign. To prevent that from happening, the Bloc has said it wants the Liberals to support their legislation that would increase pension payments for seniors aged 65 to 74. The Quebec separatist party also wants Ottawa to devolve more powers over immigration to the province, because of concerns about an influx of asylum seekers.
The NDP, meanwhile, has said it wants to advance policies to make housing more affordable, help Indigenous Peoples and fight the climate crisis. On Monday, NDP Leader Jagmeet Singh said he would cut public financing to fossil fuel companies, and suggested any carbon pricing regime should be lighter on consumers who are currently paying a "fuel charge" - and receiving rebate payments - under the existing federal plan.
The Liberals in turn have accused the NDP of caving on climate action, and Trudeau on Monday said Singh's party was running away from making their alliance work.
"As soon as hard things got hard they turned tail and run," Trudeau said in the House.
The Liberals also seem to be taking an aggressive stance on Poilievre. Days after the prime minister derided the Conservative leader as a "liar," Liberal House Leader Karina Gould labelled Poilievre a "fraudster." She suggested it was the right term because of how Poilievre promises to scrap the federal consumer carbon price without mentioning this would also cancel the rebates that go to households where the policy applies.
An Ontario family of four, for example, is slated to get more than $1,100 in rebates this year.
"Our job is to be up front and honest with Canadians. It's to make sure that we are holding (Poilievre) to account to ensure that he faces the proper scrutiny, because as Canadians get closer and closer to an election, he has to answer those tough questions," Gould said.
Residents in two ridings were also voting in byelections Monday, each with different stakes for the parties involved. In Montreal's LaSalle-Émard-Verdun, the governing Liberals were hoping to avoid a fate similar to the June defeat in Toronto-St. Paul's. That loss in a long-held Liberal riding uncorked a wave of discontent over Trudeau's leadership. On Friday, Trudeau told a Montreal radio station that regardless of the result this time, he will stick around as Liberal leader for the next general election.
Singh made the same commitment on Monday when asked about his party's chances in the byelections.
With a file from The Canadian Press
CAPTION: Prime Minister Justin Trudeau makes his way to the House of Commons on Parliament Hill in Ottawa on Sept. 16, 2024. Members of Parliament have returned following their summer break.

Document 26 of 500

Crisis of priorities: Why governments are failing the climate test and us

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/crisis-priorities-why-governments-are-failing/docview/3105447456/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Crisis+of+priorities%3A+Why+governments+are+failing+the+climate+test+and+us&amp;title=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-09-16&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: As the world grapples with the escalating threat of climate change, it's shocking to note our governments seem to be getting their priorities backwards.
Instead of focusing on the future and the well-being of the planet, they're mired in short-term thinking and misguided investments.
Rather than investing in renewable energy solutions like windmills and heat pumps and vital public services like health care, our governments are squandering billions on weapons and white-elephant projects like carbon capture and storage.
The Intergovernmental Panel on Climate Change (IPCC) has issued this warning and a challenge.
The warning: The pace and scale of climate action are insufficient to tackle climate change.The challenge: Cut global greenhouse gas emissions by nearly half by 2030. To do this, we need political commitment, inclusive governance, international co-operation and a sharing of diverse knowledge.
It has stated the choices and actions implemented in the next six years will have impacts now and will reverberate for hundreds, even thousands, of years. It said we need a rapid and systematic transition away from fossil fuels to limit global warming to 1.5 C above pre-industrial levels.
This transition away from fossil fuels has been defined by the IPCC as a comprehensive energy system transformation, involving rapidly increasing renewable energy sources, phasing out the continued extraction, production and combustion of fossils fuels, improving energy efficiency, and electrifying transportation, industry and buildings.
Although the IPCC includes carbon capture and storage (CCS) for residual emissions from things like cement and steel production and power plants, as part of the transition away from fossil fuels, it is a solution we should not put our hopes on. CCS is hindered by complex engineering, high costs, a lack of storage facilities, plus it requires energy to operate, which can reduce the overall efficiency of power plants and industrial processes.
Relying on widespread carbon capture deployment, even if it could work, would take too long and be largely responsible for delaying decisive action now to accelerate the urgently needed energy transition to renewable energies.
Considering this, why then on May 7 did Canada's Energy and Natural Resources Minister Jonathan Wilkinson defend carbon capture and storage technology as both effective and affordable when its long track record of failure and its promise is so poor?
It's deeply concerning our government's enthusiasm for carbon capture technology and the continued use of natural gas to heat homes coincides with the oil and gas industry's vigorous lobbying efforts to promote them. The revolving door between industry executives and government regulators raises serious concerns about the influence of fossil fuel interests on our climate policy.
It appears the government is more interested in protecting the profits of polluters than in protecting the planet.
In Canada, pivotal elections are looming that will determine the political landscape for the remainder of the decade. In those elections, does the guidance of the IPPC and many wise world scientists matter?
Saying yes, and then ignoring that guidance, as Ontario Premier Doug Ford did when he overturned the decision by the Ontario Energy Board to continue his love affair with Enbridge Gas, produces a crisis of priorities. It also undermines public trust, fosters confusion, sows division, amplifies cynicism, threatens the future of the planet and reduces hope for humanity.
We need to see a radical shift now. On Dec. 31, we will have only 60 months remaining until the year 2030.
That is why our looming elections are so important. To have a chance to cut the world's carbon emissions in half, our governments need to address their crisis of priorities now. At this time, the most important thing we citizens can do to help fight climate change is vote for who will.
Robert Hicks and is a member of Seniors for Climate Action Now (SCAN!) www.seniorsforclimateactionnow.org
CAPTION: In May, Canada Energy and Natural Resources Minister Jonathan Wilkinson defended carbon capture and storage technology as effective and affordable despite it having a track record of failure.
CREDIT: Robert Hickscontributing columnist
