Document 37 of 500

Premier shows indifference to his MPPs

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/premier-shows-indifference-his-mpps/docview/3104427833/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=unknown&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Premier+shows+indifference+to+his+MPPs&amp;title=The+Spectator&amp;issn=11899417&amp;date=2024-09-14&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.19&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Spectator&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Greed is the deadliest of the seven deadly sins - so distasteful it distorts our thinking.
So when Doug Ford casually lashed out at Jagmeet Singh the other day, accusing him of greedily lusting after a parliamentary pension, it was a low blow.
Not because it wounded the federal NDP leader. Perched in Ottawa, Singh is probably impervious to the premier's pugnacity. No, Ford's words sounded a death knell for the secret hopes of his long-suffering fellow Tories at Queen's Park who quietly worry about money. Unlike the premier, who was born into wealth and inherited a job for life at the family firm before running for politics, backbenchers from all parties are mindful of their financial insecurities.
Little wonder Ford likes to boast that he's not in it for the money. He's been swimming in it from birth.
"There's nothing more important to these greedy politicians than collecting a pension," the premier proclaimed with righteous wrong-headedness.
"Let's face it, he wants his pension," Ford said dismissively of Singh when asked if the NDP might force an early federal election. "There's him and a whole bunch of other politicians that want to grab the taxpayers' money" by holding out until they qualify for generous pensions.
Allow me to push back against Ford's penchant for pressing our buttons about money-grubbing, greedy politicos. Let me remind the premier that not everyone inherits a motherlode from his father. I've watched politicians up close since I was a cub reporter decades ago. I can tell you that people don't go into public office to get rich.
There are far more lucrative and long-lasting career paths to choose from. Most are motivated by public service or personal ego - or both.
The base salary of an MPP is $116,550 a year, roughly comparable to what many school principals can earn. That salary has been frozen since 2008 after the last Liberal government opted for symbolic sacrifice (Ford's Tories have perpetuated the cold war on salaries ever since).
Know anyone else who's been stuck at zero for 16 years? Adjusted for inflation, that $116,550 is worth the equivalent of $83,400 in 2008 dollars, according to the Bank of Canada - in effect a massive pay cut.
It's not just pay but pensions that are much misunderstood.
PC premier Mike Harris abolished provincial pensions after taking power in 1995, leaving MPPs with little more than a glorified defined contribution savings plan ever since.
Ford has resisted appeals from his own caucus colleagues to bring back a decent pension plan. Which means experienced politicians can spend their prime earning years fighting for improved defined pension benefits for their constituents, only to find themselves without a serious pension of their own when they retire. Or are retired by voters.
That's a major argument for better salaries. Politicians live perilous lives, prone to defeat at any time through no fault of their own if they get caught in a province-wide downturn due to their leader's sagging performance.
They can emerge without a job waiting, without a pension to speak of, without a family firm to return to. Ultimately, that would leave the legislature with only rich folks like Ford who don't need the money - but are too close to the moneyed classes, and too far removed from people in need.
At one level, Ford gets it. Responding earlier this month to the sudden resignation of his popular energy minister, Todd Smith, the premier publicly sympathized with the need to secure his financial future.
"If there's other opportunities to make three, four, five times as much money, take care of his family, God bless him," Ford opined.
That's it? If you want to be well paid, leave public life?
In fairness, cabinet ministers make considerably more than backbenchers. But Smith spent most of his 13 years at Queen's Park out of power and out of pocket, a backbencher with a frozen salary.
All that said, Ford's Tories quietly take care of their own (like Liberals before them) by boosting the salaries of almost all government MPPs, either through appointments as committee chairs or parliamentary assistants.
By contrast, the pay on Parliament Hill is far more generous: MPs now earn $203,100 annually. Far from frozen, their salaries are automatically indexed to inflation, which takes the perennial politics out of the equation.
Previously, MPP salaries were supposed to be pegged at 75 per cent of what their federal counterparts earned; now, it's barely 57 per cent. That growing gap may partly explain why two New Democrat MPPs - Monique Taylor and Joel Harden - have announced they want to leave Queen's Park for Parliament Hill.
Those are the numbers, yet Ford keeps feeding the public perception that MPPs are overpaid. Bad enough that we are a society of envy; we need not stereotype our leaders as greedy. Public service requires an oath of office. It should not demand a vow of poverty.
Martin Regg-Cohn covers provincial politics
and internatioinal affairs for Torstar.
CAPTION:

Document 38 of 500

This Ford only goes in reverse

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/this-ford-only-goes-reverse/docview/3104427585/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=unknown&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=This+Ford+only+goes+in+reverse&amp;title=The+Spectator&amp;issn=11899417&amp;date=2024-09-14&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.19&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Spectator&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: The campaign song for Premier Doug Ford's next election run should really be the Britney Spears' hit "Oops! ... I Did It Again."
Last month the Ford government announced many of Ontario's supervised consumption and treatment sites would close after a study by international medical journal The Lancet showed these harm reduction centres have dropped Toronto's fatal overdose rate by a whopping 67 per cent.
Similarly, Scotland proved by raising the price of booze and making it harder to purchase, their country's alcohol-related hospital admissions dropped 40 per cent and deaths by almost half.
Knowing this, the Ford government just made beer and wine available in convenience stores so it's even easier for everyone to get as well as cheaper to buy.
In this head-scratcher, brewers including Molson and Labatt stand to profit $225 million from taxpayers (some say it could cost as much as a billion) for doing nothing more than ripping up a contract that was going to expire at the end of next year anyway.
The folks at MADD must be furious.
The outrage from health experts means the decision to close the centres, where the sickness of addicts is supervised and helped to treatment, means Ford's decision will likely be reversed.
With the sun and wind the answer to our clean and renewable energy sources, the Ford government immediately cut 758 green energy projects after he was elected in 2018.
You can almost hear the gears grinding into reverse as it did on previous decisions to cut education, francophone schools, autistic funding, legal aid, public service wage cap, dissolving Peel Region and production of those dark-blue licence plates that crooks loved but police couldn't read.
From that $50,000 tricked-out upgrade to his personal recreational vehicle to his sanctioning of "pen hunting," in which hunting dogs run rabbits, coyotes and foxes to ground in large enclosures and then tear them apart, Ford is a walking, talking warehouse of extremely bad ideas.
He backtracked and apologized not once but twice in his attempts to gift our Greenbelt to his development cronies, the same ones who brought lavish gifts for his daughter's wedding. When Ford looks at a farmer's field, he smells hot tar and hears the shudder of a paving machine.
Too much air pollution in southern Ontario? Then let's build another 401-size highway and put more cars on the road.
It's as if these knee-jerk disastrous decisions are being made late at night by a heavy drinker, except "buck-a-beer" Ford does not touch alcohol.
Ontario needs another casino and posh spa like we need coal-burning generating plants and yet here it comes. Our place, Ontario Place, will become "Ford's Emporium" with boutique restaurants and valet parking.
If Ford came up to a light with two clear choices, stop or proceed, he'd hang a quick right and put the car in the ditch.
And health-care funding? I need an MRI and here in Niagara the wait time is 12 to 14 months. Please, Uncle Doug, if I promise to buy several hundred dollars of booze at my local convenience store, could you let me jump the queue?
Ford's idea of solving unemployment is appointing more of his already-rich friends to high-paying government positions.
Looking past political life, Ford may be preparing for a second career ... as an advice columnist.
Wading into the Wasaga Beach defecation scandal he said: "Folks, don't be pooping on the beach. It's as simple as that."
Very sound wisdom. He could have added: "But if you must urinate in public, make sure it's not on the family pet or a downed power line."
A puzzle within a capsule that keeps making 180-degree turns - I just cannot understand Doug Ford. He's not Donald Trump evil or even Pierre Poilievre sly.
He's ... OK, if Ford ever won a gold medal, he'd get so excited, the first thing he'd do is have it bronzed.
William Thomas is a
Niagara-based humour
columnist. He can be reached atwilliamjthomas@gmail.com.
CAPTION: Premier Doug Ford attends a news conference at a convenience store in Toronto, where he discussed his policy to expand availability of alcohol products in corner stores.Chris Young The Canadian Press file photo

CREDIT: William Thomas OPINION;

Subject: Alcohol; Convenience stores

Business indexing term: Subject: Convenience stores

Title: This Ford only goes in reverse

Publication title: The Spectator; Hamilton, Ont.

Document 39 of 500

Justin Trudeau's 'carbon tax' is losing more allies as Pierre Poilievre's relentless assault continues

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/justin-trudeaus-carbon-tax-is-losing-more-allies/docview/3104599565/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Justin+Trudeau%27s+%27carbon+tax%27+is+losing+more+allies+as+Pierre+Poilievre%27s+relentless+assault+continues&amp;title=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-09-13&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: OTTAWA - When it comes to its national carbon pricing regime, the Liberal government is getting lonelier and lonelier.
First it was the opposition New Democrats who raised questions about their support for a policy they had unambiguously championed in the past two federal election campaigns. Earlier this spring, and again this week, NDP Leader Jagmeet Singh suggested his party is looking at other carbon pricing options as it designs a new climate plan for the next campaign. Whatever they come up with, Singh claimed, would not impose a burden on everyday Canadians.
Then, hours later, one of the last pro-consumer carbon price premiers in Canada - British Columbia's David Eby, also a New Democrat - told reporters he's not so keen on the policy anymore. The province has had a consumer price on carbon since 2008, and until recently it wasn't a big political issue.
Until, that is, federal Conservative Leader Pierre Poilievre roared forward to dominate national public opinion polls with a relentless assault on carbon pricing. In B.C., the provincial Conservatives have now shaken off decades of irrelevance to challenge the NDP government in Victoria with an echo of Poilievre's drive to "axe the tax."
Eby told reporters in Vancouver he would ditch B.C.'s existing carbon tax if the federal government removes the requirement - introduced under the federal Liberals in 2018 - for all provinces to maintain a minimum carbon pricing system to drive down greenhouse gas emissions that fuel the climate crisis.
"Two things will happen. One is we'll remove the carbon tax for everyday British Columbians, for the farmers, for the truckers, for the average British Columbian," Eby said.
"The second thing is we believe that climate change is a real and present threat, unlike (B.C. Conservative Leader) John Rustad, who thinks it's a hoax. And so we will continue to ensure ... that the big polluters are paying their fair share."
The rhetoric is similar to that of Singh, who has said climate policy should target heavy industry rather than consumers. And it comes after sustained carbon price opposition from governments from Alberta to Ontario and the Atlantic - including Manitoba's NDP regime under Wab Kinew, and Liberal Premier Andrew Furey's government in Newfoundland.
The Trudeau Liberals have long argued their policy is an essential piece of their arsenal in the fight against climate change, as Canada strives to meet its international pledge to slash greenhouse gas pollution to at least 40 per cent below 2005 levels by 2030.
On Friday, Trudeau told reporters at a news conference near Quebec City that the NDP is "totally caving" to pressure from Poilievre's Conservatives to back away from consumer carbon pricing. He accused the party of walking away from a "world-leading" carbon pricing scheme that returns revenues to households in rebates that get doled out four times per year.
A family of four in Ontario, for example, is set to receive $1,120 in rebates this year.
"I do believe that Jagmeet Singh and the NDP actually do care about the environment," Trudeau said. "It's just increasingly obvious that they have no idea what to do to fight against climate change."
Asked to comment on Trudeau's criticism, a spokesperson for the NDP pointed to a news release on Friday that denounced the Liberal government's carbon pricing system as too soft on "big polluters," like the oil and gas company Suncor. The NDP also accused Trudeau of protecting "big oil profits" instead of "trying to make life easier for everyday families."
The federal pricing regime has two components: the consumer carbon price, and a separate system for industrial polluters that the NDP has long criticized as too lenient.
But it's not only opposition parties and provinces casting doubt on the carbon price. Some federal Liberals have questioned it, and concerns from Atlantic Canadian MPs prompted the government to exempt home heating oil from the federal carbon price last year, while boosting funds for a program to replace that fuel with electric heat pumps.
On The Herle Burly podcast this summer, Liberal MP Marco Mendicino said it may be time for the government to revisit the consumer carbon price.
"I think that is an area that we need to look at. There's no doubt that when we did the carve out for Atlantic Canadians that people really started to scratch their heads and ask, you know, what is the point?" Mendicino said.
"And I know this is a contentious issue, particularly with people who care a lot about fighting climate change and protecting the environment. And it became a lightning rod. And it has become a very difficult issue for the government to navigate."
Mendicino declined Friday to discuss his comments further.
Environmentalists like Caroline Brouillette are concerned that politicians who are dropping their support for carbon pricing are wrongly blaming climate policies for inflation that has fuelled affordability woes. Brouillette pointed to a July report from the International Institute for Sustainable Development that found rising fossil fuel prices accounted for 33 per cent of Canada's overall inflation between February 2021 and June 2022.
"I am worried when I see politicians try to compete about who's most excited to axe the tax," Brouillette said.
In a statement, Mark Zacharias from the climate policy think tank Clean Energy Canada also blamed B.C.'s retreat from consumer carbon pricing on "politicians who misled Canadians" about the policy for political gain.
To Andrew Leach, an economist and law professor at the University of Alberta, politicians backing away from carbon pricing are reacting to widespread challenges with the rising cost of living. But he predicted that - as an economist who sees carbon pricing as the least disruptive way to push society away from greenhouse gas pollution - most observers will regret it if Canada loses its national pricing regime.
He also said it's wrong for politicians to suggest there is a "magical policy" that can combat climate change without impacting the economy.
With files from Tonda MacCharles and The Canadian Press
CAPTION: B.C. Premier David Eby speaks during a campaign event in Vancouver on Sept. 12, 2024.

Document 40 of 500

'I'm not going anywhere': Justin Trudeau says he won't resign if Liberals lose Montreal byelection

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/im-not-going-anywhere-justin-trudeau-says-he-wont/docview/3104599537/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=%27I%27m+not+going+anywhere%27%3A+Justin+Trudeau+says+he+won%27t+resign+if+Liberals+lose+Montreal+byelection&amp;title=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-09-13&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: OTTAWA - A combative Prime Minister Justin Trudeau said Friday he's not going to resign as Liberal leader if his party loses a high-stakes Montreal byelection on Monday, the same day his minority government is slated to return to Parliament without the NDP promising to prop it up.
In an interview on the Montreal radio station CJAD 800, Trudeau said he will stay on regardless of the result in LaSalle-Émard-Verdun, a riding held by the Liberals' former justice minister David Lametti from 2015 until he resigned from federal politics earlier this year.
Asked what it means for him if his party loses the contest, Trudeau said it would show the Liberals simply have more work to do to convince voters to support them.
He also suggested the federal Conservatives under Pierre Poilievre would take actions that harm Canadians if they take power in the next election.
"I'm not going anywhere," Trudeau said. "I've got a fight to lead against people who want to hurt this country, who want to hurt our communities, and who want to take the country in directions that - quite frankly - are exactly the opposite of where the world needs to go."
The byelection in Montreal is one of two set for Monday, with the other contest taking place in Elmwood-Transcona, a Winnipeg riding long held by the New Democratic Party.
It also comes more than two months after Trudeau faced a wave of pressure over his leadership when the Liberal candidate lost to the Conservative in a byelection in Toronto-St. Paul's, which had been a Liberal stronghold. The defeat unleashed calls for Trudeau to resign from Liberals like former environment minister Catherine McKenna and former B.C. Premier Christy Clark, who told the Star she's not ruling out a future campaign for the federal leadership.
New Brunswick MP Wayne Long remains the only sitting Liberal MP to publicly call for Trudeau's resignation, although Toronto MP Nathaniel Erskine-Smith has said the party's members should vote on the prime minister's continued leadership.
Ahead of the Montreal byelection, some Liberals have suggested another loss in long-held Grit territory could put even more pressure on Trudeau.
"If we lose the Montreal one, people are going to be up in arms," one MP told the Star in July.
In the interview Friday, Trudeau expressed excitement about returning to Parliament after the summer break, even without the formal support of the NDP.
Last week, the New Democrats dropped out of an alliance with the Liberals, in which they propped up Trudeau's minority government in exchange for policies like dental care for lower-income Canadians without coverage, public coverage of birth control and diabetes medication costs, and a new law banning replacement workers during strikes or lockouts.
The prime minister also took a direct shot at Poilievre's integrity after the radio station played a clip of the Conservative leader denouncing the federal government's price on carbon by claiming it has "deleted" $25 billion of annual gross domestic product (GDP) and is therefore costing "every family in Canada" $1,400 per year.
Without elaborating, Trudeau said what Poilievre claimed is false and derided him as dishonest.
"There's a convention around parliamentarians that we're not supposed to call each other liars," he said, adding, "Pierre Poilievre is a liar."
Trudeau noted that the federal carbon pricing system includes rebates that return proceeds from the consumer levy to households four times a year. A family of four in Ontario, for instance, will get more than $1,100 in rebates under that system this year.
A spokesperson for Poilievre did not immediately respond to a request for comment.
CAPTION: Prime Minister Justin Trudeau makes an aerospace announcementin Ste.Anne-de-Bellevue, Quebec onSept. 13, 2024.

Document 41 of 500

A jury found Umar Zameer not guilty of murder. His case raises tougher questions about all of us

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/jury-found-umar-zameer-not-guilty-murder-his-case/docview/3104599525/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=A+jury+found+Umar+Zameer+not+guilty+of+murder.+His+case+raises+tougher+questions+about+all+of+us&amp;title=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-09-13&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Doug Ford crossed every line when he condemned the courts for granting bail to an accused cop-killer in a controversial 2021 case.
Letting Umar Zameer out of jail "was beyond comprehension" and "completely unacceptable," according to Ford.
That said, the premier wasn't the only one to say it: John Tory, then Toronto's mayor, uttered similar improprieties and hyperboles.
Let's be honest. In truth, these two top leaders were merely voicing publicly what many Torontonians were likely thinking privately at the time.
Yet we look to our leaders to lead - to do (and say) the right thing in tough times, not just tell people what they want to hear. Today, it's time for a reckoning about them, but also about us - our society's strengths and weaknesses.
In reality, the righteous indignation of the premier and mayor of the day was dead wrong - not merely a misreading of their leadership roles, but a misunderstanding of the case.
In fact, there was good reason to release the suspect on bail, because the case against him was so weak and the flight risk so low (with house arrest and an ankle bracelet). As my colleague Betsy Powell revealed in the Star a week ago, the case was full of holes and ultimately collapsed in court, culminating in a jury acquittal.
What's so compelling about Powell's in-depth analysis is not just the contradictions of the case but the complexities. When the full story came out in court, public opinion shifted dramatically - instead of questioning the suspect's bail conditions, people were doubting police testimony and the entire prosecution.
I won't relitigate the case here, but what's so interesting about Powell's explanatory piece is how it addresses and answers questions of policing and prosecuting. Flowing from that are some societal questions that still remain unanswered.
First, prosecutorial discretion isn't as simple as it sounds.
It's worth noting that even Zameer's lawyer agreed, after a preliminary hearing testing the testimony and evidence, that there was enough to proceed to trial. The defence acknowledged that questions of credibility are up to jurors as triers of fact.
Still, as more details emerged at the full trial, first-degree murder charges looked less and less plausible. Zameer had been getting into his car after a Canada Day celebration with his pregnant wife and child when they were suddenly accosted by a couple of tough-looking plain clothes police officers.
They feared being robbed in an underground garage and escaped in a panic - running over one of the cops. The jury believed them, and seemingly disbelieved the police testimony that Zameer had wilfully killed their colleague.
It was a vindication of sorts for the accused, a verdict that resonated with the public. But it took a trial to clear the air.
Zameer paid a heavy price for that vindication - incarceration for three months before he got bail, years of house arrest, and onerous lawyers' bills. Initially, he faced public vilification and suspicion given his roots as an immigrant from Malaysia, originally from Pakistan.
There can be no doubt that the trial imposed an unfair burden on the accused, but justice isn't about social justice. It's about adjudicating disputes and ascertaining truth.
And yet Zameer had a hideous introduction to Canada so soon after arriving as an immigrant. Was he discriminated against as a brown Muslim with a foreign name and no citizenship?
Initially, almost certainly. Here's another question, at a time when white privilege is so often offered as the sole explanation for everything: Did Zameer ultimately benefit from stereotypes, or more precisely, positive archetypes - not his immigration status but his social status, including education and religious faith?
It could not have escaped the notice of any jury, which is only human, that he was an accountant driving a BMW, accompanied by his pregnant wife in a hijab and his innocent child - images of assiduity, piety and even maternity.
That's not to deny the reality of white privilege, just a reminder that other class and social indicators are also influential (at least in multicultural Toronto).
Zameer had the misfortune to stumble into a perfect storm - a plain clothes operation gone awry, a police death, and a controversial prosecution. In truth, that fate could befall any of us.
The test of law and order is not that it achieves perfection, but that it strives for a just resolution. Our premier and mayor behaved badly (Tory later apologized), the police chief spoke badly, and the front-line cops may have testified wrongly.
But the courts handled the cases dispassionately and the jury acted wisely, with full credit to Zameer's defence team. Toronto police Chief Myron Demkiw later asked the OPP to conduct an independent review into the officers' testimony, their procedures and training - notably in plain clothes operations.
Ultimately, Zameer was given the benefit of the doubt - not just in the law courts but the court of public opinion - and a fundraising campaign has raised hundreds of thousands of dollars to defray the cost of his defence. As for the prosecutors, they got a bum rap in the end - a trial seems to have been the correct course - but that's part of the job description in our judicial system.
CAPTION: Umar Zameer and his lawyers walk away from Toronto courthouse following his not-guilty verdict on April 21, 2024.

National briefs

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/national-briefs/docview/3103746087/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=National+briefs&amp;title=The+Spectator&amp;issn=11899417&amp;date=2024-09-13&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.6&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Spectator&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Nova Scotia
Domestic violence an epidemic: NDP
Nova Scotia's opposition New Democrats have tabled legislation to declare domestic violence an epidemic in the province. Leader Claudia Chender says the bill would legislate a recommendation to all levels of government from the commission of inquiry that investigated the 2020 Nova Scotia mass shooting. Chender says designating intimate partner violence as an epidemic would underscore the seriousness of the issue.
Ontario
More Science Centre layoffs coming
More workers at the Ontario Science Centre are set to lose their jobs as the abruptly shuttered attraction contemplates a move to a temporary home. Dexterra Group, which provided cleaning services for the attraction, sent a letter to the Ontario Public Service Employees Union last week, saying 28 employees will lose their jobs by Oct. 31.
Quebec
How merchants can ask for tips to be regulated
Quebec wants to curb excessive tipping in legislation the government says will help people save money. Simon Jolin-Barrette, minister responsible for consumer protection, tabled a bill Thursday that would regulate how merchants calculate suggested tips and how grocery stores display food prices. The bill would force businesses to calculate tips based on the price before tax.

Document 43 of 500

Not much to see at Ford's beer utopia

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/not-much-see-at-fords-beer-utopia/docview/3103745964/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=unknown&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Not+much+to+see+at+Ford%27s+beer+utopia&amp;title=The+Spectator&amp;issn=11899417&amp;date=2024-09-13&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.10&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Spectator&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: It's a Saturday night at Stanley's Variety, an unassuming bodega wedged between Victorian row houses on Gerrard Street East, near Jarvis in Toronto. It's 9:30 p.m., but the store is devoid of customers, save for a taxi driver buying lottery tickets.
Near the store's entrance is the only thing marking this night as different from any other, a small fridge stocked with beer; cans, bottles, mostly from Molson Coors. The clerk tells me he hasn't sold any yet. Clerks at two other variety stores on Jarvis tell me they're still waiting on full shipments - presumably from the LCBO - before selling to the public.
"When do you think it might get here?" I ask one of them.
"I don't know," he says, waving his hand lazily from behind the counter. "Probably later."
New provincial regulations kicked in recently allowing more than 4,000 convenience and grocery stores across Ontario to sell beer, wine, cider and pre-mixed alcoholic drinks. It hasn't been a politically smooth transition. Doug Ford's government is spending hundreds of millions of dollars to make it not just happen, but happen this year, before an existing contract with the Beer Store runs out at the end of 2025.
I wanted to understand the urgency, to get sense for all the pent-up demand driving this sudden change. So last weekend, I spent two nights touring convenience stores to check out the stock and quiz their clerks.
What did I find? Not much.
After wandering around corner stores downtown, I took the streetcar to Humewood, a mostly residential neighbourhood centred around Cedarvale Park. There, it was a similar story. One variety store was waiting on a shipment, although the clerk directed me to a handful of six-packs on a shelf at the back of the store beside the toilet paper. A variety store across the street had assembled a DIY beer cabinet in one of the aisles with plywood framing and two padlocks. But that clerk, too, told me sales have been minimal.
The only place I encountered that actually seemed to be moving product was a 7-Eleven at College and Spadina, on the border of the University of Toronto campus. Within 10 minutes of my arrival, around 11 p.m., I saw three groups of student-age customers schlepping six-packs of Coors Lite that a clerk had plucked from inside a locked fridge between the cat food and chips aisles. The clerk told me that, so far, booze sales had been unremarkable during the daytime, but were ramping up somewhat after the LCBOs close. During the time I was there, it still seemed like most customers were making conventional late-night purchases: Slurpees, Twizzlers, two-litre bottles of Coke.
It is, of course, early days for the rollout. Maybe in six months things will be different: Ford would have us believe that a sizable chunk of Ontario's population has been clamouring for the option to purchase alcohol at their local corner store at all hours of the day, perhaps while picking up cigarettes and some peanut butter. But browsing the dusty aisles of the city's variety stores, pacing past three cases of Molson wedged in a back fridge between cartons of milk, it was hard for me to imagine this taxpayer-funded, $225-million (at least) rollout was worth it for the small business "growth opportunities" it purports to support.
Supposedly, one of the stipulations of this more permissive licensing is that at least 20 per cent of the beer, cider, wine and ready-to-drink beverages on display in grocery and convenience stores must be produced by craft producers. But in my - admittedly limited - tour of various variety stores, I didn't see any craft beer. The stock I saw was dominated by Molson Coors products. Maybe, as my pal on Jarvis Street claims, the craft beer is coming "later."
Nothing I found on my after-dark variety store tour helped me understand why this had to happen now, why the government couldn't have just pushed this transition down the road a year and saved the public a heap of cash. Ford says it's about supporting local consumers and small businesses. But much like the Coors Lite next to the toilet paper, I'm not buying it.
For now, it looks like the government spent all that cash just to make sure some university students can get a six-pack after dark.
Lana Hall is a Toronto-based journalist who writes about urban affairs.
CAPTION:

CREDIT: Lana Hall;

Document 44 of 500

'Removed from our shelves': Concerns over mould in margarine sold through Loblaws, No Frills, Shoppers Drug Mart, Real Canadian Superstore and Zehrs plus vegan chocolate and more recalls trigger Health Canada warnings to shoppers

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/removed-our-shelves-concerns-over-mould-margarine/docview/3104160592/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=%27Removed+from+our+shelves%27%3A+Concerns+over+mould+in+margarine+sold+through+Loblaws%2C+No+Frills%2C+Shoppers+Drug+Mart%2C+Real+Canadian+Superstore+and+Zehrs+plus+vegan+chocolate+and+more+recalls+trigger+Health+Canada+warnings+to+shoppers&amp;title=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-09-12&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Several items have been recalled by Health Canada over the past week due to a number of health and safety concerns.
PC Blue Menu brand original margarine and margarine with olive oil
Health Canada says these products have been recalled over concerns of mould. Consumers are advised "not to use, sell, serve or distribute the affected products."
Loblaw said the products are no longer being sold at its locations nationwide.
"Food safety and quality assurance are fundamental to our business," a spokesperson from Loblaw said.
"The PC Blue Menu brand Original Margarine and Margarine with Olive Oil recall impacted Loblaw grocery (i.e. Real Canadian Superstore, No Frills, Zehrs) and Shoppers Drug Mart stores nationally, and these products have since been removed from our shelves."
Ceiling hoists, field spray and vegan chocolate sold through Amazon
A number of products sold through Amazon have been recalled over the past week.
"Our Product Safety Team proactively investigates and addresses reported safety complaints and incidents to ensure customer protection from potential product-related safety risks," Amazon says on its website.
"We closely monitor public recall alert websites and receive notifications from vendors and sellers. Upon discovering a product recall, we immediately halt the affected product offerings and promptly inform both the customers and sellers involved about the recall."
Delta Cycle ceiling hoists with straps have been recalled over a potential injury hazard.
"The plastic clip used to hold larger items may fail, causing the larger items to fall and presenting an injury hazard," said Health Canada in its recall notice. "Use of this product without the use of straps (ie. just a bicycle) does not present a hazard."
According to Health Canada, the company has received no reports of incidents or injuries in Canada while there have been four reported incidents, one of which involved a minor injury in the United States as of Aug. 7.
The recall was also posted on The Home Depot's website.
"These straps are used in storing a kayak, canoe, ladder or other larger items," Delta Cycle said in its recall.
"If you are not using the straps and only using the hooks, no further action is required."
For more information, including how to get replacement straps from Delta Cycle, consumers can contact the company by phone at 1-508-698-0606, Monday to Friday, 9 a.m.-5 p.m. EST, by email at hoiststraprecall@deltacycle.com or visit the company website.
Dead Down Wind odour eliminating field spray has also been recalled due to improper labelling.
The recalled products do not have the proper consumer chemical labelling as required by the Consumer Chemicals and Containers Regulations, 2001, under the Canada Consumer Product Safety Act, Health Canada said in its recall.
"The lack of appropriate labelling information could result in unintentional exposure to the products and lead to serious illness or injury."
Consumers are advised to immediately stop using the product and dispose of it according to municipal hazardous waste guidelines.
Arcus Hunting, LLC can be reached by telephone at 1-678-342-9000, Monday to Friday, 8 a.m. - 5 p.m. EST for more information about the recall.
Nutrabolics brand Feed Me vegan real food protein & oats bars (chocolate coconut flavour) have also been recalled because of undeclared milk in the product, prompting a warning to not consume the product if you are allergic or sensitive to milk.
Health Canada advises those who may be affected by consuming the product to throw it out or return it to the location where it was purchased.
Unauthorized sexual enhancement products
A wide range of sexual enhancement products have been recalled due to concerns they contain ingredients that may pose "serious health risks."
The full list of products can be found here.
Health Canada says some of the products have been found to include ingredients such as sildenafil and/or tadalafil, prescription drugs used to treat erectile disfunction but may cause "potentially life-threatening low blood pressure."
The agency also says other recalled products were found to include levodopa - a prescription drug is combined with other drug ingredients in anti-Parkinson's medications - and/or prasterone - recognized as a controlled substance in Canada and illegal to sell without proper authorization from Health Canada.
"Health Canada is advising about unauthorized sexual enhancement products that may pose serious health risks," the agency said in its recall.
"The products are labelled to contain or have been tested and found to contain dangerous ingredients."
The products were sold out of a number of convenience stores and adult shops in Mississauga, Markham, Whitby, Woodbridge, Niagara Falls, Toronto, Etobicoke, North York, Kitchener, Vaughan and St. Catharines.
CAPTION: PC Blue Menu brand original margarine and margarine with olive oil have been recalled.Delta Cycle ceiling hoists with straps have been recalled.Dead Down Wind odour eliminating field spray has been recalled.Nutrabolics brand Feed Me vegan real food protein & oats bars (chocolate coconut flavour) have been recalled.PC Blue Menu brand original brand margarine and margarine with olive oil- sold out of Loblaws and other stores under the Loblaw banner- have been recalled over concerns of mould.

Document 45 of 500

Doug Ford pledges jobs for uprooted Ontario Science Centre workers

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/doug-ford-pledges-jobs-uprooted-ontario-science/docview/3104160561/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Doug+Ford+pledges+jobs+for+uprooted+Ontario+Science+Centre+workers&amp;title=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-09-12&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Premier Doug Ford is promising no one working at the shuttered Ontario Science Centre will be left unemployed as the museum is moved to a new location.
"We'll find jobs for those people within the system, so we'll help them out," Ford told reporters Thursday in Toronto.
"I don't like anyone ever losing their jobs," he said.
Ford's comments came after The Canadian Press reported Dexterra Group, which provided the museum's cleaning services, informed the Ontario Public Service Employees Union that 28 employees will lose their jobs by Oct. 31.
"They're devastated," said Martin Fischer, president of OPSEU Local 549, which represents 500 science centre workers.
"With the plans for science centre to continue at a new location, they really wanted to be part of that," said Fischer.
In June, the Don Mills facility was abruptly closed after an engineering report warned the aerated concrete used in the roof could collapse.
Opened in 1969, the science centre was due to be moved to Ontario Place in 2028 as part of the controversial redevelopment of that waterfront park, which includes a privately owned Therme water spa.
Ford's political opponents claimed he wanted the 55-year-old building to become unusable so he could more easily relocate the science centre.
Critics have said the heritage building, designed by Canadian architect Raymond Moriyama, could and should be salvaged.
Green Leader Mike Schreiner said last week the "science centre closure was a blatantly political move orchestrated by the Ford government alone."
"Other buildings - like the ROM or the CN Tower - are properly repaired and restored because we recognize their historical, cultural and educational value. Why not this building in this community?" said Schreiner.
A temporary home for the science centre is not expected to open until January 2026.
Ford said Thursday that no interim site has yet been chosen amid reports a Mississauga convention centre could serve as its home for two years.
"What I want to do is listen to other options, because I know Toronto has options. Mississauga does and everyone else," he said.
"I just want to get a new one built as quickly as possible."
The Don Mills Road property is owned by the city and was leased to the province for a century in 1964.
But city hall - already struggling to maintain the recreational facilities it now operates, such as parks that are increasingly sites of homeless tent encampments - has said it could not afford to operate a museum there.
With files from The Canadian Press
CAPTION: The Ontario Science Centre was closed in June after a report showed some concrete roofing panels were deteriorating and needed to be replaced.

Subject: Science

Title: Doug Ford pledges jobs for uprooted Ontario Science Centre workers

Document 46 of 500

Sept. 12: Ford should focus on local issues, what's really behind CTS closures and other letters to the editor

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/sept-12-ford-should-focus-on-local-issues-whats/docview/3103433103/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Sept.+12%3A+Ford+should+focus+on+local+issues%2C+what%27s+really+behind+CTS+closures+and+other+letters+to+the+editor&amp;title=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-09-12&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Ford should focus on local issues
Re: Doug Ford says Jagmeet Singh is a 'greedy politician' who won't topple Trudeau government, Sept. 9
Premier Doug Ford calling the leader of the federal New Democratic Party, Jagmeet Singh, "a greedy politician" was a supreme example of the pot calling the kettle black. I believe Premier Ford should focus on the provincial issues needing the attention, such as homelessness, health care and education.
Theresa Flynn-Purchase, Hamilton
Thanks for the interesting stories
Thank you for "Remembering Jennifer, my stillborn sister" (Sept. 7) and also the three-page article by Matt Van Dongen on how Montreal has developed ideas on addressing some homelessness issues in a most incredible way (First right of refusal: A way for cities to fight back against gentrification and keep housing affordable, Sept. 5). This is why I still read my Spectator daily and sometimes the next day in case I've missed many more articles. They make me realize what good people there are out there.
Jane Evans, Hamilton
Learn about the issues before voting
Mark Bulgutch's opinion piece "Being Tired of Trudeau ..." (Sept. 8) was a timely piece. I couldn't have said it any better and I agree it's important to cast our votes with intention and thoughtfulness. A quote by (journalist) Tony Snow reminds us "voting is a right best exercised by people who have taken time to learn about the issues." But more importantly, let's "be careful for what you wish for."
Rocco Gizzarelli, Hamilton
Ulterior motive behind closure?
What is the real reason the Ford government wants to close supervised consumption sites? Does he have friends who plan to open "clinics" to help our residents who use drugs and other substances, which will be paid for by our taxpayer dollars? Will they cost more than the supervised sites we presently have? Probably.
Jackie Oke, Dundas
No sign of unhoused in Ottawa
Re: Do something about homelessness, Sept. 10
I agree with Bernadette Flis. While my friend and I were driving home from a fishing trip in pristine northern Quebec on the weekend, we drove through Ottawa. What a beautiful, historical, clean and decorated city. A city with minimal road construction disorder, no trash on the streets, a beautifully landscaped downtown and absolutely no sign of tent encampments for the unhoused.
Albeit we followed a route through downtown skirting many federal properties with no sign of what many other cities in Canada are experiencing regarding the homelessness issue. Did we miss it or is Ottawa exempt from the plight of so many who are less fortunate? If our view was real, then what is the secret? It needs to be shared with the rest of many Canadian cities.
Sheldon Frank, Grimsby
Can't we learn from others?
I recently returned from a trip in Europe, visiting the beautiful Scandinavian countries and not once did I see an unhoused person. Not one tent, no begging, just a beautiful expanse of well-run, clean cities with absolutely beautiful parks and downtown areas, areas one can walk through without fear. These cities put our Canadian cities to shame!
Our once beautiful parks like Gage Park, Bayfront and others have become scattered with tents - an ever-increasing disgust. Our downtown area has become a war zone with shootings and rampant drug use, major businesses pulling out of the downtown core, afraid for their employees to walk around once they park their car.
Something has to be done. Maybe look and see what Europe or other communities closer to home have done. Is it an impossible task, maybe, but you don't know unless you try.
Linda Ferrie, Waterdown
Happy to have surprise visit
The other day, I heard an unexpected knock at my door. Figuring I must have forgotten about a parcel delivery, I opened it. Instead there was my MPP, Sarah Jama, asking if there was anything she could help with. I've never been approached directly by a sitting politician outside of an election campaign and we had a great conversation about fixing Ontario's broken health-care system and Hamilton's air quality. Apparently she's been active at Queen's Park on those and other topics and the media never bothered covering it - she's got my vote, I know that much.
Pat Antila, Hamilton
CAPTION: "Premier Doug Ford calling the leader of the federal New Democratic Party, Jagmeet Singh, 'a greedy politician' was a supreme example of the pot calling the kettle black," a reader writes.

Document 47 of 500

Doug Ford's slap at Jagmeet Singh reveals his indifference to his own MPPs

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/doug-fords-slap-at-jagmeet-singh-reveals-his/docview/3103433077/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Doug+Ford%27s+slap+at+Jagmeet+Singh+reveals+his+indifference+to+his+own+MPPs&amp;title=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-09-12&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Greed is the deadliest of the seven deadly sins - so distasteful it distorts our thinking.
So when Doug Ford casually lashed out at Jagmeet Singh the other day, accusing him of greedily lusting after a parliamentary pension, it was a low blow.
Not because it wounded the federal NDP leader. Perched in Ottawa, Singh is probably impervious to the premier's pugnacity.
No, Ford's words sounded a death knell for the secret hopes of his long suffering fellow Tories at Queen's Park who quietly worry about money. Unlike the premier, who was born into wealth and inherited a job for life at the family firm before running for politics, backbenchers from all parties are mindful of their financial insecurities.
Little wonder Ford likes to boast that he's not in it for the money. He's been swimming in it from birth.
"There's nothing more important to these greedy politicians than collecting a pension," the premier proclaimed with righteous wrong-headedness this week.
"Let's face it, he wants his pension," Ford said dismissively of Singh, when asked if the NDP might force an early federal election. "There's him and a whole bunch of other politicians that want to grab the taxpayers' money" by holding out until they qualify for generous pensions.
Allow me to push back against Ford's penchant for pressing our buttons about money-grubbing, greedy politicos. Let me remind the premier that not everyone inherits a motherlode from his father.
I've watched politicians up close since I was a cub reporter decades ago. Based on my own unscientific survey, I can tell you that people don't go into public office to get rich.
There are far more lucrative and long-lasting career paths to choose from. Most are motivated by public service or personal ego - or both.
The base salary of an MPP is $116,550 a year, roughly comparable to what many school principals can earn. That salary has been frozen since 2008, after the last Liberal government opted for symbolic sacrifice (Ford's Tories have perpetuated the cold war on salaries ever since).
Know anyone else who's been stuck at zero for 16 years? Adjusted for inflation, that $116,550 is worth the equivalent of $83,400 in 2008 dollars, according to the Bank of Canada - in effect a massive pay cut.
It's not just pay but pensions that are much misunderstood. PC premier Mike Harris abolished provincial pensions after taking power in 1995, leaving MPPs with little more than a glorified defined contribution savings plan ever since.
Ford has resisted appeals from his own caucus colleagues to bring back a decent pension plan. Which means experienced politicians can spend their prime earning years fighting for improved defined pension benefits for their constituents, only to find themselves without a serious pension of their own when they retire.
Or are retired by voters.
That's a major argument for better salaries. Politicians live perilous lives, prone to defeat at any time through no fault of their own if they get caught in a province-wide downturn due to their leader's sagging performance.
They can emerge without a job waiting, without a pension to speak of, without a family firm to return to. Ultimately, that would leave the legislature with only rich folks like Ford who don't need the money - but are too close to the moneyed classes, and too far removed from people in need.
At one level, Ford gets it. Responding earlier this month to the sudden resignation of his popular energy minister, Todd Smith, the premier publicly sympathized with the need to secure his financial future.
"If there's other opportunities to make three, four, five times as much money, take care of his family, God bless him," Ford opined.
That's it? If you want to be well paid, leave public life?
In fairness, cabinet ministers make considerably more than backbenchers - nearly $166,000 a year. But Smith spent most of his 13 years at Queen's Park out of power and out of pocket, a backbencher with a frozen salary.
All that said, Ford's Tories quietly take care of their own (like Liberals before them) by boosting the salaries of almost all government MPPs, either through appointments as committee chairs or parliamentary assistants. That can be worth as much as $16,700 more a year.
By contrast, the pay on Parliament Hill is far more generous: MPs now earn $203,100 annually. Far from frozen, their salaries are automatically indexed to inflation, which takes the perennial politics out of the equation.
Previously, MPP salaries were supposed to be pegged at 75 per cent of what their federal counterparts earned; now, it's barely 57 per cent. That growing gap may partly explain why two New Democrat MPPs - Monique Taylor and Joel Harden - have announced they want to leave Queen's Park for sunnier climes and wealthier times on Parliament Hill.
Those are the numbers, yet Ford keeps feeding the public perception that MPPs are overpaid. Bad enough that we are a society of envy; we need not stereotype our leaders as greedy.
Public service requires an oath of office. It should not demand a vow of poverty on pensions.
CAPTION: Todd Smith, then Ontario's energy minister, attends an announcement with Premier Doug Ford in Belleville, Ont. on March 1, 2024.

Subject: Pensions; Family owned businesses; Politics; Politicians; Wages & salaries

Business indexing term: Subject: Pensions Family owned businesses Wages & salaries

People: Singh, Jagmeet

What a beautiful, historical, clean and decorated city. A city with minimal road construction disorder, no trash on the streets, a beautifully landscaped downtown and absolutely no sign of tent encampments for the unhoused.

Can't we learn from others?

Not one tent, no begging, just a beautiful expanse of well-run, clean cities with absolutely beautiful parks and downtown areas, areas one can walk through without fear. These cities put our Canadian cities to shame!
Our once beautiful parks like Gage Park, Bayfront and others have become scattered with tents - an ever-increasing disgust. Our downtown area has become a war zone with shootings and rampant drug use, major businesses pulling out of the downtown core, afraid for their employees to walk around once they park their car.

Linda Ferrie, Waterdown

Document 49 of 500

Doug Ford says child protection system 'failed' girl whose body was found in Toronto dumpster

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/doug-ford-says-child-protection-system-failed/docview/3103594352/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Doug+Ford+says+child+protection+system+%27failed%27+girl+whose+body+was+found+in+Toronto+dumpster&amp;title=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-09-11&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Ontario's child protection system "failed" a four-year-old girl named Neveah whose remains were found in a Rosedale dumpster about a year after her mysterious death, Premier Doug Ford acknowledges.
While it's up to Ontario's chief coroner to call an inquest into the case - as child welfare advocates have been urging - Ford said he is open to a broader examination of what went wrong after Neveah was returned to her mother by a court order following time in the care of Children's Aid.
"We've got to make sure it never happens again, and no matter if it takes a wider scope to look at it, we need to do everything we can," the premier told reporters Wednesday in Vaughan, where he announced $26 million for new facilities at LiUNA 183's skilled trades training campus.
"I believe the system failed her," Ford added. "It's an absolute tragedy."
Although Neveah's remains were found in 2022, a recent Star investigation that uncovered new details about her death has raised troubling questions about how she died in 2021 and how a child who had been under protection could end up dead in a dumpster - with no one looking for her in the intervening months or held responsible for her death.
"It's heartbreaking," said Ford.
As the Star's Wendy Gillis and Jennifer Pagliaro reported last month, there was no missing person's report for Neveah, whose name was variation of "heaven" spelled backwards. Her last name is subject to a court-ordered publication ban to prevent identifying members of her family.
Once her decomposed remains were found wrapped in blankets by a contractor in a posh neighbourhood, Toronto police began efforts to discover her identity, eventually relying on advanced DNA testing and a lucky tip.
In a rare public court record from a Child, Youth and Family Services Act hearing held in June to consider the future of Neveah's younger siblings, it was revealed her mother, who has an extensive history with child protection agencies and substance abuse challenges, had likely hidden the pregnancy and Neveah had limited prenatal care.
Toxicology reports at her birth revealed marijuana, prompting the York Region Children's Aid Society to obtain a protection order that placed the newborn into the care of a foster family at just five days old. The court records show the mother's six children, at various points in their lives, have been taken into care by child protective services in York and Peel regions and Toronto.
While with her foster parents, Neveah was diagnosed with developmental delays and deemed non-verbal at the age of two and was diagnosed with autism spectrum disorder before her third birthday.
After Neveah's mother gave birth to boy around her second birthday, he was placed in the care of the same foster family at the age of five months over concerns about the mother's alcohol and drug use.
Then in March 2020 - as the world was locking down for the COVID-19 pandemic - came a re-evaluation of their protection status that resulted in Neveah and her younger brother placed back in the care of their mother but under supervision of child welfare authorities.
Four months after that, the York Region Children's Aid Society flagged concerns the mother had broken the supervision agreement by moving to a new residence in Toronto. The case was transferred to the Toronto Children's Aid Society. Six months later, the mother and Toronto CAS requested the courts end the supervision order for Neveah and her younger brother, a sign concerns about the children's care had eased. They had been registered to begin attending a downtown daycare centre.
The supervision order was ended on March 31, 2021 but the two children never showed up for daycare as scheduled the next day - or at all. Neveah vanished in June.
Irwin Elman, who served as Ontario's child advocate from 2008 to 2019, when the position was eliminated by the Ford government, told the Star last month there are many unanswered questions as to what went wrong and how Neveah fell through the cracks of the child protection system.
"We must learn about where those errors in judgment lay and where the mistakes happened."
New Democrat MPP Monique Taylor (Hamilton Mountain) said the death of any child who has been in care should automatically trigger a coroner's inquest, and Neveah's case screams for one.
"It's unacceptable that nobody knew this child was missing," said Taylor, her party's longtime children's services critic. "There's no 'I'm sorrys,' there's no 'we made a mistake.' It's completely unacceptable."
Ontario's chief coroner, Dirk Huyer, recently told the Star it's too soon to call an inquest into Neveah's death because a police investigation into the case is continuing.
A coroner's inquest is a quasi-judicial hearing that probes the circumstances of a death, determines how it happened and presents recommendations to prevent similar tragedies but does not assign blame.
CAPTION: Premier Doug Ford attends the US-Canada Summit in Toronto on June 11, 2024.

Document 50 of 500

TIFF won't pull controversial 'Russians At War' documentary, despite protests

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/tiff-wont-pull-controversial-russians-at-war/docview/3103594350/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=TIFF+won%27t+pull+controversial+%27Russians+At+War%27+documentary%2C+despite+protests&amp;title=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-09-11&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: The Toronto International Film Festival said Wednesday that it won't pull "Russians At War," a documentary about Russian soldiers fighting in Ukraine, from its schedule, despite protests from Ukrainian officials and the Ukrainian-Canadian community.
"This documentary is an official Canada-France co-production with funding from several Canadian agencies, at both the federal and provincial level," TIFF said in a statement.
"Our understanding is that it was made without the knowledge or participation of any Russian government agencies. In our view, in no way should this film be considered Russian propaganda."
The statement also expressed sympathy for the Ukrainian people, and the suffering resulting from Russia's invasion.
"As we engage with the art made at this politically charged time, we are guided by the democratic values of freedom of conscience, opinion, expression and peaceful assembly as protected in the Canadian Charter of Rights and Freedoms for all Canadians including Canadian artists," the statement read.
In response to TIFF's statement, Yulia Kovaliv, Ukraine's ambassador to Canada, expressed her disappointment in a post on X.
"Russian propaganda is not the same as freedom of speech, Russian war crimes have been committed and well documented by international investigators, the whole world seen them," she wrote. "By showing this film you are whitewashing killer and rapers."
The Ukrainian Canadian Congress (UCC), a non-profit umbrella organization that represents Canada's Ukrainian community, also weighed in on X. It called TIFF's statement "insulting and appalling."
"Whitewashing Russian propaganda at a time when Russia is committing war crimes against Ukrainians," the UCC wrote.
The TIFF statement arrived a day after TVO withdrew its support for TIFF, following protests from the Ukrainian community.
"TVO's Board of Directors has decided to respect the feedback we have received, and TVO will no longer be supporting or airing 'Russians at War,'" a press release posted to the public broadcaster's website on Tuesday stated.
"TVO will be reviewing the process by which this project was funded and our brand leveraged."
Meanwhile, the Documentary Organization of Canada (DOC), which represents the collective voice of Canada's independent documentary creators, expressed alarm at TVO's "unilateral decision" to withdraw support for "Russians at War."
"This project, a Canadian-French co-production, was developed through a multi-year collaborative effort involving the filmmakers and TVO's Documentary team," read a statement released on Wednesday. "TVO's team has traditionally operated with editorial independence, fostering essential collaboration with independent filmmakers through established journalistic processes of review and discussion." The statement went on: "TVO's decision risks setting a dangerous precedent and must be immediately reversed."
The documentary, which follows Russian soldiers on the front lines of the war in Ukraine, also sparked a protest on Tuesday at the Scotiabank Theatre in Toronto.
Speaking from the Liberals' caucus retreat, Finance Minister Chrystia Freeland told reporters Tuesday that both diplomats and the Ukrainian-Canadian community have expressed "grave concerns" about the film "Russians at War" - concerns which she shares.
"We as a country have to be very, very clear that there can be no moral equivalency in our understanding of this conflict," she said. "It's not right for Canadian public money to be supporting the screening and production of a film like this."
A synopsis of the film on TIFF's website described it as a first-person documentary chronicling the lives of Russian soldiers as they grow increasingly disillusioned by the war. "They all come to realize that everything they heard about the war in Russian media is false," it read. "They begin to doubt their purpose - and fight only to survive."
The documentary, which premiered at the Venice Film Festival last week and is set to make its North American debut at TIFF on Friday, was produced in association with Ontario's TVO and British Columbia's Knowledge Network. It also received $340,000 in funding from the not-for-profit Canada Media Fund.
Ukraine's consul general in Toronto, Oleh Nikolenko, previously urged TIFF to pull the documentary from its schedule, accusing the film of absolving Russian soldiers of responsibility in the ongoing invasion.
"It is irresponsible to allow the Toronto International Film Festival, one of the most reputable world film stages, to be used to whitewash the responsibility of Russian soldiers committing war crimes in Ukraine during the ongoing Russian invasion," Nikolenko wrote in a letter to TIFF CEO Cameron Bailey dated Sep. 5.
He added that representatives from the Consulate General of Ukraine in Toronto, the Embassy of Ukraine in Ottawa and the Ukrainian Canadian Congress raised concerns with TIFF management, but did not find their response satisfactory.
TIFF did not immediately respond to the Star's requests for comment.
In response to the backlash, the film's Russian-Canadian director, Anastasia Trofimova, asserted in a written statement that the film is not propaganda, and that it was filmed without the permission of the Russian government.
"I want to be clear that this Canada-France co-production is an antiwar film made at great risk to all involved, myself especially," she said.
"I unequivocally believe that Russia's invasion of Ukraine is unjustified, illegal and acknowledge the validity of the International Criminal Court investigation of war crimes in Ukraine."
In producing the film, Trofimova followed a Russian unit through parts of Russian-occupied eastern Ukraine. Nikolenko said he believed the documentary wasn't filmed legally, as Trofimova did not receive permission to cross the border into Russian-occupied Ukraine.
By screening the film, TIFF is making itself complicit, he continued.
TVO initially stood by the film, responding to the controversy last week with a statement that the documentary "is at its core an anti-war film."
In Venice, Trofimova told reporters that she witnessed no war crimes, characterizing the soldiers she accompanied as: "Absolutely ordinary guys with families, with a sense of humour, with their own understanding of what's happening in this war."
Outside the Scotiabank Theatre on Tuesday afternoon, dozens of protesters gathered to protest the TIFF's decision to air the documentary.
For her part, Freeland noted that "This is a war where Russia is breaking international law and committing war crimes.
"There is very clearly good and evil in this war. Ukrainians are fighting for their sovereignty and for democracy around the world."
With files from The Canadian Press
CAPTION: Finance Minister Chrystia Freeland is denouncing the use of public funds for a documentary about Russian soldiers. On the same day, a crowd of people protested the film's screening at the Toronto International Film Festival. Freeland says she shares the "grave concerns" raised about "Russians at War." (Sept. 10, 2024 / The Canadian Press)People protest outside of Scotiabank Theatre about the documentary "Russians at War," the press screening of which was playing at the Toronto International Film Festival on Tuesday. Public screenings start Friday.

Document 51 of 500

Sept. 11: Canada's homeless crisis was foreseeable, NDP lacks 'big vision' and other letters to the editor

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/sept-11-canadas-homeless-crisis-was-foreseeable/docview/3102859338/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Sept.+11%3A+Canada%27s+homeless+crisis+was+foreseeable%2C+NDP+lacks+%27big+vision%27+and+other+letters+to+the+editor&amp;title=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-09-11&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Humanitarian crisis was foreseeable
If the humanitarian crisis that is on display for all to see in my hometown and every other town and city in Canada had happened all at once and if the people affected were not poor, we would have acted by now. If 1,500 people in Hamilton all lost their homes in a single catastrophic event - a fire, a flood, an earthquake - we would have called in every and all organizations that can help; the Red Cross, the military, possibly even Médecins sans Frontières. But it is not just 1,500 people in Hamilton or the more than 7,000 people in Toronto, it is 150,000 to 300,000 people across all of Canada. But it didn't happen all at once. It happened slowly over decades. It wasn't a natural disaster.
The existing humanitarian crisis in Canada is the foreseeable and inevitable result of government policies. It is time for Canadian governments at all levels to act. Act now before even more Canadians are out on the streets. The longer we delay the more difficult and expensive the solutions will be. The longer we delay, the longer a significant number of Canadians are not able to participate in the economy, the more our country, our culture, and our economy will suffer.
Bruce Pilcher, Hamilton
Migrant worker story uplifting
Re: Migrant workers harvest brighter future on Norfolk farms, Sept. 9
The article in Monday's paper was uplifting to read about the workers who visit Canada every year to harvest produce. As Canadian consumers, we should be grateful for their presence to get the fruit and vegetables picked for us to enjoy as fall and winter approaches. We rely on their hard work and loyalty to Canada. No one should ever find fault with the structure in place to bring them here. Of course, we have Canadians capable of doing the harvesting but are unwilling to do so.
It is very apparent from their photos the gratitude they have for the opportunity to send monies home to support the families they must miss daily. They've become part of the Ontario agriculture and it is reassuring to know their well-being is also monitored.
Barbara Noble, Hamilton
Bringing justice into ill repute
Re: Toxic combination of drugs and guns led to Shane Grenier's shooting death, court hears, Sept. 9
Obviously, Justice Joe Fiorucci never heard of not bringing the administration of justice into ill repute. As a retired first responder, we were frequently reminded of that admonition. Either that or he slept through that part in law school. He just proved that by being sympathetic to a drug dealer who killed another drug user with an illegal gun and gave him less time for having to put up with "harsh conditions" while in custody. Well, Mr. Justice Fiorucci, jail isn't meant to be the Hilton hotel.
It just proves that Canada no longer has a justice system, but instead, has a judicial system.
Hans Schmiedeberg, Burlington
NDP needs a grand vision
Re: Singh needs an issue voters can rally behind, Sept. 9
As Janet Chafe and I marched with the railway workers in the front of the Labour Day parade, we didn't see the rest of our fellow orange-shirted New Democrats until upon leaving, well behind most of the union stalwarts. It probably doesn't mean much, but could it? (I hope they were close to the steelworkers but I was too busy searching for my car to check it out.)
Yes, we still must "force Trudeau to block the Stelco takeover and deliver a win for the 'people'," but where is the big New Democrat vision? I'm old enough to have been in it since its birth, from the then Co-operative Commonwealth Federation - a good descriptive name but enough of a mouthful that it was usually just called the CCF - to the (also shortened) NDP. We're still usually to the left of the others on some strong, positive policies, but only because they've moved so far to the right. We're yet to hear a good "I have a dream" speech. What is the "New Democracy?"
Guy Mersereau, Hamilton
CAPTION: If 1,500 people in Hamilton all lost their homes in a single catastrophic event like an earthquake, we would have called in organizations that can help, like the Red Cross, a reader writes in regards to the homelessness crisis.
