Document 52 of 500

Port Lands gas plant emissions seven times too high for new development at mouth of Don River

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/port-lands-gas-plant-emissions-seven-times-too/docview/3102859007/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Port+Lands+gas+plant+emissions+seven+times+too+high+for+new+development+at+mouth+of+Don+River&amp;title=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-09-11&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Levels of toxic emissions from the Port Lands gas plant are so high they could imperil the construction of tall residential buildings along the eastern waterfront - land that has been remediated and flood-protected at public expense to prepare for new housing.
According to an air quality assessment report prepared for the developer of towers on Villiers Island, emissions of nitrogen oxides (NOx) from the plant's smokestacks are seven times higher than the legal limit at the 160-metre height of the tallest proposed building.
This means that either the gas plant would have to drastically reduce its operations or the towers would have to be constructed without balconies or windows that open above 118 m, or about 35 stories, the report concluded. (Below that height, the report found NOx levels would be within the provincial guidelines.)
The power plant is Toronto's biggest source of both greenhouse gas emissions and NOx. The assessment, prepared for a proposed development at 309 Cherry St., some 1.7 km away, reveals how air pollution could impact new housing throughout the East Harbour and Port Lands areas, which have been earmarked for tens of thousands of units in the coming years.
The former industrial area along the city's eastern waterfront is one of the largest swaths of undeveloped land in the city and has undergone $1.4 billion in publicly financed flood protection work.
"This report shows that the vision for the future of Toronto's waterfront is fundamentally at odds with the long-term operation of the Port Lands gas plant," said Bryan Purcell, vice-president of policy and programs at The Atmospheric Fund, a climate agency that advocates for low carbon solutions in the Greater Toronto and Hamilton area.
"There's simply no way to reduce emissions at the plant to allow the construction of tall residential towers in the vicinity," he added.
Atura, a subsidiary of publicly-owned Ontario Power Generation, owns and operates the plant. In response to questions from the Star, spokesperson Darius Sokal wrote that the plant operates within all public health and environmental emissions limits.
"We regularly work with our neighbours - current and future - related to compatibility with other nearby land uses," he added.
A Star investigation last year found the Port Lands gas plant operates more than 21 hours a day during the summer, sending carbon emissions into the atmosphere and pollutants harmful to human health into the local community.
Despite City Council twice voting against its expansion, the plant is embarking on upgrades that will increase its output by nine per cent. At the same time, the province has extended its operating contract from 2029 to 2034.
The proposed development on Cherry St. includes towers of 49, 39, 27 and 10 stories and the report analyzed the air quality at 160 meters - the height of the tallest building. It also looked at air quality at another proposed development in the East Harbour Lands, which is closer to the 75-meter-tall smokestacks at the Portlands Energy Centre (PEC). In both cases, it found NOx far exceeding the legal limit at heights above 35 stories.
"If the development at East Harbour Lands is constructed for residential use at the heights approved in the zoning, PEC would not remain in compliance with the air quality standards for NOx," stated the report. "PEC would need to reduce its NOx emission rate ... (by) approximately a factor of seven reduction to maintain compliance."
Castlepoint Numa, the developer that commissioned the report, and the report's authors, did not respond to a request for comment. Nor did Cadillac Fairview, the developer of East Harbour.
This summer, the city unveiled a re-naturalized mouth of the Don River, which was rerouted as part of a massive public infrastructure project to prevent flooding in and around Villiers Island, which is slated to host at least 15 towers and 9,000 housing units but could contain far more, activists say.
If tower heights are capped at 35 stories to allow the Port Lands gas plant to continue to operate, it would limit the amount of housing that could be built in the surrounding area and could imperil the economics of the developments, which rely on tall towers to be profitable.
The original air quality assessment completed for the Port Lands gas plant in 2003 said the pollution levels from the plant would not restrict future development in the area. But the report did not consider buildings over 20 stories tall.
"It is very clear that the operation of the Portlands Energy Centre could mean less housing will be built in the area near it or that the plant operation will have to be heavily restrained," said Peter Tabuns, the MPP who represents the area. "Those heights and the NOx are a really bad mix."
Exposure to NOx has been shown to cause lung and heart disease, asthma, Alzheimer's, cancer and birth complications; Health Canada estimates that there are more than 15,000 premature deaths per year due to air pollution.
"On one side of the issue is housing and clean air, on the other side is an irresponsible plan to expand gas burning for power production. The province's approach is wrong," Tabuns said.
Dr. Mili Roy, an assistant professor at U of T's faculty of medicine and the co-chair of the Canadian Association of Physicians for the Environment, stressed there is no "safe" level of NOx exposure.
Immediately after the election of Premier Doug Ford's Progressive Conservative government in 2018, Ontario halted construction of renewable energy, like wind and solar, and instead doubled down on natural gas plants to meet a growing demand for electricity.
This reversed the decline in carbon emissions from our electrical grid, which were reduced by more than 80 per cent by the previous Liberal government after it shut down all the coal plants in the province. Now, electricity emissions are on the rise as new gas plants are built and existing ones are used more often.
Climate experts have questioned the increasing use of gas to generate electricity, especially as renewable energy has plummeted in cost and is now considered the cheapest form of energy in history.
The air quality assessment introduces new questions.
"This report sharpens the conversation around the Port Lands gas plant, which has until now been about climate change and emissions. Now it's about billions of dollars in development and tens of thousands of units of housing," Purcell said.
As the health and climate impacts of relying on gas have become clear, local residents have started organizing against the Port Lands gas plant.
"Toronto can get all the energy it needs from renewables. So it's hard to understand why we're pursuing gas, especially with all the health implications," said David Smith, a longtime east-end resident and member of Toronto East Residents for Renewable Energy.
Smith is excited about the renewal of the Port Lands and has poured over the renderings of dozens of new condo towers planned for the area. He notes that none of them depict the smokestacks of the power plant next door.
"All these people want a nice place to live. But is it a nice place to live if you can't breathe the air?"
CAPTION: The Portlands Energy Centre, near the site of a series of high-profile developments near the mouth of the Don River.CI-CLIMATE-GASPLANTEMISSIONS9 TORONTO, ON - September 10 Wisps of greenish gasses are seen during the noon hour coming out of the large smoke stacks of the Portland's gas generating station in the Leslie and Unwin avenue area. The pollution levels from the portlands gas plant are 7x higher than the legal limit at heights above 35 stories, putting into risk future residential development across the eastern waterfront. September 10 2024 Richard Lautens/Toronto Star

Subject: Electricity; Height; Floods; Emissions; Carbon; Renewable resources; Air pollution; Energy; Outdoor air quality; Emission standards; Coal-fired power plants; Climate change; Industrial plant emissions

Full text: Good morning! It's Sept. 11, here are the top stories today in the Hamilton area.
Today's traffic and weather
Here comes another one, just like the other one. Sunny skies and a daytime high of 26 C. Environment Canada says it'll be a tiny bit warmer Wednesday than the normal temperature for this date which is 21. The humidex will make it feel more like 29 through the day. There are no weather alerts.
Traffic and transit updates:
ANCASTER: Garner Road West closed in both direction from Shaver Road to Hamilton Drive continues until mid-October.
DOWNTOWN HAMILTON: Reminder that starting Thursday, temporary closures will start on James Street North and York Boulevard for Supercrawl weekend.
Find the latest provincial road closures and traffic incidents via Ontario 511. Closures in Hamilton are available on the Hamilton police incident feed.
Taking transit today? Find the latest GO Transit service updates here. The latest information on local bus services are available here: Hamilton, Burlington and Oakville.
Top stories on The Spec:
Girl, 11, hit by car while waiting for school bus in central Hamilton
Alison Brooks' morning coffee routine on her north Hamilton porch is typically accompanied by the sound of chirping birds and scuttling kids.
On Tuesday it was bangs and screams.
City bylaws don't do enough to hold bad-faith tenants accountable, argues homeless landlord
One councillor 'a little troubled' that landlords are fined for tenant-created issues, while another says it's the landlord's problem to deal with.
Accused in 2021 motel stabbing death says he didn't plan to kill victim
40-year-old Christopher La Rose died of stab wounds after he was found outside a Queenston Road motel in east Hamilton.
Teen charged with alleged online threat against Haldimand school
OPP detachment received information from Interpol about threat to a school
Also, today's editorial cartoon by The Spec's Graeme MacKay.
Mark Carney's appointment to lead the Liberal task force offers hope for revitalizing the party, but it will likely require a leadership change to prevent a total collapse under Justin Trudeau's tenure.
Subscribe to our newsletters for the latest local content.

Document 54 of 500

Letters

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/letters/docview/3102665575/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=unknown&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Letters&amp;title=The+Spectator&amp;issn=11899417&amp;date=2024-09-11&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.8&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Spectator&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Migrant worker story uplifting
Re: Migrant workers harvest brighter future on Norfolk farms, Sept. 9
The article in Monday's paper was uplifting to read about the workers who visit Canada every year to harvest produce. As Canadian consumers, we should be grateful for their presence to get the fruit and vegetables picked for us to enjoy as fall and winter approaches. We rely on their hard work and loyalty to Canada. No one should ever find fault with the structure in place to bring them here. Of course, we have Canadians capable of doing the harvesting but are unwilling to do so. It is very apparent from their photos the gratitude they have for the opportunity to send monies home to support the families they must miss daily. They've become part of the Ontario agriculture and it is reassuring to know their well-being is also monitored.
Barbara Noble, Hamilton
Humanitarian
crisis foreseeable
If the humanitarian crisis that is on display for all to see in my hometown and every other town and city in Canada had happened all at once and if the people affected were not poor, we would have acted by now. If 1,500 people in Hamilton all lost their homes in a single catastrophic event - a fire, a flood, an earthquake - we would have called in every and all organizations that can help; the Red Cross, the military, possibly even Médecins sans Frontières. But it is not just 1,500 people in Hamilton or the more than 7,000 people in Toronto, it is 150,000 to 300,000 people across Canada. But it didn't happen all at once. It happened slowly over decades. The humanitarian crisis in Canada is the foreseeable and inevitable result of government policies. It is time for governments at all levels to act, before even more Canadians are out on the streets. The longer we delay the more difficult and expensive the solutions will be. The longer we delay, the longer a significant number of Canadians are not able to participate in the economy, the more our country, culture and economy will suffer.
Bruce Pilcher, Hamilton
Bringing justice into ill repute
Re: Toxic combination of
drugs and guns led to Shane Grenier's shooting death, court hears, Sept. 9
Obviously, Justice Joe Fiorucci never heard of not bringing the administration of justice into ill repute. As a retired first responder, we were frequently reminded of that admonition. Either that or he slept through that part in law school. He just proved that by being sympathetic to a drug dealer who killed another drug user with an illegal gun and gave him less time for having to put up with "harsh conditions" while in custody. Well, Mr. Justice Fiorucci, jail isn't meant to be the Hilton hotel. It just proves that Canada no longer has a justice system, but instead, has a judicial system.
Hans Schmiedeberg,
Burlington
NDP needs
a grand vision
Re: Singh needs an issue
voters can rally behind, Sept. 9
As Janet Chafe and I marched with the railway workers in the front of the Labour Day parade, we didn't see the rest of our fellow orange-shirted New Democrats until upon leaving, well behind most of the union stalwarts. It probably doesn't mean much, but could it?
Yes, we still must "force Trudeau to block the Stelco takeover and deliver a win for the 'people,' " but where is the big New Democrat vision? I'm old enough to have been in it since its birth, from the then Co-operative Commonwealth Federation - a good descriptive name but enough of a mouthful that it was usually just called the CCF - to the (also shortened) NDP. We're still usually to the left of the others on some strong, positive policies, but only because they've moved so far to the right. We're yet to hear a good "I have a dream" speech.
Guy Mersereau, Hamilton

Document 55 of 500

Chrystia Freeland, Ukrainian officials slam TIFF screening a documentary following Russian soldiers

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/chrystia-freeland-ukrainian-officials-slam-tiff/docview/3102970925/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Chrystia+Freeland%2C+Ukrainian+officials+slam+TIFF+screening+a+documentary+following+Russian+soldiers&amp;title=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-09-10&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: A documentary following Russian soldiers on the front lines of the war in Ukraine is facing backlash over its inclusion at the Toronto International Film Festival.
Speaking from the Liberals' caucus retreat, Finance Minister Chrystia Freeland told reporters Tuesday that both diplomats and the Ukrainian-Canadian community have expressed "grave concerns" about the film "Russians at War" - concerns which she shares.
"We as a country have to be very, very clear that there can be no moral equivalency in our understanding of this conflict," she said. "It's not right for Canadian public money to be supporting the screening and production of a film like this."
A synopsis of the film on TIFF's website described it as a first-person documentary chronicling the lives of Russian soldiers as they grow increasingly disillusioned by the war. "They all come to realize that everything they heard about the war in Russian media is false," it read. "They begin to doubt their purpose - and fight only to survive."
The documentary, which premiered at the Venice Film Festival last week and is set to make its North American debut at TIFF on Friday, was produced in association with Ontario's TVO and British Columbia's Knowledge Network. It also received $340,000 in funding from the not-for-profit Canada Media Fund.
Ukraine's consul general in Toronto, Oleh Nikolenko, previously urged TIFF to pull the documentary from its schedule, accusing the film of absolving Russian soldiers of responsibility in the ongoing invasion.
"It is irresponsible to allow the Toronto International Film Festival, one of the most reputable world film stages, to be used to whitewash the responsibility of Russian soldiers committing war crimes in Ukraine during the ongoing Russian invasion," Nikolenko wrote in a letter to TIFF CEO Cameron Bailey dated Sep. 5.
He added that representatives from the Consulate General of Ukraine in Toronto, the Embassy of Ukraine in Ottawa and the Ukrainian Canadian Congress raised concerns with TIFF management, but did not find their response satisfactory.
TIFF did not immediately respond to the Star's requests for comment.
In response to the backlash, the film's Russian-Canadian director, Anastasia Trofimova, asserted in a written statement that the film is not propaganda, and that it was filmed without the permission of the Russian government.
"I want to be clear that this Canada-France co-production is an antiwar film made at great risk to all involved, myself especially," she said.
"I unequivocally believe that Russia's invasion of Ukraine is unjustified, illegal and acknowledge the validity of the International Criminal Court investigation of war crimes in Ukraine."
In producing the film, Trofimova followed a Russian unit through parts of Russian-occupied eastern Ukraine. Nikolenko said he believed the documentary wasn't filmed legally, as Trofimova did not receive permission to cross the border into Russian-occupied Ukraine.
By screening the film, TIFF is making itself complicit, he continued.
TVO responded to the controversy in a statement, saying the documentary "is at its core an anti-war film. It is unauthorized by Russian officials and was made at great personal risk to the filmmaker, who was under constant threat of arrest and incarceration for trying to tell an unofficial story."
In Venice, Trofimova told reporters that she witnessed no war crimes, characterizing the soldiers she accompanied as: "Absolutely ordinary guys with families, with a sense of humour, with their own understanding of what's happening in this war."
For her part, Freeland noted that "This is a war where Russia is breaking international law and committing war crimes.
"There is very clearly good and evil in this war. Ukrainians are fighting for their sovereignty and for democracy around the world."
With files from The Canadian Press
CAPTION: Finance Minister Chrystia Freeland is denouncing the use of public funds for a documentary about Russian soldiers. On the same day, a crowd of people protested the film's screening at the Toronto International Film Festival. Freeland says she shares the "grave concerns" raised about "Russians at War." (Sept. 10, 2024 / The Canadian Press)People protest outside of Scotiabank Theatre about the documentary "Russians at War," the press screening of which was playing at the Toronto International Film Festival on Tuesday. Public screenings start Friday.

Document 56 of 500

Doug Ford is 'obsessed with alcohol in convenience stores' instead of health care, Jagmeet Singh charges

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/doug-ford-is-obsessed-with-alcohol-convenience/docview/3102970416/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Doug+Ford+is+%27obsessed+with+alcohol+in+convenience+stores%27+instead+of+health+care%2C+Jagmeet+Singh+charges&amp;title=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-09-10&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: It's the booze talking.
NDP Leader Jagmeet Singh fired back at Doug Ford on Tuesday, accusing the premier of being "obsessed with alcohol in convenience stores" instead of more pressing challenges.
One day after Ford derided Singh for abruptly ending the two-year supply and confidence agreement to prop up Prime Minister Justin Trudeau's minority Liberals, the New Democrat responded.
"Doug Ford needs to worry about delivering health care. People in Ontario can't get the health care they need, they're struggling with the cost of living and he's obsessed with alcohol in convenience stores," the federal NDP leader said in Montreal.
"I don't know any person that I've spoken to when I tour Ontario, when I'm in the communities, that says that's their number one pressing issue," said Singh, referring to the premier's booze liberalization scheme that took effect last week and will cost taxpayers between $225 million and $1 billion.
"Doug Ford should focus on those things instead of being stuck on Twitter," he said.
On Monday, the premier raised eyebrows after being asked whether the threat of an imminent federal election would force him to reconsider any plans for a potential early provincial vote next year.
"As far as I'm concerned, MP Singh is just blowing smoke," Ford said in Prince Edward County.
"There's nothing more important to these greedy politicians than collecting a pension and he was elected (in) ... 2019 and I'll be floored and I'll stand up here and apologize to all the greedy politicians if he calls the election early," the premier said, referring to the six-year threshold for federal MPs to qualify for a pension.
"But let's face it, he wants his pension. He's not going anywhere until ... Oct. 20, 2025 because there's him and a whole bunch of other politicians that want to grab the taxpayers' money. So my bet is, he's not doing anything. He's just putting on a big show like he has for the last number of years there."
Last Thursday, Singh - who has said an election could come at any time, though his party would not automatically vote against the Liberals - dismissed the pension charge as "a ludicrous suggestion."
"I'm obviously going to get re-elected in my riding (of Burnaby South), I'm not worried about that at all. This is a silly argument being brought about by Conservatives," he told Vassy Kapelos on CTV's Power Play.
A snap federal vote could throw a wrinkle in Ford's plan to have an Ontario election one year ahead of the scheduled June 2026 vote.
His Progressive Conservatives, who lead in every public-opinion poll, would prefer to face voters sooner rather than later.
That's because they're worried a Pierre Poilievre-led Conservative government might make cuts to transfer payments and to federal subsidies for electric-vehicle manufacturers in Ontario.
There's also concern at the potential for charges being laid in the ongoing RCMP investigation into the Greenbelt land swap scandal.
Political tradition is another reason why Ford could go early - historically, Ontario voters prefer to have different parties in power in Ottawa and Queen's Park at the same time.
When Ontarians elect Liberals federally, they usually choose Tories provincially.
Conversely, when they elect federal Tory governments, the Liberals - and, in one instance, the New Democrats - tend to win provincially.
The phenomenon, known as the "Underhill balance theory," has held true for about 70 of the past 81 years.
While Ford has sparred with Trudeau in the past, he has worked closely with the prime minister since the COVID-19 pandemic began in 2020.
In the wake of that health emergency, the two leaders have focused on shared economic priorities like tens of billions of dollars in planned subsidies for EV manufacturers, such as Volkswagen, Stellantis and Honda.
CAPTION: NDP Leader Jagmeet Singh speaks to the media at the party's caucus retreat in Montreal on Sept. 10, 2024.

Document 57 of 500

Help the Liberals? Oh, come on - Mark Carney is here for Canada

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/help-liberals-oh-come-on-mark-carney-is-here/docview/3102665588/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Help+the+Liberals%3F+Oh%2C+come+on+-+Mark+Carney+is+here+for+Canada&amp;title=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-09-10&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Mark Carney wants you to know he's answering a call from his country, not overtly positioning himself for the Liberal leadership, or painting himself to be the Grits' saviour.
"I'm an outsider," the former Bank of Canada governor told journalists in Nanaimo, B.C., where Grit MPs are gathered for three days of caucus meetings. On Monday, Carney was announced by the Liberal party as the chair of its leader's task force on economic growth. He is to travel across the country, meeting with stakeholders and writing up a plan to address near and longer-term economic growth and productivity issues, with an eye to shaping the party's electoral platform. On Tuesday, he met with Liberal MPs for about 40 minutes, with most of the time spent answering their questions about the economy and what he planned to do.
For those wanting to know why one of the country's most impressive economic minds - he steered Canada through the global economic crisis, earned widespread praise as a prudent financial manager and was tapped to lead the Bank of England - was hitching his wagon to the struggling Liberals, Carney suggested he wasn't joining a partisan fight, but just answering the call of duty.
"I have a couple of simple rules. One, if the prime minister of Canada asked me to do something, I will do it," he told reporters. "Secondly, you know, I'm interested. I have some experience in these issues and I'm interested in helping our country to grow."
Asked if he had been asked to run for the Liberals in either of the Sept. 16 byelections, or if he planned to run for a seat in the next general election, Carney sidestepped. "I understood the question, but my answer stands," he said. "I'm interested in doing something, not being something" - a line he repeated twice.
Carney also sidestepped when asked if there was daylight between his position on carbon pricing and that of the Grits. In the spring, "Carbon Tax Carney" as Pierre Poilievre's Conservatives are trying to define him, told a Senate committee that carbon pricing has "served a purpose up until now. I think one can always look for better solutions."
Last year, however, Carney was one of Trudeau's fiercest critics after the prime minister announced a three-year pause on the carbon levy for Canadians using heating oil. At the time, Carney said, Canadians were not struggling because of the carbon tax, but because of broad increases in energy prices, in food prices, the impact on wages and the lingering efforts of COVID, and he suggested he would have addressed affordability concerns differently.
"Being low carbon is going to be a determinant of whether we can create jobs, whether we can sell our products in the United States, in Europe, around the world," he said on Tuesday. "We have to help our companies become more competitive. There is a huge range of things that the federal government can do."
It wasn't immediately clear Tuesday what Carney had told Liberal MPs about carbon pricing. Procurement Minister Jean-Yves Duclos told the Star that Carney had been asked about it and "he still believes that if we put a price on pollution, which puts more money in the pockets of eight families out of 10, that's a great thing to do!"
Outside of the Vancouver Island Conference Centre, however, a handful of protesters reminded the Liberals that, so far, they appear to have lost the communication battle when it comes to carbon pricing and cost of living increases. They'll face another challenge on that front next spring when the carbon price is set to increase yet again.
Carney's carbon pricing stance isn't the only line of attack the Conservatives plan to hit the Liberals with.
While Carney sought to frame himself as "a catalyst" to help the government define a new course for economic growth, rather than an appointee designated to bolster the popularity of the Liberal party, so too did the Conservatives.
In a letter to the prime minister, Ontario Tory MP Michael Barrett told Trudeau he was deeply concerned that Carney had been appointed a Liberal party adviser, rather than an adviser to the government, in order to bypass conflict of interest rules.
"Ministerial advisers are reporting public office holders under the (law) and are legally required to disclose potential conflicts of interest, ethics screens, recusals and all controlled assets, liabilities and outside activities," Barrett wrote. He noted Carney had done very well for himself and sits on several corporate boards, including of asset management companies overseeing trillions of dollars. Interestingly, Barrett did not demand Trudeau cancel Carney's appointment, but instead that Carney's position be placed within the government structure - essentially, allowing the Conservatives to prod Carney's financial dealings and read his emails.
"This is a laughable stunt from the Conservative party," Liberal spokesman Parker Lund told the Star. "The Conservatives are just upset that Mark Carney supports the Liberal economic plan and not Pierre Poilievre's disastrous cuts that would damage the economy."
CAPTION: Former Bank of Canada governor Mark Carney speaks to reporters at the Liberal caucus retreat in Nanaimo, B.C., onSept. 10, 2024.

Document 58 of 500

A union helped open this medical clinic. Now its members have been dropped as patients because there aren't enough doctors

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/union-helped-open-this-medical-clinic-now-members/docview/3102454129/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=A+union+helped+open+this+medical+clinic.+Now+its+members+have+been+dropped+as+patients+because+there+aren%27t+enough+doctors&amp;title=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-09-10&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: As Premier Doug Ford's government scrambles to improve access to health care, fallout from the front lines of Ontario's growing doctor shortage is moving from the examination room to the courtroom.
A Sault Ste. Marie medical clinic that made headlines across Canada for its dramatic decision to cut loose 10,000 patients in May because of physician retirements and departures is the subject of a lawsuit from United Steelworkers Local 2251.
The lead plaintiff is local president Mike Da Prat, one of the patients who was dropped from the clinic roster and who has yet to find another doctor. His lawyer has filed a statement of claim in Ontario Superior Court of Justice against the Group Health Centre.
Among other things, it alleges that age discrimination played a role in the decision, and seeks an injunction forcing the clinic to take back Da Prat and about 200 "original subscribers" of the clinic (in addition to their immediate family members), which was founded and built with $800,000 in contributions from the union local's members in 1963, before medicare and the Ontario Health Insurance Plan.
"We were to be members of Group Health for the rest of our lives. Instead, we were de-rostered," said Da Prat.
The allegations in the lawsuit have not been proven in court, which will hold a hearing on the injunction request Oct. 30. To put it in perspective, the original $800,000 at stake is the equivalent of $8 million today, according to the Bank of Canada inflation calculator.
Group Health called the lawsuit "unfortunate," given the challenges in recruiting new doctors at a time when physician shortages are widespread across the country.
"We are disappointed to reallocate limited health-care funds and resources to legal proceedings. Our focus remains on providing quality health-care services to our community, and we believe that collaboration and constructive dialogue are the most effective ways to address issues," it said in a written statement.
"We continue our efforts to recruit additional primary care physicians and nurse practitioners."
The lawsuit points to the high stakes in the remote northern Ontario city of 80,000, says New Democrat MPP France Gelinas (Nickel Belt), a physiotherapist by training and her party's longtime health critic.
"It has divided the community," Gelinas said of the de-rostering. "You just can't imagine the hurt people feel. It's just awful what's happening."
Unlike in more populous Toronto or southern Ontario, medical alternatives are few.
"There's nowhere else to go," Gelinas said, noting the nearest major population centre is Sudbury, a four-hour drive east in perfect weather.
Concerns about medical care are certain to become a delicate issue in a provincial election campaign that Ford is expected to call as early as next spring, likely putting local Progressive Conservative MPP Ross Romano on the hot seat.
Romano could not be reached for comment despite several requests made to his office by the Star.
The de-rostering of patients at the end of May turned out to be not as severe as the 10,000 predicted by Group Health in its January bombshell.
But the situation is fluid in a province the Ontario Medical Association says is short about 2,500 doctors, with 40 per cent of working physicians considering retirement in the next five years. The OMA estimates about 2.5 million Ontarians do not have a family doctor.
Two doctors who left Group Health to start their own health team took 3,000 patients with them. However, another Group Health doctor subsequently left, prompting the de-rostering of 1,200 patients.
And while new nurse practitioners will begin working at Group Health this fall, allowing patients to be returned to the roster, it said, "known physician retirements in 2025 ... will result in additional de-rostering of approximately 6,000 patients.
Group Health's plan is to use its "access care clinic" - which de-rostered patients can call first thing in the morning in hopes of getting a same-day appointment - "to provide interim care to these patients with the intent to re-roster all patients," the health centre's statement said.
That, of course, will depend on attracting new physicians to Group Health which now has 30 primary care doctors and 10 nurse practitioners in addition to 26 specialists including a cardiologist, an ophthalmologist, dermatologists, oncologists and surgeons doing procedures to take pressure off the local hospital.
The access care clinic operates with $2.8 million in provincial funding announced in late May by Health Minister Sylvia Jones, who said at the time "we recognize there is more work to be done."
Jones said she is in touch with Romano on expanding access to primary care in the city.
A Toronto lawyer handling the lawsuit maintains the Group Health Centre made a bad choice in the de-rostering.
"We understand that there were issues with having practitioners leave, but the answer was not to just kick out the Steelworkers," said Louis Sokolov of Sotos LLP.
"In exchange for their contribution to the centre's seed funding and payment of a monthly premium, the original subscribers were guaranteed medical care from the centre," he wrote in the 24-page lawsuit that argues the introduction of medicare in Ontario in 1969 does not detract from their right.
"The de-rostered patients were chosen simply because they happened to be on the rosters of the departing physicians, without regard to their relative medical needs. This methodology discriminates on the bases of age (given that patients on the rosters of retiring physicians are older, on average), contrary to section 1 (1) of the Human Rights Code."
As to those allegations, the Group Health Centre said it "will not be commenting on the particulars of matters before the court."
In another sign of local tensions over the de-rostering, a video went viral last week picturing a man wearing a Local 2251 T-shirt who teed off on Prime Minister Justin Trudeau during his visit to Sault Ste. Marie.
"What about the 40 per cent taxes I am paying and I don't have a family doctor?" the unidentified man said as a TV camera recorded the exchange with Trudeau, who was rebuffed after touting his new tariffs on Chinese steel as a way to protect local jobs at Algoma Steel.
CAPTION: NDP health critic France Gelinas speaks at Queen's Park on Sept. 14, 2022.

Subject: Health care access; Nurse practitioners; Injunctions; Primary care; Health care policy; Litigation; Medicare; Physicians

Document 59 of 500

Stigma never reversed an overdose

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/stigma-never-reversed-overdose/docview/3102454041/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Stigma+never+reversed+an+overdose&amp;title=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-09-10&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: There is a reason nurses, public health workers and social services workers are collectively horrified by Premier Doug Ford's and Health Minister Sylvia Jones' decision to shutter supervised consumption sites across Ontario.
Last month, Jones announced 10 sites would permanently close effective early next year. The legislation slated for this fall will prohibit municipalities from creating any new sites or participating in federal safer-supply initiatives.
The Ford government did not seek advice from anyone who would be directly affected by this cruel policy before making this rash decision; Not those with lived experiences; not organizations that support these communities; and not nurses or health-care professionals who know first-hand what must be done to help some of the most marginalized Ontarians.
If Ford and Jones had consulted any of them, they would have discovered the facts are crystal clear - supervised consumption sites save lives.
The reality is chilling. One person in Ontario dies every two and a half hours due to toxic unregulated drugs and more than 21,000 people have died from unregulated drug poisoning since 2018. An estimated 3,812 drug-related deaths occurred in 2023 and an additional 1,842 suspected drug-toxicity deaths happened in the first six months of 2024.
Without supervised consumption sites, these staggering numbers will rise exponentially.
Clinical approaches to substance use need to be comprehensive and tailored to individuals. No two people who use substances are exactly alike. Quitting "cold turkey" can often be dangerous or even deadly. Mandatory treatment is neither ethical nor in alignment with best clinical practice.
Ford and Jones said this decision is about safety. But the evidence suggests these sites do not increase crime in the area surrounding them. In fact, they are linked to a reduction in neighbourhood overdoses, EMS calls and transmission of blood-borne infections such as HIV and hepatitis C. The sites are associated with less public disposal of syringes and drug use in areas visible to the public.
Cutting supervised consumption sites won't stop people from using substances. It simply stands to increase drug use in public areas like parks, sidewalks and in restaurant bathrooms, because people will have nowhere else to go.
The nurses and health-care professionals who work in supervised consumption sites provide emergency medical care, preventive and primary care, including wound care. They support clients in mental health treatment and referrals to housing and other social supports. They educate clients on safer consumption practices and refer them to rehabilitation, detox centres and other health services if and when they're ready.
What is missing from the statistics and stigma are the very people who rely on these sites. They are mothers and uncles; sons and sisters; best friends and colleagues. They come from virtually every walk of life and are simply trying to avoid harm related to addiction and substance use.
Most of us know someone who has struggled with substance use. As nurses and health-care professionals, we advocate for their right to access life-saving, dignified, low-barrier health-care services. We know they deserve compassion and support, no matter where they are in their health journeys.
People can only benefit from treatment if and when they are ready.
That's why nurses are calling on Premier Ford and his government to reverse their decision and keep supervised consumption sites open.
Ford and Jones should spend less time spreading stigmatizing views and more time listening to the nurses and health-care professionals who provide these vital services and the people who use them. Stigma never reversed an overdose.
Following the outcry at this announcement, Jones publicly promised that people would not die because of her government's policy decision.
Unfortunately, the facts tell us otherwise.
CAPTION: IfPremier Doug Ford and Health Minister Sylvia Jones had consulted anyone, they would have discovered that the facts are crystal clear: supervised consumption sites save lives.
CREDIT: Erin Ariss and Hannah Stahl

Subject: Drug overdose; Health care; Nurses; Stigma; Drug use

Full text: Do something about homelessness issue
We have read articles now on clever housing ideas for the unhoused and/or affordable housing from Waterloo, Montreal and Europe. Here's an idea for Hamilton. Do something. While the number of tents become larger at most of our tourist sights, including Gage Park and Bayfront Park and scattered throughout the city (even the escarpment stratas), the City of Hamilton does nothing.
Find a real estate entrepreneur who cares about this issue, partner with them and be done with it. The tract of land where Ivor Wynne Stadium should have been built along the north end of Barton Street would fit affordable units for the unhoused. The lack of planning and insight in this city is embarrassing.
Bernadette Flis, Hamilton
The way forward for the left
Side-by-side columns in the comment section of the Saturday Spec proposed two views of Canada's politics of the left. In one a McMaster professor advocated for education that "connects education with social responsibility, compassion and justice," or in other words produces social justice warriors. The column beside it by an NDP activist proposes progressives "take back ... freedom, family, patriotism, masculinity, normalcy."
Two more divergent views of the way forward for the far left can hardly be imagined. That the NDP could even consider championing patriotism is astounding or that they can now write "masculinity" without the previously required word "toxic" in front of it amazes one. Watching this internal fight might be the most entertainment the NDP has provided in years.
Reg McGuire, Mount Hope
Billionaires don't have all the power
I am so tired of listening to Canadians whining about a few billionaires who allegedly control most of the nation's wealth. Just last month in this paper was an article stating the middle class controlled $57 trillion in property. Fifty-seven trillion! That makes all the billionaires in North America look like a bunch of children with pocket change in comparison.
Dan Riga, Burlington
Jama needs to change her position
Re: Hamilton Centre MPP removed from magazine's list of influential women following 'threats,' Sept. 5
MPP Sarah Jama needs to let go of her anti-Zionist position. Her claim she's been muzzled in the legislature by a "Zionist lobby" is a statement that should be seen as an expression of antisemitism. As Martin Luther King put it, "When people criticize Zionists, they mean Jews. You're talking antisemitism."
Chris Asimoudis, Ancaster
Magazine needs to apologize
Anyone paying attention to or working in Canadian media should be alarmed by Elle Canada's decision to remove Ontario MPP Sarah Jama from its recent list of trail-blazing women.
As journalists, we should be ashamed. Scrubbing an article because of Ms. Jama's pro-Palestine stance because of backlash is the equivalent of unpublishing and censoring a story. This is a serious violation of journalistic standards and also an act of anti-Palestinian erasure. Excluding Palestinian perspectives and those of their allies is an explicit form of anti-Palestinian racism, according to the Arab Canadian Lawyers Association.
Elle Canada's decision sets a dangerous precedent in the Canadian media landscape. The message they are sending is that women will be disqualified from recognition as trailblazers if they stand up for Palestinian rights. Caving into the demands of bullies, especially at a time when Palestinians are facing genocide, is spineless. It is especially embarrassing as this comes from an industry built upon the belief that journalists serve not only as watchdogs, but effectively function as tools to hold institutions accountable and uplift marginalized voices.
If the outlet wishes to maintain any journalistic credibility, Elle Canada ought to restore Sarah Jama to their article and issue a public apology. Editors at Elle Canada must not erase history to evade criticism.
Anthony Issa, advocacy journalist and media analyst at Canadians for Justice and Peace in the Middle East
Trump looking to intimidate voters
Re: Trump threatens to jail adversaries in escalating rhetoric ahead of pivotal debate, Sept. 8
This story glosses over Donald Trump's true motives. In the 2020 election there was very little proven fraud, but a few voters did vote twice or sent in votes for dead relatives, etc. However, in the majority of the cases that went to court, it turned out they voted for Republicans.
Trump's threats are not intended to stop election fraud but to scare marginalized people into not voting. For many minority voters just the threat of having to fight a bogus lawsuit could be enough to keep them from voting and that is the true purpose of Trump's threats. When it comes to "unscrupulous election behaviour," Trump isn't the victim, he is the king.
Steen I. Petersen, Nanaimo, B.C.
Looking for a way to dump Trudeau
Re: Justin Trudeau shrugs off the naysayers as he heads into a Liberal caucus retreat, Sept. 9
So it seems no matter how hard we pray, we still can't get rid of Justin Trudeau. How about "Beetlejuice, Beetlejuice, Beetlejuice?"
Terry Toll, Campbell's Bay, Que.
CAPTION: We often read about communities across Canada developing projects for the unhoused, but it seems like Hamilton hasn't been able to do anything, a reader says.

Document 62 of 500

Doug Ford's beer boondoggle is nothing to brag about

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/doug-fords-beer-boondoggle-is-nothing-brag-about/docview/3102453893/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Doug+Ford%27s+beer+boondoggle+is+nothing+to+brag+about&amp;title=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-09-10&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Amazingly, Doug Ford is bringing people together over expanded alcohol sales.
The premier has miraculously united the two opposing sides in a symbiotic debate about his latest move to bring beer and wine into corner stores.
In one corner are Ford's Tories, cheerfully raising a glass to their supposedly life-changing achievement of boosting booze distribution.
In the other corner are the premier's opponents, desperately raising the alarm about the allegedly life-ending disaster of increased alcohol availability.
Neither group was attracting any attention - until recently, most Ontarians were blissfully oblivious. Now, together, they are now making common cause in creating a cause célèbre.
Today, beer in corner stores is a stale idea first trotted out 40 years ago by opposition leaders looking for attention. When David Peterson and later Mike Harris became premier, they found bigger issues facing the province.
All these years later, Ford can think of nothing more important. It's his signature issue politically (even if not personally - he's a teetotaller).
Ahead of a possible spring election, the premier has been touring Ontario touting his obsession. But Ford's Tories weren't gaining traction - until researchers and anti-alcohol activists raised the volume by warning of more drunkenness, illness and fatalities from expanded sales.
Now, on both sides of the divide, the premier and his opponents - aided and abetted by the media - are trying to convert an anticlimax into a controversy, turning a nonstory into a big deal.
Consider the premise that beer and wine will suddenly become both ubiquitous and iniquitous. In reality, the previous Liberal government of Kathleen Wynne broke up the unholy duopoly of the LCBO and The Beer Store, which had a stranglehold on sales, by giving a large piece of the action to hundreds of large supermarkets nearly a decade ago.
Those big supermarkets achieved far more economies of scale than small convenience stores can today. But it's worth recalling that the same scaremongering voices arguing against sales in convenience stores today were warning against letting it into supermarkets a decade ago.
Moreover, when COVID hit, Ford's government allowed thousands of already-licensed bars and restaurants to sell takeout alcohol. Yet there was no evidence of social upheaval then, just as there isn't any reliable predictor of it now.
In fact, governments of all political persuasions have long understood that the easiest way to raise revenue is to open more LCBO stores across the province. More outlets equals more inputs, and yet you never hear anyone complaining about new government-run stores.
Over time, Ontario has learned to restrain the temptation for prohibition or restriction. At the same time, we've seen that wider availability works just fine in other jurisdictions, not just in Quebec but across the continent and around the world.
All that said, convenience stores will not revolutionize the market, despite the pretensions and promises of this Progressive Conservative government. They have not started a stampede to stock beer, wine or pre-mixed cocktails, because they know the market for growth is limited.
Convenience stores are about convenience, not competitive pricing. You pay a premium for having a location at your corner, open late at night, offering small quantities in outlying locations with increased distribution costs and markups.
This isn't the 1980s, with corner stores on every corner. Today, supermarkets have more locations with longer hours than they did four decades ago, and the proliferation of cannabis stores has long since overtaken alcohol as a talking point.
That hasn't stopped the naysayers from saying no to the iterative convenience of corner stores. The more fuss they make, the more fun for Ford's Tories, because it makes their small beer look like a big deal.
In the premier's mind, this is a proof point for his 2022 campaign promise to "Get it Done" - on beer and wine as on homebuilding and road-building. But if booze is the best he can do, it's not saying much - and it's costing way too much.
To deliver on his ill-conceived and long-delayed pledge from the 2018 campaign, Ford had to pay off The Beer Store's ownership in order to get around the previous "master framework agreement" that phased in beer sales through 2025. The PC government paid $225 million outright, and is on the hook for hundreds of millions of dollars in potential ancillary costs, just so they buy themselves a 15-month head start - ahead of the early election Ford's campaign team wants for next spring.
If the teetotalling, bellowing Ford were in opposition, he'd be lampooning and lambasting an NDP or Liberal government for this beer boondoggle. Remarkably, he seems to have gotten away with it in power - even if he hasn't gotten much else done.
Get it done? The Greenbelt has come undone, highway construction is off track, and Metrolinx mass transit is derailed or delayed.
Beer is here belatedly, but the bottle is empty.
CAPTION: Premier Doug Ford and Finance Minister Peter Bethlenfalvy announced plans last May to allow beer sales in corner stores this summer.

Subject: Alcohol; Highway construction; Beer; Convenience stores; Distribution costs; Supermarkets

Business indexing term: Subject: Convenience stores Distribution costs Supermarkets

Title: Doug Ford's beer boondoggle is nothing to brag about

Document 63 of 500

Stigma never reversed an overdose

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/stigma-never-reversed-overdose/docview/3102303841/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=unknown&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Stigma+never+reversed+an+overdose&amp;title=The+Spectator&amp;issn=11899417&amp;date=2024-09-10&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.10&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Spectator&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: There is a reason nurses, public-health workers and social services workers are collectively horrified by Premier Doug Ford's and Health Minister Sylvia Jones' decision to shutter supervised consumption sites across Ontario.
Last month, Jones announced 10 sites would permanently close effective early next year. The legislation slated for this fall will prohibit municipalities from creating any new sites or participating in federal safer-supply initiatives.
The Ford government did not seek advice from anyone who would be directly affected by this cruel policy before making this rash decision; Not those with lived experiences; not organizations that support these communities; and not nurses or health-care professionals who know first-hand what must be done to help some of the most marginalized Ontarians.
If Ford and Jones had consulted any of them, they would have discovered the facts are crystal clear - supervised consumption sites save lives.
The reality is chilling. One person in Ontario dies every two and a half hours due to toxic unregulated drugs and more than 21,000 people have died from unregulated drug poisoning since 2018. An estimated 3,812 drug-related deaths occurred in 2023 and an additional 1,842 suspected drug-toxicity deaths happened in the first six months of 2024. Without supervised consumption sites, these staggering numbers will rise exponentially.
Clinical approaches to substance use need to be comprehensive and tailored to individuals. No two people who use substances are exactly alike. Quitting "cold turkey" can often be dangerous or even deadly. Mandatory treatment is neither ethical nor in alignment with best clinical practice.
Ford and Jones said this decision is about safety. But the evidence suggests these sites do not increase crime in the area surrounding them. In fact, they are linked to a reduction in neighbourhood overdoses, EMS calls and transmission of blood-borne infections such as HIV and hepatitis C. The sites are associated with less public disposal of syringes and drug use in areas visible to the public.
Cutting supervised consumption sites won't stop people from using substances. It simply stands to increase drug use in public areas like parks, sidewalks and in restaurant bathrooms, because people will have nowhere else to go.
The nurses and health-care professionals who work in supervised consumption sites provide emergency medical care, preventive and primary care, including wound care. They support clients in mental- health treatment and referrals to housing and other social supports. They educate clients on safer consumption practices and refer them to rehabilitation, detox centres and other health services if and when they're ready.
What is missing from the statistics and stigma are the very people who rely on these sites. They are mothers and uncles; sons and sisters; best friends and colleagues. They come from virtually every walk of life and are simply trying to avoid harm related to addiction and substance use.
Most of us know someone who has struggled with substance use. As nurses and health-care professionals, we advocate for their right to access life-saving, dignified, low-barrier health-care services. We know they deserve compassion and support.
People can only benefit from treatment if and when they are ready.
That's why nurses are calling on Premier Ford and his government to reverse their decision and keep supervised consumption sites open.
Ford and Jones should spend less time spreading stigmatizing views and more time listening to the nurses and health-care professionals who provide these vital services and the people who use them. Stigma never reversed an overdose.
Following the outcry at this announcement, Jones publicly promised that people would not die because of her government's policy decision.
Unfortunately, the facts tell us otherwise.
Erin Ariss is a registered nurse and provincial president of the Ontario Nurses' Association. Hannah Stahl is a registered nurse and bargaining unit president of the Ontario Nurses' Association.
CAPTION:

CREDIT: Erin Ariss and Hannah Stahl Erin Ariss is a registered nurse and provincial president of the Ontario Nurses' Association. Hannah Stahl is a registered nurse and bargaining unit president of the Ontario Nurses' Association.;

Document 64 of 500

Mountain MPP Taylor plans to run in the next federal election: New Democrat has represented the riding in the Ontario legislature since 2011

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/mountain-mpp-taylor-plans-run-next-federal/docview/3102303431/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Mountain+MPP+Taylor+plans+to+run+in+the+next+federal+election%3A+New+Democrat+has+represented+the+riding+in+the+Ontario+legislature+since+2011&amp;title=The+Spectator&amp;issn=11899417&amp;date=2024-09-10&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.6&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Spectator&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: A second Ontario New Democrat MPP is looking to make the leap to federal politics even though provincial parties are scrambling to nominate candidates amid expectations Premier Doug Ford will call an early election.
Monique Taylor, who has represented Hamilton Mountain in the legislature since 2011, said Monday she will seek the NDP nomination for the riding in the next federal vote.
"This decision has not come easily ... but under (Prime Minister) Justin Trudeau, things have gotten worse," Taylor, a lifelong Hamiltonian who has served as children and social services critic under Ontario New Democrat Leader Marit Stiles, told supporters.
"Seniors who have built our communities are not living the retirement dream they had expected. The cost of living is eating up more and more of their pension cheques ... and they live in terror of needing long-term care."
Hamilton Mountain is held by Liberal MP Lisa Hepfner, a former television reporter for CHCH, who was elected in 2021.
"It's good to see there are still women willing to run in this climate," Hepfner said from Nanaimo, B.C., where the Liberal caucus is meeting this week.
"I see Monique at community events all the time. I don't think it will be acrimonious."
Taylor's move comes just days after federal NDP Leader Jagmeet Singh ended an agreement to support Trudeau's Liberals in the minority Parliament, which could result in a federal election earlier than one slated for October 2025.
Two months ago, Ottawa Centre New Democrat MPP Joel Harden signalled he will seek the federal party's nomination in his riding, now represented by Liberal MP Yasir Naqvi, a former Ontario cabinet minster who last year ran for the provincial Liberal leadership won by Bonnie Crombie.
"The stakes couldn't be higher," Harden said as he announced his bid and set up a website for it. "The climate crisis, the cost of living trap, and Canadians' trust in government all need a community approach."
Should they win the nominations, neither Taylor nor Harden would have to resign as MPPs until a federal election is called.
There is a major difference in salaries for serving in the federal and Ontario parliaments, with MPP's wages frozen since 2008.
MPs earn a base salary of $203,100 versus $116,500 for MPPs, who do not have a pension plan - unlike their federal counterparts.
CAPTION: NDP MPP Monique Taylor is shown in the Ontario legislature on Feb. 20, 2019.Andrew Francis Wallace Toronto Star file photo

CREDIT: Rob Ferguson

Document 65 of 500

Hamilton Health Sciences short cash third year in a row: Union sounds the alarm about mounting deficits at area hospitals

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/hamilton-health-sciences-short-cash-third-year/docview/3102303252/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Hamilton+Health+Sciences+short+cash+third+year+in+a+row%3A+Union+sounds+the+alarm+about+mounting+deficits+at+area+hospitals&amp;title=The+Spectator&amp;issn=11899417&amp;date=2024-09-10&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.1&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Spectator&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Hamilton's largest hospital network was short $20.4 million at the end of the fiscal year.
It is the third year in a row that Hamilton Health Sciences (HHS) has been in the red on its roughly $1.6-billion budget come March 31.
"Funding is the lowest for hospitals (in Ontario) of any provincial government across Canada and that has driven hospital deficits," said Doug Allan, senior researcher at the Canadian Union of Public Employees (CUPE). "We have a very large deficit at Hamilton Health Sciences ... and that's happening at a lot of hospitals around Ontario. It's driving scrimping, cost-savings, cuts to the quality of care."
CUPE leaders were in Hamilton recently to bring attention to the shortfalls that a number of area hospitals have racked up for the fiscal year - April 1, 2023, to March 31, 2024 - and sound the alarm about the funding that is needed to maintain services.
Brant Community Healthcare System was short nearly $2 million on its roughly $220-million operating budget. The hospital network that includes Brantford General Hospital and the Willett in Paris also finished the fiscal year that ended March 31, 2023, with a deficit of $1.5 million.
Norfolk General Hospital in Simcoe was short just over $1.5 million and West Haldimand General Hospital in Hagersville was nearly $162,000 in the red on their combined operating budget of $84.5 million. It is the third year in a row that West Haldimand has had a deficit after being short about $2.7 million in 2022-23 and just over $241,000 the year before that.
Haldimand War Memorial Hospital in Dunnville has also been short for the last three fiscal years - nearly $991,000 as of March 31, nearly $409,000 the year before and just over $23,000 the year before that.
"We are looking for the government to step up significantly," said Michael Hurley, president of CUPE's Ontario Council of Hospital Unions. "The provincial government really has such a lethargic approach to what many people would say is a crisis in acute care."
Hospitals are supposed to balance their books according to accountability agreements they sign with Ontario Health, which is the super agency the province created to oversee the health-care system. But so many of them are in the red that the Ontario Hospital Association (OHA) said in February that the requirement has either been waived or the hospital boards have put conditions on the agreements.
CUPE has estimated that Ontario hospitals need $2 billion more in funding to maintain current levels of service, including $118 million for HHS and $50 million for St. Joseph's.
A spokesperson for Ontario Health Minister Sylvia Jones said the province has been putting money into the health-care system along with other solutions.
"Our government is taking bold, innovative action to connect you to the care you need when you need it," spokesperson Hannah Jensen said in a statement.
"Under the leadership of Premier (Doug) Ford, our government has made record investments in our health-care system, investing over $85 billion this year alone, a 31 per cent increase from 2018."
HHS - like many of the hospitals - did not alert the public about its mounting deficits that were $31.1 million for the fiscal year that ended March 31, 2023, and $900,000 for the year before. It's $20.4-million shortfall for 2023-24 was less than the $57.4-million deficit its board of directors approved.
"HHS is continuing to collaborate with the Government of Ontario to balance our budget while ensuring our patients have access to the care they need," the hospital network said in a statement.
The latest deficit at HHS appeared to be primarily driven by settlements related to provincial wage suppression legislation - Bill 124 - that was found unconstitutional by an Ontario court in 2022 and was repealed by the government in February.
HHS estimated retroactive salaries, benefits and other costs to be just over $19.1 million in the last fiscal year from the failed legislation that restricted public-sector wage increases to one per cent a year for three years.
The OHA advised the province in December that retroactive payments from Bill 124 were costing Ontario's hospitals more than $1 billion, with only partial reimbursement from the Conservative government.
"It remains unclear to CUPE that the government will 100 per cent fund the need created by the failure of the Bill 124," stated a research report on Ontario's hospitals by the union. "As a result, hospital deficits may become even larger this year."
St. Joseph's Healthcare has already received extra provincial funding to offset the fallout of Bill 124 and to cover some other costs, particularly for surgery, that helped it end the fiscal year with a surplus of $178,211 instead of the $46.4-million shortfall approved by the board of governors. However, the board has approved a deficit of $24.3 million for this fiscal year that started April 1 on its roughly $800-million budget.
"St. Joe's is working with Ontario Health and the Ministry of Health to address its long-term fiscal sustainability," hospital president Mike Heenan said in a statement.
Joseph's Brant Hospital in Burlington also finished the fiscal year with a nearly $2.1-million surplus after falling $156,000 short the year before on its operational budget of roughly $268 million.
Overall, CUPE says the province is falling behind the rest of Canada when it comes to hospital funding with its report showing Ontario spent the least per capita of any province on hospitals in 2023.
"Ontario is experiencing an unprecedented, hospital capacity problem," Allan said. "Hospital capacity is falling behind the growth in need. The Ford government plans for the future, and especially in the next few years, are totally inadequate and we'll see the situation become worse."
Joanna Frketich is a reporter at The hamilton Spectator.
jfrketich@thespec.com
CAPTION:

CREDIT: Joanna Frketich Reporter Joanna Frketich is a reporter at The hamilton Spectator. jfrketich@thespec.com

Document 66 of 500

Doug Ford says Jagmeet Singh is a 'greedy politician' who won't topple Trudeau government

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/doug-ford-says-jagmeet-singh-is-greedy-politician/docview/3102543073/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Doug+Ford+says+Jagmeet+Singh+is+a+%27greedy+politician%27+who+won%27t+topple+Trudeau+government&amp;title=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-09-09&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Premier Doug Ford says the threat of an imminent federal election does not alter any plans for a potential provincial vote next year.
Blasting NDP Leader Jagmeet Singh, who last week dramatically ended his supply and confidence agreement with Prime Minister Justin Trudeau's minority Liberal government, Ford predicted there would be no snap election in Ottawa.
"As far as I'm concerned, MP Singh is just blowing smoke," the premier told reporters during a campaign-style swing through Prince Edward County, where there's a Sept. 19 byelection.
"There's nothing more important to these greedy politicians than collecting a pension and he was elected on ... Oct. 19, 2019 and I'll be floored and I'll stand up here and apologize to all the greedy politicians if he calls the election early," said Ford.
"But let's face it, he wants his pension. He's not going anywhere until ... Oct. 20, 2025 because there's him and a whole bunch of other politicians that want to grab the taxpayers' money," he said, referring to the six-year threshold for federal MPs to qualify for a pension.
"So my bet is, he's not doing anything. He's just putting on a big show like he has for the last number of years there."
Singh dismissed the pension claim as "a ludicrous suggestion" in an interview Thursday with Vassy Kapelos on CTV's Power Play.
"I'm obviously going to get re-elected in my riding (of Burnaby South), I'm not worried about that at all. This is a silly argument being brought about by Conservatives," the NDP leader said.
Ford - who is ruminating about holding an Ontario election next spring, one year ahead of the scheduled June 2026 vote - has worked closely with Trudeau's Liberals since the COVID-19 pandemic began.
He emphasized "we haven't made that decision yet" about a 2025 Ontario election.
A federal campaign within the next six months, however, could complicate things for his Progressive Conservatives, who have been in power since 2018.
"I'll tell you, we aren't calling an election this year - and we're going to make sure that we continue with prosperity and creating jobs and opportunities for the people of Ontario," said Ford.
"We're going to continue giving opportunities to the regions just like this one," he said.
His comments came as he announced the province was giving the region up to $18.3 million toward waterworks infrastructure that will help build 3,895 new homes in Prince Edward County.
The cash is part of a $970 million "housing-enabling water systems fund" that Ford has been touting all summer across Ontario.
Voters in the riding of Bay of Quinte will be headed to the polls next week to elect a successor to Todd Smith, who resigned suddenly as education minister on Aug. 16 to take an executive job with nuclear power company CANDU.
To try to retain a seat the Tories have held since 2011, the governing party is fielding small businessman Tyler Allsopp, a Belleville city councillor.
Bonnie Crombie's Liberals have tapped fellow city councillor Sean Kelly, who is also a prominent radio morning show host.
Kelly has warned Belleville, which declared a state of emergency last February after 13 drug overdose calls to 911 in a one-hour period, needs more help from Queen's Park on coping with the opioid crisis.
Marit Stiles's New Democrats are running school board trustee Amanda Robertson, who said Monday that Ford's Tories are failing students in the riding.
"Students are missing school because there aren't enough (bus) drivers to get them there," said Robertson.
"Our students, teachers, and communities deserve much better than this. We need to fix this broken system, and we need urgent investment in our schools. At the doors, people are telling me they want better."
Respiratory therapist Lori Borthwick is the Green candidate.
Last Wednesday, Singh jolted the national political landscape when he ripped up the accord to prop up the Liberals that had been in place since 2022.
In exchange for supporting the Grits in confidence votes, the NDP extracted government commitments on dental care, pharmacare and anti-scab legislation.
Ford's outburst against Singh comes despite their storied shared history.
Seven years ago, the two appeared together on the most popular episode of the TVO series Political Blind Date, riding bicycles and sharing breakfast in a diner in Ford's riding.
CAPTION: Jagmeet Singh and Doug Ford appeared together in an episode of the TVO series Political Blind Date in 2017.

Another New Democrat MPP will try to run in the next federal election

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/another-new-democrat-mpp-will-try-run-next/docview/3102543016/se-2?accountid=15090
