Document 68 of 500

'Too preachy': Liberal MPs admit some of their constituents want Justin Trudeau gone

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/too-preachy-liberal-mps-admit-some-their/docview/3102303846/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=%27Too+preachy%27%3A+Liberal+MPs+admit+some+of+their+constituents+want+Justin+Trudeau+gone&amp;title=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-09-09&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: NANAIMO, B.C. - It's become too obvious to hide. Liberal MPs publicly acknowledged Monday that some of their constituents want Prime Minister Justin Trudeau to go. Using words like "too preachy" and "tired," a handful of MPs told the Star there is a clear appetite for change, and some worry the prime minister's unpopularity will affect their own bids for re-election.
There's "a sentiment of exasperation, I would say, in many people," offered Quebec MP Alexandra Mendès. "A lot of the comments bordered on, 'We are sick and tired of being preached to.'"
But while Mendès said Trudeau's presence is "not helpful," she and other Liberal MPs who are gathering for a caucus retreat this week in Nanaimo stopped short of calling for the prime minister to step down as their leader. "I personally don't think he should go, but my constituents definitely do, and that is the message they've been asking me to convey," she said.
"Some out there in the community certainly believe it's time for him to move on," said Nova Scotia MP Kody Blois. "There's others that actually think he needs to stay on the job."
"I still believe that we have the best plan to present to Canadians," offered Ontario MP Francis Drouin, "but I think people have lost an emotional connection to the prime minister."
The calls for Trudeau to go are unlikely to lead to a full blowout during the Liberals' three days of meetings, and the prime minister has made it clear he plans to stick around for another term. But there are now more MPs publicly willing to say what everyone has been hearing for the past year, and it may lead to more fireworks and tougher conversations behind closed doors this week.
Behind the scenes, there are also questions being asked about a surprise guest of honour - former Bank of Canada governor Mark Carney, who, as the Star reported Friday, has been tapped to advise Trudeau and the Liberal party on economic matters. He is expected to brief MPs Tuesday morning.
Carney's presence ruffled a few cabinet ministers last week, who were perplexed as to why Trudeau had asked a likely leadership rival to show up. Had the prime minister called in the big gun because he had little faith in his own front bench? Was he designating Carney his dauphin? Was this the beginning of the jockeying over leadership? Why was Carney going to Nanaimo? These were questions asked over and over again.
Attuned to their anxiety, Carney called a handful of ministers over the weekend, hoping to smooth some feathers.
Trudeau's team hopes Carney's presence demonstrates to Canadians that the prime minister can still attract big names to the Liberal fold - that while the news might be about staff departures, impressive Canadians like Carney are willing to get engaged with the Grits and lend the party their brand's cache.
For his part, the Carney calculations appear to be focused on dispelling any notion that he backed away from the party after being offered the role of finance minister - and yes, although no one will publicly say it, it's also about leadership positioning.
A Liberal party statement announcing his new role as chair of the Liberal "leader's task force on economic growth" billed Carney as a "globally acclaimed Canadian economist," "a public policy leader," a "sustainability advocate, and author." It said Carney would help develop and shape ideas for the next phase of Canada's strategy for near- and longer-term economic growth and productivity and help the party create a "pragmatic, focused, and high-impact vision for Canada's economic success."
The Liberals plan to send Carney on a cross-country tour to hear from Canadians in different sectors, and then have him report back to its election platform committee.
It's easy to see why those around the cabinet table who want Trudeau's job might be miffed by this challenger's exposure.
Reaction from MPs to Carney's presence in Nanaimo was mixed.
"Whatever. I mean, we'll see what he does. I don't think he's going to be the messiah," said Drouin.
Several fear Carney is too much of a technocrat - that he resembles former leader Michael Ignatieff - and that they would flounder under his leadership. Others looked forward to Tuesday's presentation and his ability to respond to media questions afterward.
"There's been a lot of talk about Mark Carney in one way or another, so it's great that he's making time to come out and share his thoughts with us," said Ontario MP Ruby Sahota.
The only parliamentarian who seemed agitated by Carney's presence was Conservative House leader Andrew Scheer, who flew all the way out to Nanaimo to tell reporters that "it doesn't matter what happens at this retreat."
"It doesn't matter who is leading the Liberal party ... It doesn't matter what kind of paint they slap on the Liberal brand," Scheer insisted. Neither Carney nor any other Liberal cabinet minister would change the direction the country is headed in now, he claimed.
If Scheer hoped to convince reporters that his party isn't worried about a Liberal leadership change, he accomplished the opposite. It's no secret that Conservative Leader Pierre Poilievre and NDP Leader Jagmeet Singh would rather run against Trudeau than against another Liberal who could represent change from a tired brand .
Change is what Liberal MPs have on their minds, whether that's changing the prime minister or changing communication strategy - something they've long griped about. (It's also far easier to champion that than calling for the leader to go, and plunging the party into disunity.)
"Right now," said Blois, change "would be the prospect of a Pierre Poilievre prime ministership, and there's a lot of my constituents that are concerned about the direction he would take the country."
Blois, Drouin and others want the party to focus Canadians' attention on Poilievre's record. There is a lot of material to mine from his disparaging comments about Indigenous Peoples to his work on the so-called Fair Elections Act.
And on that front, the team around Trudeau is listening.
Their major challenge, however, is financing - how much money to spend to get bang for their buck when their opponents have so much more than they do. So far this year, the Conservatives have raised $20.53 million, nearly three times more than the Liberals, with $6.87 million, from nearly twice as many donors.
Two years ago, the Grits shied away from spending money. Now, with an election on the horizon, their calculations may be shifting.
CAPTION: Liberals convened in Nanaimo to strategize for the upcoming election year. Still stinging from a devastating byelection loss earlier this summer, the caucus is now also reeling from news that its national campaign director has resigned and the party can no longer count on the NDP to stave off an early election. (Sep. 9, 2024 / The Canadian Press)Prime Minister Justin Trudeau answers reporters' questions following an announcement in Rocky Harbour, Nfld., on Sept. 4, 2024.

Document 69 of 500

'I've never seen anything like it': The behind-the-scenes attempts to unite against the right in a Toronto byelection

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/ive-never-seen-anything-like-behind-scenes/docview/3102058390/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=%27I%27ve+never+seen+anything+like+it%27%3A+The+behind-the-scenes+attempts+to+unite+against+the+right+in+a+Toronto+byelection&amp;title=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-09-09&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: An ex-Ontario premier and a former Toronto mayor could play pivotal roles in an upcoming city council byelection, amid concerns from some left-leaning insiders that a trio of candidates with Liberal connections are on track to split the vote and give a controversial conservative a path to victory.
Although voters won't go to the polls to elect a new councillor in Don Valley West until Nov. 4, the race is already being shaped by behind-the-scenes manoeuvring featuring Kathleen Wynne and John Tory, the Star has learned.
Wynne, the former Liberal premier who represented Don Valley West provincially for nearly two decades, confirmed she has spoken with three candidates with links to the Grits about the importance of rallying around a single challenger to block right-wing pundit Anthony Furey from winning the seat.
Meanwhile, the Star has confirmed that representatives of both Furey and Rachel Chernos Lin, the Toronto District School Board chair who is also running in Don Valley West, have reached out to Tory seeking his endorsement.
Observers say the swirling discussions involving local political heavyweights are unusual in municipal byelections, which are normally low-key affairs.
"There's so much jockeying ... I've never seen anything like it," said one insider who isn't directly involved in the race, and who like other sources spoke on the condition of anonymity to discuss private conversations.
The seat in Ward 15, Don Valley West was left empty in May after the death of longtime representative Jaye Robinson. As of Friday, 14 candidates had signed up to replace her. While political parties play no official role in municipal elections, at least three contenders have strong ties to the federal or provincial Liberals.
Dhruv Jain, who is on leave from the office of the TTC chair, is executive vice president of the Don Valley West Provincial Liberal Association, and worked as executive assistant to Stephanie Bowman, the Liberal MPP for the riding.
Evan Sambasivam recently left his job as a federal Liberal government staffer, and his campaign is being managed by Alex Keys, Ontario field manager for the Liberal party of Canada. Sambasivam is also the nephew of former Ontario Liberal deputy premier Deb Matthews.
Chernos Lin has had help from Mandy Moore, former executive director of the Ontario Liberal party, while Jane Rounthwaite, a principal at the Osborne Group consultant firm and Wynne's partner, is chairing her fundraising efforts.
In an interview with the Star, Wynne said she had spoken to the three candidates about her concern that Furey could take advantage of the crowded field. Bowman, who has announced her support for Chernos Lin, has held similar talks with the candidates, according to sources.
Furey, a former Toronto Sun columnist and editorial director of a right-wing charity, has faced accusations of Islamophobia over his columns about the threat of what he called "political Islam," as well as criticism for his ties to anti-trans activists. He placed fourth in last year's mayoral byelection.
Wynne said she was concerned Furey's "right-wing ideology would not be helpful" to residents in Ward 15, and claimed he wasn't interested in representing the community but hopes to use the council seat as a platform for another mayoral run.
Furey said he wasn't paying attention to conversations among his opponents, but voters want a representative who can address local issues like rising car thefts, and would be turned off by insiders "brokering backroom deals."
He rejected the accusations of Islamophobia and transphobia against him as "out-of-context smears." Asked whether he sees the Ward 15 seat merely as a stepping stone to the mayor's office, Furey said he couldn't rule out contesting the 2026 mayoral election but for now was focused on Don Valley West.
According to Jain's campaign, Wynne asked their candidate not to sign up for the byelection. Wynne gave a different account, telling the Star she respects anyone who decides to run for office, and it would be an "overstatement" to say she had asked any of the Liberal-linked candidates to step aside. The deadline to sign up for or withdraw from the byelection is 2 p.m. on Sept. 19.
But Wynne said she hoped "there will be a clear, progressive, candidate who will emerge" soon. She said she has yet to make a public statement about who that candidate should be, but described Chernos Lin as having a higher profile than Jain or Sambasivam, and suggested she could make an endorsement in the "next couple of weeks."
Chernos Lin told the Star she's spoken to Wynne and Bowman "about me being the strongest candidate," and is expecting the former premier's endorsement. She said she hadn't enlisted Wynne and Bowman to try to push Jain or Sambasivam out.
"I think people are endorsing me because they believe in me, because they've seen I have a proven track record of getting things done," said Chernos Lin, who has been chair of the TDSB for two years and a trustee for six. Her platform includes making the ward safer and more walkable.
Jain and Sambasivam told the Star they are out campaigning every day and don't intend to step aside.
"It would be very cynical to ask the most energetic, hardworking campaign to drop out ... And I don't think that Kathleen and Stephanie are cynical people," said Sambasivam, who is promising to tackle gridlock and housing issues in the ward.
"I have a lot of respect for Kathleen Wynne," said Jain. "But I'm the one who's been at the doors every day, and both my neighbours and I know that I'm the best candidate to represent Don Valley West." He has vowed to promote family-sized housing options and improve public safety.
Multiple sources told the Star that both Chernos Lin's and Furey's camps have put out feelers to Tory seeking his support.
The former mayor, who resigned in February 2023 after admitting to an inappropriate relationship with a former staffer, won 72 per cent of the vote in Don Valley West in the 2022 mayoral election. His endorsement could go a long way to either cementing Chernos Lin as the most viable left-leaning candidate - particularly if paired with Wynne's support - or vaulting Furey into a prime position.
In a text message, Tory said he hadn't "made any plans or decisions on involvement in the byelection at this point."
Other candidates on the ballot include architect and environmental advocate Sheena Sharp, food and lifestyle educator Lesley Stoyan and international development consultant Habiba Desai.
With files from David Rider

Document 70 of 500

Hamilton Health Sciences short cash for third year in a row

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/hamilton-health-sciences-short-cash-third-year/docview/3102058178/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Hamilton+Health+Sciences+short+cash+for+third+year+in+a+row&amp;title=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-09-09&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Hamilton's largest hospital network was short $20.4 million at the end of the fiscal year.
It is the third year in a row that Hamilton Health Sciences (HHS) has been in the red on its roughly $1.6-billion budget come March 31.
"Funding is the lowest for hospitals (in Ontario) of any provincial government across Canada and that has driven hospital deficits," said Doug Allan, senior researcher at the Canadian Union of Public Employees (CUPE). "We have a very large deficit at Hamilton Health Sciences ... and that's happening at a lot of hospitals around Ontario. It's driving scrimping, cost-savings, cuts to the quality of care."
CUPE leaders were in Hamilton recently to bring attention to the shortfalls that a number of area hospitals have racked up for the fiscal year - April 1, 2023, to March 31, 2024 - and sound the alarm about the funding that is needed to maintain services.
Brant Community Healthcare System was short nearly $2 million on its roughly $220-million operating budget. The hospital network that includes Brantford General Hospital and the Willett in Paris also finished the fiscal year that ended March 31, 2023, with a deficit of $1.5 million.
Norfolk General Hospital in Simcoe was short just over $1.5 million and West Haldimand General Hospital in Hagersville was nearly $162,000 in the red on their combined operating budget of $84.5 million. It is the third year in a row that West Haldimand has had a deficit after being short about $2.7 million in 2022-23 and just over $241,000 the year before that.
Haldimand War Memorial Hospital in Dunnville has also been short for the last three fiscal years - nearly $991,000 as of March 31, nearly $409,000 the year before and just over $23,000 the year before that.
"We are looking for the government to step up significantly," said Michael Hurley, president of CUPE's Ontario Council of Hospital Unions. "The provincial government really has such a lethargic approach to what many people would say is a crisis in acute care."
Hospitals are supposed to balance their books according to accountability agreements they sign with Ontario Health, which is the super agency the province created to oversee the health-care system. But so many of them are in the red that the Ontario Hospital Association (OHA) said in February that the requirement has either been waived or the hospital boards have put conditions on the agreements.
CUPE has estimated that Ontario hospitals need $2 billion more in funding to maintain current levels of service, including $118 million for HHS and $50 million for St. Joseph's.
A spokesperson for Ontario Health Minister Sylvia Jones said the province has been putting money into the health-care system along with other solutions.
"Our government is taking bold, innovative action to connect you to the care you need when you need it," spokesperson Hannah Jensen said in a statement. "Under the leadership of Premier (Doug) Ford, our government has made record investments in our health-care system, investing over $85 billion this year alone, a 31 per cent increase from 2018."
HHS - like many of the hospitals - did not alert the public about its mounting deficits that were $31.1 million for the fiscal year that ended March 31, 2023, and $900,000 for the year before. It's $20.4-million shortfall for 2023-24 was less than the $57.4-million deficit its board of directors approved.
"HHS is continuing to collaborate with the Government of Ontario to balance our budget while ensuring our patients have access to the care they need," the hospital network said in a statement.
The latest deficit at HHS appeared to be primarily driven by settlements related to provincial wage suppression legislation - Bill 124 - that was found unconstitutional by an Ontario court in 2022 and was repealed by the government in February.
HHS estimated retroactive salaries, benefits and other costs to be just over $19.1 million in the last fiscal year from the failed legislation that restricted public-sector wage increases to one per cent a year for three years.
The OHA advised the province in December that retroactive payments from Bill 124 were costing Ontario's hospitals more than $1 billion, with only partial reimbursement from the Conservative government.
"It remains unclear to CUPE that the government will 100 per cent fund the need created by the failure of the Bill 124," stated a research report on Ontario's hospitals by the union. "As a result, hospital deficits may become even larger this year."
St. Joseph's Healthcare doesn't mention Bill 124 in its financial statements that show it ended the fiscal year with a surplus of $178,211. The hospital network didn't provide comment about how it turned around a $46.4-million shortfall approved for the fiscal year by its board of governors on its roughly $775-million budget.
Joseph's Brant Hospital in Burlington also finished the fiscal year with a nearly $2.1-million surplus after falling $156,000 short the year before on its operational budget of roughly $268 million.
Overall, CUPE says the province is falling behind the rest of Canada when it comes to hospital funding with its report showing Ontario spent the least per capita of any province on hospitals in 2023.
"Ontario is experiencing an unprecedented, hospital capacity problem," Allan said. "Hospital capacity is falling behind the growth in need. The Ford government plans for the future, and especially in the next few years, are totally inadequate and we'll see the situation become worse."
Joanna Frketich is a reporter at The Spectator. jfrketich@thespec.com
CAPTION: Michael Hurley is president of CUPE's Ontario Council of Hospital Unions.Michael Hurley, left, and Doug Allan of the the Canadian Union of Public Employees (CUPE) are sounding the alarm about the funding that is needed to maintain hospital services.Doug Allan is a senior researcher at the Canadian Union of Public Employees (CUPE).

Document 71 of 500

'He's not getting the message': Justin Trudeau shrugs off the naysayers as he heads into a Liberal caucus retreat

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/hes-not-getting-message-justin-trudeau-shrugs-off/docview/3102058160/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=%27He%27s+not+getting+the+message%27%3A+Justin+Trudeau+shrugs+off+the+naysayers+as+he+heads+into+a+Liberal+caucus+retreat&amp;title=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-09-09&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: OTTAWA - The summer ride is over. So too is the Justin Trudeau listening tour.
The prime minister spent much of two months meeting in small groups or individually with nervous Liberal MPs, many angry after the June loss of a supposedly safe Toronto seat to the Conservatives.
Trudeau also spent two months running, paddling, boxing and bounding from one summer festival to another, taking thousands of selfies with Canadians who appeared not to have read polls about how disliked he is.
So what did the prime minister take away from it all?
His practiced answers at a cabinet retreat and again this week after his co-operation deal with the NDP fell apart show clearly he does not believe he is the problem.
Rather, Trudeau believes he is the solution.
In public and in private, Trudeau has expressed his determination to stay at the helm of his party through to the next election.
And he believes he now has most of his caucus behind him.
The Star spoke to more than a dozen Liberals inside and outside the Prime Minister's Office, cabinet and caucus, and agreed to requests they not be identified in order to gauge where they believe the prime minister's head is at.
All said Trudeau plans to stay the course.
Not all are thrilled with that.
But most said Trudeau is newly energized for the fight ahead. And the death of the NDP co-operation deal doesn't bother the prime minister or many of those around him.
If anything, after his summer listening tour, Trudeau is, surprisingly, "more emboldened," said one Liberal.
Another said, "I think there is a path to victory that he's confident about. Obviously it's not what it was in 2015 by any stretch of the imagination, but it's there, and I don't think anyone doubts his capacity to win the last two weeks of the campaign."
Trudeau, several said, is deeply concerned about the rise of a right-wing populism that threatens Canadian social programs and cohesion. He sees Conservative Leader Pierre Poilievre as an agent of that.
Some said Trudeau believes he must stay on to counter Poilievre, even in the face of polls showing he is leading the Liberal party to likely disaster.
"He's not getting the message. He thinks he's the only one who can fight for the progressive agenda in Canada against the big, evil Conservatives," said one.
Some Liberals genuinely believe Trudeau is the best leader the party's got for that fight, and all they need to do in the months ahead is a better job of prosecuting the case against Poilievre.
They and the prime minister are relying on a conviction that the more Canadians see of Poilievre, the more they will dislike him.
Trudeau also thinks that most of the negative spin about his leadership comes from "groupthink" in the Ottawa press gallery.
That there is an unending stream of negative stories is certainly true, because there is a seemingly endless stream of polls documenting the mood of Canadians.
So what has Trudeau's own outreach taught him?
The prime minister has often talked in the past about how he discounts the excesses of both his "haters" and admirers.
Yet after a bruising early summer, where MPs spoke openly about their concerns to reporters (although only one, New Brunswick's Wayne Long, agreed to be identified), Trudeau seems to have refuelled his tank on the encouragement he encountered in crowds.
From the Vancouver Pride parade to the St. John's Regatta, Trudeau has told those around him that people were eager and happy to meet "the prime minister" not least because he brings "the celebrity factor."
The son of a former prime minister who grew up in the public spotlight, he creates a buzz most places he shows up.
Yet one Liberal cautioned it's not an indication Canadians want him to stay. "He confuses the selfie with support and it's not," they said. "Some of it may be support, but the selfie is largely celebrity-driven and not politically driven."
A clear sign that Trudeau's public relations and security staff worried he would encounter protesters were Trudeau's carefully sanitized public itineraries. Early in the summer, his office stopped issuing timely public notices of where the prime minister would be, sometimes concealing Trudeau's whereabouts until after an event was over.
And there was one telling, unscripted moment in Trudeau's summer travels.
In Sault Ste-Marie, a steelworker declined to shake Trudeau's hand, complained about taxes, the lack of a family doctor, the cost of dental care and about the "lazy neighbour" who doesn't work but lives the same life he, the ordinary worker, does.
Trudeau engaged the man and quickly tried to talk his way out of the awkward encounter, but the worker wanted no part of it and looked forward to Trudeau not being around "in another year."
It's a strategy: talking your way through difficult meetings, and talking over your critics.
It's one some say Trudeau has employed in caucus and in other tense meetings.
Whether it's the strategy the Liberal leader intends to employ at this week's national caucus retreat in Nanaimo - the first face-to-face gathering of all Liberal MPs since the loss of Toronto-St. Paul's - could determine whether his MPs think Trudeau has truly listened.
It may also determine what face they put on as they emerge before the cameras.
One senior government official said the Nanaimo caucus meeting is important because people will compare notes, and express their views very directly to the prime minister, the PMO, and to each other. Yet that same Liberal - along with others - is convinced the vast majority of the caucus is united behind Trudeau leading them into the next campaign, and will be focused on how to win that election, not on leadership changes.
So will Trudeau offer a major course correction - say, on the Middle East, the economic direction of the country, or the carbon pricing regime that has caused him endless political headaches? Don't expect that, said several.
Trudeau plans to stay on the same track, even if the prospect of a federal election before October 2025 suddenly looms large. Certainly, Trudeau's favourite catchphrase is, "We will continue to ..."
At the cabinet retreat, Industry Minister Francois-Philippe Champagne summed up the strategy: "Deliver, deliver, deliver."
In an interview Friday, Champagne said, "We just need to double down and focus more on delivering and let others play their political game."
Canadians still feel "a lot of anxiety" as they look for economic relief with lower inflation and interest rates in sight, he said. "People have little appetite for the kind of spectacle you see in Ottawa, of gamesmanship and political play and, you know, House tactics and all that. They say, 'Listen, get on with it. You've been elected. Just do it.'"
Even Trudeau's ardent supporters say the path forward is set.
"The biggest course correction would be him - him leaving," said one.
And that is not something anyone is predicting, for now.
CAPTION: Prime Minister Justin Trudeau visits with city transit workers during a scheduled event at a transit facility in Sault Ste.-Marie on Aug.29, 2024.

Document 72 of 500

Government policy may not be insane, but it sure lacks imagination

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/government-policy-may-not-be-insane-sure-lacks/docview/3102058080/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Government+policy+may+not+be+insane%2C+but+it+sure+lacks+imagination&amp;title=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-09-09&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: With NHL training camps opening soon, I'll likely resume listening to sports talk radio.
For anyone who has spent any time tuning in to this genre, you'll be familiar with the testosterone-fuelled banter, hyperbolic speculation and cliché-laden rhetoric.
Ear candy for any hockey fan.
Among the most frequently enunciated clichés is one that goes something like this: "the definition of insanity is doing the same thing over and over again and expecting a different result." It gets passed off as a witty rejoinder, and is often directed at people making foolish decisions. Foolish at least according to the jocks on sports radio.
I hear this statement often and it never sits right with me. It sounds too simplistic.
I used to work in community mental health, and I can't remember it being used in any clinical setting. Nor do I recall any of the clients I worked with, even those with debilitating mental illness, "doing the same thing over and over again and expecting a different result."
It turns out that when you Google its origin, it is attributed to Albert Einstein who, not only apparently never said it, practised physics and not psychiatry.
I thought of this as I reflected on what appears to me to be a complete lack of imagination on the part of our federal and provincial governments when it comes to solving some of the serious problems we face.
Far be it from me to suggest the people in charge - or their ideas - are insane, but they do seem intent on rolling out the same old tired policies. The question is: 'do they really expect a different result?'
This seems to be the case for housing and income support policy at the provincial level, and economic policy federally.
Let's start with housing. In 1995, the government of Progressive Conservative Premier Mike Harris completely transformed public housing policy. According to a 2015 edition of On Policy magazine, the Tories "terminated Ontario's affordable housing programs and dumped the cost of running provincial social housing onto municipalities."
Thirty years on, we have a homelessness crisis and cities struggling to manage the costs.
To say nothing of the outcry from the public over rising municipal taxes and debris strewn streets. And yet the signals from the current Conservative government suggest more of the same failed policies.
On the matter of income support, the attack against welfare recipients intensified under the Harris government. Eligibility requirements were tightened, rates were effectively slashed and there were no increases for the two, full-term duration of the Harris government.
The current Tory administration has taken a page from the Harris playbook.
OntarioWorks rates have remained the same since Doug Ford took office in 2018, at levels less than half the official poverty level. A truly dismal performance. Like the aforementioned housing policy, there is no appetite from the current Conservative government to depart from its chosen path of doing the same thing over and over again regarding income support.
The result? Widespread despair and growing wealth and income inequality.
At the federal level, successive Liberal and Tory governments have stayed the neo-liberal course for four decades. Top bracket income and corporate tax rates are well below those of the post Second World War-era, a time often nostalgically cited across the political spectrum as a golden age.
Also a time when income inequality was relatively low, fledgling social programs robust and activist government the norm. All that has been abandoned in favour of a neo-liberal creed of privatization, deregulation and top bracket tax reductions.
For the past 40 years, federal governments have been doing the same thing over and over again with underwhelming results.
The stakes of behaving this way in the sports world are low; your favourite team might miss the playoffs. In the real world, this insanity from our federal and provincial governments is sleepwalking us toward dystopia.
CAPTION: Former Premier Mike Harris's policies were devastating and yet the signals from the current Conservative government suggest more of the same is coming, Tyler Firth writes.
CREDIT: Tyler Firth

Subject: Neoliberalism; Affordable housing; Income inequality; Government; Housing policy

Politicians need some imagination

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/politicians-need-some-imagination/docview/3101919177/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=unknown&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Politicians+need+some+imagination&amp;title=The+Spectator&amp;issn=11899417&amp;date=2024-09-09&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.9&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Spectator&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: With NHL training camps opening soon, I'll likely resume listening to sports talk radio.
For anyone who has spent any time tuning in to this genre, you'll be familiar with the testosterone-fuelled banter, hyperbolic speculation and cliché-laden rhetoric.
Among the most frequently enunciated clichés is one that goes something like this: "the definition of insanity is doing the same thing over and over again and expecting a different result."
I hear this statement often and it never sits right with me. It sounds too simplistic. I used to work in community mental health, and I can't remember it being used in any clinical setting. Nor do I recall any of the clients I worked with, even those with debilitating mental illness, "doing the same thing over and over again and expecting a different result."
It turns out that when you Google its origin, it is attributed to Albert Einstein who, not only apparently never said it, practised physics, not psychiatry.
I thought of this as I reflected on what appears to me to be a complete lack of imagination on the part of our federal and provincial governments when it comes to solving some of the serious problems we face.
Far be it from me to suggest the people in charge - or their ideas - are insane, but they do seem intent on rolling out the same old tired policies. The question is: "do they really expect a different result?"
This seems to be the case for housing and income support policy at the provincial level, and economic policy federally.
Let's start with housing. In 1995, the government of Progressive Conservative Premier Mike Harris transformed public housing policy. According to a 2015 edition of On Policy magazine, the Tories "terminated Ontario's affordable housing programs and dumped the cost of running provincial social housing onto municipalities."
Thirty years on, we have a homelessness crisis and cities struggling to manage the costs.
To say nothing of the outcry from the public over rising municipal taxes and debris strewn streets. And yet the signals from the current Conservative government suggest more of the same failed policies.
On the matter of income support, the attack against welfare recipients intensified under the Harris government. Eligibility requirements were tightened, rates were effectively slashed and there were no increases for the duration of the Harris government.
The current Tory administration has taken a page from the Harris playbook. OntarioWorks rates have remained the same since Doug Ford took office in 2018, at levels less than half the official poverty level. Like the aforementioned housing policy, there is no appetite from the current Conservative government to depart from its chosen path of doing the same thing over and over again regarding income support.
The result? Widespread despair and growing wealth and income inequality.
At the federal level, successive Liberal and Tory governments have stayed the neo-liberal course for four decades. Top bracket income and corporate tax rates are well below those of the post Second World War-era, a time often nostalgically cited across the political spectrum as a golden age.
Also a time when income inequality was relatively low, fledgling social programs robust and activist government the norm. All that has been abandoned in favour of a neo-liberal creed of privatization, deregulation and top bracket tax reductions.
For the past 40 years, federal governments have been doing the same thing over and over with underwhelming results.
The stakes of behaving this way in the sports world are low; your team might miss the playoffs. In the real world, this insanity from our federal and provincial governments is sleepwalking us toward dystopia.
Tyler Firth lives in Hamilton.

Document 74 of 500

Who was the biggest loser in the Liberal-NDP breakup?

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/who-was-biggest-loser-liberal-ndp-breakup/docview/3101793103/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Who+was+the+biggest+loser+in+the+Liberal-NDP+breakup%3F&amp;title=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-09-08&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: In a recurring feature, Susan Delacourt, a small-l liberal, and Matt Gurney, a small-c conservative, bring their different perspectives - and shared commitment to civil disagreement - to the political debates of the moment.
Matt Gurney: Well, that was a week. I'd hoped for a bit more of a gradual re-entry to reality as the summer wrapped up, but Jagmeet Singh and Jeremy Broadhurst, the man who will no longer be leading the next Liberal election campaign, had other plans. There is a LOT to take in here, but I'll start with a basic question: have the developments of this week cemented Pierre Poilievre's already pretty good position, or is this shakeup an opportunity for either (or both!) of the Liberals and NDP to reinvigorate themselves ahead of the next campaign, which feels closer today than it did a week ago?
Susan Delacourt: Whenever Justin Trudeau does write his memoirs, I wonder if the title will be, "Didn't See That Coming." I think we need to break this all down into component parts. In answer to your basic question, yes, this is obviously good news for the Conservatives. They get to spend the next weeks and months trying to tip Parliament into an election they would dearly love to have sooner than later. But I think they are going to have to watch their tone. Calling the NDP leader "Sellout Singh" reminds me of another politician who loves to nickname his rivals. I think as Canadians watch that spectacle in the U.S. this fall, they may develop a heightened aversion to nasty schoolyard politics.
MG: I remain skeptical of that. I think any attempt to force those comparisons just makes Poilievre look more normal by comparison. I know the Liberal party brain trust disagrees with me on that. We'll see. As for the new dynamic politically, I am weirdly the least interested in how the Liberals respond. I think it's fairly obvious what they have to do: survive. I won't be shocked if they prorogue to get through the coming byelections and then the U.S. election. I'm much more interested in the pressure campaign the Conservatives are now going to hit the Bloc and the NDP with. And what the government may be forced into conceding to prevent the Conservative campaign from working.
SD: That's an interesting idea - prorogation. That does present an opportunity for renewal of some kind, as does the need for a new campaign director. At the risk of sounding like a wild-eyed optimist, let me suggest one possible upside in the collapse of the Liberal-NDP deal.
I have heard - as have you, I'm sure - from lots of Liberals who were a bit queasy with the tilt to the left in the Trudeau government. These are the people who winced when Conservatives kept hammering away at the "Liberal-NDP coalition." I know that was coming up a lot at the doors in the St. Paul's byelection. So a little distance with the NDP may not be the worst thing in the world for the so-called blue Liberals.
MG: Maybe? I dunno. I am wary of ever underestimating Justin Trudeau. He's a very good politician. Better than Singh, as I think Singh may soon discover anew. But, honestly, I don't think Trudeau or the Liberals can meaningfully pivot, tilt, renew, reinvent or whatever. They are too baked in. They're the known quantity. I think any kind of major overhaul or shift would be really, really hard, even if they were very good at responding with deft, swift moves to developing events. And as it so happens, I think they're actually pretty objectively bad at doing those things. So yeah. I honestly just don't see that.
SD: I'd like to pause here to lament the collapse of the deal. Not because of any tactical considerations or the fact that it puts this Parliament closer to collapse - doesn't everyone love elections? - but because I thought it was one of the most interesting developments of the last couple of years. The public likes when politicians work together - witness the success of Trudeau-Doug Ford collaboration on so many fronts. The fact that Trudeau and Singh forged this agreement during the height of the convoy protest impressed me; it was a pact of stability in unstable times. I remember asking the NDP what could possibly be a breaking point. "Implementation" was the answer. Singh's in a tough spot here, it seems. He has to boast about all the things the deal did implement - pharmacare, a dental program, tougher labour laws. But now he has to say that Trudeau can't do anything?
MG: I didn't like the agreement. I think I could have liked an agreement. A coalition, a pure case-by-case bargaining before every confidence vote, or even something like a confidence-and-supply agreement - those are all legitimate. And I'd have respected the right of Messrs. Trudeau and Singh to pursue any of those arrangements. But that's in theory. In practice, I think Singh has humiliated himself. He wanted the benefits of signing an agreement to support the government for a set period of time and also the benefits of talking and acting as if he was an opposition leader. It's pure having-cake-and-eating-it-too delusion. Singh never navigated this well. Maybe that was impossible, but I think he came away looking ridiculous, and that he could have gotten all that he got without sacrificing so much pride. So it's not the idea of a confidence-and-supply agreement I object to, it was how Singh navigated it. It was genuinely mortifying to watch, and I wish we hadn't had to.
SD: The news conference last week was difficult to watch, too. The NDP leader was trying just a bit too hard to climb aboard the I-hate-Trudeau-too train. I never did hear exactly what was the straw that broke the back of this deal. It was like he came to the news conference with three lines and a keen desire to say as little as possible. What I guess we need to see next is whether this gambit pays off, either in the polls or those two byelections on Sept 16 in Quebec and Manitoba. The NDP is a contender in both. A victory in either could persuade the NDP that breaking up with the Liberals was the right thing to do. So much again for that idea of a gradual re-entry to fall.
CAPTION: Justin Trudeau and Jagmeet Singh, prepare for the start of the federal election English-language leaders' debate in Gatineau, Que., onSept. 9, 2021.

Document 75 of 500

'Too little, too late'? Trudeau government plans ad campaign to promote carbon pricing - and rebates

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/too-little-late-trudeau-government-plans-ad/docview/3101793028/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=%27Too+little%2C+too+late%27%3F+Trudeau+government+plans+ad+campaign+to+promote+carbon+pricing+-+and+rebates&amp;title=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-09-08&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: OTTAWA - The Trudeau government will launch a multimillion-dollar advertising campaign in the year ahead of the next election to promote its carbon pricing plan and the rebates it gives to Canadians, the Star has learned.
Environment and Climate Change Canada is planning a $7-million "climate literacy and action" advertising campaign that would focus on how "carbon pricing reduces emissions and benefits Canadians through the Canada Carbon Rebate," in addition to other actions to combat climate change, according to a copy of the department's advertising plan obtained by the Star through an Access to Information request.
It would mark the latest shift in the way the Trudeau Liberals communicate their flagship climate policy - which makes fossil-fuel products more expensive but encourages consumers to use less of them off with quarterly rebates - in the face of mounting Conservative attacks that label it as a driver of inflation.
The Liberals have long asserted that the rebates send more money to Canadians than the plan costs them. However, Conservative Leader Pierre Poilievre has vowed to "axe the tax" and has called for a "carbon tax election." Combined with the more significant industrial price on pollution, the Liberals' carbon pricing plan is expected to account for a third of Canada's greenhouse gas reductions by 2030, according to government estimates.
In an acknowledgment that the Conservatives have dominated the narrative, the Trudeau government this year rebranded the plan's rebates, forced banks to more clearly label payments and made a push to counter Conservative claims.
But the Liberal government had previously avoided calls urging it to use large paid advertising campaigns to make its case to Canadians.
Running advertisements would be one more way to raise awareness and boost support for the plan, a spokesperson for Environment Minister Steven Guilbeault said, pointing to public polling that suggested Canadians were more likely to know they received a rebate last March than they would have in November 2023. That same polling, however, suggested a majority of Canadians support reducing or eliminating the consumer price on carbon.
The ads will include "plain language messaging designed to cut through misinformation and disinformation that educates Canadians on the Government of Canada's climate plan," said spokesperson Kaitlin Power.
"Our plan is working, the economy is strong and we're building resilient communities."
Senior government officials had previously told the Star that advertising restrictions brought in by the Liberals after the previous Conservative government was criticized for overly partisan advertising had made it difficult to advertise the carbon pricing plan. The Trudeau government has generally spent less on advertising than the Harper government did.
In 2019, for example, a proposed Canada Revenue Agency (CRA) ad campaign was flagged by the watchdog Ad Standards as "self-congratulatory" for stating that carbon pricing was "making a cleaner economy more affordable for everyone," a key point of the Liberal argument. The CRA had spent just over $2 million on marketing what was then known as the Climate Action Incentive, which was a refundable tax credit rather than a quarterly rebate.
Any coming ad campaign will have to respect such restrictions, even though "it really dulls and blunts advertising so that it's not very effective," said Alex Marland, a political science professor at Acadia University.
It could also be seen as "too little, too late," he said.
"Usually these types of advertising campaigns need to be done at the time of the launch, not like reactive messaging," he said. "It's actually really hard to change people's minds."
Last year, the Liberals ran a similar climate literacy ad campaign, which cost $6.8 million and included a variety of media buys, including during the Super Bowl, though it did not touch on the contentious price on carbon.
The Conservative Party of Canada routinely runs ads opposing the plan, and fossil-fuel lobby groups and Conservative-led provinces like Alberta and Ontario have also advertised against carbon pricing.
CAPTION: The federal government'sflagship climate-change policy makes fossil-fuel products more expensive but encourages Canadians to use less of them off with quarterly rebates.

Subject: Rebates; Canadians; Decades; Carbon; Climate change; Literacy; Advertising campaigns; Tax elections

Document 76 of 500

How Staples Canada is pivoting to the new hybrid reality as tech replaces paper and pens

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/how-staples-canada-is-pivoting-new-hybrid-reality/docview/3101548909/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=How+Staples+Canada+is+pivoting+to+the+new+hybrid+reality+as+tech+replaces+paper+and+pens&amp;title=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-09-07&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: The pandemic forever changed how we work and learn, but this year's back to school shopping trends suggest some habits have real staying power.
Online shopping was supposed to kill in-person retail, yet 88 per cent of parents planned to do at least half of their back to school shopping in person this year according to a survey by Staples.
Digital learning was supposed to replace more analogue education, yet demand for supplies like crayons and notebooks remains high. Remote work was supposed to kill the office, but demand for office supplies is increasing.
"It is absolutely evolving, but it is not swinging to fully digital," said Rachel Huckle, the CEO of Staples Canada. "There's still a human aspect, and I think that people realized over the pandemic how much they missed and value human connection."
Huckle, who joined the U.S. office supply giant in 2019, now oversees its 11,000 associates across 300 stores in Canada, a position she couldn't have anticipated when she studied nursing, following in her mother's footsteps.
After kick-starting her career with Shoppers Drug Mart, Huckle ended up staying with the pharmacy giant for the next 25 years, as well as its parent company Loblaw Companies Ltd., eventually going back to school to earn an MBA from Rotman in 2017. Along the way she developed a reputation for giving familiar, legacy retail brands a modern makeover.
Huckle spoke to the Star from Staples Canada's head office in Richmond Hill about this year's back to school shopping trends, how buying habits have changed since the pandemic, and how shopping patterns provide a unique insight into everything from flexible work policies to classroom experiences to AI adoption.
What inspired you to pursue a career in nursing?
My mother is a nurse, and growing up I saw how much she loved working with patients. She's 82-years old and still working at St. Michael's Hospital.
How did you end up in retail?
When I graduated there were a lot of changes in the health care field, and there weren't many jobs available. One day I was walking by a Shoppers Drug Mart and saw a pharmacy technician role available, and so I applied, and I got it. Once I got in, I committed to the role, and I loved it.
You spent the next 25 years with Shoppers, and following the acquisition, its parent company Loblaw. What kept you there for so long?
It's an outstanding organization that gave me opportunities to grow. I had some great associate-owners that I worked with, and great leaders when I moved into field operations and later, the corporate office. We had amazing senior leaders that took chances on me and gave me the opportunity to do things like help bring PC-Plus together with Shoppers Optimum, designing new store concepts and working on a digital health app.
It was a great culture, a great brand, a great mission, and I think great leaders who gave me opportunities, and I always put my hand up to grow and learn.
Why did you ultimately decide to move on to Staples in 2019?
Having been there for a good chunk of my adult life, there was a part of me that wondered what else was out there. When I met the leadership team, I was inspired by their vision around reforming the company to the Working and Learning Company, and I thought it was a great opportunity for me to once again challenge my thinking and grow.
What is the Working and Learning Company concept all about?
It started with understanding who our customers are, and being very clear that we service everything from the solopreneurs to large enterprise customers, from students to educators to parents and employees.
Then we considered how that shapes the customer experience, the product and service offering, our physical retail, our digital offering, our delivery capabilities, even our sales force. It underpins how we're thinking about curating our assortment, our strategic partnerships, our store design, and everything in between.
What was the result of that effort?
One of the major changes was we bolstered the services offering - everything from print and marketing solutions to passport photos - and introduced several new partners. We now work with all five major shipping carriers, we offer Amazon returns, introduced Bell telecommunications for individuals, students and businesses. We also partnered with Allstate on our warranty program.
Then we spent time working with our associates coast-to-coast on processes to help them direct more of their time to supporting customers with new tools, training and development to keep them fresh and knowledgeable on what's happening in the market.
We also partnered with major technology manufacturers like Apple, Microsoft, Lenovo, Samsung and Google to provide our associates with training on new generative AI and AI-enabled technology.
You joined the company in 2019, just before the pandemic. How did COVID affect those plans?
We had a national meeting already in the schedule for the third week of March of 2020, and at the time we knew something was happening, but we didn't really understand what. There was this energy in the room, you could tell people were nervous. I remember pulling up a chair and saying, "I know we have an agenda, but let's just talk."
I started by sharing that my mother, who was elderly and living with me, was still going into the hospitals every day. When I told them about sending my mom off to work that morning my voice cracked a little, and in that moment, I realized how nervous I was for my mom. So, I said, "this is how I'm feeling; let's talk about how you're feeling."
After that, we got to work. I think we were one of the first retailers in Canada that repurposed our store development and construction folks and our vendors to start working on store renovations for things like Plexiglas dividers, because we were considered an essential service.
After the pandemic online shopping was supposed to kill in-person retail. Why didn't it?
Even in the digital era, sometimes you just want to go and grab what you need and maybe explore and discover what else is out there.
We hear from customers that they still love the touch and feel, they still want someone to talk to about the products. People realized over the pandemic how much they missed and value human connection.
One customer sent me a letter saying that after his son was born, he started taking photos and writing stories about him, and when he turned 18, he had hundreds of photos. So, he went to our store in Scarborough and asked for some help with the photo machine, and the associate in our print shop found out what he was working on and helped him turn it into a beautiful bound book to give to his son on his 18th birthday. He would have been there a long time if he had to do it all alone, but our associate took the time to help him. So to me, it isn't binary; digital is table stakes, but we love the retail experience as well.
Are digital tools replacing paper and pencils in the classroom?
In our most recent study, we found much more of a combination of online and offline, digital and manual. We are still seeing demand for paper and notebooks and crayons, but tech is making it into baskets at a much earlier grade than we've seen historically. We're also seeing students in higher grades asking more about AI-enabled tools, and we find that parents are more comfortable providing those tools when the school has identified and endorsed them.
What are some of the hottest back-to-school products this year?
Tech is on a lot of people's lists, both educators and students, but we see a lot of our curated 'Prep Rally' collections of totes, backpacks, water bottles, binders - with all matching design patterns - in high demand this season. Things like calculators, binders, and dividers, there's still huge demand, and in our kid's area, Crayola and art books continue to do well, so there's still an emphasis on art and creativity.
Does your sales data also give you unique insight into flexible work policies
Yes, and we are seeing more people going back into offices, and more demand from mid-to-large enterprises who are making bulk orders of facilities products - like cleaning, bathroom and breakroom supplies - but we're also seeing that hybrid is here to stay. We see commuter bags and travel accessories, mobile technology and audio equipment like headphones doing exceptionally well, which suggests that people are preparing to bring their work back and forth.
Have school supply costs gone up significantly since last year due to inflation?
We are aware that it's getting harder for our customers, and we've been focused on adding promotions and special pricing. Customers will see, both online and in store, a new program called Red Dot Savings, where you can find our best deals. We also offer a finance program with Flexiti that enabled customers to take products home today and make monthly payments for them later, and we continue to offer price matching to ensure our customers have access to what they need.
Does your background in nursing shape your leadership style?
I think it's a culmination of all my experiences, but there's an element of compassion, an element of listening, an element of identifying the needs of the person you're working with. Whether it's being employee-centric, customer-centric or patient-centric, if you're constantly focusing on the needs of others and how you can provide solutions or challenge them, it enables you to have impact.
CAPTION: Staples Canada CEO Rachel Huckle was following in her mother's footsteps as a nurse when she happened on a job at Shoppers Drug Mart where she spent 25 years climbing the ranks before moving to Staples in 2019.

Subject: Office supplies; Customer services; Leadership; Students; Educational technology; Shopping; Electronic commerce; Pharmacy; Trends; Learning; Pandemics

Business indexing term: Subject: Customer services Leadership Electronic commerce

Location: Canada

Company / organization: Name: Shoppers Drug Mart Corp; NAICS: 456110

Sept. 7: Give up on Connolly redevelopment plans; we want more jets, not buses; and other letters to the editor

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/sept-7-give-up-on-connolly-redevelopment-plans-we/docview/3101548822/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Sept.+7%3A+Give+up+on+Connolly+redevelopment+plans%3B+we+want+more+jets%2C+not+buses%3B+and+other+letters+to+the+editor&amp;title=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-09-07&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Give up on Connolly plans
Re: Decade-long downtown condo dream crumbles again, Sept. 5
Over the past 10 years, we have watched four developers try, and subsequently fail, in their efforts to make a condo out of the remains of a downtown church. The "Connolly" is not going to happen. City hall should face it and tear down the façade before it tumbles and creates its own brand of havoc. Let's move on so much-needed housing can rise in its place.
Paul Charles, Hamilton
Expanding alcohol sales hurtful
While banks and major groceries made record profits during the COVID-19 pandemic, small business and in particular restaurants and bars that rely on liquor sales are struggling to pay back their Canada Emergency Business Account loans. While many small business have had to close and declare bankruptcy, Premier Doug Ford's solution to their plight is to saturate Ontario with as many locations as possible that sell alcohol. As well, anyone who has had the misfortune of having to visit the emergency department know that hospital staff are overworked and understaffed. Hospitals, once pristine, are now dirty because maintenance staff numbers have been stretched thin. Beds are at a severe shortage, with many patients having to sleep on gurneys for days in hallways. The screams of people in agony echo through the halls as they wait for hours to be attended to. Premier Ford seems to be incapable of doing anything for citizens of Ontario that isn't related to alcohol. Perhaps Ford should hire bartenders and serve booze 24 hours a day in hospitals so patients that languish in excruciating pain in emergency wards can at least see someone to give them advice and something to kill the pain.
Gary Johnson, Hamilton
Time to overhaul our laws
Re: Teen charged in shooting near Lime Ridge Mall, Sept. 5
The teen can't be named because of the Young Offenders Act. He breached a youth probation order. It seems that almost 100 per cent of the time that a person is arrested in Hamilton, they are in breach of the terms of their release. It's way past time to kill the Young Offenders Act. A 10-year-old knows the difference between right and wrong. Violent crime and theft by youth is on the rise. Let's change the laws to protect the public. Build more jails if necessary. Our laws are way too easy on criminals. There should be stricter laws and they should be applicable regardless of age.
Pat Stevens, Grimsby
More jets and fewer buses, please
There's more Air Canada buses coming and going from Hamilton airport than flights. Don't we already have high-priced bus terminals in this city? We have a flight to Calgary by WestJet and once in a while flights to Iceland and Halifax by largely unknown carriers. We just got back from Edmonton and had to take a bus to Pearson airport for that trip. Crazy! Everyone on the bus said the same thing: Why are we busing it to Pearson when we have an airport right in our city? Even with the problems Swoop had, it was still the best thing to come to our airport. At least we had plenty of Canadian flight choices. Since city council loves to waste time voting on things, maybe they can vote on this issue.
Red Taylor, Hamilton
Does being a Jew equal being a Zionist?
Re: 'Tsunami of antisemitism' should be a 'call to action and responsibility': Israeli special envoy speaks in Hamilton, Sept. 6
If the woman who answered the questions asked inside the article above is completely right, then I'm supposed to be known as antisemitic due to the fact I'm not a Zionist despite my Jewish theology.
Despite anything she and others might want to believe, it's completely possible for Jews to not be Zionists, thinking Israel's high and holy, better than any other nation on earth. Zionism, doing a 180, has drastic potential to provoke isolation among others; it was due to my ex-synagogue's decision to politicize worship through making sure the Israeli anthem always helped communicate its conclusion once the war started that I stopped feeling able to appear, eventually resigning my membership due to the war's failure to stop.
Does being a Jew equal being a Zionist? Some Jews, myself included, don't think Israel's the best nation on earth and thus able to do whatever it wants.
Hope she doesn't have it too hard learning a potentially painful lesson.
Amy Soule, Hamilton
NDP ending deal raises concerns
In light of the recent decision by Jagmeet Singh and the NDP to end the confidence-and-supply agreement with the Liberals, I feel compelled to express my concerns about the implications of this choice for Canadians, particularly those most vulnerable in our society.
The past few years have presented Canada with numerous urgent challenges, from the ongoing impacts of the COVID-19 pandemic to the escalating climate crisis and rising inequality. The confidence-and-supply agreement allowed the NDP to play a crucial role in addressing these pressing issues, leading to significant accomplishments such as the introduction of the Canada Recovery Benefit, increased funding for health care, the implementation of dental care for low-income families, and commitments to tackle climate change through investments in green technology. Additionally, the agreement paved the way for initiatives aimed at reducing the cost of pharmaceuticals, making essential medications more accessible to Canadians. These achievements underscore the importance of collaboration in achieving meaningful progress for all Canadians.
By stepping away from this arrangement, the NDP risks weakening its influence over the government's response to these critical matters. The party has the potential to continue advocating for policies that prioritize the needs of the most vulnerable, but this is best achieved through partnership rather than isolation.
The NDP has always been about building coalitions and fostering collaboration among various groups and communities. The agreement represented an opportunity to unite progressives across the political spectrum. By working with the Liberals, the NDP could have demonstrated that meaningful change is possible through collaboration rather than division. This approach could inspire other progressive movements and parties to come together, creating a stronger front against the conservative agenda and fostering a more inclusive political environment.
As a lifelong supporter of the NDP, I believe that the party's future success hinges on its ability to navigate these complex political landscapes with a focus on collaboration and effective governance. The NDP must remain a key player in Canadian politics, advocating for progressive policies while ensuring stability and governance for all Canadians. Let's hope this decision is not akin to the "throwing the baby out with the bathwater" saying!
Rudy Giacchetti, Stoney Creek
CAPTION: There's more Air Canada buses coming and going from Hamilton airport than flights, a reader writes.Financial woes continue to plague developers hoping to build condos at the James Street Baptist site in downtown Hamilton.

Subject: Agreements; Small business; Emergency medical care; Collaboration; Buses; Pandemics; Airports; Antisemitism; Jewish people; Canadians; Alcohol; Zionism; Juvenile offenders; Condominiums; COVID-19

Business indexing term: Subject: Small business Airports
