Document 78 of 500

'You just ran over a cop': The inside story of the failed prosecution of Umar Zameer

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/you-just-ran-over-cop-inside-story-failed/docview/3101548789/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=%27You+just+ran+over+a+cop%27%3A+The+inside+story+of+the+failed+prosecution+of+Umar+Zameer&amp;title=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-09-07&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: "Officer down. Officer down," Const. Lisa Forbes screamed into her radio as she ran to her partner, lying mortally wounded on the floor of the underground parking garage.
Immediately, two other officers pulled up in an unmarked police van. "I got Jeff, you go get him," Forbes shouted to her plainclothes colleagues, telling them to chase the man who had just run over Const. Jeffrey Northrup.
Just past midnight on July 2, 2021, as the downtown streets grew quiet following a subdued Canada Day celebration amid the pandemic, a tragic event was taking place in the parking garage underneath Nathan Phillips Square, one that would profoundly change many lives.
About 30 seconds after Northrup was run over, Consts. Tony Correa and Scharnil Pais rammed their van into the fleeing BMW, blocking it from leaving. The officers leapt from their vehicle, their weapons drawn while a third car, with Spanish-speaking occupants, broke through the parking barricade to flee in terror.
Soon, the BMW's driver, a GTA accountant named Umar Zameer was handcuffed and on his knees. His pregnant wife, Aida Shaikh, stood by, distraught. Their son, aged two, wailed in her arms.
In that moment, Northrup was dying or already dead. Forbes was with him. More Toronto police officers, some uniformed, rushed to the scene.
At 12:21 a.m., Pais punched the kneeling Zameer in the face, and told him what he had done.
"You just ran over a cop."
Months after Zameer's acquittal in one of the highest-profile court cases in Toronto history, the Star can now present the most complete account yet of what happened in the days, weeks and months following the death of Toronto police Const. Jeffrey Northrup.
The timeline is central to answering the key questions that perplexed many observers after the Crown closed its case in court earlier this spring. Foremost among them: Why was Zameer prosecuted for murder?
Drawing on more than a dozen interviews with sources close to the police investigation and prosecution, along with a review of court transcripts and decisions, this story reveals how some police leaders within the force were skeptical about the case from Day 1; how the prosecution nonetheless forged ahead based on the accounts of three eyewitness officers - Forbes, Correa and Pais - despite eventually having expert and video evidence that contradicted their accounts; and, for the first time, reveals the trial judge's scathing written decision, delivered post-verdict, about the "troubling" possibility the officers lied to put themselves in the best light, regardless of the repercussions for Zameer.
The interviews also reveal that, to this day, key figures maintain that the police were justified in bringing the charge and that there was enough evidence to proceed with the prosecution.
Said one police source: If investigators "had not laid the charge, hypothetically, and the Crown dropped it, you would forever be having the (Toronto Police) Association and Northrup's family saying 'Hold on a second, these officers said he was standing in front of the car ... yet we don't believe them?'
"The evidence still has to be tested in court."
Since the end of the trial, Toronto police Chief Myron Demkiw has asked the Ontario Provincial Police to conduct a review of officer testimony, conduct procedures, practices and training, in addition to an internal review of all aspects of plainclothes policing. Those investigations are ongoing.
The three principal officers present in the parking garage on that night are prevented from making any public statements, and did not speak to the Star for this story. Similarly, lead prosecutor Mike Cantlon, who faced verbal abuse online and criticism from both defence and prosecution colleagues, declined to be interviewed.
Shortly after Zameer's acquittal, Cantlon released a short statement to the media, saying: "The events surrounding (Northrup's) tragic death warranted a trial to determine accountability."
The minutes afterAt 12:21 a.m., Const. Ryan D'Souza arrived to find Zameer with a "fixed stare" as Pais informed him of the initial charge against him - dangerous operation of a motor vehicle.
"We didn't know you were police. You weren't wearing your vests," Shaikh, Zameer's wife, said after Pais donned a vest with the word POLICE written across the front.
Speaking to D'Souza, Zameer told him he and his family were about to leave the parking garage when a man and woman started hitting the window and door of his BMW. He had no idea they were police. He was scared and trying to get away.
"You didn't see a badge?" D'Souza asked.
No, Zameer said. If he had known Northrup and Forbes were officers, he would not have gone.
A still from video evidence taken that night shows Northrup - a towering, 300-pound man known as the "gentle giant" who often shared homemade chili and brownies with his coworkers - wearing baggy black shorts, an unbuttoned short-sleeved shirt over a camo T, with a faded ball cap. Forbes, his partner, was in jean shorts and a dark hoodie.
It remains unclear whether Northrup was wearing or showing his badge when he approached the Zameer vehicle; it was later recovered from the scene.
Forbes wore hers on a long chain around her neck.
By 12:50 a.m., Zameer was whisked to nearby 52 Division, home base for Northrup and the other officers on duty that night.
Meanwhile, Shaikh and her toddler were taken to hospital for observation.
Forbes was still at the scene and received treatment in a ground-level stairwell vestibule at Nathan Phillips Square. Hyperventilating, she asked to be recorded by an officer equipped with a body camera.
"I was worried about forgetting details and wanted to document them," Forbes later testified, explaining she feared she might faint.
Amid a mix of background noise, Forbes' agitated state and her use of an oxygen mask, parts of her statement are difficult to comprehend.
What can be discerned is the following: The vehicle that hit Northrup was likely an SUV, operated by a man with brown skin fitting the description of a stabbing suspect she and her fellow officers were looking for. Displaying her badge, she commanded the driver to halt but he began to move forward, then put the car into reverse and manoeuvred erratically. Her partner was hit around the knee by the vehicle, after which the driver accelerated rapidly. Northrup could not get out of the way. (Forbes's initial account that Zameer matched the description of a heavy-set, brown-skinned suspect with big hair was later challenged at trial).
Already, while Forbes was giving her statement, the news of a Toronto police officer's critical injury was circulating within the force. Alerts soon reached then-interim Chief James Ramer and Supt. Glenn Cole, the head of 52 Division. Jon Reid, president of the Toronto Police Association, was also awakened and headed to St. Mike's hospital.
Homicide Det.-Sgt Terry Browne, who recently retired, and his team, including Dets. Jeff Allington and Trevor Grieve, were on call that night. They gathered at 52 Division while Ramer, Cole and a TPA representative drove to Brampton to notify Northrup's wife Margaret.
She was woken up by her son around 3:30 a.m.
"He said 'Mom there's a whole bunch of police at the front door,'" she later told Global News. "I couldn't verbalize it, my knees started shaking, and all I could say was "Oh god, oh god."
By 4 a.m., Northrup was confirmed dead.
The morning afterAt 8 a.m., Shaikh provided a videotaped statement that echoed her comments at the scene: She and her husband did not know the man and woman outside their vehicle were officers, and her husband locked the doors because they were afraid of being robbed. Shaikh said she saw the woman show what looked like a police badge, but it didn't look authentic.
"She could have bought that somewhere, that's what I thought."
Zameer also gave a videotaped statement, again asserting he was unaware the people knocking on his window were police. He explained that he drove the way he did because he was frightened and believed his family was at risk. He was also unaware their car had struck anyone, let alone a police officer.
Allington, a 25-year veteran, the last six of those years in the homicide squad, was put in charge of co-ordinating the investigation. The team set about securing the scene, conducting witness interviews and gathering evidence - including surveillance video showing the Zameer family out and about that Canada Day night.
Also called into work was Jeff Bassingthwaite, a veteran TPS traffic collision reconstructionist who worked separately from the investigation team.
The mood was sombre; off-duty officers were arriving with cards, flowers and a show of solidarity with Northrup, who'd served for 31 years and had been with the division since 2008. He was known as the type of officer who was always ready to undertake any task, doing so with a smile.
Mayor John Tory was among those who showed up at 52 Division early that Friday, as did police leaders, including Supt. Peter Code, who would later be master of ceremonies at Northrup's funeral.
Amid all this, the homicide team needed the police witness officers - especially Forbes, Correa and Pais - to give formal videotaped statements.
In her second statement, Forbes described Zameer accelerating and hitting Northrup as he was standing directly in front of the BMW. The impact caused Northrup to go flying back and land on the ground, she said. The vehicle then accelerated and drove over him.
Pais gave his statement at 8:29 a.m., followed by Correa at 8:37 a.m. Similar to Forbes, both officers said Northrup was standing upright, directly facing Zameer's BMW when he was knocked to the ground and run over.
All emphasized that despite wearing plainclothes, Forbes and Northrup were identifiable as police officers.
But there were discrepancies. Pais said Northrup had his badge out, while the others didn't see that. He also said he heard Forbes and Northrup shouting, "police, police," while Correa said he couldn't make out any words due to loud fan noise in the underground garage.
Nevertheless, the homicide detectives felt they had enough evidence to charge Zameer with first-degree murder, based on the officers' descriptions. (Under the Criminal Code, the intentional killing of a police officer is first-degree murder.)
"It was the appropriate charge to lay," said a senior police source, noting the police collision report that eventually undermined the Crown's theory was not completed until months later. "This was an officer killed in lawful execution of his duties on that evening, and he was ... identifiable as a police officer."
Or so police believed, based on the officers' accounts.
Homicide investigators had no choice but to press charges, based on the statements of the three officers, said another law enforcement official. "It's not up to the investigating officer to weigh the evidence, whether they believe those first responding officers or not, they still have that evidence. That was the problem in that case."
After Browne briefed Ramer on the decision, the chief sought advice from Meaghan Gray, the longtime Toronto police corporate communications guru, on what to tell the media. (Gray has since left the service to work for the TPA.)
Reporters and photographers were already gathered at the entrance to the Nathan Phillips Square underground lot; Ramer arrived just after 9 a.m.
"During the course of this dynamic event, Const. Northrup was struck by a vehicle. We believe this was an intentional, deliberate act," he said, with Tory, the TPA's Reid and others standing nearby.
Back at headquarters, Ramer's words were heard live on TV, leaving some, including at least two senior officers, shaking their heads in disbelief. Why the rush to say anything beyond confirming that a revered officer had died and a suspect was in custody? Pais and Correa had only just given their formal statements a half-hour earlier.
The scene was secured and there was a ton of evidence to sort through. And there was also the surveillance video - it captured Zameer, his pregnant wife and young son, appearing to innocently wander the downtown core before heading down to the parking garage.
Already, the question of motive had no clear answer: Why would this man run down Northrup?
But after Ramer's public statement, there was "no more discussion, it's done," a veteran police officer told the Star.
10 days laterOn July 12, 2021, thousands of uniformed officers marched through the city to BMO Field for Northrup's funeral, joined by Premier Doug Ford, Mayor John Tory, and police leaders from across Canada.
The tributes were heartfelt.
"Northrup made the ultimate sacrifice in the line of duty," said Ford, speaking near the coffin adorned with the Canadian flag. "He did so as a hero, laying down his life to serve and protect his community."
Northrup was remembered as a devoted husband and father, who volunteered with his daughter's Special Olympics T-ball team and coached his son's lacrosse team.
The livestreamed service featured repeated glimpses of Margaret Northrup and her three children seated together, clasping hands, their black COVID masks not concealing their sorrow. (Northrup declined the Star's interview request.)
That summerAllington instructed his team to investigate Zameer's history, including his social media activity. They inquired about the brief encounter the Zameer family had with a man at Nathan Phillips Square and probed why the couple had driven two vehicles downtown that Canada Day. They contacted Canada Border Services for Zameer's travel details and examined the couple's employment and educational backgrounds.
Ultimately, the couple's story was verified. The two-car trip? The couple were renting their second car out on a car-sharing app.
That summer, Mike Cantlon, in charge of one of Toronto's four Crown attorney's offices, assigned himself to the case along with Karen Simone, an experienced prosecutor with numerous murder trials under her belt.
They opposed Zameer's release on bail - a choice that surprised some police insiders who were aware of the weaknesses in the prosecution's case. The broader public, however, was expecting Zameer to remain behind bars.
A police officer was dead. The chief had said it was an intentional and deliberate act, and this was the prosecution's position at Zameer's bail hearing, held over Zoom on Sept. 7, 8, and 9, 2021.
His "attack on the deceased indeed demonstrates a callous indifference to human life. The fact that (Zameer) took off to make good on his escape without checking on the officer, clearly shows this indifference," Cantlon and Simone wrote in court filings.
Letting Zameer out of jail would "offend the public's confidence in the judicial system."
Zameer didn't have a criminal record, they noted. However, "he is not a Canadian citizen. He continues to have strong ties to his country of birth, Pakistan, as his mother and brother still reside there. He faces a life sentence with no chance of parole for 25 years. Given the seriousness of his charge and potential sentence, there is a risk he will not attend court."
At the hearing, Toronto defence lawyer Nader Hasan laid out a summary of his case, an unusual move at such an early stage.
This was a tragic accident, he told Justice Jill Copeland. Zameer and his wife did not believe the people who approached them aggressively were police officers, he panicked and was trying to protect his family when he inadvertently ran over the officer.
But, Cantlon told the judge, three key eyewitness officers - Forbes, Pais and Correa - "are telling a consistent story, not identical, but a consistent story about a series of decisions made by Mr. Zameer."
What about the absence of motive? Cantlon agreed there was none - but stressed police were continuing to investigate. (This was two months after Northrup died, not days, the judge would later note.)
There were also already questions about the officers' evidence.
Both Correa and Pais did not complete their notes on Northrup's death until after they had taken time off due to the traumatic event they'd witnessed. After they returned to duty, they went back to the parking garage to walk around the scene together. Then, on Aug. 4, 2021, they sat down with other members of the team - but not Forbes - in the same room at 52 Division and finally wrote up their versions of what they saw. (The trial judge would later refer to this as an "outrageous breach of proper protocol.")
For his part, Cantlon warned Justice Copeland to consider Shaikh's evidence cautiously due to her apparent bias as Zameer's wife. To this, the judge interjected. "The same could be said about the officers ... a close colleague, you know, has been killed in front of their eyes," Copeland said. "Police officers don't get special treatment in terms of assessment of their credibility and reliability."
That September, Copeland released Zameer on bail. He had spent three months in jail; missing the birth of his daughter.
However, a standard publication ban meant the public did not learn about the evidence heard at the hearings, nor an explanation of the judge's logic, until after the verdict, two-and-a-half years later.
In the 53 pages of her reasons, Copeland - who has since been elevated to the Ontario Court of Appeal - described the case for first-degree murder as "weak" more than a dozen times.
The prosecution's theory that Zameer - "out for a normal family evening with his pregnant wife and young son" - would suddenly and intentionally kill a police officer "runs contrary to logic and common sense," she wrote.
Her critique foreshadowed what the trial judge would repeatedly tell prosecutors - and ultimately the jury - at Zameer's trial: There was no evidence to support a murder conviction. However, Copeland did find there was a "reasonably strong" case for manslaughter, which would not require the Crown to prove Zameer knew Northrup was an officer and meant to kill him.
Zameer's release, under strict house arrest and subject to electronic monitoring, was met with immediate outrage. Ford tweeted, "it was beyond comprehension" and "completely unacceptable" that he would be released on bail. Patrick Brown, mayor of Brampton, called it "disgusting and disturbing." John Tory, then Toronto mayor, also tweeted, "It is almost impossible to imagine a circumstance in which an accused in a case of first-degree murder would be granted bail." (He has since apologized.)
Amid this outrage, Hasan went back to court to ask Copeland to partially lift the publication but the judge declined, reasoning that even partially lifting the ban "is likely to do more harm than good to the fairness and integrity of the trial process."
She did, however, point out that the evidence "paints a very different picture" than the "troubling" comments made by police and politicians.
Publication bans routinely prevent the reporting of certain arguments or evidence that are otherwise discussed in open court and can be found in court records. Generally speaking, these bans are meant to protect an accused person's right to a fair trial but do not prevent journalists - or anyone else, including politicians and their staff - from attending hearings or reviewing documents for their own information. (A Star reporter monitored Zameer's September 2021 bail hearing and was aware of Copeland's reasons.)
The next yearIn March 2022, lawyer Nader Hasan was in his downtown office when the accident report of Toronto police reconstruction expert Bassingthwaite landed in his email inbox.
Hasan had to read it twice. It was entirely consistent with the defence case.
Bassingthwaite reported the BMW first struck Northrup while reversing, causing him to fall to the ground towards the centre of a parking lot laneway. The BMW was momentarily not visible to an underground camera, due to obstruction by a pillar. Northrup was then prone on the ground as the BMW then ran over him. The officer could not have been struck head-on while standing up, as Forbes, Pais and Correa had said.
The dirt on the car's front fender was smeared in a manner suggesting it had brushed against the clothing of a person struck as the vehicle moved in reverse, Bassingthwaite noted. Additionally, there were no dents or significant damage on the hood to indicate a direct collision with a large man of six-foot-three.
Bassingthwaite later testified that after submitting his report in the spring of 2022, he did not have any in-depth discussions with the police or prosecutors until the eve of the trial, two years later.
By then, Hasan had hired his own accident reconstructionist, Barry Raftery, who reached the same conclusion as Bassingthwaite: Northrup fell to the ground following glancing contact with the driver's side fender of the reversing BMW. If he had been hit while standing in front of Zameer, he would have fallen onto the hood and caused damage.
(Prosecutors received Raftery's report only after they closed their case at the trial.)
The preliminary hearing got underway in February 2023 at Old City Hall. Margaret Northrup was there, supported by members of the Toronto Police Association. At seven days, the hearing was relatively short, but it gave Hasan his first opportunity to cross-examine Forbes, Correa and Pais.
At the end, Hasan told the judge that although the defence had "significant concerns about the credibility and reliability of certain Crown witnesses," there was sufficient evidence for Zameer to stand trial for murder.
Asked by the Star why he did not fight committal at this stage, Hasan noted that the legal test at a preliminary hearing is whether there is evidence that, if believed, could establish the elements of the offence. The question of whether witnesses may be lying does not enter into the analysis, he explained.
Within prosecution and police circles, Hasan's decision continues to be regarded as confirmation of their reasoning to move forward.
The trial of Umar ZameerUltimately, the trial in the spring of 2024 hinged largely on the accounts of the three police witnesses who said firstly, that Northrup and Forbes had clearly identified themselves as police officers, and secondly, that Northrup was standing directly in front of Zameer's BMW as he drove forward and fatally ran him over him.
If what the three officers said they saw was true, this was an act of first-degree murder.
In his opening statement, Cantlon explained the nuances to the jury. "The issue in this case is why Mr Zameer chose to make a series of (driving) manoeuvres," the veteran prosecutor said. "The issue is also whether Mr. Zameer knew or was wilfully blind that officer Northrup was a police officer acting in the execution of his duties."
The Crown's theory, he explained, wasn't necessarily that Zameer intended to kill Northrup, but that he intended to inflict bodily harm and knew the officer's death was likely.
As the trial proceeded, Cantlon and Simone continued to believe they still had a "reasonable prospect of conviction."
That standard does not require a "probability of conviction," law enforcement sources stress. "If the evidence makes out the essential elements of the offence, and there are challenges and inconsistencies between the experts and eyewitnesses ... That's why we have trials," said one senior legal official.
Some law enforcement officials believe that the pressure on prosecutors to proceed with the trial should not be underestimated, particularly because it involves the death of a revered officer, whose widow had been vocal about her desire for "justice."
Toward the end of the trial, after the jury had heard all of the evidence, Cantlon and Simone acknowledged the inconsistencies in the accounts of Pais, Correa and Forbes. Simone told the jury to consider whether the officers were not lying, but rather genuinely mistaken that Northrup was hit standing up in the laneway - a reasonable assumption because that is where his body ended up.
Justice Molloy saw it differently. In a remarkably frank written ruling - released after the verdict - the judge summed up her opinion:
"It is possible that Officers Correa and Pais did not see what happened, but decided to back up what Officer Forbes said, honestly believing she must have gotten it right. It is also possible that all three of these officers deliberately made up this story because they did not see what happened and thought that this version was logical. More troubling, it is possible they invented this version to put themselves and Officer Northrup in a better light and were indifferent to the implications for Mr. Zameer."
Forbes? Her memory could have been affected by the trauma of losing her partner, Molloy wrote.
But Pais and Correa?
"The one thing I know for sure ... is that Officers Pais and Correa did not see Officer Northrup standing upright while being run down by Mr. Zameer. Further, the fact that their versions dovetail so closely with each other and with Officer Forbes leads me to the inexorable conclusion that they not only lied, but they colluded to lie."
(At the close of trial, Molloy pointed jurors toward this conclusion, posing the question: "When three versions of an event are wrong, and wrong in the same way, you must also consider whether there has been collusion between those witnesses," she said - adding, "all of the officers denied collusion.")
The experts' evidence received significant media attention during the trial for how it contradicted the Crown's theory; the prosecutors tried to downplay this discrepancy by reminding jurors that even experts can make mistakes or have shortcomings.
Bassingthwaite, for example, could not explain how Northrup's body got from the point of contact to where it ended up seconds later. And Raftery's explanation, that Northrup was pulled or pushed by the BMW, also lacked physical proof - there was no debris or dust movement on that dirty garage floor.
The prosecutors stressed to the jury how improbable it was that Zameer drove over a 300-pound man and he and his wife did not know it. The couple both testified they thought they were driving over a speed bump.
When it came time to deliberate, Zameer's jury had the option to consider the lesser included offences of second-degree murder and manslaughter. By the end, Cantlon and Simone's closing arguments pointed more strongly to those lesser charges.
Still, the prosecutors did not concede that there was no reasonable prospect of conviction on the charge of first-degree murder.
According to the Crown Prosecution Manual, when prosecutors ask whether or not they have a reasonable prospect of conviction, they must make "an assessment of the credibility and competence of witnesses, without taking on the role of the trier of fact."
In other words, they must not take on the role of judge or jury - that's why trials are held.
On April 21, 2024, Zameer was acquitted.
The jury's verdict on both murder and manslaughter definitively rejected the Crown's theory that Zameer had intentionally hit Northrup, knowing he was an officer.
Behind the scenes at trial, in discussions between the sides, without the jury present, Molloy had been strikingly critical of the Crown's case; after the verdict, she made the rare move of apologizing to Zameer over his prosecution.
"You have my ... deepest apologies for what you have been through," the judge told him. (Those who felt the jury got it wrong believed Molloy should have also addressed Northrup's widow.)
Within hours of the verdict, Toronto police Chief Myron Demkiw released a statement that was widely condemned for sending a "dangerous" message that risked undermining the justice system. Police had been "hoping for a different outcome," he said.
He soon apologized and said he accepted the verdict, while also announcing he'd asked the OPP to conduct an independent review into the officers' testimony.
The OPP said it cannot speculate on how long the investigation will take or what the outcome will be. "Our mandate in this matter is to thoroughly investigate to determine whether three involved Toronto police officers committed any criminal offences and/or any violation of the Police Services Act," wrote Gosia Puzio, of the OPP's corporate communications bureau. OPP investigators must acquaint themselves with the full case, reviewing thousands of pages of court transcripts that will likely take several months.
The Ministry of the Attorney General, meanwhile, has not responded to a request for comment about the criticism directed at Cantlon and Simone - some of it intense and vitriolic - in the aftermath of the trial. Nor has the ministry explained how the prosecution properly exercised their discretion in this case.
Ultimately, critics and law enforcement sources remain divided on whether prosecutors should have pulled the plug, either before or during the trial, as the evidentiary challenges of the case mounted.
"Zameer got a fair trial and a just verdict," said a prosecutor who watched the case from the sidelines. "The justice system worked as it should."
The Zameers, meanwhile, received an outpouring of public support, including more than $300,000 in donations to cover their legal expenses.
The family "is doing very well," Hasan wrote in an email last week. "Aaida and Umar are happy and focused very much on their three young children."

Document 80 of 500

If ALS hadn't taken Sheila, she'd be knocking on Ford's door

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/if-als-hadnt-taken-sheila-shed-be-knocking-on/docview/3101493867/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=unknown&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=If+ALS+hadn%27t+taken+Sheila%2C+she%27d+be+knocking+on+Ford%27s+door&amp;title=The+Spectator&amp;issn=11899417&amp;date=2024-09-07&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.19&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Spectator&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: My sister Sheila was a keener.
Five years younger, with a disposition far sunnier than mine, she was: a volunteer, an activist, a photographer, a belter-outer of songs, a skilled cook, a committed NDP supporter, a music lover. She believed in people, cherished her family and friends with quiet ferocity, and routinely dispersed whatever clouds she spotted on someone else's horizon. She laughed easily and smiled often.
She never lost that contagious smile either, even when she'd lost everything else.
Ten years ago, she wore it while participating in the Ice Bucket Challenge, the phenomenon that raised $220 million globally for ALS clinical care and research. Canada's $17.2 million was more per capita than any other country.
Sheila could still talk then, though her speech had a drunken slur to it, which she hated. But sitting there in her wheelchair, friends around her, local Vancouver TV cameras rolling, she endured the icy water poured over her head and laughed.
That was Sheila and her ALS - indomitable spirit captured in a smile. By the time she died a year later in hospice, age 63, she was trapped inside an unresponsive body, no longer able to move, eat, breathe without help, talk or communicate in any way other than by using her eyes to spell out a sentence, letter by agonizing letter. Amazingly, though, she could still smile.
ALS (Amyotrophic Lateral Sclerosis) is a neurodegenerative disease that attacks the body's voluntary muscles progressively and relentlessly. Ten per cent of cases are genetic, while the rest, like my sister's, are random. Despite ongoing research and some discoveries, there is still no cure, and most people diagnosed with it will die within five years. ALS is unspeakably cruel.
Roughly 4,000 Canadians are living with ALS at any one time (in the grim calculus, 1,000 annual deaths cancel out 1,000 new diagnoses), so, unlike cancer, it is not one of health care's headliners. ALS is much easier for governments to ignore. Which is tragic.
"It's such a horrendously expensive disease," says Tammy Moore, CEO of ALS Canada. The need for complex equipment and assistive devices evolves for patients with dismaying speed, but sufferers and their caregivers are often left to the kindness of strangers. Government help across Canada for such needs is uneven and, in Ontario, nonexistent.
ALS Canada provides nearly 40 different kinds of necessary equipment to patients, involving purchase, maintenance, shipping, setting up, education - and gets zero dollars from Ontario.
"We're actually augmenting the system," says Moore. "We're funding it through philanthropy."
And don't get her started on the disparities in clinical care. You're in good hands if you live near the five multidisciplinary ALS clinics in Toronto, London, Hamilton, Kingston and Ottawa. But good luck if you live in Kapuskasing or Kirkland Lake.
ALS Canada and the Ontario clinics are currently lobbying the government for $6.6 million in funding, says Moore. "After all, one stay in ICU for an ALS patient at Sunnybrook costs $30,000. We're just asking for $5,000 per patient annually." Sounds reasonable.
If Sheila were still here, I suspect she'd be at Doug Ford's door, knocking with gusto. She was tireless that way, a committed activist to the end. Six months before she died in 2015, she received the "Exceptional Public Awareness" award from ALS BC, whose executive director spoke at her memorial service.
From the beginning, Sheila knew her nearly four-year battle with ALS would end in defeat. That didn't stop her from preparing gracefully for her death while animating each painful day with her own guiding spirit. With assistance, she voted early in the October federal election. On Thanksgiving, a week before she died, she insisted on posting a message. It exhausted her, but she knew she was leaving soon, and saying a final thank you to those she'd loved was urgent.
No science will ever prove this, but I suspect that the ALS monster chooses its victims from the ranks of exceptional people, people with great hearts and stunning courage.
The Ice Bucket Challenge is history now, with ALS associations back to less frenzied days of lobbying and fundraising. But the hope - for donations, for government support, for a cure - that hope remains.
It's stubborn that way. Like a smile that will not fade.
Janice Kennedy is a retired journalist living in Ottawa. For more information, go to als.ca.
CAPTION: Sheila Tynan, centre, attended Vancouver's annual ALS Walk with her family in June 2015, four months before she died.Courtesy of Janice Kennedy

Letters

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/letters/docview/3101493743/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=unknown&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Letters&amp;title=The+Spectator&amp;issn=11899417&amp;date=2024-09-07&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.18&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Spectator&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Give up on the
Connolly project
Re: Decade-long downtown condo dream crumbles again, Sept. 5
Over the past 10 years, we have watched four developers try, and subsequently fail, in their efforts to make a condo out of the remains of a downtown church. The "Connolly" is not going to happen. City hall should face it and tear down the façade before it tumbles and creates its own brand of havoc. Let's move on so much-needed housing can rise in its place.
Paul Charles, Hamilton
We want more
planes, not buses
There's more Air Canada buses coming and going from Hamilton airport than flights. Don't we already have high-priced bus terminals in this city? We have a flight to Calgary by WestJet and once in a while flights to Iceland and Halifax by largely unknown carriers. We just got back from Edmonton and had to take a bus to Pearson airport for that trip. Crazy! Everyone on the bus said the same thing: Why are we busing it to Pearson when we have an airport right in our city? Even with the problems Swoop had, it was still the best thing to come to our airport. At least we had plenty of Canadian flight choices. Since city council loves to waste time voting on things, maybe they can vote on this issue.
Red Taylor, Hamilton
Booze expansion will end badly
There will be so many consequences from increasing access to alcohol. We will no doubt see more people driving under the influence, and thus, drunk driving deaths. Our youth will have easier access to alcohol, in the marketplace and maybe also at home. There will be increased health care costs as Ontarians drink more and have alcohol-related ailments. It's too bad that Premier Doug Ford cannot be held liable himself for contributing to these increases!
Mike Benson, New Dundee
Expanding alcohol sales is hurtful
While banks and major groceries made record profits during the COVID-19 pandemic, small business and in particular restaurants and bars that rely on liquor sales are struggling to pay back their Canada Emergency Business Account loans. While many small business have had to close, Premier Doug Ford's solution to their plight is to saturate Ontario with as many locations as possible that sell alcohol. As well, anyone who has had the misfortune of having to visit the emergency department know that hospital staff are overworked and understaffed. Hospitals, once pristine, are now dirty because maintenance staff numbers have been stretched thin. Beds are at a severe shortage, with many patients having to sleep on gurneys for days in hallways. The screams of people in agony echo through the halls as they wait for hours to be attended to. Premier Ford seems to be incapable of doing anything for citizens of Ontario that isn't related to alcohol. Perhaps Ford should hire bartenders and serve booze 24 hours a day in hospitals so patients that languish in excruciating pain in emergency wards can at least see someone to give them advice and something to kill the pain.
Gary Johnson, Hamilton
Time to overhaul our laws
Re: Teen charged in shooting near Lime Ridge Mall, Sept. 5
The teen can't be named because of the Young Offenders Act. He allegedly breached a probation order. It seems almost 100 per cent of the time that a person is arrested in Hamilton, they are in breach of the terms of their release. It's way past time to kill the Young Offenders Act. A 10-year-old knows the difference between right and wrong. Violent crime and theft by youth is on the rise. Let's change the laws to protect the public. Build more jails if necessary. Our laws are way too easy on criminals. There should be stricter laws and they should be applicable regardless of age.
Pat Stevens, Grimsby

Document 82 of 500

Funds cut from daycares not in $10-a-day program: Province says focus on non-profits is hampering growth

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/funds-cut-daycares-not-10-day-program/docview/3101493573/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Funds+cut+from+daycares+not+in+%2410-a-day+program%3A+Province+says+focus+on+non-profits+is+hampering+growth&amp;title=The+Spectator&amp;issn=11899417&amp;date=2024-09-07&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.14&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Spectator&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Ontario child-care centres that aren't participating in the national $10-a-day program will soon lose provincial funding to offer new fee subsidies to lower-income families and their staff could see a pay cut of $2 an hour.
In a memo sent to licensed operators in Ontario outlining changes to how the province is funding the $10-a-day program, an assistant Deputy Minister of Education wrote that starting in 2025, non-participating centres will no longer receive routine funding such as "general operating, fee subsidy or wage enhancement grants."
Families who already get fee subsidies for child care will continue to benefit until their child ages out or leaves their provider, the memo said, but any new families won't be able to access subsidies for kids aged five and under in centres outside the $10-a-day system.
That money will instead go into funding the $10-a-day program "to ensure the success of that system," according to the memo.
The accompanying changes that will see funding cut off from non-participating centres will make it harder for them to operate, and therefore harder for families to access care outside the $10-a-day system, said Andrea Hannen, executive director of the Association of Day Care Operators of Ontario.
"When these centres lose access to provincial funding, including the opportunity for their staff to receive provincial wage enhancements and the families they serve to receive provincial fee subsidies, they will have to either close their doors, or dramatically raise their fees," she said. "This means fewer licensed child care choices for families of modest means."
Any low-income families seeking child care who aren't currently enrolled can apply to their municipality to get a placement in a centre within the $10-a-day program offering subsidies, said a spokesperson for Education Minister Jill Dunlop.
"They may need to wait for a spot, but when it is available, they will be prioritized," Edyta McKay wrote in a statement.
Ontario still offers a tax credit for up to 75 per cent of child-care expenses for lower-income families, she noted.
Operators outside the $10-a-day program have the flexibility to set the fees parents pay based on operational costs, and can offer subsidies and staff pay increases out of their own budgets, McKay said.
Ontario's deal with the federal government to join the $10-a-day program committed the province to create 86,000 new child-care spaces. But, so far, while there have been about 51,000 new spaces, only 25,500 of those are within the $10-a-day system, officials say.
The province says a federal cap on the percentage of for-profit spaces within the system is hampering growth, as municipalities are having to turn down applications for thousands of potential spaces because they are created by for-profit operators.
"We continue to call on the federal government to lift their cap on for-profit providers, which is limiting the opportunity for operators to join the $10-a-day program and access funding, and is limiting the availability of affordable child-care spaces close to home," Dunlop's spokesperson wrote in a statement.
NDP child care critic Teresa Armstrong said the government's most recent moves don't make sense.
"We should be making it easier for child-care centres to offer affordable child care and encourage people to work in a sector that offers fair wages for fair work," she said. "Giving a $2-an-hour pay cut for daycare workers? Who does that?"
The Canadian Press
CAPTION: OntarioPremier DougFord visitsa Brampton daycarecentre in March afterreaching anagreement withthe federalgovernment in the$10-a-daychild-careprogram.Nathan DenetteThe Canadian Press File Photo

Document 83 of 500

Singh should take a page from the Harris playbook

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/singh-should-take-page-harris-playbook/docview/3101493523/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=unknown&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Singh+should+take+a+page+from+the+Harris+playbook&amp;title=The+Spectator&amp;issn=11899417&amp;date=2024-09-07&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.18&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Spectator&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Now that NDP leader Jagmeet Singh has pulled the pin on the NDP's governing agreement with the Liberals, he has an opportunity to distance himself from both Trudeau's unpopular party and fire-breathing Poilievre. To do so, though, he and his people need to rethink how they think and talk and dream.
Fortunately, they have a rather prominent example south of the border in Kamala Harris and the Democratic Party. In three short weeks, Harris took her party from being on the ropes to being jubilant, cheeky, appealing and very much in contention.
The dynamics in Canadian politics are entirely different but there are lessons to be learned. Memes, pop star endorsements - if they can get some - and an upbeat image will help Singh, as they're helping Harris. The transformation Democrats are undertaking goes much deeper, though.
The Democrats clearly have found a playbook, and they're using it to call every play they make. This book has become an American bestseller and tool kit essential for Democrats.
In "The Persuaders: At the Front Lines of the Fight for Hearts, Minds, and Democracy," by Anand Giridharadas, the former columnist for The New York Times profiles left-wing activists, including Bernie Sanders and Alexandria Ocasio-Cortez, a Democratic Party maverick a few years ago and most recently a star at the Democratic National Convention (DNC), who take brilliant new approaches that work.
Giridharadas preaches about the need for progressives to take back key frames the Right has laid claim to for years. Freedom. Family. Patriotism. Masculinity. Normalcy. It's no coincidence that after Giridharadas advice, the DNC repeatedly beamed all these values into the heads and hearts of everyone who watched. Democrats will now take these values out on the stump.
What politicians have done in the past and what they promise for the future are important. They are secondary, though, to how they make people feel about themselves and their vision for the future. Singh and the NDP need, at this point, to decouple themselves entirely from the Liberals and work on connecting with voters on their values.
In "The Persuaders," communications whiz Anat Shankar Osorio - a disciple of the godfather of political framing, George Lakoff - advises "Sell the brownie, not the recipe." That Kamala Harris hasn't detailed her platform is not as important as the sense you get from her and her running mate Minnesota Gov. Tim Walz about the world they envision for voters and their families.
More advice from "The Persuaders": Democrats need to offer "blue meat." (For the NDP, this might be called "orange meat.") This is a takeoff from the advice of revolutionary Ronald Reagan pollster and strategist Frank Luntz who preached that rather than worrying about the mushy middle, Republicans should offer "red meat," meaning ideas that excite their base, upset the opposition and drive the narrative and all discussion. Democrats are now doing this. The NDP needs to do the same, to make mouth-watering offers to voters the Liberals can't match, and the Tories revile.
Advertising the other side is never a good thing, as Lakoff and "The Persuaders" tell us, yet the NDP never stops talking about the other guys. They waste precious time denouncing the big-money ties of their opponents.
Singh and company need to reframe the narrative. They need Canadians to understand they will be freer if they don't have to worry about costs of dental care. That their families will be more secure if we help homeless people get off the street. That it's patriotic for Canada to take the lead on climate change and to make polluters pay (and unpatriotic not to). That real men support others, not just themselves. That it's weird to want to kill medicare, which is what Poilievre wants to do by allowing more private health-care companies.
What the NDP has accomplished through its deal with the Liberals is not to be sneezed at, but it's not enough.
Closing the deal for the NDP may mean what it appears to mean for American Democrats, namely reclaiming those key concepts and frames.
Ish Theilheimer is a writer,
theatre producer and
community activist in Golden Lake, ON. He is a former Ontario NDP Vice-President and advisor.
CAPTION: NDP Leader Jagmeet Singh gives remarks during a press conference Thursday in Toronto. On Wednesday the NDP has pulled out of a confidence and supply agreement with the Liberals.Christopher KatsarovThe Canadian Press

CREDIT: Ish Theilheimer;

Subject: Meat; Voters; Homeless people; Political advertising; Political parties

People: Harris, Kamala; Singh, Jagmeet

Company / organization: Name: Democratic Party; NAICS: 813940

Document 84 of 500

Strip searches of children must stop now

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/strip-searches-children-must-stop-now/docview/3101493499/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=unknown&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Strip+searches+of+children+must+stop+now&amp;title=The+Spectator&amp;issn=11899417&amp;date=2024-09-07&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.17&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Spectator&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: The Ontario government is allowing private contractors to strip children as young as 12, to examine their naked bodies, to make them squat and cough, to keep them fully naked while they search and take notes on everything they see.
The government can't, or won't, tell you how often this happens. No government minister will say anything about it all - nothing about the sanctioned, degrading practice of stripping children nude, of humiliating them in the name of safety, of leaving some of the most vulnerable and damaged kids in this province with even more lasting psychological scars.
Those are the headline takeaways from the Toronto Star's reporting this week and last on strip searches in Ontario youth jails. To call the stories, by crime reporter Jennifer Pagliaro, outrageous would be an understatement. They are heartbreaking, both in minute detail and broad scope.
They make it very clear how much this government cares about the children - and again, these are children - who are being routinely strip searched in private and public facilities in Ontario, both before and after they've been put on trial. Put short, they don't even bother to keep accurate stats on how often this is going on.
Almost everything Pagliaro was able to dig out about the prevalence and details of this practice came not from the ministry in charge, not from the facilities or private companies holding the kids, and certainly not from Michael Parsa, the Progressive Conservative minister ultimately in charge of all of this, but from lawsuits, court records and testimony in criminal trials.
The Ontario Ministry of Children, Community and Social Services has so far stonewalled Pagliaro's attempts to obtain any kind of comprehensive data on youth strip searches. In the process, it managed to reveal that it doesn't even keep particularly detailed stats on how often kids in Ontario are being subjected to a practice the Supreme Court calls "inherently humiliating and degrading."
Pagliaro asked the ministry how many strip searches took place in the province's 27 youth detention facilities (five public, 22 private) between 2022 and 2023. The ministry told her it couldn't answer because it doesn't know. It doesn't collect strip search data from its own facilities in a way that would allow it to be aggregated.
That's bad enough. What's worse is that the government doesn't even bother to collect strip search data at all from the private contractors that run the bulk of all youth facilities in the province. "We do mandate that the records be produced and kept," a spokesperson told Pagliaro in a statement, "but we do not collect them."
Running a jail, any jail, isn't easy. Contraband - especially drugs and weapons - is a real issue in any facility holding people of any age charged or convicted of a crime. Guards, like any other worker, have a right to be safe on the job. They also have a duty to keep other prisoners safe from attack. But strip searching is not the answer.
As Pagliaro's reporting, and years of litigation and research, show, forcing prisoners to strip, expose their genitals and have their bodily orifices inspected, not just when they are suspected of smuggling contraband, but routinely, anytime they want to enter or leave a facility, is more than just humiliating. It's traumatic, for prisoners of any age, let alone children. People in prisons have been convicted of crimes, but their punishment is the removal of their liberty, not their dignity.
It's also unnecessary. Full body scanners, like the kind used in all major Canadian airports, could achieve the same aims, without the degradation and trauma. The government, in other words, could have solved this problem years ago.
The ministry told Pagliaro a review of strip search practices in youth jails is now underway. Whatever data that review collects, the correct policy answer is already clear. Doug Ford's government needs to stop strip searching children, now.
CAPTION:

Document 85 of 500

Ontario sells brow lands for LTC beds, affordable housing: Complicated, multi-partner agreement has not been publicly announced

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/ontario-sells-brow-lands-ltc-beds-affordable/docview/3101493373/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Ontario+sells+brow+lands+for+LTC+beds%2C+affordable+housing%3A+Complicated%2C+multi-partner+agreement+has+not+been+publicly+announced&amp;title=The+Spectator&amp;issn=11899417&amp;date=2024-09-07&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.4&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Spectator&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Ontario has quietly reached a complicated deal for its controversial former hospital lands on the Mountain brow that calls for two long-term-care facilities, a retirement home, a college expansion - and affordable housing built within a redeveloped Century Manor.
The massive, multi-party deal for the former Hamilton Asylum lands on Fennell Avenue West has been under negotiation for a year - and still has not been publicly announced.
But land registry documents obtained by The Spectator show a brow lands transfer to long-term care industry giant Schlegel Villages for about $24.3 million on Aug. 29. The total value of the multi-tentacled development project is not stated - but a $350-million charge was registered on the property with the province at the same time as the sale.
In response to Spectator questions about the sale Friday, company president James Schlegel said via email he was "thrilled" to complete the land deal, but cautioned there remain "many steps to navigate" in needed approvals. As a result, he said the organization will wait to share more specific project details, but added "we're confident we'll be able to make our vision a reality at the site."
Notably, registry documents outlining the agreement with the province show plans over time for two long-term care facilities with a combined 512 beds. That would be double the 256-bed goal originally announced by the Progressive Conservative government when it put the land on the market in 2022.
In response to a request for an interview, a Ministry of Long-Term Care spokesperson said the province will say more about the project "in due course," but emphasized the government is making progress on plans to build or upgrade 58,000 long-term care beds by 2028.
The updated plan for two new facilities rather than one comes as the wait list for beds at other west Mountain long-term care facilities ranges between 170 and 623 people.
Registry documents also outline a planned Schlegel-run "campus of care" on the brow that is expected to include a retirement home, independent-living seniors' apartments and a related "health hub." The agreement leaves open the opportunity to include resident amenities like stores, hair salons, libraries or a community centre within the sprawling development.
Non-profit social housing group Indwell is also named as a partner on an unspecified amount of new, affordable rental housing. Those units appear destined to be built both on the brow lands property and, eventually, in the lower city.
That plan could also breathe new life into trespasser-plagued Century Manor, the last remnant of a former provincial psychiatric hospital that is designated as a heritage building but has been falling apart for years. Heritage advocates have accused the province in the past of demolition by neglect.
In a statement, CEO Jeff Neven said Indwell hopes to convert the building into "supportive affordable housing," but noted planning is in the "very early stages" for the project. "Ultimately, every door we open together becomes a solution to homelessness," he said.
Other transactions reflected late last month show some brow property transferred for $7.3 million by Schlegel to Mohawk College and some natural lands effectively given to the City of Hamilton for a $2 fee. (The city has previously sought ownership or protection of green space and trails along the provincially owned brow.)
When contacted by The Spectator, Mohawk president Paul Armstrong said in a statement he is "excited" to partner with Schlegel and Indwell to allow its campus to grow just across the street from its main location - a long-sought goal for the space-starved college - but added there are "no specific expansion plans" that can be shared yet.
The college already partners with Schlegel on "living classrooms" at some of the company's long-term care and retirement facilities in Hamilton and Burlington.
The sale and ambitious redevelopment plans come nearly six years after the Tory government spurred local outrage by cancelling an earlier deal for the land with Mohawk that would have provided millions for affordable housing and turned the crumbling Century Manor into an educational facility.
Ontario later imposed contentious zoning changes over city objections to allow tall residential buildings before putting the land on the market.
Ward councillor John-Paul Danko, formerly a vocal opponent of the province's decisions around the land, said he is "excited" about the latest plan but is not allowed to share what he knows until the province releases details publicly.
"What I can say is there have been significant discussions with service providers and other parties interested in property (and) ... I'm looking forward to a final announcement," he said Friday.
Last week, The Spectator reported on plans to amend property zoning yet again to allow for taller retirement homes and long-term care buildings. At that time, Mayor Andrea Horwath signalled her expectation that unspecified confidential plans for the property would be "forthcoming to council."
Matthew Van Dongen is a reporter at The Hamilton Spectator. mvandongen@thespec.com
CAPTION: The fate of Century Manor remains unclear as the province finalizes a sale of former hospital lands on the Mountain brow.the Hamilton Spectator file photo

CREDIT: Matthew Van Dongen Reporter

Massive deal for asylum lands calls for LTC beds, affordable housing in Century Manor

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/massive-deal-asylum-lands-calls-ltc-beds/docview/3101578767/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Massive+deal+for+asylum+lands+calls+for+LTC+beds%2C+affordable+housing+in+Century+Manor&amp;title=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-09-06&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Document 87 of 500

Fred Hahn survives CUPE push for his resignation

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/fred-hahn-survives-cupe-push-his-resignation/docview/3101493402/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Fred+Hahn+survives+CUPE+push+for+his+resignation&amp;title=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-09-06&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: The country's largest public-sector union acknowledges it cannot oust vice-president Fred Hahn for posting a video denounced as antisemitic, but says his refusal to resign as requested is fuelling a damaging "conflict."
There are no provisions in the constitution of the Canadian Union of Public Employees to remove the elected Hahn, who is also the elected president of the union's Ontario arm, CUPE's national executive board said in a statement Friday.
"I appreciate the recognition ... that union democracy must be respected," Hahn, who has previously rejected charges of antisemitism, told the Star in a text message.
CUPE's two-page missive to members caps a rift within the union since the national executive board declared it had "lost confidence" in Hahn - an outspoken critic of Israeli attacks on Gaza - over the video he posted on social media on Aug. 11 and removed a week later.
It depicted a Jewish athlete with a Star of David arm tattoo leaping off a diving board at the Paris Olympics and turning into a bomb that explodes.
Critics raised concerns that the video conflates Judaism with
military strikes by Israel in Gaza that have killed more than 40,000 Palestinians in the wake of a surprise Oct. 7 attack by Hamas that killed some 1,200 people in Israel and saw more than 200 hostages taken.
"We are deeply saddened to have heard from many of you about the profound hurt that has been caused by the conflict within our union," said the statement from the CUPE board that includes CUPE national president Mark Hancock, who had urged Hahn to quit his national VP post.
"We have seen a conservative, anti-worker government use this moment to attack and attempt to further divide us by interfering in internal union business," the statement said in reference to Premier Doug Ford, who called Hahn "a disgusting human being" and called for him to be disciplined.
"None of this has been good for CUPE, or for the labour movement generally," the statement continued.
The video has resulted in CUPE locals, members and outside groups speaking out for or against Hahn - depending on their point of view - after he was accused by the national executive board of violating CUPE's equality statement.
"I've been honoured and humbled by the support ... I want to see our union united and strong because we have real enemies to fight," Hahn said in the text.
The CUPE equality statement stipulates the union "should neither condone nor tolerate behaviour that undermines the dignity or self-esteem of any individual or creates and intimidating, hostile or offensive environment."
CUPE Ontario is the subject of a human rights complaint filed last November alleging systemic discrimination and promotion of antisemitism that isolates Jewish members.
Last Friday, CUPE Ontario issued a statement saying its own executive board passed motions in support of Hahn continuing as both a national vice-president and president of the Ontario arm, but called for more training and a "greater awareness around the issues of antisemitism and anti-Palestinian racism."
The next election for CUPE Ontario president will be held in May. Hahn faced several opponents at the last one and took 60 per cent of the vote. The next election for national vice-presidents is October 2025. In the last one, Hahn had one opponent and emerged victorious with 55 per cent support.
CUPE national, which has been highly critical of Israel's treatment of Palestinians for years, said "nothing about the position taken by our national executive board has been about silencing voices - Brother Fred Hahn's or otherwise - on the issue of Palestine."
CAPTION: Canadian Union of Public Employees Ontario president Fred Hahn speaks at the pro-Palestine encampment at the University of Toronto in May 2024.

Document 88 of 500

Doug Ford's booze dreams might turn out to be small beer

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/doug-fords-booze-dreams-might-turn-out-be-small/docview/3101205428/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Doug+Ford%27s+booze+dreams+might+turn+out+to+be+small+beer&amp;title=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-09-06&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: At just before 2 p.m. on Thursday, I was the fifth-ever customer to buy beer from the Little Bee Mart at the corner of Runnymede and Annette, when I picked up a few $4 tall boy cans of Mill Street Organic from among the seven varieties of suds on offer there. I missed out on being first by about an hour, Charlie, the owner told me, and in that time a few regular customers had picked up a six pack or a can mostly for the novelty.
"Honestly, it's going better than I thought," he said with a shrug. No problems yet, none really expected, but no sales bonanza either. (He also offers two kinds of wine - though he was still waiting to sell his first bottle.)
As for me, buying alcohol in a corner store in Toronto for the first time, it was ... well, no big deal, really. Although that may be what you want in a beer purchase - just in and out and done. A lot of ritual or fanfare might seem appropriate when you buy a house or a car or an engagement ring. But a six pack? Let's just get it over with.
As of Wednesday, there were four retail spots within roughly a 15-minute walk of my house where you could buy alcohol - two LCBO locations and two Wine Racks. That seemed set to change Thursday, as Ontario's 4,200 convenience stores got the right, at long last, to sell beer and wine.
There are at least 17 places you might call convenience stores within that same 15-minute walking radius of my home - places that sell lottery tickets, cigarettes, pop and snacks. In the first few hours that they were legally able to sell booze, I set out to visit each and see how it was going.
For most of them it wasn't going at all. As in, they were choosing so far not to offer it for sale. "We're not doing that, too much hassle," the owner of High Park Variety told me as she monitored a lunch-hour lineup of middle schoolers crowding the candy counter.
"Too much work," the owner of Bruce's Gift & Variety said.
The owner of T.J.'s Variety on Bloor West said that with an LCBO right next door, he wanted to wait and see what others in the area were doing, to judge whether it would be worth the time, effort and shelf space.
In all, of the 17 places that seemed to qualify, only three had beer and wine on sale.
And in those places, no great crowds of drunks were rushing in to take sudden advantage of the convenience.
At Jug Mart convenience on Jane Street, where they have a separately enclosed area for vape products as well as lottery, cigarettes and junk food - a one-stop vice shop alongside the usual variety-store groceries and knick-knacks - they had a few major brews in the fridge and a handwritten sign advertising the introduction of the new product line.
Through a language barrier that initially had her trying to explain to me that her store didn't sell newspapers (journalism: a vice too far), the woman who runs the place with her husband said they'd sold their first six pack after opening in the morning, and had seen nothing further on the sales front by noon. "We think it's good," she said.
In anticipation of this day, some people expected Toronto to turn into a wild untamed party, while othersexpected some business sales revolution. Many, one way or the other, portrayed it as a big deal. But in the first hours, at least in one area of the city, it seemed to be neither bonanza nor blight. Instead it seemed a bit blah.
Which, as I say, might be what you want from a routine beer purchase. If you can walk a few minutes to get it rather than driving a few minutes, so much the better. But either way it's probably fine, right? (Maybe it's worth mentioning that in my walk past the 17 corner stores and four more traditional booze outlets, I passed dozens and dozens of licensed bars and restaurants. So it's not as if alcohol was ever scarce around here.)
This could be a repeat of past issues like drinking in parks, or expanded patios, or so many others that generated much discussion in advance and seemed like much ado about nothing once they were implemented.
Which makes it all the more puzzling that Premier Doug Ford seems to think this is a policy change he base a re-election campaign on, as my Queen's Park colleagues were reporting Thursday. He paid - depending on whose numbers you go on - between $225 million and $1 billion in taxpayer money to end an agreement with the Beer Store a year early to rush these bottles onto corner-store shelves.
After that expensive regulatory rush, it seems many retailers are in no rush to take advantage.
Likely it will help the bottom lines of many of those small retailers who participate. It will certainly seem like a welcome convenience to those who can get what they're looking for a few steps closer to home.
It's a good deal. But what it's not, it appears so far, is a big deal.
CAPTION: More than 4,200 convenience stores in Ontario were allowed to sell beer and wine starting Thursday morning.

Subject: Alcohol; Sales; Beer; Liquor; Convenience stores

Business indexing term: Subject: Sales Convenience stores

Document 89 of 500

Federal politics could derail Doug Ford's plans to distract from his fading fortunes

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/federal-politics-could-derail-doug-fords-plans/docview/3101204938/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Federal+politics+could+derail+Doug+Ford%27s+plans+to+distract+from+his+fading+fortunes&amp;title=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-09-06&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: As Doug Ford counts down to an early spring election, watch out for the fallout from a federal election he hadn't counted on - even earlier next spring.
It's now looking as if his best-laid plans to get out ahead of events could be overtaken by events in Ottawa. This week, the federal NDP announced a breakup by "ripping up" its two-year nonelection accord with Prime Minister Justin Trudeau.
That's politics. When you play politics, there's always a faster player in town.
The premier had fervently hoped to beat the clock on a police probe into the Greenbelt imbroglio. But there was always a second motive for flouting the province's fixed election date law, which mandates a vote nearly two years from now in June 2026.
That's an eternity in politics. Ford's provincial Tories have long worried that their fellow Tory, federal Conservative Leader Pierre Poilievre, could already be in the prime minister's office by then.
Which would make for an awkward federal-provincial byplay, pitting these two fellow travellers - who are far from pals - against one another on the public stage. Far better to steer clear of a collision course that could be personal, political, fiscal and ideological.
For Ford and Poilievre are utterly unlike one another:
The premier can be personable when he is feeling folksy; Poilievre is prone to being impersonal and impetuous.
Ford is a salesman at heart, cutting deals in the business world; his federal Conservative counterpart is a career politician who is quick to cut people down.
The premier is a people pleaser who'd rather spread money around; Poilievre is all about cutting programs to eliminate deficits.
Ford sees himself as a populist, not an ideologue; Poilievre is a party man who backs the party line.
Against that backdrop, the last thing Ford wants is to run for re-election with future prime minister Poilievre cutting Ontario down to size and pushing the premier around. Far better to campaign with Trudeau still in power, tapping into anti-Liberal animus across the province, while tarring the New Democrats with the same cloth.
For years, Ford's Tories have taunted the Ontario NDP by falsely claiming they joined a formal coalition with the previous minority governments of former premiers Kathleen Wynne and Dalton McGuinty. In fact, there was never any arrangement, formal or informal, between the two parties to keep the minority legislature alive from 2011-14.
Instead of targeting the Liberals and New Democrats for the supposed sins of their federal counterparts, Ford could find himself taunted to explain or excuse the excesses of Poilievre in power should a federal election take place first. All that said, gaming it out only goes so far.
The long life of this federal minority Parliament may offer lessons for a future minority in Ontario. Just as it drew inspiration from this province's past experiment with a durable NDP-Liberal minority accord from 1985-87.
Political posturing aside, the latest federal pact was a credit to federal NDP Leader Jagmeet Singh, even though he got little credit for it from pundits and public opinion. He wielded the balance of power to make the most of this minority, winning successes on pharmacare, dental care and low-income protection.
By granting stability to the Liberals - promising to support the minority government on confidence and money matters - Singh not only made progress on progressive issues, he also avoided the constant scrutiny and squirming over whether or not he'd support the government on every budget measure. But the NDP must now pre-position, pre-election, to promote its own brand as distinct from the Liberals.
Consider it a collaborative divorce, or perhaps a separation with room for reconciliation in future. Singh certainly played his cards better than the Ontario NDP in 2014, when then-leader Andrea Horwath pulled the plug on Wynne's progressive budget only to propel the Liberals to a majority government in the election that followed (as an MPP at the time, he doubtless learned from Horwath's blunder).
The 40th anniversary is fast approaching for the 1985 minority accord between Liberals and New Democrats that achieved great things. Will there be another minority in 2025, if Ford's early election goes awry?
In a campaign, you never know how votes will split and campaigns will come together at the finish line. But just as there's no telling how the next Ontario election will turn out, no one can say when that early campaign call will come - if ever.
I began by noting the premier's plan for a premature election has been overtaken by events, but perhaps we are all being taken in by his team. Possibly this is merely a call to arms, a way for the Progressive Conservatives to whip their team into following the party line.
The only certainty is that by launching so much pre-election speculation, the Tories have got the media talking about suspense instead of substance. When you have a fading attraction, distraction can be a fail-safe.
CAPTION: Premier Doug Ford walks through the halls of the Ontario legislature after speaking to media, in Toronto on May 6, 2024.

Subject: Prime ministers; Political parties
