Document 90 of 500

Canadians are furious. Can Jagmeet Singh give them what they need?

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/canadians-are-furious-can-jagmeet-singh-give-them/docview/3101204902/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Canadians+are+furious.+Can+Jagmeet+Singh+give+them+what+they+need%3F&amp;title=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-09-06&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: There is certainly a vibe on the campaign trail in Montreal, Craig Sauvé told me this week: Rage.
When I sat down with the NDP candidate in this month's federal byelection, he had just come back from knocking on doors. At one home, he met a guy still seething from downpour that flooded his basement. It was just another problem, on top of crime, a housing crisis, and more. "He feels like he's not getting help from anybody," Sauvé told me inside his campaign office "Nobody's here."
Sauvé excluded, of course. The high-profile city councilor made his name on precisely those issues. Now he's running to channel that anger into an upset win. On the campaign trail in LaSalle-Émard-Verdun, long considered one of the safest Liberal seats in the country, Sauvé says he's hearing from long-time Liberal after long-time Liberal that the prime minister needs to go.
Sauvé is a pretty mild-mannered guy. (He's been a friendly acquaintance for years.) But he says he's come to identify with the "palpable anger" he's hearing from voters, especially in his working class corner of Montreal. "The Liberal Party has been really dragging its feet (on housing) over the past nine years," he says.
That kind of criticism makes political sense. It's also somewhat awkward. The NDP has been keeping that same Liberal government alive through a confidence-and-supply agreement for the past two years. But no longer. The day after I met Sauvé, NDP Leader Jagmeet Singh announced he had "ripped up" that deal.
Politically, it's a smart move, but it came under duress. Conservative leader Pierre Poilievre has successfully whipped up voter fury at Singh for keeping Trudeau's government going. The Conservatives now dominate the narrative on just about every issue that matters to Canadians. More importantly, they're in tune with the national vibe: angry, frustrated and ready for change.
Prime Minister Justin Trudeau has mostly met that vibe with relentless and insufferable optimism. Accosted by a disgruntled steelworker in Ontario last week, Trudeau touted dentalcare and Chinese steel tariffs, to little effect. The guy shrugged: "I don't believe you for a second."
It's a feeling his caucus shares. "People are really f------ pissed off," one Liberal Member of Parliament told me recently.
After their last byelection loss in June, Liberal MPs began agitating for big changes, like a major cabinet shuffle. Change never came.
So the party is staggering into the next election with the same game plan as before: Paint Poilievre as an extremist. The same Liberal MP rolled their eyes: "You can't do contrast without vision," they said. Jeremy Broadhurst, a longtime Trudeau advisor, seems to agree: He resigned as the Liberal campaign director this week.
A byelection win in Montreal wouldn't stop the bleeding. But it might at least provide a little relief. The Liberals are sure they'll retain the seat, held for decades by Paul Martin and, later, by justice minister David Lametti, who resigned after Trudeau fired him from cabinet.
But one well-connected local Liberal told me the smart money is on Sauvé. If he does win, the loss will certainly deepen the Liberal misery.
The NDP, however, need a selling point. Sauvé makes a good case that it ought to be housing. He nods towards the Lachine Canal, where Ottawa is planning to sell off a huge parcel of federal land to developers. Sauvé says Ottawa - and Poilievre - can't fix this problem by dumping more high-price high-rises into the market. "Federal lands should go to public goods," he says.
On other files, the NDP seem at a loss. When Singh came to town to campaign with Sauvé, I asked three times for specific policies the NDP are offering. The only thing I got was a pitch to maybe put a price cap on groceries.
The fact is that people are mad. They're facing declining services on top of an affordability crisis. They want to hear a plan for how to fix it. Failing that, they'll vote for whoever can amplify their anger.
For now, that's the Conservatives. But if the New Democrats can win on the 16th, they may actually get a chance to present themselves as a real alternative: If they can't, then why are they still in the race?
CAPTION: NDP leader Jagmeet Singh puts up campaign posters with candidate Craig Sauve in Montreal. A federal by-election will be held in the riding of Lasalle-Emard-Verdun Sept. 16, 2024.Toronto Star contributing columnist Justin Ling.

Document 91 of 500

A loveless marriage, strategic split

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/loveless-marriage-strategic-split/docview/3101087205/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=unknown&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=A+loveless+marriage%2C+strategic+split&amp;title=The+Spectator&amp;issn=11899417&amp;date=2024-09-06&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.10&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Spectator&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: It was a marriage of convenience that ended because it was no longer convenient. No need for progressives to shed a tear, or for Liberals to fear the fall of the government, or for Conservatives to celebrate.
NDP Leader Jagmeet Singh did what he needed to do, what caucus members wanted him to do, what many New Democrat members believed should have been done last year.
On Wednesday, days before New Democrat MPs were set to meet in Montreal, and less than two weeks before two important byelections, Singh pulled the plug on his agreement with Prime Minister Justin Trudeau - 10 months before it was set to expire. The supply and confidence agreement is no more.
The deal was framed by Trudeau and Singh as one that offered stability and delivered on the mandate voters gave parliamentarians in 2021, when they returned MPs to Ottawa in nearly the same formation as in the previous Parliament.
Canadians wanted MPs to "work together to put people and families first. Deliver results and build a better future," Trudeau said in announcing the deal. "This is what Canadians expect from their politicians."
But what Canadians have also learned to expect from politicians is that they make decisions that best serve their partisan interests.
Singh signed on because the agreement provided a good answer to the question of why the NDP was propping up the Grits. With a minority of seats in the House of Commons, the Liberals needed a dance partner to avoid an early election call. The NDP thought it could be seen as extracting concessions from the Liberals - action on climate change, reconciliation, affordable housing, and big dream items such as a new national dental care program, progress toward universal pharmacare, and a ban on the use of "scabs" during lockouts or strikes - in return for its continued support. The agreement allowed the NDP to vote with the Liberals without appearing spineless.
But as the deal wore on, it became less of a blessing and more of a liability.
In June, Singh's team concluded there was little left to accomplish. They had extracted all they thought they could get from it. They also faced a bigger problem: it was the Liberals who took most of the credit from programs such as dental care. There was no bump in support for the New Democrats.
Meanwhile, Singh and the NDP spent months being defined by the Conservatives as one and the same as the Liberals, an "NDP-Liberal coalition" responsible for all the ills plaguing the country.
With the next election gearing up to be a change election, the Conservatives' framing wrote off Singh as a change option.
It was a brilliant strategic move by the Conservatives, and a big hurdle for the NDP.
It's one the party hopes to overcome in announcing that the deal is ripped up, with an election-style ad showcasing Singh as ready to defeat both Pierre Poilievre's Conservatives and Trudeau's Liberals in order to fight for the middle class.
The next few months are guaranteed to be rockier but, unless the parties miscalculate, this announcement won't bring much immediate change. Neither the NDP nor the Liberals want an election campaign with the Conservatives leading massively in public support.
Poilievre is itching to go to the polls. On Wednesday, he said he'd test the NDP leader's resolve to distance himself from the Grits with a non-confidence motion at the first chance he gets. But that opportunity might not come until November, if the Liberals delay offering opposition days to the Conservatives. It could come even later if the Liberals decide to prorogue Parliament.
If the House of Commons returns as planned on Sept. 16, the NDP's national director is already laying the groundwork for continued support of the Liberal government. Speaking to CBC last week, Anne McGrath said that while the "Liberals have kind of run out of gas," there are things the NDP wants to accomplish, such as getting the government to "improve the EI system."
The party may still have to play that game - the we-demanded-the-Liberals-act-and-they-acted game - to save face and ensure the continuity of this Parliament. Few New Democrats want a repeat of 2005, when the NDP pulled support from Paul Martin's Liberal minority government and ushered in a decade of Conservative rule.
Other than the NDP, only the Bloc Québécois can offer the Liberals a lifeline. But it too has been targeted by the Conservatives, who exaggerate the BQ's closeness to the Liberals in order to score points in Quebec. With the Bloc still strong in public support, however, it has less to fear about triggering a trip to the polls.
So until Singh and the NDP are confident their new rebranding has them leading as the progressive option for change, the result of Wednesday's announcement may be more sizzle than substance.
Althia Raj is a national
affairs columnist
with Torstar.
CAPTION: NDP Leader Jagmeet Singh, right, propped up the minority Liberal government to get priority legislation passed.The Canadian Press file photo

CREDIT: Althia Raj OPINION;

Document 92 of 500

'People finally have more options': Beer, wine, cider and coolers now on the shelves at Ontario convenience stores

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/people-finally-have-more-options-beer-wine-cider/docview/3101338884/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=%27People+finally+have+more+options%27%3A+Beer%2C+wine%2C+cider+and+coolers+now+on+the+shelves+at+Ontario+convenience+stores&amp;title=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-09-05&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: You'll now be able to grab a six-pack when gassing up, as convenience stores have added beer, wine, cider and coolers to their shelves.
It's the second phase of the province's plan to expand ways to purchase alcohol.
"Today's expansion delivers on our government's promise to give people in Ontario the same choice and convenience enjoyed by people throughout the rest of Canada," Premier Doug Ford said in a press release. "With today's announcement, we're also creating new opportunities to produce and sell world-class Ontario-made beer, wine and ready-to-drink beverages in nearly 4,200 convenience stores across the province."
Stores will be able to sell from 7 a.m. to 11 p.m. daily.
Another 3,000 locations throughout Ontario already are licensed to sell alcoholic beverages, as well as bars and restaurants.
"People finally have more options to responsibly and conveniently support local Ontario breweries and wineries - or whatever their drink-of-choice may be - even closer to home, and today's announcement is another milestone in the government's commitment to deliver more choice and convenience to consumers," Minister of Finance Peter Bethlenfalvy said in a press release. "In addition to supporting Ontario retailers, domestic producers and workers in the alcohol industry, our vision for a better marketplace has come to life thanks to people and businesses across the province."
A complete list of all convenience and grocery stores in Ontario that have been issued liquor retail licences is available daily on the Alcohol and Gaming Commission of Ontario's (AGCO) data inventory page. Please see the AGCO's website for the most up-to-date information.
You can also search this interactive map to learn more.
The final phase of the province's plan to expand alcohol sales happens Oct. 31, when other licensed grocery and big-box grocery stores will be able to sell alcoholic beverages.
CAPTION: Beer, wine and coolers will be available for sale in corner stores across the province starting Sept. 5.

Document 93 of 500

'This is a significant change': How Liberals are reacting to the resignation of their party's campaign director

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/this-is-significant-change-how-liberals-are/docview/3101086674/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=%27This+is+a+significant+change%27%3A+How+Liberals+are+reacting+to+the+resignation+of+their+party%27s+campaign+director&amp;title=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-09-05&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: OTTAWA-Trailing in the polls and suddenly facing the prospect of an early federal election, many Liberals say there is a welcome chance for renewal now that the governing party's national campaign director is leaving his post.
Jeremy Broadhurst, a veteran strategist who was set to quarterback the Liberals' election campaign after serving as a top adviser in Prime Minister Justin Trudeau's government, announced Thursday that he is stepping down from his party role on Sept. 30.
The decision, first reported in the Star, came one day after the New Democratic Party declared it was pulling out of the parliamentary alliance that was supposed to keep the Liberal minority government in power until at least June 2025. However, the Broadhurst departure was not directly related to the possibility an election could come sooner than later - a prospect multiple Liberals also downplayed.
Rather, after more than a year of national polls giving the opposition Conservatives a significant lead in voting intentions, many Liberals greeted the news of Broadhurst's departure as an opportunity to change course and improve their chances in the next federal campaign. Many doubted an election would come quickly, arguing the NDP doesn't have the money or public support to go to the polls at this time.
Nathaniel Erskine-Smith, the Liberal MP for the Toronto riding of Beaches-East York who has called for a partywide vote on Trudeau's leadership, said Broadhurst's decision to quit is a much-needed change in the Liberals' top ranks.
"The way we communicate to Canadians, the national director sets that agenda as much as anyone ... and we obviously need to change that approach in a serious way," Erskine-Smith said.
"I've got nothing against Jeremy," he added, "but we need significant change, and guess what? This is a significant change."
Speaking to reporters at the party's Quebec caucus meeting Thursday, Foreign Affairs Minister Mélanie Joly also said the move gives the Liberals a chance to look at "a new approach" and "new ideas," according to a report from Radio-Canada.
A cabinet minister who spoke to the Star on condition they not be identified said although Broadhurst had left the Prime Minister's Office to become campaign director more than a year ago, "the view inside was that he had not accomplished what he had intended."
That source said the party is financially ready for an election campaign, and fundraising has been surprisingly strong in spite of what polls suggest, but there is a lot of work to do on policy and the party's candidate roster, with questions remaining about how many of its incumbent MPs are leaving or running again.
Asked about such complaints, a senior government official who spoke on a background-only basis acknowledged that some caucus and cabinet members had aired their concerns to both the prime minister and to Broadhurst. However, the insider also defended Broadhurst and said he was "definitely not pushed out."
Liberal party spokesman Parker Lund said the party has 105 nominated candidates to date, and had its best overnight email fundraising effort of the year in the wake of the NDP withdrawal from the deal, but declined to specify how much money was raised.
Ottawa Liberal MP Yasir Naqvi downplayed the significance of Broadhurst's resignation, saying "people come and go" in politics, but added that Broadhurst's departure could be an opportunity for fresh ideas to counter the Liberals' slump in the polls. "We need a campaign team that is focused and disciplined and adaptive to new technology," he told the Star. "But it's never about just one person ... politics is a team sport."
Another Liberal MP, who agreed to speak on condition of anonymity, told the Star that it's good that Broadhurst is leaving because the Liberals need a "street fighter" leading their campaign against Pierre Poilievre's Conservatives, who are positioning themselves as scrappy populists.
"Going into a campaign like this, renewal is good," the MP said.
Under the Liberals' deal with the NDP, the New Democrats pledged to prop up the government in exchange for progress on progressive policies like dental care for low-income Canadians and broader public pharmacare coverage.
But several Liberals who spoke to the Star on Thursday said the pact never made for smooth sailing with the NDP. One said it will be a challenge to get vote-by-vote support to get laws passed in Parliament, but characterized dealing with the NDP as always "a slog. It will continue to be a slog."
Meanwhile, several Liberal MPs acknowledged to the Star that an earlier election is more likely now that the deal has been scrapped. Some expressed disappointment over the sudden shift in the political landscape, while others downplayed its significance since they don't believe the NDP is willing to immediately join forces with the Bloc Québécois and the Conservatives to topple the government.
"I don't know if it's a well-thought-through strategy," said Liberal MP John McKay. He predicted the NDP will face "relentless pressure" from the Conservatives to declare non-confidence in the government, which will force the NDP to side with the Liberals if it wants to prevent an election over the coming weeks and months.
McKay also said the death of the NDP deal cements Trudeau's position as Liberal leader. The prime minister has insisted he will stay on for the next campaign, but after the party lost a Toronto byelection in June, several Liberals called for him to step down.
McKay said the return to a more precarious minority Parliament should put that issue to rest.
"The prime minister is pretty well locked in now for the next election," he said.
Marcus Powlowski, a Liberal MP from northern Ontario, said he still believes there would be a way to quickly install a new leader if Trudeau changes his mind and decides to resign, but that he's "not contemplating that" since it is outside of his control.
"The prime minister's the prime minister," and he's "earned the right" to decide his own fate, Powlowski said.
At the same time, Powlowski argued that it is in the government's interest to stave off an election. He said he believes voters will start to sour on Poilievre if they have more time to watch him in action, and predicted the Liberals' standing in the polls would improve if economic growth picks up, inflation keeps cooling, and interest rates that have jacked up mortgage payments continue to come down.
Others, like Kingston and the Islands MP Mark Gerretsen, said the Liberals could compete in an earlier election.
"I'm as ready now as I will be a year from now," he said.
CAPTION: Prime Minister Justin Trudeau answers media questions following an announcement in Rocky Harbour, Nfld., on Sept. 4, 2024.

Document 94 of 500

Delivering on a six-year-old campaign promise gives Doug Ford something to run on in a snap election

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/delivering-on-six-year-old-campaign-promise-gives/docview/3100901998/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Delivering+on+a+six-year-old+campaign+promise+gives+Doug+Ford+something+to+run+on+in+a+snap+election&amp;title=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-09-05&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Six years after first campaigning to liberalize alcohol sales in Ontario, Premier Doug Ford has kept a promise that sets the table for the next election.
With beer, wine, cider and premixed cocktails available in 4,187 licensed convenience stores across Ontario as of Thursday morning, Ford has finally realized a cornerstone 2018 election pledge.
"Today's expansion delivers on our government's promise to give people in Ontario the same choice and convenience enjoyed by people throughout the rest of Canada," the premier said in a statement.
"With today's announcement, we're also creating new opportunities to produce and sell world-class Ontario-made beer, wine and ready-to-drink beverages in nearly 4,200 convenience stores across the province," he said.
As first announced in May, the Progressive Conservatives are paying the privately run Beer Store $225 million to break the 10-year "master framework agreement" former Liberal premier Kathleen Wynne signed in 2015, which allowed six-packs and wine in 450 supermarkets.
While that deal would have ended on Dec. 31, 2025 with minimal cost to taxpayers, Ford's Tories scrambled to deliver the promise earlier.
That's because they wanted to give themselves the flexibility to call a snap election next spring, a year earlier than the scheduled June 2026 vote.
Liberal Leader Bonnie Crombie has warned Ford's bar tab could skyrocket, dubbing his gambit "a $1-billion booze boondoggle."
But Finance Minister Peter Bethlenfalvy said the plan, which will be fully implemented by the end of October when big box outlets and other grocery stores can be licensed, is about treating Ontarians as adults.
"People finally have more options to responsibly and conveniently support local Ontario breweries and wineries - or whatever their drink of choice may be - even closer to home, and (this) is another milestone in the government's commitment to deliver more choice and convenience to consumers," said Bethlenfalvy.
"In addition to supporting Ontario retailers, domestic producers and workers in the alcohol industry, our vision for a better marketplace has come to life thanks to people and businesses across the province," he said.
Bethlenfalvy noted that, as part of the reforms, owners of multiple bars and restaurants will be allowed to move wine and spirits between locations to help them manage inventory with less red tape.
As well, supermarkets will be allowed to display alcohol throughout their stores instead of just on designated aisles.
The provincially owned Liquor Control Board of Ontario will retain its long-time monopoly on the sale of spirits such as whisky, gin, vodka, rum and tequila.
Against the backdrop of the changes, Ford was asked Tuesday if he was planning to trigger an early election.
The premier would only confirm that "we aren't having an election this year."
But he was less definitive about whether he would call a $150-million vote next spring - even though his Tories already have a massive majority in the legislature, where the opposition parties cannot thwart his governing agenda.
When reporters queried the premier whether PC cabinet ministers and MPPs are being instructed to decide by Christmas if they are running again, Ford emphasized that "you always have to be prepared."
"That's what it comes down to - no matter which way you look at it, we aren't too far from any election, no matter if it's next year (or) the following year," he said.
Ford is ruminating about the early vote because he is leading in public-opinion polls and is concerned about the next federal election that could see Conservative Leader Pierre Poilievre topple Prime Minister Justin Trudeau's Liberals.
Ontario voters traditionally prefer different parties to be in power at Queen's Park and in Ottawa at the same time - the Tories dominated federally during the provincial Liberal dynasty from 2003 to 2018.
So Ford's team worries that a Poilievre victory could be bad for their fortunes if they wait until 2026, especially if a new federal Tory government makes deep cuts in transfer payments to the provinces.
There is also the looming spectre of the RCMP investigation in the Greenbelt land swap scandal that could jolt the political landscape provincially.
CAPTION: Employees at a 7-Eleven store in Toronto on Wednesday prepare the new beer section for sales to begin at 7 a.m. on Thursday.

Document 95 of 500

My sister was tireless. If ALS hadn't claimed her life, I'm sure she'd be knocking on Doug Ford's door

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/my-sister-was-tireless-if-als-hadnt-claimed-her/docview/3100901976/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=My+sister+was+tireless.+If+ALS+hadn%27t+claimed+her+life%2C+I%27m+sure+she%27d+be+knocking+on+Doug+Ford%27s+door&amp;title=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-09-05&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: My sister Sheila was a keener.Five years younger, with a disposition far sunnier than mine, she was: a volunteer, an activist, a photographer, a belter-outer of songs, a skilled cook, a committed NDP supporter, a music lover. She believed in people, cherished her family and friends with quiet ferocity, and routinely dispersed whatever clouds she spotted on someone else's horizon. She laughed easily and smiled often.She never lost that contagious smile either, even when she'd lost everything else.Ten years ago, she wore it while participating in the Ice Bucket Challenge, the phenomenon that raised $220 million globally for ALS clinical care and research. Canada's $17.2 million was more per capita than any other country.Sheila could still talk then, though her speech had a drunken slur to it, which she hated. But sitting there in her wheelchair, friends around her, local Vancouver TV cameras rolling, she endured the icy water poured over her head and laughed.That was Sheila and her ALS - indomitable spirit captured in a smile. By the time she died a year later in hospice, age 63, she was trapped inside an unresponsive body, no longer able to move, eat, breathe without help, talk or communicate in any way other than by using her eyes to spell out a sentence, letter by agonizing letter. Amazingly, though, she could still smile.ALS (Amyotrophic Lateral Sclerosis) is a neurodegenerative disease that attacks the body's voluntary muscles progressively and relentlessly. Ten per cent of cases are genetic, while the rest, like my sister's, are random. Despite ongoing research and some discoveries, there is still no cure, and most people diagnosed with it will die within five years. ALS is unspeakably cruel.Roughly 4,000 Canadians are living with ALS at any one time (in the grim calculus, 1,000 annual deaths cancel out 1,000 new diagnoses), so, unlike cancer, it is not one of healthcare's headliners. ALS is much easier for governments to ignore. Which is tragic."It's such a horrendously expensive disease," says Tammy Moore, CEO of ALS Canada. The need for complex equipment and assistive devices evolves for patients with dismaying speed, but sufferers and their caregivers are often left to the kindness of strangers. Government help across Canada for such needs is uneven and, in Ontario, nonexistent.ALS Canada provides nearly 40 different kinds of necessary equipment to patients, involving purchase, maintenance, shipping, setting up, education - and gets zero dollars from Ontario."We're actually augmenting the system," says Moore. "We're funding it through philanthropy."And don't get her started on the disparities in clinical care. You're in good hands if you live near the five multidisciplinary ALS clinics in Toronto, London, Hamilton, Kingston and Ottawa. But good luck if you live in Kapuskasing or Kirkland Lake.ALS Canada and the Ontario clinics are currently lobbying the government for $6.6 million in funding, says Moore. "After all, one stay in ICU for an ALS patient at Sunnybrook costs $30,000. We're just asking for $5,000 per patient annually." Sounds reasonable.If Sheila were still here, I suspect she'd be at Doug Ford's door, knocking with gusto. She was tireless that way, a committed activist to the end. Six months before she died in 2015, she received the "Exceptional Public Awareness" award from ALS BC, whose executive director spoke at her memorial service.From the beginning, Sheila knew her nearly four-year battle with ALS would end in defeat. That didn't stop her from preparing gracefully for her death while animating each painful day with her own guiding spirit. With assistance, she voted early in the October federal election. On Thanksgiving, a week before she died, she insisted on posting a message. It exhausted her, but she knew she was leaving soon, and saying a final thank you to those she'd loved was urgent.No science will ever prove this, but I suspect that the ALS monster chooses its victims from the ranks of exceptional people, people with great hearts and stunning courage.The Ice Bucket Challenge is history now, with ALS associations back to less frenzied days of lobbying and fundraising. But the hope - for donations, for government support, for a cure - that hope remains.It's stubborn that way. Like a smile that will not fade.
CAPTION: Thi group shot, June 2015, four months before Sheila Tynan died, shows her family with her at the annual ALS Walk in Vancouver. Sheila, front centre, was joined by, seen left to right brother-in-law Dan Kennedy, sister Janice Kennedy, niece Jessica Kennedy, niece Sarah Kennedy, and nephew-in-law Brien Whalen.In this photo from August, 2014, Amir Johnson and Tim Leiweke douse Nazem Kadri in the ALS Ice Bucket Challenge outside the Air Canada Centre.
CREDIT: Janice Kennedy, Contributor

Document 96 of 500

Sept. 5: The homeless need more than housing, drunk driving must be stopped and other letters to the editor

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/sept-5-homeless-need-more-than-housing-drunk/docview/3100901804/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Sept.+5%3A+The+homeless+need+more+than+housing%2C+drunk+driving+must+be+stopped+and+other+letters+to+the+editor&amp;title=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-09-05&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: We need more than housing
Re: 'Our city is basically dying': How do we stop downtown Hamilton's progress from unravelling? Aug. 29
I used to believe that a city would never allow its inhabitants to live in ways that could be compared to the favela-like conditions, where people may live in decrepit shacks. I was wrong. Tents have become the new shacks, allowing people to exist in a deplorable state between life and not-life.
The high cost of homelessness includes numerous visits to hospital emergency departments, the routine maintenance of tent cities, dependence on food banks and propping up dozens of agencies that cater to the poor on various levels.
It is glaringly obvious that the Mental Health Act needs a complete overhaul, so that people who are a danger to themselves and others can be directed to appropriate care. If you look at the "History of Mental Health Reform" on the Canadian Mental Health website, you can easily see the massive divide in what is written there and what actually takes place.
You can allow people to live in parks and starve on the streets, but this is neither healthy nor moral. Some people refuse to move to indoor spaces, many have addiction issues, some suffer from mental illness and all of them deserve better than to freeze or broil outside.
They not only need a safe place to live, but the kind of care that will help them navigate their way back to living a decent life.
Grace Bellefeuille, Hamilton
Ford has the power to act
When I read any news about the state of our province and watch the weekly decimation of our quality of life, I realize that the Conservative government under Doug Ford holds the burden of responsibility for much of it.
He has the power to change and better the outcomes for many in dire need but chooses not to. Federally, Pierre Poilievre's platform blatantly insinuates he will start cutting (with fervour) everything he doesn't see of value. If you're struggling now just wait, so don't vote Conservative provincially or federally.
Robert Panchyson, Burlington
Punishment should fit the crime
Re: Teen 'jumped' and robbed of Nike Jordan shoes: police, Aug. 31
Let the punishment fit the crime. In recent news, a teenager was robbed and assaulted by a gang of other youths for his sneakers in the middle of the afternoon in Hamilton. The police have since apprehended the assailants. The courts can't do this of course, but I suggest the judge sentence the street punks to one month in India, working in a Nike sweatshop. I can hear them crying and bawling for their mothers now.
Walt Fisher, Hamilton
Drunk driving must be stopped
Re: The senseless tragedy that cost Johnny and Matthew Gaudreau their lives hits home on many levels, Aug. 31
The tragedy of the deaths of two outstanding young men, Matthew and Johnny Gaudreau has resulted in the outpouring of grief from all walks of life and across two countries. I cannot imagine how the families of these young men will be able to cope. However, every day we hear of drunk drivers taking away the lives of innocent people.
This has got to stop! "Don't drink and drive" is not working. Putting drunk drivers in jail and having huge fines and sentences will not work!
When someone is intoxicated he/she cannot make correct decisions. Now is the time to honour Johnny and Matthew and the countless others who have died as the result of a drunk driver. Make it mandatory for car manufacturers to put breathalyzers in every car built. Stop deaths from drunk drivers at the source - the car. Prevent drunks from driving drunk.
No excuses about the cost, as lots of safety features have been added to cars to make cars safer. Preventing the death of innocent victims from a drunk driver is the goal.
Breathalyzers are expensive to add after manufacturing, however, if every car was required to have one the cost of the equipment would go down. Technology to prevent cars from being operated by drunks is here. Use it!
Obviously this will take time and thought to put this in place, but now is the time to start. Trying to figure out where and who to send a letter like this to someone who could be in charge of instigating this is mind boggling.
The time to start this going forward is now.
Donna Paterson, Hamilton
Our society has become one of rage
Re: A menacing stranger harassed an MP at a lake. Onlookers stood up for democracy, Aug. 27
While on a bus tour of Italy in 2019, I was part of a lineup at a restaurant that included many tourists when I was introduced to a fellow Canadian, a man from Alberta who was told I was from Toronto.
The man turned around, looked at me, sneered and said "Toronto eh? I bet you're voting for Trudeau."
I was a little taken aback, but not surprised. I quietly and calmly told him I don't discuss politics while on vacation. He didn't say anything, sneered again and turned his back. I was embarrassed by the disrespect he showed not only to me but to our country. I know the situation could have escalated, but I didn't want to engage or encourage him further.
I suppose, considering the political atmosphere these days, I got off lightly. It frightens me terribly to think that this, and, probably worse, is ahead for my grandchildren.
Bonnie Beaudry, Burlington
CAPTION: The homeless not only need a safe place to live, but the kind of care that will help them navigate their way back to living a decent life, a reader writes.

Subject: Automobiles; Mental health; Homeless people; Mental disorders; Drunk driving

Document 97 of 500

Sept. 5 editorial cartoon

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/sept-5-editorial-cartoon/docview/3100901785/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Document 98 of 500

Doug Ford's government needs to stop strip searching kids

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/doug-fords-government-needs-stop-strip-searching/docview/3100901608/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Doug+Ford%27s+government+needs+to+stop+strip+searching+kids&amp;title=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-09-05&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: The Ontario government is allowing private contractors to strip children as young as 12, to examine their naked bodies, to make them squat and cough, to keep them fully naked while they search and take notes on everything they see.
The government can't, or won't, tell you how often this happens. No government minister will say anything about it all - nothing about the sanctioned, degrading practice of stripping children nude, of humiliating them in the name of safety, of leaving some of the most vulnerable and damaged kids in this province with even more lasting psychological scars.
Those are the headline takeaways from the Star's reporting this week and last on strip searches in Ontario youth jails. To call the stories, by crime reporter Jennifer Pagliaro, outrageous would be an understatement. They are heartbreaking, both in minute detail and broad scope.
They make it very clear how much this government cares about the children - and again, these are children - who are being routinely strip searched in private and public facilities in Ontario both before and after they've been put on trial. Put short, they don't even bother to keep accurate stats on how often this is going on.
Almost everything Pagliaro was able to dig out about the prevalence and details of this practice came not from the ministry in charge, not from the facilities or private companies holding the kids, and certainly not from Michael Parsa, the Progressive Conservative minister ultimately in charge of all of this, but from lawsuits, court records and testimony in criminal trials.
The Ontario Ministry of Children, Community and Social Services has so far stonewalled Pagliaro's attempts to obtain any kind of comprehensive data on youth strip searches. In the process, it managed to reveal that it doesn't even keep particularly detailed stats on how often kids in Ontario are being subjected to a practice the Supreme Court calls "inherently humiliating and degrading."
Pagliaro asked the ministry how many strip searches took place in the province's 27 youth detention facilities (five public, 22 private) between 2022 and 2023. The ministry told her it couldn't answer because it doesn't know. It doesn't collect strip search data from its own facilities in a way that would allow it to be aggregated.
That's bad enough. What's worse is that the government doesn't even bother to collect strip search data at all from the private contractors that run the bulk of all youth facilities in the province. "We do mandate that the records be produced and kept," a spokesman told Pagliaro in a statement, "but we do not collect them."
Running a jail, any jail, isn't easy. Contraband - especially drugs and weapons - is a real issue in any facility holding people of any age charged or convicted of a crime. Guards, like any other worker, have a right to be safe on the job. They also have a duty to keep other prisoners safe from attack. But strip searching is not the answer.
As Pagliaro's reporting, and years of litigation and research, show, forcing prisoners to strip, expose their genitals and have their bodily orifices inspected, not just when they are suspected of smuggling contraband, but routinely, anytime they want to enter or leave a facility, is more than just humiliating. It's traumatic, for prisoners of any age, let alone children. People in prisons have been convicted of crimes, but their punishment is the removal of their liberty, not their dignity.
It's also unnecessary. Full body scanners, like the kind used in all major Canadian airports, could achieve the same aims, without the degradation and trauma. The government, in other words, could have solved this problem years ago.
The ministry told Pagliaro a review of strip search practices in youth jails is now underway. Whatever data that review collects, the correct policy answer is already clear. Doug Ford's government needs to stop strip searching children, now.
CAPTION: The Roy McMurtry Youth Centre in Brampton.

Subject: Children & youth; Prisons; Prisoner treatment; Strip searches

Letters

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/letters/docview/3100793189/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=unknown&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Letters&amp;title=The+Spectator&amp;issn=11899417&amp;date=2024-09-05&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.15&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Spectator&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: We need more than housing
Re: How do we stop downtown Hamilton's progress from unravelling? Aug. 29
I used to believe that a city would never allow its inhabitants to live in ways that could be compared to the favela-like conditions, where people may live in decrepit shacks. I was wrong. Tents have become the new shacks, allowing people to exist in a deplorable state between life and not-life.
The high cost of homelessness includes numerous visits to hospital emergency departments, the routine maintenance of tent cities, dependence on food banks and propping up dozens of agencies that cater to the poor on various levels.
It is glaringly obvious that the Mental Health Act needs a complete overhaul, so that people who are a danger to themselves and others can be directed to appropriate care. If you look at the "History of Mental Health Reform" on the Canadian Mental Health website, you can easily see the massive divide in what is written there and what actually takes place.
You can allow people to live in parks and starve on the streets, but this is neither healthy nor moral. Some people refuse to move to indoor spaces, many have addiction issues, some suffer from mental illness and all of them deserve better than to freeze or broil outside. They not only need a safe place to live, but the kind of care that will help them navigate their way back to living a decent life.
Grace Bellefeuille, Hamilton
Ford has the power to act
When I read any news about the state of our province and watch the weekly decimation of our quality of life, I realize that the Conservative government under Doug Ford holds the burden of responsibility for much of it. He has the power to change and better the outcomes for many in dire need but chooses not to. Federally, Pierre Poilievre's platform blatantly insinuates he will start cutting (with fervour) everything he doesn't see of value. If you're struggling now just wait, so don't vote Conservative provincially or federally.
Robert Panchyson, Burlington
Punishment should fit crime
Re: Teen 'jumped' and robbed of Nike Jordan shoes: police, Aug. 31
Let the punishment fit the crime. In recent news, a teenager was robbed and assaulted by a gang of other youths for his sneakers in the middle of the afternoon in Hamilton. The police have since apprehended the assailants. The courts can't do this of course, but I suggest the judge sentence the street punks to one month in India, working in a Nike sweatshop. I can hear them crying and bawling for their mothers now.
Walt Fisher, Hamilton
Drunk driving must be stopped
Re: The senseless tragedy that cost Johnny and
Matthew Gaudreau their lives hits home on
many levels, Aug. 31
The tragedy of the deaths of two outstanding young men, Matthew and Johnny Gaudreau has resulted in the outpouring of grief from all walks of life and across two countries. I cannot imagine how the families of these young men will be able to cope. However, every day we hear of drunk drivers taking away the lives of innocent people.
This has got to stop! "Don't drink and drive" is not working. Putting drunk drivers in jail and having huge fines and sentences will not work! When someone is intoxicated he/she cannot make correct decisions. Now is the time to honour Johnny and Matthew and the countless others who have died as the result of a drunk driver. Make it mandatory for car manufacturers to put breathalyzers in every car built. Stop deaths from drunk drivers at the source - the car. Prevent drunks from driving drunk. No excuses about the cost, as lots of safety features have been added to cars to make cars safer. Preventing the death of innocent victims from a drunk driver is the goal. Breathalyzers are expensive to add after manufacturing, however, if every car was required to have one the cost of the equipment would go down. Technology to prevent cars from being operated by drunks is here. Use it!
Obviously this will take time and thought to put this in place, but now is the time to start. Trying to figure out where and who to send a letter like this to someone who could be in charge of instigating this is mind boggling. The time to start this going forward is now.
Donna Paterson, Hamilton
Society seems full of rage
Re: A menacing stranger harassed an MP at a lake. Onlookers stood up for democracy, Aug. 27
While on a bus tour of Italy in 2019, I was part of a lineup at a restaurant that included many tourists when I was introduced to a fellow Canadian, a man from Alberta who was told I was from Toronto. The man turned around, looked at me, sneered and said "Toronto eh? I bet you're voting for Trudeau." I was a little taken aback, but not surprised. I quietly and calmly told him I don't discuss politics while on vacation. He didn't say anything, sneered again and turned his back. I was embarrassed by the disrespect he showed not only to me but to our country. I know the situation could have escalated, but I didn't want to engage or encourage him further. I suppose, considering the political atmosphere these days, I got off lightly. It frightens me terribly to think that this, and, probably worse, is ahead for my grandchildren.
Bonnie Beaudry, Burlington

Document 100 of 500

Ford's trying to beat the clock

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/fords-trying-beat-clock/docview/3100782023/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=unknown&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Ford%27s+trying+to+beat+the+clock&amp;title=The+Spectator&amp;issn=11899417&amp;date=2024-09-05&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.14&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Spectator&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Doug Ford has been underestimated by the media and misunderstood by his opponents in two elections, paving his way to two majority government victories. Will history repeat itself yet again?
With Labour Day out of the way, the campaign now moves into high gear. The premier is seeking a pretext for a premature spring vote, one year ahead of schedule. With an RCMP criminal probe looming over the Greenbelt imbroglio, his survival is more about beating the clock than defeating his opponents.
What is less well known is how Ontario's two untested opposition leaders - the NDP's Marit Stiles and the Liberals' Bonnie Crombie - are preparing for the showdown.
Later this month, both provincial parties are holding strategy and policy meetings that will set the stage for the coming campaign. But what they first need to understand is that Ford's governing Progressive Conservatives are a moving target, which means the Liberals and NDP need to fight the next war - not the last one.
Ford's critics frequently make the mistake of assuming everyone thinks like them. The opposition likes to think they need only diss the premier and disseminate their critiques to win the day on election day. That's a misreading of opinion polling about Ford and a misunderstanding of the public's low opinion of opposition leaders. The premier's strongest antagonists can't fathom the strength of his enduring appeal.
The media typically stress Ford's high negatives as evidence of his polarizing personality. But the numbers in the latest poll by Abacus Data for the Toronto Star tell a more revealing story. In fact, 44 per cent considered the premier to be "friendly," versus 20 per cent who picked "mean."
To be sure, there are clear signs of vulnerability for the premier: 42 per cent described him as "dishonest" and 41 per cent as "corrupt" in the wake of the Greenbelt scandal (a remarkable 82 per cent of respondents were familiar with it).
That said, the Abacus poll showed Ford's Tories enjoying 42 per cent support, far ahead of Crombie's Liberals at 26 per cent and Stiles's NDP at 21 per cent. In other words (and numbers), people have already taken into account Ford's conduct or misconduct. Which means Ford's biggest fear is the cops closing in. That's why his PC campaign team is in such a hurry to flout the fixed election date law (which dictates a June 2026 election) - it wants the election out of the way before an investigation leads to any formal charges in the $8.28-billion Greenbelt land swap.
But the opposition can't count on the police to do their political work for them. Corruption catches people's attention - until it doesn't.
The worst thing New Democrats and Liberals could do is base their campaigns on the premier's thinking and timing, rather than doing their own timely thinking. And yet, time after time, the opposition falls into Ford's traps.
When word first leaked out of an early election, Stiles boasted that New Democrats were ready - and goaded the Tories to "bring it on." Why play into Ford's playbook?
Crombie, by contrast, is calling Ford to account for jumping the gun with an early election costing $150 million, noting that governments are "typically punished" for such self-interest.
Crombie's Liberals are convening a policy conference on the Sept. 21 weekend to help cobble together an election platform while distancing herself from her unpopular federal Liberal counterpart, Justin Trudeau.
The New Democrats are also holding a provincial council meeting the same weekend to lay down the party line. But both parties remain prisoners of Ford's timelines, and captive of federal polling trends that bleed into provincial results.
Rather than focusing on events beyond their control - a police probe and federal politics - far better to dwell on voter preoccupations closer to home. Unfortunately, the opposition parties have failed to lay claim to a governing mandate of their own. Neither has outlined their visions, delineated their policies, or distinguished themselves from one another.
As for the premier, he hasn't remotely made the case for a renewed mandate so far ahead of schedule. If Ford is running out of gas early, he should turn over the keys.
Martin Regg-Cohn covers provincial politics
for Torstar.
CAPTION:

CREDIT: Martin Regg cohn OPINION Martin Regg-Cohn covers provincial politics for Torstar.;

Subject: Meetings; Scandals

Document 101 of 500

Jagmeet Singh had a good reason to tear up his deal with Justin Trudeau - and it's not that he wants an election

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/jagmeet-singh-had-good-reason-tear-up-his-deal/docview/3100963535/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Jagmeet+Singh+had+a+good+reason+to+tear+up+his+deal+with+Justin+Trudeau+-+and+it%27s+not+that+he+wants+an+election&amp;title=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-09-04&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: It was a marriage of convenience that ended because it was no longer convenient. No need for progressives to shed a tear, or for Liberals to fear the fall of the government, or for Conservatives to celebrate.
NDP Leader Jagmeet Singh did what he needed to do, what caucus members wanted him to do, what many New Democrats members believed should have been done last year.
On Wednesday, days before NDP MPs were set to meet in Montreal, and less than two weeks before two important byelections, Singh pulled the plug on his agreement with Prime Minister Justin Trudeau - 10 months before it was set to expire.
The supply and confidence agreement - signed in March of 2022 in the shadows of a capital blockaded by a convoy of truckers and protesters angry over COVID vaccination requirements, and the invasion of Ukraine by Russia - is no more. The deal was framed by Trudeau and Singh as one that offered stability and delivered on the mandate voters gave parliamentarians in the fall of 2021, when they returned MPs to Ottawa in nearly the same formation as in the previous Parliament. Canadians wanted MPs to "work together to put people and families first. Deliver results and build a better future," Trudeau said in announcing the deal. "This is what Canadians expect from their politicians."
But what Canadians have also learned to expect from politicians is that they make decisions that best serve their partisan interests.
Singh signed on because the agreement provided a good answer to the question of why the NDP was propping up the Grits. With a minority of seats in the House of Commons, the Liberals needed a dance partner to avoid an early election call. The NDP thought it could be seen as extracting concessions from the Liberals - action on climate change, reconciliation, affordable housing, and big dream items such as a new national dental care program, progress toward a universal pharmacare, and a ban on the use of "scabs" during lock outs or strikes - in return for its continued support. The agreement allowed the NDP to vote with the Liberals without appearing spineless.
But as the deal wore on, it became less of a blessing and more of a liability.
In June, Singh's team concluded there was little left to accomplish. They had extracted all they thought they could get from it.
They also faced a bigger problem: it was the Liberals who took most of the credit from programs such as dental care. There was no bump in support for the New Democrats who sit at 18 per cent, according to the latest polling by Abacus Data .
Meanwhile, Singh and the NDP spent months being defined by the Conservatives as one and the same as the Liberals, an "NDP-Liberal coalition" responsible for all the ills plaguing the country.
With the next election gearing up to be a change election - 86 per cent of those polled say it's time for one - the Conservatives' framing wrote off Singh as a change option.
It was a brilliant strategic move by the Conservatives, and a big hurdle for the NDP.
It's one the party hopes to overcome in announcing that the deal is ripped up, with an election-style ad showcasing Singh as ready to defeat both Pierre Poilievre's Conservatives and Trudeau's Liberals in order to fight for the middle class.
The next few months are guaranteed to be rockier but, unless the parties miscalculate, this announcement won't bring much immediate change.
Neither the NDP nor the Liberals want an election campaign with the Conservatives leading massively in public support.
Poilievre is itching to go to the polls. On Wednesday, he said he'd test the NDP leader's resolve to distance himself from the Grits with a non-confidence motion at the first chance he gets.
But that opportunity might not come until November, if the Liberals delay offering opposition days to the Conservatives. It could come even later if the Liberals decide to prorogue Parliament.
New Democrats are getting ready for any possibility. If the House of Commons returns as planned on Sept. 16, the NDP's national director is already laying the groundwork for continued support of the Liberal government.
Speaking to CBC last week, Anne McGrath said that while the "Liberals have kind of run out of gas," there are things the NDP wants to accomplish such as getting the government to "improve the EI system."
The party may still have to play that game - the we-demanded-the-Liberals-act-and-they-acted game - to save face and ensure the short-term continuity of this Parliament. Few New Democrats want a repeat of 2005, when the NDP pulled their support from Paul Martin's Liberal minority government and ushered in a decade of Conservative rule under Stephen Harper.
Other than the NDP, only the Bloc Québécois can offer the Liberals a lifeline. But it too has been targeted by the Conservatives, who greatly exaggerate the BQ's closeness to the Liberals in order to score points in Quebec. With the Bloc still strong in public support, however, it has less to fear about triggering a trip to the polls.
So until Singh and the NDP are confident their new rebranding has them leading as the progressive option for change, the result of Wednesday's announcement may be more sizzle than substance.
CAPTION: Prime Minister Justin Trudeau and NDP Leader Jagmeet Singh prepare for the start of the federal election English-language leaders' debate in Gatineau, Que., on Sept. 9, 2021.

Document 102 of 500

NDP kills deal that propped up Justin Trudeau's Liberals, putting his minority government at risk

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/ndp-kills-deal-that-propped-up-justin-trudeaus/docview/3100784508/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=NDP+kills+deal+that+propped+up+Justin+Trudeau%27s+Liberals%2C+putting+his+minority+government+at+risk&amp;title=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-09-04&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: OTTAWA-New Democrat Leader Jagmeet Singh is ripping up his 2022 deal with Prime Minister Justin Trudeau to support the Liberal government- a move that immediately makes for a more unstable minority Parliament and could create the conditions for a snap election call.
It does not automatically trigger an election because the 24 NDP members of Parliament could continue to support the 154-member minority Liberal government on a vote-by-vote basis - which is exactly what the Liberals are counting on - rather than automatically moving to declare lost confidence in Trudeau.
The collapse of the agreement does, however, mean Singh could introduce a non-confidence vote or vote for any such motion the Conservatives or Bloc Québécois could present, potentially plunging the country into a federal campaign - which the Conservatives immediately demanded.
In a recorded video statement released Wednesday on social media, Singh said he is cancelling the "supply and confidence agreement" 10 months early because the Liberals cannot be trusted to fight or win what he says is a battle "for the future of the middle class."
Singh said the governing Liberals have proved they "will always cave to corporate greed," and "don't deserve another chance from Canadians," adding the Liberals are "careering towards irrelevancy."
Singh talked tough about getting ready for the next federal vote, suggesting the Conservatives would form a government that would make deep cuts that will hurt workers, retirees, young people, patients, and families "in order to give more to big corporations and wealthy CEOs."
"The fact is, the Liberals are too weak, too selfish and too beholden to corporate interests to fight for people. They cannot be changed, they cannot restore the hope, they cannot stop the Conservatives," he said. "But we can."
"In the next federal election, Canadians will choose between Pierre Poilievre's callous cuts or hope - hope that when we stand united, we win, that Canada's middle class will once again thrive together."
"I am running for prime minister because together, we can and will stop Conservative cuts," he said.
Trudeau, speaking less than an hour after Singh's withdrawal from the deal, turned the NDP leader's comments back on the New Democrats.
He said he would remain focused on ensuring Canada's economic recovery continues, hailing the Bank of Canada's decision Wednesday to cut the interest rate to 4.25 per cent as proof his government's approach is working. He said there is more work to do to deliver dental care, free insulin and contraceptives, and environmental policies that will improve the lives of Canadians.
In Rocky Harbour, Newfoundland to highlight federal money for its school lunch program, Trudeau said he looked forward to speaking with Singh "about how we're going to continue to demonstrate that confident countries invest in their citizens, invest in the future, because that's what we're doing."
"The NDP is going to have to make decisions about whether or not they want to stand with Pierre Poilievre who's going to cut the school food programs, cut dental care, not deliver on pharmacare, or whether they're going to focus on what it means to Canadians to be there to give them support through this difficult time.
"Every politician gets to choose whether they want to play politics or whether they want to serve Canadians, and I certainly hope the NDP will stay true to its fundamental values, which is making sure that Canadians get the support they need, and keeping away the austerity, the cuts and the damage that will be done by Conservatives if they get the chance."
Just last week, Liberal House Leader Karina Gould at a cabinet retreat predicted the deal would endure, saying they had made good progress on things like dental care and pharmacare, and there was more to do.
However, polls show the NDP is at between 15-20 per cent approval ratings, has gained nothing at all from supporting the Liberals, and the Liberals under Trudeau have plunged in the polls, in the face of withering criticism by the Conservatives that they had formed a "costly coalition."
Conservative Leader Pierre Poilievre wrote Singh last week to pull the plug on the deal with Trudeau and called for a fall "carbon tax" election, and in a statement after Singh's announcement, Poilievre called it a "media stunt."
"Sellout Singh refuses to state whether the NDP will vote with non-confidence to cause a carbon tax election at the first chance," said Poilievre, saying the NDP leader's support for the Liberals' increased carbon tax "will drive Canadians to food banks and grind our economy to a halt- killing hundreds of thousands of jobs."
New Democrats say Singh's move comes after a summer in which senior representatives discussed next steps with party members and whether now was the time to pull out of the deal.
Just a few short months ago, Singh told the Star's Althia Raj that he viewed the deal as a way to leverage additional changes from the Trudeau government, underscoring the ongoing need to improve the dental care program and benefits for disabled Canadians. "We want to see the work finished," he said then.
However, a New Democrat with a senior position in Canada's labour movement, who spoke to the Star earlier this week on the condition they not be named, said the Liberals' decision to impose binding arbitration to end a work stoppage after Canada's two largest railways and the union representing their workers failed to reach a new contract, crossed a line for the NDP.
Singh's response to that move - calling the Trudeau Liberals "cowardly, anti-worker and proof that they will always cave to corporate greed" - the language Singh used Wednesday - was evidence the New Democrats were approaching the "natural end" of the agreement, the source said.
"That decision was pretty bruising to the relationship," they told the Star.
In an interview with the Star last month, veteran NDP MP Charlie Angus said he felt his party had "achieved what we were going to achieve in the agreement."
"There's an election coming. So now we need to begin a transition. Where are we going next?" he said.
In the aftermath of chaos caused by the truckers' convoy blockade of Ottawa and border crossings in the spring of 2022, Trudeau and Singh negotiated and signed the co-operation agreement.
Although the Conservatives, then in the midst of a leadership race, blasted it as a Liberal-NDP "coalition," it was never a formal coalition. It never guaranteed the NDP positions in cabinet or a veto on decision-making.
Instead, the deal secured advancements on a series of progressive policies, which the Liberals agreed to in exchange for Singh's support in votes of confidence in the Commons and in committees on a range of budgetary and key policy matters like dental care, pharmacare and federal anti-scab legislation.
The NDP's backing on confidence votes until June 2025, four months before the legislated fixed date for an October 2025 election, was meant to avert triggering an early vote.
The law only applies to majority governments, and is not binding on minority parliaments, which can face a confidence test at any time.
With the NDP support, the Liberals could count on its bills passing with majority support in the Commons. Without it, the Liberals could try to woo Bloc Québécois support.
Currently, the party standings in the House of Commons are Liberals at 154, Conservatives at 119, Bloc Québécois at 32, NDP at 24, Green Party at two. There are three Independent MPs, and four seats are vacant (with two byelections scheduled for Sept. 16, the day that Parliament resumes sitting for the fall session).
Singh was elected leader of the NDP in 2017 to replace Thomas Mulcair, and in his first federal campaign in 2019 he led the party to a 20-seat drop, placing it as fourth in the Commons. In the following 2021 campaign, he managed to increase the party's total by one seat.
Although it is being cancelled 10 months early, both Singh and Trudeau can check off - in some form - most of the deal's 24 objectives.
The pact saw the creation of a national dental coverage plan that has delivered oral health services to hundreds of thousands of Canadians.
It resulted in the introduction and passage of "anti-scab" legislation, which banned the use of replacement workers in situations where employees in federally regulated industries were locked out from the job or went on strike.
Another bill aimed at protecting jobs in the transition into a clean energy economy also became law under the arrangement.
Both parties also took steps to introduce election reforms, while other goals, like tabling legislation focused on improving long-term care and ushering in a homebuyers' bill of rights, have yet to fully materialize.
Perhaps the most contentious of the deal's major pillars was a central promise to pass legislation committing to a universal, national pharmacare program by the end of 2023.
Negotiations to hash out the details of that framework nearly pushed the New Democrats to prematurely abandon the deal; eventually, a bill was introduced earlier this year that outlined a plan to first cover diabetes and birth control drugs and devices, and laid out a road map for wider government-paid drug coverage. That bill has passed the House of Commons, but not the Senate.
But as much as the deal - which was struck in early 2022 in the shadow of a receding global pandemic, the historic invocation of the Emergencies Act and Russia's invasion of Ukraine - was meant to secure parliamentary stability, it also boxed Singh into an increasingly precarious political position.
As the Liberals began stumbling in the polls and Poilievre's Conservatives ascended to new heights, Singh's decision to stand by Trudeau while simultaneously claiming to keep him in check did little to boost the NDP's fortunes - or, at times, its leader's credibility.
And while the arrangement did give the fourth-party-status New Democrats a tantalizing taste of what governing might be like, they too felt pressure inside the deal.
One year in, faith in the pact - which operated on a foundational principle of "no surprises" - was shaken when the NDP were blindsided by proposed changes to the Liberals' then-firearms bill. Nearly a year after that, facing heat from party membership, Singh's inner circle planned for a potential election scenario when pharmacare talks began to stall.
But the New Democrats wanted to see the health-care centrepieces of the deal through, and frequently pointed to issues outside the deal structure, like affordability measures, the Canada Disability Benefit and accountability on foreign interference, as areas where they could push for additional action.

Document 103 of 500

Doug Ford is trying to beat the clock. What are the opposition leaders doing to beat him?

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/doug-ford-is-trying-beat-clock-what-are/docview/3100457615/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Doug+Ford+is+trying+to+beat+the+clock.+What+are+the+opposition+leaders+doing+to+beat+him%3F&amp;title=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-09-04&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Doug Ford has been underestimated by the media and misunderstood by his opponents in two elections, paving his way to two majority government victories.
Will history repeat itself yet again?
With Labour Day out of the way, the campaign now moves into high gear. Never mind the U.S. presidential race, Ontario's election preparations are ramping up on the home front.
The premier is seeking a pretext for a premature spring vote, one year ahead of schedule. With an RCMP criminal probe looming over the Greenbelt imbroglio, his survival is more about beating the clock than defeating his opponents.
What is less well known is precisely how Ontario's two untested opposition leaders - the NDP's Marit Stiles and the Liberals' Bonnie Crombie - are preparing for the anticipated showdown. How will these two rookie leaders respond?
To defeat your adversary you must know your enemy. And define yourself to your audience.
Later this month, both provincial parties are holding key strategy and policy meetings that will set the stage for the coming campaign. But what they first need to understand is that Ford's governing Progressive Conservatives are a moving target, which means the Liberals and New Democrats need to fight the next war - not the last one.
Ford's critics frequently make the mistake of assuming that everyone thinks like them. The opposition likes to think they need only diss the premier and disseminate their critiques to win the day on election day.
That's a misreading of opinion polling about Ford and a misunderstanding of the public's low opinion of opposition leaders. The premier's strongest antagonists can't fathom the strength of his enduring appeal.
The media typically stress Ford's high negatives as evidence of his polarizing personality. But the numbers in the latest poll by Abacus Data for the Toronto Star tell a more revealing story.
In fact, 44 per cent considered the premier to be "friendly," versus only 20 per cent who picked "mean." That's a far cry from Ford's bombastic image in 2018 when he campaigned provincially for the first time after an equally cantankerous career on city council.
To be sure, there are clear signs of vulnerability for the premier: 42 per cent described him as "dishonest" and 41 per cent as "corrupt" in the wake of the Greenbelt scandal (a remarkable 82 per cent of respondents were familiar with it).
All that said, the Abacus poll showed Ford's Tories enjoying 42 per cent support, far ahead of Crombie's Liberals at 26 per cent and the Stiles's NDP at 21 per cent. In other words (and numbers), people have already taken into account - discounted - Ford's conduct or misconduct.
Which means that Ford's biggest fear isn't Stiles or Crombie coming after him, it's the cops closing in. That's why his PC campaign team is in such a hurry to flout the province's fixed election date law (which dictates a June 2026 election) - it wants the election out of the way before an RCMP investigation leads to any formal charges in the $8.28-billion Greenbelt land swap.
But the opposition can't count on the police to do their political work for them. Corruption catches people's attention - until it doesn't.
The worst thing New Democrats and Liberals could do is base their campaigns on the premier's thinking and timing, rather than doing their own timely thinking. And yet, time after time, the opposition falls into Ford's traps.
When word first leaked out of an early election, Stiles boasted that New Democrats were ready - and goaded the Tories to "bring it on." Why play into Ford's playbook?
Crombie, by contrast, is calling Ford to account for jumping the gun with an early election costing $150 million, noting that governments are "typically punished" for such self-interest: "We know what the lesson has been in the past. It would be very disrespectful to the taxpayer."
Crombie's Liberals are convening a policy conference on the Sept. 21 weekend to help cobble together an election platform while distancing herself from her unpopular federal Liberal counterpart, Justin Trudeau. In an unmistakable signal, the Ontario Liberals have invited two outspoken ex-politicians who have publicly called for the prime minister to leave (former cabinet minister Jane Philpott and ex-B.C. premier Christie Clark).
The New Democrats are also holding a provincial council meeting that same weekend to help lay down the party line. But both parties remain prisoners of Ford's timelines, and captive of federal polling trends that bleed into provincial results.
Rather than focusing on events beyond their control - a police probe and federal politics - far better to dwell on voter preoccupations closer to home.
Unfortunately, the opposition parties have failed to lay claim to a governing mandate of their own. Neither has outlined their visions, delineated their policies, or distinguished themselves from one another.
As for the premier, he hasn't remotely made the case for a renewed mandate so far ahead of schedule. If Ford is running out of gas early, he should turn over the keys.
CAPTION: Premier Doug Ford speaks to reporters at the Council of the Federation meetings in Halifax on July 15, 2024.
