national briefs: Suspended cop facing charges

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/national-briefs/docview/3100457469/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=national+briefs%3A+Suspended+cop+facing+charges&amp;title=The+Spectator&amp;issn=11899417&amp;date=2024-09-04&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.5&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Spectator&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Ontario
Ford rules out election in 2024
Ontario Premier Doug Ford has ruled out calling an early election this year, but has left the door open to calling one in 2025. The province's next fixed election date isn't until June 2026, but Ford says he and his Progressive Conservative team must always be prepared. He has given his party's members of provincial parliament a December deadline to decide if they will run again. The premier previously left open the possibility of an early election when asked about one last spring.
Suspended cop facing charges
Police in Sarnia, Ont., say a suspended officer has been charged with multiple offences, including assault and criminal harassment. Police say they were called Friday to a dispute between two people who know each other, one of them a 47-year-old suspended constable. They say the officer was arrested Sunday. He's also charged with animal cruelty and mischief. Police say the constable has since been released on bail, with conditions. Sarnia's police chief says in a statement that the officer has been suspended from duty since late August 2023.
Alberta
Opt-in to sex ed
in schools
The Alberta government says it's moving ahead this fall with legislation that would require parents to proactively sign up to have their children opt-in, rather than the usual practice of opting out, for sex education. It's a move that has teachers wondering what problem the province is looking to solve. Education Minister Demetrios Nicolaides, in a statement, said school boards, teachers, superintendents and parents are being consulted on the changes.

Document 105 of 500

Canada's clean-tech funding created a global powerhouse. Now scandal threatens to tear it down

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/canadas-clean-tech-funding-created-global/docview/3100440350/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Canada%27s+clean-tech+funding+created+a+global+powerhouse.+Now+scandal+threatens+to+tear+it+down&amp;title=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-09-04&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: OTTAWA-It was once the driver of Canada's nascent clean-technology industry, supported by Liberal and Conservatives alike.
Publicly funded but arms-length from government bureaucracy since 2001, Sustainable Development Technology Canada (SDTC) was a lifeline to entrepreneurs desperate to find money for their clean-tech innovations.
That was until a damaging mismanagement scandal rocked the agency and the House of Commons last year, forcing the Trudeau government on its heels and prompting it to launch a drastic overhaul of the fund.
Now, in the backdrop of the crisis, industry insiders worry the growth of a sector critical to Canada's transition to a green economy is under threat.
"There are tons of jobs at stake, tons of fundamental business opportunities," said Peter McArthur, chair of the Ontario Clean Technology Industry Association.
"That brilliant idea that's coming out of a lab in Hamilton, in Edmonton, in Sherbrooke, is at risk of not seeing the light of day without this sort of funding. Maybe that solution is going to reduce the likelihood of those forest fires in Alberta and B.C., maybe it's going to ... help us go in a different direction regarding the flooding in Ontario and Quebec this year, or the heat that's been so punitive to so many people."
"This gives us a chance."
Here's the story of how a once lauded federal fund became a punching bag for Justin Trudeau's political rivals, and the uncertainty it faces going forward.
What is SDTC?
Even while acknowledging it's a cliché, industry experts who spoke to the Star say Canada has always punched above its weight when it comes to clean tech or products that help reduce the negative environmental impacts of using natural resources.
As of 2022, more than 2,000 companies contributed $37 billion to Canada's GDP, good enough for the country to claim 13 of the top 100 clean-tech companies in the world in the latest rankings from the Global Cleantech Innovation Index, only second behind the U.S.
SDTC was integral to that success, McArthur said. Launched in 2001 by the Jean Chrétien government, it was a public fund led by experts without political interference that identified the best early stage startups in an industry that required heavy capital investment.
It was "totally necessary, and it didn't exist anywhere else in the world," said Jane Kearns, the co-founder of the Canada Cleantech Alliance and a partner at Evok Innovations, a clean energy venture capital firm. "It set Canada apart."
By 2022, SDTC had provided more than $13 billion in funding to clean-tech projects, leading to billions in revenues and creating at least 25,000 Canadian jobs. Companies that received SDTC funding were seen as most credible by other organizations, said Lynn Côté, the former clean-tech lead at Export Development Canada.
Successive governments of different stripes saw its value and in 2021, the federal government gave it an injection of $1 billion to spend over five years.
How did scandal shut down SDTC's funding?
Behind the scenes, concerns about rampant financial and workplace mismanagement were brought to the government in early 2023 and quickly blew things up.
By October 2023, a third-party report spurred by whistleblower complaints raised enough red flags that Industry Minister François-Philippe Champagne froze funding and said SDTC's governance model would have to be reviewed. The embattled president and the chair of the board would both resign weeks later.
The consequences for business were devastating, said McArthur. In a survey of members by the Canada Cleantech Alliance, two-thirds of companies said they had their businesses interrupted and many more revealed they were unable to find alternative funding. To this day, McArthur told the Star, the money has yet to start flowing again.
"It has resulted in some layoffs and people having to sell off portions of their business, at the same time that our American cousins have turned up their focus on this sector dramatically through the Inflation Reduction Act," he said. "It's been a very tough situation, so companies are being lured to the United States."
An auditor general probe concluded in June would later find tens of millions in funding went to ineligible projects, conflict of interest rules were violated in funding decisions, and requirements in the law governing the foundation were not met. A long list of recommendations in the report were accepted, and misspent funds would be recouped.
Conservative MPs dubbed it the "green slush fund," arguing it was wasteful, corrupt, potentially criminal, and pursued parliamentary hearings on what went wrong. One employee at the company told the Star that's when things "went off the rails."
Champagne took a drastic measure, announcing SDTC would be shut down and transitioned to the National Research Council of Canada, which reports directly to the minister rather than operates at arms-length. Eventually, Champagne said, the fund would move to the Canada Innovation Corporation, a crown corporation first promised by the Trudeau government in 2021. Its establishment, however, is not expected until after the next election.
Even Canada's auditor general Karen Hogan told MPs it was the first time she was aware of a critical report leading to such measures.
Later, a Tory motion to compel the auditor general to cough up extensive records obtained during her investigation to the RCMP succeeded, despite protests from Liberal MPs and Hogan, who argued it would have "unintended consequences" on her access to public servants and agencies.
Over the summer, the Mounties also quietly raised concerns about the motion, with commissioner Mike Duheme declaring in a letter to the House law clerk obtained by the Star that it was "highly unlikely" information received due to the motion could be used in an RCMP investigation. The RCMP had not yet found any criminal wrongdoing in the available reports but would continue its review, Duheme added.
Can clean tech industries survive?
Now, clean-tech industry experts worry a history of consistent funding is at risk.
Champagne's response addressed the governance problems head on, but it's left too much uncertainty and its ramifications on the industry that relied on SDTC could be severe, said several sources who spoke to the Star. The fund's success was attributed to fast, bold decision-making typical only of the private sector. Now, unprecedented political spotlight could lead to slower, more risk-averse decisions that fall short of innovators' expectations, some fear.
"If the money is in a government department, I'd be worried about political interference," said Jonathan Rhone, a serial clean tech investor who has received SDTC funding in the past. "Honestly, I worry that the process of securing funding becomes overly onerous and costly, and takes too long and (is) bureaucratic."
That's why moving the fund under direct government oversight was a "real overreach" and a "political overreaction," said Stewart Elgie, an environmental law and policy professor at the University of Ottawa. In a statement, a spokesperson for SDTC said funding should resume in the coming weeks and a "seamless transition that meets everyone's needs" was in the works.
"Yes, it needs to be publicly accountable, it needs to show that it's delivering results ... it needed to have its governance tightened up," Elgie said. "But you didn't need to strangle SDTC to solve the governance problem."
"If you get a speeding ticket, well, then you're a little bit cautious," Côté said. "But money hates uncertainty."
Those fears are compounded by questions about how a potential Conservative government whose leader has claimed his climate plan would centre on "technology, not taxes" would approach funding.
"When Pierre Poilievre was a cabinet minister in (the Stephen Harper) government, they poured hundreds of millions of dollars into what he's now calling a green slush fund," Elgie said. "And now, to call that a failure that should be killed is really, shallow opportunism of something that's been a massive Canadian economic success story."
Conservative ethics critic Michael Barrett said in a statement the fund is "nothing but a fraud" that has been "used solely to benefit Trudeau's friends and Liberal insiders."
Spokespeople for Innovation, Science and Economic Development Canada and the NRC said the federal government remains committed to homegrown clean-tech funding. The NRC, they said, has a history of supporting clean-tech innovation with a dedicated team that has doled out more than $400 million in clean-tech funding since 2020, and the incoming changes will "enhance accountability, transparency, and public confidence."
To Rhone, whether Ottawa bureaucrats find a way to continue funding clean-tech startup success is a choice in the type of country we want to be.
"It's important for us to realize that Canada has a choice to make about whether we're going to be a supplier of clean-tech solutions into this, you know, trillion dollar global clean-tech market, or we're going to be a buyer," he said. "How do we create the conditions for success?"
CAPTION: A helicopter buckets water onto smouldering fires outside of Jasper, Alta., on July 26, 2024. Peter McArthur, chair of the OntarioClean Technology Industry Association, says clean tech could one day provide the solutions that help reduce the likelihood of forest fires, flooding or heat waves.

Subject: Innovations; Economic development; Technological change; Success; Forest & brush fires; Scandals; Clean technology; Startups; Politics; Funding; Venture capital

Business indexing term: Subject: Economic development Startups Venture capital

Location: United States--US; Canada

Title: Canada's clean-tech funding created a global powerhouse. Now scandal threatens to tear it down

Document 106 of 500

Poilievre's attack on me a symptom of larger problem

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/poilievres-attack-on-me-symptom-larger-problem/docview/3100438714/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=unknown&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Poilievre%27s+attack+on+me+a+symptom+of+larger+problem&amp;title=The+Spectator&amp;issn=11899417&amp;date=2024-09-04&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.8&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Spectator&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: When nurses speak out, we do so based on evidence and on health and clinical knowledge. Our advocacy for healthy public policy is grounded on the lived experiences of those we care for and by our expertise on social determinants of health.
This is one element that makes nursing the most trusted profession, except apparently for Conservative Leader Pierre Poilievre. His office recently mocked the expertise of myself and others with "too many letters after their names." This strange behaviour reveals the divisive and belittling leadership we can expect from Poilievre and his team if given the opportunity.
There is a history to his lack of empathy and consistent undermining of scientific evidence and expertise. We are still reeling from extremist politicians who openly sabotaged vaccination efforts during and after the COVID-19 pandemic. As recently as October 2023, Poilievre and at least one of his colleagues pushed a private member's anti-vaccine mandate bill in Parliament to tie the hands of public health officers - a bill the majority of MPs wisely agreed to drop.
From a healthy public policy perspective, the callous message coming from Poilievre's office after I voiced support for supervised consumption site (SCS) is dangerous on many fronts. He is shutting down dialogue on an escalating and preventable human tragedy that needs immediate attention.
And unfortunately, Poilievre and team are not alone in this rhetoric. For example, the Ford government announced on Aug. 20 a misguided decision to gut consumption site services in the province. The next day, Premier Doug Ford slammed the federal government's prior approval of 16 safer supply projects in Ontario, stating, "As far as I'm concerned the federal government is the biggest drug dealer in the entire country."
Ford's overall message is deceptive. His claim that we need to get rid of safe supply begs a serious question: what will happen when there are no guardrails against unsafe supply? The answer? Ontario's communities will look like some of the big cities in the U.S., with people convulsing and dying in the streets.
In a reckless move that amounts to a death sentence for even more vulnerable people, the Ford government has given notice that it is withdrawing life-saving health-care services offered at supervised consumption sites under the pretence of safety and security. Yet the facts show that crime has decreased in communities that have consumption sites.
Poilievre's referring to these sites as "drug dens" is callous and deceptive. These sites offer a lifeline to those struggling with substance use. The very same harm reduction programs that some leaders are targeting don't only save lives; they also open the door to treatment and recovery.
For many struggling with substance use, these sites are their only hope when they are ready for treatment and recovery. Shutting sites down will lead to more preventable deaths, increased HIV and hepatitis C infections and cost taxpayers more.
Your readers likely know people who struggle with substance use. They are sons, daughters, mothers, fathers, friends and neighbours. They need our compassion and support, not stigma, sarcasm and withdrawal of life-saving solutions that can put them onto healthier paths.
For these reasons, Ford must reverse the decision to gut supervised consumption site services in Ontario.
As for Poilievre's office's recent mocking of actual expertise, yes, we nurses - including me - have many letters after our names. We are proud of our education. It affords us the in-depth knowledge to care for people in health and illness.
Instead of mocking, Poilievre and his team could benefit from experts to understand the evidence on the importance of supervised consumption sites as an essential pillar of a multi-pronged approach to address this health crisis and to make our communities safer for all.
Doris Grinspun is chief
executive officer of
Registered Nurses'
Association of Ontario.
CAPTION:

CREDIT: Doris Grinspun Doris Grinspun is chief executive officer of Registered Nurses' Association of Ontario.;

Document 107 of 500

Doug Ford rejects comparison of booze sales in corner stores to safe-injection sites

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/doug-ford-rejects-comparison-booze-sales-corner/docview/3100256223/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Doug+Ford+rejects+comparison+of+booze+sales+in+corner+stores+to+safe-injection+sites&amp;title=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-09-03&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Premier Doug Ford insists there's "absolutely no comparison" between convenience stores selling booze near schools and the supervised drug consumption sites he is restricting.
With thousands of corner stores licensed to sell beer, wine and premixed cocktails as of Thursday, Ford scoffed at suggestions the retailers should face limits similar to those imposed on injection facilities or cannabis shops.
"Let's be fair, you're comparing a convenience store to a safe consumption site, injection sites," the premier told reporters Tuesday in Brampton.
"Last time I checked, the convenience stores don't have needles lying around the front of their stores," he said, noting variety stores are "well equipped" for dealing with restricted products, having sold tobacco and lottery tickets for decades.
"They're going to be very responsible. There's absolutely no comparison."
Two weeks ago, the premier decreed 10 of Ontario's 17 consumption and treatment service (CTS) sites in Toronto, Hamilton, Guelph, Kitchener, Ottawa and Thunder Bay will close by March 31 because such facilities must be 200 metres away from schools and daycares.
At the same time, he earmarked $378 million for 19 new "Homelessness and Addiction Recovery Treatment" (HART) hubs that will boast 375 supportive housing units and addiction recovery and treatment beds.
The HART facilities are designed to help thousands of people transition to long-term housing annually, but they will not offer supervised drug consumption or safe supply of narcotics or needle exchange programs.
Some proponents have argued the sites are essential to harm-reduction measures for those addicted to drugs.
Under Ontario law, cannabis shops must be at least 150 metres from schools, but there are no restrictions on where LCBO outlets, Beer Stores or licensed restaurants and supermarkets can be located.
Ford, who shares his cellphone number with the public, said he has been inundated with messages about the CTS initiative amid concerns about crime near supervised injection sites.
Among the five closing in Toronto is one in Leslieville's South Riverdale Community Health Centre, near where mother-of-two Karolina Huebner-Makurat was killed by stray bullet last summer.
That brazen daylight crime against an innocent bystander shocked the city.
"I've never had more phone calls in support about closing down the safe consumption sites - I call them unsafe consumption sites - and ... making sure that we support these people," said Ford, touting the new funding to help those with addiction issues.
The premier stressed the additional dollars will "get them social assistance, to make sure that they have housing, make sure that they have addiction treatment beds and detox beds."
With an eye toward an election next spring, one year ahead of the scheduled June 2026 vote, Ford is also spending hundreds of millions of dollars to expedite the liberalization of booze sales.
While the government maintains it will cost taxpayers $225 million to hasten an end to the 10-year contract with the privately owned Beer Store that was set to expire on Dec. 31, 2025, Liberal Leader Bonnie Crombie has warned the price tag could balloon to $1 billion.
As first disclosed by the Star in May, Ford is spending that money to give himself the flexibility to call an election one year earlier because he will have delivered a key campaign pledge dating back to 2018.
On Tuesday, the premier would only confirm that "we aren't have any election this year."
But he was coy about whether he would trigger a $150-million vote next spring - despite enjoying a huge Progressive Conservative majority in the legislature, where the opposition parties cannot slow his agenda.
Pressed on why MPPs and cabinet ministers are being told to decide by Christmas if they will seek re-election, Ford said, "You always have to be prepared."
"That's what it comes down to - no matter which way you look at it, we aren't too far from any election, no matter if it's next year (or) the following year."
The premier was in Brampton, where the Tories hold all five seats, for a campaign-style announcement promising up to $29.7 million to boost flood-protection and water systems.
Brampton Mayor Patrick Brown said the money would enable the construction of 12,900 homes in the city's downtown, which has been prone to flooding due to aging infrastructure.
"Our downtown - like many old communities and old downtowns - has challenges that come with it," said Brown.
"Since the 1820s, we've had some significant challenges with flooding in our downtown because of the impact of the Etobicoke Creek," he said, noting that upgrading 600 metres of the creek as part of the city's Riverwalk project will improve storm water runoff.
Ford has been touring Ontario municipalities all summer, doling out money from the $970-million "housing-enabling water systems fund" in a bid to tackle the province's demand for additional homes.
CAPTION: Premier Doug Ford attends the U.S.-Canada Summit in Toronto on June 11, 2024.

Document 108 of 500

Booze is coming to corner stores this week. Who's in charge of policing the new rules?

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/booze-is-coming-corner-stores-this-week-whos/docview/3099790561/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Booze+is+coming+to+corner+stores+this+week.+Who%27s+in+charge+of+policing+the+new+rules%3F&amp;title=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-09-02&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: The Alcohol and Gaming Commission of Ontario has staffed up in advance of booze coming to corner store shelves next week and says it plans to carry out "unannounced inspections" to make sure the new rules are being abided by.
One thing it won't be looking for, however, is whether alcohol carrying stores are near schools. Unlike with cannabis stores or supervised drug consumption sites, the province is not creating bubble zones around students to protect them from alcohol.
The AGCO said that with increased provincial funding it now has more than 100 inspectors to monitor the 4,146 convenience stores approved so far to sell beer, wine and canned cocktails starting Thursday. The commission is the sole enforcer when it comes to ensuring only eligible stores get to sell eligible beverages.
These inspectors are supported by "back-office analysts and advanced, data-driven monitoring tools," said AGCO spokesperson Raymond Kahnert.
"Proactive inspections allow us to help educate licensees and address potential issues before they escalate, while responsive inspections follow up on any public complaints or issues flagged by local authorities," he added.
The city of Toronto said it had no influence on the rollout and will not be watching over it.
"There are no plans for the city of Toronto to track retail stores that sell alcohol, as the AGCO regulates the sale, service and delivery of liquor in Ontario, including licences and permits," said spokesperson Elise von Scheel in an email to the Star.
Toronto police also defer to the AGCO, but are able to conduct inspections, issue tickets and lay charges should the situation demand.
"Depending on the circumstances, police should be called in emergencies or if there is an immediate safety concern and the AGCO should be called with concerns about liquor licences or sales," said Toronto police spokesperson Shannon Eames.
Even before the new rules have taken effect, the AGCO has had to punish an offending retailer. An Etobicoke shop this week jumped the gun and sold alcohol before the official start date. It also sold spirits, which is still forbidden under the new regulations, and didn't buy the liquor through the LCBO.
The penalty was a three-week suspension of its licence to sell liquor and lottery tickets.
Depending on how severe it considers a particular violation, the AGCO has the ability to fine retailers up to $100,000. The most costly offence for a store owner is knowingly selling liquor to a minor.
While Ontario's Cannabis Licence Act prohibits cannabis stores from being located less than 150 meters from a school, its Liquor Licence and Control Act puts no geographic restrictions on alcohol outlets.
In contrast, it was announced in August that 10 of the 17 supervised consumption sites in Ontario will be closed by springtime because of forthcoming legislation from the province mandating they stay at least 200 meters away from schools and daycares.
Both decisions could be harmful to Ontarians' health, according to experts. In May, the Canadian Public Health Association wrote that allowing alcohol in corner stores is a public health risk.
"Expanding the sale of alcohol to more retail locations will increase its accessibility and consumption. Research shows that greater availability of alcohol leads to higher rates of consumption and, consequently, more alcohol-related harms."
Meanwhile, supervised consumption has been shown to save lives. A study this year in The Lancet found overdose deaths were reduced by 67 per cent in neighbourhoods within 500 meters of supervised consumption sites.
Newly shuttered drug consumption clinics will not have the chance to relocate. The province is banning the creation of new sites.
When asked why the province wasn't also imposing restrictions on alcohol sales near schools, Premier Doug Ford's office forwarded the Star's inquiry to the Ministry of the Attorney General.
A spokesperson for the ministry said it wouldn't be fair to force store owners to relocate away from schools to sell booze.
"As grocery and convenience stores are existing businesses in the marketplace, it would be inappropriate for these businesses to relocate should proximity requirements prevent their businesses from selling alcohol," said Jack Fazzari.
Fazzari dismissed the comparison with drug consumption sites.
"The legal and regulated sale of alcohol in our province has been occurring in communities for nearly 100 years," he said. "Places where alcohol is sold, and the locations of drug consumption sites are not comparable."
As for cannabis, Ontario had to take "careful steps" to regulate it, including keeping sales away from schools, as it was a "brand-new market" when it was legalized federally.
Fazzari said the province's tools for mitigating the harms alcohol causes are still in use. This includes only allowing sale from 7 a.m. to 11 p.m., enforcing the legal drinking age of 19, making sure alcohol doesn't get any cheaper than the legal minimum retail price of beer at $2.81 per litre, putting up signs warning of the dangers of drinking while pregnant and mandatory staff training for all new retailers.
"Our government will continue upholding some of the highest existing standards of social responsibility through safe and responsible implementation of our plan to bring Ontario in-line with the rest of Canada," he said.
CAPTION: Beer, wine and coolers will be for sale in corner stores across the province starting Sept. 5.

Subject: Public health; Retail stores; Liquor laws & regulations; Alcohol; Sales; Beer; Convenience stores; Schools; Marijuana

Document 109 of 500

Doug Ford's 'HART hubs' move is heartless

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/doug-fords-hart-hubs-move-is-heartless/docview/3099130111/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Doug+Ford%27s+%27HART+hubs%27+move+is+heartless&amp;title=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-08-31&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: The Ford government has a history of ignoring expert recommendations in favour of options that serve its political interests, and Ford's latest plan could be fatal.
The province has announced that it will shut down 10 supervised drug consumption and treatment services (CTS) sites, as well as ban future ones, despite two external reviews calling for an increase in locations across the province.
The announcement, which comes amid overlapping housing, cost of living and opioid addiction crises and the most toxic drug supply ever seen, shows a cruel and continued lack of compassion and commitment to solutions rooted in best practice.
The decision to close CTS sites within 200 metres of a school or child-care centres, or transform them into "Homelessness and Addiction Recovery Treatment (HART) hubs" is a cruel switch when CTS operators have been calling for more addiction treatment options and highly supportive transitional housing beds as part of a holistic service since long before this government took office.
This announcement seems to be an attempt to conceal the Ford government's lack of investment in our communities overall. To give an idea of just how deficient this announcement is, consider this: supportive housing providers in Hamilton are ready to build more than 400 units in Hamilton. The new 375 supportive housing beds referenced in the announcement would not even meet current demand in our city, let alone across the province.
The move to close these sites also pits vulnerable communities against one another - framed as a need to protect young people, especially children in Ontario. But it is children who are most at risk when drug users have no choice but to use in public spaces - often parks, and even playgrounds. Providing an indoor clinical area for use is the only proven defence against drug use and abandoned paraphernalia where it can seriously harm a child, a parent or waste worker.
Tragically absent from the conversation is the fact that these sites are health-care facilities. They offer connection to primary care providers, wound care, STI testing and contraception and clean needles (in a time when we are seeing more blood-borne illness, including syphilis, HIV and hepatitis). These sites and their courageous staff offer a lifeline and dignified care that most people who use drugs will not find elsewhere - hopefully long enough to build the rapport, strength and mental health necessary to seek detox services.
Workers in harm reduction are absolutely bereft in reaction to this news, fearing a return to dark times before this life-saving service with overwhelmed emergency departments and full morgues.
In Toronto, no deaths have occurred from overdose within 500 metres of a supervised injection site. CTS sites in Hamilton have seen over 90 per cent reduction in ambulance calls, taking massive pressure off the health-care system and reducing the cost of first responders for the city of Hamilton.
These sites create safety for everyone in the community and must be part of a network of services for people experiencing street level homelessness, addiction and other challenges.
If Doug Ford's government truly wants to support those who struggle with addiction to find a path out of it, and for them to stay alive in the meantime, we need proven detox models inclusive of consumption sites, clean needle exchange and a better plan for supportive housing investment.
Whatever your perspective on this issue, I hope we agree that $225 million to cancel the Beer Store contract early would be much better spent solving this crisis.
We cannot encourage people to consider treatment if they are dead. How many more people will be on the street or in the morgue before this government invests in proven solutions instead of playing politics with people's lives?
CAPTION: If Doug Ford's government truly wants to support those struggling with addiction to find a path out of it, and for them to stay alive in the meantime, we need proven detox models inclusive of consumption sites, clean needle exchange and a better plan for supportive housing investment, MPP Sarah Jama writes.The province has announced it will shut down 10 supervised drug consumption and treatment services sites despite external reviews calling for an increase in locations across the province, MPP Sarah Jama writes.
CREDIT: Sarah Jama

Subject: Addictions

Title: Doug Ford's 'HART hubs' move is heartless

Document 110 of 500

Ontario liquor sales set for expansion: More than 4,000 corner stores approved to sell alcohol next week as new rules take effect Sept. 5

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/ontario-liquor-sales-set-expansion/docview/3099054590/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Ontario+liquor+sales+set+for+expansion%3A+More+than+4%2C000+corner+stores+approved+to+sell+alcohol+next+week+as+new+rules+take+effect+Sept.+5&amp;title=The+Spectator&amp;issn=11899417&amp;date=2024-08-31&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.14&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Spectator&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: More than 4,000 convenience stores across Ontario have been licensed to sell alcohol as new liberalized rules are set to take effect next week.
The Alcohol and Gaming Commission of Ontario said Friday that it has approved 4,146 such licences ahead of convenience stores being allowed to sell beer, wine and ready-to-drink cocktails as of next Thursday.
The expansion of liquor sales fulfils a pledge Premier Doug Ford made in the 2018 election.
Last December, Ford announced that all convenience and grocery stores could sell beer, wine, cider and ready-to-drink cocktails by 2026. In May, he announced plans to speed that up, moving up the timeline for corner stores to Sept. 5.
The government has said its alcohol expansion plans will give Ontarians more choice and convenience.
The AGCO said it was committed to helping licensees meet their obligations for the safe and responsible sale of alcohol.
One store in Toronto's west end had allegedly been selling liquor even before the new rules kick in, the AGCO said. The commission said it served a notice of suspension to the store after a surprise visit from inspectors two weeks ago.
"As the next phase in Ontario's expansion of the liquor retail market approaches, the AGCO is focused on ensuring licensees understand and comply with their obligations for the responsible sale of alcohol," Karin Schnarr, CEO of the commission, wrote in a statement.
"The AGCO has a wide-ranging compliance monitoring process and will take strong measures to enforce social responsibility and ensure a smooth transition to the new, expanded marketplace."
The government allowed grocery stores licensed to sell beer and wine to start selling pre-mixed cocktails and large packs of beer last month. Newly licensed grocery stores can start selling beer, wine and ready-to-drink cocktails on Oct. 31.
The province's alcohol sale expansion saw Ford strike a deal with The Beer Store for $225 million as the government broke a 10-year agreement with the company. Part of the new agreement with The Beer Store includes keeping at least 386 stores open until July 2025 and at least 300 until Dec. 31, 2025.
The decision to allow the sale of ready-to-drink cocktails outside the province's main liquor retailer became a sticking point in talks over the summer between the Liquor Control Board of Ontario and its unionized workers. About 10,000 workers went on strike in July. The strike, which shut 700 stores, lasted more than two weeks before both sides struck a deal.
The Canadian Press
CAPTION:

CREDIT: Liam Casey The Canadian Press

Document 111 of 500

Ford's 'HART hubs' move is heartless

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/fords-hart-hubs-move-is-heartless/docview/3099047989/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=unknown&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Ford%27s+%27HART+hubs%27+move+is+heartless&amp;title=The+Spectator&amp;issn=11899417&amp;date=2024-08-31&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.18&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Spectator&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Tragically absent from the conversation is the fact that these sites are health-care facilities. They offer connection to primary care providers, wound care, STI testing and contraception and clean needles (in a time when we are seeing more blood-borne illness, including syphilis, HIV and hepatitis). These sites and their courageous staff offer a lifeline and dignified care that most people who use drugs will not find elsewhere - hopefully long enough to build the rapport, strength and mental health necessary to seek detox services.
Workers in harm reduction are absolutely bereft in reaction to this news, fearing a return to dark times before this life-saving service with overwhelmed emergency departments and full morgues.
In Toronto, no deaths have occurred from overdose within 500 metres of a supervised injection site. CTS sites in Hamilton have seen over 90 per cent reduction in ambulance calls, taking massive pressure off the health-care system.
These sites create safety for everyone in the community and must be part of a network of services for people experiencing street level homelessness, addiction and other challenges.
If Doug Ford's government truly wants to support those who struggle with addiction to find a path out of it, and for them to stay alive in the meantime, we need proven detox models inclusive of consumption sites, needle exchange and a better plan for supportive housing investment.
Whatever your perspective on this issue, I hope we agree that $225 million to cancel the Beer Store contract early would be much better spent solving this crisis.
We cannot encourage people to consider treatment if they are dead. How many more people will be on the street or in the morgue before this government invests in proven solutions instead of playing politics with people's lives?
Sarah Jama is the MPP
for Hamilton Centre.
CAPTION: The province has announced it will shut down 10 supervised drug consumption and treatment services sites despite external reviews calling for an increase in locations, MPP Sarah Jama writes.Cathie Coward The Hamilton Spectator file photo

CREDIT: Sarah Jama;

Document 112 of 500

Is Pierre Poilievre's election push driven by fear of Doug Ford?

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/is-pierre-poilievres-election-push-driven-fear/docview/3099171735/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Is+Pierre+Poilievre%27s+election+push+driven+by+fear+of+Doug+Ford%3F&amp;title=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-08-30&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Pierre Poilievre and Doug Ford don't have a lot in common, except maybe their desire to hold elections early.
Yes, they are conservatives and chances are that the many Ontarians who have voted for Ford are likely leaning toward voting for the federal Conservatives when the next election comes.
Both leaders in their own ways would call themselves populists, too.
But that's where Ford and Poilievre have very different approaches. Ford is what many would call a happy populist - a guy who just wants everyone to vote for him, whether that requires giving out his phone number or shovelling someone's vehicle out of a snowbank.
Poilievre, on the other hand, seems happiest when shaking his fist at his enemies, rattling off all the ways in which the country has been broken by government. His press conference this week was a perfect example.
"Happy end of summer," the Conservative leader greeted reporters gathered outside Parliament's West Block. "But really, what's there to be happy about after Justin Trudeau's recent cabinet retreat?"
The stated reason for the press conference was to call for an election. Poilievre wrote a letter to NDP Leader Jagmeet Singh, imploring him to end his working deal with the Trudeau government, so Canada can have an "axe the tax election" as soon as possible.
In the letter, Poilievre tells Singh to "pull out of the costly coalition and vote non-confidence in the government this September to trigger a carbon tax election in October of this year. Or you will forever be known as 'Sell-Out Singh.'"
Well, since he is asking so nicely, how could the NDP refuse?
It is, of course, extremely unlikely Singh will go along with Poilievre's request. Just this week at the retreat in Halifax, Trudeau and his House Leader Karina Gould said they expected the supply and confidence arrangement (not a coalition) to keep sparing Canadians an election until the fall of 2025.
But as my colleagues at Queen's Park have been reporting for months now, there's a very good chance Ontarians will be voting in a provincial election before then.
"Sources say Ford is worried that if, as polls suggest, Pierre Poilievre wins an election expected in October 2025, there would be reduced transfer payments to the provinces, a scrapping of Prime Minister Justin Trudeau's electric-vehicle strategy that is a cornerstone of Ontario economic policy and other slashed spending that would hurt the Progressive Conservatives," Queen's Park Bureau Chief Robert Benzie wrote in May.
Poilievre hasn't said publicly how he feels about an Ontario vote possibly upstaging the Conservatives' much-anticipated romp to victory. The fact that Ford sees a Poilievre victory as not great for Ontario, however, is fascinating and further underlines that there are serious tensions between the Ford and Poilievre brands of conservatism.
What remains to be seen is whether an early Ontario election could harm Poilievre's chances. For instance, what if Ontarians use a provincial election as their chance to vent at Trudeau and get some of the anti-Liberal sentiment we keep hearing about? What if Ontarians decide that as long as Ford remains premier, they might as well have a prime minister with whom he has a good working relationship?
Maybe that's one of the reasons Poilievre was out there this week, agitating for Canada to go to the polls this fall, not next fall.
Now, anyone who is looking at a 20-point lead in the polls, as Poilievre is, would naturally be eager to get that election show on the road. Being prime minister is definitely more fun than being leader of the opposition.
But if this summer in the U.S. has taught us anything, it's that a lot can change in a matter of weeks in politics these days. One wonders if Conservatives remain worried they're peaking too soon and the next year is going to be a slog of more intense scrutiny, more attention to mistakes and a struggle to maintain discipline over complacency.
Again, in the United States, there's a sense that politics may be turning against all the negativism of Donald Trump and his angry brand of populism, and tuning in more to a positive, progressive message coming from Kamala Harris and Tim Walz.
Poilievre doesn't like being compared to Trump, but the nickname tossing in Singh's direction this week sure seemed like an echo of the former president's schoolyard style.
Right now, as for most of the past year, Poilievre is having a moment. His sudden keenness for an October election may be rooted in concern that two other elections - one in Ontario, one in the U.S. - may make his moment fleeting.
CAPTION: Conservative Party Leader Pierre Poilievre speaks at a press conference on Parliament Hill in Ottawa on Aug. 29, 2024.

Subject: Press conferences; Provincial elections; Politics; Prime ministers; Tax elections

Business indexing term: Subject: Tax elections

Location: United States--US; Canada

People: Poilievre, Pierre; Trudeau, Justin

Document 113 of 500

School bus operators warn of delays and cancellations saying Ford government's new funding falls short

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/school-bus-operators-warn-delays-cancellations/docview/3099047981/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=School+bus+operators+warn+of+delays+and+cancellations+saying+Ford+government%27s+new+funding+falls+short&amp;title=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-08-30&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: School bus operators warn of a bumpy road ahead after a change in provincial funding they say will lead to delays and cancellations.
The Ontario government, while increasing transportation funding overall, still does not cover actual costs and is not providing enough to cover "adequate driver compensation, driver retention bonuses and new buses," said Nancy Daigneault, executive director of School Bus Ontario.
"To make matters worse, the Ministry of Education imposed a devastating reduction of between 56 to 58 cents per litre of fuel, which has left operators in certain regions worried about filling their tanks to cover the millions of kilometres a day travelled," and some operators are still owed money from the previous school year, added Daigneault.
Daigneault called the problem "unintended consequences" of the new funding model, which was announced last year and since then "we've been working to try to rectify some issues" with it.
"We also need to warn people that over the next couple of weeks and months, things could get worse before they get better. This could result in slowdowns ... there are some operators that are just going on a wing and a prayer right now, and relying on local banks to finance them."
The province provides the funding for transportation, but there is a patchwork of contracts between the operators and individual school boards.
Education Minister Jill Dunlop, who has been on the job two weeks, said in a statement that "providing stability for students, parents and our schools is critical, that why our government has increased funding to school boards, enhanced wages for bus drivers and invested in strengthening safety of school buses to ensure children can reliably get to and from school."
The province is spending $80 million more in student transportation, for a total of $1.3 billion, Dunlop added, and "ensured every board received an increase of at least three per cent this school year, with many receiving double digit increases" as well as monies to pay a driver retention bonus of up to $2,000 a year.
"That includes a focus on recruiting and retaining drivers with our driver retention program that provides each eligible driver with bonus payments of up to $2,000 per year," she said.
But Michael Bellmore, president of the Ontario Catholic School Trustees Association, said transportation has been a concern and "truth be told is that funding just doesn't meet funding needs."
The reality is "that wages have gone up ... (as has) the cost of fuel," and the association has told the ministry about its concerns, he added.
"I'm sure we're going to hear a lot next week, once the school year starts officially, of the issues and pitfalls that they've experienced," he said. "I suspect strongly that transportation is going to be one of the top items on that list that we hear feedback on" from boards.
Kathleen Woodcock, president of the Ontario Public School Boards' Association, said "while the most recent round (of education funding) included a welcome increase in transportation funding and addressed some key concerns identified by boards - such as the need for funding for special purpose vehicles - there are still challenges that remain."
She said rural, remote and northern boards in particular face busing challenges.
NDP Education critic Chandra Pasma, who represents Ottawa West-Nepean, said boards are struggling "and it's going to be more and more school boards every year, unless the government is actually willing to admit that they made a mistake and fix the funding formula for student transportation."
She said the amounts allocated are based on pre-pandemic pricing "and costs have massively escalated," and when it comes to wages, "the benchmark doesn't cover all the time that a bus driver is actually driving the bus," including on the way to pick up students.
"That's why you saw in Ottawa last year, an operator walked away from the contract, which is what contributed to huge chaos," she added.
CAPTION: The Ontario government provides the funding for school transportation, but there is a patchwork of contracts between the operators and individual school boards.

Subject: School buses; Funding; Education; Student transportation; Wages & salaries

Document 114 of 500

Aug. 30: Gage Park tents must go, memorials should have time limits and other letters to the editor

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/aug-30-gage-park-tents-must-go-memorials-should/docview/3098789149/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Aug.+30%3A+Gage+Park+tents+must+go%2C+memorials+should+have+time+limits+and+other+letters+to+the+editor&amp;title=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-08-30&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Gage Park tents must go
Tuesday morning there was a fire which necessitated the closure of Main Street and a response from emergency services. My frustration with the Gage Park situation and the lack of an effective response from the city is immeasurable.
I am not the only taxpayer who is frustrated and angry by what the city is allowing to carry on in one of the most beautiful parks in Hamilton: homeowners and residents alike on either side of the park have had enough.
In addition to the drugs and needles, the garbage and the items being amassed in the park, we are dealing with increased break-ins and fear for our safety, not just in the park but also in our neighbourhoods.
Please honour the rights of all citizens, not just "the unhoused," and remove the tents ASAP. Sadly, the encampments seem to be attracting drug dealers, thieves and God knows what else. The longer you allow this to continue, the worse it will get and the more difficult it will be to undo. Please remove the tents from Gage Park.
Lidia Wesolow, Hamilton
Move services to former school
I work full-time on James North, near Barton. I have a lot of sympathy for unhoused people, and I work across the street from a major outreach program - the Cathedral Cafe at Christ's Church Cathedral (252 James St. N.).
The James North small business community is in a lot of trouble. Even long-established shops are closing up or being forced to consider it because of out-of-control drug use, harassment, defecation/lewd acts, etc. on the street. It has become a poisoned environment.
But unhoused people deserve food and shelter, beyond question. Let's move the program to the unused Sir John A. Macdonald school, which has sat empty since 2019. If Doug Ford won't foot the bill, it's time to stop asking and time to start telling.
Pat Antila, Hamilton
Memorials should have time limits
Re: Ghost bike honouring Burlington hit-and-run victim removed by city, Aug. 27
Condolences to all who have lost a person through no fault of their own. Setting up a ghost bike site for a loved one does need a time limit. It is very disturbing having people come all hours of the day and night when the spot is across from your home. My first thoughts were, am I being watched? How else does one move on? To have a reminder every time you pass that spot is a distraction for everyone. If one needs to have a reminder, then set up a special place in your home. Many cultures have a space designated for a loved one gone.
Cindy Addley, Hamilton
Turn frustration into action
Re: Empathy in short supply today, Aug. 29
Thank you Christy France for your reminder that what is really behind the use of tents by "them." It would be better if instead of complaining about tents, we would fight for affordable housing and better mental health care. Compassionate action would make everyone's life better.
A.J. Williams, Hamilton
Protest photo from India disturbing
Re: Indian police fire tear gas and water cannons at rally against rape and killing of trainee doctor, Aug. 27
The picture in the article says so much; the man in the centre doesn't look like he's doing much other than being verbal. The police officer appears to be approaching the man from behind, swinging his baton and looking like he's enjoying his baton-wielding far too much.
Robert Coxe, Hamilton
Recalling a memorable quip
The discussion around whether or not we should keep building basements due to climate change flooding takes me back to comments made by former prime minister Pierre Trudeau during a visit to Hamilton. Beach strip residents complained to the PM that their basements were flooding due to a lack of federal control over the level of Lake Ontario. Trudeau thought for a minute then responded, "If the water is still in your basement after two weeks, consider it yours."
Phil Beard, Dundas
CAPTION: A police officer hits a man during a protest against the rape and murder of a resident doctor at a government hospital earlier this month in Kolkata, India.Readers continue to write about their frustration with encampments following a recent tent fire at Gage Park.

Location: India

Document 116 of 500

Doug Ford has faced controversy after controversy - yet remains as popular as ever. Here's why Ontarians say they still like him

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/doug-ford-has-faced-controversy-after-yet-remains/docview/3098891733/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Doug+Ford+has+faced+controversy+after+controversy+-+yet+remains+as+popular+as+ever.+Here%27s+why+Ontarians+say+they+still+like+him&amp;title=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-08-29&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Premier Doug Ford appears to be the Teflon Tory.
In his lucky seventh summer as Ontario premier, Ford is emerging as a politician to whom little seems to stick.
At least for now.
In new polling for the Star, Abacus Data found voters are so far willing to forgive - if not quite forget - transgressions that would have derailed the electoral careers of others.
But significant concerns around Ford's honesty and his stewardship of taxpayers' money loom large because of the $8.28-billion Greenbelt land swap scandal now being probed by the RCMP.
"If something bad comes out of that investigation, it could be the thing that breaks through that Teflon," Abacus president David Coletto said this week, referring to the non-stick coating that seems to protect the premier.
Despite that risk, Progressive Conservative insiders confided to the Star they are ruminating about an early election next year, well before the scheduled June 2026 vote.
That's because the Tories are confident about the two-term premier's skills on the stump and his ability to connect with Ontarians in their everyday lives.
"It's an enigma - he's withstanding the same pressures that a lot of other political leaders haven't been able to and so the question is why? He's polarizing in the sense that if you don't like him, you don't like him. And there's a lot of Ontarians who don't like Doug Ford," said Coletto.
"But he has enough who do and they think he's just a friendly, nice guy who isn't perfect, but admits mistakes when he makes them and tries to fix them," the pollster said.
"He's forgiven because he asks for forgiveness."
Revealingly, when asked how to describe Ford, 44 per cent of respondents felt he was "friendly," while 20 per cent said he was "mean."
Similarly, 39 per cent said he "gets things done" while 38 per cent insisted he "fails to deliver"; 38 per cent said he is "normal" while 28 per cent said he's "weird"; and 37 per cent said he "admits mistakes and corrects them" while 38 per cent said he "refuses to admit mistakes."
"That's a winner - during the last campaign that became his new brand: the guy who gets things done," said Coletto, hearkening to the Tories' successful 2022 re-election slogan, Get It Done.
However, there are potentially ominous findings for the premier in the poll that may concern the governing party looking ahead to a spring campaign.
Fully 42 per cent felt he was "dishonest," while only 25 per cent said he was "honest"; 42 per cent said he was "reckless with taxpayers' money" compared to 25 per cent who said he was "careful" with it; 41 per cent said he's "corrupt" with 23 per cent saying he's "ethical"; and 39 per cent said he's "out of touch," while 35 per cent said he's "down to earth."
"The Greenbelt scandal did signal, perhaps, a recklessness," said Coletto, expressing surprise that so many respondents believed Ford wasted money given his frequent boasts of fiscal probity.
"That's why that scandal was so damaging. He is running large deficits. He's not raising people's taxes, but he's still spending a lot of money," he said, adding the $225 million to an estimated $1 billion Ford has earmarked to expedite liberalization of alcohol sales in the coming days may also be hurting the premier.
Abacus surveyed 1,028 Ontarians Aug. 14-17 using online panels based on the Lucid exchange platform. While opt-in polls cannot be assigned a margin of error, for comparison purposes, a random sample of this size would have one of plus or minus 3.057 percentage points, 19 times out of 20.
Overall, Abacus found Ford's Tories at 42 per cent, well ahead of Bonnie Crombie's Liberals at 26 per cent, Marit Stiles' New Democrats at 21 per cent and Mike Schreiner's Greens at eight per cent.
That healthy PC lead is despite the fact there's strong awareness of the government's problems.
Fully 50 per cent of respondents were "familiar" with the Greenbelt debacle, in which Tory-connected developers were "favoured" in the unexpected November 2022 rezoning of 7,400 acres of environmentally sensitive protected land to build housing.
Another 32 per cent were "aware of it, but not familiar" with the land swap an embattled Ford cancelled last Sept. 21, and 18 per cent didn't know.
Of the 82 per cent aware of the Greenbelt saga, 58 per cent were "less likely to support" him because of it, 12 per cent were "more likely to support" him while 28 per cent said it would have no impact.
About one-third - 35 per cent - were familiar with Ford's invoking of the Constitution's "notwithstanding" clause in 2022 to try to impose a contract settlement on 55,000 education workers. Under pressure from labour unions and the opposition, he backed off.
Forty per cent said they were aware of the constitutional controversy, but not familiar with it and 25 per cent didn't know anything about it.
Of the 75 per cent aware of the imbroglio, 43 per cent were less likely to support Ford over it, 26 per cent were more likely to back him while 28 per cent said it would have no impact.
Almost one-third - 31 per cent - were familiar with Ford's unilateral move to give "strong mayor" powers to the heads of city councils, while 31 per cent had heard about it but weren't familiar with the issue and 37 per cent didn't know about it. That enabled mayors to override council majorities on issues deemed "provincial priorities," such as fast-tracking construction of new housing.
Of the nearly two-thirds aware of the mayoral maelstrom, 43 per cent were less likely to back the premier over it, 23 per cent were more likely to support him while 32 per cent said it would have no impact.
Finally, Abacus asked respondents about last summer's contentious King's Counsel patronage award to 91 lawyers, many of whom had strong connections to the Tories, including MPPs, cabinet ministers and donors.
Just 16 per cent were familiar with the botched revival of an honorific former Liberal premier David Peterson had scrapped in 1985 because it had been "corrupted" by previous Tory governments rewarding cronies. (Peterson, now chair of the Star's parent company, Torstar, has criticized Ford for reinstituting the award.)
Another 28 per cent had heard of it but were not familiar with the KC kerfuffle, while 56 per cent didn't know about it.
Of the 44 per cent aware of it, 49 per cent were less likely to support Ford due to the flap, 16 per cent were more likely to support him while 33 per cent said it would have no impact.
Those lingering difficulties aside, Coletto said the poll may encourage Ford to forge ahead with a 2025 campaign because "all of these positive and favourable conditions, I believe, still likely outweigh the risk of that early election."
In June, Abacus found 44 per cent of Ontarians would be "unhappy" or "very angry" if the premier triggered a snap election, which would cost taxpayers at least $150 million.
"But if people already kind of think you're reckless with their money, spending another $150 million more may not be the thing that causes his own base to leave him," he said.
One senior Conservative, speaking confidentially in order to discuss the internal debate over whether to head to the polls early, said the hefty cost of what could be deemed "an unnecessary election" may be a reason to worry.
"When (Ford) has been asked by you guys why he needs (an earlier vote), he hasn't had a great answer yet," admitted the insider, pointing to the premier's responses to journalists' questions on such a gambit.
"The opposition is useless, so it's not like he can't get his agenda through," the source said, referring to the massive majority the Tories have in the legislature.
"As we saw with Peterson (whose Liberals were at 50 per cent in the polls when he called a snap election in 1990 only to lose to Bob Rae's New Democrats), things can change fast in a campaign."
Another top Tory said while "there may be stuff in (this latest Abacus) poll that gives the premier pause," the PC team trusts his judgment and his knack for reading public opinion.
"He's not ideological. It's like golf. He aims for the middle of the fairway because that's how you win," said the second insider.
Indeed, as longtime supporter Irene Borecky told the Star's Peter Edwards at the annual Ford Fest picnic in Milton two weeks ago, the premier's missteps - and his accessibility to the public - only humanize him.
"It's service above self. He's very easy to talk to," said Borecky, a retired program analyst.
To Coletto, "that is at the core of Doug Ford's brand," the perception of a folksy populist that has developed since he came to office in 2018.
"He is the guy who will call you back. You run into him in the airport or on the street, he's going to shake your hand, he wants to meet you - he's that ultimate retail politician," he said.
"That said, ethics and being careful with taxpayers' money and honesty remain areas of vulnerability."
CAPTION: Doug Ford is in his seventh summer as Ontario premier and new polling for the Star has found voters are so far willing to forgive - if not quite forget - transgressions that would have derailed the electoral careers of others.

Subject: Scandals

Document 117 of 500

Hallway diplomacy for Haldimand County at municipal conference

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/hallway-diplomacy-haldimand-county-at-municipal/docview/3098264989/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Hallway+diplomacy+for+Haldimand+County+at+municipal+conference&amp;title=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-08-29&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Haldimand County councillors raised eyebrows when they barred independent MPP Bobbi Ann Brady from meetings with provincial cabinet ministers at the Association of Municipalities of Ontario (AMO) conference in Ottawa last week.
In June, council voted to exclude Brady, who represents Haldimand-Norfolk, from the annual conference over fears her presence would anger Premier Doug Ford - with whom she has publicly sparred at Queen's Park - and hurt the municipality's chances of getting provincial dollars.
But Brady went to AMO anyway, and was soon in the thick of things alongside Haldimand Mayor Shelley Ann Bentley.
"In the first five minutes we were at AMO, we ran into Prabmeet Sarkaria, the minister of transportation," Brady told The Spectator.
"We went right up to him and had a 10-minute chat about Caledonia bridge reconstruction (and) creating a safer situation at Haldibrook Road and Highway 6."
The county's request for a formal meeting with the transport ministry to discuss replacing the long-delayed Argyle Street bridge and addressing the collision-prone intersection - along with the need for a Highway 6 bypass to alleviate traffic congestion in Hagersville - had been denied prior to the conference, which ran Aug. 18 to 21.
But Bentley hopes the "impromptu" hallway chat with the minister will spur action.
"He told me that he would be getting back to me, (and) we will follow up with him this week," Bentley said in an interview on Monday.
Brady, who attended many AMO conferences as executive assistant to former Conservative MPP Toby Barrett, said sideline meetings are often where the real work is done.
"Delegations are just one part of AMO," Brady said. "It's the other conversations that are actually really vital."
During a delegation with the Ministry of Health, ministry officials confirmed the county's community paramedic program will continue to receive provincial funding.
The program, which launched in 2021, sees paramedics visit patients to check vital signs and manage medications, keeping residents out of the hospital for routine issues that can be taken care of more comfortably - and much more affordably - at home.
"That leaves people in their homes longer. They can age in place," Bentley said.
The Ministry of Long-Term Care turned down a meeting to talk about the planned 64-bed expansion of Edgewater Gardens, a municipally-run long-term care home in Dunnville, but officials are open to future discussions, Haldimand CAO Cathy Case told The Spectator in an email.
Haldimand agreed to cancel a previously granted delegation with the Ministry of Infrastructure since Minister Kinga Surma toured the Nanticoke water treatment plant in late July, where she learned about the county's $132-million plan to upgrade the plant to meet future development needs and pipe water to Norfolk and Six Nations.
"It is a big ask," said Bentley, who is banking on financial support from Queen's Park and Ottawa for the tripartite water project.
"The province wants to develop, so they're going to have to come through with some funding if they want more development in Norfolk."
As for Brady, she remains disappointed at being snubbed at AMO over sniping between her and the premier during Question Period.
"Nobody was going to keep me from going," Brady said.
"If (Haldimand councillors) don't understand that question period is separate from the work (MPPs) do behind the scenes every single day, then they need to do a little more research, or come to Queen's Park and really find out what goes on."
J.P. Antonacci's reporting is funded by the Canadian government through its Local Journalism Initiative. jpantonacci@thespec.com
CAPTION: Haldimand County Mayor Shelley Ann Bentley, left, and Haldimand-Norfolk MPP Bobbi Ann Brady chatted with Ontario Federation of Agriculture president Drew Spoelstra at the Association of Municipalities of Ontario (AMO) conference in Ottawa last week.Haldimand County Mayor Shelley Ann Bentley, far left, and Haldimand-Norfolk MPP Bobbi Ann Brady, far right, caught up with Tillsonburg councillors Dave Beres and Kelly Spencer at the Association of Municipalities of Ontario (AMO) conference in Ottawa last week.

Document 118 of 500

Aug. 29: Encampment fires endanger the public, the premier should listen to experts and other letters to the editor

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/aug-29-encampment-fires-endanger-public-premier/docview/3098264966/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Aug.+29%3A+Encampment+fires+endanger+the+public%2C+the+premier+should+listen+to+experts+and+other+letters+to+the+editor&amp;title=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-08-29&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Tent fires endanger the public
Re: Encampment catches fire at Gage Park, sending plume of smoke above east Hamilton, Aug. 27
Tuesday morning in Gage Park near the main children's playground, a dangerous fire erupted in one of the encampments. It was a plastics fire with intense flames and smoke shooting more than 20 feet into the air and surrounding trees. Explosions occurred with propane tanks flaring inside the fire. After the fire trucks had left, we found scorched, full-sized propane tanks, smaller propane canisters and numerous aerosol cans.
Dog walkers, joggers, children and park workers were all nearby. There could have been serious injuries or worse had any of the propane tanks blown, or had anyone been in the tents at the time. The city must do more to protect not only the homeless, but citizens at large.
Andrew Mutch and Jan Martin, Hamilton
Lucky no one injured in blaze
One of the larger tents in Gage Park went up in flames at around 8 a.m. on Tuesday.
Flames, fuelled by propane tanks and cans of spray paint, reached 30 feet high and were visible across the park. Thankfully, this tent was not in a cluster, but anyone walking among the gardens that run parallel to Main Street East could easily have been injured.
Gage Park is among many parks where people live in tents. Sadly, it seems inevitable that there will be more fires as the weather turns cooler.
Doug Sykes and Alison Rogers, Hamilton
Empathy in short supply today
It is no secret that a hot topic of conversation these days has been the encampments. Everyone seems to have an opinion.
Last week, I was talking to someone who was bothered by "those people." "They" were noisy. "They" left a mess. "They" shouldn't be allowed to live in the parks. It was an inconvenience for her. An inconvenience.
These comments were coming from a place a privilege. A place where housing or food scarcity or addictions or mental illness were not a part of her world.
We are all one crisis away from losing everything. Illness. Death of a partner. Injury. Addiction. Trauma. Life can be cruel and unforgiving.
On the same day this conversation took place, my rent was increased for the third time in a 16-month period by over 36 per cent.
The system is not set up to help those in need. It's set up quite the opposite. The rich get richer. The poor get poorer.
Everyone seems to have an opinion. Those are not in short supply. But what is missing these days is compassion. Empathy. Understanding.
So next time the unhoused become an inconvenience for you, remember that there are some terrible people who live on the street. There are also some terrible people who own million-dollar mansions. Where you live doesn't make you who you are. The sooner we all realize this, the sooner we can start helping to lift people up again, instead of kick them when they are down.
Christy France, Hamilton
Ford should listen to experts
Re: We need both harm reduction and treatment, Aug. 27
Having read the excellent article by Dr. Vincent Lam (an addictions physician), I would ask the provincial government to listen to the experts. He speaks of the complexity of addiction and the need for a multifaceted approach. These supervised consumption sites can be the beginning of recovery for addicts. Maybe not the first time they attend one, but over time, with the addict close to professional staff who can plant the seeds of recovery. Let's hope Doug Ford will heed the advice of experts in the field and keep supervised consumption sites.
Janet Connelly, Stoney Creek
Socialized health care has failed me
I once believed the Canadian medical system was modern, efficient and world-class. I was wrong.
My illness was first diagnosed while on vacation. Doctors there put me on prescription drugs, recommended I return home and discuss options, including surgery, with a Canadian doctor. Upon return, I was referred to a specialist. Each time I asked about surgery, I was told the meds needed more time to work. Each time I complained about pain, I was ignored or told to take Tylenol. I've been admitted to the ER numerous times due to complications. On two occasions, the attending physicians voiced concern surgery hadn't been done.
After a year and a half, I was told my case was more complicated and I was passed onto a second specialist. I am now on a waiting list for surgery. The system does not consider quality of life or lost productivity. My course of treatment has cost OHIP more than surgery. My original disorder has gotten worse and my overall health deteriorated because I cannot get a simple operation. The same operation can be done in a private clinic in Canada using newer and safer operating procedures, but is not covered by OHIP. I regret putting my faith in socialized health care. I am not a rich man, but had I known, in the beginning, my health would regress to the state I'm now in, I would have spent my hard-earned savings and gone to a private clinic a year ago.
Donald Zelem, Simcoe
CAPTION: Readers worry that a tent fire in Gage Park on Tuesday could've been resulted in injuries or worse had propane tanks exploded.

Subject: Tents; Parks & recreation areas; Addictions; Surgery; Homeless people; Injuries
