Document 119 of 500

Dealing with all the messes that get left behind

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/dealing-with-all-messes-that-get-left-behind/docview/3098162694/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=unknown&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Dealing+with+all+the+messes+that+get+left+behind&amp;title=The+Spectator&amp;issn=11899417&amp;date=2024-08-29&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.8&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Spectator&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: "The sins of the father are to be laid upon the children," wrote William Shakespeare in "The Merchant of Venice."
Although the Bard was not referring to the toxic industrial legacies that we've all inherited from our predecessors, the shoe fits.
The real tragedy is that racialized, marginalized and poorer people are more likely to be affected.
Bill C-226, introduced by the Green party's Elizabeth May, received Royal assent to remedy that in June this year.
The mandate is to "introduce legislation to develop an environmental justice strategy and examine the link between race, socio-economic status, and exposure to environmental risk."
Like all federal legislation, the process from law to action is lengthy.
The next step seeks public input for Canada's Environmental and Racial Justice Symposium, scheduled for Sept. 13 and 14 in Ottawa. The deadline to register to attend in person is Friday, and to attend online, it's Sept 6. Full details are on the dedicated website enviroequity.ca.
There are countless examples across the country where communities of racialized and Indigenous people have been sickened - often for generations - by industrial pollution of land, air and water.
Local governments approved garbage dumps and polluting industries where people have less economic power to fight back.
This includes the historic Africville in Nova Scotia, Grassy Narrows in northern Ontario, and Chemical Valley in Sarnia. Wealth and race should not be the determinants of being able to live in a safe, clean environment.
But are any of us immune?
If you've lived in Waterloo Region long enough, for example, you'll know about the 35-years-and-counting cleanup of Elmira's contaminated municipal wells and aquifers, the homes on Kitchener's Ralgreen Crescent that began to crack and ooze poisons from the garbage dump they were built on, the 60,000 tonnes of toxic coal tar that were found and excavated under what is now the beautiful new entrance to Victoria Park, and the trichlorethylene (TCE) contaminated water, soil and air in Cambridge's Bishop Street neighbourhood.
It's easy to blame our forefathers' industrial sins on a time with fewer people, seemingly endless space at the edge of our towns, and lax or non-existent protective regulations. But now we can add flawed laws and legal action that suck up funds that could have gone to cleaning up existing polluted sites.
We're still saddled with seven unresolved claims under Chapter 11 in the North American Free Trade Agreement (NAFTA), that was the brainchild of the then-federal Conservative government. Although it was removed under a Liberal government with the revised Canadian United States Mexico Agreement, foreign investors were allowed to seek compensation for cancelled projects up until June 2023 under "legacy investment protection provisions."
One such case, Ruby River vs. Canada, is seeking billions - an unspeakable amount of money - for the cancellation of a liquified gas plant project in Quebec.
But where are all the big investors and job-creating industries when it comes time to cleaning up the messes they leave behind?
Susan Koswan is a freelance contributing columnist for the Waterloo Region Record. Reach her at greyandstillgreen
@gmail.com.
CAPTION:

CREDIT: Susan Koswan OPINION Susan Koswan is a freelance contributing columnist for the Waterloo Region Record. Reach her at greyandstillgreen @gmail.com.;

Document 121 of 500

Letters

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/letters/docview/3098162456/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=unknown&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Letters&amp;title=The+Spectator&amp;issn=11899417&amp;date=2024-08-29&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.8&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Spectator&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Tent fires pose a risk to the public
Re: Encampment catches fire at Gage Park, sending plume of smoke above east Hamilton, Aug. 27
Tuesday morning in Gage Park near the main children's playground, a dangerous fire erupted in one of the encampments. It was a plastics fire with intense flames and smoke shooting more than 20 feet into the air and surrounding trees. Explosions occurred with propane tanks flaring inside the fire. After the fire trucks had left, we found scorched, full-sized propane tanks, smaller propane canisters and numerous aerosol cans.
Dog walkers, joggers, children and park workers were all nearby. There could have been serious injuries or worse had any of the propane tanks blown, or had anyone been in the tents at the time. The city must do more to protect not only the homeless, but citizens at large.
Andrew Mutch and
Jan Martin, Hamilton
Lucky no one
injured in blaze
One of the larger tents in Gage Park went up in flames at around 8 a.m. on Tuesday.
Flames, fuelled by propane tanks and cans of spray paint, reached 30 feet high and were visible across the park. Thankfully, this tent was not in a cluster, but anyone walking among the gardens that run parallel to Main Street East could easily have been injured.
Gage Park is among many parks where people live in tents. Sadly, it seems inevitable that there will be more fires as the weather turns cooler.
Doug Sykes and
Alison Rogers, Hamilton
Empathy in short supply today
It is no secret that a hot topic of conversation these days has been the encampments. Everyone seems to have an opinion.
Last week, I was talking to someone who was bothered by "those people." "They" were noisy. "They" left a mess. "They" shouldn't be allowed to live in the parks. It was an inconvenience for her. An inconvenience.
These comments were coming from a place a privilege. A place where housing or food scarcity or addictions or mental illness were not a part of her world.
We are all one crisis away from losing everything. Illness. Death of a partner. Injury. Addiction. Trauma. Life can be cruel and unforgiving.
On the same day this conversation took place, my rent was increased for the third time in a 16-month period by over 36 per cent.
The system is not set up to help those in need. It's set up quite the opposite. The rich get richer. The poor get poorer. Everyone seems to have an opinion. Those are not in short supply. But what is missing these days is compassion. Empathy. Understanding.
So next time the unhoused become an inconvenience for you, remember that there are some terrible people who live on the street. There are also some terrible people who own million-dollar mansions. Where you live doesn't make you who you are. The sooner we all realize this, the sooner we can start helping to lift people up again, instead of kick them when they are down.
Christy France, Hamilton
Ford should listen to the experts
Re: We need both harm
reduction and treatment,
Aug. 27
Having read the excellent article by Dr. Vincent Lam (an addictions physician), I would ask the provincial government to listen to the experts. He speaks of the complexity of addiction and the need for a multifaceted approach. These supervised consumption sites can be the beginning of recovery for addicts. Maybe not the first time they attend one, but over time, with the addict close to professional staff who can plant the seeds of recovery. Let's hope Doug Ford will heed the advice of experts in the field and keep supervised consumption sites.
Janet Connelly, Stoney Creek

Document 122 of 500

Province plans to boost electricity production

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/province-plans-boost-electricity-production/docview/3098162288/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Province+plans+to+boost+electricity+production&amp;title=The+Spectator&amp;issn=11899417&amp;date=2024-08-29&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.13&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Spectator&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Ontario is touting protections for farmland and the possibility of more carbon-free power as it seeks proposals for 5,000 megawatts of electricity generation - enough to power five million homes.
"The message we're sending today is renewables are very much part of this," Energy and Electrification Minister Stephen Lecce said Wednesday in a switch from Premier Doug Ford's decision to cancel 758 green energy projects after taking office in 2018.
Lecce added the approach to the electricity procurement is "technology-agnostic" with officials ready to weigh proposals using "affordability" as the main determinant to avoid shocks on consumers' hydro bills.
"Ontario must produce more affordable energy to keep up" with a fast-growing population and electrification needs fuelled by electric vehicles and artificial intelligence and data services, he said at a farm in King City, north of Toronto.
Keith Brooks of Environmental Defence cautioned against over-optimism about more green energy from the Conservatives.
He noted Lecce's "agnostic" reference makes the procurement "open to natural gas and nukes" and other forms of electricity generation in addition to renewables like solar and wind power.
"We look forward to the opportunity to compete," Leonard Kula of the Canadian Renewable Energy Association told reporters at the announcement with Lecce.
While applauding the government's "U-turn" on renewables, Green Leader Mike Schreiner said the province has been increasingly relying on natural gas, which produces carbon emissions that contribute to climate change.
"Renewables are cheaper, cleaner and make far more economic sense than fossil gas," he added in a statement.
As recently as 2017, Ontario's grid was 96 per cent free of carbon emissions but that has fallen to 87 per cent with nuclear reactors offline for refurbishments. It remains one of the cleanest systems in the world and the emission-free level will rise once nuclear plants return to service.
Promised protections for agricultural lands come in the wake of the government's $8.28-billion Greenbelt land swap scandal, now under investigation as Ford considers an early election next year.
The protections include a prohibition on energy projects in "specialty crop areas" such as the Holland Marsh, ensure no ground-mounted solar installations in prime agricultural areas, and require "agricultural impact assessments" for projects proposed for prime farmland.
This approach "strikes the right balance between protecting how we grow our food and how we generate our energy," said Agriculture, Food and Agribusiness Minister Rob Flack.
The procurement will be handled by Ontario's Independent Electricity System Operator. Energy developers proposing projects will be required to get resolutions of support from local municipal councils to ensure new facilities are welcome.
"We are ready to move quickly to secure supply from all available technologies," Lesley Gallinger, chief executive of the IESO, said.
CAPTION:

CREDIT: Rob Ferguson Toronto Star

Document 123 of 500

'Star Wars Outlaws' heavy with Canadian talent: Game's featured voice artist honed her craft studying theatre while growing up in Alberta

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/star-wars-outlaws-heavy-with-canadian-talent/docview/3098162263/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=%27Star+Wars+Outlaws%27+heavy+with+Canadian+talent%3A+Game%27s+featured+voice+artist+honed+her+craft+studying+theatre+while+growing+up+in+Alberta&amp;title=The+Spectator&amp;issn=11899417&amp;date=2024-08-29&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=G.3&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Spectator&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: "Star Wars Outlaws" would have had a distinctly Canadian touch if the game's narrative director had his way.
Early in the development process for the upcoming video game, Navid Khavari suggested an idea that ultimately never made it out of the writers' room.
"I wanted to write a scene that was just two smugglers apologizing to each other the entire time," said Khavari, who is based in Toronto along with lead writer Nikki Foy and lead voice actor Humberly Gonzalez.
Developed by Ubisoft, "Outlaws" is the first open-world "Star Wars" game and is set between the events depicted in the movies "The Empire Strikes Back" and "Return of the Jedi."
While Sweden-based Massive Entertainment led the development, support provided by other Ubisoft studios included motion capture work that Khavari says was done in Toronto and Montreal.
As its name suggests, "Outlaws" explores the criminal underbelly of the galaxy, a side of "Star Wars" that has rarely been seen.
Players will have the opportunity to explore several planets, including the classic Tatooine, but also lesser-known and even new locales.
Foy says it was a fun challenge to create a brand new story in a well-established universe.
"Getting to work on this project, in this galaxy, at this time period, it was like a cheat code that got all of us on the same page right away," Foy said.
"You didn't have to worry about what's the world going to be like, what's the tone, what's the vibe - that stuff we already know."
Khavari, who was also the narrative director for "Far Cry 6" and has worked on a number of other Ubisoft titles, says Massive contacted him early on about joining the "Star Wars" project.
"We have a huge abundance of writing talent in Toronto," he said. "The idea really became, let's build a story team in Toronto. This was years and years ago, and Massive was up for it."
In a departure from recent "Star Wars" video games, the main character in "Outlaws" is not a lightsaber-wielding, Force-pushing Jedi but a blaster-packing scoundrel.
Gonzalez voices protagonist Kay Vess, a charming and witty rogue trying to find her way in the galaxy.
Gonzalez was born in Venezuela and learned English in the nearby Caribbean island nation of Aruba before moving with her family to Fort McMurray, Alta., where she spent nearly 10 years and studied theatre.
"Being in a small city, I think it really allowed me to home in on my art," said Gonzalez, who later lived in Montreal and is now based in Toronto.
"Moving to Canada changed everything for me," she added.
"Much like Kay - she left everything behind, she had big dreams, she went to an unknown place where there were a lot of opportunities, and you just have to find them and seize them."
"Star Wars Outlaws" will be released Friday for Xbox Series S and X, PS5 and PC.
The Canadian Press
CAPTION: Humberly Gonzalez, the main voice actor who plays the character Kay Vess in the upcoming video game "Star Wars Outlaws," at Ubisoft's Toronto offices.Chris YoungTHE CANADIAN PRESS File Photo

CREDIT: Curtis Ng The Canadian Press

Document 124 of 500

Ford government plans to boost electricity production to keep up with demand

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/ford-government-plans-boost-electricity/docview/3097838891/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Ford+government+plans+to+boost+electricity+production+to+keep+up+with+demand&amp;title=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-08-28&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Ontario is touting protections for farmland and the possibility of more carbon-free power as it seeks proposals for 5,000 megawatts of electricity generation, enough to power five million homes.
In a switch from Premier Doug Ford's cancellation of 758 green energy projects after taking office in 2018, "the message we're sending today is renewables are very much part of this," Energy and Electrification Minister Stephen Lecce said Wednesday.
But Lecce added the approach to the electricity procurement is "technology-agnostic," meaning that officials will consider proposals with "affordability" being the main determinant to avoid shocks on consumers' hydro bills.
"Ontario must produce more affordable energy to keep up" with a fast-growing population and electrification needs fuelled by electric vehicles and artificial intelligence services, he said at a farm in King City, north of Toronto.
Keith Brooks of Environmental Defence said Lecce's "agnostic" reference makes the procurement "open to natural gas and nukes."
Green Leader Mike Schreiner said the change in tune on renewables is welcome but addedthe Progressive Conservative government "has a lot further to go if it wants to undo the years of damage its fossil gas obsession has caused."
As recently as 2017, Ontario's grid was 96 per cent free of carbon emissions but that has fallen to 87 per cent, although it remains one of the cleanest systems in the world.
Ontario is tak
"We are putting municipalities in the driver's seat," Lecce said in a statement, faulting a previous Liberal government for foisting projects on some areas more than a decade ago.
The goal is to secure another 5,000 megawatts of power. To put that in perspective, Tuesday's peak demand amid sweltering, humid temperatures was roughly 23,400 megawatts. Ontario's electricity consumption is expected to rise 60 per cent in the next 25 years.
Giving cities and towns the power to nix projects they don't want would spare Premier Doug Ford's government the embarrassment of rejection and delays, as happened when Halton Hills and Loyalist Township said "no" to new natural gas-fired plants amid concerns about climate change.
Protections for agricultural lands come in the wake of the government's $8.28-billion Greenbelt land swap scandal, now under investigation by the RCMP as Ford considers an early election next year.
The protections include a prohibition on energy projects in "specialty crop areas," ensure no ground-mounted solar installations in prime agricultural areas, and require "agricultural impact assessments" for projects on prime farmland, a government statement said.
A source said discussions are underway with the agriculture ministry on how to conduct such assessments.
Lecce has directed Ontario's Independent Electricity System Operator (IESO) to make the procurement process "technology-agnostic," a phrase the government uses to signal that projects will be considered on their merits, including wind and solar.
"We are ready to move quickly to secure supply from all available technologies," Lesley Gallinger, chief executive of the IESO, said in a statement.
Shortly after taking over the energy portfolio in June, Lecce said the province can't afford an "ideological" push to wean its electricity system off fossil fuels despite criticism the province has been increasing its reliance on natural gas-fuelled plants to meet peak demand.
As recently as 2017, Ontario's grid was 96 per cent free of carbon emissions but that has fallen to 87 per cent, although it remains one of the cleanest systems in the world.
The IESO aims to arrange the procurements of new electricity projects by late February 2026.
There will also be incentives for projects in northern Ontario, with a plan to make Crown lands available for renewable energy. Incentives will be provided for projects by Indigenous communities on their traditional territories.
Upon taking office in 2018, the Ford government cancelled 758 green energy projects, saying they were too expensive.
CAPTION: Energy and Electrification Minister Stephen Lecce said there will be protections for prime agricultural lands while local councils must approve of proposed projects within their boundaries.

Document 125 of 500

Can Justin Trudeau's Liberals seize on the U.S. Democrats' success? Here's where they should start

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/can-justin-trudeaus-liberals-seize-on-u-s/docview/3097838870/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Can+Justin+Trudeau%27s+Liberals+seize+on+the+U.S.+Democrats%27+success%3F+Here%27s+where+they+should+start&amp;title=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-08-28&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: It was a bit of bad luck for Justin Trudeau's Liberals, having to hold their annual cabinet retreat on the heels of the wildly successful Democratic convention in the U.S.
The comparisons and the questions were going to be inevitable - why can't the Trudeau Liberals do that kind of progressive political revival here in Canada?
As minister after minister explained, no one is talking about showing Trudeau the exit door, as the Democrats did with Joe Biden.
But leaving that nuclear option aside, one presumes that ministers would be drawing some lessons from how the Democrats rebranded themselves and their new leader, Kamala Harris, at last week's convention.
Some of the strategies on display in Chicago last week could be transferable techniques - at least to some measure.
The first one, the "joy" factor, is the most obvious but also the toughest. Government House Leader Karina Gould alluded to this when she was talking to reporters about possible parallels between Liberals here and the rebranded Democrats.
"Just because the Democrats do something in the United States does not then mean that we should be doing that here in Canada as Liberals," Gould said. "What I do think is that progressive voters are looking for continued hope."
Hope, positivity and joy can be hard to pull off, though, if you're a nine-year-old government, languishing as much as 20 points behind the Conservatives in the opinion polls.
The cabinet retreat featured a small flurry of announcements, but it would be a reach to call them sunny measures - tariffs on Chinese vehicles, limits to temporary foreign workers, a task force on trade barriers. They were meant to show a government hard at work, which is serious, as opposed to joyous business.
There have been signs the Liberals would dearly love to reframe their battle against Pierre Poilievre and the Conservatives as the Democrats have shifted their framing of Donald Trump - from scary to "weird," from object of fear to object of ridicule.
We can probably expect to see Liberals making more forays in this direction, similar to Immigration Minister Marc Miller, talking in Halifax about Poilievre's "crap slogans." But can they diminish a 20-point-lead with punchlines or mockery? That won't be easy.
There was another new Democratic strategy on display in Chicago - or maybe it's more properly noted at what was not on display, conspicuous by its absence.
Harris was being very careful how she came at the idea of being the first female president or the first Black and South Asian female president. Instead, she spoke more of herself as someone from humble beginnings, evidence that ambitious dreams are available to all in America.
This was no doubt a legacy of what happened to Hillary Clinton and her bid for the presidency, which constantly reminded voters of the identity stakes in the 2016 election.
But it's also a product of many voters' fatigue with identity politics - even voters on the progressive left. The debates, often pitting one demographic against another, have been exhausting.
One hears this in Canada, too. Trudeau and his Liberals are often criticized for spending too much time on these issues - the accusation that they're too "woke." If Liberals are taking some cues from Harris and the Democrats in the wake of Chicago, they might want to take a closer look at the caution with which their American progressives are handling identity politics in 2024.
Thanks to their change in leadership, Democrats do have a luxury that is not available to the Trudeau Liberals - an opportunity to rebrand the party on a more fundamental level. CNN commentator Van Jones, a veteran Democrat, spoke often of this last week while watching the convention.
Whether it was in reclaiming the word "freedom" from Republicans or recasting what it means to be a patriot, Jones said, Harris is staking out a new definition of progressive politics.
"They are asserting that the value of freedom is not just the freedom to not pay your taxes, or the freedom to, say, get off my lawn. It's the freedom to live a full life," Jones said.
Though Liberals can hardly set about redefining progressive politics on their own right now - especially in a party that Trudeau has already rebranded in his own image a decade ago - they should be paying close attention to where the Democrats are moving the markers on the left-right political spectrum. If nothing else, political trends that start in the U.S. often end up here.
In front of the cameras, Trudeau and his team took every opportunity to point out that it would be a mistake to do too much comparing between Liberals in Canada and Democrats in the U.S. Behind the scenes though, it wouldn't be a surprise if Trudeau's ministers were seeing if their playbook could take a few pages from Chicago.
CAPTION: Vice President Kamala Harris briefly takes to the stage at the Democratic National Convention on Aug. 19, 2024, in Chicago.

Subject: Political conventions; Progressivism; Identity politics; Voters; Political leadership; Political parties

Location: Chicago Illinois; United States--US; Canada

Title: Can Justin Trudeau's Liberals seize on the U.S. Democrats' success? Here's where they should start

Aug. 28: Early election speculation welcome, housing takes too long to complete and other letters to the editor

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/aug-28-early-election-speculation-welcome-housing/docview/3097838574/se-2?accountid=15090

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Aug.+28%3A+Early+election+speculation+welcome%2C+housing+takes+too+long+to+complete+and+other+letters+to+the+editor&amp;title=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-08-28&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Happy to hear early election possible
Re: Ontario parties spend summer preparing for possibility of an early election, Aug. 21
Oh boy, this is the best news I've heard in a long time; a chance to send Ford and his band of inept sycophants packing. This can allow a chance for Ontarians to have a real government, one hopefully focused on people and the important life-enhancing and necessary issues, not just making money then throwing it away on pet projects while those in need suffer. Bring it on Doug, because we have nothing to lose but you.
Robert Panchyson, Burlington
Let Chinese EVs into Canada
Re: China accuses Canada of protectionism over 100% tariffs on electric vehicles, Aug. 27
Perhaps western car manufacturers should let the Chinese take over the car manufacturing business since China produces smaller, cheaper and more earth-friendly cars than we do in North America or Europe.
Renate Manthei, Hamilton
We have a government of contradictions
Doug Ford and Sylvia Jones are running one heck of a good dog and pony show. That had a great photo op for the opening of an animal hospital which gets the family pet better access to MRI/CT scan/ultrasounds than to the aging and ailing human beings of Ontario. In a great contradiction, they are making alcohol - with its well-documented health risks - readily available to everyone everywhere, but are putting vulnerable people at risk with closures of supervised injection sites. As the radio commercials say, "Paid for by the Government of Ontario." Isn't that us?
Al Dionisi, Stoney Creek
Housing takes too long to complete
The politicians still get on their soap boxes and call for more affordable housing to be built to address the need. But there's one thing they forgot!
Building new housing, say a 10-storey geared-to-income building, from concept to completion is in the range of 24 to 48 months. That is if all the stars align and there are no material or labour shortages or some bureaucrat inspector shutting the project down or causing delays.
In that time span, the number of homeless or working poor will triple. It's like putting lipstick on a pig. Politicians believe the problem can be solved as easily as getting a coffee at a drive-thru. Think again. We need streamlined approvals and where there are shovel-ready properties, to encourage development and not impede it.
Steven Hill, Hamilton
Positive vibes from the DNC welcome
I am a Canadian who loves the reclaiming of the narrative that America isn't a bad place to be, which was a message from Donald Trump that got him votes under the smoke and mirrors promise that he would make the country great again. The positive energy at the Democratic convention was palpable across digital lines, and all of a sudden I am interested in visiting that country again.
Rachel Griffin, Ancaster
Toxic drug crisis a complex issue
I wish Premier Doug Ford a good night's sleep after choosing to gamble with people's lives in the decision to close several drug consumption and treatment sites. To be fair, I do not have an answer to this complex problem, but it is evident that he doesn't either with his half-baked "solution." You would think that, after so many embarrassing missteps, Ford would finally figure out that he indeed is not the smartest man in the room. But he can't help pretending that he is and continues to blindly flail at issues far more complex than he is capable of solving. Experts be damned!
David Ottenbrite, Cambridge
CAPTION: The province's next fixed election date is in June 2026, but Premier Doug Ford set off alarm bells in political circles this past May when he repeatedly refused to commit to that date at a press conference. Laryssa Waler, a former director of communications for Ford and founder of Walman Strategies, said a spring 2025 election seems more likely than this fall.

Full text: Happy to hear
the early election speculation
Re: Ontario parties
spend summer preparing for possibility of an early election, Aug. 21
Oh boy, this is the best news I've heard in a long time; a chance to send Ford and his band of inept sycophants packing. This can allow a chance for Ontarians to have a real government, one hopefully focused on people and the important life-enhancing and necessary issues, not just making money then throwing it away on pet projects while those in need suffer. Bring it on Doug, because we have nothing to lose but you.

Re: China accuses Canada

Renate Manthei, Hamilton
We have a
government of contradictions
Doug Ford and Sylvia Jones are running one heck of a good dog and pony show. That had a great photo op for the opening of an animal hospital which gets the family pet better access to MRI/CT scan/ultrasounds than to the aging and ailing human beings of Ontario. In a great contradiction, they are making alcohol - with its well-documented health risks - readily available to everyone everywhere, but are putting vulnerable people at risk with closures of supervised injection sites. As the radio commercials say, "Paid for by the Government of Ontario." Isn't that us?
Al Dionisi, Stoney Creek
Housing projects take too long
to complete
The politicians still get on their soap boxes and call for more affordable housing to be built to address the need. But there's one thing they forgot!
Building new housing, say a 10-storey geared-to-income building, from concept to completion is in the range of 24 to 48 months. That is if all the stars align and there are no material or labour shortages or some bureaucrat inspector shutting the project down or causing delays.
In that time span, the number of homeless or working poor will triple. It's like putting lipstick on a pig. Politicians believe the problem can be solved as easily as getting a coffee at a drive-thru. Think again.
We need streamlined approvals and where there are shovel-ready properties, to encourage development and not impede it.
Steven Hill, Hamilton

Document 128 of 500

Behind closed doors, Justin Trudeau ponders the road ahead - as one of his former advisers says the PM faces a massive challenge

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/behind-closed-doors-justin-trudeau-ponders-road/docview/3097921237/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Behind+closed+doors%2C+Justin+Trudeau+ponders+the+road+ahead+-+as+one+of+his+former+advisers+says+the+PM+faces+a+massive+challenge&amp;title=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-08-27&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: HALIFAX-There was one unusually candid moment as a parade of Liberal ministers went before cameras at a cabinet retreat in a soulless downtown conference centre.
Immigration Minister Marc Miller, a friend of the prime minister's, knew he'd be asked about the party's prospects under Justin Trudeau and whether the Liberals had learned any lessons from a summer of talking to cranky voters or from the fluid presidential race south of the border.
Miller's blunt answer: Sure. But it's not for public discussion.
"The reality is, we have to have these conversations behind closed doors. It's important for the health of the party" and cabinet "cohesion," he said.
But one of the prime minister's key former advisers, David MacNaughton, Trudeau's former ambassador to Washington who briefed cabinet Tuesday, says Trudeau faces a massive challenge to persuade a weary electorate.
MacNaughton told the Star it's up to the prime minister if he wants to stay or go, but if he stays "you're going to have to do stuff that is really tough" because all the polls show voters have an intense desire for change.
Facing a "change election" means at the least the Liberals have to make bold policy decisions, said MacNaughton, a former Ontario campaign co-chair for Trudeau. He means big things: like taking on supply management in the agriculture sector, or jumping on AI to push Canadian companies big and small to embrace it and become a global leader. MacNaughton sees AI as an opportunity to address health-care issues, and productivity challenges. The Trudeau government, he said, is "reactive," addressing problems as they arise, but it is not proactive enough, looking ahead to determine what needs to be done to goose economic growth.
MacNaughton is not one of those Liberals who thinks there is a magic solution - like replacing Trudeau with former Bank of Canada governor Mark Carney - that can fix what ails them.
If there is reflection still going on within Liberal cabinet ranks about Trudeau's leadership, it is rarely voiced aloud.
Several ministers who spoke to the Star said nobody at the cabinet table is actively elbowing him out. Many seem resigned to a year of hard work, if not all that enthusiastic.
Trudeau himself publicly dodged the question of his own takeaways from the momentum the Democrats south of the border are suddenly seeing.
Asked whether the Democrats' change of party leader made him think twice about his own decision to stay on, Trudeau - and some of his ministers - insisted the U.S. is a "very different country than Canada," has a "very different" political dynamic (without elaborating) and said Canadians don't worry about such questions, but about whether there are economic opportunities for them and their children.
Trudeau repeated what he's said on the rare occasion he's answered media questions since losing a key Toronto seat in a byelection in late June: he intends to lead the Liberal party into the next campaign.
And that's coming no time soon.
"We're still a year-plus away from the election," said Trudeau, and his focus is "on making sure we're delivering through that election the kinds of things that Canadians need."
Trudeau and his cabinet tout their economic record, saying over and over again they will "continue" to deliver things like dental care, child benefits and affordable daycare, and will put a "very clear choice" before Canadians, as they sharpened attack lines aimed at the Conservatives under leader Pierre Poilievre.
They're shifting direction on immigration, cutting temporary foreign workers and flagging there could be robust cuts to permanent resident numbers unveiled this fall. They are prioritizing housing shortages and affordability, but even economist Mike Moffatt, who's briefed them before, says they haven't been bold enough.
"They need to go much further, much faster," as Canada's housing problem "continues to get worse," in his view. In an interview, Moffatt said the country's housing issue has "seen some tinkering around the edges," but that his message to the Liberals is "we need some particularly bold action." He urged them to use the infrastructure program to force provinces to freeze and drastically cut development charges.
Publicly, minister after minister pledged confidence in Trudeau, albeit in remarks that felt scripted, if not defensive.
Foreign Affairs Minister Mélanie Joly said cabinet was busy "focusing on what Canadians need, and for the rest, the prime minister has my full support."
Industry Minister François-Philippe Champagne said Canadians want a government that "takes action," offers "a clear vision" and an "optimistic message."
"Optimism is contagious," he said, adding although the economic picture is brightening, and inflation is coming down, there's more work to do.
Deputy Prime Minister and Finance Minister Chrystia Freeland was astonishingly brief when asked if a leadership swap would revive Liberal party fortunes.
"Absolutely not," she said, stepping back. Pressed to explain why not, she stepped theatrically forward again. "I, all of us here, have tremendous confidence in the prime minister."
As if to underline how Trudeau might have (but didn't) similarly voice confidence in her performance when news of his wooing of Carney came to light in July, Freeland listed the ways she backs Trudeau: "We have confidence in him as leader of our government, as the prime minister of Canada, and we have confidence in him as our party's leader, as the guy who's going to lead us into the next election."
Calls for Trudeau to step down may emerge at a bigger caucus retreat in September. One minister said MPs who haven't made it into cabinet after nine years are expected to air grievances there.
Still, behind the scenes, several acknowledged Trudeau may be a good campaigner - as evidenced by weeks of gripping-and-grinning with Canadians at summer festivals and barbecues - and may want to stay on, but the Liberals have a deep hole to climb out of.
Public opinion polls give a massive advantage to the Conservatives.
Several ministers and senior staffers who spoke to the Star on a background-only basis said they have worked hard during the summer to push initiatives out the door to deliver those "results" Trudeau wants to see - an effort one minister said was easier in the pandemic when everyone was working "in the same direction."
And there are questions about just how committed other ministers are to that job.
Transport Minister Pablo Rodriguez tried to punt speculation he was leaving the federal ranks to run for the Quebec Liberal Party leadership, saying he had nothing to announce immediately. Still, several of his colleagues privately wondered whether he's with them for the long haul.
Trudeau's team made a stream of announcements: tariffs on Chinese-made EVs, steel and aluminum imports; the release of federal properties for housing development; and cuts to the temporary foreign workers program. Then there are minor process-oriented steps that never seem to change anything: a working group on productivity, plans by two ministers to meet premiers to reduce interprovincial trade barriers, and efforts to reduce regulatory red tape to expedite Canada-U.S. trade flow.
On the last day, unlike previous retreats, Trudeau took no media questions.
It was left to House leader Karina Gould, fresh off a seven-month mat leave, to sum up the Liberals' thought process heading into a key caucus retreat in September, and a pre-election House of Commons.
Liberals are grappling with that desire for change seen in the polls, and asking "is it change for change's sake, or is it because we need to be doing something differently?" she said. "What is the change that Canadians are looking for?"
She insisted it's not a change in progressive policies, but admitted, "there are things that need to be different."
"We've been in government for nine years. That's a long time, and we've done a lot in that period of time. But now we need to show Canadians what's next."
CAPTION: Prime Minister Justin Trudeau and members of his cabinet arrive at the retreat in Halifax on Aug. 26, 2024.

Subject: Leadership; Canadians; Election results; State elections; Voters; Prime ministers; Immigration

Business indexing term: Subject: Leadership

Location: United States--US; Canada

People: Trudeau, Justin

Document 129 of 500

Does Doug Ford want drug users dead?

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/does-doug-ford-want-drug-users-dead/docview/3097482760/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Does+Doug+Ford+want+drug+users+dead%3F&amp;title=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-08-27&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: "We need to get rid of safe supply."
These are the words of Premier Doug Ford in speaking about the planned closure of safe consumption sites announced last week by the Ontario government and, clearly, the end desire to get rid of any idea of safe supply.
Think about this little sentence of eight words.
What would the alternative be to safe supply? Trick question because we know the answer. Unsafe supply.
There's only safe and unsafe supply. There's no 'little bit' safe. And isn't safe better than unsafe? Unless you're looking to kill the problem completely, which unsafe supply would most certainly help with; but as we've seen throughout our history, substance use is endemic among humans. I think even some animals find a way to get high. Cats like catnip.
The question has to be asked. Does Doug Ford want drug addicts to die?
Does he want contaminated drugs flowing through our streets? Drug addiction is bad enough without certain death from unsafe supply. Is he favouring the criminal element, the current purveyors of unsafe drugs? That's who stands to benefit from eliminating government supply.
You have to know organized crime has lost territory to the government. Or maybe it's the police, who also benefit from the war on drugs as they focus on taking down the above mentioned criminal element while demanding larger and larger police budgets.
I'm in full support of increased public treatment and detox beds. Not private for-profit operations, but publicly funded and resourced with trained health-care personnel under the Canada Health Act. The provinces are not doing their jobs in delivering health care on many fronts, but in particular they are failing spectacularly on mental health and addiction.
The results are on our city streets.
Public intoxication of any substance disturbs the public peace and places people in potentially dangerous situations. I've seen more alcohol fuelled arguments resulting in fisticuffs than I care to remember. Ugly drunks are the worst. No one should have to step over a passed out crack addict lying on the pavement, whether they're taking their kids downtown shopping or to school.
We are all addicted to something, but we manage our addictions, some more successfully than others. If a person can't manage their addictions, they need help. But we have nowhere near the resources we need to help all the people who need it, in the way that they need, when they need it.
Our health professionals are woefully unprepared. The lack of training among health professionals over cannabis is quite alarming. This is the direct result of drug criminalization and the resulting restrictions on research into cannabis or any other illegal substance. You don't know what you don't study.
The last century demonstrates the failure of our ability to come to grips with recreational and illicit drug use. From prohibition in the early 20th century to the opioid drug crises of the early 21st, the attempt to control the consumption of mind-altering substances has failed spectacularly, with policies that haven't saved lives, only saddled people with criminal records and the associated stigma of "drug addict."
I bet the areas around the supervised consumption sites are tough for the community. But in this present moment, every community is struggling with or without SCSs. The problem is bigger than SCSs, but they are a slice of the solution.
Decriminalizing drugs will remove the imperative of the criminal element. It would be ridiculous to think all crime would just disappear - anything of value is worth stealing to some. But it addresses the issue of safe supply to ensure people aren't dying from criminal, unregulated production. Like they did from moonshine and bathtub gin.
Ford also accuses the federal government of being the biggest drug dealer in the country.
He's wrong about that, too. They should be, but they aren't yet. All the drugs we take should be regulated and safe. That should be a no-brainer, even for Doug Ford.
CAPTION: "We need to get rid of safe supply and put money into treatment and detox beds, that's what we need to do, not continue to give people free drugs,"Premier Doug Ford said recently. Does that mean he wants contaminated drugs flowing through our streets? Margaret Shkimba asks.

Subject: Drug addiction; Canada Health Act; Medical personnel; Contamination

Document 130 of 500

The cost of procrastination is adding up

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/cost-procrastination-is-adding-up/docview/3097482751/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=The+cost+of+procrastination+is+adding+up&amp;title=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-08-27&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: We are woefully unprepared to deal with runaway climate change.
Denialism, corporate greed and politics have gotten in the way of climate change progress. The obvious response to mitigate climate impacts would be to reduce fossil fuel use. In an increasingly energy-hungry world, however, particularly in developing countries, energy use has only increased, further driving destructive droughts, wildfires and storms. Worldwide losses due to flooding have reached $100 billion annually and are increasing.
It will be costly to climate-proof our infrastructure - roads, bridges, rail lines, electrical grid, sewers, etc. - but far less than the cost to repair climate change damage. The great ice storm of 1998, affecting Eastern Canada and the Northeastern U.S., caused massive damage to electrical infrastructure, left millions in the dark for weeks or longer, resulted in 56 fatalities and, essentially, shut down Montreal and Ottawa. The cost to repair the damage was $7 billion (U.S.).
As cities experience increasingly more powerful and frequent storms and flash flooding, stormwater infrastructure, built for a different time - with a gentler climate and fewer people - will continue to be overwhelmed. So far, 2024 has seen unprecedented flooding worldwide, dams breached and destroyed, cities flooded and villages washed away.
Worldwide, there is a massive stormwater infrastructure deficit, particularly in undeveloped and developing countries. With few resources, these countries suffer greater flooding as a result. Landslides are also more common, as saturated soil on unstable slopes, stripped of trees and other vegetation, slips, engulfing and destroying whatever is in its path.
The Seine, like many European rivers, was heavily polluted from sanitary and industrial waste. Paris spent $2 billion to clean up the Seine ahead of the Olympics, with upgraded wastewater treatment plants, a 50,000-cubic-metre combined sewer overflow (CSO) storage tank and renovated sewer infrastructure. In spite of these mitigation efforts, bacteria-laden stormwater runoff made the river unsafe and sickened some athletes. Going forward, it is unlikely that Paris' sewers will be able to handle increasingly higher stormwater volumes, with increased flooding and combined sewer overflows.
Stormwater management comes at a cost. We look to our governments to take action on climate change, including stormwater management, as long as it doesn't cost us anything. Stormwater fees, like carbon fees, are an unpopular but necessary tool to generate revenue that can be used to build greater stormwater collection and storage capacity. Stormwater fees may also encourage on-site capture and storage, reducing pressure on the stormwater system and reducing combined sewer overflows and flooding.
We can't engineer our way out of the problem with "grey" infrastructure alone, more concrete and steel - dams, seawalls, roads, pipes or wastewater treatment plants. "Sponge" cities incorporate more "green" infrastructure - rain ponds, bioswales, urban forests and wetlands - to slow down and absorb rainwater and pollutants, with the added benefit of providing habitat, protecting biodiversity and reducing urban heat islands.
Copenhagen, having experienced a once-in-a-100-year cloudburst event in 2011, resulting in $910 million in damage, adopted a $2-billion, 300-project, public/private cloudburst management plan, using both "grey" and "green" infrastructure, to capture and store stormwater. Other cities have taken notice and are following suit, but it requires government will, public consultation and resources to do so.
Reducing urban sprawl would also take pressure off stormwater infrastructure. With more people moving into urban centres, however, this becomes difficult. We could also stop paving over greenspaces, farmland and wetlands. Doug Ford, are you listening?
Hamilton has mitigated stormwater flooding with construction of nine CSO storage tanks and expanded capacity at the Woodward Avenue wastewater treatment facility - grey infrastructure - but more must be done to make Hamilton a sponge city, more green space, rain gardens and protected wetlands.
Volunteers are needed to plant new community rain gardens at the Westinghouse site on Sanford Avenue, on Sept. 5. If you would like to participate in expanding Hamilton's green infrastructure, you may contact Green Venture for more information.
CAPTION: As cities experience increasingly more powerful and frequent storms and flash flooding, stormwater infrastructure, built for a different time, will continue to be overwhelmed.
CREDIT: Wayne Poole

Subject: Storms; Dams; Developing countries--LDCs; Landslides & mudslides; Infrastructure; Water treatment; Floods; Stormwater management; Climate change; Rain; Wetlands

Business indexing term: Subject: Developing countries--LDCs Infrastructure

Document 131 of 500

'We need that hospital built yesterday': New hospital on Brant County's priority list at municipal conference

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/we-need-that-hospital-built-yesterday-new-on/docview/3097482667/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=%27We+need+that+hospital+built+yesterday%27%3A+New+hospital+on+Brant+County%27s+priority+list+at+municipal+conference&amp;title=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-08-27&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: County of Brant staff and councillors pushed hard for a new hospital at the Association of Municipalities of Ontario (AMO) conference in Ottawa last week.
Specifically, the county asked the province to grant Brant Community Healthcare System (BCHS) the remaining $8.5 million needed to complete the next planning stage and put the new hospital on the 2025 budget, Coun. Lukas Oakley wrote in a conference summary he shared with The Spectator.
Brantford General is the main hospital in the area, serving residents of Brantford, Brant County, Six Nations of the Grand River and Mississaugas of the Credit First Nation.
It has been challenged to keep up with the rapidly growing population, and reached 125 per cent capacity in the winter, councillors heard at an administration and operations meeting in May.
Sections of the building are nearing 100 years old, and staff are dealing with space and infrastructure challenges - including an eight-foot-deep sinkhole over a utility tunnel last September.
The BCHS and local municipalities began trying to get a new hospital in 2010, but it has never advanced past the planning stage.
The 14-year lag is something Bonnie Camm, incoming president and CEO of BCHS, told councillors was "unusual."
Following Brantford's AMO delegation with Minister of Health Sylvia Jones, and the county's meeting with her parliamentary assistant Anthony Leardi, Oakley said he's "optimistic" the Ministry of Health understands the need.
In the lead-up to the Aug. 18 to 21 conference, both municipalities collaborated with each other and the hospital to ensure they were presenting a unified message that "we need that hospital built yesterday," Coun. Jennifer Kyle told The Spectator in a call Friday.
Another key meeting was with Minister of Agriculture, Food and Agribusiness Rob Flack to advocate for protecting farmland from urban boundary expansions and non-agricultural uses, Oakley said.
The county highlighted the importance of provincial guidance and standardization when it comes to agricultural impact assessments, so the county can "best select the places where we need to have the growth," while protecting and keeping "the most important farmlands that we have" in production, Kyle said.
The county met with several other ministries, and NDP and Green Party leaders, as part of the South Central Ontario Region Economic Development Corporation (SCOR EDC), which includes Brant, Norfolk, Oxford, Middlesex and Elgin, Oakley said.
One such meeting was in support of the Southwest Community Transit (SCT) network project - an intercommunity transit system that includes Brant Transit - which only has approved government funding until March 2025.
The county spoke to the importance of continuing the program, "as the transit has become critical for members of our shared community," Oakley said.
As for the impact of the AMO meetings, Kyle said they sometimes lead to followups from a minister. In the case of the hospital, Brantford invited Jones for a visit.
"So hopefully she'll take us up on that invitation to come and to see the hospital for herself, and to really see the dire need we're in," she said.
Celeste Percy-Beauregard's reporting is funded by the Canadian government through its Local Journalism Initiative. The funding allows her to report on stories about Brant County. Reach her at cpercybeauregard@torstar.ca.
CAPTION: County of Brant councillors John MacAlpine, John Bell, Lukas Oakley and Jennifer Kyle, and county staff Alysha Dyjach andPhilip Mete met with MPP Anthony Leardi (centre) at AMO last week.

Document 132 of 500

Does Ford want drug users dead?

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/does-ford-want-drug-users-dead/docview/3097381412/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=unknown&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Does+Ford+want+drug+users+dead%3F&amp;title=The+Spectator&amp;issn=11899417&amp;date=2024-08-27&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.9&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Spectator&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: "We need to get rid of safe supply."
These are the words of Premier Doug Ford in speaking about the planned closure of safe consumption sites announced last week by the Ontario government and, clearly, the end desire to get rid of any idea of safe supply.
Think about this little sentence of eight words.
What would the alternative be to safe supply? Trick question because we know the answer. Unsafe supply.
There's only safe and unsafe supply. There's no "little bit" safe. And isn't safe better than unsafe? Unless you're looking to kill the problem completely, which unsafe supply would most certainly help with; but as we've seen throughout our history, substance use is endemic among humans. I think even some animals find a way to get high. Cats like catnip.
The question has to be asked. Does Doug Ford want drug addicts to die?
Does he want contaminated drugs flowing through our streets? Drug addiction is bad enough without certain death from unsafe supply. Is he favouring the criminal element, the current purveyors of unsafe drugs? That's who stands to benefit from eliminating government supply.
You have to know organized crime has lost territory to the government. Or maybe it's the police, who also benefit from the war on drugs as they focus on taking down the above mentioned criminal element while demanding larger and larger police budgets.
I'm in full support of increased public treatment and detox beds. Not private for-profit operations, but publicly funded and resourced with trained health-care personnel under the Canada Health Act. The provinces are not doing their jobs in delivering health care on many fronts, but in particular they are failing spectacularly on mental health and addiction.
The results are on our city streets.
Public intoxication of any substance disturbs the public peace and places people in potentially dangerous situations. I've seen more alcohol fuelled arguments resulting in fisticuffs than I care to remember. Ugly drunks are the worst.
No one should have to step over a passed out crack addict lying on the pavement, whether they're taking their kids downtown shopping or to school.
We are all addicted to something, but we manage our addictions, some more successfully than others. If a person can't manage their addictions, they need help. But we have nowhere near the resources we need to help all the people who need it, in the way that they need, when they need it.
Our health professionals are woefully unprepared. The lack of training among health professionals over cannabis is quite alarming. This is the direct result of drug criminalization and the resulting restrictions on research into cannabis or any other illegal substance. You don't know what you don't study.
The last century demonstrates the failure of our ability to come to grips with recreational and illicit drug use. From prohibition in the early 20th century to the opioid drug crises of the early 21st, the attempt to control the consumption of mind-altering substances has failed spectacularly, with policies that haven't saved lives, only saddled people with criminal records and the associated stigma of "drug addict."
I bet the areas around the supervised consumption sites are tough for the community. But in this present moment, every community is struggling with or without SCSs. The problem is bigger than SCSs, but they are a slice of the solution.
Decriminalizing drugs will remove the imperative of the criminal element. It would be ridiculous to think all crime would just disappear - anything of value is worth stealing to some.
But it addresses the issue of safe supply to ensure people aren't dying from criminal, unregulated production. Like they did from moonshine and bathtub gin.
Ford also accuses the federal government of being the biggest drug dealer in the country.
He's wrong about that, too. They should be, but they aren't yet. All the drugs we take should be regulated and safe.
That should be a no-brainer, even for Doug Ford.
Margaret Shkimba is a
Hamilton writer. For more
visit margaretshkimba.
Substack.com. You can follow her on X (@menrvasofia)
and friend her on Facebook.
CAPTION: "We need to get rid of safe supply and put money into treatment and detox beds, that's what we need to do, not continue to give people free drugs," Premier Doug Ford said recently. Does that mean he wants contaminated drugs flowing through our streets? Margaret Shkimba asks.Darren Calabrese The Canadian Press file photo

Document 133 of 500

The cost of stalling is adding up: We can't engineer our way out of our flooding problem with just more pipes and wastewater treatment plants

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/cost-stalling-is-adding-up/docview/3097381139/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=unknown&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=The+cost+of+stalling+is+adding+up%3A+We+can%27t+engineer+our+way+out+of+our+flooding+problem+with+just+more+pipes+and+wastewater+treatment+plants&amp;title=The+Spectator&amp;issn=11899417&amp;date=2024-08-27&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.9&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Spectator&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: We are woefully unprepared to deal with runaway climate change.
Denialism, corporate greed and politics have gotten in the way of climate change progress. The obvious response to mitigate climate impacts would be to reduce fossil fuel use. In an increasingly energy-hungry world, however, particularly in developing countries, energy use has only increased, further driving destructive droughts, wildfires and storms. Worldwide losses due to flooding have reached $100 billion annually and are increasing.
It will be costly to climate-proof our infrastructure - roads, bridges, rail lines, electrical grid, sewers, etc. - but far less than the cost to repair climate change damage. The great ice storm of 1998, affecting Eastern Canada and the Northeastern U.S., caused massive damage to electrical infrastructure, left millions in the dark for weeks or longer, resulted in 56 fatalities and, essentially, shut down Montreal and Ottawa. The cost to repair the damage was $7 billion (U.S.).
As cities experience increasingly more powerful and frequent storms and flash flooding, stormwater infrastructure, built for a different time - with a gentler climate and fewer people - will continue to be overwhelmed. So far, 2024 has seen unprecedented flooding worldwide, dams breached and destroyed, cities flooded and villages washed away.
Worldwide, there is a massive stormwater infrastructure deficit, particularly in undeveloped and developing countries. With few resources, these countries suffer greater flooding as a result. Landslides are also more common, as saturated soil on unstable slopes, stripped of trees and other vegetation, slips, engulfing and destroying whatever is in its path.
The Seine, like many European rivers, was heavily polluted from sanitary and industrial waste. Paris spent $2 billion to clean up the Seine ahead of the Olympics, with upgraded wastewater treatment plants, a 50,000-cubic-metre combined sewer overflow (CSO) storage tank and renovated sewer infrastructure. In spite of these mitigation efforts, bacteria-laden stormwater runoff made the river unsafe and sickened some athletes. Going forward, it is unlikely that Paris' sewers will be able to handle increasingly higher stormwater volumes, with increased flooding and combined sewer overflows.
Stormwater management comes at a cost. We look to our governments to take action on climate change, including stormwater management, as long as it doesn't cost us anything. Stormwater fees, like carbon fees, are an unpopular but necessary tool to generate revenue that can be used to build greater stormwater collection and storage capacity. Stormwater fees may also encourage on-site capture and storage, reducing pressure on the stormwater system and reducing combined sewer overflows and flooding.
We can't engineer our way out of the problem with "grey" infrastructure alone, more concrete and steel - dams, seawalls, roads, pipes or wastewater treatment plants. "Sponge" cities incorporate more "green" infrastructure - rain ponds, bioswales, urban forests and wetlands - to slow down and absorb rainwater and pollutants, with the added benefit of providing habitat, protecting biodiversity and reducing urban heat islands.
Copenhagen, having experienced a once-in-a-100-year cloudburst event in 2011, resulting in $910 million in damage, adopted a $2-billion, 300-project, public/private cloudburst management plan, using both "grey" and "green" infrastructure, to capture and store stormwater. Other cities have taken notice and are following suit, but it requires government will, public consultation and resources to do so.
Reducing urban sprawl would also take pressure off stormwater infrastructure. With more people moving into urban centres, however, this becomes difficult. We could also stop paving over greenspaces, farmland and wetlands. Doug Ford, are you listening?
Hamilton has mitigated stormwater flooding with construction of nine CSO storage tanks and expanded capacity at the Woodward Avenue wastewater treatment facility - grey infrastructure - but more must be done to make Hamilton a sponge city, more green space, rain gardens and protected wetlands.
Volunteers are needed to plant new community rain gardens at the Westinghouse site on Sanford Avenue, on Sept. 5. If you would like to participate in expanding Hamilton's green infrastructure, you may contact Green Venture for more information.
Wayne Poole lives in Dundas.
CAPTION:

CREDIT: Wayne Poole Wayne Poole lives in Dundas.;

Document 134 of 500

Doug Ford's Greenbelt scandal and health-care neglect has Liberals and NDP talking upset in Conservative stronghold

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/doug-fords-greenbelt-scandal-health-care-neglect/docview/3097541703/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Doug+Ford%27s+Greenbelt+scandal+and+health-care+neglect+has+Liberals+and+NDP+talking+upset+in+Conservative+stronghold&amp;title=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-08-26&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: It's been a Progressive Conservative stronghold since 2011 but rival parties are hoping to end that in the Sept. 19 Bay of Quinte byelection prompted by the surprise resignation of Ford cabinet minister Todd Smith.
In a race of local Belleville-area politicians hoping to make the leap to Queen's Park, the NDP is running school board trustee Amanda Robertson and the Liberals have tapped city councillor Sean Kelly - a radio morning show host.
They are looking to wrest votes from the Conservatives and their candidate Tyler Allsopp, also a Belleville city councillor, in the riding where Smith took 49 per cent of the votes for Premier Doug Ford's PCs in the 2022 election.
That was more than double the second-place New Democrat challenger at 20.9 per cent, 18.5 per cent for the Liberals and 6.3 per cent for the Greens, who do not yet have a candidate.
Despite Ford's commanding lead in province-wide public opinion polls, the opposition parties say the lack of progress improving access to doctors, long hospital emergency room waits and affordable housing will hurt the Tories as the RCMP investigates the government's $8.28-billion Greenbelt land swap scandal.
"People in Bay of Quinte deserve health care they can rely on, homes they can afford and schools that set kids up for success," said Robertson, who is also vice-chair of the Hastings and Prince Edward and District School Board.
In London on Monday, Ford said he is confident there will be an "explosion" in housing starts as interest rates fall.
"I've witnessed firsthand how Doug Ford's cuts are having a devastating impact on our community," said Kelly, in his second term on council in a city where officials declared a state of emergency last February after 13 drug overdose calls to 911 during a one-hour period.
Allsopp, a small businessman, said he is campaigning on keeping costs down for families and businesses as the PC government works to widen Highway 401 and build a new Prince Edward County Memorial Hospital.
"I understand the struggles many families like mine face," he said.
Smith had been education minister for 10 weeks, following a three-year stint as minister of energy and time in other cabinet portfolios, before he unexpectedly quit as MPP 10 days ago to take an unspecified job in the private sector.
CAPTION: Todd Smith speaks at Queen's Park in Toronto on June 20, 2019. Smith resigned from cabinet to accept a job in the private sector.
