Document 135 of 500

Trudeau government to match U.S. tariffs on Chinese EVs

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/trudeau-government-match-u-s-tariffs-on-chinese/docview/3097156592/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Trudeau+government+to+match+U.S.+tariffs+on+Chinese+EVs&amp;title=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-08-26&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: HALIFAX-Canada is following the U.S. lead and slapping steep new tariffs on Chinese-made electric vehicles and on heavily subsidized Chinese steel and aluminum imports, Prime Minister Justin Trudeau said Monday - move that drew swift condemnation by Chinese officials.
As the Star first reported, Canada's tariffs on Chinese EVs will match the U.S. level, which quadrupled in May, at 100 per cent in an attempt to protect domestic EV carmakers from a rising tide of cheap imports. Canada's EV tariffs will take effect Oct. 1.
In addition, Ottawa will match the U.S. with a 25-per-cent tariff on Chinese-made steel and aluminum imports, to take effect Oct. 15.
The Liberal government is now weighing whether to impose additional punitive surtaxes on a broad range of other Chinese-made goods like semiconductors, solar cells, batteries and battery parts and critical minerals, as Washington has.
Since many auto and battery makers use a lot of Chinese inputs, it's a more complicated step to take without knowing the collateral damage that could be caused for domestic producers, and so the Trudeau government kicked off another intense 30-day consultation period.
The Chinese embassy in Ottawa denounced Canada's moves in a statement to Bloomberg, condemning them as an act of trade protectionism that disregards global free trade rules.
It warned the tariffs "will undermine the normal economic and trade co-operation between China and Canada, harm the interests of Canadian consumers and enterprises, and be detrimental to Canada's green transformation and global efforts to combat climate change." It suggested there would be retaliation if Canada didn't reverse course: "China will take all necessary measures to safeguard the legitimate rights and interests of Chinese enterprises," the statement said.
China blocked Canadian exports of canola, pork and beef during a dispute with Canada over the 2018 arrest of a Chinese Huawei executive.
When asked if he is concerned about Chinese government retaliatory action on Canadian exports to China, Trudeau dodged a direct answer. China's government is not only subsidizing its automakers, he said, it is "not playing by the same rules as other countries" when it comes to labour and environmental standards.
"What is important about this is we're doing it in alignment and in parallel with other economies around the world that recognize that this is a challenge that we are all facing, and unless we want to get in a race to the bottom, we have to stand up" to China's unfair trade practices, the prime minister continued.
The new 100-per-cent EV tariff applies to electric and certain hybrid passenger automobiles, trucks, buses, and delivery vans, the government said, and it comes on top of a 6.1 per cent tariff that Canada currently applies to EVs produced in China and imported into Canada.
Ottawa will also limit rebates for EV purchases to cars coming from countries that have free trade deals with Canada - in other words, not China.
Officials who briefed reporters said the main supplier of Chinese-made EVs in Canada - imports that are valued at about $2.2billion - is Tesla, which produces EVs in Shanghai.
However, they said Tesla has four other manufacturing facilities that supply the American market and it could shift production for the Canadian market to any of those.
Canada has invested billions of taxpayer dollars to draw investments by international automakers to build EV battery plants and an industrial supply chain here, yet the Liberal government says there is a big difference in what it is doing and what China does.
"The reality is, China has an intentional state directed policy of overcapacity and oversupply designed to cripple our own industries," said Deputy Prime Minister and Finance Minister Chrystia Freeland. "Talk to the steel sector, talk to the aluminum sector, to understand how effective that has already been, and we simply will not allow that to happen to our EV sector, which is showing such promise and in which we have invested so effectively."
The announcement comes a day after a top adviser to U.S. President Joe Biden urged Canada to join the Americans and EU in imposing penalties on cheap Chinese state-subsidized products flooding global markets.
U.S. national security adviser Jake Sullivan said Chinese EVs are not just an economic threat but also a national security concern.
"Canada will make its own determinations, but the U.S. does believe that a united front, a co-ordinated approach on these issues benefits all of us," Sullivan said.
Sullivan spoke to reporters Sunday evening in Nova Scotia where he met with Trudeau and his cabinet - a surprise visit at the Liberal retreat - and ahead of a high-level visit Sullivan makes this week to Beijing.
Sullivan said the U.S. sees "two distinct challenges" connected with Chinese EVs: the "massive subsidies" that "have eliminated a level playing field," and concerns about "data security, with critical infrastructure, and with the underlying questions of national security associated with connected vehicles, electric vehicles."
Canadian officials agreed that with the development of sophisticated software used in those vehicles comes a higher risk of privacy and security breaches, one that Ottawa is now examining.
Sullivan underscored that the EU has also hiked tariffs on Chinese EVs and the G7 has made strong statements in support of the U.S. view.
The EU set its tariffs at a lower rate, up to 36 per cent, and Canadian officials said those were based directly on the rate of subsidization, whereas Canada's are punitive surtaxes intended to target not just China's state subsidy rate, but its poor labour and environmental standards as it seeks to dominate markets abroad, not just in EVs, but in steel and aluminum production.
Since 2018, China has sharply increased its steelmaking capacity - despite softening global demand - by more than 18 million metric tonnes, an amount that exceeds all of Canada's steel production capacity, and has now become the world's largest steel producer. It is likewise jacking up aluminum production, Canadian officials suggested.
Sullivan is now en route to Beijing to meet with China's foreign affairs minister Wang Yi, one of a series of high-level meetings that the White House said are key to managing an "intensely competitive relationship" and keep it from "veering into conflict."
Conservative Leader Pierre Poilievre and Premier Doug Ford have both called on Ottawa to slap heavy surtaxes on Chinese EV exports.
On Monday, Poilievre's deputy leader Melissa Lantsman said Trudeau did not go as far as the Conservatives would, and commit to tariffs on semiconductors and other products.
Ford applauded the move.
"This is welcome news for Canadian workers that puts us onside with the U.S., our closest friend and trading partner. It's never been more important to do everything we can to strengthen Canada-U.S. supply chains," Ford said Monday on X.
Some environmentalists, however, said raising the price of EV imports only makes achieving Canada's climate goals more elusive.
"Unfortunately, Canada made a decision today that will result in fewer affordable electric vehicles for Canadians, less competition, and more climate pollution," said Joanna Kyriazis, of Clean Energy Canada.
Domestic automakers and auto parts manufacturers welcomed the move to align Canadian and American tariff policy.
Brian Kingston, head of the Canadian Vehicle Manufacturers' Association, said that with an upcoming review of the Canada-United States-Mexico Agreement in 2026 "there is simply too much at stake for the automotive industry and broader economy if Canada is misaligned."
Flavio Volpe, head of the Canadian Autoparts Manufacturers Association, posted on "X" his members "feel vindicated and motivated" by the government's move, after a yearlong lobbying effort to highlight "the importance of standing up to the Chinese government's challenge to Western manufacturing economies."
The Liberal cabinet is examining ways to advance the government's key priorities for the fall and the months ahead, particularly housing and economic supports for the middle class, as well as examining how to shore up political support in Atlantic Canada.
Ministers have heard briefings by economic and housing policy experts, current and former mayors, along with the current and former Canadian ambassadors in Washington, and a former Trump Administration trade official.
With a file from Rob Ferguson, Queen's Park bureau.
CAPTION: Prime Minister Justin Trudeau arrives to the Sunday evening cabinet retreat dinner at the Halifax Convention Centre on Sunday.

Document 136 of 500

Ontario parties spend summer preparing for the possibility of an early election

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/ontario-parties-spend-summer-preparing/docview/3096852726/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Ontario+parties+spend+summer+preparing+for+the+possibility+of+an+early+election&amp;title=The+Spectator&amp;issn=11899417&amp;date=2024-08-26&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.1&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Spectator&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: TORONTO Ontario's major political parties have been spending the summer nominating candidates, running "campaign schools," and canvassing after remarks from the premier this spring fuelled speculation he will call an early election.
The province's next fixed election date isn't until June 2026, but Doug Ford set off alarm bells in political circles this past May when he repeatedly refused to commit to that timeline during a news conference, saying only that he wants to make sure he fulfils his agenda and keeps the promises his majority Progressive Conservative government has made.
Liberal co-campaign directors Genevieve Tomney and Chad Walsh - officially named to their positions soon - looked at each other and said, "now it's time to go," Tomney said in an interview.
"We've kind of been treating this as a campaign summer," she said.
"What I would say about campaigns is that I sometimes kind of compare it to an iceberg, where so much of the work that's being done is happening below the surface, but we're gearing up for a pretty big end of summer into the fall."
Some of that below-the-surface work entails putting calls out to potential campaign staff and volunteers. Gone are the majority Liberal government days when the party had a campaign army at the ready - most of them have moved into different roles and into the private sector.
"Pulling all of those people back together again is kind of like a huge volunteer-slash-HR effort as well," Tomney said.
Some of the public-facing election preparations include nominating candidates. The Liberals are starting by nominating their incumbents, but that will only fill nine out of 124 ridings, so the task ahead is daunting. It's unclear how much time they will have - elections tend to happen in the fall or spring, and Tomney said the Liberals are focusing on three main scenarios: fall 2024, spring 2025 and the as-scheduled spring 2026.
Ford told Ottawa radio station CFRA that Ontario would "100 per cent" not have an election this summer or fall.
But parties like the NDP are not banking on that.
"We're going to prepare as such," Leader Marit Stiles said in an interview. "If it's not this fall and it's, say, next spring we will be just even more ready."
Laryssa Waler, a former director of communications for Ford and founder of Walman Strategies, said a spring 2025 election seems more likely than the fall.
"You saw how the last election got called, there was essentially a referendum on the budget that they presented and they won more seats," she said in an interview. "So I wouldn't be surprised if they did something similar, because it worked so well last time."
The NDP was starting some early election campaign planning this year, but Ford's comments "kicked it up a notch," Stiles said. Summers are always a good time for members of provincial parliament to connect with their constituents, and this year the politicians and volunteers are out knocking on doors, Stiles said.
"Our MPPs are on the road and some of them have been out across the province and will continue to be to help engage with our activists, connect with potential voters, help us to identify candidates, which of course is a big push right now.
"In a lot of ridings, where we don't have incumbents and we don't even have a candidate yet (organizers) are canvassing this summer. They're trying to cover as much of the riding as they can. Many of them are trying to get through the entire riding ... before the fall."
The NDP are also running campaign schools this summer to help teach people in local riding associations how to run campaigns, so the party is ready on the ground locally, not just centrally, Stiles said.
Both opposition parties are also working on some ads to help introduce their leaders to voters, since it will be the first provincial general election each of them fight.
The Green Party of Ontario, which now has two seats in the legislature, has also been busy over the past couple of months.
"We've spent the summer assembling localized mobilizing teams and boosting our ground game capacity in target ridings across the province, with our leader and deputy leaders participating in regular riding canvasses," executive director Jaymini Bhikha wrote in a statement. "We are also preparing to open nominations in the very near future."
The Progressive Conservatives currently hold 78 seats, so they have a significant advantage when it comes to getting 124 candidates in place for an election.
The party is also ahead in polling and is raising more money than the others, Waler said, which are big factors in considering election readiness. As well, they are constantly polling and conducting research, she said.
Tory caucus members have been out door knocking too this summer, but Ford encourages them to do so year-round, no matter the time in the election cycle, Waler said.
The Canadian Press
CAPTION: The province's next fixed election date isn't until June 2026, butDoug Ford set off alarm bells in political circles this past May when he repeatedly refused to commit to that timeline.The Canadian Press

Alcohol at corner stores is coming, is Ontario prepared for the health cost of convenience?

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/alcohol-at-corner-stores-is-coming-ontario/docview/3096660947/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Alcohol+at+corner+stores+is+coming%2C+is+Ontario+prepared+for+the+health+cost+of+convenience%3F&amp;title=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-08-25&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: It's where you go when you run out of milk or need a loaf of bread. You turn to its aisles of potato chips, chocolate bars and candy to satisfy a craving. Often, you can find an OK cup of coffee to go with a weekly splurge on lottery tickets.
And early next month, your neighbourhood convenience store will be able to sell you beer, wine and an assortment of ready-to-drink cocktails.
For many Ontarians, including perhaps yourself, the change coming on Sept. 5 will be little more than a convenience - making a routine purchase less of a hassle. But behind the scenes, health professionals and experts are watching with bated breath to see how Ontario's push to allow booze in thousands of new stores will play out across the province.
With emerging evidence linking alcohol to a growing list of costly outcomes, experts warn the biggest increase in alcohol availability in Ontario in nearly 100 years will lead to a cascade of health and social harms, including an increase in alcohol-related deaths.
The question is: Is Ontario prepared?
"Harm will increase in Ontario. That is straightforward," said Dr. Daniel Myran, who has studied the costs of previous changes to alcohol sales, including Ontario's last, when some grocery stores started selling beer and wine.
A chorus of experts is now calling on the province to have a plan: If we're going to modernize alcohol sales to be more in line with our neighbours, it's best to prepare ahead of time to help minimize the hangover.
"We need to have some safety measures put in place," said Ian Culbert, executive director of the Canadian Public Health Association, "so that it's not the wild, wild west when it comes to alcohol sales in this province."
What you may not know about the risks of alcoholFor decades, you may have heard that the occasional beer or glass of wine is harmless, even healthy for you. Shared so broadly, the notion has become widely accepted.
But recent research is proving this idea wrong.
One of the main messages in Canada's Guidance on Alcohol and Health is that no amount of alcohol is good for your health.
The updated guidelines, released in 2023 by the Canadian Centre on Substance Use and Addiction, caught Canadians by surprise after drastically reducing the threshold for "low-risk" drinking.
Previous advice recommended no more than 10 drinks a week for women and 15 for men, with anything above that jeopardizing long-term health.
The new guidance, funded by Health Canada and based on emerging evidence on how alcohol harms health, set low-risk drinking at just one to two drinks per week; moderate risk is defined as three to six standard drinks a week, with each additional weekly drink increasing the alcohol-related consequences.
Adam Sherk, senior scientist and special policy adviser at the Canadian Centre on Substance Use and Addiction, said the aim of the 2023 advice is to make clear that "any reduction that we can do in our drinking will improve our health."
Drinking less reduces the chance of injury but also lowers the risk of a host of health problems, such as heart disease and stroke, liver disease and at least seven types of cancer, including breast and colon cancer.
According to the updated guidelines, heavy drinking - defined as 3.5 drinks a day, or more - can double or even triple the risk of mouth and throat cancers, while those who consume 14 or more drinks a week face a 20 per cent increased risk of colorectal cancer.
And despite red wine's long-held halo, the guidance states that all types of alcohol come with health costs.
It's important to note that when experts discuss "harm," they don't mean that a typical low-to-moderate drinker can expect to face all of the most severe health consequences. People tend to connect alcohol consumption with dire outcomes, such as alcohol-use disorder, liver cirrhosis and impaired driving, said Myran, a family doctor and public health physician and Canada Research Chair in social accountability at the University of Ottawa.
But, he said, there needs to be a greater awareness that alcohol is linked to more than 200 different health conditions - and that it's not just heavy drinkers who will see an impact on their health.
The cost of convenienceResearch has shown that making alcohol easier to access leads to higher rates of consumption, which in turn leads to an increase in alcohol-related harms.
With Ontario expanding alcohol sales to an additional 8,500 stores - up from about 2,935 outlets - experts say they are bracing for these negative outcomes.
"People are going to drink more and there's going to be more emergency room visits, deaths, chronic health conditions and acute health problems and social problems," said Myran.
Already, alcohol leads to some 18,000 deaths a year in Canada. In Ontario alone, research shows that in 2020 alcohol was estimated to cause 6,201 deaths and led to about 47,500 hospital admissions and 258,700 emergency department visits.
And the burden on the health system comes with a stark economic cost, said Myran, an investigator at the Bruyère Research Institute at The Ottawa Hospital.
"For every standard drink - every bottle of beer, every glass of wine - that's sold in Canada, it costs the taxpayer 34 cents, when you add up the health and social problems from it."
He notes the 2015 move by the former Liberal government to allow grocery stores to sell beer - also billed as a way to deliver consumer choice and convenience - offers evidence of an increase in associated harms.
A 2019 study that he conducted found that in the two years following the policy change, emergency department visits due to alcohol increased by six per cent more in regions with grocery stores that sold beer and wine compared to regions that didn't introduce alcohol in their grocery stores.
While it's not clear how much the levels of drinking in each province are the effects of more or less restrictive alcohol laws, Ontario has long bought less alcohol per person than the national average.
According to Statistics Canada's most recent data from 2022, Ontario also had the lowest rate of self-reported heavy drinking of any province. And in 2023, the province also had by far the lowest police-reported rates of alcohol-impaired driving.
In the coming months, researchers will be watching to see how much these trends increase.
"I don't think that we're going to see a 20 per cent increase in how much people drink or a 20 per cent increase in health harms due to alcohol," Myran said. "But we might see something on the order of a three, four or five per cent increase. And that is concerning because there is so much harm that already occurs from alcohol."
What should Ontario be doing?As the days tick closer to booze being sold in corner stores, calls from federal and provincial health organizations have been growing stronger for Ontario to address the health and safety concerns from expanded alcohol sales.
In May, the Canadian Public Health Association (CPHA) "condemned" Ontario's expansion of alcohol into convenience stores, more supermarkets and big-box stores, calling the move "alarming in light of the evidence" and urging the province to reconsider.
But with the change weeks away and the Ford government firm in its plans, Culbert of the CPHA said its priority now is to press for "guard rails" as alcohol is rolled out to thousands of stores in the province.
A coalition of 10 health organizations, including the Canadian Cancer Society, the Ontario Public Health Association and Toronto's Centre for Addiction and Mental Health, have been pushing for Ontario to adopt a comprehensive alcohol strategy.
The group penned an open letter to Premier Doug Ford in May, pointing out that the province "does not have a co-ordinated action plan for reducing harms from alcohol use." The letter also noted that "alcohol-related harms cost Ontario more than $7 billion annually, which is greater than the costs of harms from tobacco and opioids combined."
Earlier this month, the coalition sent a followup letter to the Premier's Office that laid out a 10-point plan. Among its recommendations are to: Allow municipalities to opt out of the expansion; Prohibit stores within 150 metres of a school or daycare from selling alcohol; Prohibit convenience stores at gas stations from selling alcohol; And prohibit convenience stores and supermarkets from cross-promoting alcohol with food or other products.
"We're not asking for the moon," said Culbert of the health care coalition, of which the CPHA is a member. "We're asking for straightforward, logical, common-sense initiatives to help reduce the harms associated with the expansion of alcohol sales."
In a statement to the Star, a spokesperson for Health Minister Sylvia Jones said the province continues "to believe that the people of Ontario should be treated like adults," and that the government "has a clear mandate to give them the same choice and convenience that Canadians in every other province have when it comes to buying beer and wine."
Alexandra Adamo said the government is investing an additional $10 million "to support social responsibility and public health efforts to ensure alcohol continues to be sold and consumed safely in the expanded marketplace." She said that funding is in addition to the $396 million announced in this year's budget for mental health and addiction services.
Full steam aheadWarnings about alcohol's growing harms have also come from inside the Ontario government.
In his 2023 Annual Report, Ontario's Chief Medical Officer of Health Dr. Kieran Moore noted the COVID-19 pandemic led to "a significant increase in alcohol sales and alcohol toxicity deaths" - just one of the "disturbing trends" that also include "a growing number of youths in grades 7 to 12 who reported using alcohol, cannabis, and vaping products more frequently."
Among other things - including a call for a comprehensive alcohol strategy - Moore recommended strengthening alcohol policies through pricing and taxation, limiting the number of retail outlets that sell alcohol and exploring the possibility of raising the drinking age from 19 to 21.
Dr. Leslie Buckley, Chief of Addictions at the Centre for Addiction and Mental Health (CAMH), noted that other jurisdictions, including Scotland, Lithuaniaand the Nordic countries, are looking for ways to reduce alcohol availability in their countries to curb consumption. With Ontario pursuing a different course, Buckley said the province needs to have a greater focus on education, prevention and treatment to reduce alcohol-related harms.
"There's a lot of potential gain in providing better treatments, and getting treatment to people earlier."
Asked about Moore's position, Adamo said while the Health Ministry appreciates his viewpoint, his position would "set Ontario apart from the rest of the country."
How to sell booze betterAlcohol is a psychoactive substance that affects the way people think and behave. This means it shouldn't be marketed and sold like most other consumer goods, said Sherk.
Because alcohol is potentially addicting and dependency-inducing, moves that lead to more drinking will have a "reinforcing effect or feedback loop," he said. "Lower prices increase sales, which increases alcohol use that pulls more people into dependency, which further increases sales because people are dependent or addicted to alcohol itself."
Sherk said it's critical that Ontario maintain - or even modestly increase - the minimum price of alcohol in the province to offset the upcoming expansion in sales. The cheapest alcohol is the most harmful because it attracts new drinkers and continues to draw in those already addicted to alcohol, he explained.
"Those cheapest products fly off the shelves, and they cause a lot of harm. We know the more expensive we can make those cheapest products - that's really important for public health."
So far, the province has said it will continue to impose minimum price restrictions, though private retailers will be able to set their own prices - which the province says will benefit consumers.
Another key way to lower harms is to require warning labels on individual bottles and cans, said Erin Hobin, a senior scientist in the department of health promotion, chronic disease and injury prevention at Public Health Ontario.
She said a recent study - the most comprehensive review of its kind, of which she was the senior author - showed that such labels reduce alcohol consumption, in part because of their wide reach.
"They essentially reach everyone who handles the container, and they expose those who are handling the container repeatedly to key health information," she said.
While Canada is a world leader in designing and mandating strong warning labels on cigarette packages, and most recently cannabis packages, alcohol containers are largely exempt from these labelling requirements, Hobin said.
Container labels can also help people learn about alcohol's health risks, especially alcohol's links to cancer, she said, adding this is important because evidence suggests that this makes people more likely to support stronger alcohol policies, including those that have to do with pricing.
The people most at riskFor people who don't have a problem with alcohol and aren't one of the unlucky few who may experience the cancer risk, popping into a corner store to grab a bottle of wine for dinner may be nothing more than a time-saver.
But the impacts are much larger, and carry more weight, for those who already experience problems from alcohol - or importantly, people that will do so in the future - Sherk said, noting how booze will now be much more visible in their lives.
He noted that about one-quarter of all people who drink alcohol at any point will become dependent at some level in their lives.
"It's not a benign product."
CAPTION: Dr. Daniel Myran, who studied the increase in alcohol consumption following Ontario's last move to liberalize alcohol sales.Eggs. Milk. Beer. Consumer choice and convenience are the powerful keywords in the government messaging behind Ontario's move.Examples of warning labels used ingovernment-run liquor stores in Whitehorse during a 2017-18 study.The view of beer shelves in a typical Toronto grocery store. For some, the sight is a pure convenience. To others, it may be a reminder of addiction.Ontario Premier Doug Ford announces the change to allow booze sales in corner stores at a Toronto Circle K on Dec. 14, 2023.

People who use drugs are human beings who deserve the best possible care

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/people-who-use-drugs-are-human-beings-deserve/docview/3096557303/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=People+who+use+drugs+are+human+beings+who+deserve+the+best+possible+care&amp;title=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-08-24&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: I write this with a heavy heart, knowing that my words may not fully capture the devastation that comes with the announcement of supervised consumption site closures. But I can't stay silent. This is not just a policy decision; this is a death sentence.
Every day, people who use drugs walk through the doors of supervised consumption sites, not just for a place to use, but for a lifeline. These spaces represent more than just harm reduction - they are beacons of hope, where judgment is left at the door and dignity is restored.
The people who come to these sites are not statistics; they are our neighbours, our friends, our family members. They are human beings who deserve the best possible care, including access to safer drugs and compassionate treatment.
When we talk about closing these sites, what we're really talking about is turning our backs on some of the most vulnerable members of our community. We are condemning them to a world where they are left to fend for themselves in alleys, parks and other unsafe places. We are forcing them to make choices between bad and worse, all while the stigma of their circumstances continues to suffocate them.
It breaks my heart to think of the lives that will be lost because of this decision. And yes, make no mistake - lives will be lost. The closure of these sites is not just a logistical issue; it is a moral failing. We have the power to save lives, to offer dignity and care, and yet, we are choosing not to.
But it's not too late to change the course we are on. We need to stand up and say, "No more." We need to demand that our leaders prioritize human lives over politics. We need to fight for the right of every person, no matter their circumstance, to be treated with the humanity they deserve.
That's why I'm asking you to take action now. We've started a petition to keep these life-saving consumption sites open. By signing, you're not just adding your name to a list - you're standing up for compassion, for dignity and for the right of every person to be treated with respect and care.
People who use drugs are not the problem - our society's lack of compassion and understanding is. We must do better. We owe it to them, to ourselves, and to the future we want to create.
To sign the "Save Our Safe Consumption Sites" petition, visit https://chng.it/JXpgnS5rzX.
CAPTION: When we talk about closing consumption and treatments sites, what we're really talking about is turning our backs on some of the most vulnerable members of our community. We are condemning them to a world where they are left to fend for themselves in alleys, parks, and other unsafe places, Jennifer Bulbrook writes.Ontario is set to ban consumption and treatment services sites within 200 metres of schools and child-care centres, which will lead to the closure of 10 facilities. Sylvia Jones, deputy premier and minister of health, and Premier Doug Ford need to prioritize human lives over politics, Jennifer Bulbrook writes.
CREDIT: Jennifer Bulbrook

Aug. 24: New heritage district welcome, cyber attack questions linger and other letters to the editor

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/aug-24-new-heritage-district-welcome-cyber-attack/docview/3096557278/se-2?accountid=15090

Full text: New heritage district welcome
Re: Newly proposed Dundas heritage district, Aug. 22
This is a really good news story for our local heritage community. My home will be a "contributing" property for the new Melville Street Heritage Conservation District and I couldn't be more pleased.
Sincerest thanks go to the city's heritage department for working hard to put this all together.
I attended the public information event at Dundas Museum and Archives and the atmosphere seemed overwhelmingly positive for establishing a new heritage district in Dundas. That being said, I wish the article had input from the stakeholders and homeowners impacted by this process, so it would be nice to hear the "whole story" from both sides; those for and those against.
Perhaps a followup article could discuss the potential economic benefits and the effect this will have on future renovations and dispel the myths that a new heritage district will paralyze any future home improvements.
I truly believe those folks lucky enough to reside in heritage neighbourhoods, like the one in question, are stewards of our heritage, and while modern advantages can be realized, a major goal should be the conservation of our built heritage for the next homeowner - and posterity.
Stan Nowak, Dundas
Community station already exists
Re: The future of Hamilton's airwaves: A call for community radio, Aug. 22
Hamilton has had a community radio station, CFMU 93.3 FM, for 46 years. It is one of the oldest in Canada. Commercial free, not for profit and volunteer driven. What is really needed is more support for this treasured resource.
Robert Kendrick, Hamilton
Breathe life back into CHML
I'll put this out there. I have never cared for MP Matthew Green, his grandstanding politics, that carding incident and his embarrassing support for MPP Sarah Jama. However, his commentary in The Spec about CHML makes a lot of sense.
If his innovative and exciting idea is possible, I'd love to see him direct his energy toward a worthy cause like bringing back our beloved "Hometown Radio."
Wray Brown, Dundas
Cyber attack questions linger
Re: Hamilton cyberattack: 61 per cent of affected applications restored, Aug. 22
As a taxpayer I would like to know how much of our tax dollars (minus "cyber insurance") is being spent on rebuilding our IT network. We are told $7.4 million has been spent to date, but why is "our" insurance coverage not been disclosed? Is it because it's a pittance in relation to the cost of rebuilding? Or, the city didn't have sufficient coverage to lessen the burden on the taxpayers? So many unanswered questions.
Catherine Castellan, Hannon
A moving story of resilience
Re: Hamilton woman named 2024 Eureka Fellow, Aug. 22
Joelle Kabisoso, way to go! I'm so sorry you went through what you did and, as a youth, then to have the officials treat it like it was your fault. You turned that experience around and used it to help others and hopefully yourself. You should definitely be very proud.
Robert Coxe, Hamilton
You have to be alive to recover
Re: Doug Ford says safe injection sites are a 'failed policy' that harms communities and people with addictions, Aug. 21
Premier Doug Ford is closing safe consumption sites and refusing to fund them because Health Minister Sylvia Jones thinks keeping people alive is enabling. Well, these misguided people don't realize an addict has to survive in order to go into recovery.
This move will kill many and people will find discarded syringes everywhere.
If the opioid crisis hasn't touched you yet, be patient. It will.
Russ Young, Fort Erie
Trip to the ER was enlightening
I would like to extend a heartfelt thank you to the staff who were working in the emergency room (ER) at St. Joseph's hospital on Monday and early Tuesday morning. I took my wife to the ER Monday afternoon and although it was extremely busy, everyone we interacted with was respectful and compassionate. They are truly exceptional people who treat their "patients with patience."
We were in the ER for 12 hours and I had lots of time to observe and reflect. The population of Hamilton is estimated to increase by another 236,000 people by 2051. There is no way our existing infrastructure can support a population growth of that magnitude. If our infrastructure changes don't keep pace with the population increases, Hamilton will become just another big city that no one will want to live in or even visit. Our existing hospitals are extremely short-staffed now in large part because the Ford government's wage-suppression legislation (Bill 124) drove medical personnel to leave our hospitals to look for greener pastures elsewhere or to retire.
I hope someone, somewhere is thinking ahead and planning what infrastructure changes will have to be made in order to handle the predicted population explosion, but I doubt such planning is being done at any level of government. Perhaps Hamilton should put the brakes on building condos on every piece of available land in the city. These condos do nothing to ease the homeless problem. Bigger isn't always better.
Al Knapp, Hamilton
NDP needs to act on its convictions
NDP Leader Jagmeet Singh accused the federal government of undermining workers. Singh stated: "The Liberals' actions are cowardly, anti-worker and proof that they will always cave to corporate greed; and Canadians will always pay for it."
If Singh doesn't immediately move to initiate a vote a non-confidence in the Trudeau government he will absolutely have zero respect with workers, NDP supporters and Canadians. If there is any delay in taking this action when the House is sitting again, every NDP member should rise up and demand Singh's resignation as leader of the NDP.
Chris Robertson, Stony Plain, Alta.
CAPTION: The home at 223 Park St. W. is considered a contributing property within the boundaries of the new Melville Street Heritage Conservation District extending west from the existing Cross-Melville Heritage Conservation District, which the former town of Dundas created in 1990. There are 250 homes considered to be "contributing properties" between Sydenham, Brock, Park and Victoria streets. All were built between 1880 and 1930.

Document 140 of 500

Letters

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/letters/docview/3096520219/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=unknown&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Letters&amp;title=The+Spectator&amp;issn=11899417&amp;date=2024-08-24&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.22&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Spectator&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: New heritage district welcome
Re: Newly proposed Dundas heritage district,
Aug. 22
This is a really good news story for our local heritage community. My home will be a "contributing" property for the new Melville Street Heritage Conservation District and I couldn't be more pleased.
Sincerest thanks go to the city's heritage department for working hard to put this all together.
I attended the public information event at Dundas Museum and Archives and the atmosphere seemed overwhelmingly positive for establishing a new heritage district in Dundas.
That being said, I wish the article had input from the stakeholders and homeowners impacted by this process, so it would be nice to hear the "whole story" from both sides; those for and those against.
Perhaps a followup article could discuss the potential economic benefits and the effect this will have on future renovations and dispel the myths that a new heritage district will paralyze any future home improvements.
I truly believe those folks lucky enough to reside in heritage neighbourhoods, like the one in question, are stewards of our heritage, and while modern advantages can be realized, a major goal should be the conservation of our built heritage for the next homeowner - and posterity.
Stan Nowak, Dundas
Community radio station exists
Re: The future of Hamilton's airwaves: A call for community radio, Aug. 22
Hamilton has had a community radio station, CFMU 93.3 FM, for 46 years. It is one of the oldest in Canada. Commercial-free, not for profit and volunteer driven.
What is really needed is more support for this treasured resource.
Robert Kendrick, Hamilton
Breathe life back into CHML
I'll put this out there. I have never cared for MP Matthew Green, his grandstanding politics, that carding incident and his embarrassing support for MPP Sarah Jama. However, his commentary in The Spec about CHML makes a lot of sense.
If his innovative and exciting idea is possible, I'd love to see him direct his energy toward a worthy cause like bringing back our beloved "Hometown Radio."
Wray Brown, Dundas
Cyber attack questions linger
Re: Hamilton cyberattack: 61 per cent of affected applications restored, Aug. 22
As a taxpayer I would like to know how much of our tax dollars (minus "cyber insurance") is being spent on rebuilding our IT network. We are told $7.4 million has been spent to date, but why is "our" insurance coverage not been disclosed? Is it because it's a pittance in relation to the cost of rebuilding? Or, the city didn't have sufficient coverage to lessen the burden on the taxpayers? So many unanswered questions.
Catherine Castellan, Hannon
You have to be alive to recover
Re: Doug Ford says safe injection sites are a 'failed policy' that harms communities and people with addictions, Aug. 21
Premier Doug Ford is closing safe consumption sites and refusing to fund them because Health Minister Sylvia Jones thinks keeping people alive is enabling. Well, these misguided people don't realize an addict has to survive in order to go into recovery.
This move will kill many and people will find discarded syringes everywhere. If the opioid crisis hasn't touched you yet, be patient. It will.
Russ Young, Fort Erie
Trip to the ER was enlightening
I would like to extend a heartfelt thank you to the staff who were working in the emergency room (ER) at St. Joseph's hospital on Monday and early Tuesday morning. I took my wife to the ER Monday afternoon and although it was extremely busy, everyone we interacted with was respectful and compassionate. They are truly exceptional people who treat their "patients with patience."
We were in the ER for 12 hours and I had lots of time to observe and reflect. The population of Hamilton is estimated to increase by another 236,000 people by 2051.
There is no way our existing infrastructure can support a population growth of that magnitude. If our infrastructure changes don't keep pace with the population increases, Hamilton will become just another big city that no one will want to live in or even visit. Our existing hospitals are extremely short-staffed now in large part because the Ford government's wage-suppression legislation (Bill 124) drove medical personnel to leave our hospitals to look for greener pastures elsewhere or to retire.
I hope someone, somewhere is thinking ahead and planning what infrastructure changes will have to be made in order to handle the predicted population explosion, but I doubt such planning is being done at any level of government. Perhaps Hamilton should put the brakes on building condos on every piece of available land in the city. These condos do nothing to ease the homeless problem. Bigger isn't always better.
Al Knapp, Hamilton

Document 141 of 500

Shipyard gets piece of $10M for skills training: Ontario Shipyards gets a boost from fund that cuts risk for employers

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/shipyard-gets-piece-10m-skills-training/docview/3096520054/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Shipyard+gets+piece+of+%2410M+for+skills+training%3A+Ontario+Shipyards+gets+a+boost+from+fund+that+cuts+risk+for+employers&amp;title=The+Spectator&amp;issn=11899417&amp;date=2024-08-24&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.5&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Spectator&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Ontario Shipyards is getting a $10-million skills training boost for new and current employees at its trio of locations in St. Catharines, Hamilton and Thunder Bay.
Premier Doug Ford made the announcement Wednesday in St. Catharines at the company's Port Weller facility with employees, city officials and business people on hand.
"This investment will help reskill and upskill existing workers for careers in the millwright, welding, electrical and painting trades and many more," said Ford, accompanied by Labour, Immigration, Training and Skills Development Minister David Piccini and Niagara West MPP Sam Oosterhoff.
"It will give new workers hands-on experience in shipbuilding and ship repair, and it will help recruit and retain the next generation of workers and leaders in our shipbuilding industry."
The $10 million is being provided through the province's Skills Development Fund training stream.
Ontario Shipyards, which changed its name from Heddle earlier this year, has about 250 employees across the three sites, with 130 at the St. Catharines shipyard.
The company previously received $3.7 million from the Skills Development Fund in July 2023 for training and modernization in Hamilton and Port Weller.
Shaun Padulo, president and chief executive officer of Ontario Shipyards, said the Ford government has played a pivotal role in the revitalization of the shipyards, allowing the company to advance multiple phases of the Ontario Shipyard Modernization Project.
"On a personal note, the Skills Development Fund has been crucial during times when work was scarce, allowing us to keep our workforce intact through various training initiatives," he said.
"This support has directly benefited over 250 families, ensuring their stability, and many of those families are represented by the fine folks right behind me here."
Ted Kirkpatrick, director of Ontario Shipyards business development and government relations, said afterwards the funds really are helping to bring shipbuilding back to Ontario, explaining the impact of having to train a workforce.
The company signed a long-term lease at the Port Weller dry docks in 2017 when the site was dormant and had been through years of instability.
"At one point when we took these facilities over, we took them over out of bankruptcy. So it's a tremendous amount of not just capital in terms of what you need to build and to buy equipment, but to train the people that are no longer there," he said.
"Today's announcement, it's hugely important to rebuilding this industry in Ontario."
Kirkpatrick said the fund will be used to train current employees who want to increase their skills, as well as for people who may have no experience in shipbuilding to bring them into apprenticeship programs through the various trades at the shipyard.
"We employ a vast array of tradespeople. And that's why this money is so effective - we can deploy that across all layers of skills."
Jordan Sherlock, executive director of Niagara Industrial Association, was at the announcement and said afterwards the government's fund takes the risk out of employer-led training.
"It makes it a lot easier for large and small businesses to train and bring in employees that they need to do what they have to do without having to incur the cost and the risk," he said.
"There's a lot of risk associated with workforce development today so de-risking that is is a huge advantage for our industrial folks."
Mayor Mat Siscoe told people gathered that shipbuilding and ship repair have played a vital role in St. Catharines for generations and has become a cornerstone of the local economy and a key contributor to the broader national economic landscape. He said he was excited to see another investment in "upskilling" workers in St. Catharines and the Niagara region.
"We know that our industrial base is capable of meeting the demands of the 21st century with the right training opportunities," he said during the announcement.
"The provincial government's investment is a welcome step for the workers of our community, and it's a clear demonstration of their dedication to empowering individuals to thrive in the modern workforce by providing them with the necessary tools and the knowledge that they need to succeed."
The province launched its Skills Development Fund in 2021.
Applications are open for the next round of funding from the SDF training stream.
CAPTION: Doug Ford, with St. Catharines Mayor Mat Siscoe, meets with workers at Ontario Shipyards' Port Weller facility while announcing the Ontario government is investing $10 million to train and upskill workers in the shipbuilding and repair sector.Bob Tymczyszyn The St. Catharines Standard

CREDIT: Karena Walter Reporter

Document 142 of 500

CTS decision is a death sentence disguised as policy

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/cts-decision-is-death-sentence-disguised-as/docview/3096520015/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=unknown&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=CTS+decision+is+a+death+sentence+disguised+as+policy&amp;title=The+Spectator&amp;issn=11899417&amp;date=2024-08-24&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.22&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Spectator&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: I write this with a heavy heart, knowing that my words may not fully capture the devastation that comes with the announcement of supervised consumption site closures. But I can't stay silent. This is not just a policy decision; this is a death sentence.
Every day, people who use drugs walk through the doors of supervised consumption and treatment sites, not just for a place to use, but for a lifeline. These spaces represent more than just harm reduction - they are beacons of hope, where judgment is left at the door and dignity is restored.
The people who come to these sites are not statistics; they are our neighbours, our friends, our family members. They are human beings who deserve the best possible care, including access to safer drugs and compassionate treatment.
When we talk about closing these sites, what we're really talking about is turning our backs on some of the most vulnerable members of our community. We are condemning them to a world where they are left to fend for themselves in alleys, parks and other unsafe places. We are forcing them to make choices between bad and worse, all while the stigma of their circumstances continues to suffocate them.
It breaks my heart to think of the lives that will be lost because of this decision. And yes, make no mistake - lives will be lost. The closure of these sites is not just a logistical issue; it is a moral failing. We have the power to save lives, to offer dignity and care, and yet, we are choosing not to.
But it's not too late to change the course we are on. We need to stand up and say, "No more." We need to demand that our leaders prioritize human lives over politics. We need to fight for the right of every person, no matter their circumstance, to be treated with the humanity they deserve.
That's why I'm asking you to take action now. We've started a petition to keep these life-saving consumption sites open. By signing, you're not just adding your name to a list - you're standing up for compassion, for dignity and for the right of every person to be treated with respect and care.
People who use drugs are not the problem - our society's lack of compassion and understanding is. We must do better. We owe it to them, to ourselves, and to the future we want to create.
To sign the "Save Our Safe Consumption Sites" petition, visit https://chng.it/JXpgnS5rzX.
Jennnifer Bulbrook is a women's community development worker at Positive Health Network
in Hamilton.
CAPTION: Sylvia Jones, deputy premier and minister of health, and Premier Doug Ford need to prioritize human lives over politics, Jennifer Bulbrook writes.The Canadian Press file photo

CREDIT: Jennifer Bulbrook;

Subject: Capital punishment

Document 143 of 500

Closure of Hamilton supervised drug-use site 'raises many concerns': operator

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/closure-hamilton-supervised-drug-use-site-raises/docview/3096255535/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Closure+of+Hamilton+supervised+drug-use+site+%27raises+many+concerns%27%3A+operator&amp;title=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-08-23&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: The operator of a supervised drug-consumption service facing closure in downtown Hamilton says it's "well positioned" to take on a new hub model the province plans to roll out to tackle homelessness and addiction.
Hamilton Urban Core Community Health Centre must close its consumption and treatment services (CTS) site at St. Paul's Presbyterian Church by next March under new provincial restrictions.
On Tuesday, Health Minister Sylvia Jones announced CTS sites within 200 metres of schools and child-care centres will be barred, forcing the closure of 10 of 17 in Ontario, including Urban Core's at the James South church.
"This news was unexpected and raises many concerns for the vulnerable clients accessing these vital services," Sandy Ezepue, Urban Core's executive director, said in a statement posted to social media.
The CTS model "saves lives and plays a vital role" in the community, Ezepue wrote, noting Urban Core plans to meet with the Health Ministry to "explore our options" for mental-health and addiction-support services.
"Despite this setback, we believe we are well positioned to apply" to operate a homelessness and addiction recovery treatment (HART) hub that Jones announced. "The model aligns closely with the work we are already doing, and we are committed to pursuing this opportunity."
Jones, who associated consumption sites with spikes in crime, said the $379-million plan to open 19 HART hubs in Ontario would mark a shift "toward a model that emphasizes recovery, treatment and community safety."
They are to combine a variety of services, including primary care, addiction and mental-health treatment, social services and feature "up to 375 supportive housing units."
"This is part of our commitment to avoid gaps in care," said Jones, noting CTR operators facing closure will be given priority to transition to the HART model.
A CTS site offers people who use drugs a place to consume under the supervision of trained staff to prevent overdose deaths, but it also connects them with other health and social services.
Mayor Andrea Horwath expressed concern over the loss of CTS sites but also optimism that a "robustly funded" HART hub network would help saves lives and "reduce the crises that are unfolding in our city."
The city plans to explore collaborations with Urban Core and other community partners on a HART hub, Horwath told The Spectator.
Hamilton and other municipalities have pressed Premier Doug Ford's Conservative government for more help in responding to a worsening homelessness crisis with encampments dotting urban landscapes.
Untreated mental illness and a volatile supply of street drugs are layered into the housing affordability crunch.
Urban Core opened Hamilton's first CTS in 2018 at its longtime Rebecca Street location.
Then, the agency relocated the service to St. Paul's, where it was supposed to operate temporarily while displaced from Rebecca Street due the property owner's redevelopment plans and until it could open its new home on Cannon Street East.
It's not clear why the CTS hasn't moved already to the health centre's new location.
Ezepue hasn't responded to The Spectator's requests for comment. "At this moment, we need time to fully understand the details of this decision before providing further comment," she wrote in her social-media post.
Health-care professionals, meanwhile, have warned closing CTS sites will increase the risk of overdoses and serious infections from unsanitary equipment.
They "save lives and provide evidence-based, life-saving services and comprehensive mental-health support; you can't help a dead person," Lhamo Dolkar, president of the Registered Nurses' Association of Ontario, said in news release.
Hamilton resident Storm Mallinson, who is familiar with Urban Core's CTS, called it "one of the most useful services" in the city.
"It's saved many, many lives," said Mallinson, a 32-year-old who is struggling with homelessness.
Closing Urban Core's CTS will result in "an even bigger epidemic" of overdose deaths and more infections caused by dirty equipment, he predicted.
Urban Core has been an "exemplary tenant," Rev. Mark Lewis, interim moderator at St. Paul's, said in an email praising staff's "tireless dedication and care."
"Innumerable peer-reviewed scientific studies indicate that safe injection sites are effective in helping people recover from opioid addiction and saving lives that would otherwise have been lost to overdoses," he wrote.
"Our government is ignoring scientific evidence and putting the public at greater risk under the pretext of protecting children. This action will put children and the general public at greater risk."
Teviah Moro is a reporter at The Hamilton Spectator.
CAPTION: Urban Core has been an"exemplary tenant," says Rev. Mark Lewis, interim moderator at St. Paul'sPresbyterian Church. He praised staff's "tireless dedication and care."A naloxone kit and a fact sheet at the International Overdose Awareness Day at St. Paul's Presbyterian Church on James Street South put on by Hamilton Urban Core Community Health Centre in 2022.

Document 144 of 500

Aug. 23: Railways should be designated an essential service, community radio station a great idea and other letters to the editor

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/aug-23-railways-should-be-designated-essential/docview/3096253080/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Aug.+23%3A+Railways+should+be+designated+an+essential+service%2C+community+radio+station+a+great+idea+and+other+letters+to+the+editor&amp;title=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-08-23&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Railways are an essential service
Given that railways are an essential lifeline for Canadian businesses and individuals, it is shameful that CN and CPKC are able to lock out its employees rather than being required to continue to bargain in good faith with their workers. It certainly does not look good on CN and CPKC to shut down vital business and commuter traffic and create chaos across the country so they can strong-arm the workers into an agreement - or force the federal government into legislating an end to the work stoppage.
Railroads should be designated an essential service in Canada. No lockouts or strikes should be permitted. The parties should be forced to proceed to arbitration to negotiate new collective agreements. Put an end to this very disruptive nonsense once and for all.
Ralph D'Angelo, Hamilton
Rail lock out was wrong move
How dare CN and CPKC corporations hold Canadian families and business hostage out of fear for their shareholders profits. In a disgusting display of manipulation of workers' rights, CPKC is quoted by the CBC as saying "binding arbitration was the only responsible way to end the impasse."
Binding arbitration only exists if the federal government interjects into the barraging process and orders it through legislation. The union hasn't gone on strike. CN and CPKC have chosen to punish Canadians in order to coerce the federal government to interject into the bargaining process.
CN and CPKC chose to lock out workers rather than negotiate real changes to safety systems that protect Canada businesses and communities - something they could do with a stroke of their pen.
My family and I live just 500 metres from a CPKC rail yard. We stand in solidarity with the workers fighting for the right to have safer workplace conditions, especially when it comes to fatigue management.
Lac-Mégantic was a tragedy that devastated Canadians and wiped an entire community off the map. It was caused was a systemic failure to maintain railway safety systems in favour of shareholder profits.
The East Palestine Ohio derailment was a devastating ecological disaster and again its cause was a systematic and intentional deregulation of railway safety systems in favour of shareholder profits.
CN and CPKC need to return to the table and negotiate real changes to working conditions. I for one will be contacting my MP to ensure that it's made clear Canadian families and their safety come before shareholder profits. Better yet, I will be advocating that the federal government does interject by legislating worker safety and fatigue management; clearly Canadians can't trust the free market to regulate itself.
Edward Harris, Hamilton
Community radio a great idea
Re: The future of Hamilton's airwaves: A call for community radio, Aug. 22
What a great idea to initiate a community radio for the Hamilton and surrounding area. Kootenay Co-Op Radio in Nelson, B.C. has an excellent community radio. It represents the arts, sports, cultural events, public opinion, local interviews, news and plays great music, especially "The Blues Hour," a favourite of mine. It also has community DJs who maintain the spirit of the eclectic Kootenays. A Hamilton community radio station could look at the Kootenay Co-Op Radio as a starting point for ideas on how to create the perfect community radio station for all to enjoy.
Judith Keczan, Lincoln
Help needed to deal with homelessness
We live adjacent to Gage Park, where encampments have existed for the past few years. This year has seen a dramatic increase in their numbers, which has resulted in severe and ever-growing challenges for the surrounding neighbourhood and park users. Personally, we have cleaned up fecal matter and garbage behind our home, have had our property vandalized and have found homeless people sleeping overnight in our backyard.
On a daily basis, our neighbours and park users see people in various states of mental distress, open drug dealing and use, with many wandering around in a zombie state. Theft and vandalism have increased across the neighbourhood and some tent clusters appear to be organized criminal gangs engaged in drug dealing, theft and prostitution. Many neighbours have been and continue to be subject to intimidation and harassment.
Individual citizens and neighbourhoods cannot be expected to shoulder the burden of inadequate mental health services, scarce housing supply and rampant drug use. The city of Hamilton alone is not equipped to tackle these growing problems. We need dedicated provincial and federal support.
Jan Martin, Hamilton
We need to stand against antisemitism
Re: Hamilton Jewish institutions among more than 100 in Canada to receive terror threats, Aug. 22
I am disturbed by the recent terror threats received by more than 100 Jewish organizations, synagogues and hospitals, not only across Canada, but here in our city.
According to StatsCan, violent attacks against Jews have increased over the past year, not only worldwide, but also in Canada and our local communities. As a longtime Hamiltonian, I have recently heard antisemitic remarks in our community. In response to the recent rise of antisemitism, people of all faiths, racial backgrounds and sexual orientation must unite against hatred and violence. We must also continue to send a message of zero-tolerance of any form of hatred.
It is only through ongoing anti-racism education that we can take the initial steps necessary to stand in solidarity against the rise of antisemitism and white supremacy in Canada, as well as locally. In addition, we must continue to educate our youth about the horrors of the Holocaust to prevent the mistakes of the past from being repeated in the future.
Andrea Rado, Hamilton
Shutting down injection sites a death sentence
Re:Doug Ford to ban safe injection sites within 200 metres of schools and child-care centres, Aug. 20
Given that addiction has been in his family, you would think Premier Doug Ford might understand addiction a little better. By shuttering supervised-consumption sites, the province will be in part responsible for any deaths that could have been prevented. The research is clear that the road to recovery starts with safe consumption. Without such sites, syringes will be discarded everywhere, bodies will be found and overdoses will be instantly noticeable.
Russ Young, Fort Erie
Fantasy thinking on homelessness
Re: Municipalities ask Ontario to appoint minister for housing needs, addiction, Aug. 18
Leaders at the Association of Municipalities of Ontario (AMO) conference asked the province to create a new ministry to tackle homelessness. That will certainly create more government jobs and might trickle down to a few less unemployed. That is about the sum total of the difference it will make.
There are already several bureaucratic offices working on the problem. A new minister will add another series of meetings with all the other ministries to review and discuss the existing strategies they have in place.
AMO is dreaming when it thinks adding a ministry for homelessness will streamline anything or produce some new magic bullet solution. It will just add more administrative cost and stretch out timelines.
The AMO meeting with the province to discuss how Ontario public services can be funded and delivered more effectively seems far more productive.
Bruce Timms, St. Catharines
Ontarians deserve a better system of home-care
Re: The entire process of choosing home care contractors needs an independent sober second look, Aug. 13
Thank you to Dr. Patricia Spindel for her excellent opinion piece on the awarding of home-care contracts by Premier Doug Ford's Conservative government.
Spindel points out the lack of transparency in the pre-qualification process for home-care providers, including the criteria used, the decision-making process and selection of the decision makers in the awarding of these contracts. Most of these contracts were awarded to for-profit companies, many with close ties to the Ford government, and some operated by people with little or no experience delivering health care.
Ontario seniors continue to say they wish to age at home in their communities and need access to reliable, professional home-care services. However, many families report a failing home-care system that puts them at risk. Home-care contracts should not be a way to reward friends of the government. The people of Ontario deserve better.
Kathryn Murrell, New Hamburg
CAPTION: Rail workers stand in front of CN headquarters Thursday on the first day of a nationwide rail shutdown, after workers were locked out by CN and CPKC when new contract agreements weren't reached.

Document 145 of 500

One question Trudeau needs to stop dodging

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/one-question-trudeau-needs-stop-dodging/docview/3095903502/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=unknown&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=One+question+Trudeau+needs+to+stop+dodging&amp;title=The+Spectator&amp;issn=11899417&amp;date=2024-08-23&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.10&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Spectator&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Justin Trudeau got a bit of a reprieve this summer from the questions about whether he was staying on to fight the next election.
Two months since those questions rose to a fever pitch after the Liberals' loss of their St. Paul's seat in Toronto, Trudeau and his team are probably thinking they rode out that storm - as they have ridden out so many storms over the past decade.
"This too shall pass" is an integral part of the Trudeau crisis-management method.
It's been proven true in many cases. Donald Trump, a trigger of existential drama for Canada-U.S. relations, eventually did go away. And while the prospect of another Trump presidency is not totally gone, Trudeau's government appears braced to handle it, as it did before. Doug Ford, similarly, has ceased to be the thorn in the side of Trudeau as he promised to be.
A pandemic, a convoy protest, foreign-interference blow-ups with China and India - all eventually receded from the headlines, although of course they took their toll on the government. And while it would be a mistake to say Trudeau and his Liberals simply hunkered down and waited for all these crises to go away, they have learned that today's catastrophe can become tomorrow's distant memory.
This is what nine years in government can teach you: the long game. But there's a risk associated with that big-picture view: complacency.
Trudeau definitely has managed to escape the whirlwind of questions over his future that dominated the news in late June, some by his own design, others by bigger events.
U.S. politics, for instance, is an excellent distraction for political junkies. Joe Biden stepping down, the assassination attempt on Trump, and the rise of Kamala Harris are at least as interesting to Canadians, I'll bet, than another round of speculation over whether Trudeau is sticking around.
Then there's this whole issue of Trudeau travelling incognito. Journalist Paul Wells sent up the biggest flare about this earlier in August, accusing the prime minister and his office of blatantly lying about the itinerary posted daily.
"This has become standard operating procedure for Justin Trudeau and his staff during the difficult summer of 2024: they claim in public every day that the PM has 'no public events scheduled.' Even though he is in a different city every day. And he has public events scheduled. In fact, he is in the city in question so he can attend the public events he claims aren't on his schedule," Wells wrote on Substack.
It's worth remembering that prime ministers before Trudeau didn't issue daily itineraries, which are standard for leaders in other countries.
I'm betting that Trudeau faced some opposition from the RCMP and his security detail when he vowed to be transparent about where he could be found, especially when opposition to him turned into violence and threats in recent years.
But it's also true that Trudeau has, for the most part, avoided putting himself into a position to face more questions about his future.
These demands too, for now, have passed. That doesn't mean they've gone away. Trudeau is meeting with his cabinet this weekend in Halifax, and with the full caucus in Nanaimo, B.C., in early September.
But there's a sharper question to be answered at these gatherings - and it's not just about whether Trudeau is staying on.
David Cochrane, host of CBC's "Power and Politics," put his finger on that question when he interviewed Trudeau in June. Rather than asking the prime minister (again) whether he was staying to fight the next election, Cochrane asked why he wanted another term in office. Trudeau didn't have a quick answer, or at least a sufficient one. He said he wanted to continue to "meet the moment."
The No. 1 job for the cabinet and caucus meetings in the next two weeks should be to come up with a better answer. Asking for a fourth term is a major demand and Liberals should be clear with voters about what they want to do, rather than simply reacting to events and meeting moments.
Susan Delacourt covers national politics for
Torstar.
CAPTION:

CREDIT: Susan Delacourt OPINION;

Subject: Politics; Prime ministers

Location: United States--US

People: Trudeau, Justin
