Document 146 of 500

Closure of supervised drug-use site 'raises many concerns,' operator says: But Urban Core says despite setback, it's 'well positioned' to take on the province's new homelessness and addiction hub model

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/closure-supervised-drug-use-site-raises-many/docview/3095903476/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Closure+of+supervised+drug-use+site+%27raises+many+concerns%2C%27+operator+says%3A+But+Urban+Core+says+despite+setback%2C+it%27s+%27well+positioned%27+to+take+on+the+province%27s+new+homelessness+and+addiction+hub+model&amp;title=The+Spectator&amp;issn=11899417&amp;date=2024-08-23&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.1&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Spectator&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: The operator of a supervised drug-consumption service facing closure in downtown Hamilton says it's "well positioned" to take on a new hub model the province plans to roll out to tackle homelessness and addiction.
Hamilton Urban Core Community Health Centre must close its consumption and treatment services (CTS) site at St. Paul's Presbyterian Church by next March under new provincial restrictions.
On Tuesday, Health Minister Sylvia Jones announced CTS sites within 200 metres of schools and child-care centres will be barred, forcing the closure of 10 of 17 in Ontario, including Urban Core's at the James South church.
"This news was unexpected and raises many concerns for the vulnerable clients accessing these vital services," Sandy Ezepue, Urban Core's executive director, said in a statement posted to social media.
The CTS model "saves lives and plays a vital role" in the community, Ezepue wrote, noting Urban Core plans to meet with the Health Ministry to "explore our options" for mental-health and addiction-support services.
"Despite this setback, we believe we are well positioned to apply" to operate a homelessness and addiction recovery treatment (HART) hub that Jones announced. "The model aligns closely with the work we are already doing, and we are committed to pursuing this opportunity."
Jones, who associated consumption sites with spikes in crime, said the $379-million plan to open 19 HART hubs in Ontario would mark a shift "toward a model that emphasizes recovery, treatment and community safety."
They are to combine a variety of services, including primary care, addiction and mental-health treatment, social services and feature "up to 375 supportive housing units."
"This is part of our commitment to avoid gaps in care," said Jones, noting CTR operators facing closure will be given priority to transition to the HART model.
A CTS site offers people who use drugs a place to consume under the supervision of trained staff to prevent overdose deaths, but it also connects them with other health and social services.
Mayor Andrea Horwath expressed concern over the loss of CTS sites, but also optimism that a "robustly funded" HART hub network would help saves lives and "reduce the crises that are unfolding in our city."
The city plans to explore collaborations with Urban Core and other community partners on a HART hub, Horwath told The Spectator. Hamilton and other municipalities have pressed Premier Doug Ford's Conservative government for more help in responding to a worsening homelessness crisis with encampments dotting urban landscapes.
Urban Core opened Hamilton's first CTS in 2018 at its longtime Rebecca Street location.
Then, the agency relocated the service to St. Paul's, where it was supposed to operate temporarily while displaced from Rebecca Street due the property owner's redevelopment plans and until it could open its new home on Cannon Street East.
It's not clear why the CTS hasn't moved already to the health centre's new location.
Ezepue hasn't responded to The Spectator's requests for comment. "At this moment, we need time to fully understand the details of this decision before providing further comment," she wrote in her social-media post.
Health-care professionals, meanwhile, have warned closing CTS sites will increase the risk of overdoses and serious infections from unsanitary equipment.
Hamilton resident Storm Mallinson, who is familiar with Urban Core's CTS, called it "one of the most useful services" in the city.
"It's saved many, many lives," said Mallinson, a 32-year-old who is struggling with homelessness.
Closing Urban Core's CTS will result in "an even bigger epidemic" of overdose deaths and more infections caused by dirty equipment, he predicted.
Urban Core has been an "exemplary tenant," Rev. Mark Lewis, interim moderator at St. Paul's, said in an email praising staff's "tireless dedication and care."
"Innumerable peer-reviewed scientific studies indicate that safe injection sites are effective in helping people recover from opioid addiction and saving lives that would otherwise have been lost to overdoses," he wrote.
Teviah Moro is a reporter at The Hamilton Spectator.
CAPTION: Urban Core has been an "exemplary tenant," says Rev. Mark Lewis, interim moderator at St. Paul's Presbyterian Church. He praised staff's "tireless dedication and care."Hamilton Spectator file photo

Document 147 of 500

Ford helps factory farms keep secrets

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/ford-helps-factory-farms-keep-secrets/docview/3095897214/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=unknown&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Ford+helps+factory+farms+keep+secrets&amp;title=The+Spectator&amp;issn=11899417&amp;date=2024-08-23&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.10&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Spectator&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: For years, images of the horrific suffering of animals trapped in our factory farming system were making their way onto our TV screens.
The secretly filmed videos were having a powerful and disturbing effect on a public that had simply assumed governments were regulating and monitoring agribusiness to assure basic animal welfare.
One such video, shown on CTV's "W5" in 2020, captured footage of a very ill pregnant pig being roughly treated as she's sliced open with a knife and her unborn piglets are ripped out and thrown onto a concrete floor. The viewers then see piglets squealing in pain as they're castrated and have their tails cut off.
Inside the executive offices of agribusiness and the Doug Ford government, there was only one way to deal with the growing public anger: these videos must be stopped!
So, the Ford government brought in a law, known as an agriculture gag law (or ag-gag) making it illegal, punishable with fines up to $25,000, to get hired at a factory farm or slaughterhouses while concealing an intention to make an undercover video documenting animal abuse.
Interestingly, the farm featured in the pig video shown on "W5" pleaded guilty to charges of animal cruelty and was fined $20,000 - a prosecution that would never have happened without the video evidence. Despite this conviction, demonstrating the value of undercover videos, the government has persisted with its ag-gag law, which has accomplished exactly what the industry wanted: an end to exposés of the miserable lives of animals behind farm factory walls.
Not one such video has been made since the law came into effect almost four years ago, says Camille Labchuk, of the group Animal Justice, which shot the pig video.
However, last April, Animal Justice won an important victory when the Ontario Superior Court struck down key sections of the ag-gag law. Justice Markus Koehnen ruled parts of the law violated the Charter-protected right to free expression of activists, journalists and whistleblowers.
But the Ford government is appealing the ruling, so the battle continues.
The determination of government and industry to keep the curtain drawn on corporate farming practices suggests that there's a lot they want to remain hidden. For instance, they don't want us to know that calves spend the entire four or five months of their lives confined to narrow crates that prevent them from even turning around.
The crates are designed to stunt their muscle growth so that their flesh is tender, when it's served as veal.
The replacement of the family farm with industrial farming in recent decades has meant a dramatic worsening of living conditions for millions of animals - who, while different from humans, suffer as we do.
As renowned anthropologist Jane Goodall put it: "Farm animals feel pleasure and sadness, excitement and resentment, depression, fear and pain. They are far more aware and intelligent than we ever imagined."
What range of emotions must they feel spending their lives as captives inside our wretched factory farming system?
We can get a hint of how much they suffer by watching videos of the lucky few who end up in animal rescue sanctuaries. The joy of these suddenly-released animals as they frolic outdoors for the first time is not something easily forgotten.
Certainly, we live today with a strange disconnect between the world of dogs and cats, who are increasingly loved, spoiled, indulged and treated as part of the family, and the hidden world of suffering farm animals, who typically experience the outdoors for the first time as they're loaded onto trucks bound for the slaughterhouse.
LINDA MCQUAIG IS AN AUTHOR, JOURNALIST AND A FREELANCE
CONTRIBUTING COLUMNIST FOR TORSTAR.
CAPTION:

CREDIT: Linda McQuaig OPINION;

Full text: Canada
Nicotine pouches going behind counter
Health Minister Mark Holland is putting Health Canada-approved nicotine pouches behind pharmacy counters starting next week as part of his crackdown on the sale of new nicotine products to children. He also says he's banning flavours other than mint or menthol for pouches and any new nicotine products on the market, and putting restrictions on the labels and advertising of all nicotine replacement therapies.
Manitoba
Free birth control prescriptions
Manitobans will have access to free prescription birth control starting Oct. 1. The NDP government says that's the date it will fulfil a promise it made in last year's election campaign. The plan will cover methods including birth control pills and intrauterine devices and cost an estimated $11 million a year.
Ontario
Man grabbed stroller with child inside
Toronto police say they've arrested and charged a man who allegedly grabbed a stroller with a child in it and fled before being stopped. Police say the grandparent chased after the suspect and was assaulted by him before the man fled.

Document 149 of 500

Deadline passes, CUPE vice-president remains silent after union's board asks for his resignation

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/deadline-passes-cupe-vice-president-remains/docview/3096378368/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Deadline+passes%2C+CUPE+vice-president+remains+silent+after+union%27s+board+asks+for+his+resignation&amp;title=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-08-22&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Embattled CUPE leader Fred Hahn is keeping mum on his future with the union, which is seeking his resignation over a video that has been characterized as divisive and antisemitic, a criticism being voiced by a growing number of his own members.
A noon Thursday deadline passed without word from Hahn on whether he would quit as a national vice-president as requested by CUPE's executive board, which said Wednesday it had "lost confidence" in him.
"CUPE's national executive board made the difficult decision to ask for Fred Hahn's resignation as general vice-president because he reposted a deeply problematic video that was a clear violation of our union's equity statement," CUPE's national officers said in a statement Thursday.
Hahn did not immediately reply to messages left by the Star with the union's Ontario division, of which he is the elected president in a separate role, and on his mobile phone.
At issue is a digitally manipulated video that the veteran unionist posted on his Facebook page Aug. 11. It depicted a Jewish athlete with a Star of David arm tattoo jumping off a diving board at the recent Paris Olympics and turning into a bomb that explodes.
Hahn took down the post on Sunday, the day after CUPE's executive board decided to take action against him and called a virtual meeting for Tuesday night. Hahn did not attend the meeting but was informed of the board's decision and asked for time to consider its request, said CUPE national president Mark Hancock.
Hancock told the Star that because Hahn was elected to the national vice-president post, he cannot be dismissed as would be the case with a typical employee.
"I have a (CUPE) constitution to live by and firing is ... it's not a job, per se, so it's different," said Hancock, who noted this is the first time in living memory the union has felt compelled to seek a senior leader's resignation.
If Hahn does not resign voluntarily, "that'll be new ground again for CUPE and me as a national president," Hancock added.
"If not, then I will review options available to me."
While the executive board has asked Hahn to resign as a national vice-president, that request does not directly apply to his presidency of CUPE's 290,000-member Ontario wing. But a union source, speaking confidentially to discuss internal deliberations, said "it's hard to see how he would have the legitimacy to stay on."
In explaining his decision to take down the video post at Hancock's urging, Hahn expressed "deep regret" on Facebook at causing pain "for some who viewed it."
"It is a terrible mistake, and antisemitic, to conflate abhorrent actions by the state of Israel with Jewish humanity or identity," he wrote, referring to the Israel-Hamas war that followed last year's Oct. 7 attack on Israel by Hamas.
The Hamas attack killed about 1,200 people and saw more than 200 hostages taken. The subsequent attack by Israel in neighbouring Gaza has killed more than 40,000 Palestinians, according to Gaza's health ministry.
Premier Doug Ford's surprise intervention in the Hahn matter - on Wednesday he called him " a disgusting human being" for posting the video and called for the union to discipline him - appears to have complicated the CUPE executive board's efforts to secure Hahn's departure.
The board noted in its statement Thursday that the decision to ask for Hahn's resignation was made days before Ford and Labour Minister David Piccini spoke out against Hahn, with Piccini saying "your members deserve better" and calling the video "antisemitic."
"We do not take advice from a union-hating, public service-gutting premier trying to distract from his own scandals and track record," said the statement, calling the attacks on Hahn from the two Progressive Conservative politicians "completely revolting and unacceptable."
The statement added the union's position on "the bombardment of Gaza by Israel" has not changed.
"CUPE continues to call for an immediate and permanent ceasefire, a release of all hostages from Gaza, and an end to the blockade of Gaza and the restoration of humanitarian aid."
Taking aim at opponents of the move to oust Hahn, the CUPE statement said "to suggest that asking for Fred's resignation ... is to throw in with those who support the genocide in Gaza, or is in response to Conservative bullying, is frankly ludicrous."
Israel disputes allegations that the war against Hamas is a genocide, saying it is a military action in response to the attack by Hamas, which is designated as a terrorist group by Canada and other Western governments.
Pushback over Hahn's video post is growing within the ranks at CUPE, including the local representing paramedics in Windsor-Essex, developmental support workers in Vineland who called the depiction of the diver "harmful, hurtful and divisive," and City of Hamilton workers.
The executive of Hamilton Local 5167 called Thursday on X for a focus on "shared labour issues" as well as "the immediate resignation or removal of Fred Hahn as the president of CUPE Ontario ... Fred's actions have contributed to the fracturing of the CUPE Ontario labour movement."
The Friends of Simon Wiesenthal Center said CUPE's decision to seek Hahn's resignation "sends a clear message that antisemitism and the toxic environment he created for Jewish CUPE members are no longer acceptable."
CUPE is facing a complaint at the Ontario Human Rights Tribunal alleging systemic discrimination and promotion of antisemitism that isolates Jewish members of the massive union, which represents almost 300,000 workers in Ontario.
The complaint was filed last November in the wake of the Oct. 7 Hamas attack and an Oct. 8 statement from Hahn celebrating Palestinian resistance. Hahn apologized in late October for that statement, saying "the timing was wrong."
Kathryn Marshall, a lawyer representing CUPE workers in the complaint, said the diver video has resulted in more of the union's members seeking to be part of the case.
CAPTION: Fred Hahn, Ontario president and national vice-president of the Canadian Union of Public Employees, speaks at a rally in support of pro-Palestinian encampment protesters at the University of Toronto on May 27, 2024.

Subject: Antisemitism; Resignations; Vice Presidents; Employees; Tribunals & commissions; Genocide; Truces & cease fires

Business indexing term: Subject: Resignations Employees

Location: Gaza Strip; Israel

Company / organization: Name: Hamas; NAICS: 813940

Title: Deadline passes, CUPE vice-president remains silent after union's board asks for his resignation

Publication title: The Hamilton Spectator (Online); Hamilton

Publication year: 2024

Publication date: Aug 22, 2024

Section: politics/provincial

Publisher: Torstar Syndication Services, a Division of Toronto Star Newspapers Limited

Document 150 of 500

If Justin Trudeau really wants to be re-elected, there's one question he needs to stop dodging

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/if-justin-trudeau-really-wants-be-re-elected/docview/3095625622/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=If+Justin+Trudeau+really+wants+to+be+re-elected%2C+there%27s+one+question+he+needs+to+stop+dodging&amp;title=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-08-22&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Justin Trudeau got a bit of a reprieve this summer from the questions about whether he was staying on to fight the next election.
Two months since those questions rose to a fever pitch after the Liberals' loss of their St. Paul's seat in Toronto, Trudeau and his team are probably thinking they rode out that storm - as they have ridden out so many storms over the past decade.
"This too shall pass" is an integral part of the Trudeau crisis-management method.
It's been proven true in many cases. Donald Trump, a trigger of existential drama for Canada-U.S. relations, eventually did go away. And while the prospect of another Trump presidency is not totally gone, Trudeau's government appears braced to handle it, as it did before.
Doug Ford, similarly, has ceased to be the thorn in the side of Trudeau as he promised to be, long before COVID made him best pals with Deputy Prime Minister Chrystia Freeland and the Ontario premier got into the habit of doing friendly, smiling joint announcements with the prime minister.
A pandemic, a convoy protest, foreign-interference blow-ups with China and India - all eventually receded from the headlines, although of course they took their toll on the government.
And while it would be a mistake to say Trudeau and his Liberals simply hunkered down and waited for all these crises to go away - they were highly active on all the files (too activist, some might argue) - they have learned that today's catastrophe can become tomorrow's distant memory.
This is what nine years in government can teach you: the long game. But there's a risk associated with that big-picture view: complacency.
Trudeau definitely has managed to escape the whirlwind of questions over his future that dominated the news in late June, some by his own design, others by bigger events.
U.S. politics, for instance, is an excellent distraction for political junkies. Joe Biden stepping down, the assassination attempt on Trump, and the rise of Kamala Harris are at least as interesting to Canadians, I'll bet, than another round of speculation over whether Trudeau is sticking around.
Then there's this whole issue of Trudeau travelling incognito. Journalist Paul Wells sent up the biggest flare about this earlier in August, accusing the prime minister and his office of blatantly lying about the itinerary posted daily.
"This has become standard operating procedure for Justin Trudeau and his staff during the difficult summer of 2024: they claim in public every day that the PM has 'no public events scheduled.' Even though he is in a different city every day. And he has public events scheduled. In fact, he is in the city in question so he can attend the public events he claims aren't on his schedule," Wells wrote on Substack.
It's worth remembering that prime ministers before Trudeau didn't issue daily itineraries, which are standard for leaders in other countries. I'm betting that Trudeau faced some opposition from the RCMP and his security detail when he vowed to be transparent about where he could be found, especially when opposition to him turned into violence and threats in recent years.
I've been watching these itineraries grow more cryptic and vague since the convoy of 2022 and the incident in which the gates of Rideau Hall were crashed by an armed man looking to "talk" to the prime minister.
But it's also true that Trudeau has, for the most part, avoided putting himself into a position to face more questions about his future, whether from the media, or from his caucus members, who were demanding a meeting so urgently in late June. These demands too, for now, have passed.
That doesn't mean they've gone away. Trudeau is meeting with his cabinet this weekend in Halifax, and with the full caucus in Nanaimo, B.C. in early September.
But there's a sharper question to be answered at these gatherings - and it's not just about whether Trudeau is staying on.
David Cochrane, host of CBC's "Power and Politics," put his finger on that question when he interviewed Trudeau in June. Rather than asking the prime minister (again) whether he was staying to fight the next election, Cochrane asked why he wanted another term in office.
Trudeau didn't have a quick answer, or at least a sufficient one. He said he wanted to continue to "meet the moment."
The number-one job for the cabinet and caucus meetings in the next two weeks should be to come up with a better answer. Asking for a fourth term is a major demand and Liberals should be clear with voters about what they want to do, rather than simply reacting to events and meeting moments.
Trudeau may well get through another few weeks without being asked whether he is staying on. But a harder question to dodge is: why?
CAPTION: Prime Minister Justin Trudeau arrives to deliver a statement on the potential rail strike following an event in Gatineau, Que., on Aug. 21, 2024.

Subject: Politics; Prime ministers

Location: United States--US

People: Trudeau, Justin

Title: If Justin Trudeau really wants to be re-elected, there's one question he needs to stop dodging

Document 151 of 500

CUPE demands resignation of vice-president for posting 'antisemitic' video

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/cupe-demands-resignation-vice-president-posting/docview/3095396226/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=CUPE+demands+resignation+of+vice-president+for+posting+%27antisemitic%27+video&amp;title=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-08-22&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: The Canadian Union of Public Employees has "lost confidence" in national vice-president Fred Hahn and is demanding his resignation after he posted a video denounced as antisemitic.
"We've given him pretty strong direction," CUPE national president Mark Hancock told the Star in an interview Wednesday, a day after the union's executive board met over the growing controversy surrounding Hahn.
Hahn was re-elected earlier this year in a separate but related role as president of the union's Ontario wing, which also faces a human rights complaint filed last November in the wake of the Oct. 7 Hamas attack on Israel and the subsequent attack by Israel in Gaza that has killed more than 40,000 Palestinians. The complaint alleges systemic discrimination and promotion of antisemitism that isolates Jewish members of the massive public sector union.
Saying "the timing was wrong," Hahn apologized in late October for an Oct. 8 statement celebrating Palestinian resistance in the wake of the attack on Israel that killed some 1,200 people and saw more than 200 hostages taken.
"We've asked him to reply by noon Thursday," Hancock said of the resignation request. "I'm very optimistic and hopeful that he will do the right thing."
Hahn has not replied to requests for comment made through the union.
A union source, speaking on background, said the resignation demand does not directly apply to his role as Ontario president, but "it is going to trigger a chain reaction. It's hard to see how he would have the legitimacy to stay on."
A digitally manipulated video posted on Hahn's Facebook page on Aug. 11 pictured a Jewish athlete with a Star of David arm tattoo leaping off a diving board at the Paris Olympics and turning into a bomb that explodes.
Hahn removed the video Sunday and posted an explanation, but by then it was too late and the damage was done, said Hancock, who noted he considered the video "antisemitic. I felt it was inappropriate."
Hahn was informed of the executive board's decision Tuesday night and chose not to participate in the meeting, added Hancock, who spoke personally with Hahn in a call Wednesday morning.
"The board stated we have lost confidence in Fred to carry on as a national vice-president," said Hancock, referring to Hahn's other role in the union.
"This is a really sad day. Fred is a colleague and a friend."
In addition to concerns from the national executive about Hahn's activities, two CUPE locals in Ontario posted their thoughts on it, with developmental support workers in Vineland calling the diver video "harmful, hurtful and divisive."
In his Facebook explanation, Hahn expressed "deep regret" at causing pain "for some who viewed it."
"It is a terrible mistake, and antisemitic, to conflate abhorrent actions by the state of Israel with Jewish humanity or identity," he wrote.
The Centre for Israel and Jewish Affairs said on X that Hahn has "an anti-Israel obsession."
The Friends of Simon Wiesenthal Center said CUPE's decision to secure Hahn's resignation "sends a clear message that antisemitism and the toxic environment he created for Jewish CUPE members are no longer acceptable."
While Hahn got supportive messages from many Facebook friends, others called him to task on the post and the explanation.
"Fred, aren't you a union leader? Keep your lousy opinions on Mideast politics to yourself," wrote Sam Jonas.
"You left that post up for days - your apology rings totally hollow," said Naomi Levinson.
Hahn also faced a broadside Wednesday from Premier Doug Ford, who called him "a disgusting human being" and urged CUPE to discipline him.
"Here in Ontario, we have 110 nationalities speaking 200 languages, and we all need to get together," Ford said in St. Catharines.
"I applaud CUPE National for asking Fred Hahn to step down. Now it's time for Fred to put his members first and do the right thing by resigning from all his positions of leadership," the premier wrote late Wednesday night on X.
The premier applauded David Piccini, his labour minister, for accusing Hahn of being an "antisemite."
The confrontation between Piccini and Hahn is shown in a video that Piccini posted on X Tuesday from this week's meeting of the Association of Municipalities of Ontario conference in Ottawa. Hancock said it came out as the CUPE executive board was meeting.
"Your members deserve better, Fred," Piccini told Hahn in a 44-second exchange that one CUPE official described privately as an "ambush."
"As a labour leader you've got to represent your members and what you have said about Jews ... is antisemitic and your members reach out to me," Piccini said.
"They just re-elected me, man," Hahn retorted at one point in the conversation.
"I am representing my members and I'm not talking about Jews, I'm talking about the state of Israel and I represent 290,000 workers," Hahn added.
"I get that you're filming this and I get that you're trying to do it for something. But you are the minister of labour," Hahn continued, chiding Piccini.
"This is about how you behave," Piccini shot back before walking away.
The controversial diving video has resulted in more CUPE members seeking to be part of the human rights complaint, said lawyer Kathryn Marshall, who is handling the case.
"My inbox has been flooded with people who want to join the action and it's not just Jewish members," she added.
"Hahn must resign but CUPE is ultimately responsible for the environment they have created where someone with his horrific views could thrive and hold leadership positions."
CAPTION: Fred Hahn, the Canadian Union of Public Employees' Ontario president, speaks at a news conference on June 6, 2024.

Subject: Antisemitism; Resignations; Jewish people; Leadership; Vice Presidents; Human rights

Business indexing term: Subject: Resignations Leadership

Location: Israel

Document 152 of 500

Taking away life-saving programs cruel

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/taking-away-life-saving-programs-cruel/docview/3095393743/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=unknown&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Taking+away+life-saving+programs+cruel&amp;title=The+Spectator&amp;issn=11899417&amp;date=2024-08-22&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.12&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Spectator&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: After the provincial government's announcement that it will force the closure of more than half the supervised consumption drug sites in the province, can we start by acknowledging that we'd pretty much all prefer such places didn't need to exist?
They're not easy to have in your neighbourhood. They can wind up surrounded by desperate and dangerous characters, their very existence is a sad acknowledgment of our failings as a society. The approach they are part of is called "harm reduction," a name that acknowledges the real harm caused by what takes place in and around them.
Pretty much all of us would prefer that those who wind up addicted would seek successful treatment rather than continue using. Pretty much all of us would prefer there be no demand at all for these sites. But we don't live in the world we prefer. And in this one, the choice is not between consumption sites and an addiction-free environment.
These places exist because our choice is between desperation under supervision or death on our neighbourhood streets. They exist because they are proven to save lives.
A study published in the Lancet this year found that, in the year after Toronto safe consumption sites opened, overdose deaths in the immediate vicinity of them (within 500 metres) dropped 67 per cent. The administrator of two Toronto sites told the Toronto Star they have reversed 776 overdoses within the past year. Overdoses that likely would have happened anyway.
As Gillian Kolla, a Memorial University substance abuse expert told the Star, the sites in Toronto were chosen in locations where data showed drug use was already happening. "Before the South Riverdale site was opened, people were dying in the alleys behind the centre because there wasn't a safe place to go," Kolla said.
When the centre closes, we can guess people will go back to dying in alleys. We don't want them to, but that's what seems likely to happen.
So Premier Doug Ford can spare us his stated preference for treatment over supervised consumption sites, because it is a false dichotomy. And because we all share that preference. But some addicts are not at a point where they are seeking treatment, and they don't magically go away if you force them outdoors into back alleys.
Moreover, many who seek treatment cannot find it. This is the most galling part of the provincial government's rhetoric here. Here's Ford in 2018: "I don't believe in safe-injection areas, as I call them. I believe in supporting people, getting them help." Great. Six years of his alleged leadership on the issue later, we learned this year that wait times for addiction treatment stretch as long as one year. Here's the thing: if these sites were closing because the provincial government's addiction treatment supports were so successful that there was no more demand for supervised consumption sites and the risk of overdoses had disappeared, I think virtually everyone would be cheering. But that isn't what's happening.
Certainly, the province is announcing $378 million in funding for treatment and recovery centres, including some supportive housing units, alongside its new restrictions on safe consumption sites. That new funding is welcome. But no one can expect it is likely to immediately solve the problem the consumption sites are meant to address. By all means, please spend more on treatment, and on counselling and outreach services that help people find their way into treatment.
Like with emergency homeless shelters and food banks, we'd all prefer to see the underlying problem resolved so that safe consumption sites aren't needed anymore. But like in those other cases, the underlying problems only seem to have recently gotten worse. And taking away such life-saving approaches to the worst symptoms of societal problems while demand for them is still high is worse than ineffective. It's cruel.
It is like taking a life preserver away from an actively drowning person because you strongly prefer they learn how to swim. Even if you're investing in some long-term learn-to-swim programs at the same time, taking the flotation devices away from those currently thrashing away in the water is only going to kill people.
Edward Keenan is a
Torstar columnist.
CAPTION:

CREDIT: Edward Keenan Edward Keenan is a Torstar columnist.;

Subject: Drug overdose; Addictions; Homeless people; Substance abuse treatment

Title: Taking away life-saving programs cruel

Publication title: The Spectator; Hamilton, Ont.

First page: A.12

Publication year: 2024

Publication date: Aug 22, 2024

Section: Editorial

Publisher: Torstar Syndication Services, a Division of Toronto Star Newspapers Limited

Place of publication: Hamilton, Ont.

Country of publication: Canada

Publication subject: General Interest Periodicals--Canada

CUPE demands resignation of vice-president for posting 'antisemitic' video

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/cupe-demands-resignation-vice-president-posting/docview/3095750163/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=CUPE+demands+resignation+of+vice-president+for+posting+%27antisemitic%27+video&amp;title=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-08-21&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: The Canadian Union of Public Employees has "lost confidence" in national vice-president Fred Hahn and is demanding his resignation after he posted a video denounced as antisemitic.

Document 154 of 500

Doug Ford finally got his way on safe injection sites. That doesn't solve our bigger problem

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/doug-ford-finally-got-his-way-on-safe-injection/docview/3095750072/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Doug+Ford+finally+got+his+way+on+safe+injection+sites.+That+doesn%27t+solve+our+bigger+problem&amp;title=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-08-21&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: The war on drugs is now part of the culture wars in Doug Ford's Ontario.
The premier is shutting down many of the province's safe injection sites by declaring their locations unsafe - too close to schools and child-care centres.
He has finally found a way, bureaucratically, to have his way, politically. In practice, that means government-funded safe sites must be a safe distance of 200 metres or more from schools and child-care centres starting next March.
Ford points out that his government requires all cannabis storefronts to be at least 150 metres from any school. So why should needle exchange sites be any different?
Fair point, but he added an extra 50 metres of distancing for good measure - conveniently ensuring that most existing sites will henceforth be deemed too close for comfort. And destined to be closed down.
It's a deft approach by the usually blundering and blustering politician. Ford has bristled about supervised sites for years, biding his time until times changed.
Now, times are indeed different, compared to when governments began experimenting with safe injection sites - Toronto's first supervised consumption service opened in 2017. All these years later, an addiction crisis is decimating a generation of young people, while the fallout from fentanyl is disrupting neighbouring communities.
Karolina Huebner-Makurat, a mother of two, was killed by a stray bullet outside the South Riverdale Community Health Centre consumption and treatment site (CTS) last year. The government cites increased crime near safe sites, where assault and robbery incidents are roughly double those of other neighbourhoods in Toronto (and still higher in other cities); it also points to police reports of free hydromorphone from those sites being "diverted and trafficked" outside.
Now, Ford is using proximity as a pretext for putting these sites out of business. It's one way to thread the needle.
Ford claims he's not moralizing about drug users, merely protecting children from the harmful effects of harm reduction sites too close to home. But for all the focus on proximity as a measure of safety, Ontario's Progressive Conservative government is not allowing disqualified sites to relocate and reopen further from schools.
Which means it's not really about relocating. It's about reallocating resources from harm reduction to recovery.
Toronto now has 10 such sites. Six are provincially funded CTS centres covered by this week's announcement, of which five will be required to close due to their locations.
This week, Health Minister Sylvia Jones announced an extra $378 million for 19 new Homelessness and Addiction Recovery Treatment (HART) Hubs offering a more holistic approach. But that won't satisfy critics who insist an overdose emergency requires urgent measures like needle exchanges.
Safe drug sites are like a Rorschach blotter test. Proponents view them as harm reduction, while opponents see them as harm attraction.
Where you stand on these sites might depend on where you live, what drugs you've done, what harm(s) you've seen. If your child's school is near a so-called "safe injection site," it's unsafe; if your child is using dangerous drugs and dirty needles, maybe it's a safer site.
Ford styles himself an authority on how drug dealers operate: "I'm not sold on these safe injection sites that are in neighbourhoods and needles are all flowing around - it's a haven for drug dealers," he said last week.
Defenders of safe drug sites say they save the lives of hundreds of people with addictions who would otherwise die without help. What the statistics don't make clear is how many lives go unsaved, because safe sites don't change behaviours, they perhaps only perpetuate them (523 people died from opioid toxicity last year, according to Toronto Public Health figures).
"This government is cynically attacking health infrastructure and putting ideology over evidence," Ontario's NDP said in response.
Left unsaid was that B.C.'s New Democratic Party government faced an intense public backlash about drug use, forcing it to do a U-turn this year on a pilot project decriminalizing possession of small amounts of hard drugs. No politician can ignore public sentiment on public drug use near so-called safe spaces.
Perhaps that's why Liberal Leader Bonnie Crombie seemed more measured in her reaction, acknowledging that "these sites should not be next to schools." While criticizing Ford's funding shortfalls, she promised only to ensure "treatment to those who need it" - which is different from defending existing safe sites.
The realities in Ontario and B.C., and the responses of politicians in both provinces, are a reminder that safe sites were always a quick fix that bought us more time, not more success. The premier is unquestionably determined to rip off the Band-Aid, but the bigger question is whether he can show as much passion for finding - and funding - a comprehensive approach to a problem that is only getting worse over time, safe sites or not.
CAPTION: Security guards stand outside a safe injection site at 277 Victoria St. in Toronto on Aug. 20, 2024.

Document 155 of 500

St. Catharines shipyard gets piece of $10M for skills training

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/st-catharines-shipyard-gets-piece-10m-skills/docview/3095750048/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=St.+Catharines+shipyard+gets+piece+of+%2410M+for+skills+training&amp;title=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-08-21&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Ontario Shipyards is getting a $10-million skills training boost for new and current employees at its trio of locations in St. Catharines, Hamilton and Thunder Bay.
Premier Doug Ford made the announcement Wednesday in St. Catharines at the company's Port Weller facility with employees, city officials and business people on hand.
"This investment will help reskill and upskill existing workers for careers in the millwright, welding, electrical and painting trades and many more," said Ford, accompanied by Labour, Immigration, Training and Skills Development Minister David Piccini and Niagara West MPP Sam Oosterhoff.
"It will give new workers hands-on experience in shipbuilding and ship repair, and it will help recruit and retain the next generation of workers and leaders in our shipbuilding industry."
The $10 million is being provided through the province's Skills Development Fund training stream.
Ontario Shipyards, which changed its name from Heddle earlier this year, has about 250 employees across the three sites, with 130 at the St. Catharines shipyard.
The company previously received $3.7 million from the Skills Development Fund in July 2023 for training and modernization in Hamilton and Port Weller.
Shaun Padulo, president and chief executive officer of Ontario Shipyards, said the Ford government has played a pivotal role in the revitalization of the shipyards, allowing the company to advance multiple phases of the Ontario Shipyard Modernization Project.
"On a personal note, the Skills Development Fund has been crucial during times when work was scarce, allowing us to keep our workforce intact through various training initiatives," he said.
"This support has directly benefited over 250 families, ensuring their stability, and many of those families are represented by the fine folks right behind me here."
Ted Kirkpatrick, director of Ontario Shipyards business development and government relations, said afterwards the funds really are helping to bring shipbuilding back to Ontario, explaining the impact of having to train a workforce.
The company signed a long-term lease at the Port Weller dry docks in 2017 when the site was dormant and had been through years of instability.
"At one point when we took these facilities over, we took them over out of bankruptcy. So it's a tremendous amount of not just capital in terms of what you need to build and to buy equipment, but to train the people that are no longer there," he said.
"Today's announcement, it's hugely important to rebuilding this industry in Ontario."
Kirkpatrick said the fund will be used to train current employees who want to increase their skills, as well as for people who may have no experience in shipbuilding to bring them into apprenticeship programs through the various trades at the shipyard.
"We employ a vast array of tradespeople. And that's why this money is so effective - we can deploy that across all layers of skills."
Jordan Sherlock, executive director of Niagara Industrial Association, was at the announcement and said afterwards the government's fund takes the risk out of employer-led training.
"It makes it a lot easier for large and small businesses to train and bring in employees that they need to do what they have to do without having to incur the cost and the risk," he said.
"There's a lot of risk associated with workforce development today so de-risking that is is a huge advantage for our industrial folks."
Mayor Mat Siscoe told people gathered that shipbuilding and ship repair have played a vital role in St. Catharines for generations and has become a cornerstone of the local economy and a key contributor to the broader national economic landscape.
He said he was excited to see another investment in "upskilling" workers in St. Catharines and the Niagara region.
"We know that our industrial base is capable of meeting the demands of the 21st century with the right training opportunities," he said during the announcement.
"The provincial government's investment is a welcome step for the workers of our community, and it's a clear demonstration of their dedication to empowering individuals to thrive in the modern workforce by providing them with the necessary tools and the knowledge that they need to succeed."
The province launched its Skills Development Fund in 2021 and through it has invested $1.4 billion.
Applications are open for the next round of funding from the SDF training stream.
CAPTION: Premier Doug Ford makes his skills training funding announcement at Ontario Shipyards Wednesday.The funding "will give new workers hands-on experience in shipbuilding and ship repair, and it will help recruit and retain the next generation of workers and leaders in our shipbuilding industry," Premier Doug Ford says.Doug Ford, with St. Catharines Mayor Mat Siscoe, meets with workers at Ontario Shipyards' Port Weller facility while announcing the Ontario government is investing $10 million to train and upskill workers for careers in the shipbuilding and repair sector.

Document 156 of 500

Doug Ford says safe injection sites are a 'failed policy' that harms communities and people with addictions

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/doug-ford-says-safe-injection-sites-are-failed/docview/3095750037/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Doug+Ford+says+safe+injection+sites+are+a+%27failed+policy%27+that+harms+communities+and+people+with+addictions&amp;title=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-08-21&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Premier Doug Ford has denounced safe injection sites as a "failed policy" experiment that harms communities as well as people battling drug addiction.
In his first public comments since his government announced 10 of Ontario's safe injection sites will close by March 31 due to zoning restrictions forcing them to be 200 metres away from schools and daycares, Ford did not mince words.
"This was supposed to be the greatest thing since sliced bread - it's the worst thing that could ever happen to a community to have one of these safe injection sites in their neighbourhood," he said Wednesday in St. Catharines, the day after his changes were announced.
"How you would feel if I stuck one of these beside your house? You wouldn't like it ... neither would your neighbours and neither would anyone else."
Touting the new $378 million in funding for treatment hubs that will replace many of the sites, Ford said "giving someone, an addict, a place to do their injections, we haven't seen it get better."
Asked about health advocates warning there could be more overdose deaths by closing five of Toronto's 10 safe injection sites, Ford dismissed such concerns as "typical scare tactics that these folks have been saying for years."
Angela Robertson, executive director at Parkdale Queen West Community Health Centre, which will be forced to close one of its two safe injection sites, said Ford's decision could mean more deaths among drug users who might otherwise survive an overdose.
Robertson said her two sites reversed 766 overdoses last year.
The premier - who has spoken candidly in the past about his late brother Rob Ford, the former Toronto mayor who was captured on video using crack cocaine - said treatment and rehabilitation services are more effective.
"You don't give them more drugs," he said. "We're trying to help these people, we aren't going to give them a safe place to shoot up heroin and crack and every other thing they can get their hands on."
The premier noted he has been inundated with calls from residents, who live near the sites, praising the move due to concerns about drug dealing and other criminal activity.
At the same time, Health Minister Sylvia Jones announced $378 million for 19 new "Homelessness and Addiction Recovery Treatment" (HART) hubs with 375 supportive housing units as well as addiction recovery and treatment beds.
The new HART facilities, which will not offer supervised drug consumption or safe supply of narcotics or needle exchange programs, aim to help people transition to long-term housing.
NDP Leader Marit Stiles noted Ford was closing CTS sites against the advice of experts who have reported back to his government that they can be effective.
"People deserve a government that uses every tool they have to take on the addictions crisis, and that bases that support on evidence, not ideology," Stiles said Wednesday.
Five of the shuttered consumption and treatment service (CTS) sites are in Toronto, including Leslieville's South Riverdale Community Health Centre, near where mother-of-two Karolina Huebner-Makurat was killed by stray bullet last summer.
Outcry over that shocking crime put a new focus on the community concerns over safe injection sites.
CTS facilities in Hamilton, Guelph, Kitchener, Ottawa and Thunder Bay will also close by next spring.
"The status quo of drug consumption sites is not working," Jones said Tuesday at the Association of Municipalities of Ontario (AMO) conference in Ottawa.
"Parents are worried about discarded needles that their children could pick up. Some parents no longer feel comfortable sending their children to the local elementary school or have pulled them out of their local daycare," she said.
While Liberal Leader Bonnie Crombie said "of course these sites should not be next to schools," she expressed concern that the Progressive Conservatives aren't doing enough to address addiction and mental health issues.
"It is so important we find smart, compassionate solutions to helping our loved ones and neighbours fight these challenges," said Crombie, who also has spoken about the toll of addiction in her family.
Green Leader Mike Schreiner predicted "Ontario will lose more lives because of this government's attack on harm reduction services."
While AMO president Colin Best said municipalities welcomed Tuesday's decision as "an important step towards the comprehensive, long-term strategy that is needed to address root causes of homelessness," there are concerns.
"Supervised safe consumption sites are a critical part of tackling the opioid crisis. The evidence shows that harm reduction saves lives, saves money, and reduces crime. It takes the pressure off our police, paramedics and hospitals," said Best.
Mayor Olivia Chow's office said she was "concerned that the closure of existing harm reduction services in Toronto will have significant consequences, such as increased overdose deaths, greater strain on first responders and emergency rooms, and more public drug consumption."
CAPTION: Premier Doug Ford speaks to reporters at the Council of the Federation meetings in Halifax on July 15, 2024.

Subject: Drug overdose; Addictions; Schools; Harm reduction

Ontario Liberals to meet in London, Ont. to plot election strategy

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/ontario-liberals-meet-london-ont-plot-election/docview/3095159340/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Ontario+Liberals+to+meet+in+London%2C+Ont.+to+plot+election+strategy&amp;title=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-08-21&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: As Premier Doug Ford's Progressive Conservatives gear up for a possible early election next spring, Bonnie Crombie is signalling her Liberals are also getting ready.
Crombie announced Wednesday the Grits will gather in London, Ont. on Sept. 20-22 for a policy conference featuring some high-profile speakers.
"Ontario's Liberals are coming together to chart a path forward for our party and present an ambitious vision to deliver more for you and your family from a government that works for you," said the rookie leader, who took her party's helm last Dec. 2.
"After over six years of Doug Ford's failed leadership and giveaways to his rich friends and insiders, Ontario families are struggling," she said.
"We're going to get Ontario back on track with a plan to fix and protect our health-care system, cut class sizes, combat climate change, create jobs, and make house and your life more affordable."
Crombie has invited former British Columbia premier Christy Clark, former Toronto chief planner Jennifer Keesmaat, former federal health minister Jane Philpott and Dr. Samir Sinha, director of geriatrics at Mount Sinai and the University Health Network Hospitals to speak to delegates.
"This is meant to send exactly the signal it looks like it's sending. That Bonnie Crombie is a different kind of Liberal," said an adviser, speaking confidentially in order to discuss strategy.
Both Clark and Philpott have publicly distanced themselves from Prime Minister Justin Trudeau's struggling federal Liberals.
Ontario Liberals privately believe the national party's current unpopularity is contributing to their own challenges to break through against Ford's Tories.
The insider said Philpott, dean of health sciences at Queen's University, and Sinha, a leading expert on seniors' care, will discuss how Ontario's publicly funded health-care system can be improved.
Clark, who governed B.C. from 2011 until 2017, will share her insights building an election platform that can help boost economic growth while improving health and education and protecting the environment.
Keesmaat, runner-up to John Tory in the 2018 Toronto mayoral race, will speak to how Ontario can tackle its housing crisis.
While the next Ontario election is scheduled for June 2026, Ford, who leads in every public-opinion poll, has been considering a vote for next year.
"Stay tuned," the premier said coyly in May.
In June, NDP Leader Marit Stiles ralled her party faithful, noting the last time a sitting premier with a majority in the legislature called a snap election, the New Democrats won.
Recalling the 1990 vote triggered by then Liberal premier David Peterson, who was well ahead in the polls until the campaign, Stiles reminded Ford that Bob Rae's NDP came from behind to win.
"If you're going to call an election ... early, I'm ready to fight that election on the things that matter to the people of Ontario," she said June 15.
"So bring it."
Peterson, now chair of Torstar, the Toronto Star's parent company, was circumspect when asked in May about his decision to call an early election 34 years ago.
"Every single situation is different - different players, different times and I'm in no position to judge," said the former premier, who governed from 1985 to 1990.
"If I was so smart, I'd still be there," he joked.
CAPTION: Ontario Liberal Leader Bonnie Crombie speaks to the Save the Science Centre rally crowd in June.

Full text: Good morning! It's Aug. 21, here are the top stories today in the Hamilton area.
Today's traffic and weather
Expect another cooler day. Environment Canada calls for mostly sunny weather with a high of 22 C at the airport weather station. There are no weather alerts.
TRAFFIC UPDATES:
Find the latest provincial road closures and traffic incidents via Ontario 511. Closures in Hamilton are available on the Hamilton police incident feed.
Taking transit today? Find the latest GO Transit service updates here. The latest information on local bus services are available here: Hamilton, Burlington and Oakville.
Top stories on The Spec:'A voice in my head told me ... '
In the months leading up to Jackie Buckle's murder, the son who killed her sought help in hospital on 18 occasions and received 16 different diagnoses. Susan Clairmont examines the failures of the mental-health and justice systems, and the in-depth psychiatric report that provides insight into the mind of a killer who was increasingly dangerous as his mother tried, but failed, to help.
Fallout study looks at pollution residue
Many Hamiltonians have learned air pollution can come in all the colours of the rainbow in a city prone to industrial fallout. The types of fallout differ, but the questions are often the same: "What's this grime?" A new study could give residents some answers.
'I'm not sold on these safe injection sites'
Hamilton's only supervised drug-consumption site - along with several others in Ontario - will close under new provincial rules. A new restriction on where Consumption and Treatment Services can operate, prohibits sites within 200 metres of schools and child-care centres. A spokesperson for Premier Doug Ford's office confirmed that this ban will shutter Hamilton Urban Core Community Health Centre's CTS.
Roofing scammers look legitimate: police
Two men from the United Kingdom have been arrested in what Hamilton police say is a roofing repair scam that targeted seniors and vulnerable people. The pair allegedly used "high-pressure tactics" and a company name that closely resembled a legitimate roofing company.
Also, a Port Dover clothing shop is picking up the pieces after a car crashed into the storefront.

Document 159 of 500

Ford's Greenbelt woes are no laughing matter

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/fords-greenbelt-woes-are-no-laughing-matter/docview/3095021037/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=unknown&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Ford%27s+Greenbelt+woes+are+no+laughing+matter&amp;title=The+Spectator&amp;issn=11899417&amp;date=2024-08-21&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.8&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Spectator&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Doug Ford is having one funny summer. Funny as in politically peculiar - without having much fun or hearing many laughs from the home crowd.
First, he appealed to people not to "go pooping" on Wasaga Beach. It made late-night TV in the U.S. as a laugh line, but locals squirmed. Then Ford joked a new animal hospital in King City could reduce lineups for humans needing health care.
After the negative headlines, the premier was poised to party last Friday night, giving out free food and drinks to a "Ford Fest" crowd in Milton. But just before party time, it was quitting time for popular education minister Todd Smith, who left a big hole in cabinet by bailing out on short notice.
If the summer of 2024 feels unfunny, the summer of 2025 might be that much more miserable. For all the fuss about Ford's latest wisecracks in the news media, there is bigger (and badder) news behind the scenes.
As the Toronto Star's Robert Benzie has revealed, a quiet RCMP investigation is fast gaining momentum, gathering critical statements about alleged wrongdoing in the $8.28-billion Greenbelt imbroglio of two summers ago.
Interviews with members of the RCMP "O" Division's Sensitive and International Investigations unit, the elite Ottawa-based branch that probes political crimes and corruption, are taking place. Sources say current and former staff in Ford's Progressive Conservative government are systematically being questioned about the controversial land swap.
The probe comes in the wake of a devastating report by the provincial auditor general last summer, which placed an $8.28-billion value on the windfall from Ford's Tories opening up previously protected lands to development. Ford has gone back and forth on the Greenbelt and his apologia eased the public pressure - his polling numbers bounced back to record highs. But the police pressure isn't going away.
Indeed, the RCMP probe appears to be a major consideration behind Ford's remarkable game plan to call an election as early as next spring. That would be more than a year ahead of the fixed election date mandated by law for June 2026.
What political pretext could Ford's Tories conjure up to seek a fresh mandate? How could a government that won re-election with a massive majority a mere two years ago justify going back into pre-election mode so early in the legislative cycle?
Ford's priority, bizarrely, is the boutique issue of beer in corner stores, given his determination to "keep the promise" he made in 2018 after allowing it to languish for so many years. That decision came at considerable cost - hundreds of millions of dollars to pacify the Beer Store (and the big brewers that own it) in order to move up the 2026 end date for a previous contract governing beer sales.
There are other distortions and contortions tied to Ford's fear of an RCMP probe. Remember that the premier came to power by promising to clean up what he claimed was corruption by the previous Liberal government of Kathleen Wynne giving preferential treatment to insiders and political friends, though he never produced any evidence; now the tables may be turned on the Tories.
The allegations of racketeering and distractions of electioneering are stressing out the Tories, and the loss of influential cabinet ministers doesn't help: Smith's departure will weaken the government at the very time it is trying to put its best face(s) forward. As Ontario's long-serving energy minister, he kept the Tories out of trouble by disarming the opposition and stakeholders alike. But Smith was reluctant to soldier on when shuffled to the education portfolio this summer.
The ministerial heavyweights who left before him - Monte McNaughton, Rod Phillips and Christine Elliott - have also left big gaps at the cabinet table and on the front benches.
Ford may yet prove the doubters wrong, romping to yet another election victory next spring. Perhaps he'll be sitting pretty by next summer, should the RCMP come calling for top Tories in and out of government. But it doesn't sound like much fun.
Martin Regg-Cohn
covers provincial politics and international affairs for Torstar.

Document 160 of 500

Hamilton's supervised drug-use site to close under new rules: Premier's office

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/hamiltons-supervised-drug-use-site-close-under/docview/3095248011/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Hamilton%27s+supervised+drug-use+site+to+close+under+new+rules%3A+Premier%27s+office&amp;title=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-08-20&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: A supervised drug-consumption site in Hamilton - along with others in Ontario - will close under tighter restrictions as the province pivots to a new model to tackle addictions, mental illness and homelessness.
The Conservative government unveiled its $379-million plan to open 19 "homelessness and addiction recovery treatment (HART) hubs" Tuesday during the Association of Municipalities of Ontario (AMO) conference in Ottawa.
Health Minister Sylvia Jones said consumption and treatment services (CTS) would be barred within 200 metres of schools and child-care centres.
That means Hamilton Urban Core Community Health Centre's CTS and nine others among 17 across Ontario will have to close by March 2025.
Mayor Andrea Horwath expressed trepidation over the loss of life-saving CTS sites but welcomed the HART hubs as a sign that the province "actually acknowledges that it has to do more" when it comes to helping municipalities respond to intersecting crises.
"That they have not been good partners and they need to step up," Horwath said in a call from Ottawa, where she is attending the conference with other city officials this week.
The devil will be in the details, the mayor suggested, explaining the model must come with "robust" funding and a provincial commitment to amplify it if it falls short.
"It could help us save lives. It could help us reduce the crises that are unfolding in our cities."
Meanwhile, experts in addiction medicine and harm reduction argue the government's announcement to close consumption sites is a setback that will inflict more harm on those struggling with addiction.
A CTS site provides people who use illicit drugs a place to consume under the supervision of trained staff in an effort to prevent overdose deaths - but it also connects them with other health and social services.
"The closing of those sites is now going to result in people likely using substances in an unsupervised manner and increasing the risk of an overdose occurring with no one around to respond, so I anticipate we're going to see a worsening of the overdose crisis locally," Dr. Robin Lennox, head of service for St. Joseph's Healthcare Hamilton's substance-use service, told The Spectator.
The loss of sanitary spaces that provide sterile equipment, such as needles and cookers, is another problem, added Lennox, who noted concerns over rising rates of serious conditions like endocarditis, an infection of the heart valves that intravenous drug use can cause.
Jones, however, described supervised consumption services as detrimental to the plight of people struggling with addiction.
"It feels like every day we turn on the news and learn more about the tragic outcome of an approach that accepts and promotes the use of illegal drugs over treatment and recovery."
Jones, who also associated consumption sites with spikes in crime, said the HART hubs aspire to "shift addiction toward a model that emphasizes recovery, treatment and community safety."
They will combine a range of services: primary care, addiction and mental-health treatment, social services and add "up to 375 supportive housing units."
"I want to be clear. They will not provide 'safer' supply (of drugs), supervised drug consumption or needle-exchange programs," Jones emphasized.
The province plans a call for proposals later this month to establish as many as 10 HART hubs. CTS operators who face closure will be given priority to transition to the model, she noted.
Urban Core opened Hamilton's only CTS at its former longtime centre at the old Rebecca Street bus station downtown initially as a temporary site in 2018.
In 2021, Urban Core relocated the service to St. Paul's Presbyterian Church, where it was supposed to operate under a temporary lease while displaced from Rebecca Street due the property owner's residential development plans and until it could open its new home on Cannon Street East.
It's not clear why the CTS hasn't yet moved to the health centre's new location.
Executive director Sandra Ezepue didn't respond to The Spectator's request for comment Tuesday.
The local YWCA also operates a safer drug-use space at its MacNab Street South location in partnership with Keeping Six and the Hamilton Social Medicine Response Team, but under a different regulatory model.
It's not a CTS, but a temporary urgent public health needs site under the auspices of Health Canada.
CEO Medora Uppal said doesn't believe the provincial announcement affects the YWCA's program.
"It's absolutely been life-saving," Uppal said of the initiative for women and gender-diverse people that opened in April 2022.
The safer-use initiative and the CTS at nearby St. Paul's have improved safety for people who use drugs and those in the community by focusing consumption inside their supervised environments, she said.
"So to me, this is not a solution that is going to address a problem of safety for children and families," Uppal said of the increased provincial restrictions on sites.
Over the years, Hamilton has struggled to open more CTS operations, despite a staff report that called for more than one site.
In late 2023, the local AIDS Network (now called the Positive Health Network) confirmed it would no longer pursue a CTS on Barton Street East amid a provincial review.
The government's pause on applications was among the factors that influenced the agency's decision to withdraw its application for a supervised site at 746 Barton St. E., executive director Tim McClemont said at the time.
At the request of partners, including the city, the AIDS Network took on the application - a two-year effort that came with no additional funding - while maintaining its other services, he noted.
"And we just couldn't see when this was going to be resolved."
Teviah Moro is a reporter at The Hamilton Spectator. tmoro@thespec.com
CAPTION: Hamilton Urban Core Community Health Centre's supervised drug-use site at St.St. Paul's Presbyterian Church on James Street South will close under new provincial rules, Premier Doug Ford's office says.Health Minister Sylvia Jones is expected to announce new rules for consumption and treatment services sites in Ontario this afternoon.

Subject: Public health; Presbyterian churches
