Document 172 of 500

Why are some dentists still brushing off the new federal dental plan?: They are among Canada's top earners, but some oral health care providers aren't smiling about getting paid a little less to clean your teeth

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/why-are-some-dentists-still-brushing-off-new/docview/3093816163/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Why+are+some+dentists+still+brushing+off+the+new+federal+dental+plan%3F%3A+They+are+among+Canada%27s+top+earners%2C+but+some+oral+health+care+providers+aren%27t+smiling+about+getting+paid+a+little+less+to+clean+your+teeth&amp;title=The+Spectator&amp;issn=11899417&amp;date=2024-08-17&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=G.1&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Spectator&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Breakups are never easy, but this one was especially painful.
We'd been together 20 years. He knew my intense anxiety about going to the dentist (thanks to a traumatic incident when I was five and a mean, old small-town dentist slapped my face after I cried when he pulled out a tooth).
Dr. D., let's call him, happens to also be movie-star handsome. Beyond his good looks, he is gentle and kind and patient.
When he said, "open wide," I happily complied.
Last month, we parted without saying goodbye.
It turns out our bond wasn't as strong as I hoped.
Blame the new Canadian Dental Care Plan (CDCP), a federal benefit first introduced by the NDP and passed by the Liberal government that will provide free dental care for basic services such as cleanings, fillings, X-rays and extractions for millions of Canadians who can't afford the hefty cost of such care.
Dr. D. chose not to be part of the CDCP, not even for long-standing patients like me. His secretary broke the news. When I asked her if he could give me a call so we could talk about it, she said, "He suggests you call the Ontario Dental Association (ODA) with your concerns."
Dr. D. ghosted me - this, after 20 years of me and my kids sitting in his chair. This, after he earned tens of thousands of dollars from our family's dental insurance plan over that time.
The program is free for eligible people under 18 or 65 and older with a net family income of less than $90,000 and no access to dental insurance.
Next year it will be rolled out for eligible Canadians of any age, which means our country will have joined the likes of Norway and Sweden, who have progressive dental care policies that relieve the suffering of millions of uninsured people unable to afford the high cost of taking care of their teeth.
No one in this day and age should have to worry about having to afford to pay for a filling or a tooth extraction. No one should have to go to emergency when they are in excruciating dental pain because they can't afford a dentist. No one should have to cover their mouth when they speak or lose out on employment opportunities because they are ashamed of how their teeth look. No kid should have to forgo teeth cleaning for years and then deal with the long-term consequences of poor oral health (including diabetes, heart disease and other chronic conditions) when they are older because their parents couldn't afford a dentist.
If you have the means to pay for your kids' braces or your own root canal, consider yourself privileged. Lots of Canadians, including the 50 per cent of lower-income Canadians without health benefits, can't afford to do the same.
Dental care shouldn't be a luxury. It's a necessity. But apparently not all dentists think so.
Back in June, only 50 per cent of dentists had signed up for the CDCP. The others complained the paperwork was too complicated (this, from people who have eight years of higher education?). Last month, Health Minister Mark Holland simplified things, telling them they could submit individual claims for reimbursement without officially joining the CDCP. Now 70 per cent of dentists are in the program.
So, what's with the other 30 per cent?
Some people think it comes down to money, but Dr. Maneesh Jain, president of the ODA, says it ain't so.
"Dentists are not comfortable signing contracts that could be unilaterally changed by Health Canada," he said in an interview, while also noting that dentists fear being audited by the government if they signed up for the program.
Why would they be audited?
"If they suspect fraud," he said.
That seems like a good reason, doesn't it?
It was like pulling teeth to get him to admit that signing up for the CDCP will result in lost income for dentists. When pressed, he finally conceded, "Obviously it's a subsidy, and so some patients are going to pay less."
There are dentists who will tell you that it is indeed about the money.
Dr. A. (he asked that his last name not be used because he does not want to be seen as publicly criticizing members of his profession) is a recently retired Ontario dentist who operated a few clinics in rural communities. He notes that dentists who sign up for the CDCP will receive about 10 per cent less than what the dental association fee guides suggest for basic services.
Some dentists - who happen to be among the highest income-earning professionals in the country with personal corporations that pay them anywhere from $200,000 to $500,000 a year (often far more than family physicians) - think that is too high a price to pay.
Dr. A put it bluntly: "I don't think most dentists mind taking a 10-per cent cut. If a filling is normally $200, they will now get $180. The real fear is that two years from now the government might change the rules and reduce their fee to $100," he says. "In the end, it comes down to money."
He also suggests something more sinister may be at work - namely, that some dentists don't want lower-income people in their chairs.
"Some dentists spend $1 million or more on their upscale clinics to attract a certain clientele. They'd rather fill their chair with people paying full price for lucrative cosmetic dentistry rather than basic stuff like fillings."
However, he also notes some dentists may stand to gain more income by seeing all those patients who never would have gone to them in the first place because they couldn't afford it.
"Eventually, the majority of dentists will accept this plan because it is a competitive market and if the dentist across street is accepting it, they will too because some money is better than no money," he said.
(Dentistry as a profession has a long unfortunate history of being resistant to government plans for dental care. Dentists did not want to be part of Medicare in the 1960s and opted out of OHIP in the 1970s so that they would not have to accept government oversight and set fees.)
When a privileged professional class chooses to resist a subsidized program because they will face a tiny loss in earnings, you have to wonder about their commitment to their existing clients who were loyal patients for years. You have to wonder about their commitment to the health of kids and older people or anyone, for that matter, who can't afford high dental costs. You have to wonder why they got into the profession in the first place.
It's time for that 30 per cent of withholding dentists to do their part - if they truly care about the health of Canadians then they should put their money where their mouth is.
As for me, I'm still getting over my breakup with my dentist, but I've found a new partner in my oral health care - Dafna Czudner, a regulated dental hygienist who operates Healing Roots on Dundurn Street in Hamilton. A couple of weeks ago, I went for a cleaning (I paid $150 compared to my dentist's usual $270) and, yes, she is part of the CDCP.
"I am excited that the public has this program in place," says Czudner. "This is a big opportunity for hygienists like myself to help more patients."
All dentists would do well to take a cue from her.
Anne Bokma is an author, journalist, and writing coach in Hamilton. Find her at annebokma.com.

She questions why more dentists haven't signed up for the Canadian Dental Care Plan.Minister of Health Mark Holland makes an announcement on the Canadian Dental Care Plan at a dental office in Ottawa, on Aug. 7. About 70 per cent of dentists are now in the program.DreamstimeJustin Tang Canadian Press file photo

Document 173 of 500

Preserving our history shouldn't been viewed as just more red tape: We cannot go back to the time when Indigenous archaeological sites and burials could be bulldozed away for the sake of progress

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/preserving-our-history-shouldnt-been-viewed-as/docview/3093808659/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=unknown&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Preserving+our+history+shouldn%27t+been+viewed+as+just+more+red+tape%3A+We+cannot+go+back+to+the+time+when+Indigenous+archaeological+sites+and+burials+could+be+bulldozed+away+for+the+sake+of+progress&amp;title=The+Spectator&amp;issn=11899417&amp;date=2024-08-17&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.17&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Spectator&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Ontario needs homes.
Doug Ford has set some aggressive goals for increasing the housing supply, which by all metrics are not being met. Indeed, new housing builds have gone down since 2022. Generationally-high mortgage rates and a lack of affordability in the market are mostly to blame.
However, there is a temptation in some quarters to blame red tape for the lack of progress. As anyone who's had any dealings with government knows, bureaucracy can be infuriatingly slow. That said, when stakeholders talk about eliminating "absurd" elements of the development process, exactly what are they taking aim at?
I earn my living doing archeology for the development industry. For some people, the red tape may be me. Here in Ontario, anyone proposing to develop land must carry out an archeological assessment to ensure no significant archeological remains will be impacted (read destroyed) by the project. It's not a job that pays particularly well, but it feels like a vital service.
The archeological history of Ontario stretches back at least 13,000 years, and for 12,600 of them, it was exclusively Indigenous. Those people lived lightly on the land. Much of their archeological legacy consists of stone tools, ceramics and, more rarely, items made of more perishable materials such as antler, bone and wood.
The further back in time we explore, the less likely it is that organic items will be preserved. Even so, after countless generations living here, there is a surprisingly rich and varied archeological record that encompasses items that range from the prosaic (debris left by stone tool manufacture) to high art (ceramic effigies, bone and antler carvings, etc.). For an archeologist, the material you find here is every bit as exciting and interesting as anything from the "Old World."
The fact Ontario does not come quickly to mind when one thinks about archeology is an unfortunate legacy of old colonial stereotypes that sought to portray Indigenous cultures as primitive and unworthy of comparison with the "great" cultures of Eurasia. Archaeologists here know better, but we've seldom done a great job of passing that knowledge on to the public. Perhaps, too, that public simply wasn't interested in what we had to say. Either way, those stereotypes became the basis for public policy regarding Indigenous archeology.
For most of our shared history, little thought was given to preserving Indigenous archeological sites (including burials). Accordingly, over the past two centuries, untold thousands of them were unceremoniously scoured from the landscape in the name of development. In response to public discomfort with the situation, Ontario passed the Heritage Act in 1975. It afforded some legal protection to known archeological sites. Over the next decade, archeological assessments became a requirement of most development, transportation, energy and resource projects.
As archeology has grown, so has the interest among First Nations in playing a stewardship role over their own history. Most Indigenous communities now employ staff whose job it is to monitor archeological work and ensure it is being done in a careful and respectful way. The costs for this, and the archeology itself, is added to the myriad other charges developers must pay if they are to see their projects taken to completion.
I have no doubt it is onerous. But there is no easy fix for it. We cannot go back to the time when Indigenous archeological sites and burials could be bulldozed away for the sake of progress. Even now, the development process is heavily freighted toward getting archeological sites out of the way. If anything, the system needs to get better at conserving archeological sites - not digging them all up.
Archaeology is one cost among many in the development process. Other professionals are there to protect the environment and to ensure essential services, such as roads, power, water and sewers, work so well that we can have the luxury of hardly ever thinking about them. Which of these is red tape? Which should we ignore?
Paul Racher is managing principal at Archaeological Research Associates Ltd.
CAPTION: The fact that Ontario does not come quickly to mind when one thinks about archaeology is an unfortunate legacy of old colonial stereotypes, writes archeologist Paul Racher, seen at a dig.For most of our shared history, little thought was given to preserving Indigenous archeological sites including burials.Paul Racher photoDave Carter Torstar file photo

Education minister departs for private sector

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/education-minister-departs-private-sector/docview/3093808633/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Education+minister+departs+for+private+sector&amp;title=The+Spectator&amp;issn=11899417&amp;date=2024-08-17&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.12&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Spectator&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: TORONTO Ontario Education Minister Todd Smith resigned his seat and from cabinet Friday to accept a job in the private sector less than three months after being given the education portfolio, prompting Premier Doug Ford to shuffle his cabinet.
Smith has served in cabinet since Ford's government was first elected in 2018, but spent the longest amount of time in the energy portfolio.
Sources who were not authorized to publicly discuss the issue have told The Canadian Press that Smith was not happy about the move to education in a cabinet shuffle in June, following three years as energy minister.
Ford named Jill Dunlop as the new education minister. She moves to the portfolio after being colleges and universities minister for three years. Nolan Quinn, who was promoted to cabinet a little over two months ago as associate minister of forestry, takes over as minister of colleges and universities.
Kevin Holland will move from the backbenches to become associate minister of forestry and Ford also created a new cabinet position, naming Graham McGregor as associate minister of auto theft and bail reform. That brings the size of his cabinet to 37 people.
Smith served as the representative for Bay of Quinte and was first elected when the Conservatives were in Opposition in 2011.

Document 175 of 500

A 'serial pooping problem' or something else? What's really behind the rumours about people defecating at Wasaga Beach

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/serial-pooping-problem-something-else-whats/docview/3093808651/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=A+%27serial+pooping+problem%27+or+something+else%3F+What%27s+really+behind+the+rumours+about+people+defecating+at+Wasaga+Beach&amp;title=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-08-16&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: WASAGA BEACH, Ont. - It might be the busiest summer season in 18 years of operation for Grandma's Beach Treats, a milestone measured in homemade butter tarts and scoops of ice cream headed out the door, with visitors drawn to the sand two blocks away that just happens to be part of the longest freshwater beach in the world.
This summer, Wasaga Beach has seen its highest number of tourists in a decade, with visitation rising to 20 per cent above even pre-pandemic levels. It's a welcome boon to the Ontario tourist town on the lip of Georgian Bay, a beachside retreat that has staked its reputation on selling, as one town official recently put it, "family-friendly fun."
But as the crowds have swelled and then settled in, so too has a persistent rumour - that some of those visitors are pooping in the sand.
Locally, it's been debated and joked about. Mayor Brian Smith, who called the rumour "misinformation" in a public statement earlier this month, declined an interview about what he termed a "non-issue," and pointed to the more pressing problems the town is dealing with, like "crumbling" park infrastructure. Premier Doug Ford was pulled into the fray too when, in response to a question, he noted that while there "wasn't 100 per cent proof" of any incidents, he still implored, "Folks, don't go pooping on the beach, simple as that, man." Most news coverage treated the story as a simple scuffle over beach etiquette, after concerns were raised by a local citizen who first posted the allegation to TikTok. Even talk show host Stephen Colbert made a joke about it. But the conversation online goes much deeper - and much darker - than that, and shows how local issues can get co-opted to push racist narratives on social media.
"The whole poop thing, it's a difficult thing to talk about, because the reality is, we don't want people to focus on such a small issue for an incredible community like Wasaga Beach," said Mark Winegarden, the co-owner of Grandma's. "When huge issues like this come up, whether it's huge or not, sometimes it's how it's responded to that makes it huge."
****
When Natasha Lynn sat down and opened TikTok on July 9, she was fired up over the fact that her friend had told her she'd seen someone defecating on the beach. "I've decided that I am going to start doing segments. I don't know when I'm going to start, maybe this weekend, because I need the weather to be really nice for this to be a thing," she says emphatically in a video post, her phone capturing her seated in front of a brick wall.
"I'm going to go start catching people pooping on the beach. I'm going to do it. I'm not scared of people."
According to other videos on her page, Lynn - who asked that her last name not be used because of online harassment, but posts under the handle @itsnattylxnn2.0 - has lived in Wasaga Beach for almost five years.
In some videos, she explained how she could see people set up tents from her backyard and when they left, fecal matter would be left behind. She described how she wouldn't let her kids play on Beach 1, a heavily trafficked strip of stores and restaurants known locally as the Main End, and that other locals wouldn't either. Her concerns were swiftly picked up by news organizations, who described her as a local resident and mother of three.
"It's not locals doing it, it is people coming from out of town and it is immigrants that think it's OK to dig a hole and pop a squat," she claims in a video posted July 10.
What was not mentioned in media stories and the general conversation were her theories about who exactly was responsible for the bad beach behaviour. "There is people using the beaches and parks as outhouses and I don't think I can talk about it because they are immigrants. I'm scared to be called racist," she wrote in a video posted on July 11. A few days earlier, she'd been more specific, saying it was "Indian families" who were responsible for digging holes on the beach and pooping in them.
Lynn is adamant she is not racist - one of her children has Indian heritage, she points out - and that she sees her videos as speaking up for her community. She denies she is targeting South Asian people in general and that, if anyone is to blame for the issue, it's the government for increasing immigration numbers. (As she put it in one video, "This is not about everyone that's an immigrant, it's about the ones who bring bad habits from where they came from with them.")
She said most of the feedback to her posts has been supportive, particularly from other town residents. (Many other residents who spoke to the Star said they were appalled by the videos.)
"I may not have a lot of money but I have a lot of care to give," she wrote in the caption of a video on July 25. "For once in my life I feel heard. I feel seen."
Still, actual evidence for these claims is thin. In a recent phone call with the Star, Lynn said she's been recognized from her videos, which has made it challenging, particularly as a mother of young children, to get down to the beach to monitor behaviour as she'd planned. When pressed for more concrete evidence that defecation is a widespread problem, she said her videos speak for themselves and hung up on a Star reporter.
Her claims continue to be swiftly denied by authorities, by the town and by government officials - the waterfront strip is a provincial park, ensuring access to 14 kilometres of sandy shore - who said there was no evidence to back up her allegations. "Park wardens are being extra vigilant when patrolling park areas and beachfronts to ensure that visitors are being respectful of the park," noted an email from a spokesperson for Ontario Parks.
On a recent afternoon, many beachgoers said the rumours were concerning, ridiculous - funny, even - but it wasn't enough to keep them away from the sand. But still the possibility lingered.
"I've heard it a few times," said one longtime resident, who didn't want to be named because of fears over ruffling feathers in a small town, as she was sitting in a chair reading a novel in a slightly less crowded area of the beach. "Enough talk that, yeah, I do, I do believe it," she added. "Maybe I'm wrong to believe it without seeing proof. But when enough people start talking about something, you start to think maybe there was something to it."
"Maybe nobody's doing that, and everybody's blaming an entire culture of something they're not doing. But where did it start and why?"
****
Regardless of her original intentions, Lynn's videos have been taken out of her hands and used to bolster the anti-South Asian sentiment that is increasingly being pushed in this country.
"Wasaga Beach has gained a lot of attention in certain kinds of Canada-focused, far-right social media spaces," says Peter Smith, a researcher and journalist with the Canadian Anti-Hate Network.
"In particular, South Asian people are being blamed both in the videos that went viral, and kind of writ large for a number of other things by the far right."
Lynn's videos were quickly used to prop up a growing sense on far-right social media in Canada that people of South Asian descent - men who had immigrated from India, in particular - were responsible for an outsize chunk of society's problems, Smith says. The specific phrase "they have to go back" has become popular.
Now the idea that people from India are pooping on Canadian beaches is being used to bolster that narrative, and has been widely shared by X (formerly Twitter) accounts associated with the right. "I don't think this is about race, I think this is about massively irresponsible immigration just coming to fruition," says a video posted by Real Toronto Newz, an account with half a million followers on Instagram.
On a livestream for her more than 700,000 followers devoted to discussing the perceived problems of "mass immigration" and the recent riots in the U.K. - which are believed to have been spurred by social media posts - alt-right YouTuber Lauren Southern also referred to Canada's "serial pooping problem" being caused by new immigrants.
The Wasaga Beach claims neatly feed a wave of anti-South Asian sentiment that goes back a few months, Smith argues. The Global Project Against Hate and Extremism, a U.S.-based organization that tracks far right movements, argues the focus on South Asians found root this spring in Prince Edward Island after a student protest against new rules limiting the extension of work permits. Members of Diagolon, a Canadian white supremacist group, are now using anti-Indian messages in their recruitment and training, the organization argues.
There are all sorts of legitimate debates about immigration - how it affects taxes, or schools, say - but the conversation tips into racism when it starts pushing the idea that people from a certain country are dirty or smell or are otherwise scapegoated for widespread issues, said Heidi Beirich, one of the group's co-founders.
Anti-immigration sentiment is on the rise in many parts of the world, including Europe and in the U.S., but tends to take different forms depending on the country, Beirich said. While in the U.S. the focus is on Latinos, Beirich argues that in Canada it's South Asians who currently bear the brunt, given the number of people here with roots in that part of the world.
"It's the same as when Trump and many people on the far right (in the U.S.) call immigrants 'invaders' and 'criminals,'" she said. "We're not having a reasonable discussion about immigration, we're having a discussion about race."
It's an increasingly toxic sentiment that is now arguably leaching back into the real world. In early August, the World Sikh Organization raised the alarm about what it described as the overlap between Canadian alt-right groups and Indian nationalist social media content, which were both amplifying and promoting "anti-Sikh hate content" online. According to Statistics Canada, reported hate crimes targeting the South Asian community grew by 143 per cent between 2019 and 2022.
****
Adsaya Jegatheesan and her four childhood friends refused to let hearsay ruin their much-anticipated beach day.
Beneath blue skies Thursday, the 21- and 22-year-old friends lay on beach blankets listening to music as they enjoyed their spaghetti and meatball lunch. For most of them, it was their first visit to the beach town this year.
This year, Wasaga Beach will celebrate its 50th anniversary, marking a half century since the farming and logging outpost officially incorporated, paving the way for a defined community, but one that depends on the business of outsiders, too. These days, even mid-week afternoons see a diverse crowd of young families, couples and friends set up shop in the sand, music blasting as food trucks peddle ice cream and burritos and Italian sausages on a bun.
The friends had made a plan two weeks ago to take a day off work and make the two-hour drive north from Scarborough - right around the time they came across Lynn's viral video. "There was no proof. It was way too crazy," Jegatheesan said.
While the group agreed their happy feelings about Wasaga Beach were largely intact, they mused that the anti-immigration rhetoric directed at South Asian people specifically can be dangerous, and questioned why people are "so easy" to blame one ethnic group over others.
"It can be dangerous," Jegatheesan added. "Some people believe it."
CAPTION: People cross the street to get to Wasaga Beach on Aug. 14, 2024.Wasaga Beach, shown on Aug. 14, 2024.Public washrooms at Wasaga Beach, shown on Aug. 14, 2024.A park warden patrols Wasaga Beach on Aug. 14, 2024.

Subject: Extremism; Asian people; White supremacy; Parks & recreation areas; Racism; Video recordings; Immigrants; Immigration; Beaches; Social networks

Business indexing term: Subject: Social networks

Location: Canada; India

Company / organization: Name: TikTok Inc; NAICS: 518210

Title: A 'serial pooping problem' or something else? What's really behind the rumours about people defecating at Wasaga Beach

Publication title: The Hamilton Spectator (Online); Hamilton

Publication year: 2024

Publication date: Aug 16, 2024

Section: politics/federal

Publisher: Torstar Syndication Services, a Division of Toronto Star Newspapers Limited

Place of publication: Hamilton

Document 176 of 500

Doug Ford's 'Don't be pooping on the beach' comment met with laughs on Stephen Colbert show

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/doug-fords-dont-be-pooping-on-beach-comment-met/docview/3093582554/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Doug+Ford%27s+%27Don%27t+be+pooping+on+the+beach%27+comment+met+with+laughs+on+Stephen+Colbert+show&amp;title=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-08-16&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Premier Doug Ford's pooping plea has made it to late-night TV.
During a segment on Thursday night's "The Late Show with Stephen Colbert," the American comedian brought up comments made by Ford earlier this month during a press conference in Mississauga in which he said, "Don't be pooping on the beach," while addressing rumours of people defecating in the sand at Wasaga Beach, on Lake Huron outside Collingwood.
Almost two weeks later, the comment has made it south of the border on late night TV.
"I love. There's a lot to love there," Colbert said as audience members laughed after seeing a clip of Ford's press conference. "I particularly love how defeated he seems at having to say that out loud."
Colbert ended the bit by doing an impression of Ford saying, "Don't be showing your boobs to a moose."
Ford and others have stressed that the rumours of people pooping at Wasaga Beach, which is the longest freshwater beach in the world, are not confirmed. At the press conference earlier this month, Ford said the province has given the beach $1 million to build more bathrooms and cover the costs of repairs and upgrades to beach grooming equipment.
With files from Andy Takagi

Document 177 of 500

Doug Ford's education minister resigns after just 10 weeks on the job

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/doug-fords-education-minister-resigns-after-just/docview/3093582522/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Doug+Ford%27s+education+minister+resigns+after+just+10+weeks+on+the+job&amp;title=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-08-16&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Veteran Progressive Conservative MPP Todd Smith has resigned suddenly as Premier Doug Ford's education minister after just 10 weeks on the job.
Smith, who has represented Bay of Quinte since 2011, stepped down Friday in a surprise move that will trigger a byelection within six months.
The unexpected departure will force Ford to appoint a new education minister later Friday afternoon with schools set to reopen in a fortnight.
"The life of a politician is hard and takes its toll, especially when I was away from home as often as I've been over the past 13 years," said Smith, 53, who recently returned from a trip to Joshua Tree park near Palm Springs in the California desert with his wife, Tawnya, a vice-principal.
"To my constituents in Bay of Quinte and Prince-Edward-Hastings, thank you for your trust and support - which I am proud to say has grown significantly in each of the last four Ontario elections," he said in a statement Friday.
"I have made the very difficult decision to leave politics and accept a position in the private sector."
Contacted by the Star, the affable Smith, who is well-regarded on both sides of the house, said he would let his prepared statement stand, but emphasized there were no hard feelings with Ford.
"Love the premier and the team. Not a negative thing to say! It's been a great ride and the honour of a lifetime," he said.
Ford said he wished Smith well.
"From the bottom of my heart, I want to thank Todd for his many years of service to our government, our province and our party," the premier said in a statement.
"He leaves politics with a record that he can be incredibly proud of, including and especially fixing the previous government's energy policies and bringing electricity prices down for hardworking families and businesses," he said.
"Todd will always be a friend and I can't wait to watch his every success as he starts this next chapter of his life."
The former radio broadcaster has been a strong performer in the legislature's question period and has been a top cabinet minister since the Tories were elected in 2018, serving in economic development, consumer services, community and social services, and as house leader before becoming energy minister in 2021.
A source told the Star that Smith - who thrived at the energy ministry and had served as shadow energy minister in opposition - was displeased when Ford swapped him for Stephen Lecce in a June 6 cabinet shuffle.
"No Conservative would be happy being minister of education," said a Tory, who had previously predicted Smith would step away.
"I told you so," the insider, who spoke confidentially in order to discuss internal machinations, said Friday, moments after the MPP announced his resignation around 11 a.m.
The source said the fractious relations between Queen's Park and teachers' unions are especially tough on Tory education ministers.
Underscoring that, the Elementary Teachers Federation of Ontario said of Smith's exit, "Well, at least we now clearly know where his priorities were."
Ontario Secondary School Teachers Federation president Karen Littlewood threw some shade.
"Sadly, another loss to privatization from the public education sector," said Littlewood.
Another senior Tory confided that Smith "wasn't happy about being moved" from energy, an area where he had a lot of expertise.
At the time of the June shuffle, he was in Europe promoting Ontario's nuclear sector.
NDP Leader Marit Stiles said Smith was "throwing in the towel and heading to the private sector" just before schools reopen.
"Families deserve a government that will invest in schools, reduce class sizes and put students first. Instead it's more chaos and uncertainty from a government that always treats kids as an afterthought," said Stiles.
Liberal Leader Bonnie Crombie blasted the Tories.
"After six years of Doug Ford, our schools are overcrowded, and Ontario's economic growth is falling behind our rivals," said Crombie.
"We don't need a new education minister - we need a new government focused on giving more to children, educators, and families, not even more to Ford's rich friends."
Green Leader Mike Schreiner said it was "yet another blow to a government fraught with scandal."
"How many cabinet ministers will have to resign before the premier gets his head out of the sand, takes accountability, and starts delivering for ordinary people, not wealthy insiders?" said Schreiner.
Smith took his leave as Tories and supporters of the premier were set to gather Friday night in Milton for the annual Ford Fest picnic, which was the target of protests organized by education and public sector unions.
Ford has been ruminating about calling an early election next spring, a year before the scheduled June 2026 vote.
CAPTION: Todd Smith answers questions during the daily briefing at Queen's Park in Toronto in this 2020 file photo. He is stepping down.

Subject: Schools; Politics; Education; Teachers

Document 178 of 500

Provincial changes 'undermine' Hamilton's frozen urban boundary

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/provincial-changes-undermine-hamiltons-frozen/docview/3093582513/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Provincial+changes+%27undermine%27+Hamilton%27s+frozen+urban+boundary&amp;title=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-08-16&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: The city is bracing for a slew of urban-expansion bids with provincial changes opening the door to boundary-busting development of any size at any time in Hamilton.
The legislative moves threaten to undo council's decision to freeze its urban-rural dividing line in order to avoid sprawl into the farmland, city officials say.
And so the city must be well-armed for Ontario Land Tribunal (OLT) battles that more developers are bound to wage, Coun. John-Paul Danko says.
In fact, the "entire point" of Bill 185, the Cutting Red Tape to Build More Homes Act, is to "undermine the local decisions that were not in line" with the province's goal.
"They were forced to rescind their urban boundary expansion, so they're doing the same thing, but with a different mechanism," Danko told The Spectator.
In 2022, Premier Doug Ford's government imposed a 4,500-acre urban expansion on the city and opened up tracts of the Greenbelt for development.
Last year, in the face of scathing watchdog reports pointing to undue developer influence and an RCMP probe, the province walked back the boundary expansion and reversed the Greenbelt grabs.
This past June, Bill 185 came into effect, allowing developers to appeal council "nondecisions" or rejections of urban boundary applications to the OLT.
Also, a newly drafted provincial planning statement axes the requirement of comprehensive municipal land-use studies before boundary-expanding bids can be made.
That clears the way for urban expansion applications "at any time and with no limit on the size," a city report points out.
In response, the city is preparing requirements to evaluate urban expansion applications, creating a new team of staff to respond to them, setting new application fees and earmarking an additional $1.5 million to fight OLT appeals.
"We've been committed to implementing council's (firm-boundary) decision to the best of our ability," Steve Robichaud, acting general manager of planning and economic development, told The Spectator.
"So in no way does the adoption of that evaluation framework waver from the city's position that growth is to be accommodated within the existing urban area at this point."
If staff reject urban expansion applications, and developers file OLT appeals, the requirements will form the basis for cases before the provincial tribunal, Robichaud also noted.
Appeal rights for boundary expansions - excluding the Greenbelt - allow "development applications to be considered in a balanced manner," Bianca Meta, press secretary for Municipal Affairs and Housing Minister Paul Calandra, told The Spectator via email Thursday.
The draft provincial planning statement, for which the government sought feedback, is meant to bolster Ontario's housing supply and protect the environment, prime agricultural areas and public health and safety, Meta wrote.
"The government is carefully considering the input received and will determine whether changes are to be made in the final (statement)."
Hamilton's proposed framework presents considerations for boundary expansions, including their environmental, financial and land-use impact.
Staff plan to seek feedback from the public, agencies and First Nations this fall. In the meantime, the city plans to use the draft requirements for applications and OLT cases.
The city already faces appeals launched by developers hoping to build in the Twenty Road West and Whitechurch Road East areas in Glanbrook.
Another appeal relates recognizing Elfrida as a "priority area" for urban expansion.
City staff point out a key factor is servicing (think sewer pipes and pumping stations).
Accommodating urban expansions shouldn't reduce the capacity for existing residential or industrial development, Robichaud said. "You don't want to rob Peter to pay Paul."
Developers are expected to pay for the upfront costs of new infrastructure, but the city is on the hook for operational and maintenance expenses of services - from firefighting to snow-clearing and recreation centres - "in perpetuity," the city report notes.
The province, builders who own would-be expansion lands and industry groups have argued urban expansion is necessary if Ontario hopes to solve its housing-supply crunch.
In a letter to council, the West End Home Builders' Association says the provincial changes give the city "an additional opportunity to set up a framework for collaborative discussions to work together and move more quickly towards building more attainable housing supply in new complete communities."
City planners, councillors and anti-sprawl advocates, however, have insisted Hamilton has enough land in its existing urban area for growth to the year 2051.
This week, in supporting staff's recommendations, councillors emphasized it's important to keep the public up to speed on the urban boundary file.
Casting a wide net is crucial, added Ian Borsuk, executive director of Environment Hamilton.
"We should just be notifying the entire city as best that we can," said Borsuk, who called the provincial changes an "infuriating situation."
Teviah Moro is a reporter at The Hamilton Spectator. tmoro@thespec.com
CAPTION: Coun. John Paul Danko warns the city's frozen urban boundary is at risk under new provincial changes.A consortium of developers hopes to build homes in the Elfrida area.This map shows Hamilton's urban boundary and "white belt" lands, where developers aim to build homes along Twenty Road, White Church Road and in Elfrida. The city has frozen the urban boundary, making those areas off-limits to development.The Upper West Side Landowners Group aims to build homes on Twenty Road West near the Hamilton airport.

Document 179 of 500

Waterdown residents fear 'fortress' townhouse plan: Councillors delay decision on contested development

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/waterdown-residents-fear-fortress-townhouse-plan/docview/3093431123/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Waterdown+residents+fear+%27fortress%27+townhouse+plan%3A+Councillors+delay+decision+on+contested+development&amp;title=The+Spectator&amp;issn=11899417&amp;date=2024-08-16&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.1&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Spectator&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Aiming for a compromise, city councillors have delayed a decision on a staff-backed plan for 18 townhomes in Waterdown that has sparked a local outcry.
The hope is that a meeting between residents, city officials and concerned residents will bear fruit before the 30-day deferral period expires, Coun. Ted McMeekin says.
"I believe in ways forward," said McMeekin, who has sided with would-be neighbours of the proposed development in protesting the plan.
But other councillors argue Hawk Ridge Homes Inc.'s application for Dundas Street East will lead to badly needed homes during a housing crisis.
And an appeal to the Ontario Land Tribunal (OLT) if the proposal is rejected - with staff's favourable analysis on record - or if council fails to make a decision within 120 days, also looms.
In the meantime, local residents who oppose the plan are nervous about what could emerge beyond the fences of their sloping backyards.
"We feel like ants in an ant colony," Jennifer Gallant told The Spectator.
That's a reference to her family's feared loss of privacy with the two- and three-storey towns built on the higher-ground lots behind their single-detached home of 12 years.
Hawk Ridge Homes Inc. also plans to backfill the development property, build a retaining wall and erect a fence at its rear.
"It's going to be like a fortress ... It's going to be ugly," Gallant predicts.
Earlier this week, she and her neighbours made that point at city hall as councillors considered staff's recommendation to approve the application.
In addition to unease over loss of privacy and unpleasant views, the residents raised other concerns, which they say are not rooted in a not-in-my-backyard reflex, or NIMBYism.
Stormwater runoff from the higher site into their flood-prone backyards; increased traffic on a busy, cresting and dangerous stretch of Dundas Street East; parking congestion on their streets.
"Common sense must prevail and people must be prioritized over profits. Lives must be more important than the bottom line and residents' concerns must be taken into consideration," Kim Parkes-Hallmark told the planning committee Tuesday.
Hawk Ridge Homes Inc. looks for council's approval as the city aims to corral growth within Hamilton's urban area through residential intensification in an effort to meet housing targets and avoid sprawl into farmland.
Stephanie Card, a 20-year resident of the neighbourhood, says the sloping site is the sticking point.
"If Hawk Ridge was putting this on a flat piece of ground, I'm sure it would be a lovely development, but here it just doesn't make any sense," she told The Spectator.
Card, who has spearheaded a six-year campaign against the drawn-out proposal, noted 57 people have signed a petition and dozens of others have written city officials to express their disapproval.
A representative of the developer and city staff, however, say provisions in the blueprint and studies analyzing traffic, overlook, shadowing and stormwater management address residents' concerns.
Moreover, 18 townhouses along a transit route like Dundas Street East, close to the local public library, parks, schools and shopping represent sound planning.
"So all in all, it's a good location for intensification," developer consultant Gerry Tchisler told the planning committee.
But like McMeekin, other city politicians say they're not convinced, despite planning staff's take.
Hawk Ridge Homes Inc.'s proposal is "quite an intrusion of a very different esthetic," Coun. Jeff Beattie said.
In a recent split vote, council rejected another staff-supported application for 553 rental units at a boarded-up shopping plaza on Greenhill Avenue in the east end.
That decision became the focus of a federal Conservative campaign-style video that characterized it as municipal obstruction of housing, despite funding from the Liberal government to incentivize development.
Under the gaze of Premier Doug Ford's provincial government, it's important for the city to "walk the talk," Coun. Maureen Wilson offered during Tuesday's discussion.
Local officials and residents were vocal opponents of Ford's initial - but then walked-back - moves to open up the Greenbelt to development and expand Hamilton's urban area into farmland, despite the city's plan to freeze its boundary.
More housing is "key" to the city's economy, including when it comes to recruiting and retaining workers that keep it humming, Wilson said.
In years gone by, "it may have been appropriate" to reject certain applications based on resident discomfort, the west end councillor also said.
"But we know now that collectively when we say no, that's affecting everything from encampments to our economy."
Likewise, Coun. Craig Cassar argued Hawk Ridge Homes Inc.'s proposal must be viewed "from the big picture," which takes into account planning policy and the need for housing within the urban area.
Neighbourhoods of single-detached homes haven't changed for decades, the Ancaster councillor noted.
"But the reality is we can't expect that that's going to continue."
Owing to his predilection for "compromise," McMeekin said, he'd never opposed a development in Ward 15.
But if Hawk Ridge Homes Inc.'s plan were sound, dozens of residents wouldn't have written the city in protest or shown up to air their concerns, he maintained.
"Bottom line ... I have never seen a project proposal in Ward 15 that is less compatible in an existing neighbourhood than this one."
The developer held a community meeting in 2018 to discuss the project with local residents but hasn't done so again despite requests, he said.
In response, Tchisler told the committee Hawk Ridge Homes Inc. has done its "best to resolve the issues" and questioned "what else we can deliver" in another meeting.
Raising the spectre an OLT battle, Coun. John-Paul Danko said city politicians "just can't deny planning applications because we don't like them."
The tribunal "will inevitably overturn" council's rejection of the staff-backed application after a costly hearing, Danko warned.
Overwhelmingly, the OLT tends to side with developer interests, acknowledges Card but says local residents hope their case can buck the trend.
"Because we really feel strongly that this is an inappropriate location."
Teviah Moro is a reporter
at The Hamilton Spectator.
tmoro@thespec.com
CAPTION: Jennifer Gallant and her neighbours on Scott Street in Waterdown are opposed to a developer's plan to build 18 townhomes on a pair of lots that back onto their sloping backyards. The residents have cited an array of concerns, including flooding, traffic safety and privacy.City staff recommend the proposal but Coun. Ted McMeekin has sided with a group of concerned residents in opposing the plan.City councillors have delayed the decision on the townhomes proposal in Waterdown after pushback from residents.Teviah Moro photos The Hamilton SpectatorHawk Ridge Homes Inc.

Document 180 of 500

Readers weigh in on the state of the city's downtown

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/readers-weigh-on-state-citys-downtown/docview/3093427218/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=unknown&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Readers+weigh+in+on+the+state+of+the+city%27s+downtown&amp;title=The+Spectator&amp;issn=11899417&amp;date=2024-08-16&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.15&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Spectator&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: We asked Hamilton Spectator readers about their thoughts on the downtown in an online survey and, over a period of 24 hours, 335 people responded from around the city and beyond. Most respondents reported feeling unsafe, when asked to rate their feeling of safety in the core.
Here's a sample of the comments, edited for clarity:

"I have worked downtown for about 30 years. The downtown has struggled throughout this period but things were getting better before the pandemic. Since COVID, unfortunately, it has deteriorated. Many people who used to travel to the downtown to work, now work from home. Many restaurants and shops have closed.
"Condos have been built, but it doesn't seem that they are filling up with people. There has been negligible impact on the downtown economy thus far. It's sad to see but it will require a sustained effort from government, business and ordinary citizens to make the downtown a better place to be. Right now, it is not an attractive place either to live or work."
Franco Ciarlo,
Hamilton Mountain
"The most unsafe I have felt in my 50 years of living in Downtown Hamilton. Open drug use, repeat petty crime and regular shootings. I will no longer be having my relatives come and visit me."
James, Bay and Jackson
" ... We have to treat Gore Park with more respect. Stop treating that park like a soup kitchen, and move the services that are provided in the park to a location downtown that might be currently empty or underutilized. The park is beautiful and people need to feel safe and undisturbed sitting there. We also need to crack down, hard, on the owners of buildings surrounding Gore Park who have let their buildings become derelict."
John, West Mountain
"I'm hoping that with the new developments, things will pick up, and foot traffic, specifically when the sun sets, will encourage more people to feel safe."
Zachary, St. Clair
"King and James, the core's epicentre, should be the jewel of city, and looks like someone's nightmare or a post-apocalyptic wasteland."
Jill Hemingway,
West Mountain
"It's a complete dump. The abandoned buildings, graffiti, tents and open drug use is disgraceful. How can we look at the former Sir John A. Macdonald (school), Eaton Centre, Jamesville complex and other dishevelled areas and not come up with some sort of comprehensive solution to our housing crisis? How did it get to this?"
Julia, Hamilton
"Downtown revitalization has been buzzwords for the city for decades ... there is no revitalization, just decay that costs more and more of my taxes."
Jean, West Mountain
"It's unfortunate to see so many people doing so poorly. I wish I could take children to local parks without people living in them. I don't think that makes things unsafe for me though."
Doug MacKenzie, Beasley
"I was so proud of Hamilton and the changes it was making. I defended it to all my friends and would say you have to come and check it out. Over the last two to three years, my opinion has changed and I would no longer say that. I see human waste around the building I work in and other garbage including needles frequently. I have sat with younger people waiting for the bus around the MacNab terminal because they did not feel safe.
"I have called 911 several times for people in distress. I see people walking in traffic frequently. It just keeps getting worse with no end in sight."
Catherine, West Mountain
"Being drunk or high in public needs to be put to an end. If I was acting in that way, I would be arrested."
Joanne, North End
"I think downtown needs support in the form of rent control, safe-use sites, and other systemic changes to help people from falling through the cracks. I don't feel unsafe downtown because I know that the people the most in danger are the people who are unhoused, not me shopping at the market and going home."
Rachel, Durand
"Seems like the most overlooked area of the entire city when it comes to cleanliness. I've seen garbage in the same spot for weeks at a time."
Katie, Gage Park
"I love the downtown core of our city. It's gone through so many ups and downs and always recovers - but we seem to stagger out of the downs instead of charging out. Downtown needs to be an expressive and productive hub, but that requires planning, action and commitment."
Jeremy, Stoney Creek
"There shouldn't be so many services in the core which (creates clustering) of people who just feed off each other."
Debra Schreiber, Stipley
"I always have to have one eye on my property to make sure it's not getting stolen."
Greg, Hess Village Area
"What is needed is leadership from city council, a realistic vision, an appropriate mix of low-rise and highrise living, and commercial development that fulfils a real need. What must be avoided is turning it into a collection of highrise office towers and condos - in other words, a sunless wind tunnel that discourages foot traffic and removes any sense of community."
Jonathan Nicholls, Durand
" ... Outreach, shelters, etc., can't be in your immediate core when the city is so small and fledgling. It is not safe."
Jamie Roberts, Kirkendall
"There needs to be a bigger police presence, and more mental-health assistance to go with it."
Kim, Durand
"We have to stop fooling ourselves and see the offenders for what they really are: greedy speculators operating in investment and property holding firms.
"As a full-time working person who can't afford to live even in such an unappetizing place as this, I urge everyone to look well around and match the symptoms to the source."
J.B., Main Street West

"There have been several random shootings this summer, making it scary to be on the streets. I live in a retirement residence and one of the shootings saw the offenders literally running through the parking lot, requiring the police to keep us indoors."
Ruth, Downtown
"I can't wait to see the vibrancy that will hopefully come with the new residential buildings, the LRT, the entertainment district and the various other changes. I just hope we don't lose too many people before we see these changes."
Michael, Beasley
"Lane reductions are causing horrible congestion and the LRT is an extremely bad idea."
Adrian Phillips, Downtown (but planning to leave)
"I wish people weren't living in tents ... I would like to see more co-op housing or subsidized housing on city-owned land - instead of huge parking lots."
Jesse, St. Clair

"The provincial government isn't appropriately funding services that will help unhoused people and isn't prioritizing affordable housing. Ford needs to be held accountable."
Bob, Ainslie Wood West
"I am not without empathy but I firmly believe we cannot have people living wherever they want. Our parks are for the neighbouring taxpayers to use for themselves and their families. We need to have the homeless living in a 'controlled' environment no matter what/who has to bear the cost."
John, West Mountain
"I don't want to have to consider whether I will die from a stray bullet while I'm walking for coffee or lunch. This is extremely scary for myself and my co-workers"
Josh, Ward 2
"The city is a crazy wild jungle where you can't turn your back for a minute."
Melanie, North End

Document 181 of 500

Is Ford for the people or developers?

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/is-ford-people-developers/docview/3093426120/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=unknown&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Is+Ford+for+the+people+or+developers%3F&amp;title=The+Spectator&amp;issn=11899417&amp;date=2024-08-16&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.14&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Spectator&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: When politicians are on the campaign trail, they'll often say they're for the people. After they get elected, however, many seem to switch lanes and listen to powerful industries and their lobbyists. Why bother to vote at all then, many will say. Politicians are all the same, they say one thing to get elected, and do the opposite when in power.
Former U.S. president George H.W. Bush's famous promise was: "Read my lips. No new taxes." But that soon morphed into more taxes. Premier Doug Ford's slogan, "For The People" had wide appeal during the past elections, but after the Greenbelt land scandal, he's been often labelled as "for the developers."
The premier has a lot of explaining to do. What hasn't helped this pro-developer image is his government's recent legislation, the "Cutting Red Tape to Build More Homes Act." This law takes away the voices of ordinary citizens (third parties) to challenge municipal plans and zoning changes at the Ontario Land Tribunal (OLT).
These hearings usually take place in a courtroom-like setting and individuals or citizens groups presenting their views have, in the past, needed an experienced and costly lawyer to navigate the complex rules and procedures. But since Ford's new law he passed in June this year, no third-party appeals can be heard at the OLT. Very concerning for a premier who sold himself to us as a leader for the people.
Residents who object to the excessive height of a 90-storey condo building in their neighbourhood, for example, or the elimination of medical labs or doctors' offices, should be able to be heard in the planning process, even if their views are rejected in the end.
People who've had to take an issue to court to get it resolved are more likely to accept a negative outcome, if they think they've had a fair trial and a fair chance to be heard.
Minister of Municipal Affairs and Housing Paul Calandra said the purpose of the new legislation is to "remove obstacles" and cut red tape to build more homes faster. So it seems citizens are obstacles and red tape to be removed from the process. But these are the very people politicians will be asking to vote for them in the next election.
To find out what people want in particular areas of the city, why they moved there in the first place, it might be important to ask them and bring more of what consumers are asking for at prices they can afford.
There's a disconnect between what city officials and developers are planning and what consumers want, or can afford. Why not get more consumer input into housing problems then, and open discussions with all stakeholders, instead of legislating certain voices into silence?
Even if citizen views are not accepted in the final planning decisions, the fact that they've been heard is part of our democracy and part of building trust in our institutions.
To eliminate third-party appeals gives the impression the process may be rigged in favour of one party. It's clothed in all the language of building more homes faster, cheaper and cutting red tape, but it turns out part of the red tape being cut out is the public's voice.
In the midst of a housing crisis, this is a time when government needs to build public trust, not further decrease it, especially in the aftermath of the Greenbelt scandal, where developers seem to have had very effective connections at Queen's Park.
Once broken, trust is hard to gain back. Preventing the public from speaking out in the planning process may encourage more people to speak out at election time. In the premier's own words, best not to throw your friends under the bus.
Barbara Captijn is an
independent consumer
advocate, with a focus on
new home buyer protection.
CAPTION:

CREDIT: Barbara Captijn;

Subject: Consumers; Planning; Politicians

Document 182 of 500

Provincial changes 'undermine' Hamilton's frozen urban boundary: City aims to defend growth strategy against developer tribunal challenges

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/provincial-changes-undermine-hamiltons-frozen/docview/3093425713/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Provincial+changes+%27undermine%27+Hamilton%27s+frozen+urban+boundary%3A+City+aims+to+defend+growth+strategy+against+developer+tribunal+challenges&amp;title=The+Spectator&amp;issn=11899417&amp;date=2024-08-16&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.1&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Spectator&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: The city is bracing for a slew of urban-expansion bids with provincial changes opening the door to boundary-busting development of any size at any time in Hamilton.
The legislative moves threaten to undo council's decision to freeze its urban-rural dividing line in order to avoid sprawl into the farmland, city officials say.
And so the city must be well-armed for Ontario Land Tribunal (OLT) battles that more developers are bound to wage, Coun. John-Paul Danko says.
In fact, the "entire point" of Bill 185, the Cutting Red Tape to Build More Homes Act, is to "undermine the local decisions that were not in line" with the province's goal.
"They were forced to rescind their urban boundary expansion, so they're doing the same thing, but with a different mechanism," Danko told The Spectator.
In 2022, Premier Doug Ford's government imposed a 4,500-acre urban expansion on the city and opened up tracts of the Greenbelt for development.
Last year, in the face of scathing watchdog reports pointing to undue developer influence and an RCMP probe, the province walked back the boundary expansion and reversed the Greenbelt grabs.
This past June, Bill 185 came into effect, allowing developers to appeal council "nondecisions" or rejections of urban boundary applications to the OLT.
Also, a newly drafted provincial planning statement axes the requirement of comprehensive municipal land-use studies before boundary-expanding bids can be made.
That clears the way for urban expansion applications "at any time and with no limit on the size," a city report points out.
In response, the city is preparing requirements to evaluate urban expansion applications, creating a new team of staff to respond to them, setting new application fees and earmarking an additional $1.5 million to fight OLT appeals.
"We've been committed to implementing council's (firm-boundary) decision to the best of our ability," Steve Robichaud, acting general manager of planning and economic development, told The Spectator.
"So in no way does the adoption of that evaluation framework waver from the city's position that growth is to be accommodated within the existing urban area at this point."
If staff reject urban expansion applications, and developers file OLT appeals, the requirements will form the basis for cases before the provincial tribunal, Robichaud also noted.
Appeal rights for boundary expansions - excluding the Greenbelt - allow "development applications to be considered in a balanced manner," Bianca Meta, press secretary for Municipal Affairs and Housing Minister Paul Calandra, told The Spectator via email Thursday.
The draft provincial planning statement, for which the government sought feedback, is meant to bolster Ontario's housing supply and protect the environment, prime agricultural areas and public health and safety, Meta wrote.
"The government is carefully considering the input received and will determine whether changes are to be made in the final (statement)."
Hamilton's proposed framework presents considerations for boundary expansions, including their environmental, financial and land-use impact.
Staff plan to seek feedback from the public, agencies and First Nations this fall. In the meantime, the city plans to use the draft requirements for applications and OLT cases.
The city already faces appeals launched by developers hoping to build in the Twenty Road West and Whitechurch Road East areas in Glanbrook.
Another appeal relates recognizing Elfrida as a "priority area" for urban expansion.
City staff point out a key factor is servicing (think sewer pipes and pumping stations).
Accommodating urban expansions shouldn't reduce the capacity for existing residential or industrial development, Robichaud said. "You don't want to rob Peter to pay Paul."
Developers are expected to pay for the upfront costs of new infrastructure, but the city is on the hook for operational and maintenance expenses of services - from firefighting to snow-clearing and recreation centres - "in perpetuity," the city report notes.
The province, builders who own would-be expansion lands and industry groups have argued urban expansion is necessary if Ontario hopes to solve its housing-supply crunch.
In a letter to council, the West End Home Builders' Association says the provincial changes give the city "an additional opportunity to set up a framework for collaborative discussions to work together and move more quickly towards building more attainable housing supply in new complete communities."
City planners, councillors and anti-sprawl advocates, however, have insisted Hamilton has enough land in its existing urban area for growth to the year 2051.
This week, in supporting staff's recommendations, councillors emphasized it's important to keep the public up to speed on the urban boundary file.
Casting a wide net is crucial, added Ian Borsuk, executive director of Environment Hamilton.
"We should just be notifying the entire city as best that we can," said Borsuk, who called the provincial changes an "infuriating situation."
Teviah Moro is a reporter at The Hamilton Spectator. tmoro@thespec.com
CAPTION: The Upper West Side Landowners Group aims to build homes on Twenty Road West near the Hamilton airport.Spectator file photo

Document 183 of 500

Doug Ford hints at changes around safe injection sites as municipalities urge help for mental health and addiction services

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/doug-ford-hints-at-changes-around-safe-injection/docview/3093427098/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Doug+Ford+hints+at+changes+around+safe+injection+sites+as+municipalities+urge+help+for+mental+health+and+addiction+services&amp;title=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-08-15&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: With Premier Doug Ford hinting provincial moves are afoot to restrict supervised drug consumption sites, municipalities say more help is needed on mental health, addiction and homelessness.
"I'll be very frank, I'm not sold on these safe injection sites that are in neighbourhoods and needles are all flowing around - it's a haven for drug dealers," Ford said last week in Thunder Bay.
"Let's get these people the support they need and build more detox beds and I know our ministers will be making announcements shortly regarding that," the premier said.
That suggests Ford's cabinet members will be unveiling changes around consumption and treatment service (CTS) sites at the annual Association of Municipalities of Ontario conference in Ottawa next week.
On the eve of that confab, Cambridge Mayor Jan Liggett commissioned a provincewide poll, which she shared with the Star and will be distributing to other civic leaders at the convention, that found 78 per cent of respondents want Queen's Park to earmark special funding for mental health and addiction services.
"It's a catastrophe that we are in right now," said Liggett, stressing "there's not a single mayor or councillor in this province" who isn't wrestling with mental health and addiction crises in their communities.
"We've been spinning in circles for years. We're all struggling with what needs to be done," she said in an interview Thursday.
Liggett, whose city does not have a CTS site, said she hoped the poll by Campaign Research, the same research firm used by Ford's Progressive Conservatives, would give mayors "some guidance" on addressing mental health and addiction across Ontario.
Asked what "should be prioritized with new funding," the poll found 34 per cent of respondents felt youth and adult mental health services, followed by 26 per cent for mental health crisis intervention services, 15 per cent for rehabilitation programs and facilities, nine per cent for community supports such as Alcoholics Anonymous and eight per cent for in-patient detox centres.
Some 45 per cent said "governments must spend much more on mental health and addiction services to make significant progress on getting ahead of this ever-growing problem" while 33 per cent said "the overall problem isn't a lack of funding" and 22 per cent were unsure.
Campaign Research surveyed 2,027 Ontarians July 30-31 using the Maru Blue online panel. While opt-in polls cannot be assigned a margin of error, for comparison purposes, a random sample of this size would have one of plus or minus two percentage points, 19 times out of 20.
The firm found 67 per cent believe "that Ontario's laws needed to change to allow families and physicians to provide mental health and addiction treatment to involuntary patients" with 14 per cent opposed to such mandatory intervention and 19 per cent unsure.
But Health Minister Sylvia Jones said two weeks ago she has "concerns that involuntary treatment would not lead to the outcomes that we want."
"Having said that, when we see the opportunity and the need for intervention, and people are willing to take on those treatments to make a difference, that's when we can show them our government is committed," Jones said on July 31.
Campaign Research also found 68 per cent of respondents would like to see more "drug-treatment courts" that provide judicially supervised rehab instead of incarcerating people with addiction issues related to their criminal activities.
Only 16 per cent opposed the expansion of such courts and 16 per cent weren't sure.
Liggett said drug-treatment courts are "a tool that is underused" because they can help people tackle their addiction while avoiding jail.
"We have to reach those that have that capacity (for rehabilitation)," she said.
Mental health issues and addiction are also big factors in the rise in homelessness - AMO estimates there were 1,400 homeless encampments across Ontario last year.
The largest such tent encampment in Toronto is in Dufferin Grove Park, where local residents have repeatedly complained to the city and local councillor about drug use, fires, garbage, broken glass, public urination and other unsanitary conditions.
Encampment dwellers insist they have nowhere else to go because city shelters are full or unsafe and Toronto housing is expensive.
Finance Minister Peter Bethlenfalvy said he expects to hear about such concerns in some of his 35 scheduled meetings with municipal leaders over two days at the AMO conference.
"We're going to continue to have robust conversations and talk about the shared priorities that we have - and of course, homelessness, mental health and addiction, building more homes, building infrastructure," Bethlenfalvy said Tuesday.
Liberal MPP Adil Shamji (Don Valley East), an emergency room physician who has worked with homeless people, said the province needs to improve the overall delivery of mental health and addiction services.
"I have seen how red tape, bureaucracy, and, frankly, a lack of clarity about who has the power to fund certain programs and services does create a quagmire that slows down getting help to the people that need it most," Shamji said Wednesday.
"That process needs to be clarified."
While Ford has long been wary of supervised consumption sites - especially after mother-of-two Karolina Huebner-Makurat was killed by a stray bullet outside the South Riverdale Community Health Centre CTS in July 2023 - advocates argue they are safer for addicts than injecting in alleyways where fatal overdoses are common. Some research has shown the sites can save lives.
The city of Toronto has 10 safe injection sites, six of which are provincially designated as a CTS site and are subject to Ontario public health standards.
CAPTION: Premier Doug Ford speaks to reporters in Halifax on July 15, 2024.
