Document 184 of 500

Justin Trudeau's government radically transformed Canada's temporary foreign worker program. Young people and low wage workers are paying the price

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/justin-trudeaus-government-radically-transformed/docview/3093161690/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Justin+Trudeau%27s+government+radically+transformed+Canada%27s+temporary+foreign+worker+program.+Young+people+and+low+wage+workers+are+paying+the+price&amp;title=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-08-15&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: If you know a young person who struggled to find a summer job they are not alone. This has been the worst summer on record for youth employment outside of the pandemic. Many factors - from a weak economy to a population boom of young people - are at play with one of the largest being the federal government's 2022 decision to deregulate the low-wage stream of the temporary foreign worker program.
On April 4, 2022, a mere 13 days after the Liberals and NDP signed their Supply and Confidence Agreement, the federal government announced arguably the largest deregulation of the Temporary Foreign Worker program in Canadian history. The program's low-wage stream, which allows employers not in the agricultural industry (they have a separate stream) to bring in workers and pay them wages under the provincial median (currently $28.39 in Ontario), was radically transformed. The government removed the rule that employers could only bring in workers in some low-wage occupations if the local unemployment rate was less than six per cent allowing firms in areas of high unemployment to access the program. Companies had been limited to having only 10 per cent of their workforce be low-wage temporary foreign workers; this was raised to 20 per cent. In seven sectors, including accommodation and food services, this was raised to 30 per cent.
The government argued that the plan was necessary to reduce labour shortages, noting that "much of the unmet demand is in low-wage occupations." Accommodation and food services were cited as having the largest shortages. Business groups, such as the Canadian Federation of Independent Business, applauded the move, particularly the removal of the six per cent rule and raising the cap for low-wage employers. Companies responded by rapidly expanding the use of the program. The number of approvals for the low-wage program more than doubled in 2022 alone rising from an estimated 30,000 to more than 80,000. The total number of temporary foreign worker positions approved in all streams rose by 52 per cent in 2022 alone to 227,000. A Bloomberg report found that Tim Hortons' franchisees in Ontario hired 714 temporary foreign workers in 2023, up from just 59 in 2019.
While this deregulation worked as intended, it came at the expense of low-wage workers in Canada, as it reduced employment opportunities and suppressed wages. To bring in a temporary foreign worker, employers must show that there are no qualified domestic applicants at the "prevailing wage." A coffee shop owners' inability to find workers at the current wage is plausible in places with low unemployment. However, the government's reforms allowed employers in high-unemployment areas to access the program as well. But the term "prevailing wage" is important here. In a capitalist society, if a company cannot find someone to work for $17 an hour, they either need to raise their wage to attract more workers or do without. But the federal government has given them a third option, temporary foreign workers, which prevents employers from having to offer raises to attract new workers.
Liberals and New Democrats once understood that the low-wage temporary foreign worker program works by suppressing wage increases. When the Conservative Harper government deregulated the program, Liberal MP Scott Brison asked in the House, "Does the minister recognize that some economists are linking the growth in the number of low-skilled temporary foreign workers under the Conservatives with higher youth unemployment in Canada and wage suppression for Canadian youth?" And Liberal leader Justin Trudeau called on the Harper government to "reverse its wage-suppressing policies and fix its broken program."
Between the increase in the number of temporary foreign workers and international students, young workers are having to compete harder for jobs, and many are coming up empty-handed. The employment rate for 15- to 19-year-olds dropped from 55.3 per cent in July 2022 to 46.4 per cent this July, the lowest July on record outside of the lockdown year of 2020. The trend for 20- to 24-year-olds is similar, with this summer being the worst non-pandemic year for employment since 1997. Had the employment rates for these groups stayed at 2022 levels, an additional 200,000 15 - to 19-year-olds and 80,000 20-to 24-year-olds would have jobs this summer. The economists that the Liberals cited in 2014 were right then, and they are right today - deregulation of the low-wage temporary foreign worker stream suppresses wages and increases youth unemployment.
The federal government must phase out the non-agricultural low-wage stream of the temporary foreign worker program. The harms it causes to workers far outweigh the benefits to corporations.
CAPTION: Deregulation of theTemporary Foreign Workerprogram allowed many employers to increase temporary foreign worker hires. It's been so successful that many young Canadians can't find summer jobs.
CREDIT: Mike Moffatt, Contributor

Document 185 of 500

Waterdown residents fear 'fortress' townhouse plan

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/waterdown-residents-fear-fortress-townhouse-plan/docview/3093161686/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Waterdown+residents+fear+%27fortress%27+townhouse+plan&amp;title=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-08-15&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Aiming for a compromise, city councillors have delayed a decision on a staff-backed plan for 18 townhomes in Waterdown that has sparked a local outcry.
The hope is that a meeting between residents, city officials and concerned residents will bear fruit before the 30-day deferral period expires, Coun. Ted McMeekin says.
"I believe in ways forward," said McMeekin, who has sided with would-be neighbours of the proposed development in protesting the plan.
But other councillors argue Hawk Ridge Homes Inc.'s application for Dundas Street East will lead to badly needed homes during a housing crisis.
And an appeal to the Ontario Land Tribunal (OLT) if the proposal is rejected - with staff's favourable analysis on record - or if council fails to make a decision within 120 days, also looms.
In the meantime, local residents who oppose the plan are nervous about what could emerge beyond the fences of their sloping backyards.
"We feel like ants in an ant colony," Jennifer Gallant told The Spectator.
That's a reference to her family's feared loss of privacy with the two- and three-storey towns built on the higher-ground lots behind their single-detached home of 12 years.
Hawk Ridge Homes Inc. also plans to backfill the development property, build a retaining wall and erect a fence at its rear.
"It's going to be like a fortress ... It's going to be ugly," Gallant predicts.
Earlier this week, she and her neighbours made that point at city hall as councillors considered staff's recommendation to approve the application.
In addition to unease over loss of privacy and unpleasant views, the residents raised other concerns, which they say are not rooted in a not-in-my-backyard reflex, or NIMBYism.
Stormwater runoff from the higher site into their flood-prone backyards; increased traffic on a busy, cresting and dangerous stretch of Dundas Street East; parking congestion on their streets.
"Common sense must prevail and people must be prioritized over profits. Lives must be more important than the bottom line and residents' concerns must be taken into consideration," Kim Parkes-Hallmark told the planning committee Tuesday.
Hawk Ridge Homes Inc. looks for council's approval as the city aims to corral growth within Hamilton's urban area through residential intensification in an effort to meet housing targets and avoid sprawl into farmland.
Stephanie Card, a 20-year resident of the neighbourhood, says the sloping site is the sticking point.
"If Hawk Ridge was putting this on a flat piece of ground, I'm sure it would be a lovely development, but here it just doesn't make any sense," she told The Spectator.
Card, who has spearheaded a six-year campaign against the drawn-out proposal, noted 57 people have signed a petition and dozens of others have written city officials to express their disapproval.
A representative of the developer and city staff, however, say provisions in the blueprint and studies analyzing traffic, overlook, shadowing and stormwater management address residents' concerns.
Moreover, 18 townhouses along a transit route like Dundas Street East, close to the local public library, parks, schools and shopping represent sound planning.
"So all in all, it's a good location for intensification," developer consultant Gerry Tchisler told the planning committee.
But like McMeekin, other city politicians say they're not convinced, despite planning staff's take.
Hawk Ridge Homes Inc.'s proposal is "quite an intrusion of a very different esthetic," Coun. Jeff Beattie said.
In a recent split vote, council rejected another staff-supported application for 553 rental units at a boarded-up shopping plaza on Greenhill Avenue in the east end.
That decision became the focus of a federal Conservative campaign-style video that characterized it as municipal obstruction of housing, despite funding from the Liberal government to incentivize development.
Under the gaze of Premier Doug Ford's provincial government, it's important for the city to "walk the talk," Coun. Maureen Wilson offered during Tuesday's discussion.
Local officials and residents were vocal opponents of Ford's initial - but then walked-back - moves to open up the Greenbelt to development and expand Hamilton's urban area into farmland, despite the city's plan to freeze its boundary.
More housing is "key" to the city's economy, including when it comes to recruiting and retaining workers that keep it humming, Wilson said.
In years gone by, "it may have been appropriate" to reject certain applications based on resident discomfort, the west end councillor also said.
"But we know now that collectively when we say no, that's affecting everything from encampments to our economy."
Likewise, Coun. Craig Cassar argued Hawk Ridge Homes Inc.'s proposal must be viewed "from the big picture," which takes into account planning policy and the need for housing within the urban area.
Neighbourhoods of single-detached homes haven't changed for decades, the Ancaster councillor noted. "But the reality is we can't expect that that's going to continue."
Owing to his predilection for "compromise," McMeekin said, he'd never opposed a development in Ward 15.
But if Hawk Ridge Homes Inc.'s plan were sound, dozens of residents wouldn't have written the city in protest or shown up to air their concerns, he maintained.
"Bottom line ... I have never seen a project proposal in Ward 15 that is less compatible in an existing neighbourhood than this one."
The developer held a community meeting in 2018 to discuss the project with local residents but hasn't done so again despite requests, he said.
In response, Tchisler told the committee Hawk Ridge Homes Inc. has done its "best to resolve the issues" and questioned "what else we can deliver" in another meeting.
Raising the spectre an OLT battle, Coun. John-Paul Danko said city politicians "just can't deny planning applications because we don't like them."
The tribunal "will inevitably overturn" council's rejection of the staff-backed application after a costly hearing, Danko warned.
Overwhelmingly, the OLT tends to side with developer interests, acknowledges Card but says local residents hope their case can buck the trend.
"Because we really feel strongly that this is an inappropriate location."
Teviah Moro is a reporter at The Hamilton Spectator. tmoro@thespec.com

Document 186 of 500

Waterloo Region purchased farm land for development, then destroyed the corn crop. Now, councillors want answers

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/waterloo-region-purchased-farm-land-development/docview/3093161661/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Waterloo+Region+purchased+farm+land+for+development%2C+then+destroyed+the+corn+crop.+Now%2C+councillors+want+answers&amp;title=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-08-15&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Waterloo Region council has directed staff to investigate why a corn crop was plowed under as part of the industrial land assembly process in Wilmot Township.
Council wants a report within four weeks. The vote was passed by council on Tuesday with little discussion following a closed session, prior to a regional committee meeting.
The corn crop was about five weeks from harvest when it was destroyed in late July as the region was in the process of completing the purchase of a 160-acre farm. The action appears to be in direct conflict with the region's official plan and "growing with care" vision, as well as the province's agri-food strategy, states the council motion, which commits to ensuring any future crops associated with the land assembly be assessed and that regional council be notified in advance before any action is taken.
The requested report is expected to be made public, subject to confidentiality obligations.
Last month the region paid farmers from Strathroy to come to Wilmot and plowed under the corn crop. In a statement, the region said the plowing was required to complete various studies, including topographic surveys and archeological assessments, prior to closing land purchase transactions.
The destruction drew criticism from local farmers and environmentalists who said the corn could have been used to feed animals or people.
The Waterloo Federation of Agriculture estimates that the region squandered a significant amount of revenue by destroying the crop. "Corn valued at approximately $5 per bushel, with an average yield of 200 bushels per acre on this prime farmland, represents potential revenues of around $1,000 per acre," it said. "This totals a staggering $160,000 in lost revenue, for which the landowner must be compensated by the region, not to mention the additional cost incurred for crop destruction services. To put this into perspective, this field could have produced 2.5 million boxes of 400g Corn Flakes cereal."
"The destruction of crop weeks out from harvest is an attack on our community and the values we hold," said Wilmot Coun. Harvir Sidhu in a Facebook post, who was the first municipal politician to publicly criticize the region for its actions as it attempts to assemble 770 acres of prime farmland in Wilmot, roughly bound by Bleams Road, Highway 7 and 8, and Wilmot Centre Road.
During an interview with a local media outlet last week, regional Coun. Rob Deutschmann said councillors were told the soil on the land would be tested but weren't told the corn crop would be destroyed.
"Members of council I've interacted with are very upset about what's happened there; we need to find out what occurred," he said on the Mike Farwell radio show.
Last winter, a group Wilmot landowners were approached with offers by consultant acting on behalf of the region. They were told their properties would be expropriated if a purchase agreement couldn't be reached.
In a press release issued Tuesday, the Fight for Farmland group representing residents and landowners, said people are "still confounded by the lack of information, the lack of answers, and the lack of even a single public meeting about such massive development in Wilmot Township contrary to all previous planning."
The release continues questioning of the province's involvement in the land assembly process due to conflicting statements by cabinet ministers such as Ontario's agriculture minister Rob Flack, who recently said the government supports the land purchase.
During a past visit to Kitchener, Ontario Premier Doug Ford stated the industrial site shouldn't be forced on an unwilling community and the Fight for Farmland group has approached Township of Wilmot council, asking it to pass a motion saying that it is an unwilling host.
"That's with the mayor and councillors now," however it wouldn't likely pass, said Alfred Lowrick, spokesperson for the group.
"There are some councillors who are adamant development is required," he said. "The only reason it would pass would indicate, let's pause this now and go through a proper communication exercise and consultations, and do what we've always done in this region with constituents before making rash decisions."
Regional Chair Karen Redman has been firm in her stance that the land assembly is needed for future economic development opportunities to support a growing population, a position that's supported by other politicians and prominent community leaders promoting commerce and job growth.
As Wilmot Township embarks on a new strategic plan for the community, Lowrick said a large industrial site could change its entire complexion.
"If you have a thousand people coming on shift to an industrial complex or whatever is going in there, can you still farm this community? I don't think those studies have been done."
with files from Luisa D'Amato
CAPTION: A map of the properties involved in the Wilmot land assembly, as of Aug. 2, 2024. In red, these properties are owned by private farms. In blue, these properties were acquired by the Region of Waterloo. In green, these properties (8 acres, and 36.6 acres) are owned by the Ministry of Transportation of Ontario. The property lines and ownership status was provided by Alfred Lowrick, a farmer whose family members are among the landowners being pressured to sell. The map was shared with the Region of Waterloo at the start of August to confirm property lines and current land ownership. A spokesperson did not comment on the map, but said the region "is committed to reaching fair and equitable agreements for all landowners. Negotiations are ongoing and progressing, and the terms of the agreements are confidential."A few lone corn stalks stand at the edge of a corn field along Bleams Road in Wilmot Township that was recently plowed under. The field is owned by the Region of Waterloo, part of the land assembly they're pursuing for a future industrial site.

Aug. 15: Sad to see CHML radio station silenced, health-care cuts got us here and other letters to the editor

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/aug-15-sad-see-chml-radio-station-silenced-health/docview/3093161115/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Aug.+15%3A+Sad+to+see+CHML+radio+station+silenced%2C+health-care+cuts+got+us+here+and+other+letters+to+the+editor&amp;title=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-08-15&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Sad to see radio station silenced
It's likely you've heard by now that 900 CHML has been silenced. Hamilton, Ontario and Canada have lost another legacy radio station thanks to those who put profit ahead of product. On a personal level, I also find it especially sad that, I believe, the longest-serving female anchor in the station's long history, Shiona Thompson, will not get the recognition she deserves.
Tedd Colbear, Hamilton
Health-care cuts got us here
Re: Solving the homelessness crisis involves revisiting a 30-year-old mental health policy failure, Aug. 14
I am responding to the excellent commentary by Tyler Firth who hit the nail on the head when he related homelessness to the closing of Hamilton Psychiatric Hospital in 2000.
These closings occurred throughout North America, however, the community services needed to replace the hospital service never developed to meet the needs of all the mentally ill people. Then, many ill people were often found wandering the streets with nowhere to go.
So with limited mental health outpatient treatment centres and limited supportive housing, we end up with tent encampments.
Mental health and addictions go hand in hand. If we don't have sufficient services, we get homelessness at its worst.
This issue is not going away until we address mental health and addictions services in a safe environment. And yes, this plan will cost money.
Janet Connelly, Stoney Creek
The experiment clearly failed
Finally! Tyler Firth's commentary is the first and only sound voice of reason, experience and concrete proposal to deal with the homelessness issue that plagues us.
While this has been my personal belief for years, after the province closed the psychiatric hospitals, my opinion lacked Firth's expertise and therefore, validity. But he has hit the nail squarely on the head. His solution is the only solution - amend the Ontario Mental Health Act. Our experiment failed. And if it requires a change in government to one more forward thinking, then so be it.
Susan Ruddle, Waterdown
Is there something in the water?
Re: Seeing double: Hagersville residents celebrate sibling bond at Twins Days, Aug. 13
Twins definitely have a special connection. In the summer of 1954 there was a gathering of 18 sets of twins living in, or near, Caledonia. The picture appeared in the Caledonia Sachem and Rev. A.N. MacMillan was quoted as saying "There must be something in the water."
Betsy Smith, Cayuga
Trump getting his due reward
Donald Trump is said to be furious over the polls that show Kamala Harris surging ahead in the presidential race. Apparently, when you're a misogynist famous for sleeping with porn stars and bragging "you can grab them by the p----" you start to wince when you hear a woman has a firm grip and is crushing it.
Frieda Huffman, Hamilton
Workplace diversity still lacking
Re: Let's make it easier for newcomers to work in their fields, Aug. 11
I am discouraged by the continued lack of diversity in numerous professions within our workforce.
Despite the recently passed Workers for Working Act, banning the use of Canadian work experience as a requirement in job postings or on application forms, highly qualified newcomers continue to be under-represented.
A change in equity policies, not just on paper, but also in practice, is long overdue. It is only by doing so that Canada can not only improve its productivity, but also become the equitable and welcoming country we claim to be.
Andrea Rado, Hamilton
City can't shift the blame any longer
CBC reported some time ago as follows: "According to the Ontario government, it allocated more than $52 million for the 2022-2023 fiscal year for housing and homelessness programs in Hamilton.
Fast forward to today and Mayor Andrea Horwath is droning the same wearying lament: "to "put .. pressure on the provincial government." That is to say, Horwath is continuing the modus operandi she honed when leading the NDP: blaming someone else for her demonstrable failure to effectively use the millions that have been directed to the city.
A "hollow downtown" and her imagination share the same adjective. Whatever possessed people to vote for her?
Neville Newman, Hamilton
CAPTION: Hamilton's 900 CHML announced Wednesday that the radio station would be closing after nearly a century of broadcasting.

Document 188 of 500

New party to appear on two upcoming byelection ballots

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/new-party-appear-on-two-upcoming-byelection/docview/3093086787/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=New+party+to+appear+on+two+upcoming+byelection+ballots&amp;title=The+Spectator&amp;issn=11899417&amp;date=2024-08-15&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.16&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Spectator&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: A new political party will appear on the ballot in two upcoming byelections as the Canadian Future Party seeks to introduce itself officially as a centrist option for voters it argues are growing weary of an increasingly polarized environment.
The party, approved by Elections Canada last month, will field candidates in the LaSalle-Émard-Verdun and Elmwood-Transcona races in September, which are being watched closely for what they might herald for the governing Liberals, official Opposition Conservatives and New Democrats.
Dominic Cardy, the interim leader of the party, told the Toronto Star in an exclusive interview that he's under no illusions his rookie venture will be easy to get off the ground.
But Cardy - a former cabinet minister in New Brunswick's Progressive Conservative government - says he sees room for his party's message as he surveys the current political landscape.
"We're the no-nonsense party. We're going to be here to talk about things that are real, not try and spin them, not have lots of hyperbole on either side, and actually offer concrete solutions around the problems that I think have been totally ignored by the Liberals and Conservatives particularly," he said ahead of an event in Ottawa on Wednesday to officially launch the party.
"Our tag line is saying we're not left, not right, (we're) going forward."
Future Canada started two years ago as a movement known as Centre Ice Conservatives, a coalition of small-c conservatives frustrated by their party's inability to gain traction with centrist voters.
At the time, the federal Tories were running their third leadership race in five years. Centre Ice was set up as a forum to discuss how the party could appeal to a block of voters who had eluded the party in previous general elections and kept them out of power.
The ultimate winner of that leadership race was Pierre Poilievre, whose campaign hit a nerve among those growing increasingly skeptical of the Liberal government, and his party has since secured a dominating lead in the national polls over the Trudeau Liberals.
The races in the upcoming byelections aren't clean fights between the Liberals and Tories.
In the Quebec riding of LaSalle-Émard-Verdun, the Liberals will try to hold onto a seat with the NDP and Bloc Québécois chomping for a win. In Manitoba's Elmwood-Transcona, a NDP-held riding, it is the Conservatives who are expected to put up the toughest fight.
Cardy said his rookie party will not be easy to get off the ground, recalling how another member of the movement once said starting a new political party would be like having 338 root canals - a reference to the 338 federal seats in Parliament at the time.
"It turns out that in the last couple of years there are things worse than having 338 root canals, which is watching as our current political system continues to fight about things that don't matter and ignore existential threats against Canada," Cardy said, citing defence, trade and foreign policy as examples.
CAPTION: Dominic Cardy, right, interim leader of the Canadian Future Party, and Tara McPhail, interim party president, launch their new federal party in Ottawa on Wednesday.Justin Tang The Canadian Press

CREDIT: Stephanie Levitz Toronto Star

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=National+briefs&amp;title=The+Spectator&amp;issn=11899417&amp;date=2024-08-15&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.16&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Spectator&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Ontario
Ford criticized after patient 'overflow' joke
Ontario's opposition parties are criticizing Premier Doug Ford for joking about sending "overflow" human patients to a new animal hospital. Ford spoke Tuesday at the opening of a large new veterinary facility north of Toronto and said, "by the looks of it we know where we can send the overflow patients now for MRIs and CAT scans and everything else." A spokesperson for the premier says he was making a joke referring to the size of the four-storey hospital and that the government has made record investments in the public health-care system. But opposition parties say the state of Ontario's health-care system isn't humorous, as patients can face long waits for diagnostic tests and emergency care.
Canada
Liberals won't say if CBC boss will get bonus
The Liberal government is refusing to say if it approved a bonus for the head of the Canadian Broadcasting Corp. It is up to the federal government to determine if Catherine Tait is eligible for a bonus following a review of her performance and recommendation by the board of directors at CBC/Radio-Canada. Citing privacy laws, a spokesperson for the Privy Council Office says it cannot disclose details, even though some of that information was made public in past years. This year, CBC paid out $18.4 million in bonuses to nearly 1,200 employees, managers and executives after eliminating hundreds of jobs.

Document 190 of 500

Opposition slams Doug Ford for joke about animal hospital

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/opposition-slams-doug-ford-joke-about-animal/docview/3093240106/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Opposition+slams+Doug+Ford+for+joke+about+animal+hospital&amp;title=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-08-14&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Premier Doug Ford's offhanded joke that a new King City animal hospital could ease Ontario's health crunch has left his political rivals galled and appalled.
"By the looks of it, we know where we can send the overflow patients now for MRIs and CAT scans and everything else," Ford quipped Tuesday at the grand open of the massive King Animal Hospital, a private veterinary facility 45 kilometres north of Toronto.
The premier's office emphasized he was making a lighthearted remark about the impressive four-storey, 60,000-square-foot clinic that boasts MRI, CT scan, X-ray, ultrasound and endoscopy services for horses, dogs, cats and other animals.
"Our government made record investments in our publicly funded health-care system, increasing the health-care budget by over 31 per cent since 2018, and investing over $85 billion this year alone," Grace Lee, the premier's director of media relations, said Wednesday.
"We are adding over 49 new MRI machines and 50 new CT scanners in hospitals across the province, increasing capacity to perform nearly 500,000 hours of MRI and CT scans. We recently announced an investment for 100,000 new publicly funded scans, which will continue to help Ontario achieve some of the shortest wait times for surgeries in Canada," she said.
But opposition leaders pounced on a quip they argued was no laughing matter.
"Remember, this is part of the plan," NDP Leader Marit Stiles said Wednesday on social media.
"Doug Ford (and) his politicians are dismantling our health-care system (and) giving it over to folks like Galen Weston to make more billions off," said Stiles, referring to the wealthy owner of Shoppers Drug Mart, where more publicly funded health services are being made available by pharmacists.
"It's all a giant inside deal. Corrupt," the NDP chief said.
Liberal Leader Bonnie Crombie blasted the premier for his insensitivity.
"After six years of deliberately underfunding our health-care system in order to justify his agenda to sell it off and privatize, Ford is now joking that if you're waiting for an MRI, you should just visit a veterinary clinic," Crombie said in a statement.
Liberal MPP Adil Shamji (Don Valley East), an emergency room doctor, said "jokes can be funny, but they can also be revealing," and called Ford's "cruel, callous and out of touch."
"Nearly 10,000 people died while waiting for MRIs and CT scans alone through the watch of Doug Ford," Shamji told reporters at Queen's Park.
"Another 2,000 ... Ontarians died while waiting for surgery. So against that backdrop, you don't joke that the hundreds of thousands of people who are still on waiting lists line up like animals at a veterinary hospital."
With 2.5-million Ontarians lacking access to a doctor, Ford's Progressive Conservatives are poised to give pharmacists more authority to prescribe medications for 14 common ailments such as insomnia, shingles, hepatitis A and B, rabies and human papillomavirus, as well as lab tests for strep throat.
While those additional powers for pharmacists would likely alleviate the strain on emergency rooms and doctors' offices, they would generate more revenue for pharmacy giants.
"For me, this is about access," Health Minister Sylvia Jones said three weeks ago.
The Ontario Medical Association - which represents the province's 43,000 physicians, medical students and retired doctors - has argued that's a "Band-Aid solution for a health-care system in crisis."
It maintains that the doctor shortage will worsen in the years ahead due to demographic changes and other factors.
As Ford ponders an early election next year - ahead of the scheduled June 2026 vote - the political parties are gearing up for a campaign in which health care is expected to be a major issue.
CAPTION: Premier Doug Ford speaks during a news conference about police helicopter funding in Mississauga on July 29, 2024.

Document 191 of 500

As RCMP investigates Greenbelt scandal, finance minister says Ford government is 'an open book'

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/as-rcmp-investigates-greenbelt-scandal-finance/docview/3092922518/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=As+RCMP+investigates+Greenbelt+scandal%2C+finance+minister+says+Ford+government+is+%27an+open+book%27&amp;title=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-08-13&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: As RCMP detectives interview current and former Progressive Conservatives staffers about the Greenbelt scandal, Finance Minister Peter Bethlenfalvy insists the government is "an open book."
Speaking to reporters at Queen's Park on Tuesday, Bethlenfalvy said he has not been contacted by the Royal Canadian Mounted Police and, as far as he knows, neither have any of his past aides.
Asked if he were prepared to sit down with investigators, the treasurer nodded.
"We're an open book - as the premier said, 'Nothing to hide.' They've got to do their job," Bethlenfalvy said as he announced first quarterly growth in Ontario was 0.7 per cent and the deficit of $9.8 billion remains unchanged from his March budget.
After the Star revealed Friday that Tory aides are being questioned as potential witnesses in the $8.28-billion Greenbelt land swap scandal, Premier Doug Ford signalled to the police, "Come in and do whatever you have to do."
"But I want full co-operation - they know that - because there's nothing to hide there. Let's get on with it," he said in Thunder Bay.
"We have nothing to hide."
Ford has not been interviewed by police.
The RCMP would only "confirm that the investigation is ongoing" into the premier's controversial 2022 decision to open up 7,400 acres of the two-million-acre Greenbelt to housing development.
While Ford cancelled his Greenbelt land-swap scheme last Sept. 21, the Mounties launched a criminal probe 19 days later.
Their investigation came in the wake of separate reports into the debacle by the auditor general and the integrity commissioner last August.
Then-auditor general Bonnie Lysyk found developers connected to Ford's Tories had been "favoured" in the government's push to allow houses to be built on land previously deemed as environmentally sensitive.
Lysyk calculated that the rezoning inflated the value of the 15 properties in the Greater Toronto and Hamilton Area to $8.28 billion.
Her 97-page report - and a subsequent 166-page review by integrity commissioner J. David Wake - are apparently serving as road maps for the RCMP investigation.
Some of the 62 witnesses named by Wake in his report are in the process of being questioned, sources say.
Detectives from the RCMP "O" Division's Sensitive and International Investigations unit, the elite Ottawa-based branch that probes corruption and political crimes, are quietly conducting interviews with past and present PC staffers this month at the force's Greater Toronto Area detachment.
Sources, speaking confidentially in order to discuss sensitive matters, emphasized the officers are meeting with political aides and bureaucrats viewed as potential witnesses, and not with anyone suspected of wrongdoing.
Revelations about the escalation of the RCMP investigation comes as the Tories, who lead in public opinion polls, are gearing up for an early election call next year instead of waiting for the scheduled June 2026 vote.
Bethlenfalvy was asked Tuesday if the governing party was reconsidering a gamble on a snap vote.
"We're very focused on executing on our transparent plan for the people of Ontario," he said, noting the Tories ran and won on their 2022 budget in the last provincial election.
Last summer, two of Ford's ministers and a pair of top political aides resigned over the Greenbelt affair.
The premier was so bruised by the debacle that he admitted "it was a mistake to open the Greenbelt."
"I'm very, very sorry. I made a promise to you that I wouldn't touch the Greenbelt. I broke that promise. As a first step to earning back your trust, I'll be reversing the changes," he said last September.
"We moved too quickly and we made the wrong decision ... it caused people to question our motives."
CAPTION: Finance Minister Peter Bethlenfalvy listens to Premier Doug Ford speak during a media event in July.

Subject: Criminal investigations; Scandals; Mounted police; Politics

We asked Spec readers what they think about downtown Hamilton. Here's what they said

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/we-asked-spec-readers-what-they-think-about/docview/3092922280/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=We+asked+Spec+readers+what+they+think+about+downtown+Hamilton.+Here%27s+what+they+said&amp;title=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-08-13&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: We asked Hamilton Spectator readers about their thoughts on downtown in an online survey and, over a period of 24 hours, 335 people responded from around the city and beyond. Most respondents reported feeling unsafe, when asked to rate their feeling of safety in the core.
Here's a sample of the comments, edited for clarity:
"I have worked downtown for about 30 years. The downtown has struggled throughout this period but things were getting better before the pandemic. Since COVID, unfortunately, it has deteriorated. Many people who used to travel to the downtown to work, now work from home. Many restaurants and shops have closed.
Condos have been built, but it doesn't seem that they are filling up with people. There has been negligible impact on the downtown economy thus far. It's sad to see but it will require a sustained effort from government, business and ordinary citizens to make the downtown a better place to be. Right now, it is not an attractive place either to live or work."
Franco Ciarlo, Hamilton Mountain
"The most unsafe I have felt in my 50 years of living in Downtown Hamilton. Open drug use, repeat petty crime, and regular shootings. I will no longer be having my relatives come and visit me."
James, Bay and Jackson
"... we have to treat Gore Park with more respect. Stop treating that park like a soup kitchen, and move the services that are provided in the park to a location downtown that might be currently empty or underutilized. The park is beautiful and people need to feel safe and undisturbed sitting there. We also need to crack down, hard, on the owners of buildings surrounding Gore Park who have let their buildings become derelict."
John, West Mountain
"I'm hoping that with the new developments, things will pick up, and foot traffic, specifically when the sun sets, will encourage more people to feel safe."
Zachary, St. Clair
I feel scared most of the time when I'm not inside my office. I feel concerned about the well-being of folks. I find the mornings the most difficult in terms of safety. We have witnessed two gun shootings and two overdoses that have lead to death in the last three months alone.
Danielle Hulan, Westdale
"King and James, the core's epicentre, should be the jewel of city, and looks like someone's nightmare or a postapocalyptic wasteland."
Jill Hemingway, West Mountain
"It's a complete dump. The abandoned buildings, graffiti, tents, and open drug use is disgraceful. How can we look at the former Sir John A. Macdonald (school), Eaton Centre, Jamesville complex, and other dishevelled areas and not come up with some sort of comprehensive solution to our housing crisis? How did it get to this?"
Julia, Hamilton
"Downtown revitalization has been buzzwords for the city for decades ... there is no revitalization, just decay that costs more and more of my taxes."
Jean, West Mountain
"It's unfortunate to see so many people doing so poorly. I wish I could take children to local parks without people living in them. I don't think that makes things unsafe for me though."
Doug MacKenzie, Beasley
"I was so proud of Hamilton and the changes it was making. I defended it to all my friends and would say you have to come and check it out. Over the last two to three years, my opinion has changed and I would no longer say that. I see human waste around the building I work in and other garbage including needles frequently. I have sat with younger people waiting for the bus around the MacNab terminal because they did not feel safe. I have called 911 several times for people in distress. I see people walking in traffic frequently. It just keeps getting worse with no end in site."
Catherine, West Mountain
"Being drunk or high in public needs to be put to an end. If I was acting in that way, I would be arrested."
Joanne, North End
"I think downtown needs support in the form of rent control, safe-use sites, and other systemic changes to help people from falling through the cracks. I don't feel unsafe downtown because I know that the people the most in danger are the people who are unhoused, not me shopping at the market and going home."
Rachel, Durand
"Seems like the most overlooked area of the entire city when it comes to cleanliness. I've seen garbage in the same spot for weeks at a time."
Katie, Gage Park
"I love the downtown core of our city. It's gone through so many ups and downs and always recovers - but we seem to stagger out of the downs instead of charging out. Downtown needs to be an expressive and productive hub, but that requires planning, action and commitment."
Jeremy, Stoney Creek
"There shouldn't be so many services in the core which (creates clustering) of people who just feed off each other."
Debra Schreiber, Stipley
"I always have to have one eye on my property to make sure it's not getting stolen."
Greg, Hess Village Area
"What is needed is leadership from city council, a realistic vision, an appropriate mix of lowrise and highrise living, and commercial development that fulfils a real need. What must be avoided is turning it into a collection of highrise office towers and condos - in other words, a sunless wind tunnel that discourages foot traffic and removes any sense of community."
Jonathan Nicholls, Durand
"... outreach, shelters, etc., can't be in your immediate core. When the city is so small and fledgling. It is not safe."
Jamie Roberts, Kirkendall
"There needs to be a bigger police presence, and more mental-health assistance to go with it."
Kim, Durand
"We have to stop fooling ourselves and see the offenders for what they really are: greedy speculators operating in investment and property holding firms. As a full-time working person who can't afford to live even in such an unappetizing place as this, I urge everyone to look well around and match the symptoms to the source."
J. B., Main Street West
"Jackson Square needs to be revitalized into a community hub and place (people) want to spend time in, it's too unsafe currently."
Anonymous, Downtown
"There have been several random shootings this summer, making it scary to be on the streets. I live in a retirement residence and one of the shootings saw the offenders literally running through the parking lot, requiring the police to keep us indoors."
Ruth, Downtown
"I can't wait to see the vibrancy that will hopefully come with the new residential buildings, the LRT, the entertainment district and the various other changes. I just hope we don't lose too many people before we see these changes."
Michael, Beasley
"Lane reductions are causing horrible congestion and the LRT is an extremely bad idea."
Adrian Phillips, Downtown (but planning to leave)
"I wish people weren't living in tents ... I would like to see more co-op housing or subsidized housing on city-owned land - instead of huge parking lots."
Jesse, St. Clair
"There's a reason people feel safest during Open Streets and Art Crawl events. We should stop making them 'events,' start making them normal."
Anonymous, Downtown
The provincial government isn't appropriately funding services that will help unhoused people and isn't prioritizing affordable housing. Ford needs to be held accountable.
Bob, Ainslie Wood West
"I am not without empathy but I firmly believe we cannot have people living wherever they want. Our parks are for the neighbouring taxpayers to use for themselves and their families. We need to have the homeless living in a 'controlled' environment no matter what/who has to bear the cost."
John, West Mountain
"I don't want to have to consider whether I will die from a stray bullet while I'm walking for coffee or lunch. This is extremely scary for myself and my co-workers"
Josh, Ward 2
"The city is a crazy wild jungle where you can't turn your back for a minute."
Melanie, North End
CAPTION: Protestors rallying for affordable housing set up an encampment at city hall in March 2024. Here's what Spectator readers had to say about downtown and its many problems.

Document 193 of 500

A former Progressive Conservative who calls Pierre Poilievre 'terrifying' is launching a new political party

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/former-progressive-conservative-who-calls-pierre/docview/3092922214/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=A+former+Progressive+Conservative+who+calls+Pierre+Poilievre+%27terrifying%27+is+launching+a+new+political+party&amp;title=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-08-13&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: OTTAWA - A new political party will appear on the ballot in two upcoming by-elections as the Canadian Future Party seeks to introduce itself officially as a centrist option for voters it argues are growing weary of an increasingly polarized environment.
The party, approved by Elections Canada last month, will field candidates in the LaSalle-Émard-Verdun and Elmwood-Transcona races in September, which are being watched closely for what they might herald for the governing Liberals, Official Opposition Conservatives, and New Democrats.
Dominic Cardy, the interim leader of the party, told the Star in an exclusive interview Tuesday that he's under no illusions his rookie venture will be easy to get off the ground.
But Cardy - a former cabinet minister in New Brunswick's Progressive Conservative government - says he sees room for his party's message as he surveys the current political landscape.
"We're the no-nonsense party. We're going to be here to talk about things that are real, not try and spin them, not have lots of hyperbole on either side, and actually offer concrete solutions around the problems that I think have been totally ignored by the Liberals and Conservatives particularly," he said ahead of an event in Ottawa on Wednesday to officially launch the party.
"Our tag line is saying we're not left, not right, (we're) going forward."
Future Canada started two years ago as a movement known as Centre Ice Conservatives, a coalition of small-c conservatives frustrated by their party's inability to gain traction with centrist voters.
At the time, the federal Tories were running their third leadership race in five years. Centre Ice was set up as a forum to discuss how the party could appeal to a block of voters who had eluded the party in previous general elections and kept them out of power.
The ultimate winner of that leadership race was Pierre Poilievre, whose campaign hit a nerve among those growing increasingly skeptical of the Liberal government, and his party has since secured a dominating lead in the national polls over the Trudeau Liberals.
The races in the upcoming by-elections aren't clean fights between the Liberals and Tories.
In the Quebec riding of LaSalle-Émard-Verdun, the Liberals will try to hold onto a seat with the NDP and Bloc Québécois chomping for a win. Meanwhile, in Manitoba's Elmwood-Transcona, a NDP-held riding, it is the Conservatives who are expected to put up the toughest fight.
Cardy said he's under no illusions that his rookie party will be easy to get off the ground, recalling how another member of the movement once said starting a new political party would be like having 338 root canals - a reference to the 338 federal seats in Parliament at the time.
"It turns out that in the last couple of years there are things worse than having 338 root canals, which is watching as our current political system continues to fight about things that don't matter and ignore existential threats against Canada," Cardy said, citing defence, trade and foreign policy as examples.
Cardy also accused those on the left and right of twisting facts and evidence to suit their political narratives, arguing they are taking Canada to a place where the country can't even agree on its basic challenges.
And politicians like Poilievre who challenge evidence and experts are "terrifying," Cardy said, citing in particular the Conservative leader's calls to fire so-called "gatekeepers."
"This is a very new and more complicated world," Cardy said. "We need trust in our experts, and that does not come from politicians playing games with expertise."
Meanwhile, the Liberals are no better, he said, citing the current "disaster" in the immigration system.
"All they do is undermine faith in the things that Mr. Trudeau says are important, which is Canadians working together and the power of government to do good," he said.
"Unless you can make democracy deliver, all you're doing is giving power to the populists by showing that it can't."
While the genesis of the new party dates back to Centre Ice Conservatives, it rebranded after Poilievre won the Tory leadership to broaden its appeal to frustrated Liberals, before moving on to actually set up a new party last year.
It counts among its current leadership team a former Quebec NDP MP, a former Liberal party candidate, and grassroots organizers from the federal Conservative party.
Cardy is currently sitting as an Independent MLA in New Brunswick after breaking with that province's PC government over Premier Blaine Higgs's leadership.
He said he's not pursuing re-election provincially, and will eventually seek a seat for Future Canada.
CAPTION: New Brunswick MLA Dominic Cardy is shownin Fredericton onAug. 20, 2021.

Subject: Conservatism; Endodontics; Voters; Political leadership; Special elections; Political parties

Document 194 of 500

Doug Ford has to make it clear: Is he for the people or for the developers?

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/doug-ford-has-make-clear-is-he-people-developers/docview/3092354415/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Doug+Ford+has+to+make+it+clear%3A+Is+he+for+the+people+or+for+the+developers%3F&amp;title=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-08-13&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: When politicians are on the campaign trail, they'll often say they're for the people. After they get elected, however, many seem to switch lanes and listen to powerful industries and their lobbyists. Why bother to vote at all then, many will say. Politicians are all the same, they say one thing to get elected, and do the opposite when in power.
Former U.S. President Bush's famous promise was,: "Read my lips. No new taxes." But that soon morphed into more taxes. Premier Doug Ford's slogan, "For The People" had wide appeal during the past elections, but after the Greenbelt land scandal, he's been often labelled as "for the developers."
The Premier has a lot of explaining to do. What hasn't helped this pro-developer image is his government's recent legislation, the "Cutting Red Tape to Build More Homes Act." This law takes away the voices of ordinary citizens (third parties) to challenge municipal plans and zoning changes at the Ontario Land Tribunal (OLT).
These hearings usually take place in a courtroom-like setting and individuals or citizens groups presenting their views have, in the past, needed an experienced and costly lawyer to navigate the complex rules and procedures. But since Ford's new law he passed in June this year, no third party appeals can be heard at the OLT. Very concerning for a premier who sold himself to us as a leader for the people.
Residents who object to the excessive height of a 90-storey condo building in their neighbourhood, for example, or the elimination of medical labs or doctor's offices, should be able to be heard in the planning process, even if their views are rejected in the end. People who've had to take an issue to court to get it resolved are more likely to accept a negative outcome, if they think they've had a fair trial and a fair chance to be heard.
To see the process as open and fair is key. "Justice must not only be done, but be seen to be done," as the famous justice Lord Hewitt quote goes. How can you see the process as fair if you've been excluded from it at the outset?
Minister of Municipal Affairs and Housing Paul Calandra said the purpose of the new legislation is to "remove obstacles" and cut red tape to build more homes faster. So it seems citizens are obstacles and red tape to be removed from the process. But these are the very people politicians will be asking to vote for them in the next election.
To find out what people want in particular areas of the city, why they moved there in the first place, it might be important to ask them and bring more of what consumers are asking for at prices they can afford.
There are multiple approved condo towers in midtown Toronto at the moment, which have few buyers, while local amenities are being forced away and neither current residents or newcomers seem happy with the offerings. There's a disconnect between what city officials and developers are planning and what consumers want, or can afford. Why not get more consumer input into housing problems then, and open discussions with all stakeholders, instead of legislating certain voices into silence?
Even if citizen views are not accepted in the final planning decisions, the fact that they've been heard is part of our democracy and part of building trust in our institutions. To eliminate third party appeals gives the impression the process may be rigged in favour of one party. It's clothed in all the language of building more homes faster, cheaper, and cutting red tape, but it turns out part of the red tape being cut out is the public's voice.
In the midst of a housing crisis, this is a time when government needs to build public trust, not further decrease it, especially in the aftermath of the Greenbelt scandal, where developers seem to have had very effective political connections at Queen's Park.
Once broken, trust is hard to gain back. Preventing the public from speaking out in the planning process may encourage more people to speak out at election time. In the pemier's own words, best not to throw your friends under the bus.
CAPTION: Ontario Premier Doug Ford, left speaks during a press conference regarding housing development in the Greater Toronto Area, as Toronto Mayor Olivia Chow, right, looks on at Toronto City Hall, in Toronto on Feb. 22.
CREDIT: Barbara Captijn Contributor

Subject: Consumers; Planning; Politicians

Business indexing term: Subject: Consumers

Document 195 of 500

The entire process of choosing home care contractors needs an independent sober second look

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/entire-process-choosing-home-care-contractors/docview/3092354255/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=The+entire+process+of+choosing+home+care+contractors+needs+an+independent+sober+second+look&amp;title=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-08-13&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: As Ontario ages, and elders continue to say they want to age in their own homes and communities, home care is becoming a vital source of assistance to many.
With Ontario having introduced a new pre-qualification process for home-care providers, Ontario taxpayers should have been able to expect more transparency and accountability and a higher quality of home care delivery. But that is now being questioned when examining who received those new home-care contracts, and which organizations and companies continue to be home-care providers.
With the release of the new 2024 pre-qualified home-care providers list one might have expected highly qualified organizations and individuals, especially non-profits, to be featured. That does not appear to be the case.
The majority of new contracts have gone to for-profit companies.
It should also be reasonable to expect that government would be careful about awarding major contracts to companies with a close relationship to government - either as friends or political donors - in order to avoid the appearance or fact of favouritism. How then do we explain a lucrative home-care contract having been awarded to Care Company Inc. owned by Laura Harris, the wife of Mike Harris, former premier of Ontario?
And how do we explain another company, Medavie Blue Cross, headed by a former PC premier of New Brunswick and former appointed head of Ontario Power Generation also receiving a home-care contract?
The chair of the advisory board of another company that received a contract is a former deputy minister appointed during the Harris/Eves governments.
Another company is owned by someone who was a significant donor to the PC Party.
What would cause the government to continue to award a home-care contract to ParaMed, a subsidiary of Extendicare - a company that was prosecuted by the U.S. Department of Justice and found liable in a $38-million lawsuit for billing "Medicare and Medicaid for materially substandard nursing services that were so deficient that they were effectively worthless and billed Medicare for medically unreasonable and unnecessary rehabilitation therapy services ..."?
It is also a mystery how companies whose owners, managing directors and managers with backgrounds in sales, accounting, hairdressing and esthetics, engineering and as corporate consultants or senior corporate executives managed to land home-care contracts.
The list goes on.
Older and disabled Ontarians should be able to expect that their government will deliver a home-care system that is non-profit, where competitive bidding is a thing of the past, where a solid track record matters, and where profits go into direct care, rather than the pockets of wealthy corporations.
The thousands of home care users in Ontario who rely on home care to be able to stay out of long-term care institutions should be able to feel confident that their government will actually serve their interests not those of private sector friends of the government and political donors.
All of us should be able to expect transparency and accountability in the criteria used, the decision-making process, and in knowing who has made these decisions in the awarding of home care contracts, but that is not the case.
The entire process of choosing home-care contractors needs an independent sober second look. Elders, people with disabilities and their families are counting on that because too many are reporting that continuing to have a failing home-care system is putting them at risk. Unions have alleged the Ford government is breaking Ontario's home-care system. They are absolutely right with current evidence of questionable home care contracts being awarded to these companies.
Home care contracts should not be a way to reward friends of any government with lucrative contracts. Ontarians deserve so much better. They deserve a real, functioning, accountable home-care system.
CAPTION: Older and disabled Ontarians should be able to expect that their government will deliver a home-care system that is non-profit, where competitive bidding is a thing of the past, where a solid track record matters, and where profits go into direct care, rather than the pockets of wealthy corporations, Patricia Spindel writes.
CREDIT: Patricia Spindel

Subject: Transparency; Corporate profits; Medicare; Nonprofit organizations; Accountability

Home-care contract process questioned

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/home-care-contract-process-questioned/docview/3092194112/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=unknown&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Home-care+contract+process+questioned&amp;title=The+Spectator&amp;issn=11899417&amp;date=2024-08-13&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.8&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Spectator&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: As Ontario ages, and elders continue to say they want to age in their own homes and communities, home care is becoming a vital source of assistance to many.
With Ontario having introduced a new pre-qualification process for home-care providers, Ontario taxpayers should have been able to expect more transparency and accountability and a higher quality of home care delivery. But that is now being questioned when examining who received those new home-care contracts, and which organizations and companies continue to be home-care providers.

The chair of the advisory board of another company that received a contract is a former deputy minister appointed in the Harris/Eves governments.

Home care contracts should not be a way to reward friends of any government with lucrative contracts.
Ontarians deserve so much better. They deserve a real, functioning, accountable home-care system.
Patricia Spindel is the chair of Seniors for Social Action Ontario and a former
associate dean of Health Sciences at Humber College.

Document 197 of 500

Trudeau and Ford lure $575M Goodyear investment in Napanee with $64.3M in subsidies

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/trudeau-ford-lure-575m-goodyear-investment/docview/3092444191/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Trudeau+and+Ford+lure+%24575M+Goodyear+investment+in+Napanee+with+%2464.3M+in+subsidies&amp;title=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-08-12&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: The rubber is hitting the road for Prime Minister Justin Trudeau and Premier Doug Ford's electric-vehicle push.
Buoyed by up to $44.3 million in federal subsidies and another $20 million in provincial funding, Goodyear is expanding its Napanee factory to produce specialized tires for EVs and all-terrain vehicles.
Goodyear Tire & Rubber Company said its more than $575-million investment will create another 200 skilled positions in Napanee, 225 kilometres east of Toronto, ballooning its workforce to 1,000 workers by 2027.
Trudeau and Ford boasted Monday that Goodyear's gambit inflates recent EV investments to $46 billion across Canada in recent years.
Lured by federal and provincial production subsidies and other incentives, most of that money is coming to Ontario.
"Companies from across the world are choosing Canada. It's another vote of confidence in Canada's auto sector workers," said Trudeau, who is soon expected to match U.S. trade tariffs on heavily subsidized Chinese-made EVs to protect the domestic industry.
The prime minister noted "Goodyear is modernizing its Napanee plant, (which) will create manufacturing jobs, grow our EV industry and use modern technology to keep our air clean."
Ford added the company's expansion was "another significant boost to Ontario's growing electric-vehicle sector, building on the billions of dollars that have been invested in the sector over the past four years."
With the premier considering a provincial election next spring, a year ahead of the scheduled June 2026 vote, the campaign-style announcement helped him to showcase how his Progressive Conservatives are working well with Trudeau's Liberals.
"Today's announcement is another great example of all levels of government - of everyone - coming together to support local businesses and workers and to help create better jobs and bigger paycheques," said Ford.
"Across Ontario, we're seeing major investments and new jobs created as we build out our end-to-end EV supply chain, connecting critical minerals in Northern Ontario to world-class manufacturing across the province," said Ford.
"Companies are choosing Ontario because of our transportation infrastructure, our competitive business environment and our skilled workforce."
Goodyear's announcement that it would soon manufacture another two million tires annually in Napanee may also help Trudeau's struggling Liberals, who are expected to face voters in October 2025.
Trailing Pierre Poilievre's Conservatives in public-opinion polls, the prime minister attacked the Tory leader, accusing him of choosing "to write off Canada's auto sector" by questioning the subsidies.
Trudeau also railed against Poilievre for having "no plan to fight climate change (and) ... no plan for the economy either."
"It's a bit of a joke that Poilievre is suddenly talking about workers in the auto industry - he has said repeatedly that he wouldn't be making these investments in our auto industry," he said.
But Tory MP Kyle Seeback (Dufferin-Caledon) countered that Trudeau "has failed to stand up to the regime in Beijing to defend our industries against flooding our market with cheap EVs, steel, aluminum and other products."
"Common-sense Conservatives will stand up for our workers, take a stand against authoritarian governments threatening our industries, and we will bring home investment so we can build things in Canada again and create powerful paycheques for our workers," said Seeback.
Goodyear said its Napanee plant will be at net-zero emissions by 2040, a decade ahead of Ottawa's goal of a clean, green economy by 2050.
It pledged to reduce greenhouse gas emissions by 10 per cent by 2030 and by 100 per cent within the next 16 years.
Goodyear CEO and president Mark Stewart - who joined the rubber giant from Stellantis earlier this year after being key to the auto company's EV transformation - said the announcement "reinforces our long-term commitment to Canada."
"We are appreciative to the federal, provincial and local governments for their support and to our Napanee associates for their dedication to building the next generation of tires," said Stewart.
Goodyear, which has been in Napanee for 35 years, is that region's largest single employer.
"They are an integral part of the growth and success of our community," said Greater Napanee Mayor Terry Richardson.
Flavio Volpe, president of the Automotive Parts Manufacturers' Association, who has worked closely with Ottawa and Queen's Park on the EV strategy, said Goodyear's investment was significant.
Because EVs are heavier and quieter than traditional gasoline-powered cars and trucks, they require high-tech tires.
"We needed to win this from American and Mexican competition," said Volpe, noting it comes despite U.S. President Joe Biden's Inflation Reduction Act (IRA), which gives huge subsidies to encourage companies to remain stateside.
"A U.S. company investing in Canada in an IRA era is very interesting," he said.
CAPTION: Premier Doug Ford and Prime Minister Justin Trudeau look over a vehicle along an assembly line at an event announcing plans for a Honda electric vehicle battery plant in Alliston in April 2024.Canadian Prime Minister Justin Trudeau is set to make an economic announcement during a visit to Lennox and Addington County, Ont. on Monday.Trudeau will be joined by Ontario Premier Doug Ford, as well as Economic Development Minister Mary Ng and her provincial counterpart, Vic Fedeli.In May, Trudeau and Ford announced that Honda, the vehicle manufacturer, was investing $15 billion to build four electric vehicle plants in Ontario.And back in February, the pair signed a $3.1 billion health care deal, to improve and expand Ontario's health sector and increase job opportunities.Though details are unknown about Trudeau and Ford's Monday morning meetings, it could have big implications for Ontarians.For more info, please go to https://globalnews.caSubscribe to Global News Channel HERE: http://bit.ly/20fcXDcLike Global News on Facebook HERE: http://bit.ly/255GMJQFollow Global News on X HERE: http://bit.ly/1Toz8mtFollow Global News on Instagram HERE: https://bit.ly/2QZaZIB#GlobalNews #JustinTrudeau #Ontario #DougFord #Canada
