Document 198 of 500

Former Stoney Creek mayor Bob Hodgson launched Flag Day celebration

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/former-stoney-creek-mayor-bob-hodgson-launched/docview/3091972514/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Former+Stoney+Creek+mayor+Bob+Hodgson+launched+Flag+Day+celebration&amp;title=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-08-12&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Former Stoney Creek mayor Bob Hodgson was a politician, businessperson, salesperson and even a wrestler, but he is probably best remembered as the person behind Stoney Creek's annual Canada Flag Day celebration.
The celebration for the Maple Leaf flag was first held in June 1969 and Hodgson served as its first chair. The flag had become the country's new flag in February 1965 and Hodgson hoped the celebration would spread across the country.
"It is the feeling of all concerned that such a day would be one positive step forward in aid of national unity," he told The Spectator in 1972.
Sadly, Canada Flag Day folded in 2003 due to money troubles. It came back a few years later, but was finally cancelled in 2012.
When it was run by Hodgson - who died at 92 on July 24 - it was a going concern. Hodgson served as chair and co-ordinator of Flag Day between 1969 and 1973, and then as honorary chair in 1974.
In June 1972, the Flag Day celebration ran a week and featured a flag-raising ceremony, a lobster dinner, a church social, a Bavarian beer garden, pancake breakfast and sidewalk sales. The kicker was the parade down King Street to Battlefield Park. It had 4,000 participants and 18 marching bands.
"He was always trying to do stuff for people," said his daughter Lori Peters, a retired court worker. "He liked helping people . . . Everyone liked him and he did many things during his life."
Peters said her father was always cracking jokes. She recalled when she was growing up her dad had an answer to any question.
"I always thought he was the smartest man around," she said. "He always had an answer. When I got older, I would say to him that doesn't sound right. He said, 'Well, you wanted an answer'."
Hodgson started his political career in 1975 after working in insurance, liquor sales and serving as the first general manager of the Better Business Bureau in Hamilton. He ran in the provincial election for the Progressive Conservatives in Hamilton East but the riding was won by the NDP's Bob Mackenzie.
Hodgson was elected Ward 7 councillor in Stoney Creek in 1985 and deputy mayor in 1988. He became acting mayor in 1991 upon the death of Stan Napper and was elected that year in the municipal election. He served three years and was defeated by Ward 7 councillor Anne Bain. He ran for the mayor's job in 1997 but Bain was re-elected.
Hodgson was born Nov. 23, 1931 to Alfred and Winnifred Hogdson. His father was a wrestler, using the name Jack Wentworth, and wrestled across Great Britain, Europe and South Africa before the Second World War. After the war, he wrestled in Canada and in the southern United States. Wentworth later ran the Queenston Health Studio in east Hamilton.
Hodgson was schooled in Hamilton, London, Capetown, Birmingham, Alabama and Amarillo, Texas. He wrestled himself for six months in Europe, using the name Bobby Wentworth or Bobby Wayne.
His daughter said her father almost became a radio announcer in Texas in about 1950, but her grandfather told the station owners his son was coming back to Canada. After 1975, Hodgson worked as executive vice-president of the Canadian BBB and executive director of the Canadian Transmission Association.
Hodgson was a member of many groups, including the Stoney Creek Kinsmen, and was a diehard Ticat fan who had season tickets since 1949. He chaired the 1996 Hamilton Grey Cup volunteer committee.
He jokingly told The Spec in 1993 that when he died he wanted his ashes spread at Ivor Wynne. His daughter says the urn with his ashes is black and gold.
Hodgson is survived by his wife Ruth, son Rob, daughter Lori Peters, three grandchildren and sister Audrey. He was predeceased by son Larry in 2012.
Daniel Nolan can be reached at dannolanwrites@gmail.com
CAPTION: Former Stoney Creek mayor Bob Hodgson - who was a diehard Ticat fan - at a recent game with his wife Ruth. The urn carrying his ashes is gold and black.Bob Hodgson, former mayor of Stoney Creek, died at 92.Former Stoney Creek mayor Bob Hodgson, and the originator of Canada Flag Day, in a recent family photo.

Title: Former Stoney Creek mayor Bob Hodgson launched Flag Day celebration

Document 199 of 500

Canada's premiers are in a fight for federalism, not funding

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/canadas-premiers-are-fight-federalism-not-funding/docview/3091962705/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Canada%27s+premiers+are+in+a+fight+for+federalism%2C+not+funding&amp;title=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-08-12&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: The recent Premiers' Council of the Federation meeting in Halifax had a bold new tone. Instead of begging for federal handouts, they talked about rejecting them.
Is this the start of real change?
During the meeting, the premiers expressed intense frustration over the lack of federal support and partnership in health care, which remains their top priority. This frustration comes just four months after Quebec signed its own side deal with the federal government, finalizing details from last year's $196.1-billion health care funding agreement.
Since the council was formed in 2003, meetings have centred on money and control.
Provinces want more federal money with less federal control, while the federal government wants more control and to pay as little as possible. This year, discussions seem to have gone beyond funding to address deeper frustrations.
Alberta Premier Danielle Smith highlighted the provinces' rejection of federal funding for boutique programming, noting that provinces end up funding the "lion's share" of operational costs. Historically, provinces have grumbled but accepted federal funding - bribes designed to build unaffordable programs - with one exception.
The final pillar of medicare, the Medical Care Act of 1966, came with an offer from the federal government to fund 50 per cent of medical services through a new "social development" tax. This tax would increase regardless of whether provinces accepted the new funding.
Then Ontario premier John Robarts criticized this move, calling it "the greatest Machiavellian fraud ever perpetrated on the people of Canada."
The current group of premiers reject federal bribery cloaked in the constitutionally questionable use of federal spending power. If they stick together, the provinces can neutralize federal power. The federal government is aware of this dynamic, which might explain why it used "side deals" in the 2023 federal-provincial funding agreement mentioned above.
In fairness, not all funding is bribery. It hinges on how it is structured.
Nova Scotia Premier Tim Houston, chair of the council, sent a letter to PM Justin Trudeau on behalf of the premiers, calling for "true partnership to revitalize co-operative federalism in Canada."
A true partnership, according to Peter Block, an organization development expert, includes an exchange of purpose, the right to say no, joint accountability, and absolute honesty. This means provincial and federal leaders should meet to build a shared purpose before planning new programs.
Each party should have the right to stop plans, share accountability for performance, and be honest about resources and intentions.
True partnership captures the essence of what federalism was meant to be in Canada. Federalism provides a foundation for negotiation, not a power play for forcing governments to take actions they otherwise wouldn't.
In his book "American Covenant: How the Constitution unified our nation - and could again," Yuval Levin makes a similar point about the American constitution. It exists to facilitate peace and co-operation despite diverse interests. Though different from American republicanism, Canadian federalism serves the same purpose - peace and compromise, not power and force exerted by one government on another.
The premiers' call for "true partnership to revitalize co-operative federalism in Canada" will require compromise from all parties. We cannot tolerate one level of government wielding power over the other. Governments must embrace true partnership, or Canadian federalism will become a charade.
The premiers have taken the high ground and asked for change. Will Trudeau listen?
CAPTION: Manitoba Premier Wab Kinew, centre, speaks to reporters beside Prince Edward Island Premier Dennis King, left, and Ontario Premier Doug Ford as Canada's premiers hold a press conference to close the Council of the Federation meetings in Halifax on July 17. The premiers are demanding a true partnership in federalism and want an end to federal funding with strings attached, Shawn Whatley writes.
CREDIT: Shawn Whatley

Document 200 of 500

Chinese mining firm subsidiary disputes Ottawa's review of Peruvian transaction

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/chinese-mining-firm-subsidiary-disputes-ottawas/docview/3091535609/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Chinese+mining+firm+subsidiary+disputes+Ottawa%27s+review+of+Peruvian+transaction&amp;title=The+Spectator&amp;issn=11899417&amp;date=2024-08-12&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.6&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Spectator&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: A subsidiary of a Chinese state-owned mining firm says Canada is wrongly considering a national security review in its agreement to purchase a gold and copper mine in Peru.
In May, Vancouver-based Pan American Silver Corp. announced an agreement worth almost $300 million (U.S.) to sell its stake in Peru's La Arena gold mine to Jinteng (Singapore) Mining, a subsidiary of China's Zijin Mining Group.
Pan American said then that the agreement was "subject to customary conditions and receipt of regulatory approvals."
Since then, however, Canada Industry Minister François-Philippe Champagne has found the agreement "could be injurious to national security" and told the company in late June he "may" order a formal review under the act.
Certain types of foreign investments involving Canadian companies are reviewed on national security grounds, and Jinteng voluntarily notified the director of investments at Innovation, Science and Economic Development Canada shortly after the agreement was announced.
The federal government maintains a list of nearly three dozen critical minerals "essential to Canada's economic or national security," and reviews of investments involving foreign companies like Zijin are a protective measure to maintain Canadian control of materials essential to "the green and digital economy."
Zijin is partially owned by the Chinese government and overseen by members of the Chinese Communist Party.
Canada's Critical Minerals Strategy outlines how allies in Europe have "experienced the consequences of dependence upon non-like-minded countries for strategic commodities."
Jinteng claims in a judicial review application filed in Federal Court in late July that the minister "lacks jurisdiction under the act" to order a national security review of the La Arena deal. "The targets are Peruvian entities. They do not have a place of operations in Canada or otherwise carry on operations in Canada, they do not have individuals in Canada who are employed or self-employed in connection with their operations and they do not have asset in Canada used in carrying on their operations," the application says.
National security experts have warned of the geopolitical consequences of letting foreign actors scoop up Canadian companies in the sector, and Jinteng's actions to skirt the national security review process represent a test of Ottawa's reach on companies incorporated in Canada, but that have no domestic operations and exist only to hold foreign assets.
Despite the voluntary notification sent to Ottawa about the deal, Jinteng claimed it doesn't involve a "Canadian business" as defined by the act because the target companies and their assets are in Peru, though they're owned by Pan American subsidiaries incorporated in B.C. and Ontario.
The company claims in its application the minister's decision is "based on an untenable and unreasonable interpretation of the act, and is therefore wrong in law."
Jinteng claims the minister "lacks jurisdiction under the act" to order a national security review of the La Arena deal.
Aaron Shull, managing director and general counsel at the Centre for International Governance Innovation in Ontario, said the case presents a "fairly complicated story that is actually quite simple."
He said the deal boils down to a Canadian parent company selling Peruvian assets to a Chinese company, and the structure of the subsidiaries involved could be for various reasons such as shielding liability and tax purposes.
Shull said the Canadian government has indicated its intention to scrutinize and "get tougher on" foreign investments involving things like strategic minerals involving "hostile states."
"Especially from state-owned enterprises or enterprises that are so closely affiliated with the state," he said.
The deal, he said, is not only for gold-mining assets, but also for a nearby gold-copper mine and a power transmission facility.
"You could probably make a fairly compelling case that this is part of a strategic play on China's part in Latin America," he said. "The Canadian government, the American government, a whole bunch of others have been making a lot of noise about being tougher on this type of stuff. I think that's what you're seeing here is the kind of implementation of that sabre rattling in this kind of contested geopolitical environment."
Innovation, Science and Economic Development Canada declined to speak about Jinteng's Federal Court application.
"The Government of Canada does not comment on matters before the court. Due to confidentiality provisions of the Investment Canada Act, the Government cannot comment on specific transactions," the agency said in a statement.
The federal government announced "significant changes" to the act in March this year.
"While foreign investment is essential to economic prosperity, the Investment Canada Act is a key lever that allows the government of Canada to act quickly and decisively when foreign investment would threaten national security," the department said at the time. "As the world changes and threats evolve, Canada needs new tools to continue protecting the economy and keeping Canadians safe."
Shull said the case documents don't indicate what specific national security concerns the minister may have, but said he'll be watching the case closely for the outcome.
He said if the company's successful in staving off a national security review, it would put Canada in an "odd spot" by potentially giving foreign companies a means of structuring deals outside of the legislative regime with "just a bunch of creative lawyering."
The underlying issues beyond the specifics of the case involve the intersection of national security, prosperity and economic security, which "we tend not to think about ... in a strategically integrated fashion in this country," Shull said. "It's part of a much bigger puzzle, " he said. "The primary concern that I have is that hostile states and strategic adversaries are looking at this stuff in an integrated way."

Document 201 of 500

Former Stoney Creek mayor launched Flag Day celebration: Once-bustling festival was known for its large parade to Battlefield Park

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/former-stoney-creek-mayor-launched-flag-day/docview/3091535594/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Former+Stoney+Creek+mayor+launched+Flag+Day+celebration%3A+Once-bustling+festival+was+known+for+its+large+parade+to+Battlefield+Park&amp;title=The+Spectator&amp;issn=11899417&amp;date=2024-08-12&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.5&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Spectator&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Former Stoney Creek mayor Bob Hodgson was a politician, businessperson, salesperson and even a wrestler, but he is probably best remembered as the person behind Stoney Creek's annual Canada Flag Day celebration.
The celebration for the Maple Leaf flag was first held in June 1969 and Hodgson served as its first chair. The flag had become the country's new flag in February 1965 and Hodgson hoped the celebration would spread across the country.
"It is the feeling of all concerned that such a day would be one positive step forward in aid of national unity," he told The Spectator in 1972.
Sadly, Canada Flag Day folded in 2003 due to money troubles. It came back a few years later, but was finally cancelled in 2012.
When it was run by Hodgson - who died at 92 on July 24 - it was a going concern. Hodgson served as chair and co-ordinator of Flag Day between 1969 and 1973, and then as honorary chair in 1974.
In June 1972, the Flag Day celebration ran a week and featured a flag-raising ceremony, a lobster dinner, a church social, a Bavarian beer garden, pancake breakfast and sidewalk sales. The kicker was the parade down King Street to Battlefield Park. It had 4,000 participants and 18 marching bands.
"He was always trying to do stuff for people," said his daughter Lori Peters, a retired court worker. "He liked helping people . . . Everyone liked him and he did many things during his life."
Peters said her father was always cracking jokes. She recalled when she was growing up her dad had an answer to any question.
"I always thought he was the smartest man around," she said. "He always had an answer. When I got older, I would say to him that doesn't sound right. He said, 'Well, you wanted an answer'."
Hodgson started his political career in 1975 after working in insurance, liquor sales and serving as the first general manager of the Better Business Bureau in Hamilton. He ran in the provincial election for the Progressive Conservatives in Hamilton East but the riding was won by the NDP's Bob Mackenzie.
Hodgson was elected Ward 7 councillor in Stoney Creek in 1985 and deputy mayor in 1988. He became acting mayor in 1991 upon the death of Stan Napper and was elected that year in the municipal election. He served three years and was defeated by Ward 7 councillor Anne Bain. He ran for the mayor's job in 1997 but Bain was re-elected.
Hodgson was born Nov. 23, 1931 to Alfred and Winnifred Hogdson. His father was a wrestler, using the name Jack Wentworth, and wrestled across Great Britain, Europe and South Africa before the Second World War. After the war, he wrestled in Canada and in the southern United States. Wentworth later ran the Queenston Health Studio in east Hamilton.
Hodgson was schooled in Hamilton, London, Capetown, Birmingham, Alabama and Amarillo, Texas. He wrestled himself for six months in Europe, using the name Bobby Wentworth or Bobby Wayne.
His daughter said her father almost became a radio announcer in Texas in about 1950, but her grandfather told the station owners his son was coming back to Canada. After 1975, Hodgson worked as executive vice-president of the Canadian BBB and executive director of the Canadian Transmission Association.
Hodgson was a member of many groups, including the Stoney Creek Kinsmen, and was a diehard Ticat fan who had season tickets since 1949. He chaired the 1996 Hamilton Grey Cup volunteer committee.
He jokingly told The Spec in 1993 that when he died he wanted his ashes spread at Ivor Wynne. His daughter says the urn with his ashes is black and gold.
Hodgson is survived by his wife Ruth, son Rob, daughter Lori Peters, three grandchildren and sister Audrey. He was predeceased by son Larry in 2012.
Daniel Nolan can be reached at dannolanwrites@gmail.com
CAPTION: Above, former Stoney Creek mayor Bob Hodgson, and the originator of Canada Flag Day, in a recent family photo. Left, Hodgson, who was a diehard Ticat fan, at a recent game with his wife Ruth. The urn carrying his ashes is gold and black.Lori Peters Photos

Document 202 of 500

A brief history of flipping the bird: From ancient Greece, to Pierre Trudeau, to one Toronto police officer

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/brief-history-flipping-bird-ancient-greece-pierre/docview/3091274961/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=A+brief+history+of+flipping+the+bird%3A+From+ancient+Greece%2C+to+Pierre+Trudeau%2C+to+one+Toronto+police+officer&amp;title=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-08-11&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: In ancient Greece, while Athens was being ravaged by the plague and under siege by Sparta, comedic playwright Aristophanes put an insult into writing.
It appeared in "Peace," a 421 B.C. comedy, telling the story of a farmer searching for a lost goddess.
"Don't you see how the sickle-maker is happy," Aristophanes wrote, according to one translation, "and how he gave the spear maker the finger?"
That is, the middle finger.
The bird.
The one-finger salute.
That prodigious protrusion of pent-up frustration.
It's the same finger raised by a Toronto police officer, and made viral this past week in an online video, sparking a Toronto Police Service investigation for inappropriate behaviour.
The through-line from ancient times to today is, of course, not that straight.
The middle finger has a complicated history, one that has taken it from the Greeks to baseball players in the late 1800s to the hand of a prime minister, with stops in between.
Through time, it has been employed in different ways and attributed different meanings. It has been debated legally. And it has become perhaps the most recognizable gesture in the world, landing prisoners, politicians and police officers alike in hot water.
A questionable parking job; a finger of blame
An undated video posted online this week showed two police officers being confronted on a Toronto street. The man taking the video claimed the officers had parked illegally in a loading zone while getting coffee. One of the officers said she needed caffeine while working an 11-hour shift. Later, as the cruiser waited at an intersection, she said, "We're doing our job, bro."
"I'm not your bro," the man taking the video responded. The officer flipped him off and the video ended.
It generated a ton of criticism online.
"We acknowledge the officer's response was not appropriate," Const. Shannon Eames said in a statement. The force's Professional Standard Unit is now investigating.
Premier Doug Ford took a different view of the encounter.
"Let's thank them, rather than getting Google glasses and trying to harass our police. That's what I call those. Harassment," Ford said Wednesday. "As far as I'm concerned, they did nothing wrong."
So just where does this particular gesture sit in the spectrum of the rude and obscene? History has its suggestions, but no easy answers.
Poking a hole in some theories
Evidence of the middle finger being used in various insulting gestures dates back to Aristophanes' time, according to Max Nelson, a professor of Greek and Roman studies at the University of Windsor. Aristophanes referenced the finger in multiple works in the 420s B.C.
Later, in the second and third centuries A.D., Greek philosopher Epictetus and biographer Diogenes Laertius referenced it in their own writings.
The meaning of the finger likely differed from today, however. The way they gave it probably did, too. It sometimes involved snapping the middle finger and thumb, which was considered insulting. Other times, the middle finger was used to point horizontally at another person as a sexual insult.
There is, in fact, no evidence linking those middle finger insults of the ancient Greeks and Romans to modern times, according to Nelson.
"People like to say things go back to the Greeks and Romans, but there are very few things that have this kind of longevity," Nelson said. "Especially hand gestures. They're very culturally specific and change quite a bit. So it's kind of ridiculous to say something has survived all the way since ancient times."
Which doesn't mean there isn't more recent history.
From Beaneaters to the Trudeaus
In more modern times, the middle finger likely began to emerge as an insult in the U.S. in the late 19th century, according to American University Prof. Ira Robbins. It was documented on American soil for the first time in an 1886 team photo of baseball's Boston Beaneaters and New York Giants.
Its use has grown exponentially since - even playing a role in international relations.
In 1968, American soldiers imprisoned in North Korea flipped the finger in propaganda photos, explaining to their captors it was a Hawaiian good luck sign. It took U.S. media reports for the North Koreans to catch on.
The finger is now known the world over, in part thanks to the influence of American media, according to a 2019 analysis by University of California, San Diego cognitive science professor Benjamin Bergen.
It also has one enduring and uniquely Canadian nickname: the Trudeau Salute.
That finds its origins in August 1982, when then-prime minister Pierre Trudeau was vacationing in the British Columbia Rockies. Protesters upset with the economic hardship in Canada at the time - unemployment shot up to 11 per cent that year, while GDP dropped nearly three per cent - tossed tomatoes at Trudeau's train, UPI reported at the time.
Trudeau came to the window. He "smiled, then scowled," one protester said, "then gave us the old Trudeau one-finger salute and pulled down the shade."
Trudeau would not be the last politician to do so. In May 2003, the front page of the Star showed Ontario MPP John O'Toole flipping off the opposition. In April 2004, Calgary East MP Deepak Obhrai was accused of giving a Scarborough MP the finger in the House of Commons. Last year, a Thunder Bay councillor was accused of flipping off residents.
And, of course, the middle finger has been weaponized as part of a countrywide political rally cry: "f--- Trudeau." In recent years, it has found its way onto bumper stickers, flags and hats.
A 'God-given' Charter right
While rude - and some would say unbecoming of elected officials - there appears little doubt that it is legal.
A 45-year-old Quebec teacher tested that out in 2021. Returning home from a walk in Beaconsfield, just outside Montreal, Neall Epstein gave double-fisted middle fingers to a neighbour with whom he had previously clashed over children playing in front of Epstein's home.
Epstein was arrested for criminal harassment and prosecuted. But in 2023, a judge acquitted him. He ruled that giving someone the middle finger is protected by the Charter of Rights and Freedoms.
"It is not a crime to give someone the finger," the judge wrote in his decision. "Flipping the proverbial bird is a God-given, Charter enshrined right that belongs to every red-blooded Canadian. It may not be civil, it may not be polite, it may not be gentlemanly. Nevertheless, it does not trigger criminal liability."
The same was found by a U.S. appeals court in 2019. It ruled that flipping someone off is a form of free speech, matching at least two earlier cases that reached similar conclusions.
In 2018, a Vermont man was arrested for giving the finger to a state police trooper - only to have the charges of disorderly conduct dismissed. He then launched a lawsuit against the state and the trooper and received $175,000 US this June to settle it.
But how about a police officer flipping off a citizen, as that Toronto cop did?
That may run up against Ontario's Community Safety and Policing Act, which stipulates that officers "shall not conduct themselves in a manner that undermines, or is likely to undermine, public trust in policing."
It could also be at odds with the Toronto Police Service's values, which include acting professionally and "treating all people with empathy, respect, equity and dignity."
In 420 B.C. or 2024 A.D., the middle finger has never been that.

Document 203 of 500

Aug. 11: Doug Fords booze plan is excellent news for his buddies, ER overcrowding and other letters to the editor

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/aug-11-doug-fords-booze-plan-is-excellent-news/docview/3091274939/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Aug.+11%3A+Doug+Fords+booze+plan+is+excellent+news+for+his+buddies%2C+ER+overcrowding+and+other+letters+to+the+editor&amp;title=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-08-11&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Story just the tip of the iceberg
Re: Len King could walk and talk when he waved goodbye to his family from the ambulance. He ended up bedridden, incoherent and died in hospital, Aug. 8
I am compelled to address the pressing issue of overcrowding in Ontario's emergency rooms, particularly as it pertains to our senior population. Joanna Frketich's recent article on Len King's experience shines a much-needed light on a problem that is both pervasive and under-reported.
As the chief operating officer of the Canadian Association of Retired Persons, I receive daily accounts from our members about the struggles they face within our health-care system. I have witnessed this with my own elderly parents. My father, who suffers from Alzheimer's disease, was left in a hallway for days, leading to delirium, the use of restraints and sedation. These experiences are not isolated incidents but rather indicative of a systemic failure.
We are merely seeing the tip of the iceberg as our health-care system crashes into a crisis, exacerbated by the growing number of older adults requiring care. Emergency rooms are beyond capacity, and ageism plays a significant role in the lack of action from authorities. There is an urgent need for comprehensive reform to address these issues and ensure that our elderly citizens receive the dignity and care they deserve.
As I advocated for my father, I felt an overwhelming sense of shame - not only for his treatment but for the countless other older adults who were on their own, left without a voice in the understaffed emergency rooms and overcrowded hallways, unable to navigate a broken system.
It is imperative we, as a society, recognize and address this growing crisis. Our elders, who have contributed so much to our communities, should not have to endure such indignities in their most vulnerable moments.
Thank you for bringing attention to this dire situation. I hope that through continued reporting and advocacy, we can spur the necessary changes to improve our health-care system for all, particularly our aging population.
Anthony Quinn, Canadian Association of Retired Persons (CARP)
City hall needs to tighten its belt
Re: Should businesses in construction zones get financial help from the city? Aug. 8
We have a tax crisis in our city. Ask any resident or business owner. Should businesses be compensated during prolonged construction? Of course. Destroying small businesses will never return the city to a more balanced tax system we so desperately need. But where does the money come from? How about eliminating unnecessary spending?
Recently we had a speed bump installed on our street at the request of our councillor. No traffic study required. No public consultation. Governance by edict. Entire sidewalks are being replaced rather than repaired. Why? Every day I witness hundreds of thousands of dollars in savings without any impact on services. Is the city waiting for more people to move or default on their taxes before they realize they need to prioritize projects to reduce our deficit? The silence from councillors on this issue is truly astounding.
David Borsellino, Hamilton
Provide relief where it's needed
Premier Doug Ford was successful with pushing through his plan to allow alcohol products to be sold in grocery stores and gas station convenience outlets. That is fantastic economic news, especially for some of his business friends. We all know that oil companies and large grocery chains have been suffering and barely scraping by for years. Maybe now with Doug's help, they might finally be able to post a tiny little profit at year's end!
Pete Wignall, Burlington
Columnist a delight to read
Re: Columnist jumps on bandwagon, Aug. 7
Say what you want about Vinay Menon, but his column is a delight to read. Not only is he spot on about Donald Trump, but he definitely has many of us saying "Thank God, I am Canadian." Trump is the only candidate and person I know who wants a catastrophic event to happen to his people and blame it on his opponent. Narcissism at its finest! Thank you Vinay for your most insightful columns.
Linda Ferrie, Waterdown
CAPTION: 'There is an urgent need for comprehensive reform to address many health-care issues and ensure our elderly citizens receive the dignity and care they deserve,' Anthony Quinn writes.

Aug. 10: Biden dropping out was no conspiracy, watch Canadians send a message to Liberals and other letters to the editor

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/aug-10-biden-dropping-out-was-no-conspiracy-watch/docview/3091144339/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Aug.+10%3A+Biden+dropping+out+was+no+conspiracy%2C+watch+Canadians+send+a+message+to+Liberals+and+other+letters+to+the+editor&amp;title=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-08-10&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: No conspiracy at play here
Abraham Lincoln is credited with the proverb "Don't change horses in midstream." It was the U.S. president's advice not to alter your plan, or action, or to change leader in the middle of a project or at an inopportune moment. To do so is an extremely bold move and an act of calculation brought on by necessity.
To have Fox News and Spectator readers think the removal of President Joe Biden was cunningly planned and manipulated by Kamala Harris months in advance speaks of the paranoia and fake news that swirls around the American election. The Democratic Party simply made a desperate Hail Mary pass that Biden's time had passed and so far their gamble seems to be working.
President Biden should be commended for putting his love of country ahead of his own ego. The world needs more leaders with class and dignity, and far less of ones with massive self-conceit.
Jon Lisowski, Hamilton
Priorities need realignment
I was really disappointed to read about expanding drinks to convenience stores. It saddens me to hear about our income from taxes being chipped away at by private business. It already enrages me to be bombarded by advertisements on public broadcasting spending our tax money selling the same story. Oh to hear "paid for by the Government of Ontario" about them investing in health care or people in need.
Irene Hough, Hamilton
Name calling won't work
You can tell the federal Liberals are in total chaos with Justin Trudeau just surfing while the fortune of the party is completely sinking. The latest example of the Liberals grasping at straws is them stealing the current Democrat tactic of calling anyone who votes Republican "weird." So Trudeau's Liberals think they are going to wash away their years of bad performance by calling Canadians who won't vote for them as weird.
Neither Democrats in America or Liberals in Canada learned anything from the mistake and backlash against Hillary Clinton when she called Americans who wouldn't vote for her as "deplorables." They say people who don't learn from history are doomed to repeat it. Get you popcorn and enjoy the fun of watching all Canadians send a message to the Liberals by crushing the politicians who called them weird!
Chris Robertson, Stony Plain, Alta.
CAPTION: Democratic presidential nominee Vice-President Kamala Harris speaks at a campaign rally Wednesday. Democratic Party members made a desperate Hail Mary pass when they switched their choice to Harris, a reader says.

Subject: Presidents; Conspiracy; Political parties

Document 205 of 500

Letters

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/letters/docview/3091093849/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=unknown&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Letters&amp;title=The+Spectator&amp;issn=11899417&amp;date=2024-08-10&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.18&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Spectator&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Sad story is just
the tip of the iceberg
Re: Len King could walk and talk when he waved goodbye to his family from the ambulance. He ended up bedridden, incoherent and died in hospital, Aug. 8
I am compelled to address the pressing issue of overcrowding in Ontario's emergency rooms, particularly as it pertains to our senior population. Joanna Frketich's article on Len King's experience shines a much-needed light on a problem that is both pervasive and under-reported.
As the chief operating officer of the Canadian Association of Retired Persons, I receive daily accounts from members about the struggles they face within our health-care system. I have witnessed this with my own elderly parents. My father, who suffers from Alzheimer's disease, was left in a hallway for days, leading to delirium, the use of restraints and sedation. These experiences are not isolated incidents, but rather indicative of a systemic failure.
We are merely seeing the tip of the iceberg as our health-care system crashes into a crisis, exacerbated by the growing number of older adults requiring care. Emergency rooms are beyond capacity and ageism plays a significant role in the lack of action from authorities. There is an urgent need for comprehensive reform to address these issues and ensure our elderly citizens receive the dignity and care they deserve.
As I advocated for my father, I felt an overwhelming sense of shame - not only for his treatment but for the countless other older adults who were on their own, left without a voice in the understaffed emergency rooms and overcrowded hallways, unable to navigate a broken system.
It is imperative we address this growing crisis. Our elders, who have contributed so much to our communities, should not have to endure such indignities.
Thank you for bringing attention to this dire situation. I hope that, through continued reporting and advocacy, we can spur the necessary changes to improve our health-care system for all, particularly our aging population.
Anthony Quinn, Canadian
Association of Retired Persons (CARP)
Provide relief
where it's needed
Premier Doug Ford was successful with pushing through his plan to allow alcohol products to be sold in grocery stores and gas station convenience outlets. That is fantastic economic news, especially for some of his business friends. We all know that oil companies and large grocery chains have been suffering and barely scraping by for years. Maybe now, with Doug's help, they might finally be able to post a tiny little profit at year's end!
Pete Wignall, Burlington
City hall needs
to tighten its belt
Re: Should businesses in construction zones get financial help from the city? Aug. 8
We have a tax crisis in our city. Ask any resident or business owner. Should businesses be compensated during prolonged construction? Of course. Destroying small businesses will never return the city to a more balanced tax system we so desperately need. But where does the money come from? How about eliminating unnecessary spending?
Recently we had a speed bump installed on our street at the request of our councillor. No traffic study required. No public consultation. Governance by edict. Entire sidewalks are being replaced rather than repaired. Why? Every day I witness hundreds of thousands of dollars in savings without any impact on services. Is the city waiting for more people to move or default on their taxes before they realize they need to prioritize projects to reduce our deficit? The silence from councillors on this issue is truly astounding.
David Borsellino, Hamilton

a delight to read
Re: Columnist jumps

Say what you want about Vinay Menon, but his column is a delight to read. Not only is he spot on about Donald Trump, but he definitely has many of us saying "Thank God, I am Canadian." Trump is the only candidate and person I know who wants a catastrophic event to happen to his people and blame it on his opponent. Narcissism at its finest! Document 207 of 500

Pierre Poilievre calls for stiff tariffs, punitive taxes on Chinese-made EVs

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/pierre-poilievre-calls-stiff-tariffs-punitive/docview/3091195676/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Pierre+Poilievre+calls+for+stiff+tariffs%2C+punitive+taxes+on+Chinese-made+EVs&amp;title=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-08-09&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: OTTAWA - Federal Conservative Leader Pierre Poilievre is adding his voice to the chorus of calls for stiff tariffs on Chinese-made electric vehicles, pledging he'd enact a punitive tax regime should his party win the next election.
That pledge could end up a moot point, with Prime Minister Justin Trudeau's government currently working on a policy response aimed at safeguarding Canada's burgeoning EV industry from Chinese competition, a move that would also represent efforts to align Canadian policy with the U.S approach.
Still, Poilievre's decision to weigh in - a moment that marks a rare show of unity with Premier Doug Ford - is a new foray into trade and tax policy for the federal Conservative leader, and particularly on the EV industry.
He has not, for example, said whether he'd continue the billions of dollars in support the federal government is currently giving the EV battery manufacturing sector, and wasn't asked about during Friday's event.
But he said a Conservative government would introduce a 100-per-cent tariff on made-in-China electric vehicles, a 50-per-cent tariff on semiconductors and solar cells, and a 25-per-cent tariff on other related products and parts. He would also remove rebates for made-in-China EVs.
The tariff regime Poilievre is proposing would match the one unveiled by U.S. President Joe Biden earlier this year, a move that led to the Trudeau government's current work on creating a Canadian approach. Public consultations ended on Aug. 1.
Speaking Friday during a campaign-style event at a Hamilton steel plant, Poilievre had harsh words for China, accusing the country's government of stealing Western technology and exploiting weak labour and environmental standards.
"They're doing this with a goal of crushing our steel, our aluminum and our automotive production and taking our jobs away," he said, echoing comments the federal Liberals and Ford have made about the issue.
But he also accused the Liberals of doing nothing about it.
"Trudeau has failed to protect our workers against this economic predation," he said.
A spokesperson for Finance Minister Chrystia Freeland called it "incredibly rich" for Poilievre to try to repackage the Liberal government's own position that action is necessary to protect Canadian autoworkers and the EV sector from Chinese actions.
"Poilievre is two months late to the conversation and is showing his hypocrisy, given his long anti-union record and continuous efforts to delay the implementation of the government's major investment tax credits, which are essential to securing Canada's auto sector's competitiveness in the 21st century," Katherine Cuplinskas said in an email.
Cuplinskas said the government will have more to say "soon" on what its approach will be.
China is the largest manufacturer and exporter of electric vehicles in the world, and has ramped up exports, which jumped by 20 per cent last month, as domestic demand lags.
Its critics accuse China of deliberately oversupplying the global market to drive down competition, and there are concerns that cheaper Chinese vehicles will undercut sales of North American products, in turn undermining the billions Ottawa is putting into creating a domestic industry.
The only made-in-China EVs currently sold in Canada are from Tesla, and they are subject to a six-per-cent import tariff.
But despite the threat of much larger tariffs, the massive Chinese EV maker BYD has plans to enter the Canadian market. BYD sold more three million vehicles last year, and its entry-level model has a starting price of about $13,000. The cheapest EVs currently for sale in Canada cost about three times as much.
The European Union has also imposed provisional tariffs on Chinese electric vehicles.
On Friday, China filed a complaint with the World Trade Organization, saying the tariffs violate WTO rules and undermine global co-operation on climate change.
With files from the Associated Press
CAPTION: Conservative Leader Pierre Poilievre speaks during a rally in Montreal in June.

Document 208 of 500

RCMP interviewing potential witnesses linked to Doug Ford's government in Greenbelt criminal probe

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/rcmp-interviewing-potential-witnesses-linked-doug/docview/3091195669/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=RCMP+interviewing+potential+witnesses+linked+to+Doug+Ford%27s+government+in+Greenbelt+criminal+probe&amp;title=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-08-09&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: RCMP detectives investigating the $8.28-billion Greenbelt land swap scandal are interviewing witnesses linked to Premier Doug Ford's government.
As first disclosed by the Star, current and former Progressive Conservative aides are sitting down with the Mounties to discuss Ford's controversial 2022 decision to open up 7,400 acres of the two-million-acre Greenbelt to housing development.
"We have nothing to hide," the premier told reporters in Thunder Bay on Friday, three hours after the Star broke the news.
"Come in and do whatever you have to do ... but I want full co-operation - they know that - because there's nothing to hide there. Let's get on with it," said Ford.
Interviews with members of the RCMP "O" Division's Sensitive and International Investigations unit, the elite Ottawa-based branch that probes political crimes and corruption, are being held at the force's Greater Toronto Area detachment.
In a statement Friday, Ford's office confirmed that aides and former staffers were being interviewed.
"We've always said we would co-operate. That co-operation would include the premier and current or former staff conducting interviews as witnesses, which are currently underway," the premier's office said.
"Any further questions should be directed to the RCMP."
The Royal Canadian Mounted Police would only "confirm that the investigation is ongoing."
But officials close to Ford said the premier has not yet been approached by the RCMP.
The Star is not identifying anyone who has been contacted by the Mounties because they are not authorized to discuss what they are telling - or will tell - detectives.
But sources say the RCMP appears to be currently focusing on potential witnesses rather than anyone actually suspected of wrongdoing.
The revelation came on the first anniversary of the auditor general's explosive report into the land swap that ultimately triggered the police investigation.
Ford scrapped his Greenbelt land-swap scheme last Sept. 21 in the wake of an Aug. 9 report by then-auditor general Bonnie Lysyk that found developers connected to his Progressive Conservatives had been "favoured" in the government's surprise move to allow houses to be built on the environmentally sensitive land.
Lysyk, who told the Star this week she has not been contacted by the Mounties, said the rezoning ballooned the value of the 15 properties in the Greater Toronto and Hamilton Area to $8.28 billion.
Her 97-page report - and a subsequent review by integrity commissioner J. David Wake released on Aug. 30, 2023 - appear to be serving as guidebooks for the RCMP investigation.
Some of the witnesses now being interviewed were named by Wake in his 166-page report.
He interviewed 62 people - most of whom were in the presence of their legal counsel - during the course of his review.
News that the RCMP investigation is heating up comes as Ford, whose Tories have a large lead in public opinion polls, has been considering an early election call next year instead of waiting for the scheduled June 2026 vote.
Last month's Abacus Data tracking poll found his PCs at 44 per cent - their highest level of support in more than two years - with Bonnie Crombie's Liberals at 26 per cent, Marit Stiles's New Democrats at 19 per cent and Mike Schreiner's Greens at seven per cent.
Abacus surveyed 1,000 Ontarians July 16-21 using online panels based on the Lucid exchange platform. While opt-in polls cannot be assigned a margin of error, for comparison purposes, a random sample of this size would have one of plus or minus 3.1 percentage points, 19 times out of 20.
Two of Ford's ministers and two senior political aides resigned last summer after the Lysyk and Wake reports.
With his popularity plummeting last September, the premier reversed course on his Greenbelt scheme.
"It was a mistake to open the Greenbelt. I'm very, very sorry. I made a promise to you that I wouldn't touch the Greenbelt. I broke that promise," Ford said on Sept. 21.
"As a first step to earning back your trust, I'll be reversing the changes. We moved too quickly and we made the wrong decision ... it caused people to question our motives," he said.
But Stiles said "one year after the auditor general's revelations about Ford's Greenbelt grab, he hasn't learned a thing."
"You deserve a government that tells the truth. That cares about regular people and does right by them. A government that isn't under criminal investigation," the NDP leader said.
Crombie said Friday was "a sad day for the people of Ontario, who deserve and need so much more than a government embroiled in criminal investigation."
"The people of this province need to know exactly how Doug Ford was involved in this scandal. Ontario deserves better," the Liberal leader said.
Schreiner, for his part, maintained "the premier has not learned from the Greenbelt scandal."
CAPTION: Premier Doug Ford speaks to reporters at the Council of the Federation meetings in Halifax on July 15, 2024.

Subject: Criminal investigations; Scandals; Mounted police; Interviews

Document 209 of 500

Constituency worker was sexually harassed by former NDP MPP, arbiter says

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/constituency-worker-was-sexually-harassed-former/docview/3091195640/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Constituency+worker+was+sexually+harassed+by+former+NDP+MPP%2C+arbiter+says&amp;title=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-08-09&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: A constituency assistant fired by the Ontario NDP after being sexually harassed by MPP Michael Mantha was not terminated for just cause, an arbitration decision has found.
Mantha was suspended from caucus in April 2023 and then permanently removed last August, after a workplace investigator found he pressured the woman to "engage in non-consensual sexual interactions with him," required her to regularly hug him, asked her to send sexually explicit photos of herself and to share sexual dreams about him, and pressured her to work from the office so he could be near her and "continue his practice of sexually touching her."
Mantha did not seek to participate in the arbitration proceedings, despite being notified by the NDP, arbitrator Jasbir Parmar said in the decision released Thursday.
Reached by phone Friday afternoon, Mantha told the Star he had not yet seen the arbitration decision, but added "there are many things that I didn't agree with" regarding the workplace investigation findings. He said he did not participate in the arbitration proceedings following advice from lawyers, and that he would review the decision with them before commenting.
At Queen's Park, NDP Leader Marit Stiles said Mantha, who now sits as an Independent MPP, should resign from the legislature.
"As leader, I take my responsibility to our staff and their safety very seriously. It's why I took swift action to remove MPP Mantha from my caucus when allegations against him were substantiated by an independent, third-party investigation," Stiles said Friday.
"In light of the arbitration decision being made public, I want to reaffirm the Ontario NDP's commitment to zero tolerance for sexual violence and harassment. Elected officials at all levels must be held to a higher standard. Those who take advantage of the trust and power they have been given are unfit to remain in office. They must be held accountable for their actions."
According to Parmar's decision, the woman first started working for the Algoma-Manitoulin MPP in his Elliot Lake office in 2015. She went on a paid leave of absence after raising concerns to the NDP in August 2022 about an "unsafe work environment," with the Canadian Office and Professional Employees union filing a grievance on her behalf later that year. The NDP hired an external workplace investigator in January 2023 to look into the situation.
In January 2024, just over four months after Mantha was permanently removed from caucus due to the workplace investigation findings, the woman received a letter from the NDP caucus indicating that "given the fact that MPP Mantha no longer held an elected seat within the ONDP Caucus," her employment no longer fell under the collective agreement and had been terminated as of April 1, 2023, Parmar wrote in the arbitration decision.
The union then filed another grievance in January 2024 saying the woman had been unjustly terminated.
Because NDP political aides at Queen's Park and in constituency offices are unionized, Mantha's staff all lost their positions in the union's bargaining unit when he was removed from caucus last year. Sources say the woman in question remained on the NDP payroll months after her unionized job was eliminated.
Parmar found this week that the woman's employment "was frustrated through no fault of her own" following Mantha's removal from caucus "based on the substantiated findings" that he had sexually harassed and discriminated against her, and had created a toxic workplace.
"I have concluded that the grievor's employment was not terminated for just cause," Parmar wrote, sending the matter back to the NDP and the union to work out a solution.
With files from Robert Benzie

An arbitration decision released this week found a worker in Mantha's constituency office was wrongly fired following Mantha's removal from caucus after a workplace investigator found he sexually harassed the woman.

Document 210 of 500

Dundas doctor is Canada's most influential family medicine researcher

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/dundas-doctor-is-canadas-most-influential-family/docview/3090934302/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Dundas+doctor+is+Canada%27s+most+influential+family+medicine+researcher&amp;title=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-08-09&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: A Dundas doctor was found to be Canada's most influential researcher in family medicine.
The work of Dr. Dave Davis has been cited 18,551 times, concluded a study on the impact of the country's primary care (PC) researchers published in May in the journal Canadian Family Physician.
Citations are a key metric used to measure the success of research. It means the work has been referenced as a source of information in other studies.
"It means you've been read and the finding of your research has been applied in somebody else's thinking," said Davis. "It's been used to create other research that's built on it so I'm very pleased to see that."
The importance of primary care studies was highlighted in the May publication, authored by researchers from McMaster University, the University of Toronto, the University of Manitoba, Dalhousie University, the College of Family Physicians of Canada and the National Institute of Nursing Research in Maryland.
"Canada is home to a cadre of primary care researchers who are highly cited in the medical literature, suggesting that their work is of high quality and relevance," the study stated. "Building on this foundation, further investments in primary care research could accelerate needed improvements in Canadian primary care policy and practice."
The study also flagged potential consequences of Canada's "underinvestment" in primary care research, which the authors note coincides with the current family doctor shortage.
The paper stated that 14 per cent of Canadians do not have a regular primary care provider and that the country has higher-than-average rates of seniors aged 65 and older being unable to get timely access to a family physician or nurse and having to go to an emergency department for health issues that should be seen in a doctor's office.
"This underinvestment in PC research, evidence and knowledge exacerbates our ability to adequately navigate the current crisis," the research stated. "In 2008, the World Health Organization highlighted the importance of producing knowledge and research to accelerate primary care reform. Despite this recognition, investment in PC research and the number of publications are low in Canada and abroad."
Davis has been an author on 159 peer-reviewed publications. What put him on top was that 9,081 of his citations were for studies in which he was the first author.
"I was actually quite surprised," Davis said about discovering the influence of his work. "I don't think of myself necessarily as a researcher. I still think of myself as a clinician who likes to teach and likes to learn."
The 82-year-old, now-retired physician spent his career researching the most effective way to provide continuing education to doctors to improve patient care. His research proved that traditional methods of doctors travelling to large lectures given by an authority on the topic did not have the best results.
"It needs to be interactive," said Davis. "Learning from colleagues is as good in many ways as learning from the authority."
Education was also better in small groups and when it filled knowledge gaps identified by the doctors themselves.
Davis says the work has helped slowly change continuing education over many years but there is still more that needs to be done.
"Has it changed to the degree that I expected it to? No," said Davis. "Old traditions die hard. It has changed our thinking about continuing education, and to that extent has improved patient care."
Davis got started on the research when he became the director of continuing education for health sciences at McMaster in 1989 and the dean asked him if the professional development being provided to doctors worked.
He also had a practice in Burlington and worked at what was then the North Hamilton Community Health Centre on Hughson Street North.
He continued the research when he moved to the University of Toronto in 1994 and later went on to positions in the United States and the United Arab Emirates before returning to Dundas when he retired from medicine to become a writer, including a contributing columnist for The Spectator.
The list of Canada's most influential included a total of 50 primary care researchers who have collectively published 2,555 papers as first authors and been cited 93,216 times. About 60 per cent of them were men and 86 per cent were clinician scientists, which means they divided their time between practicing medicine and doing research.
"High-performing primary care is widely acknowledged as the cornerstone of effective and efficient health care systems," stated the study. "This research has the potential to influence clinical practice, health care policy and patient-oriented outcomes."
The publication listed four of the researchers as being from McMaster. However, at least two others also have some affiliation with Hamilton.
Davis is listed under the University of Toronto in the study but gives part of the credit to McMaster for the foundational work he did there.
"Not only has it perhaps inspired some change in the practice of continuing education, but also inspired more research," said Davis. "The end goal of all that we do as clinicians is improving patient care so if I can see the research that I've done has inspired some of that thinking, that's quite gratifying."
Joanna Frketich is a reporter at The Hamilton Spectator. jfrketich@thespec.com
CAPTION: Dr. Dave Davis was found to be Canada's most cited primary care researcher.

Subject: Primary care; Medicine; Continuing education; Physicians; Medical research

East Hamilton's Ann Pirvu plays in new show 'Poly is the New Monogamy'

Document 213 of 500

When the Greenbelt scandal broke, Doug Ford was all apologies. What a difference a year makes

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/when-greenbelt-scandal-broke-doug-ford-was-all/docview/3090986497/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=When+the+Greenbelt+scandal+broke%2C+Doug+Ford+was+all+apologies.+What+a+difference+a+year+makes&amp;title=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-08-08&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Contrition may not quite be what it once was.
With Friday marking the first anniversary of the explosive auditor general's report into the Greenbelt land swap, Premier Doug Ford was asked about the lessons learned from a scandal that still looms over his Progressive Conservative government.
Ford shrugged at the reporter's question.
"We made that decision not to move forward with it - and we're going keep moving forward and building homes everywhere other than the Greenbelt," he said Wednesday in Mississauga.
"So that's it."
While the premier conceded his government was still in the process of implementing all 15 recommendations from then-auditor general Bonnie Lysyk's report, his tone was a far cry from the plaintive plea when he cancelled the land deal at the end of last summer.
"It was a mistake to open the Greenbelt. I'm very, very sorry. I made a promise to you that I wouldn't touch the Greenbelt. I broke that promise," Ford said on Sept. 21, 2023.
That was when he announced 7,400 acres of environmentally sensitive land would no longer be removed from the two-million-acre Greenbelt around the Greater Toronto and Hamilton Area.
"As a first step to earning back your trust, I'll be reversing the changes. We moved too quickly and we made the wrong decision ... it caused people to question our motives," he said at the time.
It was Lysyk's report - and a separate review by integrity commissioner J. David Wake released on Aug. 30, 2023 - that set in motion events that dog Ford's government to this day.
The RCMP "O" Division's Sensitive and International Investigations unit, the branch that investigates corruption and political crimes, has an ongoing probe of the affair.
Lysyk, who retired last September after a 10-year term as the financial watchdog, told the Star this week she has not been contacted by the RCMP.
The reports by her and Wake are widely viewed as potential road maps for police investigators because the independent legislative watchdogs had access to people who do not necessarily have to play ball with the Mounties.
She found developers with ties to Ford's Tories were "favoured" in the process led by then-housing minister Steve Clark's chief of staff, Ryan Amato, and said land they owned that was selected to be opened for housing construction could have soared in value to $8.28 billion as a result.
Amato, who resigned last August, has always denied any wrongdoing and his supporters at Queen's Park - including some past and present PC cabinet ministers - believe he has been unfairly scapegoated.
Clark, who has also insisted he did nothing wrong and has promised to co-operate with the Mounties, stepped down from cabinet last September, languishing as a backbencher for nine months until Ford promoted him to government house leader in the June 6 shuffle.
"He's one of the most experienced and qualified members we have - he knows Queen's Park inside and out. A very credible individual and I support him 1,000 per cent," the unrepentant premier said earlier this week.
Others seen as collateral damage remain politically sidelined.
Kaleed Rasheed resigned as Ford's business minister last September.
Rasheed no longer sits as a Tory MPP after misleading Wake about a 2020 Las Vegas trip with Shakir Rehmatullah, a developer who attended Ford's daughter's wedding and who had a stake in about 23 acres of land removed from the Greenbelt near Markham and Whitchurch-Stouffville.
The affable Mississauga East-Cooksville Independent MPP maintains he made "an honest mistake" on the date of the Vegas jaunt and is trying to return to the PC fold.
Jae Truesdell, who was also on that trip, quit as Ford's housing adviser after Rasheed's defenestration.
The imbroglio has remained in the headlines - eight weeks ago, the Star and the Narwhal shared the Michener Award, Canada's most prestigious journalism prize, for their work examining the Greenbelt affair.
A joint Star and Narwhal investigation found eight of the 15 parcels of land affected had been bought up by developers after Ford's election in 2018 - when the properties were still protected.
NDP Leader Marit Stiles said Thursday that "people power, investigative journalism and a strong NDP opposition helped thwart Ford's plan to carve up the Greenbelt, but he hasn't learned a thing."
"A year later, the minister who took the fall is back ... and Ford is still making backroom deals that benefit wealthy developers over everyone else," she said.
Liberal Leader Bonnie Crombie said, "Doug Ford isn't sorry for trying to give the Greenbelt away to his rich friends, he's only sorry he got caught."
"He even brought his friend, Steve Clark, the Greenbelt fall guy, back," said Crombie.
Green Leader Mike Schreiner expressed concern Thursday that "the premier has not learned from the Greenbelt scandal."
"He is still refusing to legalize gentle density to build homes people can afford in the communities they love," said Schreiner.
"And he is destroying farmland by imposing urban boundary expansions in places like Waterloo region that threaten the land that feeds us."
CAPTION: Premier Doug Ford listens as Steve Clark, then his minister of housing, speaks during a news conference in Mississauga on Aug. 11, 2023.

Subject: Criminal investigations; Scandals

Title: When the Greenbelt scandal broke, Doug Ford was all apologies. What a difference a year makes

Publication title: The Hamilton Spectator (Online); Hamilton

Publication year: 2024

Publication date: Aug 8, 2024

Section: politics/provincial
