Document 214 of 500

Who really posted those awkward tweets praising a Pierre Poilievre rally? Here's what might be going on

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/who-really-posted-those-awkward-tweets-praising/docview/3090986312/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Who+really+posted+those+awkward+tweets+praising+a+Pierre+Poilievre+rally%3F+Here%27s+what+might+be+going+on&amp;title=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-08-08&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: If online chatter was to be believed, the northern Ontario town of Kirkland Lake - founded on gold, sustained by hockey, population roughly 8,000 - was abuzz last week.
"Just got back from Pierre Poilievre's rally," read one post on X, formerly Twitter. The user, who wrote her bio in Spanish and listed her location as France, continued the emphatic message to her zero followers: "I'm buzzing from the energy! As a northerner, it's refreshing to see a leader who actually listens to our concerns and prioritizes our needs."
A nearly identical message was posted by a user with seven followers who listed their location as Russia - same buzz, same proud northern identity, same praise for the Conservative leader's "palpable" commitment to the region. Then came a message from New York, then another from the United Kingdom. Then another, and another, then dozens more. Between their boilerplate wording and the users' impersonal profiles, the signs seemed to point to a bot campaign, suggesting the posts likely came not from real people but computer programs designed to spread the same message - and fast.
Speculation that the Poilievre camp might have been behind the messages set politicos aflutter. The NDP has written to the Commissioner of Canada Elections asking for an investigation to ensure, among other things, that the Conservative Party of Canada hadn't employed the services of a bot farm. The Liberals called the suspected use of bots "extremely alarming," and urged their opponents to the support the Online Harms Act, which has taken steps toward labelling bot-amplified content. (Elsewhere, a Reddit thread titled "Pierre Poilievre Caught Red Handed Using Foreign Bots and I Can't Stop Laughing" had been upvoted some 3,700 times by Thursday afternoon.)
It's an allegation the Conservatives vehemently deny: "The CPC does not pay for bots and has no idea who is behind these accounts," party spokesperson Sarah Fischer said in an email. "We are seeking the support of actual Canadians, as witnessed by large in-person turnouts at our events."
But at a time when bots are increasingly recognized as a threat that can sway public opinion - and when people are also more likely than ever to recognize them - researchers point out something else about the pro-Poilievre messages: they aren't very good.
"This is pretty rudimentary - like, it's just copying and pasting and amplifying messages," said Fenwick McKelvey, an associate professor in the department of communication studies and co-director of the Applied AI Institute at Concordia University. "The way I would think about doing this is using a large language model to generate messages that are slightly varying in tone," he added, referring to artificial intelligence programs used to create text. "It's a bit weird to me."
There is no dispute the Poilievre event happened - a CTV news report that described the venue as "packed" showed the Conservative leader, dressed in an "Axe the Tax" T-shirt, addressing a standing-room-only crowd. There's nothing illegal about using bots, either.
But the allegation has put a new spotlight on Poilievre, who is known for his online savvy. He's also faced criticism for tactics that include tagging his official YouTube videos with a hashtag used by a misogynistic movement.
Still, the suspected use of bots - which researchers say isn't unique to the right side of the political spectrum - raises questions about who might be paying for them, what they're being used for, and whether those who deploy them are being up front about what they're doing.
Thanks to a lack of transparency from social media platforms, it's almost impossible to know who is behind a bot campaign, says Elizabeth Dubois, an associate professor and University Research Chair in qualitative communication and technology at the University of Ottawa.
But thanks to the overuse of unique, easy-to-search words like "buzzing" and seemingly international attention on an event in a rural outpost, where even the nearest regional airport is over an hour away, this campaign became noticeable. Indeed, it was almost immediately flagged by other users. The lack of sophistication suggests a couple of possibilities for who might be pulling the puppet strings, Dubois says.
The instigator could have been an amateur who was trying to drum up political support, she suggested, or someone who was just testing the limits of what's possible with bots for some future purpose and didn't care if their efforts were seen.
It's also possible this campaign was easy to detect by design. "Sometimes, the point of this is not to actually convince anybody that a bunch of people were at a rally and they were really jazzed about it," Dubois said.
"Sometimes the point of it is to make a conversation messy, to create confusion. That way, it doesn't actually matter that it's a whole bunch of copy-and-pasted stuff. And in fact, you want to be detected because - if that's the motivation behind it - your goal is to make people think, 'I don't know if I can trust my democracy anymore.'"
While it remains unclear who was behind the tweets in question, the general chaos created by bots, which can be relatively cheap and easy to spin up, is the reason why they tend to be useful as a foreign interference strategy, she says.
While bots have been used in Canadian politics as far back as 2012 - Dubois and McKelvey have written about an automated account that tweeted 11,000 times to promote the Coalition Avenir Quebec during the Quebec election that year - they've drawn growing attention for their potential for meddling in electoral processes.
The recent foreign interference inquiry heard about instances of bots completing polls during the 2021 federal election campaign that had been flagged afterwards by staffers. While they'd assumed it was a ploy to collect the $0.50 incentive for completing the survey, they still noted the tactic could be used to influence party or government policy.
There was also a so-called "spamouflage" campaign last summer, in which dozens of MPs from both sides of the political aisle were targeted by posts from new or hijacked social media accounts that claimed their targets had been accused of criminal or ethical violations.
Part of the issue is that social media companies aren't enforcing any rules about how much automation is allowed on their platforms, Dubois says. Studies suggest that networks of bots continue to operate on X despite owner Elon Musk's claims he would crack down on scams on the platform.
But it would also help if political parties would step up and agree to be open and transparent about how they're using new technologies like automation and artificial intelligence, McKelvey says.
At the individual user level, it would also help if we all stopped putting so much stock in what is popular on social media. "As a researcher who has put a lot of time into this, one of the findings is that what is popular on the internet is more complicated than a popularity contest," he said.
"Just because something's trending or a lot of people are tweeting a message - it means nothing."
With files from Stephanie Levitz
CAPTION: tweet1tweet2Conservative Leader Pierre Poilievre speaks during a rally in Montreal on June 19, 2024.

Aug. 7: Free flowers an uplifting sight, rent control is the answer and other letters to the editor

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/aug-7-free-flowers-uplifting-sight-rent-control/docview/3089865157/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Aug.+7%3A+Free+flowers+an+uplifting+sight%2C+rent+control+is+the+answer+and+other+letters+to+the+editor&amp;title=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-08-07&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Free flowers an uplifting sight
Re: Local business owner gives away free sunflowers from his Ancaster field, July 27
I can't tell you how touched I was by the article about Peter Vanderboom who is giving away sunflowers after seeding his empty acreage at Wilson Street and Tradewind Drive in Ancaster. After reading Cathie Coward's story, I dropped by and cut a beautiful bouquet. Such a beautiful and uplifting sight! In a world full of so much hate and selfishness, to see such a thoughtful gesture offers hope and confirmation that there is still goodness out there. Here's to the power of good news.
Connie Smith, Ancaster
Rent control is the answer
Re: Encampments a 'public policy failure' by successive governments, Aug. 6
Bryan Patterson states "upper levels of government need to take action." My thought on this is why are Toronto and Ottawa not imposing rent control? If people could afford to direct 30 per cent of their income (not 50 per cent) to housing and have enough money left over to feed themselves and pay bills would that not help to get the homeless off the streets? With rents the way they are today, what kind of job must one have to afford to live in an apartment in Hamilton or anywhere else in this country?
Catherine Castellan, Hannon
Use cash to tip your server
Re: Tip skimming is illegal, Aug. 3
I read with considerable interest Ed Canning's commentary on tipping. I, for one, never tip using my credit card that I use to pay for the meal. I always carry some cash to tip directly to the server so I know who gets my gratuity. It also gives me an opportunity to directly thank them for their service.
If the server decides to give it to an employee tip pool or her employer, it is his or her choice. Many servers work two or three jobs to make ends meet. They are overworked and underpaid. I have no intention of lining their management's pockets with my discretionary tipping for their good service.
Jim Casey, Hamilton
Primary care should be Ford's priority
Re: Turning doctor care over to pharmacists isn't a solution, Aug. 1
I applaud the article by Dr. Mark Unger, focusing on roles of primary care being taken over by independent pharmacists, which both fragments and compromises health care. Similarly, Premier Doug Ford's solution is to support privatization of clinics, which benefits those who can afford it, or afford supplementary health insurance. The way to increase primary-care providers is to fund them appropriately, and in the case of nurses, honour their contracts. Many medical students are no doubt daunted by the cost of managing a family doctor's office and instead go into specialties with higher pay. A further support to family physicians would be to cover independent counsellors, social workers and psychologists under OHIP, redirecting the mental health cases to skilled providers.
Barbara A. Mead, Hamilton
Lack of decency all around
Re: We must stand up for decency, Aug. 1
I believe Christopher Holcroft's commentary deserves a rebuttal. Not that Pierre Poilievre needs defending from me or anyone else for that matter, I found Mr. Holcroft conveniently forgot to mention the lack of decency or decorum from our prime minister during the COVID-19 pandemic. Justin Trudeau was quoted as mentioning that many of the protesters in Ottawa were white misogynists. Let us not forget that our leader was found to be lacking in ethics, by our very own ethics commissioner! I ask that Mr. Holcroft look at both sides of the decency debate next time.
Frank De Clara, Hamilton
Olympic swimmer is a star
Re: A phenom in the water, Aug. 3
I totally agree with Paul Bacon's comments in Saturday's Spectator given Summer McIntosh's accomplishments at the Olympics. If Toronto plans to name a street after someone, I feel we should be naming the route from Rogers Centre to Nathan Philips Square "Summer McIntosh Way" for a few months to celebrate her accomplishments. She is truly a star in my opinion.
Mary Hickey, Burlington
Province should tackle homelessness
It's August 2024, and the epic homeless problem is no closer to being solved than it ever was. Encampments are growing with all the problems of NIMBYism, and so is compassion fatigue. New research suggests homelessness could be even bigger than the current data shows, and the numbers could increase by more than 80 per cent by 2030.
The cure for homelessness is housing. But since the 1990s, the responsibility for social housing and homelessness has been moved from the federal level to the province, then downloaded by the province to municipalities. But municipalities are not permitted to run deficits, and they can't fund adequate housing programs without either raising taxes or cutting other programs.
All political parties have to share the blame. The Liberals had years to do something about it, but they passed the buck. Currently, Premier Doug Ford's party is running amok under the banner of conservatism. It's not conservative at all. If it were, we'd see public policy that emphasizes the value of traditional institutions and practices leading to continuity and stability. Instead, we're seeing Greenbelt incursions, climate change denial, unnecessary superhighways, favours for developers, privately run clinics, ministerial zoning orders, and the Science Centre/Ontario Place debacle. They also cancelled the basic annual income pilot, scheduled to run for three years, after only one year, so no results could be determined. This was vital research. Instead, billions and billions of taxpayer dollars are being spent, and for what - pet projects, power and the next election? Obviously, funding for homelessness could be made available.
So stop blaming city hall. Municipalities are not the cause of this - the problem needs to be fixed upstream! The legislative assembly's long summer break doesn't end until Oct. 21 so you have plenty of time to express your strong displeasure to the premier.
James Newman, Hamilton
CAPTION: An offer of free sunflowers was a thoughtful gesture that confirms there is still goodness out there, a reader says.

Document 216 of 500

Doug Ford says he's 'happy as punch' Kamala Harris picked Tim Walz

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/doug-ford-says-hes-happy-as-punch-kamala-harris/docview/3089865120/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Doug+Ford+says+he%27s+%27happy+as+punch%27+Kamala+Harris+picked+Tim+Walz&amp;title=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-08-07&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Premier Doug Ford says he's "happy as punch" U.S. Vice President Kamala Harris chose Minnesota Gov. Tim Walz as her Democratic running mate.
"We share a lot in common," Ford told reporters Wednesday in a campaign-style stop in Mississauga where he announced $35 million to expand the G.E. Booth water resource recovery facility.
As the Star reported Tuesday, the premier hosted Walz at Queen's Park two months ago and the pair developed a warm rapport.
"I was happy as punch to see the governor nominated as ... (Harris's) running mate. We tossed the football around a little bit in my office - we gave him a CFL football," the premier said, adding their private meeting focused on the importance of Canada-U.S. trade.
"He understands that. We're stronger together," said Ford, acknowledging the worrying protectionist rhetoric in the run-up to the Nov. 5 U.S. presidential election.
The premier, who has been on a "Buy North American" push with other provincial leaders and U.S. governors, emphasized the importance of Ontario as a trading partner and the need to keep goods and services flowing.
"I explained to him that we're the third largest (U.S.) trading partner in the world - Ontario is. We do approximately $500 billion in (annual) two-way trade," he said.
"We're the number one trading partner to 17 states, number two to 13 other states - and we're the number one customer to those states. We're an economic powerhouse ... in North America," he said.
"We're going to work well together if he gets elected."
Ford has expressed concern about the protectionist talk on the hustings stateside - especially from Republican nominee Donald Trump, the ex-president, and his running mate J.D. Vance.
Because of the looming mandatory review of the United States-Mexico-Canada Agreement (USMCA) in 2026, Canadian politicians are closely watching the American election.
Still, Ford hastened to emphasize he "will work with anyone, I don't care if it's Republican or Democrat."
Ontario's governing Progressive Conservatives were encouraged by Harris's choice Tuesday of the two-term Minnesota governor, who is well-regarded at Queen's Park.
Walz beat out better-known potential running mates, including Pennsylvania Gov. Josh Shapiro, Arizona Sen. Mark Kelly and President Joe Biden's transportation secretary, Pete Buttigieg.
Like his Tory, Liberal and NDP predecessors as premier, Ford has made building relationships with Democratic and Republican U.S. governors a priority since winning power in 2018.
But the June 10 meeting with Walz in his office at the legislature went particularly well.
"There was such an excellent rapport and really, really positive statements from the governor on bilateral (Ontario-Minnesota) and Canada-U.S. trade," confided one senior provincial official, who attended the closed-door session.
"They're both very salt-of-the-Earth and 'of the people' so it was a very positive meeting."
As an avid NFL fan, Ford gave Walz, a former high school football coach who once guided his team to a Minnesota state championship, an official Wilson CFL ball.
The two men, born seven-and-a-half months apart in 1964, threw the football together like schoolchildren in the premier's massive second-floor office while aides watched.
CAPTION: Premier Doug Ford and Minnesota Gov. Tim Walz share a moment in the premier's office at Queen's Park in June.

is the answer

Document 218 of 500

Doug Ford's football friend Tim Walz is Kamala Harris's running mate

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/doug-fords-football-friend-tim-walz-is-kamala/docview/3089919239/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Doug+Ford%27s+football+friend+Tim+Walz+is+Kamala+Harris%27s+running+mate&amp;title=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-08-06&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Two months ago, Premier Doug Ford was talking trade and tossing a football around his Queen's Park office with Minnesota Gov. Tim Walz.
With Walz now U.S. Vice President Kamala Harris's running-mate atop the Democratic presidential ticket, Ford is hopeful that could be good news for Ontario - and for his "Buy North America" push against protectionist rhetoric stateside.
On Tuesday, Harris opted for the two-term Minnesota governor - who for 12 years was a congressman representing a traditionally Republican district bordering the key swing state of Wisconsin - to be her vice-presidential nominee.
Walz edged out high-profile hopefuls such as Pennsylvania Gov. Josh Shapiro, Arizona Sen. Mark Kelly and Pete Buttigieg, who is President Joe Biden's transportation secretary.
The premier, who has cultivated relationships with key Democratic and Republican U.S. governors and trade officials over his six years in office, was pleased with Harris's pick - even though he is remaining agnostic on the election.
"There was such an excellent rapport and really, really positive statements from the governor on bilateral (Ontario-Minnesota) and Canada-U.S. trade," said one senior provincial official, speaking confidentially in order to discuss the private meeting at Queen's Park on June 10.
"They're both very salt-of-the-Earth and 'of the people' so it was a very positive meeting," said the high-ranking insider, who was among about a dozen at the confab dominated by shared concerns over trade.
Ford, a huge NFL fan, presented Walz, a former high school football coach who led his team to the Minnesota state championship in 1999, with an official Wilson CFL ball.
Behind the closed doors of the premier's capacious second-floor office in the Ontario legislature, the two leaders began throwing the pigskin back and forth to the amusement of their aides.
Insiders said the impromptu game of catch - away from media cameras - symbolized a connection between this province and Walz's state that could have broader trade implications.
That's because the next American administration will preside over the scheduled mandatory review of the United States-Mexico-Canada Agreement (USMCA) in 2026.
Mindful of the protectionist hyperbole on the campaign trail - especially from former president Donald Trump, the Republican nominee, and his running mate J.D. Vance - Ford has been pushing a "Buy North America" strategy with premiers and U.S. governors.
Provincial sources said Walz, whose state is home to major multinational corporations like 3M and General Mills that have significant operations in Ontario, was simpatico to the premier's pitch.
"As you know, two-way trade between Minnesota and Ontario reached an impressive $5.6 billion (U.S.) in 2023, supporting thousands of jobs on both sides of the border," Ford wrote in a letter to the governor on June 19.
"At a time of increasing global instability, it's never been more important for us to work together to build on this important and growing relationship," the premier wrote.
"As we confront the challenge of global instability, I agree with you that we cannot take our relationship for granted and, as neighbours, we need to stick together," he continued.
"Moving forward, it will be important to consider ways in which we can boost trade between our two jurisdictions as we embrace a 'Buy North America' mindset," added Ford.
"This approach will help provide certainty, stability and prosperity to the hundreds of millions of Americans and Canadian workers that rely on us to maintain an open trading relationship, regardless of political circumstances or external events."
The Progressive Conservative premier noted Ontario can help Minnesota with "critical minerals and clean, affordable energy" to power its businesses.
"I look forward to exploring how we can better secure our North American supply chains across all sectors to become less reliant on countries further afield that cannot be relied on and that do not share our values," he concluded.
Should Harris and Walz win the Nov. 5 U.S. election, the Minnesota governor would follow in the footsteps of Trump's former vice-president, Mike Pence.
In October 2014, Pence, then the governor of Indiana, was promoting his state's relations with Ontario at Queen's Park with then Liberal premier Kathleen Wynne and "boosting bonds with existing partners in Toronto."
CAPTION: Vice President Kamala Harris has chosen Minnesota Gov. Tim Walz as her running mate, looking to strengthen the Democratic ticket in Midwestern states. She'll introduce Walz at a rally Tuesday evening in Philadelphia. (AP Video / Aug. 6, 2024)Premier Doug Ford and Minnesota Gov. Tim Walz share a moment in the premier's office at Queen's Park in June 2024.

Document 219 of 500

Environmental and residents groups call new provincial rules barring OLT third-party appeals an attack on 'citizen rights'

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/environmental-residents-groups-call-new/docview/3089467145/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Environmental+and+residents+groups+call+new+provincial+rules+barring+OLT+third-party+appeals+an+attack+on+%27citizen+rights%27&amp;title=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-08-06&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: A provincial law change that curtailed third-party groups' ability to appeal development decisions has left environmental and ratepayer groups saying they've been silenced in a move that puts developer interests ahead of citizen concerns.
Ontario residents can no longer appeal development decisions at the Ontario Land Tribunal - a quasi-judicial body designed to adjudicate planning and other land disputes - after the Doug Ford government introduced legislation that removes the ability of third-parties such as ratepayer groups or environmental groups to do so.
The province says the changes to the third-party appeal rights in the Cutting Red Tape to Build More Homes Act (Bill 185), which passed in June, will "help communities get quicker planning approvals for housing projects."
But critics say the legislation is another attack on "citizen rights" and the public's participation in the development process and will ultimately lead to poorly planned and less safe communities across the province.
"This is the loss of an important mechanism for the public to make sure that environmental and public health concerns were raised and properly considered in development decisions across the province," said Jacqueline Wilson, counsel for the Canadian Environmental Law Association, who says his organization has been part of local resident appeals for decades.
"It's really a problem to cut out active engaged citizens, who deeply care about their communities, from the equation."
According to the OLT, between April 1, 2022, to March 31, 2023, the Tribunal handled 1,422 cases, totalling 2,161 appeals. The OLT said they don't track the number of appeals made by developers versus individuals or third-party groups. However, since the law came into effect in June, 62 third-party appeals that didn't "meet the transition provisions," which include a hearing date scheduled before April 10, 2024, were dismissed, a spokesperson for the OLT said.
The new legislation places restrictions on the ability of the public to appeal amendments to official plans and zoning bylaws made through development applications. The only groups who can appeal are "public bodies," specified persons who are defined as government, utility boards, and agencies. Landowners and developers can also appeal municipal development decisions they don't agree with.
Justine Teplycky, a spokesperson for Housing Minister Paul Calandra, says the changes to the legislation "provide the appropriate balance between speeding up planning approvals and maintaining an avenue for impacted landowners to voice their concerns."
She says between 2021 and 2023, approximately 67,000 housing units were subject to third-party appeals of official plans and rezoning.
Teplycky says "the public still has opportunities to provide full input in the planning process to their locally elected councils, who are best positioned to make decisions that reflect the needs of their communities."
Environmental lawyer David Donnelly says in his research of third-party appeals, he believes just a handful of appeals in the GTA have hindered or slowed down the process.
Some residents whose cases have been dismissed this past month also disagree with the notion their voices are better heard at the municipal level.
Suzi Recine, with the group Friends to Conserve Kleinburg, who had filed an appeal at the OLT against plans to build about 800 single-detached homes and townhomes on part of the Copper Creek golf club in Vaughan, said the community turned to the OLT once they realized they had no support from the city and there was a "lack of consideration of their concerns."
"This was a way for residents to have a voice. Why are we being silenced?" said Recine. "Bills like Bill 185 ... give more powers to the government and developers, while taking away the public's opportunity to be heard."
The Copper Creek community is owned by both Fieldgate Developments and by TACC Developments, development companies run by Jack Eisenberger and Silvio De Gasperis. The development is being built by Block 55 Northwest Landowners Group Inc., which consists of three landowners, said Alana De Gasperis, a spokesperson for TACC Developments.
Recine says the residents appealed the development to the OLT amidst concerns of overdevelopment of the pristine area, and environmental degradation of an area that borders the Greenbelt and the Humber River watershed.
In a statement, Alana De Gasperis said there have been "numerous opportunities for public input" in addition to statutory public hearings. She also said the TACC and the landowners have worked with staff to preserve trees, create a buffer zone between the new and existing communities, and ensure appropriate environmental protections are in place, including the completion of several studies that have been reviewed by the city and the conservation authority.
She said the appeal, which was previously dismissed by the OLT and heard at Divisional Court, has "delayed the project approximately 3 years."
Recine says her group's case was dismissed by the OLT in July, despite having been given a hearing date prior to the bill's passing.
The Kleinburg group was told on April 10 that their case would be heard in June 2025.
Recine said within hours of their hearing being scheduled, the province introduced Bill 185, which included transition provisions that said any third-party appeal that didn't have a hearing date scheduled prior to April 10, 2024, was immediately dismissed. And even though the law didn't pass until June, this provision was applied retroactively.
"We got our date, and then a short while later, we found out our entire case was dismissed," said Recine. "Where is the fairness to the residents and the community?"
In an email, the province said the law was retroactively applied as that was the day the legislation was introduced, said Teplycky.
Environmental lawyer Donnelly said several groups he is representing have had their cases dismissed as a result of Bill 185.
"It's not just about the rights of one group of residents, it's about the rights of the public to fight for environmental protections for all," he said. "You should care about the fact that developers have all these rights, and citizens have none."
Julie Neves, the chair of Stop Petersburg Sand Pit Inc., says her group's appeal of an aggregate pit in Wilmot, Ont., was dismissed in July as her group was not one of the parties allowed to file an appeal per the new legislation. The group had been opposing an application for an aggregate pit, and had tried to petition their town council to monitor the air quality in the area where six others already operate, to find ways to "clean them up and improve conditions."
When those efforts failed, Neves' group filed an appeal with the OLT. But that appeal route was dismissed before it even began, she said.
"At this point, all our citizen rights have been taken away," said Neves. "There are no more options left ... and the development always gets its way."
Justin Sherwood, a spokesperson with the Building Industry and Land Development Association (BILD), said they support "efforts to increase the speed and efficiency of the approvals process" but says it's too early to know if this move by the province will do just that.
"We understand, and can appreciate, that one of the intentions to limit third party appeals was to eliminate those that were frivolous or vexatious. However, the results and outcome of this are yet to be tested since the legislation was only passed in early June," said Sherwood.
The OLT is the latest iteration of what has been more commonly known as the Ontario Municipal Board and the Local Planning Appeal Tribunal - administrative tribunals that were created to adjudicate planning matters. The OLT was created in 2021, after the government consolidated several different boards including the Conservation Review Board, the Environmental Review Tribunal, the Local Planning Appeal Tribunal and the Mining and Lands Tribunal.
Donnelly said removing third party appeals - or what he calls "citizen appeals" - at the OLT has little to do with NIMBYs (not-in-my-backyard), a term that refers to those who oppose development in their own neighbourhoods. He fears it will instead remove the ability of concerned citizens and environmental groups who have historically used the OLT (and its predecessors) to push cities and developers to make better planning decisions.
"Nobody has made the case that having residents, neighbours, environmentalists, First Nations involved in the planning process is a bad thing. They have always been the check and the balance in the system, and now that's being taken away," Donnelly said.
He cites several cases where appeals led to outcomes in the public interest, including the enactment of the Oak Ridges Moraine Protection Act, which came as the result of a 12-month appeal by a third-party group called Save the Rouge Valley System Inc. to a development on the environmentally sensitive lands. He said other appeals against aggregate quarries and mines have led the projects to be denied, or have a significantly reduced footprint.
CELA lawyer Wilson says that without a place for the public to appeal development decisions, many will have no choice but to turn to the courts by seeking a judicial review of a planning decision, leading to delays and high costs for all parties.
Recine says the Kleinburg group has already spent thousands on legal fees and experts at the OLT. In light of the recent dismissal, she says she's not sure if the group will continue their fight.
"I don't know where this leaves us," she said. "With a dismissal of the entire case, it leaves us without a voice."
CAPTION: A spokesperson for the developers behind the housing planned for Copper Creek say the third-party appeal has delayed the project by three years.Suzi Recine, left, and Uzzo Calderaro stand near a planned development in Kleinburg. Their residents group was appealing a development adjacent and abutting the Greenbelt, but that right was taken away from them with the passing of Bill 185.

Subject: Landowners; Legislation; Outdoor air quality; Third party; Appeals; Public hearings; Planning; Judicial reviews; Tribunals & commissions

Document 220 of 500

How Rob and Doug Ford's police helicopter hopes finally took off in Toronto

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/how-rob-doug-fords-police-helicopter-hopes/docview/3087695887/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=How+Rob+and+Doug+Ford%27s+police+helicopter+hopes+finally+took+off+in+Toronto&amp;title=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-08-04&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Something felt a little retro this week when Premier Doug Ford announced plans to purchase five choppers for police and cover ongoing operating costs.
Helicopters can conjure all kinds of images. Think of the Los Angeles news chopper over the slow-speed police chase of O.J. Simpson and his white Ford Bronco back in 1994, before Simpson was arrested and charged with murdering his ex-wife Nicole Brown Simpson and her friend, Ron Goldman. (That white Bronco is now in a Tennessee crime museum.) Others might equally hearken back to 1980s' TV shows - think "Magnum P.I." or "Airwolf."
Perhaps a little nostalgia is appropriate. These new helicopters are hardly a new idea.
Choppers for coppers was a hot topic 20 years ago, when Ford's late brother Rob was a city councillor, and outspoken proponent for equipping police with an air fleet.
That move ultimately fizzled, but not for lack of trying by Rob Ford and then-Toronto police chief Julian Fantino.
Back then, the Toronto Police Service Board said it didn't see roto-coppers as a priority.
This week, Ford's argument finally won the day, thanks to his brother, the premier.
'We've been dither-dallying'
Two decades ago, Toronto police would call on either the York or Durham forces to loan them their choppers, when needed. That's a practice that continues.
In 2000, Toronto police operated a pair of leased helicopters for six months in what was called (no pun intended) a pilot project.
In a review of that pilot project, former city auditor Jeff Griffiths found the helicopter made police and some citizens feel safer but had mixed results as a law-enforcement tool.
Griffiths' report also said the city would need more than one to patrol the city effectively.
Fantino was undaunted by drawbacks outlined in the auditor's report, which neither recommended nor opposed the helicopters.
The two helicopters used in the pilot project more than 20 years ago were paid for following a fundraising drive dubbed "Choppers for Coppers" with a provincial grant and private donations that Fantino collected from a mixed bag of benefactors, including air, sea and army cadets, major banks, a tobacco company, a brewery, a taekwondo studio and the Toronto Maple Leafs.
York Police also turned to private donors to get their fleet off the ground.
That included a mystery donor who was later revealed to be Charles R. Burnett III, the grandson of Garfield Weston, of the wealthy Weston family, nephew of Galen Weston, head of the family's multibillion-dollar retail, grocery and real estate empire, and nephew-through-marriage of then Ontario Lt.-Gov., Hilary Weston.
Rob Ford chafed in 2004 at a budget meeting that it was high time to finally make a decision.
"There's no sense in waiting for a report," Rob Ford said at the time. "We have to get on to this immediately. We've been dither-dallying for years and years and years."
Rob Ford unsuccessfully pressed on despite comments from Toronto police board chair Alan Heisey that he'd rather add 15 to 20 officers to the force with the money.
The council vote was 27-12 to refer it to the Toronto Police Service Board, where it was quashed.
'Every tool will help'
This week saw Doug Ford pledge $134 million for five new police helicopters to patrol the GTA and Ottawa to combat crimes such as auto theft, carjackings, street racing and impaired driving.
In Ford's plan, two helicopters will be purchased for the Ontario Provincial Police to work with the Ottawa and Toronto police, and three more helicopters will be purchased and operated by Durham, Halton and Peel police.
As Ford announced the establishment of the Greater Toronto Area Joint Air Support Unit (JASU), he said the new helicopter fleet would be just the thing to combat sky-high auto theft rates, that saw Toronto lose more than 12,000 autos to theft last year.
Critics told the Star's Kristin Rushowy that the choppers are helpful but "not the end all, be all."
A security official with Équité Association, the insurance industry investigative arm, said it welcomes helicopters in its fight against auto theft.
"Absolutely," says Bryan Gast, a former OPP officer. "Every tool will help to combat crime, including vehicle crimes."
GTA police were also buoyant at the news.
"Equipped with the latest technology, the helicopters will help keep Toronto safe from violent carjackings, Toronto Police Chief Myron Demkiw said as he addressed the Toronto Police Service Board on Wednesday.
"Auto thefts, street racing and impaired driving, and will also assist with missing persons and other police operations, at no cost to the city," Demkiw said.
"This is a monumental investment that will provide much-needed support to our officers and help them do the difficult work they do every day to enhance community safety," Peel Regional Police Chief Nishan Duraiappah said.
That means assistance in patrols, suspect and vehicle pursuits, missing person searches and combating street racing and stunt driving, Peel police said.
What other Canadian cities have in the sky
Calgary police have had helicopters for years, and upgraded their fleet in 2020 with two replacement aircraft.
The previous fleet had been in service more than 15 years.
Calgary police say the helicopters allow them to travel from one end of the city to the other in just eight minutes.
Montreal police have often borrowed a chopper from the provincial Sûreté du Quebéc to increase their visibility around popular gathering spots.
This was a particularly big priority for policing the Old Port, the downtown core and Mount Royal between 10 p.m. and 3 a.m. during the height of the COVID-19 pandemic, when large gatherings were forbidden.
Some Montreal residents complained that low-flying police choppers were intimidating and irritating, especially for children and pets.
Durham Regional Police Service have had a helicopter, dubbed "Air1," for more than 20 years.
York Regional Police boasts an Air Support Unit with a new state-of-the-art aircraft called "Air2," with what it calls "cutting-edge technology."
York police said the force's old "Air2" proved its worth "time and again," while logging 20 years of service and 20,000 operational hours.
"With the growth of our communities and changing dynamics of policing in Ontario, the York Regional Police Services Board approved a request from the York Regional Police Executive Command Team for the $7.1 million purchase of a replacement aircraft for Air2," York police announced in 2023 as it changed choppers.
"In 2022 alone, Air2 supported 1,373 calls for service, assisted officers on 266 traffic stops and helped make 213 arrests, including for commercial robberies, carjackings, break and enters, impaired driving, street racing and other offences," York police said in a statement. "Additionally, Air2 located 15 missing and vulnerable people."
Amid the debate over effectiveness, one salient point may have been made by former Toronto mayor Mel Lastman, who once said he liked the idea of helicopters because it makes people feel safe.
"I like to feel safe," Lastman said.
CAPTION: Premier Doug Ford on Monday announced $134 million for five new police helicopters to patrol the GTA and Ottawa to combat crimes such as auto theft, carjackings, street racing and impaired driving.In 2000, Const. Nicole Campbell was one of five officers assigned to the Toronto police's six-month helicopter pilot project.York Regional Police's first "Air2" chopper, seen in 2015, logged 20 years of service and 20,000 operational hours. The force has a new state-of-the-art aircraft, also called "Air2."

Document 221 of 500

Off-duty Toronto police officer shot downtown after spotting wanted man, assisting colleagues in foot chase

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/off-duty-toronto-police-officer-shot-downtown/docview/3087695877/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Off-duty+Toronto+police+officer+shot+downtown+after+spotting+wanted+man%2C+assisting+colleagues+in+foot+chase&amp;title=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-08-04&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: An off-duty Toronto police officer was shot while taking a man wanted on a Canada-wide warrant into custody in the city's downtown core late Saturday night.
Police said they were conducting an investigation in the Bathurst Street and King Street West area just before 11:45 p.m. when the off-duty officer was shot in the upper leg.
The officer was transported to hospital with non-life-threatening injuries and was later discharged Sunday morning. Police added he is currently recovering at home.
Det. Sgt. Brandon Price from the force's Homicide and Missing Person Unit told reporters on Sunday morning that the officer was in the area when he recognized a man wanted on a Canada-wide warrant for attempted murder and numerous other charges.
Price said the officer, who works in 51 Division, "promptly alerted on-duty officers" who then started to approach the man to apprehend him.
This led to a short foot chase by uniformed officers during which the accused, who was believed to alone at the time, shot the off-duty officer in the leg while he tried to assist his colleagues, he said.
The incident occurred in the southwest corner of the busy intersection as many people were out enjoying the long weekend at the bars and restaurants in the area.
According to information police have at this time of the investigation, there was no interaction between the accused man and officers, but the suspect started running when he observed police, Price said.
He added that there were no shots fired by police during the takedown by on-duty officers.
The 30-year-old-suspect, identified as Javell Jackson from Toronto, was arrested at the scene. He was wanted for the attempted murder of a man in a near-fatal shooting from December 2022 in the area of Church Street and Front Street East.
Police added a firearm was recovered.
"Additionally, he was wanted for unauthorized possession of a firearm, possession of a firearm contrary to a prohibition order, and failure to comply with the release order," Price said.
Jackson will be facing another count of attempted murder and numerous additional firearms charges.
"The incident demonstrates that our officers, even when off-duty (officers) are committed to keeping our city safe, sometimes at great risk to themselves," Price said.
"There are weapons on the streets, and we need to always be aware of that, and these officers did an amazing job taking a very dangerous individual off our streets," Price said.
The Toronto Police Association said in a post on X that they are "grateful the officer is alive."
Mayor Olivia Chow posted on X on Sunday morning wishing the officer a quick recovery.
"Last night, a Toronto police officer was shot while performing their duties. I wish the officer a quick recovery from their injuries and offer my full support. Violence is never acceptable," Chow said.
Ontario Premier Doug Ford said he is "relieved to hear the officer is alive."
"This terrible shooting is a reminder of the risks our brave women and men in uniform face every day as they work to keep our communities safe, and why it's so important that we all have their backs," Ford said in a post on X. "I'm relieved to hear the officer is alive and receiving treatment and I'm confident those responsible for this terrible act will be held accountable."
CAPTION: Det. Sgt. Brandon Price gives an update to reporters on Sunday morning at 14 Division after an off-duty police officer was shot during an arrest late Saturday night in downtown Toronto.A 30-year-old-man was arrested, according to police.Toronto police investigate the scene of a shooting at King and Bathurst Streetsearly Sunday morning.

Document 222 of 500

Aug. 3: Gage Park still full of children, fund research instead of beer sales and other letters to the editor

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/aug-3-gage-park-still-full-children-fund-research/docview/3087557635/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Aug.+3%3A+Gage+Park+still+full+of+children%2C+fund+research+instead+of+beer+sales+and+other+letters+to+the+editor&amp;title=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-08-03&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Gage Park is still full of children
Re: 'Compassion fatigue': Gage Park neighbours frustrated with encampments, Aug. 2
I was baffled by the opening passage of the Aug. 2 article about encampments in Gage Park. It stated that neighbours "remember the days" when children frolicked in the playground, and when it was possible to have birthday parties in the park. A Delta East resident, I visit Gage five to seven days per week. The park is still full of children. Picnic tables with birthday balloons remain a common sight. I recently went with a friend and her two children, ages five and nine, who had a wonderful time in the splash park, at the (crowded) playground, and chasing squirrels. These activities are all still in full swing at Gage Park. I hate that people reading your article, who are not familiar with the park, might come away with a different impression.
The nine-year-old asked why there were tents in the park. We told her that our provincial government is failing to take care of people and to ensure that rents are affordable. We told her that the people living in those tents - good people, our neighbours - deserve better.
Compassion fatigue is real, but it's nothing compared to the fatigue of surviving unsheltered, day after day, in the face of constant vitriol. If our unhoused neighbours are able to push through their fatigue to keep fighting to stay alive, the least we can do is push through our compassion fatigue, keep treating people with kindness, and direct our frustrations where they belong: toward our provincial government.
Alyssa Stryker, Hamilton
Fund research, not beer sales
Re: Ontario halts wastewater testing for COVID, other viruses, July 31
The Doug Ford government is ending the expanded wastewater surveillance program, begun in 2020 to monitor COVID-19, but found to be useful as a powerful detector of many illnesses. Sylvia Jones, our lapdog health minister, claims we are returning to what she calls a "normal state of affairs." In Conservative-governed Ontario, this means valuable, low-cost prevention programs such as this ($10 million to $15 million a year), are sacrificed so that our money can be spent to break contracts in order to make alcohol more available in local stores. The $225 million-plus spent on that could have covered the cost of the surveillance for decades to come. And what will it cost to "wind down" the program? Will we ever know? The cynicism and waste of this government boggles the mind.
Carol Town, Hamilton
A phenom in the water
Summer McIntosh is built differently. She is a phenom in the water and becoming a Canadian superstar and legend in this country. I've said it before and I'll say it again, she should be the closing ceremonies flag-bearer in my opinion.
Paul Bacon, Hallandale Beach, Fla.
Australians a good measuring stick for Canada
Australia is considered among the world's top athletic nations as they excel in so many sports. For a lesser-populated country, they compete above their weight class, and are surprisingly competent in traditional North American sports such as baseball. One must admire them for that. To see our Canadian men's basketball team and our women's rugby team finally better the Aussies gave great delight to those of us that are Olympics-watchers. Well done to both of Canada's teams!
Mike Brown, Burlington
Banks must support trafficking survivors
Re: Canada must do more for sex trafficking survivors, July 29
Outrageous. I am speaking about the financial exploitation of vulnerable victims of human trafficking in Canada.
It is an injustice to hold these victims accountable for debts incurred and imposed upon them, and I would suggest it is something our courts of law should do more to ban outright, and prevent our financial institutions from continuing what could be defined as an illegal cash grab.
In fact, it shouldn't require legislation to move financial institutions to do the right thing, and stop them from becoming predators themselves, in sheep's clothing.
Until now, I was unaware of Victim Services Toronto. I congratulate them for the work they are doing to address these injustices, and their coalition with other parties, that helped pass legislation to prevent further exploitation of survivors. But it appears more action is warranted.
Unless these financial institutions change, we may need to create a wall of shame, and not hesitate to identify and profile them as perpetrators of further abuse upon innocent victims.
Norman Damaren, Kitchener
CAPTION: Canada's Summer McIntosh celebrates winning the the women's 200-metre butterfly final Thursday at the 2024 Summer Olympics in Nanterre, France.Steve Jorgensen pets Duke at the campsite he has maintained at Gage Park for about five months. Despite the encampments, the public still enjoys the use of the park, a reader writes.

Document 223 of 500

'NIMBY' or not, rejection of east Hamilton housing pitch enters federal political arena

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/nimby-not-rejection-east-hamilton-housing-pitch/docview/3087557632/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=%27NIMBY%27+or+not%2C+rejection+of+east+Hamilton+housing+pitch+enters+federal+political+arena&amp;title=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-08-03&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: City politicians' rejection of a plan for 553 apartments in a suburban east Hamilton neighbourhood in the midst of a housing crisis has sparked accusations of NIMBYism from the federal Conservatives.
But "not-in-my-backyard" sentiments have nothing to do with local opposition to the proposal for the shuttered commercial plaza, Coun. Matt Francis says.
"It's not NIMBY at all because my residents know that change is coming to this location. They've accepted the fact that something's going to be built here."
But they "didn't feel the balance was right," the Ward 5 councillor said of local resistance to the Davis Creek neighbourhood application.
And so the 8-8 council vote that denied Medallion Development Inc.'s pitch for 399 Greenhill Ave. boiled down to "local democracy," Francis said.
In a campaign-style video posted to social media, Tory housing critic Scott Aitchison takes aim at the decision to reject Medallion's plan for two 12-storey and two four-storey buildings.
"The bureaucrats and the planning department actually recommended in favour of the project," says Aitchison, noting comparable mid-rise buildings already stand in the area of the old Mount Albion Plaza.
"They said it represented good planning and it was good, compact urban form, and yet city council, they voted against it. They voted against it."
The Parry Sound-Muskoka MP goes on to say the "Justin Trudeau government gave" Hamilton $93.5 million from its Housing Accelerator Fund to "speed up building and here we are delaying building."
"Well, when (Conservative Leader) Pierre Poilievre is prime minister, we'll have a very different approach. We will reward results. We're not going to pay for promises anymore. You see what that gets us. No more housing."
Francis argues Aitchison's rant "slams local democracy" and suggests the Conservatives ought to hold developers' feet to the fire for buying up properties and sitting on them for years without breaking ground.
"This was an attack on a community and a community's voice," he said, noting Medallion has since filed an appeal with the Ontario Land Tribunal (OLT).
Francis has backed residents in their misgivings over increased traffic and parking congestion, blocked vistas and the loss of a local grocery store with no commitment for a replacement in the project's commercial space.
"This development's located in a very car-centric neighbourhood, and people are going to have cars and lots of them if they're going to live here," he told planning committee about parking concerns.
But some fellow city politicians have taken to social media to side with at least some of Aitchison's points, particularly his framing of those councillors who turned down Medallion's proposal.
"Not often I retweet a Conservative MP," but Aitchison was "exactly right on the issue," Coun. Maureen Wilson posted on X.
Half of council "voted to kill desperately needed, good quality rental housing" within Hamilton's urban boundary that staff had recommended for approval, Wilson wrote.
"Pandering to a few NIMBY's in a housing crisis undermines our case for (federal/provincial) financial support and represents the height of political irresponsibility."
The Ward 1 councillor predicted Medallion's OLT challenge would make for the "shortest tribunal hearing ever," but cost "taxpayers nonetheless."
It's not accurate to frame council's overall record on planning decisions as "irresponsible," Coun. John-Paul Danko said in an interview.
"But this specific decision is obviously being seized upon by people that have ... a vested interest in trying to undermine local decision-making in order to consolidate planning decisions ... and I don't think that's in the city's best interest."
The federal Liberals announced Hamilton's $93.5-million allocation in October with the promise that it would "fast track" roughly 2,600 units over the next three years.
The city landed the funding in exchange for its commitments to residential zoning reform, streamlined building approvals and various development incentives.
The "seven-point" plan that Hamilton submitted "really reached the gold standard," Liberal MP Filomena Tassi (Hamilton West-Ancaster-Dundas) remarked during the announcement.
But Aitchison's video unfairly characterizes municipalities as "the villains," says Liberal MP Chad Collins, who represents Hamilton East-Stoney Creek.
"We don't see them as gatekeepers. We see them as partners as we're incentivizing them to change the way that they do business."
Before the COVID-19 pandemic, Canadian cities, including Hamilton, experienced strong residential growth, but the public health emergency led to labour shortages and supply-chain issues, said Collins, noting high interest rates continue to slow housing starts.
Builders, not municipalities, "determine when shovels go in the ground," he added.
Aitchison didn't respond to The Spectator's request for an interview to explain how the Conservatives would ensure developers follow through on housing plans once approvals are in hand.
But Poilievre's press secretary provided an emailed statement attributed to the shadow minister for housing.
It pointed to escalating rents and mortgages, tent encampments, rising interest rates, as well as "inflationary taxes and spending" under Trudeau's watch.
"While Trudeau continues to reward city gatekeepers who block housing, Common Sense Conservatives will require municipalities to remove gatekeepers and to build 15% more homes every year, providing a building bonus for exceeding the target or imposing a fine if they don't.
"We will also cap wasteful inflationary government spending and fix the budget to bring down inflation and interest rates so builders can build the homes Canadians can afford."
Collins argued the Conservatives' hard line against municipal control of planning applications would likely fuel donations from "salivating" developers.
But local input into proposed development remains "paramount," the former Ward 5 councillor said.
"Otherwise, we wouldn't have planning committee meetings and we wouldn't have applications. We'd just let people do whatever they want."
Teviah Moro is a reporter at The Hamilton Spectator. tmoro@thespec.com
CAPTION: Council voted 8-8 to deny Medallion Development Inc.'s pitch for 399 Greenhill Ave.Conservative MP Scott Aitchison takes aim at Hamilton council's rejection of a 553-unit rental housing proposal and criticizes a federal Liberal housing program in a video posted to social media.Coun. Matt Francis says a decision to reject a proposal for 553 rental units in an east Hamilton neighbourhood wasn't fuelled by NIMBY sentiments.Coun. Maureen Wilson says half of council "voted to kill desperately needed, good quality rental housing" in rejecting a proposal for Greenhill Avenue.City politicians' rejection of a plan for 553 apartments at 399 Greenhill Ave. has sparked accusations of NIMBYism from the federal Conservatives.

Document 224 of 500

Reaching for the rock: They - and their hair - were huge in the '80s. Now, with an upcoming arena show headlined by Billy Idol, can Toronto's Platinum Blonde make a comeback?

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/reaching-rock/docview/3087527094/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Reaching+for+the+rock%3A+They+-+and+their+hair+-+were+huge+in+the+%2780s.+Now%2C+with+an+upcoming+arena+show+headlined+by+Billy+Idol%2C+can+Toronto%27s+Platinum+Blonde+make+a+comeback%3F&amp;title=The+Spectator&amp;issn=11899417&amp;date=2024-08-03&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=G.7&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Spectator&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: You have your Halley's Comet. You have your solar eclipses. But an even rarer phenomenon - a national tour pairing two rock acts who played together 40 years ago - will be visible in Toronto when Billy Idol and Platinum Blonde perform at Scotiabank Arena on Aug. 9.
Back in January 1984, when music videos reigned supreme, the sneering, spiky British punk Billy Idol, then riding high on his Rebel Yell tour, invited Toronto's ascendant glam rockers Platinum Blonde to open for him in Ontario, including a Massey Hall appearance.
Four decades later, with many hits, kilometres and history behind them, the bands began a cross-Canada tour in Vancouver on Tuesday.
So how does Platinum Blonde bassist and singer Mark Holmes feel about this full circle moment?
"When (promoter) Live Nation talked to me about it, they wanted to hearken back to those times, because we were both fresh and green and it was exciting," said Holmes, who has piloted the fashionable group since its inception in 1979. "And now, after all this time, we get to do it again. It's almost like a gift."
In '84, he recalled, "we were really catching on. It was more of a doubleheader (billing). We were both on the rise, pretty much neck and neck."
He's not kidding: Canadians quickly took to the Blondes, which in addition to Holmes - a native of Mansfield, England - featured Sergio Galli on guitar and Chris Steffler on drums. Aided by colourful videos and punchy, hook-laden songs such as "Not in Love" and "Doesn't Really Matter," they ruled the airwaves.
Their debut album, "Standing in the Dark," released in early '84, ended up selling several hundred thousand copies. A free concert at Nathan Phillips Square on May 25 of that same year, expected to draw 2,500 people, attracted a crowd estimated at between 25,000 and 40,000, resulting in more than 100 people being treated for various ailments and front-page coverage in the Toronto Star.
Guitarist Kenny MacLean joined the band a year later, in time for their sophomore album, "Alien Shores," which yielded the No. 1 single "Crying Over You" and sold more than 500,000 copies.
"We had teenage girls screaming like the Beatles, and every time we stopped a song we had to cover our ears, they were so loud," Holmes recalled during a recent interview conducted on his 64th birthday.
"Fans loved us. The industry really hated us. We were rock stars: crazy kids doing crazy stuff, getting drunk and getting into fights."
They had no handbook, so Holmes and crew played up their celebrity stature.
"You feel like it's your path," he said. "Because everything is happening for the first time, you don't know what's going to be happening the next time."
Some misunderstanding
Despite the hysteria, Holmes maintained that Platinum Blonde may have been one of the world's most misunderstood bands.
"Did anybody know what we were singing about?" he asked rhetorically. "We were singing about fallout from nuclear war. We were singing about acid rain. We were singing about f--ked up psychological things, like 'Not in Love,' which is about denial.
"We weren't singing, 'I love you, baby,' and I'm f--king proud about that. I wanted to be remembered for being socially conscious."
"Doesn't Really Matter," for example, was inspired by the time Holmes was travelling to an airport but didn't make it because the Irish Republican Army had blown up an entrance to the highway.
"That was our first hit and all these teenage girls were screaming at us," he said. "Listen to that first record, it's dark. 'Standing in the Dark' - that's how I'd like to be remembered.
"Will people remember us that way? No, because people still remember the hair."
As much as fans may remember those magnificent, massive coifs, they haven't forgotten the music: Sony Music Canada recently announced that "Alien Shores" has sold 860,000 units.
Platinum Blonde's winning streak ended with 1987's "Contact," an album that reimagined the group as a funk band (with new drummer Sascha Tukatsch) - a direction that Holmes conceded may have been a bit premature in the wake of the "Alien Shores" triumph.
"The '80s were a changing, volatile time," Holmes said. "Holy cow: every six months there was a new sound, a new vibe." When the supergroup Power Station released their debut album with the of-the-moment gated drum sound, Platinum Blonde enlisted their producer Bernard Edwards to work on "Contact."
"That music was so great and of course we got to play with a real funk-rock legend," Holmes said.
"But this is what I learned: when you're in a band, you've heard your own songs and played them so much and they (may not even be) released yet, but you find yourself musically moving on to different things.
"You have to slow yourself down. You've got to think, 'If we want to take our fans with us, we've got to bring this in a little bit more with our classic sound.'
"During that 'Contact' record, there were a lot of influences because now, all of a sudden, we're big stars: big money, everybody's got things on the line. 'Contact' put wedges between band members."
Holmes admitted that drugs also played a part in the internal strife.
"It absolutely was in the band," he said. "It was a coke-fuelled industry and I can say that now without having people say to me, 'Oh, you'll never work again.'"
A new beginning
Although they didn't tour "Contact," they regrouped as the Blondes for the album "Yeah Yeah Yeah," but after 1989 didn't play together live for 10 years. They reconvened for the occasional gig between 2000 and 2002, and then Holmes put Platinum Blonde on hold to co-found Toronto's Mod Club, where he was also known as DJ MRK.
While he was spinning, his old bandmate Kenny MacLean kept in touch.
"He would call me when I had found a lot of success as a DJ and with remixes," Holmes recalled. "That's where my life was: I had no time to talk or rehearse with the band. I'm trying to stay atop of the DJ world. You talk about pressure? That's pressure: always being the guy who is trying to come up with the new thing. It's an interesting world and I became fully involved in it.
"One day, I just decided to soften up. Kenny had called me every six months to say, 'Let's write some songs and do a tour with the band.' I was leading a double life: my DJ fans didn't know I was in Platinum Blonde. All they knew was MRK is this big English DJ. They had no clue of any of my past.
"I didn't want that knowledge to cross over to a band, because I don't know what that would have done to my rep. All my fans were in their early 20s."
But Holmes finally relented and agreed to reunite Platinum Blonde onstage, joining MacLean at the Mod Club during the release party for one of the musician's solo albums to sing an old hit or two.
"You should have seen his face," Holmes said. "He was so happy."
That was on Nov. 22, 2008, the last time Holmes saw MacLean alive.
"Kenny was so happy and I was so happy to see him so happy. I was invigorated and I thought, we're going to do this! This is going to be awesome! We had the after-party upstairs in what we call the Orange Room and then he went home that night.
"When he was brushing his teeth, it was over." On Nov. 24, MacLean was found dead of natural causes.
Holmes said he decided to honour MacLean's memory by reuniting with Sergio Galli and recording 2012's "Now & Never."
"I couldn't say that was the end," said Holmes. "We did a record, released it and toured it, and it was a success. Every moment I played, Kenny was with us. It's still heartbreaking."
After having his band feted at Canada's Walk of Fame last September, Holmes - also co-owner of the Jean Darlene Piano Room - said Platinum Blonde is back and prepping new music, some of which will be heard at Scotiabank Arena.
They're also yearning for another hit.
"When you're a creative artist and all you do is write and produce, that never goes away," he said.
"Even with Platinum Blonde, we'll never lose that feeling of wanting to put out another hit and, who knows, maybe one day you get a solid hit."
In 2010, Platinum Blonde got a boost when Toronto electro act Crystal Castles, with help from the Cure's Robert Smith, covered "Not in Love." "When we released our song 'Beautiful,' we got hits in places we'd never had before. That was in 2012," Holmes said.
"So it's always there, if you want it. The key component to being a functioning, creative musician and songwriter is to never ever, under any circumstances, grow up. You grow up, your art is done. It's over."
CAPTION: Platinum Blonde's Chris Steffler, Mark Holmes and Sergio Galli in their high-hair heyday.A Platinum Blonde concert at Toronto City Hall in May 1984 drew tens of thousands.Mark Holmes performed with Platinum Blonde at Peterborough's Del Crary Park in August 2016.Dimo SafariFrank Lennon Toronto Star file photoClifford Skarstedt Peterborough Examiner file photo

CREDIT: Nick Krewen Special to the Star

People: Idol, Billy

Document 225 of 500

Ontario won't adopt new federal riding boundaries

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/ontario-wont-adopt-new-federal-riding-boundaries/docview/3087526102/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Ontario+won%27t+adopt+new+federal+riding+boundaries&amp;title=The+Spectator&amp;issn=11899417&amp;date=2024-08-03&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.11&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Spectator&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Premier Doug Ford says Ontario is breaking with tradition and not adopting federal electoral boundary changes for the next provincial election, meaning there will be 124 ridings contested.
"No," said Ford on Thursday when asked whether Queen's Park would follow Ottawa's lead and increase the number of constituencies in the province.
His surprise move also means Toronto city council boundaries won't be redrawn either, so there will remain 25 councillors - instead of being cut to 24 - after the 2026 municipal election.
"Why change something that works? Just because the feds want to do it, jerry-rig the ridings," said Ford, alleging, without providing evidence, that the independent federal boundary commission gerrymanders electoral districts.
"It's no secret people do that, governments do that, I'm not doing that," he told reporters at an unrelated press conference at Lakeridge Health Oshawa.
"The less politicians, the better it is," said the premier, whose cabinet has 36 ministers, by far the largest in Ontario history. "I'm going to leave the boundaries alone and people will decide if they want to move forward with our government on prosperity and health care and on the economy - or if they want to go backwards and look for the other guys. They'll have that choice."
Ford's unexpected comments followed a Star report this week that Elections Ontario was awaiting instructions from the legislature on how to proceed on redistribution.
The uncertainty came against the backdrop of Ford considering an early election call next spring, which would cost taxpayers $150 million, one year ahead of the scheduled June 2026 vote.
With the exception of Northern Ontario, the province's current electoral ridings are based on the Elections Canada map.
Federal boundaries will change for an election expected in October 2025, jumping from 338 electoral districts to 343 across Canada.
That includes boosting Ontario's 121 federal ridings to 122 with the addition of three seats in Brampton, Durham and Milton and the elimination of one in Toronto and another in the north due to demographic changes.
Polls suggest that provincial redistribution would have favoured Ford's Tories, who won 83 of 124 seats in the 2022 election.
The premier's decree clears the air for Elections Ontario, which had warned "Ontario does not have legislation in place for a scheduled review of electoral districts and boundaries."
"Provincial electoral district boundaries in Ontario will therefore remain unchanged until new legislation has passed," the independent arm's length agency told the Star last week.
"The legislature has a choice in how to respond to the new federal electoral boundaries. It could, for example, establish its own commission to determine what the new electoral boundaries would be, it could adopt the new federal electoral boundaries in part or in their entirety, or it could choose to retain the existing 124 electoral boundaries."
Under Ontario's 2017 Representation Act, passed by former premier Kathleen Wynne's Liberals, there are now "111 southern electoral districts whose names and boundaries are identical to those of the federal electoral districts."
Wynne - after a report from the non-partisan Far North Electoral Boundaries Commission - kept 13 Northern Ontario ridings instead of the 10 federal constituencies at the time in order to improve Indigenous and northern representation.
Ford's intervention ensures that Toronto will not lose the constituency of Don Valley East at Queen's Park. Federally, it's being absorbed into Don Valley West and Don Valley North, with the rest merged with Scarborough Centre to create a renamed Scarborough Centre-Don Valley East.
It also means that city council boundaries won't be amended for the 2026 municipal election, sparing Ward 16 (Don Valley East) represented by Coun. Jon Burnside.
But fast-growing Brampton, Durham and Milton won't get the additional provincial representation they will receive federally.
The federal riding of Brampton North has been divided into Brampton North-Caledon, including portions of Dufferin Caledon, and Brampton-Chinguacousy Park, which takes part of Brampton Centre.
And the federal constituency of Durham has been split into two new ridings called York-Durham and Bowmanville-Oshawa North, while Milton has been divvied up into Burlington North-Milton West and Milton East-Halton Hills South, including a piece of Wellington-Halton Hills.
CAPTION: Premier Doug Ford rejected the new federal electoral map that adds one riding to the GTA and removes one from the city.Frank Gunn The Canadian Press

CREDIT: Robert Benzie Toronto Star

Document 226 of 500

Rejection of Hamilton housing pitch enters federal political arena: Conservatives seize council's denial to pounce on Liberal housing plan

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/rejection-hamilton-housing-pitch-enters-federal/docview/3087525661/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Rejection+of+Hamilton+housing+pitch+enters+federal+political+arena%3A+Conservatives+seize+council%27s+denial+to+pounce+on+Liberal+housing+plan&amp;title=The+Spectator&amp;issn=11899417&amp;date=2024-08-03&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.1&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Spectator&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: City politicians' rejection of a plan for 553 apartments in a suburban east Hamilton neighbourhood in the midst of a housing crisis has sparked accusations of NIMBYism from the federal Conservatives.
But "not-in-my-backyard" sentiments have nothing to do with local opposition to the proposal for the shuttered commercial plaza, Coun. Matt Francis says.
