In a campaign-style video posted to social media, Tory housing critic Scott Aitchison takes aim at the decision to reject Medallion's plan for two 12-storey and two four-storey buildings.
"The bureaucrats and the planning department actually recommended in favour of the project," says Aitchison, noting comparable mid-rise buildings already stand in the area of the old Mount Albion Plaza.
"They said it represented good planning and it was good, compact urban form, and yet city council, they voted against it. They voted against it."
The Parry Sound-Muskoka MP goes on to say the "Justin Trudeau government gave" Hamilton $93.5 million from its Housing Accelerator Fund to "speed up building and here we are delaying building."
"Well, when (Conservative Leader) Pierre Poilievre is prime minister, we'll have a very different approach. We will reward results. We're not going to pay for promises anymore. You see what that gets us. No more housing."
Francis argues Aitchison's rant "slams local democracy" and suggests the Conservatives ought to hold developers' feet to the fire for buying up properties and sitting on them for years without breaking ground.
"This was an attack on a community and a community's voice," he said, noting Medallion has since filed an appeal with the Ontario Land Tribunal (OLT).
Francis has backed residents in their misgivings over increased traffic and parking congestion, blocked vistas and the loss of a local grocery store with no commitment for a replacement in the project's commercial space.
"This development's located in a very car-centric neighbourhood, and people are going to have cars and lots of them if they're going to live here," he told planning committee about parking concerns.
But some fellow city politicians have taken to social media to side with at least some of Aitchison's points, particularly his framing of those councillors who turned down Medallion's proposal.
"Not often I retweet a Conservative MP," but Aitchison was "exactly right on the issue," Coun. Maureen Wilson posted on X.
Half of council "voted to kill desperately needed, good quality rental housing" within Hamilton's urban boundary that staff had recommended for approval, Wilson wrote.
"Pandering to a few NIMBY's in a housing crisis undermines our case for (federal/provincial) financial support and represents the height of political irresponsibility."
The Ward 1 councillor predicted Medallion's OLT challenge would make for the "shortest tribunal hearing ever," but cost "taxpayers nonetheless."
It's not accurate to frame council's overall record on planning decisions as "irresponsible," Coun. John-Paul Danko said in an interview.
"But this specific decision is obviously being seized upon by people that have ... a vested interest in trying to undermine local decision-making in order to consolidate planning decisions ... and I don't think that's in the city's best interest."
The federal Liberals announced Hamilton's $93.5-million allocation in October with the promise that it would "fast track" roughly 2,600 units over the next three years.
The city landed the funding in exchange for its commitments to residential zoning reform, streamlined building approvals and various development incentives.
The "seven-point" plan that Hamilton submitted "really reached the gold standard," Liberal MP Filomena Tassi (Hamilton West-Ancaster-Dundas) remarked during the announcement.
But Aitchison's video unfairly characterizes municipalities as "the villains," says Liberal MP Chad Collins, who represents Hamilton East-Stoney Creek.
"We don't see them as gatekeepers. We see them as partners as we're incentivizing them to change the way that they do business."
Before the COVID-19 pandemic, Canadian cities, including Hamilton, experienced strong residential growth, but the public health emergency led to labour shortages and supply-chain issues, said Collins, noting high interest rates continue to slow housing starts.
Builders, not municipalities, "determine when shovels go in the ground," he added.
Aitchison didn't respond to The Spectator's request for an interview to explain how the Conservatives would ensure developers follow through on housing plans once approvals are in hand.
But Poilievre's press secretary provided an emailed statement attributed to the shadow minister for housing.
It pointed to escalating rents and mortgages, tent encampments, rising interest rates, as well as "inflationary taxes and spending" under Trudeau's watch.
"While Trudeau continues to reward city gatekeepers who block housing, Common Sense Conservatives will require municipalities to remove gatekeepers and to build 15% more homes every year, providing a building bonus for exceeding the target or imposing a fine if they don't.
"We will also cap wasteful inflationary government spending and fix the budget to bring down inflation and interest rates so builders can build the homes Canadians can afford."
Collins argued the Conservatives' hard line against municipal control of planning applications would likely fuel donations from "salivating" developers.
But local input into proposed development remains "paramount," the former Ward 5 councillor said.
"Otherwise, we wouldn't have planning committee meetings and we wouldn't have applications. We'd just let people do whatever they want."
Teviah Moro is a reporter at The Hamilton Spectator. tmoro@thespec.com
CAPTION: City politicians' rejection of a plan for 553 apartments at 399 Greenhill Ave. has sparked accusations of NIMBYism from the federal Conservatives.Cathie Coward The Hamilton Spectator

Document 227 of 500

Letters

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/letters/docview/3087525200/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=unknown&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Letters&amp;title=The+Spectator&amp;issn=11899417&amp;date=2024-08-03&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.16&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Spectator&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Gage Park is still full of children
Re: 'Compassion fatigue': Gage Park neighbours frustrated with encampments, Aug. 2
I was baffled by the opening passage of the Aug. 2 article about encampments in Gage Park. It stated that neighbours "remember the days" when children frolicked in the playground, and when it was possible to have birthday parties in the park.
A Delta East resident, I visit Gage five to seven days per week. The park is still full of children. Picnic tables with birthday balloons remain a common sight.
I recently went with a friend and her two children, ages five and nine, who had a wonderful time in the splash park, at the (crowded) playground, and chasing squirrels.
These activities are all still in full swing at Gage Park. I hate that people reading your article, who are not familiar with the park, might come away with a different impression.
The nine-year-old asked why there were tents in the park. We told her that our provincial government is failing to take care of people and to ensure that rents are affordable. We told her that the people living in those tents - good people, our neighbours - deserve better.
Compassion fatigue is real, but it's nothing compared to the fatigue of surviving unsheltered, day after day, in the face of constant vitriol. If our unhoused neighbours are able to push through their fatigue to keep fighting to stay alive, the least we can do is push through our compassion fatigue, keep treating people with kindness, and direct our frustrations where they belong: toward our provincial government.
Alyssa Stryker, Hamilton
Fund research, not beer sales
Re: Ontario halts wastewater testing for COVID,
other viruses, July 31
The Doug Ford government is ending the expanded wastewater surveillance program, begun in 2020 to monitor COVID-19, but found to be useful as a powerful detector of many illnesses. Sylvia Jones, our lapdog health minister, claims we are returning to what she calls a "normal state of affairs." In Conservative-governed Ontario, this means valuable, low-cost prevention programs such as this ($10 million to $15 million a year), are sacrificed so that our money can be spent to break contracts in order to make alcohol more available in local stores. The $225 million-plus spent on that could have covered the cost of the surveillance for decades to come. And what will it cost to "wind down" the program? Will we ever know? The cynicism and waste of this government boggles the mind.
Carol Town, Hamilton
A phenom in the water
Summer McIntosh is built differently. She is a phenom in the water and becoming a Canadian superstar and legend in this country. I've said it before and I'll say it again, she should be the closing ceremonies flag-bearer in my opinion.
Paul Bacon, Hallandale Beach, Fla.
Australians show the way
Australia is considered among the world's top athletic nations as they excel in so many sports. For a lesser-populated country, they compete above their weight class, and are surprisingly competent in traditional North American sports such as baseball.
One must admire them for that. To see our Canadian men's basketball team and our women's rugby team finally better the Aussies gave great delight to those of us that are Olympics-watchers. Well done to both of Canada's teams!
Mike Brown, Burlington
Banks must support survivors
Re: Canada must do more for sex trafficking
survivors, July 29
Outrageous. I am speaking about the financial exploitation of vulnerable victims of human trafficking in Canada.
It is an injustice to hold these victims accountable for debts incurred and imposed upon them, and I would suggest it is something our courts of law should do more to ban outright, and prevent our financial institutions from continuing what could be defined as an illegal cash grab.
In fact, it shouldn't require legislation to move financial institutions to do the right thing, and stop them from becoming predators themselves, in sheep's clothing.
Until now, I was unaware of Victim Services Toronto. I congratulate them for the work they are doing to address these injustices, and their coalition with other parties, that helped pass legislation to prevent further exploitation of survivors. But it appears more action is warranted.
Unless these financial institutions change, we may need to create a wall of shame, and not hesitate to identify and profile them as perpetrators of further abuse upon innocent victims.
Norman Damaren, Kitchener

Document 228 of 500

Haldimand-Norfolk loses early warning system for viral diseases as province pulls plug on wastewater testing program

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/haldimand-norfolk-loses-early-warning-system/docview/3087311385/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Haldimand-Norfolk+loses+early+warning+system+for+viral+diseases+as+province+pulls+plug+on+wastewater+testing+program&amp;title=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-08-02&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Haldimand-Norfolk has lost its early-warning system for viruses like RSV and influenza now that the province has stopped funding for wastewater testing.
Data about the viral load present in wastewater collected at treatment plants in Simcoe and Caledonia stopped flowing to the local health unit as of Wednesday, when the province ended its wastewater surveillance program.
Tracking wastewater "provides a better picture of disease activity in the community than testing and hospitalizations (and) emergency department visits alone," environmental health program manager Alexis Atkinson and epidemiologist Megan McCallum from the Haldimand-Norfolk Health Unit (HNHU) told The Spectator over email.
Local wastewater had been tested for SARS-CoV-2 (the virus behind the COVID-19 pandemic), influenza A and B, and RSV.
Samples were sent for analysis at Western University, with the results coming back to the health unit through the Ontario Wastewater Surveillance Initiative Data and Visualization Hub, run by the Ministry of Environment, Conservation and Parks.
The provincially funded program - offered at no cost to the health unit - helped officials gauge "the level of disease activity in an area at a point in time," Atkinson and McCallum said.
Wastewater data was "not typically used to identify outbreaks in higher-risk settings" during the COVID-19 pandemic, they added. But as wastewater samples encompass both symptomatic and asymptomatic carriers, testing gave the health unit a heads up about possible outbreaks.
"Wastewater can be used to assist in making informed decisions about potential public health interventions (and) help inform emergency preparedness and surge planning by identifying potential increases in disease activity," Atkinson and McCallum said.
In an interview with the Canadian Press, Dr. Fahad Razak, former scientific director of the COVID-19 Ontario Science Table, called the province's decision to stop testing wastewater "incredibly disappointing," arguing the program helped dozens of communities monitor and respond to the threat posed by viruses like COVID and avian flu.
The Environment Ministry told the Canadian Press the program was "winding down" to coincide with the Public Health Agency of Canada expanding its wastewater surveillance sites. But a spokesperson for the federal agency said the added test sites would not replace the provincial program.
That leaves Haldimand-Norfolk without a canary in the RSV coal mine as flu season nears.
"At this time, HNHU cannot continue with wastewater testing if not provided funding for this activity, as we do not have the resources available," Atkinson and McCallum told The Spectator.
In a statement, Ontario NDP Leader Marit Stiles called on the Ford government to restore wastewater testing and expand the program "to monitor and prepare for future public health emergencies."
But independent Haldimand-Norfolk MPP Bobbi Ann Brady told The Spectator ending the program makes sense, noting the "extra layer of surveillance" prompted by the pandemic "was not used to identify outbreak settings" in her riding.
"I trust that returning to pre-pandemic surveillance was not an easy decision," Brady said in an email.
"However, it is the surveillance that has been in place for decades in Ontario, whereby the federal government works with our public health units and clinicians," she said.
"I feel that returning to a normal state of affairs will perhaps allow the government to funnel these monies to vital services at this point in time."
J.P. Antonacci's reporting is funded by the Canadian government through its Local Journalism Initiative. jpantonacci@thespec.com
CAPTION: The Haldimand-Norfolk Health Unit no longer has access to wastewater testing data after the province ended a program designed to detect increasing levels of RSV, influenza and COVID-19.

Document 230 of 500

Libel lawsuit threat against Ford expires

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/libel-lawsuit-threat-against-ford-expires/docview/3087188019/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Libel+lawsuit+threat+against+Ford+expires&amp;title=The+Spectator&amp;issn=11899417&amp;date=2024-08-02&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.4&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Spectator&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: A threatened $2.5-million libel lawsuit against Premier Doug Ford has fizzled after Independent MPP Sarah Jama (Hamilton Centre) and her lawyer failed to serve papers before a mid-July deadline.
An eight-page statement of claim was filed with the Ontario Superior Court of Justice and dated Jan. 11. But no further action was taken in the lawsuit, which alleged Ford defamed the former NDP MPP in the wake of a statement she made following the Oct. 7 attack on Israel by Hamas and subsequent retaliation in Gaza.
"The deadline expired and it was never served," Ford lawyer Gavin Tighe said in an email to the Star. "We offered from the outset, in writing, to accept service. Under the rules, a statement of claim 'shall' be served within six months of issuance.
"That period has now lapsed."
Jama and her lawyer, Stephen Ellis, didn't respond Thursday to requests seeking comment.
Ford accused Jama - then a member of the NDP caucus - of "a long and well-documented history of antisemitism" and of "publicly supporting the rape and murder of innocent Jewish people" in an Oct. 11, 2023 post.
It was a day after she issued a statement on her MPP letterhead calling for an immediate ceasefire in the Israel-Hamas war. That statement didn't mention the Israeli casualties in the Oct. 7 attack by Hamas.
Jama described the Palestinian situation as an "apartheid in real time through the continued violation of human rights in Gaza ... for 75 years, violence and retaliation rooted in settler colonialism have taken the lives of far too many innocent people. I call for an immediate ceasefire and de-escalation."
Hours after Ford's statement on X and at the previous urgings of NDP Leader Marit Stiles for a retraction of her original statement, Jama issued a clarification and apology.
Stiles ejected Jama from the NDP caucus last Oct. 23 for insubordination on the same day she was censured by a majority vote of Ford's Progressive Conservatives in the legislature, a move that prevents Jama from asking questions of Ford and his cabinet ministers or otherwise speaking in the chamber.
CAPTION: Independent MPP Sarah Jama speaks to reporters at Queen's Park in May.Christopher Katsarov Canadian Press file photo

CREDIT: Rob Ferguson Toronto Star

Document 231 of 500

5 reasons why Canada should consider moving to a 4-day work week

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/5-reasons-why-canada-should-consider-moving-4-day/docview/3087187738/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=unknown&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=5+reasons+why+Canada+should+consider+moving+to+a+4-day+work+week&amp;title=The+Spectator&amp;issn=11899417&amp;date=2024-08-02&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.11&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Spectator&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Nine out of 10 Canadians are interested in a four-day work week, according to a recent survey, making it their most desirable benefit ahead of health care, flexible scheduling and additional vacation days.
To be clear, a four-day work week in this context does not refer to a condensed five-day schedule, but rather the 100-80-100 model, where employees work 80 per cent of the time while maintaining 100 per cent of their productivity and pay. This means Canada, which already struggles with productivity levels, won't see a dip if it switches to this model.
Globally, the four-day work week has proven successful. 4 Day Week Global, a not-for-profit organization, has run pilot programs in six continents. It reports that 91 per cent of companies that have tried this model chose to continue with it.
Could the four-day work week be Canada's new reality? While we can't predict the future, here are five compelling reasons why a reduced work week makes sense for the country.
1. Declining work hours over time
Over the past 150 years, work hours have been consistently decreasing despite resistance from employers. In the past, Canadian factory workers used to endure up to 12-hour workdays, six days a week, totalling a gruelling 72-hour work week.
In 1872, when printers in Toronto requested a nine-hour workday, their demands were dismissed as "foolish," "absurd," and "unreasonable" by employers. This led to the historic Printers' Strike, a part of the Nine Hour Movement advocating for shorter workdays.
Over the next century, unionized workers continued to fight against employer skepticism and achieve shorter work weeks. The standard work week was progressively reduced until it reached 40 hours in the 1960s.
Throughout history, efforts to reduce work hours have consistently faced strong resistance from employers. However, these efforts have ultimately prevailed, aligning with evolving societal norms and economic conditions. The five-day work week has been our model for 60 years. It is time to try something different.
2. The outdated 5-day work week
The traditional five-day work week, once suitable for single-income families, is now incompatible with today's dual-income households. While the typical work week has remained unchanged for decades, family dynamics have shifted significantly over the past 60 years.
Decades ago, one salary often sufficed for a family. Today, most Canadian families have dual incomes, working twice as much without a reduction in family responsibilities. This shift has significantly increased the burden of household management, making families more reliant on external support.
In 1979, Canada had 110,000 licensed child care spaces, covering seven per cent of children. Today, this has increased tenfold, yet many parents still face wait lists.
Research also shows that dual-income families experience greater work-family conflict and stress compared to single-income families, with women shouldering 50 per cent more unpaid housework according to Statistics Canada than men.
A combined 60-hour work week is more optimal according to research, offering parents substantial benefits in job flexibility and family satisfaction.
3. Sustainability benefits
Canada has an ambitious plan to reduce CO2 emissions by 40 to 45 per cent below 2005 levels by 2030, aiming for net-zero emissions by 2050.
Reducing the number of hours worked is one of the most effective strategies to lower our carbon footprint, as it means using less machinery and reducing commuting, both of which cut emissions.
Additionally, a four-day work week could lead to reduced consumption. When people work long hours, they tend to consume more. To encourage people to engage in sustainable activities like cooking food at home, biking and making eco-friendly home improvements, they need more time.
Research on French families' work and consumption patterns confirms that, to be more sustainable, families need to work less.
4. Success around the world
Many countries around the world have experimented with and implemented shorter work weeks with remarkable success. In Iceland, a large-scale trial of a four-day work week with no reduction in pay led to increased productivity, improved employee well-being and reduced stress levels.
Similarly, in the United Kingdom, various companies have run four-day work week trials, reporting positive outcomes such as enhanced employee morale, greater retention rates and sustained or improved productivity.
France has maintained a 35-hour work week since 1998, with no government reverting to a 40-hour standard.
The rapid dissemination of these success stories through social media and news feeds appears to be influencing expectations and might lead more Canadians to advocate for a four-day work week, knowing that others have already obtained it.
5. The 4-day work week in Canada
Canadian companies have also been adopting the four-day work week with success, as seen in Ontario, British Columbia and Quebec. These initiatives have been spearheaded by forward-thinking leaders, despite the lack of a nationwide proposal from any political party.
Notably, the B.C. Green Party called for a four-day work week pilot in 2023, highlighting growing political interest in the model.
Historically, unions have played a pivotal role in reducing work hours for employees. Today, although union influence may have waned, the momentum for shorter work weeks continues, driven by regular employee turnover and individual negotiations.
Average yearly work hours have declined from 1,840 in 1997 to 1,716 in 2022, while GDP has continued to rise. This trend indicates that many Canadians are working fewer hours with no detriment to economic performance, providing a compelling case for the broader adoption of the four-day work week.
A shift is underway
While there is no nationwide law mandating reduced work hours and no major unions have publicly called for it, an increasing number of businesses are adopting four-day work weeks. Given the worldwide popularity and positive results of the approach, this trend is likely to continue to grow in Canada.
The four-day work week presents significant opportunities for organizations. Top-performing employees are drawn to jobs with the best benefits, and the four-day work week is quickly becoming the most coveted benefit. Companies that implement this model are well-positioned to attract and retain talented workers.
For the average worker, a four-day work week means improved work-life balance, more time to spend with family and friends, and opportunities to pursue personal interests and hobbies. This shift can lead to increased job satisfaction, better mental health and overall quality of life.
As more organizations adopt this model, employees can look forward to a future where the balance between work and personal life is more manageable and enriching.
Jean-Nicolas Reyt is a management professor at McGill
University. This was originally published on The Conversation.
CAPTION: The five-day work week has been our model for 60 years. It is time to try something different, Jean-Nicolas Reyt writes.Dreamstime

Document 232 of 500

Ford government to break with tradition and not adopt federal boundary changes for next election

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/ford-government-break-with-tradition-not-adopt/docview/3087402060/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Ford+government+to+break+with+tradition+and+not+adopt+federal+boundary+changes+for+next+election&amp;title=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-08-01&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Premier Doug Ford says Ontario is breaking with tradition and not adopting federal electoral boundary changes for the next provincial election, meaning there will be 124 ridings contested.
"No," said Ford on Thursday when asked whether Queen's Park would follow Ottawa's lead and increase the number of constituencies in the province.
His surprise move also means Toronto city council boundaries won't be redrawn either, so there will remain 25 councillors - instead of being cut to 24 - after the 2026 municipal election.
"Why change something that works? Just because the feds want to do it, jerry-rig the ridings," said Ford, alleging, without providing evidence, that the independent federal boundary commission gerrymanders electoral districts.
"It's no secret people do that, governments do that, I'm not doing that," he told reporters at an unrelated press conference at Lakeridge Health Oshawa.
"The less politicians, the better it is," said the premier, whose cabinet has 36 ministers, by far the largest in Ontario history.
"I'm going to leave the boundaries alone and people will decide if they want to move forward with our government on prosperity and health care and on the economy - or if they want to go backwards and look for the other guys. They'll have that choice."
Ford's unexpected comments followed a Star report earlier this week that Elections Ontario was awaiting instructions from the legislature on how to proceed on redistribution.
The uncertainty came against the backdrop of Ford considering an early election call next spring, which would cost taxpayers $150 million, one year ahead of the scheduled June 2026 vote.
With the exception of northern Ontario, the province's electoral ridings are based on the Elections Canada map.
Federal boundaries will change for an election expected in October 2025, jumping from 338 electoral districts to 343 across Canada.
That includes boosting Ontario's 121 federal ridings to 122 with the addition of three seats in Brampton, Durham and Milton and the elimination of one in Toronto and another in the north due to demographic changes.
Polls suggest that provincial redistribution would have favoured Ford's Tories, who won 83 of 124 seats in the 2022 election.
The premier's decree clears the air for Elections Ontario, which had warned "Ontario does not have legislation in place for a scheduled review of electoral districts and boundaries."
"Provincial electoral district boundaries in Ontario will therefore remain unchanged until new legislation has passed," the independent arm's length agency told the Star last week.
"The legislature has a choice in how to respond to the new federal electoral boundaries. It could, for example, establish its own commission to determine what the new electoral boundaries would be, it could adopt the new federal electoral boundaries in part or in their entirety, or it could choose to retain the existing 124 electoral boundaries."
Under Ontario's 2017 Representation Act, passed by former premier Kathleen Wynne's Liberals, there are now "111 southern electoral districts whose names and boundaries are identical to those of the federal electoral districts."
Wynne - after a report from the non-partisan Far North Electoral Boundaries Commission - kept 13 northern Ontario ridings instead of the 10 federal constituencies at the time in order to improve Indigenous and northern representation.
There were 124 ridings up for grabs in the 2018 and 2022 elections, up from 107 in 2014, 2011 and 2007.
Ford's intervention ensures that Toronto will not lose the constituency of Don Valley East at Queen's Park. Federally, it's being absorbed into Don Valley West and Don Valley North with the rest merged with Scarborough Centre to create a renamed Scarborough Centre-Don Valley East.
It also means that city council boundaries won't be amended for the 2026 municipal election, sparing Ward 16 (Don Valley East) represented by Coun. Jon Burnside.
But fast-growing Brampton, Durham and Milton won't get the additional provincial representation they will receive federally.
The federal riding of Brampton North has been divided into Brampton North-Caledon, including portions of Dufferin Caledon, and Brampton-Chinguacousy Park, which takes part of Brampton Centre.
And the federal constituency of Durham has been split into two new ridings called York-Durham and Bowmanville-Oshawa North while Milton has been divvied up into Burlington North-Milton West and Milton East-Halton Hills South, including a piece of Wellington-Halton Hills.
CAPTION: Premier Doug Ford takes speaks during a press conference about police helicopter funding in Mississauga on July 29, 2024.

Document 233 of 500

Doug Ford promised to end the King's Counsel controversy. Now he says no one cares

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/doug-ford-promised-end-kings-counsel-controversy/docview/3087402057/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Doug+Ford+promised+to+end+the+King%27s+Counsel+controversy.+Now+he+says+no+one+cares&amp;title=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-08-01&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: You could call it the abdication of the King's Counsel patronage award for lawyers.
One year after promising "to fix the process" surrounding his controversial reinstatement of the ceremonial legal honorific, a defensive Premier Doug Ford conceded Thursday the scheme is effectively on the backburner.
"People don't give two hoots if they have K.C. behind their names," said Ford, who criticized the Star for pursuing "a nothing story."
"The average person, they're worried about health care that we're announcing today. They're very worried about affordability and the economy and crime," he said at Lakeridge Health Oshawa, where he announced there would eventually be a new hospital in Whitby.
"Someone who does work for the government, they get (a) K.C., they aren't getting any more money, they aren't doing anything, it's just something behind their name."
While the title indeed carries no special privileges aside from allowing lawyers to put the initials "K.C." next to their name and wear silk robes to court, the Progressive Conservatives' quiet reviving of the moniker on the eve of Canada Day last year sparked an outcry.
That's because former Liberal premier David Peterson eliminated what was then known as the Queen's Counsel in 1985 because previous Tory governments had "corrupted" the designation.
Peterson, now chair of Torstar, the Star's parent company, said past PC premiers had treated it as a patronage award for loyalists.
"It lost all meaning, and so any principled approach would have been to stop them," the former Grit premier said last July.
"I just think it's unbelievable that they would reinstitute this," said Peterson, a K.C. himself dating back to the early 1980s.
On the Friday afternoon before last year's July 1 long weekend, Attorney General Doug Downey announced 91 lawyers - including Tory ministers, MPPs, PC donors and party loyalists - were receiving the award.
There were no calls for nomination, selection criteria or information about the appointment process prior to Downey's June 30 press release.
But no new K.C. recipients have been named in the 13 months since.
Stung by the fallout, Ford pledged last July "to implement" a nomination protocol.
"Should there have been probably ... another process, yes," the premier said at the time.
However, there is little evidence anything has happened behind the scenes to rectify that.
"There are no updates at this time," Downey's office said in a terse statement Tuesday.
The Star's Jacques Gallant reported last summer that the elected head of the Law Society at the time, Jacqueline Horvat, declined the government's offer of a K.C. due to the lack of selection criteria. However, several Law Society board members accepted the honorific.
Last November, the Advocates' Society, a national organization representing lawyers who appear in court, wrote Downey to say the main criterion for the designation should be professional excellence, not political connections.
"If the designation is permitted to fall into such disrepute again, public confidence in the legal profession will be undermined," wrote then-president Dominique Hussey, chief executive officer of the prestigious law firm Bennett Jones.
CAPTION: Premier Doug Ford speaks to reporters at the Council of the Federation meetings in Halifax on July 15, 2024.

Document 234 of 500

Aug. 1: Leadership needed on gun violence, LCBO plays a beneficial role and other letters to the editor

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/aug-1-leadership-needed-on-gun-violence-lcbo/docview/3086940539/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Aug.+1%3A+Leadership+needed+on+gun+violence%2C+LCBO+plays+a+beneficial+role+and+other+letters+to+the+editor&amp;title=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-08-01&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Downtown becoming 'Wild West'
Re: Safety concerns spur federal tax court's exit from downtown Hamilton, July 27
We see more and more employers leaving the core because of unsafe conditions for their employees. It's turning into a lawless Wild West. Encampments aren't safe for the unhoused, for children or for residents. They're unsafe and unsanitary for everyone.
Advocates can try to minimize the criminality occurring within these encampments, but it doesn't detract from evident drug use, sex trafficking, intimate partner violence, etc.
I invite city council and Mayor Andrea Horwath to come spend an afternoon alongside an encampment.
Blanche Baldwin, Hamilton
Leadership needed on violence
A recent announcement by the Tax Court of Canada of its downtown office closure due to safety concerns has Mayor Andrea Horwath disappointed. However, the disappointment isn't directed toward the continuous gun violence in our city. Instead, she ignores the safety concerns of the staff and public and on CHCH shames the federal government for its decision calling it "problematic" and saying that they need to "take responsibility and be part of the community."
She doesn't offer empathy to those who really feel unsafe being in the downtown core. She doesn't talk about plans, ideas or initiatives to make our community safer - because there are none. I believe it is Mayor Horwath that is "running away." This lack of leadership will only result in further closures.
Unless some action is taken soon, our downtown will soon suffer the same fate as San Francisco. Not even a gold-plated LRT will save it.
Steve Budz, Wionna
LCBO plays a beneficial role
Re: Cheers to Ford's alcohol plans, July 30
Clearly this writer is not thinking of the roughly $2.5 billion that the LCBO provides to the coffers of Ontario for uses in health care and education.
It cannot be compared to the United States or Ireland where there is two-tier health care, nor the Netherlands where care is universal, but citizens must pay an average of 100 Euros per month (roughly $150 Canadian). I will not buy from corner stores and I say thank you to the LCBO workers for taking one for the team.
Marg MacNaughton, Hamilton
Alcohol is already easy to access
Re: Cheers to Ford's alcohol plans, July 30
When I first was legally able to drink, the age limit was 21. There were only two LCBOs in the area; at the Gray/8 mall and the Queenston traffic circle. The stores were open to 5 p.m. during the week and closed on Sunday. The liquor bottles were kept behind glass doors and you had to fill out a form and a LCBO employee would get your order for you.
Fifty years later there are numerous Beer Store and LCBO locations throughout cities in Ontario, many located in shopping malls. Beer and wine is also available in large grocery stores. In small towns, local variety stores are allowed to sell beer and liquor. You can also buy directly from breweries, wineries and distilleries.
I'm not sure how much more convenient it is to shop for alcohol in Europe, but I do know watching the U.S. news variety stores with liquor tend to get robbed a lot there. I also know threatening the livelihood of 9,000 workers and Premier Doug Ford willing to pay over $225 million of taxpayers money to do so is not in the best interest of Ontario, even if it means you have to drive five minutes out of your way.
Buddy Winston, Hamilton
CAPTION: Hamilton Police investigate the shooting of a man in May. Citing safety concerns - including over gun violence and encampments - the Tax Court of Canada has left its downtown Hamilton location. The city needs to show some leadership on these issues, readers say.

Document 236 of 500

Ontario health minister's concerns about Pierre Poilievre's interest in forced drug rehab reveals latest tension between the two parties

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/ontario-health-ministers-concerns-about-pierre/docview/3086992868/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Ontario+health+minister%27s+concerns+about+Pierre+Poilievre%27s+interest+in+forced+drug+rehab+reveals+latest+tension+between+the+two+parties&amp;title=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-07-31&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Ontario Health Minister Sylvia Jones is taking issue with federal Conservative Leader Pierre Poilievre's talk of forcing people with drug addictions into treatment as the country grapples with a deadly opioid crisis.
In another sign of tensions between the provincial and federal Tories, with polls suggesting Poilievre is poised to become Canada's next prime minister, Jones said mandatory rehab is the wrong path.
"I have concerns that involuntary treatment would not lead to the outcomes that we want," Jones said Wednesday at Mount Sinai Hospital.
"But having said that, when we see the opportunity and the need for intervention, and people are willing to take on those treatments to make a difference, that's when we can show them our government is committed."
Jones had been asked about recent remarks from Poilievre, who mulled the concept of involuntary treatment July 25 during a campaign-style stop in London, Ont.
"I don't know if you can take someone off the street that has not committed a prison offence and successfully rehabilitate them. If we can, I'm open to it, but I'd need to see more evidence at this point," he said.
"I need to study it more. I need to understand how it would work. I want everybody who's on drugs to be in treatment and rehab to get off drugs. What I haven't been able to figure out is, if someone doesn't want to be rehabilitated, can you require them to be?"
Poilievre's remarks were made at a news conference in a public park where he criticized a safe opioids supply program at a health centre a few blocks away.
He blamed safe supply strategies for fuelling the addictions crisis and said a Conservative federal government would cut any federal funding for them.
Jones said the province, where Premier Doug Ford has expressed reservations about safe consumption sites, has a new addictions plan coming in which "treatment is a very large portion."
While Poilievre has railed against safe supply programs as "drug dens," proponents say providing users with prescription opioids free of dangerous and potent add-ons in street opioids reduces overdose deaths. But in London, for example, police said some of the safe supply is being diverted to the streets.
Ford's Progressive Conservative government froze approvals of supervised drug consumption sites last fall to conduct a review after 44-year-old mother of two Karolina Huebner-Makurat was stuck and killed by a stray bullet.
She was walking near Queen Street and Carlaw Avenue in east end Toronto when she was caught in the crossfire of a shooting near a consumption site.
The premier has made no secret of his differences with Poilievre.
In a thinly veiled shot at the federal Conservative leader last month, Ford signalled he will "fight like a pit bull" for Ontario's interests if Ottawa acts against them after the next federal election expected in fall 2025.
The wording was telling because Ford has worked closely with the Trudeau government on issues such as health care and providing billions of dollars in joint support for electric vehicle battery plants to create next-generation auto industry jobs.
Sources have told the Star's Robert Benzie that Ford is considering an early election next year to get to the polls before the expected federal vote, fearing a Poilievre government would cut transfer payments to the provinces and cut the cord on Trudeau's electric vehicle strategy.
Ford is also concerned some of his MPPs and cabinet ministers could jump ship to Poilievre's team, following in the footsteps of Parm Gill, the minister of red tape reduction who resigned earlier this year to run federally.
CAPTION: Premier Doug Ford chats with Health Minister Sylvia Jones at Queens Park on Feb. 26 2024.

Subject: Drug overdose; Addictions; Electric vehicles; Narcotics

People: Poilievre, Pierre

July 30: Cheers to Ford's alcohol plans, Jasper fire is just the beginning and other letters to the editor

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/july-30-cheers-fords-alcohol-plans-jasper-fire-is/docview/3086044569/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=July+30%3A+Cheers+to+Ford%27s+alcohol+plans%2C+Jasper+fire+is+just+the+beginning+and+other+letters+to+the+editor&amp;title=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-07-30&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Cheers to Ford's alcohol plans
I recently travelled in the last three weeks. In the U.S., I could buy liquor wherever I wanted. Before the anti-American bashers seek their moral high ground, I travelled also to the Netherlands and Ireland. Again, liquor could be purchased anywhere.
Premier Doug Ford's recent plan to expand alcohol sales into other locations is indeed the thin edge of the wedge. Personally, I am tired of being told where and when I can shop, not to mention sick of subsidizing overpaid, less than helpful glorified stock clerks. Keep it coming, Doug.
Dave Pasian, Ancaster
Don't penalize players for spy scandal
So FIFA penalizes the women's team for something they have no control over? They should penalize Soccer Canada, Canada's dysfunctional governing body for the sport. Why should the women who have worked and dreamed of one day competing for their country have to pay the cost of Soccer Canada's ineptitude?
A better solution would have FIFA reinstate the penalized points in return for Canada forfeiting the 13 games being held in Toronto and Vancouver for the 2026 men's World Cup. At this point, it would be easy to just add the games to the U.S. schedule. Plus, Canadians all save the ridiculous amount of tax money that is being planned for security.
Money is better spent on seeing that every youngster has the opportunity to play a sport, join a team, have fun and maybe be a future Olympian.
Don Robertson, Stoney Creek
Wildfire disaster was predicted
Re: Wildfires send us a clear message, July 27
As is to be expected, the Henny Pennies of the climate change world are demanding action. It's too bad that the action that has been demanded by people in the know who realized and warned years and years ago that unless action was taken to clear the increasing amount of dead wood in the Jasper National Park, a disaster would occur.
Well, it has occurred. And no amount of hand-wringing about fossil fuel will do anything about it.
The deadwood in the forest is being "fired" - the same should happen to the bureaucracy in Parks Canada who ignored the warning about a situation that anyone could see was an increasing threat.
Neville Newman, Hamilton
Jasper fire is just the beginning
Alberta Premier Danielle Smith's tears over the destruction in Jasper, one of Canada's most iconic treasures, won't put out the wildfires. Were they tears of regret, of contrition?
It's time for the irresponsible Smith and the UCP to stop playing politics and get real, and for Alberta's fossil fuel industry to wake up to the realty of climate change, now happening in their backyard.
Jasper is just the beginning. They ignore the warnings at their peril, endangering Albertans and the province's natural and historic treasures.
Wayne Poole, Dundas
Who does the city really care about?
What a myopic view city council has. Let's put bicycle lanes all over the city. Let's also put those cute little traffic lights with the bicycles on them to tell them when they can stop and go because they don't know how to use the grown up light standards. What an additional waste of money.
Now let's limit the disabled in our city by removing the reduced fare assist program for them on our public transit and forcing them off the bus! So, who does the city really care about? Certainly not our less fortunate.
They can't get a plan together for our homeless citizens and now they are taking away the independence of our disabled population. What a shame.
The next election for city council can't come fast enough!
R.J. Kovljenic, Ancaster
Tents don't belong in city park
I grew up on the East Mountain, where I enjoyed skating and other activities at Mountain Park. I recently moved back to the neighbourhood, where I observed five to six tents located at the same park. In recent weeks, the number of tents has doubled, now occupying both sides of the walkway.
I can only describe this area as a tax-free subdivision. I say tax-free because the occupants enjoy better trash collection and other services than the neighbouring residences, who are paying thousands of dollars in property taxes. Additional trash collection and other clean up services are subcontracted by an outside company. Paid for by who?
There are houses for sale on the Mountain Brow and Concession Street. I would venture to say that the growing number of tents nearby must have an impact on the potential sale of these homes. In speaking with neighbours and others walking through the park, they feel intimidated, frustrated and just plain fed up. Walking by this morning, I observed one tent area that appeared to host a party. Garbage, food, beverage cans strewn about.
I do not see anyone utilizing this park in the fashion that I enjoyed years ago.
My one neighbour contacted our councillor and was told nothing can be done. That is not true - there are alternatives to a neighbourhood park being taken over by the tents.
Rick Arnold, Hamilton
Plan ahead to avoid traffic delays
Re: Better traffic planning needed, July 23
It is unfortunate that letter writer Maureen Dwyer missed her show, but how does she know that the travel time on York Boulevard should be 20 minutes?
What if there had been an accident? Would she expect the emergency vehicles to allow her to pass because she wanted to be on time for the show?
Road barriers during construction are put up to protect the workers. They also protect the drivers and their cars from damage from using partially completed roads.
Her idea of removing barriers for the weekend because she, a resident of Burlington, might possibly wish to use the road, would use half a day to open incomplete lanes on Friday and then another half-day after the weekend to restore the protection. A five-week project then becomes a six-week project.
Trying to go to the show via the QEW, accessing it by means of the North Shore ramp, the author is unaware that this is in Burlington. As is the side of the subsequent lift bridge where she was detained. These two places are not under Hamilton's control. Nor are they under Burlington's. The work on the QEW is a provincial project and I believe the lift bridge is a federal project.
When going to a show, one must anticipate the possibility of traffic problems. If there are no delays, then relax and walk around at the venue. Have a snack. Sit on a park bench. Look in the shops. By allowing additional travel time, you will be on time and in a better mood to enjoy the show.
If the author is planning on a cruise, she should get a flight arriving the day prior and not the day of departure. A ship with 1,000 or more passengers will not wait for her because her flight was delayed.
Hans-Peter Boergers, Hamilton
It's naïve to think Biden chose to leave
Re: What's old is news again: The story of Beowulf and Joe Biden, July 26
To have read the poem "Beowulf" in the old English is a feat for any English scholar. To use the story as an analogy of recent events in American politics is disingenuous in the extreme.
A career politician of five decades is far from "a heroic warrior in his country's service." The American president did not "choose" to "yield his place to others." Only the extremely naïve would swallow his fabrication.
Canadians may be fascinated with the politics of our southern neighbour for good reason, but we should not be taken in by false analogies or machinations behind the scenes not meant for the eyes of the ordinary citizen.
Henrietta Wasik, Stoney Creek
CAPTION: Premier Doug Ford is right to make buying alcohol in Ontario more convenient, as so many American states and countries have already done, a reader says.

Document 238 of 500

Province to buy five police helicopters for $134 million: New fleet to help keep highways and roadways safe, Ford says

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/province-buy-five-police-helicopters-134-million/docview/3085841362/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Province+to+buy+five+police+helicopters+for+%24134+million%3A+New+fleet+to+help+keep+highways+and+roadways+safe%2C+Ford+says&amp;title=The+Spectator&amp;issn=11899417&amp;date=2024-07-30&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.7&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Spectator&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Ontario will buy five new police helicopters at a cost of about $100 million more than what the province earmarked for leasing the choppers in this year's budget.
Premier Doug Ford said the province decided to buy the helicopters rather than lease them at the request of several police forces.
"This new fleet of helicopters will be a massive boost for community safety," Ford said on Monday.
"It will help keep our highways and roadways safe from violent carjackings, auto thefts, street racing and impaired drivers."
The province initially said in March it would lease four police helicopters for the Toronto area for $36 million, plus $10 million to operate them.
The Ontario Provincial Police will receive two helicopters to support Ottawa and Toronto police forces. Three other helicopters will go to Durham, Halton and Peel Regional Police services.
A car is stolen every 14 minutes in Ontario, the worst jurisdiction for vehicle theft in North America, Ford said. "That is simply unacceptable," he said.
The province has yet to purchase the helicopters, but plans to do so shortly with the goal of having them in the air by 2026.
Ontario Liberal Party Leader Bonnie Crombie said Ford's government has failed to tackle auto thefts.
"He complains about auto theft and blames everyone - but himself," she said in a statement.
"The truth is that after six years of inaction, Ford isn't interested in fixing what's broken."
Ford also said skyrocketing insurance premiums are largely due to the auto theft crisis in certain parts of the province. Rates are much higher in and around Toronto because of the high auto theft rate.
"If we stop the cars being stolen, the premiums should be coming down," he said.
Two years ago, Ford pledged to end the so-called insurance practice of "postal code discrimination."
Yet on Tuesday, he struck a different tone when asked if he would end the practice via legislation.
"We're going to make sure that we reduce the cars being stolen and work with the Insurance Bureau of Canada, of Ontario, too, to reduce the fees," he said.
He stopped short of pledging a legislative change to bring down car insurance rates for those living in high-priced insurance neighbourhoods and cities.
The Canadian Press
CAPTION: Premier Doug Ford and Deputy Premier Sylvia Jones, right, meet with police officers following a press conference about police helicopter funding in Mississauga on Monday.Frank Gunn The Canadian Press

CREDIT: Liam Casey The Canadian Press

Subject: Automobiles; Theft; Robbery

Document 240 of 500

Doug Ford may be getting ready for an early election - but it's not clear how many ridings Ontario will have

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/doug-ford-may-be-getting-ready-early-election-not/docview/3086111082/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Doug+Ford+may+be+getting+ready+for+an+early+election+-+but+it%27s+not+clear+how+many+ridings+Ontario+will+have&amp;title=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-07-29&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: It is not yet known how many ridings will be up for grabs in the next Ontario election.
Even though Premier Doug Ford is preparing for an early campaign next year, Elections Ontario is still awaiting direction from the legislature on whether there will be 124 constituencies contested as there were in the 2022 and 2018 votes.
"No decision has been made at this time," Attorney General Doug Downey's office said Monday when asked about any changes to the provincial electoral map.
Current public-opinion polls suggest that redrawing the electoral boundaries could be politically advantageous to Ford's governing Progressive Conservatives, who won 83 of the 124 seats in the last election.
Ontario's provincial ridings are largely based on those of Elections Canada - except for some northern constituencies.
Federal boundaries are changing for a national election expected in October 2025 from 338 electoral districts to 343 across the country.
That includes increasing Ontario's 121 federal ridings to 122 by adding three seats in Brampton, Milton and Durham while losing Don Valley East in Toronto and one in northern Ontario due to population changes.
The federal riding of Brampton North has been split into Brampton North-Caledon, which includes part of Dufferin Caledon, and Brampton-Chinguacousy Park, taking in sections of Brampton Centre.
Similarly, the federal riding of Durham has been divided into two new constituencies called York-Durham and Bowmanville-Oshawa North.
The federal riding of Milton has been carved into Burlington North-Milton West and Milton East-Halton Hills South, which includes a chunk of Wellington-Halton Hills.
In Toronto, sections of Don Valley East were absorbed into Don Valley West and Don Valley North and the rest merged with Scarborough Centre to create a renamed Scarborough Centre-Don Valley East.
Because federal and provincial riding boundaries are identical in southern Ontario, Ottawa's changes are being closely watched at Queen's Park, where Ford is considering a vote a year ahead of the scheduled June 2026 election.
But Elections Ontario is hamstrung until MPPs act.
"While the last provincial redistribution followed federal redistribution, Ontario does not have legislation in place for a scheduled review of electoral districts and boundaries," Elections Ontario said in a statement to the Star.
"Provincial electoral district boundaries in Ontario will therefore remain unchanged until new legislation has passed," the independent arm's length agency said.
"The legislature has a choice in how to respond to the new federal electoral boundaries," Elections Ontario added.
"It could, for example, establish its own commission to determine what the new electoral boundaries would be, it could adopt the new federal electoral boundaries in part or in their entirety, or it could choose to retain the existing 124 electoral boundaries."
Under the provincial Representation Act, passed by former premier Kathleen Wynne's Liberals in 2017, there are currently "111 southern electoral districts whose names and boundaries are identical to those of the federal electoral districts."
Concerned about the lack of Indigenous MPPs in the legislature, Wynne's government - bolstered by a report from the non-partisan Far North Electoral Boundaries Commission - ensured there were 13 northern ridings instead of the 10 federal constituencies at the time.
Two-term MPP New Democrat Sol Mamakwa (Kiiwetinong) is the only Indigenous member of the legislature.
Buoyed by recent public and internal polls, Tory insiders believe they could win the rejigged Brampton and Durham seats - though they concede a victory in the May 2 Milton byelection would not necessarily translate into picking up the additional riding there due to demographics.
The disappearance of the riding of Don Valley East, currently held by Liberal Adil Shamji, could be a setback to the Grits.
Don Valley East was one of only seven ridings the Liberals won in the 2018 election and one of eight in the 2022 campaign.
However, a new provincial Scarborough Centre-Don Valley East riding could pit Shamji against Tory MPP David Smith (Scarborough Centre) with boundaries more favourable to the Liberals.
CAPTION: Ontario's provincial ridings are largely based on those of Elections Canada-except for some northern constituencies.

Subject: Election districts; Legislatures; Elections

Location: Canada

Company / organization: Name: Elections Canada; NAICS: 921190

Title: Doug Ford may be getting ready for an early election - but it's not clear how many ridings Ontario will have

Document 241 of 500

IF BONNIE CROMBIE IS PREMIER: Ontario Liberal leader says 'downloading has to stop' on municipalities, will 're-evaluate strong-mayor powers,' bring back co-op solutions for housing crisis: She said she's ready to look at new revenue opportunities for Ontario municipalities, try new approaches on housing crisis

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/if-bonnie-crombie-is-premier-ontario-liberal/docview/3085668976/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=IF+BONNIE+CROMBIE+IS+PREMIER%3A+Ontario+Liberal+leader+says+%27downloading+has+to+stop%27+on+municipalities%2C+will+%27re-evaluate+strong-mayor+powers%2C%27+bring+back+co-op+solutions+for+housing+crisis%3A+She+said+she%27s+ready+to+look+at+new+revenue+opportunities+for+Ontario+municipalities%2C+try+new+approaches+on+housing+crisis&amp;title=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-07-29&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: When it comes to municipal issues and provincial and federal downloading, Ontario Liberal leader Bonnie Crombie knows all about it.
After serving for 12 years as a member of Mississauga municipal council - the last eight as mayor - Crombie has been on the other side trying to deal with steadily increasing demands on municipal budgets due to higher costs for issues like social housing, homelessness, mental health and a host of other complicated problems that were once fully funded at the provincial and/or federal level.
So what would she do about the downloading problem if she became premier of Ontario.
Metroland asked Crombie, who was elected leader in December 2023, but does not sit in the Ontario legislature and has said she'll wait until a general election is called to contest a seat - likely in a Mississauga riding - either in June 2026 or sooner, about downloading, the ongoing housing crisis, strong-mayor powers, and other issues in an interview in Whitby on July 24.
Crombie says downloading has to "stop"
1. You went to AMO (Association of Municipalities of Ontario) conferences a lot as a mayor. I'm sure when you met with provincial ministers you probably asked them about the imbalance in how much municipalities have to pay for downloading and for the amount for services like housing, homelessness, asylum seekers, shelters - both federal and provincial responsibilities. Now, if you become premier and you have a majority Liberal government, will you do something to redress the balance so property taxpayers don't have to pay so much for what are provincial responsibilities?
Crombie: So, municipalities were created at a time when Canada was largely an agrarian country. And municipal budgets were designed to pay for community services like community centres and rinks and splash pads and libraries and then they expanded to policing and public transit. Today, there has been so much downloading that municipalities are forced to deal with complex issues that need a broader strategy like mental health, addiction and homelessness issues, shelters, and asylum seekers, prisoner transport and the downloading continues by the provincial government.
The downloading has to stop but in addition to that, there needs to be a strategy for long-term sustainable funding for municipalities that is different from what it is today because the property tax bill is largely very regressive and is punitive for families and municipalities because they are being forced to cope with so much, like investment in infrastructure and transit and these are very large, costly, aspirational projects, that their (municipal) budgets can't handle.
So, municipalities need other sources of revenue. This is something we will be discussing at AMO with the municipalities that come to visit us. We know the City of Toronto has the Toronto Act which gives them the authority to raise revenues in different ways and now the cities of Toronto and Ottawa have made deals with the provincial government to upload some transit costs, which are a good thing.
There are other cities, like Mississauga, or Brampton, or Hamilton or London, that run large transit services (and) are still suffering because it's very costly on your budget. Police services are very costly on your budget.
2. Would you, as a new premier, do something about strong-mayor powers and bring forward legislation to alter the Strong Mayors, Building Homes Act so that it only applies to provincial priorities and get rid of this situation where mayors are just firing people without any controls like we've seen in Mississauga and Caledon and Windsor and other places? It seems like taxpayers are getting hammered with contracts, and severance packages. Would you do something about that?
Crombie: Very good question. I won't speak of any specific municipality, but it's something we have to have a specific debate about. I look forward to the discussion at AMO. As chair of Big City Mayors, I would hear both perspectives. There were mayors who felt it was very undemocratic, who felt that they were consensus-builders, as I did, and I will reference an example where I did use strong-mayor powers, but those mayors didn't see a reason to use those powers. Other mayors said, you know, we have a dysfunctional council and there's a little block of councillors who always want to seem to oppose the mayor and it's hard for us to get things done according to the agenda that we were voted on. So there were some mixed feelings about it. But now that it's been before us for a year, we see it in action and it is time for a pause and to re-evaluate it, because they (mayors) are given three authorities: one is over budget, one is over hiring of the chief administrative officer and one is over provincial priorities.
When I used it the single time I used it, it was to satisfy provincial priorities to introduce housing and gentle density into communities, and it would have left $120 million in federal financing on the table. As it turned out, I used the strong-mayor powers to bring back the motion on the floor to have another open discussion with council, because the vote had been 5-5, so it fell based on a tie vote. So when I brought it back, with me in the chair, we passed it 8-4. We had some consultation around it to dispel some myths around it. So, I think it just requires consensus building and some leadership.
What will you do specifically?
Crombie: Around AMO we will float it with municipalities. I was chair of the Big City Mayors, and I have since met with the small mayors and small municipalities. The property tax base can't support the kinds of programs residents are demanding, but downloading is a part of that and for instance, there are subtle types of downloading like community contribution for a local hospital. Municipalities, at the expense of their own priorities, have to come up with their own contributions, 10 per cent for local hospitals, and health care is a provincial responsibility.
Liberal leader believes old is new for housing
3. What do you think needs to be done to improve the situation with the housing crisis? What does the federal government need to do, the provincial government need to do, that it isn't doing? Bill 23 has been used.
Crombie: There have been so many bills, we can name them all, 109 and 23, and 185, I don't know, there are five or six. So here's what we need to do, it's very simple. The three levels of government and our partners, the home-building community, which is the development community, need to sit together and work this out. We need incentives to build housing that's affordable, we need incentives for purpose-built rental, we need the government to step up on deeply subsidized. I'd like to see the government back in the co-op business. I remember in the '70s and '80s when the federal government invested in co-ops and how popular they were and how people competed to live in one of those co-ops.
So why is it different today? What's changed?
Crombie: Government has left the building and management of housing. They want to fund it. But we need to have the partners who are the municipalities and the development community at the table to hammer out solutions. The reality is, this is a very complex issue, it's not a simple solution. You have high-cost real estate, you have labour issues, you have supply-chain issues and a shortage of labour, skills talent, a shortage of lumber and access to it, that's starting to be addressed. There are high interest rates, starting to come down. It's very complex. Not one level of government is going to solve this on their own. It's not fair to be villainizing a sector or blaming a level of government for what's not happening. For instance, municipalities are being evaluated on housing starts. Municipalities issue permits. They don't put shovels in the ground. One of the reasons is the development community is sitting on those permits, isn't building. It's complex.
Those issues need to be addressed and we need to sometimes incentivize them better if they will build purpose-built rental or deeply affordable or deeply subsidized housing.
CAPTION: Former Mississauga mayor Bonnie Crombie and mayor-elect Carolyn Parrish after the latter won a June 10 byelection. Asked about strong mayor powers in an interview July 25 in Whitby, and how she would deal with them if elected premier, Crombie did not want to comment about any specific mayor. Parrish has used the strong mayor powers to fire a CAO and hire a replacement.Ontario Liberal Leader Bonnie Crombie was in Whitby Wednesday, July 25, and sat down for a one-on-one interview at Brock Street Brewing Company to discuss a wide range of provincial and municipal issues she'd handle differently as premier.
