Document 252 of 500

MPP Sarah Jama's threat of defamation lawsuit against Doug Ford expires

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/mpp-sarah-jamas-threat-defamation-lawsuit-against/docview/3084675081/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=MPP+Sarah+Jama%27s+threat+of+defamation+lawsuit+against+Doug+Ford+expires&amp;title=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-07-25&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: A threatened $2.5-million libel lawsuit against Premier Doug Ford has fizzled after Independent MPP Sarah Jama and her lawyer failed to serve papers before a mid-July deadline.
An eight-page statement of claim from Jama (Hamilton Centre) was filed with Ontario Superior Court of Justice and dated Jan. 11.
But no further action was taken in the lawsuit, which alleged Ford defamed the former New Democrat MPP in the wake of a statement she made following the surprise Oct. 7 attack on Israel by Hamas and subsequent Israeli retaliation in Gaza.
"The deadline expired and it was never served," Ford lawyer Gavin Tighe said in an email to the Star.
"We offered from the outset, in writing, to accept service. Under the rules, a statement of claim 'shall' be served within six months of issuance," he added.
"That period has now lapsed."
Jama and her lawyer, Stephen Ellis, did not respond Thursday to phone calls and emails seeking comment on the status of the lawsuit.
Ford accused Jama - then a member of the NDP caucus - of "a long and well-documented history of antisemitism" and of "publicly supporting the rape and murder of innocent Jewish people" in an Oct. 11, 2023 statement posted on X, formerly Twitter.
It was a day after she issued a statement on her MPP letterhead calling for an immediate ceasefire in the Israel-Hamas war.
That statement focused on the plight of Palestinians under Israeli occupation and did not mention or condemn the Israeli casualties in the surprise attack by Hamas.
In the statement, Jama described the Palestinian situation as an "apartheid in real time through the continued violation of human rights in Gaza ... for 75 years, violence and retaliation rooted in settler colonialism have taken the lives of far too many innocent people. I call for an immediate ceasefire and de-escalation."
Hours after Ford's statement on X and at the previous urgings of New Democrat Leader Marit Stiles for a retraction of her original statement, Jama issued a clarification and apology.
It stated: "To be clear, I unequivocally condemn terrorism by Hamas on thousands of Israeli civilians. I also believe that Israel's bombardment and siege on civilians in Gaza ... is wrong.
"I understand the pain that many Jewish and Israeli Canadians, including my own constituents, must be feeling. I apologize," her statement continued.
In the statement of claim, Jama called Ford's accusations "false."
Jama also apologized to the Jewish community on the eve of her March 2023 byelection win, when she acknowledged making "harmful" comments about Israel and a "poor choice of words" in a video circulating online.
Stiles ejected Jama from the NDP caucus last Oct. 23 for insubordination on the same day she was censured by a majority vote of Ford's Progressive Conservatives in the legislature, a move that prevents Jama from asking questions of Ford and his cabinet ministers or otherwise speaking in the chamber.
Jama repeatedly declined to comment on her ejection and censure as she left the legislature that day.
In May, a court rejected Jama's push for a judicial review of the censure, saying it had "no jurisdiction to review matters that fall within parliamentary privilege."
Jama called that court decision "disappointing."
CAPTION: Independent MPP Sarah Jama speaks to reporters at Queen's Park on May 6, 2024.

Subject: Court decisions; Defamation; Judicial reviews; Apologies; Truces & cease fires

Location: Gaza Strip; Israel

Company / organization: Name: Hamas; NAICS: 813940

July 25: Premier's commercial was repulsive, how about booze in hospitals and other letters to the editor

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/july-25-premiers-commercial-was-repulsive-how/docview/3084394711/se-2?accountid=15090

Full text: Premier's commercial was repulsive
Re: Don't compensate striking workers, July 23
I'm in agreement with the Spectator reader who feels strongly that bureaucrats and the minister should be removed from their positions for wasting taxpayers' money, just not for his reasons. Premier Doug Ford and the rest of his party should be tossed out of office for creating the LCBO strike and for being willing to spend $225 million of taxpayers' money to break a contract with the Beer Store. The premier's barbecue commercial advising Ontarians where to go for liquor at the beginning of strike was nothing short of repulsive. The LCBO strike was just the latest misstep by a man who claims to work for the people while trying to find ways to line the pockets of his rich, influential friends.
Walt Fisher, Hamilton
How about booze in hospitals?
Perhaps Doug Ford's obsession with having ready-made cocktails more readily available is a bit short-sighted. Further distribution could include hospital emergency rooms and long-term-care facilities. One could then soothe the wait for medical attention, or ease the loneliness of being sent to a facility away from the support of family.
Marlene Radke, Hamilton
Ford continues to fail Ontarians
Doug Fords picks up ideas from various people around him who give him ideas for their benefit and he thinks "What a great idea!" This lacking-higher-education politician, however, fails over and over again drastically at the expense to taxpayers without first doing a cost vs. benefit analysis. He is doing this with the LCBO, as he did with the Beer Store. Look at Greenbelt sprawl, without considering small municipalities' costs for servicing etc. Let's go back to the licence plate stickers. We save a few bucks at the expense of Service Ontario and their employees and the income generated, which also went into health care, etc. It is time for Doug to smarten up, educate himself and stop destroying the various services we are dependent on. I will never vote Conservative again. What a mistake many of us made.
Jo-anne Laframboise, Hamilton
Elect an Indigenous candidate
Re: Hamilton votes down study of Indigenous council seat, July 13
As "persistent" as the group advocating for the study may be, creating or even exploring the idea of a dedicated Indigenous council seat would be divisive and antithetical to the effective government of this municipality. The citizens of Hamilton have had their share of councillor ward heelers, whose narrow vision was counterproductive to the development of the community as a whole. Divisiveness of this sort and the creation of dedicated seat is detrimental to all.
Surely, if the goal is to have Indigenous representation, the "persistence" of this group would be best directed to supporting an Indigenous candidate in any one of the many wards already created in what we hope is an "ambitious" city.
Henrietta Wasik, Stoney Creek
Generosity can still be found
My friend and I just want to say thank you to the lovely gentleman sitting on the bench at Spencer Smith park recently.
We walk regularly along there and stop to enjoy a cup of coffee at our halfway mark. We both realized that neither one of us had a method of payment with us. Pure oversight. A kind gentleman heard us laughing about it and said "Coffee is on me today ladies" and insisted we take $20 and get our coffee with the parting words "happy Monday."
Thank you, kind sir. You validated the fact that among all the chaos of today's world good people and kindness can still be found.
We have both paid it forward by making a donation to the Burlington Food Bank in your honour.
Hope you, too, had a happy Monday!
Catherine Brady, Burlington
We need to stand up against hate
Re: Far-right group Diagolon and its 'terror tour' are 'not welcome,' says Hamilton mayor, July 13
I was disturbed to read that Jeremy Mackenzie, a far-right podcaster and founder of the extremist network Diagolon, is planning to bring his so-called "Road Rage Terror Tour" to Hamilton on July 31.
As Canadians, we pride ourselves on living in a society which is less racially and culturally divided than our American neighbours. However, Hamilton police reported a 26-per-cent increase of hate incidents in 2023, making it the city with the second-highest incidents of hate crimes in Canada after Thunder Bay. In addition, a rise in anti-LGBTQ sentiment indicates we are far less tolerant than we claim.
To prevent the occurrence of this event, Hamiltonians must take a proactive approach by actively opposing all forms of racism and public demonstrations of hate. It is only by doing so, that we can ensure that everyone feels safe and welcome in schools as well as in public spaces in the community. In this case, indifference is not an option.
Andrea Rado, Hamilton
Highway was leased, not sold
Re: Ford's zealous desire to privatize alcohol sales will be costly for Ontario taxpayers, July 12
The dictionary defines privatization as selling a government asset to a private for-profit corporation.
In her column, on July 12, Linda McQuaig says Mike Harris privatized Highway 407 by selling it. Well, Highway 407 was never sold. It was leased and still is. Check Highway 407 and MTO web pages. It says Highway 407 is leased.
When the story broke, media outlets said the highway had been sold. Some said in the same story it had been sold and leased. To correct this, MTO issued a news release saying the highway had been leased not sold. See how much take that got.
She also claims "the selling" by Harris cost us billions. Since the highway is leased, Ontario still owns the land, and the highway will revert at the end of the lease - it has not cost us one cent.
Jim McPeak, Hamilton
Politics a young person's sport
Politics is a very demanding, thankless at times and recently, a dangerous position. The recent announcement of U.S. President Joe Biden not to seek re-election shows that modern politics is indeed for those of a younger age. Candidates now need to weather a constant storm of criticism and dissent from within their own party and externally. Whoever replaces Biden as Democrat nominee for president in the upcoming election will have their work cut out for them to derail the Trump machine, unite a divided nation and show the world that America is indeed not broken.
Mike LoSchiavo, Stoney Creek
CAPTION: The LCBO strike was just the latest misstep by a premier who claims to work for the people while trying to find ways to line the pockets of his rich influential friends, a reader writes.

Document 254 of 500

Federal documents reveal what happened behind the scenes with Toronto's request to decriminalize drugs

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/federal-documents-reveal-what-happened-behind/docview/3084394536/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Federal+documents+reveal+what+happened+behind+the+scenes+with+Toronto%27s+request+to+decriminalize+drugs&amp;title=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-07-25&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: OTTAWA-Health Canada put dozens of questions to Toronto about the practical implications of the city's plans for drug decriminalization, newly released documents reveal, a plan ultimately quashed in the midst of a political firestorm earlier this year.
Records from Health Canada, obtained by the Star under Access to Information law, explain what was happening behind the scenes in the months before the Liberal government rejected the city's application, a move that followed weeks of political pressure during which there were few details about what was going on with Toronto's request.
Against the backdrop of a toxic drug supply killing hundreds of people a year, Toronto had applied for an exemption under the Controlled Drugs and Substances Act in 2022 to decriminalize drugs. Exemptions allow for the personal possession of otherwise illegal drugs, an approach that public health advocates say reduces the harms associated with criminalizing people for personal drug use.
Toronto's proposal, sent by Toronto Public Health (TPH), came after B.C. was granted an exemption and launched a three-year pilot project in 2023.
The documents show Health Canada came back within months of Toronto's application with questions including: asking for more information on how potential risks would be mitigated, why no thresholds were set for possession amounts and about the choice not to place age restrictions on the policy.
Toronto updated its application, answered the questions, provided a letter of support from the police and detailed plans on risk mitigation, communications and monitoring and evaluation for the program.
It wasn't enough to pass muster with Health Canada and the federal agency went back to the city.
"Given the very broad request by TPH (i.e. the inclusion of all youth, all substances in the [Controlled Drugs and Substances Act] and no maximum possession amount), Health Canada continues its assessment of the request and levers available to them in this context," the department wrote in a May 12, 2023 letter.
The two sides continued talking, but email exchanges show in the fall of 2023, Toronto told Health Canada it wanted to pivot its focus onto a broader mental health and addictions strategy, and said talks on the drug decriminalization application could resume in 2024.
By then, B.C.'s program was underway and was facing challenges. It began with a more limited scope than Toronto was seeking - B.C. only asked for certain illicit drugs to be included, placed limits on how much a person could possess, and set age limits.
Then, B.C. scaled back the program even further in the fall of 2023 by putting more controls over where the drugs could be used. But stories began emerging about drug use in hospitals placing people at risk, and police also began voicing concerns that their hands were too tightly tied to address public safety concerns around drug use, even though they did broadly support the approach.
Critics of not just the policy - but also the B.C. and federal government more broadly - seized on the issue as a political pressure point, and began demanding Ottawa and the province reverse course.
In April, B.C. asked Ottawa to further curtail its exemption, and during the ensuing political debate, federal Conservative Leader Pierre Poilievre argued Ottawa must not just agree with B.C.'s request but also reject Toronto's application.
Confusion then followed about the status of Toronto's request - was it just sitting on Addictions Minister Ya'ara Saks' desk unread? Back with the city? Caught in the bureaucratic machine? The Liberal government refused to be clear about it, with Saks referring to the application as dormant, and Health Canada saying they were waiting on information from Toronto, while the city said it had answered Ottawa's concerns.
Meanwhile, Prime Minister Justin Trudeau inferred it didn't matter because if the provincial government wasn't onside, Ottawa wouldn't be either. By this point, Ontario Premier Doug Ford had come out against the idea.
Who Health Canada was corresponding with directly in Toronto is redacted from the records obtained by the Star.
But the records show that as the political hyperbole around Toronto's application hit a fever pitch in early May, a senior Health Canada official met a Toronto official in person, and then followed up with an email.
"Further to that conversation and public statements made, I'm hoping we could find a few minutes to connect in the next day or so," Jennifer Saxe, an associate assistant deputy minister at Health Canada, wrote to a Toronto counterpart on May 13.
On May 17, Saks announced Toronto's application was denied.
CAPTION: Addictions Minister Ya'ara Saks rejected Toronto's request to decriminalize the possession of controlled drugs, citing concerns about public safety.

Subject: Decriminalization; Public health; Addictions; Public safety; Politics; Drug use

Location: Canada

Company / organization: Name: Health Canada; NAICS: 923120

Title: Federal documents reveal what happened behind the scenes with Toronto's request to decriminalize drugs

Document 255 of 500

Loblaws customers' complaints over PC Optimum accounts spark privacy commissioner investigation

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/loblaws-customers-complaints-over-pc-optimum/docview/3084394533/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Loblaws+customers%27+complaints+over+PC+Optimum+accounts+spark+privacy+commissioner+investigation&amp;title=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-07-25&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: The Office of the Privacy Commissioner of Canada (OPC) says it has opened an investigation after receiving "several" complaints against Loblaws from customers alleging that they have not been able to delete their PC Optimum accounts. One of Canada's largest retail loyalty programs, PC Optimum has more than 16 million active annual users, while more than $1 billion worth of points have been redeemed by customers since the program's inception, according to Loblaws' 2023 annual report.The OPC said it could not provide further details about the complaints as the investigation is ongoing. Loblaws spokesperson Catherine Thomas told the Star the company has a process in place to close PC Optimum accounts as requested, "generally within a few weeks and often much sooner than that."During the process, customers will receive an email acknowledging their request, and one confirming the closure. When calls and/or online requests for our customer service team are higher than normal, it may take a little longer for them to respond," she said in an email. "We apologize to anyone who felt the timeline was unreasonable."The move by the privacy commissioner comes after a few rough months on the public relations front for Loblaw Companies Ltd., the nation's largest retailer.In March, the Competition Bureau of Canada opened an investigation into the parent companies of both Loblaws and Sobeys for alleged anti-competitive conduct surrounding leasing agreements that supposedly restrict retail grocery competition from other potential tenants. In June, the bureau said at that time "there is no conclusion of wrongdoing."Then in May, Loblaw stores became the target of a boycott organized by a Reddit group intent on forcing the grocery giant to lower food prices at a time when many Canadians were feeling the pinch of high inflation. PC Optimum was formed in 2018 when Loblaw brought the Shoppers Optimum and PC Plus programs together. The now-popular unified program allows customers to earn and redeem points at nearly 2,500 Loblaw-owned stores across the country, including Shoppers Drug Mart, Real Canadian Superstore, No Frills, Independent, Zehrs, and Loblaws.When it comes to security of personal information online, it is understandable that people who request their accounts and personal data be deleted "would be disappointed and frankly confused by an organization that wasn't able to do that in a very timely fashion," said Charles Finlay, executive director of the Rogers Cybersecure Catalyst, a hub for innovation and collaboration in cybersecurity at Toronto Metropolitan University. "It is important because of course the more that your private data is held by more entities, the more exposed you are to having that personal information released or exploited in the event of a cybersecurity attack against an entity that holds your data."David Young, a Toronto-based privacy lawyer, says that under federal privacy law, organizations are generally required to obtain consent for collection, use and disclosure of personal information and that individuals can withdraw consent at any time, with some exceptions. "That exists under our current law today and ... if these individuals had a complaint with Loblaws, that would be the basis under the law which that complaint will be analyzed by the privacy commissioner," Young said. He added that current legislation, the Personal Information Protection and Electronic Documents Act, or PIPEDA, does not have an express right for customers to force organizations to dispose ofpersonal information, known as "the right to be forgotten," but such a right is contained in the current draft of the new Consumer Privacy Protection Act before Parliament.With files from The Canadian Press

Subject: Cybersecurity; Complaints; Personal information; Customers; Privacy

Business indexing term: Subject: Customers

Location: Canada

Re: Don't compensate striking workers, July 23
I'm in agreement with the Spectator reader who feels strongly that bureaucrats and the minister should be removed from their positions for wasting taxpayers' money, just not for his reasons. Premier Doug Ford and the rest of his party should be tossed out of office for creating the LCBO strike and for being willing to spend $225 million of taxpayers' money to break a contract with the Beer Store. My friend and I just want to say thank you to the lovely gentleman sitting on the bench at Spencer Smith park recently.
We walk regularly along there and stop to enjoy a cup of coffee at our halfway mark. We both realized that neither one of us had a method of payment with us. Pure oversight. Catherine Brady, Burlington
We all need to stand up against hate
Re: Far-right group Diagolon and its 'terror tour' are 'not welcome,' says Hamilton mayor, July 13
I was disturbed to read that Jeremy Mackenzie, a far-right podcaster and founder of the extremist network Diagolon, is planning to bring his so-called "Road Rage Terror Tour" to Hamilton on July 31.
As Canadians, we pride ourselves on living in a society which is less racially and culturally divided than our American neighbours. However, Hamilton police reported a 26-per-cent increase of hate incidents in 2023, making it the city with the second-highest incidents of hate crimes in Canada after Thunder Bay. In addition, a rise in anti-LGBTQ sentiment indicates we are far less tolerant than we claim.
To prevent the occurrence of this event, Hamiltonians must take a proactive approach by actively opposing all forms of racism and public demonstrations of hate. It is only by doing so, that we can ensure that everyone feels safe and welcome in schools as well as in public spaces in the community. In this case, indifference is not an option.
Andrea Rado, Hamilton
Highway 407 was leased, not sold
Re: Ford's zealous desire to
privatize alcohol sales will be
costly for taxpayers, July 12
The dictionary defines privatization as selling a government asset to a private for-profit corporation.
In her column, on July 12, Linda McQuaig says Mike Harris privatized Highway 407 by selling it. Well, Highway 407 was never sold. It was leased and still is. Check Highway 407 and MTO web pages. It says Highway 407 is leased. When the story broke, media outlets said the highway had been sold. Some said in the same story it had been sold and leased.
To correct this, MTO issued a news release saying the highway had been leased not sold. See how much take that got.
She also claims "the selling" by Harris cost us billions. Since the highway is leased, Ontario still owns the land, and the highway will revert at the end of the lease - it has not cost us one cent.
Jim McPeak, Hamilton

Document 257 of 500

Are Chinese-made EVs 'spy machines' that pose an 'invisible threat' to Canada? This expert is raising the alarm

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/are-chinese-made-evs-spy-machines-that-pose/docview/3084493131/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Are+Chinese-made+EVs+%27spy+machines%27+that+pose+an+%27invisible+threat%27+to+Canada%3F+This+expert+is+raising+the+alarm&amp;title=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-07-24&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Canada should move quickly to slap hefty tariffs on Chinese electric vehicles that could be used to spy for Beijing, warns a leading expert on China's booming auto industry.
With Canada widely expected to impose levies on China-made EVs next month, Michael Dunne told the Star on Wednesday that "a connected car is a kind of invisible threat because it is hiding in plain sight - right there in our driveway."
Beyond the trade implications of cheaper Chinese EVs on Canadian roads, Dunne - a former president of GM Indonesia and director of JD Power and Associates in China - said there are profound security concerns due to spyware and malware.
"Some people may think this sounds alarmist, but that's a powerful super computer parked in front of your house," said the San Diego-based CEO of Dunne Insights, a firm with expertise in global EV markets and battery supply chains, and the author of American Wheels, Chinese Roads: The Story of General Motors in China.
With their cameras, the Chinese cars could be used for covert surveillance, collecting data on drivers and their surroundings and even weaponized for co-ordinated cyberattacks.
"These things could be used as mobile spy machines," he said, pointing out that the vehicles could disrupt 5G cell service or Wi-Fi networks.
His comments come against the backdrop of heightened concerns about foreign interference in Canadian politics by hostile states.
Dunne said the Chinese government understands the danger posed by "spy cars" - that's why Beijing strictly limits where U.S.-engineered Tesla EVs are allowed to go in China.
"The Chinese themselves are acutely aware of the risks. They are restricting where Teslas can drive in China," he said, noting the American-designed EVs are barred from many Chinese highways, military installations, airports, prisons and other infrastructure deemed sensitive.
Even Chinese-made Tesla EVs are affected - despite the company promising that all data generated from its vehicles will be stored locally in China.
"There are already 400 million cameras inside China tracking everyone's movements so they don't want (the U.S.) spying on them (with camera-laden American EVs)," said Dunne.
Dan Gittleman, managing partner at SanBoca Insights, an automotive software consultant that advises manufacturers, said the U.S. Commerce Department is considering new restrictions on the EV software imported from China.
"They're looking at barring certain types of software and hardware in any kind of vehicle in addition to the 100 per cent (U.S.) tariff on Chinese EVs," Gittleman said in interview from France.
"But we're playing catch-up," he said.
Canada currently has a six per cent tariff on Chinese EVs, but Ottawa gives buyers - such as those who purchase Teslas or Volvos made in China - a $5,000 rebate.
Prime Minister Justin Trudeau, who has been holding consultations with stakeholders that conclude next week, is expected to soon match U.S. President Joe Biden's stiff tariffs on Chinese EVs.
"We're watching closely what the Americans and other allies have done," Trudeau said in June.
It's been more than two months since Biden boosted American levies on the China-built cars from 25 per cent to 100 per cent.
Premier Doug Ford has been urging Trudeau to do the same in order to protect Ontario's burgeoning EV industry from being swamped by inexpensive imports.
"I'm calling on the federal government to immediately match or exceed U.S. tariffs on Chinese imports, including at least a 100 per cent tariff on Chinese electric vehicles," Ford said June 21.
"Taking every advantage of low labour standards and dirty energy, China is flooding the market with artificially cheap electric vehicles," the premier said at the time.
Dunne said "the 100 per cent tariffs are a showstopper for now, but look for the Chinese to do workarounds" by building their cars in other countries.
He noted that Volvo, a Swedish-based firm wholly owned by China's Geely Holding since 2010, is shifting production of its EX30 SUV to Europe to avoid the 100 per cent duties. American-owned Tesla is expected to supply the Canadian market with more U.S.-made vehicles instead of from its Shanghai factory.
"The tariffs are a good first step, but China has a 10-year head start on (the rest of the world) on EVs ... so we need to sustain that urgency. We are the underdogs."
Trudeau and Ford have earmarked tens of billions of dollars in production subsidies and tax breaks for Stellantis, Volkswagen, Honda and other major manufacturers to invest in Ontario's EV sector.
Flavio Volpe, president of the Automotive Parts Manufacturers' Association, who has worked closely with Ottawa and Queen's Park on an EV strategy that is a cornerstone of Ontario economic policy, said the Chinese threat is real.
"We know the Chinese don't play by the rules - there's evidence of election interference, covert police operations here and the arbitrary detention of the Michaels," he said, referring the three-year incarceration of Canadians Michael Kovrig and Michael Spavor and ongoing concern about Chinese meddling in domestic affairs.
"So to believe that they're going to play by the rules after they've deployed an incredible amount of state-owned technology on Canadian roads is naïve."
China has always denied it interferes in Canada's domestic affairs.
CAPTION: A Sealion car by Chinese electric vehicle manufacturer BYD is seen at the Bangkok International Motor Show in Nonthaburi on March 27, 2024.

Document 258 of 500

Is the King's Counsel dead - again? One year on from patronage scandal, Ford government silent on future of controversial lawyers' title

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/is-kings-counsel-dead-again-one-year-on-patronage/docview/3083998251/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Is+the+King%27s+Counsel+dead+-+again%3F+One+year+on+from+patronage+scandal%2C+Ford+government+silent+on+future+of+controversial+lawyers%27+title&amp;title=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-07-24&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Is the King's Counsel designation for lawyers - revived by the provincial government last year, only to spark a patronage scandal - once again dead?It's been just over a year since Premier Doug Ford promised to "fix" a secretive process that saw the awarding of the K.C. title to dozens of Tory cabinet ministers, MPPs, political staff, donors and other party loyalists.Amid public outcry, Attorney General Doug Downey's office said last July that, going forward, there would be a public application process to receive the designation, which does little else but grant lawyers the privilege of adding the letters K.C. next to their names.Despite legal organizations providing Downey with suggestions, there is still no sign of a new process, or even any public indication that the government remains interested in handing out the designation.Downey's office did not return the Star's repeated requests for comment this month."It would be nice if the government for once could admit that they made a mistake and acted without all the information," said Ottawa criminal defence lawyer Michael Spratt."Or, if you think that the K.C. program is such a vital and important core function that you need to be involved in," Spratt said of the government, "maybe follow through with what you're going to do and tell the truth about what your intentions are."In 1985, the Liberal government of David Peterson stopped handing out the designation - when it was known as Queen's Counsel during the reign of Queen Elizabeth II - because it had become "corrupted" and "lost all meaning," Peterson told the Star last year. (Peterson, a K.C. himself, is the chair of Torstar, the Star's parent company.)The backlash was swift after the Ford government announced the first 91 recipients last year on the Friday afternoon of the Canada Day long weekend. While many individuals were closely linked to the Tories - including Downey himself and his predecessor, Caroline Mulroney - new K.C.s also included senior bureaucrats and the head of the Ontario Human Rights Commission.Further controversy followed after the Star revealed that Mulroney, who was Transportation Minister at the time, was called to the Ontario bar just three days before the government's announcement, in order to qualify for her K.C. Until that point, Mulroney had only ever been licensed to practise in New York, even during her time as Ontario's attorney general.She was called to the bar thanks to an amendment the Tories made to the Barristers Act that allowed for former provincial attorneys general to bypass the usual licensing requirements of the legal regulator, the Law Society of Ontario, including examinations. The elected head of the Law Society at the time, Jacqueline Horvat, told the Star that she declined the government's offer of a K.C. due to the lack of selection criteria. (Several Law Society board members did, however, accept the offer.)The issue dogged Ford for days, as he tried to distance himself and declare that he did not see a list of recipients. He finally stated that the government would "fix" the process."I still think there are legitimate questions to be asked about the whole idea of the government being involved in designating a class of 'super-lawyers,'" said University of Ottawa law professor Amy Salyzyn, who specializes in legal ethics."But, if the government decides that it must go down this road, having public and non-partisan criteria and processes are clearly in the public interest."There has been no shortage of suggestions on how to improve the process.The Advocates' Society, the national organization representing lawyers who appear in court, told Downey in a letter last November that the primary criterion for the designation should be professional excellence, and that the government should explicitly state that a lawyer's political affiliation will play no role in whether they get a K.C.A public nomination process should involve an independent, non-partisan advisory committee to screen applications, the society said, noting that similar committees in other provinces include judges, lawyers and sometimes members of the public.The society referred in their letter to the patronage concerns that led to the Liberals' decision to stop handing out the title in the 1980s. "If the designation is permitted to fall into such disrepute again, public confidence in the legal profession will be undermined," wrote then-president Dominique Hussey.The Federation of Ontario Law Associations also pushed for a depoliticized process, and suggested to Downey that priority should be given to lawyers "who have applied their skills in the course of reputable civic engagement," with a special emphasis on volunteer work.The federation also proposed that lawyers in certain positions automatically receive the title, including MPPs, MPs, heads of tribunals and senior judges.The federation's chair told the Star that he's surprised at the lack of clarity from the government one year later, having heard nothing aside from an acknowledgement that the federation's proposals were received."Maybe they looked at everything they received and said 'there isn't really support in the legal community for this, and maybe we should move on,'" federation chair Douglas Judson told the Star."But I'd be surprised they wouldn't just come out and say that."
CAPTION: Ontario Attorney General Doug Downey is seen here in an Oct. 24, 2023, file photo.

Document 259 of 500

Doug Ford adds to confusion surrounding plans for Ontario Place's contentious parking garage

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/doug-ford-adds-confusion-surrounding-plans/docview/3083998244/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Doug+Ford+adds+to+confusion+surrounding+plans+for+Ontario+Place%27s+contentious+parking+garage&amp;title=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-07-24&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: As city and provincial officials start to negotiate the fate of one of the most controversial parts of the Ontario Place remake, the premier is suggesting a contentious new option for creating thousands of parking spots at the treasured waterfront site.
Doug Ford's government originally suggested a multi-level underground lot at Ontario Place to accommodate motorists visiting a planned new spa resort and expanded Budweiser Stage concert venue.
Last fall, as part of the new financial deal for Toronto, Ford agreed to put the lot at neighbouring city-owned Exhibition Place, subject to negotiations between city and provincial representatives. Visitors to a relocated Ontario Science Centre would also use the lot.
Asked Monday if he is rethinking an underground lot, given the recent massive flooding of Lake Shore Boulevard that separates Ontario Place and Exhibition Place, Ford seemed to float what would be the most controversial option yet - an above-ground lot near the Ontario Place waterfront.
"We haven't confirmed the three underground parking (levels) because it costs so much to build underground," Ford said. "We'd like to build as much on top without prohibiting the view," he told reporters. "Stay tuned on the parking lot. We're working with the city as well - maybe we'll put it on the other side."
Ford's comments injected further uncertainty into a key element of the redevelopment plan that has been shrouded in secrecy. Even with early construction underway at the site, the location, size, cost and form of the parking facility remain undecided.
The idea of a towering new parkade on the provincially owned site is being panned by Ontario Place for All, an activist group fighting Ford's plans to grant Austrian spa company Therme a 95-year lease. The group argues the shuttered amusement park should remain fully public and as natural as possible, including Lake Ontario views.
"An overground parking lot would be an absolute blight at Ontario Place," said the group's co-chair Norman Di Pasquale.
"Imagine a five-storey lot, maybe with the Science Centre on top? You're not going to be able to see the pods or the Cinesphere at all," he said, referring to the site's landmark buildings. "We oppose any new parking structure there."
The premier's newly expressed aversion to the cost of burying a parkade near the waterfront could be a sticking point in the city-provincial talks on the parking lot that are just getting underway as part of the "new deal" agreement announced by Ford and Mayor Olivia Chow last November.
Two sources with knowledge of the talks, but not authorized to speak, said provincial officials seem to want the big provincially funded parkade - if it's built at Exhibition Place - to be above ground.
But the city's guiding principles, as decided by the Exhibition Place board chaired by Deputy Mayor Ausma Malik, state that the parking solution must "be completely underground to ensure highest and best use of land."
In a statement, Malik said the province must front not only construction costs if it is built at Exhibition Place but also "long-term costs such as the impact on Exhibition Place and CNE operations."
Any new garage must also not hurt the city's revenue generation from existing parking lots or Toronto's ambitious target to become a carbon-neutral city by 2040, in part by encouraging alternatives to driving.
Ford's office, asked for an update on plans for the lot, said only that city-provincial discussions are at a "preliminary" stage and no decisions have been made.
A spokesperson for Chow, who agreed to stop fighting Ford's Ontario Place redevelopment plan as part of the new deal, and who welcomed the proposal to put the parkade at Exhibition Place, said: "There are many options and outcomes for the Ontario Place garage. Talks are ongoing between city staff, the province and our office."
The parking plans have been shrouded in mystery since Ford announced the surprise redevelopment in 2021. Early talk of the lot being built deep underground on a man-made island for private-sector tenants, potentially costing taxpayers hundreds of millions of dollars, triggered fierce criticism.
Ontario's auditor general pierced provincial secrecy on the plans last December, revealing in a report that the province's agreement with Therme requires Ontario to provide the firm with dedicated parking spots within 650 metres of the spa entrance or face a financial penalty.
The spa, concert venue and Science Centre are expected to require 2,700 parking spots, or 2,000 more than currently exist at Ontario Place. Ontario's infrastructure ministry pegged the cost of building the new spots underground at $307 million.
The auditor general found that the requirement to build new parking was one reason behind the Ford government's belated decision to move the Science Centre to the waterfront from its existing Don Mills site, "in order to dispel public/stakeholder concerns relating to cost and impact on the environment."
Ford defended the relocation on Monday, saying: "We're building a brand spanking new state-of-the-art (facility), more exhibit space in the right location - not in a sleepy little neighbourhood in the suburbs that no one goes to.
"(Downtown) is where all the action is - where the soccer is, and the stadium and LiveNation, the Exhibition - so it's just common sense, a no-brainer."
It's Ford's comments that lack brains, the co-chair of Save Ontario Science Centre told the Star in an email.
Jason Ash said that more than 50 million Ontarians who have visited the interactive science museum since it opened in Flemingdon Park in 1969 "wholeheartedly disagree" with the premier.
Ash called the neighbourhood "one of the most densely-populated neighbourhoods in Toronto, and in the country," with an additional 27,000 homes planned or under construction.
"Inclusive of Mr. Ford's two subway expansions, Flemingdon Park is attracting a lot of investment for a sleepy little neighbourhood in the suburbs that no one goes to."
With files from Robert Benzie
CAPTION: Even with construction underway at Ontario Place, the location, size, cost and form of its new parking lot remain undecided.

Document 260 of 500

Premier urges BoC to cut interest rate: Ford wants figure slashed down to 3% to help build homes

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/premier-urges-boc-cut-interest-rate/docview/3083835874/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Premier+urges+BoC+to+cut+interest+rate%3A+Ford+wants+figure+slashed+down+to+3%25+to+help+build+homes&amp;title=The+Spectator&amp;issn=11899417&amp;date=2024-07-24&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.9&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Spectator&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Amid concerns about Ontario's housing crisis, Premier Doug Ford is stepping up calls for the Bank of Canada to cut its key lending rate Wednesday.
"People in Ontario and across Canada need help," Ford said on social media Tuesday.
"The best way to boost the economy and give people stuck with sky-high mortgage rates some relief is for the Bank of Canada to cut interest rates again tomorrow. They need to get this done," the premier said.
Ford's latest intervention comes as the Bank of Canada is widely expected to lower its key overnight lending rate for the second consecutive time Wednesday as the economy appears to be slowing.
Analysts believe the central bank will cut the rate to 4.5 per cent from 4.75 per cent.
But the premier, who has been publicly lobbying Bank of Canada governor Tiff Macklem for months to slash the cost of borrowing, said rates must be even lower to jump-start housing construction.
"I can't help myself with this but the Bank of Canada, they need to continue cutting these rates," Ford told reporters Monday in Kitchener.
Blasting the bank as an "antiquated group ... living back in the 1970s," the premier said drastic action must be taken.
"Folks, you want to build homes? We get the Bank of Canada to knock the interest rate down to about three per cent, you'll see them pop up like mushrooms everywhere," he said.
That would lead to developers borrowing additional money to build homes and more buyers getting mortgages to afford them, Ford said. Prior to last month's cut, the Bank of Canada raised rates 10 times - from 0.25 per cent in March 2022 to five per cent in July 2023.
"I encourage the governor of the Bank of Canada (to) get his act together and start lowering interest rates at a lot faster pace - just like you did when you raised the interest rates 10 times (at a) record speed," the premier said.
"It's easy to raise things but we need to start moving on that," he said.
The bank's rationale for increasing rates was to slow the economy to reduce inflation - because making it more expensive to borrow leads to less spending, driving down prices. According to Statistics Canada, inflation was at 2.7 per cent last month, down from 2.9 per cent in April.
CAPTION:

Document 261 of 500

LCBO stores reopen in Ontario today; here's where you can buy and strike details

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/lcbo-stores-reopen-ontario-today-heres-where-you/docview/3084061795/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=LCBO+stores+reopen+in+Ontario+today%3B+here%27s+where+you+can+buy+and+strike+details&amp;title=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-07-23&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Union members with Liquor Control Board of Ontario (LCBO) stores are back on the job today (July 23) following a two-week strike.
It was the first time members have been on strike, but a new, three-year collective agreement was ratified by employees on Monday.
"In the new contract, there is a guarantee of no store closures and a cap on the number of agency stores (akin to private liquor stores)," the Ontario Public Service Employees Union (OPSEU) said on its website. "Most importantly, there is a shared commitment to a future in which the LCBO, and its revenues, continue to grow with Ontario."
"As we welcome back our valued employees, we are focused on returning to our normal operations in support of our retail and wholesale customers and of Ontario's beverage alcohol industry," the company said on its website.
Back in April, LCBO union members voted 93 per cent in favour of going on strike.
After providing a few days of prior notice to the public and extending store hours, the strike began at midnight on Friday, July 5 as more than 9,000 LCBO workers hit the picket lines throughout the province.
Ten days later, on Monday, July 15, Premier Doug Ford announced he was expediting his timeline to allow licensed grocery stores to order ready-to-drink beverages and large beer pack sizes, with sales starting later that week.
He also shared a specific webpage on Ontario.ca that included a map for LCBO patrons to search areas where they could still purchase beverages from agencies like The Beer Store, local breweries and wine shops.
Also announced July 15, LCBO shared it wouldn't be temporarily opening 32 selected locations Friday, July 19 as initially planned for restaurants to restock. It said all stores would remained closed through the strike.
Then, on Tuesday, July 16, LCBO took out full-page newspaper ads and 30-second broadcast spots, explaining its side of the labour dispute. OPSEU had released its own satirical YouTube video months earlier, criticizing the privatizing of alcohol sales.
Talks between both sides resumed Wednesday, July 17 as they worked to find a new contract.
Select grocery stores started selling ready-to-drink beverages and large-pack sizes Thursday, July 18.
A tentative deal was reached Saturday, July 20, however the union paused the ratification as it raised a new issue, and LCBO said it was going to file an unfair labour practice in response.
The new contract was officially ratified by employees Monday, July 22.
On Sept. 5, licensed convenience stores will be able to sell beer, cider, wine and ready-to-drink beverages.
CAPTION: Unionized LCBO employees are back at work again following a two-week strike.

Document 262 of 500

Doug Ford urges Bank of Canada to cut interest rate

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/doug-ford-urges-bank-canada-cut-interest-rate/docview/3084061599/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Doug+Ford+urges+Bank+of+Canada+to+cut+interest+rate&amp;title=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-07-23&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Amid concerns about Ontario's housing crisis, Premier Doug Ford is stepping up calls for the Bank of Canada to cut its key lending rate Wednesday.
"People in Ontario and across Canada need help," Ford said on social media Tuesday.
"The best way to boost the economy and give people stuck with sky-high mortgage rates some relief is for the Bank of Canada to cut interest rates again tomorrow. They need to get this done," the premier said.
Ford's latest intervention comes as the Bank of Canada is widely expected to lower its key overnight lending rate for the second consecutive time Wednesday as the economy appears to be slowing.
Analysts believe the central bank will cut the rate to 4.5 per cent from 4.75 per cent.
But the premier, who has been publicly lobbying Bank of Canada governor Tiff Macklem for months to slash the cost of borrowing, said rates must be even lower to jump-start housing construction.
"I can't help myself with this but the Bank of Canada, they need to continue cutting these rates," Ford told reporters Monday in Kitchener.
Blasting the bank as an "antiquated group ... living back in the 1970s," the premier said drastic action must be taken.
"Folks, you want to build homes? We get the Bank of Canada to knock the interest rate down to about three per cent, you'll see them pop up like mushrooms everywhere," he said.
That would lead to developers borrowing additional money to build homes and more buyers getting mortgages to afford them, said Ford.
Prior to last month's cut, the Bank of Canada raised rates 10 times - from 0.25 per cent in March 2022 to five per cent in July 2023.
"I encourage the governor of the Bank of Canada (to) get his act together and start lowering interest rates at a lot faster pace - just like you did when you raised the interest rates 10 times (at a) record speed," the premier said.
"It's easy to raise things but we need to start moving on that," he said.
The bank's rationale for increasing rates was to slow the economy to reduce inflation - because making it more expensive to borrow leads to less spending, driving down prices.
In the wake of the COVID-19 pandemic, inflation skyrocketed to 8.1 per cent in June 2022, well above the central bank's target of two per cent.
According to Statistics Canada, inflation was at 2.7 per cent last month, down from 2.9 per cent in April.
Because Ford is pondering an early election next spring - one year ahead of the scheduled June 2026 vote - he is under pressure to demonstrate some success in tackling Ontario's housing crisis.
But his goal to build 1.5 million new homes by 2031 appears to be falling short, with annual housing starts far below the average levels needed to achieve that.
The $8.28-billion Greenbelt land swap scandal, which is now under investigation by the RCMP, has also hindered the province's efforts to get more homes built.
CAPTION: Premier Doug Ford speaks to reporters at the Council of the Federation meetings in Halifax on July 15, 2024.

Subject: Borrowing; Central banks; Interest rates; Housing starts

Document 263 of 500

Green MPPs' summer tour of Ontario finds unhappy voters

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/green-mpps-summer-tour-ontario-finds-unhappy/docview/3083666060/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Green+MPPs%27+summer+tour+of+Ontario+finds+unhappy+voters&amp;title=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-07-23&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: When you are trying to broaden support for the Green Party from a base of two seats in the Ontario legislature, you spend the summer travelling the province, and listening to voters.
Party leader and Guelph MPP Mike Schreiner and Kitchener Centre MPP Aislinn Clancy are taking advantage of the extra-long summer recess from Queen's Park to visit small towns and big cities around Ontario.
Health care, housing and the climate crisis are the top-of-mind issues for voters, said Schreiner.
He's heard about emergency ward closures in rural areas, speculators buying up thousands of acres of land for sprawl-style suburban development, and a small town that can't afford its only swimming pool as residents endure record-breaking temperatures.
"And the Ford government has no plans for dealing with any of these issues," said Schreiner.
A developer from Waterloo Region recently bought thousands of acres of land in the Bruce-Grey area, and nearby residents are worried about the expense of servicing land for new suburbs, said Clancy.
"When we have a sprawl agenda it is more expensive to maintain that kind of infrastructure, it takes longer to build, it's not affordable housing that is being built," said Clancy. "A lot of folks are worried - is this sustainable?"
The Green Party was founded in 1979 in then-West Germany. It united political groups working on disarmament, human rights and the environment. It became an influential political party in several European countries, but has not achieved a broadly-based breakthrough in Canada.
Schreiner and Clancy are the two Green MPPs in Ontario, but both are hopeful about expanding support and winning more seats in the next provincial election, scheduled for June 2026.
Schreiner said he is the only party leader who lives outside the GTA, and he wants the Greens to focus more on rural communities and medium-sized cities.
In the 2022 election, the Green Party candidate in Parry Sound-Muskoka had 40 per cent of the vote.
The climate crisis, encampments of unhoused people, overdose deaths, the increasing costs of buying a house or renting an apartment and the continued loss of farmland combined with changing demographics, could translate into more support for the Greens.
"Aislinn and I did a great event in Markdale, in Grey County, the other night," said Schreiner. "People are just tired of the three other status-quo parties."
Volunteers and candidates are stepping foward now, almost two years before the next election.
"A lot of people in rural communities and urban communities are really worried," said Clancy.
"What I hear from folks is the premier if more focused on helping wealthy, well-connected land speculators cash-in rather than building homes people can actually afford," added Schreiner.
There was enough land zoned for new residential developments within existing urban areas to build two million homes in Ontario, said Schreiner, but the Ford government expanded urban boundaries in several southern Ontario municipalities that will only benefit the speculators and developers who own that land.
In Waterloo Region, the boundary expansions opened 6,400 acres to new suburban development.
"Why is the premier wasting so much time, money and energy focused on opening land for development when we already have enough land approved, let's just get to building homes that people can afford," said Schreiner.
Terry Pender is a Waterloo Region Record reporter. Reach him via: tpender@therecord.com
CAPTION: Guelph MPP Mike Schreiner, leader of the Ontario Green Party, and Kitchener Centre MPP Aislinn Clancy, are touring small communities and cities around the province during the months long recess from the provincial legislature. Housing, health care and the climate crisis are top of mind issues, they say.

Document 264 of 500

LCBO has bright future, Ford says: 'Great quality, great people serving you and at a competitive price,' Premier says as strike ends

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/lcbo-has-bright-future-ford-says/docview/3083464578/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=LCBO+has+bright+future%2C+Ford+says%3A+%27Great+quality%2C+great+people+serving+you+and+at+a+competitive+price%2C%27+Premier+says+as+strike+ends&amp;title=The+Spectator&amp;issn=11899417&amp;date=2024-07-23&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.6&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Spectator&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Ontario's main liquor retailer has a bright future, Premier Doug Ford said Monday as thousands of employees returned to work after a two-week strike.
The Liquor Control Board of Ontario said its stores will be open for business on Tuesday.
"I have confidence in the LCBO," Ford said at a news conference in Kitchener.
"They meet the first three thresholds: great quality, great people serving you and at a competitive price, so we're going to keep moving forward on it."
He also said the LCBO makes most of its money as the primary alcohol wholesaler in the province rather than through its retail stores.
"The fact is, per capita, we make the least amount of money in the entire country to the exception of P.E.I when it comes to alcohol sales," he said.
Ford's previous plan was to get beer, wine and ready-to-drink cocktails in convenience stores and all grocery stores by 2026, completing a 2018 election campaign promise. But in May he announced that would instead happen this year, capping speculation of an early election that Ford did not outright deny.
Convenience stores will be allowed to sell beer, wine and coolers starting Sept. 5 while newly licensed grocery stores can do so starting Oct. 31.
An "early implementation agreement" with The Beer Store involves the province paying the company up to $225 million to help it keep stores open and workers employed. The province is also giving brewers a rebate on an LCBO fee that normally brings in $45 million a year, and it is giving retailers a 10 per cent wholesale discount.
The Ontario Public Service Employees Union said it faced an existential crisis with Ford's plan to open up the alcohol market.
The union representing the LCBO workers had said it believed Ford's plan to expand alcohol sales to convenience and grocery stores would threaten union jobs and the public revenue the LCBO provides to the province.
The union said the sticking point was Ford's plan to allow convenience and grocery stores to sell ready-to-drink cocktails, the fastest growing market within alcohol sales in the province. It was seeking job security, wage increases and better benefits.
Ford admitted ready-to-drink cocktails are fast sellers and make up about nine per cent of the LCBO's sales.
"They can't even keep ready-to-drink on the shelves, so we can see that market growing within the LCBO," he said.
CAPTION:

Document 265 of 500

Ford pledges $260M for skills training: Province boosting fund to help employers fill nearly 300,000 open jobs

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/ford-pledges-260m-skills-training/docview/3083464101/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Ford+pledges+%24260M+for+skills+training%3A+Province+boosting+fund+to+help+employers+fill+nearly+300%2C000+open+jobs&amp;title=The+Spectator&amp;issn=11899417&amp;date=2024-07-23&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.9&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Spectator&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Hindered by a shortage of skilled labour, Premier Doug Ford is earmarking as much as $260 million for employers and unions to train workers.
Ford said Monday he was boosting the province's "skills development fund" in order to help fill some of the nearly 300,000 available jobs in Ontario.
"By continuing to ... make these important investments, we are also tackling Ontario's labour shortage and bringing back good-paying jobs in manufacturing and the skilled trades," the premier told reporters Monday in Kitchener.
Ford said starting next Monday, municipalities, hospitals, private companies, labour unions, community organizations, Indigenous groups and training providers can apply to tap in to the funding to tackle their employment needs.
This latest cash infusion brings to $1.4 billion the amount Queen's Park has spent on skills training subsidies since 2021.
In those three years, the government said the program has helped train nearly 600,000 people, including 36,000 health care and personal support workers, some 66,000 in construction and more than 92,000 in manufacturing.
Ford said the money is "helping connect workers here in Kitchener and across the province to better jobs and bigger paycheques."
Karen Renkema, Ontario vice-president of the Progressive Contractors Association of Canada, said the funding "has been a game changer for the construction industry."
"The funds have enabled innovative training partnerships, pathways and programs that have served to attract and support many new participants into the skilled trades," said Renkema.
One of the many challenges Ford's government has had trying to get housing built in Ontario is the dearth of skilled tradespeople.
The premier has promised to have 1.5 million new homes built by 2031, but housing starts are falling well short of his targets.
CAPTION: Premier Doug Ford said starting next week, municipalities, hospitals, private companies, labour unions, community organizations, Indigenous groups and training providers can apply to tap in to the funding to tackle their employment needs.

CREDIT: Robert Benzie toronto star

Document 266 of 500

Doug Ford says the 18-day LCBO strike could have been avoided

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/doug-ford-says-18-day-lcbo-strike-could-have-been/docview/3083726992/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Doug+Ford+says+the+18-day+LCBO+strike+could+have+been+avoided&amp;title=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-07-22&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: As liquor stores reopen Tuesday after an 18-day strike, Premier Doug Ford says the first job action in LCBO history could have been avoided.
"We learned many things with this strike - people were still able to get their alcohol ... don't mess around with people's booze in Ontario, because they aren't too happy," Ford told reporters Monday in Kitchener.
"This strike should have never ever happened and we could have easily sat around the table," the premier said, blaming Ontario Public Service Employees Union (OPSEU) brass for the stoppage.
More than 9,000 unionized workers hit the bricks on July 5, shuttering 680 Liquor Control Board of Ontario outlets and forcing consumers to buy from other retailers, including the 450 grocery stores licensed to sell beer and wine by former Liberal premier Kathleen Wynne in 2015.
It was Ford's decision two months ago to spend $225 million to widen Wynne's liberalization to 8,500 supermarkets, convenience stores and big box outlets - and allow the expanded sale of ready-to-drink beverages - that spurred OPSEU to strike the LCBO for the first time since its creation in 1927.
The union felt that losing the LCBO's monopoly on selling premixed drinks, such as Mike's Hard Lemonade and Dillon's gin cocktails, would undermine the future of the Crown retailer - even though it will still enjoy exclusive rights to spirit sales.
After a settlement that included eight per cent raises over three years, giving full-time jobs to 1,000 part-time employees and guarantees the LCBO would retain its existing retail footprint and cap the number of private agency stores, OPSEU president JP Hornick insisted "this fight is far from over."
"Ontarians are more aware than ever that Ford's plan isn't for them, it's for big box CEOs. We have no doubt that Ford's webs of corporate buddies and corrupt backroom deals will continue to be exposed," Hornick said Sunday.
The premier, who gives out his cellphone number to the public, maintained he "was able to connect with over 500 people" in the union through the more than two-week strike.
"I can tell you 98 per cent were super, super nice, they were understanding and all they kept saying is, 'I just want to go back to work,'" said Ford.
"You get the odd nasty call but that goes with anything, but the vast majority were super people and they just want to earn a paycheque," he said.
"I've always said I differentiate between labour and labour leadership. I support the frontline labour folks that work their backs off and I differentiate between public- and private-sector unions. I love the private sector and I get along well with some public-sector unions..."
Colleen MacLeod, chair of the OPSEU bargaining team, said her members "won" because they "stood strong" and "talked to their communities" about what was at stake.
"We went on strike to protect good jobs and public revenues and to win more permanent jobs with benefits and guaranteed hours," said MacLeod.
"In my 27 years at the LCBO, the employer has continuously casualized the workforce, so that people wouldn't get guaranteed hours, benefits or any hope of permanent work. Permanent part-time nearly went extinct," she said.
"I am beyond proud that we fought back and won these permanent jobs - it will improve the lives of workers and their families for many years to come."
Beer and wine and - as of this week - ready-to-drink beverages are available in the 450 licensed supermarkets with the thousands of other retailers expected to be stocking booze by Oct. 31.
Internal Progressive Conservative government polling obtained by the Star last week concluded there was significant support for liberalized alcohol sales even though 54 per cent backed the OPSEU strike while 32 per cent opposed it and 14 per cent had no opinion.
The Campaign Research survey suggested the job action had little impact on the public with about three-quarter of respondents - 73 per cent - saying it was not "inconvenient" while 28 per cent said it was.
Some 69 per cent backed Wynne's liberalization and 57 per cent supported Ford's expansion while 61 per cent favoured more availability of premixed drinks.
Campaign Research surveyed 1,995 Ontarians July 12-13 using the Maru Blue online panel. While opt-in polls cannot be assigned a margin of error, for comparison purposes, a random sample of this size would have one of plus or minus two percentage points, 19 times out of 20.
Ford's Tories were at 41 per cent to 26 per cent for Bonnie Crombie's Liberals, 18 per cent for Marit Stiles's New Democrats and 10 per cent for Mike Schreiner's Greens.
That's similar to last month's Abacus Data tracking poll for the Star, which had the PCs at 41 per cent, the Liberals at 25 per cent, the NDP at 22 per cent and the Greens at eight per cent.
CAPTION: Premier Doug Ford walks past reporters after arriving late for a meeting between Canada's premiers and Indigenous leaders at the Council of the Federation meetings in Halifax on July 15, 2024.

Subject: Beverages; Liquor; Strikes; Liberalization; Employment; Supermarkets; Part time employment

Document 268 of 500

Chocolate biscuits, chicken bites and fruit pouches succumb to shrinkflation at Metro, No Frills and Walmart, says new report

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/chocolate-biscuits-chicken-bites-fruit-pouches/docview/3083200809/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Chocolate+biscuits%2C+chicken+bites+and+fruit+pouches+succumb+to+shrinkflation+at+Metro%2C+No+Frills+and+Walmart%2C+says+new+report&amp;title=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-07-22&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Your eyes aren't deceiving you, grocery store package sizes have shrunk in some areas - particularly for products with higher sugar and/or fat content.
According to researchers with communityresearchers.org, 11 out of 20 categories from Canada's Food Guide experienced the highest 'shrinkflation' from 2019 to 2024.
Researchers used old store flyers to compare package sizes from Metro, No Frills and Walmart stores.
Although prices were not listed, the term shrinkflation typically suggests package size is smaller, while the price remains the same or increases.
Products with shrinkflationTreasure Mills' School Safe brownies went from 320g to 296g at Metro.Our Finest chocolate biscuits went from 1,000g to 800g at Walmart.Gogo Squeez fruit sauce pouches went from 1,400g to 1080g at No Frills.Janes chicken bites went from 900g to 710g at Metro.Great Value smoked ham went from 1,700g to 1,400g at Walmart.Crisco vegetable oil went from 1,420ml to 1,180 mL at Metro.Products with no shrinkflation
Fairlife lactose-free milk stayed at 1,500 mL at Walmart.
Green Branch bok choy stayed at 1,890g at Metro.PC Organic gala apples stayed at three pounds at No Frills.Sabra hummus remained at 283g at Walmart.No Name trail mix stayed at 1,000g at No Frills.Irresistibles cranberry cocktail stayed at 1,890ml at Metro.
The study overall found that beverages, dark green vegetables, fruit, nuts and seeds, seafood, chips and whole grains did not experience shrinkflation in the last five years.
To see the full report, visit communityresearchers.org.
CAPTION: A recent study compared package sizes for 20 items on Canada's Food Guide over the last five years, looking for 'shrinkflation'.
