Document 282 of 500

Ontario's rushed planning leaves little time for residents to weigh in

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/ontarios-rushed-planning-leaves-little-time/docview/3082291611/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Ontario%27s+rushed+planning+leaves+little+time+for+residents+to+weigh+in&amp;title=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-07-18&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Tuesday marked the last regular Burlington council meeting until September - but there's one in August to meet Premier Doug Ford's tight deadline for planning applications. Otherwise cities now must refund application fees.
It is another change that has cut out the "middle man": citizens.
Burlington prides itself on soliciting public input on development proposals. Years ago planning reports contained recommendations. Residents asked, "Why ask our opinion if you've already made up your mind?" The process was changed, seeking input first, then making a recommendation report.
Now, with the clock ticking on Ford's mandatory timelines, it's changed again. The "statutory" meeting - required under the planning act - has a recommendation. Council asks developers to host public meetings early - and most accede to the request. The city seeks written input early, too, but now the "stat meeting" is the public's only chance to speak to committee and council. Again, there's a recommendation in the report.
There are many projects approved, but few underway, mainly because of interest rates. So cities "hurry up and wait," and residents feel rushed. Public consultation is not a high priority for the province, whose vast planning changes have upended a previously enviable system that allowed proper time to tackle public concerns.
There were two "stat meetings" last week, with approvals granted by council this week. One sought minor changes to the Paradigm condo project underway near the Fairview GO station. The other sought six townhouses on a small site on Plains Road near Shadeland. The willingness of the consultants and developers to resolve concerns is worthy of note, and these two have done it. The city has co-operative developers and consultants, and others.
Ed Fothergill is the planning consultant on the former project, and the Molinaro Group is a frequent city developer. On the latter, Martin Quarcoopome (Weston Consulting) is the planner and Albert Faccenda (a small local builder) is the developer. A big issue with the townhouses is fear of losing the huge, healthy trees on-site, and/or damaging their root systems and those of neighbours. Faccenda plans to preserve them and their presence helps the marketability.
Height is also an issue. The townhouses are four storeys, backing onto a two-storey condo enclave. They're building "missing middle" units, he said, and the fourth storey is intended only as outdoor space. Compromises were offered on ways to make it less intrusive for neighbours.
Council approved it, with modifications yet to be finalized. Council also approved 20-page terms of reference for a community improvement plan (CIP). When completed it will open the door to federal and provincial dollars to assist in providing affordable, assisted and special-needs housing. The total cost of the actual CIP will range from $150,000 to $200,000.
Burlington Hydro offered an update on its distribution system capability, with timelines, to accommodate the dramatic growth expected. Seasonally, summer sees the highest hydro use, but that is expected to shift to winter, mainly because of increased use of heat pumps and electric vehicles (EV). In the near term, it will expand feeder lines. After 2030, mainly because of EV growth, the system will need upgrading.
Likely the agenda item that affects the most people is the city's new zoning, being drafted now. I'm learning to hate the word "flexible" - shorthand for "anything goes" in today's Ontario - and that word is writ large in the current thinking of the city's consultant. It's fine for developers, but what about residents who want some certainty about neighbourhoods they're buying into when they put their life savings on the line? That's going, going, gone! Consultants spoke of using few zones, with lots of flexibility. Imagine the fun the Ontario Land Tribunal will have interpreting a loosey-goosey zoning bylaw. It already grants most developers' wish lists.
Freelance columnist Joan Little is a former Burlington alderperson and Halton councillor. Reach her at specjoan@cogeco.ca.
CAPTION: New pressures on planning timelines from Premier Doug Ford's government have cut out opportunities for residents to give their opinions, writes Joan Little.

Document 283 of 500

What tone will Doug Ford strike as the new spokesman for Canada's premiers?

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/what-tone-will-doug-ford-strike-as-new-spokesman/docview/3082291608/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=What+tone+will+Doug+Ford+strike+as+the+new+spokesman+for+Canada%27s+premiers%3F&amp;title=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-07-18&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Listening to Doug Ford and his fellow premiers shedding a few tears in Halifax this week, you'd think the world - and the country - were coming to an end.
With predictable passion, they rattled off a list of perennial perils:
Federal interventionism is high. American protectionism is nigh. Asylum-seekers are on the rise. Equalization is inequitable.
Feasting on seafood, washed down with sour grapes while sharing a few private laughs, the premiers used their annual conference to toast one another for prudent stewardship of their own provinces. While raising the alarm about everyone else:
With a Donald Trump presidency looming, Canadians face renewed risk. With Justin Trudeau's prime ministerial reign lagging, Canadians face prolonged risk.
Climate change is in the air, but most of the premiers called for the federal carbon levy to be buried. Federal meddling in provincial jurisdiction is intolerable, yet the premiers cheerfully waded into Ottawa's bailiwick by demanding more defence spending and cuts to interest rates (by the independent Bank of Canada).
Ottawa-bashing (and hectoring) is apparently part of every premier's job description. But what if it's your turn to serve as leader of the pack - not merely as one premier among 13, but the one who represents them all?
What then? What tone might you strike, what topics would you like?
For Ford, that challenge is now at hand. For the next year, he will hold the gavel as chair of the Council of the Federation (as this parley of provinces and territories is called).
Next July in Ontario, the premier will convene his counterparts to frame a national agenda for collaboration in the Confederation. Can Ford rise to the challenge?
To be sure, there's no certainty he will still be premier, given his private (now public) fantasy of springing an early election on Ontario next spring. Even if one assumes he wins re-election - his Progressive Conservatives enjoy a commanding lead in the polls - there's no guarantee Trudeau will still be prime minister, leaving him a sitting duck for quacking premiers.
As Ford mused in an interview with my colleague Kristin Rushowy, he knows little of Pierre Poilievre, the federal opposition leader whose Conservatives are far ahead in the polls. If Poilievre wins power before the next annual summit in Ontario, there's no assurance Ford or his fellow premiers would be any kinder toward the new prime minister.
But situational politics, like situational ethics, is a sometime thing. Ford has always aspired to have a national voice, so if he wants to make his mark as a leader among premiers, he needs to have something more enduring to say.
At the podium in Halifax on Wednesday, Ford couldn't resist taking a shot at the federal government for the allegedly costly way it delivers social programs for low-income Canadians, which leaves the premiers "pissed." But as a populist, Ford knows better than to think Canadians care for these traditional jurisdictional squabbles.
Give him credit for trying to be constructive. Ford travelled to Halifax armed with a proposal to speed up approvals for life-saving drugs, and placed it squarely on the agenda for the next year.
All that said, the fastest way to get the green light for new medicines would be through a national pharmacare program that integrates the approval, procurement, pricing and distribution processes across all boundaries - bureaucratic and geographic. Ford has been hostile to pharmacare since taking power, targeting the embryonic OHIP+ program developed by the previous Liberal government to deliver universal coverage to people up to age 25 or 65 and older (the program now helps only those young people without private benefits while seniors still get free drugs).
A little policy coherence on this growing health-care contradiction - a free, universal medicare program without free medicines for all - would make Ford sound more convincing on the national stage as he takes on the challenge of being chair. Pharmacare is no longer about ideology, it's about efficiency and equity.
Where Ford might truly make his mark, however, is not so much on national issues but bilateral problems. If Trump wins the November election and the Republicans are ascendant, the premier will have his work cut out for him; even if the Democrats somehow hold on, protectionist impulses will surge.
Ford is planning a trip to Washington in the new year, after the January inauguration. He will have limited influence as a premier - even one who speaks for all premiers - but he is well placed to lobby on our behalf.
Perhaps it takes a populist to understand a populist. More than that, it takes someone who has lived in America to comprehend the lived American experience (Ford lived in Chicago for many years running a branch of the family's printing business).
As an expatriate Canadian, Ford understood how inward-looking the American outlook can be. Now as an incoming chair of the premiers' platform, he needs to be rise above the inward-looking impulses among the provinces.
CAPTION: Premier Doug Ford speaks to reporters as Canada's premiers hold a news conference to close the Council of the Federation meetings in Halifax on July 17, 2024.

Document 284 of 500

LCBO strike affecting 15% of poll respondants: About half are supportive of union's demands

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/lcbo-strike-affecting-15-poll-respondants/docview/3082121477/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=LCBO+strike+affecting+15%25+of+poll+respondants%3A+About+half+are+supportive+of+union%27s+demands&amp;title=The+Spectator&amp;issn=11899417&amp;date=2024-07-18&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.15&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Spectator&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: A new poll suggests just 15 per cent of Ontarians are personally affected by the LCBO's ongoing strike, as the provincial government expands access to alcohol at other retailers.
That's despite nearly 90 per cent of respondents in a new Leger poll saying they are aware the job action is happening.
Fewer than one-third of Ontarians say they want the provincial government to intervene to end the 12-day strike at Ontario's main liquor retailer, while about half are supportive of the striking union's demands.
The Leger poll of 601 residents can't be assigned a margin of error because online surveys are not considered truly random samples.
Approximately 10,000 workers at the LCBO walked off the job July 5 after negotiations broke down.
The union representing the workers said the sides were headed back to the bargaining table Wednesday.
The poll conducted last weekend asked whether the government should use binding arbitration or legislation to ensure that LCBO stores open back up as soon as possible.
Twenty-nine per cent of respondents supported such a move, while 44 per cent opposed it.
The poll also asked if respondents support the union's stated goals, including wage increases and more permanent positions. Just under half, 49 per cent, answered in the affirmative, while 25 per cent said they were not supportive.
The Ontario Public Service Employees Union has said the main issue is the province's alcohol expansion plans, which would see ready-to-drink cocktails sold outside LCBO stores - a move it maintains poses an existential threat to the LCBO and could lead to major job losses.
Colleen MacLeod, chair of the union's LCBO bargaining unit, has said the plan would "mean thousands of lost jobs, fewer hours for the 70 per cent of LCBO retail workers who are casual and struggling to make ends meet and hundreds of millions in dollars of lost public revenues drained from health care, education and infrastructure."
The LCBO, a Crown corporation, nets the province $2.5 billion a year.
On Monday, the Ontario government sped up its expansion plan. The 450 stores across Ontario already licensed to sell beer, wine and ciders will be able to start ordering coolers and seltzers on Thursday and sell them as soon as they arrive.
Stephanie Ross, an associate professor in the school of labour studies at McMaster University, said Premier Doug Ford doesn't have a great reputation when it comes to labour, given the high-profile disputes in recent years with health-care and education workers. And he's faced accusations of making policy moves that benefit friends in the private sector, a criticism that's been levied against him in the LCBO dispute.
"There is a base of support for the union's message here, both in terms of the working conditions that they're trying to fight to improve, and in terms of the role that the LCBO plays in funding public services in the province," she said.
The Canadian Press

Document 285 of 500

New party is one way to stop Poilievre

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/new-party-is-one-way-stop-poilievre/docview/3082121437/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=unknown&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=New+party+is+one+way+to+stop+Poilievre&amp;title=The+Spectator&amp;issn=11899417&amp;date=2024-07-18&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.14&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Spectator&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: If ever there was a time for a "big tent" left wing party in Canada, now is it.
Not a merger of the Liberals and NDP, mind you. This misses the point that Canadians are dissatisfied with current leadership. Canada already has a de facto merger of the two parties anyways. But in trying to be all things to all people, this merged entity has alienated many Canadian progressives. The Liberals have governed as though they owe nothing to the party keeping them in power, while the NDP has asked for far too little in exchange for too much. Though the limited dental and pharmaceutical care agreements are worthwhile, they would be insufficient for a party with an unencumbered progressive agenda.
What we need is a new party comprising the disaffected progressive members of all federal parties. With a clear progressive vision for the future, such a party would pose a more formidable challenge to the Conservatives than either the Liberals or the NDP can do today. Recent examples from around the world demonstrate that "big tent" progressive efforts have considerable public appeal.
Many political experts were surprised by the recent electoral victory of France's New Popular Front, which goes to show how left-wing coalitions can form governments even when underestimated. The New Popular Front ran on a sensible progressive platform: taxing the rich, free school lunches, building a million new homes, investing in France's infrastructure. That they further committed to recognizing Palestine didn't hurt either, given the widespread sense of helplessness felt by many witnessing the horrors in Gaza.
What is needed most in Canada is a rearticulation of shared progressive values - for the public, not the political class - as the foundation for such a movement. An open door, big tent, progressive party where shared values and goals serve as the main attraction, rather than the personality cults of career politicians, would be a welcome and refreshing change of pace.
A concerted effort at rebuilding the social safety net by significant increases in taxation on the highest earners and corporations, in addition to major investments to decarbonize the energy and transport grids and buttress against climate change, would be crucial focus areas to improve quality of life and stimulate the economy. A rejuvenation of our democracy through electoral, Senate, and constitutional reforms, could make Canada a more participatory and representative democracy. A stronger federal democracy would have the mandate needed to fight back against encroaching privatization of health care, and disinvestment in education. If these sound like radical proposals, recent world events show they're also saleable in this moment.
Where France's right-wing defined itself in opposition to everything from immigration to "le wokeisme," France's left was open, inclusive, and accepting.
Canada has had seven elections in the last 20 years, and the highest participation level reached was in 2015, when 68.3 per cent of eligible voters cast ballots. That was the election Trudeau promised would be the last using "first past the post" and arguably one won on a platform of progressive promises. Despite Trudeau's comfortable majority at the time, many of those promises were abandoned. Over the past 20 years anywhere from 32 to 42 per cent of the electorate hasn't voted, and it's this "silent plurality" - combined with disaffected Liberal, NDP, Green, Bloc, and even Communist Party supporters - that could sweep a new progressive alternative to power in Canada.
Canadians do not have to accept Pierre Poilievre as an inevitability, just as the French and British didn't have to accept a government led by Marine Le Pen or Nigel Farage. A new progressive big tent could be a powerful antidote to the toxicity on the Canadian right.
Taylor C. Noakes is an
independent journalist
and public historian
from Montreal. He is a
freelance contributor
for the Toronto Star.
CAPTION:

Men require more support to help limit abuse of partners, experts say

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/men-require-more-support-help-limit-abuse/docview/3082121373/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Men+require+more+support+to+help+limit+abuse+of+partners%2C+experts+say&amp;title=The+Spectator&amp;issn=11899417&amp;date=2024-07-18&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.11&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Spectator&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Any Ontario strategy to end intimate partner violence must include more programs for men at risk of becoming perpetrators, an area in which the province is currently sorely lacking, experts told a committee Wednesday.
There is a "smattering" of services available, said Katreena Scott, academic director of the Centre for Research and Education on Violence Against Women and Children at Western University, but it is too few and far between.
"The reality in Ontario is that we live, right now, in a system where if there is somebody who is engaging in - or is at risk for - hurtful, abusive or coercive behaviour, it's very, very difficult to find and get help," Scott told a legislative committee studying intimate partner violence.
"Really, what has to happen is they need to wait until they're involved in the criminal justice system."
The Progressive Conservative government said this spring that it would support an NDP bill to declare intimate partner violence an epidemic, but then-government house leader Paul Calandra also tasked the justice committee with a broader study of the issue. Those hearings began Wednesday.
The move came nearly a year after the province rejected calls from an inquest into the deaths of three women at the hands of their former partner to make the declaration, saying it couldn't label intimate partner violence an epidemic because it was not an infectious or communicable disease.
Experts at a legislative committee Wednesday held up an Alberta program as a good example of support, which offers men counselling, a 24-hour telephone support line, online resources and toolkits.

Document 287 of 500

Public input is no longer a high priority

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/public-input-is-no-longer-high-priority/docview/3082121294/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=unknown&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Public+input+is+no+longer+a+high+priority&amp;title=The+Spectator&amp;issn=11899417&amp;date=2024-07-18&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.14&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Spectator&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Tuesday marked the last regular Burlington council meeting until September - but there's one in August to meet Premier Doug Ford's tight deadline for planning applications. Otherwise cities now must refund application fees.
It is another change that has cut out the "middle man": citizens. Burlington prides itself on soliciting public input on development proposals. Years ago planning reports contained recommendations. Residents asked, "Why ask our opinion if you've already made up your mind?" The process was changed, seeking input first, then making a recommendation report.
Now, with the clock ticking on Ford's mandatory timelines, it's changed again. The "statutory" meeting - required under the planning act - has a recommendation. Council asks developers to host public meetings early - and most accede to the request. The city seeks written input early, too, but now the "stat meeting" is the public's only chance to speak to committee and council. Again, there's a recommendation in the report.
There are many projects approved, but few underway, mainly because of interest rates. So cities "hurry up and wait," and residents feel rushed. Public consultation is not a high priority for the province, whose vast planning changes have upended a previously enviable system that allowed proper time to tackle public concerns.
There were two "stat meetings" last week, with approvals granted by council this week. One sought minor changes to the Paradigm condo project underway near the Fairview GO station. The other sought six townhouses on a small site on Plains Road near Shadeland. The willingness of the consultants and developers to resolve concerns is worthy of note, and these two have done it.
Ed Fothergill is the planning consultant on the former project, and the Molinaro Group is a frequent city developer. On the latter, Martin Quarcoopome (Weston Consulting) is the planner and Albert Faccenda (a small local builder) is the developer. A big issue with the townhouses is fear of losing the huge, healthy trees on-site, and/or damaging their root systems and those of neighbours. Faccenda plans to preserve them and their presence helps the marketability.
Height is also an issue. The townhouses are four storeys, backing onto a two-storey condo enclave. They're building "missing middle" units, he said, and the fourth storey is intended only as outdoor space. Council approved it, with modifications yet to be finalized. Council also approved 20-page terms of reference for a community improvement plan (CIP). When completed it will open the door to federal and provincial dollars to assist in affordable, assisted and special-needs housing.
Burlington Hydro offered an update on its distribution system capability, with timelines, to accommodate the dramatic growth expected. Seasonally, summer sees the highest hydro use, but that is expected to shift to winter, mainly because of increased use of heat pumps and electric vehicles (EV). In the near term, it will expand feeder lines. After 2030, mainly because of EV growth, the system will need upgrading.
Likely the agenda item that affects the most people is the city's new zoning, being drafted now. I'm learning to hate the word "flexible" - shorthand for "anything goes" in today's Ontario - and that word is writ large in the current thinking of the city's consultant. It's fine for developers, but what about residents who want some certainty about neighbourhoods they're buying into when they put their life savings on the line? That's going, going, gone! Consultants spoke of using few zones, with lots of flexibility. Imagine the fun the Ontario Land Tribunal will have interpreting a loosey-goosey zoning bylaw. It already grants most developers' wish lists.

Joan Little, a freelance
contributing columnist
for the Spec, is a former
Burlington alderman
and Halton councillor.

Doug Ford tells the Star he won't rule out an early election and vows to defend Ontario 'like a pit bull' against Ottawa

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/doug-ford-tells-star-he-wont-rule-out-early/docview/3082348320/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Doug+Ford+tells+the+Star+he+won%27t+rule+out+an+early+election+and+vows+to+defend+Ontario+%27like+a+pit+bull%27+against+Ottawa&amp;title=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-07-17&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: HALIFAX-Premier Doug Ford insists it does not matter whether the Liberals or the Conservatives win the next federal election.
But in a veiled shot at Pierre Poilievre's federal Tories, Ford - who has worked closely with Prime Minister Justin Trudeau's Liberals - says regardless of who is elected, he will fight "like a pit bull" for the province if Ottawa acts against its interests.
Speaking to the Star in an exclusive interview at the annual meeting of Canada's 13 premiers, held this week in Nova Scotia, Ford also said calling an Ontario election in 2025 - a year before the June 2026 scheduled vote - is not something he's thinking about now.
"It's not on my mind," he said. "I never rule out anything, but my main focus is to continue moving the province forward and really focusing on the things that people really care about - and that's the economy that drives everything else, health care," as well addressing increasing concerns about crime.
An early election call "is probably the last thing on my mind right now. And we'll see when we get closer."
The Star reported in May that Ford is considering moving up the Ontario vote in order to go to the polls before an expected October 2025 federal election.
That's because he is concerned about budget cuts and policy changes a future Poilievre government might impose.
"I just want to make sure on our federal election that Ontario has a strong voice," Ford told the Star.
"No matter what party it is - and I don't care what party does win - if it's not in the best interest of Ontario, and the federal government does something to hurt Ontario, I'm going after them like a pit bull."
Asked Wednesday about the prospect of a Poilievre government, the premier said, "I can't have concerns about him - I've never sat down and really negotiated anything with him."
The premier said he is "focusing on dealing with the government that's in there right now, and I'll always focus on that. But if for any reason if that changes, my number one priority is Ontario."
Ford is the most powerful Conservative politician in Canada after winning back-to-back majority governments in a province his federal counterparts need if they want to form government. But he's had a rocky relationship with Poilievre's team, some of whom privately maintain the premier isn't really a conservative.
Just last March, Ford was visibly displeased by an attack on his then-education minister Stephen Lecce by newly elected federal Tory MP Jamil Jivani - who the premier had personally appointed as his communities opportunities advocate in 2019.
In his victory speech after winning the byelection in Durham, Jivani railed about "the liberal elites who run the Ontario Ministry of Education in this province."
That prompted Ford to retort that when Jivani "showed up - no one even knew who he was - he came in my office, I gave him an incredible opportunity" five years ago.
"To be very frank - and go to root cause - he wouldn't even be where he is if I didn't give him that opportunity," Ford said at the time.
"Maybe he should come in, I'll give him a couple of lessons on how to speak to ... his constituents and that."
Ford takes the helm of the Council of the Federation - comprising the 10 provincial and three territorial leaders - on Aug. 1, and said they are united in advocating their needs to Ottawa.
"I've always said to the other premiers, the smaller provinces - we have a duty to help our smaller provinces, to make ourselves a lot stronger as a country," Ford said. "They appreciate that. I've always taken that approach."
Whether the Liberals should call a federal election before October 2025 "is up to the people, it's up to the federal government," Ford also said. "It all depends on what they decide, but either way, I'm going to focus number one on Ontario and support our other premiers, the territories and the provinces.
"They look at Ontario as a leader and we'll always lead."
Ford flew to Halifax on Monday with wife Karla, but said she had to rush home after massive downpours in the Toronto area on Tuesday that flooded highways, roads and homes also poured into their Etobicoke property.
"We got the videos - there was four feet of water piling through the downstairs, and a couple of feet of water" in the basement, he said, adding his brother went in there with pumps to help clear it out.
"This is one of the million different stories - everyone had a lot of damage," he said.
CAPTION: Premier Doug Ford, second from left, speaks to reporters with Manitoba Premier Wab Kinew, left, Nova Scotia Premier Tim Houston, and Quebec Premier Francois Legault as Canada's premiers hold a press conference to close the Council of the Federation meetings in Halifax on July 17, 2024.

Document 289 of 500

'North America first': Doug Ford has a message for America as premiers discuss U.S. relations

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/north-america-first-doug-ford-has-message-as/docview/3081756253/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=%27North+America+first%27%3A+Doug+Ford+has+a+message+for+America+as+premiers+discuss+U.S.+relations&amp;title=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-07-17&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: HALIFAX - Ontario is asking the federal government to pay up and cover the costs of supporting and housing asylum-seekers, as Quebec's premier said his province is willing to give up some funding so that other provinces can take in larger numbers.
Speaking at the final news conference at the Council of the Federation's annual summer meeting - this year hosted by Nova Scotia Premier Tim Houston - Premier Doug Ford said Ontario spends $1 billion a year and that asylum-seekers are often left in limbo by the federal government.
"When it comes to asylum-seekers, Quebec and Ontario bear the brunt of it," Ford told reporters. "We're just asking the federal government to give our fair share. We've spent over a billion dollars in Ontario every single year."
Ford said he talks to newcomers "and they want to work. They want a better life, and it's taking way too long to get their working visa from the federal government."
The premiers' demand follows Mayor Olivia Chow's long-standing request to Ottawa for additional help for her cash-strapped city.
While the federal government finally came forward with $97 million for Toronto last year to help refugees, there are still asylum-seekers lined outside of the Peter Street homeless intake centre.
That was part of a one-time infusion of $362.4 million for refugee resettlement across Canada.
City hall says there are still more than 4,000 refugees and asylum-seekers in shelters each night, which nearly matches the number of homeless people in them.
Ford said families are often stuck "staying in a hotel like they do on the airport strip up in Toronto ... or a couple of kids down playing in a parking lot in Scarborough" when they "want a better life. They want to work like everyone else ... but we have to make sure we have shelter, we have homes for them. We really need the support of the federal government."
Quebec Premier François Legault said "it's not fair" that his province receives $750 million for asylum-seekers, while other provinces "receive nothing. We would prefer to receive less in the future and have less asylum-seekers."
Alberta is also taking in proportionally more newcomers, said Premier Danielle Smith, noting some 70,000 Ukrainian evacuees have recently settled in her province.
"Aside from a small amount of money at the beginning, there are hotels that need to be funded, there are schools that need to be funded" as well as health-care needs and other programming, she said.
"We want to do our part, but ... when provinces are shouldering larger burdens" Ottawa needs to step up, Smith added.
The 13 provincial and territorial leaders held talks on a number of issues over the past three days, including health care, federal equalization payments and relations with the U.S.
Looming large is the outcome of the U.S. presidential election and the impact that could have on trade relations.
Earlier Wednesday, Ford noted that when tariffs were placed on Canadian goods by the Trump administration, talks were held with U.S. trade representatives and the matter was resolved within a few weeks.
"We won't hesitate if they put tariffs on any of our products," Ford said when asked about tough talk coming from presidential candidates regarding trade.
"We do $500 billion of trade in Ontario alone with the U.S. ... We're the number-one customer to 17 states and number two to 11" Ford said, adding other provinces have strong trading ties as well.
"We are an economic powerhouse," the premier added.
Prince Edward Island Premier Dennis King said no matter the outcome of the election, there will "be good discussions and (we'll) continue the good relationship" with the U.S.
Ford takes over as chair of the premiers' group Aug. 1, and said U.S. relations will be key.
"Hopefully we'll plan a trip as the new administration, whoever it is, gets sworn in" after the November election, Ford said. "We'll have a conversation ... and (stress) it's not just 'buy America,' it's about 'buy North America.'
"At the end of the day, it's Canada, the U.S. and you can put Mexico in there, against the rest of the world, no matter if it's South America, Asia, Europe," Ford told reporters.
Ford also said federal government duplication in some programs - notably in the recent student nutrition program - overlaps with provincial efforts and costs more, wasting taxpayer dollars.
"And you know what happens?" Ford said, "The premiers get pissed. It's as simple as that. They just jump in there and think they know better what we know, what we deal with on a daily basis."
With files from Robert Benzie

Document 290 of 500

Doug Ford optimistic as contract talks resume in LCBO strike

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/doug-ford-optimistic-as-contract-talks-resume/docview/3081756219/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Doug+Ford+optimistic+as+contract+talks+resume+in+LCBO+strike&amp;title=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-07-17&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: It's not just the booze talking.
Talks are continuing between the Liquor Control Board of Ontario and the union representing more than 9,000 workers who have been off the job for two weeks.
Contract bargaining resumed Wednesday for the first time since July 4, leaving Premier Doug Ford optimistic a deal can be reached to get employees back on the job and 680 LCBO stores reopened.
Ford said he was "quite excited" that the Ontario Public Service Employees Union had returned to the negotiating table.
"I've talked to hundreds and hundreds of frontline workers, they just want to get back to work," the premier told reporters at the annual Council of the Federation meeting in Halifax.
"Hopefully we can get this done. We've offered benefits, we've offered a (seven per cent) pay increase (over three years and 400) part-time folks full-time opportunities," he said, praising the striking OPSEU members who have contacted him for being "so polite."
"They told me their stories (and it) hits you in the heart."
Colleen MacLeod, chair of the OPSEU bargaining team that represents the striking workers, insisted "we will not back down from our core demands to protect good jobs and public revenues."
"We are showing up today in good faith that a fair deal can finally be reached. What we're putting forward are ideas that are good for workers and good for Ontario," said MacLeod.
The first strike in LCBO history came after Ford announced the province would pay The Beer Store $225 million to move up the Dec. 31, 2025 deadline for expanded liberalization of alcohol sales.
Under the premier's changes, beer, wine and "ready-to-drink" beverages can be sold at 8,500 licensed supermarkets, convenience stores and big-box outlets by Oct. 31.
While the LCBO currently enjoys a monopoly on most premixed drinks - the fastest-growing alcohol category - Ford said "that ship has sailed" and such products will be more widely available.
The 450 supermarkets licensed to sell beer and wine by former Liberal premier Kathleen Wynne in 2015 can order ready-to-drink booze - like Bacardi Breezer and Mike's Hard Lemonade - as of Thursday with cans and bottles expected on grocery shelves next week.
Ford emphasized the LCBO will retain exclusive rights to sell spirits, such as whisky, vodka, gin, rum and tequila, and it will control the wholesale distribution of all wine and premixed drinks.
But OPSEU fears "the Ford government's expanded alcohol-everywhere scheme" will lead to reduced public revenues and inevitable job losses for the Crown asset.
Internal Progressive Conservative government polling obtained by the Star found strong support for more liberalized alcohol sales even though 54 per cent back OPSEU's "decision to go strike" while 32 per cent opposed it and 14 per cent had no opinion.
The Campaign Research survey suggested the job action is so far having little impact on the public with about three-quarter of respondents - 73 per cent - saying the stoppage was not "inconvenient" while 28 per cent said it was.
There is 91 per cent awareness of the strike with only nine per cent unaware.
With 69 per cent backing Wynne's liberalization and 57 per cent supporting Ford's expansion - and 61 per cent favouring more availability of premixed drinks - the Tories maintain public opinion is with them.
Campaign Research surveyed 1,995 Ontarians last Friday and Saturday using the Maru Blue online panel. While opt-in polls cannot be assigned a margin of error, for comparison purposes, a random sample of this size would have one of plus or minus two percentage points, 19 times out of 20.
According to Campaign Research, Ford's Tories were at 41 per cent to 26 per cent for Bonnie Crombie's Liberals, 18 per cent for Marit Stiles's New Democrats and 10 per cent for Mike Schreiner's Greens.
That's similar to the findings in last month's Abacus Data tracking poll for the Star, which found the PCs at 41 per cent, the Liberals at 25 per cent, the NDP at 22 per cent and the Greens at eight per cent.
CAPTION: Premier Doug Ford holds a flat of beer cans during a photo-op at Cool Beer Brewing Company in Toronto.

Document 291 of 500

One way for progressives to beat Pierre Poilievre: Create a new party

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/one-way-progressives-beat-pierre-poilievre-create/docview/3081756218/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=One+way+for+progressives+to+beat+Pierre+Poilievre%3A+Create+a+new+party&amp;title=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-07-17&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: If ever there was a time for a "big tent" left wing party in Canada, now is it.
Not a merger of the Liberals and NDP, mind you. This misses the point that Canadians are dissatisfied with current leadership.
Canada already has a de facto merger of the two parties anyways. But in trying to be all things to all people, this merged entity has alienated many Canadian progressives. The Liberals have governed as though they owe nothing to the party keeping them in power, while the NDP has asked for far too little in exchange for too much. Though the limited dental and pharmaceutical care agreements are worthwhile, they would be insufficient for a party with an unencumbered progressive agenda.
What we need is a new political party comprising the disaffected progressive members of all federal parties. With a clear progressive vision for the future, such a party would pose a more formidable challenge to the Conservatives than either the Liberals or the NDP can do today. And recent examples from around the world demonstrate that 'big tent' progressive efforts have considerable public appeal.
Many political experts were surprised by the recent electoral victory of France's New Popular Front, which goes to show how left-wing coalitions can form governments even when underestimated. Recent electoral successes in the United Kingdom, Mexico and India also underscore the appeal of progressive leadership in this era of economic uncertainty.
The New Popular Front ran on a sensible progressive platform: taxing the rich, free school lunches, building a million new homes, investing in France's infrastructure. That they further committed to recognizing Palestine didn't hurt either, given the widespread and growing sense of helplessness felt by many witnessing the horrors committed against the people of Gaza.
What is needed most in Canada is a rearticulation of shared progressive values - for the public, not the political class - as the foundation for such a movement. An open door, big tent, progressive party where shared values and goals serve as the main attraction, rather than the personality cults of career politicians, would be a welcome and refreshing change of pace.
A concerted effort at rebuilding the social safety net by significant increases in taxation on the highest earners and corporations, in addition to major investments to decarbonize the energy and transport grids and buttress against climate change, would be crucial focus areas to improve quality of life and stimulate the economy. A rejuvenation of our democracy through electoral, Senate, and constitutional reforms, could make Canada a more participatory and representative democracy. A stronger federal democracy would have the mandate needed to fight back against encroaching privatization of healthcare, and disinvestment in education, committed by conservative governments ideologically opposed to the values of social democracy.
If these sound like radical proposals, recent world events show they're also salable in this moment.
Where France's right-wing defined itself in opposition to everything from immigration to "le wokeisme," France's left was open, inclusive, and accepting.
Canada has had seven elections in the last 20 years, and the highest participation level reached was in 2015, when 68.3 per cent of eligible voters cast ballots. That was the election Trudeau promised would be the last using "first past the post" and arguably one won on a platform of progressive promises. Despite Trudeau's comfortable majority at the time, many of those promises were abandoned.
Over the past 20 years anywhere from 32 to 42 per cent of the electorate hasn't voted, and it's this "silent plurality" - combined with disaffected Liberal, NDP, Green, Bloc, and even Communist Party supporters - that could sweep a new progressive alternative to power in Canada.
Creating a new party isn't easy-nothing worthwhile ever is. There will be resistance from those inside the political bubble who will argue this is splitting the vote. But the progressive vote is already split several ways, and the entrenched leadership of extant political parties are resisting cooperation to their detriment.
Canadians do not have to accept Pierre Poilievre as an inevitability, just as the French and British didn't have to accept as inevitable a government led by Le Pen or Farage.
A new progressive big tent could be a powerful antidote to the toxicity on the Canadian right.
CAPTION: Participants gesture toward a giant banner that reads as "France is the fabric of migration" during an election night rally following the first results of the second round of France's legislative election, at Place de la Republique in Paris on Sunday. A loose alliance of French left-wing parties thrown together for snap elections was on course to become the biggest parliamentary bloc and beat the far right, according to shock projected results.

Document 292 of 500

July 17: Dandelions ruin Hamilton's look, Ford's next step - wine with your morning Timbits and other letters to the editor

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/july-17-dandelions-ruin-hamiltons-look-fords-next/docview/3081756185/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=July+17%3A+Dandelions+ruin+Hamilton%27s+look%2C+Ford%27s+next+step+-+wine+with+your+morning+Timbits+and+other+letters+to+the+editor&amp;title=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-07-17&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Dandelions will make city look dirty and unkept
Dandelions blanketed the grasslands all over the city earlier this year.
The weeds were flourishing in crevices in sidewalks and in the roadways, too. One stubborn dandelion was even growing out of a storm grate at the steps near a hospital, though I thought I had nipped it a few weeks before.
It seemed that homeowners could not care less, their lawns infested with weeds.
By early June, the thousands of yellow flowers had changed into white puffballs. The wind then separated them into white puffy seeds, as they danced about the city for weeks. They've all gone now, leaving their mark behind.
Come next year, the dandelions will be back tenfold. Hamilton will look even more dirty and unkept.
Tina Maini, Hamilton
Is Ford interfering with LCBO strike bargaining?
Premier Doug Ford promised to not get involved in the LCBO strike.
He had advanced the sale of alcoholic drinks in convenience stores and grocery stores by two months.
If this is not underhanded interference in bargaining, what is it called?
I hope that the Labour Board sees this for what it is.
Annette Webber, Freelton
Not all stores want to sell alcoholic drinks
Not all stores want the extra responsibility of selling alcohol. There's more inventory to worry about, plus all employees must be at least 19 years old and have Smart Serve training.
How about theft? No one in Ontario cares about this. Convenience? The LCBO is open seven days a week.
As for those greediest opportunists known as the grocers ... Remember bread price fixing and the ridiculous food prices?
It's nice to know that our government cares more about the grocers' profits than LCBO employees.
Robin Magder, Dundas
What's next from Ford? Wine with our Timbits?
Premier Doug Ford seems to have a maniacal drive to get alcohol into as many locations as possible. He will give, at a minimum, $225 million to The Beer Store (owned by foreign corporations) to cancel a contract two years before it is due for renewal.
He will allow thousands of corner stores to sell alcohol. I am surprised he hasn't given carte blanche to Tim Hortons to sell a glass of wine with their Timbits.
Ford will allow you to walk 10 minutes to the local store and pick up a six-pack of mixed drinks, and he will also allow you to wait 12 hours in your local hospital emergency room to see a doctor.
Ford will spend $13 billion to build a highway that parallels the underused Highway 407. He will partially finance this misadventure by cutting spending in health care.
At a time when climate change is a direct threat to every human on the planet, Ford saw fit to cancel millions of dollars in green energy, then brag he is the Jolly Green Giant by giving billions to car battery plants.
If you like to drink and drive (not at the same time), Ford is your man. If you want an education or are sick, good luck. You will need it.
Bill Sullivan, Hamilton
CAPTION: Dandelions cover a stretch of grass.

Subject: Alcohol; Liquor; Grocery stores

Document 294 of 500

Internal poll for Doug Ford finds strong support for booze liberalization plan - and striking LCBO workers

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/internal-poll-doug-ford-finds-strong-support/docview/3081867105/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Internal+poll+for+Doug+Ford+finds+strong+support+for+booze+liberalization+plan+-+and+striking+LCBO+workers&amp;title=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-07-16&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Ontarians overwhelmingly support more liberalized alcohol sales even though a majority back the LCBO strikers, according to internal government polling obtained by the Star.
The poll commissioned by Premier Doug Ford's Progressive Conservatives found 54 per cent support the Ontario Public Service Employees Union's "decision to go strike" while 32 per cent opposed it and 14 per cent had no opinion.
But the Campaign Research survey suggested the job action that has closed 680 Liquor Control Board of Ontario stores since July 5 is so far having little impact on the public or on the Tories' popularity.
Only about one-quarter of respondents - 28 per cent - said the job action was "inconvenient" while 73 per cent said it wasn't.
That's despite the fact 91 per cent were aware of the work stoppage with only nine per cent unaware.
Some 69 per cent said they agreed with former Liberal premier Kathleen Wynne's 2015 decision to allow beer and wine to be sold in 450 grocery stores, while only 23 per cent opposed it and eight per cent didn't know.
More than half - 57 per cent - backed Ford expanding booze sales to other supermarkets, convenience stores and big-box outlets, with 35 per cent opposed and eight per cent unsure.
And 61 per cent support the premier's push to sell "ready-to-drink" cocktails - currently only available at the LCBO - at 8,500 private retailers, with 27 per cent opposed and 12 per cent unsure.
A senior government official, speaking confidentially in order to discuss the ongoing labour dispute, said the poll underscores why Ford is moving forward with opening up the market while ensuring the LCBO remains a key player.
"He actually deeply cares about the workers (and) the LCBO ... having a special and important place in our alcohol ecosystem," said the high-ranking insider.
Campaign Research principal Nick Kouvalis said Tuesday that "the public clearly wants liberalization to continue."
Campaign Research surveyed 1,995 Ontarians on Friday and Saturday using the Maru Blue online panel. While opt-in polls cannot be assigned a margin of error, for comparison purposes, a random sample of this size would have one of plus or minus two percentage points, 19 times out of 20.
"The union clearly believes that they're having an impact politically, but the ballot test (response) suggests they're not," said Kouvalis.
According to Campaign Research, Ford's Tories were at 41 per cent in public support to 26 per cent for Bonnie Crombie's Liberals, 18 per cent for Marit Stiles's New Democrats and 10 per cent for Mike Schreiner's Greens.
That echoes the findings in last month's Abacus Data tracking poll for the Star, which had the Tories at 41 per cent, the Grits at 25 per cent, the NDP at 22 per cent and the Greens at eight per cent.
"It doesn't look like there's any negative impact for the Ford government," said Kouvalis, noting "there's Ford voters who support the union's decision to go on strike for better wages, benefits and job security."
The LCBO has offered the workers seven per cent raises over three years and promised to make 400 casual employees full-time.
But OPSEU leaders say Ford's move to allow pre-mixed beverage sales in other stores will undermine the Crown booze giant and inevitably lead to jobs losses.
The poll found 41 per cent believe the premier's opening up of the market "poses an existential threat to the LCBO that will lead to major jobs losses," while 40 per cent say it was something he "promised in the election so that people in Ontario would have more choices and more convenience" and 18 per cent were unsure.
About one-third - 34 per cent - said "Ford's plan is to privatize the LCBO," while 44 per cent said he's "allowing the LCBO to become the sole distributor to all restaurants, bars, grocery stores, convenience stores and big-box stores in Ontario" and 22 per cent were unsure.
CAPTION: Premier Doug Ford is shown at Cool Beer Brewing Company in Toronto on July 10, 2024.

Subject: Liquor; Convenience stores; Strikes; Liberalization; Grocery stores

Document 295 of 500

Flooding in Toronto: DVP lanes still closed, transit riders in limbo at Union Station for afternoon rush hour commute

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/flooding-toronto-dvp-lanes-still-closed-transit/docview/3081867010/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Flooding+in+Toronto%3A+DVP+lanes+still+closed%2C+transit+riders+in+limbo+at+Union+Station+for+afternoon+rush+hour+commute&amp;title=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-07-16&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: During the afternoon rush hour, all southbound lanes of the DVP remain closed at Bayview/Bloor due to flooding. Northbound DVP at Dundas Street East is still closed.
Drivers had to be rescued from their stranded vehicles as Toronto streets and major highways saw significant flooding on Tuesday. Massive power outages are still affecting tens of thousands of customers as Toronto Hydro crews work frantically to field a backlog of calls.
Toronto transit was messier than usual, as service was disrupted for many commuters and flooding at Union Station forced the subway to close.
Environment Canada said approximately 125 mm is expected to fall throughout the day, with Pearson airport issuing a warning about the significant rain. "During a 10 minute span, we recorded 26 mm of rain," the airport said in a post on X.
Power outages
3:55 p.m. Premier Doug Ford said he's personally spoken to the CEO of Toronto Hydro and had put a call into the head of Hydro One. Flooded stations have been pumped out now "and I understand power is going back on," Ford said from Halifax on Tuesday, where he is attending the annual meeting of Canada's premiers. "We've put into flood management over $2 billion to help communities and we are pouring money into Toronto, too, for their old infrastructure, to support them," he told reporters.
Ford said even his own home in Etobicoke didn't escape the heavy rainfall. "My house got flooded out - the basement is all flooded right now I was told," said Ford. "It's unfortunate when we have natural, little disasters - these are mini ones compared to what it could be. But we are pouring money into infrastructure - we'll continue to upgrade all the pipes ... We are making sure that we upgrade a lot of antiquated piping throughout the entire city. But we are always there to support our municipalities across Ontario." He said he will immediately reach out to Toronto Mayor Olivia Chow. "Everyone's safe and sound, thank God," the premier said. - Kris Rushowy
3:19 p.m. Toronto Hydro said suspected flooding at a Hydro One transmission station may have caused the widespread power outages across the city, with approximately 123,000 customers still in the dark as of 3 p.m. Hydro One is the provincial utility that provides electricity to regional companies like Toronto Hydro. Power will be restored to most areas by midnight, Toronto Hydro estimates. "Please know that we are working closely with Hydro One to resolve the issue and restore power as quickly and safely as possible," Toronto Hydro spokesperson Daniel McNeil told the Star in an email.
At one point, Toronto Hydro reported more than 170,000 customers were left in the dark and thousands of people waiting in a virtual queue to submit additional outages. Toronto Hydro has blamed some of its widespread outages on HydroOne, the provincial electricity supplier.
Don't call the dispatch centre if your power is out, says Toronto Hydro, asking the public to keep the lines open for public safety hazards and emergencies. - Mark Colley
TTC service disruptions due to flooding
4:32 p.m. Some GO trains appear to be running now, though hundreds of commuters are still in limbo. The station's food court has been closed for hours, cordoned off by yellow caution tape and security guards stationed at different stairwells. Maintenance workers are blasting music on a speaker, as they vacuum water and mop the floors. - Calvi Leon
4:08 p.m. St. Patrick, Osgoode and St. Andrew are now running trains through the stations. Union is still being bypassed and remains fully closed off. The 509 Harbourfront streetcar can't enter the station so it's being replaced with the 510 Spadina replacement bus along Queens Quay and up to Bay and Front streets. There's still no service at Pape Station on Line 2, and Line 2 subway service is still suspended between Jane and Kipling stations due to a west end power outage where shuttle buses are still operating. A number of service disruptions are still taking place across the streetcar system due to traffic congestion and effects of flooding.
3:53 p.m. At Union Station, security guards are preventing commuters from accessing the subway platform due to the flooding, advising them to take the shuttle buses running outside instead. One of the guards said he's optimistic the service will resume within the next hour now that power has been restored, but cautioned that nothing has been announced officially. Further down the corridor, dozens of Go Transit passengers are standing around the information screens, trying to determine if their train is one of many that are either cancelled or delayed.
Down the stairs at the lower level of Union Station, Go Train passengers sound exasperated. "It's messed up here. No trains are leaving," one passenger could be heard saying over the phone. "There's only one way out of the city," another said, as they pushed through the crowds.
Kingsman Lai said he's been waiting nearly two hours for his train to depart to Missisauga."There's nothing we can do," he said. "We're just waiting around. My office has no power anyway." - Calvi Leon
2:42 p.m. Regular service has resumed between St. George and King stations after an earlier power failure.
1:41 p.m. "The TTC is closed!" a Union Station guide yells, trying to field questions from commuters. "The station is flooded. Unless you want to go down there and ride a submarine, the TTC is closed," she told frustrated passengers. The subway at Union Station remains closed. - Andy Takagi
1:39 p.m. TTC monitoring Kennedy and Spadina stations but trains still running at both. No streetcar service to Union - turning at Fleet Loop at Bathurst.
1:16 p.m. Trains also not running between Kipling and Jane stations due to power failure. Shuttle buses are operating.
12:26 p.m. Water slowly leaked through the bottom floor of Union Station, blocking off the GO station platform. A lone security guard turned away would-be travellers. "Go back, I'm sorry, go back." A frustrated man tries to make his way forward, pushing against the security guard until he finally gives up, turning back out into the rain in a huff. Outside of Union's York St. exit, cars slosh through flooded roads, one sits idling in a pool of water, its hazards on as others push through nearly a foot of water. - Andy Takagi
The entrance to Yorkdale Mall from Yorkdale TTC station has been closed due to reports of flooding, the TTC said.
The TTC said trains were bypassing Christie subway station around 9:30 a.m. due to flooding but normal service resumed shortly after.
The 510 Spadina route saw a brief detour southbound at Spadina Avenue and Queens Quay West due to water pooling, but it has since resumed normal operations.
The 507 Long Branch TTC streetcar route was not running in service between Long Branch Loop and Humber Loop at The Queensway due to flooding, the TTC said in a post on X. Shuttle buses were operating before normal service resumed around 11:10 a.m.
DVP and Highway lane closures due to flooding
On the DVP at Dundas, traffic was at a standstill as a water completely flooded underneath the bridge. A couple was trapped in their car in the water just underneath the bridge as onlookers watched from above. The Fire department was on scene preparing to rescue cars and passengers. Fireman were lashing themselves to the guard rail with knotted ropes when doing rescues, as to make sure they didn't get pushed away by the current. - Raju Mudhar
Reports of flooding in the areas of Lakeshore Boulevard and Strachan Avenue and at Pottery Road and Bayview Avenue have caused hazardous road conditions for drivers, Toronto police say.
Delays are expected as drivers are advised to use alternate routes and Lake Shore Boulevard from British Columbia Road to Strachan Avenue has been closed in both directions
The Ontario Provincial Police are advising drivers to slow down and take caution while on the highways in the GTA as they say multiple lanes have reportedly seen flooding.
The Ontario Provincial Police are advising drivers to slow down and take caution while on the highways in the GTA as they say multiple have reportedly seen flooding.
In an update just after 11 a.m., Environment Canada says the rainfall warning is calling for amounts of up to 125 mm with extremely heavy showers that will gradually reduce over the next few hours.
"The ground, already near saturation, has little ability to absorb further rainfall," the weather agency said.
Environment Canada says there may be 40 mm of rainfall per hour in the city and surrounding areas.
"Rapidly rising rivers and creeks can sweep away bridges, culverts, buildings, and people. Abandon stalled cars if water is rising rapidly. Keep children and pets away from creeks and river banks. Consider moving valuable items to higher levels."
In a post on X, Peel police said manhole covers in the region were reportedly lifting due to the "volume of rain."
Drove through a flooded street? How to know if your car is damaged
Certain streets were underwater Tuesday as Toronto weathered a downpour that left thousands without power and drivers stranded on roads that had transformed into canals.
Warnings of localized flooding were issued as videos of cars driving through - and getting stranded in - wheel-height water circulated on social media.
Driving experts and mechanics warned never to drive in flooded areas if possible, lest you risk permanently damaging your vehicle or potentially sparking an electrical fire.
Read the full story from the Star's Kevin Jiang
Flights impacted by storm
Billy Bishop airport says some flights have been impacted as of 1 p.m. The pedestrian tunnel at the airport was swamped due to the downpour, requiring its temporary closure at around noon."Tunnel is currently closed and all passengers and staff will be redirected to the ferry for access to and from the airport," Billy Bishop airport said in a post on X.
Today was supposed to be Daphne Rely's first time flying out of Billy Bishop Airport. And she picked one of the worst days to do it. In nearly three decades of living in the city, Rely said she's never seen the flooding or rain this bad.
"It was pouring, like somebody just had a bucket and was pouring water."
Her trek down to Union from North York bypassed several stops, but now she waits for her cousin, bags in hand, stranded on the wrong side of the Western Channel.
"I'm still hoping that everything will be okay," Rely said, an optimistic smile on her face.- Andy Takagi
City of Toronto
City manager Paul Johnson said he'd been receiving regular updates throughout the day, and Toronto's office of emergency management was meeting early Tuesday afternoon to perform its latest assessment. He said projections of rainfall had increased significantly from when the city first started monitoring the storm Monday night.
"We've got roads closed. We've got trains that are bypassing certain stations. We have a lot of calls for service. I think people just need to be very careful when they're out there on the roads," he said.
Johnson said severe rain events were occurring more frequently and while the city has been taking steps to mitigate their impact, Toronto's infrastructure "was not designed to handle this."
"Right now, we're just trying to make sure everybody's safe, respond to the calls and the emergencies that we have. And then we'll deal with the aftermath, as soon as the rain stops," he said. - Ben Spurr
Why is it raining so much?
If Tuesday's rainfall reaches the upper range of what Environment has predicted - the forecast says up to 125 millimetres - it would put the downpour on par with the worst the city has ever seen. That record was notched on July 9, 2013, when gauges at Pearson International Airport recorded 126 millimetres n a single day, breaking a record that had stood since 1954 when Hurricane Hazel caused extensive and fatal flooding.
The 2013 storm cost almost $1 billion in insured damages and remains Ontario's most expensive weather disaster, according to the Insurance Bureau of Canada; the city received more than 4,700 flooding complaints because of the storm. Just five years later, another significant rainfall event, in August 2018, resulted in a further $80 million in insured damages.
Both storms, at times, registered rainfall that would classify them as "100 year storms" - ones so intense that they have a one percent chance of occurring in any given year. Climate scientists have reported that global warming will increase rainfall extremes and intensify urban flooding, raising the chances of extreme events like this occurring. - Kate Allen
Flood watch issued for Toronto and GTA
The Toronto and Region Conservation Authority (TRCA) issued a flood watch for the city and the GTA on Tuesday.
Severe thunderstorm watch ends for GTA
The severe thunderstorm alert, which was issued just after 8:20 a.m., has been lifted after it stated that weather conditions were "favourable for the development of dangerous thunderstorms that may be capable of producing strong wind gusts, large hail and torrential rain."
Tips to drive safely during heavy rain
Driving in the rain and on wet surfaces can lower one's visibility, putting vehicles at risk of skidding or hydroplaning and raising the chances of collisions in general. But there are simple ways to lower the risk for yourself and your loved ones.
Naureen Raziq, the director of communications and an instructor at Toronto's Globe Driving Academy provided the Star with safe driving tips for those travelling in the rain.
Raziq says to plan out your route before heading out. Make sure you know of the weather conditions at your destination and along your route, and plan accordingly. If it is raining, make sure to leave earlier as you can expect traffic conditions to be worse than usual.
No matter the weather conditions, drivers should always perform a circle check before heading out, Raziq explained. This means circling around the vehicle looking for any signs of damage or malfunction.
Read the full story from the Star's Kevin Jiang
This week's weather forecast
Wednesday morning will be sunny but it will start getting partially cloudy later in the afternoon. In the afternoon, there will be a 40 per cent chance of showers and the risk of a thunderstorm late in the afternoon.
The temperature is expected to reach a high of 28 C with a humidex of 33 and a low of 14 C.
The skies will start clearing in the evening leading to a sunny Thursday and Friday with temperatures expected to hit 26 C.
There will be some cloudy periods on Saturday and the temperature is expected to hit a high of 27 C.
Sunday morning will be cloudy again with a 30 per cent chance of showers with a high of 26 C.
With files from Kevin Jiang.
This is a developing story.
CAPTION: Environment Canada is calling for up to 125 mm of rain on Tuesday. A number of motorists were stranded on the DVP as it completely flooded at Dundas street.Heavy rains hammered Toronto on Tuesday morning. Document 296 of 500

Fewer than 4% of grocery stores have applied for Ontario's new alcohol license - here's why many don't want one

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/fewer-than-4-grocery-stores-have-applied-ontarios/docview/3081242721/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Fewer+than+4%25+of+grocery+stores+have+applied+for+Ontario%27s+new+alcohol+license+-+here%27s+why+many+don%27t+want+one&amp;title=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-07-16&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Convenience stores are snapping up Ontario's new alcohol-retailing licences in droves. Grocery stores? Not so much.
According to official data, fewer than four per cent of the province's 2,000 grocery stores have applied for new licences allowing them to sell beer, wine, cider and ready-to-drink cocktails beginning Oct. 31.
Grocery industry insiders say the biggest reason is a requirement that any stores selling alcohol under the new licenses also have to accept bottle and can returns for recycling.
"Food safety is the number one concern. They don't want empty beer bottles with cigarette butts being dragged into their stores," said Gary Sands, vice president of the Canadian Federation of Independent Grocers.
Just 53 grocery stores across the province had been issued new alcohol-retailing licenses as of July 14 (out of 75 applications), according to the Alcohol and Gaming Commission of Ontario. More than 3,000 convenience stores - or roughly 40 per cent of the total - had been given licences (out of 3,587 which applied). Convenience stores won't be required to accept empties when their licences come into force Sept. 5.
The Retail Council of Canada, which represents large grocery chains including Loblaws, Sobeys and Metro, and big box chains like Costco and Walmart, says the cost of installing recycling infrastructure makes the alcohol licenses unappealing.
"The primary concern is the plan to force consumers to return their empty alcohol containers to grocery stores. Operating return-to-retail systems are expensive, take up valuable floor and backroom space, and create health and safety concerns," said RCC spokesperson Michelle Wasylyshen. "Grocery stores were not built for this purpose and adaptation of stores will cost hundreds of millions of dollars - costs which Ontario consumers would ultimately bear."
A spokesperson for Ontario finance minister Peter Bethlenfalvy said the government has already tried to be flexible with recycling requirements, including exemptions for smaller grocery stores and convenience stores.
"The (size) requirement was determined to exempt smaller retailers from accepting empties ... while continuing to require returns for large grocery stores, who have more square footage for storage and sorting," said Bethlenfalvy spokesperson Colin Blanchar.
Under the new rules for grocery stores - and big-box stores like Walmart - starting Oct. 31, any store 4,000 square feet or larger needs to have its own recycling set up to handle empties. Until Jan. 1, 2026, there's an exemption for stores that are within five kilometres of a Beer Store outlet.
Monday, the Ontario government announced that roughly 450 grocery stores which already have licences to sell beer and wine under the old rules will be allowed to sell ready-to-drink cocktails and cases of beer starting Thursday, as opposed to Aug. 1 - a shot across the bow of striking LCBO workers.
The Ontario Public Service Employees Union has argued that allowing RTDs - the fastest-growing alcohol segment - to be sold at other retailers would hurt the LCBO's revenue. Premier Doug Ford, says the union's insistence on allowing the LCBO to keep a monopoly on RTD sales is a futile effort, arguing "that ship has sailed."
Those 450 licenses were issued under the Master Framework Agreement (MFA), which expires at the end of 2025.
As part of an agreement with The Beer Store and its owners, the provincial government is paying $225 million to get an early jump on wide-open alcohol retailing that would otherwise have had to wait until the beginning of 2026, after the official end of the MFA.
While convenience stores have been more inclined to apply for the alcohol licenses, it's not a no-brainer for them, either, said Anne Kothawala, CEO of the Convenience Industry Council of Canada.
"I'm not surprised a lot of our members have applied, because this is something we've been asking for for a while. But there are still a lot of details they're concerned about, including how they'll get it delivered, and how much that will cost."
CAPTION: In May, Premier Doug Ford and Finance Minister Peter Bethlenfalvy announced the sale of beer and wines at corner stores.

Subject: Retail stores; Licenses; Alcohol; Liquor; Beer; Convenience stores; Grocery stores
