July 16: Poilievre's full response to Trump's shooting should have been published, Ford's plans for the Science Centre raise suspicions, and other letters to the editor

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/july-16-poilievres-full-response-trumps-shooting/docview/3081242700/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=July+16%3A+Poilievre%27s+full+response+to+Trump%27s+shooting+should+have+been+published%2C+Ford%27s+plans+for+the+Science+Centre+raise+suspicions%2C+and+other+letters+to+the+editor&amp;title=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-07-16&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Poilievre's full response to shooting should be published
Re: Canadian leaders say democracy must prevail following Trump rally shooting, July 15
I read with interest the Canadian Press Article in the Spectator about Trump's shooting. When quoting Opposition leader Pierre Poilievre on his statement, the editor removed part of what Polievre said on social media.
After the portion of the quote offered by the Canadian Press, Polievre went on to state "I am also happy that the suspected shooter is dead. Democracy must prevail."
This portion of the quote should not have been omitted as it offers some insight into the mind of Poilievre.
That he could be "happy" about the violent killing of any human being is revealing, but not surprising. But that he equates this killing with democracy "prevailing" is absolutely something that the public should be aware of.
Killing this individual was definitely not democratic in any way.
It is disappointing that the Canadian Press chose to edit this quote from the man who wants to be the Prime Minister of Canada.
Don Courtnage, Ancaster
Shelters and rehab are better than living in tents
Shelters, that is what the city needs to provide, more shelters. If the root cause for the person being homeless is not addressed, the person will remain homeless. Treatments for addictions or mental health issues are what is needed, not more leniency to allow them to live where they want to.
Shelter should be provided as long as the person is getting rehab for their issue. No rehab, no hotel room and no pitching a tent in a park.
Diana MacKenzie, Hamilton
Ford's plans for Science Centre are suspicious
It is a shame that Doug Ford is going to destroy the Science Centre. Unfortunately, his agenda does not include the centre in its current location.
While his "bought" consultants claim that the building is unsafe, other professionals have said it can be repaired despite the years of neglect and deferred maintenance.
Even if it is demolished, why wouldn't the new building be built on the same beautiful property? Is building it at Ontario Place part of a secret deal made with private interests?
Doug Ford has been outed on many ventures such as the Green Belt Scandal, the expensive Booze Boondoggle and Highway 413, to name a few, that will make his well-connected associates even wealthier than they are now. I feel this is just another example in that long list.
Paul John Phillips, Dundas
Don't like gun violence? Don't let people have guns
After watching many political members in the United States talking about the attempted assassination of Donald Trump, I couldn't help but notice the hypocrisy in their statements.
In general terms, they all said: "We have to stop this violence, this is not who we are."
The statistics tend to tell another story. As of the end of June, the U.S. has had 302 mass shootings, with 390 killed, which is a carry-over from previous years and is considered "normal" in their country. In almost every incident, if anyone tries to put in controls on guns, even background checks, it gets defeated, simply because the National Rifle Association provides millions of dollars to many of these same politicians.
As long as politicians are allowed to be bought, these issues will never end - the money is worth more than the lives of their citizens.
Terry Horne, Hamilton
CAPTION: Republican presidential candidate former President Donald Trump is helped off the stage by U.S. Secret Service agents at a campaign event in Butler, Pa., on Saturday, July 13, 2024.

Subject: Political campaigns; Shootings; Gun violence; Mass murders; Democracy; Assassinations & assassination attempts

Location: United States--US

People: Trump, Donald J

Document 298 of 500

Doug Ford appeals to LCBO union to return to bargaining

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/doug-ford-appeals-lcbo-union-return-bargaining/docview/3081242680/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Doug+Ford+appeals+to+LCBO+union+to+return+to+bargaining&amp;title=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-07-16&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: HALIFAX-As the LCBO launches an advertising blitz aimed at ending an 11-day strike, Premier Doug Ford is urging the Ontario Public Service Employees Union to return to bargaining.
"I've talked to hundreds and hundreds of front-line people - all they want to do is get back to work," Ford told the Star in Halifax, where he is attending the annual Council of the Federation meeting of the premiers.
"They are frustrated with the way their labour leader is right now - they want to get back. That's all they want," he said.
Ford said striking Liquor Control Board of Ontario workers have told him they are hurting financially while on picket lines and not earning their usual salaries.
"It breaks my heart when I speak to these people. It's very tough to pay the bills and they just want to get back to work ... I hear their stories and they are struggling."
In a surprise salvo at OPSEU, Ford announced Monday that "ready-to-drink" cocktails will be sold at 450 licensed supermarkets as early as next week.
Expanding the sale of the pre-mixed drinks - such as Bacardi Breezer and Mike's Hard Lemonade - has been a key concern of the union, which fears the loss of the LCBO's monopoly on such beverages will undermine the Crown corporation.
Under Ford's scheme, the products could be sold at as many as 8,500 convenience stores, supermarkets and big box outlets by Oct. 31.
The LCBO would remain the only retailer allowed to sell spirits - such as whisky, rum, gin, vodka and tequila.
"The premier needs to stop interfering with our members' right to bargain and stop escalating his destruction of public alcohol sales," said OPSEU president JP Hornick.
"Our members saw that their employer was directed never to bargain with them at the table," said Hornick.
"They were handed an insulting offer that made it clear their employer did not have a mandate to address workers' core demands: protecting jobs and public revenues."
On Tuesday, the LCBO took out full-page newspaper ads and 30-second broadcast spots explaining its side of a labour dispute that began July 5.
"This strike did not need to happen," says LCBO president and CEO George Soleas in the ads.
"In fact the LCBO worked hard to avoid it," says Soleas.
At the same time, OPSEU has a new digital ad depicting "a relatable billionaire grocery CEO" - who looks like Loblaw's Galen Weston - sneeringly claiming he needs the profits from expanded booze sales in supermarkets.
"I worked really hard to be born into money - don't I deserve more of yours?" says the actor playing the Weston-esque character.
"What's a billionaire got to do to get some extra zeroes around here? Stop funding public services, start funding me."
The LCBO is offering seven per cent raises over three years to the more than 9,000 OPSEU members who work at 680 LCBO stores as well as distribution warehouses.
As well, the LCBO has promised to give full-time jobs to 400 casual employees.
It's the first time in the 97-year history of the booze giant that workers have hit the bricks.
The job action came just six weeks after Ford announced he would pay The Beer Store $225 million to speed up the previously announced Dec. 31, 2025 deadline for expanded alcohol sales.
Currently, the LCBO has a monopoly on most "ready-to-drink" beverages, which is the fastest growing alcohol category.
NDP Leader Marit Stiles, Liberal Leader Bonnie Crombie and Green Leader Mike Schreiner have blamed Ford's rushed alcohol liberalization push for the strike.
On Monday, the Tourism Industry Association of Ontario implored the government to end the dispute, which is hindering the operations of bars and restaurants.
The Ontario Restaurant Hotel and Motel Association also warned "the strike is already impacting licensed operations."
CAPTION: Premier Doug Ford speaks to reporters upon arriving at a meeting at the Council of the Federation meetings in Halifax on Monday.

Subject: Alcohol; Sales; Liquor; Employment; Supermarkets

Document 299 of 500

Province speeds up alcohol expansion: 'Our government is keeping our promise to give people in Ontario choice,' finance minister says

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/province-speeds-up-alcohol-expansion/docview/3080953426/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Province+speeds+up+alcohol+expansion%3A+%27Our+government+is+keeping+our+promise+to+give+people+in+Ontario+choice%2C%27+finance+minister+says&amp;title=The+Spectator&amp;issn=11899417&amp;date=2024-07-16&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.6&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Spectator&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Ontario Premier Doug Ford's government is rushing to get ready-to-drink cocktails on grocery store shelves amid a strike at the province's main liquor retailer that has focused on that very issue.
Finance Minister Peter Bethlenfalvy said Monday that grocery stores that are already licensed to sell beer and wine can start ordering the pre-mixed cocktails, as well as large packs of beer, to sell starting on Thursday - sooner than the planned Aug. 1 launch of that step.
"Our government is keeping our promise to give people in Ontario choice and convenience while supporting Ontario-made beverage producers across the province, including the Ontario businesses that produce more than 80 per cent of the ready-to-drink beverages sold here in our province," Bethlenfalvy wrote in a statement.
The sped-up move is part of an already fast-tracked plan to expand alcohol sales in the province. Ford's previous plan was to get beer, wine and ready-to-drink cocktails in convenience stores and all grocery stores by 2026, but in May he announced that would instead happen this year.
Leadership at the Ontario Public Service Employees Union, whose approximately 10,000 workers at the Liquor Control Board of Ontario walked off the job July 5, has said wages are not the main issue in the labour dispute, rather it is the expanded sale of ready-to-drink cocktails.
Previous rounds of alcohol market expansion in Ontario have kept spirits sales in the hands of the LCBO, and OPSEU worries undoing that will threaten the LCBO and union jobs.
Getting ready-to-drink beverages in grocery stores even sooner is an attempt to undercut the LCBO and amounts to interfering in bargaining, said OPSEU president JP Hornick.
"Ford is clearly trying to deliver on a promise he made to prospective corporate donors from the convenience and grocery industries during the elections," Hornick wrote in a statement.
"Doug Ford should stop playing politics with the lives of our members and taxpayers' money."
Ford has noted that LCBO revenues have increased through previous rounds of alcohol sales expansions.
Under Ford's plan, convenience stores will be allowed to sell beer, wine and coolers starting Sept. 5 while newly licensed grocery stores can do so starting Oct. 31.
The Canadian Press
CAPTION:

CREDIT: Allison Jones The Canadian Press

Document 301 of 500

Doug Ford wants Canada to approve drugs faster. Is that possible?

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/doug-ford-wants-canada-approve-drugs-faster-is/docview/3081423324/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Doug+Ford+wants+Canada+to+approve+drugs+faster.+Is+that+possible%3F&amp;title=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-07-15&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: HALIFAX-Canada is a much smaller market than the U.S. or U.K. and that affects how fast drugs are submitted for approval, but there are ways for the federal and provincial governments to help move the process along so patients get faster access to them, say experts.
Talk of the delay getting drugs to patients is top of mind for Premier Doug Ford, who arrived in Nova Scotia on Monday as the annual meeting of Canada's 13 provincial and territorial leaders began.
Ford, who takes the helm of the Council of the Federation Aug. 1, has said he will raise the issue and make it a focus for the coming year.
"Canada currently ranks last in the G7 in the time it takes to approve and provide patients access to innovative and often life-saving medicines," Ford said in a recent letter to the pan-Canadian Pharmaceutical Alliance, which was founded by the country's premiers.
"This needs to change," Ford wrote. "We owe it to Canadians to do everything we can to give them the same timely access to life-changing treatments as patients in the rest of the world."
University of Toronto Prof. Mina Tadrous, who researches pharmaceutical policies and drug effectiveness, said Canada does lag behind when comparisons are made to the American market from when they first get drugs, but "there are some very important caveats you need to consider ... The first is that the decision to submit to the regulator, Health Canada or the (American) FDA, is not made by governments, it's made by companies."
They choose when to apply for a drug to enter a market, and with Canada sitting as the ninth largest, "companies will always choose to go with the larger U.S., Europe and then Japan markets first," said Tadrous, who is with U of T's Leslie Dan Faculty of Pharmacy.
"Doug Ford isn't going to be able to make us on par with the size of the American market, just given our population size compared to the U.S., so a lot of this is kind of outside of the control of government," added Matthew Herder, director of the Health Justice Institute at Dalhousie University.
Tadrous noted that in the U.S., once the FDA (Food and Drug Administration) approves a drug, it's allowed to enter the market. Here, a similar process occurs with Health Canada but differs after that stage. With so much government spending on drugs, it must go through a health technology assessment, and the provinces then negotiate the price together. That process is currently going through improvements, he added.
That process "is slower because (drug companies) want to get listed on the public formulary," he said. "If (governments) resource that a little more, that might go a bit faster," he said.
He noted there are already special access programs for innovative drugs - especially for rare cancers - to get them to patients quickly, with co-operation and additional resources for the reviews, something Ford has pointed to as a way to get priority approval for more medications.
Improved co-operation and simultaneous assessments between provincial governments helped during the COVID pandemic to get vaccines and medications to market faster, noted Jennifer Lake, a professor in U of T's pharmacy faculty.
Herder, who is also a professor of pharmacology at Dalhousie, said what could make a difference is all premiers getting onside with a system of national pharmacare, eliminating the time it takes for each province to decide what is covered.
"If the premiers want to get together (Tuesday) in Halifax and really have a serious discussion, they should be talking about how they can support pharmacare instead of opposing it," he said, as some provinces have criticized it as federal overreach into an area of provincial jurisdiction.
Cancer groups have said patients are desperate to get access to the latest treatments, and are urging the provinces to take action.
"Today, people with blood cancer are offered the best of science and the worst of bureaucracy. Despite tremendous advances in research and an incredible number of revolutionary therapies approved in Canada, our crumbling health-care system denies most patients access to these life-saving treatments," said Jessy Ranger of Myeloma Canada and Christina Sit of the Leukemia & Lymphoma Society of Canada, in a joint statement to the Star.
"The danger they face is just one example of what awaits Canadians impacted by cancer if our governments fail to take concerted action and urgently address the issues which sicken our health-care systems."
Tadrous cautioned, however, that there is "a very important nuance of risk and balance ... We want to make sure that it's well studied in safety, and it's not rushed."
CAPTION: Premier Doug Ford speaks to reporters upon arriving late for a meeting between Canada's premiers and Indigenous leaders at the Council of the Federation meetings in Halifax on July 15, 2024.

Document 302 of 500

LCBO strike continues, stores won't open Friday as planned but customers can order online

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/lcbo-strike-continues-stores-wont-open-friday-as/docview/3081419917/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=LCBO+strike+continues%2C+stores+won%27t+open+Friday+as+planned+but+customers+can+order+online&amp;title=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-07-15&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Even though there were rumblings the LCBO was set to open 32 selected locations Friday, July 19 for a few days, to allow restaurants to restock, that plan has changed.
On its website, the LCBO now says "no LCBO retail stores will open throughout the duration of the strike."
"Workers saw this announcement coming," Ontario Public Service Employees Union (OPSEU) said in a press release. "Without LCBO workers, the LCBO doesn't work."
The strike is now entering its second week, after contract negotiations broke down between 9,000 LCBO workers with OPSEU. The strike began at midnight July 5.
"We understand that this strike impacts our customers and communities," the union said. "Our members want stores to be open and to be back at work as soon as possible. Our members miss their regulars and want to get back to work. It is on Ford to end this strike, after all - he forced it to justify his rushed alcohol everywhere scheme."
In the meantime, LCBO patrons needing to find alcohol supplies are encouraged to go online to lcbo.com, or the free mobile app, sign in and set up an order.
"Because of this success and because of our confidence in our ability to continue serving retail customers online, we will be reallocating the personnel that were planned to open LCBO retail stores for in-store shopping to other parts of the operations to further enhance support," the company said in a July 14 press release.
"This pivot means that we will be able to improve how we serve Ontario bars and restaurants to help increase product selection, availability, and expedited delivery. Details will be shared directly with these customers."
There are some limitations for customers when ordering online.
For spirits: up to six or 12 units are allowed, depending on productFor wine: only 24 units per product are allowedBeer, cider and ready-to-drink: 96 units per product are allowed.
The strike does not impact LCBO Specialty Services and duty-free, and LCBO Convenience Outlets and grocery stores already authorized throughout the province.
On Monday, the province announced ready-to-drink alcoholic beverages and large beer pack sizes would become available at grocery stores sooner than expected - starting Thursday.
By the end of October, every convenience, grocery and big-box store in Ontario will be able to sell beer, cider, wine and ready-to-drink alcoholic beverages if they choose to do so, the government said.
Back in 2015, the province first permitted beer sales at grocery stores.
And a month ago, Premier Doug Ford announced he was expanding to allow every convenience, big box and grocery store to sell beer, wine and ready-to-drink products this fall.
"Spirits, like vodka, gin and whiskey will continue to be sold at the LCBO," Ford said at the time.
The union said its bargaining team is "prepared to return to the bargaining table as soon as it is clear that Ford and the LCBO are prepared to bargain in good faith a future that will protect good jobs and protect public revenues as well as create more stable and permanent jobs."
The LCBO thanks customers for their flexibility and patience as the company works to meet expected service timelines.
"We want this strike to end, remain committed to reaching an agreement with OPSEU, and encourage them to respond to our fair offer," the company said.
CAPTION: Striking LCBO employees from around Hamilton walk on the picket line.

Document 303 of 500

Trudeau government's carbon rebates went out Monday - but one major bank still isn't using their official name

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/trudeau-governments-carbon-rebates-went-out/docview/3081419235/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Trudeau+government%27s+carbon+rebates+went+out+Monday+-+but+one+major+bank+still+isn%27t+using+their+official+name&amp;title=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-07-15&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: All but one of Canada's major banks have adopted clear labels for the government's quarterly carbon rebate payments after a lengthy battle with Ottawa concluded with changes to a law that now forces them to comply.
On Monday, eligible Canadians received their quarterly rebates, which the federal government sends out to offset increased costs of heat, fuel and other goods due to its carbon pricing plan.
They are the first rebate payments to reach Canadians since the Trudeau government amended the Financial Administration Act to compel banks to label the payments in a way specified by Ottawa.
The change is among a host of tweaks, including a rebrand of the rebates first reported by the Star, introduced by the Trudeau government this year, that aimed to address points of contention and communication mishaps with the politically controversial carbon pricing regime.
The Liberals insist the Canada Carbon Rebates return more money to most households than the carbon levy costs them, while preserving an incentive across the economy to slash greenhouse gas emissions that are causing the climate crisis. Meanwhile, opposition politicians have blamed the so-called "carbon tax" for juicing recent spikes in inflation. Consumer and industrial carbon pricing is expected to account for about one-third of Canada's emissions reductions by 2030.
Ottawa's decision to force the hands of banks came after the Star reported some were "being less than helpful" in response to government requests to label the payments "CDACarbonRebate" in English and "CarboneRemiseCA" in French, citing character limits, bilingual requirements and "various purported technical reasons," according to a senior government official.
"Most frustrating," the official said at the time, is that "one of the big banks also says they aren't willing to make these changes at all," although they did not specify which bank.
Now only CIBC, which labels the rebate payments as "Deposit Canada" on its customers' accounts, has yet to make the change.
CIBC spokesperson Tom Wallis told the Star the bank is "on track to implement a labelling change by later this summer." Because CIBC has committed to comply by the next rebate payment in October, a government official who spoke to the Star on condition of anonymity said they don't expect the federal government to pursue any enforcement actions, but will track the situation closely.
The Liberals hope the bank labelling will increase awareness of the rebates. In Ontario, those eligible receive a base rebate of $140, with an additional $70 for a second adult in the family and $35 for each child under 19.
"There are still many Canadians who are receiving the benefits of the Canada Carbon Rebate but, until now, may not yet know it," Environment Minister Steven Guilbeault said in a statement to the Star. "Getting the banks to properly label the payments will go a long way for Canadians to see for themselves that the Canada Carbon Rebate leaves them better off, while they are helping to fight climate change."
That government-wide push has also included more aggressive promotion of the rebates, including on social media and in MPs' communications with constituents.
A video shared by Environment Canada on Monday says disinformation is "one of the biggest challenges" facing carbon pricing. The government source pointed to a flyer distributed recently by Calgary Conservative MP Greg McLean, which claimed "Canada's carbon tax will cost Alberta families an additional $2,943 in 2024-25."
That number, a spokesperson for the Parliamentary Budget Officer told the Star, includes both the direct costs and indirect economic impacts of the price on carbon, but does not include the quarterly rebates Canadians receive. The real number should be $911 on average, although low-income Albertans who pollute less would ultimate see a gain. With only the direct costs of the price on pollution accounted for, the average Albertan would receive $559 more than they pay, the spokesperson said.
In a statement, Conservative critic for finance Jasraj Singh Hallan accused the Liberals of lying about the costs of carbon pricing.
"The Liberals are desperate to keep raising their carbon tax, but if they really believe it isn't hurting Canadians who are struggling to heat their homes and feed their families, they should call a carbon tax election and let Canadians decide."
With files from Alex Ballingall
CAPTION: Federal Environment and Climate Change Minister Steven Guilbeault speaks at a news conference in St. John's on July 10, 2024.

Subject: Rebates; Canadians; Costs; Emissions; Carbon; Public officials; Climate change; Tax elections; Environmental tax

Business indexing term: Subject: Rebates Costs Tax elections Environmental tax

Company / organization: Name: Canadian Imperial Bank of Commerce; NAICS: 522110

Document 304 of 500

Doug Ford's changes will allow 'ready-to-drink' cocktails in 450 supermarkets by next week

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/doug-fords-changes-will-allow-ready-drink/docview/3080596119/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Doug+Ford%27s+changes+will+allow+%27ready-to-drink%27+cocktails+in+450+supermarkets+by+next+week&amp;title=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-07-15&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: In a salvo at striking LCBO workers, Premier Doug Ford is pushing up the sale of "ready-to-drink" cocktails in 450 supermarkets to this week.
"This is an important milestone for grocery retailers and consumers alike as we continue our work modernizing Ontario's alcohol marketplace," Finance Minister Peter Bethlenfalvy said Monday.
"As the next step in the government's plan to give people in Ontario more choice and convenience, licensed grocery stores will be able to order ready-to-drink beverages and large beer pack sizes starting Thursday ... and begin selling them immediately upon arrival," said Bethlenfalvy.
"This new timeline accelerates the first phase of the government's plan to expand alcohol sales to grocery, convenience and big-box stores by allowing the 450 grocery stores that are currently licensed to sell beer, cider or wine to sell them once they arrive in store, rather than Aug. 1," he said.
That means Ontarians should be able to actually purchase the ready-to-drink beverages - such as Bacardi Breeze and Mike's Hard Lemonade - in supermarkets as early as next week.
For the time being, such drinks would only be available in the 450 stores licensed by former premier Kathleen Wynne's Liberals in 2015.
The gambit comes as LCBO chief executive George Soleas on Monday blamed the 10-day liquor store strike on the Ontario Public Service Employees Union (OPSEU).
Soleas insisted the government-owned Liquor Control Board of Ontario wants to get back to contract bargaining with the representatives of more than 9,000 striking workers.
But JP Hornick, president of OPSEU, said the onus is on Ford to get things on track.
Hornick told the Star on Monday "it is absolutely not true" the union walked away from the table, saying "the government squandered - and the LCBO squandered - the second day of mediation entirely, and then tabled a proposal that they knew wouldn't hit the mark.
"The mediator, to my understanding, determined that we were at an impasse and called off talks," said the OPSEU chief, adding union negotiators "had indicated we had readiness to talk, but (the LCBO) would have to come back with improvements in the proposal."
"So there were two main issues at that point, and they remain the same - protection for revenue and job security. We want to protect the jobs, and grow the revenue ... We are absolutely committed to coming back to the table."
But Hornick said the union has not heard directly from the LCBO or the mediator about a resumption of talks.
The labour dispute has shuttered the LCBO's 680 stores for the first time in the 97-year history of the government booze monopoly.
"OPSEU has repeatedly stated in the media that they did not walk away from negotiations and that they are willing to return to the table at any moment. I want you to know this is simply not true," the LCBO CEO said.
"The truth is LCBO's expectation was that both parties would work hard and in good faith to reach a tentative agreement before the union's strike deadline at 12:01 a.m. on July 5," he said.
Soleas said OPSEU told the liquor giant "no deal was possible unless the government changed its current policy on ready-to-drink beverages in grocery and conveniences stores, and revoked regulations passed by the government and legislature of Ontario."
He stressed the LCBO does "not set government policy and/or alcohol regulation in Ontario."
The strike came six weeks after Ford announced he would pay The Beer Store $225 million to expedite the previously announced Dec. 31, 2025 deadline for expanded liberalization of alcohol sales.
Under his changes, beer, wine and premixed beverages will be sold at 8,500 licensed supermarkets, convenience stores and big-box outlets by Oct. 31.
Currently, the LCBO has a monopoly on most "ready-to-drink" alcoholic beverages, which is the fastest growing booze category.
Ford told the union last Wednesday that "if they want to negotiate over (ready-to-drink beverages), the deal's off ... that ship has sailed - it's halfway across Lake Ontario."
But the premier has repeatedly said the LCBO would remain a public asset and retain its exclusive rights for the sale of spirits, such as whisky, vodka, gin, rum and tequila.
And it would control the wholesale distribution of wine and ready-to-drink products to all private retailers.
Last week, Ford said the LCBO had made a fair offer to OPSEU, including seven per cent raises over three years and a pledge to move 400 casual employees into full-time jobs.
Soleas said Monday that "the union has never responded to the LCBO's proposal."
"The offer stands and we remain prepared to negotiate collective bargaining issues," he said.
On Sunday, the LCBO scrapped plans to re-open 32 stores for limited hours later this week.
Instead, it will focus resources to online shopping on its website.
NDP Leader Marit Stiles, Liberal Leader Bonnie Crombie and Green Leader Mike Schreiner have blamed Ford's booze liberalization push for the job action.
This is a developing story.
CAPTION: LCBO workers picket at the Queens Quay flagship store location in Toronto.

Document 305 of 500

'NEW TIMELINE': Ontario Premier Doug Ford government releases update on grocery stores selling ready-to-drink alcohol and large beer packs

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/new-timeline-ontario-premier-doug-ford-government/docview/3080595444/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=%27NEW+TIMELINE%27%3A+Ontario+Premier+Doug+Ford+government+releases+update+on+grocery+stores+selling+ready-to-drink+alcohol+and+large+beer+packs&amp;title=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-07-15&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Ontario residents will be able to get ready-to-drink alcoholic beverages and large beer pack sizes at grocery stores sooner than expected, the government announced Monday (July 15).
Licensed grocery stores will be able to order ready-to-drink beverages and large beer pack sizes starting Thursday, July 18, and begin selling them immediately upon arrival, the government announced in a release.
This "new timeline" accelerates the first phase of the government's plan to expand alcohol sales to grocery, convenience and big-box stores by allowing the 450 grocery stores currently licensed to sell beer, cider or wine to sell them once they arrive in store, rather than Aug. 1, the government said.
Finance Minister Peter Bethlenfalvy said Ontario businesses produce more than 80 per cent of the ready-to-drink beverages sold in the province.
"This is an important milestone for grocery retailers and consumers alike as we continue our work modernizing Ontario's alcohol marketplace," he said in a statement.
By the end of October, every convenience, grocery and big-box store in Ontario will be able to sell beer, cider, wine and ready-to-drink alcoholic beverages if they choose to do so, the government said.
This announcement comes in the midst of an LCBO strike that has closed LCBO stores across the province.
Restaurants Canada is urging the LCBO, the government and striking workers to keep negotiating to reach a quick resolution and bring the labour dispute to an end.
"The summer season is a critical time for restaurants and bars who rely on patio weather, tourists and sports fans to visit our establishments and support the local community and economy," Restaurants Canada said in a release.
Restaurants and bars in Ontario rely on the LCBO as their primary provider for wine and spirits, with alcohol sales accounting for a significant part of their revenue, Restaurants Canada said.
"Potential disruptions in alcohol supply reaching our establishments could have significant impacts on our sector, particularly at a time when operators are already faced with so many challenges," said Kris Barnier, vice-president Central Canada at Restaurants Canada, in a news release.
"Many restaurants are still in recovery from pandemic debt, and now dealing with a one-two punch of weaker consumer spending combined with operating cost pressures that have grown by more than 20 per cent in the last two years alone."
CAPTION: Ontario Premier Doug Ford

Document 306 of 500

Alcohol is to Doug Ford as gold was to Midas

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/alcohol-is-doug-ford-as-gold-was-midas/docview/3080587898/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Alcohol+is+to+Doug+Ford+as+gold+was+to+Midas&amp;title=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-07-15&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Alcohol is to Ontario Premier Doug Ford what gold was to Midas, what artisanal coffee is to millennials, what Ivanka Trump is to Donald Trump. It's an essential material, the core around which life swirls.
Now I don't understand the concept of craving something that is readily available to the point of forcing. We can obtain booze at home, at other people's homes, at the LCBO, at other stores, in restaurants and bars and all day at the airport. We can drink it in parks.
Same with coffee. Ubiquitous. Ivanka Trump and her husbandette moved to Florida to be closer to her father. Reach out and there it is.
That I have long COVID and wine now tastes like room-temperature bear urine is immaterial. (Why is the bear in a room though?) Ford makes sure alcohol is as reachable for me as it is for alcoholics desperately trying to avoid booze-triggers.
When the LCBO strike began, Ford even published what looked like a road map to perdition, guiding Ontario booze hounds to Wine Rack stores and such, the kind of comedy gold we remember from his brother Rob Ford's days as mayor. Not so long ago, how we longed for a similar map of pharmacies stocked with COVID vaccines.
But now I wonder. Perhaps I couldn't see the forest for the beer jokes. Maybe it's not that Ford, who says he doesn't drink, doesn't like the booze so much as he detests the Ontario Public Service Employees Union (OPSEU), which represents 9,000 striking LCBO workers and donates to his opponents.
Similarly, the existence of The Beer Store offended Ford so he paid it at least $225 million to let beer be sold in a wider world.
But why? Because The Beer Store was ugly, soul-destroying and industrial? Nah. Ford doesn't care about the esthetics of getting hammered. Because he has a principled opposition to monopolies? No, Ford has no principles, not as such. But he has preferences.
He likes privatization. Small-minded Conservatives like privatized highways, private retail and outsourced jobs.
If there had been a buyer wanting to run the Ontario Science Centre in Don Mills as a going concern, attached to, yes, a condo development, maybe he would have given it to them as long as they built a new roof. I wait agog.
A new Disneyfied science centre at Ontario Place is what Ford envisions. It's his idea of family fun.
It would be a place to store the kids while prosperous parents spend the day at that private Austrian spa, drinking small-batch alcohols, soaking, gambling, and contracting a urinary tract infection from poorly cleaned tubs. No, a nail fungus. Trichophyton rubrum will be our word du jour.
I have a stock tank at home but right now it's full of zucchini plants. So there I'd sit at Therme, staring out at Lake Ontario and sloppily singing old Blue Rodeo songs in a privatized tank, thinking about Ford.
What does our premier want? Better yet, what do we want in a premier? A Keir Starmer, I think. Someone who'd put an end to all the nonsense on Day One, as the new British prime minister did. No one will be sent to Rwanda. It's over, he said.
We want a premier who prioritizes health care, education, housing and transit along with a green plan for climate change. What we have is a strange man obsessed with booze, online gambling, and raising speed limits while giving good Ontario land to real estate developers building great stupid swathes of oversized homes for buyers who'll be sunk in regret by 2032.
We want a sensible person. I'm tired of writing columns about peripheral Ford entertainments. I want a government based on thought and substance. I won't get it of course but this is what I want.
CAPTION: Premier Doug Ford moves a flat of beer cans during a photo-op at Cool Beer Brewing Company on July 10.

Document 307 of 500

Privatization sins committed by Ford

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/privatization-sins-committed-ford/docview/3080194220/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=unknown&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Privatization+sins+committed+by+Ford&amp;title=The+Spectator&amp;issn=11899417&amp;date=2024-07-15&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.9&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Spectator&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: After six years of Doug Ford in power, here are six privatized provincial things that will never be the same:
ServiceOntario outlets are moving into big chain stores.
The LCBO is under pressure from private beer and wine outlets.
Private health-care clinics are thriving.
The old Ontario Place is never coming back and the Ontario Science Centre can't be saved.
Private construction firms have turned public infrastructure into big business.
Ontario Hydro isn't the public utility it once was.
Six sins committed by the premier. How did he get away with remaking Ontario in his own right-wing image?
Answer: He didn't - he's just doing more or less what previous premiers did before him.
That's the paradox of politics in this province. While the six changes listed above largely predate Ford's turn in power, most people and pundits talk as if he has orchestrated a revolutionary U-turn in Ontario's political culture and economic outlook.
Thanks to his pugnacious persona - brash and in your face, populist and provocative - people assume the worst and blame him for all of it. If you listen to opposition and unions you'd think that Ford's agenda of privatization is unprecedented in the province's history.
In fact, the premier's less bombastic Liberal predecessors - Dalton McGuinty and Kathleen Wynne - got the ball rolling and the beer flowing in many of these areas. That's politics - and that's perception.
To be sure, much of the premier's agenda is controversial. To be clear, many of the things he says are nonsensical - from liberating the Greenbelt to axing the carbon levy.
But in 2024, it's surely time for an honest conversation about privatization - where it helps, where it hurts. The least we could do is have an informed debate, rather than misinformed diatribes and misleading dogma.
The biggest and boldest liberation of beer and wine in Ontario's history came under Wynne's Liberal government, when she phased in supermarket sales that cut the (privately held) Beer Store down to size. Ford's incessant boasts about bringing beer to corner stores are a pale echo of Wynne's willingness, a decade ago, to stare down the big breweries (plus their union allies and the NDP) by easing the stranglehold of The Beer Store's unholy duopoly with the LCBO.
Equally with Ontario Place, people are blaming Ford for finishing it off as a public enterprise. Yet it was McGuinty's Liberals who shuttered the site more than a decade ago because attendance and revenues had dropped to unjustifiable levels.
Belatedly, Ford has embraced a long-standing recommendation from public servants to relocate the outdated Ontario Science Centre to a reborn Ontario Place, alongside a water park rebranded as a Therme "spa." Predictably, that has raised the hackles of the hair-shirt brigade, but what business does government have in the waterpark business?
It's a similar story of perception versus reality - and history - with ServiceOntario, which helps people renew driver's licences and OHIP cards. When the media reacted with astonishment that some outlets would be relocated to Staples stores, I happened to be shopping at my local Canadian Tire location where - shock - a ServiceOntario branch, already privately owned, has been located for years. Same with public-private partnerships for building new hospitals, which the Liberals long relied on.
None of this is to argue that privatization and devolution are good or bad on ideological grounds. The point is to consider it on efficiency and equity grounds.
The debate gets more complicated with private health-care clinics. Opposition politicians and health-care unions love to scaremonger about people being asked for credit cards instead of OHIP cards, but private delivery of publicly financed health care (by self-employed doctors in stand-alone clinics or labs) is as old as medicare itself.
It matters little, from a public policy perspective, whether you buy stamps or renew a driver's licence from a government building, a Staples or a Canadian Tire. It matters a great deal whether people are getting health care from the right provider without being out of pocket.
But after all these years (not just the last six under Ford), isn't it time for an intellectually honest conversation about the perils of privatization, versus the exaggeration about experimentation?
CAPTION:

CREDIT: Martin Regg Cohn OPINION;

Canada Briefs: Sexual assault charges laidLCBO keeping stores closed

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/canada-briefs/docview/3080194176/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Canada+Briefs%3A+Sexual+assault+charges+laidLCBO+keeping+stores+closed&amp;title=The+Spectator&amp;issn=11899417&amp;date=2024-07-15&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.6&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Spectator&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Ontario
Kidnap victim found safe
Police say a man who was allegedly kidnapped from outside his workplace north of Toronto has been found in good health, though the search is still on for multiple suspects in the case. York Regional Police say Roberto Marchioni, 57, was found at around 3 a.m. Sunday near Highway 401 in east Toronto. He had sustained minor injuries and was treated at the scene. Police previously alleged Marchioni was leaving his workplace in Vaughan at 2:15 Friday morning when two suspects forced him into a grey 2020 Honda CRV LX. They say a black Dodge Ram pickup may also have been involved.
Sexual assault charges laid
Police say an educational assistant at St. Mary's Catholic School in the City of Welland has been charged with sexual assault and interference over an alleged intimate relationship with a 14-year-old male. Niagara Regional Police say a 48-year-old female was in a relationship with the victim, who is a former student. Detectives have been investigating the allegations of sexual assault and interference since June of this year.
LCBO keeping stores closed
Ontario's main liquor retailer said Sunday it has abandoned plans to open a handful of locations for in-store shopping amid the ongoing strike by thousands of its employees. The Liquor Control Board of Ontario previously said it planned to open 32 stores three days a week with limited hours if the strike by members of the Ontario Public Service Employees Union went on for longer than two weeks. LCBO workers represented by OPSEU walked off the job on July 5 after negotiations broke down. The LCBO says it has been able to serve retail customers since the strike began through online shopping, fulfilling orders within a week. OPSEU said Friday it's fighting for the future of the LCBO and Ontario and repeated criticisms of Premier Doug Ford's plans to open up the province's alcohol market by allowing ready-to-drink cocktails to be sold outside LCBO stores.
News Services

Document 309 of 500

Doug Ford to focus on getting medications approved faster at annual premiers' conference

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/doug-ford-focus-on-getting-medications-approved/docview/3080841295/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Doug+Ford+to+focus+on+getting+medications+approved+faster+at+annual+premiers%27+conference&amp;title=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-07-14&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: HALIFAX - Getting medications approved and into patients' hands can take about two years in Canada - when other countries approve in half that time, or less - and it's a concern Doug Ford wants to make a key issue at this year's meeting of premiers.
The Ontario premier arrives Monday morning for the annual gathering of all 10 provincial and three territorial leaders, hosted this summer by Nova Scotia Premier Tim Houston, the current chair of the Council of the Federation. Ford takes over as head of the federation in August, and will host next summer's meeting in Ontario.
"To ensure Canada maintains its advantage as a leader in health care innovation, we also need to get new publicly funded medicines to people as fast as possible," a Ford spokesperson said Saturday.
"Currently, patients in Canada wait almost two years to access life-saving breakthrough medicines, a year longer than in other developed countries, placing us last in the G7. As Premier Ford takes over as chair of the Council of the Federation, one of his focuses will be doing everything possible to give Canadian patients the same timely access to life-saving treatments as patients in the rest of the world."
The 13 premiers will meet with Indigenous leaders on Monday before holding a number of closed-door sessions on a range of topics over the following two days, including energy resources and independence, arctic security and sovereignty - amid worries of ongoing Russian aggression - as well as the U.S. election and what to expect from a Biden or Trump administration, especially in terms of protectionist trade policies.
They are also expected to address "revitalizing co-operative federalism in Canada," which Houston recently wrote to Prime Minister Justin Trudeau about on behalf of all premiers, asking him to "work with us in a true partnership."
"Canadians are facing immense and complex challenges that require all orders of government to work together," Houston said in his July 11 letter.
"To revive the collaborative spirit of the federation, we urge the federal government to refrain from unilateral actions in areas of provincial and territorial jurisdiction, particularly in health care, education and housing. In areas of shared responsibility, timely outreach and engagement will ensure that our respective programs, funding and policies complement one another and are effective in each jurisdiction across Canada."
Houston said "when all governments work together, we can find the best solutions for the challenges we face. This meeting will allow premiers to engage on the issues that matter most to Canadians."
Ford's spokesperson also said "in the face of global uncertainty, it's never been more important for Ontario and Canada to have a secure and affordable homegrown energy supply that people and businesses can rely on."
Ford "is keen to discuss the future of clean, reliable nuclear energy, including small modular reactors, and how provinces can work together to make it easier to build energy infrastructure across borders."
Newly appointed Ontario Energy Minister Stephen Lecce recently held talks with U.S. Department of Energy officials and has since reached out to U.S. Secretary of Energy Jennifer Granholm for a meeting, in part to highlight the province's technology as well as exporting nuclear energy to the states.
The country's three northern premiers are hoping to highlight their unique needs on housing and infrastructure, as well as broader security issues.
"The NWT has benefitted greatly from the provincial-territorial relationships at the CoF table," said Agata Gutkowska, press secretary for Northwest Territories Premier R.J. Simpson.
"With the system of consensus government to the co-management of lands and resources legislation with Indigenous governments the NWT is a unique jurisdiction in Canada."
Yukon Premier Ranj Pillai is looking for better collaboration with the federal government and what the provinces and territories can do as a group to foster that given their "strong and productive" relations, as well as looking at keeping Arctic sovereignty "top of mind for all Canadians."
That's also a major concern for Nunavut Premier P.J. Akeeagok, who has been able to move new housing into high gear but more needs to be done to encourage people to live in remote communities - including investments in things like hydro or even fibre optics - which is part of the broader plan to boost security in the north.
That issue received unanimous support from all 13 premiers at their meeting two summers ago, just months after Russia first invaded Ukraine.
"Arctic sovereignty and security are priorities for Nunavut at this year's national premiers' conference," Akeeagok said in a statement to the Star.
"Our territory wants to see investments in multi-use infrastructure such as deep sea ports that can address Canada's security needs while revitalizing Nunavut's economic potential. These are nation-building projects that address reconciliation while serving local Arctic communities."
Last summer, when the premiers met in Winnipeg, Ford's main focus was getting Ottawa to work with provinces to help big infrastructure projects move along faster, in particular to reduce delays and duplication, especially on environmental assessments.
In March, the province and federal government ended their spat over Ottawa's insistence on environmental assessments on Highway 413 that Ontario officials said was causing construction delays.
The two levels of government instead signed a memorandum of understanding to create a collaborative working group to look at addressing environmental impacts. That came after a Supreme Court decision that found Ottawa had "overstepped" its authority on the provincial highway project, a key campaign promise of Ford's.
CAPTION: Premier Doug Ford speaks at Cool Beer Brewing Company in Toronto on July 10, 2024.Halifax waterfront: Artwork swiped from a city pub was restored after the owners used social media to appeal for its return.

Document 310 of 500

Justin Trudeau is reckoning with the decision of his political life. Here's why few know what he will do

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/justin-trudeau-is-reckoning-with-decision-his/docview/3079952865/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Justin+Trudeau+is+reckoning+with+the+decision+of+his+political+life.+Here%27s+why+few+know+what+he+will+do&amp;title=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-07-14&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: This is a moment of reckoning for Prime Minister Justin Trudeau.
The question of whether to stay or go, or to deliver some other kind of dramatic change the Liberal caucus is clamouring for, is one of the most sensitive and solitary exercises a prime minister can undertake.
Sometimes the moment arrives when bitter internal rivalries push a leader out, as happened to Jean Chrétien. Sometimes it comes on the night of a general election defeat, as it did for Paul Martin and Stephen Harper.
Sometimes it is imposed on a prime minister by devastating poll results, as it was for Brian Mulroney, or as in Trudeau's case now, by dint of a brutal byelection loss that telegraphs future trouble for his party.
However it arrives, it is a decision laden with political and policy implications. At the same time, it is deeply personal and is contemplated in privacy if not secrecy, in large part due to the nature of the job.
Most cabinet ministers would not be in the loop, and sources who spoke to the Star said Trudeau has not shown them his cards.
That makes sense, said several sources, including current and former government officials, all of whom spoke to the Star on condition they not be identified in order to speak frankly.
One said it is "very difficult to have candid conversations with anyone" in cabinet or caucus about it, not just because he might want the element of surprise, but because the prime minister would rightly fear immediate leaks.
If Trudeau doesn't choose a major cabinet shuffle or staff shakeup at the Prime Minister's Office or Liberal party headquarters, and opts instead for his own departure, his intention would likely only be known to a couple of people, most suggested.
Unlike the choice to run for party leadership, when a politician must consult widely, cajole support, and call on friends to commit their energies, ideas and money to campaigning, another source said, "Leaving is a solitary act."
"You cannot for one second - unless there are people very, very, very close to you - hint that you're going, because you still have to command respect and be able to get people to follow you.
"You are there until the minute you decide you're not. There's no (Mont-)Tremblant-type of exercise that's going to go on, where 15 people are going to sit around a hot tub and decide whether or not he's going to stay or go."
That's a reference to Trudeau's memoir, "Common Ground," in which he describes a three-day retreat with a couple of dozen friends and advisers. They puzzled through the future of the Liberal party and whether Trudeau should dive into the leadership contest to replace Michael Ignatieff, the leader who had dragged down Liberals to third place in the House of Commons.
Trudeau wrote that he learned while travelling internationally with his father, prime minister Pierre Trudeau, to make big decisions after listening to many voices and opinions.
But Trudeau's own leadership style since he took the top office hasn't always followed that path.
Current and former caucus members describe Trudeau as someone with whom they do not have a close relationship.
Three former cabinet ministers have written books that go farther, and call Trudeau's approach to government overly centralized, and reliant on only a few trusted advisers. Jody Wilson-Raybould, Bill Morneau and Marc Garneau portray Trudeau as a distant, aloof prime minister with whom they never had the access or the input they expected.
Others disagree. They say Trudeau is open to challenge and debate, yet ultimately is one to take decisions, certain in his own judgment.
A lot of people think they know Justin Trudeau.
Few really do.
Trudeau himself has described a kind of personal defence mechanism acquired over a lifetime in the public eye as the son of a prime minister.
He is swayed by neither his most adamant haters nor admirers.
An introvert despite the enthusiastic grip-and-grin impression he leaves as he glad-hands his way through a crowd, Trudeau has a broad circle of people he knows and has relied upon over the years.
But he has only a small circle of people he remains close to and trusts.
By their own accounts, once someone leaves his inner circle, they're out. It's not that Trudeau dumps them. Rather, they become distanced or removed from his day-to-day world, and are no longer among those he turns to for advice.
And now, for all Trudeau has talked about "engaging" with Liberal colleagues and caucus members following last month's defeat by the Conservatives in Toronto-St. Paul's, most do not know what he is thinking as he mulls the way forward.
After nine years in government, a yearlong plunge in public opinion polls, and the humbling loss of a long-held Liberal riding in downtown Toronto, some who consider themselves his friends wonder where his head is at.
Publicly, Trudeau says only that he believes now is the time for the Liberal government to double down on delivering services that improve the lives of Canadians hit hard by inflation and high interest rates, and that's where his focus lies. Trudeau frames his determination to push through his progressive agenda as an antidote to the loss of public faith in leaders and institutions, to democratic disengagement, and as a counterweight to the rise of the populist right - as he sees it.
That strikes some Liberals as hubris.
One said it seems like Trudeau thinks there isn't anyone else who can do that job, but there always is, the source said, and "there has to be. That's the nature of democracy. Someone else always has to be" able to do the job.
Another Liberal who supports Trudeau described grappling with the dilemma of "what's the best option for him, what's the best option for the party, what's the best option for the country."
"I still think he's the best option for the country, and he still represents the best option for the party," that person said. However it is not clear to him if staying on "is the right call" for the prime minister personally or if Trudeau should leave now with his head high and a strong policy legacy intact.
Trudeau can be a risk-taker. He took a big risk in calling a snap election, mid-pandemic, in 2021.
But since the byelection loss that triggered angst in Liberal ranks, he has not taken the risk of convening a national caucus meeting, as many are calling for, to let members of Parliament vent or let MPs in on his thinking.
To some, that makes tactical sense in that it keeps nervous MPs away from the national media.
To others, it is strategically the wrong thing to do. They say it makes Trudeau seem unwilling or unable to take the chance that he can't hold caucus together.
Still, Trudeau likes to surprise, as when he announced he would decriminalize cannabis, required all Liberal candidates to support abortion rights, bought a pipeline, and ditched electoral reform.
He is unafraid of upending Liberal orthodoxies - ousting senators from the Liberal caucus, creating a Senate appointment process intended to remove patronage, or dropping fees for party membership to expand his base.
At the NATO summit in Washington, he announced Canada would meet its defence spending promise to the alliance by 2032, a declaration he has avoided making for nearly nine years.
By his own description, Trudeau likes to be underestimated - as he was in his political curtain-raiser boxing match with a young Conservative senator.
Now in his 13th year of elected office, has he ruled anything out? Probably not.
Trudeau once said he never viewed himself as a lifelong politician, and wasn't his father who spent nearly 20 years in parliament. Near the end of his first term, then still with a majority government, the prime minister privately expressed that whenever he left politics, he would have no regrets.

He has even mused about taking up some kind of teaching role in a post-political career.
But at age 52, is Justin Trudeau about to give up what is probably the best job he'll ever have?
Text me if you know.
CAPTION: Prime Minister Justin Trudeau arrives for a meeting at the 2024 North Atlantic Treaty Organization summit in Washington on July 11, 2024.

Subject: Political leadership; Prime ministers

People: Trudeau, Justin

Company / organization: Name: North Atlantic Treaty Organization--NATO; NAICS: 928120

Document 311 of 500

Sunny ways? How the Trudeau government slept in during the 'dawn of the solar age'

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/sunny-ways-how-trudeau-government-slept-during/docview/3079872292/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Sunny+ways%3F+How+the+Trudeau+government+slept+in+during+the+%27dawn+of+the+solar+age%27&amp;title=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-07-13&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: It might come as a surprise that solar power will provide about six per cent of the world's electricity this year.
After all, the world is still using record amounts of oil.
And among alternatives to fossil fuels, solar continues to trail hydro, nuclear and wind in Canada as a source of clean power.
Some governments have put up roadblocks to rapid adoption of solar.
For instance, the U.S. government this year increased tariffs on solar equipment from China, the world's biggest supplier.
Closer to home, the governments of Ontario and Alberta imposed restrictions on renewable energy development other than hydro and nuclear.
If the world is at the "dawn of the solar age," as The Economist recently declared, Canada didn't get the memo.
Canada relies on solar power for just 0.5 per cent of total energy consumption, lagging most major economies including the U.S. (2.4 per cent), Germany (5.0 per cent), Japan (5.2 per cent), and Australia (7.0 per cent).
Yet how quickly realities can change.
A reality for Ontario is that dynamic economic and population growth require that the province more than double its power production by mid-century.
So, Ontario Premier Doug Ford has committed to a multibillion-dollar expansion of the province's nuclear power capacity.
And in a reversal of his 2018 cancellation of green power projects, Ford is now encouraging development of 2,000 megawatts (MW) of renewable power projects, including solar, wind and bioenergy.
The Paris-based International Energy Agency (IEA) forecasts that global solar electricity generation "is set to almost triple over the 2022-2027 period, surpassing coal and becoming the largest source of power capacity in the world."
And by the 2040s, solar, already the cheapest source of electricity, is expected to be about half as expensive as today's least costly power sources.
A virtuous circle is at work here.
As solar capacity increases, the cost of producing solar power drops and so does the price. That increases demand for cheap solar power, which in turn spurs generation of still more and even cheaper solar power.
Another reality is Canada's enormous potential in solar power generation.
Canada is already a clean-energy leader with its abundance of hydro and nuclear power.
That helps explain the lack of a Canadian federal commitment to solar to match that of governments in Europe, Asia-Pacific and Latin America.
Yet even B.C. and Atlantic Canada, which register the country's lowest levels of annual sunlight, have potential to produce as much as 17 terawatt hours (TWh) and 10 TWh of solar power, respectively.
And the sun-drenched Prairies could produce as much as 28 TWh of solar power, according to a February report by CanmetEnergy, a federal energy research centre in Varennes, Que.
A single terawatt hour can fully power about 70,000 homes per year.
In the absence of a federal plan to significantly boost solar power production, the dawn of the Canadian solar age is evident in the scores of small-scale community-based solar installations across the country.
They include the 117 Ontario solar projects sponsored by the Community Energy Development Co-operative (CEP Co-op), headquartered in Kitchener, Ont.
Toronto-based SolarShare, another solar project provider, has 51 Ontario projects including solar-panel arrays on industrial rooftops and nonarable fields.
For now, community-based projects are driving Canada's solar evolution, though some large utilities have developed solar projects and collaborate with local projects by purchasing their surplus power.
The "solar garden" in Nelson, B.C., a town of about 10,000 people in B.C.'s Southern Interior, is one Canada's first solar projects of its kind, launched in 2017.
A "garden" is a centralized array of solar panels smaller than a utility-grade power plant but producing more power and at lower cost than solar panels on household rooftops.
At the other end of the country, Summerside, P.E.I., obtains about a quarter of the power required by the city of 15,000 people from its solar farm.
Many of Canada's solar power projects have been sponsored by Indigenous communities in remote regions that often rely on costly diesel that must be shipped in.
The solar farm under construction near Anahim Lake in Central B.C., home of the Ulkatcho First Nation, will provide electricity to about 350 homes.
The Anahim project, Canada's largest "off-grid" solar project (not connected to the provincial grid) is heralded by the Ulkatcho First Nation as a step toward "energy sovereignty."
It's also good business sense.
Pelee Island Winery, in Kingsville, Ont., on Lake Erie, saves more than $48,000 a year in electricity costs from its rooftop solar panel installations.
The winery's managing director, Annemarie Heikenwälder, is committed to ecologically sustainable agriculture.
But Heikenwälder is also running a business. And she says that "more businesses can profit from the ability to produce your own energy."
And as they say in the solar power industry, your investment is low risk since it is backed by the sun.
CAPTION: A solar panel array is seen in North Vancouver, B.C.Among alternatives to fossil fuels, solar continues to trail hydro, nuclear and wind in Canada as a source of clean power, David Olive writes.

Subject: Solar farms; Solar energy; Electricity; Alternative energy sources; Fossil fuels; Energy consumption; Nuclear power plants; Wineries & vineyards

Business indexing term: Subject: Nuclear power plants Wineries & vineyards

Location: United States--US; Canada

Company / organization: Name: Ulkatcho First Nation; NAICS: 921150; Name: International Energy Agency; NAICS: 541720, 926130, 928120

July 13: MPP Jama has chosen her own path, Trudeau will reach the NATO spending target, but by shrinking the economy, and other letters to the editor

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/july-13-mpp-jama-has-chosen-her-own-path-trudeau/docview/3079872197/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=July+13%3A+MPP+Jama+has+chosen+her+own+path%2C+Trudeau+will+reach+the+NATO+spending+target%2C+but+by+shrinking+the+economy%2C+and+other+letters+to+the+editor&amp;title=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-07-13&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: PM will reach NATO target by shrinking economy
Re: Canada expects to hit NATO target in 2032, Trudeau says, July 12
Prime Minister Justin Trudeau says we will reach two per cent of our gross domestic product for spending on defence, as required by NATO, by 2032.
He will do this by killing all money-generating industries (e.g. oil and gas) that don't conform with his beliefs. If these industries don't bring in revenue, the gross domestic product will drop drastically, thus bringing his goal in sight.
It's time Trudeau was voted out of office, and we became the rich country that we should be!
Richard Hewins, Hamilton
Sometimes it's the tenants who are the oppressors
I am all for protecting renters from being evicted by landlords in order to raise rents.
But, increasingly I am reading stories of landlords being abused by renters.
The renters have no stake in the property, so they let it deteriorate, dump garbage or even destroy property.
Why is it when a landlord wants to evict a tenant they suddenly stop paying rent?
I would tend to take the side of the tenant if they maintained their rent payments up to date as they disputed an eviction.
Instead, it seems, many of them want to play the system, stop paying rent, stop cleaning the property and squat on the property during the very long process.
All the while forcing the landlord to make his payments, with no compensation from the tenant.
If you are not paying rent, you should be evicted. If you damage the property, you should be billed.
This would happen if you were in a hotel.
Andy Price, Ancaster
MPP Sarah Jama has chosen her own path
Re: The re-education of Sarah Jama, July 2
Columnist Margaret Shkimba seemed to overlook the reality that many people may have voted for MPP Sarah Jama because she was running as the New Democratic Party's candidate to be the representative for Hamilton Centre.
When Ms. Jama agreed to run as a member of the NDP, she undertook to act on behalf of her constituents and to support the NDP's values and platform; not as an independent activist.
Party leader Marit Stiles has advised Ms. Jama on what she needs to do in order to re-establish her membership in the party. Ms. Jama has chosen not to comply. Ms. Jama has indicated her intention to run in the next election as an independent. I trust the NDP will have their own candidate who will also run.
Theresa Flynn-Purchase, Hamilton
Hurricane's early arrival is a warning to us all
With Hurricane Beryl reaching unprecedented strength so early in the hurricane season, and record heat waves scorching the United States, is there any doubt that we have a climate problem?
Such extreme shifts in our climate cannot and should no longer be brushed aside. No doubt, those in Atlantic Canada are hoping such a hurricane does not have their area in its crosshairs in the future. Many east coasters are still recovering from Hurricane Fiona and recent forest fires. Another catastrophic hurricane would devastate the area.
The human race must act swiftly and decisively now to address this climate crisis. It threatens our very existence on our home, Earth.
Mike LoSchiavo, Stoney Creek
CAPTION: Independent MPP Sarah Jama speaks to media outside the legislature in Toronto on May 6.

Subject: Gross Domestic Product--GDP; Evictions; Economic growth

Business indexing term: Subject: Gross Domestic Product--GDP Economic growth

Canada missing out on this sunny way

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/canada-missing-out-on-this-sunny-way/docview/3079739447/se-2?accountid=15090
