Document 347 of 500

Do Canadians want more or less government in their lives?

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/do-canadians-want-more-less-government-their/docview/3076421840/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Do+Canadians+want+more+or+less+government+in+their+lives%3F&amp;title=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-07-07&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: In a recurring feature, Susan Delacourt, a small-l liberal, and Matt Gurney, a small-c conservative, bring their different perspectives - and shared commitment to civil disagreement - to the political debates of the moment.
Susan Delacourt: Just before the long Canada Day weekend, Matt, you wrote a piece that has stayed with me, even if it wasn't exactly a rallying, hooray-for-Canada tale. You wrote that you're worried about people giving up on this country because many things, big or small, seem to be too hard for governments to do competently. For those who haven't read the piece already (and really, they should) do you want to give us a quick recap of why you felt the need to write it?
Matt Gurney: I mean, I mainly write because if I don't write I go crazy and make everyone around me miserable. Writing just makes some of the readers miserable, which is (for me) a huge upgrade. But the column I wrote was a product of a few things. And I admit they're mostly anecdotal. I know the quip that the plural of anecdote is not "data." But when I get whacked over the head repeatedly with something, I start to pay attention. And over the last few years, I've come to suspect that people are leaving Canada, for economic or sometimes (though less often) political reasons, or, interestingly, to access health care they can't reliably get at home. To be clear, I don't think this is a huge number of people, and I 100 per cent understand that for many, Canada is still the promised land. But I think that some of those with means are either leaving outright (this was something my colleague Jen Gerson wrote about recently) or are at least coming up with a Plan B - a backup location where they have a "vacation" property, a bank account, a health plan and maybe residency rights. I stress again that I don't think this is a huge number of people. But I think it might be a lopsided amount of money and talent, both of which Canada badly needs. And for what it's worth, I was reading about something similar just a few days ago right here in the Toronto Star.
SD: It is undeniably true that all kinds of government services to citizens are stretched, especially coming out of the pandemic. But it got me thinking about the whole notion of activist government and the weird place we find ourselves in North America. If polls are correct, a lot of Canadians and Americans are flirting with hard-right conservatism. At the same time, maybe as a product of the pandemic, the public seems to want more activist government. They want fixers in power. I wonder how this can be reconciled with politics that used to be all about getting the government out of people's lives?
MG: I don't know how hard-right Canadian conservatives will get. Will they be hard-right enough to fund the military, end supply management and open up domestic markets to foreign competition? Or will they mainly just tweet meanly? In any case, I sort of view the problem from the opposite perspective: I think a problem more progressive-leaning governments have, including very much our incumbent federal government, is that they want full value for their proposals. "We are proposing to do X, aren't we good people for doing X?" The problem is, me and about 41 million of my buddies have noticed what you very charitably described as stretched government services. I've put it more bluntly: things are literally and visibly falling apart. And every time a government proposes to do a new thing, I'm not bowled over with gratitude, I'm suspicious they're offering me something new to distract me from the old stuff that's breaking.
SD: I too would be grateful, if only out of exasperation with talking points, if all politicians stopped telling me the government has my back. But clichés are built around ideas that are commonplace. There is an assumption now that the public needs to be assured constantly that government is there to meet an ever-expanding list of citizens' needs. It's something that Pierre Poilievre, if he does become prime minister, is going to have to grapple with, as you point out with those examples of military spending and supply management. Poilievre grew up in the old Reform Party, when then leader Preston Manning was repeating the old joke about the scariest words in the English language: "I'm from the government and I'm here to help." I'm not sure that joke would get the same laughs now.
MG: I'm not the first person to note this, so give me no credit. But what we need - desperately - is a government that's focused on competency in service delivery and policy execution. I don't think anyone suggests we have that today. So a political party that was based around the notion of "Make Government Competent Again" would, I think, be popular. And that's non-partisan. That could mean more taxes to better fund a big government. It could mean lower taxes to sustain a smaller one. I don't care! Just make me an offer. But we won't get that. We'll get social media wedges and performative grandstanding. And I think we'll keep getting what we started talking about earlier: a slow trickle of at least money and business activity out of Canada, into jurisdictions where you can buy with cash what governments in Canada can no longer provide.
SD: Competence, one assumes, should be the low bar. But I guess what I'm saying is that I think citizens have to do some hard thinking too about wants and needs. We're in a housing mess now in part because successive governments of all stripes got out of that business and now everyone wants the government back in. It would be great to have an election debate that got voters assessing just how much they want activist government.
MG: You're right that competence should be the low bar. But it isn't. Even talking about it feels like a journey into "if wishes were horses" territory. I'd love a debate about what kind of government we want. I'd love a whole election about that. But everything I've seen in recent years tells me what we'll get is this: a variety of options all basically offering us the same dysfunction, but in a variety of "Don't you just hate those other bastards?" flavours. No one votes for competency. We vote to punish those that annoy or terrify us.
CAPTION: There is no denying that all kinds of government services, including health care, are stretched and appear to falling apart, Susan Delacourt and Matt Gurney write.

Document 348 of 500

Will independent stores, restaurants and bars run out of wine and liquor if LCBO strike drags on? Here's what you need to know

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/will-independent-stores-restaurants-bars-run-out/docview/3076459538/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Will+independent+stores%2C+restaurants+and+bars+run+out+of+wine+and+liquor+if+LCBO+strike+drags+on%3F+Here%27s+what+you+need+to+know&amp;title=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-07-06&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: It's barely been a day since the LCBO strike began, but its impacts are already being felt by Ontarians bracing for a hot summer weekend.
Temperatures are expected to reach 28 C this weekend and consumers have been flocking to the nearest Wine Rack or Beer Store seeking refreshments, while restaurant owners worry that they'll run out of raspberry vodka and vintage wines, especially if the strike continues for the next few weeks.
Why are workers striking?
On midnight Friday, more than 9,000 LCBO employees hit the picket lines, demanding, among other things, job security in light of the government's expansion plans for alcohol sales in the province.
Last year, Premier Doug Ford announced that the province would allow the sales of beer, wine, cider and ready-to-drink cocktails in convenience stores and all grocers by 2026, starting this September.
"Doug Ford wants to make life better for his wealthy friends," said Ontario Public Service Employees Union president JP Hornick in a statement Thursday.
"It's why he's wasting upwards of a billion dollars of our money to fast-track privatized alcohol sales and hand more of the public revenues generated by the LCBO over to the CEOs and big box grocery and convenience chains like Loblaws and Circle K."
At Queen's Park on Friday, Finance Minister Peter Bethlenfalvy reiterated his call for "OPSEU to return to the negotiating table and work towards a deal that prioritizes Ontario consumers and producers."
Bethlenfalvy said the government is "more committed than ever to fulfilling our promise of choice and convenience by expanding access to beer, cider, wine and ready-to-drink beverages in convenience, grocery and big-box stores starting later this summer," while retaining the provincial LCBO monopoly.
In a statement, Convenience Industry Council of Canada spokesperson Anne Kothawala said "it is unfortunate that OPSEU leadership are afraid of competition and are fighting for a closed, restrictive market with fewer options for consumers.
"Their claim that expanding the sale of beverage alcohol will somehow hurt jobs and reduce tax revenues defies logic and ignores the benefits that added convenience will provide to consumers."
How long is the strike expected to last?
It's uncertain how long the strike will last, but Hornick said the union is prepared with a "very healthy strike fund" and that "we can weather a strike as long as necessary."
If the strike drags on, the LCBO said it will close its stores for two weeks before opening up 32 select stores, which will operate during restricted hours from Fridays to Sundays, starting on July 19.
Still, store closures won't be as "big a deal as they would have been 30 years ago, when the LCBO was the main point of access," said Brock University professor of health sciences Dan Malleck.
During the COVID-19 pandemic, Ford's government changed licensing rules so more than 19,000 bars and restaurants are allowed to sell bottles and cans to go, which could alleviate the pressure from consumers in the cities, while more than 400 independent alcohol outlets will remain serving smaller communities.
Where can I buy alcohol?
Online ordering will be available through LCBO.com and the LCBO's mobile app with free home delivery during the strike, with no minimums. There are maximums though: a maximum two units for spirits, 12 units for wine, and 12 units for beer, cider and ready-to-drink.
The LCBO said it will keep processing wholesale orders through a separate online system from LCBO Convenience Outlets, licensed grocery stores, The Beer Store, and winery, brewery, cidery and distillery outlets, as well as from bars and restaurants.
Industry experts, however, say the online-only process might pose an inconvenience to these establishments, as they must order a case with, typically, a minimum of twelve bottles.
"In some cases, you have to buy a case of, say, scotch, and you might need a year to get through it," explained Tony Elenis, president and CEO at Ontario Restaurant Hotel and Motel Association.
Starting Wednesday, the LCBO will also open five locations in Toronto, Mississauga, Woodbridge, London and Ottawa that will exclusively serve licensees, including bars, restaurants, nightclubs and lounges, so they can shop by the bottle rather than the case, but the locations are unknown at this point.
"We have measures in place across our inventory build, our warehouse operations, and our fulfillment approach that reflect the importance of beverage alcohol availability to our wholesale, especially during the busy summer months," said an LCBO spokesperson in a statement.
When could liquor run out?
Most of the largest restaurants and hotel chains bought enough supply to last them around four weeks, while smaller ones may be able to last up to two weeks, said Elenis.
Mom-and-pop shops are especially vulnerable to run into issues as they might not have enough cash reserves to order alcohol in bulk and stock up in advance of possible shortages. (This, the LCBO says, might be alleviated with the opening of five stores exclusive to licensees next week.)
Consumers will generally find the bar rail basics, but could see limited availability of specialty drinks such as high-end scotches, vintage wine or flavoured spirits, said Kris Barnier, vice-president of Restaurants Canada.
Meanwhile, there will be plenty of local wine available, says Wine Rack spokesperson Mark Wasserman.
Wine Rack's owner, Arterra Wines Canada, had been preparing for a possible strike by increasing operating hours, staffing and inventory, he said. Arterra operates eight wineries in Ontario's wine regions, and is the producer behind brands such as Jackson-Triggs, Inniskillin, Sandbanks Estates, Bodacious and others.
"It's a good opportunity, when other places are shut, for people to rediscover Ontario" wines, said Wasserman.
"We have super-high conversion rates with consumers when they walk into a Wine Rack," he said. "I'm hopeful that we can retain some of those customers post-strike."
With files from Robert Benzie
CAPTION: The LCBO said it will keep processing wholesale orders from bars and restaurants through a separate online system.

Document 349 of 500

Justin Trudeau is caught in a different kind of Liberal leadership struggle

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/justin-trudeau-is-caught-different-kind-liberal/docview/3076281538/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Justin+Trudeau+is+caught+in+a+different+kind+of+Liberal+leadership+struggle&amp;title=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-07-06&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: When Justin Trudeau took over the leadership of his party in 2013, he vowed there would be no more "hyphenated Liberals."
That was a reference to the leadership dramas of the past, particularly the long-running battle between Jean Chrétien and Paul Martin, during which partisans came to define themselves by their loyalty to the prime minister or his likely successor.
That tension coursed through the Liberals for the better part of a decade, until it exploded in 2002. It culminated in Chrétien's announcement late that summer that he would finally step down, but only at the end of 2003 - a long goodbye. Take that, Martin Liberals.
Late last week, with rumours and leaks swirling about Liberal unrest and speculation about a Trudeau goodbye, there was a summer-of-2002 vibe developing. I wrote a book on that long-ago drama, travelling with Martin through the country that summer to get a close look at how the party was shifting its loyalty from one leader to another. It was a heady time for political reporters.
There's a huge temptation to see the current Liberal drama as history repeating itself, but hold on - in so many ways, what's going on now is vastly different.
First of all, if Trudeau is Chrétien in this repetition-of-history scenario, staring down a party that's keen for change, who is the Martin waiting in the wings?
The emerging answer seems to be Mark Carney, the former governor of the Bank of Canada. But apart from his interest in being finance minister, which my colleague Althia Raj wrote about last weekend, Carney does not share much in common with Martin.
He does not, as far as I can tell, have a huge network of organizers, as Martin did, nor any critical mass of MPs ready to proclaim their loyalty to his cause. Nor has a Carney team assumed control of riding associations or young Liberal groups, as the Martin team so massively did in the years before the 2002 explosion.
One intense scuffle in the summer of 2002 revolved around a running list in the Hill Times, identifying which MPs were Martin loyalists and which remained true to Chrétien. It got a bit silly at some points, with MPs being lobbied to get their names on and off one column or the other each time the list was updated.
Now, there was a bit of that feeling in the air last weekend, when all my media colleagues were scrambling to see which MPs had attached their names to emails and open letters about Trudeau's leadership. That kind of fizzled this week.
The old Liberal battles can't really be replayed because it is simply not the same party. Devastated in the 2011 election, which reduced it to third-party status for the first time, the red team was vastly diminished.
Trudeau can claim, quite rightly, that he rebuilt the Liberals and that they owed their phoenix-like rise in 2015 to him. That strength waned as their government went from majority to minority status, but the party built in his image - with "supporters," not members, for instance -- would have to be rebuilt with Trudeau gone.
That's the other big difference between now and 2002. Back then, when it came to power, the Liberals were pretty much the only game in town. The right was divided between the Canadian Alliance and the Progressive Conservatives. Stephen Harper had arrived on the scene to lead the Alliance, but uniting the right was still to come.
In other words, the Liberals could afford a little leadership drama. Trudeau has often said he believes the party took its eyes off the ball. In his debut speech as leader in 2013, he put it this way: "Liberals became more focused on fighting with each other than fighting for Canadians."
I heard echoes of that this week when he spoke with the media for the first time since the disastrous byelection loss in Toronto-St. Paul's.
"I can tell you that, in my conversations with MPs from one end of the country to the other, the emphasis is on how we're going to continue to be there for Canadians," Trudeau said. He slipped in a mention of Pierre Poilievre in his remarks, too - a reminder that unlike in 2002, the Liberals could easily be replaced if Canadians get weary of their leadership drama.
Trudeau was largely successful in excising the hyphens from the Liberal party. Now, as he navigates this summer, he has to avoid letting the party fall into hyphenated camps again: the Trudeau Liberals versus the no-Trudeau Liberals.
CAPTION: Prime Minister Justin Trudeau speaks to supporters at a Liberal party fundraiser in Montreal on July 3, 2024.

Document 350 of 500

The NDP has failed to gain from Liberal losses

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/ndp-has-failed-gain-liberal-losses/docview/3076281452/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=The+NDP+has+failed+to+gain+from+Liberal+losses&amp;title=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-07-06&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Since Prime Minister Justin Trudeau's Liberal party went down to a disastrous defeat in a byelection on June 24 in the riding of Toronto-St. Paul's, pressure has mounted on Trudeau to resign. But Trudeau isn't the only party leader who is in deep trouble.
Jagmeet Singh, leader of the New Democratic Party (NDP), I believe is also in a political "death spiral."
In the 2021 federal election, the NDP garnered 16.8 per cent of the vote in Toronto-St. Paul's. On June 24, they won only 10.9 per cent of the vote. This is a riding in a very "progressive" part of Toronto.
One would think that if the voters were finished with the Liberals, their next choice would be the NDP. And that sure didn't happen! The Conservatives, led by Pierre Poilievre, won that riding in a shocking upset.
And it isn't just the byelection results. According to a recent Leger poll only 14 per cent of Canadian voters believe Singh would make the best prime minister.
The trends are not looking good for Singh and the NDP. In the 2019 election, the NDP led by Singh fell from 39 seats to 24. In 2021 they saw an increase of one seat. In 2022, Singh signed a "supply and confidence" agreement with the Liberals, promising to support them in confidence votes until 2025, in return for the Liberals agreeing to pass certain legislation.
Much of that legislation, such as federal dental care and drug coverage for birth control and diabetes medications, has been passed. But both parties continue to sink in the polls. And Singh is now sending very odd messages to Canadians.
"We look at this as really people sending their message. They are frustrated with Trudeau. They're done with Trudeau, and they are frustrated with the Liberal party, and they've got reasons to (be)," Singh told the National Post about the byelection results.
"We hear this again and again: things are getting worse, not better. The Liberals have had nine years to make improvements and they haven't."
And yet, despite what he says about voter fatigue with Trudeau and the Liberals, Singh won't pull his support from them.
So we see that on one hand Singh says voters are "done with Trudeau." And on the other hand he says he will not terminate the supply and confidence agreement with the Liberals that is keeping Trudeau in office. As Star Trek's Mr. Spock would say, "Mr. Singh is being most illogical."
History has shown that supporting a Liberal government doesn't benefit the NDP.
In the 1972 federal election, the Liberals led by Pierre Trudeau won 109 seats, the Progressive Conservatives 107, and the NDP 31. The NDP agreed to keep the Trudeau government in office in return for the Liberals passing certain legislation. However, when the Trudeau government fell in 1974, the NDP lost 15 seats in the resulting election and the Liberals won a large majority.
Working with the Liberals didn't in any way help the NDP.
We saw something similar in Ontario in 1985. In the provincial election of that year, the Conservatives won a very slim minority. The Liberals and NDP promptly voted no confidence and brought down the Conservative government. The NDP led by Bob Rae supported the Liberal government for the next two years.
In the 1987 provincial election, the Liberals won a massive majority while the NDP, despite becoming the official opposition, lost six seats. Once again, supporting the Liberals did not help the NDP in the following election.
Keeping Justin Trudeau in office clearly isn't helping the NDP. I predict it will lead to a backlash from voters, angry at Singh's continued support for a very unpopular prime minister and government. The next federal election may be ugly for Singh and the NDP.
CAPTION: Looking at the results of the recent Toronto byelection, Craig Wallace says keeping PM Justin Trudeau in office clearly isn't helping the NDP or its leader, Jagmeet Singh.
CREDIT: Craig Wallace

Subject: Provincial elections; Polls & surveys; Election results; Legislation; Voters; Prime ministers

Document 351 of 500

Developers take Hamilton urban boundary expansion battle to provincial tribunal

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/developers-take-hamilton-urban-boundary-expansion/docview/3076281396/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Developers+take+Hamilton+urban+boundary+expansion+battle+to+provincial+tribunal&amp;title=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-07-06&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: A contentious new Ontario housing law has allowed developers to resurrect on-again, off-again urban boundary expansion pitches in Hamilton - this time, in front of a tribunal.
It's the latest episode in a back-and-forth growth battle that famously featured the Doug Ford government reversing a 4,500-acre urban boundary expansion last year in the face of allegations of undue developer influence.
A group of developers, the Upper West Side Group, has long sought permission to expand Hamilton's urban boundary to allow for construction of thousands of homes on Twenty Road West farmland near the airport employment growth district.
Late last week, the group resurrected three dormant, piecemeal urban boundary applications dating back to 2020 through an appeal to the Ontario Land Tribunal.
That appeal option "did not exist" until Ontario's controversial housing Bill 185 became law last month, said Hamilton planning head Steve Robichaud, whose department is bringing an update report to councillors Tuesday.
That bill, described by the government as a means of "cutting red tape" to facilitate needed housing construction, allows developers to apply for urban boundary expansions whenever they wish - and appeal rejections or council "nondecisions" to an administrative tribunal.
Environmental Defence lawyer Phil Pothen earlier characterized the changes as a "stealth" attempt to overturn the victory of Hamilton anti-sprawl advocates in pressuring Ontario to walk back its imposed urban boundary expansion, which would have opened thousands of rural acres to residential development all at once in Stoney Creek and Glanbrook.
"Now it looks like the province is just trying to accomplish the same thing by a different route," said Hamilton councillor Craig Cassar. "Instead of opening (protected farmland) to development all at once, it is more like death by a thousand cuts."
As it stands, council has adopted a frozen urban boundary aimed at protecting rural farmland via a strategy of intensification across urban Hamilton.
A tribunal hearing does not change that position, said Cassar. "We have to stick to our principles," he said.
Council formally objected to Bill 185 ahead of it becoming law, sending Robichaud to the provincial legislature in May to outline concerns about the myriad proposed policy changes.
In his delegation, Robichaud noted the province appeared to endorse Hamilton's commitment to growing within its existing footprint after repealing the contentious 4,500-acre urban boundary expansion late last year.
But the slew of recent provincial planning and policy changes linked to housing, he suggested, seem to represent "a shift from an intensification-first approach to a greenfield-first approach" and risks subjecting Hamilton to "multiple independent, unco-ordinated urban boundary expansion applications and appeals."
The Spectator was unable to reach Upper West Side Group spokesperson John Corbett on Friday about the developers' newly resurrected urban boundary applications.
Last year, he said by email that the development group had "essentially abandoned" the piecemeal requests first made in 2020 after the province briefly imposed a wholesale urban boundary expansion on the city.
In that email, he argued the Twenty Road West lands are "distinctly different" than other proposed boundary expansions in Hamilton because they represent an "infill" home-building opportunity in an area already surrounded by residential or airport employment lands.
Combined, the three resurrected applications going to the tribunal represent more than 200 acres of rural land pegged for development of around 2,450 homes or residential units.
The applications also seek policy changes to allow residential building in areas otherwise off-limits under "noise exposure" thresholds for lands near the John. C Munro Hamilton International Airport.
Robichaud said the update report Tuesday is primarily to ensure "the public is aware" that the urban boundary appeal is happening, given "the high level of interest" in the never-ending growth debate.
Council will eventually be asked to provide closed-door legal direction to staff on how to approach the impending tribunal appeal.
"But right now, the policy of council ... is that all growth is to be accommodated within the existing urban area," he said. "So that is what we are working toward."
Matthew Van Dongen is a reporter at The Spectator. mvandongen@thespec.com
CAPTION: The urban boundary expansion battle over farmland along Twenty Road West is headed to a provincial tribunal.

Document 352 of 500

NDP fails to gain from Liberal losses

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/ndp-fails-gain-liberal-losses/docview/3076228048/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=unknown&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=NDP+fails+to+gain+from+Liberal+losses&amp;title=The+Spectator&amp;issn=11899417&amp;date=2024-07-06&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.20&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Spectator&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Since Prime Minister Justin Trudeau's Liberal party went down to a disastrous defeat in a byelection on June 24 in the riding of Toronto-St. Paul's, pressure has mounted on Trudeau to resign. But Trudeau isn't the only party leader who is in deep trouble.
Jagmeet Singh, leader of the New Democratic Party (NDP), I believe is also in a political "death spiral."
In the 2021 federal election, the NDP garnered 16.8 per cent of the vote in Toronto-St. Paul's. On June 24, they won only 10.9 per cent of the vote. This is a riding in a very "progressive" part of Toronto.
One would think that if the voters were finished with the Liberals, their next choice would be the NDP. And that sure didn't happen! The Conservatives, led by Pierre Poilievre, won that riding in a shocking upset.
The trends are not looking good for Singh and the NDP. In the 2019 election, the NDP led by Singh fell from 39 seats to 24. In 2021 they saw an increase of one seat. In 2022, Singh signed a "supply and confidence" agreement with the Liberals, promising to support them in confidence votes until 2025, in return for the Liberals agreeing to pass certain legislation.
Much of that legislation, such as federal dental care and drug coverage for birth control and diabetes medications, has been passed. But both parties continue to sink in the polls. And Singh is now sending very odd messages to Canadians.
"We look at this as really people sending their message. They are frustrated with Trudeau. They're done with Trudeau, and they are frustrated with the Liberal party, and they've got reasons to (be)," Singh told the National Post about the byelection results.
"We hear this again and again: things are getting worse, not better. The Liberals have had nine years to make improvements and they haven't."
And yet, despite what he says about voter fatigue with Trudeau and the Liberals, Singh won't pull his support from them. So we see that on one hand Singh says voters are "done with Trudeau." And on the other hand he says he will not terminate the supply and confidence agreement with the Liberals that is keeping Trudeau in office. As Star Trek's Mr. Spock would say, "Mr. Singh is being most illogical."
History has shown that supporting a Liberal government doesn't benefit the NDP.
In the 1972 federal election, the Liberals led by Pierre Trudeau won 109 seats, the Progressive Conservatives 107, and the NDP 31. The NDP agreed to keep the Trudeau government in office in return for the Liberals passing certain legislation. However, when the Trudeau government fell in 1974, the NDP lost 15 seats in the resulting election and the Liberals won a large majority.
Working with the Liberals didn't in any way help the NDP.
We saw something similar in Ontario in 1985. In the provincial election of that year, the Conservatives won a very slim minority. The Liberals and NDP promptly voted no confidence and brought down the Conservative government. The NDP led by Bob Rae supported the Liberal government for the next two years.
In the 1987 provincial election, the Liberals won a massive majority while the NDP, despite becoming the official opposition, lost six seats. Once again, supporting the Liberals did not help the NDP.
Keeping Justin Trudeau in office clearly isn't helping the NDP. I predict it will lead to a backlash from voters, angry at Singh's continued support for a very unpopular prime minister and government. The next federal election may be ugly for Singh and the NDP.
Craig Wallace is a Hamilton resident and author of
five books.
CAPTION:

CREDIT: Craig Wallace OPINION;

Urban boundary expansion fight heads to tribunal: Builders want permission to add 2,000-plus homes on rural land along Twenty Road West

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/urban-boundary-expansion-fight-heads-tribunal/docview/3076227309/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Urban+boundary+expansion+fight+heads+to+tribunal%3A+Builders+want+permission+to+add+2%2C000-plus+homes+on+rural+land+along+Twenty+Road+West&amp;title=The+Spectator&amp;issn=11899417&amp;date=2024-07-06&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.1&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Spectator&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: A contentious new Ontario housing law has allowed developers to resurrect on-again, off-again urban boundary expansion pitches in Hamilton - this time, in front of a tribunal.

"But right now, the policy of council ... is that all growth is to be accommodated within the existing urban area," he said. "So that is what we are working toward."
Matthew Van Dongen is a reporter at The Spectator. mvandongen@thespec.com

Urban boundary roller-coaster:
a brief timeline
2020Developers submit applications for a series of small urban boundary expansions in the Twenty Road West and Glancaster Road area. With growth studies still underway, council does not make any decision on these applications.
2021Hamilton city council votes to freeze its urban boundary in the face of a massive grassroots "anti-sprawl" campaign.
2022The Ford government overrules the city and imposes a 4,500-acre urban boundary expansion - while opening parts of the Greenbelt to development at the same time.
2023Premier Doug Ford reverses Greenbelt plans in the face of constant media coverage, scathing audit findings and a police probe into allegations of undue developer influence. Weeks later, the government also walks back the urban boundary expansion in Hamilton.
2024Ontario introduces Bill 185, the Cutting Red Tape to Build More Homes Act, which is enacted in June. Developers use new provisions under the law to take their circa-2020 urban boundary expansion applications to the Ontario Land Tribunal for a decision.

Document 354 of 500

LCBO closed for two weeks: Employees hit picket line, arguing plan to open up booze market is a threat to workers' jobs

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/lcbo-closed-two-weeks/docview/3076227306/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=LCBO+closed+for+two+weeks%3A+Employees+hit+picket+line%2C+arguing+plan+to+open+up+booze+market+is+a+threat+to+workers%27+jobs&amp;title=The+Spectator&amp;issn=11899417&amp;date=2024-07-06&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.16&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Spectator&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Workers for Ontario's main liquor retailer, who say the government's plan to open up the alcohol market poses an existential threat to their jobs, are now on strike, with stores expected to stay closed for at least 14 days.
Thousands of workers at the Liquor Control Board of Ontario went on strike Friday morning in the first such labour disruption in the retailer's history, after months of contract negotiations between their union and management failed to result in a new deal.
Wages are not the issue, the union said, rather they take umbrage with Premier Doug Ford's sped-up plan to open up the alcohol market, in particular the increased access to ready-to-drink cocktails.
After the midnight strike deadline, the Ontario Public Service Employee Union shared a video of workers picketing outside an LCBO warehouse east of Toronto.
"Welcome to Doug Ford's dry summer," read the video caption, posted to OPSEU's social media page.
The union is fearful of job losses after Premier Doug Ford's government announced plans to allow convenience stores and all grocery stores to sell beer, wine and ready-to-drink cocktails.
The first step is set to start in less than a month, when grocery stores that already sell beer and wine will be able to sell ready-to-drink cocktails. Convenience stores can start selling beer, wine, cider and ready-to-drink cocktails on Sept. 5. The Alcohol and Gaming Commission of Ontario said in June that it had issued nearly 1,900 licences to convenience stores just days after applications opened.
OPSEU officials said they believe Ford's fast-tracked plan to further privatize alcohol sales threatens the future of the LCBO, as well as the $2.5 billion dividend that goes to the province for services such as health care and education.
"We'll be at the table if the government wants to actually address some of our core concerns about how to ensure that there is a future for the LCBO, that they can compete on a level playing field with these private interests," OPSEU president JP Hornick said in a Thursday news conference.
Hornick said the union is not opposing Ford fulfilling his 2018 election promise to put beer and wine in corner stores, but OPSEU wants to keep the ready-to-drink cocktails out of those locations.
Finance Minister Peter Bethlenfalvy wrote in a statement that the government is "more committed than ever" to its plan.
CAPTION:

Document 355 of 500

On the lam, an international pursuit as justice closes in: A sinister double-cross in Hamilton. A panicked getaway across Ontario. An international pursuit over three European countries. A shooting victim must face her boyfriend's accused killers. In this three-part series, The Spectator's Nicole O'Reilly brings us a story of betrayal, greed and murder

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/on-lam-international-pursuit-as-justice-closes/docview/3076227150/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=On+the+lam%2C+an+international+pursuit+as+justice+closes+in%3A+A+sinister+double-cross+in+Hamilton.+A+panicked+getaway+across+Ontario.+An+international+pursuit+over+three+European+countries.+A+shooting+victim+must+face+her+boyfriend%27s+accused+killers.+In+this+three-part+series%2C+The+Spectator%27s+Nicole+O%27Reilly+brings+us+a+story+of+betrayal%2C+greed+and+murder&amp;title=The+Spectator&amp;issn=11899417&amp;date=2024-07-06&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.1&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Spectator&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Tyler Pratt and Jordyn Romano expected to meet their friends for a routine real-estate inspection in Hamilton, and a dinner together afterward at a nice restaurant in Oakville.
Their friends, Oliver Karafa and Lucy Li, planned something more sinister.
When Pratt and Romano arrived in their usual white Range Rover, Karafa and Li were already there in a car Pratt and Romano didn't recognize, a white Audi.
The two vehicles parked facing each other at the back of an industrial building at 347 Arvin Ave. in Stoney Creek.
Where's the realtor? Pratt asked.
They were expecting a man named Michael to show them around, but he was nowhere to be seen. What's more, they were locked out of the building.
Li was sent to look for a lockbox; she didn't find one - that's because no one had actually made plans to view the property.
Romano, who was 13 weeks pregnant and cold, returned to their Range Rover to warm up. Li was directed to join her while the guys talked.
Romano was dressed up, in a tan sweater and thigh-high Christian Louboutin boots. She believed the foursome was going out for dinner at the restaurant Soto Soto in Oakville afterward. It was strange, she thought, that Li - usually impeccably dressed - was in sweats. She "showed up in rags," Romano said.
Romano and Pratt had known Karafa about four months, since they started investing in one of his businesses. Over the preceding months, they met often. Pratt and Karafa spoke almost daily. Romano believed the two couples had become good friends.
Indeed, Pratt and Romano bought Karafa a diamond chain as a gift for his birthday a week earlier. And two days earlier, Karafa messaged them about taking a trip to Italy in June. Li spoke to Romano about her upcoming baby shower.
But inside the car that day, something seemed off.
Li was "nervously texting," Romano said. "She comes in the car and couldn't even look at me."
When Li got out of the car, Romano slid over into the driver's seat.
Treachery revealed
Her window was down when she looked up and froze. Karafa, his face shaking, was pointing a gun at her.
"Get out of here, Jo," is all Romano could remember him saying before she was shot. Panicked, she reversed her car and crashed into something. She heard Pratt saying, "What the f--k, bro," before more gunshots.
More than an hour later, about 7:20 p.m., a passing motorist - Annette Bright - found Romano crawling on the road in front of 347 Arvin Ave. Police later found Pratt's body lying on the cold ground behind the property.
Both cars, damaged in the chaos, were gone. The Audi was found abandoned nearby parked at Green Road and South Service Road.
Li and Karafa fled in Romano's Range Rover. Karafa - who was prohibited from driving after being convicted of impaired driving causing death years earlier - had to hold the door closed as they drove on the highway.
They dumped the vehicle near Mimico GO Station in Etobicoke. Li says Karafa later told her he also dumped the gun somewhere near there, but it was never found.
Karafa arranged for a guy he met through a Kijiji ad, named Mohamad Morad, to buy both vehicles for $2,000, strip them for parts and ship them out of the country. But police found the vehicles first.
As Romano fought for her life, Karafa and Li took a taxi back to their condo on Erskine Avenue, near Yonge Street and Eglinton Avenue in Toronto.
Before the shooting, they had switched cellphone SIM cards on one of their phones with Phi Chinh - an unsuspecting friend of Karafa's - and then switched them back later that evening.
When data from cell towers was inspected later, it appeared as if Li and Karafa stayed home, while Chinh travelled to the shooting scene. Indeed, Chinh was later arrested for murder. When he explained to police what actually happened, he was let go.
Back at their condo, Karafa and Li tried to get some rest, but early the next morning they saw an article about the shooting in The Hamilton Spectator: one male was dead and one female critically injured.
Now they had a problem. Romano could identify them.
They thought their careful planning would allow them to continue their lives without paying Pratt and Romano back. Pratt and Romano had invested nearly $500,000 in a business of Karafa's selling medical supplies in Europe. Karafa had promised big profits but the couple had not been repaid a penny.
Now, with a witness, they had to run.
On the lam
Surveillance video shows Karafa, carrying a garbage bag, and Li, carrying a UPS envelope, leaving their condo shortly before 3 a.m. on March 1. They drove Li's Mercedes north to Muskoka, stopping at the Glen Orchard General Store near Port Carling, which also includes a gas station, restaurant, tackle shop and other properties off Butterfly Lake.
When the store's general manager, Charlene Smith, arrived at work around 6:30 a.m. on March 1, she noticed the vehicle in the parking lot. She opened the store at 7 a.m. and, about an hour later, saw an attractive young couple come in to use the washroom.
They bought some drinks and left in a taxi.
Chris Whittingham, who owns the Glen Orchard store, didn't think much of the abandoned Mercedes until his daughter found a garbage bag in the recycling bin filled with 40 nine-millimetre bullets, a blond wig, clothing and other suspicious items. He called police.
Ontario Provincial Police documented the evidence found in the bag, including a pair of Li's pants with blood drops and a phone charger with blood on it. The key fob to an Audi was also found in the bag.
Inside the abandoned Mercedes, police found more clothing and items, including the UPS folder that contained documents from the life-insurance scheme. Before the shooting, Romano and Pratt believed Li was finalizing a $1.5 million life-insurance policy that would help them transfer some of the money they were owed from Europe; Li later admitted she never intended to file the application and only pretended to go along with it to buy Karafa more time.
By the time police found the evidence in Muskoka, Li and Karafa were long gone.
Sandy Sethi, the taxi driver, was going to drop them off at the bus station in Barrie, but the couple changed their plans and she drove them to Toronto. She dropped them off at a subway station, earning $400 for the trip.
Li and Karafa then travelled to Union Station, where their movements could be traced on surveillance video buying tickets and then boarding a train to Montreal. In Montreal, they sat silently on a shuttle bus that took them to Montreal-Trudeau International Airport. They arrived at 4:40 p.m., and checked in for a flight to Germany and a connecting flight to Prague.
Li carried nothing. Karafa had a bag filled with cash: between $16,000 and $17,000. Because of this, Li said they were stopped at the gate until she showed them her banking records for the money she had withdrawn to buy the Audi from Karafa's friend the day before.
The prosecution would later argue the money was likely Pratt's, which he brought back from Vancouver the day of the shooting. That money was never recovered.
In messages between Karafa and Li just two days before the shooting, Karafa said, "I hope these retards have money."
By March 4, Hamilton police had announced warrants for Karafa and Li for first-degree murder and attempted murder. Later that month, they said the fugitives had fled to eastern Europe and were now wanted on international warrants.
Jim Callender, then a detective sergeant with Hamilton police and the case manager in charge of the investigation, vowed they would find them. It was a promise Callender, now an inspector, and his team, including Det. David Tsuchida, who was the lead detective, made good on several months later.
An international pursuit
What happened between the time Li and Karafa landed in Prague and were arrested in Budapest, Hungary, months later has been the subject of speculation. The details that are known largely come from Li, who testified about their run from the law.
Karafa was an entrepreneur with multiple businesses. Most were in Europe. Karafa's business partner, Martin Macek, was based in Prague.
After landing there, they took an Uber to Macek's condo and then spent a few days at an Airbnb.
Business records obtained by The Spectator show their partnership in at least one business was dissolved a few days after Karafa's arrival. In 2021, Macek declined to speak with The Spectator and said he found out about the murder only after Karafa left Prague.
After a few days in Prague, Li and Karafa took a train to Slovakia, where his dad picked them up and drove to their family home, Li said. It was just Karafa's parents and their cat in the house.
Karafa and Li were both exhausted; they showered and fell asleep.
But Li awoke around midnight when her phone rang. It was one of her sisters telling her warrants for their arrests had been reported in the media.
Li woke her husband, who woke his dad. The Karafa family home would be the first place authorities would look for the fugitive couple, so they would have to leave.
Karafa's father didn't want to lose his son again to the justice system in Canada - Karafa had already served a prison sentence for the drunk-driving death.
In 2021, there were strict pandemic lockdown rules in Eastern Europe. And, as a Chinese woman, Li said she stood out. She was made to crouch in the back of a truck while Karafa's dad drove them to a friend's house.
Li said she called her mom, Hong Wei 'Winnie' Liao, who hysterically yelled at her daughter while Li sobbed. Her mom was telling her to come home.
It was then that Liao, a high-profile businessperson with political ties, released a statement in Chinese-language media.
In the statement, which the Spectator translated from Mandarin, the family said they were "shocked, disturbed and puzzled by Lucy's involvement in the tragic incident" and offered condolences to the victims and their families.
Addressing Li directly, they said they were sure she would not be involved in such an incident voluntarily and would not do "such a ridiculous thing."
"We can hardly imagine the shock and anxiety you experienced after the incident."
The family implored Li to co-operate.
Li's mom arranged for her to speak with a lawyer on the phone while they were in Slovakia. Karafa told her he would not turn himself in.
That night, she awoke to hear Karafa speaking Slovak in the kitchen with men who claimed to be Europol police officers. The men, who called themselves Paul and Milo, drove vehicles with diplomatic plates and claimed they could help make the warrants go away.
Li said Karafa's father paid 250,000 euros that night - and more money later - for their help. She also said her mom paid about 200,000 euros through a lawyer after another man, Sasa (pronounced Sasha), who appeared to be a higher-ranked officer, became involved.
They were first moved to an apartment that belonged to Paul, where their phones were taken from them and fake passports issued.
Li's fake Slovak passport listed her name as Ying Feng Nichta, born in Cebu, Philippines.
Karafa's said his name was Jakub Nichta born in Bratislava.
With their fake passports, Li said they were driven in military-style vehicles across the border into Hungary, where they spent April and half of May in a safe house overseen by a family from Syria in the city of Gyor. Li called it the "pink house" due to its colour.
She said they were told on May 15 that the international warrants were gone, but that Hamilton police were slow to update. They were given their phones back and travelled to Budapest, where Li said they wrongly believed they were free.
Justice closes in
The couple stayed in an Airbnb and Li - who wanted to come home - started looking into getting new Canadian passports. She said they spent a lot of time just walking around the city and visiting a church.
It was outside a church where the pair were arrested June 12.
Police in Hungary released photos of the pair, hands cuffed and heads bowed to avoid cameras.
Li immediately waived extradition and agreed to come back to Canada. Hamilton police travelled to Budapest and escorted her back. She landed in Toronto a month after her arrest on July 13 and was charged.
In Hamilton, she was interviewed for hours by Det. Troy Ashbaugh, an expert interrogator who is now retired. Li, used to flying first class, told him she was "not used to sitting in tight spaces" while being escorted home in economy. Her mom, she said, had offered to fly them all back privately.
Throughout the interview, Li was polite but refused to answer many questions. She repeatedly said there was more to the story, but her lawyer and mother had told her not to talk.
She admitted to being at the scene of the shooting, but claimed to be far away when shots were fired.
Back in Budapest, Karafa had perks in jail, including a contraband cellphone. He fought extradition and instead wanted to go home to Slovakia.
According to court records there, Karafa argued that corruption within Hamilton police and the Hamilton-Wentworth Detention Centre, where Karafa was expected to be held, made it unsafe for him to return to Canada.
His lawyers also argued that they had hired a private investigator to look into Pratt and his "extensive criminal connections."
However, the court there - including the Budapest Court of Appeal - dismissed those concerns. Canadian authorities provided detailed information about Karafa's rights and security plans.
Just over a week after the Appeal Court upheld Karafa's extradition and while Canadian authorities were working out the plan to transport him back to Hamilton, Li was granted bail.
Many people charged with murder in Canada are not granted bail. But granting bail to someone who was an international fugitive is exceptionally rare.
In Li's case, her mother and other sureties promised strict control, including bars on her windows at the family's North York property. Li was released on bail with a $2.7-million bond on Jan. 20, 2022.
Only four months later, Li was again in custody after allegedly breaching her bail conditions by going out to a party and being in contact with someone she was prohibited from communicating with.
By March 30, Karafa - escorted by Hamilton police homicide detectives - arrived back in Canada and was transported to jail. He has been behind bars ever since.
Nicole O'Reilly is a crime and justice reporter at The Spectator. noreilly@thespec.com

Document 356 of 500

Union says 'we can weather a strike as long as necessary' as all LCBO locations shut down

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/union-says-we-can-weather-strike-as-long/docview/3076300111/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Union+says+%27we+can+weather+a+strike+as+long+as+necessary%27+as+all+LCBO+locations+shut+down&amp;title=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-07-05&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: LCBO workers are now on strike for the first time in the liquor monopoly's history.
More than 9,000 LCBO workers hit the picket lines at midnight on Friday after talks between the Crown corporation and its employees broke down just hours before the deadline.
As of Friday, LCBO stores will be closed for 14 days, followed by a partial reopening. Online ordering and free home-delivery services are still available.
"Tonight, Ford's dry summer begins," OPSEU bargaining committee chair Colleen MacLeod said at a news conference Thursday evening where Ontarians were urged to voice their displeasure to Premier Doug Ford.
The union reiterated that the fundamental issue is job security, expressing concern that thousands of jobs will be lost in the coming years due to the government's expansion plans for alcohol sales in the province.
"We haven't even got to the wages and benefits portion of it ... that's not what this is about," MacLeod said. "If we don't have a job on the go-forward, a wage increase really doesn't make much of a difference."
OPSEU president JP Hornick said the union has a "very healthy strike fund" and that "we can weather a strike as long as necessary."
Hornick described the LCBO as facing an existential crisis. "Do we want to have a public provider of alcohol as we've had for the past hundred years, or are we just moving into a wild west private model?"
The LCBO said in a statement Thursday evening that OPSEU's leadership made clear "numerous times" at the bargaining table that workers would strike "solely over their demand" that the government reverse its decision to expand the sale of ready-to-drink beverages like coolers and seltzers in grocery and convenience stores.
The Crown corporation said it tabled its latest offer just after 4 p.m. on Thursday, which included wage increases for the next three years (2.5 per cent in years one and two, and two per cent in year three), and converting about 400 casual employees to permanent full-time positions.
Instead of presenting a counterproposal, the union held a news conference, the LCBO said.
"The LCBO is calling on OPSEU's leadership and bargaining team to counter our latest offer and work with us to achieve a negotiated agreement and avoid a strike."
The LCBO has said that 32 stores will reopen July 19 for limited shopping every Friday, Saturday and Sunday. It also said that the strike does not impact 2,300 private retail points of sale across the province, including LCBO Convenience Outlets, grocery stores, The Beer Store, and bars and restaurants.
Finance Minister Peter Bethlenfalvy's office said Thursday evening it was disappointed in the union's decision to walk away from the bargaining table.
"We are particularly disappointed that OPSEU is opposed to giving people in Ontario the choice and convenience of buying ready-made drinks, like coolers and seltzers, in grocery and convenience stores," said a statement from the minister's office.
"We are more committed than ever to fulfilling our promise of choice and convenience by expanding access to beer, cider, wine and ready-to-drink beverages in convenience, grocery and big-box stores starting later this summer."
OPSEU has repeatedly criticized the provincial government's decision to open up Ontario's alcohol retail market, arguing that it puts LCBO jobs and profits at risk, while helping private retailers, including grocery and big box stores, as well as convenience stores.
The union was proposing to add more LCBO stores and increase operating hours to meet demand and improve convenience.
OPSEU also says that 70 per cent of LCBO workers are "casual," meaning they have no guaranteed hours, benefits or sick pay. The LCBO, meanwhile, said that casual employees do have access to benefits and at least 50 per cent of them are guaranteed at least 1,000 hours per year.
The union says LCBO wages range from $17.65 an hour to roughly $30. The LCBO says the range for customer service representatives goes from $17.85 to $32.58.
Liberal MPP Stephen Blais, the party's critic for labour issues, called on the government to make a deal.
"LCBO workers deserve job security, livable wages and fair treatment. Doug Ford needs to do the right thing and come to a fair deal that respects workers, keeps Ontario working and doesn't disrupt the lives of hardworking Ontarians," Blais said in a written statement. "We know OPSEU workers are at the table ready to bargain for a fair deal, the government needs to do the same."
Green Party Leader Mike Schreiner said in a statement Thursday, "The chaos in the beverage industry is entirely the Ford government's doing, and now Ontarians are the ones paying for it.
"The premier's rush to get beer into corner stores without proper planning or consultation has upended the industry and raised workers' fears about job security in an uncertain future," said Schreiner, adding "the Ford government needs to step up and ensure that workers get a fair deal that protects jobs and provides stability in a changing industry."
It's a more complicated set of negotiations than typical contract talks, where the main bone of contention is strictly money, noted labour relations professor Rafael Gomez.
"Because of all the changes in alcohol retailing, the union knows that there will be fewer workers at the LCBO by 2030. And likely, fewer LCBO stores," said Gomez, director of U of T's Centre for Industrial Relations and Human Resources.
"The government is going to push the union to either have high wages in return for less job security, or low wages and higher job security," Gomez said. "Either way, through attrition or store closures, the government will have fewer LCBO outlets and fewer LCBO employees by 2030."
LCBO on strike: Our wine critic found the best bottles at Wine Rack and the Wine Shop
CAPTION: The LCBO had said all its stores would be closed for two weeks if there's a strike, but that 30 will reopen July 19 for limited shopping.The LCBO said online ordering would be available in the event of a strike, but said details wouldn't be available until a strike begins.LCBO employees picket outside store at 125 The Queensway.

Document 357 of 500

July 5: We need to shorten CFL games, the complaining has just begun and other letters to the editor

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/july-5-we-need-shorten-cfl-games-complaining-has/docview/3075990177/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=July+5%3A+We+need+to+shorten+CFL+games%2C+the+complaining+has+just+begun+and+other+letters+to+the+editor&amp;title=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-07-05&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Should we shorten CFL games?
Re: It's time to get concerned about the Ticats, July 1
I have the perfect solution. Let's lobby the CFL to move the games from 60 minutes to 58 minutes. That should do it! Go Cats!
Rob Baboth, Ancaster
Canadians aren't happy
Why do politicians have trouble understanding the high level of anger directed at governments in our country? Perhaps the best explanation of that anger was written by podcaster Jenn Gerson, who described it as "the sound of an entire society grieving the death of its hopes and dreams."
Reg McGuire, Mount Hope
The complaining has just begun
Re: Enough ranting about Ford, June 29
This letter writer is sick to death of people complaining about Premier Doug Ford. It seems that he seems to be perfectly OK with him gutting heath care, education and lining his developer buddies' pockets, which was not part of his job to do. Instead of making things better, he continues to make everything worse. Guess what, sir - we Ontarians will not stop complaining about this corrupt premier, and make no mistake, we will be speaking with our votes when Ford is defeated in the next election.
Lisa Johnston, Stoney Creek
More balanced approach needed
Re: The re-education of Sarah Jama, July 2
I am disappointed that Margaret Shkimba's column about Sarah Jama was published.
Sarah Jama was elected to support her Hamilton constituency in Ontario's legislature for provincial matters. Her views on the Middle East were not to be her mandate on being elected, yet she made this her fight since Oct. 7. Regardless of what Ms. Jama may say, her views are strongly antisemitic and anti-Israel. She has been seen protesting in favour of the Palestinians at various venues with signs reading From the River to the Sea. These protests call for the destruction of the only free and democratic state in the Middle East. This is the reason she was sanctioned.
Ms. Shkimba has written numerous anti-Israel op-eds. It is time for the Spectator to bring a more balanced approach to its editorial pages.
Janet Weisz, Hamilton
Shelter shouldn't be about profit
Shelter, a basic human need, was not too long ago affordable to most individuals and families. Then, somewhere along the way, the entrepreneurial spirit of developers, realtors and investors moved housing from a basic need to an investment opportunity.
The extent of homelessness is more than a shame. We read stories of fully employed people unable to afford rent for any kind of units. And young families unable to afford homes since the notion of housing has shifted from a basic need to a huge money-making venture. Then we hear of families who forego other basic needs merely to provide shelter. Many should have seen this dire situation coming and not sustainable, and yet as a society, we did not have the tools or the will to put a halt to it. Now what? Will it take a crash or a collective undertaking to get back to basics?
Phil Bruckler, Stoney Creek
Sports tickets now beyond reach
Despite Gary Bettman, hockey is the most exciting sport out there. It is fast-paced and entertaining! Just look at the recent playoffs between Edmonton and Florida. It doesn't get any better than that! But to be perfectly fair, we as fans continue to pay exorbitant ticket prices not only for hockey, but baseball as well. Yes, you see all these excessive advertisements, but as fans we contribute to this. Yes, owners are wealthy because the price of a ticket puts lots of money in their pockets. To attend a hockey or baseball game in Toronto with your family costs you a small mortgage. But such is the world of sport and as a fan of both, I will continue to watch from home and not contribute any more revenue to the already rich, greedy owners.
Linda Ferrie, Waterdown
CAPTION: Ottawa Redblacks kicker Lewis Ward (10) watches his kick, leading to his game-winning field goal in the last seconds of the second-half CFL football action against the Hamilton Tiger-Cats in Ottawa on Sunday, June 30, 2024.
