Document 359 of 500

The double-cross, the shooting and the surviving witness: A sinister double-cross in Hamilton. A panicked getaway across Ontario and an international pursuit over three European countries. A shooting victim faces her boyfriend's accused killers. In a three-part series, The Spectator's Nicole O'Reilly brings us a story of betrayal, greed and murder

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/double-cross-shooting-surviving-witness/docview/3075885362/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=The+double-cross%2C+the+shooting+and+the+surviving+witness%3A+A+sinister+double-cross+in+Hamilton.+A+panicked+getaway+across+Ontario+and+an+international+pursuit+over+three+European+countries.+A+shooting+victim+faces+her+boyfriend%27s+accused+killers.+In+a+three-part+series%2C+The+Spectator%27s+Nicole+O%27Reilly+brings+us+a+story+of+betrayal%2C+greed+and+murder&amp;title=The+Spectator&amp;issn=11899417&amp;date=2024-07-05&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.1&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Spectator&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: It was late on a cold Sunday afternoon, the last day of February 2021, when the Audi Q5 pulled into 347 Arvin Ave. and stopped in front of the gate.
Like most businesses in the industrial area of Stoney Creek on the eastern edge of Hamilton, Colonial Pillars was closed for the weekend. The street was quiet, save for the odd passing car. The property was secured, the gate locked.
Oliver Karafa left his wife, Yun (Lucy) Lu Li, in the passenger seat, and approached the gate, returning once when someone walked by. Then it opened - with the padlock now cut - and the Audi disappeared around back.
There were no cameras to capture what happened next.
Just that morning, Karafa and Li had bought the white Audi. They paid Karafa's best friend Jaime Gutierrez about $18,000, using about $4,000 from Li's line of credit.
Karafa had already arranged for someone in Montreal to buy it, along with another vehicle: a Range Rover that didn't belong to them and was also destined for 347 Arvin that day.
From the surveillance cameras of businesses on Arvin, the Audi was unrecognizable, its rims crudely painted black, and untraceable to Karafa or Li.
Colonial Pillars had a for-sale sign out front. No arrangements had been made with a realtor to look at the property, but that is what Tyler Pratt and his pregnant girlfriend, Jordyn Romano, had been led to believe.
The couple thought they were meeting their friends to look at the property for a legal marijuana growing operation. They had already invested nearly a half a million dollars in another of Karafa's businesses and were anxious to see it returned.
"We considered them really good friends," Romano said.
But this Feb. 28 meeting was a ruse.
Karafa did not have the money to pay them back, and he did not want to set up a grow-op on Arvin or do any more business with the couple.
At 5:21 p.m. Karafa messaged Pratt and Romano on the encrypted messaging app Signal to say they would be at the property in 10 minutes, even though they were already there.
Just two minutes after receiving the message, Pratt and Romano arrived in their white Range Rover and drove back to meet their friends.
The shooting
For 31 minutes the two couples remained at the back of the property, unseen by anyone.
What exactly happened may never be known.
What is known is that Karafa shot at Romano three times as she sat inside her Range Rover, one of the bullets lodging in her heart and the other two missing her and hitting the vehicle.
Then he shot Pratt five or six times, with bullets hitting his arm, going through the back of his neck and a graze to his chin that may have been caused by the same bullet that hit his chest. This gunshot to the chest is what killed him, but his death would not have been immediate. He bled out on the cold ground as chaos ensued around him.
Both the Audi and Range Rover were damaged in the melee, either crashing into each other or into one of the warehouses at the back of the large property.
It is "difficult to imagine a more planned murder," said the judge who presided over Karafa's and Li's trials, after a jury found the pair guilty. He described it as an "outright execution."
It's not just that they travelled in an unknown car - Li's identifiable Mercedes was safely parked at their Toronto condo - but there were other steps taken to make it look like they never left their Erskine Avenue residence in the Yonge Street and Eglinton Avenue area of Toronto.
Karafa took the stairs where there are no cameras. Li wore a blond wig in the elevator. They switched cellphone SIM cards with a friend to give themselves a "digital alibi" that made it look like their phones never left the city.
The survivor
There was only one problem: Romano didn't die.
As Karafa and Li fled to Europe, Romano underwent life-saving surgery and spent days in a coma before she was able to tell police the identity of her killers.
Her memory of that day is foggy and, in some spots, unreliable because of the trauma she went through. But there are crucial moments she remembers in flashes.
Karafa staring at her with an odd look, his face shaking, he raised a gun and pointed it at her. Romano froze.
"Get out of here, Jo," was all she could remember Karafa saying before she was shot. Panicked, she put the car in reverse and crashed.
She doesn't remember seeing Pratt, who was standing outside, being shot. But she heard him scream, "What the f--k, bro."
Another flash is Karafa dragging her out of her car. Police would later find one of her thigh-high Christian Louboutin boots, the top folded over as if pulled down while she was dragged out, on the ground in the parking lot.
Karafa and Li took both vehicles from the scene, leaving Pratt and Romano for dead.
Video from neighbouring properties shows the Audi, and then Pratt and Romano's Range Rover, coming and going several times during the hour after the shooting. The Audi was left nearby on Green Road. Then the Range Rover returned to Arvin twice.
The prosecution would later argue that was Karafa - and possibly Li - returning to the scene looking for Romano to finish the job. They finally left for good around 6:52 p.m. in the Range Rover.
The getaway
Eleven minutes later, Karafa messaged his car buyer in Montreal, Mohamad Morad.
"Both cars are f--ked," and would need to be towed, Karafa wrote. He offered both the Audi and Pratt and Romano's Range Rover for just $2,000, but only if they were sent out of the country in a shipping container with no trace to Karafa. With Li in the passenger seat, Karafa drove the Range Rover to Etobicoke, and left it parked on Audley Street near the Mimico GO Station.
Back on Arvin Avenue in Hamilton, about 25 minutes after the last time the Range Rover passed, Annette Bright drove by with her boyfriend in the passenger seat.
She saw something on the road in the dark. At first, she thought it was an animal. But something nagged at her, so she turned around. That's when she saw a woman crawling on the road.
Romano was wearing one boot. Her sweater bloody. She said she was cold.
"Help me, I've been shot," she pleaded.
Police arrived by 7:22 p.m., soon followed by paramedics, who took Romano to hospital, where she would live, but lose her unborn baby. She was about 13 weeks pregnant; a fact Li and Karafa knew.
After discovering Romano on the road, police found Pratt's body on the pavement behind the warehouse, his arms outstretched, bullets, shell casings and other debris around him.
The ensuing investigation by Hamilton police followed a complex web of shady business investments and false friendships. The tale spans from Vancouver to Toronto to Hamilton to Europe.
It is a story about money, desperation and betrayal. Perhaps more than anything it is about greed.
The killers
Yun Lu Li was born in Shenzhen, China, a city north of Hong Kong, in 1995. She is a triplet, and the sisters are the oldest children in the family.
Like the rest of her siblings, Li's English name is adapted from her Chinese name Yun Lu, with the Lu becoming Lucy.
When the girls were little, their parents moved to Toronto, and the triplets stayed with family while their parents established themselves in Canada, spending years apart until the girls joined their parents when they were seven. In Canada, the family grew, with a younger sister and brother.
At first, their dad ran a driving school for Chinese immigrants, where their mom also worked. But soon their mom's wealth management business took off. Respon International grew to one of the top financial institutions working with Chinese-Canadians, with Hong Wei (Winnie) Liao becoming a prominent businessperson, photographed rubbing shoulders with political elites, including Prime Minister Justin Trudeau.
According to Lucy, the bread and butter of her mom's business is selling 20-year life insurance policies that allow clients, after an initial premium paid in Canada, to transfer funds from overseas without being taxed.
This type of insurance policy later plays a role in the murder plot.
Li said she initially struggled at school and was behind her sisters in learning English. Her first language is Mandarin. The family moved to North York, and it was at Earl Haig Secondary School where she met her future husband.
"He was older and cool from the beginning," Li said. "I was pretty nerdy with braces and bangs."
Karafa was born in Slovakia and immigrated to Canada in 2001 with his family, first living in Windsor and then Toronto. Li said she liked Karafa right away.
They went on dates, but Li claimed the short-lived relationship ended when Karafa wanted to have sex and Li wasn't ready. She also claimed he slept with a friend of hers at a party.
In April 2012, when Karafa was 19, he crashed his Range Rover on Mount Pleasant Road in Toronto.
He was only a G2 driver at the time. Karafa walked away, but his passenger - a friend and personal trainer - was killed.
Karafa was sentenced to five years in prison in 2014 and prohibited from driving for 15 years.
When Karafa first applied for parole in January 2016, he was denied because of two incidents in Beaver Creek Institution, where he was involuntarily transferred from minimum to medium security.
"The board suggested your behaviours since being incarcerated is indicative of a young man with an abnormal sense of entitlement; you agreed," the parole decision says. "You present as a person who believes rules made for society are for everyone else to live by and not you."
By December 2016, Karafa was granted day parole, but denied full parole.
Karafa appealed the decision to deny his full parole, and that was rejected. But six months later, he was released on full parole in August 2017. He was 24 and the parole board believed he was motivated to get his life on track. He had a job as a manager at a café and was supported by his sister, her husband and his unnamed girlfriend, the document says.
His parents had already returned to Europe, where they lived in the family home in Levice, Slovakia - a location that would play a role later when Karafa and Li were on the run.
Karafa's first prison sentence for the fatal drunk-driving offence officially expired on May 4, 2019, while the driving ban continued.
Li said her upbringing was strict. Her mother was a "tiger mom" with resolute plans for her children.
All were encouraged to study business. Li's mom offered a reward of a car to the first triplet to acquire accreditation to sell life insurance. Li was the first to do this, focusing on that more than her studies at Western University in London, Ont. Eventually she transferred Ryerson University (now Toronto Metropolitan University) to finish school, while working with insurance companies.
Li, working with her mother, struggled to attract bigger clients herself and realized that she needed more life experience. She, along with her triplet sisters, asked for a break from work at Christmas 2018. The family often had annual meetings around the holidays where they would discuss what they accomplished that year and plans for the future. Li said she was given two years of freedom, with her mom offering a $5,000 a month allowance.
The triplets had an account on Instagram together where they attracted sponsors who paid for the sisters to model their products. Li said they made about $500 to $1,000 per sponsored post, but that the money was split three ways and was more for fun and not enough to live on. One sponsor gave her the blond wig.
It was during this time, when Li was about 23, that Karafa came back into her life. They messaged on Instagram and met for a date.
The relationship moved fast, with Li moving in with Karafa after their second date. Li's mom stopped giving her an allowance because Karafa paid for most things.
Out of prison, Karafa worked as an entrepreneur and seemed to be making good money. According to Li, his businesses included everything from exporting cars, renting out a studio to film social-media ads, to selling weighted blankets. But much of his business was in Europe, where he had a partner in Prague.
The couple got a two-story penthouse condo at 18 Erskine Ave. in Toronto. Because Karafa was a prohibited driver, Li drove him everywhere, including to multiple business meetings.
This included frequent meetings hosted at the Mississauga offices of Karafa's longtime friend Alex (also known as Sasha), where Karafa would later meet Pratt and Romano.
Karafa and Li travelled often, including to Europe to see his family and conduct business. In spring 2020, about two months into lockdowns at the start of the COVID-19 pandemic, the couple were going a little "stir crazy," and Karafa wanted to return to Europe.
They bought tickets to Zurich for May 16. But when they got to the airport, they had a problem. Karafa, who had dual citizenship in Canada and Slovakia, was allowed to return to Europe, but Li was not.
At the suggestion of someone working at the airline they decided to get married, Li said. Karafa was going to leave with or without her.
"We were not planning on marrying, but I was very in love with him and due to travel restrictions I made the irresponsible choice and decided to ... marry him on the spot," Li said.
They stepped out of line and married with an officiant presiding over Zoom. They spent months in Europe before returning to Toronto that summer.
The victims
Tyler Pratt was raised in a large and loving family in British Columbia, where he coached kids' hockey and, like Karafa, was also an aspiring entrepreneur.
Pratt grew up in Taylor, B.C., then, as a young man, he lived in Kelowna before relocating to Vancouver, where his two children were born.
Described by family as hardworking, his businesses included a diamond company called Hard as Diamonds and a wellness supplement company, Elite Organic Nutrition.
But he made most of his money selling cocaine. He wasn't a street-level dealer - he arranged shipments by phone. He once made $1 million in a day, Romano said, but that was rare.
By 2020, he had cash and was looking to invest and move away from cocaine.
"He wanted to just become a more legitimate businessman," Romano later said in court. "He wanted to get out of the cocaine life; we wanted to have a family and be normal and escape that negativity."
She grew up in Ontario, with family in Hamilton. In her early 20s, she went back and forth to B.C. for a few years before moving there when she was about 24.
Romano worked as an exotic dancer and met Pratt about six months before they started dating. She stopped working and he paid for their lifestyle, which included luxury goods and travel. Their Toronto condo, photographed by police after the murder, was filled with boxes of designer shoes, bags and other items.
Romano agreed that Pratt was careful to live off the radar because of his cocaine business, but she said he was kind and had an even temper. Pratt's former wife - the mother of his two children - also said she never had issues with Pratt and that he always put his children first.
Romano and Pratt were together for about a year before he was killed, first living in Vancouver and then moving to Toronto not long before the shooting. They wanted to start a new life together.
In 2020, the couple travelled back and forth between Toronto and Vancouver often, both for work and so Pratt could see his kids. On the day he was killed, he had just returned from a trip for his daughter's birthday.
In July 2020, Romano took a solo trip to Toronto and messaged a girlfriend she hadn't seen in a while. This friend was dating a guy named Alex (Sasha) and the group went out for dinner.
The swindle
Later, Romano introduced Sasha, described in court as the owner of a Mississauga construction company, and Pratt. The two men hit it off. Pratt was looking for a business to invest in and Sasha helped arrange meetings.
Among those Sasha introduced was his friend, Oliver Karafa, who he had known for a decade and vouched for.
At that first meeting, Karafa told them: "Yo, I have something that can make us a lot of money."
Soon after, Pratt and Romano invested $170,000 in Karafa's business, selling truckloads of personal protective equipment (PPE) in Europe. Selling everything from hospital gowns to syringes promised to be lucrative, especially during the pandemic. Soon after, they invested more cash and, by late fall, they'd given Karafa roughly $470,000.
"I really liked Oliver," Romano said. "He was just always nice and easygoing. Tyler loved Oliver, they had a great friendship. He adored him."
And they trusted him. The couple never signed a formal contract.
There were a lot of meetings with Karafa and Pratt, often at Sasha's office. It was there they first met Li. By late 2020, Sasha was dating one of Li's sisters, Jane, one of the triplets, and Li would visit with her sister after driving Karafa to the meetings.
Li later testified that she knew Pratt had invested money in the PPE business, but didn't know the particulars. She wasn't involved in the PPE business meetings.
Karafa promised big profits, but claimed the money was stuck in Europe. Romano said he showed them a European account with millions in it. He promised $3 million was their share.
But by January, Karafa had failed to pay Romano and Pratt and was desperately trying to find ways to delay.
His answer: an insurance scheme. This is where Li really enters the story.
Li had let her licence lapse, but she was toying with getting back into insurance and knew the industry well. Karafa sold Pratt on one of the 20-year life insurance policies and set up meetings with the four of them.
The held several meetings, including at the North York home of Li's parents - which the family continued to own despite her parents primarily living in Vancouver - as well as at Pratt and Romano's new Cumberland Street condo in Toronto, where they moved in February.
Romano said Li initially suggested a $2.8-million policy, but she and Pratt couldn't afford that so it was reduced to $1.5 million. Romano believed if they paid the first premium in cash from Canada, their money stuck in Europe could be added later, allowing them to transfer the money without paying tax or risk the attention of authorities, including Canada's national financial intelligence agency, FINTRAC (Financial Transactions and Reports Analysis Centre of Canada). Romano also said she was told they could borrow against the policy, allowing them to get a loan in Canada to expand business.
The truth is that insurance premiums cannot be paid in cash. But Pratt and Romano didn't understand how it worked.
Li later admitted the insurance scheme was a ruse to buy Karafa more time to come up the money owed. She said she initially was sincere in trying to sell them a policy, but that after learning the premium would need to be in cash, she played along to help Karafa. She never intended to submit the application.
Li, who said she never like Romano and Pratt, claimed she offered to pretend to pay the first premium. Police would later find a $1.5-million cheque in an envelope of insurance documents. But Romano said she never saw that cheque and instead believed Li was going to loan them $1 million - taken from her mom's account where she "wouldn't notice" it missing.
Pratt needed the loan because he was having cash-flow problems, including losing a cocaine shipment that was seized at the border, court heard.
Romano said the two couples were set to meet at Li's mom's offices on March 1 to finalize the insurance application. Karafa was going to be paying them back the initial $500,000 investment, with the $1-million loan from Li that would make up the $1.5 million required. Then they were going to sign paperwork finalizing their positions with the European PPE business at Karafa's lawyer's office afterward.
Instead, by March 1, Li and Karafa were on a flight to Europe, Pratt was dead and Romano in a coma, all while Hamilton police worked to put together what happened the day before at 347 Arvin Ave.
Nicole O'Reilly is a crime and justice reporter at The Spectator. noreilly@thespec.com

Document 360 of 500

LCBO workers will strike, union says as talks fail

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/lcbo-workers-will-strike-union-says-as-talks-fail/docview/3075884370/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=LCBO+workers+will+strike%2C+union+says+as+talks+fail&amp;title=The+Spectator&amp;issn=11899417&amp;date=2024-07-05&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.10&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Spectator&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: The union representing workers at Ontario's main liquor retailer says they will be going on strike after talks broke down ahead of a deadline.
The Ontario Public Service Employees Union had set a strike deadline of 12:01 a.m. Friday for its approximately 10,000 workers at the Liquor Control Board of Ontario.
Finance Minister Peter Bethlenfalvy's office said in a statement that it is disappointed by the union's decision to walk away from the bargaining table hours before the deadline.
A union spokesperson had earlier said they were committed to bargaining a good deal for workers.
The LCBO has said that if a strike takes place, all locations will close for 14 days and, after that point, if the strike continues, the Crown corporation will open 30 stores three days a week, on Fridays, Saturdays and Sundays, with limited hours.
It has also said it will continue operating its online retail services, though what customers can purchase may be limited.
"Recognizing the impact of a strike on distribution and operations, the LCBO will need to institute reasonable caps on products in store and online," the LCBO wrote in its strike plan.
A strike wouldn't affect LCBO convenience outlets in smaller communities and sales would also continue at grocery stores and other outlets.
The workers are seeking wage increases and more full-time jobs, saying part-time roles have become 70 per cent of their workforce.
The union is also fearful of job losses after Premier Doug Ford's government announced plans to open up the alcohol market and expand sales in convenience stores and grocery stores.
CAPTION:

Document 361 of 500

Will the LCBO close for two weeks? Sources say two sides are far apart as strike deadline looms

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/will-lcbo-close-two-weeks-sources-say-sides-are/docview/3076062824/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Will+the+LCBO+close+for+two+weeks%3F+Sources+say+two+sides+are+far+apart+as+strike+deadline+looms&amp;title=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-07-04&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: They're still at the table - at least for now.
Contract talks between the LCBO and the union representing thousands of its workers are continuing, just hours ahead of a 12:01 a.m. Friday strike deadline, but it's looking more likely that workers are headed for the picket lines.
Sources familiar with negotiations said the two sides are talking but that the LCBO and the Ontario Public Service Employees Union are still far apart, with several major issues - including job security - not even having been discussed in detail yet.
"The province is sticking to its guns," one source said.
OPSEU has repeatedly criticized the provincial government's decision to open up Ontario's alcohol retail market, arguing that it puts LCBO jobs and profits at risk, while helping private retailers, including grocery and big box stores, as well as convenience stores.
"Front-line LCBO workers are proud that the revenues we help to generate give back to their communities, and we won't back down in our fight for a strong future for the LCBO and the public services funded by LCBO revenues," OPSEU bargaining committee chair Colleen MacLeod told the Star earlier this week. "Without a strong commitment to that future, we will have to strike."
The union also says that 70 per cent of LCBO workers are "casual," meaning they have no guaranteed hours, benefits or sick pay. The LCBO, meanwhile, argued that at least 50 per cent of casual employees are guaranteed at least 1,000 hours per year.
The union says LCBO wages range from $17.65 an hour to roughly $30. The LCBO says the range for customer service representatives goes from $17.85 to $32.58.
Colin Blachar, a spokesperson for Ontario's finance minister, said in an email that it's "disappointing that OPSEU could be heading toward an unnecessary strike that threatens people's ability to enjoy their summers. It's never been more clear that Ontario consumers need and deserve the same choice and convenience every other Canadian enjoys.
"We will not be privatizing the LCBO. The LCBO will continue to be a publicly owned retailer, as well as operating as the exclusive wholesaler for all retail, bars and restaurants selling alcohol and spirits."
Liberal MPP Stephen Blais, the party's critic for labour issues, called on the government to make a deal.
"LCBO workers deserve job security, livable wages and fair treatment. Doug Ford needs to do the right thing and come to a fair deal that respects workers, keeps Ontario working and doesn't disrupt the lives of hardworking Ontarians," Blais said in a written statement. "We know OPSEU workers are at the table ready to bargain for a fair deal, the government needs to do the same."
The LCBO has said all its stores will be closed for two weeks if there's a strike, but that 30 would reopen July 19, for limited shopping every Friday, Saturday and Sunday. It also said online ordering would still be available in the event of a strike, but said details wouldn't be available until a strike begins.
It's a more complicated set of negotiations than typical contract talks, where the main bone of contention is strictly money, argued labour relations professor Rafael Gomez.
"Because of all the changes in alcohol retailing, the union knows that there will be fewer workers at the LCBO by 2030. And likely, fewer LCBO stores," said Gomez, director of U of T's Centre for Industrial Relations and Human Resources.
"The government is going to push the union to either have high wages in return for less job security, or low wages and higher job security," Gomez said. "Either way, through attrition or store closures, the government will have fewer LCBO outlets and fewer LCBO employees by 2030."
CAPTION: The LCBO said online ordering would still be available in the event of a strike, but said details wouldn't be available until a strike begins.

Document 362 of 500

With early election looming, Doug Ford is promoting the controversial Highway 413

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/with-early-election-looming-doug-ford-is/docview/3076062817/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=With+early+election+looming%2C+Doug+Ford+is+promoting+the+controversial+Highway+413&amp;title=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-07-04&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: As the Progressive Conservatives rev up for an early election next year, Premier Doug Ford has filmed a campaign-style video touting progress on Highway 413.
The minute-long promotional spot posted to Ford's social media accounts on Thursday boasts that the Tories are "getting it done" on the controversial 52-km freeway linking Highway 401 in Milton to Highway 400 in Vaughan.
While building the highway was the cornerstone promise of the Tories' successful 2022 re-election campaign, there has been little visible progress since - save for some blue and white billboards posted along nearby existing routes.
"Highway 413: part of our $28 billion plan to build roads and highways," blare the signs that are a familiar sight along Highway 400.
With Ford considering an election in early 2025 - more than one year before the scheduled June 2026 vote - the Tories are scrambling to demonstrate that they have kept previous campaign promises.
That's a major reason why the government has earmarked $225 million to liberalize the sale of beer, wine and premixed cocktails by Oct. 31, though critics warn it could cost taxpayers $1 billion to expedite booze in variety stores, more supermarkets and big box outlets.
In the video of him on a tour with Transportation Minister Prabmeet Sarkaria, shot without any journalists or media cameras present, Ford assures viewers "preliminary work is well underway" on the highway.
"Crews are currently on site flying in drilling equipment by helicopter to test soil and bedrock conditions. It's great to see firsthand ... the crews drilling boreholes and assessing the sample conditions," the premier says.
"This step is critical as we move forward into our next phase of designing the bridges that will cross the Humber Valley," he continues.
"With construction tracking to start early next year, we will be adding 3,500 good-paying jobs each year - along with the highway itself as it will contribute $350 million to Ontario's GDP (gross domestic product)."
A major roadblock to construction was cleared in April when Queen's Park and Ottawa agreed to establish a joint working group for provincial and federal officials to "recommend appropriate measures to minimize environmental impacts in areas of federal environmental jurisdiction."
Even though the freeway was the Tories' signature campaign pledge two years ago - and they won every riding along the proposed route - it remains contentious.
NDP Leader Marit Stiles, Liberal Leader Bonnie Crombie and Green Leader Mike Schreiner all oppose the highway and warn it could be a reprise of Ford's $8.28-billion Greenbelt land swap scandal now under investigation by the RCMP.
The opposition parties charge the Tories could use the freeway to reward favoured land owners along the route because adjacent property would become more valuable for potential housing development.
Stiles has criticized Ford for "spending billions of taxpayers' money on a project with no end date in sight ... a project that also threatens Ontario's farmlands and gives preferential treatment to insiders."
Crombie has predicted that "just like the Greenbelt scandal, the ineffective $10-billion Highway 413 is designed to benefit one group of people: Doug Ford's wealthy insiders, Conservative donors and rich land speculators."
Schreiner has dubbed the highway "an expensive sprawl supercharger that will cost Ontarians at least $10 billion and pave over 2,000 acres of prime farmland - all to save commuters a few extra seconds."
Environmental Defence, an activist group, has pointed out the 413 would cut through protected lands, pave over thousands of acres of farmland and cross streams and rivers more than 100 times.
But in the video, Ford emphasizes something must be done to tackle gridlock.
"The government promised to build Highway 413 - and we're getting it done," he says.
"We know too many people across the GTA are stuck in traffic and are counting on us to help them get where they need to go faster."
CAPTION: Premier Doug Ford takes to the stage to address the U.S.-Canada Summit, in Toronto on June 11, 2024.

Subject: Roads & highways

Document 363 of 500

40 bullets, a blond wig and bloody clothing: Clues the killers left behind in escape to Europe

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/40-bullets-blond-wig-bloody-clothing-clues/docview/3075692307/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=40+bullets%2C+a+blond+wig+and+bloody+clothing%3A+Clues+the+killers+left+behind+in+escape+to+Europe&amp;title=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-07-04&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Tyler Pratt and Jordyn Romano expected to meet their friends for a routine real-estate inspection in Hamilton, and a dinner together afterward at a nice restaurant in Oakville.
Their friends, Oliver Karafa and Lucy Li, planned something more sinister.
When Pratt and Romano arrived in their usual white Range Rover, Karafa and Li were already there in a car Pratt and Romano didn't recognize, a white Audi.
The two vehicles parked facing each other at the back of an industrial building at 347 Arvin Ave. in Stoney Creek.
Where's the realtor? Pratt asked.
They were expecting a man named Michael to show them around, but he was nowhere to be seen. What's more, they were locked out of the building.
Li was sent to look for a lockbox; she didn't find one - that's because no one had actually made plans to view the property.
Romano, who was 13 weeks pregnant and cold, returned to their Range Rover to warm up. Li was directed to join her while the guys talked.
Romano was dressed up, in a tan sweater and thigh-high Christian Louboutin boots. She believed the foursome were going out for dinner at the restaurant Soto Soto in Oakville afterward. It was strange, she thought, that Li - usually impeccably dressed - was in sweats. She "showed up in rags," Romano said.
Romano and Pratt had known Karafa about four months, since they started investing in one of his businesses. Over the preceding months, they met often. Pratt and Karafa spoke almost daily. Romano believed the two couples had become good friends.
Indeed, Pratt and Romano bought Karafa a diamond chain as a gift for his birthday a week earlier. And two days earlier, Karafa messaged them about taking a trip to Italy in June. Li spoke to Romano about her upcoming baby shower.
But inside the car that day, something seemed off.
Li was "nervously texting," Romano said. "She comes in the car and couldn't even look at me."
When Li got out of the car, Romano slid over into the driver's seat.
Treachery revealed
Her window was down when she looked up and froze. Karafa, his face shaking, was pointing a gun at her.
"Get out of here, Jo," is all Romano could remember him saying before she was shot. Panicked, she reversed her car and crashed into something. She heard Pratt saying, "What the f--k, bro," before more gunshots.
More than an hour later, about 7:20 p.m., a passing motorist - Annette Bright - found Romano crawling on the road in front of 347 Arvin Ave. Police later found Pratt's body lying on the cold ground behind the property.
Both cars, damaged in the chaos, were gone. The Audi was found abandoned nearby parked at Green Road and South Service Road.
Li and Karafa fled in Romano's Range Rover. Karafa - who was prohibited from driving after being convicted of impaired driving causing death years earlier - had to hold the door closed as they drove on the highway.
They dumped the vehicle near Mimico GO Station in Etobicoke. Li says Karafa later told her he also dumped the gun somewhere near there, but it was never found.
Karafa arranged for a guy he met through a Kijiji ad, named Mohamad Morad, to buy both vehicles for $2,000, strip them for parts and ship them out of the country. But police found the vehicles first.
As Romano fought for her life, Karafa and Li took a taxi back to their condo on Erskine Avenue, near Yonge Street and Eglinton Avenue in Toronto.
Before the shooting, they had switched cellphone SIM cards on one of their phones with Phi Chinh - an unsuspecting friend of Karafa's - and then switched them back later that evening.
When data from cell towers was inspected later, it appeared as if Li and Karafa stayed home, while Chinh travelled to the shooting scene. Indeed, Chinh was later arrested for murder. When he explained to police what actually happened, he was let go.
Back at their condo, Karafa and Li tried to get some rest, but early the next morning they saw an article about the shooting in The Hamilton Spectator: one male was dead and one female critically injured.
Now they had a problem. Romano could identify them.
They thought their careful planning would allow them to continue their lives without paying Pratt and Romano back. Pratt and Romano had invested nearly $500,000 in a business of Karafa's selling medical supplies in Europe. Karafa had promised big profits but the couple had not been repaid a penny.
Now, with a witness, they had to run.
On the lam
Surveillance video shows Karafa, carrying a garbage bag, and Li, carrying a UPS envelope, leaving their condo shortly before 3 a.m. on March 1. They drove Li's Mercedes north to Muskoka, stopping at the Glen Orchard General Store near Port Carling, which also includes a gas station, restaurant, tackle shop and other properties off Butterfly Lake.
When the store's general manager, Charlene Smith, arrived at work around 6:30 a.m. on March 1, she noticed the vehicle in the parking lot. She opened the store at 7 a.m. and, about an hour later, saw an attractive young couple come in to use the washroom.
They bought some drinks and left in a taxi.
Chris Whittingham, who owns the Glen Orchard store, didn't think much of the abandoned Mercedes until his daughter found a garbage bag in the recycling bin filled with 40 nine-millimetre bullets, a blond wig, clothing and other suspicious items. He called police.
Ontario Provincial Police documented the evidence found in the bag, including a pair of Li's pants with blood drops and a phone charger with blood on it. The key fob to an Audi was also found in the bag.
Inside the abandoned Mercedes, police found more clothing and items, including the UPS folder that contained documents from the life-insurance scheme. Before the shooting Romano and Pratt believed Li was finalizing a $1.5 million life-insurance policy that would help them transfer some of the money they were owed from Europe; Li later admitted she never intended to file the application and only pretended to go along with it to buy Karafa more time.
By the time police found the evidence in Muskoka, Li and Karafa were long gone.
Sandy Sethi, the taxi driver, was going to drop them off at the bus station in Barrie, but the couple changed their plans and she drove them to Toronto. She dropped them off at a subway station, earning $400 for the trip.
Li and Karafa then travelled to Union Station, where their movements could be traced on surveillance video buying tickets and then boarding a train to Montreal. In Montreal, they sat silently on a shuttle bus that took them to Montreal-Trudeau International Airport. They arrived at 4:40 p.m., and checked in for a flight to Germany and a connecting flight to Prague.
Li carried nothing. Karafa had a bag filled with cash: between $16,000 and $17,000. Because of this, Li said they were stopped at the gate until she showed them her banking records for the money she had withdrawn to buy the Audi from Karafa's friend the day before.
The prosecution would later argue the money was likely Pratt's, which he brought back from Vancouver the day of the shooting. That money was never recovered.
In messages between Karafa and Li just two days before the shooting, Karafa said, "I hope these retards have money."
By March 4, Hamilton police had announced warrants for Karafa and Li for first-degree murder and attempted murder. Later that month, they said the fugitives had fled to Eastern Europe and were now wanted on international warrants.
Jim Callender, then a detective sergeant with Hamilton police and the case manager in charge of the investigation, vowed they would find them. It was a promise Callender, now an inspector, and his team, including Det. David Tsuchida, who was the lead detective, made good on several months later.
An international pursuit
What happened between the time Li and Karafa landed in Prague and were arrested in Budapest, Hungary, months later has been the subject of speculation. The details that are known largely come from Li, who testified about their run from the law.
Karafa was an entrepreneur with multiple businesses. Most were in Europe. Karafa's business partner, Martin Macek, was based in Prague.
After landing there, there they took an Uber to Macek's condo and then spent a few days at an Airbnb.
Business records obtained by The Spectator show their partnership in at least one business was dissolved a few days after Karafa's arrival. In 2021, Macek declined to speak with The Spectator and said he found out about the murder only after Karafa left Prague.
After a few days in Prague, Li and Karafa took a train to Slovakia, where his dad picked them up and drove to their family home, Li said. It was just Karafa's parents and their cat in the house.
Karafa and Li were both exhausted; they showered and fell asleep.
But Li awoke around midnight when her phone rang. It was one of her sisters telling her warrants for their arrests had been reported in the media.
Li woke her husband, who woke his dad. The Karafa family home would be the first place authorities would look for the fugitive couple, so they would have to leave.
Karafa's father didn't want to lose his son again to the justice system in Canada - Karafa had already served a prison sentence for the drunk-driving death.
In 2021, there were strict pandemic lockdown rules in Eastern Europe. And, as a Chinese woman, Li said she stood out. She was made to crouch in the back of a truck while Karafa's dad drove them to a friend's house.
Li said she called her mom, Hong Wei 'Winnie' Liao, who hysterically yelled at her daughter while Li sobbed. Her mom was telling her to come home.
It was then that Liao, a high-profile businessperson with political ties, released a statement in Chinese-language media.
In the statement, which the Spectator translated from Mandarin, the family said they were "shocked, disturbed and puzzled by Lucy's involvement in the tragic incident" and offered condolences to the victims and their families.
Addressing Li directly, they said they were sure she would not be involved in such an incident voluntarily and would not do "such a ridiculous thing."
"We can hardly imagine the shock and anxiety you experienced after the incident."
The family implored Li to co-operate.
Li's mom arranged for her to speak with a lawyer on the phone while they were in Slovakia. Karafa told her he would not turn himself in.
That night, she awoke to hear Karafa speaking Slovak in the kitchen with men who claimed to be Europol police officers. The men, who called themselves Paul and Milo, drove vehicles with diplomatic plates and claimed they could help make the warrants go away.
Li said Karafa's father paid 250,000 euros that night - and more money later - for their help. She also said her mom paid about 200,000 euros through a lawyer after another man, Sasa (pronounced Sasha), who appeared to be a higher-ranked officer, became involved.
They were first moved to an apartment that belonged to Paul, where their phones were taken from them and fake passports issued.
Li's fake Slovak passport listed her name as Ying Feng Nichta, born in Cebu, Philippines.
Karafa's said his name was Jakub Nichta born in Bratislava.
With their fake passports, Li said they were driven in military-style vehicles across the border into Hungary, where they spent April and half of May in a safe house overseen by a family from Syria in the city of Gyor. Li called it the "pink house" due to its colour.
She said they were told on May 15 that the international warrants were gone, but that Hamilton police were slow to update. They were given their phones back and travelled to Budapest, where Li said they wrongly believed they were free.
Justice closes in
The couple stayed in an Airbnb and Li - who wanted to come home - started looking into getting new Canadian passports. She said they spent a lot of time just walking around the city and visiting a church.
It was outside a church where the pair were arrested June 12.
Police in Hungary released photos of the pair, hands cuffed and heads bowed to avoid cameras.
Li immediately waived extradition and agreed to come back to Canada. Hamilton police travelled to Budapest and escorted her back. She landed in Toronto a month after her arrest on July 13 and was charged.
In Hamilton, she was interviewed for hours by Det. Troy Ashbaugh, an expert interrogator who is now retired. Li, used to flying first class, told him she was "not used to sitting in tight spaces" while being escorted home in economy. Her mom, she said, had offered to fly them all back privately.
Throughout the interview, Li was polite but refused to answer many questions. She repeatedly said there was more to the story, but her lawyer and mother had told her not to talk.
She admitted to being at the scene of the shooting, but claimed to be far away when shots were fired.
Back in Budapest, Karafa had perks in jail, including a contraband cellphone. He fought extradition and instead wanted to go home to Slovakia.
According to court records there, Karafa argued that corruption within Hamilton police and the Hamilton-Wentworth Detention Centre, where Karafa was expected to be held, made it unsafe for him to return to Canada.
His lawyers also argued that they had hired a private investigator to look into Pratt and his "extensive criminal connections."
However, the court there - including the Budapest Court of Appeal - dismissed those concerns. Canadian authorities provided detailed information about Karafa's rights and security plans.
Just over a week after the appeal court upheld Karafa's extradition and while Canadian authorities were working out the plan to transport him back to Hamilton, Li was granted bail.
Many people charged with murder in Canada are not granted bail. But granting bail to someone who was an international fugitive is exceptionally rare.
In Li's case, her mother and other sureties promised strict control, including bars on her windows at the family's North York property. Li was released on bail with a $2.7-million bond on Jan. 20, 2022.
Only four months later, Li was again in custody after allegedly breaching her bail conditions by going out to a party and being in contact with someone she was prohibited from communicating with.
By March 30, Karafa - escorted by Hamilton police homicide detectives - arrived back in Canada and was transported to jail. He has been behind bars ever since.
Nicole O'Reilly is a crime and justice reporter at The Spectator. noreilly@thespec.com

Document 364 of 500

Leaders share a similar problem

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/leaders-share-similar-problem/docview/3075597394/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=unknown&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Leaders+share+a+similar+problem&amp;title=The+Spectator&amp;issn=11899417&amp;date=2024-07-04&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.14&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Spectator&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Justin Trudeau announced on Monday he's headed for Washington next week. What will he and U.S. President Joe Biden be talking about, behind the scenes at the NATO summit?
Endurance might be one topic. It's been a bad week for Trudeau and Biden - arguably their worst weeks - with increasingly open questions of whether either leader should be hanging on.
Their two situations are not exactly similar, but they speak to the pall hanging over progressive governments in Canada and the U.S. and how the discontent is rattling the very top of the Liberal party here and the Democrats south of the border.
For Trudeau, it was the Liberals' loss in the Toronto-St. Paul's byelection on June 24 that is being seen as a verdict on his leadership. For Biden, it was the incredibly weak performance in last Thursday night's CNN debate with Donald Trump.
Now it's not just their rivals saying they shouldn't be the men to fight the next election, but people within their own party, too. That is a prime condition for political chaos: it's rarely your enemies in politics who do you in; it's your friends.
On Tuesday, a Democrat in the House of Representatives became the first lawmaker to say it publicly. Texas Rep. Lloyd Doggett said in a statement that Biden should "make the painful and difficult decision to withdraw." In Canada, Liberal MP Wayne Long wrote an open letter saying "new leadership" is required.
Trump has had a good week. Not only did Biden help make him look good, but the U.S. Supreme Court handed down a decision on presidential immunity that went Trump's way, meaning he's unlikely to be tried for the Jan. 6 insurrection before Americans go to the polls.
What's worse for Biden is no one is listening to his warnings that Trump is dangerous to democracy. Incredibly, a Washington Post poll last week among swing-state voters showed that 44 per cent of the citizens there trusted Trump to better handle threats to democracy, compared to just 33 per cent who thought Biden was better placed.
A new Abacus poll in Canada contains echoes of that finding. In a survey conducted last week, Abacus asked about Canadian concerns with Trump's potential election victory as a threat to democratic stability. Fewer than half - 41 per cent - said they were deeply concerned.
The contrast is especially sharp between Conservative supporters and those for other parties. Only 34 per cent of Conservative-leaning respondents said Trump could have an impact on democratic stability, compared to 59 per cent of Liberals and 67 per cent of New Democrats who view Trump as dangerous to democracy.
Overall, says Abacus CEO David Coletto, "the risk hasn't really sunk in and I think that's meaningful when it comes to the political implications here in Canada."
Abacus also asked about how Canada's international relationships might have to change if Trump wins. Canadians were either divided or neutral about how our economic ties should change under a Trump threat to democracy, with 31 per cent saying Canada should reduce its ties, 29 per cent saying it shouldn't and 40 per cent with no leaning either way.
"Throughout the poll results, Conservatives in Canada are far less concerned and less wanting to see our relationship with the U.S. change if Trump is elected in November. I think This reflects the affinity some Conservative-oriented voters in Canada have for Trump and likely their belief that things could actually get better," Coletto said.
The survey was conducted online with 1,900 Canadian adults from June 20 to 25. The margin of error for a comparable probability-based random sample of the same size is within 2.248 percentage points, 19 times out of 20.
Trudeau and Biden have been saying for some time that they're confident that once the voters take a close look at the alternatives, the next election choice will be clear and it will land in their favour. But after the past bad week for both of them, these leaders have to confront the possibility the main existential threat weighing in the balance is to their own leadership. That's no doubt something they probably should discuss when they get together next week.
Susan Delacourt covers national politics for
Torstar.
CAPTION:

CREDIT: susan delacourt OPINION;

Document 365 of 500

Justin Trudeau and Joe Biden are two leaders with one problem: Many of their supporters think their time is up

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/justin-trudeau-joe-biden-are-two-leaders-with-one/docview/3075329969/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Justin+Trudeau+and+Joe+Biden+are+two+leaders+with+one+problem%3A+Many+of+their+supporters+think+their+time+is+up&amp;title=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-07-03&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Justin Trudeau announced on Monday that he's headed for Washington next week. What will he and President Joe Biden be talking about, behind the scenes at the NATO summit?
Endurance might be one topic. It's been a bad week for Trudeau and Biden - arguably their worst weeks - with increasingly open questions of whether either leader should be hanging on.
Their two situations are not exactly similar, but they speak to the pall hanging over progressive governments in Canada and the U.S. at the moment and how the discontent is rattling the very top of the Liberal party here and the Democrats south of the border.
For Trudeau, it was the Liberals' loss in the Toronto-St. Paul's byelection on June 24 that is being seen as a verdict on his leadership. For Biden, it was the incredibly weak performance in last Thursday night's CNN debate with Donald Trump.
Now it's not just their rivals saying they shouldn't be the men to fight the next election, but people within their own party, too. That is a prime condition for political chaos: it's rarely your enemies in politics who do you in; it's your friends.
On Tuesday, a Democrat in the House of Representatives became the first lawmaker to say it publicly. Texas Rep. Lloyd Doggett of Texas said in a statement on Tuesday that Biden should "make the painful and difficult decision to withdraw."
In Canada, no MP has directly said that to Trudeau during calls the prime minister is making, sources say. But there have been a lot of panicked conversations and calls for meetings, including one Liberal MP, Wayne Long, saying in an open letter that "new leadership" is required. The PM is still saying, as he told CBC on Canada Day, that he's "committed" to the job he now holds and that there's still lots to do.
There's another thing Biden and Trudeau might want to discuss: the prospect of a Trump victory doesn't seem as unthinkable to their voting publics as it might be to either leader. Trump has had a good week. Not only did Biden help make him look good, but the U.S. Supreme Court handed down a decision on presidential immunity that went Trump's way, meaning he's unlikely to be tried for the Jan. 6 insurrection before Americans go to the polls.
What's worse for Biden is no one is listening to his warnings that Trump is dangerous to democracy. Incredibly, a Washington Post poll last week among swing-state voters showed that 44 per cent of the citizens there trusted Trump to better handle threats to democracy, compared to just 33 per cent who thought Biden was better placed.
A new Abacus poll in Canada contains echoes of that finding. In a survey conducted last week, Abacus asked about Canadian concerns with Trump's potential election victory as a threat to democratic stability. Fewer than half - 41 per cent - said they were deeply concerned.
The contrast is especially sharp between Conservative supporters and those for other parties. Only 34 per cent of Conservative-leaning respondents said Trump could have an impact on democratic stability, compared to 59 per cent of Liberals and 67 per cent of New Democrats who view Trump as dangerous to democracy.
Overall, says Abacus CEO David Coletto, "the risk hasn't really sunk in and I think that's meaningful when it comes to the political implications here in Canada."
Abacus also asked about how Canada's international relationships might have to change if Trump wins. Canadians were either divided or neutral about how our economic ties should change under a Trump threat to democracy, with 31 per cent saying Canada should reduce its ties, 29 per cent saying it shouldn't and 40 per cent with no leaning either way.
"Throughout the poll results, Conservatives in Canada are far less concerned and less wanting to see our relationship with the U.S. change if Trump is elected in November. I think this reflects the affinity some Conservative-oriented voters in Canada have for Trump and likely their belief that things could actually get better," Coletto said.
The survey was conducted online with 1,900 Canadian adults from June 20 to 25. The margin of error for a comparable probability-based random sample of the same size is within 2.248 percentage points, 19 times out of 20.
Trudeau and Biden have been saying for some time that they're confident that once the voters take a close look at the alternatives, the next election choice will be clear and it will land in their favour.
But after the past bad week for both of them, these leaders have to confront the possibility that the main existential threat weighing in the balance is to their own leadership, at least on the immediate horizon. That's no doubt something they probably should discuss when they get together in Washington next week.
CAPTION: Prime Minister Justin Trudeau, left, and U.S. President Joe Biden talk as they wait for the start of a skydiving exhibition during the G7 Summit in Savelletri Di Fasano, Italy on June 13, 2024.

Subject: Polls & surveys; Democracy; Voters; Political leadership; Prime ministers; Political parties

Location: Texas; United States--US; Canada

People: Biden, Joseph R Jr; Trudeau, Justin

Title: Justin Trudeau and Joe Biden are two leaders with one problem: Many of their supporters think their time is up
