Document 366 of 500

'Help me, I've been shot': Inside a story of betrayal, greed, murder ... and the survivor who foiled the getaway

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/help-me-ive-been-shot-inside-story-betrayal-greed/docview/3075328510/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=%27Help+me%2C+I%27ve+been+shot%27%3A+Inside+a+story+of+betrayal%2C+greed%2C+murder+...+and+the+survivor+who+foiled+the+getaway&amp;title=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-07-03&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: It was late on a cold Sunday afternoon, the last day of February 2021, when the Audi Q5 pulled into 347 Arvin Ave. and stopped in front of the gate.
Like most businesses in the industrial area of Stoney Creek on the eastern edge of Hamilton, Colonial Pillars was closed for the weekend. The street was quiet, save for the odd passing car. The property was secured, the gate locked.
Oliver Karafa left his wife, Yun Lu (Lucy) Li, in the passenger seat, and approached the gate, returning once when someone walked by. Then it opened - with the padlock now cut - and the Audi disappeared around back.
There were no cameras to capture what happened next.
Just that morning, Karafa and Li had bought the white Audi. They paid Karafa's best friend Jaime Gutierrez about $18,000, using about $4,000 from Li's line of credit.
Karafa had already arranged for someone in Montreal to buy it, along with another vehicle: a Range Rover that didn't belong to them and was also destined for 347 Arvin that day.
From the surveillance cameras of businesses on Arvin, the Audi was unrecognizable, its rims crudely painted black, and untraceable to Karafa or Li.
Colonial Pillars had a for-sale sign out front. No arrangements had been made with a realtor to look at the property, but that is what Tyler Pratt and his pregnant girlfriend, Jordyn Romano, had been led to believe.
The couple thought they were meeting their friends to look at the property for a legal marijuana growing operation. They had already invested nearly a half a million dollars in another of Karafa's businesses and were anxious to see it returned.
"We considered them really good friends," Romano said.
But this Feb. 28 meeting was a ruse.
Karafa did not have the money to pay them back, and he did not want to set up a grow-op on Arvin or do any more business with the couple.
At 5:21 p.m. Karafa messaged Pratt and Romano on the encrypted messaging app Signal to say they would be at the property in 10 minutes, even though they were already there.
Just two minutes after receiving the message, Pratt and Romano arrived in their white Range Rover and drove back to meet their friends.
The shooting
For 31 minutes the two couples remained at the back of the property, unseen by anyone.
What exactly happened may never be known.
What is known is that Karafa shot at Romano three times as she sat inside her Range Rover, one of the bullets lodging in her heart and the other two missing her and hitting the vehicle.
Then he shot Pratt five or six times, with bullets hitting his arm, going through the back of his neck and a graze to his chin that may have been caused by the same bullet that hit his chest. This gunshot to the chest is what killed him, but his death would not have been immediate. He bled out on the cold ground as chaos ensued around him.
Both the Audi and Range Rover were damaged in the melee, either crashing into each other or into one of the warehouses at the back of the large property.
It is "difficult to imagine a more planned murder," said the judge who presided over Karafa's and Li's trials, after a jury found the pair guilty. He described it as an "outright execution."
It's not just that they travelled in an unknown car - Li's identifiable Mercedes was safely parked at their Toronto condo - but there were other steps taken to make it look like they never left their Erskine Avenue residence in the Yonge Street and Eglinton Avenue area of Toronto.
Karafa took the stairs where there are no cameras. Li wore a blond wig in the elevator. They switched cellphone SIM cards with a friend to give themselves a "digital alibi" that made it look like their phones never left the city.
The survivor
There was only one problem: Romano didn't die.
As Karafa and Li fled to Europe, Romano underwent life-saving surgery and spent days in a coma before she was able to tell police the identity of her killers.
Her memory of that day is foggy and, in some spots, unreliable because of the trauma she went through. But there are crucial moments she remembers in flashes.
Karafa staring at her with an odd look, his face shaking, he raised a gun and pointed it at her. Romano froze.
"Get out of here, Jo," was all she could remember Karafa saying before she was shot. Panicked, she put the car in reverse and crashed.
She doesn't remember seeing Pratt, who was standing outside, being shot. But she heard him scream, "What the f--k, bro."
Another flash is Karafa dragging her out of her car. Police would later find one of her thigh-high Christian Louboutin boots, the top folded over as if pulled down while she was dragged out, on the ground in the parking lot.
Karafa and Li took both vehicles from the scene leaving Pratt and Romano for dead.
Video from neighbouring properties shows the Audi, and then Pratt and Romano's Range Rover, coming and going several times during the hour after the shooting. The Audi was left nearby on Green Road. Then the Range Rover returned to Arvin twice.
The prosecution would later argue that was Karafa - and possibly Li - returning to the scene looking for Romano to finish the job. They finally left for good around 6:52 p.m. in the Range Rover.
The getaway
Eleven minutes later, Karafa messaged his car buyer in Montreal, Mohamad Morad.
"Both cars are f--ked," and would need to be towed, Karafa wrote. He offered both the Audi and Pratt and Romano's Range Rover for just $2,000, but only if they were sent out of the country in a shipping container with no trace to Karafa. With Li in the passenger seat, Karafa drove the Range Rover to Etobicoke, and left it parked on Audley Street near the Mimico GO Station.
Back on Arvin Avenue in Hamilton, about 25 minutes after the last time the Range Rover passed, Annette Bright drove by with her boyfriend in the passenger seat.
She saw something on the road in the dark. At first, she thought it was an animal. But something nagged at her, so she turned around. That's when she saw a woman crawling on the road.
Romano was wearing one boot. Her sweater bloody. She said she was cold.
"Help me, I've been shot," she pleaded.
Police arrived by 7:22 p.m., soon followed by paramedics, who took Romano to hospital, where she would live, but lose her unborn baby. She was about 13 weeks pregnant; a fact Li and Karafa knew.
After discovering Romano on the road, police found Pratt's body on the pavement behind the warehouse, his arms outstretched, bullets, shell casings and other debris around him.
The ensuing investigation by Hamilton police followed a complex web of shady business investments and false friendships. The tale spans from Vancouver to Toronto to Hamilton to Europe.
It is a story about money, desperation and betrayal. Perhaps more than anything it is about greed.
The killers
Yun Lu Li was born in Shenzhen, China, a city north of Hong Kong, in 1995. She is a triplet, and the sisters are the oldest children in the family.
Like the rest of her siblings, Li's English name is adapted from her Chinese name Yun Lu, with the Lu becoming Lucy.
When the girls were little, their parents moved to Toronto, and the triplets stayed with family while their parents established themselves in Canada, spending years apart until the girls joined their parents when they were seven. In Canada, the family grew, with a younger sister and brother.
At first, their dad ran a driving school for Chinese immigrants, where their mom also worked. But soon their mom's wealth management business took off. Respon International grew to one of the top financial institutions working with Chinese-Canadians, with Hong Wei 'Winnie' Liao becoming a prominent businessperson, photographed rubbing shoulders with political elites, including Prime Minister Justin Trudeau.
According to Lucy, the bread and butter of her mom's business is selling 20-year life insurance policies that allow clients, after an initial premium paid in Canada, to transfer funds from overseas without being taxed.
This type of insurance policy later plays a role in the murder plot.
Li said she initially struggled at school and was behind her sisters in learning English. Her first language is Mandarin. The family moved to North York, and it was at Earl Haig Secondary School where she met her future husband.
"He was older and cool from the beginning," Li said. "I was pretty nerdy with braces and bangs."
Karafa was born in Slovakia and immigrated to Canada in 2001 with his family, first living in Windsor and then Toronto. Li said she liked Karafa right away.
They went on dates, but Li claimed the short-lived relationship ended when Karafa wanted to have sex and Li wasn't ready. She also claimed he slept with a friend of hers at a party.
In April 2012, when Karafa was 19, he crashed his Range Rover on Mount Pleasant Road in Toronto.
He was only a G2 driver at the time. Karafa walked away, but his passenger - a friend and personal trainer - was killed.
Karafa was sentenced to five years in prison in 2014 and prohibited from driving for 15 years.
When Karafa first applied for parole in January 2016, he was denied because of two incidents in Beaver Creek Institution, where he was involuntarily transferred from minimum to medium security.
"The board suggested your behaviours since being incarcerated is indicative of a young man with an abnormal sense of entitlement; you agreed," the parole decision says. "You present as a person who believes rules made for society are for everyone else to live by and not you."
By December 2016, Karafa was granted day parole, but denied full parole.
Karafa appealed the decision to deny his full parole, and that was rejected. But six months later, he was released on full parole in August 2017. He was 24 and the parole board believed he was motivated to get his life on track. He had a job as a manager at a café and was supported by his sister, her husband and his unnamed girlfriend, the document says.
His parents had already returned to Europe, where they lived in the family home in Levice, Slovakia - a location that would play a role later when Karafa and Li were on the run.
Karafa's first prison sentence for the fatal drunk-driving offence officially expired on May 4, 2019, while the driving ban continued.
Li said her upbringing was strict. Her mother was a "tiger mom" with resolute plans for her children.
All were encouraged to study business. Li's mom offered a reward of a car to the first triplet to acquire accreditation to sell life insurance. Li was the first to do this, focusing on that more than her studies at Western University in London, Ont. Eventually she transferred Ryerson University (now Toronto Metropolitan University) to finish school, while working with insurance companies.
Li, working with her mother, struggled to attract bigger clients herself and realized that she needed more life experience. She, along with her triplet sisters, asked for a break from work at Christmas 2018. The family often had annual meetings around the holidays where they would discuss what they accomplished that year and plans for the future. Li said she was given two years of freedom, with her mom offering a $5,000 a month allowance.
The triplets had an account on Instagram together where they attracted sponsors who paid for the sisters to model their products. Li said they made about $500 to $1,000 per sponsored post, but that the money was split three ways and was more for fun and not enough to live on. One sponsor gave her the blond wig.
It was during this time, when Li was about 23, that Karafa came back into her life. They messaged on Instagram and met for a date.
The relationship moved fast, with Li moving in with Karafa after their second date. Li's mom stopped giving her an allowance because Karafa paid for most things.
Out of prison, Karafa worked as an entrepreneur and seemed to be making good money. According to Li, his businesses included everything from exporting cars, renting out a studio to film social-media ads, to selling weighted blankets. But much of his business was in Europe, where he had a partner in Prague.
The couple got a two-story penthouse condo at 18 Erskine Ave. in Toronto. Because Karafa was a prohibited driver, Li drove him everywhere, including to multiple business meetings.
This included frequent meetings hosted at the Mississauga offices of Karafa's longtime friend Alex (also known as Sasha), where Karafa would later meet Pratt and Romano.
Karafa and Li travelled often, including to Europe to see his family and conduct business. In spring 2020, about two months into lockdowns at the start of the COVID-19 pandemic, the couple were going a little "stir crazy," and Karafa wanted to return to Europe.
They bought tickets to Zurich for May 16. But when they got to the airport, they had a problem. Karafa, who had dual citizenship in Canada and Slovakia, was allowed to return to Europe, but Li was not.
At the suggestion of someone working at the airline they decided to get married, Li said. Karafa was going to leave with or without her.
"We were not planning on marrying, but I was very in love with him and due to travel restrictions I made the irresponsible choice and decided to ... marry him on the spot," Li said.
They stepped out of line and married with an officiant presiding over Zoom. They spent months in Europe before returning to Toronto that summer.
The victims
Tyler Pratt was raised in a large and loving family in British Columbia, where he coached kids' hockey and, like Karafa, was also an aspiring entrepreneur.
Pratt grew up in Taylor, B.C., then, as a young man, he lived in Kelowna before relocating to Vancouver, where his two children were born.
Described by family as hardworking, his businesses included a diamond company called Hard as Diamonds and a wellness supplement company, Elite Organic Nutrition.
But he made most of his money selling cocaine. He wasn't a street-level dealer - he arranged shipments by phone. He once made $1 million in a day, Romano said, but that was rare.
By 2020, he had cash and was looking to invest and move away from cocaine.
"He wanted to just become a more legitimate businessman," Romano later said in court. "He wanted to get out of the cocaine life; we wanted to have a family and be normal and escape that negativity."
She grew up in Ontario, with family in Hamilton. In her early 20s, she went back and forth to B.C. for a few years before moving there when she was about 24.
Romano worked as an exotic dancer and met Pratt about six months before they started dating. She stopped working and he paid for their lifestyle, which included luxury goods and travel. Their Toronto condo, photographed by police after the murder, was filled with boxes of designer shoes, bags and other items.
Romano agreed that Pratt was careful to live off the radar because of his cocaine business, but she said he was kind and had an even temper. Pratt's former wife - the mother of his two children - also said she never had issues with Pratt and that he always put his children first.
Romano and Pratt were together for about a year before he was killed, first living in Vancouver and then moving to Toronto not long before the shooting. They wanted to start a new life together.
In 2020, the couple travelled back and forth between Toronto and Vancouver often, both for work and so Pratt could see his kids. On the day he was killed, he had just returned from a trip for his daughter's birthday.
In July 2020, Romano took a solo trip to Toronto and messaged a girlfriend she hadn't seen in a while. This friend was dating a guy named Alex (Sasha) and the group went out for dinner.
The swindle
Later, Romano introduced Sasha, described in court as the owner of a Mississauga construction company, and Pratt. The two men hit it off. Pratt was looking for a business to invest in and Sasha helped arrange meetings.
Among those Sasha introduced was his friend, Oliver Karafa, who he had known for a decade and vouched for.
At that first meeting, Karafa told them: "Yo, I have something that can make us a lot of money."
Soon after, Pratt and Romano invested $170,000 in Karafa's business, selling truckloads of personal protective equipment (PPE) in Europe. Selling everything from hospital gowns to syringes promised to be lucrative, especially during the pandemic. Soon after, they invested more cash and, by late fall, they'd given Karafa roughly $470,000.
"I really liked Oliver," Romano said. "He was just always nice and easygoing. Tyler loved Oliver, they had a great friendship. He adored him."
And they trusted him. The couple never signed a formal contract.
There were a lot of meetings with Karafa and Pratt, often at Sasha's office. It was there they first met Li. By late 2020, Sasha was dating one of Li's sisters, Jane, one of the triplets, and Li would visit with her sister after driving Karafa to the meetings.
Li later testified that she knew Pratt had invested money in the PPE business, but didn't know the particulars. She wasn't involved in the PPE business meetings.
Karafa promised big profits, but claimed the money was stuck in Europe. Romano said he showed them a European account with millions in it. He promised $3 million was their share.
But by January, Karafa had failed to pay Romano and Pratt and was desperately trying to find ways to delay.
His answer: an insurance scheme. This is where Li really enters the story.
Li had let her licence lapse, but she was toying with getting back into insurance and knew the industry well. Karafa sold Pratt on one of the 20-year life insurance policies and set up meetings with the four of them.
The held several meetings, including at the North York home of Li's parents - which the family continued to own despite her parents primarily living in Vancouver - as well as at Pratt and Romano's new Cumberland Street condo in Toronto, where they moved in February.
Romano said Li initially suggested a $2.8-million policy, but she and Pratt couldn't afford that so it was reduced to $1.5 million. Romano believed if they paid the first premium in cash from Canada, their money stuck in Europe could be added later, allowing them to transfer the money without paying tax or risk the attention of authorities, including Canada's national financial intelligence agency, FINTRAC (Financial Transactions and Reports Analysis Centre of Canada). Romano also said she was told they could borrow against the policy, allowing them to get a loan in Canada to expand business.
The truth is that insurance premiums cannot be paid in cash. But Pratt and Romano didn't understand how it worked.
Li later admitted the insurance scheme was a ruse to buy Karafa more time to come up the money owed. She said she initially was sincere in trying to sell them a policy, but that after learning the premium would need to be in cash, she played along to help Karafa. She never intended to submit the application.
Li, who said she never like Romano and Pratt, claimed she offered to pretend to pay the first premium. Police would later find a $1.5-million cheque in an envelope of insurance documents. But Romano said she never saw that cheque and instead believed Li was going to loan them $1 million - taken from her mom's account where she "wouldn't notice" it missing.
Pratt needed the loan because he was having cash-flow problems, including losing a cocaine shipment that was seized at the border, court heard.
Romano said the two couples were set to meet at Li's mom's offices on March 1 to finalize the insurance application. Karafa was going to be paying them back the initial $500,000 investment, with the $1-million loan from Li that would make up the $1.5 million required. Then they were going to sign paperwork finalizing their positions with the European PPE business at Karafa's lawyer's office afterward.
Instead, by March 1, Li and Karafa were on a flight to Europe, Pratt was dead and Romano in a coma, all while Hamilton police worked to put together what happened the day before at 347 Arvin Ave.
Nicole O'Reilly is a crime and justice reporter at The Spectator. noreilly@thespec.com

A person gets out and walks back to the property, while another moves the Audi and parks. Document 367 of 500

Doug Ford did a good thing for Canadian media. Justin Trudeau should follow his lead

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/doug-ford-did-good-thing-canadian-media-justin/docview/3075328472/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Doug+Ford+did+a+good+thing+for+Canadian+media.+Justin+Trudeau+should+follow+his+lead&amp;title=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-07-03&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Across Canada, governments have supported journalism for generations. Since the founding of Canada's first newspaper, the Halifax Gazette, which dates to 1752, government advertising has been an important source of revenue for newspapers. Even prior to Confederation, direct supports, like the former Publications Assistance Program, which subsidized the postal delivery of nondaily newspapers, ensured that Canadians have access to high-quality Canadian news.
Today, community, regional and national news publishers are struggling with declining advertising revenue. In 2012, Canadian newspaper advertising revenue stood at $3.55 billion. Today, it is under $1 billion. Yet, four out of five Canadians still read newspapers, regardless of format, at least once a week - essentially unchanged since 2012.
While things will never go back to the way they were, and nor should they, governments can do a much better job supporting our domestic news businesses by addressing the advertising revenue challenge through their own advertising spending and procurement policies.
We were very encouraged by the recent news coming out of Premier Doug Ford's government in Ontario that it is directing crown corporations like the LCBO, the Ontario Cannabis Retail Corp. (OCRC), the Ontario Lottery and Gaming Corporation (OLG), and Metrolinx to invest 25 per cent of their advertising budget in Ontario-based news businesses. This is forward-looking smart policy that other provinces, municipalities, and the federal government can follow, and it does not involve any additional taxpayer dollars.
Ford recognizes the importance of journalism to Ontario's economy. According to the Government of Canada's Job Bank, there are 4,350 journalists working in Ontario. The impact of those journalists goes beyond the economy as they keep communities across the province connected, informed, and engaged.
One of the most powerful tools in any government's policy tool kit is procurement. Procurement can help governments advance socio-economic policy objectives, including creating jobs and delivering better outcomes. When the Government of Canada spends more per year on advertising with China-based TikTok ($1,113,056) than it does on all of Canada's print publications combined, something is seriously wrong with government advertising procurement policies. The same holds true for other orders of government.
Ontario isn't the first jurisdiction to adopt an "earmark" approach when it comes to government advertising. New York City's Local Law 83 mandates the creation of an annual spending report on advertising, displaying each agency's spending and compliance toward spending 50 per cent of advertising on ethnic and community media.
Subscription revenue alone may be enough to fund websites devoted to intelligent commentary, but fact-based, fact-checked coverage of schools, cops, courts, politicians, and businesses - holding the powerful to account - costs real money and demands advertising revenue.
We encourage governments at all levels, as well as our leading corporations - the large banks, telcos, and retailers - to take a cue from the Government of Ontario and follow its lead in supporting our domestic news publishing industry by spending 25 per cent of their advertising budget in newspaper and news publications. This will allow us to reinvest in high-quality Canadian journalistic content and in innovative techniques that harness the power of digital tools to engage audiences and sustain viable news businesses that serve the public interest.
CAPTION: Ontario Premier Doug Ford takes part in a news conference at Ottawa city hall on April 29, 2024. Ford is directing crown corporations like the LCBO, the Ontario Cannabis Retail Corp. (OCRC), the Ontario Lottery and Gaming Corporation (OLG), and Metrolinx to invest 25 per cent of their advertising budget in Ontario-based news businesses.
CREDIT: Paul Deegan Contributor

Subject: Journalism; Advertising revenue; Newspapers; Budgets; Advertising expenditures; Government

Document 368 of 500

July 3: Plenty of questions around police body cameras, encampment debate a game of whack-a-mole and other letters to the editor

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/july-3-plenty-questions-around-police-body/docview/3075328432/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=July+3%3A+Plenty+of+questions+around+police+body+cameras%2C+encampment+debate+a+game+of+whack-a-mole+and+other+letters+to+the+editor&amp;title=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-07-03&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Plenty of questions around body cameras
In 2015, the Information and Privacy Commissioner of Ontario stated: "(Body worn cameras) should not be adopted simply because they may be considered a popular enforcement tool. They must be judged necessary to address specific operational circumstances in the jurisdiction they are deployed in."
On June 27, in the course of asking the Hamilton Police Service Board to approve the purchase of police body cameras, Chief Frank Bergen mentioned a number of police services that are using body cameras and said, should the board approve the expenditure, "(the police) have to come back to the board with the privacy impacts, the policies, the procedures and the governance of what that is. We have to have town halls and conversations with our community to understand the impacts of introducing body worn cameras in interactions with the community."
In other words, the chief told the board body cameras are becoming a popular enforcement tool. How they will be used will be determined later. He did not say why they are necessary or cost-effective.
The board did not even begin to deal with privacy concerns or the myriad other issues relating to body cameras. None of (the board members) appeared to have done any independent research. The board approved the expenditure of more than $9 million without an adequate inquiry. It treated taxpayer dollars like confetti.
Andrew Bell, Stoney Creek
A narrow minded approach
Observing Hamilton councillors debate the location of homeless encampments in parks has the same depth of vision and integrity as a game of whack-a-mole. The "not in this park" approach is narrow minded (in every sense). This city needs leadership that comprises cleverness, courage and competency. Sadly, these adjectives have been absent in many recent chapters (ongoing computer infrastructure hack, location of isolated Tim Hortons field). This city needs more than ambition.
Dave Pasian, Ancaster
Cappelli family commended
Re: 'Our tears are not for your death, dear brother, but for your life:' A story of schizophrenia, a family's love and a man who suffered 'every minute of every day,' June 29
Once again, a beautiful article by Susan Clairmont about an amazing family's unfaltering love and support for their brother John, who lived with schizophrenia for 55 years. His heartfelt obituary in May detailed their efforts to help him throughout his many struggles, including buying a home to save him from homelessness. The Cappelli family should be commended; indeed, even after John's death to advocate and bring an awareness of mental illness, specifically schizophrenia. With knowledge comes understanding and acceptance.
Jan Peirson-Reid, Carlisle
A heartwarming story
Thank you to the Cappelli family for having the courage to share the story of your brother/son John. It was a heartwarming, yet tragic, story that I hope people read, listen to and ultimately change their views toward individuals who struggle with any kind of mental illness. Growing up, my mother, a nurse before having a family, always would say to us that mental health would be an issue in the years ahead. Hopefully this is a step in the right direction. Sending blessings and condolences to the Cappelli family.
Mary Hickey, Burlington
Cappelli experience all too familiar
My sincere condolences to the Cappelli family.
I know Dea and her parents, as I was on the first executive committee of the Ontario Friends of Schizophrenics Society, Hamilton Chapter, with them. With the guidance of Patricia Saunders and Dr. Raymond Prudeau, we developed a group whose mandate was to help family members to understand and cope.
I also had a brother named John who, too, "suffered every minute of every day" with schizophrenia. I was his advocate. When he died in a nursing home at the age of 57, I was somewhat ashamed of my feelings of relief (like a burden had been lifted from my shoulders) mixed in with a continuation of feelings of sorrow for his poor quality of life.
My brother was at the Hamilton Psychiatric Hospital (HPH) for 20 years, lived in a home for special care (administered by the HPH) in the community for 20 years, and spent his last two years at the Brant Centre in Burlington. I would give his treatment at the HPH a grade of C for their lack of caring, the homes for special care and the HPH's administration of it an F for their unwillingness to follow their own guidelines, and the Brant Centre nursing home an A for their compassion.
The same problems exist today as they did back then, such as anosognosia, whereby the patient has a lack of insight of their psychiatric condition, resulting in the patient refusing medication and family involvement. Community supports are still lacking. Supportive housing for people with severe mental illness needs to be separate from housing for people who are addicted to drugs, and qualitative evaluations are needed to gauge the success or failure of these programs.
There has been little progress in our hopes for a wonder drug or a cure for schizophrenia in the 45 years since I met the Cappellis. Maybe Artificial intelligence will help researchers come up with ways to alleviate the suffering of severe mental illness in the near future.
Steve Liddycoat, Burlington
Cannabis harms being ignored
Thank you for the insightful and moving story about the struggles of John Cappelli and his family in dealing with schizophrenia. One small but significant point that was mentioned had to do with John's use of drugs at an early age.
Without meaning to sound presumptuous or insensitive, there is plenty of scientific evidence linking cannabis use to schizophrenia among young adults with a genetic disposition to this mental illness - young adults who would otherwise not see it manifest if it weren't for the use of this powerful psychoactive drug on a developing mind. This fact is glossed over and ignored by society in general, particularly by ignorant politicians and unscrupulous cannabis investors who are out to make money at any cost.
We should stop promoting cannabis as if it were a boon to Canada's economy. We should stop branding and marketing pot like it was a fine wine. Stop flying pot banners in front of shopping centres and start recognizing the potentially devastating effect this drug has on the lives of many unsuspecting youth.
Chris Asimoudis, Ancaster
Interesting developments in the U.S.
The CNN presidential debate was a disaster for the Democratic Party and for U.S. President Joe Biden. The American people and world leaders publicly saw what many people already knew, that Biden has slipped both physically and, more importantly, mentally. If the election were held today, although Biden might win the popular vote, it would not be close in the electoral college, where Donald Trump leads in almost all of the important swing states.
The Democrats have three choices. 1. Stick with Biden until the bitter end. 2. Convince him and his wife and close supporters to get him to step down as the candidate. 3. Invoke the 25th amendment that he is unfit for office and therefore must be replaced by the vice president. (Takes a majority of his cabinet and the VP)
On the other side, Trump has his own challenges. He actually may be sentenced to jail time in his New York case effective July 11. Trump just obtained a political and legal boost from the Supreme Court that ruled presidents have some immunity from criminal prosecution for official acts when they are president. Unofficial acts were not specified.
November 5 is only four months away.
David Gallo, Ancaster
CAPTION: Hamilton police are moving forward with a plan to purchase 500 body-worn cameras for officers.

Document 369 of 500

Doug Ford promises 25 per cent of government agency, provincial advertising budgets to local media outlets

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/doug-ford-promises-25-per-cent-government-agency/docview/3075328243/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Doug+Ford+promises+25+per+cent+of+government+agency%2C+provincial+advertising+budgets+to+local+media+outlets&amp;title=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-07-03&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: The Ontario government is throwing the beleaguered journalism industry a financial lifeline.
In memos to government agencies, boards and commissions including the LCBO and Metrolinx, Ontario Premier Doug Ford has mandated that the organizations must spend at least 25 per cent of their advertising budget with Ontario-based news organizations.
"Ontario's major government agencies are some of the largest advertisers in the province, together spending well over $100 million on marketing each year," Ford wrote in a memo sent to the LCBO late last week.
"These significant advertising budgets should help support Ontario-based publishers and the Ontario-based workers creating local news content for people in our province," Ford added. Other agencies, boards and commissions received similar memos.
Ford also committed to spending 25 per cent of the government's own advertising budget with Ontario-based news organizations. Sources familiar with government finances estimated conservatively that the government itself spends at least $100 million per year in advertising.
Toronto Star owner and publisher Jordan Bitove, who has called for a similar commitment from Canadian businesses, praised the government decision, saying it comes at a crucial time for the industry, and for democracy.
"Premier Ford and his government have shown outstanding leadership in supporting Canadian-owned and operated journalism," said Bitove. "This support will have a profound impact in ensuring Ontarians are better informed."
With the proliferation of misinformation on social media eating away at democracy's foundations, traditional journalism stands as a beacon of reliable information, Bitove added.
"There is no better investment in democracy than supporting quality Canadian journalism. Given what is going on in the world, it has never been more important," Bitove said.
The Ontario government's new mandate will be in place by September, and will apply to Ontario-based publishers defined as "qualified Canadian journalism organizations" by the Canada Revenue Agency.
Paul Deegan, president of industry association News Media Canada, also praised Ford's decision, and called on other levels of government to follow suit.
"This is brilliant. We need municipalities, other provinces and the feds to adopt a similarly smart approach to advertising," Deegan said.
"Governments should be spending taxpayer dollars with trusted news mastheads, rather than with foreign social media behemoths that foment misinformation and disinformation for cheap clicks."
In the 2022-23 fiscal year, the federal government spent just over $86 million on advertising.
Since 2019, the federal government has funded the Local Journalism Initiative, a program that gives journalism organizations funding to hire reporters to cover underserved communities. In March, the federal government announced a three-year, $58.8-million extension of the program.
The Ontario decision comes at a time when journalism organizations across the country are struggling for survival.
In March, SaltWire Network and Halifax Herald Ltd. were forced into receivership by their biggest creditor, which said the companies owed more than $90 million. Last week, the companies received an extension to their protection from creditors until Aug. 9, amid reports that a potential purchaser was closing in on a deal that would see at least some parts of the companies kept alive.
In January, creditors approved an insolvency proposal by Metroland, a sister company of the Star.
Metroland is owned by Torstar Corp., which is in turn controlled by NordStar Capital.
As part of a restructuring plan announced last September, Metroland cut 605 jobs - roughly two-thirds of its workforce - and turned 71 weekly newspapers into online-only publications. Just over 100 of the terminated workers are unionized and represented by Unifor.
CAPTION: Ontario Premier Doug Ford also committed to spending 25 per cent of the government's own advertising budget with Ontario-based news organizations.

Document 370 of 500

Ejected by Doug Ford, MPP Goldie Ghamari breaks her silence

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/ejected-doug-ford-mpp-goldie-ghamari-breaks-her/docview/3075418971/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Ejected+by+Doug+Ford%2C+MPP+Goldie+Ghamari+breaks+her+silence&amp;title=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-07-02&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Former Progressive Conservative MPP Goldie Ghamari says she's "disappointed with Doug Ford's unilateral decision" to remove her from his caucus.
But the outspoken Ghamari, who is close to federal Tory Leader Pierre Poilievre, said she would "continue to align myself with and support conservative policies."
"I am a conservative and that will never change," she said in her statement Tuesday.
In her first detailed comments since the premier defenestrated her Friday, an unrepentant Ghamari said she would continue to "raise awareness about (Islamic Revolutionary Guard Corps) terrorists in Canada" and "will continue to speak out on this issue."
Ford turfed the two-term Carleton MPP for what his office called her "serious lapses in judgment," which culminated in an online video conversation with far-right Briton Tommy Robinson last week.
Robinson, a convicted criminal whose real name is Stephen Yaxley-Lennon, is the founder of the anti-Islam English Defence League and was recently arrested in Calgary after allegedly misrepresenting himself to visit Canada.
Ghamari has insisted she was "not aware of Mr. Robinson's history prior to our meeting."
"As the first Iranian-Canadian woman elected to office in Canada, I'm proud to use my personal time to raise awareness and speak to people in Canada and around the world about the terrorist Islamic regime in Iran, its countless human rights violations, and its very real threat to Canadians in Canada," she said in a social media statement.
"The recent decision by the federal government to put the Islamic Revolutionary Guard Corps (IRGC) on the Canadian terror list after the NSICOP found the Islamic Regime in Iran is one of the main culprits of foreign interference in Canada is proof."
That was a reference to last month's National Security and Intelligence Committee of Parliamentarians report that found some foreign governments were meddling in Canadian democracy.
Now sitting as one of six Independent MPPs in the 124-member legislature, Ghamari is the provincial seatmate to Poilievre and has been mentioned as a possible federal Tory candidate in an election expected in October 2025.
"I don't yet know what the future holds for me, but I am feeling very hopeful and optimistic. I will be taking some time to reflect on my next steps. When one door closes, another opens," she said.
"To the people of Carleton, my office is still open as usual and my team and I will continue to assist you with all your requests. I am still your MPP and I will continue to be your voice at Queen's Park, as I have been doing for the last six years."
In a terse statement Friday, the premier's office said she was being kicked out of the PC caucus "effective immediately."
"This decision follows repeated instances of serious lapses in judgment and a failure to collaborate constructively with caucus leadership and as a team member. While this decision did not come easily, it has become clear that MPP Ghamari can no longer continue in her role within our caucus," Ford's office said.
The National Council of Canadian Muslims had been imploring its supporters to email and call the premier to urge him to fire Ghamari.
Ford previously hailed her as a "strong voice" speaking out against Islamophobia and antisemitism.
On social media, Robinson said he reached out to the MPP "to ask her out on a date."
CAPTION: Premier Doug Ford is shown with MPP Goldie Ghamari at a news conference in Toronto on Jan. 16, 2020. Ford ejectedGhamari from the Progressive Conservative caucus last week after what he called repeated and serious lapses in judgment.

Subject: Social networks

Document 371 of 500

The lone Liberal MP to call for Justin Trudeau's resignation speaks out about the surprising response

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/lone-liberal-mp-call-justin-trudeaus-resignation/docview/3074971120/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=The+lone+Liberal+MP+to+call+for+Justin+Trudeau%27s+resignation+speaks+out+about+the+surprising+response&amp;title=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-07-02&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: It was a political call to arms that no Liberal MP appears to have taken up.
In an email to his colleagues last week, New Brunswick MP Wayne Long gave voice to feelings Grit MPs were confidentially sharing, in chat groups and with reporters, that the prime minister should step down. He expected further calls for Justin Trudeau's resignation would follow. But so far ... crickets.
"I won't lie, it's a bit of a lonely feeling. But ... I feel that it needed to be said."
In a long conversation with the Star, Long said he has no regrets about speaking up, or for possibly being judged a "bad guy," or shunned for his comments. But he also expressed surprise no other Liberal MP had piped up.
"I'm just kind of puzzled - like, I just don't understand the silence," he said over the phone. "It has been quieter than I thought it would be."
He expected to spur "a more robust, open conversation, but clearly, that's not what's occurred to date."
In a five-line email to his parliamentary colleagues Friday, Long said the byelection results in Toronto-St. Paul's, where the Liberals lost a seat they had held since 1993, led him to conclude the party needed new leadership and a new direction.
"I think the way to reconnect with Canadians is a new leader, a new fresh face," he told the Star.
It wasn't just the byelection loss that prompted Long's reflection. It was also the prime minister's response in June to a 20-point lead in public support for the Conservatives, when Trudeau suggested voters aren't in a "decision mode" yet, and that Liberals just need to "work harder connecting with Canadians," he said.
"With respect, I beg to differ," said Long. "We've been 15 to 20 points down in the polls for a better part of a year now ... Seventy per cent (of those surveyed) are saying that we don't deserve to be re-elected and ... 84 per cent are saying that they want change. I mean, let's listen to what Canadians are telling us! I don't want to see, you know, a Kathleen Wynne example happen to us nationally."
(In Ontario's 2018 election, Wynne led the governing Liberals to a third-place finish, losing official party status in the process.)
The problem, for Long, is the prime minister. Whatever good work and popular policies the Liberals have adopted - from dental care to the billions invested in housing - he said the message is just "getting lost because people can't get past the, "'Yeah, but your leader ...'
"There's just a tuning out, and I want to see us win. I want to see us be competitive."
He feels that can't happen with Trudeau at the helm, but it might happen with a new leader - and that someone needed to sound the alarm. "The alarm bells have been ringing for a year and a half, as far as I'm concerned."
Long doesn't have a preferred leadership candidate - he lists Housing Minister Sean Fraser, Public Safety Minister Dominic LeBlanc and former Bank of Canada governor Mark Carney as possibilities - and he's open to the idea that an outsider could come in and swing the contest. "I don't know. That's what the process is about."
Like many in the Liberal caucus, he'd like to see his party realign closer to the political centre. Long's Saint John-Rothesay's riding is a blue Liberal/red Tory kind of seat that often flips between Liberals and Conservatives. He feels Canadians have seen the pendulum swing too far to the left and now too far to the right, and that there is a thirst and an opportunity for centrist policies. "I'm convinced that there's a lot of people that are parking their vote for Pierre Poilievre ... because they just won't vote for Justin Trudeau another time," he said.
"I hear it every day."
A backbencher for nine years, Long is no stranger to speaking his mind. He once called for an inquiry into the SNC-Lavalin affair, amid allegations Trudeau had inappropriately pressured his then-attorney general to offer the Quebec engineering firm a deferred prosecution agreement. But one gets the sense that this time he's particularly concerned about being seen as knifing his party's leader.
"I like the prime minister," he offered, unprompted. "I think the prime minister has done a good job, you know, since 2015, by and large. I mean, every government has some, you know, peaks and valleys." Long speaks highly of Trudeau's progressive policies, and commends his leadership during COVID.
"I just think that a lot of our good work ... a lot of it gets lost and is getting lost and people just can't see through the unfortunate dislike of the prime minister."
And while some in caucus think Trudeau's leadership and the Liberals' fortunes can be salvaged through some big bold moves, Long wonders what those could be. "We had a cabinet shuffle, like, last August. Maybe I missed something but ... I didn't see anything change." Same thing with the spring budget, he said.
"There's a time when you need to read the room."
Maybe his Liberal colleagues aren't talking in that room. But on their doorsteps, Canadians certainly are.
Long says the prime minister is ignoring that message at his - and his party's - peril.
"You're gonna hear what you want hear if you block out what you don't want to hear."
CAPTION: Wayne Long, the MP for Saint John-Rothesay, speaks in Saint John, N.B. on Jan. 17, 2024.

Document 372 of 500

July 2: Something is wrong when a homeowner must live in her car, Canada is great enough to survive even a bad leader, and other letters to the editor

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/july-2-something-is-wrong-when-homeowner-must/docview/3074794381/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=July+2%3A+Something+is+wrong+when+a+homeowner+must+live+in+her+car%2C+Canada+is+great+enough+to+survive+even+a+bad+leader%2C+and+other+letters+to+the+editor&amp;title=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-07-02&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Something's wrong when a homeowner must live in her car
Re: She saved up to buy a townhome, but she's forced to live in her car (June 26)
Our society has gone absolutely off the rails when tenants have more rights to a house they don't own, and are likely delinquent on the rent, than the homeowner.
These people have no problem abusing a building that is not theirs and causing the rightful owner thousands in damages.
When the homeowner, who pays the mortgage, the utilities and property taxes and any other expenses relating to their house, has to live in their car, there is something woefully wrong with the system.
The police should be able to go in and remove the illegal tenants from the building.
Shame on the Landlord and Tenant Board for creating this situation.
Diana MacKenzie, Hamilton
Cyclists, be responsible for your own safety
I almost hit a cyclist today. Despite my checking both ways, this young man came flying down the sidewalk on Brant Street, going the wrong way and without a helmet. He literally came out of nowhere (from behind an obstructive tree) and never paused at the intersection to account for other vehicles.
Our miss was within inches, after which he voiced his supreme displeasure with my jeopardizing his near-death experience.
For those of you who practise this behaviour, I suggest you might want to consider your responsibility and personal safety on bicycles, etc., given there are some drivers who don't really account for your decisions.
Rob Panchyson, Burlington
Canada is great enough to survive even a bad leader
Re: Trio is 'hopelessly unqualified' (June 26)
According to writer Craig Wallace, none of our three present leaders of major political parties are qualified for the position of prime minister of Canada.
Would any of our former prime ministers pass his leadership test? I am doubtful.
Can we agree that Canada is one of the best countries in the world? (In spite of Conservative leader Pierre Poilievre's constant haranguing and fearmongering.)
Perhaps it doesn't matter who is prime minister. Canada will survive and continue to thrive as it has in the past, not because of, but regardless of, who is prime minister.
Julia Kinar, Hamilton
Ontario needs a better leader
Re: Is this the Ontario we actually want? (June 25)
Thank you for publishing this article by Laura Babcock. Premier Doug Ford has blinders on when it comes to the issue of the homeless, and affordable housing is not a consideration.
His idea of building homes and highways on some of the best farmland and wetlands is just appalling, especially when experts say that there is no reason or need to build Highway 413, nor to expropriate urban land for housing.
Furthermore, concern is deepened when we remind ourselves of the ever-present risk of several hands being in the cookie jar whenever expropriation comes up for these pet projects.
It's time to say goodbye to Mr. Ford. Let's hope our next premier has the foresight to truly make Ontario what it can be, without destroying our farmlands, wetlands and the environment.
Kids, our future, need a solid education. Our health-care system needs support. It will certainly be a challenge, but with the right person at the helm, it can be done.
Cathy Pengelly, Dundas
CAPTION: Ayesha Asghar says she has been forced to couch surf with friends and sleep in her car because tenants in the Hamilton townhome she bought won't leave, despite being issued eviction notices more than 80 days before she took ownership of the property.

Document 373 of 500

Imagine how much more effective MPP Sarah Jama could be within the party

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/imagine-how-much-more-effective-mpp-sarah-jama/docview/3074793999/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Imagine+how+much+more+effective+MPP+Sarah+Jama+could+be+within+the+party&amp;title=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-07-02&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: I met with my MPP, Sarah Jama, last week to find out where's she at in terms of her plans for a spring election, should there be one.
She plans to run as an independent.
To recap for those new here, MPP Jama sits as an independent after being kicked out of the NDP caucus, ostensibly for her remarks calling for a ceasefire in Gaza ahead of and without the appropriate approvals of the NDP party structure. The NDP effectively threw her to the wolves as the Conservatives moved to censure her by falsely accusing her of antisemitism, exposing how quickly the Conservatives can exploit anti-Palestinian racism within their party for political gain. This was further buttressed in later months by the kaffiyeh ban initiated by the Speaker of the legislative assembly, Conservative MPP Ted Arnott.
It was a disappointing development for those who voted for Jama, who won the riding with a majority of votes. It wasn't a fake majority either, where too many candidates split the vote so many ways a loser wins. She won with 54 per cent of the vote. Clearly the people spoke.
And it was just as equally disappointing to those looking to NDP Leader Marit Stiles for a revived leadership to see her jettison a young, popular member of her party for reasons we may never fully know, so shrouded are they in undocumented decision-making and lack of any due process. This is troublesome for a party that wants to be taken seriously or in any way appeal to a younger, diverse demographic of voters.
Jama sits as the second youngest MPP, a few years older than Conservative Niagara West MPP Sam Oosterhoff, who has himself combined activism with politics in his vocal support of anti-choice policies and his vow to make abortion unthinkable in his lifetime. And not in the good way either, like bringing in policies that would prevent unwanted pregnancies, encouraging or enhancing sex education in schools, or supporting struggling young families in financial need; but in the bad way of wanting to restrict access to birth control, sex education and reproductive choice. Women have more to fear of provincial policies that restrict reproductive choice in health care than attempts to criminalize abortion at the federal level.
Despite the fact that Premier Doug Ford promised to maintain abortion access, he did not kick Oosterhoff out of his caucus. In fact, he now holds an associate minister position. Why is that? Because Oosterhoff has popular support for an anti-choice position among the constituents in his riding that vote Conservative, which is a lot. He won with a 52 per cent majority in his inaugural term. Besides, Ford can't be trusted to tell the truth anyway. Basic income anyone? Greenbelt?
How did the NDP get so hard and disrespectful of the support given them by the voters in Hamilton-Centre?
Jama has done yeoman's work partnering with local groups who have the data and know the issues in helping develop policies that address the needs of all Ontarians, not just people in Hamilton-Centre. Environment Hamilton, ODSP Action Coalition and ACORN Hamilton have worked with Jama to create polices that deal with reasonable, actionable responses to the problems facing our communities, for they are all the same: environment, housing, health care, poverty.
Imagine how much more effective she could be within the party? Is that a problem to anyone in the party?
It's not good for the riding of Hamilton-Centre to have the representative most of them voted for be cut adrift from the party that was once happy to have her aboard. Bad look. The voters know who Sarah Jama is and most of them are willing to stand behind her re-education while she transforms herself from a vocal and passionate activist into an effective and capable legislator.
There are people working toward a rapprochement. Jama has support within caucus. I hope Stiles reconsiders. All hands are needed on deck to defeat Ford.
Margaret Shkimba is a Hamilton writer. For more on my interview with Sarah Jama visit: margaretshkimba.Substack.com. You can follow me on X (@menrvasofia), and friend me on Facebook.
CAPTION: The voters know who MPP Sarah Jama is and most of them are willing to stand behind her re-education while she transforms herself from a vocal and passionate activist into an effective and capable legislator, Margaret Shkimba writes.

'FULL REFUND': Major recall on sausages sold at No Frills, Shoppers Drug Mart, Fortinos, Loblaws, Real Canadian Superstore, Zehrs, Valu-Mart and other grocery stores in Canada triggers warning to shoppers

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/full-refund-major-recall-on-sausages-sold-at-no/docview/3074793390/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=%27FULL+REFUND%27%3A+Major+recall+on+sausages+sold+at+No+Frills%2C+Shoppers+Drug+Mart%2C+Fortinos%2C+Loblaws%2C+Real+Canadian+Superstore%2C+Zehrs%2C+Valu-Mart+and+other+grocery+stores+in+Canada+triggers+warning+to+shoppers&amp;title=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-07-02&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: A significant recall is hitting some major Canadian grocery stores and the public is being advised.
Loblaw Companies Ltd. said that, "out of an abundance of caution," it is voluntarily recalling PC Cheddar Jalapeno Smokies, 1 kg, UPC: 060383200213, with a best before date of BB: 2024 JL 25.
"While there have been no reported cases of injury relating to the product, it is being recalled from the marketplace due to the possible presence of soft coloured plastic," the company said in a news release warning shoppers of the recall.
The product was sold at Real Canadian Superstore, Fortinos, Loblaws, Valu-Mart, Your Independent Grocer, Zehrs, No Frills, Atlantic Superstore, Dominion, Provigo, Maxi, Axep, Intermarche, Extra Foods, Real Canadian Wholesale Club and Shoppers Drug Mart, Loblaw Companies Ltd. said in the release.
"All affected units have been removed from store shelves. Customers can return the product to the place of purchase and the customer service desk will provide a full refund," Loblaw said. "We sincerely apologize for any inconvenience this recall may have caused. The health and safety of customers is our top concern."
There has been plenty of reaction online to this recall, as many shoppers report using these sausages.
"Oh wow, I use this kind at one of my private lunches that I cater at," Theressa Hill wrote on Facebook. "The guys love the jalapeno & cheddar."
For more information regarding the recall, customers can contact customer service via loblaw.ca or 1-888-495-5111.
CAPTION: Loblaw Companies Ltd. said that, "out of an abundance of caution," it is voluntarily recalling PC Cheddar Jalapeno Smokies, 1 kg, UPC: 060383200213, with a best before date of BB: 2024 JL 25.Recall at No Frills and other Loblaw Companies Ltd. stores.

Letters

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/letters/docview/3074316561/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=unknown&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Letters&amp;title=The+Spectator&amp;issn=11899417&amp;date=2024-07-02&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.10&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Spectator&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Something's wrong when a homeowner must live in her car
Re: She saved up to buy a
townhome, but she's forced
to live in her car (June 26)
Our society has gone absolutely off the rails when tenants have more rights to a house they don't own, and are likely delinquent on the rent, than the homeowner.
These people have no problem abusing a building that is not theirs and causing the rightful owner thousands in damages.
When the homeowner, who pays the mortgage, the utilities and property taxes and any other expenses relating to their house, has to live in their car, there is something woefully wrong with the system.
The police should be able to go in and remove the illegal tenants from the building.
Shame on the Landlord and Tenant Board for creating this situation.
Diana MacKenzie, Hamilton
Cyclists, be
responsible for your own safety
I almost hit a cyclist today. Despite my checking both ways, this young man came flying down the sidewalk on Brant Street, going the wrong way and without a helmet. He literally came out of nowhere (from behind an obstructive tree) and never paused at the intersection to account for other vehicles.
Our miss was within inches, after which he voiced his supreme displeasure with my jeopardizing his near-death experience.
For those of you who practise this behaviour, I suggest you might want to consider your responsibility and personal safety on bicycles, etc., given there are some drivers who don't really account for your decisions.
Rob Panchyson, Burlington
Canada is great enough to survive even a bad leader
Re: Trio is 'hopelessly
unqualified' (June 26)
According to writer Craig Wallace, none of our three present leaders of major political parties are qualified for the position of prime minister of Canada.
Would any of our former prime ministers pass his leadership test? I am doubtful.
Can we agree that Canada is one of the best countries in the world? (In spite of Conservative Leader Pierre Poilievre's constant haranguing and fearmongering.)
Perhaps it doesn't matter who is prime minister.
Canada will survive and continue to thrive as it has in the past, not because of, but regardless of, who is prime minister.
Julia Kinar, Hamilton
Ontario needs
a much better leadership
Re: Is this the Ontario we
actually want? (June 25)
Thank you for publishing this article by Laura Babcock. Premier Doug Ford has blinders on when it comes to the issue of the homeless, and affordable housing is not a consideration.
His idea of building homes and highways on some of the best farmland and wetlands is just appalling, especially when experts say that there is no reason or need to build Highway 413, nor to expropriate urban land for housing.
Furthermore, concern is deepened when we remind ourselves of the ever-present risk of several hands being in the cookie jar whenever expropriation comes up for these pet projects.
It's time to say goodbye to Mr. Ford.
Let's hope our next premier has the foresight to truly make Ontario what it can be, without destroying our farmlands, wetlands and the environment.
Kids, our future, need a solid education. Our health-care system needs support.
It will certainly be a challenge, but with the right person at the helm, it can be done.
Cathy Pengelly, Dundas

Document 376 of 500

The re-education of Sarah Jama

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/re-education-sarah-jama/docview/3074314132/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=unknown&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=The+re-education+of+Sarah+Jama&amp;title=The+Spectator&amp;issn=11899417&amp;date=2024-07-02&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.11&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Spectator&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Document 377 of 500

He depends on his power wheelchair - but is frustrated he can't count on the Ontario government to service it

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/he-depends-on-his-power-wheelchair-is-frustrated/docview/3074126024/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=He+depends+on+his+power+wheelchair+-+but+is+frustrated+he+can%27t+count+on+the+Ontario+government+to+service+it&amp;title=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-07-01&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Three years after a debilitating stroke left him largely immobile, retired lawyer Dan MacDonald is in a constant battle to get his power wheelchair to fit properly.
The struggle - faced by others, disability advocates say - is raising new questions about Ontario's Assistive Devices Program (ADP), which helps people with long-term disabilities afford essential items like wheelchairs, hearing aids, insulin pumps, home oxygen and more.
MacDonald's emailed complaints to Health Minister Sylvia Jones and Premier Doug Ford's office on June 7 about an exclusive power wheelchair supplier arrangement under the ADP, and concerns that clients have nowhere to turn if service is unsatisfactory, got an answer Thursday.
He was not impressed, and shared it with the Star.
"The Ministry of Health is sorry to hear of the challenges you've faced with your wheelchair," said the unsigned email reply from the ministry's correspondence and corporate support unit.
"The ADP strives to provide customer-oriented support ... ADP staff will take your concerns into consideration," the reply added, providing MacDonald with a separate ADP email address to contact with any "further concerns."
MacDonald says he'd already emailed that address, and feels "very frustrated."
"That correspondence begs the question of why ADP has not seen fit to respond to my previous emails concerning the difficulties I have encountered," he said.
Jones's office declined to comment specifically on MacDonald's situation, although the Ministry of Health sent a separate statement saying it stands by the program and noting it has procedures for handling complaints.
But the ministry does not have statistics on complaints readily available and does not track client satisfaction.
MacDonald feels like he's hit a brick wall and filed a complaint with the office of Ontario's ombudsman, hoping the staff there can get action.
"The chair is what I'm in most of the day. It's essential that I'm comfortable," said MacDonald, 66. He suffered a serious brain stem stroke in 2021 that left the retired litigation lawyer with limited use of his hands and arms.
Getting the chair's cushions and other components to fit and not cause pressure sores - the wheelchair equivalent of bedsores - is a "trial-and-error" process that can entail many appointments with a technician, he says.
"The disabled population faces numerous challenges, physically, emotionally and financially," MacDonald wrote in a recent appeal to Jones, which he shared with the Star.
"Obtaining reliable, dependable and timely service for something which is essential to their mobility and independence should not be an additional challenge."
Frustrated and now dealing with a retired power wheelchair seating technician as a last resort, MacDonald is urging the Ford government to open the power wheelchair repair business under the ADP to competition.
"There has to be a better way," said MacDonald, who is frustrated by the continuing challenges following the stroke that "upended" his life with his wife Marie, a former senior IT executive who is now his chief caregiver.
The unexpected stroke hit 18 months after he retired, putting him in hospital for weeks, and causing breathing problems that resulted in two intubations and six months on a feeding tube.
"I took care of myself. I was reasonably fit, wasn't overweight," he said. "I asked one of my doctors, 'Why did this happen?' He said, 'You've lost the lottery.'"
Ministry of Health officials say the wheelchair contract was assumed by a company called Motion LP - which has branches across the province selling and servicing "high technology power wheelchairs" - from Shoppers Home Health Care in 2016.
As such, the ministry funds Motion to run the "central equipment pool" or CEP, first established in 1996, for the power wheelchairs. The pool provides clients with "new and recycled" wheelchairs "at discounted prices and provides a rebate on the client's costs once the equipment is returned."
"Due to the cost and complexity of power dynamic positioning wheelchairs, only a few select vendors have the expertise and technology to undertake management of the CEP," ministry spokesperson Adriana Dutkiewicz said in a statement to the Star.
"The ministry receives and tracks complaints ... the complaints are reviewed, investigated and resolved by the program. Any concerns related to client safety are taken extremely seriously, in accordance with the government's agreement with Motion."
Ontario's auditor general found eight years ago that while the contract with Shoppers was reached through a competitive bidding process, the ministry "assigned" the contract to Motion.
"We noted concerns ... about the quality of services provided (by Motion), including a lack of responsiveness to client inquiries," the auditor general's office said in a statement to the Star this week.
The company was required to submit an annual service plan to the ministry and year-end reports "to ensure the appropriate oversight," including performance metrics, according to a follow-up report in 2020.
But MacDonald's troubles with Motion have included leaving detailed voice-mail messages but not getting return calls, a lack of promised followup from in-person appointments, delays in getting parts and a wheelchair battery, and being called in for a battery replacement that had to wait until the next day for a more experienced technician to install it.
These are problems for disabled people who rely on their power wheelchairs for everything from getting around the house to getting to work or medical and physiotherapy appointments.
MacDonald and others say service provided by Motion is uneven. After his repeated complaints, the company has agreed to service MacDonald, who lives in Mississauga, from its more distant Hamilton office.
Motion says it strives to provide "the best possible client experience in support of our vision to make life accessible for everyone" and notes the customization of power chairs is complicated - something MacDonald acknowledges.
"Each individual has unique needs," chief executive officer Sue Gilpin said in a statement to the Star.
Gilpin said the timeline for repairs and adjustments can depend on recommendations from therapists and approvals from third-party funders, and the ordering and delivery of parts.
"We are not permitted to make changes to a client's equipment without approval and direction from the therapist," she added.
"Given such complexities and the wide range of mobility and accessibility needs of our clients, there have been exceptional circumstances in which we did not achieve the customer service standards that we set out for our business," she acknowledged.
"In such cases we always try to work directly with the client to understand their experience and identify any opportunities for improvement."
In MacDonald's case, that included sending staff for extra training, as Gilpin acknowledged in a letter the retired lawyer shared with the Star.
Jeff Preston, an associate professor of disability studies at King's University College in London, Ont., says the monopoly on service enjoyed by Motion is a "disservice" to the disabled community.
"The result has been to wipe out the service, repair and sales industry," said Preston, who has used a wheelchair for 24 years after being born with a rare form of muscular dystrophy.
"I've heard wildly different reports of the quality of care and quality of support that people receive from one unit to another based on where they're living," he said. "People are trapped as a result."
He applauded MacDonald for going public and drawing attention to the situation.
"Not everybody has the energy, the training, the ability, the passion to be able to speak up."
A leading advocate for the disabled says problems like MacDonald's are a "recurring issue" in a way that other Ontarians might not understand.
"When you take your car in for service, you can rent a car or take an Uber but you can't do that with a wheelchair because a lot of them are customized," says David Lepofsky of the Accessibility for Ontarians with Disabilities Act Alliance.
Lepofsky supports MacDonald's call to end the service monopoly.
"You want the providers to compete, to give good service. You don't want to give anyone a monopoly over basic service."
Green MPP Aislinn Clancy (Kitchener Centre) - who has been helping Kitchener-area mother Laurie O'Brien get answers six years after her adult son, David, died while waiting for wheelchair improvements - said it's time for a review of ADP.
"We need a robust system to make sure that people are being served properly. A lot of the people I know are worried, if they speak up, are they going to get worse service."
Clancy is planning a town-hall meeting where ADP clients can air their concerns "so that we can start nipping this in the bud."
Previous examinations of the ADP by the auditor general have also focused on weak oversight of sales and claims.
CAPTION: Dan MacDonald is a retired lawyer who relies on an electric wheelchair since he had a stroke.

Subject: Complaints; Stroke; Correspondence; Pressure ulcers; Wheelchairs; Handicapped accessibility; Disability; Ontarians with Disabilities Act-Ontario
