Document 378 of 500

The Ontario Science Centre never should have been Doug Ford's to close

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/ontario-science-centre-never-should-have-been/docview/3074125598/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=The+Ontario+Science+Centre+never+should+have+been+Doug+Ford%27s+to+close&amp;title=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-07-01&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: It could be the feel-good story of the year: a beloved and popular attraction is saved from closure and demolition by concerned citizens. Working together, they find wealthy benefactors who pledge the funds to execute needed repairs, and an architect willing to do the work pro-bono. The community effort spares an impoverished government from having to make an unpopular decision, as much as sparing taxpayers the additional financial burden.
Thanks to the altruism of our social betters, and the "outside the box" thinking of a plucky white-collar class, the Ontario Science Centre is saved. A new generation of children are thus encouraged to pursue rewarding careers in STEM.
This might be the spin applied to the saga of the science centre if Ontario Premier Doug Ford can be convinced to accept the numerous generous offers that have come in over the last week to save it.
The problem is that it didn't have to be this way.
That the science centre needed saving in the first place is demonstrative of the features - not the bugs - of our political and governance system.
For your consideration: If the science centre was the city's responsibility, would we be in this predicament? Probably not. This deluge of public support for the science centre has led city council to pass a motion looking into the feasibility of taking over its operations.
Consider as well that the lion's share of public opposition to the science centre's closing has come from the citizens of Toronto. Similarly, it's the people of Toronto who have been most vocal about the privatization and disfigurement of Ontario Place.
This isn't simply a reflection of the fact these public spaces are immediately accessible to most Torontonians, but that it is Torontonians who most need immediate access to public spaces. It shouldn't surprise anyone that the people of Toronto would be most vocal in their opposition to Ford's ideologically-motivated process of public disinvestment, as it has had a debilitating effect on Toronto and its citizens first and foremost.
The consequences of chronic disinvestment in urban areas by upper levels of government has bedevilled North American cities for about 50 years. The problem - particularly in American cities in the area now known as the "rust belt" - was initially one of suburban flight sapping cities' urban tax bases, thus resulting in shrinking budgets, austerity, and eventually, severe population and economic loss. Upper levels of government - either at the county or state level - attempted to rein-in depopulation and deindustrialization, to varying degrees of success and failure.
Toronto experienced aspects of these processes while also being shielded from some of their worst manifestations in part because it evolved into becoming the nation's corporate and business capital around the same time as depopulation and deindustrialization were ravaging other major cities. That Toronto is Ontario's capital certainly helped as well. Most premiers want their capital cities to shine and understand - at least - that the needs of big cities can be very different from other regions of the province, or the province as a whole.
Where Doug Ford is unique amongst premiers is that he seems to take no interest in the fact his often-reckless decisions can negatively impact the lives of so many people he ostensibly represents. He seems to govern in spite of Toronto, all while seeking to remake the city according to his whims. What Torontonians want never seems to enter into consideration.
If Toronto had the financial tools and political sovereignty necessary to provide the services and protect and promote the spaces its citizens desire, we wouldn't be discussing the science centre's closure, but likely its expansion. We likely wouldn't be spending public money to privatize Ontario Place either. Our schools probably wouldn't be falling into disrepair, and our hospitals wouldn't be so overcrowded, because we'd be acting on the recommendations of our public health officials, rather than dismissing them.
That private individuals have come forward to save the science centre - and that this option was not immediately accepted by the government - is indeed suspect, but let's not let this distract us from the underlying problem. Toronto is denied the right to do what's best for itself by politicians who campaign on their contempt for the city. They see public assets as exploitable business opportunities, and they spend public money seemingly without consulting the public.
This is the system we have, and it consistently doesn't work for us. That we are so consistently denied the right to do better for ourselves isn't a bug, it's a feature.
It begs the question: why do we continue to put up with this?
CAPTION: Judging from the outcry of Torontonians at the closure of the Ontario Science Centre, writer Taylor C. Noakes ponders if the public attraction would be in this position if it fell under the city's responsibility. Probably not, he writes.
CREDIT: Taylor C. Noakes Contributor

Subject: Public spaces; Privatization; Science; Deindustrialization; Population decline; Cities

Business indexing term: Subject: Privatization Deindustrialization

Document 379 of 500

Ontario Science Centre insiders recount the rise and fall of a beloved institution

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/ontario-science-centre-insiders-recount-rise-fall/docview/3074182151/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Ontario+Science+Centre+insiders+recount+the+rise+and+fall+of+a+beloved+institution&amp;title=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-06-30&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: For a 10-year-old boy, it was a rock from out of this world that left him wanting to reach the stars.
"My biggest 'whoa' moment at the Ontario Science Centre was not just running into this hall of all these theories turned into practice, where I could run around and actually try stuff out, but to see a small piece of something that I had been imagining," said former Canadian astronaut Chris Hadfield. "A small, actual piece of the moon that Neil Armstrong and Buzz Aldrin had picked up and brought all the way back on Apollo and put it into a container and then shipped to Toronto and put right there for me to see it."
Hadfield was featured in a fundraising video for the Science Centre in 2020. His moon rock experience happened shortly after the centre opened and, like generations of children in Ontario, he had fun and memorable moments created at the institution. Things came full orbit for Hadfield, who was the basis of an exhibit there in 2015. It is that kind of a spark that has motivated countless people that have worked at the Science Centre and was the key behind architect Raymond Moriyama's vision of a place where people could interact with and immerse themselves in science.
With the abrupt closing of the Science Centre last Friday due to an engineering report that said roof panels are in danger of collapsing, there has been an outpouring of emotion and soul searching about what the centre means to us. There have been pledges of financial support, demonstrations by people in the neighbourhood and an outcry from scientists, architects and philanthropists here and around the globe.
But how did we get to this point, where a building designed by one of Canada's most famous architects has fallen into such a state of disrepair? Where a place that should be a beacon for evidence-based discussion has become fraught with differing opinions about what should be done with it now?
In discussions with former and current staff, members of the board of trustees and former CEOs, a number of long-standing issues have been cited. The Science Centre's tight control from the provincial government has left it chronically underfunded, leading to a failure to innovate and evolve and a lack of maintenance on an aging building. Some also point to the issues with an ill-fitting and powerful union that also limits the room to manoeuvre.
To start, we have to look at its origin story. The Ontario Science Centre was a grand experiment for a number of reasons. It was away from downtown, nestled into a ravine at Don Mills and Eglinton. The Exploratorium in San Francisco and our Science Centre were the first museums of their kind, and have now inspired over 300 similar ones all over the world, with many using the Toronto institution as the blueprint for how they bring science to the masses.
Architect Moriyama and the other collaborators behind the Science Centre wanted to create something different, something interactive where children could touch and feel the science in their hands. In the late '60s, when it was being conceived, it was in vogue for governments to have a strong hand in projects, which meant that it was tightly controlled by the province, as opposed to other institutions, like the Royal Ontario Museum or the Art Gallery of Ontario.
"Bill Davis conceived the Ontario Science Centre as a Centennial project, which actually opened in 1969," said Nancy Lockhart, who was board chair from 1998 to 2006. "And it was the style at the time to have Schedule One government agencies, which tied you very, very closely to government and that became a real impediment as time went on. But when the Science Centre was constituted, it got 92 per cent of its funding, I believe, from the provincial government. The rest was generated through parking and admission."
The year 2006 saw the last major influx of provincial cash for the centre, $15 million as well as a successful $40 million fundraising campaign. The money was used to give the institution a re-energizing facelift and attract blockbuster draws, most notably in 2009 with Body Worlds and the Story of the Heart, an internationally created exhibit focusing on the human body, which helped boost attendance.
But subsequent governments weren't willing to invest, and the institution's ability to generate funds on its own was limited through being beholden to the province.
"We needed government approval for everything, whereas the ROM or the AGO would have had more freedom to operate or to act," said Lockhart.
One example she points out was during her tenure, there was an opportunity identified that would have generated $11 million a year by building exhibitions that would tour the world. But there was no space to do the work, and the ability to staff up or staff down depending on work was limited because of union constraints. She said they needed $2 million as a startup cost, but there was no appetite from government to provide it.
"I'd say benign neglect would be a good way of describing what happened over successive governments of every stripe," said Lockhart. "Each and every year, the Science Centre had to prepare a very detailed business plan using a very lengthy and repetitive government template. That business plan had to be submitted by the end of the year, by the beginning of December, I think. There wasn't a single year I was there where that plan was approved before the following November. So that tells you that nobody was looking at it.
"It was really frustrating. Each and every year we put in requests for repairs, for modifications, but basically most years, we ended up with a flat budget, which meant a negative budget because the wages, which were the biggest part of the budget, went up and we had no control over them."
That was another issue. Because it was run by the government, all the employees are Ontario public servants and part of a much bigger union, which gave Science Centre leadership no negotiating power over their employees.
"The Ontario Science Centre should always have been run like a business," said Lockhart.
The world was also changing, and the centre's information and interactivity had to compete with what people had in the palm of their hands, said Lesley Lewis, who was CEO from 1998 to 2014.
"People had access to vast amounts of information on their computers and ultimately on their phones," Lewis said. "So, it was a different competitive environment."
By the 2010s, bigger screens - and one of the Science Centre's big draws - were becoming much more commonplace.
"Another thing that created some problems in the Science Centre, and similar places around the world, is that many had invested big time in Imax theatres," Lewis said. "And for a while, they brought in a huge amount of people, but suddenly it wasn't drawing unique visitors, because in a city like Toronto, suddenly there were lots of Imax screens, not just the one in Ontario Place and the one at the Science Centre."
Budgets remained flat during the 2010s, and that started to manifest itself in other ways.
"What I saw during my time there was an increasing reliance on what we called partnerships, but were really kind of sponsorships of various spaces," said Kevin Von Appen, who worked at the Science Centre in various capacities over 40 years, before retiring three years ago as the director of science communications.
"So you had the Shoppers Drug Mart Imax Theatre, not the Ontario Science Centre Imax Theatre. You had the Procter and Gamble Great Hall, not simply the Great Hall. Those changes started in the '80s in earnest when I first came to the Science Centre, and they only accelerated over the time that I was there."
He said the problem with that is it leads to hiring more people in roles promoting sponsorships or seeking more entrepreneurial activities.
"It's about where you're putting your resources, and over time, you wind up with maybe fewer scientists, designers and researchers."
A good year for Science Centre attendance was always around one million visitors, with some banner years fuelled by bigger exhibitions.
With a lack of funding, there were a dearth of those big draws.
Maurice Bitran, who was CEO and chief scientist from 2014 to 2019, recently wrote an opinion piece for the Star, saying: "I discovered the many challenges it faced: the roof leaked whenever it rained, there was no working capital to develop new exhibits or to bring new shows, and the auditors had even put a liquidity note in the financial statements. Not surprisingly, attendance was in decline. For decades, and under governments of all political stripes, this cherished Ontario gem had suffered from chronic underfunding and insufficient infrastructure maintenance."
In 2019, a report - and a series of columns by the Star's Martin Regg Cohn - supported the idea of revitalizing Ontario Place by moving the science centre to the waterfront. Over the next few years, the tourism ministry and Infrastructure Ontario released reports on putting the two projects together, calling it a priority project and commissioning business cases and reports on the feasibility of the plan. Then COVID-19 hit, and in March 2020 the centre was shut down for almost two years, shifting to virtual projects.
In 2022, the bridge linking the main hall was closed because of the risk of structural failure, and a report estimated repair costs at $16 million. Since then, shuttle buses have been ferrying people between buildings.
In April 2023, Infrastructure Ontario put a proposal together that said moving the Science Centre to the waterfront, with integrated parking with the Therme Spa, would help meet the province's obligations to that company. On April 18, Premier Doug Ford announced the Science Centre's planned relocation to Ontario Place.
Then, as reported last week, the Rimkus engineering report landed, which found that some of the roof panels were in danger of collapsing due to a snow load or heavy rain. That report said it could cost $22 million to $40 million and take two to five years to repair the three buildings, it also said fixes in "high risk" locations could cost as little as $522,500.
What the report did not say was that the Ontario Science Centre needed to be closed immediately. That was a decision made by the current board of trustees, and supported by the government.
"One must remember that this was a warning, a health and safety warning, and as a representative of government, I have to take that seriously," said Kinga Surma, Ontario's infrastructure minister, in a press conference Monday. "There is nothing more important than the health and safety of people, of workers and of children."
Lewis said the shutdown has been felt throughout the community of science museums around the world, shocking many of her colleagues that hold this Canadian institution in high regard.
On its last day of operation, families from across Ontario were still heading toward the admission desk as the news broke, while workers put up fencing at the entrance to the parking lots. Employees also learned of the closing that afternoon, with a few walking around the entrance near closing time, in a daze.
Some hugged and consoled each other. A building steeped in science had left so many unknowns.
CAPTION: In this 2016 Star file photo, architect Raymond Moriyama poses with a model of Science North in Sudbury, one of his creations.A general view of the Ontario Science Centre, in Toronto, Friday, May 5, 2023. Toronto city staff are looking into what legal requirements the province may have to operate or maintain the Ontario Science Centre, after Premier Doug Ford's government abruptly shuttered the facility citing structural concerns. THE CANADIAN PRESS/Chris YoungThe reliance on government and the way the Ontario Science Centre was managed were part of the problem, insiders say.

Document 380 of 500

84 candidates on a ballot? That's a stunt, and not the way we should be saving democracy

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/84-candidates-on-ballot-thats-stunt-not-way-we/docview/3074182078/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=84+candidates+on+a+ballot%3F+That%27s+a+stunt%2C+and+not+the+way+we+should+be+saving+democracy&amp;title=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-06-30&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: The trouble with the most righteous democracy reformers is how they revel, wrongly, in running down what we already have. And slowing it down unless they get their way.
Case in point: The delayed vote count in this week's hotly-contested Toronto-St. Paul's byelection, which degenerated into an all-night marathon.
Election results are usually revealed within a few hours of the polls closing. Wonder why the vote count dragged on until nearly dawn, far beyond the usual timeline?
Chalk it up to a pointless protest by self-appointed democracy vigilantes ready to bog down the existing system so they can build one of their own design. Thanks to their overzealous recruitment of protest candidates, there were 84 names jammed into a supersized paper ballot on voting day last Monday, most of them utterly unknown - and unknowing.
Quite apart from testing the patience of every voter, the interminable ballots tested the stamina of the Elections Canada staff. What was the point of the so-called "Longest Ballot" protest?
To push proportional representation (PR) upon the people of Canada - like it or not. As one triumphant organizer told the CBC, "If that message hasn't gotten across yet, then next time it won't be 84 candidates, it will be 184 candidates."
You get the idea: We must diminish democracy in order to save it. Which seems an awfully anti-democratic way to get their way.
A better way would be to persuade all Canadians, and their political leaders, to change the existing system - if that is indeed what people want. Trouble is, previous votes on various forms of proportional representation have failed in Ontario, B.C. and P.E.I. in recent years.
That said, the protest got me thinking again about the politics of electoral reform - and the bogus premise that it is a purely non-partisan undertaking. The challenge is that each side is a prisoner of its own ideological, electoral, political and partisan preferences.
Where you stand on this issue depends a great deal on where you sit - and which party you support.
I can see the benefits of a proportional representation outcome that is more aligned with the overall popular vote. But I also worry about distortions from empowering smaller parties (fringe or not) that will always wield a disproportionate balance of power (not just in occasional minority governments).
I've always preferred the ranked ballot, or electronic runoff, by which you rank your candidates in order of preference. That's how most political conventions work - if no single candidate wins a majority of the vote, the second and third choices of the defeated candidates are tallied until someone wins the runoffs. The virtue of ranked ballot is that it inculcates consensus and civility, encouraging rival candidates to court one another - and their supporters - to earn their second-ballot support.
The shortcomings in our current system, known as first past the post, are obvious: a winner-takes-all outcome for the candidate who finishes first with merely a plurality - not a majority - of votes. For example, in the Toronto-St. Paul's byelection, the Conservative candidate fell well short of a majority of votes cast, winning 42.1 per cent compared to 40.4 for the Liberal and 11.0 for the New Democrat.
As a thought experiment, if those numbers were mirrored provincially or nationally, you could imagine support for the latter two parties (now allied in Ottawa) adding up to a putatively progressive majority of 51.4 per cent.
One enduring myth is that Prime Minister Justin Trudeau promised to implement PR after winning power in 2015. In fact, he merely promised electoral reform to change the existing system, and his stated preference is the ranked ballot.
Of course, New Democrats and Greens are diehard PR parties because they covet the balance of power. Conservatives oppose PR because they lack natural allies among the other progressive parties, and prefer to wait for the occasional lopsided majority under the existing system.
Overlooked in this equation is the common front among Tories, New Democrats and Greens in opposing the ranked ballot - because an electronic runoff tends to favour the centrist, consensus party - the Liberals. New Democrats and Greens want change because they want to wield power - not by winning a majority, merely the balance of power. Tories don't want to be frozen out of power in perpetuity by PR or the ranked ballot.
That shared self-interest - the impulse to perpetuate and protect one's own political interests - is what bogged down electoral reform in the past. Liberals love the ranked ballot because it gives them the best shot of winning one election after another, but the other parties are in no position to compromise.
As for ordinary voters, proportional representation doesn't achieve the required threshold (or command a decent turnout) in any referendum. That's the political reality the pro-PR side is so reluctant to accept.
Too bad some diehards are now ready to hold the rest of us hostage on voting day until they get their way. That's not democracy, that's sophistry.
CAPTION: The challenge with changing our voting system is that each side is a prisoner of its own ideological, electoral, political and partisan preferences, Martin Regg Cohn writes.

Subject: Balance of power; Preferences; Electoral reform; Referendums; Democracy; Candidates; Elections; Political parties

Document 381 of 500

Developer seeks designation change for Grimsby property in Greenbelt

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/developer-seeks-designation-change-grimsby/docview/3073792335/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Developer+seeks+designation+change+for+Grimsby+property+in+Greenbelt&amp;title=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-06-29&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: A Burlington developer's request for a town and regional planning change affecting nearly six hectares of land on Winston Road in Grimsby may have to wait a while.
The land is owned by 502 Winston Road Inc., whose parent company is New Horizon Development Group.
Matt Johnston, a principal with Urban Solutions and agent for the landowner, said during a June 5 statutory public meeting of Grimsby's planning and development committee the desire to change the designation from specialty crop area tender fruit and grape lands to rural area is essentially a housekeeping matter.
"Basically, the purpose of this application is just to recognize the existing conditions of this parcel and bring it into land use designation which more accurately aligns with that condition of the site and nothing more than that," Johnston said.
Much of the site is an open field with some trees, a meeting hall and parking lot.
"Please accept this as confirmation that no development of the lands is proposed as part of these applications," said Johnston in an email to Niagara This Week.
But Walter Basic, director of planning for the Town of Grimsby, noted the land falls under the province's Greenbelt, and Queen's Park must take the lead on any changes.
"What we're saying is that the local official plan amendments, meaning the Grimsby official plan and the (Niagara) Region's official plan amendments that are proposed, now are premature unless and if the province changes their policy in the provincial planning documents for these lands," Basic said.
After controversy last year over proposed changes to the Greenbelt that the Doug Ford government walked back, it appears the province is not about to make any further changes.
"The government is not considering any changes to the Greenbelt," said Bianca Meta, press secretary at the office of the minister of municipal affairs and housing.
Back at Grimsby town hall, Basic said a staff report on the matter will be going to the July 3 planning and development committee meeting.
Current permitted uses for the site under the specialty crop designation include agricultural uses; one single-detached residence as an accessory use to an agricultural operation; home occupations within the dwelling unit: limited home industries; bed and breakfast; farm holidays and farm tours; and a help-house.
Rural area designation would permit agricultural uses; a single-detached residence; home occupation within dwelling unit; limited home industry; bed and breakfast; farm holidays and tours; help-house; garden suites; forestry, conservation of plants and wildlife; nurseries; small-scale institutional uses; animal kennels; stockyards; grain drying and grain mill; farm supply stores and farm equipment sales and service; certain types of low-intensity non-agricultural development; public and private parks; camp grounds; and golf courses and cemeteries.
During the June 5 planning and development committee meeting, one environmental group suggested the site has been targeted for development.
"We know that the developer in this case is intending to develop the property in terms of high-density housing," said Mike Marcolongo from Environmental Defence, an environmental advocacy organization.
"I encourage the proponent to speak to their intentions as opposed to slowly but surely erode the protection for the property because there are motives here and they are not being stated."
CAPTION: The site on Winston Road in Grimsby where the landowner is looking to change the designation from specialty crop area tender fruit and grape lands to rural area.Grimsby planning officials say provincial changes to the Greenbelt are required before the town and Niagara Region can consider redesignating this parcel of land on Winston Road from specialty crop area tender fruit and grape lands to rural area.

Document 382 of 500

Doug Ford couldn't abandon the Science Centre quick enough. So now who will pay to fix it?

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/doug-ford-couldnt-abandon-science-centre-quick/docview/3073789776/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Doug+Ford+couldn%27t+abandon+the+Science+Centre+quick+enough.+So+now+who+will+pay+to+fix+it%3F&amp;title=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-06-29&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Imagine that you notice a leak in the roof of your home.
Do you: a) put a bucket under the drip; b) patch or replace the roof; or c) scramble to immediately abandon the house, fence off the entire property, and seek temporary accommodations until you can build a new place to live somewhere else in a few years.
In the case of the Ontario Science Centre, Doug Ford's government has chosen option C.
The analogy was suggested to Star reporters by Elsa Lam, the editor of Canadian Architect magazine, looking into the roof assessment the provincial government said justified the immediate closure of the Ontario Science Centre last week. The opinion Lam and others, including both the architecture firm that built the place and the consultants the province is relying on, are a far cry from the Ford government's claims of an emergency threatening life and limb.
The report did not recommend immediate closure. It said a small percentage of the roof should be replaced immediately, the rest monitored and replaced eventually.
The cost (between $500,000 and $7 million this year, and something like $25 million total over the next decade) is peanuts compared to the possible $1 billion this same provincial government is planning to spend in order to sell booze in corner stores a year ahead of schedule, or the tens of billions they're spending on a subway line originally pitched as delivering riders to the Science Centre, or the maybe $500 million they may spend to build a parking lot at the Science Centre's planned new site.
The roof is in disrepair, but it is not some five-alarm emergency on the verge of imminent collapse.
The building was not condemned - it was abandoned.
That is, the Science Centre was not suddenly forced by physical circumstances to move out. The provincial government chose to move it out because they wanted to. And only the timing is a surprise: the move to a dramatically smaller facility at Ontario Place to open by 2028 was announced long ago.
The frenzied shuttering of the current home only makes sense, I think, if the Ford government started with the conclusion that they wanted to get the heck out. And thus see no value in spending even a single penny to maintain the building. And in the face of a public outcry, they've decided to get out sooner rather than later (cementing the impression there's no going back).
Here's the thing: if the building is going to be put to any future use, these repairs are going to need to be done. There are no savings in moving out now, unless the building is going to be torn down.
Perhaps more so than Ford and his gang anticipated, a lot of people in Ontario love that building. It is massive (more than twice the square footage of the Royal Ontario Museum, for instance), situated in a location of natural beauty, and serves as an attraction that anchors a neighbourhood. Tastes in building styles vary, but it is a significant and globally influential example of mid-century institutional architecture built by one of Canada's foremost architects.
Moreover, many of us have deep attachments to it. A friend I was speaking to had lived across the street after his family moved here from Poland when he was a child, and he'd go often after school during hours when admission was free. "For a time, it was my favourite place in the whole world," he said. I have heard similar sentiments from a lot of people. Mayor Olivia Chow reported this week the city had heard from 35,000 Torontonians upset about the sudden closure.
So can the building so many people love be saved? Maybe put to a new purpose?
Toronto city council took up that question Thursday. The complication is the city doesn't want to be on the hook for paying for repairs resulting from provincial neglect. The mayor and city council are hoping there's a contractual way to force the province to pay for it - and have asked for a report on optionsfor the site. Chow told me in a podcast appearance Friday that the "new deal for Toronto" she struck with Ford last year included negotiations over keeping science programming at the site, and she'll continue to pursue that - expecting that the province would need to fund such a project.
But spending money on it is one thing it seems Ford's government won't consider. I mean, they wouldn't even put a bucket under a leak, so to speak, keeping the building in service long enough to move out properly. What are the chances they're going to spend that money (and more) just to give the building away?
This beloved place has been abandoned by choice. It would be a shame if it is now condemned by budget restraints.
CAPTION: If the Ontario Science Centre building is going to be put to any future use, the repairs are going to need to be done.

Subject: Architecture; Science

Business indexing term: Subject: Architecture

June 29: Enough ranting about Ford, hockey advertising is out of control and other letters to the editor

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/june-29-enough-ranting-about-ford-hockey/docview/3073641999/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=June+29%3A+Enough+ranting+about+Ford%2C+hockey+advertising+is+out+of+control+and+other+letters+to+the+editor&amp;title=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-06-29&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Enough ranting about Ford
It seems that Doug Ford is the lightning rod for anything remotely uncomfortable in our lives. Almost every article and letter I read is a complaint about Ford. The comments published in The Spec leave me assuming that Premier Ford controls everything from weather, to the rotation of the Earth. The projection of blame and constant whining about him is nauseating.
Ford was elected by a majority of voters to do a job. A job, that many of these whiny people, couldn't comprehend. If you don't like Ford's decisions or policies, then speak with your vote and spare us the entitled and myopic ranting and protesting.
Troy Smillie, Hannon
Who gets to be the PM?
Re: Would you hire any of Canada's federal leaders to run your business? (June 26)
Well, I'm sure we all appreciate Craig Wallace enlightening us on who is really qualified to be prime minister of Canada. I have a couple of issues with his list of qualified candidates.
The first one is that this is still supposed to be a democracy - so every Canadian should really be eligible for the role, not simply those of power, privilege and wealth. The civil service is there to provide the expertise to advise on policy and manage implementing legislation.
The second issue is that at least two of the three candidates Wallace suggests are just another set of neo-liberals who think of people as disposable liabilities and who are driving this country into the ground while privatizing everything from education to medical care.
What we need is a prime minister and a government to put an end to the endless cutting of services for the poor, sick and working class, while handing tax cuts to the already too-wealthy oligopoly in this country. We need someone to reverse the race to turn every aspect of public life into a profit centre. We need a PM and party for whom the idea of Canadians living in tents is a national disgrace; jurisdiction be dammed - not one single Canadian should be living through the winter in a tent and standing in a food bank lineup. And frankly, the much-lamented deficit is a manufactured artifact made and used by the neo-liberals to justify all the cuts and misery for average Canadians.
Wayne Stansfield, Hamilton
But will any units be affordable?
Re: Chedoke development on Mountain brow moves forward (June 26)
I read the article about all the new housing units proposed on the Brow, and the preservation of the Cross of Lorraine, that was erected as a symbol of hope in the fight against tuberculosis. It is commendable that we are preserving history. The plans call for lots of new housing. The only thing I didn't see was any mention of affordable housing units. People living in tents are in desperate need of sustainable housing. Without affordable housing, the encampments will grow. Issues of poverty, mental health and addiction will grow.
The fight to find a cure for the epidemic of homelessness is just as vital as the fight waged against TB in the past. Million-dollar houses will not fix the situation. We need a symbol of hope for this epidemic. Build affordable housing.
Sharon McKibbon, Hamilton
Hockey advertising is out of control
Thanks to the players in the finals between Edmonton and Florida who are proof that despite the incompetence of Gary Bettman, hockey remains the most exciting sport in the world. Thanks to the immense greed of the NHL owners the game itself has become a backdrop to what they're really presenting: advertisements. The owners, not content to be billionaires, have made the game a visual eyesore with signs, logos and superimposed animation everywhere. From the ice, to the boards, seating, scoreboard, uniforms, skates and sticks they push products, not the game. How long before the players wear sandwich board advertisements over their long-johns underwear and the owners bring the Goodyear blimp out of retirement and fly it among the rafters?
Gary Johnson, Hamilton
Thanks for the extra care
I am writing to commend the service we have had from our very conscientious Spectator delivery fellow. He has always been respectful with the paper neatly folded in the mailbox daily.
However, we have recently had construction work at the front of our house making the regular mailbox delivery impossible. We now find our morning read carefully slipped under the door of the carport where it never gets wet. We are impressed.
Ann Lawton-Barry, Hamilton
Understanding heat pumps
Re: Not buying the heat pump hype (June 27)
In Richard Ronchka's letter he claims to know about energy production but doesn't understand how heat pumps work. He will not have to throw out his forced air furnace as the heat pump works in conjunction with it. The blower from the furnace is used with the heat pump just like it is with a central air unit.
I suggest Richard learn a bit more about how they work before inadvertently spreading misinformation.
Dan Smith, Hamilton
Happy to have heat pump
Re: Here's a low-cost emission reduction action for Hamilton (June 25)
After suffering many years from not being able to cool the bedrooms of our aging side split, we decided to install a heat pump. With only one unit on the upper level and one below in an area with a concrete floor. What a difference being able to sleep comfortably and also heating a chilly place in winter making it much more functional. When our furnace broke down during a particularly hot spell, these two units kept our home bearable. We still have a gas furnace and air conditioner, but now have the option of using one or both systems keeping our indoor climate perfect.
Irene Hough, Hamilton
CAPTION: Doug Ford was elected by a majority of voters to do a job. A job that many of these whiny people couldn't comprehend, a reader writes.

Document 384 of 500

Doug Ford would upset nearly half of all Ontarians with an early $150M election, poll suggests

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/doug-ford-would-upset-nearly-half-all-ontarians/docview/3073641989/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Doug+Ford+would+upset+nearly+half+of+all+Ontarians+with+an+early+%24150M+election%2C+poll+suggests&amp;title=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-06-29&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Nearly half of all Ontarians would be "unhappy" or "very angry" if Premier Doug Ford called an early $150-million election, a new poll suggests.
With Ford considering a vote a year before the scheduled June 2026 provincial election, Abacus Data used its monthly tracking survey for the Star to ask how Ontarians would feel about heading to the polls early.
"The price tag is a problem," said Abacus president David Coletto.
Indeed, when the cost of the election is factored in, 19 per cent said they would be "very angry" at a snap call while 25 per cent would be "unhappy" - a total of 44 per cent.
Some 39 per cent were "neutral" while nine per cent were "happy" and eight per cent "very happy," suggesting only a total of 17 per cent like the idea.
Findings changed when respondents were asked about an early election without the $150-million cost included - only five per cent were "very angry" while 10 per cent were "unhappy" and 50 per cent "neutral" with 20 per cent "happy" and 16 per cent "very happy."
Coletto said if sticker-shocked voters come to believe an early election is "a very expensive endeavour that's not needed," the premier could pay a price.
"It's risky for Doug Ford to do that in this context because he's built a brand about being frugal," he said, noting the Tories already govern with a majority in the legislature so there is no electoral urgency.
"So he would have to justify the need for an election and the expense."
When former premier David Peterson - now chair of Torstar, the Star's parent company - called a snap election in 1990, its $40-million price tag was a key factor in his Liberals' surprise loss to Bob Rae's New Democrats.
Abacus surveyed 1,000 Ontarians from June 20 until Tuesday using online panels based on the Lucid exchange platform. While opt-in polls cannot be assigned a margin of error, for comparison purposes, a random sample of this size would have one of plus or minus 3.1 percentage points, 19 times out of 20.
The firm also asked about Ford moving "to take advantage of Justin Trudeau's unpopularity to hold the provincial election before the federal election" expected in October 2025.
Ten per cent said they would be "very angry" at that rationale while another 10 per cent said they would be "unhappy" while 50 per cent were "neutral," 20 per cent "happy" and 16 per cent "very happy."
Overall, Ford's Tories were at 41 per cent to 25 per cent for Bonnie Crombie's Liberals, 22 per cent for Marit Stiles's New Democrats and eight per cent for Mike Schreiner's Greens.
While that's largely unchanged from last month's poll, the PC government's disapproval rating jumped from 40 per cent in May to 45 per cent in June with its approval rating falling from 37 per cent to 34 per cent.
"Something has happened in the last month that may have turned off some people, because that's a change outside the margin of error," said Coletto.
On May 24, Ford announced the government would pay The Beer Store $225 million to expedite the liberalization of beer, wine and premixed cocktail sales in convenience stores, supermarkets and big-box outlets by Halloween.
The premier - whose original plan was for that to be completed by Dec. 31, 2025 - wants to be seen keeping a major 2018 campaign promise in case he opts for an election early next year.
But Crombie's Liberals insist the tab for speeding up the changes could be three or four times more than $225 million, which is why she has dubbed the rushed scheme "a billion-dollar booze boondoggle."
Asked for their impressions of the four party leaders, 35 per cent had a positive impression of Ford while 45 per cent had a negative impression with 18 per cent neutral and two per cent didn't know enough to say.
That's a net impression rating for the premier of minus 10 per cent.
Crombie was 28 per cent positive, 30 per cent negative, 26 per cent neutral and 15 per cent unsure for a minus two per cent net rating.
Stiles had a 28 per cent positive, 24 per cent negative, 30 per cent neutral and 18 per cent unsure for a net rating of plus four per cent.
Schreiner had a 23 per cent positive, 18 per cent negative, 36 per cent neutral and 23 per cent unsure for a net rating of plus five per cent.
CAPTION: Premier Doug Ford attends an announcement in Toronto on May 24, 2024.

Subject: Provincial elections

Title: Doug Ford would upset nearly half of all Ontarians with an early $150M election, poll suggests

Publication title: The Hamilton Spectator (Online); Hamilton

Document 385 of 500

For opportunity, community, or because they can't afford to leave: Why these Toronto residents have made the choice to stay

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/opportunity-community-because-they-cant-afford/docview/3073641970/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=For+opportunity%2C+community%2C+or+because+they+can%27t+afford+to+leave%3A+Why+these+Toronto+residents+have+made+the+choice+to+stay&amp;title=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-06-29&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: When asked why she stays in Toronto, Lil Moore says it boils down to this: "Better the devil you know than the devil you don't."
That's why she chooses to stay in her "disgusting" rental instead of moving to a more affordable city.
The adult entertainer is 35 now, but she experienced five years of homelessness when she was younger. And with rent control removed for Ontario homes built or first occupied after 2018, she fears losing the stability she has now could land her in a worst-case scenario, again.
Moore is among those choosing to stay in Toronto at a time when people are leaving the city in droves. The number of residents moving to other parts of the province or country from Toronto's census metropolitan area has been trending upwards since about 2010, according to Statistics Canada data, and the figures have more than doubled in recent years. From July 1, 2022 to July 1, 2023 alone, nearly 110,000 more people left Toronto to go somewhere else in Canada than the reverse, and about a third of them were aged 25 to 39.
At just 15, Moore became well-acquainted with the city's crowded shelters, cold streets and scarce affordable housing options. She signed up for the wait-list for affordable housing, but 20 years later, she's still waiting.
"I've been on the public housing list since I was 15 years old. I've never received an offer," she said. (In the first quarter of this year, there were nearly 87,000 people waiting to access social housing in Toronto, according to city data.)
At age 20, a shelter worker helped her get into a program that provided social assistance to help pay for a market-priced apartment.
About a decade later, Moore moved to a different rent-controlled house in Scarborough - one with a backyard so that her dogs could have more space - but she's still "kicking herself" for leaving that cheaper and more stable home for a place that she says has been rife with problems from floods and mould, to disrepair and problematic tenants.
Moore said there's two major reasons she feels she can't leave - one is financial and the other is cultural.
On the first, despite the high cost of living across Ontario, Premier Doug Ford's decision to cut rent control for units built after 2018 means an increasing number of apartments provincewide could be subject to large rent increases. She also describes what she sees as a pervasive trend of bad landlords (in her personal experience and as a former paralegal), and she fears any movement could cost her the stability she has now.
In addition, Moore said she doesn't believe she could find culture that mirrors what's in Scarborough, and that she needs to be somewhere that's safe for her partner, who is transgender.
"I will never leave Scarborough because the connection to Caribbean culture and Asian culture is so strong over here," she said, noting that her father was Indonesian-Chinese and that Caribbean and Indigenous women on the street raised her. "They are community to me. They are culture that I understand. Where am I going to find that?"
Still, Moore dreams of a future where she can own a house. But she knows for that to happen, it can't be in Toronto.
The cost of opportunitiesHilary Henderson, a comedian originally from Hamilton, has higher hopes for Toronto - despite some less-than-ideal experiences she's had in her few years here.
In 2021, Henderson moved to the city with the goal of taking her comedy career all the way. The 27-year-old with a master's degree in public administration works a full-time job in the field, plus comedy gigs, while making money on social media. But after two years of living alone in a one-bedroom apartment, she realized she would need to get a promotion at her day job or downsize if she ever wanted to save enough money to achieve her long-term financial goals.
"I was left with a choice of, do I advance in my career in a way that I can still afford to stay in the place that I was? But advancing in my career means that I would have less time and energy to contribute to my creative work."
Ultimately, she decided to downsize. When plans to move in with a friend fell through, she found someone on Facebook Marketplace to move in with, and rent that cost about $1,000 less than what she was paying. But soon after settling in, she found a mouse infestation and an additional, unexpected roommate. She left after four months.
Now, Henderson is in a studio apartment where she only saves about $200 compared to the one-bedroom she had before. She's looking to the future knowing being in the city will provide more opportunities to entertain, connect with audiences, and network with other comedians - even if for now, the process is costly and exhausting.
'Anything less than $120,000 ... it's just not financially feasible'Leaving Toronto isn't an option for accountant and mother-of-one Alesha Ford, at least for now.
On her $70,000 salary, covering rent and groceries requires precise calculations, the Canada Child Benefit payment and, this month, some help from her child's father - which means the 38-year-old has no money leftover to save for a moving truck, a flight, or first and last month's rent somewhere new.
"I'm going to be getting rid of my Netflix subscription ... but that's only $11," she said. "There's not much I can do with $11 in savings; it's just going to go right back to food."
Ford said she'll stay in Toronto while her teen finishes up French immersion school, but she can see herself landing in Belgium in the future, since she's bilingual and it's more affordable than Toronto; or in New York, where there's a bigger Caribbean community and where she believes she could make a higher income.
For now, she's hoping to change career paths in order to earn enough money to get by.
"I don't know if I'm in a place financially to increase my student loans ... so I have opted to do a self-taught course in product management," Ford said.
"In two to three years, I could be making $120,000, which is exactly what I need to live. Anything less than $120,000, especially if you have children, it's just not financially feasible, in my opinion."
CAPTION: Lil Moore, a 35-year-old adult entertainer who experienced homelessness as a teen in Toronto, says she doesn't want to lose the stability she's found in the city.Hilary Henderson creates a TikTok video in her studio apartment in Toronto. Henderson, 27, moved to Toronto from Hamilton to pursue a career in comedy.Lil Moore, a 35-year-old adult entertainer who experienced homelessness as a teen in Toronto, says she doesn't want to lose the stability she's found in the city.

Subject: Comedians; Careers; Rents; Cost control; Affordable housing; Social networks; Rent control

Business indexing term: Subject: Careers Cost control Affordable housing Social networks Rent control

Location: Canada

Title: For opportunity, community, or because they can't afford to leave: Why these Toronto residents have made the choice to stay

Publication title: The Hamilton Spectator (Online); Hamilton

Publication year: 2024

Publication date: Jun 29, 2024

Section: news/ontario

Publisher: Torstar Syndication Services, a Division of Toronto Star Newspapers Limited

Document 386 of 500

Seven more accusers allege sexual assault by Frank Stronach

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/seven-more-accusers-allege-sexual-assault-frank/docview/3073539453/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Seven+more+accusers+allege+sexual+assault+by+Frank+Stronach&amp;title=The+Spectator&amp;issn=11899417&amp;date=2024-06-29&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.12&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Spectator&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Billionaire businessman Frank Stronach stands charged with sexually assaulting seven additional complainants from 1977 to as recently as February, court documents filed Friday show, bringing the total number of accusers in his case to 10.
The 91-year-old Stronach, who was first charged in early June with five sex-related offences involving three complainants, was faced with eight additional charges this week after Peel Regional Police said they had identified more victims.
Court documents filed in a Brampton, Ont., courthouse shed more light on the latest charges, which stem from incidents that allegedly occurred in Toronto, its surrounding areas and elsewhere in Ontario. Stronach is accused of attempted rape and indecent assault on a woman between June 1977 and January 1978, and six other sexual assaults that allegedly occurred in the 1980s, 1990s, early 2000s and this year, the documents show.
The five previously filed charges against the founder of auto parts giant Magna involve allegations dating from the 1980s to 2023.
Stronach's lawyer Brian Greenspan has said that his client denies all allegations against him and plans to "vigorously" defend his reputation.
"He has spent his lifetime committed to the betterment of the Canadian community and industry," Greenspan wrote in a statement this week, saying Stronach plans to continue his advocacy work.
Stronach is scheduled to appear at the Brampton courthouse on July 8.
Stronach, who was born in Austria, became one of Canada's wealthiest people by creating Magna in his garage in 1957 and building it into one of the world's largest suppliers of auto parts.
He also founded The Stronach Group, a company that specializes in horse racing, and founded Stronach International in 2018, a company focusing on organic foods and "micro-electric mobility."
Stronach resigned as Magna's chairman in 2011 and founded his own political party in his native Austria the following year. He is the father of former Canadian politician Belinda Stronach, who served as a Member of Parliament for the Conservative Party before famously crossing the floor of the House of Commons to join the Liberals.
In 2018, Frank Stronach sued his daughter, two grandchildren and a former business associate for more than $500 million in Ontario Superior Court, alleging they mismanaged the family's assets and conspired to take control of them. The lawsuit was settled in 2020 with a deal that split the family fortune across two factions.
The Canadian Press

Document 387 of 500

The Liberals are 'done like dinner'

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/liberals-are-done-like-dinner/docview/3073539418/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=unknown&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=The+Liberals+are+%27done+like+dinner%27&amp;title=The+Spectator&amp;issn=11899417&amp;date=2024-06-29&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.16&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Spectator&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: In the spring of 1977 the Toronto Maple Leafs stunned the heavily favoured Philadelphia Flyers by winning games 1 and 2 of their Stanley Cup quarter-final series right in Philadelphia. Feeling supremely confident, Maple Leafs forward Dave (Tiger) Williams declared that the Flyers were "done like dinner."
Williams was proved wrong. The Flyers, enraged by his comments, came roaring back, winning the next four consecutive games and indeed the series.
But I believe the Williams quote can be used to describe the current Liberal government under Prime Minister Justin Trudeau.
This is due to their shocking defeat in a byelection held on Monday in the riding of Toronto-St. Paul's by Conservative Party candidate Don Stewart. Since the riding was created in 1935 the Liberals have held it for almost 60 years, including 10 consecutive elections dating back to 1993. Even in their electoral disaster of 2011, when the Liberals were decimated and fell to third place, they held onto Toronto-St. Paul's very comfortably. This was a riding that seemed to be painted Liberal red permanently.
And then came June 24. To be sure it was a close election and Liberal candidate Leslie Church lost by only 590 votes. A razor-thin margin, but a loss all the same.
Going into this byelection the Liberals had been practically unbeatable in the Toronto area. The Tories and NDP may win ridings in rural Ontario, but Toronto belonged to the Liberals. That is, until now.
It wasn't as if the Liberals didn't put in a strong effort, or took the riding for granted. The Liberals threw a lot of resources at keeping Toronto-St. Paul's. Senior cabinet ministers campaigned door to door for Church, and Church herself ran a good, well-financed campaign. Justin Trudeau made an appearance in the riding. The result was still a stunning loss. It is hard to argue that they could have run a better campaign.
What situation do the Liberals find themselves in now? They still trail in national polls. Some polls have them trailing the Conservatives by up to 20 points. Even before Monday's disaster they knew if an election was held today, Pierre Poilievre would win a massive, even record-setting majority. But that would only mean a period of time in exile for the Liberals as the official opposition.
That is now being called into question. If the Liberals can (and indeed did) lose Toronto-St. Paul's, this means there are no safe Liberal seats anywhere in Canada. Before June 24 the Liberals knew even if an election went bad, they were fairly safe in major cities like Toronto, Montreal, Ottawa, as well as the Maritimes. They can't count on any of that anymore. Adding to this is Quebec.
While Quebec is hardly a prime region for the "hard-right" angry politics of Poilievre and the Conservatives, they also have a history of wanting a say in government. When previous Conservative prime ministers such as John Diefenbaker and Brian Mulroney won record-setting majorities, they did it with large numbers of Quebec MPs. In the next election, will we see a substantial number of Quebec ridings go "Tory blue" to ensure Quebec has a strong voice in the new government? History says that is very possible and indeed probable.
Looking at all the above, the Liberals are facing an electoral disaster of epic proportions. Remember how the Progressive Conservatives went into the 1993 federal election with a majority government and were reduced to two seats? We may be looking at something similar here.
It's time for the Liberals to "save the furniture." Focus on a few possibly winnable seats, prepare for disaster and then rebuild. As Tiger Williams said in 1977 they are "done like dinner."
Craig Wallace is a Hamilton resident and author of
five books.
CAPTION:

CREDIT: Craig Wallace OPINION;

Subject: Elections

Location: Canada; Quebec Canada

People: Trudeau, Justin

Letters

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=unknown&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Letters&amp;title=The+Spectator&amp;issn=11899417&amp;date=2024-06-29&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.17&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Spectator&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Enough ranting about Ford
It seems that Doug Ford is the lightning rod for anything remotely uncomfortable in our lives. Almost every article and letter I read is a complaint about Ford.
The comments published in The Spec leave me assuming that Premier Ford controls everything from weather, to the rotation of the Earth. The projection of blame and constant whining about him is nauseating.
Ford was elected by a majority of voters to do a job. A job, that many of these whiny people, couldn't comprehend. If you don't like Ford's decisions or policies, then speak with your vote and spare us the entitled and myopic ranting and protesting.
Troy Smillie, Hannon
Who gets to be the PM?
Re: Would you hire any of Canada's federal leaders to run your business? (June 26)
Well, I'm sure we all appreciate Craig Wallace enlightening us on who is really qualified to be prime minister of Canada. I have a couple of issues with his list of qualified candidates.
The first one is that this is still supposed to be a democracy - so every Canadian should really be eligible for the role, not simply those of power, privilege and wealth. The civil service is there to provide the expertise to advise on policy and manage implementing legislation.
The second issue is that at least two of the three candidates Wallace suggests are just another set of neo-liberals who think of people as disposable liabilities and who are driving this country into the ground while privatizing everything from education to medical care.
What we need is a prime minister and a government to put an end to the endless cutting of services for the poor, sick and working class, while handing tax cuts to the already too-wealthy oligopoly in this country.
We need someone to reverse the race to turn every aspect of public life into a profit centre. We need a PM and party for whom the idea of Canadians living in tents is a national disgrace; jurisdiction be dammed - not one single Canadian should be living through the winter in a tent and standing in a food bank lineup. And frankly, the much-lamented deficit is a manufactured artifact made and used by the neo-liberals to justify all the cuts and misery for average Canadians.
Wayne Stansfield, Hamilton
Will any units be affordable?
Re: Chedoke development on Mountain brow moves forward (June 26)
I read the article about all the new housing units proposed on the Brow, and the preservation of the Cross of Lorraine, that was erected as a symbol of hope in the fight against tuberculosis. It is commendable that we are preserving history.
The plans call for lots of new housing. The only thing I didn't see was any mention of affordable housing units.
People living in tents are in desperate need of sustainable housing. Without affordable housing, the encampments will grow. Issues of poverty, mental health and addiction will grow.
The fight to find a cure for the epidemic of homelessness is just as vital as the fight waged against TB in the past. Million-dollar houses will not fix the situation. We need a symbol of hope for this epidemic. Build affordable housing.
Sharon McKibbon, Hamilton
Advertising is out of control
Thanks to the players in the finals between Edmonton and Florida who are proof that despite the incompetence of Gary Bettman, hockey remains the most exciting sport in the world. Thanks to the immense greed of the NHL owners the game itself has become a backdrop to what they're really presenting: advertisements.
The owners, not content to be billionaires, have made the game a visual eyesore with signs, logos and superimposed animation everywhere. From the ice, to the boards, seating, scoreboard, uniforms, skates and sticks they push products, not the game.
How long before the players wear sandwich board advertisements over their long-johns underwear and the owners bring the Goodyear blimp out of retirement and fly it among the rafters?
Gary Johnson, Hamilton
Thanks for the extra care
I am writing to commend the service we have had from our very conscientious Spectator delivery fellow. He has always been respectful with the paper neatly folded in the mailbox daily.
However, we have recently had construction work at the front of our house making the regular mailbox delivery impossible. We now find our morning read carefully slipped under the door of the carport where it never gets wet. We are impressed.
Ann Lawton-Barry, Hamilton

Doug Ford removes MPP Goldie Ghamari from caucus after she met with British far-right figure

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/doug-ford-removes-mpp-goldie-ghamari-caucus-after/docview/3073217107/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Doug+Ford+removes+MPP+Goldie+Ghamari+from+caucus+after+she+met+with+British+far-right+figure&amp;title=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-06-28&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Goldie Ghamari has been thrown out of the Progressive Conservative caucus, after Premier Doug Ford called her meeting with a controversial, far-right Briton "deeply regrettable."
"The premier has made the decision to remove MPP Goldie Ghamari from the Ontario PC caucus, effective immediately," Ford's office said in a statement Friday morning.
"This decision follows repeated instances of serious lapses in judgment and a failure to collaborate constructively with caucus leadership and as a team member. While this decision did not come easily, it has become clear that MPP Ghamari can no longer continue in her role within our caucus."
Ghamari responded to the move on Friday morning in a post on X.
"When one door closes, another one opens," she wrote.
Ghamari, who represents the Ottawa-area riding of Carleton, met with Tommy Robinson and had briefly posted on social media about their video conversation before deleting it.
Ford's office had previously criticized Ghamari for giving "a platform to an individual whose behaviour and beliefs are at odds with our government."
Robinson, who founded the anti-Islam English Defence League and has been convicted of a number of offences in the United Kingdom, was arrested in Calgary this week after being accused of misrepresenting himself in coming to Canada.
Ghamari has maintained she had no idea of Robinson's views.
"I was not aware of Mr. Robinson's history prior to our meeting," she posted on social media.
"I chose to meet with him because I am an Iranian-Canadian immigrant who has been speaking out on behalf of human rights violations by the Islamic regime in Iran." She added that the two discussed the Islamic Revolutionary Guard Corps, a branch of the Iranian armed forces that the Canadian government recently listed as a terrorist entity.
On social media, Robinson said he reached out to Ghamari "to ask her out on a date."
The National Council of Canadian Muslims said the interaction was "completely unacceptable" and called for Ghamari's ouster.
Ford has previously said Ghamari is a "strong voice for anti-Islamophobia" and antisemitism.
More to come.
With files from Alex Boyd

June 28: The people of Ontario deserve better, local Jewish community won't be intimidated and other letters to the editor
