Full text: People of Ontario deserve better
Re: Doug Ford's Ontario is less yours to discover than it is yours to fix (June 25)
Laura Babcock's column on the current state of Ontario certainly put concisely the problems with Doug Ford.
We are seniors that are appalled and actually terrified by the shambles in this province. Homelessness, health care and the myriad other poor decisions make us very afraid for our grandchildren's future. Ford and his government are oblivious to the needs that should be prioritized immediately.
If an election is called, all eligible voters must exercise that right and send Ford a strong message that the people of Ontario deserve better.
Ann O'Hare, Hamilton
Election call would be welcome
Special thanks to the Spectator for printing Laura Babcock's poignant column about Doug Ford's handling of Ontario's health care and education.
Remember, come election time ask yourself: what has Ford really done for you?
Remember his commercial saying "my lines are always open?"
The problem is he doesn't hear them over the ringing bells of slot machines and the funding parties he attends with his rich cronies.
David Intini, Stoney Creek
Voters have a short memory
"Does it feel like Ontario is still ours to discover, or does it feel more like it is ours to fix?" Laura Babcock asks about the Conservative Party of Ontario.
Two things continue to surprise me about my fellow Ontario citizens: their almost pathological avoidance of voting and the degree to which amnesia infects all of us minutes after the last government has left the building.
John Cartwright's recent primer on the Mike Harris government's "Common Sense Revolution" reminded me of its long-term impact: social housing projects slashed, mental hospitals closed and welfare reduced by 21 per cent. Privatization of public projects began, leading to today's struggle over long-term health care as the downloading of costs to municipalities accelerates. The gradual disintegration of local social services and transportation is the result of slashing our taxes and the rise in every cost associated with government that effects Ontario families.
It has been the singular achievement of Premier Doug Ford to reduce potential income even further, as massive amounts are transferred from the public purse into the pockets of his benefactors.
Pierre Poilievre promises a new "Common Sense" slash and dash at the federal level, including an assault on the Constitution if he does not get his way. I shudder and hope my fellow voters who missed the last version awake from their Rip Van Winkle naps before it is too late.
Virginia Aksan, Hamilton
Health care getting shortchanged
Enough is enough. I don't know about you, but it seems to me now that more and more hospitals are having lotteries to buy equipment.
The latest is Joseph Brant Hospital having a lottery to purchase a CT scanner. This is criminal, asking taxpayers to buy tickets when they have been paying taxes to fund health care. It's time governments funded the health-care system the way it should be. I don't think anybody would disagree with me.
My suggestion would be instead of sending billions of dollars to Ukraine and Israel, use that money to buy equipment needed to look after the people who have being paying taxes here for years. Instead, have lotteries to fund those wars that have nothing to do with Canada.
Health care is our right and should be the No. 1 priority and it seems our elected officials have forgotten that.
Jake Lombardo, Hamilton
Happy to hear of progress
Re: Chedoke development on Mountain brow moves forward (June 26)
I have just read Wednesday's article on the movement of the Chedoke development. It was exciting to see they are planning to preserve the Cross of Lorraine on the brow.
The cross represents a large piece of history of the sanatorium and Chedoke hospital. It would be a delight for many Hamiltonians to see it glow once again in the dark of night as it glowed many years ago.
Ed Barton, Hamilton
Gwynne Dyer column 'repugnant'
Re: Joe Biden's polling numbers will soon force a peace deal in Gaza (June 22)
In his June 20 column, Gwynne Dyer - a habitual anti-Israel antagonist - made the ridiculous claim that Israel, the world's only Jewish state, is the puppet-master controlling the United States for its own selfish and nefarious purposes. This is just a modern-day iteration of the historic lie parroted by antisemites that the Jews controlled the levers of world power and Dyer's unfounded accusation is repugnant.
Beyond the total lack of any evidence behind the claims that Israel's Prime Minister Netanyahu is the "tail that wags the American dog," as Dyer put it, is his apparent inability to grasp how any government could possibly support Israel, believing that apparently only a conspiracy theory could answer this conundrum.
Israel, despite its small size, is a liberal democratic state amid a sea of despotic, often fanatical, regimes. It is a stable, affluent society and one which, cultural differences aside, strongly resembles modern western societies. It should come as little wonder, then, why Israel is a natural ally of any western country. The bigger question, however, is why Dyer seems unable to understand this.
Cheri Elson, Dundas
Columns full of conspiracy theories
Once upon a time, I remember reading Gwynne Dyer's columns with interest and feeling that he had something intelligent to say. I no longer feel that way, particularly when he writes thatIsrael's Prime Minister Netanyahu is "the tail that wags the U.S. dog." Is he delusional as well as antisemitic?
Your newspaper should be publishing news rather than columns which are untrue and full of conspiracy theories
Preston Thom, Toronto
Strong leaders need to step up
Re: Would you hire any of Canada's federal leaders to run your business? (June 26)
What job outside the home could be more important than the role of prime minister? Yet as correctly asserted by Craig Wallace in his article, all of the three major party leaders are unqualified for the position.
Lack of good candidates in our elections is not a new thing. I recall with despair over several decades of casting my vote too many times as not in support of a good candidate, but rather in opposition to a poor one.
So where are the leaders we need for public service? Surely capable people must be out there, but my guess is they are busy raking in millions as CEOs where their skills are appreciated and fully compensated (although by way too much! Don't get me started on that one).
If someone has a solution to this problem, I will be very relieved to hear it. I can only hope that a person with strong leadership skills might leave the private sector and have enough civic mindedness (and energy!) to take on the tough and thankless task of running the country.
Emil Andrew Sekerak, Beamsville
Add Doug Ford to the list
Craig Wallace's column outlines what is wrong in politics in Ontario and Canada. Doug Ford's name could have been included in the article about Justin Trudeau, Pierre Poilievre and Jagmeet Singh in not having the qualities anyone would hire to run a large business.
Ford was lucky enough to inherit a successful business, so has learned nothing about running a province other than to keep his developer friends happy. His continued push for the completely unnecessary Highway 413 and the neglect of the Ontario Science Centre in favour of the new location at Ontario Place are just the latest examples of his lack of concern for taxpayers (read employers).
Unfortunately, nothing is going to happen to change this. What successful business person would want to step into these shoes?
Dennis Martin, Binbrook
We will not be intimidated
As a newcomer to Canada, I am proud and joyful to call this wonderful country home. Furthermore, since moving to Hamilton, my family has quickly learned how lucky we are to be part of this amazing community at large. This, our home, is a wonderful and diverse place, where relationships grow deep and spout into beautiful possibilities. Being a rabbi in this community, representing a Jewish community that has thrived for decades upon decades, is a gift.
This past Tuesday the 25th, as protesters chose the Westdale branch of Scotiabank for an anti-Israel protest, I was left with a need to let Hamiltonians know that we, the Jewish community, have - and continue to feel - more unsafe than we have ever before. Since the bank has many branches in town, choosing the branch closest to most of the Jewish community in town feels like an act of intimidation.
The conflation of the Jewish community in Hamilton with the war between Israel and the terrorist organization Hamas is also troublesome and racist. According to a federal government's fact sheet on antisemitism in Canada, available online, advocating for boycotting the state of Israel is one of six major manifestations of antisemitism in the country.
We, Hamiltonians, can and must do better. The Jewish community is not going anywhere. We will not be intimidated and will continue to stand proud and tall of our impact, past and future, in this wonderful community that we are lucky to call home.
Rabbi Beni Wajnberg, Hamilton
Play is witty and fast paced
Re: Tranquilizers all around, please - this comedy will bounce you off the walls (June 22)
Gary Smith missed the boat with his highly critical review of "Murder at Ackerton Manor" playing at the Lighthouse Festival in Port Dover. We attended this hilariously funny play today. The play was witty and fast paced. Maybe it's time Mr. Smith takes a tranquillizer and stay home.
Mary Anne McDougall, Hamilton
Did we watch the same play?
"Murder at Ackerton Manor" at Lighthouse Festival Theatre is a clever, comic campy send-up of Agatha Christie's work, not a "misguided homage" as Gary Smith called in his June 22 review. Christie's work can take a good joke and audiences can too. It's summer theatre and our group loved it. We actually wondered if Smith had seen the same play we had.
Sharon Bray, Mount Hope
Hoping minister gives this a pass
I just read that farmers in Denmark will soon be paying a carbon on their flatulent cows, sheep and pigs to reduce their greenhouse gas emissions.
I hope Environment Minister Steven Guilbeault doesn't get wind of this (excuse the pun) or it will be top of his "to do" list.
Les Farrington, Ancaster

It would be a delight for many Hamiltonians to see it glow once again in the dark of night, a reader says.Premier Doug Ford spoke at the Bay Area Economic Summit at the Royal Botanical Gardens Tuesday. Document 391 of 500

Letters

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/letters/docview/3073048371/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=unknown&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Letters&amp;title=The+Spectator&amp;issn=11899417&amp;date=2024-06-28&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.11&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Spectator&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: People of Ontario deserve better
Re: Doug Ford's Ontario is less yours to discover than it is yours to fix (June 25)
Laura Babcock's column on the current state of Ontario certainly put concisely the problems with Doug Ford.
We are seniors that are appalled and actually terrified by the shambles in this province. Homelessness, health care and the myriad other poor decisions make us very afraid for our grandchildren's future. Ford and his government are oblivious to the needs that should be prioritized immediately.
If an election is called, all eligible voters must exercise that right and send Ford a strong message that the people of Ontario deserve better.
Ann O'Hare, Hamilton
Election would
be welcomed
Special thanks to the Spectator for printing Laura Babcock's poignant column about Doug Ford's handling of Ontario's health care and education.
Remember, come election time ask yourself: what has Ford really done for you?
Remember his commercial saying "my lines are always open?"
The problem is he doesn't hear them over the ringing bells of slot machines and the funding parties he attends with his rich cronies.
David Intini, Stoney Creek
Voters have a short memory
"Does it feel like Ontario is still ours to discover, or does it feel more like it is ours to fix?" Laura Babcock asks about the Conservative Party of Ontario.
Two things continue to surprise me about my fellow Ontario citizens: their almost pathological avoidance of voting and the degree to which amnesia infects all of us minutes after the last government has left the building.
John Cartwright's recent primer on the Mike Harris government's "Common Sense Revolution" reminded me of its long-term impact: social housing projects slashed, mental hospitals closed and welfare reduced by 21 per cent. Privatization of public projects began, leading to today's struggle over long-term health care as the downloading of costs to municipalities accelerates. The gradual disintegration of local social services and transportation is the result of slashing our taxes and the rise in every cost associated with government that effects Ontario families.
It has been the singular achievement of Premier Doug Ford to reduce potential income even further, as massive amounts are transferred from the public purse into the pockets of his benefactors.
Pierre Poilievre promises a new "Common Sense" slash and dash at the federal level, including an assault on the Constitution if he does not get his way. I shudder and hope my fellow voters who missed the last version awake from their Rip Van Winkle naps before it is too late.
Virginia Aksan, Hamilton
Health care gets shortchanged
Enough is enough. I don't know about you, but it seems to me now that more and more hospitals are having lotteries to buy equipment.
The latest is Joseph Brant Hospital having a lottery to purchase a CT scanner. This is criminal, asking taxpayers to buy tickets when they have been paying taxes to fund health care. It's time governments funded the health-care system the way it should be.
I don't think anybody would disagree with me.
My suggestion would be instead of sending billions of dollars to Ukraine and Israel, use that money to buy equipment needed to look after the people who have being paying taxes here for years. Instead, have lotteries to fund those wars that have nothing to do with Canada.
Health care is our right and should be the No. 1 priority and it seems our elected officials have forgotten that.
Jake Lombardo, Hamilton
Happy to hear
of progress
Re: Chedoke development on Mountain brow moves forward (June 26)
I have just read Wednesday's article on the movement of the Chedoke development. It was exciting to see they are planning to preserve the Cross of Lorraine on the brow.
The cross represents a large piece of history of the sanatorium and Chedoke hospital. It would be a delight for many Hamiltonians to see it glow once again in the dark of night as it glowed many years ago.
Ed Barton, Hamilton
We will not
be intimidated
As a newcomer to Canada, I am proud and joyful to call this wonderful country home. Furthermore, since moving to Hamilton, my family has quickly learned how lucky we are to be part of this amazing community at large. This, our home, is a wonderful and diverse place, where relationships grow deep and spout into beautiful possibilities. Being a rabbi in this community, representing a Jewish community that has thrived for decades upon decades, is a gift.
This past Tuesday the 25th, as protesters chose the Westdale branch of Scotiabank for an anti-Israel protest, I was left with a need to let Hamiltonians know that we, the Jewish community, have - and continue to feel - more unsafe than we have ever before. Since the bank has many branches in town, choosing the branch closest to most of the Jewish community in town feels like an act of intimidation.
The conflation of the Jewish community in Hamilton with the war between Israel and the terrorist organization Hamas is also troublesome and racist. According to a federal government's fact sheet on antisemitism in Canada, available online, advocating for boycotting the state of Israel is one of six major manifestations of antisemitism in the country.
We, Hamiltonians, can and must do better. The Jewish community is not going anywhere. We will not be intimidated and will continue to stand proud and tall of our impact, past and future, in this wonderful community that we are lucky to call home.
Rabbi Beni Wajnberg,
Hamilton
Dyer column
'repugnant'
Re: Joe Biden's polling
numbers will soon force
a peace deal in Gaza (June 22)
In his June 20 column, Gwynne Dyer - a habitual anti-Israel antagonist - made the ridiculous claim that Israel, the world's only Jewish state, is the puppet-master controlling the United States for its own selfish and nefarious purposes. This is just a modern-day iteration of the historic lie parroted by antisemites that the Jews controlled the levers of world power and Dyer's unfounded accusation is repugnant.
Beyond the total lack of any evidence behind the claims that Israel's Prime Minister Netanyahu is the "tail that wags the American dog," as Dyer put it, is his apparent inability to grasp how any government could possibly support Israel, believing that apparently only a conspiracy theory could answer this conundrum.
Israel, despite its small size, is a liberal democratic state amid a sea of despotic, often fanatical, regimes. It is a stable, affluent society and one which, cultural differences aside, strongly resembles modern western societies. It should come as little wonder, then, why Israel is a natural ally of any western country. The bigger question, however, is why Dyer seems unable to understand this.
Cheri Elson, Dundas
Columnist
loses a fan
Once upon a time, I remember reading Gwynne Dyer's columns with interest and feeling that he had something intelligent to say. I no longer feel that way, particularly when he writes that Israel's Prime Minister Netanyahu is "the tail that wags the U.S. dog." Is he delusional as well as antisemitic?
Your newspaper should be publishing news rather than columns which are untrue and full of conspiracy theories.
Preston Thom, Toronto
Strong leaders need to step up
Re: Would you hire any of
Canada's federal leaders to run your business? (June 26)
What job outside the home could be more important than the role of prime minister? Yet as correctly asserted by Craig Wallace in his article, all of the three major party leaders are unqualified for the position.
Lack of good candidates in our elections is not a new thing. I recall with despair over several decades of casting my vote too many times as not in support of a good candidate, but rather in opposition to a poor one.
So where are the leaders we need for public service? Surely capable people must be out there, but my guess is they are busy raking in millions as CEOs where their skills are appreciated and fully compensated (although by way too much! Don't get me started on that one).
If someone has a solution to this problem, I will be very relieved to hear it. I can only hope that a person with strong leadership skills might leave the private sector and have enough civic mindedness (and energy!) to take on the tough and thankless task of running the country.
Emil Andrew Sekerak,
Beamsville
Add Doug Ford
to this list
Craig Wallace's column outlines what is wrong in politics in Ontario and Canada. Doug Ford's name could have been included in the article about Justin Trudeau, Pierre Poilievre and Jagmeet Singh in not having the qualities anyone would hire to run a large business.
Ford was lucky enough to inherit a successful business, so has learned nothing about running a province other than to keep his developer friends happy. His continued push for the completely unnecessary Highway 413 and the neglect of the Ontario Science Centre in favour of the new location at Ontario Place are just the latest examples of his lack of concern for taxpayers (read employers).
Unfortunately, nothing is going to happen to change this. What successful business person would want to step into these shoes?
Dennis Martin, Binbrook
Play is witty
and fast paced
Re: Tranquilizers all around, please - this comedy will bounce you off the walls
(June 22)
Gary Smith missed the boat with his highly critical review of "Murder at Ackerton Manor" playing at the Lighthouse Festival in Port Dover. We attended this hilariously funny play today. The play was witty and fast paced. Maybe it's time Mr. Smith takes a tranquillizer and stay home.
Mary Anne McDougall,
Hamilton
Did we watch
the same play?
"Murder at Ackerton Manor" at Lighthouse Festival Theatre is a clever, comic campy send-up of Agatha Christie's work, not a "misguided homage" as Gary Smith called in his June 22 review. Christie's work can take a good joke and audiences can too. It's summer theatre and our group loved it. We actually wondered if Smith had seen the same play we had.
Sharon Bray, Mount Hope
Hoping minister gives this a pass
I just read that farmers in Denmark will soon be paying a carbon on their flatulent cows, sheep and pigs to reduce their greenhouse gas emissions.
I hope Environment Minister Steven Guilbeault doesn't get wind of this (excuse the pun) or it will be top of his "to do" list.
Les Farrington, Ancaster

Document 392 of 500

PM needs to hear that it's time to go

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/pm-needs-hear-that-time-go/docview/3073046576/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=unknown&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=PM+needs+to+hear+that+it%27s+time+to+go&amp;title=The+Spectator&amp;issn=11899417&amp;date=2024-06-28&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.10&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Spectator&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: If Justin Trudeau's life were a country music song, now would be the point in the proceedings where he's all sad and lonesome, watching his girl dance with another man.
That's because Pierre Poilievre has just swooped into downtown Toronto, the Big Smoke, Liberal party HQ, and done stole his woman. You can practically hear the pedal steel guitar lick accompanying the prime minister's tears to the floor.
At least, the prime minister should be sad. Because what's happened is bad. Catastrophically bad. If Toronto is now fertile Conservative ground, not much of anything Liberal will be growing come the next general election.
Whether the prime minister is actually sad is another question. Trudeau has never scored highly on humility or self-awareness. It's therefore more likely he's angry. No matter what Trudeau might end up saying in the aftermath of the shocking, razer thin byelection upset to the Conservatives in the safe Liberal riding of Toronto-St. Paul's there is unquestionably a part of him raging about the result.
Of all the people he could lose to, he doesn't want to lose to Conservative leader Pierre Poilievre. Not to the man they used to call "Skippy." Not that (expletive) guy.
I imagine a similar thought once flitted through Stephen Harper's mind as Trudeau began pulling away to victory in 2015. But here's the thing: Harper was in the midst of an election. He had no choice but to fight on, even if that meant dragging Rob and Doug Ford up on stage.
Trudeau has another choice. He can leave. He should leave. There is, in theory, a record of accomplishment from the past nine years. Better to tout it now and give someone else the chance of plotting a new course. Because the voters are starting to make themselves heard, even in places where Liberals have seldom heard a discouraging word.
Sure, things could, in theory, turn around. Trump might return to the White House. And Poilievre might get caught French-kissing Alex Jones at a Diagolon rally as a group of Proud Boys livestream it for QAnon. But who's to say these things are going to make people like Justin Trudeau any more?
Trudeau has binned half of his cabinet and ... nothing changed in the polls. He tabled another budget and ... nothing changed. He sent most of his new cabinet to Toronto-St. Paul's to tout the budget and ... the people said "no." What more of a signal does one man need?
This is where a politician's political staff and other trusted advisers need to step in and have a frank conversation. It is the time to say the things that need to be said, not to say the things the boss wants to hear.
Whatever the words, it will need to be a human conversation. The prime minister deserves respect. Life at the top is unforgiving. But it's also a choice. Nobody is forcing him to stay. There is time for another career.
Put differently, just what does the prime minister hope to accomplish by toughing it out until the next election? And how does that stack up to time he could be spending with his children, or pursuing change through a different vehicle other than elected office?
The answer appears to be: nothing.
The choice is leave gracefully now, or get smoked later. That's why it's time for those close to Trudeau to pat him warmly on the back and tell him he's done his job. That the Liberal party, despite all appearances, is more than one man, and has more than the past 11 years with him at the top as its history.
He rebuilt the party after its decimation in the 2011 election and now it's time for someone to do the same, hopefully ahead of the next election.
There is still time for the Liberals to change their tune, even if time has run out for Justin Trudeau to do the same.
Andrew MacDougall is a director at Trafalgar
Strategy, a U.K.-based
consultancy. He was a
director of communications to prime minister Stephen Harper.
CAPTION:

Document 393 of 500

Architects firm behind science centre calls closure 'political': Infrastructure minister says she could not risk safety of workers, children

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/architects-firm-behind-science-centre-calls/docview/3073046365/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Architects+firm+behind+science+centre+calls+closure+%27political%27%3A+Infrastructure+minister+says+she+could+not+risk+safety+of+workers%2C+children&amp;title=The+Spectator&amp;issn=11899417&amp;date=2024-06-28&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.6&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Spectator&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: The firm of the late architect who designed the Ontario Science Centre says the province's decision to immediately close its doors over a problem with the roof was "absurd" and motivated by politics rather than safety concerns.
Brian Rudy, a partner with Moriyama Teshima Architects, said news of the science centre's abrupt closure last week left them "dumbfounded."
"It's absurd to think that the whole building needs to be immediately shut down," Rudy told The Canadian Press. "It's so obviously a political move."
Raymond Moriyama, who died last year, designed the science centre that opened in 1969 on a ravine near the west branch of the Don River in Toronto's east end.
At a hastily called news conference last Friday, the Ministry of Infrastructure and Infrastructure Ontario announced the science centre would close by 4 p.m. due to health and safety concerns over the roof.
The closure sparked outrage from local residents, science lovers and opposition politicians. Many have called on the government to reverse course.
The province blamed failing roof panels made with a material called reinforced autoclaved aerated concrete, a lightweight form of concrete that was popular in the 1960s and '70s.
The government said it acted fast after receiving an engineer's report earlier in the week that laid out problems with the roof. The science centre's board decided to close the institution.
The report, written by the engineering firm Rimkus Consulting, did not recommend an immediate closure.
Some roof panels are at risk of collapse and Infrastructure Minister Kinga Surma said the engineers told government officials that the roof should be replaced in its entirety. That would take two to five years and cost upwards of $40 million.
Surma said it was a health-and-safety matter and that she was not "going to risk the safety of workers and children."
But Rudy said the roof panel problem should not have come as a surprise to the government.
"It needn't have been because it has been known for years," Rudy said. "This has been an issue identified decades ago."
Several reports over the years painted a sad story of the science centre's state of disrepair. Successive governments, from Progressive Conservatives to Liberals to New Democrats, deferred maintenance.
Rudy said his firm analyzed the engineer's report and he thinks there are a number of ways forward, and questioned the government's actions.
"It's not as urgent as they would let on," Rudy said.
"Most of the panels actually aren't even above exhibition spaces. So the urgency to close exhibition spaces is unfounded. In fact, all of the permanent exhibit spaces are free of this type of roof panel entirely, so it's not even a concern."
Rudy takes no issue with the thrust of the engineer's report.
"There's no doubt that repairs need to be made and that's no different than any building of this age," he said.
"Any building or museum of this size is going to have periodic maintenance at this age, so this is something that they know and that they do in phases, even if there are panels above public spaces. They're easy to rope off and make secure from public while the repairs are done."
The firm wants the province to reverse its decision and has offered its services free of charge to help.
The province does not appear to be wavering. It is looking for an interim spot for the centre before a new one, half the size, opens at a redeveloped Ontario Place.
Milton said the full cost to repair everything at the science centre "would be at least $478 million."
The Canadian Press
CAPTION: The architectural firm that designed the Ontario Science Centre said the province's decision to close its doors immediately over a problem with its roof was "absurd."Chris Young The Canadian Press

Document 394 of 500

How two kids from Scarborough and East York became the go-to tailors for star NBA prospects, including Zach Edey

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/how-two-kids-scarborough-east-york-became-go/docview/3073306242/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=How+two+kids+from+Scarborough+and+East+York+became+the+go-to+tailors+for+star+NBA+prospects%2C+including+Zach+Edey&amp;title=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-06-27&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: For the past three months, two Toronto tailors have been designing suits for four NBA prospects taking centre stage in the 2024 NBA draft tonight.
Nota Bene Bespoke, run by business partners Shana Uthaya and Trevis Villafana, is the company behind the suits worn by Duke's Kyle Filipowski, Kansas' Johnny Furphy, Kentucky's Justin Edwards and Toronto's own Zach Edey.
Ahead of the NBA draft, the Star got a peek behind the magic of how the local tailor company created the suits for four players headed to the NBA.
The NBA draft has become one of the most talked-about fashion events in sports. It's where future stars get to make their mark and introduce themselves to the world while dressing to impress.
Draft night fashion has gone down in history, from Raptors guard Gradey Dick making headlines last year for his "Wizard of Oz''-inspired red suit to LeBron James' classic all-white outfit from 2003.
Duke's Kyle Filipowski and Kansas' Johnny Furphy walk the red carpet at the 2024 NBA draft at Barclays Arena in Brooklyn tonight, wearing custom-made suits designed by Nota Bene Bespoke. Filipowski and Furphy received an invite to the NBA draft green room, where soon-to-be drafted players are invited with family to hear their name called by the NBA's commissioner.
Furphy went for a royal blue, single-breasted peak lapel tuxedo with a James Bond-esque look, with a black bow tie. "Johnny Bond" is the nickname Uthaya and Villafana stamped for Furphy while designing his suit.
"I wanted to keep it simple, nothing too crazy, nothing in your face. The idea was sharp but calm," Furphy told the Star.
When brainstorming his look for draft night, Furphy, a 19-year-old from Melbourne, Australia, said he wanted to show love to his country. So the suit's inner lining includes a map outline of Australia pinpointing Melbourne with a yellow star. It also pays homage to every Australian basketball player that has been drafted to the NBA.
"There haven't been many Australians in the position I am now," said Furphy. "It's cool to be a part of the small group of guys that have been in the same position."
The names of Australian NBA players including Ben Simmons, Joe Ingles, Dante Exum, Matthew Dellavedova and Mattise Thybulle are included in the suit's lining. Adding to the growing list of Australian-drafted players, Furphy's name is highlighted in green.
Every detail and every seam was carefully considered by the tailors. "We want the guys to be able to wear [these suits] a decade from now," said Uthaya. "We put in extra fabric because your body won't be the same 10 years from now."
Duke's Filipowski is wearing an emerald green suit with a floral pattern. It's double-breasted and accented with gold buttons and a colourful paisley pocket square. The fabric on his suit is rare, a one-of-one Vitale Barberis Canonico textile made in 1663.
The inner lining of Filipowski's suit has his nickname "Flip" repeated in silver on the left side, while on the right is an image of him hooping at Duke. His full last name is showcased on the back of the lining with the words "Welcome to the league."
"What I love so much about it is that it's very me," said Filipowski. "It's a little bit more than how I usually express myself but it's not too much."
The seven-foot man doesn't often express himself through clothing, but for draft night, he thought it was important to come out of his comfort zone. "I think they did a tremendous job. They executed my vision perfectly and they were just trying to make me the most happiest I can be with it and most comfortable," said Filipowski. "I never thought I could be so attached to a piece of clothing."
When Filipowski saw the final version of his suit ahead of the draft, he couldn't stop smiling. His partner got emotional seeing him in it. "Getting to this moment and her being such a focal point in getting here ... it's going to be a memory that I have and a very special one for me for the rest of my life," said Filipowski.
While he won't be in New York, Kentucky's Edwards went for a classy, timeless look for the draft. His suit is a custom deep black tuxedo made with a blend of 80 per cent silk and 20 per cent wool with silk sparkle on the top. The black pattern of Edward's suit is known as jacquard, invented by the French textile artisan Joseph Marie Jacquard in 1804.
When Villafana met with Edwards at the NBA draft combine, the player had one ask for his suit: to find a way to honour his mother. A very family-oriented guy, Edwards grew up in a single-parent household with two younger siblings.
On Edwards' inner lining, there is a photo of his mother hugging him from a photo shoot in the Philadelphia Inquirer. The lining also features the area code "215" as a shoutout to his hometown of Philly, and an action shot of him playing college basketball in Kentucky.
Edey is by far Nota Bene Bespoke's largest client in stature. The seven-foot-four, 300-pound big man born and raised in Toronto received an invite to the NBA draft green room but instead decided to host a watch party with family, friends and teammates in Purdue, where he went to college. Edey is expected to wear the suit created by Nota Bene Bespoke for his first press conference with his new team after the draft.
Edey's agent called the Toronto company to request a suit for the draft, and in late April, the tailors flew to Chicago to meet with Edey to brainstorm, present him with potential mock-ups and take measurements.
In the fitting process, Edey's size left the tailors in awe. He had the longest wingspan they'd ever seen; his seven-foot-10 wingspan took up the room. The back of his jacket was about 35 inches; a typical suit would be about 10 inches shorter.
"I shook his hand and right away I was like, 'Man, I gotta use a ladder. There's no way we're going to be able to do this,'" said Uthaya, who took about 30 measurements of Edey's entire body.
While doing the fitting, they chatted about college basketball and the ongoing Drake vs. Kendrick Lamar beef, and laughed about rivalries at the high schools they attended in Toronto.
"He's probably the biggest Canadian [athlete] in terms of size, to be drafted in the NBA. And he's reached out to a small business in Toronto to do his look. That's really important for us," said Uthaya.
Uthaya and Villafana have known each other since they were kids. Before becoming business partners, they grew up playing competitive soccer against each other.
Uthaya started the business in 2011 while studying at McMaster University. For the first few years, his clientele consisted of friends and family who trusted his eye for fashion. Villafana was a bar manager at the time on Ossington and Uthaya would often stop by; they'd talk about the work he was doing growing his business. Villafana's grandmother used to make wedding dresses so tailoring is in his blood, and he was interested in working with Uthaya.
Soon, Villafana started helping Uthaya with styling and doing trade shows. Nota Bene means "attention" or "take notice" in Italian, and people certainly did. Their business grew as they started getting wedding referrals and clients on Bay Street, and word-of-mouth spread. That's how the business manager for former Raptor Danny Green heard about them and soon they were fitting the 16-year NBA veteran during the Raptors championship season.
The sports world started to take notice of their work. Uthaya and Villafana went from childhood soccer rivals to eventually suiting up Brazilian soccer star Richarlison de Andrade, designing his suit ahead of the 2022 FIFA World Cup in Qatar. They've since worked with Jannik Sinner, the No. 1 ranked tennis player in the world, Atiba Hutchinson from Canada soccer as well as NFL and NHL players.
But their tallest task was creating the perfect suit for Edey. His main request was to highlight two things: that he's Canadian and Purdue, where he dominated college basketball, racking up almost every accolade possible.
After they met with Edey in Chicago, Filipowski, Furphy and Edwards also jumped on board as clients.
The Toronto tailors had about four weeks to make Edey's suit come alive. There were a lot of sleepless nights, as they wanted it to be perfect. A week and a half before the NBA draft, they travelled back to Chicago to show Edey the final version: A custom-made black, double-breasted, peak lapel suit. The detailing has micro diamonds with a black sparkle; the fabric is from Loro Piana, one of the most luxurious mills in the world. There's a silver crown design on some of the buttons.
The inner lining of the suit has the Purdue Boilermakers logo stamped across it, just as Edey requested. And if you look closely, the Canadian maple leaf is displayed in the design.
When Edey put on his suit, he did a runway walk through his agency's office in sunglasses, to cheers. Nota Bene Bespoke's work was complete.
"For two guys from Toronto - I'm from Scarborough, Trev's from East York - to come in and be a part of such a big American event, not just one guy in the draft but four guys," said Uthaya, "it's meaningful."
CAPTION: Kyle Filipowski, flanked by the Nota Bene Bespoke tailors.Nota Bene Bespoke's tailor Shana Uthaya shows the outline of Australia inside the suit of Johnny Furphy, who is from Melbourne.Johnny Furphy walks the red carpet before the 2024 NBA draft.The custom-made lining inside the suit of NBA prospect draft pick Kyle "Flip" Filipowski.Kyle Filipowski in his custom Nota Bene Bespoke suit on the NBA 2024 draft red carpet.Nota Bene Bespoke's tailor Travis Villafana with the suit for NBA prospect draft pick Justin Edwards.Nota Bene Bespoke's Toronto tailors Trevis Villafana (right) and Shana Uthaya with the custom-made suits they created for NBA draft prospect picks (left to right) Zach Edey, Johnny Furphy and Kyle "Flip" Filipowski.Nota Bene Bespoke's tailors Travis Villafana (left) and Shana Uthaya show Zach Edey's wingspan as they hold the suit shirt they made for him.ONLINE AND PRINT RIGHTS PURCHASED - INVOICE #5119 - TORONTO, ONT.: JUN 22, 2024 - Nota Bene Bespoke's tailors Travis Villafana (right) and Shana Uthaya are photographed with the custom made suits for NBA draft prospect picks (left to right) Zach Edey, Johnny Furphy, and Kyle "Flip" Filipowski, at Nota Bene Bespoke atelier, on Saturday, June 22, 2024, in Toronto, Ontario. Document 395 of 500

Some Liberal insiders worry they're seen as too 'woke' under Justin Trudeau - and that it may be too late for him to go

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/some-liberal-insiders-worry-theyre-seen-as-too/docview/3073306225/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Some+Liberal+insiders+worry+they%27re+seen+as+too+%27woke%27+under+Justin+Trudeau+-+and+that+it+may+be+too+late+for+him+to+go&amp;title=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-06-27&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: OTTAWA - Shock and disappointment over Monday's byelection defeat in midtown Toronto is still rippling through federal Liberal circles, with some insiders stating their party has veered too far from the political centre and gained a damaging association with the "woke" left.
It's one of the many factors Liberals are pointing at - alongside souring views on Prime Minister Justin Trudeau's leadership - to explain how they lost to Pierre Poilievre's Conservatives on Monday, in a riding that has elected Liberals reliably in 10 previous campaigns since 1993. On Thursday, former B.C. Liberal premier Christy Clark became the latest prominent voice to call on Trudeau to resign.
For Marcus Powlowski, a Liberal MP from northern Ontario, part of the problem is a public perception - which he thinks is wrong - that the party is connected to a closed-minded political correctness that prioritizes "identity politics" over "universal human values."
Powlowski described this type of politics as including "cancel culture," in which people are shamed for expressing certain opinions, and said the party needs to counter the perception it is too "woke" by better showcasing its centrist values of fiscal discipline, support for free enterprise, and the "traditional" liberalism of free speech, civil liberties and equality.
"In a lot of people's minds, the Liberal party has become synonymous with concepts like 'woke' culture, cancel culture and identity politics," Powlowski said.
"I think it has alienated a lot of people."
Sen. Percy Downe, who was chief of staff to prime minister Jean Chrétien, agreed the Liberals have "gone too far left" under Trudeau's leadership, pointing to how Environment Minister Steven Guilbeault declared earlier this year in the House of Commons that he is "proud socialist."
"He's in the wrong party," Downe said.
He also believes the Liberal government has earned a reputation for lengthy consultations without concrete results, which is now hurting its chances with the electorate. Voters, he said, "think they're a collection of words as opposed to action."
The soul-searching comes as Trudeau continues to distance himself from journalists' questions amid renewed speculation over whether he will stay on as Liberal leader. Trudeau's official schedule for Thursday said the prime minister - who hasn't taken questions since the stunning loss on Monday - was in the GTA for a party fundraiser and was set to deliver prepared remarks.
So far, those who have openly called for a new leader are outside the sitting Liberal caucus. CBC reported Wednesday that former Liberal MP Frank Baylis is weighing a possible leadership bid to replace Trudeau. Baylis did not respond to an interview request from the Star.
At the same time, several cabinet ministers and sitting MPs have publicly stated they believe Trudeau should stay, including Guilbeault, Deputy Prime Minister Chrystia Freeland, Immigration Minister Marc Miller and Health Minister Mark Holland. Trudeau, in his first public statement on the byelection result, suggested he's focused on his job and earning back Canadians' support.
The Prime Minister's Office has not ruled out convening a national caucus meeting to hash out feedback about the disappointing result and to plot a path forward for the party. Because of the summer parliamentary recess, the next scheduled caucus meeting isn't until September.
Downe was one of the first Liberals to publicly call for Trudeau to go, in an op-ed published last fall. But on Thursday, the senator suggested it might now be too close to the next general election for Trudeau to resign without saddling his successor with an unwinnable political situation.
"The window for him leaving may have closed," Downe said.
Many other Liberal insiders who spoke with the Star after the byelection loss stopped short of calling for Trudeau to leave, even if some of them felt the sting of the defeat should spark significant changes in how the party is preparing for the next general election campaign, currently set for the fall of 2025.
For Downe, the party should focus on a handful of core issues, like economic prosperity, immigration and health care. It should also "stop the cheap shots at the Conservatives," he said, referring to a statement Freeland made this week when she said the opposition Tories have a vision that is "cold, cruel and small."
"To mock that support is not helpful," Downe said. "It looks like political separation."
Powlowski, meanwhile, said he still has faith in Trudeau's leadership and believes the party is focusing on the right issues - including a promised school food program, dental care and public coverage of diabetes medication and birth control. He suggested if people are willing to "actually listen" to Trudeau and the policies he's promoting, that will dispel what he sees as a problematic association with the "woke" left.
"I think that's part of the political centrism ... back to the universal human values and getting away from the identity politics," he said. "I don't think it's just white males - I think a lot of different cultures and a lot of people from different countries ... feel the same way about a lot of these issues."
Others say their confidence in the current leadership of the Liberal government has been shaken. One MP, who agreed to speak Thursday on condition they aren't named, said many in the Liberal caucus are now wondering whether their own seats are at risk. The MP said Canada is heading for a "massive" Conservative majority government unless the Liberals make changes - possibly in the party organization, the Prime Minister's Office, or even Trudeau himself.
"I'm not yet prepared to say he needs to step down, but there needs to be some self-reflection," they said.
One thing that won't work, according to this MP, is for the Liberals to continue with the same strategy and assume their standing will improve.
"We can cling to, 'Things will get better, don't worry, hope is on the horizon,' but Canadians may have moved past that," the MP said.
"It's tough to shake that notion that they dislike you."
With files from Tonda MacCharles
CAPTION: Prime Minister Justin Trudeau rides a horse while attending community celebrations to mark the 10th anniversary of the Tsilhqot'in decision, in Nemaiah Valley, B.C., on June 26, 2024. In 2014, the Supreme Court of Canada granted Tsilhqot'in aboriginal title to more than 1,750 square kilometres of land in the Nemaiah Valley.

Subject: Liberalism; Identity politics; Political leadership; Prime ministers; Immigration; Ostracism

Document 396 of 500

Doug Ford calls Tory MPP's meeting with British far-right figure 'deeply regrettable'

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/doug-ford-calls-tory-mpps-meeting-with-british/docview/3073305940/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Doug+Ford+calls+Tory+MPP%27s+meeting+with+British+far-right+figure+%27deeply+regrettable%27&amp;title=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-06-27&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: A meeting between Goldie Ghamari and British far-right figure Tommy Robinson is "deeply regrettable," Premier Doug Ford's office said after the Carlton MPP posted - and then deleted - a screenshot of their video conversation.
In an emailed statement, Ford's office condemned the decision to "give a platform to an individual whose behaviour and beliefs are at odds with our government." Robinson was arrested in Calgary earlier this week on an outstanding immigration warrant in connection with allegations he misrepresented himself when entering the country. A founder of the anti-Islam English Defence League, he has been convicted in the U.K. of multiple offences, including assault.
For her part, Ghamari says she did not know who Robinson was. "I was not aware of Mr. Robinson's history prior to our meeting," the MPP posted to X on Wednesday, after removing a post from Instagram that showed the two in conversation, above the caption, "Oh hi there Tommy Robinson." She added that she condemned both Islamophobia and antisemitism.
"I chose to meet with him because I am an Iranian-Canadian immigrant who has been speaking out on behalf of human rights violations by the Islamic regime in Iran." She added that the two discussed the Islamic Revolutionary Guard Corps, a branch of the Iranian armed forces that the Canadian government recently listed as a terrorist entity.
Robinson, whose real name is Stephen Christopher Yaxley-Lennon, also took to X with his own explanation: "I contacted @gghamari to ask her out on a date as I'm in Canada, we didn't talk politics," he posted. "You clowns don't control who can & cannot talk to each other."
The interaction drew fire from the National Council of Canadian Muslims, who called it "completely unacceptable conduct" from an elected official.
"This is not only deeply offensive to Ontarians who care about combating hate in our province," the organization said in a petition renewing its calls to remove Ghamari, who has said she grew up in a Muslim family, from the government caucus.
In the wake of protests last fall, Ghamari was also criticized by the NCCM for "recycl(ing) some of the worst Islamophobic fear-mongering" after she took to social media to criticize the Muslim leaders she said had "justif(ied) Hamas's barbarity." She also said hearing the prayer Allahu akbar in the streets - meaning "God is great," a phrase commonly used by Muslims to express joy or faith - is "concerning" and was sometimes appropriated by radicals.
At the time, the premier said he would not remove her from caucus. "She has really been a strong voice for anti-Islamaphobia, making sure we have zero tolerance" he told reporters. "No matter if it's antisemitism or Islamophobia, it has no place here in Ontario."
CAPTION: In this 2020 photo, Premier Doug Ford is joined by MPP Goldie Ghamari to honour the victims of Ukraine airlines Flight 752, which was shot down near Tehran.

Subject: Antisemitism; Muslims; Islamophobia; Xenophobia

Document 397 of 500

Ford government slammed over decision to close science centre as workers laid off, opposition argues it's cheaper to repair it

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/ford-government-slammed-over-decision-close/docview/3073305912/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Ford+government+slammed+over+decision+to+close+science+centre+as+workers+laid+off%2C+opposition+argues+it%27s+cheaper+to+repair+it&amp;title=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-06-27&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: It would be cheaper for the provincial government to repair the existing Ontario Science Centre rather than shut it down and move it to the waterfront by Ontario Place, says NDP Leader Marit Stiles.
Speaking in front of the now-shuttered science centre on Don Mills Road, Stiles said Thursday efforts to save the facility will be increased.
"There's a very large part of it not even impacted at all," said Stiles. "So they could get in there right now. They could even make some of the repairs right now. And then if we need to close it, just temporarily - for weeks, whatever it takes - to repair it, not to shut it down forever" at its current location.
The Ontario Science Centre was abruptly closed last Friday after a new engineering report found parts of the roof to be at risk of collapsing because of the aerated concrete used in its construction.
The Ministry of Infrastructure announced the building, designed by the late Raymond Moriyama, only had a few months before it would not be structurally sound, and that the immediate shutdown would allow staff to clear out the building.
All planned summer camps have been relocated to a nearby public school. A weekend wedding planned at the centre was moved to the nearby Aga Khan museum.
The search for a temporary location for the science centre is now underway.
Green MPP Aislinn Clancy, her party's deputy leader, said the government had assured science centre workers would not lose their jobs, and now more than 50 will be laid off.
"This is a classic move out of the Conservative playbook: delay repairs, disregard the public good and disrespect the workers who show up every day to serve the people of Ontario," she said in a written statement.
"The premier just doesn't get it, and Ontarians won't stand for it. We're going to keep fighting to repair and reopen the Science Centre so it can continue to provide jobs, programming and lifelong memories for the people of Don Valley and Ontario as a whole."
The centre has about 250 workers.
Its closure comes against the backdrop of the government's controversial decision to move the science museum to Ontario Place and become part of the redevelopment there along with a privately owned spa.
Toronto city council will be discussing the science centre at its regular meeting Thursday.
CAPTION: The Ontario Science Centre, shown on June 26, 2024.

Subject: Construction; Science; Engineering firms; Aerated concrete

Business indexing term: Subject: Engineering firms

Document 398 of 500

June 27: Sidelining MPP is outrageous, Kelowna model worth emulating and other letters to the editor

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/june-27-sidelining-mpp-is-outrageous-kelowna/docview/3072777623/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=June+27%3A+Sidelining+MPP+is+outrageous%2C+Kelowna+model+worth+emulating+and+other+letters+to+the+editor&amp;title=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-06-27&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Sidelining MPP is outrageous
Re: Why is this provincially elected representative not allowed at meetings with the province? (June 24)
Absolutely disgusting that our local MPP Bobbi Ann Brady has been barred from attending meetings between Haldimand County and Doug Ford's government. She fought hard for this job and was elected. She represents all the people in Haldimand and has our best interests at heart and should not be banned by a few councillors who disagree with her. As for the few councillors who approved this outrageous motion, that won't be forgotten next election.
Joy Stewart, Dunnville
Not buying the heat pump hype
Re: Here's a low-cost emission reduction action for Hamilton (June 25)
Yet another article extolling the environmental benefits of cold air heat pumps versus gas furnaces. If I understand correctly, I am supposed to replace my perfectly good 95 per cent efficient gas furnace with an air source heat pump that admittedly is more efficient when the temperatures are above freezing, but whose efficiency declines as the temperature decreases.
Oh, and on those days when the heat pump is used, how is the electricity produced? Gas-fired generators. Not to mention the waste of tossing perfectly good equipment.
Fortunately those of us who understand the details of energy production in Ontario aren't wasting our money on this nonsense.
Richard Ronchka, Carlisle
Homelessness response is disappointing
The encampments are destroying our downtown, just when Hamilton was getting close to being the "Ambitious City" that it has been purported to be. For 15 years, I have lived in Ward 2 and experienced a blossoming and rejuvenation that was wonderful to see. But things are changing and at the recent general issues committee council meeting, many Ward 2 and 3 residents communicated the new and rising problems with garbage, noise, theft and insecurity that come from laissez-faire governance of the encampments.
Front and centre is the very real issue of a drug addiction and mental health epidemic and how it has hit our community. Where, also, is the provincial government to provide the services these people need? We don't need booze on every corner; we need help. And what do our self-serving councillors do just in time for Canada Day? They are moving all tents from Bayfront Park to Strachan, a location that caused a huge backlash from North End residents last year. A great city is only a sum of its many parts and I am incredibly disappointed that our representatives opted to think only of themselves and not of the health of the city as a whole.
Marta Stiteler, Hamilton
Provide shelter for those in need now
Re: City dragging its feet on homelessness (June 24)
Regarding the human beings who are less a home in Hamilton, every day I tiredly ask "why?" Why are we allowing things to deteriorate so badly?
If we don't want to see homeless people in tents, make some type of inexpensive homes for them to live in ASAP. There sits a handful of wonderful "tiny homes" unused due to squabbles about zones and NIMBYism. Is that ridiculous or what? It's also unintelligent.
Don't want to see their clothes, belongings and garbage? Give them a home, any type of safe structure to live in. We know they will be grateful.
Don't want needles and other drug paraphernalia in the parks where children play? Make a place of their own in a simple structure, very far away from the parks.
Don't want some of them at every street corner begging for change? Give them a small safe space to live immediately with enough money for food, etc. It certainly won't eliminate all panhandling, but will reduce it significantly.
For the millionth time, I have just stated what thousands upon thousands have already said. Any human less a home, is not going to be a healthy productive one. Why are we allowing homelessness to grow?
We don't have to have both intelligence and compassion; just pick one and there is a solution before our eyes every day. The solution is to provide safe homes for homeless people. What's Hamilton going to choose to do?
Ana Long, Stoney Creek
Kelowna model should be emulated
It's clear after a trial period that the city's present encampment policy is not working and needs a total revamp.
After a recent trip to Kelowna, B.C., it seems that city has a more effective model in place that I believe could work here for the betterment of all.
There are no tents in any of their public parks or spaces, but instead a specific place is set aside in a non-residential section of the city where they are permitted.
There in that same area as well are several portable toilets along with garbage containers and a running water.
I would suggest the city look at remediating some of the lands in the Caroline Street North/Stuart Street area or some other brownfield within city boundaries to set up a new way of tackling this societal issue that won't be going away any time soon.
With such a new approach, a security and an ongoing wellness check-in model will be needed until we can get this population of our fellow citizens housed with the proper supports where needed.
Interesting to note as well that Kelowna, with provincial help, also has 60 tiny homes in place, which is proving to be very effective in helping to combat the homelessness issue in their area.
Having worked with the homeless population off and on for 36 years in various cities across Canada, I know we can do better than what we are presently doing for all of the people in our area who find themselves living in tents.
Victor Cyr, Hamilton
Congratulations to the Oilers
Thank you to the Edmonton Oilers and their fans in the recent Stanley Cup final. With backs to the wall, down three games, you fought back, denying the Panthers an easy victory. You forced a Game 7 and just came up a little short. Nevertheless, you showed Canada, Canadians and hockey fans all over the globe that hockey is indeed a truly Canadian game with your grit and determination. Well done Oilers!
Mike LoSchiavo, Stoney Creek
Foreign aid worth supporting
Foreign aid is more than a hand out. These initiatives often focus on addressing global issues like climate change, health, pandemics and economic instability, which have direct repercussions for Canada. By contributing to global solutions, we protect our own interests and ensure a safer, more stable world, benefiting Hamilton and all of Canada.
Foreign aid also promotes Canadian values of compassion and humanitarianism. It reflects our commitment to a fair and just world, fostering goodwill and strengthening diplomatic ties. Hamiltonians can take pride in knowing our community contributes to these noble efforts, reinforcing our city's reputation for empathy and global citizenship.
It is not merely a charitable gesture, but a strategic investment in a better future for all. Its benefits are far-reaching, encompassing economic growth, international collaboration and the promotion of global stability - all of which positively impact Hamilton. Let us continue to support and advocate for robust foreign aid policies, recognizing that in helping others, we are also enhancing our own community's well-being.
Koko Sanginga, Hamilton
Schools need A/C for learning
Re: Do students really need A/C? (June 25)
How ironic that an educated person on climate change has written an article on the same page as an opinionated person who lacks climate change background and expresses that schools don't need air conditioning. The former provides valuable information of the effects of extreme heat listing health symptoms and the seriousness of heat stroke. The latter states her opinion. It is not based on knowledge or experience.
As an educator I have taught in elementary schools without air conditioning. This was before climate change and extreme temperatures recorded today. It's very difficult for children to learn when the room is stifling and there is no air movement. Indeed, as the day progresses and the room becomes even more of an oven, it is impossible. Lights can be turned off and children can put their head down for a break, but it doesn't improve the learning curve. They sweat, they smell and it becomes increasingly uncomfortable. They cannot perform to their best ability on any kind of learning task, especially on exams.
The second half of my career was in a school with air conditioning. The difference in the learning environment is extreme. It is conducive to academic activities, acquiring knowledge, tests and evaluations. There are many jobs where adults have to work without air conditioning; however, children and teens cannot have viable learning experiences in classrooms that are uncomfortably hot as the world feels the effects of climate change.
Jan Peirson-Reid, Carlisle

Document 399 of 500

Province seeks much smaller home for temporary Ontario Science Centre: 'No one is buying Ford's excuses for permanently closing,' NDP leader says

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/province-seeks-much-smaller-home-temporary/docview/3072640487/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Province+seeks+much+smaller+home+for+temporary+Ontario+Science+Centre%3A+%27No+one+is+buying+Ford%27s+excuses+for+permanently+closing%2C%27+NDP+leader+says&amp;title=The+Spectator&amp;issn=11899417&amp;date=2024-06-27&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.12&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Spectator&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Ontario is searching for a temporary home for the Ontario Science Centre at a fraction of the size of the building the province abruptly closed last week due to structural concerns.
Government officials announced midday Friday that the science centre would permanently close in its east Toronto location at the end of the day after the province received an engineering report on the state of the roof.
The government was already planning to relocate the science centre to the waterfront Ontario Place attraction, but that is not slated to open until 2028, so officials said they would look for a temporary space to house some of the programming and exhibits in the meantime.
Infrastructure Ontario has now released its request for proposals for retail or commercial space to accommodate a temporary Ontario Science Centre, and it is seeking about 50,000 to 100,000 square feet - much smaller than the original building's 568,000 square feet.
Opponents of the science centre relocation were already critical of the fact the new building at Ontario Place is set to be half the original building's size, and the temporary location would be less than half of that smaller facility.
"We have consistently termed the Ontario Place plan as a half-size, mini OSC," said Jason Ash, co-chair of the group Save Ontario's Science Centre.
"We would easily term this a micro Ontario Science Centre, and I would personally go so far as to say Premier (Doug) Ford is offering the people of Ontario a Chuck E. Cheese-size facility for the next four to five years."
NDP Leader Marit Stiles is among those urging the government to repair faulty roof panels rather than permanently close the whole building.
"No one is buying Ford's excuses for permanently closing our Science Centre; we all know it's about sweetening the deal for his luxury spa at Ontario Place," Stiles wrote in a statement.
Stiles and many others have been fighting a waterpark and spa by European company Therme planned for Ontario Place, and a government-commissioned business case on the science centre said relocating it to Ontario Place could help temper criticism of the project.
The government is seeking a start date of "no later than" Jan. 1, 2026, and is looking for a property centrally located.
The Canadian Press
CAPTION: Visitors take in an installation at the Ontario Science Centre in Toronto in May 2023. Opponents of the science centre relocation were already critical of the fact the new building at Ontario Place is set to be half the original building's size.Chris Young The Canadian Press file photo

CREDIT: Allison Jones The Canadian Press

MPP has support of residents as she sounds off against council: Haldimand councillors ratify a motion to bar MPP Bobbi Ann Brady from provincial meetings

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/mpp-has-support-residents-as-she-sounds-off/docview/3072638870/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=MPP+has+support+of+residents+as+she+sounds+off+against+council%3A+Haldimand+councillors+ratify+a+motion+to+bar+MPP+Bobbi+Ann+Brady+from+provincial+meetings&amp;title=The+Spectator&amp;issn=11899417&amp;date=2024-06-27&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.5&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Spectator&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Haldimand councillors have ratified a motion asking Haldimand-Norfolk MPP Bobbi Ann Brady to avoid meetings with county officials and provincial ministers at the Association of Municipalities of Ontario (AMO) conference in Ottawa this August.
At a June 24 council meeting, councillors heard public delegations, including the MPP herself, urging them to change course. But councillors approved the original motion - moved by Coun. Stewart Patterson - directing staff to draft a letter to Brady indicating the meetings are considered private and that council does not wish her to attend.
Haldimand Mayor Shelley Ann Bentley and Coun. Marie Trainer voted to rescind the motion at Monday's council meeting, but were outvoted by councillors Stewart Patterson, Dan Lawrence, Patrick O'Neill, Rob Shirton and John Metcalfe.
In a delegation to council, Brady said she refuses to be silenced.
"Today I am here to defend democracy and to stand up for the people who are tired of party politics and special interests suppressing political representation," she said. "Motion after motion against me can be brought up if you wish, but you will not silence me. I will not back down."
Brady added she spoke positively with provincial Infrastructure Minister Kinga Surma during the minister's visit to Norfolk in May and maintains a solid working relationship with the government despite her previous criticism of Tory policies.
"If you had your wish for me to sit in silence, then I ask if you believe in democracy," said Brady.
In closing, Brady said her job is to represent the 110,000 people of Haldimand-Norfolk and not the five councillors who voted to block her from AMO delegations.
"For those of you that are headed to Ottawa in August to AMO, I will see you there," she said.
Patterson said the motion isn't a personal attack against Brady.
"This motion was moved so our municipal council keeps the focus on municipal affairs and removes any provincial political debates," Patterson said, citing Brady's past criticism of the provincial Tories and some heated exchanges in the legislature between Brady and Premier Doug Ford.
"Our council supported my motion to keep this delegation purely municipal in order to keep the focus on issues at hand rather than on provincial political manoeuvring."
Those who spoke in support of Brady called the motion an affront to democracy.
"Going forward, I do not support this motion and I am embarrassed that it was considered and passed," Bentley said. "We should be pleased that our MPP represents Haldimand residents and wants to be involved in municipal matters."
Bentley said Brady was part of an impromptu meeting with provincial Housing Minister Paul Calandra during the last Rural Ontario Municipal Association Conference in January that helped the county secure official plan approval that had been delaying an affordable housing project in Dunnville.
Hagersville resident Beverly Petheram also called the motion undemocratic.
"Your actions go against our wishes. Your actions appear to the citizens of Haldimand as politically excluding, shunning and silencing the MPP from delegations, which is unacceptable and highly inappropriate," Petheram told councillors.
Petheram asked councillors to apologize to Brady and called for councillors to receive refresher training on inclusivity and gender bias.
"Our public display of disrespect to our MPP is insulting and embarrassing," Petheram added. "Instead of casting blame on our MPP for different matters, I urge you to focus on collaboration and constructive dialogue, versus divisiveness."
In voting to support the original motion, Coun. Patrick O'Neill said the county must present "a strong united front" at the AMO conference, especially as Haldimand, Norfolk and Six Nations petition the province for hundreds of millions of dollars for a new water system that would connect the three areas.
"I believe that we, as elected Haldimand County councillors, have a better chance of getting the funding for this infrastructure project without our MPP's presence and therefore I will be doing what's best for my constituents and supporting the motion requesting that our MPP not attend the delegations," O'Neill said.
In addition to the tri-party water project, Haldimand councillors have also requested Ministry of Transportation delegations for a new traffic light at Haldibrook Road and Highway 6 in Caledonia; a Highway 6 bypass through Hagersville due to current and future growth projections; and replacement of the Argyle Street bridge in Caledonia. A delegation request to the Ministry of Long-Term Care also seeks funding for the expansion of the Edgewater Gardens long-term care home in Dunnville.
CAPTION: Haldimand council voted Monday to direct staff to draft a letter to Haldimand-Norfolk MPP Bobbi Ann Brady, seen here during an Ontario Trillium Foundation funding announcement at Birds Canada in Port Rowan last fall, requesting she not attend meetings between Haldimand County and the provincial government at an upcoming conference.Jeff Tribe Hamilton Spectator file photo

CREDIT: Mike Pearson Reporter
