Full text: Sidelining MPP
is outrageous
Re: Why is this provincially elected representative not
allowed at meetings with
the province? (June 24)
Absolutely disgusting that our local MPP Bobbi Ann Brady has been barred from attending meetings between Haldimand County and Doug Ford's government. She fought hard for this job and was elected. She represents all the people in Haldimand and has our best interests at heart and should not be banned by a few councillors who disagree with her. As for the few councillors who approved this outrageous motion, that won't be forgotten next election.
Joy Stewart, Dunnville
A disappointing
response
The encampments are destroying our downtown, just when Hamilton was getting close to being the "Ambitious City" that it has been purported to be. For 15 years, I have lived in Ward 2 and experienced a blossoming and rejuvenation that was wonderful to see. But things are changing and at the recent general issues committee council meeting, many Ward 2 and 3 residents communicated the new and rising problems with garbage, noise, theft and insecurity that come from laissez-faire governance of the encampments.
Front and centre is the very real issue of a drug addiction and mental health epidemic and how it has hit our community. Where, also, is the provincial government to provide the services these people need? We don't need booze on every corner; we need help. And what do our self-serving councillors do just in time for Canada Day? They are moving all tents from Bayfront Park to Strachan, a location that caused a huge backlash from North End residents last year. A great city is only a sum of its many parts and I am incredibly disappointed that our representatives opted to think only of themselves and not of the health of the city as a whole.
Marta Stiteler, Hamilton
Provide shelter for those in need
Re: City dragging its feet
on homelessness (June 24)
Regarding the human beings who are less a home in Hamilton, every day I tiredly ask "why?" Why are we allowing things to deteriorate so badly?
If we don't want to see homeless people in tents, make some type of inexpensive homes for them to live in ASAP. There sits a handful of wonderful "tiny homes" unused due to squabbles about zones and NIMBYism. Is that ridiculous or what? It's also unintelligent.
Don't want to see their clothes, belongings and garbage? Give them a home, any type of safe structure to live in. We know they will be grateful.
Don't want needles and other drug paraphernalia in the parks where children play? Make a place of their own in a simple structure, very far away from the parks.
Don't want some of them at every street corner begging for change? Give them a small safe space to live immediately with enough money for food, etc. It certainly won't eliminate all panhandling, but will reduce it significantly.
For the millionth time, I have just stated what thousands upon thousands have already said. Any human less a home, is not going to be a healthy productive one. Why are we allowing homelessness to grow?
We don't have to have both intelligence and compassion; just pick one and there is a solution before our eyes every day. The solution is to provide safe homes for homeless people. What's Hamilton going to choose to do?
Ana Long, Stoney Creek
Emulate the
Kelowna model
It's clear after a trial period that the city's present encampment policy is not working and needs a total revamp.
After a recent trip to Kelowna, B.C., it seems that city has a more effective model in place that I believe could work here for the betterment of all.
There are no tents in any of their public parks or spaces, but instead a specific place is set aside in a non-residential section of the city where they are permitted.
There in that same area as well are several portable toilets along with garbage containers and a running water.
I would suggest the city look at remediating some of the lands in the Caroline Street North/Stuart Street area or some other brownfield within city boundaries to set up a new way of tackling this societal issue that won't be going away any time soon.
With such a new approach, a security and an ongoing wellness check-in model will be needed until we can get this population of our fellow citizens housed with the proper supports where needed.
Interesting to note as well that Kelowna, with provincial help, also has 60 tiny homes in place, which is proving to be very effective in helping to combat the homelessness issue in their area.
Having worked with the homeless population off and on for 36 years in various cities across Canada, I know we can do better than what we are presently doing for all of the people in our area who find themselves living in tents.
Victor Cyr, Hamilton
Schools need
A/C for learning
Re: Do students really need A/C? (June 25)
How ironic that an educated person on climate change has written an article on the same page as an opinionated person who lacks climate change background and expresses that schools don't need air conditioning. The former provides valuable information of the effects of extreme heat listing health symptoms and the seriousness of heat stroke. The latter states her opinion. It is not based on knowledge or experience.
As an educator I have taught in elementary schools without air conditioning. This was before climate change and extreme temperatures recorded today.
It's very difficult for children to learn when the room is stifling and there is no air movement. Indeed, as the day progresses and the room becomes even more of an oven, it is impossible. Lights can be turned off and children can put their head down for a break, but it doesn't improve the learning curve. They sweat, they smell and it becomes increasingly uncomfortable. They cannot perform to their best ability on any kind of learning task, especially on exams.
The second half of my career was in a school with air conditioning. The difference in the learning environment is extreme. It is conducive to academic activities, acquiring knowledge, tests and evaluations. There are many jobs where adults have to work without air conditioning; however, children and teens cannot have viable learning experiences in classrooms that are uncomfortably hot as the world feels the effects of climate change.
Jan Peirson-Reid, Carlisle
Congratulations to the Oilers
Thank you to the Edmonton Oilers and their fans in the recent Stanley Cup final. With backs to the wall, down three games, you fought back, denying the Panthers an easy victory. You forced a Game 7 and just came up a little short. Nevertheless, you showed Canada, Canadians and hockey fans all over the globe that hockey is indeed a truly Canadian game with your grit and determination. Well done Oilers!
Mike LoSchiavo,
Stoney Creek
Not buying the heat pump hype
Re: Here's a low-cost emission reduction action for Hamilton (June 25)
Yet another article extolling the environmental benefits of cold air heat pumps versus gas furnaces. If I understand correctly, I am supposed to replace my perfectly good 95 per cent efficient gas furnace with an air source heat pump that admittedly is more efficient when the temperatures are above freezing, but whose efficiency declines as the temperature decreases.
Oh, and on those days when the heat pump is used, how is the electricity produced? Gas-fired generators. Not to mention the waste of tossing perfectly good equipment.
Fortunately those of us who understand the details of energy production in Ontario aren't wasting our money on this nonsense.
Richard Ronchka, Carlisle

Document 402 of 500

Simple science and complex politics: Some refuse to believe science centre should be closed, despite safety concerns or declining attendance

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/simple-science-complex-politics/docview/3072638693/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=unknown&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Simple+science+and+complex+politics%3A+Some+refuse+to+believe+science+centre+should+be+closed%2C+despite+safety+concerns+or+declining+attendance&amp;title=The+Spectator&amp;issn=11899417&amp;date=2024-06-27&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.11&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Spectator&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Follow the science at the Ontario Science Centre.
But beware the dubious political science over the Ontario Place controversy.
By suddenly closing off the aging science centre to the public, citing a safety scare at the aging facility, Doug Ford reignited every possible conspiracy theory.
Behold his corrupt motives, the critics cry: a scam and a sham!
So is this really a ruse to cover up the premier's scheme to relocate the science centre to Ontario Place while enriching his friends? That reaction, so soon after the deception and bungling over last year's Greenbelt boondoggle, is understandable and predictable. But disregarding an engineering report that warns of an imminent roof collapse would be incomprehensible. To do so would be to assume that an entire government - not just the politicians but the civil servants - is putting political calculations ahead of civil engineering.
Surely it is possible for a politician who is sometimes dishonest to be honest some of the time. Just as it's entirely possible for the critics to be intellectually dishonest at times.
Much of the incredulity over the sudden closing of the science centre comes from people who refuse to believe it should ever be closed - whether it's the risk of a collapsing roof or the reality of declining attendance amid structural decline. Much of the opposition to relocating the science centre to the old Ontario Place site comes from waterfront minimalists who want more of the same green space for the personal enjoyment of privileged downtown residents.
I've long argued - long before the Ford government came onside - that the best way to revitalize the waterfront was with an ambitious redesign that relocated a neglected Don Mills science centre to a reinvigorated Ontario Place. I watched the last Liberal government shutter Ontario Place (barely noticed), all the while neglecting the Ontario Science Centre (barely a peep).
To its credit, the Ford government dusted off previous reports - long ignored by the Liberals - that recommended a strong anchor tenant for a waterfront project built not on nostalgia but a future vision, noting that Singapore had similarly relied on a cutting edge science exhibition.
To be sure, Ford's proposal for a foreign-owned spa with an over-the-top plan for underground parking made it a harder sell with a distinctive smell. The proposed spa rubs people the wrong way.
I'm not a spa person, but if you think of it as a water park by another name, alongside an ambitious science centre attraction, against the backdrop of a beautifully landscaped Ontario Place setting, it will be a crowd-pleaser. Notwithstanding the naysayers.
Against that backdrop, I see the engineering report (and the government's response) not as a contrived scenario but a predictable outcome.
But back to the politics. And the science.
People assume that Ford is pulling a fast one to shutter the science centre before anyone can stop him. That's a misreading of his deal-making with an unlikely ally, Toronto's Mayor Olivia Chow, to plunge full speed ahead with the relocation.
When the two politicians shook hands last year on an entente to shore up Toronto's financing, Chow agreed to get out of the way - politically and legally. It's already a done deal, so the last thing Ford needed was to trigger a predictable public outcry by moving up the closing last week.
As for the science, it has become urban lore that the Don Mills centre is on par with the pyramids, destined to endure forever. Designed by famed Canadian architect Raymond Moriyama, time has not been kind to the structure that dates to 1969 - nor to its aging exhibits.
In fact, the 52-page Rimkus Consulting Group engineering report, released last week, found the air-filled concrete used by Moriyama in construction - similar to that in more than 100 now-closed British schools built in the same era - is nearing the end of its life. The report, which will be peer-reviewed for a second opinion by another engineering firm, said the risk of rain or snow buildup means the reinforced steel underneath could further corrode and fail.
Engineers quantify risk. So do politicians.
If a collapsed roof killed members of the public, our judgment of the collective failing would be scathing. It was a decade ago, after a rooftop parking lot crumbled at an Elliot Lake mall, and the judicial inquiry blamed "its designers and builders, its owners, some architects and engineers, as well as the municipal and provincial officials charged with the duty of protecting the public."
It's easy to criticize the "provincial officials" - not just the premier - who have publicly defended the closure of the Ontario Science Centre. But when lives are on the line, they have a duty to do the right thing - even if it's not necessarily a politically easy thing. Follow the science. Mind the politics.
Martin Regg-Cohn covers provincial politics and
international affairs
for Torstar.
CAPTION: The reaction to the Ontario Science Centre closure is understandable and predictable. But disregarding an engineering report that warns of an imminent roof collapse would be incomprehensible, Martin Regg Cohn writes.Andrew Francis WallaceToronto Star file photo

CREDIT: Martin Regg cohn OPINION Martin Regg-Cohn covers provincial politics and international affairs for Torstar.;

Document 403 of 500

Ontario launches automatic licence plate renewals on Monday

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/ontario-launches-automatic-licence-plate-renewals/docview/3072877256/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Ontario+launches+automatic+licence+plate+renewals+on+Monday&amp;title=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-06-26&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Premier Doug Ford is finally eliminating the need to renew licence plates - four-and-a-half months after the Star revealed there were one million expired plates on Ontario roads.
As of Monday, plate renewal will be automatic for all passenger vehicles unless motorists owe provincial or municipal traffic fines, or have unpaid Highway 407 tolls.
Scofflaws owing fines or tolls will be notified by mail or via email or text that they must pay their penalties or their plates will not be renewed.
"Under the leadership of Premier Ford, our government is making life more affordable and convenient for drivers," said Transportation Minister Prabmeet Sarkaria.
"First, we eliminated licence plate sticker fees for passenger vehicles, saving drivers $120 a year for every car they own. Now we're saving drivers valuable time and making their lives easier by automatically renewing licence plates," he said Wednesday.
While Ford scrapped the renewal fee in the run-up to the June 2022 provincial election - at a cost to the treasury of more than $1 billion - the onus was still on vehicle owners to keep their plates up to date or face Highway Traffic Act fines of as much as $1,000.
That fuelled confusion among motorists, many of whom were unaware they had to take any action.
In February, the Star found that more than one million of the 13 million plates in Ontario had expired, which led to a surge in charges by police.
Under the changes that take effect July 1, plates for passenger vehicles, light-duty trucks, motorcycles and mopeds will renew automatically 90 days before expiry - if the owner has valid insurance and no outstanding levies.
In the event the Ministry of Transportation cannot renew the plate, the holder will be contacted and required to submit their updated information online or in person at a ServiceOntario outlet.
Plates that are set to expire between now and Monday still must be manually renewed online for free at ontario.ca/platerenewal.
Public and Business Services Minister Todd McCarthy said "by utilizing innovative technological solutions to streamline and improve government services, we are saving Ontarians precious time and money."
But the Star reported two weeks ago that a Progressive Conservative pledge from 2020 to include Ontario drivers' licences and health cards on smartphone digital wallets is "on the back burner" due to political and privacy concerns.
Some Tory supporters are bristling at having government-issued identification added to Apple Wallets or Google Wallets - even though the province's COVID-19 vaccination cards were digitized in 2021.
"They're worried about being tracked (by the government), so privacy was an issue," a Tory insider confided earlier this month.
According to the Ontario Provincial Police, Highway Traffic Act charges skyrocketed after paid renewal stickers were phased out two years ago.
In 2023, there were 15,631 tickets issued to motorists lacking a "currently validated permit for the vehicle" compared with 5,341 charges in 2022 and 1,556 in 2021 - although pandemic restrictions could have been a factor.
CAPTION: Ontario vehicle owners will have their licence plates renewed automatically - so long as they don't have any outstanding fines or toll road charges - as of July 1.

Subject: Fines & penalties; Automobiles; Automobile drivers; Privacy; Roads & highways; Vehicles

'Poor living conditions' in Ontario's overcrowded jails top complaints to ombudsman

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/poor-living-conditions-ontarios-overcrowded-jails/docview/3072877220/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=%27Poor+living+conditions%27+in+Ontario%27s+overcrowded+jails+top+complaints+to+ombudsman&amp;title=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-06-26&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Concerns about "poor living conditions" in Ontario's overcrowded jails - including rodents - topped complaints to the provincial ombudsman last year.
In his annual report, Paul Dubé said complaints from citizens about government services including child welfare, delays at the Landlord and Tenant Board and supports to developmentally disabled adults and other issues were up 10 per cent to 27,030 in the 12 months ending March 31.
"The systems are struggling," the independent officer of the legislature told a news conference Wednesday. "Urgent attention is needed."
In jails, complaints centred on medical care, overcrowding, solitary confinement and use of force by correctional officers, prompting Dubé and members of his team to visit the Sudbury Jail, Maplehurst Correctional Complex in Milton, the Vanier Centre for Women and the Hamilton-Wentworth Detention Centre.
For example, "poor living conditions and a mouse infestation" were the subject of discussions with management at the Sudbury Jail while overcrowding at the Vanier Centre led to complaints of "no access to services, including showers, due to construction projects," the ombudsman wrote in his 104-page report.
Several shortfalls on medical care in jails were highlighted.
In one case, an inmate transferred to a new jail did not get his regular prescription medication for three weeks until the ombudsman's team got involved.
Dubé would not comment on whether the push for bail reform from Premier Doug Ford and others would worsen overcrowding in jails, but said of inmates, "most of them are awaiting trial and are presumed innocent."
Despite regulations making it unlawful for jails to put inmates known to have "serious mental illness" in segregation, complaints about this happening continue to come in.
"We are making inquiries with facilities and senior ministry officials about these cases, and about how the ministry plans to comply with the regulation."
The problems cited in the report show Ford's Progressive Conservative government has a lot of work to do on improving services and oversight, said Green Leader Mike Schreiner.
"The premier's disregard for the people of this province shows up everywhere," Schreiner said.
In the 104-page report, Dubé points to a number of disturbing cases where his staff has intervened.
They include a young man who missed four months of payouts because of bureaucratic delays in transferring his file to the Ontario Disability Support Program from the Assistance for Children with Severe Disabilities Program. He was granted $4,500 in retroactive benefits.
When a youth complained about "irregular and insufficient" meals at his group home, government inspectors were sent in to order improvements.
As part of a soon-to-be-completed investigation into adults with developmental disabilities who are inappropriately housed in hospitals due to a lack of support in the community, Dubé's staff is reviewing the case of a 22-year-old man with autism and a history of self-injury.
He had been in hospital for three years, "spending much of that time in restraints." The ombudsman's office flagged his situation to senior bureaucrats and a home was found for him in the community where he has "adjusted well," the report said.
While the Landlord and Tenant Board now has 133 adjudicators, up from 78 a year ago, Dubé noted it is "still grappling" with a backlog of cases as renters and owners in disputes seek justice.
CAPTION: Maplehurst Correctional Complex is a correctional facility located in Milton.

Subject: Prison overcrowding; Prisons

Title: 'Poor living conditions' in Ontario's overcrowded jails top complaints to ombudsman

Document 405 of 500

Whether or not Justin Trudeau should quit now is the wrong question. Just ask Kathleen Wynne

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/whether-not-justin-trudeau-should-quit-now-is/docview/3072877212/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Whether+or+not+Justin+Trudeau+should+quit+now+is+the+wrong+question.+Just+ask+Kathleen+Wynne&amp;title=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-06-26&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Should Justin Trudeau stay or should he go?
Kathleen Wynne has been there, done that - and didn't go.
After a bitter byelection defeat for the federal Liberals in Toronto-St. Paul's riding this week, Wynne has been asking herself the same question anew. As premier, she stayed to fight another campaign in 2018, paying a high price in the election defeat that followed.
So what would Wynne tell the prime minister if he called for advice about whether he should quit federal politics?
Good question. A better question for him, she argues, is why exactly he should stay.
The calculations aren't as simple as they sound. All these years after her own ups and downs, Wynne says a bad byelection result, even in a traditionally Liberal riding, is not "a line in the sand."
That's because a byelection can mean everything and nothing - or something in between. Typically, they are protest votes - and rarely predictive of a general election outcome.
History also suggests that changing leaders is no panacea for unpopular premiers or prime ministers. After Wynne left, the Ontario Liberals under Steven Del Duca lost badly again to Doug Ford; after Brian Mulroney quit as PM in 1993, his successor Kim Campbell was wiped out; same with Pierre Trudeau's successor, John Turner, in 1984.
Justin Trudeau insisted this week he's sticking it out until the next election. But that doesn't mean he can just keep saying - and doing - more of the same.
"I think what's going to happen now is there's going to be a lot of internal soul-searching," Wynne told me. Over the next few weeks, MPs will be "trying to figure out now how to have the tough conversation with him," because "he's going to have to make a decision."
Not necessarily an epiphany to go away, but a pathway to stay and fight another day.
"There is going to be such pressure on him now - not to leave so much as come up with a reason that he should stay."
Wynne predicts Liberal MPs across the country will be going door to door this summer hearing their constituents say, "We love you, we hate your leader."
That's what happened to Wynne in her last years, and it's the fate befalling Trudeau now.
"I lived through that and it's tough."
MPs have to decide whether they can stand it. And the PM must determine if he can withstand it.
But there are other calculations behind his personal unpopularity: Who comes next? In what context?
"I'm worried about who's in the wings that's ready to take this on," Wynne muses. "Anybody who's been in his cabinet is going to be seriously marked by what's gone on."
That weighed heavily on her own decision to stay on - wondering whether her successor would be handed a poisoned chalice. The calculation any incumbent must make is whether they are better placed to win - or worst placed to lose.
Back then, I thought if Wynne could reintroduce herself to voters and somehow rehabilitate her image, she had a better chance to eke out a narrow victory in 2018 than any of the other pretenders to the throne. But as readers know, I'm always wrong - and as it turned out, the downside risk of an unloved leader was a massive loss on voting day.
Similarly, Trudeau may be the best bet for a longshot Liberal minority victory in the federal election due in late 2025. But by virtue of the personal hostility he engenders - akin to Wynne - he could very well be the worst bet if things don't go their way, leading to even bigger losses.
That's the dilemma: Go for broke by staying put, or hedge your bets by bailing out.
Another complication weighing on the minds of Liberals as they contemplate a wide open leadership race: These are turbulent times in the Middle East and at home, with bitter divisions on both sides that could divide the party further if new members are motivated by a single issue - choosing someone to lead Canada into the future based on an intractable historical conflict in Israel, Gaza and Lebanon.
Party nominations and leadership races are messy affairs at the best of times. In the worst of times, they are prone to piling on by one large group or another.
"It's going to bring out the factions, and I think that's really dangerous - I don't think it bodes well for choosing the right person to bring people together," she says.
Wynne agonized over her own decision to stay on as premier in 2018, and is reliving some of that now - joking about how the talk of Trudeau's future is triggering a form of political PTSD from her past.
"My heart has gone out to Justin all day - I've just been thinking about him, what a tough place he's in."
Been there, felt that.
CAPTION: Prime Minister Justin Trudeau and then-Ontario Premier Kathleen Wynne greet each other at an event in Toronto in 2017.

Subject: Leadership; Prime ministers

Business indexing term: Subject: Leadership

People: Trudeau, Justin; Wynne, Kathleen

Document 406 of 500

How two Toronto tailors designed custom suits for four NBA draft prospects - including our Zach Edey

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/how-two-toronto-tailors-designed-custom-suits/docview/3072640486/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=How+two+Toronto+tailors+designed+custom+suits+for+four+NBA+draft+prospects+-+including+our+Zach+Edey&amp;title=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-06-26&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: For the past three months, two Toronto tailors have been designing suits for four NBA prospects taking centre stage in the 2024 NBA draft tonight.
Nota Bene Bespoke, run by business partners Shana Uthaya and Trevis Villafana, is the company behind the suits worn by Duke's Kyle Filipowski, Kansas' Johnny Furphy, Kentucky's Justin Edwards and Toronto's own Zach Edey.
Ahead of the NBA draft, the Star got a peek behind the magic of how the local tailor company created the suits for four players headed to the NBA.
The NBA draft has become one of the most talked-about fashion events in sports. It's where future stars get to make their mark and introduce themselves to the world while dressing to impress.
Draft night fashion has gone down in history, from Raptors guard Gradey Dick making headlines last year for his "Wizard of Oz''-inspired red suit to LeBron James' classic all-white outfit from 2003.
Duke's Kyle Filipowski and Kansas' Johnny Furphy walk the red carpet at the 2024 NBA draft at Barclays Arena in Brooklyn tonight, wearing custom-made suits designed by Nota Bene Bespoke. Filipowski and Furphy received an invite to the NBA draft green room, where soon-to-be drafted players are invited with family to hear their name called by the NBA's commissioner.
Furphy went for a royal blue, single-breasted peak lapel tuxedo with a James Bond-esque look, with a black bow tie. "Johnny Bond" is the nickname Uthaya and Villafana stamped for Furphy while designing his suit.
"I wanted to keep it simple, nothing too crazy, nothing in your face. The idea was sharp but calm," Furphy told the Star.
When brainstorming his look for draft night, Furphy, a 19-year-old from Melbourne, Australia, said he wanted to show love to his country. So the suit's inner lining includes a map outline of Australia pinpointing Melbourne with a yellow star. It also pays homage to every Australian basketball player that has been drafted to the NBA.
"There haven't been many Australians in the position I am now," said Furphy. "It's cool to be a part of the small group of guys that have been in the same position."
The names of Australian NBA players including Ben Simmons, Joe Ingles, Dante Exum, Matthew Dellavedova and Mattise Thybulle are included in the suit's lining. Adding to the growing list of Australian-drafted players, Furphy's name is highlighted in green.
Every detail and every seam was carefully considered by the tailors. "We want the guys to be able to wear [these suits] a decade from now," said Uthaya. "We put in extra fabric because your body won't be the same 10 years from now."
Duke's Filipowski is wearing an emerald green suit with a floral pattern. It's double-breasted and accented with gold buttons and a colourful paisley pocket square. The fabric on his suit is rare, a one-of-one Vitale Barberis Canonico textile made in 1663.
The inner lining of Filipowski's suit has his nickname "Flip" repeated in silver on the left side, while on the right is an image of him hooping at Duke. His full last name is showcased on the back of the lining with the words "Welcome to the league."
"What I love so much about it is that it's very me," said Filipowski. "It's a little bit more than how I usually express myself but it's not too much."
The seven-foot man doesn't often express himself through clothing, but for draft night, he thought it was important to come out of his comfort zone. "I think they did a tremendous job. They executed my vision perfectly and they were just trying to make me the most happiest I can be with it and most comfortable," said Filipowski. "I never thought I could be so attached to a piece of clothing."
When Filipowski saw the final version of his suit ahead of the draft, he couldn't stop smiling. His partner got emotional seeing him in it. "Getting to this moment and her being such a focal point in getting here ... it's going to be a memory that I have and a very special one for me for the rest of my life," said Filipowski.
While he won't be in New York, Kentucky's Edwards went for a classy, timeless look for the draft. His suit is a custom deep black tuxedo made with a blend of 80 per cent silk and 20 per cent wool with silk sparkle on the top. The black pattern of Edward's suit is known as jacquard, invented by the French textile artisan Joseph Marie Jacquard in 1804.
When Villafana met with Edwards at the NBA draft combine, the player had one ask for his suit: to find a way to honour his mother. A very family-oriented guy, Edwards grew up in a single-parent household with two younger siblings.
On Edwards' inner lining, there is a photo of his mother hugging him from a photo shoot in the Philadelphia Inquirer. The lining also features the area code "215" as a shoutout to his hometown of Philly, and an action shot of him playing college basketball in Kentucky.
Edey is by far Nota Bene Bespoke's largest client in stature. The seven-foot-four, 300-pound big man born and raised in Toronto received an invite to the NBA draft green room but instead decided to host a watch party with family, friends and teammates in Purdue, where he went to college. Edey is expected to wear the suit created by Nota Bene Bespoke for his first press conference with his new team after the draft.
Edey's agent called the Toronto company to request a suit for the draft, and in late April, the tailors flew to Chicago to meet with Edey to brainstorm, present him with potential mock-ups and take measurements.
In the fitting process, Edey's size left the tailors in awe. He had the longest wingspan they'd ever seen; his seven-foot-10 wingspan took up the room. The back of his jacket was about 35 inches; a typical suit would be about 10 inches shorter.
"I shook his hand and right away I was like, 'Man, I gotta use a ladder. There's no way we're going to be able to do this,'" said Uthaya, who took about 30 measurements of Edey's entire body.
While doing the fitting, they chatted about college basketball and the ongoing Drake vs. Kendrick Lamar beef, and laughed about rivalries at the high schools they attended in Toronto.
"He's probably the biggest Canadian [athlete] in terms of size, to be drafted in the NBA. And he's reached out to a small business in Toronto to do his look. That's really important for us," said Uthaya.
Uthaya and Villafana have known each other since they were kids. Before becoming business partners, they grew up playing competitive soccer against each other.
Uthaya started the business in 2011 while studying at McMaster University. For the first few years, his clientele consisted of friends and family who trusted his eye for fashion. Villafana was a bar manager at the time on Ossington and Uthaya would often stop by; they'd talk about the work he was doing growing his business. Villafana's grandmother used to make wedding dresses so tailoring is in his blood, and he was interested in working with Uthaya.
Soon, Villafana started helping Uthaya with styling and doing trade shows. Nota Bene means "attention" or "take notice" in Italian, and people certainly did. Their business grew as they started getting wedding referrals and clients on Bay Street, and word-of-mouth spread. That's how the business manager for former Raptor Danny Green heard about them and soon they were fitting the 16-year NBA veteran during the Raptors championship season.
The sports world started to take notice of their work. Uthaya and Villafana went from childhood soccer rivals to eventually suiting up Brazilian soccer star Richarlison de Andrade, designing his suit ahead of the 2022 FIFA World Cup in Qatar. They've since worked with Jannik Sinner, the No. 1 ranked tennis player in the world, Atiba Hutchinson from Canada soccer as well as NFL and NHL players.
But their tallest task was creating the perfect suit for Edey. His main request was to highlight two things: that he's Canadian and Purdue, where he dominated college basketball, racking up almost every accolade possible.
After they met with Edey in Chicago, Filipowski, Furphy and Edwards also jumped on board as clients.
The Toronto tailors had about four weeks to make Edey's suit come alive. There were a lot of sleepless nights, as they wanted it to be perfect. A week and a half before the NBA draft, they travelled back to Chicago to show Edey the final version: A custom-made black, double-breasted, peak lapel suit. The detailing has micro diamonds with a black sparkle; the fabric is from Loro Piana, one of the most luxurious mills in the world. There's a silver crown design on some of the buttons.
The inner lining of the suit has the Purdue Boilermakers logo stamped across it, just as Edey requested. And if you look closely, the Canadian maple leaf is displayed in the design.
When Edey put on his suit, he did a runway walk through his agency's office in sunglasses, to cheers. Nota Bene Bespoke's work was complete.
"For two guys from Toronto - I'm from Scarborough, Trev's from East York - to come in and be a part of such a big American event, not just one guy in the draft but four guys," said Uthaya, "it's meaningful."
CAPTION: Kyle Filipowski, flanked by the Nota Bene Bespoke tailors.Nota Bene Bespoke's tailor Shana Uthaya shows the outline of Australia inside the suit of Johnny Furphy, who is from Melbourne.Johnny Furphy walks the red carpet before the 2024 NBA draft.The custom-made lining inside the suit of NBA prospect draft pick Kyle "Flip" Filipowski.Kyle Filipowski in his custom Nota Bene Bespoke suit on the NBA 2024 draft red carpet.Nota Bene Bespoke's tailor Travis Villafana with the suit for NBA prospect draft pick Justin Edwards.Nota Bene Bespoke's tailors Travis Villafana (left) and Shana Uthaya show Zach Edey's wingspan as they hold the suit shirt they made for him.Nota Bene Bespoke's Toronto tailors Trevis Villafana (right) and Shana Uthaya with the custom-made suits they created for NBA draft prospect picks (left to right) Zach Edey, Johnny Furphy and Kyle "Flip" Filipowski.ONLINE AND PRINT RIGHTS PURCHASED - INVOICE #5119 - TORONTO, ONT.: JUN 22, 2024 - Nota Bene Bespoke's tailors Travis Villafana (right) and Shana Uthaya are photographed with the custom made suits for NBA draft prospect picks (left to right) Zach Edey, Johnny Furphy, and Kyle "Flip" Filipowski, at Nota Bene Bespoke atelier, on Saturday, June 22, 2024, in Toronto, Ontario. (Giovanni Capriotti)

Document 407 of 500

'Time-saving': Ontario Premier Doug Ford government introducing automatic licence plate renewals on Canada Day July 1 and people are reacting online

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/time-saving-ontario-premier-doug-ford-government/docview/3072638874/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=%27Time-saving%27%3A+Ontario+Premier+Doug+Ford+government+introducing+automatic+licence+plate+renewals+on+Canada+Day+July+1+and+people+are+reacting+online&amp;title=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-06-26&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Ontario Premier Doug Ford and his government say they are "the first jurisdiction" in North America to introduce automatic licence plate renewals. The change takes effect on July 1 and will save vehicle owners time, amounting to more than 900,000 hours every year, the government said.
"First, we eliminated licence plate sticker fees for passenger vehicles, saving drivers $120 a year for every car they own," said Transportation Minister Prabmeet Sarkaria. "Now, we're saving drivers valuable time and making their lives easier by automatically renewing licence plates."
Licence plates for passenger vehicles, light-duty trucks, motorcycles and mopeds will renew automatically 90 days before expiry if the vehicle owner has valid insurance and no outstanding fines or tolls, the government said in a news release.
If a licence plate cannot be renewed automatically, the vehicle owner will be notified and required to manually renew online or in person at ServiceOntario.
Until automatic licence plate renewals take effect, vehicle owners are required to manually renew their plates at no cost, the government said. Ontario drivers can check their licence plate status at ontario.ca/platerenewal.
The reaction online to this news, announced on Wednesday (June 26), has been mixed.
"That's great. It saves a lot of time to process along with the cost," Daniel Cardoso wrote on Facebook. "Now how about getting rid of the front license plates? Quebec has already done that so far."
Bill Marr called the automatic licence plate renewal "a great idea," but also wondered "why not just scrap the renewal all together?"
Joel Smith wrote on social media that the new program is "awesome."
"One of the easiest ways to save time and confusion is to change the default settings in the law," he wrote. "Changes like these are so user-friendly."
CAPTION: Ontario Premier Doug Ford and his government areintroducing automatic licence plate renewals as of July 1.

Subject: Automobiles; Renewals; Social networks; Vehicles

Title: 'Time-saving': Ontario Premier Doug Ford government introducing automatic licence plate renewals on Canada Day July 1 and people are reacting online

Public will have input if GRCA sells off land for housing

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/public-will-have-input-if-grca-sells-off-land/docview/3072281517/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Public+will+have+input+if+GRCA+sells+off+land+for+housing&amp;title=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-06-26&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: If the Grand River Conservation Authority decides to sell any land for new housing developments the public will be consulted before decisions are made, says North Dumfries Mayor Sue Foxton.
Foxton and other members of the conservation authority board will hear a presentation from the authority's chief administrative officer Friday morning about the recently completed inventory of all lands owned by the GRCA in the Grand River watershed.
The inventory and report prepared by Samantha Lawson, the conservation authority's chief administrative officer, alarmed environmentalists for two reasons - it says there are seven parcels of land that are suitable for new housing or infrastructure developments, and there is no requirement to make the inventory public.
Foxton, who has sat on the GRCA board for 10 years, said there is no cause or concern at this point. The inventory was completed under orders from Premier Doug Ford's Conservative government at Queen's Park. Every conservation authority in the province must complete a full inventory of land holdings by Dec. 31, 2024.
If there is any move to sell one or more of the seven parcels for new housing developments, the locations will be made public and the GRCA will hear from those who support or oppose the move, said Foxton.
"We will wait and see how things fall out," said Foxton. "The board has not approved anything to be sold yet."
The inventory was done to identify lands that do not fall under the authority's mandate for flood control, conservation, recreation and education, she said. In the past landowners donated properties to the GRCA that do fall within its mandate.
"I think you would know right away if we were considering selling them, that would definitely be put out to the public," said Foxton.
The GRCA owns 19,900 hectares of land or about three per cent of all the land in the Grand River watershed. The 26 members board represents 38 different municipalities in the watershed that is home to nearly a million people.
"Of these seven parcels were deemed to have potential for housing development, and two parcels will require more in-depth analysis," says the report prepared by the conservation authority's chief administrative officer.
The locations of the seven parcels are not public because at this point the lands are not up for sale, and the GRCA does not want to be hounded by developers, said Foxton.
The presentation on the land inventory Friday by the head of the conservation authority will be public and streamed online, but the document will not be released to the public, said Lisa Stocco, the GRCA's manager of communications.
That will change if the 26-member board decides to sell any of the seven parcels of land identified as suitable for housing.
"That's when they would be made public and there would be a public consultation process," said Stocco. "At this point, we are just doing what the province asked by establishing the inventory."
Terry Pender is a Waterloo Region Record reporter. Reach him via: tpender@therecord.com
CAPTION: The Grand River Conservation Authority has identified seven parcels of land suitable for new housing developments.

Document 409 of 500

The Science Centre was an ugly grey box in Don Mills. We have bigger things to worry about than keeping it alive

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/science-centre-was-ugly-grey-box-don-mills-we/docview/3072281208/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=The+Science+Centre+was+an+ugly+grey+box+in+Don+Mills.+We+have+bigger+things+to+worry+about+than+keeping+it+alive&amp;title=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-06-26&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Closing the Ontario Science Centre has distressed many people for many reasons. Some, like me, are emotional, the big ole place a warm memory from childhood, although the shabby fountainless stained concrete building in an isolated car-dependent part of Toronto makes me wince a bit now. If you love something, keep it in shape.
The bros of modernist architecture stain the concrete further with their tears, like the Globe's embittered architecture columnist mourning "Toronto's great Brutalist temple of childhood wonder, Raymond Moriyama's hillside wonderland."
Brutalism is ugly, like the gloomy concrete hulk of Robarts Library Robarts Library and the unfixable Manulife Centre, architectural manspreading that nobody loves, especially now as life gets grimmer. Sixty years ago, trendy architects wanted huge monoliths to overawe pointless little humans.
Builders now glue glass boxes to these grim buildings to make them look more inviting. It's even worse. Remember, ubiquitous cheap glass is the concrete of our future, no match for global heating.
The late Moriyama doesn't fare well. In 2023 he wrote to the Star claiming he had used "concrete to last far beyond 100 years."But he was designing grey boxes in Don Mills, not the Pyramids, and apparently used the now-notorious lightweight RAAC semi-concrete in parts of the roof.
In the glassy-eyed 1960s, brand-name Siporex might have seemed perfect for those new airy (non-air conditioned) modernist schools. Now the Star reports that 360 of Ontario's 4,500 schools are being monitored for the same aerated concrete that has left many aging British school buildings empty lest roofs collapse on children's heads.
Ontario Premier Doug Ford closed the Science Centre fast and his critics are suspicious and irate. That this was Ford's dream come true doesn't make him wrong. Ontario Place might well be a better spot for a new science centre, beautiful and accessible. Ford has money on his mind, which critics do not, not in the same way.
Toronto has to prepare for hard times. The science centre roof might not cope with a big snowfall but rainfall is an equal danger, the climate emergency having brought us ruinous rain all this month.
Some angry people have misunderstood the import of the Rimkus engineering report that doomed the buildings.
They say it wasn't that bad, not realizing that the weaker parts of the roof are in crucial areas, a steel roof replacement might take five years, and the science centre might now be uninsurable for visitors.
They're worried about the building; the province is also worried about the children inside the building and what might happen even if partial repairs began with construction walled off inside. They say "low risk." Parents want "no risk." That's what makes them parents.
Toronto needs new subway cars on Line 2, the current ones near the end of life just like the roof. Mayor Olivia Chow hopes the feds will offer help though I can't see why it's a federal matter. Prime Minister Trudeau might consider it though.
Journalists think the recent Conservative win in Toronto-St. Paul's matters hugely. But Trudeau isn't worried about ridings, he's worried about the looming U.S. election. If Trump wins, the world is in huge trouble in every sector. I doubt Canadians will think libertarian-minded Conservatives are up to the task of keeping Canada functional in a manic chaotic Trumpy world.
Like the rest of us, Trudeau, Ford and Chow have no idea what will happen next. Will Toronto get subway money? Flood money? Will taxes have to soar? Honestly, a roof in Don Mills is not the priority.
We won't always get what we want but we might get what we need. That's the hope at least.
CAPTION: The Ontario Science Centre was closed June 21, 2024 after an engineering report found problems in the roof panels.

Subject: Construction; Architecture; Modernism; Science

Business indexing term: Subject: Architecture

Document 410 of 500

Would you hire any of Canada's federal leaders to run your business?

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/would-you-hire-any-canadas-federal-leaders-run/docview/3072280929/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Would+you+hire+any+of+Canada%27s+federal+leaders+to+run+your+business%3F&amp;title=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-06-26&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: In my "day job" I am involved in the recruitment and selection of candidates for my employer. This includes executive positions.
When doing so, what are some of the "must have" qualifications I would be looking for in a candidate for a senior executive role?
The first thing is a candidate with a proven high level of leadership. Someone who has led large teams of people and who has had to make very hard decisions. This could mean a corporate executive who has successfully led organization(s) through challenging times. It could mean a former military officer who has commanded troops, perhaps even in combat. It could mean the president of a large health-care organization. These are just a few examples.
Obviously I would also be looking for a very solid background in finance. A leader must know how to spend prudently, create and stick to budgets.
There are other examples, but these would be my top "must haves." I am not considering any candidate without those two.
Keeping that in mind if I was recruiting for the role of prime minister of Canada, would I consider any of the three major party leaders?
Lets start with the incumbent, Prime Minister Justin Trudeau. He came into office in 2015 with no relevant leadership experience at all. He had been a school teacher after graduating from McGill University with a bachelor's degree in literature and a bachelor of education from the University of British Columbia. After teaching, he was the chair for the Katimavik charity and later a director of the non-profit Canadian Avalanche Association.
If I had been recruiting for the prime minister's position I would not have even finished reading Trudeau's resume. To say he was woefully unqualified would be generous. Outside of a famous last name, he "brought nothing to the table." And that has been evident in his job performance. He has shown incredibly poor judgment in such scandals as the Aga Khan, SNC Lavalin and the WE charity. A seasoned leader would have never allowed themselves to get caught up in such scandals.
Financially Trudeau has been a disaster. In April 2023 the Montreal Economic Institute reported that the gross federal debt had increased by 50 per cent since Trudeau took office in 2015! Part of that increased debt was due to COVID-19, so that is understandable. But Trudeau took a surplus budget from former prime minister Stephen Harper and immediately began running deficits. While we don't expect governments to make a profit, we do expect them to be prudent in their spending. Trudeau has failed badly here. His lack of financial experience and knowledge has been very evident.
The situation doesn't improve with Conservative Leader Pierre Poilievre or NDP Leader Jagmeet Singh.
Poilievre graduated from the University of Calgary with a bachelor's degree in international relations. While a student he worked part-time in collections for Telus. Later he worked for a short period as a journalist for Alberta Report. He got heavily involved with the Reform Party while a student and was elected to Parliament in 2004 at the age of 25.
Singh graduated from the University of Western Ontario with a bachelor of science in biology and then obtained a law degree from Osgoode Hall. He practised criminal law for about six years before entering politics in 2011.
Like Trudeau, neither Poilievre nor Singh are qualified to be prime minister. They have neither high level leadership experience, nor a background in finance. If you disagree, ask yourself this. If any of these three men applied to be the president of your company would they even be interviewed based on their qualifications?
If you are unhappy with the current state of Canada, I am afraid you will also find both Singh and Poilievre to be disappointing. Like Trudeau, they are hopelessly unqualified.
This is a sad situation for Canada.
CAPTION: Ask yourself this. If Pierre Poilievre, Justin Trudeau or Jagmeet Singh applied to be the president of your company, would they even get an interview based on their qualifications?
CREDIT: Craig Wallace

Document 411 of 500

'There's no safe Liberal seats in English Canada'

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/theres-no-safe-liberal-seats-english-canada/docview/3072280855/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=%27There%27s+no+safe+Liberal+seats+in+English+Canada%27&amp;title=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-06-26&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Hidden in the stunning results of Monday's federal byelection in Toronto is a severe warning for the MPs in Waterloo Region.
On Monday, the riding of Toronto-St. Paul's, which has voted solidly Liberal for more than 30 years - even when the party was relegated to third-party status in 2011 under Michael Ignatieff - became the riding that cracked under anti-Justin Trudeau pressure.
The counting of ballots went well into the night and Conservative Don Stewart won by 590 votes.
What happened in this Toronto riding matters, because if the Liberals had a safe seat anywhere in the country, it was there, in the centre of a cluster of central Toronto ridings that pundits jokingly describe as "Red Square."
We've known for a long while that the Liberals' popularity has sunk far below the Conservatives across Canada. People are unhappy and anxious about high interest rates, grocery bills and housing prices, to name just a few issues. Canadians are looking for a change.
"The Liberals aren't going to get routed like this unless their supporters stay home - and they did," said Sean Simpson, senior vice-president of Ipsos Public Affairs Canada, as he reflected the morning after the vote.
"If the Tories win that (riding), it means there's no safe Liberal seats in English Canada."
Waterloo MP Bardish Chagger, a former Government House Leader and cabinet minister under Trudeau, has the safest Liberal seat in the region; she won by 18 percentage points and 10,000 votes over her nearest rival in the last general election three years ago.
But even she could be in jeopardy of losing next time, if the Liberals remain as deeply unpopular as they are now.
The ridings of Tim Louis in Kitchener-Conestoga, Valerie Bradford in Kitchener South-Hespeler and Bryan May in Cambridge, are also ready to turn Conservative blue.
As for Kitchener Centre, its voters are so progressive that it is difficult to imagine this riding voting Conservative.
But that doesn't mean Green MP Mike Morrice is safe, Simpson said. It is going to depend on a wide array of factors, including whether citizens engage in strategic voting and how that might shake out.
Morrice won in 2021, partly due to his own hard work campaigning and appealing to voters, but also partly because of very unusual circumstances that are unlikely to be repeated: his Liberal opponent, Raj Saini, had ended his campaign during the election period. Saini's name was on the ballot but he was not standing for election. Morrice was the beneficiary of many of those votes.
Simpson said the opinion polls across the country are so bad for the Liberals that the best-case scenario for the party is that it might form the official opposition.
There are three options for Trudeau at this point, Simpson says:
 Step down as party leader. One in three Liberal voters believe Trudeau should resign as party leader, and MPs in his caucus are beginning to say so as well.
 Prorogue Parliament, which would mean a fresh start, in which Liberals could attempt to "take control of the narrative" and roll out a new plan, much like a campaign platform.
 Call a snap election in hopes of consolidating the anti-Conservative vote around the Liberal party. "But the headwinds are strong, and it is looking unlikely," Simpson said of that option.
In a statement Tuesday responding to the byelection, Prime Minister Trudeau said: "I want to be clear that I hear your concerns and frustrations.
"These are not easy times. And it is clear, I and my entire team, have much more hard work to do to deliver tangible, real progress that Canadians can see and feel."
The question is: Will voters give Trudeau the time and trust to make this happen?
It seems clear voters have already moved on.
Luisa D'Amato is a Waterloo Region Record columnist who writes about issues affecting day-to-day life. Reach her by email at ldamato@therecord.com
CAPTION: Prime Minister Justin Trudeau listens to Waterloo MP Bardish Chagger introduce him during a Februaryvisit to Parkwood Seniors Community in Waterloo.Conservative Leader Pierre Poilievre speaks in Februaryat Fiddleheads Health and Nutrition store in Kitchener.

Document 412 of 500

The latest casualty of Ford's policy of neglect

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/latest-casualty-fords-policy-neglect/docview/3072196864/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=unknown&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=The+latest+casualty+of+Ford%27s+policy+of+neglect&amp;title=The+Spectator&amp;issn=11899417&amp;date=2024-06-26&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.10&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Spectator&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: A failing common to successive Ontario governments in recent decades is the unconscionable neglect that has seen provincial jewels fall into decay.
Both Ontario Place and the Ontario Science Centre - the latter closed for good on Friday after a report that said its roof was in danger of collapsing - were, in their heydays, popular venues that celebrated energy and adventure, knowledge and possibility.
That they would have to be renovated and updated over time was inevitable. That they should have been, in the case of Ontario Place, shuttered and left to rot, and in the case of the science centre, poorly maintained and maligned, shames those who have led the province.
It's galling to know that governments in Ontario failed to make the necessary investments to maintain and renew prized provincial assets.
Premier Doug Ford, in particular, did nothing to reverse this decay. As the Toronto Star reported, Ontario's auditor general said in December that maintenance projects on the science centre were deferred and repeatedly denied funding, including 42 such projects since 2017, the year before the Ford government took power.
Presumably, Ford shed few tears at the closure, using it to justify a planned transfer of the science centre to Ontario Place as part of a major redevelopment on the waterfront that will include a massive, privately owned Therme spa.
Ford's proposed move has been controversial, with allegations of arbitrary decision-making that denied public consultation.
Local residents have also said the closure of the science centre marks the loss of one of the few attractions in the area.
The latest engineering report said the concrete used in construction of the science centre was nearing the end of its lifespan. To upgrade the roof would cost from $22 million to $40 million and take about two years or more, it said.
So Ford let the science centre decay and then, when it became dangerous, immediately shut down a facility he already wanted closed.
Infrastructure Minister Kinga Surma promptly touted the "eventual reopening in a new, state-of-the-art facility" on the waterfront.
An earlier report by Ernst & Young said the science centre was "in operational crisis due to a failing structure with mounting critical maintenance costs."
That report said it would cost $600 million more to repair and upgrade the existing science centre than to build a new one. It should never have gotten to that point.
After six years of government by Ford and his team, with continual impulsive decisions, pet projects and a cadre of too-close-by-half insiders, it's hard not to see the case of the science centre as emblematic of Ford's approach to infrastructure: allowing decay of prized public places and taking the opportunity his neglect created to justify new for-profit developments he wanted all along.
But much is lost along the way, as today's sad news again shows.
"For more than five decades, the Ontario Science Centre has been a beloved landmark and an integral part of our community and our province," said the centre's CEO Paul Kortenaar.
"Our building itself has been part of the experience and a cherished space for generations of visitors, sparking wonder and curiosity about science and the world around us."
But for Ford, the science centre appears merely to be someplace that we used to love.
If ever there was an example of knowing the price of everything and the value of nothing, Ontario's mishandling of these assets may be it.
CAPTION:

CREDIT: ;

Subject: Science

Title: The latest casualty of Ford's policy of neglect

Document 413 of 500

Trio is 'hopelessly unqualified'

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/trio-is-hopelessly-unqualified/docview/3072196603/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=unknown&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Trio+is+%27hopelessly+unqualified%27&amp;title=The+Spectator&amp;issn=11899417&amp;date=2024-06-26&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.10&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Spectator&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: In my "day job" I am involved in the recruitment and selection of candidates for my employer. This includes executive positions.
When doing so, what are some of the "must have" qualifications I would be looking for in a candidate for a senior executive role?
The first thing is a candidate with a proven high level of leadership. Someone who has led large teams of people and who has had to make very hard decisions. This could mean a corporate executive who has successfully led organization(s) through challenging times. It could mean a former military officer who has commanded troops. It could mean the president of a large health-care organization. These are just a few examples.
Obviously I would also be looking for a very solid background in finance. A leader must know how to spend prudently, create and stick to budgets.
There are other examples, but these would be my top "must haves." I am not considering any candidate without those two.
If I was recruiting for the role of prime minister of Canada, would I consider any of the three major party leaders?
Lets start with the incumbent, Prime Minister Justin Trudeau. He came into office in 2015 with no relevant leadership experience. He had been a teacher after graduating from McGill University with a bachelor's degree in literature and a bachelor of education from the University of British Columbia. After teaching, he was the chair for the charity Katimavik and later a director of the non-profit Canadian Avalanche Association.
If I had been recruiting for the prime minister's position I would not have even finished reading Trudeau's resumé. To say he was woefully unqualified would be generous. Outside of a famous last name, he "brought nothing to the table." And that has been evident in his job performance. He has shown incredibly poor judgment in such scandals as the Aga Khan, SNC Lavalin and the WE charity. A seasoned leader would have never allowed themselves to get caught up in such scandals.
Financially Trudeau has been a disaster. In April 2023 the Montreal Economic Institute reported that the gross federal debt had increased by 50 per cent since Trudeau took office in 2015. Part of that increased debt was due to COVID-19, so that is understandable. But Trudeau took a surplus budget from former prime minister Stephen Harper and immediately began running deficits. While we don't expect governments to make a profit, we do expect them to be prudent in their spending. Trudeau's lack of financial experience and knowledge has been very evident.
The situation doesn't improve with Conservative Leader Pierre Poilievre or NDP Leader Jagmeet Singh.
Poilievre graduated from the University of Calgary with a bachelor's degree in international relations. While a student he worked part time in collections for Telus. Later he worked for a short period as a journalist for Alberta Report. He got heavily involved with the Reform Party while a student and was elected to Parliament in 2004 at the age of 25.
Singh graduated from the University of Western Ontario with a bachelor of science in biology and then obtained a law degree from Osgoode Hall. He practised criminal law for about six years before entering politics in 2011.
Like Trudeau, neither Poilievre nor Singh are qualified to be prime minister. They have neither high level leadership experience, nor a background in finance. If you disagree, ask yourself this. If any of these three men applied to be the president of your company would they even be interviewed based on their qualifications?
If you are unhappy with the current state of Canada, I am afraid you will also find both Singh and Poilievre to be disappointing. Like Trudeau, they are hopelessly unqualified. This is a sad situation for Canada.
Craig Wallace is a Hamilton resident and author of
five books.
CAPTION:

CREDIT: Craig Wallace Craig Wallace is a Hamilton resident and author of five books.;
