Document 414 of 500

How Scotland started to kick its alcohol problem - and what Ontario could learn from it

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/how-scotland-started-kick-alcohol-problem-what/docview/3072326242/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=How+Scotland+started+to+kick+its+alcohol+problem+-+and+what+Ontario+could+learn+from+it&amp;title=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-06-25&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: GLASGOW and EDINBURGH, Scotland - One frosty winter morning, Tom Bennett awoke on the cold ground with a bare foot frozen in the snow.
The night before, the 33-year-old man had been too drunk to tie his shoelaces and one of his boots had fallen off. He was homeless and he knew that alcohol was killing him. But he was too sick to care.
"It was just a horrible place to be," said Bennett from his family home near Glasgow.
"I lost multiple jobs, multiple relationships. I lost all my dignity, self-respect and self-esteem. I nearly died on many occasions."
Today, Bennett, 47, is working to help others emerge from the same kind of darkness at a charity that helps those in the throes of addiction to heal. His journey reflects both alcoholism's toll and Scotland's transformative efforts to curb tragedy.
As Ontario moves to make alcohol more affordable and accessible, Scotland's policies are advancing in the opposite direction.
Ontario officials say they are fulfilling a 2018 election promise to increase "choice and convenience for shoppers and support Ontario retailers, domestic producers and workers in the alcohol industry."
But Scotland has cut alcohol-related hospital admissions by 40 per cent and deaths by almost half. While in Ontario, alcohol-related admissions have risen by a third and deaths by almost half, according to the Canadian Centre on Substance Use and Addiction.
While Scottish officials say that hard-hitting measures are necessary to lessen tragic outcomes, experts fear Ontario's approach makes tragic outcomes inevitable.
As in many countries, alcohol is central in Canada's social and economic life. Over three quarters of Canadians aged 15 and older report drinking alcohol, according to Statistics Canada. And booze sales contribute $26 billion annually to the national economy.
But new research is showing that drinking alcohol is more dangerous than previously thought. And alcohol's collective harms are vast because the number of people who drink is so high.
While Scottish officials say that hard-hitting measures are necessary to lessen tragic outcomes, health experts fear Ontario's approach makes tragic outcomes inevitable.
Scotland has an age-old drinking problem. For years, the country suffered more alcohol-related deaths - from conditions such as liver disease - than much of the rest of Europe. In 2003, Scots were dying from drink at a rate three times higher than the rest of Britons.
Bennett grew up deep in this tragic alcohol culture. His affluent family viewed alcohol - as many Scots did - as a fundamental part of Scottish life.
In Bennett's community, drinks flowed at the many festivals that hail ancient Celtic traditions. People drank to celebrate, to mourn, to relax and to unwind after work. Bennett enjoyed "wee nips" with his parents from the age of 10 and was buying liquor with friends from the age of 15.
"Alcohol was everywhere," Bennett said with emotion. "And cheap."
Some hard drinks cost less than soft drinks at the local store, he recalled.
"Why buy pop, when you could get 'off your face' for the same money on cheap booze?"
In those days, if you wanted to escape from the despair of drink, you were often on your own.
Bennett remembers a lonely night in jail, shaking violently in severe withdrawal, desperate for a change.
He was ignored. There was no doctor. He was released back to the frigid streets the following day.
"I was not somebody who was having two bottles of wine. ... I was going through seven or eight litres of wine a day ... and two or three bottles of spirits," he recalled as two of his three daughters chatted in the next room.
"I had nowhere to go. I didn't know what to do."
Six months later, Bennett found the remarkable strength to recover from his 15-year dependence with the support of his family and a residential program.
He says he feels lucky to be alive.
"I have known so many people who weren't so lucky .... and have gone on to die," he said.
Today fewer Scots are dying because the influences that led to Bennett's misery are being transformed by Scottish government policy.
Scotland is a global pioneer in alcohol policy. It won the inaugural European Award for Reducing Alcohol Harm in 2016 and has been recognized by the United Nations for its initiatives tackling alcohol's affordability, availability and attractiveness.
It did not happen easily.
When the Scottish government started its drive to reduce alcohol's downfalls, in the early 2000s, it looked to Canada for advice.
Among its consultants was Tim Stockwell, a professor of psychology at the University of Victoria and director of the Canadian Institute for Substance Use Research. Stockwell and his team had studied British Columbia and Saskatchewan alcohol price increases between 2002 and 2009 and found that a 10 per cent rise in average minimum prices reduced alcohol-related hospital admissions by 18 per cent two years later.
Scottish officials asked Stockwell about Canadian alcohol pricing approaches.
"I told them that we do it terribly," he said in a video interview.
Canada's alcohol pricing policy has stemmed less from public health concerns and more from desires to protect local producers and increase government revenue, Stockwell said.
Even so, the evidence Stockwell had studied about the benefits of increased prices was convincing. So Stockwell helped develop the Scottish policy and assisted over eight years to put it into action. It took two years to pass the legislation through the Scottish Parliament and six years to fight legal challenges by the alcohol industry in Scottish, European and United Kingdom courts.
The key part of Scotland's landmark policy was aimed at reducing drinking by introducing minimum unit prices to make drinking more expensive. Raising minimum prices is the most effective way of reducing alcohol's harms, Stockwell and other experts say. High prices cut the consumption of heavy drinkers - who are often partial to dirt-cheap booze - and young people, who can only drink what's low-cost.
At the beginning of 2018, the price of a beer in Scotland was about $1.00, according to public health data - the target Premier Doug Ford promised in his 'buck-a-beer' election slogan that year.
But a month before Doug Ford won a majority in Ontario, Scotland enacted a minimum price of 50 pence (C$0.87) for a unit of pure alcohol (10 mL) that meant a minimum of about $1.75 Canadian for a can of beer, $8.75 for a bottle of wine and $24.50 for a bottle of spirits, according to Dr. Peter Rice, one of the founders of the Scottish Health Action on Alcohol Problems, a medical think tank that has campaigned for change since 2006.
The new policy increased the prices of almost half the liquor on sale and led to a 15 per cent reduction in alcohol-related deaths in just three years. Last April, the Scottish Parliament unanimously voted to renew the policy and approved an increase to 65 pence per unit ($1.14).
"The pandemic hit us hard," Rice said in a phone interview. But with minimum unit pricing "we have still been able to stem the tide."
Alongside its pricing strategy, Scotland reduced the legal limit of alcohol for drivers to 50 mg per 100 mL of blood from 80 mg - the level in Ontario. This change was bolstered by a forceful media campaign that increased public opposition to drinking and driving. "It's not one for the road," a campaign slogan ran. "It's none for the road."
Scotland was also the first country to introduce "alcohol brief interventions." This involves training medical professionals to have short, structured conversations with people who abuse alcohol about their habits and how to access help.
Scotland still tolerates wide alcohol availability in supermarkets and small stores, something that flies in the face of what experts say is second only to pricing on the list of most important ways to reduce alcohol harms: reducing access.
Alex Collop sees first-hand the effects of easy access on low-income families in his work for Circle, a charity that supports families affected by alcohol and drugs.
Alcohol addiction leads to lower incomes, housing insecurity, domestic abuse and violence, Collop said from Circle's cosy meeting room in the heart of Pilton, one of Edinburgh's least-privileged communities.
"It is easier to buy alcohol than healthy food here," Collop said, noting that the stores beside a nearby pub called the Ferry Boat include a gambling house, a pizza parlour and a liquor store.
In his role as an alcohol counselling manager, Bennett also advocates for making alcohol tougher to get. He found it treacherous to go shopping when he came out of a residential rehab and was trying to stay sober.
"I was going to the supermarket for essential domestic items like toilet paper or washing-up liquid. And on the other side of the aisle.... hundreds and hundreds of bottles of whiskey, vodka, gin and everything else under the sun," he said in a video posted online.
Although alcohol is readily available in Scotland, high minimum unit prices prevent retailers from competing for customers with bargains or multi-buy promotions, something that experts worry will transpire in Ontario.
Timothy Naimi, a professor at the University of Victoria and the current director of the Canadian Institute for Substance Use Research, gives Ontario a failing grade on its plan to make alcohol more available.
"Ontario was already doing poorly" in not protecting the public from alcohol harms, Naimi said. But the government's planned tripling of retail outlets will "decimate its performance."
British Columbia research, led by Stockwell, showed that every 20 per cent increase in the number of private retail stores was associated with a three per cent increase in alcohol-related deaths.
This is mainly because increased retail competition drives prices down, Stockwell said. He added that large chains that can buy cheaply in bulk also often use alcohol as a loss leader to bring in customers who will then pay more for food and other higher-margin items.
"It's like a double public health whammy - cheap alcohol and expensive fruit and veg."
A recent study in Finland estimated that allowing alcohol sales in grocery stores, without minimum prices, would lead to a 22 per cent increase in hospital admissions and a nine per cent increase in alcohol-related deaths.
This could play out similarly in Ontario, where current minimum legal price limits are two and half times lower than levels that would curb consumption, according to University of Victoria research.
"It might not sound like a big step," Rice said, "but our experience is that moving alcohol into the grocery market will have many repercussions."
Scottish and Canadian experts questioned Ontario's decision to expand alcohol convenience especially while its hospitals and emergency rooms are unable to meet current demand. Ontario already has 320,000 alcohol-related hospital visits annually - a number bound to rise vastly with more retail access, according to the Canadian Alcohol Policy Evaluation project.
Leslie Buckley, chief of the addictions division at the Centre for Addiction and Mental Health, told the Star that she hopes Ontario will introduce alcohol brief interventions, like those in Scotland.
Ontario's direction "is also a sign that we need more health promotion around all substances, talking about the harms and making it clearer for people," Buckley said.
And as Ontario pursues its current path, Scotland's success mitigating tragedies is being recognized even in some of its oldest institutions of drink.
On a drizzly Tuesday afternoon, the Ferry Boat pub in Pilton was bustling. Four old-timers clutched pints around a wobbly table as they hooted with laughter. They said they have been meeting at the Ferry Boat almost every afternoon since the 1960s.
"We used to cut school to drink when we were nine years old," said Tommy, a retired fisherman.
"It's the working man's pastime," said David, a retired chef. "We would come in here after a shift and stay all night. Sometimes I slept here."
Even so, the regulars are not surprised to hear that fewer of their countrymen are dying of drink.
"It is too expensive now," said Alistair, 68, a retired painter. "Now I can only afford eight beers a night and I usually make it home."
CAPTION: Shelves of alcoholic drinks are displayed for sale in an Edinburgh off-licence as Scotland.In 2018 Scotland became the first country in the world to introduce minimum unit pricing for drinks.Ontario Premier Doug Ford pretends to drink from a beer can and Finance Minister Peter Bethlenfalvy holds one at an announcement saying the province is speeding up the expansion of alcohol sales in May.Ontario Premier Doug Ford pretends to drink from a beer can and Finance Minister Peter Bethlenfalvy holds one at an announcement saying the province is speeding up the expansion of alcohol sales, in May.
CREDIT: Katharine Lake Berz Special to the Star

Subject: Public health; Prices; Beer; Addictions; Alcoholism; Liquor industry; Patient admissions; Pricing policies; Drug use

Document 415 of 500

Pierre Poilievre's Conservatives managed to breach Fortress Toronto. Here's how they did it - and what it might mean for the party

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/pierre-poilievres-conservatives-managed-breach/docview/3072196682/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Pierre+Poilievre%27s+Conservatives+managed+to+breach+Fortress+Toronto.+Here%27s+how+they+did+it+-+and+what+it+might+mean+for+the+party&amp;title=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-06-25&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: OTTAWA-Pierre Poilievre's Conservatives began campaigning for the Toronto-St. Paul's byelection with the goal of rattling the foundations of Fortress Toronto: the governing Liberals have held all of the seats in the 416 for years.
They finished having knocked a brick out of the wall: Conservative Don Stewart bested the Liberals' Leslie Church by 590 votes, a result Poilievre described Tuesday as a "shocking upset."
While Stewart's victory raised questions within the Liberal and Conservative camps about Prime Minister Justin Trudeau's ability to hang on as his party's leader, for the Conservatives a broader debate is also taking place: did the stars align for one magical night, or was there a galactic implosion birthing a new electoral universe?
The road to a majority government for any party runs through the Greater Toronto and Hamilton Area, but in recent election campaigns the Conservatives have focused their energies on the "greater" part of that descriptor, rather than the 416.
Winning in Toronto, with its dense and jumbled urban mix of voters, sometimes in a single city block, has made strategic breakthroughs more challenging, especially with the Liberals dominating in every riding in the last three elections and the NDP nipping at their heels.
That's not to say the Conservatives weren't trying to win in this campaign.
Stewart was nominated in January, months before the Liberals settled on Church. His campaign manager was Amanda Kimber, a veteran politico who is close to Poilievre advisor Jenni Byrne.
When Kimber showed up Monday night outside Cafe Landwer, where volunteers were gathered to watch the election results, she was greeted with a spontaneous cheer from dozens of young volunteers spilling onto Spadina Road: the party had not just them, but also candidates from nearby ridings, sitting MPs and others to get out the vote over the last several months.
The party wasn't starting from a position of strength in the riding. It's been held by the Liberals for decades, and there was not much by way of an organized blue machine.
"This a very safe Liberal seat. When the Liberals only won 34 seats (in the 2011 election), this was one of the seats they picked up," Byrne told the Star on Monday night before the results were clear.
But the Tories approached the campaign really wanting to send a message to Canadians that they can win ridings throughout Toronto, Byrne said.
"I don't think the Liberals were ever in a position where they thought they were going to lose this. They are worried about what is the cascading effect in other ridings," she said.
And the Tories are also now contemplating the cascading effect for them.
Nomination contests in other 416 and 905 ridings, some already hotly contested, could see an influx of aspiring candidates lured by the fact the 20-point lead for Poilievre in the national polls does appear to be translating into victory.
While the Liberal campaign tried to focus on Poilievre in their campaign literature - just as the Tories used Trudeau's photo on their pamphlets - Byrne said "as people get to know Pierre, they're open and receptive to the messaging and the policies."
The veteran strategist said the Liberals' attacks on Poilievre over matters like abortion, gun control and LGBTQ issues fell flat in the byelection.
"I think it's very tired and it doesn't seem to be working for them," she said.
What worked for Stewart was focusing on some specific issues, several told the Star.
Among them was connecting the Liberals to the spate of auto thefts in the area, through literature and a focus on the "stop the crime" element of Poilievre's core messaging. Economics was also a factor - there are affluent pockets of the riding where voters might be irritated by Liberals' recently announced changes to the capital gains tax system, and receptive to Poilievre's frugality.
A focus on the riding's Jewish voters is understood to have helped as well. Their concerns about rising antisemitism in Toronto, a feeling the Liberals were not dealing with it, and what its critics characterize as the government's often confusing position on the war between Israel and Hamas were all issues the Tories pulled on to push votes their way.
There, they may have received an assist from Jewish Ally, a third-party campaign effort which targeted Jewish voters in the riding via social media ads.
Longtime conservative strategist Stephen Taylor and former provincial Progressive Conservative candidate Andrew Kirsch developed the campaign in answer to what they saw as a cold response from the Liberal government towards the Jewish community.
"It's democracy, and the way we provide feedback in a democracy is through the vote," Taylor said in an interview with the Star. "We wanted there to be a political price for the government's inaction and lack of response to the Jewish community's concerns."
The Conservatives won more votes in Toronto-St. Paul's than they did in that riding in the 2021 general election, which insiders said could portend well for future voter growth in the city, but which also caught them off guard.
Though they trusted their ability to identify potential voters, their inability to canvass inside apartment buildings left a blind spot that could be one explanation for the votes they weren't expecting.
What also mystified many Conservatives is why the New Democrats didn't do better than the 10.9 per cent of the vote their candidate captured. The NDP holds the riding provincially, and with a high number of renters, Tories expected its share of the vote to be much higher.
Jubilant as they may be, the Conservative jury was very much still out Tuesday on what it all means down the line: the specifics of what came together for them on voting day this time won't replicate themselves in the next election, several Conservatives said, meaning they also may not be able to hold on to Toronto-St. Paul's.
On the other hand, they might.
CAPTION: Conservative Leader Pierre Poilievre speaks in the House of Commons on June 12, 2024.

Document 416 of 500

June 25: Close the taps on chlorine use, Do students really need A/C and other letters to the editor

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/june-25-close-taps-on-chlorine-use-do-students/docview/3071991772/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=June+25%3A+Close+the+taps+on+chlorine+use%2C+Do+students+really+need+A%2FC+and+other+letters+to+the+editor&amp;title=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-06-25&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Close the taps on chlorine use
Re: Why is Hamilton adding more chlorine to its drinking water? (June 22)
So far, the extra disinfectant has not resulted in any related resident complaints, water director Nick Winters says.
That's because all of us who are chemically sensitive gave up on you bunch years ago and switched to bottled water. And, believe me, for an eco-conscious person like myself, that's a whole lot of guilt.
Since I moved here a decade ago, it's been like no place else in Ontario that I've ever lived. I turn on the tap and my eyes start to itch and water. If I don't boil my water long enough when making tea, I lose my voice. And showering or bathing is a nightmare. No matter how much lotion I slather on afterward, I walk around itching and scratching.
Now, given the fact (as I have read) that chlorine kills neither E. coli or giardia, which cause major problems, why for the sake of Murphy have we not switched to ozonation?
As I understand it, it's a simpler system, does the job more quickly, is cheaper to run and more effective. What in heaven's name are we waiting for? Those of us who can't drink or bathe comfortably in this city would like to know.
Marilyn Jay, Dundas
Congratulations to these grads
Re: Youth and young parents celebrated at high school graduation ceremony (June 22)
Thank you for honouring these amazing students who graduated from Good Shepherd's Notre Dame House School. These graduates are tomorrow's leaders. Every secondary school in the area should print, frame and hang this article up for all of their students to read.
Judith Keczan, Lincoln
So much for democracy
Re: Why is this provincially elected representative not allowed at meetings with the province? (June 24)
So a defeated PC candidate will represent me at a water system request rather than my democratically elected MPP Bobbi Ann Brady?
Maybe councillors Stewart Patterson and Dan Lawrence can appoint a more acceptable Ford-friendly MPP. The whole process leaves a foul taste.
We don't need democracy, just pro-Ford representatives?
Greg Rodgers, Selkirk
Do students really need A/C?
Re: Nearly half of Hamilton public schools lack air conditioning (June 20)
It's been hot - on that we can agree. But can we take a calming breath and broaden our perspectives?
The kids cannot possibly learn anything in an A/C-less classroom? But they're all happy to hit the schoolyard and tear around after a ball. Good for them!
Construction workers cannot work from home offices with A/C. Farmers tend to livestock and crops year-round in whatever weather (yes there are climate-controlled cabs). Firefighters must wear protective gear and fight fires at their source. Police and first responders attend accident victims on heat-reflecting roads. Factory and steel workers have decades of stories working in incredible temps. And all our homeless people?
Many folks' daily realities do not include A/C. There are tens of thousands around us who grew up, played and worked before A/C was commonplace, and thought nothing of it.
I'm truly grateful for compassionate community workers, cooling centres and locations where we all can cool off. There are many things essential to life. Having 24-7 A/C is not one of them.
Lynda Pierce, Mount Hope
Science centre closure another debacle
Doug Ford waited until now to announce the closure of the Ontario Science Centre. There was no discussion prior to this to advise us of the problems with the building. No warning, no public input, no voter input and no political party input was given or considered.
Basically, the Ford PC Ontario government said it is closed and that is the end of it.
This will affect Toronto and Ontario tourism during the busiest time of the year. The loss of money to the hotels, restaurants and businesses will cause more lost revenue at a time where they are still struggling to survive from the pandemic, poor economy and other mistakes that were already hindered by this government.
Families, children, summer campers and visitors from other countries who visit the site every year will not be able to come and visit the amazing displays, the hands-on programs and science experiments. Shame on you Doug Ford!
The Ford government will obviously not call for an early election now after another one of their political stunts. He forgets the people of Ontario are the voters and we will remember this as another one of his debacles.
Maureen MacKenzie, Stoney Creek

Letters

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/letters/docview/3071774878/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=unknown&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Letters&amp;title=The+Spectator&amp;issn=11899417&amp;date=2024-06-25&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.11&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Spectator&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Close the taps
on chlorine use
Re: Why is Hamilton adding more chlorine to its drinking water? (June 22)
So far, the extra disinfectant has not resulted in any related resident complaints, water director Nick Winters says.
That's because all of us who are chemically sensitive gave up on you bunch years ago and switched to bottled water. And, believe me, for an eco-conscious person like myself, that's a whole lot of guilt.
Since I moved here a decade ago, it's been like no place else in Ontario that I've ever lived. I turn on the tap and my eyes start to itch and water. If I don't boil my water long enough when making tea, I lose my voice. And showering or bathing is a nightmare. No matter how much lotion I slather on afterward, I walk around itching and scratching.
Now, given the fact (as I have read) that chlorine kills neither E. coli or giardia, which cause major problems, why for the sake of Murphy have we not switched to ozonation?
As I understand it, it's a simpler system, does the job more quickly, is cheaper to run and more effective. What in heaven's name are we waiting for? Those of us who can't drink or bathe comfortably in this city would like to know.
Marilyn Jay, Dundas
Do students really need A/C?
Re: Nearly half of Hamilton public schools lack air conditioning
(June 20)
It's been hot - on that we can agree. But can we take a calming breath and broaden our perspectives?
The kids cannot possibly learn anything in an A/C-less classroom? But they're all happy to hit the schoolyard and tear around after a ball. Good for them!
Construction workers cannot work from home offices with A/C.
Farmers tend to livestock and crops year-round in whatever weather (yes there are climate-controlled cabs).
Firefighters must wear protective gear and fight fires at their source.
Police and first responders attend accident victims on heat-reflecting roads.
Factory and steel workers have decades of stories working in incredible temps. And all our homeless people?
Many folks' daily realities do not include A/C. There are tens of thousands around us who grew up, played and worked before A/C was commonplace, and thought nothing of it.
I'm truly grateful for compassionate community workers, cooling centres and locations where we all can cool off. There are many things essential to life. Having 24-7 A/C is not one of them.
Lynda Pierce, Mount Hope
Congratulations to these special grads
Re: Youth and young parents
celebrated at high school
graduation ceremony (June 22)
Thank you for honouring these amazing students who graduated from Good Shepherd's Notre Dame House School.
These graduates are tomorrow's leaders.
Every secondary school in the area should print, frame and hang this article up for all of their students to read.
Judith Keczan, Lincoln
So much for
our democracy
Re: Why is this provincially
elected representative not
allowed at meetings with the
province? (June 24)
So a defeated PC candidate will represent me at a water system request rather than my democratically elected MPP Bobbi Ann Brady?
Maybe councillors Stewart Patterson and Dan Lawrence can appoint a more acceptable Ford-friendly MPP. The whole process leaves a foul taste.
We don't need democracy, just pro-Ford representatives?
Greg Rodgers, Selkirk

Document 418 of 500

Is this the Ontario we actually want?

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/is-this-ontario-we-actually-want/docview/3071773940/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=unknown&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Is+this+the+Ontario+we+actually+want%3F&amp;title=The+Spectator&amp;issn=11899417&amp;date=2024-06-25&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.10&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Spectator&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: I'd love to live in an Ontario where seniors mattered as much as booze, clean air mattered as much as private sector profit, science mattered as much as a casino and community mattered as much as political power. But instead, we have Doug Ford.
I'd love to live in an Ontario where the public waterfront mattered as much as private spas, accessible streets mattered as much as licence plate stickers, autism families mattered as much as Ford fests and helping vulnerable people mattered as much as LCBO bags. But instead, we have Ford.
I'd love to live in an Ontario where farmland mattered as much as highways, education mattered as much as gaming, people experiencing homelessness mattered as much as good times and affordable housing mattered as much as advertising campaigns. But instead, we have Ford.
I'd love to live in an Ontario where small businesses mattered as much as foreign corporations, cyclists mattered as much as cars, addiction mattered as much as convenience and justice mattered as much as judgment. But instead, we have Ford.
I'd love to live in an Ontario where organizations that help our most vulnerable neighbours are supported, people of all backgrounds and incomes are valued equally, the rule of law is respected and the premier is trusted. But instead, we have Ford.
I'd love to live in an Ontario where my neighbours have equal access to health care, affordable housing, long-term-care homes, emergency rooms, climate action, education and a fiscally responsible government for all. But instead, we have Ford.
The premier has the province speculating that an early election is in the works. He is flooding the airwaves and the online world with the expensive "It's Happening Here" advertising campaign, which we are paying for to promote an Ontario that seems unrecognizable to anyone needing health care, trying to get an affordable place to rent, or trying to own a home.
He has run those ads during the Grammys and the Super Bowl to project an image of our province that would lure investment and attract talent. But it feels a lot like gaslighting when we see the homeless encampments in our communities, the massive food lines queueing around downtown city blocks and the stress on the faces of students trying to afford post-secondary education or find a good job.
Ford has not met his housing targets and has focused on costly, unnecessary projects like a spa at Ontario Place and a Highway 413 that is unnecessary when the 407 is empty enough most days to land a plane on.
But the Greenbelt scandal, the closing of Service Ontario small business locations to put our tax dollars into an American company and paying up to three times more to private nursing agencies while our public health-care system collapses grates on our morale. His government making secret deals and using private email and phones erodes our trust.
After two majorities for his government, this is an opportunity to reflect on the kind of Ontario we would love to live in and examine the values that made us the envy and economic engine of Canada.
Does it feel like the future of our parents, ourselves and our children is headed in the right direction or veering off course into scandals and spending we can't afford?
Does it feel like we are on the right track or watching a gravy train wreck?
Does it feel like Ontario is still ours to discover, or does it feel more like Ontario is ours to fix?
Is Ontario a "Place to Grow" or a place to grow older worried about poverty, renoviction, private health-care costs and access to long-term care?
It's up to the other three provincial parties to promote a compelling vision for Ontario. But it's up to us to examine our values and the value of our tax dollars and decide if the next election is the one to get out the vote for change.
Laura Babcock is president
of POWERGROUP Communications and host of The OShow.
CAPTION:

CREDIT: Laura Babcock Laura Babcock is president of POWERGROUP Communications and host of The OShow.;

Document 419 of 500

How the Ontario Science Centre was suddenly shut down - and why

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/how-ontario-science-centre-was-suddenly-shut-down/docview/3072043735/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=How+the+Ontario+Science+Centre+was+suddenly+shut+down+-+and+why&amp;title=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-06-24&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: The path to the shockingly sudden shutdown of the Ontario Science Centre after 55 years in operation began quietly last fall.
While controversy swirled over Premier Doug Ford's plan to open a new science centre at Ontario Place in 2028 - and a spa - an engineering firm hired by the provincial agency Infrastructure Ontario sent experts to the iconic building as winter approached.
What they found prompted last week's technical report detailing problems with poorly maintained and crumbling aerated concrete roof panels at risk of failing. Several pages of photos were included.
"We had received notification about the specific type of concrete and the specific type of panels being used around the world in the '50s, '60s and '70s," Infrastructure Minister Kinga Surma confirmed Monday.
"We had every intention on keeping the science centre open until the new science centre is built, but I cannot ignore the health and safety warning," Surma added, pushing back at growing suspicions about the closure.
"I have an obligation to protect the public."
Despite the report, which points to water infiltration of the concrete panels causing it to deteriorate and reinforcing bar inside it to rust, critics smell a rat over the closure and want it reversed.
They maintain it conveniently pads Ford's narrative about the overall poor condition of the science centre following years of neglect by successive governments.
"We know what this is about - his vanity Ontario Place project," New Democrat Leader Marit Stiles wrote on social media.
"Everything about this feels wrong," said Liberal MPP Adil Shamji, whose riding of Don Valley East includes the science centre.
The centre's days were already numbered: the government's "business case" for a new one found it would cost $600 million more to fix up the existing building designed by the late architect Raymond Moriyama.
According to Ontario's auditor general last December, "deferred maintenance projects that were at risk of critical failure have been repeatedly denied funding" at the science centre. They include 42 such projects since 2017, the year before Ford's Progressive Conservatives took power.
What did the report find?
Last week's 52-page engineering report by Rimkus Consulting Group concluded concrete panels used in the science centre are reaching the end of their lifespan, similar to those in more than 100 now-closed British schools of the same era.
As the Associated Press reported from London last September on the schools, this type of concrete panel can fail "with very little warning," said Phil Purnell, a professor of materials and structures at the University of Leeds.
A deadline of Oct. 31 has been set to vacate the science centre, before another seasonal onslaught of rain and snow risks a collapse on staff and visitors, many of them schoolchildren.
Surma said the immediate closure to the public gives staff time to pack up and move to a temporary science centre - for which a request for proposals went out Monday - or to pop-up exhibitions being arranged.
"We have exhibits, we have animals, we have staff in the building. We have to essentially turn the building off," she added. "It's a job and we're doing all of that planning now."
Aware that the report would be questioned in today's political climate, Infrastructure Ontario chief executive Michael Lindsay pledged it will be peer reviewed by another engineering firm, providing a second opinion of sorts.
One concrete panel is 'critical'
While the report stated just one science centre roofing panel is in the "critical" category, six per cent of the panels are considered "high risk," nine per cent are "medium risk" and the remaining 84 per cent are "low risk."
But the authors warned "a significant snow or rain ... could exceed the reduced load-carrying capacity of the distressed panels, placing them at an increased risk of sudden collapse."
The recommendation? That the roof panels be replaced with steel decking, which would cost between $22 million and $40 million and take two to five years.
Officials said the lengthy time frame is because many of the roof panels are hard to reach because of the science centre's unique location straddling a ravine.
The material in question - known as RAAC for reinforced autoclaved aerated concrete - was a popular choice in institutional and industrial buildings because of its light weight and thermal insulation capacities that also made it a good sound and fire barrier, the Rimkus report said.
It is made from an aerated blend of sand, Portland cement and aluminum powder and has increasingly come on the radar for problems as water seeps in.
"As a building material, RAAC has an overall reduced robustness compared to steel decks or traditional concrete, making it more susceptible to damage from impact and raising the risk of sudden failure," the Rimkus report continues.
"Owner and facility managers are often not aware of the problems ... until there is physical evidence of a failure or deterioration."
"The consequences of RAAC failure include potential building damage and risk to public safety," the report cautions.
About 80 other public buildings across Ontario with the same type of concrete are being examined to determine if further action is necessary.
CAPTION: The Ontario Science Centre was closed June 21, 2024 after an engineering report found problems in the roof panels.

Subject: Shutdowns; Science; Infrastructure; Engineering firms; Aerated concrete

Document 420 of 500

Chrystia Freeland calls China's EVs a threat to Canada and vows to fight back

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/chrystia-freeland-calls-chinas-evs-threat-canada/docview/3072043544/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Chrystia+Freeland+calls+China%27s+EVs+a+threat+to+Canada+and+vows+to+fight+back&amp;title=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-06-24&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: OTTAWA-Deputy Prime Minister Chrystia Freeland alleged Monday that China is threatening Canadian workers by unfairly cranking out cheap electric vehicles, and vowed that the Liberal government will act to defend Canada's nascent - and heavily subsidized - EV sector.
Speaking at a factory in Vaughan on Monday, Freeland said the government will launch 30 days of consultations on July 2 before deciding how to respond to China's "intentional, state-directed policy" of overproducing EVs. She accused Beijing of "taking advantage of the global economic system" by using "very problematic" standards to produce cheap EVs to dump on the world market and undermine Canada's potential to compete in the growing global clean car industry.
The measures under consideration include a special measure to slap a surtax on Chinese-made EVs, changes to which cars are eligible for the $5,000 federal EV purchase rebates, and "broader" restrictions on investment in Canada, Freeland said.
"We are going to protect you and defend you from unfair competition, and I want you to know that," Freeland, who is also Canada's finance minister, told a room of workers Monday morning.
While Freeland declined to say whether Canada plans to join U.S. President Joe Biden's new tariff wall against Chinese EVs, she said "nothing is ruled out and all possible tools are on the table" - including higher tariffs.
"We certainly see the need to be very strong in our defence of Canada and the national economic interest," she said.
The United States announced in May that it would crank up its own tariffs on Chinese EVs from 25 per cent to 100 per cent. The European Union, citing similar concerns, later announced the bloc would raise its own tariffs on made-in-China EVs to 38 per cent.
All that fuelled expectations that Canada would follow suit, with International Trade Minister Mary Ng repeatedly stating the Liberal government was seriously looking at the question and intended to take action to protect Canada's fledgling EV sector.
A senior government official, speaking on condition they aren't named, told the Star on Monday that it is "highly likely" Canada will impose new tariffs on Chinese EVs, and that the "inclination is to match" the American tariffs. But the official said the government will try to calculate during the consultations how much of a cost advantage China gets from practices deemed unfair, and to set Ottawa's response accordingly.
The EV sector in Canada is getting a huge amount of government support, which has helped attract companies like Honda, Stellantis and Northvolt to promise to set up factories for batteries and vehicles in Ontario and Quebec. Last week, the federal Parliamentary Budget Officer calculated that the EV sector is slated to receive $52.5 billion in federal and provincial support.
The question of tariffs on China is difficult for the government to navigate, experts have told the Star, with geopolitical tensions and a potential tradeoff between protecting a heavily subsidized domestic industry and pushing hard for people to switch off gas-powered cars to confront the climate crisis.
Ontario Premier Doug Ford - whose government has also devoted billions of dollars to luring EV makers to the province - added to the pressure last week. He alleged China's "low labour standards and dirty energy" is letting it flood the market with cheap EVs, and called on Ottawa to match the American tariffs to protect Canadian jobs.
Experts such as Flavio Volpe, the head of the Automotive Parts Manufacturers' Association, argue that China will overrun Canada's fledgling EV sector unless Ottawa matches the American tariffs thatBiden has promised. Under Beijing's direction, China has pursued an industrial policy that has supported the creation of a wide range of EV companies with access to its large domestic market, lower labour costs and an abundance of raw materials.
Last year, 60 per cent of new electric cars sold globally were registered in China, according to the International Energy Agency. Chinese companies already dominate the global market for plug-in hybrids, and Shenzhen-based BYD has a growing international presence and is threatening Tesla for the biggest share of the global market in battery-operated electric vehicles, according to a February report from TrendForce, a market research firm.
The company also recently launched an EV that costs under $10,000 (U.S.).
On Monday in Vaughan, Freeland was clear that Ottawa - despite having a target for all new cars sold in 2035 to be emissions-free - does not want to fuel the green transition with cheap EVs built with what she described as lower labour and environmental standards in China.
"That is not the way to meet climate goals," she said.
As it stands, Canada has a six-per-cent tariff on EVs made in China. The only EVs made there that are currently available in Canada are Teslas manufactured in Shanghai. The federal government also doles out $5,000 to people who buy zero-emission vehicles, and the made-in-China Teslas are currently eligible for this cash.
Ng, the federal trade minister, said the moves Ottawa is considering - including new tariffs and changing which cars are eligible for the federal cash incentive - are necessary to confront China's alleged unfair practices in the EV market that undermine Canadian workers.
"It isn't just targeting companies. It is targeting the livelihood of our workers and the stability of our communities," Ng said. "We will stand up to practices that distort the market."
Asked what Canada will do if China retaliates against any new trade measures, Freeland declined to be specific but said the government is "not naive" and will be prepared "for all eventualities."
CAPTION: Finance Minister Chrystia Freeland speaks during a news conference in Ottawa on June 18, 2024.

Subject: Automobiles; Surtax; Electric vehicles; Tariffs; Factories

Document 421 of 500

The simple science and complex politics of the crumbling Ontario Science Centre

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/simple-science-complex-politics-crumbling-ontario/docview/3071773956/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=The+simple+science+and+complex+politics+of+the+crumbling+Ontario+Science+Centre&amp;title=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-06-24&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Follow the science at the Ontario Science Centre.
But beware the dubious political science over the Ontario Place controversy.
By suddenly closing off the aging science centre to the public last week, citing a safety scare at the aging Don Mills facility, Doug Ford reignited every possible conspiracy theory.
Behold his corrupt motives, the critics cry: a scam and a sham!
So is this really a ruse to cover up the premier's scheme to relocate the science centre to Ontario Place while enriching his friends? That reaction, so soon after the deception and bungling over last year's Greenbelt boondoggle, is understandable and predictable.
But disregarding an engineering report that warns of an imminent roof collapse would be incomprehensible. To do so would be to assume that an entire government - not just the politicians but the civil servants - is putting political calculations ahead of civil engineering.
Surely it is possible for a politician who is sometimes dishonest to be honest some of the time. Just as it's entirely possible for the critics to be intellectually dishonest at times.
Much of the incredulity over the sudden closing of the science centre comes from people who refuse to believe it should ever be closed - whether it's the risk of a collapsing roof or the reality of declining attendance amid structural decline. Much of the opposition to relocating the science centre to the old Ontario Place site comes from waterfront minimalists who want more of the same green space for the personal enjoyment of privileged downtown residents.
I come at it from a different direction.
I've long argued - long before the Ford government came onside - that the best way to revitalize the waterfront was with an ambitious redesign that relocated a neglected Don Mills science centre to a reinvigorated Ontario Place. I watched the last Liberal government shutter Ontario Place (barely noticed), all the while neglecting the Ontario Science Centre (barely a peep).
To its credit, the Ford government dusted off previous reports - long ignored by the Liberals - that recommended a strong anchor tenant for a waterfront project built not on nostalgia but a future vision, noting that Singapore had similarly relied on a cutting edge science exhibition. Our waterfront deserves something more compelling - not to mention modern - than the decades-old Harbourfront complex.
To be sure, Ford's proposal for a foreign-owned spa with an over-the-top plan for underground parking made it a harder sell with a distinctive smell. The proposed spa rubs people the wrong way.
I'm not a spa person, but if you think of it as a water park by another name, alongside an ambitious science centre attraction, against the backdrop of a beautifully landscaped Ontario Place setting, it will be a crowd-pleaser. Notwithstanding the naysayers.
Against that backdrop, I see the engineering report (and the government's response) not as a contrived scenario but a predictable outcome.
But back to the politics. And the science.
People assume that Ford is pulling a fast one to shutter the science centre before anyone can stop him. That's a misreading of his deal-making with an unlikely ally, Mayor Olivia Chow, to plunge full speed ahead with the relocation.
When the two politicians shook hands last year on an entente to shore up Toronto's financing, Chow agreed to get out of the way - politically and legally. It's already a done deal, so the last thing Ford needed was to trigger a predictable public outcry by moving up the closing last week.
As for the science, it has become urban lore that the Don Mills centre is on par with the pyramids, destined to endure forever. Designed by famed Canadian architect Raymond Moriyama, time has not been kind to the structure that dates to 1969 - nor to its aging exhibits.
In fact, the 52-page Rimkus Consulting Group engineering report, released last week, found the air-filled concrete used by Moriyama in construction - similar to that in more than 100 now-closed British schools built in the same era - is nearing the end of its life. The report, which will be peer-reviewed for a second opinion by another engineering firm, said the risk of rain or snow buildup means the reinforced steel underneath could further corrode and fail.
Engineers quantify risk. So do politicians.
If a collapsed roof killed members of the public, our judgment of the collective failing would be scathing. It was a decade ago, after a rooftop parking lot crumbled at an Elliot Lake mall, and the judicial inquiry blamed "its designers and builders, its owners, some architects and engineers, as well as the municipal and provincial officials charged with the duty of protecting the public."
It's easy to criticize the "provincial officials" - not just the premier - who have publicly defended the closure of the Ontario Science Centre. But when lives are on the line, they have a duty to do the right thing - even if it's not necessarily a politically easy thing.
Follow the science. Mind the politics.
CAPTION: The Ontario Science Centre stands fenced in and closed on June 24, 2024.

Subject: Science; Engineering firms; Permits; Aging; Politics; Politicians; Civil engineering

Document 422 of 500

We're staying the workforce longer, but this law isn't keeping up with the times

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/were-staying-workforce-longer-this-law-isnt/docview/3071490714/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=We%27re+staying+the+workforce+longer%2C+but+this+law+isn%27t+keeping+up+with+the+times&amp;title=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-06-24&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Doug Ford claims he is committed to working for workers and seniors. But in reality, this government continues to leave older injured workers behind by maintaining discriminatory age provisions for workers' compensation benefits.
As the province marks Seniors Month, many older workers are facing the prospect of poverty as their lost wages from the Workplace Safety and Insurance Board (WSIB) are cut off because they turned 65 or because they happened to get injured at 63 or older.
This could be you: Having just turned 63, you have a job you enjoy with no plan to retire. But a workplace injury forces you to stop working. You apply for loss of earnings (LOE) benefits through the WSIB to tide you over while you attempt to recover. However, it turns out you cannot go back to your previous job because of your injury. Suddenly, your only source of income is LOE benefits from the WSIB, which you find out will only last for two years because of your age. For a growing number of older injured workers, this is an all-too-common reality.
The current law only permits workers to access WSIB LOE benefits until they turn 65 or for a maximum of two years if they get injured at 63 or older. As more Canadians remain in the workforce beyond 63, the law is not keeping with the times and the trend of older worker injuries. From 2012 to 2023, the number of WSIB lost time claims for workers aged 60 to 64 increased by 73 per cent - and for workers 65 or older by 164 per cent.
If the government is serious about its commitment to workers and seniors, addressing this age discrimination in the workers' compensation system is a good place to start.
The Ontario Network of Injured Workers' Groups (ONIWG) and injured worker advocates have met with the labour minister and other officials from the ministry over the past two years to push for changes to the law. Unsurprisingly, the PC government has yet to take any concrete steps to address the discriminatory gap between the current law and the reality that many older workers remain in the workforce beyond 65.
For many workers, this is due to financial circumstances stemming from low wages and no or low retirement savings combined with a historic cost-of-living crisis. Just 38 per cent of Canadian workers had a registered pension plan established by their employer or union in 2021. It is clear that Ontario's workers' compensation system must respond to this obvious change.
Older injured workers must have access to crucial LOE benefits in a sustainable and just manner. To achieve this, the government must commit to getting rid of both the age 65 cut-off for LOE benefits and to scrap the two-year cap on LOE benefits for workers injured at 63 or older. It is possible to develop a system that accounts for the individual employment plans of workers, as is the case in Alberta and British Columbia. Older injured workers in those provinces can continue to receive wage-loss benefits well beyond 65, if they can prove they planned to stay in the workforce.
Eligibility to benefits should not be tied to one's age, but rather employment intentions. Ontario's law must be amended to reflect this principle. Such an approach recognizes not only the changing realities of Ontario's workforce, but respects the inherent dignity of workers to make important decisions about their futures.
This is what working for older workers actually looks like.
CAPTION: Standing on a bucket truck in 2019, Charlie Roes, 80, said he didn't want to retire from the job he loves at Network Telecom in Kitchener. Many Canadians are working into their senior years, but the rules around employee disability benefits haven't kept pace.
CREDIT: Tebasum Durrani and Chris Grawey

Subject: Older workers; Workers compensation; Wages & salaries; Workforce

Business indexing term: Subject: Older workers Workers compensation Wages & salaries Workforce

Document 423 of 500

Fearing reprisal from Ford, Haldimand bars local MPP from meetings with province: Councillors vote to exclude independent MPP Bobbi Ann Brady over criticism of the government

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/fearing-reprisal-ford-haldimand-bars-local-mpp/docview/3071391421/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Fearing+reprisal+from+Ford%2C+Haldimand+bars+local+MPP+from+meetings+with+province%3A+Councillors+vote+to+exclude+independent+MPP+Bobbi+Ann+Brady+over+criticism+of+the+government&amp;title=The+Spectator&amp;issn=11899417&amp;date=2024-06-24&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.3&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Spectator&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: When Haldimand County councillors meet with provincial ministers at the Association of Municipalities of Ontario (AMO) conference in Ottawa in August, Haldimand's local MPP will not be in the room.
Councillors recently voted to tell Bobbi Ann Brady - who represents Haldimand-Norfolk as an independent - to stay home.
"Noting that she's an independent and her relationship with the premier, I feel it would have a negative impact on our deputation, and potentially on our funding requests," Coun. Stewart Patterson said at the Wednesday council-in-committee meeting.
Haldimand, Norfolk and Six Nations are petitioning the province for hundreds of millions of dollars for a new water system that would connect the three territories.
Saying Brady's presence "would be more detrimental to the county," Patterson asked staff to send the MPP a letter saying Haldimand's meetings with the province at AMO "are considered private, and that council does not wish (Brady) to attend."
Premier Doug Ford has no love for Brady, who was former Haldimand-Norfolk MPP Toby Barrett's parliamentary assistant for 23 years before being tabbed by Barrett to succeed him as the Conservative candidate in 2022.
Instead, the party went with former Haldimand mayor Ken Hewitt, whom Brady defeated in a stunning upset.
Brady has not been shy about calling out the premier at Queen's Park, criticizing Ford's recent appointment of Hewitt to the Ontario Land Tribunal as an example of political patronage. During a heated exchange in April, she accused Ford of focusing on the carbon tax as a "publicity stunt" to distract voters from "the Greenbelt scandal" and "the health-care crisis."
Ford defended his government's record and took aim at Brady.
"By the way, you won't have a job next election," the premier snapped.
Haldimand Coun. Patrick O'Neill wondered if the "contentiousness" between Ford and Brady means projects like long-awaited repairs to Caledonia's crumbling nine-span bridge "get delayed over political influence."
"The premier doesn't dictate if she has a job. The voters do," Mayor Shelley Ann Bentley retorted.
"The bridge in Caledonia's been delayed for 18 years. It has nothing to do with Bobbi Ann Brady."
Coun. Dan Lawrence gave Brady "an A-plus for effort" but said her "antagonistic" criticisms of the Ford government could be "counterproductive."
"You've got to play ball with the provincial government, whether you like them or not," Lawrence said.
Reached by phone on Thursday, Brady told The Spectator councillors are missing the big picture about how she can help advance Haldimand's aims.
"The premier doesn't decide on monies going out the door, and the premier isn't sitting in at every single meeting at AMO. Cabinet ministers are," Brady said.
"And I worked with these people for 23 years."
Brady said contrary to the narrative put forth at Haldimand council, she has a good rapport with Conservative ministers and MPPs, pointing to invitations to appear at a funding announcement for affordable housing units in Dunnville and the groundbreaking for a new Caledonia elementary school.
As Brady tells it, she helped fast-track approval of amendments to Haldimand's official plan, which languished in bureaucratic limbo for a year and a half before she intervened.
"I leaned on the desk of the minister of municipal affairs and housing, and guess what? It was passed," she said.
Meanwhile, in Norfolk County, council has not formally declared Brady persona non grata at AMO, but the MPP was not told of two recent visits by Conservative cabinet ministers, a slight Brady considered to be bad form.
Brady said she is "not convinced" creating an inter-urban water system to connect Norfolk and Six Nations to a giant water treatment plant in Nanticoke is "the way to handle our water woes."
"But I have told our councils, and I've told Six Nations, that if they believe (in it) and it is their wish to proceed with the water project, then I will advocate for them," she said.
She rejected Patterson's premise that being an independent MPP puts her riding at risk of having funding denied for political reasons.
"You tell me what money isn't flowing to either county that's flowed in the past," Brady said. "Everything still flows. There's nothing in jeopardy here."
Ulterior motives
Brady told The Spectator she suspects an ulterior motive in Patterson's motion to bar her from AMO, as it came one day after councillors narrowly affirmed their support for a request to the province for a "minister's zoning order," or MZO, to rezone 4,200 acres of industrial land around Nanticoke.
"For these guys to use the water situation as the reason for censoring me is a bait and switch, to be honest," Brady said.
"I think they don't want me there talking about the MZO."
The rookie MPP was elected on her opposition to a proposal from Empire Communities to build 15,000 homes on land near Stelco's Lake Erie steelworks, and in May 2023 she presented a petition at Queen's Park from residents opposed to the plan.
"She was working against us - a decision we made as a body. That's not supporting Haldimand council," Lawrence said.
Brady contends council's push for the MZO goes against the wishes of voters. But she said she can disagree with some councillors about Nanticoke's future and still collaborate on other challenges.
"So if they want to base our relationship on that one issue, that's their prerogative. That's not how I want to do business," she said.
Bentley called council's decision to bar Brady from AMO "disgusting," noting the MPP "has stepped up to the plate and supported us through this trilateral water issue."
"Whether you like that person or not, or whether she's an independent or not, she's still our elected official," Bentley said.
"And to have her shunned because you think that we're not going to get the funding? Doug Ford wants to build (houses). We're certainly going to get the funding, because he wants to pave over Haldimand and Norfolk."
Patterson said his motion was not about Brady personally so much as the limitations of having an MPP outside the party system.
"It's got nothing to do with personalities. That could be my best friend being the independent member of Parliament," Patterson said.
"I'm looking out for the best interests of the people of Haldimand."
J.P. Antonacci's reporting
is funded by the Canadian
government through its
Local Journalism Initiative. jpantonacci@thespec.com

Stewart Patterson moved the motion to exclude Bobbi Ann Brady from delegations with the province at AMO.Jeff Tribe the hamilton Spectator file photo

Document 424 of 500

They risk their lives fighting deadly forest fires - but say 'scandalous' treatment by Ottawa is bleeding an essential service

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/they-risk-their-lives-fighting-deadly-forest/docview/3071391419/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=They+risk+their+lives+fighting+deadly+forest+fires+-+but+say+%27scandalous%27+treatment+by+Ottawa+is+bleeding+an+essential+service&amp;title=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-06-24&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: OTTAWA-At 27, Noah Freedman is among the most experienced wildland firefighters in his unit. In just eight years on the job, he's become a local union executive and a fire crew leader.
It's a feat that would have been unheard of a few decades ago, he says, but after years of low wages, few benefits and a climate crisis that is making weather disasters more extreme, a grave retention crisis has placed a heavy burden on those on the front lines protecting Canadians from forest fires, and forcing those who stick around to step up.
That's why Freedman believes the clock is ticking for political leaders to address this crisis as another busy wildfire season starts. He's not alone: New Democrats, Conservatives and several unions are calling on the Trudeau government to take the lead and make changes to how wildland firefighters are classified by the federal government to improve their working conditions and reflect the growing difficulties they face in their jobs.
"None of us here in this job exist for the money," Freedman told the Star. "But that doesn't mean that we're not struggling to survive and to justify these sacrifices to our families and to our loved ones."
Although wildland firefighters are provincially regulated, a technical decision in Canada's federal job classification system excludes them from jobs deemed to be in "public safety," affecting their standing under the federal Income Tax Act and directly leading to lower pensions and later retirements.
They are considered "silviculture and forestry workers" rather than "firefighters" like their colleagues working in municipalities across the country, angering many in the field who put their lives in danger every year fighting wildfires.
To one national union, it's a matter of equity. It says the Trudeau government - which has in recent years made an effort to step up its role in combating wildfires - must make a change in the classification.
"Here we are with a group who put their lives in harm's way almost every single day during the fire season, and they're not even recognized as firefighters," said Bert Blundon, president of the National Union of Public and General Employees, which represents more than 4,000 wildland firefighters across Canada. "I see it as a bit scandalous."
Blundon says classifying wildland firefighters accurately would increase their potential pension accrual rates from two to 2.33 per cent, and lower their earliest retirement age from 55 to 50 - benefits that can be available to municipal firefighters but not to forest firefighters.
Even more so, critics charge, the classification is ignorant to the realities of their jobs and puts them on lower footing than city firefighters.
"We just think it's outrageous, and it needs to be corrected," Blundon told the Star. "And it may make it easier for our provincial unions to negotiate an overall better package to protect this group of workers and to treat them fairly."
For now, the Trudeau government can only say it's reviewing the matter, according to a spokesperson for Employment and Social Development Canada.
But the change won't solve every problem. For one, working conditions vary across provinces. And because so many firefighters work a lot of overtime, for example, much of their time worked doesn't count toward their pensions, Freedman said.
"If there was a conversation about this at the federal level and it sort of permeated into provincial politics, I think it would do us a big favour," he told the Star.
That push on the federal level has received political backing. The NDP and Conservatives have called on the Trudeau government to make the change in the lead-up to this year's wildfire season, and the idea received cross-partisan support at the House of Commons public safety committee.
It's all the more reason for the federal government to take action now, Blundon said.
Often when wildfires or firefighters come up in the House of Commons, the Liberals tout their initiative in recent years to fund the training of 1,000 wildland firefighters. More than 600 have been trained so far, but fewer than 100 of them had been deployed in their communities by the end of last wildfire season.
Freedman suggested those efforts miss the bigger picture. "It's always cheaper to keep an existing customer," he told the Star.
"You spend all this energy every year, not just in the fight against fire, but in training the new people who are learning, and then it's all for nothing, and you have to do it all over again."
Not only is it a strain on resources and energy, but also on taxpayer dollars, he said.
"To put it in perspective, last year I had two brand new folks and they were amazing, and then neither of them returned."
Inaction has a cost. In the face of the crisis, provinces are increasing their reliance on other provinces, foreign firefighters, and even the Canadian Armed Forces, despite warnings from a strained military that this practice is not sustainable.
Last year, 5,500 international firefighters came to Canada to support strained provincial efforts. While it's the provinces that bear the brunt of the cost, there have been times the federal government has had to chip in.
In 2021, for example, Ottawa paid around $2.75 million for the flights and COVID-19 isolation requirements of 55 personnel from Australia and 109 from South Africa following requests from B.C., Ontario and Manitoba, documents tabled in Parliament show.
James Gault, executive vice-president of the Alberta Union of Provincial Employees, said the situation has left wildland firefighters feeling that governments look at them as "expendable."
It doesn't have to be that way, critics charge, as they call on Ottawa to show its concern by taking the lead.
"At the end of the day, what it boils down to is the respect for what these guys and girls do, right?" Gault told the Star.
But Freedman and others are growing anxious as they await action while wildfires get more intense.
Asked why he sticks around despite these conditions, he pauses to think.
"It's hard to come back watching these brilliant, amazing young people walk away, but at the same time, the job is rewarding, and in that respect, it is not hard to come back," he said. "Each and every year, all of us have a hand in helping people and changing people's lives for the better.
"That enough is reason to come back."
CAPTION: Noah Freedman, a wildland firefighter in Northwestern Ontario, wants governments across Canada to recognize the dangerous work they do.

Subject: Firefighters; Income Tax Act-Canada; Pensions; Forest & brush fires; Public safety; Job classification; Decades; Employment; Working conditions

Document 425 of 500

This age discrimination needs to be addressed

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/this-age-discrimination-needs-be-addressed/docview/3071391386/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=unknown&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=This+age+discrimination+needs+to+be+addressed&amp;title=The+Spectator&amp;issn=11899417&amp;date=2024-06-24&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.10&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Spectator&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Tebasum Durrani is a staff lawyer at the Injured Workers Community Legal Clinic. Chris Grawey is a community legal worker at the clinic.

Document 426 of 500

Community rallies for Science Centre: Facility's fate has now become a political issue

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/community-rallies-science-centre/docview/3071391372/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Community+rallies+for+Science+Centre%3A+Facility%27s+fate+has+now+become+a+political+issue&amp;title=The+Spectator&amp;issn=11899417&amp;date=2024-06-24&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.8&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Spectator&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Families and community members decried the loss of a beloved institution Sunday after the Ontario Science Centre abruptly and permanently shut its doors last week amid concerns over the state of its roof.
The provincial government already had plans to move the 55-year-old science centre from its current location to Ontario Place when it announced the sudden closure on Friday - a move it said was meant to protect the health and safety of visitors and staff.
The province said it received an engineering report this week that found a number of roof panels "in a distressed, high-risk condition" that could fail under the weight of snow this winter.
A crowd of parents, children, educators and other community members gathered in a west Toronto park Sunday to call on the province to restore the centre in its current location instead.
Children blew whistles and hoisted homemade signs declaring their love for the facility as the crowd chanted "Save our science centre."
Katarina Gligorijevic and Colin Geddes had planned to take their son Sasha to the centre on Friday but had an unexpected scheduling conflict, meaning the eight-year-old wasn't able to visit one last time before it closed.
The science centre was "one of the important cornerstones of our schedule" for Sasha, who is homeschooled and loved to spend hours looking at the frog section, his parents said. "The abruptness of the closure felt extremely unnecessary and cruel," Gligorijevic said.
"To not give the thousands of kids in the city who love the science centre a chance to go one last time ... it was just totally unnecessary and totally unreasonable," she said.
The science centre's fate has become a political issue as well, with provincial opposition legislators and local politicians joining Sunday's rally. Both NDP Leader Marit Stiles and Liberal Leader Bonnie Crombie urged the Progressive Conservative government to reverse its decision to move the centre and instead repair the existing facility.
The current building is facing $369 million in maintenance needs over the next 20 years.
The Canadian Press
CAPTION:
