June 24: City dragging its feet on homelessness, HWDSB needs to adjust its priorities and other letters to the editor

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/june-24-city-dragging-feet-on-homelessness-hwdsb/docview/3071391358/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=June+24%3A+City+dragging+its+feet+on+homelessness%2C+HWDSB+needs+to+adjust+its+priorities+and+other+letters+to+the+editor&amp;title=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-06-24&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Board needs to adjust priorities
Re: Nearly half of Hamilton public schools lack air conditioning (June 20)
It would seem that HWDSB and board trustees have an impossible task to find $52 million to provide a safe level of environmental control to our schools, as our senior students write exams that may dictate their futures in 35 C heat.
Millions of dollars per school is a lot of money. Surely, we have to set priorities in education, like meeting rooms with 80-inch flat screen TVs at HWDSB headquarters and Astroturf football fields at $2 million each.
Ray Truant, Ancaster
Exams are not by definition inappropriate
Re: Are high school final exams still needed? What happens to student grades without them? (June 16)
The Toronto Star's Janet Hurley reports that in doing away with exams, teachers have chosen to evaluate their students' skills and knowledge through a variety of methods other than just an hours-long list of questions. That's controversial because people are worried that missing out on the high stakes exam experience doesn't prepare students for post-secondary assessments, leaving them to spend thousands of dollars of tuition and then flushing it away by providing terribly ineffective instruction and demonstrably unreliable evaluation. How is our society well served by this practice?
That said, students need to arrive at college and university with some tools to succeed. Hurley reports "university exams, due to class sizes and cost efficiencies, often consist of 500 students in a lecture hall doing a multiple-choice evaluation worth 50 per cent of their grade." Isn't that the real story? Universities provide terrible instruction to their first-year students. Is there a single university department that has ever validated that their evaluation practices accurately reflect the achievement of their students?
Exams are not by definition inappropriate, and there's nothing wrong with challenging students to do difficult, time-limited tasks, but we must recognize the result may not always be a reflection of a student's ability. Let's agree that our goal is not to weed out students, but to require our young people to learn and grow and become great citizens. Let's demand great outcomes and prepare our young people to be successful contributors to society. Don't treat them like the raw material for your academic threshing machine.
Phil Davison, Burlington
City dragging its feet on homelessness
Which is worse? Living in a tent during the heat of the summer or living in a tent during the cold of the winter?
The municipal election took place 20 months ago and shelter for homeless people was identified as a high-priority issue. It is inconceivable that the new city council has been unable to find even an interim solution, let alone a permanent solution to the issue.
If a location for HATS (Hamilton Alliance of Tiny Shelters) cannot be found in a residential area, it is necessary to look farther afield. Other cities have found creative solutions. One solution was to locate the shelters in an industrial area, away from residential areas, parks, schools, etc.
How long can we allow this shameful situation to continue? For the dignity of all Hamiltonians, please, it is time to find solutions.
Hinda Levine, Hamilton
Frustrated residents feeling ignored
I attended this past Wednesday's general issues committee meeting, where one of the topics addressed was encampments. Many residents who attended were those living close to encampments in wards 2 and 3. All who spoke clearly had empathy for the unhoused residents of our city.
However, the delegates also spoke very eloquently of the fear and distress associated with living close to encampments. The themes of resident concerns were all similar: violence, threats, thefts, used needles, human excrement and large amounts of garbage.
They talked also about the stress on their lives, as well as on the lives of their children, of witnessing open drug use, intimate partner violence and animal mistreatment as well as losing access to their parks.
We left feeling that at last our concerns had been heard by at least some council members. Then to our disbelief, the majority voted not to allow any parks to be exempt from encampments. This includes the tiny space on Strachan that was the subject of such concern by residents last year.
The fact remains that the majority of encampments are located in wards 2 and 3. Consequently, the impact is disproportionately felt by residents in these areas. It's so easy for Coun. John-Paul Danko and others to support no exemptions when it isn't their constituents who are suffering.
We are a group of Hamiltonians who are distressed, frustrated and looking for support with no obvious solutions apparently forthcoming. I am strongly against vigilantism. It is worrisome to hear more and more that some feel this is the only option left.
Elizabeth Ward, Hamilton
Current pricing scheme is unfair
I used 86 cents worth of natural gas in my house last month for hot water. Enbridge charged me $31.65, including $3.64 HST.
I am charged $25.86 each month for the privilege of being a customer. It's listed on the bill as "customer charge." This amount is charged to every customer, regardless of usage and is unfair to customers with smaller living spaces, and/or who have strategically reduced their usage in keeping with the focus on our climate emergency.
I have a friend who switched his heat source to a heat pump, but has a gas fireplace he keeps for emergencies. He pays $25.85 per month, even if he uses no gas.
This is the same business model that Costco uses, but if I don't want to pay the membership fee, I can choose to shop elsewhere. Enbridge is a monopoly. I have no choice but to be their customer.
A flat fee to be a customer penalizes people with lower use - smaller houses, better insulation, lower thermostat setting, etc. - effectively forcing them to subsidize those who use excessive amounts of gas.
This pricing structure needs to be replaced with one that is fair to all consumers, a user-pay model. Roll all overhead into the price of the commodity and charge people for what they actually use. It would be revenue neutral for Enbridge, but fair to all consumers. It would reward those who reduce their use of fossil fuels.
Lee Fairbanks, Hamilton
LRT comments were ridiculous
Re: 'Trainwreck': Documentary focuses on LRT-spurred housing woes (June 18)
I am astounded by this ridiculous and unbelievable statement made by a Metrolinx spokesperson. How can LRT be more accessible if the stops are every nine to 10 blocks apart compared to HSR stops that are two to three blocks apart? Or that people will need to cross traffic lanes to get to LRT at the centre of the street compared to getting on and off buses at the sidewalk? And how can it be more "affordable" when 20 electric buses that would do everything that LRT would do, and more, would cost only $25 million compared to cost of LRT, which has been reported will cost $3.4 billion but will probably end up costing $6.8 billion or more? And there is no logical reason why LRT would result in more ridership.
Stephen Parazader, Dundas
How is this even remotely fair?
Re: Downtown Hamilton congregation opposes heritage status of Philpott church (June 19)
Philpott Memorial Church signs a sale agreement in 2021 only to discover this year the city's "intention to designate." The same thing happened to a young couple in Dundas and the buildings on the south side of Gage Park, now sitting idle. How is this remotely fair or even legal? Most heritage buildings are usually over 100 years old. As such, they should already be on a list that can be referenced by both developers and private buyers in order to conduct their own "due diligence." If it's not listed, then future heritage restrictions are void. Time for the city to be transparent with both residents and developers. Their investment is urgently needed to solve our housing crisis.
David Borsellino, Hamilton
Fashion takes consideration
Re: Trudeau's kitschy socks. Ford's ugly shoes. Social media's fashion Guy ranks Canadian politicians' style (June 14)
Is any Ontarian or Canadian affected by Premier Doug Ford's taste in shoes, or that of Prime Minister Justin Trudeau in socks? Let them have their choices, but no, they are not a fundamental statement about how they see themselves or the world. Such a shallow matter should only be an issue in the fashion industry, one that has negatively affected and wrought havoc on the psyche of too many, particularly young women. Let us at least leave this fluff out of our political commentary.
Mike Brown, Burlington
Big problem, easy fix
Tourists to Hamilton read in the pamphlets about the panoramic view from the Mountain brow, specifically Ridge Road, where the city has made three areas where people can pull over to avoid stopping on the narrow two-lane road while taking in the great view of Hamilton, the bay, Lake Ontario and, on a clear day, the CN Tower.
So, tourists drive into the roomy layby and get the camera ready. I have observed them sitting for two or three minutes with no picture taking. But the city apparently can't find the funds to trim the trees and bushes obscuring the view completely, disappointing many people, tourists and others alike.
The easy fix? Trim the trees and bushes. End of the problem.
Ingo Andersen, Grimsby
CAPTION: Philpott Memorial Church on the focus of high-rise redevelopment plans that involve its demolition.The municipal election took place 20 months ago and shelter for homeless people was identified as a high-priority issue. A reader asks why more progress hasn't been made, like securing a location for tiny shelters as other communities have done.

Document 428 of 500

Why is this provincially elected representative not allowed at meetings with the province?

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/why-is-this-provincially-elected-representative/docview/3071391324/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Why+is+this+provincially+elected+representative+not+allowed+at+meetings+with+the+province%3F&amp;title=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-06-24&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: When Haldimand County councillors meet with provincial ministers at the Association of Municipalities of Ontario (AMO) conference in Ottawa this August, Haldimand's local MPP will not be in the room.
Councillors recently voted to tell Bobbi Ann Brady - who represents Haldimand-Norfolk as an independent - to stay home.
"Noting that she's an independent and her relationship with the premier, I feel it would have a negative impact on our deputation, and potentially on our funding requests," Coun. Stewart Patterson said at the June 19 council-in-committee meeting.
Haldimand, Norfolk and Six Nations are petitioning the province for hundreds of millions of dollars for a new water system that would connect the three territories.
Saying Brady's presence "would be more detrimental to the county," Patterson asked staff to send the MPP a letter saying Haldimand's meetings with the province at AMO "are considered private, and that council does not wish (Brady) to attend."
Premier Doug Ford has no love for Brady, who was former Haldimand-Norfolk MPP Toby Barrett's parliamentary assistant for 23 years before being tabbed by Barrett to succeed him as the Conservative candidate in 2022.
Instead, the party went with former Haldimand mayor Ken Hewitt, whom Brady defeated in a stunning upset.
Brady has not been shy about calling out the premier at Queen's Park, criticizing Ford's recent appointment of Hewitt to the Ontario Land Tribunal as an example of political patronage. During a heated exchange in April, she accused Ford of focusing on the carbon tax as a "publicity stunt" to distract voters from "the Greenbelt scandal" and "the health-care crisis."
Ford defended his government's record and took aim at Brady.
"By the way, you won't have a job next election," the premier snapped.
Haldimand Coun. Patrick O'Neill wondered if the "contentiousness" between Ford and Brady means projects like long-awaited repairs to Caledonia's crumbling nine-span bridge "get delayed over political influence."
"The premier doesn't dictate if she has a job. The voters do," Mayor Shelley Ann Bentley retorted.
"The bridge in Caledonia's been delayed for 18 years. It has nothing to do with Bobbi Ann Brady."
Coun. Dan Lawrence gave Brady "an A-plus for effort" but said her "antagonistic" criticisms of the Ford government could be "counterproductive."
"You've got to play ball with the provincial government, whether you like them or not," Lawrence said.
Reached by phone on Thursday, Brady told The Spectator councillors are missing the big picture about how she can help advance Haldimand's aims.
"The premier doesn't decide on monies going out the door, and the premier isn't sitting in at every single meeting at AMO. Cabinet ministers are," Brady said.
"And I worked with these people for 23 years."
Brady said contrary to the narrative put forth at Haldimand council, she has a good rapport with Conservative ministers and MPPs, pointing to invitations to appear at a funding announcement for affordable housing units in Dunnville and the groundbreaking for a new Caledonia elementary school.
As Brady tells it, she helped fast-track approval of amendments to Haldimand's official plan, which languished in bureaucratic limbo for a year and a half before she intervened.
"I leaned on the desk of the minister of municipal affairs and housing, and guess what? It was passed," she said.
Meanwhile, in Norfolk County, council has not formally declared Brady persona non grata at AMO, but the MPP was not told of two recent visits by Conservative cabinet ministers, a slight Brady considered to be bad form.
Brady said she is "not convinced" creating an inter-urban water system to connect Norfolk and Six Nations to a giant water treatment plant in Nanticoke is "the way to handle our water woes."
"But I have told our councils, and I've told Six Nations, that if they believe (in it) and it is their wish to proceed with the water project, then I will advocate for them," she said.
She rejected Patterson's premise that being an independent MPP puts her riding at risk of having funding denied for political reasons.
"You tell me what money isn't flowing to either county that's flowed in the past," Brady said. "Everything still flows. There's nothing in jeopardy here."
Ulterior motives
Brady told The Spectator she suspects an ulterior motive in Patterson's motion to bar her from AMO, as it came one day after councillors narrowly affirmed their support for a request to the province for a "minister's zoning order," or MZO, to rezone 4,200 acres of industrial land around Nanticoke.
"For these guys to use the water situation as the reason for censoring me is a bait and switch, to be honest," Brady said.
"I think they don't want me there talking about the MZO."
The rookie MPP was elected on her opposition to a proposal from Empire Communities to build 15,000 homes on land near Stelco's Lake Erie steelworks, and in May 2023 she presented a petition at Queen's Park from residents opposed to the plan.
"She was working against us - a decision we made as a body. That's not supporting Haldimand council," Lawrence said.
Brady contends council's push for the MZO goes against the wishes of voters. But she said she can disagree with some councillors about Nanticoke's future and still collaborate on other challenges.
"So if they want to base our relationship on that one issue, that's their prerogative. That's not how I want to do business," she said.
Bentley called council's decision to bar Brady from AMO "disgusting," noting the MPP "has stepped up to the plate and supported us through this trilateral water issue."
"Whether you like that person or not, or whether she's an independent or not, she's still our elected official," Bentley said.
"And to have her shunned because you think that we're not going to get the funding? Doug Ford wants to build (houses). We're certainly going to get the funding, because he wants to pave over Haldimand and Norfolk."
Patterson said his motion was not about Brady personally so much as the limitations of having an MPP outside the party system.
"It's got nothing to do with personalities. That could be my best friend being the independent member of Parliament," Patterson said.
"I'm looking out for the best interests of the people of Haldimand."
J.P. Antonacci's reporting is funded by the Canadian government through its Local Journalism Initiative. jpantonacci@thespec.com

Document 429 of 500

'Something smells bad here': Politicians, leaders hold rally to save Ontario Science Centre and promise to pressure Ford government to reverse decision

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/something-smells-bad-here-politicians-leaders/docview/3071391401/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=%27Something+smells+bad+here%27%3A+Politicians%2C+leaders+hold+rally+to+save+Ontario+Science+Centre+and+promise+to+pressure+Ford+government+to+reverse+decision&amp;title=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-06-23&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Provincial and municipal politicians of nearly all stripes vowed Sunday to resist Doug Ford's decision to abruptly shutter the Ontario Science Centre and eventually relocate it to Ontario Place.
Speaking at a rally on Sunday, both Ontario NDP Leader Marit Stiles and Ontario Liberal Leader Bonnie Crombie, alongside several MPPs and city councillors, criticized the Ford government's decision and said the fight isn't over.
"The government needs to reverse this," Stiles told the Star in a short interview during the rally, which was attended by roughly 250 people at Wells Hill Park, near Bathurst Street and St. Clair Avenue West.
"I think (Ford) is underestimating what this means to people."
In a surprise decision Friday afternoon, the province announced the Science Centre was closing immediately and would not reopen at its current Don Mills location, following an engineering report that found structural issues with the building's roof.
The report said the facility was safe at least until the end of October, but a heavy snow or rain could increase the risk of a sudden collapse of a small percentage of roof panels.
"Any risk of collapse is too much," a senior government official told the Star's Robert Benzie and Rob Ferguson on Friday.
Stiles accused the Ford government on Sunday of "concocting an excuse" to shut down the popular tourist attraction in order to hasten its controversial relocation.
"Something smells bad here," she said. "I believe this is just the latest in a long list of scandals this government has been involved in, and just like with the Greenbelt, it has mobilized people who are concerned."
The Ford government did not respond to a question about Stiles' comments.
A government spokesperson said the decision to close the facility - recommended by the Ministry of Infrastructure and the Science Centre's CEO - was made to protect the health and safety of staff and visitors, while giving staff enough time to safely preserve and store exhibits.
In a statement announcing Friday's decision, the province said it would be releasing a Request for Proposals to identify a temporary location for the Science Centre while "work continues" to build a permanent home at Ontario Place. They estimate the new location could reopen "as early as 2028."
The Ford government announced plans to move the Science Centre to Ontario Place last year, drawing sharp criticism from city councillors and opposition MPPs. They later released a report that found the iconic building, opened in 1969 and designed by the late Raymond Moriyama, was nearing the end of its useful life and relocating it would save more than $250 million over the next 50 years.
Several speakers at Sunday's rally questioned the rationale for Friday's decision, and blamed the Ford government for the Science Centre falling into disrepair.
"The reason he's doing this is to justify the $600-million parking lot (and) the Therme spa that we'll be building on public land," Crombie said during the rally, referring to the province's Ontario Place redevelopment plans that feature a private, water-themed spa.
Among the crowd at Sunday's rally was Saren Singham, 38, with his 2-year-old son, Amaren.
Singham said he and his partner have been taking Amaren to the Science Centre on a monthly basis.
"He loves it," Singham said, gesturing to his son. "It seems like such a missed opportunity not to fix it."
Singham said the Science Centre's current location at Don Mills and Eglinton is "so important" for families like his that live in the area and can't easily travel downtown. It's also one of the few attractions in the city suitable for children of all ages, he said.
"Especially at this age, they're so curious, to get them interested in the sciences is so special."
He also worries about how long it will take to reopen and the years his son won't be able to visit.
"I just hope there's enough community support to put pressure on the government to change their mind."
CAPTION: "This is just another story of neglect," said Bonnie Crombie, leader of the Ontario Liberal Party, who blamed the Ford government for the Science Centre falling into disrepair. Crombie was among the opposition politicians who spoke at Sunday'sSave theScienceCentre rally at Wells Hill Park.Saren Singham and his 2-year-old son, Amaren, attended Sunday's Save the Science Centre rally.Roughly 250 people attended the Save the Science Centre rally on Sunday at Wells Hill Park, two days after the province announced it was abruptly shuttering the popular tourist attraction because of structural issues with the building's roof.Roughly 250 people attended the Save the Science Centre rally on Sunday at Wells Hill Park, two days after the province announced it was abruptly shuttering the popular tourist attraction because of structural issues with the building's roof.Roughly 250 people attended the Save the Science Centre rally on Sunday at Wells Hill Park, two days after the province announced it was abruptly shuttering the popular tourist attraction because of structural issues with the building's roof.

Document 430 of 500

Dental groups reject Liberal claim that Conservatives are fuelling opposition to new plan - instead claiming it's Ottawa spreading misinformation

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/dental-groups-reject-liberal-claim-that/docview/3071280954/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Dental+groups+reject+Liberal+claim+that+Conservatives+are+fuelling+opposition+to+new+plan+-+instead+claiming+it%27s+Ottawa+spreading+misinformation&amp;title=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-06-23&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: OTTAWA-The heads of several dental groups are rejecting a Liberal minister's framing that the federal Conservatives are to blame for oral health providers' opposition to aspects of Ottawa's dental care plan.The dismissal of Health Minister Mark Holland's comments, which were made earlier this week, are the latest source of friction between Canada's dental associations and Prime Minister Justin Trudeau's government over the rollout of what's been deemed a "historic" dental coverage program.Several dental association presidents told the Star that the Conservative party has nothing to do with their outstanding questions over implementing the plan, while Liberal and NDP messaging around the program do. "There has been zero pressure, really, from the Conservatives on us in any kind of fashion that would result in the low uptake of dentists registering for the program," said Dr. Robert Wolanski, president of the British Columbia Dental Association."The misinformation that has been provided has been provided almost solely by ... the Liberal government and the NDP," Wolanski said, of a program that was born out of the minority Liberals' parliamentary alliance with the New Democrats. During a news conference on Tuesday, Holland suggested that the country's dental associations were "actively seeking" to oppose the plan because the Conservative party was against its success and was "calling these dental associations and screaming at them and getting angry at them." "If you're a conservative party, (and) you want to run against taking dental care away, fine. But calling around and bullying and creating all of this negativity, you know, I think that's what the underlying force is," Holland said.Both the Canadian Dental Association (CDA) and the Conservative party have denied the minister's claims, which Holland says are rooted in fears that Pierre Poilievre would be at a political disadvantage should the program be viewed as a success. Wolanski said provincial and territorial dental associations have had "very little" contact with the federal Conservatives over the past two years, and that their health critic, Stephen Ellis, only reached out to them in April to have conversations Wolanski characterized as "inquisitive, open and respectful."The meetings mostly saw Ellis ask questions about how the program works and dentists' concerns, Wolanski said.While dental groups support the goals of the program - which is open to seniors and will expand to include eligible adults with disabilities and children under the age of 18 on June 27 - they say they still have questions about language in the plan's agreements, patient privacy and the claims process, among other concerns.They've also taken issue with messaging that suggests the program is entirely free and offered to any Canadian for any service at no cost. Holland has said he's never misguided Canadians about how the program actually works, and told the Star earlier this month that after many talks with dental groups and attempts to rectify their concerns, he no longer understands what their issues are.The minister said Tuesday that more than 200,000 seniors have accessed care under the plan and that "a little over 40 per cent" of oral health providers have signed up to deliver the program.But it is the politicization of the issue, Wolanski said, that's a new cause for concern."The politics, to be honest, have been frustrating for us because that's not how ... our minds work. We're about patients, not politics," he said."There were claims of bullying and things like that. I've spoken to every association president across the country, and none of us have experienced that."Dr. Jenny Doerksen, head of the Alberta Dental Association, said Holland is simply engaging in "campaign tactics." "We are not here to be part of this political game that they're playing," she said. The new president of the Ontario Dental Association, Dr. Maneesh Jain, told the Star that dental groups meet with parties of all political stripes. He said Holland's comments took him "by surprise" given the positive conversations he's had with the minister's office. "We've had good discussions. I think the minister has been listening," Jain said.In a statement to the Star, Holland's office said the minister has "heard some comments that aren't fact-based opposition to the program, but more so reflect partisan objections.""We need oral health care providers to get on board, this is an important program that will help nine million Canadians. Conservative MPs continue to peddle misinformation about our plan and vote against it at every opportunity," the statement read.NDP health critic Peter Julian would not directly address Holland's comments when asked by the Star if he had heard similar reports of partisan tactics, but said it's "unfortunate" Conservatives continue to sow "disinformation" about the program in the House of Commons and to their constituents.But it's largely messaging that isn't coming from Conservative quarters about the program's coverage that's prompted the CDA to launch a national campaign educating Canadians about the realities of the program: in most cases, co-payments are required, coverage doesn't start right away, additional charges may apply, and not all services are covered. Wolanski pointed to an April news conference in Victoria, in which Trudeau touted the number of seniors who had registered for the program to receive "free dental care." "We have produced papers for all of our dental offices to help them navigate this process. And one of them includes a new letter that says that the ... abuse of our dental team will not be tolerated," Wolanski said, of the reaction staff receive when patients learn the caveats of the program."We've never had to do that in my 36-year career. Now we do."
CAPTION: Federal Health Minister Mark Holland responds to a question during a news conference in Ottawa on June 18, 2024.

Document 431 of 500

Toronto-St. Paul's voters head to the polls Monday in a byelection being seen as a test for Trudeau's Liberals

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/toronto-st-pauls-voters-head-polls-monday/docview/3071280946/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Toronto-St.+Paul%27s+voters+head+to+the+polls+Monday+in+a+byelection+being+seen+as+a+test+for+Trudeau%27s+Liberals&amp;title=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-06-23&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Decked out in door-knocking shorts and sneakers, and buzzing with the energy of someone who can see the finish line, Leslie Church sounded like a professional athlete spouting clichés before a big game.
"Not taking anything for granted," she said Saturday at a farmers' market following a lightly attended campaign event featuring candidates for the upcoming Toronto-St. Paul's federal byelection. "We're working really hard, right up until the polls close."
Church is the Liberal candidate in the midtown riding, and the perceived front-runner in the high-stakes contest to replace former cabinet minister Carolyn Bennett.
Voters will be heading to the polls Monday in a race that has drawn significant national attention. Once considered the safest of Liberal seats, some political watchers believe Pierre Poilievre's Conservatives, along with local candidate Don Stewart, have a chance to flip it.
If they do, it would be the first cracks in Prime Minister Justin Trudeau's fortress in Toronto and a potential harbinger of what may be on the horizon for his party in the next general election.
Speaking to the Star on Saturday, Church said she isn't thinking about any broader implications of Monday's vote.
"I think that people always try to read the tea leaves in byelections, but the reality is we're filling a vacancy with someone who you want to represent you in Parliament," she said.
The riding, which stretches roughly from Dufferin Street to Mount Pleasant Road, between Eglinton Avenue and Dupont Street, has been in Liberal hands for more than 30 years. Bennett, who was elected nine consecutive times, won it by more than 20 percentage points in 2021.
But given Trudeau's plummeting popularity and Poilievre's commanding lead in national polls some Liberals fear Toronto-St. Paul's could slip from their grasp.
The latest polling data compiled by Abacus Data for the Star show Conservative support at 36 per cent nationally, compared to the Liberals' 19 per cent - the party's lowest share of committed voters since 2015.
Church, a longtime Liberal staffer, downplayed the suggestion that the election could be a bellwether for the national race.
"This is just about, who do you trust to be the strongest voice to deliver on your priorities?"
At Saturday's event, Church mostly spoke about her priorities - housing affordability, investing in health care and the Liberals' school food program - but she also took an opportunity to issue a warning about what a Poilievre government might look like.
In response to a question about the province's decision to abruptly close the Ontario Science Centre, Church said that if you disagree with Doug Ford's government, you should be "equally if not more concerned" about Poilievre.
"He said he's going to shut down the CBC, he's going to defund our arts and culture," she told the small crowd. "So the Science Centre will be the beginning."
Stewart, the Conservative candidate in the race, did not attend Saturday's event and has kept a low media profile throughout the campaign. He declined the Star's interview request at his campaign office on Saturday, as he has previous interview requests.
Joining Church and Stewart on the ballot are 82 other candidates - the most ever, according to Elections Canada. They include NDP candidate Amrit Parhar, the Green Party's Christian Cullis and People's Party of Canada's Dennis Wilson - who all attended Saturday's campaign event in June Rowlands Park - as well as dozens of people who signed up as part of the Longest Ballot Committee, which is trying to draw attention to Trudeau's broken promise on electoral reform.
Last week, Elections Canada estimated nearly 10,800 people cast a ballot in the advance polls.
Sara Spencer, an undecided voter who attended Saturday's event, said that while she understood the national implications of Monday's vote, she still saw it as a local race, not a contest between party leaders.
"I have to vote for the person, the person that sees what I see," she said.
A self-described political junkie, Spencer said she was a longtime supporter of Bennett, but she has grown frustrated with Trudeau's leadership. After hearing some of the candidates speak on Saturday she said she felt a little clearer about who she would vote for. She wouldn't divulge which way she was leaning, but she said she would be shocked if the longtime Liberal riding went Conservative.
"Then again, you never know."
CAPTION: The Appletree Group held an all-candidates "meet and greet" on Saturday for the Toronto-St. Paul's byelection. Voters head to the polls on Monday.Liberal Leader Justin Trudeau, centre, speaks to supporters at a campaign volunteer event for Liberal candidate for Toronto-St. Paul's, Leslie Church, in Toronto on Thursday, May 30, 2024.Three of the 84 candidates in the Toronto-St. Paul's federal byelection, from left, Liberal Leslie Church, Conservative Don Stewart and New Democrat Amrit Parhar.Toronto-St. Paul's Liberal candidate Leslie Church speaks at June Rowlands Park in Toronto on Saturday.

Document 432 of 500

It doesn't matter how this Toronto byelection shakes out - every scenario is some kind of bad for Justin Trudeau's Liberals

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/doesnt-matter-how-this-toronto-byelection-shakes/docview/3071280935/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=It+doesn%27t+matter+how+this+Toronto+byelection+shakes+out+-+every+scenario+is+some+kind+of+bad+for+Justin+Trudeau%27s+Liberals&amp;title=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-06-23&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: In a recurring feature, Susan Delacourt, a small-l liberal, and Matt Gurney, a small-c conservative, bring their different perspectives - and shared commitment to civil disagreement - to the political debates of the moment.
Matt Gurney: There's a byelection in the riding of Toronto-St. Paul's on Monday - typically a pretty safe red seat. I'll start with a couple of confessions. First, I normally hate talking about byelections. I think they're usually stories we just use to fill airtime and column inches and don't really mean much. You can draw conclusions from 338 simultaneous elections - more, now, I guess - but not much from one at a time. But I think this one matters because there's no good-news scenario for the Liberals here. They either lose a seat they shouldn't ever lose, and that's bad, or they barely hold a seat they shouldn't ever lose, and that's bad. Or they win it, and everyone is shocked and relieved, and that's a kind of bad, too. So I'd welcome your thoughts on that. But also, confession two. I'm taking time off next week, and I'm not going to be around to say much about whatever happens. I'll just read your reaction.
Susan Delacourt: I spent some time in Toronto-St. Paul's earlier this month and my sense was that it was looking pretty bad for the Liberals. Lawn signs may not be an accurate predictor, but on the streets where I was roaming, the blue ones far outnumbered the red ones. There is no sugar-coating that. I talked to someone who had been knocking on doors, who reported many people saying that Justin Trudeau had to go. My colleague Tonda MacCharles did her own reconnaissance and aptly painted that bleak picture in a story last weekend. One thing that surprised me in that story was the Conservative strategist who said they still expected to lose Toronto-St. Paul's. That sounded like spin to me, laying the groundwork for Conservatives to claim in victory that Pierre Poilievre had somehow defied the odds. Last time I checked, the odds were massively in favour of a Conservative win. They're 20 per cent ahead in the latest Abacus poll, for heaven's sake. I'm not buying the suggestion that they're the underdogs in this byelection.
MG: That's interesting. I honestly don't have a prediction. I read the polls so, yeah, I get it. But I always assume that in Canadian politics, the least interesting thing is usually the most likely thing. And the least interesting thing, I think, would be the Liberals hanging on by any margin. "Red seat stays red, film at 11." But I have friends in that riding and they agree entirely with your view and Tonda's. As for Conservatives I've talked to, I think caution has been morphing gradually into an expectation that yeah, they'll win. And that in turns leads to a realization they're going to have to defend the damn riding in a year or so.
SD: I am not making any predictions either. It's perhaps a reflection of how long I've been in this crazy business, but I don't see Toronto-St. Paul's as a Liberal stronghold. It was a Carolyn Bennett stronghold; she owned that constituency from the moment she decided to give up her job as a popular family physician and jump into politics in 1997. But I also remember it as Barbara McDougall's riding in the 1980s, a solid blue Progressive Conservative bastion in Brian Mulroney's era. I'm intrigued by the caution you heard from Conservatives. It tells me Poilievre's team is far more attached to the idea of playing offence than defence in the next year.
MG: There's a whole crop of homeowning (if they're wealthy!), job-holding, kid-having voters who have only seen a Liberal hold that riding, and it's in a pretty red part of the city. I understand it's a riding that can go blue, but it only really goes blue if the blue team is basically coming as close to running the board as it can reasonably ever hope. And like I said above, every scenario is bad for the Liberals. I don't know how the summer is going to go at party headquarters, but it might be awkward at times.
SD: When I interviewed Trudeau earlier this year, I asked him who he consults in tough times. He said, "Define 'tough times,' because that sounds like 'Monday' to me." That will be this Monday, for sure. Actually, though, I've been thinking about another quote, from Bill Clinton, who famously said that Democrats need to fall in love, while Republicans need to fall in line. When I spoke to some people in Toronto-St. Paul's, they said there's no Poilievre-mania out there - the kind of enthusiasm one might expect for a leader with those soaring poll numbers. But maybe Conservatives don't need voters to fall in love, they just need them to fall in line. And that does mean Toronto-St. Paul's is a test for them, too.
MG: Two summers ago, I observed a palpable shift in attitudes toward the Liberals in my own Toronto riding, which is just a few minutes from Toronto-St. Paul's. I know the polling didn't begin to move until about a year after that, but I felt something happening sooner. I'd be watching my daughter play soccer and some other parent would randomly confide in me, "I usually vote Liberal but I'm done with Trudeau." And I'd just sort of smile and nod and try to return my attention to the game, but that kept happening. I know that anecdotes aren't data, but after like the fifth time this happened, I began to realize that something was afoot. And maybe a perfect way of putting it is that people the Liberals count on, especially here in the Centre of the Universe, fell out of love. And then about a year later, they started falling in line. And the polls spotted that.
SD: That's the on-the-ground intelligence I don't have. Interesting. I know you're off next week, but I may try to lure you into a private chat so we can dissect what it all means when the dust settles on Monday night.
CAPTION: Conservative Leader Pierre Poilievre speaks during a rally in Montreal on June 19, 2024.

Document 433 of 500

Naheed Nenshi named Alberta's new NDP leader to replace Rachel Notley

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/naheed-nenshi-named-albertas-new-ndp-leader/docview/3071320957/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Naheed+Nenshi+named+Alberta%27s+new+NDP+leader+to+replace+Rachel+Notley&amp;title=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-06-22&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Former Calgary mayor Naheed Nenshi has officially been named the new leader of the Alberta NDP party, concluding a closely-watched competition to replace Rachel Notley and putting him on a collision course with Premier Danielle Smith.
The results were announced Saturday to a noisy crowd of party faithful in a downtown Calgary ballroom. Nenshi handily took more than half the vote in the first ballot.
There were four contenders in the running. Former health minister Sarah Hoffman, former justice minister Kathleen Ganley and Jodi Calahoo Stonehouse are all sitting MLAs, while former Calgary mayor Naheed Nenshi is a high-profile party outsider.
This is the provincial party's first leadership competition since it's metamorphosis from a handful of MLAs to a political force elected to government in 2015, ending the Progressive Conservatives'four-decade run. The hotly contested race has spurred debate about the nature of progressive politics in the Prairies and whether or not to cut ties with the federal party, as well as to how best take on Smith.
"I realized that I couldn't be a bystander anymore," Nenshi told the Star prior to the vote. "I needed to help, and it was very clear that in Alberta, the help had to come via the NDP."
Nenshi's high-profile entry into the race helped swell the province's NDP party membership fivefold, past 85,000 members, making it one of the largest provincial political bodies in the country.
Nenshi has made no secret about his concerns with Smith, his former college classmate, calling her an "existential threat" to Alberta. He's been sharply critical of the way in which her party has veered more sharply to the right, and policies that include restricting health care for transgender youth, as well as criticizing vaccines and sparring with Ottawa.
Nenshi's victory means the party will say goodbye to Notley as leader, who received a standing ovation and briefly took the stage before the final results were announced.
The daughter of Grant Notley, a previous long-time provincial NDP leader, she led the party to its historic win nine years ago and while in office raised the province's minimum wage, cut child poverty, boosted labour standards and introduced more accessible child-care options.
But after two consecutive losses to the United Conservatives, she announced in January that she would step down.
In her last speech as leader on Friday, Notley encouraged cheering delegates to be ready to rally around whoever replaces her and to fight to win again in 2027.
"So many of us made immense sacrifices to do this work, and today we live in an Alberta that is better for it," Notley said in her final address as leader at Calgary's BMO Centre.
"We can say together: it was worth it."
With files from The Canadian Press
CAPTION: On Rachel Notley's watch, the NDP won government in Alberta for the first and only time in 2015. At an event in Calgary, Notley thanked staff and party members, and, encouraged them to rally to win again in 2027. Document 434 of 500

Bottle shock: Ontario wine industry braces for impact of private label sales in grocery stores

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/bottle-shock-ontario-wine-industry-braces-impact/docview/3071083651/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Bottle+shock%3A+Ontario+wine+industry+braces+for+impact+of+private+label+sales+in+grocery+stores&amp;title=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-06-22&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: It's Saturday night. The in-laws are coming for dinner.
As you stroll the supermarket aisles, pondering which wine to serve with the main course, a few bottles catch your eye. Should you buy that juicy Napa Cabernet Sauvignon? The Aussie shiraz with the kangaroo on the label? (They complained about that one last time.)
But what about this new one, with a fancy, end-aisle display: President's Choice Select Merlot. Why not? It's gotten decent reviews and the price seems right.
That scenario could soon become reality: The Ontario government is in the middle of consultations with industry groups that could see supermarkets such as Loblaws, Metro, and Sobeys and big-box stores like Costco and Walmart selling private label wines as soon as 2026.
It might be revolutionary for Ontario consumers, but not everybody's happy. Ontario wineries and grape growers worry private label wine could hammer their sales, as retailers give their house brands a push. And it's a valid concern: In many European countries, private label wine sales account for well over a third of the market. In Norway, private label wines account for nearly three-quarters of the market. Even in wine powerhouse nations like France and Italy, private label accounts for a third of the market.
Will Ontario supermarkets jump at the chance to sell their own brands of wine? They're not saying yes just yet, at least not publicly: Requests for comment to Loblaw and Sobeys were directed to the Retail Council of Canada, while Metro declined to comment and neither Walmart nor Costco responded to requests for comment in time for the Star's deadline.
But they're not saying no, either.
"Private label could prove useful, but it will depend on the overall policy mix because there are many moving parts," said Michelle Wasylyshen, a spokesperson for the Retail Council of Canada, which includes the major supermarkets as members.
Making the situation even worse for Ontario wineries, supermarket house brands might not even be made from local grapes, according to Richard Linley, president of Ontario Craft Wineries.
"Do we want Ontario to be a captive market for a handful of large producers selling low-cost bulk wine from foreign countries? We would argue that we should be standing up for our own domestic industry," said Linley in an email.
Linley would like to see "guardrails" introduced by the provincial government that would help protect existing wineries. For example, the province could allow Ontario wineries to open up more off-site stores to showcase their own products.
And in fact, the province says guardrails - though it didn't define them - are under discussion now.
"The government will continue to consult with other market participants on the issue, including considering the appropriate guardrails if government introduces private label products no sooner than 2026," Emily Hogeveen, spokesperson for Ontario Finance Minister Peter Bethlenfalvy, told the Star.
If private label wine sales are allowed, Linley would like to make sure that only wines bearing the Vintner's Quality Alliance Ontario logo would be allowed, meaning they're made from grapes grown in Ontario and meet specific quality benchmarks.
"The government has been clear in saying that retail expansion will not come at the expense of small, local producers and we are counting on them to deliver on this promise," said Linley. "If private label is to be brought in at a future date, they should consider sourcing 100 per cent Ontario VQA first."
The head of the Grape Growers of Ontario, which supplies many Ontario wineries who don't have their own vineyards - or don't produce enough grapes on their own - said the potential move toward private label sales comes at a pivotal time for the industry.
With an extra 8,000 or so outlets able to sell alcohol across the province later this year, liberalized alcohol sales could be a huge boon to Ontario producers, said Debbie Zimmerman. But done wrong, it could be dangerous, she added.
"The province has given us this golden opportunity to shape Ontario products for the future," said Zimmerman. "If we don't get this right, and they don't get this right, then all this wonderful work that's been done will be wasted."
Even if they had to meet VQA Ontario standards, private label wines could still spell trouble for the Ontario wine industry, Zimmerman said: After all, churning out bulk wines for supermarket labels isn't exactly a high margin-business.
"I think everybody's a little cautious about private label because they see what could potentially happen. ... It becomes a commodity. It doesn't become an important value that is Ontario," said Zimmerman.
A source familiar with international trade rules and federal government policy said requiring private label wines to be 100 per cent Ontario product would likely be challenged by foreign producers.
In other jurisdictions where private label sales are allowed, they command a big share of the market. In Norway, private label wines account for a staggering 72.8 per cent of the market. In France and Spain, private label wines make up roughly a third of the market. In Alberta, private label wines now account for 10 to 15 per cent of the province's total wine market, and make up anywhere from 30 to 40 per cent of wine sales at Costco, according to estimates from industry insiders. In B.C., the country's other big wine-producing province, private label wine sales aren't allowed, either in grocery stores or big box retailers.
Whether it's wine, cookies or breakfast cereal, there's a basic reason retailers push sales of their own house brands, said Deleo De Leonardis, a retail veteran who was the vice-president for the Sobeys private label business between 2012 and 2017.
"Better margin is a big part of it, obviously," said De Leonardis, who is now CEO of a company which runs authenticity tests on ingredients for food companies and retailers. "The profit margins you can't scoff at. They're usually manufactured at a lower cost, and all of the middle men are out. You're not spending money with a big marketing campaign. You're not spending the big bucks on marketing to launch a brand like Coca Cola."
De Leonardis said a retailer's gross margin on a typical national brand cereal or other packaged product is around 25 per cent. For private label products, that rises to 35, 40 per cent or even more, she said.
While a better profit margin is a big part of private label's appeal for retailers, so is having something that other stores don't, said De Leonardis.
"The big prize is differentiation. Because that product is unique to your store, you're building up a demand and value for your store brand. And it differentiates you from your competitor," said De Leonardis.
Sometimes, the differentiator is almost solely cost, as in the case of Charles Shaw wines sold at Trader Joe's in the U.S. Better known as Two Buck Chuck for its initial selling price, it's not exactly top shelf stuff, De Leonardis acknowledged.
"Was it the best alcohol? No. If you had a good palate, you maybe had to hold your nose as you drank it," said De Leonardis. But other private label wines are actually pretty good, De Leonardis said, pointing to Costco's house brand Kirkland Signature, which sells several different wines, including some from sought-after wine regions, like Italy's Barolo.
With Ontario's minimum pricing for alcohol products, we're unlikely to ever see something like Two Buck Chuck here. But for some brands, the differentiator isn't just price anyway, said retail consultant Lisa Hutcheson. It all depends, she said, on what strategy a retailer is pursuing for its other private label products, pointing to Costco's Kirkland private label products, or PC products at Loblaws.
"Kirkland is different than President's Choice. People would expect Kirkland to be cheaper than they would PC. Because Presidents Choice has spent a lot of time building that brand," said Hutcheson, managing partner at retail consultancy J.C. Williams Group.
And that overall brand strategy, said Hutcheson, could play a role in where the retailers source their wines. A private label whose strategy is focused on price would be more likely to source their wine wherever they can find it. A Canadian company might be more likely to seek out a VQA supplier, argued Hutcheson.
"If it's a Canadian brand like Loblaws I think it would make sense for them to do it with a Canadian vineyard, versus saying the PC Black Label is from the Loire."
As for whether private label wines are likely to sell well in Ontario if the government allows it, De Leonardis has little doubt.
"If private label wines are able to offer good quality at competitive prices ... I can't see why consumers wouldn't flock to it."
CAPTION: Debbie Zimmerman, head of the Grape Growers of Ontario, was cautiously optimistic about recent moves liberalizing the sale of alcohol. "The province has given us this golden opportunity to shape Ontario products for the future. If we don't get this right, and they don't get this right, then all this wonderful work that's been done will be wasted."The government is in consultation with industry groups that could see supermarkets such as Loblaws, Metro, and Sobeys and big-box stores like Costco and Walmart selling private label wines.

Document 435 of 500

Justin Trudeau, Pierre Poilievre and Jagmeet Singh are their own worst enemies. Here's how they can use the summer to turn things around

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/justin-trudeau-pierre-poilievre-jagmeet-singh-are/docview/3071083611/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Justin+Trudeau%2C+Pierre+Poilievre+and+Jagmeet+Singh+are+their+own+worst+enemies.+Here%27s+how+they+can+use+the+summer+to+turn+things+around&amp;title=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-06-22&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: As MPs scurried out of Ottawa's oppressive heat this week for what is likely the last summer break before the next federal election - the fixed election is set for October 2025, but it's difficult to see NDP Leader Jagmeet Singh supporting the Liberals' next budget - many were looking forward to hitting the barbecue circuit and taking time to recharge. But for the parties, the reprieve from the daily to and fro on Parliament Hill is a crucial time for a reset. And they badly need it.
The Liberals
What happens to the Grits this summer may be determined by what happens Monday evening in the Toronto-St. Paul's byelection. Liberal MPs will be hitting the refresh button on Elections Canada's website for results, but not everyone will be wishing for a big win. Some MPs, who've been too scared to tell Prime Minister Justin Trudeau what they're really hearing at the doorstep, hope a defeat or near defeat gives Trudeau pause about continuing, and encourages other Liberals to call for new leadership.
But reports from the field suggest the Conservatives aren't pulling out their vote. Conservative Leader Pierre Poilievre may not want to give the Grits a reason to dump their leader.
Toronto-St. Paul's is a Liberal stronghold and should never have been in question. But Trudeau's unpopularity, a desire for change, and a significant Jewish population in the riding dissatisfied by the Liberals' approach to the war in Gaza has put the riding in play. Expectations were lowered to such a point that any win may be spun by those around Trudeau as a decisive victory.
The prime minister is lucky no leadership candidate is actively working to shove him out the door. Former Bank of Canada governor Mark Carney waits patiently in the wings. He's had conversations with Trudeau about joining the Liberal team, and his arrival would offer the Grits a jolt of energy. Many Liberals are hungry for a realignment towards the centre, and believe Carney can bring them there.
If Carney is serious about a run, he should announce his plans before the dozens of Liberal MPs who are contemplating retirement make decisions they'll regret.
Whether Carney joins Trudeau or waits to pick up the pieces after a Liberal defeat, the Liberals' biggest problem is that they've lost the drive that brought them to power in 2015. When CBC journalist David Cochrane asked the prime minister this week why he wanted a fourth term, Trudeau's response was, "Oh, it's continuing to meet the moment for Canadians." That's not a pitch. Who and what are the Liberals fighting for? They have the summer to find the answer.
The New Democrats
If Singh wants to be prime minister, the NDP leader needs to shift its strategy.
The party's reasoning for signing a supply and confidence agreement with the Liberals in 2022 was to demonstrate to progressive voters that a vote for the NDP wasn't a wasted vote, and that one did not need to vote Liberal to get big things done. With 25 (now 24) MPs, it has delivered on that goal. Dental care and free contraceptives and free diabetes medication - if they get rolled out before the next election - will have been accomplished thanks to the NDP.
But this pitch doesn't work when facing the prospect of a majority Conservative government.
Singh's challenge is twofold. First, he must create space between himself and Trudeau. The Conservatives have brilliantly branded the Trudeau government as the "NDP-Liberal government," cementing in the mind of voters the idea that the NDP is not a change option. Singh must wrestle the mantle back from the Conservatives.
His second challenge is one to which the party seems to have given little thought. If the NDP wants to capture the "anyone but Pierre Poilievre" vote from the Liberals, Singh must be seen to be prime ministerial. That's not an easy task for a leader who has never run anything, who has been abandoned by a quarter of his caucus, and who has only one MP with cabinet experience. Singh must build a team and show a maturity that convinces voters he is ready to govern.
The Conservatives
With a 20-point lead in public opinion polls, the Conservatives are poised to win a majority at a scale not seen since Brian Mulroney's 1984 government .
There is a palpable appetite for change. Abacus Data reports more than 80 per cent of survey respondents want to see the Liberals replaced. Over the past year, the "change" vote has coalesced around the Conservatives.
The only person standing in the way of the party's success is its leader. Poilievre is a bright, partisan and talented communicator, but he can also be very polarizing.
This week, in an interview with CTV, for example, Poilievre repeatedly insulted Montreal Mayor Valerie Plante, calling her "incompetent" and "radical." Poilievre may disagree with Plante's policies and think her too left-wing, but Montrealers still elected her - twice. Should the future prime minister be publicly insulting the mayor of Canada's second-largest city? Poilievre sought to define himself last summer before the Liberals framed him up, but he is now hurting his own rebranding efforts. Is he the leader shown in Conservative advertising who talks about building a country together? Or is he a leader who prioritizes waging battles against ideological opponents and further sowing division in the country?
There are 12 weeks left until MPs are back in Ottawa. That's plenty of time for the parties and the leaders to reset - if they choose to.
CAPTION: Prime Minister Justin Trudeau makes an announcement at Northumberland High School in Alma, N.S. on June 20, 2024.

Subject: Conservatism; Leadership; Election results; State elections; Voters; Prime ministers; Political advertising

Document 436 of 500

Looking for summer festival fun not far from the GTA? Take these music road trips

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/looking-summer-festival-fun-not-far-gta-take/docview/3071083579/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Looking+for+summer+festival+fun+not+far+from+the+GTA%3F+Take+these+music+road+trips&amp;title=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-06-22&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: For the third-biggest concert market in North America, Toronto has had trouble sustaining a weekend destination music festival, like Governors Ball in New York, Lollapalooza in Chicago, or Osheaga in Montreal.
It's not for lack of trying: from V Fest on Toronto Island to Field Trip at Fort York to Bestival in Woodbine Park, there have been many attempts over the past two decades to stage mega-fests in this city, but none have stuck around long enough to become annual fixtures that attract music fans from around the world.
Of course, Toronto is hardly lacking for multi-artist summer events, though these tend to take the form of club-hopping discovery experiences (Canadian Music Week, NXNE), neighbourhood gatherings (Beaches Jazz Fest), week-long citywide programming loosely affiliated by branding (Toronto Jazz Fest, Luminato) and selective genre-focused affairs (like the EDM weekender Veld).
For whatever reason, Torontonians seem largely resistant to the concept of hanging out with thousands of fellow music lovers all day in an open field, bouncing between stages to take in an eclectic array of sounds, and passing out on a blanket from sunstroke or too much beer. This either makes us the least fun or most sensible city on the continent.
But if that authentic outdoor summer-festival experience is what you crave, there are several options just outside the GTA to help fill that Coachella-sized void. And most of these events offer the opportunity to see popular artists in more intimate and unique environments than you might experience in Toronto, with less traffic hassle.
Born & Raised
Dates: July 5-6
Location: Montebello Park, St. Catharines
Approximate drive time from Toronto: Two hours
Ticket price: $121 per day
Can I camp out there? No
What to expect: Born & Raised launched two years ago in the quaint inner-city environs of Montebello Park to celebrate St. Catharines' two biggest musical exports: screamo heroes Alexisonfire and folk-rock institution City and Colour, both of which feature Dallas Green on lead vocals and guitar. The two acts will once again headline separate days on bills that nicely contrast and complement one another: City and Colour perform on July 5 with Canadian indie-rock favourites Metric and Sloan, and Alexisonfire will turn the rose-lined park into a raging mosh pit on July 6 alongside fellow early-2000s survivors the Used and electro-punk phenom Ekkstacy, among others.
Mariposa Folk Festival
Dates: July 5-7
Location: J.B. Tudhope Memorial Park, Orillia
Approximate drive time from Toronto: Two hours
Ticket price: $149-$189 for day passes, $249 for weekend passes
Can I camp out there? Yes
What to expect: Mariposa has endured for over five decades by recognizing that folk music isn't merely an acoustic-based sound, but a mode of straight-from-the-heart storytelling that transcends genre. So while this year's lineup is hardly lacking for seasoned troubadours like Bruce Cockburn and William Prince, it also accommodates the rustic indie rock of Band of Horses and Okkervil River, Americana ensemble Old Crow Medicine Show, DIY prankster B.A. Johnston, and Canadian rap royalty like Maestro Fresh Wes and Shad.
Because Beer
Dates: July 12-14
Location: Pier 4 Park, Hamilton
Approximate drive time from Toronto: One hour
Ticket price: $45 for day passes (includes two beer tastings), $99 for weekend passes (includes six beer tastings)
Can I camp out there? No
What to expect: Over the past decade, Because Beer has functioned primarily as a craft-brew tasting event with a small ancillary music tent that has more often served as the background soundtrack to boozy revelry rather than a festival focal point. But this year, organizers are exploding the format like a shaken-up can of PBR. This newly expanded three-day version of Because Beer doubles as an all-star showcase for some of Ontario's premier punk and indie acts, including PUP, the soon-to-be-retired Tokyo Police Club, Daniel Romano's Outfit and Toronto's own Billy Talent - whom you'll be able to see here for way cheaper and in a much cozier and picturesque waterfront setting than the other Southern Ontario festival they're headlining this summer (more on that later).
Hillside Festival
Dates: July 19-21
Location: Guelph Lake Island, Guelph
Approximate drive time from Toronto: Two hours
Ticket price: $75-$95 for day passes, $189-$209 for weekend passes
Can I camp out there? Yes
What to expect: Since 1984, Hillside has served as the rare music festival that encourages attendees to pack bathing suits. Located on the shores of the Guelph Lake conservation area, Hillside is less a concert event than an off-the-grid retreat into an Edenic hippie haven, complete with a green-roofed main stage, vegan-food vendors and late-night drum circles. And that carefree vibe is reflected in the anything-goes programming: At no other festival will you find British folk-guitar master Richard Thompson alongside D.C. punk-jazz ensemble the Messthetics, West African supergroup Benin International Musical and synth-pop party-starter Rich Aucoin.
Riverfest Elora
Dates: August 16-18
Location: Bissell Park, Elora
Ticket price: $85-$95 for day passes, $155-$550 for weekend passes.
Approximate drive time from Toronto: One hour and 45 minutes
Can I camp out there? Yes, at the nearby Elora Gorge
What to expect: Beginning as a backyard party back in 2009, Riverfest Elora has gradually grown into the sort of event that can lure A-list alt-rock acts up to the banks of the Grand River for exclusive appearances. This year's lineup is topped by busker-punk pioneers the Violent Femmes and indie-folk icons Fleet Foxes. Further down the bill are several other internationally acclaimed artists - like raucous rockers Bully, violin-wielding R&B queen Sudan Archives and Jurassic 5 alumni Chali 2na and Cut Chemist - you won't see at any other Southern Ontario venue this summer.
Boots and Hearts
Dates: August 8-11
Location: Burl's Creek, Oro-Medonte
Ticket prices: $89.99-$249.99 for day passes, $329.99-$669.99 for weekend passes
Approximate drive time from Toronto: Two hours and 15 minutes
Can I camp out there? Yes
What to expect: Where Burl's Creek's genre-agnostic Wayhome Festival (2015-2017) failed to become our very own Bonnaroo North, Boots and Hearts has absolutely thrived at the same site since 2012 by being ruthlessly precise in hitting its target demographic: country music lovers. This year's edition once again corrals some of Nashville's biggest guns - including Jason Aldean, Thomas Rhett and Cody Johnson - but it also acknowledges that the sound of modern country owes more to Rob Thomas than Johnny Cash, which explains the appearance by the former's '90s alt-pop band, Matchbox Twenty.
All Your Friends Fest
Dates: August 23-24
Location: Burl's Creek, Oro-Medonte
Ticket prices: $119-$269 for day passes, $249-$475 for weekend passes
Approximate drive time from Toronto: Two hours and 15 minutes
Can I camp out there? Yes
What to expect: Where flagship festivals like Coachella have experienced a dip in popularity south of the border, nostalgia-driven niche events - like the '80s-goth gathering Cruel World and the 2000s-indie celebration Just Like Heaven - have become hot tickets. In that spirit, the inaugural All Your Friends Fest will function as a high-school reunion for pop-punk-lovin' Millennials, with a lineup - Fall Out Boy, Jimmy Eat World, Dashboard Confessional, the All-American Rejects - that resembles the T-shirt wall at Hot Topic. (And if you miss Billy Talent at Hamilton's Because Beer, here's your other chance to see them in action this summer.)
Artpark
Dates: Summer concert season runs until September 7
Location: Lewiston, N.Y.
Ticket prices: Varies according to event
Approximate drive time from Toronto: Two hours and 15 minutes
Can I camp out there? No
What to expect: Artpark isn't a festival, but a rather pastoral, non-corporatized venue alternative (complete with interactive sound-sculpture garden!) to Budweiser Stage, located a stone's throw over the border in Lewiston. Its outdoor 10,000-capacity concert bowl - essentially a steep grassy hill - often hosts tours that don't make their way up into Canada: This summer's area exclusives include the Flaming Lips, Gin Blossoms, Stray Cats and Blues Traveler.
CAPTION: Sloan plays Born & Raised in St. Catharines on July 5.St. Catharines' Born & Raised festival hosts Metric on July 5.Maestro Fresh Wes will perform at the Mariposa Folk Festival on July 7.Toronto punks PUP will play Because Beer in Hamilton.Bully will rock Riverfest Elora in Bissell Park.Rob Thomas and Matchbox Twenty are coming to the country-themed Boots and Hearts festival at Burl's Creek.Wayne Coyne and his Flaming Lips headline Artpark on July 22.Riverfest Elora, seen here in 2019, will host Violent Femmes and Fleet Foxes this summer.
CREDIT: Stuart Berman Special to the Star

Title: Looking for summer festival fun not far from the GTA? Take these music road trips
