Document 437 of 500

Ontario considers revisions to data: Mayors say undercounting homes by CMHC costs them millions in funding

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/ontario-considers-revisions-data/docview/3070997235/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Ontario+considers+revisions+to+data%3A+Mayors+say+undercounting+homes+by+CMHC+costs+them+millions+in+funding&amp;title=The+Spectator&amp;issn=11899417&amp;date=2024-06-22&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=GA.4&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Spectator&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Ontario is considering revising its tallies of how many homes are built in cities and towns across the province, after some complained that undercounting has cost them millions in provincial funding.
As Premier Doug Ford's government attempts to get 1.5 million homes built by 2031, it has assigned annual housing targets to 50 municipalities and promised extra funding to those who exceed or get close to them.
To qualify for money under the Building Faster Fund, which can be spent on housing-enabling infrastructure, municipalities need to have hit at least 80 per cent of their target of housing starts as calculated by the Canada Mortgage and Housing Corporation.
But the Ontario's Big City Mayors group says there are discrepancies between the CMHC data and their own internal counts, and for four municipalities that were close to qualifying for funding it meant losing out on $23.3 million.
The town of Oakville has records of 2,701 housing starts in 2023, but the CMHC reported 1,752, which put the Greater Toronto Area municipality at 76 per cent of the province's target and it therefore narrowly missed qualifying for Building Faster Fund money.
"We have building inspectors who inspect every poured foundation," Oakville Mayor Rob Burton said.
"We keep records of those inspections: address, description, date, the whole deal. So CMHC has offered many stories over the last six months that have changed from time to time about how they do it and my submission is they're not doing it right when our documented evidence is there to contradict them."
The town of Ajax says CMHC missed counting 324 units in an apartment building. While the CMHC acknowledged the error and said it would include the units in the 2024 counts, it still means the town just missed out on qualifying for $4 million through the building fund, a spokesperson said.
The CMHC said in a statement its tracking involves site visits and it stands by the data.
"We work with all municipalities on an ongoing basis to produce the most accurate and objective data based on our methodology," the federal Crown corporation wrote. "All data is verified prior to monthly publication to ensure that no revisions or retroactive changes are needed afterwards."
Municipal Affairs and Housing Minister Paul Calandra's office said this week that the government is discussing with the CMHC how to improve data and is working with the Association of Municipalities of Ontario to evaluate the concerns and "determine if there is a need for revisions or remedies."
Calandra acknowledged at a legislative committee hearing earlier this month that at the same time the province was engaged in a dispute with the federal government over how it was counting affordable homes built in Ontario, he was hearing concerns from municipalities about the province's own tracking of their housing progress.
"We should be able to better track not only across municipalities, not only affordable housing, but as we've heard through some of the criticism of the BFF funding and how CMHC tracks shovels in the ground, I think we also have to do a better job of how do we accumulate that data from our municipal partners," he said.
Ontario has not yet met any of its annual targets toward its goal of 1.5 million homes, though it came very close last year after it started counting long-term-care beds.
Calandra has previously said he is considering counting student residences and retirement homes, and the big city mayors are asking him to confirm if that will happen, and if transitional housing will count.
The housing starts data discrepancies are just the latest concern from municipalities with the province's system for tracking and rewarding housing progress. They say housing starts shouldn't be the metric at all, no matter how the CMHC counts them.
The Canadian Press
CAPTION: Homes under construction in Milton in 2023. The Ontario's Big City Mayors group says there are discrepancies between the CMHC data on housing starts and their own internal counts.Andrew Francis Wallace Toronto Star file photo

CREDIT: Allison Jones The Canadian Press

Subject: Affordable housing; Funding; Housing starts; Municipalities

Business indexing term: Subject: Affordable housing Housing starts

Title: Ontario considers revisions to data:   Mayors say undercounting homes by CMHC costs them millions in funding

Publication title: The Spectator; Hamilton, Ont.

First page: GA.4

Publication year: 2024

Publication date: Jun 22, 2024

Document 438 of 500

How Canadian federalism has failed Hamilton's unhoused population

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/how-canadian-federalism-has-failed-hamiltons/docview/3070724691/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=How+Canadian+federalism+has+failed+Hamilton%27s+unhoused+population&amp;title=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-06-21&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: The persistent crisis of unhoused populations in Hamilton starkly highlights the failures of federalism in Canada.
At the May 15 general issues committee meeting at Hamilton City Hall, Coun. Nrinder Nann reported that 43 per cent of correspondences to her constituency office concerned Hamilton's increasing unhoused population. While there were inevitable disputes regarding the use of shared public spaces and private properties, the overwhelming majority of the correspondence, she noted, stemmed from a place of empathy. Of the 43 per cent, she posits the largest portion expressed concerns about the welfare of unhoused individuals, while the second largest inquired about ways constituents could assist.
Despite the evident empathy of Hamiltonians, the Ford administration's initiatives, such as the Homelessness Prevention Program (HPP) and investments through the Community Homelessness Prevention Initiative (CHPI), fail to coalesce into a comprehensive provincial strategy.
Ford's approach has been limited to funding and directing municipal service managers to provide local support and housing solutions. In stark contrast, British Columbia has implemented more defined strategies, such as the "Belonging in B.C." plan, which aims to make homelessness rare and brief through substantial funding and co-ordinated efforts with various partners.
During the same committee meeting, Hamilton's councillors expressed frustration over the city's increasing unhoused population, which remains unaddressed by senior levels of government. This frustration is underscored by the financial contributions to homelessness: the municipality contributes $82 million, while the federal and provincial governments contribute only $9 million and $28 million, respectively.
Coun. Cameron Kroetsch emphasized the scale of the problem stating, "this is not a million-dollar problem ... it's a billion-dollar problem." He further stressed, "until the other levels of government step up ... we're going to have to manage this crisis ... The City of Hamilton has no capacity to solve this crisis."
The federal government's National Housing Strategy (NHS), launched in 2017, aimed to address housing issues on a national scale. However, Prime Minister Justin Trudeau has often deflected responsibility onto the provinces, asserting that "housing isn't a primary federal responsibility ... It's not something that we have direct carriage of." This deflection, coupled with Premier Doug Ford's silence - likely due to distractions such as conflicts with LCBO union workers and escalating cabinet and parliamentary assistant expenses - illustrates the failure of federalism to provide a streamlined approach to the housing crisis. This is evidenced by discussions in Hamilton's general issues committee, where Coun. Brad Clarke stated, "What I hear from MPP constituents is that the city is responsible ... go talk to them," suggesting that even local MPPs use federalism to deflect responsibility onto municipalities.
This is not to suggest Canadian federalism, with its division of powers between federal and provincial governments, necessarily must complicate housing policy in Ontario through overlapping jurisdictions and responsibilities. Rather, the issue arises from senior levels of government deferring responsibilities to one another instead of collaborating with municipalities to effectively address the myriad contemporary crises affecting Canadians.
The structure of Canadian federalism, characterized by a division of powers enshrined in the Constitution Act, 1867, often leads to jurisdictional disputes. Housing policy, while historically a provincial responsibility, has seen increasing federal involvement. However, this involvement is frequently limited to funding initiatives rather than developing cohesive, nationwide strategies.
The federal government's National Housing Strategy is a step in the right direction, yet it remains hampered by the lack of enforceable mechanisms to ensure provincial co-operation and the execution of comprehensive plans.
A recent IPSOS poll reveals housing affordability is the primary concern for Canadians, while a Canada Food Banks study indicates that up to 25 per cent of Canadians are living in poverty. This underscores the urgency of addressing the housing crisis.
In response, the Ontario NDP has tabled a private member's bill to establish a Homelessness Task Force composed of front-line workers, advocates and unhoused individuals to formulate a co-ordinated and effective strategy. Despite the clear need for expert input and comprehensive solutions, the Ford government has yet to engage with this initiative. Consequently, encampments in Ontario continue to grow, highlighting the ongoing failure to address the root causes of homelessness.
The challenge of addressing homelessness in Canada is emblematic of broader collective action problems inherent in federalism. The lack of cohesive strategy and collaboration among federal, provincial and municipal governments exacerbates the crisis.
It is imperative for senior levels of government to take decisive and co-ordinated action, leveraging the expertise of local municipalities and community organizations, to combat homelessness effectively.
The situation in Hamilton is a microcosm of a national issue that demands urgent attention and a unified approach. The multiplicity of responsibilities and the tendency to defer rather than act collaboratively is a significant barrier to solving the housing crisis.
Federalism, in its current form, must evolve to allow for more integrated and effective governance if Canada is to address the pressing needs of its unhoused populations.
CAPTION: The homeless situation in Hamilton is a microcosm of a national issue that demands urgent attention and a unified approach, Elliot Goodell Ugalde writes.
CREDIT: Elliot Goodell Ugalde

Subject: Federalism; Responsibilities; Funding; Homeless people; Municipalities; Housing policy

Document 439 of 500

Every province should do this: Why Ontario's financial literacy requirement for high school students is long overdue

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/every-province-should-do-this-why-ontarios/docview/3070724677/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Every+province+should+do+this%3A+Why+Ontario%27s+financial+literacy+requirement+for+high+school+students+is+long+overdue&amp;title=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-06-21&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Premier Doug Ford and his PC government recently announced a new financial literacy graduation requirement for high school students.
The requirement is part of a series of new provincial education reforms, including the return of home economics education, consulting on building life skills, and new teachers passing a math proficiency test.
These reforms should help enhance the knowledge and understanding of high school students before they enter university, community college or the workforce. Other Canadian provinces would be wise to keep a close watch on the growth and development of these skill sets, too.
Let's begin with the financial literacy requirement.
"Financial literacy is more than just knowing about money, financial matters and having the skills to work with this knowledge," the Ontario government's news release noted. Students will, therefore, learn how to "manage their personal finances and understand financial decisions" and "save, invest and set long-term financial goals."
There will be mandatory classroom learning about financial literacy from grades 1 through 8 in mathematics to build early knowledge, along with Grade 9 mathematics and a Grade 10 career studies course. Videos and modules for children and parents will also be available.
Students will need to "achieve a mark of 70 per cent or higher" in the financial literacy requirement that will be part of the compulsory Grade 10 mathematics course. This will begin in September 2025.
Anything that improves financial literacy should be regarded as a positive development. As former Minnesota Republican Gov. Tim Pawlenty once said during his tenure as head of the Financial Services Roundtable, "Financial literacy is an important part of avoiding financial mistakes and planning for a strong, secure financial future."
This new requirement will help increase a student's knowledge about financial literacy in several ways, including the accumulation of wealth, paying off student debt and understanding interest rates, RSPs and mortgages. The earlier they learn about the value of money and how to save and invest properly now and in the future, the better.
It would be ludicrous for anyone to object to the Ford government's financial literacy graduation requirement. The introduction of this requirement is long overdue. It's something every Canadian province should require of their high school students long before they graduate and enter the real world.
Similarly, the decision to reinstate the math proficiency test beginning in February 2025 as a requirement for certification with the Ontario College of Teachers makes sense.
No one expects new teachers in Ontario to have the mathematical skills of Pythagoras, Isaac Newton, or Albert Einstein. However, some basic and grade-appropriate knowledge and understanding of math is a good idea. Teacher candidates will likely require some refresher courses to get back up to speed, but they should be able to pass comfortably.
Ontario's Appeal Court, which reversed a lower-court ruling that the math proficiency test was unconstitutional, would surely agree.
"At the end of 2021, 95 per cent of the 8,350 teacher candidates who had written the test one or more times were successful," The Canadian Press wrote about the top court's decision on Nov. 28, 2023, "including 93 per cent of candidates from racialized groups."
In addition, the test will be tied to the financial literacy graduate requirement, which, as previously mentioned, will primarily be taught in math courses.
What about life skills and home economics?
These two components are connected to the "back to basics" mentality revived during COVID-19. They're both critical to a student's development in this day and age.
Learning life skills is extremely valuable. The World Health Organization's 1999 list includes problem-solving, critical thinking, self-awareness, coping with stress, and interpersonal skills, among other things. Mastering these important skills (and others) will help students navigate life as effectively and efficiently as possible.
There's even a link between life skills and - you guessed it - financial literacy. A web page from RBC Wealth Management points out that "there are many important life skills people learn to master as they grow from young children into independent adults ... Unfortunately, money management is often overlooked."
Home economics, or domestic science, was part of the course curriculum when I went to public school. (It wasn't taught at the high school I attended.) Students were given a basic introduction to cooking, meal planning, setting the table, etc. While some called it nothing more than a "bird" course, you have to know how to prepare a bird before serving it!
Financial literacy plays a vital role in home economics, too. As reported by The Canadian Press on June 1, then-Ontario education minister Stephen Lecce suggested provincial students will learn about additional aspects of home economics "such as managing a household budget and how to protect themselves against financial fraud."
Ford and the PCs have, therefore, introduced education reforms related to financial literacy. The math seems to add up.
CAPTION: New financial literacy requirements for Ontario students will have them learn about the value of money and how to save and invest properly, Michael Taube writes.
CREDIT: Michael Taube

Subject: Mathematics education; Financial literacy; Education reform; Secondary schools; Mathematics teachers; Secondary school students; Home economics education; Learning; Skills

Document 440 of 500

Ford government closing Ontario Science Centre today after report found roof in danger of collapsing

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/ford-government-closing-ontario-science-centre/docview/3070724662/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Ford+government+closing+Ontario+Science+Centre+today+after+report+found+roof+in+danger+of+collapsing&amp;title=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-06-21&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: The Ontario Science Centre is being closed immediately after an engineering report this week found the roof is in danger of collapsing.
In a stunning move Friday afternoon - first reported by the Star - the Ministry of Infrastructure announced the iconic 1969 Don Mills building designed by the late Raymond Moriyama will no longer be structurally sound within a few months.
That means Friday is the last day of operations for the once-popular tourist attraction that successive Progressive Conservative and Liberal governments have allowed to deteriorate for years.
"The actions taken today will protect the health and safety of visitors and staff at the Ontario Science Centre while supporting its eventual reopening in a new, state-of-the-art facility," said Infrastructure Minister Kinga Surma.
Some 250 workers - including 200 unionized OPSEU members - will be affected but they are not losing their jobs.
Closure of the facility comes against the backdrop of Premier Doug Ford's controversial decision to move the science museum to a new building at Ontario Place as part of the redevelopment of the lakeside park, which includes a privately owned Therme water spa.
Political opponents charged Ford wanted the 55-year-old building to become unusable so he could more easily relocate the science centre to the waterfront.
But the 52-page Rimkus Consulting Group engineering report found that the air-filled concrete used in construction - similar to that used in more than 100 now-closed British schools built in the same era - is nearing the end of its life.
While the report said the building isn't unsafe right now, the risk of rain or snow buildup could make it so sooner rather than later because it would corrode the reinforcing steel underneath.
To that end, it must be vacated by Oct. 31.
The lightweight aerated concrete used in the construction has "the potential to degrade due to water infiltration," the review said.
One roofing panel is in the "critical" category, another six per cent of the panels are "high risk," nine per cent are "medium risk" while the remaining 84 per cent are "low risk."
"A significant snow or rain ... could exceed the reduced load-carrying capacity of the distressed panels, placing them at an increased risk of sudden collapse," the report said.
Rimkus recommended all such roof panels be replaced with steel decking, which would cost between $22 million and $40 million and take two or more years.
"These estimates are incomplete and subject to change," the report warned, noting the diagnosis was made based on visual inspections of all three science centre buildings and did not involve any "intrusive investigation."
Ford's government, which has been heavily criticized over its changes to the science centre, moved quickly due to safety concerns for children, parents and staff.
"Any risk of collapse is too much risk," said a senior official, who was not authorized to speak publicly before the announcement.
The science centre's board made the difficult decision late Thursday.
About 2,000 children scheduled to attend day camps in the next two weeks at the science centre will be given the option of relocated activities at nearby public schools. Their parents will be fully refunded.
A wedding scheduled there for Saturday is being moved to another provincially owned facility and the bride and groom refunded.
The building's condition is worse than the government thought as recently as last November.
An earlier report released by Infrastructure Ontario, in relation to the planned move of the science centre to Ontario Place, warned the ravine-straddling building "is not sustainable under status quo conditions and is in operational crisis due to a failing structure with mounting critical building maintenance costs."
That 62-page report by consulting firm Ernst & Young was dated March 8, 2023 but not revealed until November when it was presented as a "business case" for the controversial move, stating it would cost $600 million more to fix up the old science centre than to build a new one.
The material in question - known as reinforced autoclaved aerated concrete or RAAC - was a popular choice in educational, institutional and industrial buildings from the mid-1950s to the mid-1970s, and was marketed as a "go-to" product because of its light weight and thermal insulation capacities that also made it a good sound and fire barrier, the report said.
Made of an aerated blend of sand, Portland cement and aluminum powder, the product has since "raised concern amongst building managers and owners worldwide due to structural issues," the report added.
"As a building material, RAAC has an overall reduced robustness compared to steel decks or traditional concrete, making it more susceptible to damage from impact and raising the risk of sudden failure .... owners and facility managers are often not aware of the problems ... until there is physical evidence of a failure or deterioration," it said.
"The consequences of RAAC failure include potential building damage and risk to public safety."
Infrastructure Ontario CEO Michael Lindsay said the government concluded it "would require significant investment and a vacant facility to remediate."
As the Star broke the news Friday, it was business as usual as the science centre, with the parking lot approximately three-quarters full.
Parking was free as the system was supposedly down and the bus lot was full of school buses bringing students for the last week of school trips.
Two workers were putting up new fencing blocking off the road to the group and bus parking lot.
Front entrance staff were unaware of the closing.
NDP Leader Marit Stiles said "closing a world-class science and cultural institution is heartbreaking, especially when we know the government's real motive here is to justify Ford's mega-spa vanity project" at Ontario Place.
"The government could have invested in revitalizing the science centre, but instead it's using our public money to concoct a sham business case against this important community hub," said Stiles.
The Star broke the news to Coun. Jon Burnside (Ward 16 Don Valley East), who represents the ward and has for months been searching for ways to keep the science centre open at that location or, failing that, something beneficial for the community.
"I'm devastated by the news. I was literally talking to two folks from Flemingdon Park last night who said how important the science centre is to their children's learning," said Burnside.
"There was always a part of me that thought the reports of the structural issues were overstated and could be addressed for a lot less money. It looks like the board of the science centre has decided that's not the case," he said.
"A lot of parents in the area rely on day camps and after school programs. It is a neighbourhood improvement area with a dearth of opportunities and facilities for the folks of Flemingdon. This was such a wonderful resource which is why none of us wanted it to close," the councillor said.
"The problem now is that because it's closing immediately, any other plans to fill that gap aren't in place."
- With files from Raju Mudhar and David Rider
CAPTION: The Ontario Science Centre in Toronto on Nov. 29, 2023.Workers put up fencing around the Ontario Science Centre in Toronto on June 21, 2024 as the Ontario government announced the facility would be closed immediately after an engineering report found the roof is in danger of collapsing.

Document 441 of 500

Cyberattack: Hackers lurked undiscovered in Hamilton's IT network for 20 days

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/cyberattack-hackers-lurked-undiscovered-hamiltons/docview/3070724649/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Cyberattack%3A+Hackers+lurked+undiscovered+in+Hamilton%27s+IT+network+for+20+days&amp;title=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-06-21&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Hackers covertly gained access to Hamilton's IT network nearly three weeks before the city discovered the ransomware attack - but the city maintains there is "no evidence" criminals swiped any data or personal information about residents.
The cyberattack paralyzed many city services starting in late February, affecting everything from city phone lines to transit to access to records for building permits and public health inspections.
The city reported this week it has spent about $5.7 million dealing with the crisis so far, with millions of dollars more likely need to rebuild the still-ravaged network and improve cybersecurity.
Hamilton did not pay a ransom to regain access to its system - but officials at a media briefing Thursday still refused to say how much was demanded, arguing releasing the number could still put the city at risk financially.
Mayor Andrea Horwath pledged to "put that information out there as soon as we possibly can," acknowledging residents are frustrated not to know more about the attack.
"It's not a secret for the sake of it being a secret," she said in a later interview. "It's trying to protect the interests of our city."
The city's spending so far on the cyber-crisis includes almost $3 million on experts and advisers, who first helped to stave off the attack and are now advising on rebuilding and restoration efforts.
Some of those experts, from consultant Deloitte, made a presentation to councillors this week congratulating the city and its partners for being able to "contain" the attack within two days of learning it was happening.
They also said it took 20 days to "detect" attackers in the network. The consultants suggested that shows "increased sophistication" by the hackers, who moved about twice as fast as the industry average to shut down city systems.
Some experts have expressed skepticism about the city's claim that its data went untouched - in this case, for about three weeks while criminals explored the network undetected.
"I've seen enough cases where organizations had to backpedal on their 'no evidence' claim to not have a huge amount of confidence in that," B.C.-based cyberthreat analyst Brett Callow previously told The Spectator.
But the city reiterated its belief that municipal and resident data remains safe.
Acting chief information officer Cyrus Tehrani said the city cannot yet reveal anything it knows about how criminals got into Hamilton's network, a breach that is also under police investigation.
But he said experts have combed through city IT systems and conducted "forensic tracing" of the actions of the hackers and, so far, "nothing shows data being exfiltrated."
Tehrani compared the IT network to a locked safe. "In our case, they removed the combination lock, (but) everything inside is perfectly intact," he said.
Hamilton continues to slowly bring services back online - including a return to normal pay processing for most municipal employees that is expected by the end of this week.
That's a big deal for city workers - including paramedics whose union at one point threatened to refuse overtime hours if proper pay was not restored.
In addition to delayed pay, municipal employees also could not see pay stubs until now, making it impossible to determine if they were being paid the right wage amounts.
Part-time workers faced particular financial pain, with some long-term care workers reporting they had to borrow money to make ends meet because they were not receiving expected pay amounts well into May.
"I'm not going to sugar-coat it; the past number of months have been challenging for many in our community," said Horwath, who thanked municipal staff for working through the crisis under tough conditions.
She said the city is making progress to return to normal, but warned the attack fallout is "far from over," later estimating a full recovery could take more than a year.
Here's some of what we learned at the latest public update:
The city has a cyber-insurance policy, but it is not yet clear what ransomware attack costs might be covered. City manager Marnie Cluckie said the policy is being "reviewed" prior to submitting a potential claim.Hamilton has previously budgeted more than $30 million over the next decade for projects to modernize its IT network and related services. Some of that cash may be tapped to help "fast-track" the rebuilding of lost applications.A few city software applications are "unrecoverable," meaning the city will need to replace or rebuild them from scratch. Those include programs used to accept licensing and business permits and fire department records.Horwath has spoken to Premier Doug Ford about the cyberattack and suggested later meetings to share what the city has learned with the province and other cities.
-With files from Teviah Moro
Matthew Van Dongen is a reporter with The Spectator. mvandongen@thespec.com
CAPTION: Mayor Andrea Horwath has spoken to Premier Doug Ford about the cyberattack and suggested later meetings to share what the city has learned with the province and other cities.

Subject: Municipal employees; Criminal investigations; Ransomware

Document 442 of 500

Justin Trudeau's government is losing its momentum on Indigenous reconciliation, leaders say - and they're worried a Conservative government could be worse

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/justin-trudeaus-government-is-losing-momentum-on/docview/3070415064/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Justin+Trudeau%27s+government+is+losing+its+momentum+on+Indigenous+reconciliation%2C+leaders+say+-+and+they%27re+worried+a+Conservative+government+could+be+worse&amp;title=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-06-21&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: OTTAWA - Nine years after Justin Trudeau came to power campaigning on a new relationship with Indigenous people, Indigenous leaders say his government's once considerable rate of progress is slowing - and they are worried about that momentum reversing if the Conservatives topple the Liberals in the next election.
As they mark National Indigenous Peoples Day, leaders of three national organizations say the reconciliation agenda from Trudeau's 2015 platform has led to greater advancements on Indigenous priorities than previous governments.
Most importantly, they feel greater recognition of their self-government authority and believe there are stronger ties with Ottawa. But Indigenous leaders are also concerned about "going backwards," losing those relationships should a Conservative government take power in the next year, and are currently taking steps to make sure they don't lose their seat at the table.
"Hopefully, with all the changes in this country over the last 15 years or so, it's not possible to be a governing party in this country and still hold those same beliefs or treat First Nations, Inuit and Métis rights holders and rights-holding institutions in the way that the previous government did," said Inuit Tapiriit Kanatami (ITK) president Natan Obed.
"But you never really can tell."
How the Trudeau government has pursued reconcilliation
Since 2015, the Liberal government has pursued a strong Indigenous mandate, including promising to end all boil-water advisories by 2021, an inquiry on Missing and Murdered Indigenous Women, Girls and Two-Spirit People (MMIWG2S), and fulfilling the Truth and Reconciliation Commission's 94 calls to action, including investing more than $30 billion in Indigenous priorities.
Assembly of First Nations National Chief Cindy Woodhouse Nepinak told the Star earlier this year the "trajectory" of Canadians' thoughts on Indigenous people and reconciliation is changing, which she credits to the work done by the Truth and Reconciliation Commission and court settlements like on child welfare.
In October, the Federal Court approved a $23-billion settlement over child welfare discrimination. Then in February, the Supreme Court upheld legislation affirming Indigenous self-governance on child welfare over provincial jurisdiction.
Woodhouse Nepinak said it's important "for people to start speaking about the truth of the history of this country, it's not all cosy and it's not all perfect and First Nations people have been at the brunt of that."
Having worked with ITK and Inuit organizations since 2002, Obed has experienced several successive Liberal and Conservative governments. He said the Trudeau government has "transformed" its relationship with the Inuit.
ITK has seen "incredible advancements in their relationship," including the creation of a Crown-Inuit Partnership agreement, co-development of major legislation like the United Nations Declaration on the Rights of Indigenous Peoples (UNDRIP) Act and forming relationships with more than 20 government ministers.
Métis National Council (MNC) president Cassidy Caron said there has been more progress in the past nine years "than we ever have in our history for the Métis nation," including the signing of its Canada-Métis Nation Accord and "innovative" co-developed legislation with child welfare.
Getting stuck in 'incremental positive change'
The Liberal government has also been criticized for falling short on some promises. In the past year, government reports showed a lack of progress on closing long-standing socio-economic gaps between Indigenous and non-Indigenous peoples, particularly around housing, health care and policing.
Obed said the ongoing challenge with the federal government is "incremental positive change versus transformative change, and we're stuck in incremental positive change in a number of our different file areas." And while the ITK has positive relationships with some federal departments, others "still seem to be able to do whatever it is that they want with funds, including or excluding Inuit in the process."
After this year's federal budget was tabled in April, Woodhouse Nepinak called on the government to make a new commitment to reconciliation with First Nations people, saying Indigenous people are no longer a priority for the government.
Obed and Caron echoed the national chief's renewed calls for reconciliation. Caron said "the reconciliation agenda that was promised in 2015 has gone a little stale," and "a deep reflection" on what should be on a reconciliation agenda moving forward is needed.
"They respect our processes and have a willingness to work with us. However, it's not as full steam as it was nine years ago, even five years ago."
The cabinet ministers responsible for the Indigenous file aren't surprised by that response.
"When you are existing as the colonial partner in a colonial country, I think it's fair to expect all kinds of feelings and observations about the speed of transformation, whether it's fast enough, whether it's enough," Indigenous Services Canada Minister Patty Hajdu told the Star.
Crown-Indigenous Relations Minister Gary Anandasangaree said the agenda set in 2015 was "super ambitious" and Canada's journey with reconciliation is going to be multi-generational.
"We're still far from being in the ideal situation, but we have changed the trajectory of where this country needs to go and we are going in that direction. Each and every year, we're advancing on a range of issues," he said, adding reconciliation is a "one-way street" going only forward.
A turn back toward the Harper years?
With a federal election on the horizon, memories of the previous Conservative government, which saw one of the largest political movements by Indigenous people in decades with the Idle No More movement, has given Indigenous leaders cause for concern.
Obed said the approach taken by the Harper government to "discredit democratic functions of Indigenous Peoples" was something that "set back the work of our organizations, in some cases, by a generation."
Conservative Leader Pierre Poilievre has been making efforts to change his party's relationship with Indigenous people; in 2008, he said Indigenous people needed to learn the value of hard work and cast doubt on compensating residential school survivors for their trauma, comments for which he later apologized.
He now supports searching for unmarked graves at the sites of former residential schools, and announced his support for a First Nations Resource Charge, which would allow First Nations to levy taxes on resource projects on their lands, something he called "a First Nations-led solution to a made-in-Ottawa problem."
Caron said she has good working relationships with the Conservative party's critics on the Indigenous file, and has met with the opposition leader .
But both Caron and Obed said they do not want to "move backwards" by losing the voices they gained through bilateral meetings the AFN, MNC and ITK established with the Liberal government in 2016.
"I think the only way that we continue to move forward is having open and honest relationships and conversations, and if we can't even be at the table with whatever government is in power, that would set us back," Caron said.
Conservative party spokesperson Sebastian Skamski said Poilievre has prioritized meeting with Indigenous leaders, and members of his team "will continue to engage with First Nations, Inuit and Métis" and work "toward real economic reconciliation and support them in taking back control of their money, their resources, and their lives."
Reconciliation on the ballot
Hajdu said reconciliation will be kept on the agenda in the next election campaign and hopes "all parties understand that reconciliation is the right thing to do and it's the smart thing to do."
Her priority for the next year is getting the long-awaited Indigenous water legislation through the House. Hajdu also wants to see more on economic reconciliation through the Indigenous Loan Guarantee Program and including Indigenous people in resource development.
Anandasangaree said housing, closing the infrastructure gap, ending the MMIWG2S crisis and self-government through co-developed legislation and modern treaties will continue to be his priorities.
"These are things that we need to continuously work towards, and every government that comes after us - it could be in a year, it could be in 20 years or 30 years - need to continue on this direction and if anything, every government needs to double their efforts," he said.
Obed and Caron said implementing UNDRIP Act's action plan is another priority, including an Indigenous Human Rights Tribunal, which would serve as an accountability mechanism for UNDRIP's implementation.
The MNC is also focusing on the self-government agreements for Alberta, Saskatchewan and Ontario, despite the delays and controversy they have encountered through Bill C-53.
Obed said ITK plans to continue with its tuberculosis elimination plan, which Ottawa has previously pledged to fund. ITK has asked for $131 million over seven years.
"We don't want the legacies of successive governments to make outrageous promises that then aren't kept," he said.
"I think that is the direct threat that reconciliation faces."
CAPTION: Prime Minister Justin Trudeau speaks with AFN National Chief Cindy Woodhouse, left, and president of the Métis National Council Cassidy Caron, right, after his remarks at the Indigenous History Month reception in Ottawa on June 17, 2024.

Hackers lurked undiscovered in city IT network for 20 days: City still says it believes no data or resident personal information was stolen

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/hackers-lurked-undiscovered-city-network-20-days/docview/3070414904/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Hackers+lurked+undiscovered+in+city+IT+network+for+20+days%3A+City+still+says+it+believes+no+data+or+resident+personal+information+was+stolen&amp;title=The+Spectator&amp;issn=11899417&amp;date=2024-06-21&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.4&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Spectator&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Hackers covertly gained access to Hamilton's IT network nearly three weeks before the city discovered the ransomware attack - but the city maintains there is "no evidence" criminals swiped any data or personal information about residents.
The cyberattack paralyzed many city services starting in late February, affecting everything from city phone lines to transit to access to records for building permits and public health inspections.
The city reported this week it has spent about $5.7 million dealing with the crisis so far, with millions of dollars more likely need to rebuild the still-ravaged network and improve cybersecurity.
Hamilton did not pay a ransom to regain access to its system - but officials at a media briefing Thursday still refused to say how much was demanded, arguing releasing the number could still put the city at risk financially.
Mayor Andrea Horwath pledged to "put that information out there as soon as we possibly can," acknowledging residents are frustrated not to know more about the attack.
"It's not a secret for the sake of it being a secret," she said in a later interview. "It's trying to protect the interests of our city."
The city's spending so far on the cyber-crisis includes almost $3 million on experts and advisers, who first helped to stave off the attack and are now advising on rebuilding and restoration efforts.
Some of those experts, from consultant Deloitte, made a presentation to councillors this week congratulating the city and its partners for being able to "contain" the attack within two days of learning it was happening.
They also said it took 20 days to "detect" attackers in the network. The consultants suggested that shows "increased sophistication" by the hackers, who moved about twice as fast as the industry average to shut down city systems.
Some experts have expressed skepticism about the city's claim that its data went untouched - in this case, for about three weeks while criminals explored the network undetected.
"I've seen enough cases where organizations had to backpedal on their 'no evidence' claim to not have a huge amount of confidence in that," B.C.-based cyberthreat analyst Brett Callow previously told The Spectator.
But the city reiterated its belief that municipal and resident data remains safe.
Acting chief information officer Cyrus Tehrani said the city cannot yet reveal anything it knows about how criminals got into Hamilton's network, a breach that is also under police investigation.
But he said experts have combed through city IT systems and conducted "forensic tracing" of the actions of the hackers and, so far, "nothing shows data being exfiltrated."
Tehrani compared the IT network to a locked safe. "In our case, they removed the combination lock, (but) everything inside is perfectly intact," he said.
Hamilton continues to slowly bring services back online - including a return to normal pay processing for most municipal employees that is expected by the end of this week.
That's a big deal for city workers - including paramedics whose union at one point threatened to refuse overtime hours if proper pay was not restored.
In addition to delayed pay, municipal employees also could not see pay stubs until now, making it impossible to determine if they were being paid the right wage amounts.
Part-time workers faced particular financial pain, with some long-term care workers reporting they had to borrow money to make ends meet because they were not receiving expected pay amounts well into May.
"I'm not going to sugar-coat it; the past number of months have been challenging for many in our community," said Horwath, who thanked municipal staff for working through the crisis under tough conditions.
She said the city is making progress to return to normal, but warned the attack fallout is "far from over," later estimating a full recovery could take more than a year.
Here's some of what we learned at the latest public update:
The city has a cyber-insurance policy, but it is not yet clear what ransomware attack costs might be covered. City manager Marnie Cluckie said the policy is being "reviewed" prior to submitting a potential claim.
Hamilton has previously budgeted more than $30 million over the next decade for projects to modernize its IT network and related services. Some of that cash may be tapped to help "fast-track" the rebuilding of lost applications.
A few city software applications are "unrecoverable," meaning the city will need to replace or rebuild them from scratch. Those include programs used to accept licensing and business permits and fire department records.
Horwath has spoken to Premier Doug Ford about the cyberattack and suggested later meetings to share what the city has learned with the province and other cities.
With files from Teviah Moro
Matthew Van Dongen is a reporter with The Spectator. mvandongen@thespec.com

Document 444 of 500

Canadian federalism is failing the unhoused population of Hamilton: Situation here is a microcosm of a national issue that demands urgent attention and a unified approach

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/canadian-federalism-is-failing-unhoused/docview/3070411858/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=unknown&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Canadian+federalism+is+failing+the+unhoused+population+of+Hamilton%3A+Situation+here+is+a+microcosm+of+a+national+issue+that+demands+urgent+attention+and+a+unified+approach&amp;title=The+Spectator&amp;issn=11899417&amp;date=2024-06-21&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.11&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Spectator&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: The persistent crisis of unhoused populations in Hamilton starkly highlights the failures of federalism in Canada.
At the May 15 general issues committee meeting at Hamilton City Hall, Coun. Nrinder Nann reported that 43 per cent of correspondences to her constituency office concerned Hamilton's increasing unhoused population. While there were inevitable disputes regarding the use of shared public spaces and private properties, the overwhelming majority of the correspondence, she noted, stemmed from a place of empathy. Of the 43 per cent, she posits the largest portion expressed concerns about the welfare of unhoused individuals, while the second largest inquired about ways constituents could assist.
Despite the evident empathy of Hamiltonians, the Ford administration's initiatives, such as the Homelessness Prevention Program (HPP) and investments through the Community Homelessness Prevention Initiative (CHPI), fail to coalesce into a comprehensive provincial strategy.
Ford's approach has been limited to funding and directing municipal service managers to provide local support and housing solutions. In stark contrast, British Columbia has implemented more defined strategies, such as the "Belonging in B.C." plan, which aims to make homelessness rare and brief through substantial funding and co-ordinated efforts with various partners.
During the same committee meeting, Hamilton's councillors expressed frustration over the city's increasing unhoused population, which remains unaddressed by senior levels of government. This frustration is underscored by the financial contributions to homelessness: the municipality contributes $82 million, while the federal and provincial governments contribute only $9 million and $28 million, respectively.
Coun. Cameron Kroetsch emphasized the scale of the problem stating, "this is not a million-dollar problem ... it's a billion-dollar problem." He further stressed, "until the other levels of government step up ... we're going to have to manage this crisis ... The City of Hamilton has no capacity to solve this crisis."
The federal government's National Housing Strategy (NHS), launched in 2017, aimed to address housing issues on a national scale. However, Prime Minister Justin Trudeau has often deflected responsibility onto the provinces, asserting that "housing isn't a primary federal responsibility ... It's not something that we have direct carriage of." This deflection, coupled with Premier Doug Ford's silence, illustrates the failure of federalism to provide a streamlined approach to the housing crisis.
This is not to suggest Canadian federalism, with its division of powers between federal and provincial governments, necessarily must complicate housing policy in Ontario through overlapping jurisdictions and responsibilities. Rather, the issue arises from senior levels of government deferring responsibilities to one another instead of collaborating with municipalities to effectively address the myriad contemporary crises affecting Canadians.
The structure of Canadian federalism often leads to jurisdictional disputes. Housing policy, while historically a provincial responsibility, has seen increasing federal involvement. However, this involvement is frequently limited to funding initiatives rather than developing cohesive, nationwide strategies.
The federal government's National Housing Strategy is a step in the right direction, yet it remains hampered by the lack of enforceable mechanisms to ensure provincial co-operation and the execution of comprehensive plans.
A recent IPSOS poll reveals housing affordability is the primary concern for Canadians, while a Canada Food Banks study indicates that up to 25 per cent of Canadians are living in poverty. This underscores the urgency of addressing the housing crisis.
In response, the Ontario NDP has tabled a private member's bill to establish a Homelessness Task Force composed of front-line workers, advocates and unhoused individuals to formulate a co-ordinated and effective strategy.
Despite the clear need for expert input and comprehensive solutions, the Ford government has yet to engage with this initiative. Consequently, encampments in Ontario continue to grow, highlighting the ongoing failure to address the root causes of homelessness.
The challenge of addressing homelessness in Canada is emblematic of broader collective action problems inherent in federalism. The lack of cohesive strategy and collaboration among federal, provincial and municipal governments exacerbates the crisis.
It is imperative for senior levels of government to take decisive and co-ordinated action, leveraging the expertise of local municipalities and community organizations, to combat homelessness effectively.
The situation in Hamilton is a microcosm of a national issue that demands urgent attention and a unified approach. The multiplicity of responsibilities and the tendency to defer rather than act collaboratively is a significant barrier to solving the housing crisis.
Federalism, in its current form, must evolve to allow for more integrated and effective governance if Canada is to address the pressing needs of its unhoused populations.
Elliot Goodell Ugalde
is a graduate research
and teaching assistant
in McMaster University's
political science
department.
CAPTION: Senior levels of government continue to defer responsibilities to one another instead of collaborating with municipalities to effectively address the crises affecting Canadians, Elliot Goodell Ugalde writes.Cathie Coward The Hamilton Spectator file photo

Document 445 of 500

Financial literacy push long overdue

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/financial-literacy-push-long-overdue/docview/3070403645/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=unknown&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Financial+literacy+push+long+overdue&amp;title=The+Spectator&amp;issn=11899417&amp;date=2024-06-21&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.10&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Spectator&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Premier Doug Ford and his PC government recently announced a new financial literacy graduation requirement for high school students.
The requirement is part of a series of new provincial education reforms, including the return of home economics education, consulting on building life skills, and new teachers passing a math proficiency test.
These reforms should help enhance the knowledge and understanding of high school students before they enter university, community college or the workforce. Other Canadian provinces would be wise to keep a close watch on the growth and development of these skill sets, too.
Let's begin with the financial literacy requirement.
"Financial literacy is more than just knowing about money, financial matters and having the skills to work with this knowledge," the Ontario government's news release noted. Students will, therefore, learn how to "manage their personal finances and understand financial decisions" and "save, invest and set long-term financial goals."
There will be mandatory classroom learning about financial literacy from grades 1 through 8 in mathematics to build early knowledge, along with Grade 9 mathematics and a Grade 10 career studies course.
Students will need to "achieve a mark of 70 per cent or higher" in the financial literacy requirement that will be part of the compulsory Grade 10 mathematics course. This will begin in September 2025.
This new requirement will help increase a student's knowledge about financial literacy in several ways, including the accumulation of wealth, paying off student debt and understanding interest rates, RSPs and mortgages. The earlier they learn about the value of money and how to save and invest properly now and in the future, the better.
It would be ludicrous for anyone to object to the Ford government's financial literacy graduation requirement. The introduction of this requirement is long overdue. It's something every Canadian province should require of their high school students long before they graduate and enter the real world.
Similarly, the decision to reinstate the math proficiency test beginning in February 2025 as a requirement for certification with the Ontario College of Teachers makes sense.
No one expects new teachers in Ontario to have the mathematical skills of Pythagoras, Isaac Newton, or Albert Einstein. However, some basic and grade-appropriate knowledge and understanding of math is a good idea. Teacher candidates will likely require some refresher courses to get back up to speed, but they should be able to pass comfortably.
What about life skills and home economics?
These two components are connected to the "back to basics" mentality revived during COVID-19. They're both critical to a student's development in this day and age.
The World Health Organization's 1999 list includes problem-solving, critical thinking, self-awareness, coping with stress and interpersonal skills, among other things. Mastering these will help students navigate life as effectively and efficiently as possible.
There's even a link between life skills and - you guessed it - financial literacy. A web page from RBC Wealth Management points out that "there are many important life skills people learn to master as they grow from young children into independent adults ... Unfortunately, money management is often overlooked."
Financial literacy plays a vital role in home economics, too. As reported by The Canadian Press on June 1, then education minister Stephen Lecce suggested students will learn about additional aspects of home economics "such as managing a household budget and how to protect themselves against financial fraud."
The math seems to add up.
Michael Taube holds a master's degree in comparative politics from the London School of Economics.

Document 446 of 500

After nine election victories, a Toronto MP known for his straight talk and wit says he won't run again

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/after-nine-election-victories-toronto-mp-known/docview/3070785576/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=After+nine+election+victories%2C+a+Toronto+MP+known+for+his+straight+talk+and+wit+says+he+won%27t+run+again&amp;title=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-06-20&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: OTTAWA-Inside the Liberal caucus meeting on Wednesday, John McKay was having a bit of a Tom Sawyer moment.
The veteran MP had just told his colleagues that, after 27 years and nine straight election wins in his Scarborough riding, he would not run again in the next federal campaign. That uncorked a flow of praise from his fellow Liberals, including from Prime Minister Justin Trudeau, who recalled how McKay staged a session on the mechanics of being a parliamentarian after Trudeau was first elected in 2008.
"You know, it's sort of like going to your own funeral," McKay said over the phone with the Star, echoing the scene from the classic Mark Twain novel in which townspeople grieve for the boys who aren't really dead.
"People say such nice things about you, you start to wonder whether that's really you," McKay said. "I guess when you survive as many years as I have survived, you achieve a certain status in caucus and people may think you're wiser than you actually are.
"But I had some very nice things said about me, and I appreciate it."
Known around Parliament Hill for his straight talk and wry wit - when asked how he feels about his upcoming retirement, McKay deadpanned: "I'm a white male; I don't have feelings" - McKay is the latest Liberal MP to step down or decide not to run again in the next election scheduled for the fall of 2025. That includes a host of long-serving MPs and a bevy of former cabinet ministers, who are quitting at a time when national polls place Pierre Poilievre's Conservatives in a commanding lead over Trudeau and the incumbent Liberals.
But while McKay acknowledged the situation is "not good" for his party, he said the Liberals' political fortunes had almost no impact on his decision to retire from federal politics. It's more that he is at the right "age and stage" to call it quits. He's 76, has five young grandchildren, and his wife - as she put it herself when she joined the Star's interview with McKay on Thursday - has been a "political widow for 27 years."
"I didn't believe it until it happened," Carolyn Dartnell said of her husband's intention not to run again. "He has been musing about it for a while."
Indeed, as McKay explained, he informed Trudeau of his plans more than a year ago, but held off going public until he figured out who might run in his long-held Liberal riding of Scarborough-Guildwood. Turns out, since fellow Toronto Liberal MP Michael Coteau is losing his neighbouring riding of Don Valley East in the once-per-decade redrawing of federal electoral boundaries, he will shift over and vie for McKay's old seat, McKay said.
"I know Michael and admire him greatly, and I think he's a terrific candidate and knows the riding well - and lives in it," McKay said. "So things all worked out."
Coteau, a former Ontario provincial cabinet minister, confirmed the arrangement to the Star, explaining that he was in a "very awkward place" and happy to have found a place to run.
McKay was first elected when Jean Chrétien was prime minister, during the federal government's drive to slash costs to balance the budget. He said that example of fiscal discipline stuck with him, and has marked his political views ever since. He was also deeply impacted by encounters with figures like Roméo Dallaire, whose eloquence about the horrors of the Rwanda genocide have stuck with McKay.
As an MP who never entered cabinet, McKay said he found a way to leave a mark through private members' bills - legislation that comes from parliamentarians who aren't part of the sitting government. McKay's list includes a law passed in 2004 that forced tobacco companies to produce "fire-safe" cigarettes; the so-called "Better Aid Bill" of 2006, which was designed to ensure foreign aid goes to poverty reduction; and most recently a law he co-sponsored that came into effect this year, to force companies to submit annual reports on whether there is forced labour in their supply chains.
"I sometimes feel sympathy for the cabinet ministers who essentially get marching orders" from the Prime Minister's Office, McKay said. "The so-called, much-maligned backbencher can actually have an impact that is certainly of significance."
CAPTION: Liberal MP John McKay, seen here in 2019, has announced he will not seek re-election in his Scarborough riding.

Document 447 of 500

Doug Ford urges Justin Trudeau to match the U.S. and place tariffs on 'artificially cheap' Chinese EVs

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/doug-ford-urges-justin-trudeau-match-u-s-place/docview/3069990068/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Doug+Ford+urges+Justin+Trudeau+to+match+the+U.S.+and+place+tariffs+on+%27artificially+cheap%27+Chinese+EVs&amp;title=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-06-20&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Premier Doug Ford is urging Prime Minister Justin Trudeau to impose stiff tariffs on "artificially cheap" Chinese electric vehicles to protect Ontario's nascent industry from being swamped.
"I'm calling on the federal government to immediately match or exceed U.S. tariffs on Chinese imports, including at least a 100 per cent tariff on Chinese electric vehicles," Ford said Thursday.
"Taking every advantage of low labour standards and dirty energy, China is flooding the market with artificially cheap electric vehicles," the premier said.
"Unless we act fast, we risk Ontario and Canadian jobs," he said.
Ford's intervention comes as pressure is mounting on Ottawa to get in line with U.S. President Joe Biden on taxing Chinese-made EVs, which can be cheaper than domestic vehicles.
While Canada currently has a six per cent tariff on Chinese EVs, Ottawa gives buyers - like those who purchase Teslas made in Shanghai for the Canadian market - a $5,000 rebate.
Flavio Volpe, president of the Automotive Parts Manufacturers' Association, who has worked closely with Trudeau and Ford on the EV strategy that is a cornerstone of Ontario industrial policy, said Canada must act.
"While the U.S. protected their market with a serious tariff, most people don't know four out of five cars made in Canada are actually sold to Americans," said Volpe.
"We have the moral obligation to return the favour and protect the Canadian market for U.S. imports from these Chinese Trojan horses," he said Thursday.
Trudeau's government is struggling over competing pressures to boost EV use to meet climate-change targets with the need to protect Canadian jobs. Ottawa has decreed that by 2035 all new light-duty vehicles must be zero emission.
In Ottawa, Environment Minister Steven Guilbeault said "it's a very live discussion that we're having with many colleagues, including (Industry) Minister (François-Philippe) Champagne and others."
"There are trade-offs one way or the other - and we're looking into this," Guilbeault said Wednesday.
International Trade Minister Mary Ng said she's "looking at this issue very seriously."
"Canada and the United States have a very integrated auto supply chain," said Ng.
"We also have ... a lot of really amazing Canadian auto workers, so it's an issue that we are taking very, very seriously and I will have something to say about that very shortly," she said.
"For Canada, what's really important is ... trade that is underpinned by the rules of trade. Canada can't be a backdoor for products that are unfairly traded."
Ford pointed out that "over the last four years, Ontario has a secured $43 billion worth of investments in electric vehicle and battery manufacturing, securing hundreds of thousands of good, well-paying jobs."
"This has been an all-hands-on-deck achievement, working side-by-side with the federal government and our private-sector labour partners," the premier said.
"We can never take our progress for granted. Our workers are the best in the world. As governments, we need to do everything in our power to protect their jobs and the paycheques they take home," he said.
"Now's the time to work with our U.S. partners to deepen and strengthen homegrown, U.S.-Canada supply chains. Now's the time to protect good, hard-earned Ontario and Canadian jobs by matching U.S. tariffs on Chinese imports."
Five weeks ago, Biden announced he would increase its existing tariffs on Chinese EVs from 25 per cent to 100 per cent.
CAPTION: Premier Doug Ford and Prime Minister Justin Trudeau look over a vehicle along an assembly line at an event announcing plans for a Honda electric vehicle battery plant in Alliston, Ont. on April 25, 2024.

Document 448 of 500

PM still thinks he can win

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/pm-still-thinks-he-can-win/docview/3069815968/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=unknown&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=PM+still+thinks+he+can+win&amp;title=The+Spectator&amp;issn=11899417&amp;date=2024-06-20&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.16&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Spectator&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Justin Trudeau is no doubt acutely aware of just how much Liberals need a morale boost right now.
Several weeks ago, the prime minister reportedly told his cabinet that winning in 2025 is definitely still a possibility, which many Liberals - not just ministers - are desperate to believe.
On Monday, in an interview on CBC's "Power and Politics," Trudeau said something similar in his conversation with host David Cochrane. Asked whether he could be the factor holding back Liberal fortunes in the next election, Trudeau said, "Canadians are not in decision mode right now."
He added: "What you tell a pollster, if they ever manage to reach you, is very different from the choice Canadians end up making in an election campaign."
True, this is another version of the stock politician's answer about the only poll that matters is on election day. But fundamentally it reflects what Trudeau sincerely believes - that when Canadians have a chance to really stare down the choice between this prime minister and Conservative Leader Pierre Poilievre, they'll stick with a progressive government.
Intriguingly, Trudeau said he and some of his fellow G7 leaders had a talk last week in Switzerland about the challenges facing incumbent governments. If there was any time to be a fly on the wall, that would be one of them: British prime minister Rishi Sunak fighting for his life in the U.K. election; French president Emmanuel Macron in the midst of his own snap election to fight back the rise of the right. No doubt they talked about who Joe Biden is up against in November.
I'm trying to imagine what Trudeau had to say about his own battles with Poilievre in this context. All the prime minister told Cochrane was that he came out of those conversations knowing he wasn't alone.
"Everywhere, people are struggling with high inflation, with cost-of-living issues, with interest rates, with housing challenges, child-care challenges, all these things," he said. The fact that "we are doing better than these countries doesn't make a difference to someone who can't pay for their groceries."
It would have been interesting to hear, too, what Trudeau told Macron about the risky gambit the president has made with his own future - forcing France into what our PM calls "decision mode" sooner rather than later.
There's not much chance Trudeau would follow that example, as things stand. One gets the sense, listening to Liberals talk privately and publicly, that they feel they need all the time they can get before next fall.
His appearance on "Power and Politics" itself was unusual - unusual enough, in fact, that when word went out on Monday he was showing up on the set of the popular show for political junkies, there was a flurry of speculation about whether this was the announcement that the PM was quitting.
This is Ottawa's favourite guessing game right now and Trudeau, despite protestations that he's staying on, doesn't seem to mind that he's keeping people guessing.
It did occur to me, though, that the mere fact of Trudeau doing that interview at mid-year (normally the prime minister does CBC interviews at year end) may be some kind of portent, whether that's a changed communication strategy or knocking off some items on a to-do list before summer.
Trudeau and the Liberals are doing their own polling, and they may well be seeing different numbers than the ones being churned out for public consumption. The latest Abacus poll shows Trudeau mired in deep unpopularity and the Liberals continuing to trail the Conservatives by 20 points. The latest Nanos polling has that gap a little smaller, at 14 percentage points. But all of them seem to point to the reality that if Canadians were in "decision mode" right now, the Liberals would end up on the wrong side of that choice.
Cochrane asked Trudeau what he wanted to accomplish with a fourth term that he hadn't yet done in his first three. The prime minister talked about "continuing to meet the moment," which is an acknowledgment his government has had to do a lot of reacting to events since 2015.
Back in the early days, Liberals talked about "hope and hard work." What Trudeau told CBC on Monday, essentially, is that hope is hard work right now.
Susan Delacourt covers national politics for
Torstar.
CAPTION:

CREDIT: susan delacourt OPINION Susan Delacourt covers national politics for Torstar.;

National briefs: April launch eyed for national pharmacare

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/national-briefs/docview/3069815916/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=National+briefs%3A+April+launch+eyed+for+national+pharmacare&amp;title=The+Spectator&amp;issn=11899417&amp;date=2024-06-20&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.13&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Spectator&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Canada
Iran group gets terror status
Public Safety Minister Dominic LeBlanc says Canada has listed Iran's Islamic Revolutionary Guard Corps as a terrorist entity, following months of mounting political pressure to do so. Members of Parliament voted unanimously in May to list the IRGC, a branch of Iran's Armed Forces, as a terrorist entity. The Liberals have said in the past that doing so could affect a number of people who had no choice but to be drafted into the organization. The federal government had already barred tens of thousands of prominent Iranian government officials from entering Canada, including top IRGC members. It also listed the Quds Force, a branch of the IRGC, as a terrorist entity.
April launch eyed for national pharmacare
The federal health minister has asked the Senate to move as quickly as possible to pass the government's pharmacare legislation so that Canadians can start accessing medications by next spring, Mark Holland told reporters Wednesday. A pharmacare bill drafted by the Liberals and the NDP made its way through the House of Commons in June, but still needs to be studied by the Senate. The idea is to make birth control and diabetes medication available to anyone with a prescription and a health card as part of a universal, single-payer program.

Ontario
TTC workers accept contract
Members of the union representing nearly 12,000 public transit workers in Toronto have voted to ratify their new contract.
ATU Local 113 says workers voted more than 80 per cent in favour of the three-year deal with the Toronto Transit Commission. The union says the new contract includes progress on key issues like wages, benefits and job security. Contract talks between ATU Local 113 and the TTC went down to the wire earlier this month, with the two sides announcing a tentative deal minutes before a midnight strike deadline.
Calgary
Restrictions into third week
It has been two weeks since a massive water pipe ruptured in Calgary, leaving residents under restrictions. Mayor Jyoti Gondek said Wednesday also marked the fourth day in a row Calgarians have successfully used less water than the city produces, avoiding the risk of the taps going dry. "We have enough of an emergency supply to fight fires and act as backup if needed in hospitals and care facilities," Gondek said.
