Document 450 of 500

As Doug Ford contemplates early Ontario election, Bonnie Crombie makes moves to ready the Liberals

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/as-doug-ford-contemplates-early-ontario-election/docview/3070060377/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=As+Doug+Ford+contemplates+early+Ontario+election%2C+Bonnie+Crombie+makes+moves+to+ready+the+Liberals&amp;title=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-06-19&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: With Premier Doug Ford gearing up for an early election, Liberal Leader Bonnie Crombie is urging her party to get ready.
"As we move into a campaign phase, I want you to know that we have a strong new team in place to help us prepare for that fight," Crombie wrote in an email to party members Wednesday.
She announced Chad Walsh and Genevieve Tomney, key players on her successful leadership bid, will be the Liberals' campaign directors.
"With Chad's deep organizing experience and network, and Gen's immense skill set in strategy, digital engagement, and communications, we will be ready for anything Doug Ford throws at us," the Liberal leader said.
"I also want to share that Tom Allison will now move into a role as a campaign chair, with additional co-chairs to be announced over the summer," she said.
The move heralds generational change at the highest level of the Liberal campaign.
Allison - a respected veteran who ran the winning leaderships of Crombie last December, Steven Del Duca in 2020 and Kathleen Wynne in 2013 - is passing the torch to Walsh, a skilled political organizer, and Tomney, a well-regarded former journalist.
"As we move into the summer months, I want you all to know that this will be a busy season for Ontario Liberals," wrote Crombie.
"While Doug Ford adjourned the legislature early to give himself a longer summer vacation, Ontario Liberals will be organizing and getting ready to fight an election - be that in 2026 or any time sooner," continued the Liberal leader.
The Star reported May 27 that Ford is considering an election call before the scheduled June 2026 vote over concerns about cuts a future federal Conservative government led by Pierre Poilievre might impose.
Worried that Poilievre might scrap or dramatically scale back the electric-vehicle strategy the provincial Progressive Conservatives have developed in co-operation with Prime Minister Justin Trudeau's Liberals, he is considering a spring 2025 vote.
Ford also would like to face voters while he has a big poll lead over the Liberals and Marit Stiles's New Democrats.
"It's smart. It gets us in front of a tough budget from Pierre that could hurt the Conservative brand and help Bonnie," a PC insider, speaking confidentially to discuss strategy, said last month.
Last weekend, Stiles told New Democrats at a party conference in Toronto to prepare for an early election.
"You deserve a good life that's within your reach. So as the election whispers get louder and louder and louder, we say bring it," she said.
CAPTION: Liberal Leader Bonnie Crombie speaks at Queen's Park in Toronto on March 26, 2024.

national briefs: Police identify shooting victims

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/national-briefs/docview/3069488457/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=national+briefs%3A+Police+identify+shooting+victims&amp;title=The+Spectator&amp;issn=11899417&amp;date=2024-06-19&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.7&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Spectator&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Ontario
4 First Nations sign roads deal
Four First Nations have signed a deal with Ontario for new roads, other infrastructure projects and skills training as the province lays a foundation for plans to mine the mineral-rich Ring of Fire region. The province said Animbiigoo Zaagi'igan Anishinaabek, Aroland First Nation, Ginoogaming First Nation and Long Lake #58 First Nation will see their roads that connect to the provincial highway system fortified and renewed. Main thoroughfares in the area, Highway 11 and Highway 584, will be fortified and repaved. The roads, about 300 kilometres east of Thunder Bay will support critical mineral and resource development, Premier Doug Ford said.
Police identify shooting victims
Toronto police have identified the victims of a shooting that left three people dead, including the gunman, on Monday. Police say 54-year-old Arash Missaghi of Toronto, and 44-year-old Samira Yousefi of Concord, Ont., were killed at a commercial building in north Toronto. They say the third person found dead is a 46-year-old man who is believed to be responsible for the shooting. Police previously said that a dispute at a business that conducts financial transactions escalated into the shooting.

Alberta
Water main break reviewed
A panel of experts is to review why a major pipeline burst in Calgary nearly two weeks ago, forcing the city and surrounding regions to conserve water. The city's chief administrative officer says the panel will be composed of academics and industry experts and will suggest changes to prevent future water disruptions. The main ruptured area has been but further repairs could take three to five weeks.

Document 452 of 500

Will we prioritize climate change or political change?

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/will-we-prioritize-climate-change-political/docview/3069488288/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=unknown&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Will+we+prioritize+climate+change+or+political+change%3F&amp;title=The+Spectator&amp;issn=11899417&amp;date=2024-06-19&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.11&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Spectator&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Extreme heat in Arizona sent 11 people to hospital earlier this month as thousands waited to enter a campaign rally for former U.S. president Donald Trump.
"If people start dropping, don't worry. We've got people to pick you up. I don't want anybody going on me. We need every voter," Trump said in record-breaking 44 C temperatures.
It was the hottest June 6 on record in Phoenix. Is this his solution to global warming? Just pick them up after they've dropped and hope they survive the heat?
Heat-related illnesses and even death are becoming more common in Phoenix, the American southwest and also globally. The world hit 12 straight months of record-high temperatures. Last month was the hottest recorded May in history. Emissions-related illnesses are also increasing.
The irony is that Trump supporters are willing to suffer extreme heat just to hear him rail about his recent "unfair" convictions - nothing about what he would do to reduce emissions that are creating that heat.
Instead, he plans to "drill, drill, drill" to increase emissions and make global warming worse.
This is what Americans can expect if he's elected. Just more talk about himself and blaming President Joe Biden for everything rather than creating a climate change plan that will help Americans have a better quality of life.
In Canada, Pierre Poilievre also talks about change but not about climate change.
He's following Trump's trajectory by blaming Prime Minister Justin Trudeau for everything. But he still doesn't have a climate change plan, or any plan that ensures a better quality of life for the majority of Canadians.
According to an Abacus Data poll taken May 24, 54 per cent of Canadians say they want to see a change in government in the next election. However, 80 per cent of Canadians want the Conservatives to have a serious plan to deal with climate change - and Poilievre doesn't have one.
Overall, these results signal that public perceptions of what a Conservative government would do if elected are not fully formed, according to Abacus.
So 54 per cent of Canadians want change but are unsure if Poilievre will make a change that will improve their quality of life.
What these 54 per cent of Canadians don't realize is that the current Liberal government is already making these changes. Many of them will be implemented in 2025 and for the next five years after that.
Climate change initiatives are the change. They will help improve Canadians' quality of life by improving our health, our economy and our environment. Emission reduction initiatives result in cleaner air, which results in better health and less respiratory ailments.
The current government is accomplishing this with electrification of transportation and zero emissions technologies.
By 2026, 20 per cent of new vehicles sold in Canada will be electric, 60 per cent by 2030. These progressive technologies will bolster the economy, eliminate the cost of gasoline for consumers, and make travelling more affordable. The more EVs that are sold, the more affordable they will become.
Cara Clairman, president and CEO of Plug'n Drive, said the toughest part of promoting the change from gas-powered vehicles is availability.
"Long waiting lists are definitely discouraging consumers that are ready to make the switch, and if we all agree that we're in a climate emergency, we need to help consumers make the switch as soon as possible," said Clairman.
On May 14, Trudeau and Premier Doug Ford announced Asahi Kasel Corporation's investment of $1.6 billion to build an electric vehicle battery separator plant in Port Colborne, Ont. There will also soon be four new Honda EV plants in Ontario, a new battery cell gigafactory in St. Thomas and a new Ford Motor EV production plant in Oakville - just the tip of the iceberg of what positive economic and environmental changes are coming soon.
The more zero-emissions technologies the government implements, the more chance we have of stopping global warming and also reducing the cost of living because electricity is cheaper than gasoline.
These are win-win-win changes that the current government already has in place that will all be implemented if re-elected. These are the healthy, economic, environmentally progressive changes that we need.
Climate change will be the political change that's needed in Canada.
Tricia Clarkson is a climate change columnist and co-chair for Peterborough Alliance for Climate Action.
CAPTION: Hamilton's Central Library opened as a cooling station shortly after the public health department declared a heat alert in 2020. Climate change initiatives will improve Canadians' quality of life by improving our health, economy and environment, writes climate columnist Tricia Clarkson.John Rennison The Hamilton Spectator file photo

CREDIT: Tricia Clarkson Tricia Clarkson is a climate change columnist and co-chair for Peterborough Alliance for Climate Action.;

Document 453 of 500

With the polls stacked against him, here's why Justin Trudeau still thinks he can win the next election

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/with-polls-stacked-against-him-heres-why-justin/docview/3069488186/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=With+the+polls+stacked+against+him%2C+here%27s+why+Justin+Trudeau+still+thinks+he+can+win+the+next+election&amp;title=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-06-19&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Justin Trudeau is no doubt acutely aware of just how much Liberals need a morale boost right now.
Several weeks ago, the prime minister reportedly told his cabinet that winning in 2025 is definitely still a possibility, which many Liberals - not just ministers - are desperate to believe.
On Monday evening, in an exclusive interview with CBC's Power and Politics, Trudeau said something similar in his conversation with host David Cochrane.
Asked whether he could be the factor holding back Liberal fortunes in the next election, Trudeau said. "Canadians are not in decision mode right now."
He added: "What you tell a pollster, if they ever manage to reach you, is very different from the choice Canadians end up making in an election campaign."
True, this is another version of the stock politician's answer about the only poll that matters is on election day. But fundamentally it reflects what Trudeau sincerely believes - that when Canadians have a chance to really stare down the choice between this prime minister and Conservative Leader Pierre Poilievre, they'll stick with a progressive government.
Intriguingly, Trudeau said he and some of his fellow G7 leaders had a talk last week in Switzerland about the challenges facing incumbent governments. What a discussion that would have been. If there was any time to be a fly on the wall, that would be one of them: British prime minister Rishi Sunak fighting for his life in the current U.K. election; French president Emmanuel Macron in the midst of his own snap election to fight back against the rise of the right in recent European elections. No doubt they all talked about who Joe Biden is up against in the U.S. election in November.
I'm trying to imagine what Trudeau had to say about his own battles with Poilievre in this context. All the prime minister told Cochrane was that he came out of those conversations knowing he wasn't alone.
"Everywhere, people are struggling with high inflation, with cost-of-living issues, with interest rates, with housing challenges, child-care challenges, all these things," Trudeau said. The fact that "we are doing better than these countries doesn't make a difference to someone who can't pay for their groceries."
It would have been interesting to hear, too, what Trudeau told Macron about the risky gambit the French president has made with his own future - forcing France into what our PM calls "decision mode" sooner rather than later.
There's not much chance Trudeau would follow that example, as things now stand. One gets the sense, listening to Liberals talk privately and publicly, that they feel they need all the time they can get before next fall - whether that's to get Canadians feeling better about the country or for some of that frustration feeding the Conservative poll numbers to burn itself out.
Trudeau's appearance on Power and Politics itself was unusual - unusual enough, in fact, that when word went out on Monday he was showing up on the set of the popular show for political junkies, there was a flurry of speculation about whether this was the announcement that the PM was quitting.
This is Ottawa's favourite guessing game right now and Trudeau, despite his protestations that he's staying on, doesn't seem to mind that he's keeping people guessing. Personally, I haven't ruled out any possibility still, though all I've heard from him are the reasons he is staying on to fight the next campaign.
All prime ministers say that, privately and publicly, up until the minute they announce they're leaving.
It did occur to me, though, that the mere fact of Trudeau doing that interview at mid-year (normally the prime minister does CBC interviews at year end) may be some kind of portent, whether that's a changed communication strategy or knocking off some items on a to-do list before summer.
Trudeau and the Liberals are doing their own polling, and they may well be seeing different numbers than the ones being churned out for public consumption. The latest Abacus poll shows Trudeau mired in deep unpopularity and the Liberals continuing to trail the Conservatives by 20 points. The latest Nanos polling has that gap a little smaller, at 14 percentage points.
But all of them seem to point to the reality that if Canadians were in "decision mode" right now, the Liberals would end up on the wrong side of that choice.
Cochrane asked Trudeau, usefully, what he wanted to accomplish with a fourth term that he hadn't yet done in his first three. The prime minister talked about "continuing to meet the moment," which is an acknowledgment that his government has had to do a lot of reacting to events since 2015.
Back in the early days, Liberals talked about "hope and hard work." What Trudeau told CBC on Monday, essentially, is that hope is hard work right now.
CAPTION: Prime Minister Justin Trudeau makes his way to a cabinet meeting on Parliament Hill on June 18, 2024 in Ottawa.

Trudeau minister accuses Tories of 'bullying' dental associations into rejecting national program

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/trudeau-minister-accuses-tories-bullying-dental/docview/3069619246/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Trudeau+minister+accuses+Tories+of+%27bullying%27+dental+associations+into+rejecting+national+program&amp;title=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-06-18&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: OTTAWA - Pierre Poilievre's Conservatives are "bullying" Canada's dental associations into opposing the Trudeau government's national dental care plan, Health Minister Mark Holland said Tuesday.
He said the associations are suddenly "dealing with rabid Conservatives" who fear the success of the program would benefit the governing Liberals - and that's why they continue to oppose the plan despite having had their concerns addressed by the government.
"If you're a conservative party and you want to vote against this, OK," he said. "But calling around and bullying and creating all of this negativity, I think that's what the underlying force is."
Holland did not provide evidence for his claim.
A spokesperson for Poilievre denied the accusations, calling his comments "unhinged."
"It is Canada's dentists, the ones who deliver care to Canadians right across our country, who are calling out this NDP-Liberal government for delivering a broken dental program and breaking their promises to Canadians," Sebastian Skamski said in a statement.
The Canadian Dental Association and the Ontario Dental Association did not immediately reply to the Star's requests for comment on Holland's allegation.
Dental associations across the country have slammed the rollout of the plan, despite their support for its purpose, for what they say are significant administrative burdens in delivering it.
Holland previously said he didn't "even know what their issues are anymore" after the Star reported that the heads of all but one of the country's national, provincial and territorial dental associations had yet to sign on to the coverage program.
On Tuesday, he doubled down on his criticism as he upped the pressure on the dental associations to get on board with the plan.
"We've had constructive conversations with the dental associations, but it's time to move on," he said.
"If you ask the dental associations, 'What's the problem? Is it how long it takes to do it?' No. 'Is it how much you're getting paid?' No. 'Is it interaction with the program ...?' No."
Slightly more than 40 per cent of oral health professionals in Canada have signed up so far, and more than 200,000 seniors have already received care through the program, Holland said. So far, certain seniors are eligible, and dental coverage will expand to children and people with a disability tax credit on June 27.
The Liberal government has addressed administrative concerns by eliminating the contract to participate in the program effective July 8, implementing a 48-hour deadline for reimbursing dentists, and fixing privacy issues, leaving only minor concerns outstanding, he said.
But Holland suggested that because Conservatives are "calling these dental associations and screaming at them and getting angry at them," they remain opposed to the plan.
"If they can tell you, you know, what the big outstanding issue is, because I'd love to know, because I'd love to fix it," he said. "But I'm in a frustrated position now where I don't really understand what their issue is."
CAPTION: Health Minister Mark Holland responds to a question during a news conference in Ottawa on June 18, 2024.

Document 455 of 500

Canada's EV strategy could cost taxpayers $6B more than private companies are investing, watchdog says

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/canadas-ev-strategy-could-cost-taxpayers-6b-more/docview/3069199529/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Canada%27s+EV+strategy+could+cost+taxpayers+%246B+more+than+private+companies+are+investing%2C+watchdog+says&amp;title=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-06-18&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: OTTAWA-Canada's EV-building strategy could cost taxpayers some $6 billion more than the private companies themselves are investing, the federal parliamentary budget watchdog says.
A new report released Tuesday by Parliamentary Budget Officer Yves Giroux says a total of $46.12 billion in private investment across the nascent electric vehicle supply chain has been announced.
The PBO estimates the total public support exceeds that - pegging capital and operating subsidies offered by federal and provincial governments at up to $52.45 billion, which is $6.33 billion or 14 per cent higher.
Flavio Volpe, president of the Automotive Parts Manufacturers' Association, said the parliamentary officer has inflated the figures by including $38.64 billion in federal and provincial production subsidies that are only a cost to government if the companies manufacture products in Canada.
Plus, that economic activity creates jobs and generates new tax revenue, he said.
"I'd be embarrassed to put future production subsidies into this equation after having been schooled repeatedly on how they actually work," said Volpe, who has worked closely with Prime Minister Justin Trudeau and Premier Doug Ford on the EV push that is a cornerstone of Ontario's industrial strategy.
"These reports might be more useful if they tried consulting the industry they repeatedly report about," said Volpe.
The PBO looked at the announcements of 13 projects since 2020, mostly in Ontario - where Honda, Stellantis-LG, and Volkswagen are among the largest - along with the Northvolt project in Quebec.
Those have drawn the biggest government subsidies to match U.S. incentives and to kick-start "clean energy" manufacturing in Canada.
Overall, Ottawa is paying the bigger public portion, which means provinces need a willing federal partner to ensure continuity in funding for scaling up an EV supply chain industry here to keep pace with the U.S.
"We estimate federal government support to be up to $31.4 billion (60 per cent) and provincial government support to be up to $21.1 billion (40 per cent)," said the report.
So far, the Trudeau government has placed big bets on the EV manufacturing future in Canada, while federal Conservative Leader Pierre Poilievre has been, at best, noncommittal.
Poilievre has previously asked the PBO to analyze the costs to taxpayers, and been critical of projects that drew temporary foreign workers to set up complex technical machinery and processes.
The federal support comes via investment tax credits ($2.5 billion) at the front end of projects, and once plants are up and running and producing EV batteries and parts, via production subsidies, which are based on actual output. The PBO estimates production subsidies will cost the federal government $24.36 billion, which he said includes foregone corporate income tax revenue.
The federal government is providing $4.4. billion in construction and other support as well.
On the provincial side, the watchdog said production subsidies will cost $14.28 billion, along with $6.7 billion in construction and other support.
The federal Liberal government and Ford's provincial Tories have argued they are making strategic investments to match incentives provided by U.S. President Joe Biden to lure EV auto and parts manufacturers stateside.
On Tuesday, the premier was in remote Greenstone, 1,200 km northwest of Toronto, to announce partnerships with Animbiigoo Zaagi'igan Anishinaabek, Aroland First Nation, Ginoogaming First Nation and Long Lake #58 First Nation.
"I'm thrilled to announce that we've signed a new historic agreement with the four First Nations in the Greenstone area to support long-term growth and prosperity in northern Ontario," said Ford, who wants minerals mined in the north to be used in EV batteries manufactured in southern Ontario.
"This renewed partnership provides funding to drive economic development to build and upgrade the roads that improve access to critical mineral and resource development opportunities and to help connect First Nations communities to essential services," he said.
At Queen's Park, Economic Development Minister Vic Fedeli's office said the PBO report "reinforces the significant long-term benefits the Stellantis-LG Energy Solutions and Volkswagen investments will bring to the province."
"It also acknowledges that it only analyzed a fraction of the benefits gained from the investments," said Vanessa De Matteis, Fedeli's press secretary.
"These projects will create tens of thousands of good-paying direct and indirect jobs and community infrastructure benefits, like improvements to roads, highways, and police and fire services, as we establish an end-to-end electric vehicle supply chain right here in Ontario."
Editor's note: This is an updated version to clarify that the PBO report indicates government costs are higher than the announced investments by the companies.
CAPTION: Parliamentary Budget Officer Yves Girouxestimatesthe total government support for EV manufacturing will be 14 per cent higher than announced.

Document 456 of 500

Trudeau government to fund completion of new hospital in northern Ontario

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/trudeau-government-fund-completion-new-hospital/docview/3069304453/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Trudeau+government+to+fund+completion+of+new+hospital+in+northern+Ontario&amp;title=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-06-17&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: OTTAWA-Canada will provide funding to complete construction of a new hospital in northern Ontario that will primarily serve First Nations people following weeks of pressure from the federal NDP, First Nations leadership and the Ford government.
On Monday, the federal government announced $1.2 billion in funding for the Weeneebayko Area Health Authority (WAHA) Hospital Redevelopment Project. The new facility will replace the Weeneebayko General Hospital, located on Moose Factory Island on the south coast of James Bay.
"This is the completion of a project that has been long standing and I'm very thrilled to be able to commit the $1.2 billion to Ontario so they can complete the project," Indigenous Services Canada Minister Patty Hajdu told reporters Monday in Ottawa.
Hajdu said the federal government has provided $200 million to the project since 2018. However, when the federal budget was released in April, there was no mention of the money needed to complete the project, prompting calls for Ottawa to uphold its commitment to First Nations and see the project through.
On May 14, Ontario Health Minister Sylvia Jones posted a letter on X, previously Twitter, expressing the province's "disappointment" in the federal government's "unwillingness to support the redevelopment of WAHA."
And in a letter sent to Prime Minister Justin Trudeau last month, federal NDP Leader Jagmeet Singh along with NDP MPs Charlie Angus and Lori Idlout criticized the government's decision "to walk away on a commitment to build a proper hospital in the James Bay region."
Hajdu said funding the project has always been a priority for the federal government but the challenge has been an issue of payment schedules with the project's contractors, Pomerleau Inc., and questions about "the nature of the payment contracts" to the Ontario government. The province has committed nearly $1.3 billion to the project.
"I think for me, we really saw a change when we were able to engage directly with the contracting company directly. So that gave, I think, the federal government the clarity on the $1.2 billion ask," she said.
Last month, Moose Cree First Nation Chief Peter Wesley went to Ottawa to call on the federal government to follow through with a previous commitment to fund the new hospital development, stating the region currently lacks "quality and culturally appropriate health care."
"It seems like we always have to, as First Nations, we're seemingly always having to hold press conferences or new rallies and what not just to get our treaty partners to the table to recognize their obligations under the treaty," Wesley told the Star Monday.
It was a "huge relief," he said, for the community and its neighbours to know that "the project's not going to stop and it's not going to go by the wayside."
Welsey said he has invited federal and provincial government partners to come to Moose Cree Nation and see the state of their current hospital.
Hajdu said the federal government will continue to provide funding to support the maintenance of the current building providing health-care services to the 12,000 people living in the isolated communities in the area.
"I know that we have a huge gap in this country that we have to try and close when it comes to infrastructure, health, housing for First Nations people and it's pleasing and it gives us hope when we see commitments like today happen," said Assembly of First Nations National Chief Cindy Woodhouse Nepinak, who joined Hajdu during the Ottawa press conference.
The Weeneebayko General Hospital was first established in 1950 as a tuberculosis sanatorium, but in recent years had fallen into a state of disrepair.
The redevelopment project includes a culturally appropriate health campus in nearby Moosonee. Moose Factory Island will have a hospital, Elder care lodge along with a visitor hostel, staff accommodations and a new ambulatory care centre.
CAPTION: Indigenous Services Minister Patty Hajdu rises during question period on June 17, 2024 in Ottawa.

Document 457 of 500

Ontario's expansion of alcohol to convenience stores is a covert union avoidance strategy

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/ontarios-expansion-alcohol-convenience-stores-is/docview/3068702688/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Ontario%27s+expansion+of+alcohol+to+convenience+stores+is+a+covert+union+avoidance+strategy&amp;title=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-06-17&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: The Ontario government has announced it's expediting its plan to get beer into thousands of convenience stores.
The announcement received immediate backlash, primarily against the $225 million given to the Beer Store to subsidize its adjustment to a new competitive environment and avoid sudden closures of store locations.
Further criticism of the "boozedoggle" focused on the potential losses to provincial revenue and the other subsidies to corporations.
The decentralization of liquor sales, however, is nothing new in Ontario. It's a process that has been ongoing over the past few decades and part of a strategy to erode good jobs in the alcohol retail sector. It can be viewed as union-busting by stealth and the public has played its part along the way.
Casualization of work
Once considered to be good jobs, workers at the Beer Store and Liquor Control Board of Ontario (LCBO) retail outlets have been pressured for years. Employers have forced tiered agreements with unions, where part-time and casual workers earn much less than full-time workers.
The United Food and Commercial Workers (UFCW) union representing more than 6,000 Beer Store workers and the Ontario Public Service Employees Union (OPSEU) representing 8,000 LCBO workers both have agreements that have full-time workers earning almost double part-time and seasonal workers. Today, unionized full-time Beer Store workers earn more than $30 per hour while a part-time workers with four years' experience earn approximately $17 per hour.
Retailers have turned to part-time workers to reduce their labour costs and meet public demand to keep stores open during evenings and weekends. This process is known as the casualization of work.
In the most recent census, full-time workers - including management - in Ontario's beer, wine and liquor stores made up less than half of the 16,645 workers employed in the sector, but had an average annual income two to three times higher than part-time workers. In the current round of bargaining, OPSEU is demanding protection for permanent jobs in the face of "marketplace change."
Geographic shift of retail distribution
Beyond the casualization of work, the geographic shift of retail distribution is also threatening wages in the sector.
LCBO convenience outlets, also known as liquor agency stores, are small, non-union, owner-operated businesses attached to corner stores and gas stations. A few agents were established in the 1960s to get alcohol to rural areas, but the program rapidly expanded in the 2000s. Over the last decade, the number of agency stores has doubled to almost 400 locations across the province.
More recently, Ontario grocery stores have been allowed to sell beer and wine. In August, more than 450 grocery stores will add ready-to-drink alcohol products to shelves. During the pandemic, grocery and convenience outlet sales exploded.
According to recent annual reports, Ontarians purchase 10 to 15 per cent of their beer, wine and spirits from these agency outlets and grocery stores. The potential addition of more than 8,500 corner stores selling booze is the next big step in decentralizing liquor distribution away from unionized retail locations.
The potential loss of government revenue via decentralized distribution may be overstated. After all, wholesale distribution will still be controlled by the Beer Store and the LCBO, and the government is not going to stop taxing.
Real losses will be in the pocketbooks of beer and liquor workers as work shifts to small, lower wage, non-unionized workplaces. Unions are unlikely to organize chains or small, independent family-run operations, which will lead to further downward pressure on wages across the sector.
Convenience stores rely upon low-wage work and have lobbied the Ontario government to allow them to sell alcohol in order to meet the increases to the minimum wage rate.
The populist appeal
Other powerful forces driving the geographical expansion distribution are populist calls for booze in rural areas and politicians like Ford, whose "Buck-A-Beer" campaign slogan pledged to lower the minimum price of beer to $1 from $1.25.
As historian Craig Heron has noted in his book "Booze," Ontario has a long history of prohibition, temperance and disciplining workers through controlling access to alcohol.
It's not surprising that loosening government restrictions on what, where and when people drink has a populist appeal, and populist politicians like Doug Ford know it very well. Beer has long been used as a political tool in Canada.
It is here where labour unions are trapped. Unions have argued there are economic, social and environmental benefits to having a more centralized system: larger operations are more efficient and can pay higher wages and efficiency, access to alcohol for young people can be more easily restricted, and the recycling and reuse of bottles and cans is facilitated.
As unions draw attention to these advantages to protect union jobs, they risk falling into the trap of echoing the moral panics around out-of-control access or stand accused of limiting rural communities' access to alcohol.
What can unions do?
Unions do have strategic options. They will continue push back against increased decentralization and casualization, but with the addition of thousands of low-wage alcohol outlets, this is perhaps a lost battle.
Unions may choose a fortress strategy and protect remaining jobs and wages in distribution centres and remaining stores. A more aggressive strategy will be for unions to focus less on workplaces and organize workers in the sector as an occupation or trade.
In order to first reach these workers, unions could demand a role in the certification and training of all new workers who sell alcohol to the public. From here workers could be mobilized to demand minimum standards for all licensed workers.
If good jobs are to remain in the sector, unions will have to be creative and extend their power beyond their stronghold in the retail system to ensure present and future workers in the alcohol sales sector receive fair treatment and compensation.
CAPTION: The decentralization of liquor sales is part of a much longer strategy to erode good jobs in the alcohol retail sector. along the way, Steven Tufts writes.The Ontario government announced that by 2026 they will allow sales of beer, wine, cider, coolers and pre-mixed drinks to be sold at convenience stores, grocery stores and "big box" retailers.It's not surprising that loosening government restrictions on what, where and when people drink has a populist appeal, and populist politicians like Doug Ford know it very well.
CREDIT: Steven Tufts

Document 458 of 500

Union-busting by stealth: Ontario's expansion of alcohol to convenience stores is a covert strategy

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/union-busting-stealth/docview/3068702468/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=unknown&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Union-busting+by+stealth%3A+Ontario%27s+expansion+of+alcohol+to+convenience+stores+is+a+covert+strategy&amp;title=The+Spectator&amp;issn=11899417&amp;date=2024-06-17&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.9&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Spectator&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: The Ontario government has announced it's expediting its plan to get beer into thousands of convenience stores.
The announcement received immediate backlash, primarily against the $225 million given to the Beer Store to subsidize its adjustment to a new competitive environment and avoid sudden closures of store locations.
Further criticism of the "boozedoggle" focused on the potential losses to provincial revenue and the other subsidies to corporations.
The decentralization of liquor sales, however, is nothing new in Ontario. It's a process that has been ongoing over the past few decades and part of a strategy to erode good jobs in the alcohol retail sector. It can be viewed as union-busting by stealth and the public has played its part along the way.
Casualization of work
Once considered to be good jobs, workers at the Beer Store and Liquor Control Board of Ontario (LCBO) retail outlets have been pressured for years. Employers have forced tiered agreements with unions, where part-time and casual workers earn much less than full-time workers.
The United Food and Commercial Workers (UFCW) union representing more than 6,000 Beer Store workers and the Ontario Public Service Employees Union (OPSEU) representing 8,000 LCBO workers both have agreements that have full-time workers earning almost double part-time and seasonal workers. Today, unionized full-time Beer Store workers earn more than $30 per hour while a part-time workers with four years' experience earn approximately $17 per hour.
Retailers have turned to part-time workers to reduce their labour costs and meet public demand to keep stores open during evenings and weekends. This process is known as the casualization of work.
In the most recent census, full-time workers - including management - in Ontario's beer, wine and liquor stores made up less than half of the 16,645 workers employed in the sector, but had an average annual income two to three times higher than part-time workers. In the current round of bargaining, OPSEU is demanding protection for permanent jobs in the face of "marketplace change."
Retail distribution shift
Beyond the casualization of work, the geographic shift of retail distribution is also threatening wages in the sector.
LCBO convenience outlets, also known as liquor agency stores, are small, non-union, owner-operated businesses attached to corner stores and gas stations. A few agents were established in the 1960s to get alcohol to rural areas, but the program rapidly expanded in the 2000s. Over the last decade, the number of agency stores has doubled to almost 400 locations across the province.
More recently, Ontario grocery stores have been allowed to sell beer and wine.
In August, more than 450 grocery stores will add ready-to-drink alcohol products to shelves. During the pandemic, grocery and convenience outlet sales exploded.
According to recent annual reports, Ontarians purchase 10 to 15 per cent of their beer, wine and spirits from these agency outlets and grocery stores. The potential addition of more than 8,500 corner stores selling booze is the next big step in decentralizing liquor distribution away from unionized retail locations.
The potential loss of government revenue via decentralized distribution may be overstated. After all, wholesale distribution will still be controlled by the Beer Store and the LCBO, and the government is not going to stop taxing.
Real losses will be in the pocketbooks of beer and liquor workers as work shifts to small, lower wage, non-unionized workplaces.
Unions are unlikely to organize chains or small, independent family-run operations, which will lead to further downward pressure on wages across the sector.
Convenience stores rely upon low-wage work and have lobbied the Ontario government to allow them to sell alcohol in order to meet the increases to the minimum wage rate.
The populist appeal
Other powerful forces driving the geographical expansion distribution are populist calls for booze in rural areas and politicians like Ford, whose "Buck-A-Beer" campaign slogan pledged to lower the minimum price of beer to $1 from $1.25.
As historian Craig Heron has noted in his book "Booze," Ontario has a long history of prohibition, temperance and disciplining workers through controlling access to alcohol.
It's not surprising that loosening government restrictions on what, where and when people drink has a populist appeal, and populist politicians like Doug Ford know it very well. Beer has long been used as a political tool in Canada.
It is here where labour unions are trapped. Unions have argued there are economic, social and environmental benefits to having a more centralized system: larger operations are more efficient and can pay higher wages and efficiency, access to alcohol for young people can be more easily restricted, and the recycling and reuse of bottles and cans is facilitated.
As unions draw attention to these advantages to protect union jobs, they risk falling into the trap of echoing the moral panics around out-of-control access or stand accused of limiting rural communities' access to alcohol.
What can unions do?
Unions do have strategic options. They will continue push back against increased decentralization and casualization, but with the addition of thousands of low-wage alcohol outlets, this is perhaps a lost battle.
Unions may choose a fortress strategy and protect remaining jobs and wages in distribution centres and remaining stores. A more aggressive strategy will be for unions to focus less on workplaces and organize workers in the sector as an occupation or trade.
In order to first reach these workers, unions could demand a role in the certification and training of all new workers who sell alcohol to the public. From here workers could be mobilized to demand minimum standards for all licensed workers.
If good jobs are to remain in the sector, unions will have to be creative and extend their power beyond their stronghold in the retail system to ensure present and future workers in the alcohol sales sector receive fair treatment and compensation.
Steven Tufts is an associate professor in the faculty of environmental and urban change at York University. This is republished from
The Conversation.
CAPTION: The Ontario government announced that by 2026 it will allow beer, wine, cider, coolers and pre-mixed drinks to be sold at convenience stores, grocery stores and "big-box" retailers.It's not surprising that loosening restrictions on alcohol has a populist appeal, and populist politicians like Doug Ford know it very well, Steven Tufts writes.Graeme Frisque Torstar file photoCOLE BURSTON The Canadian Press file photo

CREDIT: Steven Tufts Steven Tufts is an associate professor in the faculty of environmental and urban change at York University. This is republished from The Conversation.;

Document 459 of 500

Naheed Nenshi has launched his political return. Is Alberta ready for him and his purple socks?

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/naheed-nenshi-has-launched-his-political-return/docview/3068450483/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Naheed+Nenshi+has+launched+his+political+return.+Is+Alberta+ready+for+him+and+his+purple+socks%3F&amp;title=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-06-15&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: In those times when a truck-sized pipe shatters, throttling the water supply to over a million people - let's say, in Calgary, where it happened this week - it pays to have a few extra socks.
"I've managed to avoid doing laundry the entire time because I have an incredibly deep sock drawer, which is full of purple socks that people have given me over the years," says Naheed Nenshi, speaking from home in between campaign stops. Since the beginning of his political career over a decade ago, the former mayor of Calgary has made a point of dressing to match his best-of-both-worlds political philosophy, the purple socks and purple shirts and purple ties signifying the commingling of Liberal red and Conservative blue, and a wearer who has refused to swear allegiance to either.
But in recent months his sartorial struggles have expanded to adding a pop of orange to his purple. He searched the unusual colour combination online and ordered items to be delivered. Someone gave him a tie they found at Value Village. He asked a competitor's daughter, an accomplished beader, to craft a pair of custom cufflinks.
The orange, of course, represents his first foray into party politics as he seeks to replace Rachel Notley as the head of Alberta's NDP party. He entered the race in March following several years of political retirement - he's been consulting, public speaking and, he notes, making money - and months of local speculation. He announced weeks after his competitors but still landed firmly, most have assumed, squarely in the lead. Already his star power has helped rewire the party's base of support in the prairie province, with membership numbers exploding past 85,000 - making the party, Nenshi argues, one of the biggest political organizations in the country - and throwing up new nodes of support, particularly in Calgary and rural Alberta, far from the party's traditional Edmonton stronghold.
With just over a week until the vote closes and the results are announced on June 22, Nenshi says that more than 60 per cent of those have already voted.
The race has landed on the national radar thanks, in large part, to Nenshi. The loquacious one-time business professor - not for nothing is his longtime slogan "politics in full sentences" - rocketed to prominence when he was first elected in 2010, first for being the first Muslim mayor of a major Canadian city, then for his unflappable stickhandling through the devastating floods of 2013 and his outspoken progressivism in a province usually pigeonholed as the opposite.
But it's also teeing up a reckoning on the future of prairie progressivism. While Alberta has long been stereotyped as a conservative bastion, the exact flavour of right wing sentiment has changed considerably in recent years, with Danielle Smith more populist and combative than what came before. Now it's the other side of the aisle that is a having a very public conversation about what it wants to be. The ballot question for many seems to be: is it more important to stick to the party roots, or put out a welcome mat for a wider swath of Albertans? Is this about ideology, or about taking on Smith?
But his campaign is not a clear slam dunk. Also in the race are three sitting members of the legislature; all women with more experience in the party and the provincial arena. Sarah Hoffman was a former health minister and deputy premier under Notley, Kathleen Ganley is a former justice minister and lawyer from Calgary, while Jodi Calahoo Stonehouse is a relative political newcomer, having been elected last year, but has held leadership roles in several Indigenous organizations.
There's no question that the spectre of Smith looms over the race. Nenshi has said repeatedly that he thinks Smith, one of the country's most right-wing leaders, with a sweeping agenda on everything from fighting Ottawa to restricting heath care for transgender youth, poses an "existential threat" to Alberta.
In a twist that, were it fictional, would seem too on the nose, they were once classmates at the University of Calgary in the early '90s. (The class was a mock first minister's conference; Nenshi was Quebec, Smith was the media. The professor? None other than the titan of Alberta politics and former premier Peter Lougheed.) It was a heady time at the dawn of the Reform Party when young politicos were clustering in Calgary, including Ezra Levant, Preston Manning and a "very quiet grad student" named Stephen Harper.
Nenshi remembers Smith as friendly, outgoing and much more politically mainstream, but argues her time as a columnist introduced her to more extreme views. Nowadays, neither would be accused of pulling their punches, and Nenshi was known during his time on council for veering into spats with colleagues. (He also recently raised eyebrows by comparing Smith's United Conservative Party (UCP) MLAs to "monkeys on the other side.")
When asked about whether he could get a little salty, Nenshi responded that, "you know, being a bit salty is me." He'll never be shy about calling out the UCP, he added, but there is a line. "I've never been in opposition before. And certainly the temptation in opposition, is just to fight all the time. And I think, though, that Albertans are also looking for an actual alternative, a positive alternative."
One of Nenshi's more unexpected defenders here has been Jeromy Farkas, a conservative-leaning former councillor who frequently, and publicly, butted heads with Nenshi as mayor. Farkas, who has no affiliation with the NDP, wrote a letter to the Calgary Herald to push back against arguments that Nenshi is not a team player.
"He can be frustrating, he has an ego, he really likes to be at the centre of things. He isn't quite as smart as he thinks," Farkas said in a call with the Star. "But he always made the time for me, he sat down with me at least once a month to break bread, to listen," he said. Farkas then lost the mayor race to succeed Nenshi, after which he says many of his "fair-weather friends" ran for the exit.
Nenshi, on the other hand, "had every reason to gloat," he says. "But he stepped up really meaningfully to be able to support me and everything that I wanted to do next."
The prospect of Nenshi unleashed on the provincial stage may also force the conservatives, who have never been fans, to up their game. "I think in the UCP, there's simultaneous salivation at the thought of finally getting the chance to beat him," says Farkas. "But I'd say that the more savvy campaigners also realize that he's a force to be reckoned with - as long as he can keep that ego in check."
Though Nenshi faces a different environment than what he might have been used to at Calgary city hall. When he was first elected to office, Barack Obama was still in office, and the world hadn't yet grappled with the changes forced by a Trump presidency, a global pandemic or a rise in misinformation.
"The question that I asked myself over and over, when I jumped into this race is, 'is my style of politics out of style now? Am I completely out of fashion, in this very divisive world?'" But he says he's been heartened by the sheer number of people who have bought memberships or turned out to hear his ideas.
The current toxicity has also encouraged him to dig his heels in deeper on some core principles. When he first won, he had mixed feelings about being lauded as Canada's first Muslim mayor, rather than for his ideas or his campaign, though he turned it into a story about a country that embraces pluralism.
"But I also think that multiculturalism, diversity and pluralism are under significant threat right now, and we have to speak out in favour of those issues, regardless of where we stand - and that's something I will continue to do."
In some ways, the race represents a coming of age for the Alberta NDP. Their last leadership race was a decade ago, when Notley, then one of just four NDP MLAs in the Alberta legislature, won with roughly 3,500 votes, in a competition largely overshadowed by the Progressive Conservative's own leadership race just weeks before. Of course, Notley, herself the daughter of a NDP leader, would go on to do what many thought impossible, leading the party to victory in 2015.
But part of the recipe for success was a conservative movement that had fractured into competing parties, allowing Notley's orange wave to come up the middle. After Jason Kenney stitched the two sides back together, the NDP were defeated four years later.
Math would seem to dictate that the party needs new members if they want to be competitive next time, which has raised the question of whether Nenshi's pop of orange represents a genuine ideological conversion. He first dipped a toe into party politics right before the last election with his first-ever endorsement, and while he threw his weight behind the NDP, it read a little lukewarm.
In an opinion piece at time, he noted that he didn't think either Notley or Smith had been "particularly great" at being premier; dismissing the Notley years as a "lost opportunity" when it came to moving the needle on housing or mental health. But despite his criticisms of Notley's accomplishments, he also called her a "essentially a decent person who is trying to do the right thing." He does not feel the same way about Smith. And therein lies the key.
But Nenshi pushes back against the idea that appealing to more people means changing the party's DNA. Being an NDP believer on the prairies means being a bit more "pragmatic," as he puts it. Outsiders often seem surprised at the party's general approval of pipelines, for example. The local conservatives, Nenshi says, have abandoned the space occupied by the Albertans who aren't socially conservative, who like public services but don't want to waste money.
"My challenge now, if I am successful next week, is to help more Albertans see that the party is actually them, and that more Albertans need to see themselves in the NDP," he says. "It's not about changing the party."
CAPTION: Alberta NDP leadership candidate Naheed Nenshi listens to a question during a leadership debate in Calgary, Alta., on May 11, 2024.

Document 460 of 500

This isn't just another byelection in Toronto - it might reveal the future for Justin Trudeau and Pierre Poilievre. Here's what's at stake

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/this-isnt-just-another-byelection-toronto-might/docview/3068450419/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=This+isn%27t+just+another+byelection+in+Toronto+-+it+might+reveal+the+future+for+Justin+Trudeau+and+Pierre+Poilievre.+Here%27s+what%27s+at+stake&amp;title=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-06-15&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: It's a fascinating political petri dish under the microscope by parties, pollsters and pundits alike.
Candidates and campaign volunteers will tell you what really matters to voters in the Toronto-St. Paul's federal byelection is: housing, the high cost of groceries and staples, or how to tackle acts of rising antisemitism in a midtown riding that has the fifth largest Jewish population in the country.
But ask voters on the street, and they'll tell you what really matters is Justin Trudeau and Pierre Poilievre.
It's a vague - or a visceral - feeling that the prime minister has overstayed his welcome or that they don't know if they should trust the opposition leader with the reins of power.
"I legitimately don't know yet," replied John Carthy, strolling with a friend on Yonge Street, when asked this week how he's leaning. "I would like to not vote for Trudeau. And I don't see a very good option, so that's the thing."
Many don't even know there's a byelection happening, yet advance voting for the June 24 byelection started June 14.
For Liberals and Conservatives, it's a head-to-head race where an outright loss or a steep drop in the Liberal vote share would mean essentially the same thing: embarrassment for Trudeau in fortress Toronto, and a taste of possible victory to come for Poilievre.
The Liberal and Conservative candidates have opened campaign offices right across the street from each other near Yonge and St. Clair.
Besides being pretty convenient as a hub from which to deploy volunteers, it's an in-your-face political flex.
They've called in as many resources as they can and are scrambling to identify party supporters to go vote. Turnout is always a challenge in byelections. In this one, it's crucial.
Incumbent governments often say byelections are meaningless - that what really counts is the general election result. And yet Prime Minister Trudeau has shown up at every riding where a byelection has been called since he's been in office.
What's different in this one is that the Liberal stronghold of the past three decades is clearly no longer the safe seat it was expected to be even just six or eight weeks ago.
National polls show a 20-point lead across the country for Poilievre's Conservatives. Abacus Data's latest poll, shared with the Star, shows if anything, Trudeau's approval numbers among decided voters dropped in the past week to 22 per cent - his lowest in the nine years since he took office in 2015 - while the Poilievre-led Conservatives are up two points, to 42 per cent.
So the question is whether Toronto-St. Paul's holds for the party led by an unpopular prime minister, or is an inflection point for Trudeau to contemplate leaving, as some have suggested.
For most of the past 30 years, the riding was Liberal, represented by Carolyn Bennett, a well-known physician and MP who stepped down in December after 26 years in the House of Commons.
The main candidates to replace her are all rookies, but they have political organizing experience or good connections.
Leslie Church, a lawyer and longtime senior Liberal aide who quit as Chrystia Freeland's chief of staff last fall to make her run in Toronto-St. Paul's, is a new face battling unsubtle taunts she's an outsider. An Alberta native and mother of three, she and her husband came to Toronto 22 years ago when she went to law school. They were closing a home purchase this week in the riding, because win or lose, Church told the Star, she was returning from Ottawa to live in Toronto after their kids finish the school year.
Church's main competition is Conservative Don Stewart, whose website boasts he and his two daughters "have been proud to call St. Paul's their home since 2016. His roots in the riding stretch back more than 100 years." Stewart, who has an engineering and business background, worked for a year with the firm owned by Jenni Byrne, Poilievre's key campaign adviser.
NDP candidate Amrit Parhar is a 31-year-old program director with the Institute for Change Leaders, who helps train and run collective action campaigns on social justice issues.
The Green Party candidate is Christian Cullis, a constituency organizer for city councillor Dianne Saxe.
The race is crowded. It has the dubious distinction of having the most candidates ever on a ballot. Elections Canada said 84 people have registered. They are mostly part of an organized effort to promote electoral reform that calls itself the Longest Ballot Committee. The elections agency has been forced to print a long two-column paper ballot.
So it's a compelling race even if the stakes weren't so high. And the political spin is dizzying.
Senior Liberal party officials, who spoke on condition they not be identified, said byelections are always chances for voters to send a message to incumbent governments, and that turnout in byelections is never great, so they do not take the result for granted.
The Conservatives say that's deliberate spin on the Liberals' part, to preposition what is certain to be a drastic drop in Liberal support relative to the last election and will end up being a harbinger for Trudeau's future.
A senior Conservative source, who the Star agreed not to identify, said the Liberals' move to increase taxes on capital gains will help the Tories with a certain segment of voters in the riding. It includes affluent Forest Hill and other well-off neighbourhoods. And the Liberals' attempt to "straddle both sides" of the Middle East issue may play well for Poilievre's party, which has taken a harder line in supporting Israel, and accuses Trudeau of "betraying" Canada's ally.
But the Conservative insider said the riding won't swing.
"There is a zero-per-cent chance we're winning this," the source said.
"I've seen the voter ID numbers. We're doing better than we ever have, but we never targeted this riding ... the party would never have put resources into it."
Rather, the Conservative said the result will be a cold shower for the Liberals, just how the sharp drop in Conservative vote share was in a 2013 byelection in Brandon-Souris, Manitoba. The party went from winning that by a 38-point spread in 2011 to winning it by a slim margin of two percentage points, ahead of the 2015 election where the Harper-led government lost to Trudeau's Liberals.
The senior Conservative said the Liberals' margin in this byelection won't be "that close," but it will clearly signal other GTA ridings are in play for the Conservatives, and make Liberals sweat.
"I think that what they're worried about is that this election happens, they go down 15 points in the polls or something, and their caucus members start to look at each other and say, yeah, we're f---ed."
More than a dozen cabinet ministers have gone into the riding to stump for Church. One told the Star she has a "50-50 chance" of winning. Another said the party will still win, but it is dangerously close. Some Liberals fear it could be lost outright.
The Liberals could be spinning that they're worried in order to ensure their supporters are motivated to get out . The Conservatives could be spinning that they expect to lose so Liberal supporters get complacent, and stay home.
What's clear is the fight's afoot. Poilievre, and his deputy leader Melissa Lantsman, have been on the hustings with Stewart, who appears to be winning the sign war in the riding.
What's also telling is that neither Church nor Stewart would agree to allow a Star reporter to accompany them as they went door-to-door to solicit votes.
When the reporter walked into Stewart's campaign office, the candidate spoke only for a couple of minutes, saying he had "no time" to talk, wouldn't let a reporter go door-knocking, and quickly left, heading into a back office.
Church, and her campaign staff, said it was a question of respecting "voter privacy" that they won't let reporters tag along, but she sat down for an interview where she acknowledged a fatigue with Trudeau at the door, saying because he is "front and centre on every issue, people know him."
It's an "entry point into conversations" where Church said she stresses what the Liberals "have done and how we've got more to do" to expand housing, daycare, and to improve safety around Jewish schools and synagogues.
"It's about pulling people back to remember why they have voted progressive or why they have voted Liberal as long as they have."
"I think the ballot question is who do you trust," Church said.
The trust question is definitely in play.
The NDP's Parhar, who campaigned Friday with NDP Leader Jagmeet Singh, said in an interview they are focused on the affordability crisis and housing. She pitches the NDP as the only party that can be trusted to "fix" what it says are "failures" of the Trudeau government.
The Greens agree affordability and housing top the list of voter concerns, followed by antisemitism. Cullis campaign manager John Weston said of all the campaigns he's worked, this is "the most times where I've seen people crying at the door." He suggests voters are looking for alternatives to Trudeau and Poilievre.
For voters like Sharon, 45, a freelance contractor for a brand management company who works from home and gave only her first name, the byelection represents a tough choice.
"I voted Liberal almost my whole adult life," she said, but this election reminds her of when former Liberal Premier Kathleen Wynne was running against Doug Ford and the whole province was "so anti-Kathleen that you couldn't see the party."
She's become "a bit more curious" about the NDP, saying "I would never vote Conservative because I don't relate to the Conservative party. But I'm very on the fence right now in terms of who I would vote for."
"But I feel like if maybe Justin Trudeau backed away...?" she said, trailing off.
Another voter, who said she's a millennial mom, said she has no doubts.
"I don't care who it is, I'll just vote for the Conservatives because I just don't want Trudeau. I just hate him. I can't even look at his face. I absolutely cannot stand him. I want to leave Canada because of him," she said, to her friend's surprise.
"I'm a dental hygienist and my patients come in and talk about this stuff all the time and everyone seems to feel that it's time for a change. That's my read."
Voters can cast ballots Friday through Monday at advance polls, and election day is June 24.
CAPTION: Prime Minister Justin Trudeau and Conservative Leader Pierre Poilievre.

Subject: Antisemitism; Conservatism; Electoral reform; Polls & surveys; Candidates; Voters; Prime ministers; Voter behavior; Elections

People: Poilievre, Pierre; Trudeau, Justin

Letters

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/letters/docview/3068363096/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=unknown&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Letters&amp;title=The+Spectator&amp;issn=11899417&amp;date=2024-06-15&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.20&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Spectator&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Homelessness goes on and on
Re: City hauls away RV, but homeless remain
(June 12)
I read with interest the article about the Grant family that live in trailers at the Barton-Tiffany brownfield. (Located where the football stadium should have been built.) I remembered reading about them some month ago, so the saga continues. I almost croaked when I read, "later this month, Baird's division (the city's housing director) will present a report to council on further action to address homelessness."
A report. Later this month. Come on! This should have been worked on two years ago in consultation with the Hamilton Roundtable for Poverty Reduction and all the other groups out there that have information that could have resulted in an immediate action plan. I think all the personnel in the various departments and council members need to change their modus operandi! Obviously, what they are doing is not helping to resolve the homeless problem in a timely manner.
The article also stated city politicians have pointed to a lack of federal and provincial funding. How many times have I read or heard that? Again, if the city had hired a professional lobbyist two years ago to go after some funding, maybe we would have some by now.
The only upside I see is that I can buy a bond and invest with Indwell's housing initiative. Unlike the city, I believe they make a plan, stick to it and then they get the job done.
Susan Woodrow, Hamilton
Poilievre willing to face reality
Re: Poilievre's vision should alarm all but the very rich (June 14)
Linda McQuaig goes to great lengths to paint Pierre Poilievre and his vision as a scary and cruel future for Canada. She makes no mention of the fact that the massive increase in national debt that has happened under Justin Trudeau's watch puts the very programs we hold dear as Canadians in jeopardy now and in the future.
The rich have the ways and means to largely avoid the redistribution of their wealth. Reaching into the pockets of the rich and middle class plays well with some voters, but the real danger is ignoring the future costs of continuing to pile up debt at the expense of future generations. Poilievre is willing to face this reality while Trudeau seems to avoid even thinking about how we will afford essential services in the future.
Calvon Ellens, Hannon
Be careful what you wish for
Re: Taxpayers need a break (June 14)
A letter writer bemoans his Hamilton transit levy, for a service he claims never to use. He strongly promotes a "user pay" philosophy. I can only assume he will be happy to pay his road resurfacing levy, traffic signal maintenance levy, sidewalk repair levy, parking lot operations and control levy and white line paint levy. All taxpayer-supported city costs of which drivers, as road users, seem blissfully unaware.
Jim Young, Burlington
What do we offer visitors?
Thursday's Spectator had a beautiful insert beckoning me to "Discover Brantford."
I have visitors coming from Lithuania mid-September and am racking my brain for ideas of where to take them and what to do. My friends only have a few ideas. After Dundurn Castle, Sam Lawrence Park, Albion Falls and the market, I have no ideas.
I love Hamilton and wish we could also produce such a booklet. Or should I just take them to Brantford?
Dalia Jonikas, Hamilton
Questioning the numbers
Re: Doug Ford seems to think the Greenbelt scandal is over - it's not (June 11)
The article states exactly 350 irreplaceable acres of farmland are being lost daily to urbanization, gravel mining, and other development. That's an area three times a size of Burlington and almost the size of the city of Toronto every year.
I'm not sure where these numbers come from, but they make no sense. It's certainly not land being lost in the Greenbelt. New single-family home usually take up about 1/6 of an acre. Ontario builds about 25,000 single-family homes a year. That's about 4,000 or 5,000 acres per year or about 15 acres per day. High-density development occurs mostly an existing urban areas, especially in Toronto. Thus, very few new acres are required for these new homes.
Driving south from Mount Hope, I see no development until I hit the border at Caledonia. There I see acres of farmland, being gobbled up by new homes. Obviously, the Greenbelt doesn't save all farmland. We've just created a situation where people just have to commute further to go to work. Maybe we should plan to run the LRT from Caledonia to Hamilton.
Gary Aikema, Flamborough
CAPTION:

CREDIT: ;
