Document 463 of 500

Privacy issues slow down digital wallets initiative

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/privacy-issues-slow-down-digital-wallets/docview/3068361722/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Privacy+issues+slow+down+digital+wallets+initiative&amp;title=The+Spectator&amp;issn=11899417&amp;date=2024-06-15&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.13&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Spectator&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: A promise by Premier Doug Ford's government to include Ontario driver's licences and health cards on smartphone digital wallets is on hold indefinitely due to political and privacy concerns, the Star has learned.
"It's now on the back burner," said an insider, speaking confidentially in order to discuss internal Progressive Conservative machinations.
"Our base hates the idea so it's a tough sell," said the official, noting Ford himself had backed the digital push because he saw it as a consumer-friendly measure that would make life more convenient for Ontarians.
But some Tory supporters are deeply skeptical about having government-issued identification added to Apple Wallets or Google Wallets - even though the province's COVID-19 vaccination cards were digitized in 2021.
"They're worried about being tracked (by the government), so privacy was an issue," said a second PC source.
Officially, the Tories are tight-lipped about a scheme that was a priority when then-treasury board president Peter Bethlenfalvy announced it in October 2020.
"The government currently does not have an update on its digital strategy but will provide new information as soon as it becomes available," said Matthew D'Amico, press secretary to Public and Business Service Delivery and Procurement Minister Todd McCarthy.
"The ministry continues to extend digital access to government services for those who wish to use them. The government will provide updates when available," D'Amico said in an email.
Three years and eight months ago, Bethlenfalvy, who is now finance minister, unveiled an ambitious digital strategy to improve Ontarians' access to services at a time when many were forced to work from home due to the pandemic.
"We need to adapt to Ontario's new reality. People are 100 per cent ready," he said at the time as he touted an 18-page "COVID-19 action plan for a people-focused government."
"The government will consult with industry on how the province could introduce a secure digital identity for Ontarians by the end of 2021, including the strong privacy protections that will make sure you only share what is needed when it's needed," Bethlenfalvy said on Oct. 19, 2020.
"You'll need to keep a driver's license or health care card in a real wallet, at least for a little while," he said, conceding that "making things easier for a customer - or in this case a citizen - is actually quite difficult."
"Starting with privacy, that's paramount. We have to bat 1.000 on those metrics there."
Mindful that millions of Ontarians were already storing their credit cards, loyalty points cards, flight boarding passes, sports and concert tickets, the Tories aggressively sold the proposal in 2020.
"Verified, digital information about you, such as the information found on your health card, driver's licence and birth certificate, can be securely stored in a digital wallet on your smartphone and conveniently used to prove your identity to access services when required," according to the government's action plan.
This would "provide new and improved digital health solutions to front-line care personnel, so they can rapidly and securely access a patient's health records from anywhere and from any device," the plan said.
Sources say that as recently as last summer Ontario government officials were meeting with Apple in Cupertino, Calif., to determine how to add driver's licences to iPhone wallets.
The minister overseeing the digital transformation at the time was Kaleed Rasheed, a former BlackBerry executive who introduced the digitized vaccination card and the successful Verify Ontario app that restaurants and other businesses used to check vaccine certificate QR codes at the height of COVID-19 restrictions.
Rasheed was forced from PC caucus over the Greenbelt land-swap scandal last September after he made what he called "an honest mistake" when he gave the integrity commissioner wrong information about a 2020 trip to Las Vegas with top Ford aides and a developer.
The Mississauga East-Cooksville MPP, who now sits as an Independent MPP and wants to return to the Tory fold, declined to comment when contacted this week.
CAPTION: Then-treasury board president Peter Bethlenfalvy announced plans in October 2020 tohave government-issued identification added to Apple Wallets or Google Wallets.Christopher Katsarov The Canadian Press file photo

CREDIT: Robert Benzie toronto star

Document 464 of 500

The traditional office is toast. Why corporations need to embrace hybrid and remote workplaces

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/traditional-office-is-toast-why-corporations-need/docview/3068361669/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=The+traditional+office+is+toast.+Why+corporations+need+to+embrace+hybrid+and+remote+workplaces&amp;title=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-06-15&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: When a source in the federal government leaked news that starting in September the number of days federal government workers would have to go into the office would increase to three days a week from two, Premier Doug Ford said: "Three days is a good start."
It's part of a broader push by some organizations to get people into the office for more days a week, such as UPS demanding all staff return to the office for the full Monday-to-Friday, 9-5 schedule.
Yet such top-down, full-time in the office mandates fly in the face of definitive proof that the traditional Monday-to-Friday, 9-5 is the worst of all worlds. It's based on a meta-analysis paper by researchers from Stanford University, University of Chicago, and the Instituto Tecnológico Autónomo de México on productivity in hybrid, remote, and in-office settings.
Hybrid work models, which combine remote and in-office activities, offer a balanced approach that has been shown to enhance both productivity and job satisfaction. This model allows for flexibility, enabling employees to work remotely while also capitalizing on the benefits of face-to-face interactions for certain types of work that benefit from direct engagement, such as brainstorming sessions and complex project discussions.
Research has consistently found that hybrid workers exhibit an increase in productivity of five per cent to 13 per cent and report greater job satisfaction. For example, one randomized control trial demonstrates that productivity in these settings is improved by about four per cent. Self-assessments by hybrid workers corroborate these findings, with three to 5 per cent increases in productivity. The international findings are similar, with positive reports from around the world.
Some employees can become disengaged
The picture for fully remote work is more complex. Using traditional, office-centric approaches to remote work introduce significant hurdles that can undermine efficiency. In such cases, communication difficulties and a lack of immediate feedback can slow down project progress, reduce collaboration, and diminish the quality of outputs.
Another major concern in remote work environments is the propensity for employees to become disengaged from their work tasks, if supervisors aren't using best practices and lack training in managing remote teams. The distractions of a home environment can lead to procrastination and a phenomenon colloquially known as "shirking from home." This issue is particularly acute for workers who may lack intrinsic motivation or whose tasks are boring.
For example, consider a study by economists from MIT and the University of California, Los Angeles observed two groups made up of 235 data-entry workers - one working from home, the other in the office - in Chennai, India. They hired these workers for eight weeks and compared the productivity of both groups. Their finding? Low-skilled contract workers hired for a two-month gig working from home proved 18 per cent less productive, based on net typing speed, than those working from the office.
By contrast, an earlier study by scholars at Stanford looked at 250 call centre employees of a sizable multinational corporation, split into two groups: those working remotely and those commuting to an office. Their prevailing assumption was that employees at home might indulge in leisure - sleeping or binge-watching television - instead of performing their duties. Contrary to these expectations, the researchers uncovered a startling 13 per cent surge in productivity among remote workers. The differences might stem from the fact that the latter study focused on permanent staff invested into their careers at the multinational company, compared to the short-term contract workers evaluated in the former study.
The evidence does show that fully remote work undermines collaboration and learning, unless you adopt best practices for remote work management. One study looked at software engineers at a Fortune 500 company with a main campus split across two buildings.
Remote work offers much better cost savings
Engineers stationed in the same building as their entire team benefitted from receiving 22 per cent more online feedback compared to those with teammates in different buildings. The researchers concluded that the lack of such feedback indicates that working remotely undermines collaboration and learning.
Still, no question that remote work offers much better cost savings. Remote employees require no office space and can be hired at lower wages. Specifically, consider FlexJobs' 2024 Work-From-Anywhere survey ofover 4,000 U.S. professionals. The findings revealed a significant willingness among workers to trade financial gain for geographic flexibility: in order to secure the ability to work from anywhere, 26 per cent of respondents would accept a five per cent reduction in salary, while 24 per cent indicated they would agree to a pay decrease ranging from 10 to 15 per cent. This data underscores a shift in priorities, where location independence increasingly outweighs traditional compensation structures in the professional landscape.
So overall, even with a more complicated picture of productivity, organizations may get a higher return on investment from remote workers. In other words, the reduction in productivity per employee - compared to hybrid work - would in most cases be overcome by the reduced cost of each employee. Of course, the negative impact on collaboration and learning needs to be kept in mind and addressed.
In any case, the debate is clearly between a flexible hybrid model or a fully remote model. The traditional office-centric model is toast. That's even before the fact that blanket return to office mandates, especially for full-time in-office work, harm productivity by decreasing employee engagement.
Hybrid work definitely offers higher productivity than office-centric work. Productivity in remote work is more nuanced and context-dependent, with negative implications for learning and collaboration, unless organizations adopt best practices - however, fully remote offers major cost savings.
Either hybrid or fully remote work is better than office-centric work, and there's never a reason to adopt a Monday-Friday, 9-5 way of working.
Gleb Tsipursky is CEO of the future-of-work consultancy Disaster Avoidance Experts and a freelance contributing columnist for the Star. He is based in Columbus, Ohio. Follow him on X: @Gleb_Tsipursky
CAPTION: Either hybrid or fully remote work is better than office-centric work, writes Gleb Tsipursky, and there's never a reason to adopt a Monday-Friday, 9-5 way of working.

Subject: Work at home; Best practice; Hybrid work; Job satisfaction; Collaboration; Cost control; Employees; Wages & salaries; Productivity

Business indexing term: Subject: Work at home Best practice Hybrid work Job satisfaction Cost control Employees Wages & salaries Productivity

Document 465 of 500

Families are fuming as Guelph U residence wait-list tops 1,300 - sending students scrambling to find housing

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/families-are-fuming-as-guelph-u-residence-wait/docview/3068465896/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Families+are+fuming+as+Guelph+U+residence+wait-list+tops+1%2C300+-+sending+students+scrambling+to+find+housing&amp;title=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-06-14&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Families are furious and accusing the University of Guelph of "bait and switch" and a "cash grab" after the school boosted enrolment when there weren't enough residence beds, leaving more than 1,300 incoming first-year students scrambling to find a place to live.
The situation has caused such an uproar that even the mayor of Guelph has weighed in, accusing the university of "over-recruitment" and calling the situation "completely unacceptable" - but the school says it was caught off-guard after more students than expected accepted admission offers for this fall.
"I am quite disappointed with what may occur if solutions are not immediately found, not only for the impacts to first-year students but to the greater Guelph community with the planned over-recruitment tactic employed by the university ... the ripple effects will be considerable and compounding for our community for years to come," Mayor Cam Guthrie said in a public rebuke of the school.
"It is completely unacceptable."
The university said it does not guarantee a spot in residence for first-year students, but parents say school representatives - at the universities' fair, during tours, and in offers of admission - repeatedly indicated there would be no problem accommodating everyone, and touted a housing report it commissioned that said the same.
"It feels almost like bait-and-switch," said parent James Clarke, whose daughter is near the bottom of the wait-list for a spot in residence.
While the university says it began communicating with families in April about possible bed shortages, dozens of parents who contacted the Star said it wasn't until last week that it was confirmed.
"They then came out at the beginning of June and said 'hey, we've got a waiting list of people up into the 1,300s,'" added Clarke. "Kids have already turned down other universities ... they have no other choice."
His wife, Dayna Stringer, travelled to Guelph with a real-estate agent friend on Friday to find a place for their daughter, as a number of families worry about bidding wars and outrageous rents given landlords have the upper hand.
A Toronto-area mother said she had to sign a 12-month lease at $1,750 a month for a room off campus - or $21,000 over the next year - with food and other costs on top of that. It's so far beyond what the family had budgeted that a trip planned for next year had to be cancelled.
By comparison, residence rooms at Guelph cost from $6,400 to $10,500 per school year, plus meal plan fees. And residence spots are determined by lottery.
The university said that at this time last year, it had 5,143 acceptances, and this year that jumped to 7,849. Last year, however, some residence beds were left empty - though the year prior there was also a wait-list.
For this fall, the school has again leased a former hotel, is turning some two-person apartments into three, continues to look for extra space elsewhere, and says some beds may become available in January. Even then, it will be short about 1,000 beds.
Gwen Chapman, Guelph's provost and vice-president (academic), said the university did plan for higher enrolment numbers - in part to boost the school's budget amid stalled funding from the province on top of a tuition cut and freeze - but "by April we were starting to see a bit of the higher acceptance rate and recognized that we might not be able to accommodate everyone in residence ... so we communicated that quite clearly to students and their families."
She said the school will provide "a number of supports through our student experience team" to help first-year students adjust to living off-campus while making them feel a part of campus life.
"We certainly empathize with the situation," she said. "We recognize it's not what they wanted - it's not what we want. We would like to be able to accommodate all of the students who would like to be in residence, but this is where we are now."
Green Party Leader Mike Schreiner, who represents the riding, said "what Guelph is trying to do is increase student enrolment to cover the fact that they're facing significant operating deficits" because of provincial underfunding.
The university, however, "has a responsibility to students and parents to figure out the accommodation and to be very open and transparent about what's available, what's not available," he added.
In a statement to the Star, Colleges and Universities Minister Jill Dunlop said, "I fully expect the University of Guelph to be active in helping students and families find a solution."
Her spokesperson, Brooke Campbell, added that "this is a unique situation to the University of Guelph. Publicly assisted universities in Ontario are legally autonomous institutions with full responsibility for both academic and administrative matters."
Nate Broughton, who is a part of Guelph's student government but not speaking on behalf of it, said he's trying to create a task force to address the issues that the university will face with the influx of students.
The university "needs to prepare the campus for these students - there needs to be faculty hired to teach all of these students. All of these support services on campus ... these things need to really be bumped up to support this volume of students."
Chapman said the additional tuition revenue will be shared with faculties to help them serve larger numbers of students.
However, "the library is already packed, and it's hard to even find space. We're wondering about the quality of education, or are they now going to have many online classes, which will not work for my son," said Toronto mom Jenny Blaauw, who has a daughter at Guelph and a son set to start there this fall. He's about 650 on the wait-list.
"When you let in that many people, I don't have any confidence that they have the infrastructure to support it," she said.
For families who live further afield, finding accommodation has become a huge end-of-year stress when they should be focusing on and celebrating high school graduation.
Sam Gaulin's daughter is 1,186 on the wait-list, and "we're 900 kilometres away - I'm in Kapuskasing, northern Ontario. We're freaking out quite a lot" about living accommodations, especially given her daughter's needs because of a medical condition.
"I'm genuinely very disappointed in their entire lack of communication; they knew this was coming," she said. "I'm not a rocket scientist, but if you have 1,000 rooms and you accept 2,000 kids, you know you're going to have a problem."
Her daughter made a last-minute application to another university and is waiting to hear back.
"This is just the most terrible feeling," added Deanna Nearing, whose daughter is 391 on the wait-list.
However, "a little bit of a silver lining, I guess, is that there are going to be so many students living off campus, that it will be a massive group, and maybe there will be some way to advocate for them and have something positive," she added.
Stringer likened the search for a place to "an episode of The Hunger Games," and said the school "put its financial well-being over the students' well-being - this is what really gets me."
"This was a disaster," added parent Kim Ackerman. "... If anything could come out of this year is that next year parents and students are not going through this - that they fix it, because this isn't how a kid should start university."
CAPTION: Dayna Stringer, left, and her daughter Charlotte Clarke, with realtor Michelle Martin are among the hundreds of people now scrambling to find student housing after the University of Guelph notified many first years students that they are on a wait-list for residence.

Subject: Parents & parenting; Families & family life; Students; Colleges & universities; College campuses

Document 466 of 500

High lead levels found in water at 40-plus Hamilton schools

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/high-lead-levels-found-water-at-40-plus-hamilton/docview/3068116159/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=High+lead+levels+found+in+water+at+40-plus+Hamilton+schools&amp;title=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-06-14&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: A Hamilton NDP MPP is calling on the provincial government to convene an emergency session of legislature after a new report showed high levels of lead in the drinking water of at least 40 local schools.
"This premier will use the full powers of government to end the Beer Store contract a year early as a cost of millions and millions of dollars to taxpayers, but won't do anything to protect the health of kids?" said Hamilton West-Ancaster-Dundas MPP Sandy Shaw in a Thursday interview with The Spectator. "The house is on prolonged break until October. This is the kind of thing we need to come back for."
Shaw was reacting to June 6 report by the Investigative Journalism Bureau (IJB), which found at least half of Ontario's public schools have had at least one positive test for levels of lead in drinking water so high it exceeds federal safety thresholds.
A Spectator review of the IJB data found at least 40 schools from the Hamilton-Wentworth District School Board and Hamilton-Wentworth Catholic District School Board have tested higher than the Health Canada limit of five parts per billion (ppb) in water from 2020 to 2023. At least fifteen of those school have tested higher than the more permissive provincial government threshold of 10 ppb.
The Bureau has also created an online tool to allow parents to search schools for lead contamination reports.
The highest level of lead in schools was at Dundas Valley Secondary School in 2020, when lead in the water was found to be 300 pbb.
Other schools with high levels of lead were George Armstrong School, which showed 190 ppb in 2021, Richard Beasley school, which showed 110 ppb in 2022, and Viscount Montgomery, which had a level of 93 ppb in 2023.
The Spectator's review of the IJB data is incomplete and does yet not include child-care centres and daycare centres in Hamilton. And while the data shows water tests and the amount of lead found in the water, it does not indicate what remedial steps, if any, were taken by the school board to correct the problems.
The data also does not indicate if the source of lead in water is the pipes in a school, the municipal pipes feeding the school or a combination of both.
Information requests to Hamilton Public Health Services and both school boards were not returned Thursday.
The World Health Organization has said there is no safe level of exposure to lead. Health Canada says that lead, which humans most commonly are exposed to through food and drink, is linked to damage to the brain, heart and kidneys.
"Infants and children are at most risk because of their developing brains," says a Health Canada information sheet.
"I am disgusted by this," said Shaw. "I mean, the Investigative Journalism Bureau created a new tool, and it is really helpful. But the province has this data. It is their data. So what they are doing about it?"
The provincial government ended the current session of the legislature last week, and Ontario's politicians are on an extended break until Oct. 21.
Shaw said that, given the potential health risks to children from lead, Premier Doug Ford should recall the house to deal with the matter.
"I am not saying the government should have an immediate answer, because it is a complex problem and it likely will have costly solutions," said Shaw. "But they could strike a committee that will bring in the experts and come up with a plan before school resumes in the fall."
Five years ago, Health Canada issued a more stringent lead safety threshold of five parts per billion to better protect children. Three Canadian provinces - Ontario, New Brunswick and Saskatchewan - still use the older threshold of 10 ppb.
Ontario's Ministry of Environment, Conservation and Parks told the IJB in an emailed statement that the province has stringent water testing protocols and legislation, but did not address questions about the safety threshold.
Grant LaFleche is a reporter at The Hamilton Spectator. glafleche@torstar.ca.
CAPTION: Drinking water in at least 40 schools in the Hamilton area were found to have levels of lead higher than the Health Canada safety threshold of of five parts per billion. At least 15 of those schools exceeded the less-strict provincial standard of 10 parts per billion.

A perfect weather day is in store for the Hamilton area today with sunshine and a daytime high of 24 C at the airport weather station. Environment Canada says the humidex will make it feel like 27 and the UV will remain high at 8. There are no weather alerts.

High lead levels found in water at 40-plus Hamilton schools: Report shows drinking water readings vastly higher than safety thresholds allow at many institutions

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/high-lead-levels-found-water-at-40-plus-hamilton/docview/3067797157/se-2?accountid=15090

Full text: A Hamilton New Democrat MPP is calling on the provincial government to convene an emergency session of legislature after a new report showed high levels of lead in the drinking water of at least 40 local schools.

Grant LaFleche is a reporter at The Hamilton Spectator. glafleche@torstar.ca

Lead in water at Hamilton Schools
At least 40 schools in the Hamilton area have tested positive for high levels of lead in drinking water since 2020, according to data in a June 6 report by the Investigative Journalism Bureau.
Health Canada has set a safety threshold of five parts per billion in water. Ontario continues to use the older, less strict threshold of 10 parts per billion. Here are the 15 Hamilton area schools that tested higher than the provincial threshold and the year of the highest test:
St. Martin of Tours - 20.9 ppb (2020)
Our Lady of Hope - 50.1 ppb (20200)
Dundas Valley - 300 ppb (2020)
W.H. Ballard - 51 ppb (2020)
Sir Wilfrid Laurier - 64 ppb (2020)
Bernie Custis - 22 ppb (2020)
Sir Winston Churchill -11 ppb (2021)
Norwood Park - 19 ppb (2021)
George Armstrong - 190 ppb (2021)
Richard Beasley - 110 ppb (2022)
Mountainview - 30 ppb (2002)
Sir Allan MacNab - 46 (2022)
Bennetto - 20 ppb (2023)
Earl Kitchener - 16 ppb (2023)
Viscount Montgomery - 93 ppb (2023)

Document 469 of 500

'Our base hates the idea': Insiders say politics and privacy concerns halted Doug Ford's promise to put driver's licences and health cards on digital wallets

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/our-base-hates-idea-insiders-say-politics-privacy/docview/3068180608/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=%27Our+base+hates+the+idea%27%3A+Insiders+say+politics+and+privacy+concerns+halted+Doug+Ford%27s+promise+to+put+driver%27s+licences+and+health+cards+on+digital+wallets&amp;title=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-06-13&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: A promise by Premier Doug Ford's government to include Ontario driver's licences and health cards on smartphone digital wallets is on hold indefinitely due to political and privacy concerns, the Star has learned.
"It's now on the back burner," said an insider, speaking confidentially in order to discuss internal Progressive Conservative machinations.
"Our base hates the idea so it's a tough sell," said the official, noting Ford himself had backed the digital push because he saw it as a consumer-friendly measure that would make life more convenient for Ontarians.
But some Tory supporters are deeply skeptical about having government-issued identification added to Apple Wallets or Google Wallets - even though the province's COVID-19 vaccination cards were digitized in 2021.
"They're worried about being tracked (by the government), so privacy was an issue," said a second PC source.
Officially, the Tories are tight-lipped about a scheme that was a priority when then-treasury board president Peter Bethlenfalvy announced it in October 2020.
"The government currently does not have an update on its digital strategy but will provide new information as soon as it becomes available," said Matthew D'Amico, press secretary to Public and Business Service Delivery and Procurement Minister Todd McCarthy.
"The ministry continues to extend digital access to government services for those who wish to use them. The government will provide updates when available," D'Amico said in an email.
Three years and eight months ago, Bethlenfalvy, who is now finance minister, unveiled an ambitious digital strategy to improve Ontarians' access to services at a time when many were forced to work from home due to the pandemic.
"We need to adapt to Ontario's new reality. People are 100 per cent ready," he said at the time as he touted an 18-page "COVID-19 action plan for a people-focused government."
"The government will consult with industry on how the province could introduce a secure digital identity for Ontarians by the end of 2021, including the strong privacy protections that will make sure you only share what is needed when it's needed," Bethlenfalvy said on Oct. 19, 2020.
"You'll need to keep a driver's license or health care card in a real wallet, at least for a little while," he said, conceding that "making things easier for a customer - or in this case a citizen - is actually quite difficult."
"Starting with privacy, that's paramount. We have to bat 1.000 on those metrics there."
Mindful that millions of Ontarians were already storing their credit cards, loyalty points cards, flight boarding passes, sports and concert tickets, the Tories aggressively sold the proposal in 2020.
"Verified, digital information about you, such as the information found on your health card, driver's licence and birth certificate, can be securely stored in a digital wallet on your smartphone and conveniently used to prove your identity to access services when required," according to the government's action plan.
This would "provide new and improved digital health solutions to front-line care personnel, so they can rapidly and securely access a patient's health records from anywhere and from any device," the plan said.
Sources say that as recently as last summer Ontario government officials were meeting with Apple in Cupertino, California, to determine how to add driver's licences to iPhone wallets.
The minister overseeing the digital transformation at the time was Kaleed Rasheed, a former BlackBerry executive who introduced the digitized vaccination card and the successful Verify Ontario app that restaurants and other businesses used to check vaccine certificate QR codes at the height of COVID-19 restrictions.
Rasheed was forced from PC caucus over the Greenbelt land swap scandal last September after he made what he called "an honest mistake" when he gave the integrity commissioner wrong information about a 2020 trip to Las Vegas with top Ford aides and a developer.
The Mississauga East-Cooksville MPP, who now sits as an Independent MPP and wants to return to the Tory fold, declined to comment when contacted this week.
CAPTION: Then-treasury board president Peter Bethlenfalvy announced plans in October 2020 tohave government-issued identification added to Apple Wallets or Google Wallets.

Document 470 of 500

Ontario's new energy minister rejects 'ideological' push to scrap natural gas-fired power plants

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/ontarios-new-energy-minister-rejects-ideological/docview/3067423016/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Ontario%27s+new+energy+minister+rejects+%27ideological%27+push+to+scrap+natural+gas-fired+power+plants&amp;title=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-06-13&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Ontario's new energy minister says the fast-growing province can't afford an "ideological" push to wean its electricity system off fossil fuels even as concerns about climate change mount.
"I cannot underscore this enough," Stephen Lecce said Thursday at the Darlington nuclear plant where the next phase of construction is beginning on a small modular reactor.
"We will not pursue an ideological path that will deny some forms of energy when we need all of them to help fuel our economy."
Lecce was appointed head of the rebranded Ministry of Energy and Electrification a week ago when Premier Doug Ford shuffled his cabinet and adjourned the legislature for an unusually long summer break - until Oct. 21. MPPs had been scheduled to return Sept. 9.
The government has faced criticism for a growing reliance on natural gas-fuelled power plants to keep the lights on at times of peak demand.
Green Leader Mike Schreiner said Lecce, who spent the last several years as minister of education, is "disconnected from the reality Ontario is facing."
"Ramping up expensive, dirty gas plants makes no sense when people are facing an affordability crisis and a climate emergency," Schreiner added in a statement.
Lecce said his mandate in the new ministry is to "scale up our energy using all forms of nuclear and natural gas and renewables" in what he called "an all-of-the-above approach to build for the future" as the province's population and industries grow rapidly.
That industrial growth includes new electric vehicle battery plants for Stellantis in Windsor, Volkswagen near St. Thomas and Honda in Alliston in addition to new electric arc furnaces for steelmakers.
CAPTION: Stephen Leccewas appointed head of Ontario's Ministry of Energy and Electrification a week ago when Premier Doug Fordshuffled his cabinet.

Document 471 of 500

You'd have to be crazy to go into politics

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/youd-have-be-crazy-go-into-politics/docview/3067262108/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=unknown&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=You%27d+have+to+be+crazy+to+go+into+politics&amp;title=The+Spectator&amp;issn=11899417&amp;date=2024-06-13&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.15&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Spectator&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: I have wondered for years why anyone is willing to run for public office, knowing full well that their lives will come under intense scrutiny, with every word parsed and every past misdeed, even the minor ones, dragged up and put on public display.
Many of us have secrets from our past that we'd rather forget. Justin Trudeau, no doubt, would rather have kept his "Blackface" prank as a teacher under wraps. Ontario Premier Doug Ford has his alleged high school drug-dealing history to live with. And I sometimes wonder whether Alberta Premier Danielle Smith would like to forget some of her previous endorsements of quackery, such as promoting ivermectin as a cure for COVID-19.
Yet these and other politicians endure an onslaught of judgment daily for simply being human, which is to say, for making mistakes. True, some of those mistakes are ones we'd hope never to make ourselves, but they are mistakes all the same.
Verbal abuse, insults and mockery of politicians are as old as the profession itself. It is part of the rough-and-tumble game. What is no longer acceptable, however, is the growth in physical threats to our municipal, provincial and federal politicians.
Remember "Climate Barbie?" That was the demeaning label slapped on Catherine McKenna when she was the environment and sustainability minister in the first Trudeau government nine years ago. She was essentially hounded out of her role by an ever-escalating crescendo of ridicule.
Since then, according to Patrick McDonnell, the sergeant-at-arms, MPs have been subjected to ever-more-threatening abuse via social media. McDonnell told the procedure and House affairs committee recently that his staff had opened eight files on threats or threatening behaviour directed at MPs in all of 2019. By 2023, that number had grown to 530. Online and in-person harassment increased sevenfold in the same period, he said.
As a result, more MPs are receiving protective services from the RCMP. The police provided numbers that show it cost $2.5 million to protect parliamentarians between April 1 and Dec. 31, 2023. That's 40 per cent more than the $1.8-million budget in the 12 months before that and 86 per cent more than the $1.4 million spent in 2021-22. The prime minister's security detail is over and above that amount.
Still, protection varies by jurisdiction. Citizens must wonder how aggressively authorities are willing to act in defence of public servants whose well-being is threatened. One case in Alberta underscores the point: Shannon Phillips, the Lethbridge-based former minister of the environment in the provincial NDP government of 2015-2019, was illegally spied on by two police officers who viewed her government's land protection policies as a threat to their ability to ride off-highway vehicles freely.
Phillips filed a complaint with Calgary Police, which found Lethbridge officer Keon Woronuk had conducted an unauthorized database search. Woronuk was subsequently convicted of five counts of violating the Police Act: the database search, singling her out for traffic enforcement, misleading supervisors and using his position for political reasons. The other officer, Jason Carrier, was convicted of two Police Act violations: being an accessory to Woronuk's actions and failing to report them.
And yet, what was the penalty for the two? A two-year demotion for Woronuk and a one-year demotion for Carrier. Can you say slap on the wrist?
The incident was then investigated by the Alberta Serious Incident Response Team (ASIRT). A letter to Phillips about two weeks ago stated the team "determined that there were reasonable grounds to believe that two officers had committed criminal offences by accessing Ms. Phillips' data." And yet, despite the ASIRT's findings, Alberta's Crown Prosecution Service stated it would not prosecute. "(The service's) test for prosecution was not met and therefore these individuals will not be charged," the letter to Phillips stated.
I can't help but feel that insults and threats are one of the consequences of the breakdown in citizen regard for our public institutions. Thanks in no small part to Donald Trump, that obscene thug to the south of us, actions, thoughts and behaviour once considered beyond the pale have become so commonplace they are - if not acceptable - at least insignificant to a citizenry that should be outraged by such things. Like a train tumbling off the rails in slow motion, we are witnessing a great fall in social order.
And there is one other consequence for our embattled democracy. Smart, ethical, reasonable people who might have considered entering public service in the past are more likely to think twice about taking on a role that comes with immeasurable stress and very little thanks.
I don't love most politicians. In fact, I am often astounded at how tone-deaf some of them can be. But the public doesn't see the pressures and, at times, conflicting advice they get behind the scenes.
But I know this. If all Canadians don't relearn the rules of civil behaviour - and quickly - then the world of politics will evolve into a place that is attractive only to the sort of people we really don't want to be there.
For the sake of all of us, we cannot live with that.
Doug Firby is an editorial writer with more than four decades of experience working for newspapers, magazines and online publications.
CAPTION:

CREDIT: Doug Fir Doug Firis an editorial writer with more than four decades of experience working for newspapers, magazines and online publications.;

Document 472 of 500

Doug Ford thinks love will soothe Donald Trump. He better be right

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/doug-ford-thinks-love-will-soothe-donald-trump-he/docview/3067261148/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Doug+Ford+thinks+love+will+soothe+Donald+Trump.+He+better+be+right&amp;title=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-06-13&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Time for Canada to start worrying about Donald Trump again.
With election day a mere 144 days away, the clock is ticking toward a comeback for the once and future U.S. president. If the vote were held today, he'd win.
That's the latest verdict from David Axelrod, the celebrated strategist who guided Barack Obama to the presidency before Trump moved in. Axelrod's sobering analysis set the table for a day of gloom, doom and déjà vu at a Canada-U.S. conclave of politicians, diplomats and financiers in Toronto on Tuesday.
Bottom line: If you are hedging against risk, hold tight and huddle together.
Axelrod isn't predicting the outcome on Nov. 4 with certainty, merely talking about today's trend lines with sagacity. The polls are not moving in the right direction and the battleground states are not lining up behind President Joe Biden's re-election.
"We are a deeply polarized country," he mused, but that's only the half of it. The bigger problem is that Democrats are disconnected from disgruntled voters, while Trump is disrupting and reconnecting.
Against that backdrop, Premier Doug Ford waded in to the debate.
As a disrupter in his own right, he delivered a populist pep talk to counter the pessimism. Like many of the other speakers - Axelrod aside - Ford opted for the audacity and tenacity of hope.
Trump may well win, yet all is not lost.
The premier made the case for calm, soothing the unsettled Bay Street crowd at the Ritz-Carlton Hotel. As a sweetener, he gave out his mobile number from the stage, as he is wont to do, inviting business moguls at the annual Eurasia Group summit to call or text if there's trouble.
What would he say from the privacy of his phone? Likely the same message delivered onstage all day:
Even if Trump disrupts, the show must go on, two-way trade must carry on, both sides must soldier on to keep the peace. We are too interconnected economically to disconnect from one another politically.
First, love will conquer all.
"I love the U.S.," Ford kept repeating. "I love the United States of America."
Second, have faith.
"May God bless the people of Ontario and Canada, and may God bless the people of America," he concluded.
But good faith only goes so far in bad times. Unrequited love only goes one way at any time.
If Trump returns to the White House, will he once again threaten our shared trade, as he did once before by ripping up NAFTA? Ottawa managed to salvage the old free trade deal, but it is due to be revisited at the five-year mark - which would come due in 2026 during a second Trump presidency.
Perhaps the most reassuring words emanated from the two ambassadors, from our two countries, who took the stage together and largely sang from similar diplomatic playbooks, opting for hope over hype. Despite the media scare stories, the trade deal is not slated for full renegotiation, merely a routine review, they both declared.
"There's no intention in the United States to renegotiate the agreement," argued America's top envoy to Ottawa, David Cohen, adding pointedly: "If it ain't broke, don't fix it."
That may be music to the Ontarians old enough to remember that same saying emanating from a previous premier, the late Bill Davis. The problem is that (like Davis) Cohen wouldn't be in a position to offer such sage advice to Trump if he took power, given that he is a time-limited Biden appointee.
Canada's ambassador to Washington, Kirsten Hillman, echoed her counterpart's view, stressing that both sides understand their "symbiotic relationship" attracts record trade and investment to each country. The larger bilateral relationship is bigger than any president or prime minister.
"Whether we have a Democratic or Republican White House, historically we've always needed to make sure that we have strong ties" at all levels, Hillman said. That means both houses of Congress but also state houses, city halls, labour unions and business.
Ford, for his part, remains unfazed. Long before he became a political disrupter, prior to his populist campaigns, the premier was in sales - which is all about relationships.
The premier talked about his meeting earlier that day with his Illinois counterpart, Governor J.B. Pritzker - a Democrat with whom he could do business. Pritzker followed up with a similarly upbeat message.
Beyond the boosterism, the recurring theme from these two political leaders, like the two ambassadors before them, is that the broader relationships matter - economic, diplomatic, political - when seeking stability. But disruption has a way of destabilizing those relationships.
Hearing Ford reassure his financial audience, it was hard to ignore how much of his own success story has also been based on disruption, over and over again. If Trump returns to power, brace for more of what came before - and start worrying about it anew.
CAPTION: Premier Doug Ford addresses the U.S.-Canada Summit, in Toronto on June 11, 2024.

Subject: Political campaigns; State elections; Populism; Diplomatic & consular services

Document 473 of 500

The Greenbelt fight is far from over

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/greenbelt-fight-is-far-over/docview/3067261083/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=unknown&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=The+Greenbelt+fight+is+far+from+over&amp;title=The+Spectator&amp;issn=11899417&amp;date=2024-06-13&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.14&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Spectator&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: It's hard to imagine what happened in Doug Ford's mind when he decided that the stench from the Greenbelt fiasco had dissipated enough to welcome back Steve Clark, the second-most-responsible person behind the debacle - and as house leader no less.
Maybe he thought the public would believe it was a different Steve Clark, who just looked and sounded like the old one?
Or maybe he saw the May 22 Abacus Data poll numbers, which showed his Ontario PCs were up by 13 points over the Liberals. But he may have not looked hard enough: undecideds were up to 28 per cent, and increase of seven per cent.
For many people, however, their opinion was formed nine months ago, when the wrong guy resigned. Clark took a bullet for Ford. But few of us thought it was a rubber bullet and that less than a year later Clark would be back in the cabal.
For those of us watching Ford with one eye and the Greenbelt with the other, this is merely the latest proof that the Greenbelt battle is far from over. After Ford was forced to retreat, the PCs let the public enjoy what they rightly deserved: democracy.
Then in no time, their attack on the Greenbelt resumed.
One by one, they have been pursuing new measures like the development of Highway 413 and the Bradford Bypass, two highways that will destroy hundreds of Greenbelt acres in Halton, York and Peel regions and in Simcoe County.
The new approach is "Death to the Greenbelt by a Thousand Cuts," instead of the previous attempt at one big slash.
Ford also knows that the RCMP investigation into the Greenbelt scandal is still ongoing, so it's no wonder he is talking about holding an early election.
This all means that two right things need to happen extremely fast - because as we have seen, this government will do the wrong thing even faster.
First, the RCMP must release its review before the next election, which could be as early as next spring, so that Ford and Clark and everyone else possibly implicated can be judged by voters.
And second, the Greenbelt must be expanded.
The public won the last battle because the premier was told to keep the Greenbelt promise. But now we can see, by this Clark move and others, that his promises are empty, so it's time for us to take over and make one.
It's time for all of us to make a new Greenbelt promise and pledge to do everything we can to make the Greenbelt even larger.
From the time you began reading this oped, Ontario has lost nearly one more acre of irreplaceable farmland to urbanization, gravel mining and other developments (350 acres a day to be exact). The Ford government is in support of it all. And while they want more people to move here, more people means growing more food - which means growing the Greenbelt.
The Greenbelt must be expanded in order to continue to meet Ontario's growing agricultural, climate and nature needs, encourage appropriate housing construction within the existing urban boundaries, and once and for all, tell this government that no means no.
And while the RCMP ultimately may tell them that, we can't sit back and wait for it. (Although the surreal but entirely possible prospect of Clark having to resign twice for the same thing would be unique in Canadian political history.)
The premier is clearly putting the pieces and players together for another Greenbelt attack. The people of Ontario need to remind him who the buck really stops with, by acting instead of reacting, and demanding more Greenbelt, not the cuts he tried to give us once. As the song says, we won't get fooled again.
Burkhard Mausberg is the author of "The Greenbelt - Protecting and Cultivating
a Great Ontario Treasure."
CAPTION:

CREDIT: Burkhard Mausberg Burkhard Mausberg is the author of "The Greenbelt - Protecting and Cultivating a Great Ontario Treasure.";

Document 474 of 500

Family won't pay $26K hospital bill for refusing move: More than 400 Ontarians have been put in nursing homes not of their own choosing

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/family-wont-pay-26k-hospital-bill-refusing-move/docview/3067261030/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Family+won%27t+pay+%2426K+hospital+bill+for+refusing+move%3A+More+than+400+Ontarians+have+been+put+in+nursing+homes+not+of+their+own+choosing&amp;title=The+Spectator&amp;issn=11899417&amp;date=2024-06-13&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.12&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Spectator&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: More than 400 patients have been forced into Ontario nursing homes they did not want to go to and the rate of those moves is increasing, The Canadian Press has learned.
There were 424 discharged patients who moved to a nursing home not of their choosing out of 20,261 patients who were moved to long-term care homes since a law allowing such moves came into force in late 2022, the long-term care minister's office said.
About one-third of those patients were moved in just February and March, the last two months for which data was available.
In the summer of 2022, a few months after Doug Ford and his Progressive Conservatives won a landslide victory in the election, the government introduced Bill 7 in an effort to open up much-needed hospital space. The province passed the bill into law within days, bypassing a study at committee, which sparked a firestorm of anger from the opposition and seniors.
The law is aimed at so-called alternate level of care patients who are discharged from hospital but need a long-term care bed and don't have one yet. Hospitals can send those patients to nursing homes not of their choosing up to 70 kilometres away, or up to 150 kilometres away in northern Ontario, if spaces open up there first.
If patients flat out refuse those transfers, hospitals can charge them $400 a day under the law.
Critics have said the vast majority of people in that situation would feel threatened enough by the prospect of massive fees that they would comply.
The Ministry of Health has said seven people across the province have been charged the $400-a-day penalty. It refuses to disclose the total amounts patients are being asked to pay, but at least one family has received a $26,000 bill.
Ruth Poupard and her daughter, Michele Campeau, are refusing to pay the bill and experts say it's unclear what will happen next.
"It's never gonna happen," Campeau said. "I will not stop until this bill is dead."
The southwestern Ontario family could face a lawsuit, collection agency - or nothing - by ignoring the hospital bill, experts say.
Poupard, who has lived through cancer, a heart valve transplant and progressing dementia, began her most recent health-care journey in late December. The 83-year-old fell at home, where she lived with Campeau, who is also her power of attorney. Poupard broke her hip and needed emergency surgery.
She moved to Hôtel-Dieu Grace Healthcare in Windsor, in February for rehabilitation. Her attending doctor discharged Poupard on Feb. 21, but she needed more care than her children could provide. The family decided on long-term care and made a list of five nursing homes Poupard preferred.
Those were full, like the vast majority of long-term care homes in the province, so the family agreed to add a few more. In early March, the hospital's placement co-ordinator found a nursing home in downtown Windsor and gave Campeau 24 hours to check it out and decide. Campeau walked through the home, said it was disgusting and refused to move her mother there.
A standoff began in March when Hôtel-Dieu began charging Poupard $400 a day under the provisions of the law. On May 14, Poupard left the hospital after getting into her top choice for a nursing home.
That two-month journey came with a $26,000 bill but so far there have been no followups from the hospital to pay.
"I have no idea what's happening," Campeau said.
Shortly after the first charge, Campeau went public with her family's plight. She phoned politicians of all stripes and got a return call from her MPP, Andrew Dowie, a Conservative backbencher who told her homes the hospital co-ordinator asked the family to add to her mother's list were slated for demolition because they didn't meet accessibility standards.
"I am downright angry," Campeau said. "I said to him, 'you wanted me to put my mother in there and when I refuse a place that you're going to demolish, you're charging me $26,000? It's borderline insanity."
The province is in the middle of a massive redevelopment of the long-term care system with plans to build enough spots for 60,000 people.
Several experts say the only options left for the hospital seeking penalty payouts from patients would be to get a collection agency involved, sue Poupard, or let it slide.
If the hospital wanted its money, it would likely start by enrolling a collection agency to try to get it, said Jane Meadus, a lawyer with the Advocacy Centre for the Elderly, which is taking the province to court in a Charter challenge over the law.
Pensions are non-garnishable, which means they cannot be taken in a lawsuit. Campeau said her mother's money comes from a pension.

The legislation itself does not contemplate enforcement remedies like other laws do, personal-injury lawyer Michael Smitiuch explained. If a driver loses a civil case over a car crash injury or death and does not pay the award, the legislation allows for suspension of their licence. There is nothing similar in Bill 7, he said.
"It's like they didn't think through the whole thing."
The Canadian Press

Document 475 of 500

You'd have to be crazy to go into politics

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/youd-have-be-crazy-go-into-politics/docview/3067510514/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=You%27d+have+to+be+crazy+to+go+into+politics&amp;title=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-06-12&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: I have wondered for years why anyone is willing to run for public office, knowing full well that their lives will come under intense scrutiny, with every word parsed and every past misdeed, even the minor ones, dragged up and put on public display.
Many of us have secrets from our past that we'd rather forget. Justin Trudeau, no doubt, would rather have kept his "Blackface" prank as a teacher under wraps. Ontario Premier Doug Ford has his alleged high school drug-dealing history to live with. And I sometimes wonder whether Alberta Premier Danielle Smith would like to forget some of her previous endorsements of quackery, such as promoting ivermectin as a cure for COVID-19.
Yet these and other politicians endure an onslaught of judgment daily for simply being human, which is to say, for making mistakes. True, some of those mistakes are ones we'd hope never to make ourselves, but they are mistakes all the same. It must take one huge ego and skin as thick as an elephant's hide to drive these people to seek public office. Or an insatiable thirst for power, however fleeting.
Verbal abuse, insults and mockery of politicians are as old as the profession itself. It is part of the rough-and-tumble game. What is no longer acceptable, however, is the growth in physical threats to our municipal, provincial and federal politicians.
Remember "Climate Barbie?" That was the demeaning label slapped on Catherine McKenna when she was the environment and sustainability minister in the first Trudeau government nine years ago. She was essentially hounded out of her role by an ever-escalating crescendo of ridicule, which culminated in an obscene "C-word" graffiti attack on her Ottawa office.
Since then, according to Patrick McDonnell, the sergeant-at-arms, MPs have been subjected to ever-more-threatening abuse via social media. McDonnell told the procedure and House affairs committee recently that his staff had opened eight files on threats or threatening behaviour directed at MPs in all of 2019. By 2023, that number had grown to 530. Online and in-person harassment increased seven-fold in the same period, he said.
As a result, more MPs are receiving protective services from the RCMP. The police provided numbers that show it cost $2.5 million to protect parliamentarians between April 1 and Dec. 31, 2023. That's 40 per cent more than the $1.8-million budget in the 12 months before that and 86 per cent more than the $1.4 million spent in 2021-22. The prime minister's security detail is over and above that amount.
Still, protection varies by jurisdiction. Citizens must wonder how aggressively authorities are willing to act in defence of public servants whose well-being is threatened. One case in Alberta underscores the point: Shannon Phillips, the Lethbridge-based former minister of the environment in the provincial NDP government of 2015-2019, was illegally spied on by two police officers who viewed her government's land protection policies as a threat to their ability to ride off-highway vehicles freely.
Phillips filed a complaint with Calgary Police, which found Lethbridge officer Keon Woronuk had conducted an unauthorized database search. Woronuk was subsequently convicted of five counts of violating the Police Act: the database search, singling her out for traffic enforcement, misleading supervisors and using his position for political reasons. The other officer, Jason Carrier, was convicted of two Police Act violations: being an accessory to Woronuk's actions and failing to report them.
And yet, what was the penalty for the two? A two-year demotion for Woronuk and a one-year demotion for Carrier. Can you say slap on the wrist?
The incident was then investigated by the Alberta Serious Incident Response Team (ASIRT). A letter to Phillips about two weeks ago stated the team "determined that there were reasonable grounds to believe that two officers had committed criminal offences by accessing Ms. Phillips' data." And yet, despite the ASIRT's findings, Alberta's Crown Prosecution Service stated it would not prosecute. "(The service's) test for prosecution was not met and therefore these individuals will not be charged," the letter to Phillips stated. Details of what meets "the test" were not disclosed.
It is to be hoped that federal politicians will find a higher standard of protection than Phillips has experienced.
I can't help but feel that insults and threats are one of the consequences of the breakdown in citizen regard for our public institutions. Thanks in no small part to Donald Trump, that obscene thug to the south of us, actions, thoughts and behaviour once considered beyond the pale have become so commonplace they are - if not acceptable - at least insignificant to a citizenry that should be outraged by such things. Like a train tumbling off the rails in slow motion, we are witnessing a great fall in social order that all the king's horses and all the king's men may never be able to put back together again.
And there is one other consequence for our embattled democracy. Smart, ethical, reasonable people who might have considered entering public service in the past are more likely to think twice about taking on a role that comes with immeasurable stress and very little thanks.
I don't love most politicians. In fact, I am often astounded at how tone-deaf some of them can be. But the public doesn't see the pressures and, at times, conflicting advice they get behind the scenes. More and more, it feels like they are the product of party sausage factories creating a product to be sold to voters rather than a leader.
But I know this. If all Canadians don't relearn the rules of civil behaviour - and quickly - then the world of politics will evolve into a place that is attractive only to the sort of people we really don't want to be there.
For the sake of all of us, we cannot live with that.
CAPTION: Alberta New Democrat MLA Shannon Phillips, left, is stepping down before finishing her third term in office. She was illegally spied on by two Lethbridge police officers, Doug Firby writes.
CREDIT: Doug Firby

Subject: Threats; Politics; Politicians

Document 476 of 500

If you thought Trump 1.0 was crazy, brace yourself for Trump 2.0. Here's where top officials say Canada is vulnerable

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/if-you-thought-trump-1-0-was-crazy-brace-yourself/docview/3067510435/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=If+you+thought+Trump+1.0+was+crazy%2C+brace+yourself+for+Trump+2.0.+Here%27s+where+top+officials+say+Canada+is+vulnerable&amp;title=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-06-12&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: OTTAWA-If Canadians thought Trump 1.0 was crazily chaotic, they should expect Trump 2.0 will be crazily methodic. About all of the things.
That includes his vow to impose broad 10 per cent tariffs on all imports - something Canada is unlikely to escape - his insistence that allies like Canada pony up billions more on defence; and his vow to secure U.S. borders and deport illegal immigrants, for starters.
That was a clear message delivered from the stage and behind the scenes at a conference in Toronto that drew more than 200 influential Canadian and American players from across a range of political, business and policy sectors to discuss the state of the Canada-U.S. relationship.
The other clear message?
Canadian political, business and labour leaders across all levels of government need to engage in an accelerated, more co-ordinated approach to the very real prospect of a Trump win in the U.S. presidential election.
Sure, there was lots of talk about how strong cross-border ties are, how the relationship is vital to economic prosperity in both countries, and how stable U.S. democracy is even as President Joe Biden's re-election bid struggles to counter Donald Trump's return.
Top diplomats for each country downplayed the looming impact of another Trump administration on bilateral trade, defence, security, and migration.
Yet Premier Doug Ford admitted what keeps him up at night is "American protectionism."
The gathering, organized by the Eurasia Group and BMO Financial Group, had the feel of an international summit where the hallway chatter and bilateral meetings were where the players exchanged more candid views. In private rooms two floors above the fancy speeches and snacks, Canadian cabinet ministers Mélanie Joly, François-Philippe Champagne and Mary Ng met with state governors. On the sidelines, Canada's ambassador to Washington Kirsten Hillman spoke with a senior U.S. official connected to the Biden re-election campaign and a former Trump administration official, and current and former ambassadors and premiers pressed the flesh.
Surprisingly, it was Steve Verheul, the lead Canadian negotiator during NAFTA talks that led to the current Canada-U.S.-Mexico trade agreement, who said the quiet part out loud.
We're not ready for the prospect of a Trump administration or the likelihood his trade advisers like Robert Lighthizer will want to reopen the trade pact, not just review it as scheduled in 2026, and Canada needs to up its game.
The groundwork to enlist private and public sector allies in Canada needed to start "sooner than before now," Verheul said. In an interview, he said dispute resolution mechanisms, auto sector and supply managed dairy industries would once again be on the table.
"We are entering a sprint stage from now until mid-next year" when U.S. positions and deadlines will solidify and kick in, Verheul said. A lot has changed since the last negotiation, and any trade talks now must emphasize national and economic security and supply chain resilience - factors political leaders on Republican and Democratic sides of the aisle prioritize.
Christopher Liddell, a deputy chief of staff in Trump's first administration, said Trump is "laser focused on trade imbalances" and China is first on his target list. Trade with Canada and Mexico may not be a day-one issue, "but it's going to be pretty high on the agenda."
He expects Trump will move quickly in the first six months to execute campaign positions and will announce his transition planning team soon who should be seen as responsibly planning for a return to power that seems increasingly likely.
Jason Kenney, the former Alberta premier who was Canada's longest-serving immigration minister, warned that Trump's "commitment massively to increase removals of people who are foreign nationals" means "hundreds of thousands or potentially millions" of people who have overstayed visas or never had one could head north to "take a second crack at the Canadian asylum system."
It could "overwhelm an already teetering" refugee determination system here that faces backlogs at the same time that record-high inflows of immigrants, international students and temporary foreign workers have put pressure on housing costs, and threatened the Canadian consensus that immigration is a good thing.
There's no question it's a touchy subject.
Hillman, in an earlier panel, emphatically rejected a suggestion by John Baird, another former Conservative cabinet minister, that Canada's border is a weak point or risk for the relationship.
Hillman said the renegotiated Safe Third Country agreement ensures asylum seekers must claim refugee status in the first country they arrive in - Canada or the U.S. - and border guards turn back anyone who tries to game the system and use a backdoor into the other country. Hillman called it a "strong common commitment to the security of asylum seekers, but also to the manageability of our border when it comes to human movement."
U.S. Ambassador David Cohen echoed that. "The overwhelming problem we have to manage is a southern border problem, not a northern border problem."
When it came to defence spending - a long-standing point of friction - Cohen, as well as U.S. Sen. Chris Coons, an influential lawmaker, are diplomatic.
Foreign Affairs Minister Joly was grilled over Canada's defence spending plan which still has not committed formally to the NATO target of two per cent of GDP. Striking a defensive note, she asked if Politico's editor-in-chief had even read the latest update which commits to raising Canada's military budget to 1.76 per cent of the overall economy, and to buying new subs, which she said would put Canada on a "path" to two per cent.
Coons, a Democrat from Delaware, diplomatically threw Joly an assist: "What matters most is the trajectory."
But Liddell underscored defence spending is a long-standing demand of Trump's, and Ottawa should expect him to force the issue.
Even formal central banker Mark Carney, a long-rumoured possible aspirant for the Liberal leadership, weighed in on the need for Canada to make explicit its defence commitment.
"Canada must hold our full weight in NATO. This is no longer an option. It's an imperative,' he said.
Carney said in order to ensure Canada remains "the essential trading partner" of the U.S., "we're going to need a new plan," one that builds on this country's critical minerals, stable banking, financial and agri-food industries, and builds on a nascent AI sector, and addresses lagging productivity.
If Trump wins, Eurasia Group president Ian Bremmer also flagged it would shift momentum away from American subsidies for Biden's green energy transition, with knock-on effects on the EV manufacturing industry and climate action policies in both countries.
U.S. political strategist David Axelrod, a former chief adviser to President Barack Obama, said the presidential race is incredibly tight but if a vote were held today, Trump would win it and he is far from convinced Biden can still turn the tide.
All of which means allies like Canada should not count on the luxury of time being on their side.
CAPTION: Former President Donald Trump speaks at a rally at Sunset Park on June 9, 2024, in Las Vegas.

Subject: Strategic materials; Trade agreements; Political leadership; Diplomatic & consular services; Noncitizens; Political campaigns; Defense; Presidential elections; Budgets; Immigration

The Beer Store is now allowed to sell lottery tickets. Can that save it?

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/beer-store-is-now-allowed-sell-lottery-tickets/docview/3067510366/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=The+Beer+Store+is+now+allowed+to+sell+lottery+tickets.+Can+that+save+it%3F&amp;title=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-06-12&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Could lottery tickets help The Beer Store improve its luck now that Premier Doug Ford has cleared the way for beer, wine, cider and pre-mixed drinks to be sold in corner stores starting Sept. 5?
With some experts predicting the increased competition means Beer Stores could go bust before long, the fine print in the deal announced three weeks ago does allow them to apply for licences to sell 6/49 and other lottery tickets.
But officials at The Beer Store won't say if that's the plan and are keeping mum on what else they might sell under an agreement that says "the province shall not prohibit TBS (The Beer Store) from selling any other product, including lottery tickets" except for other forms of beverage alcohol, cannabis or tobacco.
The Beer Store has pledged to maintain "a competitive retail footprint" and is "planning to adapt and thrive" by expanding its fleet of vehicles and warehousing to deliver beer to thousands of convenience and other stores while running its bottle and can deposit return service until at least 2031.
Ontario Lottery and Gaming would not comment Wednesday on whether The Beer Store - which is majority owned by Molson Coors and Labatt with Sleeman holding a minority interest - has applied to sell lottery tickets.
"OLG products are currently not available for sale at LCBO or Beer Store locations," spokesperson Tony Bitonti said in a statement.
He noted the Crown corporation is "constantly evaluating opportunities to expand points of distribution to better serve our customer and fulfil our mandate from government. We evaluate opportunities on a case-by-case basis."
Liberal Leader Bonnie Crombie said allowing Beer Stores to sell lottery tickets appears to be a contradictory policy decision by the premier and his Progressive Conservative government.
"He's now taking reliable business away from the very convenience stores he said he was trying to help," she said.
While the possibility of selling lottery tickets is widely seen as an olive branch from the government as convenience store beer sales begin shortly after Labour Day and in big box stores by Halloween, industry observers have cautioned there are no magic solutions for The Beer Store as the place to buy suds.
"I don't think there's any question that their days as a retail entity are numbered," beer author and consultant Stephen Beaumont told the Star's Josh Rubin.
A move into other products - such as salty snacks or other foods to wash down with a lager or ale - could be tricky given those products are widely available in supermarkets and other outlets that already are or will soon be selling beer.
"What are they going to add? Gourmet chips? Another food element? But then they'd have to be more unique than the convenience store," Lisa Hutcheson, a managing partner at retail consultancy J.C. Williams Group said in an interview with Rubin last month.
The "ancillary list" of products the Alcohol and Gaming Commission of Ontario now allows Beer Stores to sell is limited to beer-related clothing, hats, sandals, drinking glasses, bar ware, aprons, draught supplies, beverage holders, earrings, lighters, playing cards, key chains and fobs, umbrellas, towels, posters and patio lights.
It would have to be amended to allow sales of other products and merchandise, an industry source said.
CAPTION: The Beer Store won't say if there is a plan to sell lottery tickers and it's silent on what else it might sell under an agreement with the province that allows the sale of other products.
