Document 478 of 500

June 12: Air conditioner program raises questions, a questionable return to cabinet and other letters to the editor

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/june-12-air-conditioner-program-raises-questions/docview/3066896179/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=June+12%3A+Air+conditioner+program+raises+questions%2C+a+questionable+return+to+cabinet+and+other+letters+to+the+editor&amp;title=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-06-12&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Program raises some questionsRe: City of Hamilton handing out $350 cheques for air conditioners to low-income households (June 5)
So the city is going to give 150 lucky people (with a documented medical condition) $350 to buy an air conditioner. What if they don't have a family doctor and can't get documentation? Doesn't everyone feel uncomfortable in extreme heat? How do they know the money will be spent on air conditioners? And are they supposed to hop in their cars to the Home Depot, buy the air conditioner, take it home and install it themselves?
British Columbia is offering free air conditioners to people with low incomes. Last year it handed out 6,000 air conditioners and this year the government was prepared to fund another 19,000. I guess Hamilton only has 150 vulnerable people. Surely a company could offer services to install these air conditioners.
Cheryl Arlene Payne, Stoney Creek
Christians inaccurately labelledRe: Cult of Trump poised to topple American democracy (June 10)
I read with concern an opinion in Monday's edition of The Spec. The writer referred to some Christians as "far-right" and accused them of acting like the Taliban in the way they attack certain segments of the population. These sweeping characterizations do not accurately reflect who Christians are.
A more accurate depiction of who Christians are is found in the gospel of Matthew, where Jesus said, "Love the Lord your God with all your heart, with all your soul, and with all your mind. This is the great and first commandment. And a second is like it: You shall love your neighbour as yourself."
Andrew Douma, Ancaster
Ministry should invest in programRe: 'Big jump' in results among struggling early readers who participated in intervention program at Hamilton public school board (June 8)
Great article and research by Erin Panda. The only exception I take with it is her line toward the end that says, "it's really about leadership at the school board level." I'm sure all school boards and teachers would welcome this program. But what it is really needed for the students to be successful is for the minister of Education being on board with this program, allocating funds for it and adding additional teachers to teach it. I think we would then see tremendous results. But until this happens, our students once again are left to fall behind.
Mary Hickey, Burlington
Questionable return to cabinetRe: Premier Doug Ford shuffles cabinet as legislature rises for summer (June 6)
Steve Clark is now the government house leader. How in all that is right is that not flooring people? He was the minister of municipal affairs and housing from 2018 until 2023, when he resigned from provincial cabinet amid the Greenbelt scandal. He violated ethics rules concerning his decisions around the Greenbelt deals with developers.
How stupid does Doug Ford think Ontario voters are? Apparently he thinks the Ontario public has a very, very short memory. Or Doug just really never did see anything wrong with giving his developer friends backroom deals.
Dan McDonell, Hamilton
Neighbourhood change not welcome
My neighbourhood is turning into rentals. I pay my taxes, use my extra garbage tags, keep my property clean and park in my driveway. Not so for the rentals.
Recently a school bus that needed to pick up a child was having a problem trying to manoeuvre around the increase of parked cars.
A better look at these homes being duplexed is a must. I do understand the need for housing, but please have better insight for making our streets clean and safe again.
Heather Monette, Hamilton
Child deaths should be investigated
The tragic deaths of two children following a tonsillectomy and an adenoidectomy at McMaster Children's Hospital is very unsettling. Tonsillectomy is viewed as a relatively safe procedure in children. However, two deaths in one month, while concerning, is not a statistic. The two incidents, together with any "near misses," such as the patient with serious complications reported in the Spectator, should be investigated, looking for any errors or omissions in management.
I have long been concerned about the pressures placed on surgeons to achieve "efficiencies" in length of stay, measuring different hospitals against one another with the penalty of loss of beds, etc. if your hospital drops to a lower quartile.
Same-day discharges following surgery place extra challenges on postoperative care. Was hemostasis adequate? Was anesthesia fully recovered? Any undue swelling? Airways not compromised? Heart rate and blood pressure stable and so on? Is the patient being discharged in care of an informed, competent adult? What to do if things go wrong?
Hospital statisticians also place penalties on postoperative ER visits or hospital readmissions after surgery. What about availability of surgical staff to see recently discharged patients?
Perhaps the deaths will prove to be "happenstance" with no remedy recommended. I suspect some changes will be recommended.
Rocco de Villiers, MD, Hamilton
Museum of Hope truly inspiring
As an avid military historian and a relative of veterans from both world wars, I was deeply moved by local and international coverage of the 80th anniversary of D-Day, from the live coverage by the CBC on Juno Beach to articles in the Spectator and Facebook posts. But far more touching than any of these testimonies was a visit that my husband and I made to the Waterdown Museum of Hope at the Waterdown Royal Canadian Legion.
We were greeted by students who captivated us with their exhibits that had been meticulously curated by them. The amazing collection of war artifacts, ranging from medals to weapons to letters, came alive as our hosts took their jobs as museum guides very seriously. One young man had an excellent talk about motorcycles, while a young woman spoke eloquently about the experiences of her great grandfather in the navy. Time and time again, the D-Day veterans spoke about their fear of future generations forgetting the lessons of the past. At Waterdown District High School, under the direction of teacher Rob Flosman, not only are these lessons being taught, but also lessons about historical thinking concepts, morality and the power of personal testimony. I urge readers to support this jewel in the HWDSB's crown by visiting the museum on the dates listed at waterdownmuseumofhope.ca. You will be inspired more than you would by any speech by royalty or prime ministers.
Dawn Martens, Dundas
CAPTION: City councillors voted recently to give out $350 cheques to Hamiltonians who can't afford air conditioning and have a documented medical condition that worsens in heat.

Title: June 12: Air conditioner program raises questions, a questionable return to cabinet and other letters to the editor

Letters

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/letters/docview/3066738953/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=unknown&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Letters&amp;title=The+Spectator&amp;issn=11899417&amp;date=2024-06-12&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.11&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Spectator&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Program raises some questions
Re: City of Hamilton handing out $350 cheques for air conditioners to low-income households
(June 5)
So the city is going to give 150 lucky people (with a documented medical condition) $350 to buy an air conditioner. What if they don't have a family doctor and can't get documentation? Doesn't everyone feel uncomfortable in extreme heat? How do they know the money will be spent on air conditioners? And are they supposed to hop in their cars to the Home Depot, buy the air conditioner, take it home and install it themselves?
British Columbia is offering free air conditioners to people with low incomes. Last year it handed out 6,000 air conditioners and this year the government was prepared to fund another 19,000. I guess Hamilton only has 150 vulnerable people. Surely a company could offer services to install these air conditioners.
Cheryl Arlene Payne,
Stoney Creek
Ministry should
invest in program
Re: 'Big jump' in results among struggling early readers who participated in intervention program at Hamilton public school board (June 8)
Great article and research by Erin Panda. The only exception I take with it is her line toward the end that says, "it's really about leadership at the school board level." I'm sure all school boards and teachers would welcome this program. But what is really needed for the students to be successful is for the minister of education to be on board with this program, allocating funds for it and adding additional teachers to teach it. I think we would then see tremendous results. But until this happens, our students once again are left to fall behind.
Mary Hickey, Burlington
Questionable return to cabinet
Re: Premier Doug Ford shuffles cabinet as legislature rises for summer (June 6)
Steve Clark is now the government house leader. How in all that is right is that not flooring people? He was the minister of municipal affairs and housing from 2018 until 2023, when he resigned from provincial cabinet amid the Greenbelt scandal. He violated ethics rules concerning his decisions around the Greenbelt deals with developers.
How stupid does Doug Ford think Ontario voters are? Apparently he thinks the Ontario public has a very, very short memory. Or Doug just really never did see anything wrong with giving his developer friends backroom deals.
Dan McDonell, Hamilton
Neighbourhood change not welcome
My neighbourhood is turning into rentals. I pay my taxes, use my extra garbage tags, keep my property clean and park in my driveway. Not so for the rentals. Recently a school bus that needed to pick up a child was having a problem trying to manoeuvre around the increase of parked cars. A better look at these homes being duplexed is a must. I do understand the need for housing, but please have better insight for making our streets clean and safe again.
Heather Monette, Hamilton
Child deaths should be investigated
The tragic deaths of two children following a tonsillectomy and an adenoidectomy at McMaster Children's Hospital are very unsettling. Tonsillectomy is viewed as a relatively safe procedure in children. However, two deaths in one month, while concerning, is not a statistic.
The two incidents, together with any "near misses," such as the patient with serious complications reported in the Spectator, should be investigated, looking for any errors or omissions in management.
I have long been concerned about the pressures placed on surgeons to achieve "efficiencies" in length of stay, measuring different hospitals against one another with the penalty of loss of beds, etc. if your hospital drops to a lower quartile.
Same-day discharges following surgery place extra challenges on postoperative care. Was hemostasis adequate? Was anesthesia fully recovered? Any undue swelling? Airways not compromised? Heart rate and blood pressure stable and so on? Is the patient being discharged in care of an informed, competent adult? What to do if things go wrong?
Hospital statisticians also place penalties on postoperative ER visits or hospital re-admissions after surgery. What about availability of surgical staff to see recently discharged patients?
Perhaps the deaths will prove to be "happenstance" with no remedy recommended. I suspect some changes will be recommended.
Rocco de Villiers, MD, Hamilton

Document 480 of 500

Mississauga residents vote in Parrish as their new mayor

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/mississauga-residents-vote-parrish-as-their-new/docview/3066738623/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Mississauga+residents+vote+in+Parrish+as+their+new+mayor&amp;title=The+Spectator&amp;issn=11899417&amp;date=2024-06-12&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.7&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Spectator&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Former federal member of Parliament Carolyn Parrish has been elected mayor of Mississaug from a field a 16 candidates.
Parrish, who is also a former city councillor in Mississauga, won by a comfortable margin with 43,494 votes total with 100 per cent of polls reporting Monday night.
Her next-closest challenger, city councillor Alvin Tedjo, trailed with 35,005, followed well back by fellow councillor and former Ontario MPP Dipika Damerla at 27,119.
The race was a byelection, triggered after former mayor Bonnie Crombie stepped down in January to lead the Ontario Liberals.
Shortly after launching into her prepared victory speech, Parrish abandoned it and declared the region will be stronger because there will be "three mayors that actually get along."
"We're going to mix charm and force and we're going to take over the world!"
To run for mayor, Parrish resigned as councillor for Ward 5, whose residents also voted for a candidate to fill her seat.
With the key issue of housing availability and affordability at the forefront of the race, Parrish had said she would work to rezone land for residential use in shopping hubs to minimize disruption to existing neighbourhoods.
The byelection winner will serve as mayor for the remainder of the 2022-2026 term.
CAPTION:

CREDIT: The Canadian Press

Document 481 of 500

Doug Ford commiserates with Illinois governor over one common pain: 'gridlock traffic'

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/doug-ford-commiserates-with-illinois-governor/docview/3066460409/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Doug+Ford+commiserates+with+Illinois+governor+over+one+common+pain%3A+%27gridlock+traffic%27&amp;title=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-06-11&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Stuck in traffic? Premier Doug Ford says he feels your pain.
As he signed a new memorandum of understanding to boost the $29 billion in annual trade with Illinois Governor J.B. Pritzker in Toronto on Tuesday, Ford commiserated about the logjams.
"There's one thing I can tell the folks here what we have in common, really, gridlock traffic," said the premier, who worked in Chicago while running a division of his family's label business.
"Believe me, I lived years going down to Chicago downtown on the Stevenson or I-90 and you try to go on the Don Valley parking lot or the Gardiner, same situation. We're jammed," he said, referring to major expressways in the Windy City and here.
"We built up around the highways, but we're expanding that."
That's an apparent reference to both the proposed 52-km Highway 413 connecting Highway 401 in Milton to Highway 400 in Vaughan and the province's uploading of the Don Valley Parkway and the Gardiner Expressway from the City of Toronto last November.
Pritzker, the wealthy scion of the family that owns the Hyatt hotel chain, hailed Ontario as his state's "number one trading partner."
"Chicago and Toronto have a lot in common. We not only share a similar set of values between the two cities and of course between Ontario and Illinois, but also there is a tremendous amount of trade," he said.
With Ford set to visit Chicago on a trade mission, Pritzker joked: "I'd be happy to take you to a Blackhawks game to show you what real hockey looks like."
Deadpanned the premier, a long-suffering Toronto Maple Leafs fan: "I can't even argue that point."
As part of the new arrangement between Ontario and Illinois, the jurisdictions will establish a "procurement co-operation council as a forum for sharing information on procurement policies and to advance opportunities for suppliers in each jurisdiction."
That's designed to make the supply chains in the province and state even more efficient, reducing roadblocks for businesses.
CAPTION: File photo: Ontario Premier Doug Ford.

June 11: Remembering special teachers, Woodlands Park washroom is more than just a toilet and other letters to the editor

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/june-11-remembering-special-teachers-woodlands/docview/3066460230/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=June+11%3A+Remembering+special+teachers%2C+Woodlands+Park+washroom+is+more+than+just+a+toilet+and+other+letters+to+the+editor&amp;title=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-06-11&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: A noble profession indeed
Re: Class of '65 still indebted to 'Mr. McWilliams' (June 5)
Thank you to Susan Clairmont for her exquisite article on the impact of Mr. McWilliams on Holy Rosary Catholic Elementary School, Grade 8 class, in Burlington. Mr. McWilliams's class of '64 had a reunion this month to remember their teacher's contribution to their growth and development; his advocacy and encouragement continue to loom large in their collective memory.
Susan reminds us that "teaching is a noble profession" and whatever the subject, a teacher teaches character. Mr. McWilliams taught kindness and empathy, responsibility and courage by embodying these qualities in the classroom.
Many readers, not privileged to know Mr. McWilliams, will remember someone like him, especially in that pivotal Grade 8 transitional year. In my case, I remember Mr. Karpiak, my Grade 8 white-haired Ukrainian math teacher, and principal, who on Friday afternoons, last period, read from Victor Hugo's "Les Misérables." I kept going to school just to hear how the story might end, whether or not the good-hearted Jean Valjean would elude the fanatical Inspector Javert.
J.S. Porter, Hamilton
Special teachers remembered
Thank you Susan Clairmont for Saturday's story about Mr. McWilliams' Grade 8 class of 1964/1965. This brought back my own fond memories of Grade 7/8 at Burkholder Drive middle school which I attended on Hamilton Mountain approximately in the same period. We were blessed with a wonderful music teacher (Miss Lovejoy) and English teacher (Mr. Webb) who had us involved in Gilbert and Sullivan's "Mikado" in Grade 7 and "The Pirates of Penzance" in Grade 8. Everybody participated, either in the off- or on-stage chorus or props, costumes, whatever and we did two performances each year. It was absolutely thrilling (lots of work), but we loved it. Mr. Webb would organize trips to Stratford for us every year to see Shakespearean plays and to this day my husband and I love to take a day to see what's on at Stratford.
Without doubt, my two years at Burkholder were the best years of education I ever experienced and the impact of the learning and cultural aspects we were exposed to have stayed with me my whole life.
Colleen Dawson, Hamilton
Some suggested EQAO questions
Will the Conservatives' mandatory EQAO tests on financial literacy be including such questions as, ''explain why it makes more sense to spend $975 million to break a contract with the Beer Store a year early, rather than direct that money toward health care, education or public housing?'' Or, ''what are the most effective strategies to maximize profits and haggle with developers when arranging for construction on protected green spaces?''
Mark Dineen, Hamilton
Bring in a mobile washroom instead
Re: Reconstruction of torched Hamilton park washroom to cost up to $1.3 million (June 4)
The facility has not been in use for quite some time now. The solution would be to tear the existing building down. Place a modern mobile washroom trailer on site, with running water, toilets and sinks. The same ones that are used for special events or retail business renovations. The maintenance could be run by the city.
Tares J. Bobiak, Hamilton
It's more than just a toilet
The Woodlands Park washroom is a beacon of hope within our communities. For anyone who knows the history of Hamilton, they can recall a time when Woodlands Park name matched its appearance. A time when union advocates and community groups would gather under the shade tree's of Woodlands Park and protest the system that oppresses them.
It was this popularity that doomed the park, leading to a significant culling of trees by the then mayor and councillors. The councillors claimed the trees were dangerous, but for those activists and union advocates I have worked with over the years, they tell the story differently. The councillors hated the symbol that Woodlands Park was. It was a symbol that you can stand up to corporations and governments and demand better, that we are stronger together than we are alone and that everyone deserves dignity.
Now more than ever Woodlands Park remains a beacon for those who need a little. A private indoor space to use the toilet, a place to find five minutes of reprieve from Canadian winters. A chance to wash up or change clothes before you go and interview for a job or rental unit.
I am proud that this city council has decided to invest in people and spaces. Lets not make the mistake of eliminating another public washroom just to read disappointed letters to the editor decades later reminiscing about being able to use toilets at Gore Park.
If the city really needs to save a few bucks, I can think of a few expressways we can ask Doug Ford to buy.
Edward Harris, Hamilton
Worry about people, not raccoons
Re: Raccoons can be dangerous (June 8)
I found the rant in this Saturday's paper by the letter writer who took exception to the June 6 Eye On The Area picture of the three young raccoons rather over the top and paranoid.
I liked the picture. I like raccoons, in fact I like all wildlife. For the record, everything in his backyard carries disease and parasites: birds, skunks, squirrels, coyotes, bats, mosquitoes, you name it.
Perhaps he has forgotten about the biggest perpetrator and spreader of diseases across the globe and their recent COVID-19 pandemic. Can't catch that bad boy from a raccoon.
Lighten up.
Paul E. Casey, Ancaster
CAPTION: Mr. McWilliams's Grade 8 class at Holy Rosary school in Burlington 1964-65. Several members of the class recently attended a class reunion.

Document 483 of 500

Trudeau is Ford's 'political sunscreen'

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/trudeau-is-fords-political-sunscreen/docview/3066238754/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=unknown&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Trudeau+is+Ford%27s+%27political+sunscreen%27&amp;title=The+Spectator&amp;issn=11899417&amp;date=2024-06-11&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.10&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Spectator&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Premier Doug Ford shocked the public Thursday when he announced a snap cabinet shuffle, shifting some of his highest-profile ministers into new jobs and bringing even more backbenchers into the executive team. The move rekindled what has been the hottest rumour in Queen's Park gossip circles for weeks, that Ford is gearing up to call an early election. But the question still remains, why would a premier with a commanding majority risk it all with an early vote just halfway through his second mandate?
The conventional wisdom is that Ford and his advisers are worried about federal Conservative Party Leader Pierre Poilievre winning power next year and quickly making massive, unpopular spending cuts. Ontario voters might then decide during a provincial election in 2026 they'd like to balance out the federal Tories with a provincial Liberal government. There's precedent for that: Voters like to split the difference.
But in my view, Ford's fortunes are more tied to Prime Minister Justin Trudeau than they are to Poilievre. Ford and Trudeau may be joined at the hip. They love announcing new EV plants together. But Trudeau is also providing a much more valuable service to his provincial counterpart: He's absorbing all the ire from voters fed up with the status quo and looking for change.
Angry about traffic on the Gardiner? Pissed off you can't find a family doctor? Panicking about a rent increase? Odds are, you blame Trudeau, not Ford, even if all those problems are more the purview of Queen's Park than Parliament Hill.
For Ford, Trudeau is like political sunscreen: He blocks all the most dangerous rays. Right now, he's soaking up all the anger voters have over affordability, high interest rates and general economic malaise, the same anger currently swamping Prime Minister Rishi Sunak in the U.K. and weighing down President Joe Biden in the U.S.
Midway through his second term, Ford should be sagging under the weight of his accumulated choices in office. But he's not. His polling doesn't even reflect a spate of scandals and missteps: The RCMP investigation into the abandoned Greenbelt giveaway, the sell-off of Ontario Place, blatant patronage appointments and the decision to spend as much as a billion dollars just to speed up the sale of booze in corner and grocery stores.
It isn't Ford's everyman political charm that's making him bulletproof. It's Trudeau. The prime minister is taking all the fire while Ford relaxes in the trenches, safe behind his Justin-shaped shield.
If Ford does decide to call an early election, and Thursday's cabinet shuffle suggests it is a real possibility now, that will be a big reason why. He's worried about what voters might think of him when they don't have Trudeau to blame for all their problems anymore.
For new Ontario Liberal Leader Bonnie Crombie, that creates two related challenges. She has to carry the brand hangover from the federal party - for now, Trudeau and Liberal are still synonymous in most voters' minds - and she needs Ford's support to soften.
I'm biased. But I think Crombie has gotten a lot of big things right over the last six months. She's filled the party's war chest, grown the party's vote share in recent byelections and is winning the progressive primary with the NDP. But her polling numbers have stalled. Her success is being held back by an even bigger brand. Not Doug Ford. Justin Trudeau.
The Ontario Liberals need to focus on tying Ford to his least popular and most controversial policies while creating some distance between the provincial and federal Liberals in voters' minds. Crombie's team clearly can't count on Ford waiting until 2026 to call an election, nor can they expect the prime minister to take a long walk this summer.
What they can do is take their cue from Thursday's cabinet change and start acting like the election is already underway.
Ashley Csanady is a vice-
president at McMillan Vantage and a former communications staffer to Ontario premier Kathleen Wynne.
CAPTION:

CREDIT: Ashley Csanady Ashley Csanady is a vice- president at McMillan Vantage and a former communications staffer to Ontario premier Kathleen Wynne.;

Subject: Provincial elections; Voters; Prime ministers

People: Trudeau, Justin; Wynne, Kathleen

Full text: A noble profession indeed
Class of '65 still indebted to 'Mr. McWilliams' (June 5)
Thank you to Susan Clairmont for her exquisite article on the impact of Mr. McWilliams on Holy Rosary Catholic Elementary School, Grade 8 class, in Burlington. Mr. McWilliams's class of '64 had a reunion this month to remember their teacher's contribution to their growth and development; his advocacy and encouragement continue to loom large in their collective memory. Susan reminds us that "teaching is a noble profession" and whatever the subject, a teacher teaches character. Mr. McWilliams taught kindness and empathy, responsibility and courage by embodying these qualities in the classroom.
Many readers, not privileged to know Mr. McWilliams, will remember someone like him, especially in that pivotal Grade 8 transitional year. In my case, I remember Mr. Karpiak, my Grade 8 white-haired Ukrainian math teacher, and principal, who on Friday afternoons, last period, read from Victor Hugo's "Les Misérables." I kept going to school just to hear how the story might end, whether or not the good-hearted Jean Valjean would elude the fanatical Inspector Javert.
J.S. Porter, Hamilton
Special teachers remembered
Thank you Susan Clairmont for Saturday's story about Mr. McWilliams' Grade 8 class of 1964/1965. This brought back my own fond memories of Grade 7/8 at Burkholder Drive middle school which I attended on Hamilton Mountain approximately in the same period. We were blessed with a wonderful music teacher (Miss Lovejoy) and English teacher (Mr. Webb) who had us involved in Gilbert and Sullivan's "Mikado" in Grade 7 and "The Pirates of Penzance" in Grade 8. Everybody participated, either in the off- or on-stage chorus or props, costumes, whatever and we did two performances each year. It was absolutely thrilling (lots of work), but we loved it. Mr. Webb would organize trips to Stratford for us every year to see Shakespearean plays and to this day my husband and I love to take a day to see what's on at Stratford.
Without doubt, my two years at Burkholder were the best years of education I ever experienced and the impact of the learning and cultural aspects we were exposed to have stayed with me my whole life.
Colleen Dawson, Hamilton
Suggested EQAO questions
Will the Conservatives' mandatory EQAO tests on financial literacy be including such questions as, "explain why it makes more sense to spend $975 million to break a contract with the Beer Store a year early, rather than direct that money toward health care, education or public housing?" Or, "what are the most effective strategies to maximize profits and haggle with developers when arranging for construction on protected green spaces?"
Mark Dineen, Hamilton
Bring in a mobile washroom
Reconstruction of torched Hamilton park
washroom to cost up to $1.3 million (June 4)
The facility has not been in use for quite some time now. The solution would be to tear the existing building down. Place a modern mobile washroom trailer on site, with running water, toilets and sinks. The same ones that are used for special events or retail business renovations. The maintenance could be run by the city.
Tares J. Bobiak, Hamilton
It's more than just a toilet
The Woodlands Park washroom is a beacon of hope within our communities. For anyone who knows the history of Hamilton, they can recall a time when Woodlands Park name matched its appearance. A time when union advocates and community groups would gather under the shade trees of Woodlands Park and protest the system that oppresses them.
It was this popularity that doomed the park, leading to a significant culling of trees by the then mayor and councillors. The councillors claimed the trees were dangerous, but for those activists and union advocates I have worked with over the years, they tell the story differently. The councillors hated the symbol that Woodlands Park was. It was a symbol that you can stand up to corporations and governments and demand better, that we are stronger together than we are alone and that everyone deserves dignity.
Now more than ever Woodlands Park remains a beacon for those who need a little. A private indoor space to use the toilet, a place to find five minutes of reprieve from Canadian winters. A chance to wash up or change clothes before you go and interview for a job or rental unit.
I am proud this city council has decided to invest in people and spaces. Let's not make the mistake of eliminating another public washroom just to read disappointed letters to the editor decades later reminiscing about being able to use toilets at Gore Park. If the city really needs to save a few bucks, I can think of a few expressways we can ask Doug Ford to buy.
Edward Harris, Hamilton

Document 485 of 500

Ontario expanding prisons by hundreds of beds due to overflow: Province says 81% of inmates are awaiting trial and presumptively innocent

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/ontario-expanding-prisons-hundreds-beds-due/docview/3066238590/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Ontario+expanding+prisons+by+hundreds+of+beds+due+to+overflow%3A+Province+says+81%25+of+inmates+are+awaiting+trial+and+presumptively+innocent&amp;title=The+Spectator&amp;issn=11899417&amp;date=2024-06-11&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.7&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Spectator&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Ontario will add more beds to its beleaguered jails, which are bursting with inmates, solicitor general Michael Kerzner said Monday.
The province intends to add several hundred spots throughout the system as it grapples with a surge of inmates over the past 18 months.
"We're expanding and building facilities that will help front-line staff to do their jobs safely and effectively while increasing capacity to keep violent and repeat offenders off our streets," Kerzner said.
The province's jail population has spiked recently. Data obtained by The Canadian Press through freedom-of-information laws shows the vast majority of the province's jails are over capacity.
Meanwhile, the province has said 81 per cent of inmates in provincial jails are awaiting trial and presumptively innocent. Those institutions hold people accused of a crime but not out on bail, as well as those serving sentences of two years less a day.
As of Sept. 30, 2023, there was an average of 8,889 people in provincial jails, well over the 7,848-person capacity. Overall, the jails were operating at 113 per cent capacity at that time.
Premier Doug Ford pledged in March to build more jails to deal with an influx of inmates, the vast majority of whom are innocent and awaiting trial.
Ontario will reopen two intermittent detention centres at Toronto and London jails that had been closed in order to add up to 430 beds by 2026. The province also intends to hire 200 correctional staff to deal with an influx of inmates, Kerzner said. The province will add 18 new beds at the Quinte Detention Centre in Napanee, Ont., and will add 184 beds to the new Brockville Correctional Complex.
The union representing correctional workers has long called for more staffing, more resources and more mental-health support as bursting jails coupled with long-standing staffing shortages have led to a human resources crisis within its ranks. Correctional workers are increasingly assaulted and both operational stress injuries and post-traumatic stress disorders are on the rise. Suicides among correctional officers are also increasing, the union has said.
The union applauded the government's expansion plans, saying the extra space for inmates is "desperately needed to help the significant overcapacity issues we're seeing right across the province," said Chad Oldfield, an OPSEU spokesperson and correctional officer.
The extra space and staff will really help in the eastern, western and Toronto region jails, Oldfield said. But the same is needed in central and northern Ontario jails.
Maplehurst Correction Complex in Milton, was the most overcrowded last year, with an average inmate population of 1,188 but official capacity for 887 - meaning it was operating at 134 per cent capacity in 2023.
Elgin-Middlesex Detention Centre in London, was a close second at 133 per cent capacity, with an average 471 inmates while having operational capacity for 353.
South West Detention Centre in Windsor, was operating at 129 per cent capacity, with 337 inmates but space for only 262 people.
The province is currently building a new 345-bed jail in Thunder Bay, that will replace the current jail and double its capacity, at a cost of $1.2 billion. Ontario also plans to build a 235-bed jail in eastern Ontario but that project has met opposition from locals, who don't want to see it built on prime farmland.
The Canadian Press
CAPTION: Maplehurst Correction Complex in Milton wasthe most overcrowded Ontario prison last year, with an average inmate population of 1,188 but official capacity for 887 - meaning it was operating at 134 per cent capacity in 2023.Graham Paine Metrolandfile photo

Document 486 of 500

Premier ignores lessons of our past at our peril

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/premier-ignores-lessons-our-past-at-peril/docview/3066238588/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=unknown&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Premier+ignores+lessons+of+our+past+at+our+peril&amp;title=The+Spectator&amp;issn=11899417&amp;date=2024-06-11&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.10&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Spectator&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: One of the few things we knew for sure in the earliest, most chaotic days of the COVID-19 pandemic in Ontario was that we didn't know enough. We didn't have enough data. We had no streamlined central system for sharing information on test results, outbreaks or mortality. We didn't know how to gather the knowledge we needed or how to share it - except, in many cases, by fax - between all the organizations working on the front lines.
That was 2020. Four years on, we are left to ask how many lives might have been saved if we had better information, in real time, about what was needed and what was working. It was a predictable, preventable lapse in basic pandemic preparation, one caused by successive governments of every stripe that, despite the stark warnings of SARS, repeatedly put short-term politics over public health.
But that was all in the past, right? Things have changed, right? The mammoth human, economic and social catastrophe of COVID-19 was a lesson not even the most myopic politicians could ignore. When the next pandemic comes, Ontario will be ready. We will know enough to know about local outbreaks and viral peaks, about infection rates and testing capacities.
We'd like to believe that. Having lived through the trauma of COVID-19, Ontarians certainly have the right to expect it. But that's not what's happening, at least not if the Ford government's truly inexplicable decision to cancel funding for provincial wastewater surveillance is any indication.
Ontario's wastewater surveillance system was one of the few true triumphs to come out of the pandemic in this province. When widespread PCR (polymerase chain reaction) testing was suspended at the end of 2021, wastewater testing became the most accurate way to determine daily COVID cases in the province, the Toronto Star's Megan Ogilvie wrote last week. It works by monitoring wastewater for genetic viral material. Over time, it proved so effective, the province expanded the system to test for other existing or emerging plagues, including new COVID variants.
Until this week, Ontario had one of the most comprehensive wastewater testing systems in the world. Provincial researchers were monitoring 58 sites in Ontario, including big cities, small towns, First Nations and everything in between, Ogilvie reported. We were doing the right thing, the rare thing, in paying attention between pandemics, spending money now to improve our odds of success when disaster inevitably strikes later.
But now Premier Doug Ford and his government are throwing all that away. They informed researchers that money for the wastewater program, funded until now by Ministry of Environment, Conservation and Parks, would end July 31. Why? Because the federal government is modestly expanding its own wastewater surveillance network and the province wants to "avoid duplication," according to an official who spoke to Ogilvie.
Surely one lesson of COVID-19 is that governments must work together to confront pandemics, rather than playing jurisdictional hot potato. Moreover, the new federal network won't come close to matching the capacity of the existing provincial system. It will have fewer sites in Ontario (between five and nine total) and it won't feed data directly into Ontario institutions, like hospital networks, public health units and the provincial ministry.
Cutting the Ontario wastewater surveillance network now is a betrayal of everyone who sacrificed in the fight against COVID-19. It is a mistake on its own. More troubling, it suggests we are already repeating the mistakes we made after SARS. Ford, like his predecessors, is ignoring the lessons of the past and failing the people of the future, setting us up for more fumbling and more deaths when the next pandemic comes.
CAPTION:

CREDIT: ;

Subject: Public health; Health surveillance; Pandemics; COVID-19

Document 487 of 500

Doug Ford wants to steal Pierre Poilievre's thunder for his own re-election bid

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/doug-ford-wants-steal-pierre-poilievres-thunder/docview/3066238568/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Doug+Ford+wants+to+steal+Pierre+Poilievre%27s+thunder+for+his+own+re-election+bid&amp;title=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-06-11&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: If you're wondering what keeps Doug Ford awake at night, how he spends every waking moment during the day, I can now reveal his most frantic and fantastical obsession.
No, it's not the housing crisis, nor the health-care crisis, not even the beer boondoggle - each of which is dragging the province down. None of the above comes close.
"Axe the tax" is his mantra above all else.
Every sinew and synapse of his being is devoted to the federal carbon levy. It consumes him and he consumes it - chewing it over with unrivalled rumination and regurgitation.
"Axe the tax" is his call to arms.
In the six years since Ford first ran for premier, he's been running relentlessly on that slogan. It predates "buck-a-beer" and has outlasted everything since.
"Axe the tax" is the bumper sticker stuck on his forehead.
But will it stick? Or will it wear off - diluted by repetition, co-opted by rival Tories, overtaken by events?
After all, it is now the core theme for Ford's federal Conservative cousin, Opposition Leader Pierre Poilievre, as he pre-positions for a national election campaign later next year. Can Ford compete and still get a piece of the "axe the tax" action?
Just watch him. And hear him pre-empt Poilievre.
Today - no less than in 2018 - the slogan soaks up more of Ford's breath and bandwidth than any other issue in Ontario. Back then, he talked it up to win his first election as premier.
Now, he is banking on it to win his third term in power, sooner rather than later. Ford is toying with an early election call in 2025 - one year ahead of the fixed date mandated by law - to make the most of the country's anti-tax animus before Poilievre bites off too much of it.
Ford's sloganeering dominated the Ontario legislature for months before a summer break was announced last week. If you search through the transcripts of Ontario's legislative body, you will be inundated with "job-killing carbon tax" verbiage that is a testament to the Progressive Conservative game plan.
One after another, loyal Tories are assigned to read from a prepared script, posing their planted questions for PC government ministers to hit out of Queen's Park. The ritual is as predictable as it is pointless.
Sam Oosterhoff was the latest backbencher to rise up from his seat in the legislature and ask how the premier would put Ottawa in its place.
"What is our government doing to ensure that we are fighting this job-killing, expensive carbon tax and putting more money back into the pockets of the hard-working people?" the MPP demanded, before claiming, improbably, that voters "are flabbergasted that queen Crombie is committed to bringing forward yet another carbon tax."
Queen Crombie? That would be a reference to Liberal Leader Bonnie Crombie, whom Ford's Tories persist in calling "queen of the carbon tax."
It is a peculiar appellation and allegation, for Crombie only took over the opposition Liberals late last year - several years after Ford took power and presided over the imposition of the federal carbon levy for consumers, and ordered the introduction of a provincial carbon levy for industrial users. The irony is that while Crombie cannot logically or chronologically be connected to the levy, Ford's provenance as "king of Ontario's carbon levy" is an inconvenient fact.
For it was Ford who cancelled Ontario's little-known cap and trade system, conceived of by conservative economists who view it as a more efficient way for the market to put a price on pollution. By doing so, he automatically triggered the federal carbon backstop, designed to ensure that every province does its bit to keep Canada on track to meet its international obligations to restrain global warming.
In 2022, the Ontario government launched its own homegrown tax that charges big polluters $65 per tonne of carbon they emit, rising to $80 a tonne on Jan. 1 - which is, naturally, passed on to consumers (unlike the federal carbon levy, which is fully rebated to most families). But why let contradictions get in the way of campaigns?
Oosterhoff was merely the last on a long list of dozens of backbenchers reading from Ford's script, and he did as he was told. There was no good answer for so bad a question that day, but there was an optimal outcome for Oosterhoff mere hours after he fought the good fight last Thursday.
Ford elevated the 26-year-old loyalist to cabinet as "associate minister for energy." Henceforth, he will be one of the premier's chief mouthpieces for his undying six-year-old crusade. As King of the Carbon Tax, Ford now has another of the faithful in his record-breaking 36-member cabinet, ready to fight a new campaign with an old battle cry.
If they win the day next year with "axe the tax," an updated rallying cry will naturally follow: "The levy is dead! Long live the King!"
CAPTION: Premier Doug Ford reacts reacts during an event at the Honda of Canada Manufacturing Plant 2 in Alliston on April 25, 2024.

Document 488 of 500

Doug Ford quietly disbands ministry overseeing $1B Queen's Park renovation

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/doug-ford-quietly-disbands-ministry-overseeing-1b/docview/3066558846/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Doug+Ford+quietly+disbands+ministry+overseeing+%241B+Queen%27s+Park+renovation&amp;title=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-06-10&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Premier Doug Ford has quietly disbanded the ministry overseeing the $1-billion renovation of the Legislative Assembly building at Queen's Park.
Lost amid Thursday's major cabinet shuffle was the dissolution of the ministry of legislative affairs that had been run by Municipal Affairs and Housing Minister Paul Calandra.
As first disclosed by the Star, Ford also stripped Calandra of his duties as government house leader after the premier was blindsided by Speaker Ted Arnott's April ban on the Palestinian kaffiyeh in the legislature. The premier felt the house leader should have warned him the Speaker was banning the black and white scarves because they are "political" garments.
The planned retrofit of the building on Queen's Park Circle - dating back to 1893 and affectionately known as "the pink palace" - is supposed to begin in 2027 and will take years to complete.
Infrastructure Minister Kinga Surma, already overseeing the delayed $1.5-billion renovation of the sprawling Macdonald Block office complex as well as the controversial Ontario Place regeneration project, assumes responsibility for the legislature's repairs.
Earlier this spring, Surma spoke candidly about the challenges modernizing the 53-year-old Macdonald Block at the corner of Bay and Wellesley Streets, across from Queen's Park.
Some 6,000 bureaucrats and political aides are supposed to work there, but they are marooned in rented offices throughout the city because there's no timeline for them to return to the legislative precinct.
"This has been a very difficult time to build," the infrastructure minister said in April, noting "unforeseen challenges," including the COVID-19 pandemic, had led to rising costs.
"There are cost overruns. Fixing the issues that we faced is of great importance because these are taxpayer dollars."
While the Macdonald Block remains in limbo, Ford's dismantling of the ministry of legislative affairs creates further uncertainty.
Calandra had been a firm hand overseeing the Queen's Park Restoration Secretariat created in March 2023 to renovate the Legislative Assembly.
That secretariat, with help from an all-party legislative committee, is currently in talks with potential private landlords for a new, temporary legislature that's expected to be built in an office building near Queen's Park.
The government is negotiating to rent 500,000 square feet of space so the temporary accommodation would have the same number of offices as the existing legislature.
That is important because Ford, Arnott, Official Opposition Leader Marit Stiles, dozens of MPPs, scores of journalists in the Queen's Park press gallery as well as hundreds of political aides, librarians and legislative staffers currently work in the building.
The Star has a four-person bureau in the assembly where other major media outlets such as CBC, CTV, the Globe and Mail, The Canadian Press, the Toronto Sun, CityNews, Global, Radio-Canada, among others, also have offices.
There is an urgency to the project - the government has warned "critical building systems such as plumbing, heating, electrical, and IT services, are at or beyond end of life and at risk of total failure."
On Parliament Hill, the Centre Block, which houses the Peace Tower and House of Commons, has been under renovation for several years and is not expected to reopen until 2031 at a cost of $5 billion. MPs use a temporary Commons in the neighbouring West Block.
Unwinding the ministry of legislative affairs was but one part of Ford's cabinet reshuffle that also saw Stephen Lecce moved from education to the renamed Ministry of Energy and Electrification in a swap with Todd Smith.
The 36-member executive council is now the biggest cabinet in provincial government history, though there are only nine women. Ford added four men and one woman.
Steve Clark, who resigned from cabinet last September over the premier's $8.28-billion Greenbelt land swap scandal, will be government house leader. But Clark will not sit in cabinet or face opposition questions during the legislature's question period that resumes Oct. 21.
CAPTION: Premier Doug Ford listens as House Leader Paul Calandra answers a question during question period in the legislature at Queens Park on Oct. 19, 2023.

Subject: Legislatures; Politics

June 10: Child deaths after surgery worrisome, don't stereotype landlords and other letters to the editor

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/june-10-child-deaths-after-surgery-worrisome-dont/docview/3066037707/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=June+10%3A+Child+deaths+after+surgery+worrisome%2C+don%27t+stereotype+landlords+and+other+letters+to+the+editor&amp;title=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-06-10&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Child deaths after surgery worrisome
Re: Doctor says his granddaughter was almost a third death following tonsil surgery at McMaster Children's Hospital (June 7)
I was shocked to read about two children dying after what, in my day, was considered minor surgery. Then Rosie List's story was published and my concern grew even worse because of the ordeal she and her family went through.
Why would a child be in danger having their tonsils and adenoids removed? I can't fathom how this has become such a perilous operation.
All three of my children (now in their 60s, but were five, three and two at the time) had several infections within months of each other, so were scheduled to have theirs removed on the same day, kept overnight and able to come home late the next morning. None had a problem, recovered quickly and loved having lots of ice cream and Jell-O to eat. Were they lucky or were these operations so commonplace way back then, there was more expertise at work? Who knows?
Jackie Oke, Dundas
Lack of action incomprehensible
Re: The Liberal government's approach to foreign interference? No transparency, no accountability, and a whole lot of inaction (June 6)
I managed manufacturing companies in the private sector for most of my 50-plus years working life. If one morning I came to work and a reputable investigation agency presented me with a report that detailed that a number of the company's employees were wittingly providing company secrets to our competitors, I would take immediate action to resolve the issue.
My guess is that 100 per cent of businesspeople would immediately take action. The first concern would be to eliminate the leaks. I would be speak with the named employees to learn their side of the issues, then evaluate all available information, make decisions and implement the decisions that morning.
As head of a company, your No. 1 responsibility is to protect the company and its employees from attack. Why is this not the same for our federal government?
Ian Barrie, Ancaster
Shuffle doesn't inspire hope
Re: Premier Doug Ford shuffles cabinet as legislature rises for summer (June 6)
So Stephen Lecce has now moved from minister of education to minister of energy. Based on his track record, this feels like a good moment for everyone in Ontario to reflect upon who they want to join their Mad Max-style motor gang as they roam the barren wastelands scavenging batteries and gasoline from huddled terrified survivors of the Ford administration.
David La Rush, Hamilton
Guns no, but nukes yes?
I believe a convicted felon in the States may not possess or acquire firearms whatsoever, by law. However, Donald Trump may end up being the next president of the U.S. He cannot "own firearms," but will have under his control probably the largest and most lethal nuclear weapons on this planet. Scary times, indeed.
John H. Miller, Hamilton
Don't stereotype landlords
Landlords have a bad name. But, coming in all shapes and sizes, they should not be stereotyped. During the Great Depression, they were vilified when they evicted tenants. Understandable. But my research shows that in Hamilton they were much more likely to lose their properties to foreclosure than homeowners were. How come? Most landlords were "small." As vacancies rose, they lowered rents, but taxes stayed the same. They suffered, too. In the rental sector, as in wider spheres, there is usually more than one side to any issue.
Richard Harris, professor emeritus, McMaster University
We will never forget
Re: McMaster students in Normandy hold high torch they designed for D-Day remembrance (June 6)
What a beautiful story on the front page of Thursday's Spec, commemorating the 80th anniversary of D-Day. Congratulations to Karen Hunter and the Mac engineering students who made the journey to the northwestern coast of France, to honour the men and women who fought so bravely to preserve the democratic way of life we enjoy today. So many of those who gave the ultimate sacrifice were the same age or younger than the students in the photo.
It buoys the human spirit that these young people designed, built and delivered the Canadian Remembrance Torch to honour a generation we owe so much to, forever. It also encourages me to know, no matter how many generations come after them, we will never forget.
Hugh M. Russell, Ancaster
Here we go again
Re: Hamilton landfill construction delay sparks $5.7-million lawsuit (June 7)
A shudder came across me this morning when I read of the filing of a $5.7-million lawsuit against the city, claiming "design errors and omissions" by city staff and its engineering firms. Once again, councillors are receiving "confidential updates" and the city cannot "comment publicly on the matter." Seems to me this was the same response narrative we heard before the Sewergate scandal that cost taxpayers a $2.6-million fine on top of costly remediation expenses. Could this be déjà vu all over again?
David Shupe, Dundas
Student testing is important
I'm pleased that the Ontario government continues with EQAO testing and is focusing on improvements to curriculum to achieve better outcomes for students. But how typical is it that teachers and their unions find fault with anything remotely regarding testing. Their critique is always under the auspice of "it's not good for students" or "it'll hurt little Joey's feelings."
The reality is that teachers and their unions don't want any measurable results that may show students aren't doing well. Teachers might feel it's a reflection on them. Regardless, we need to know how our children are doing scholastically in order to identify coaching opportunities.
We can't raise the children of Ontario's future solely by issuing participation trophies. Performance matters, trying matters, comprehension matters and success matters. We want all our children to be the best they can.
Blanche Baldwin, Hamilton
CAPTION: Rosie List had tonsil surgery at McMaster Children's Hospital on May 15 and became gravely ill afterward.

Document 490 of 500

Readers write

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/readers-write/docview/3065889401/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=unknown&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Readers+write&amp;title=The+Spectator&amp;issn=11899417&amp;date=2024-06-10&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.11&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Spectator&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Deaths after surgery worrisome
Doctor says his granddaughter was almost a
third death following tonsil surgery at McMaster Children's Hospital (June 7)
I was shocked to read about two children dying after what, in my day, was considered minor surgery. Then Rosie List's story was published and my concern grew even worse because of the ordeal she and her family went through.
Why would a child be in danger having their tonsils and adenoids removed? I can't fathom how this has become such a perilous operation.
All three of my children (now in their 60s, but were five, three and two at the time) had several infections within months of each other, so were scheduled to have theirs removed on the same day, kept overnight and able to come home late the next morning.
None had a problem, recovered quickly and loved having lots of ice cream and Jell-O to eat. Were they lucky or were these operations so commonplace way back then, there was more expertise at work? Who knows?
Jackie Oke, Dundas
Lack of action is baffling
The Liberal government's approach to foreign interference? No transparency, no accountability, and a whole lot of inaction (June 6)
I managed manufacturing companies in the private sector for most of my 50-plus years of working life. If one morning I came to work and a reputable investigation agency presented me with a report that detailed that a number of the company's employees were wittingly providing company secrets to our competitors, I would take immediate action to resolve the issue.
My guess is that 100 per cent of businesspeople would immediately take action. The first concern would be to eliminate the leaks. I would speak with the named employees to learn their side of the issues, then evaluate all available information, make decisions and implement the decisions that morning.
As head of a company, your No. 1 responsibility is to protect the company and its employees from attack. Why is this not the same for our federal government?
Ian Barrie, Ancaster
Shuffle doesn't inspire hope
Premier Doug Ford shuffles cabinet as legislature rises for summer (June 6)
So Stephen Lecce has now moved from minister of education to minister of energy. Based on his track record, this feels like a good moment for everyone in Ontario to reflect upon who they want to join their Mad Max-style motor gang as they roam the barren wastelands scavenging batteries and gasoline from huddled terrified survivors of the Ford administration.
David La Rush, Hamilton
Guns no, but nukes yes?
I believe a convicted felon in the States may not possess or acquire firearms whatsoever, by law. However, Donald Trump may end up being the next president of the U.S. He cannot "own firearms," but will have under his control probably the largest and most lethal nuclear weapons on this planet. Scary times, indeed.
John H. Miller, Hamilton
Don't stereotype landlords
Landlords have a bad name. But, coming in all shapes and sizes, they should not be stereotyped. During the Great Depression, they were vilified when they evicted tenants. Understandable. But my research shows that in Hamilton they were much more likely to lose their properties to foreclosure than homeowners were. How come? Most landlords were "small." As vacancies rose, they lowered rents, but taxes stayed the same. They suffered, too. In the rental sector, as in wider spheres, there is usually more than one side to any issue.
Richard Harris, professor emeritus,
McMaster University
We will never forget
McMaster students in Normandy hold high torch they designed for D-Day remembrance (June 6)
What a beautiful story on the front page of Thursday's Spec, commemorating the 80th anniversary of D-Day. Congratulations to Karen Hunter and the Mac engineering students who made the journey to the northwestern coast of France, to honour the men and women who fought so bravely to preserve the democratic way of life we enjoy today. So many of those who gave the ultimate sacrifice were the same age or younger than the students in the photo.
It buoys the human spirit that these young people designed, built and delivered the Canadian Remembrance Torch to honour a generation we owe so much to, forever. It also encourages me to know, no matter how many generations come after them, we will never forget.
Hugh M. Russell, Ancaster

Document 491 of 500

'Extraordinary loss': Blaze destroys historic St. Anne's Church in Toronto and its 'priceless' Group of Seven murals

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/extraordinary-loss-blaze-destroys-historic-st/docview/3066105313/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=%27Extraordinary+loss%27%3A+Blaze+destroys+historic+St.+Anne%27s+Church+in+Toronto+and+its+%27priceless%27+Group+of+Seven+murals&amp;title=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-06-09&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Hamilton+Spectator+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: When Don Beyers found out his church was on fire early Sunday morning, he threw on the first pair of pants he could find and raced - on foot - to the site.
But it was too late.
"Fire was already spewing out the dome at the top," Beyers said of the roof at St. Anne's Anglican Church in Little Portugal, where he has been parish priest for three years. "And I knew there was nothing we could do."
That included rescuing murals of Jesus' life, painted by three members of Canada's iconic Group of Seven, he said.
The murals - about 16 to 18 of them that dotted the walls and ceilings of the church, Beyers said - were likely all reduced to rubble when a four-alarm fire erupted at the building on Gladstone Avenue, between College and Dundas Streets, in Toronto's west end just before 8 a.m.
Most of the church was gone.
"I was crushed," he said. "I just started to cry."
The "priceless" paintings, completed in 1923, were a landmark Beyers called "a gem of Canadian culture."
It's "an extraordinary loss," he said later in the morning in a media scrum outside the church.
"This area means a lot to us. While this is incredibly devastating for my congregation, it's devastating for this community," he said. "I cannot express enough how far reaching this church fire is going to have."
"We have our fingers crossed," Toronto Fire Services division commander Steven Darling said from a laneway near the still-smouldering building. "But it's highly unlikely there's anything we can save inside the building."
Toronto Fire hasn't determined a cause to the blaze, noting that the fire marshal and its teams will be investigating over the coming days. A spokesperson added that no one was inside the church at the time of the fire and there were no reports of injuries.
According to Beyers, the church's doors were locked and all the lights were shut off the night before.
"It's a real mystery to us how this even happened," Beyers said.
Darling said dozens of fire trucks, crews, kilometres of hose and an aerial tower that shot water onto the church's roof were dispatched to the site. Firefighters had also entered through the locked door at the back of the church during the blaze, but after about ten minutes, crews had to go on the "defensive" and retreat when the roof started to collapse, Darling said.
Fire deputy chief Jim Jessop told reporters at the scene it is possible the building could experience further collapses given the volume of water used to extinguish the fire and its age. Crews have created a "safe area" around the building as they continue to tend to the site.
Fire crews had set up hoses and command stations along Dundas Street between Gladstone Avenue and Lisgar Street, where about dozens of stores and street vendors were preparing to open for Do West Festival, a massive street party that took over that stretch of Dundas over the weekend. As black plumes of smoke shot into the sky, the festival's organizers were forced to grapple Sunday morning with how to remain open or whether it should close.
By mid-morning, fire crews had contained the blaze, downgrading it to a two-alarm fire, and moved the remaining vehicles and equipment to Dufferin Street, freeing up Dundas for the festival to proceed as planned.
But while the equipment shift offered some relief, there were still heavy emotions around the corner as locals and tourists alike gathered in an alleyway off Gladstone Avenue to watch firefighters put out any remaining hot spots at the historic church.
Laura Storey, 35, had "instant tears," she said, when her partner came home after a run and broke the news of the fire.
She'd been going to St. Anne's for years, she said, most recently to a candlelight ceremony on Easter. Not long before that she celebrated her 35th birthday at the church during evening mass on Christmas Eve.
"We had plans to be out of the city today," she said, a tear cresting down her cheek. "But we decided to just walk around here and feel it."
Keith Levenson, a former builder visiting from Vermont, stared in shock. He'd left his Airbnb earlier in the morning to grab a coffee when he saw the fire - and found his way to a fence at the back of the church.
"It's a tragedy," he said, noticing the stained glass windows that had been blown out. "It's heartbreaking to see."
A number of local politicians arrived to the scene to show their support, including Ontario NDP Leader Marit Stiles, who represents the riding of Davenport, and local councillor Alejandra Bravo, who said the destroyed art is "something we cannot replace in Canada and in the world."
St. Anne's Church was founded on the present site in 1862 before its redesign in 1907, according to Ontario Heritage Trust. The building was designated as a National Historic Site in 1996 and the City of Toronto designated it under the Ontario Heritage Act in 1980. The two-storey church was built in a Byzantine style with a domed roof, which is an unusual look for an Anglican church and the only of its kind in Canada.
Around mid-morning, as the reality of what was left of his church set in, Don Beyers, the parish priest, grew emotional.
The first thing that came to mind when he got to the site that morning, after racing over from his home near Queen and Dovercourt Street were the people that have been coming through St. Anne's doors for more than a century.
"My heart aches for them," he said. "Aches."
With files from Nathan Bawaan
CAPTION: The collapsed roof at St. Anne's in Toronto.Fire crews at the scene of the fire at St. Anne's Church on Sunday.What's left of the roof at St. Anne's Church in Toronto on Sunday.St. Anne's Reverend Don Beyers speaks to reporters at the church.St. Anne's Anglican Church with art work painted by members of the Group of Seven in Toronto, OntarioThe roof of the St. Anne's Church in Toronto has completely burned through, destroying the dome in its entirety.Crews on site trying to extinguishing a four-alarm blaze at St. Anne's church in Toronto on Saturday.Fire crews at the scene of the fire at St. Anne's Church on Sunday.Historic St. Anne's Anglican Church devastated by a four-alarm fire.Fire crews at the scene of the fire at St. Anne's Church on Sunday.Fire crews at the scene of the fire at St. Anne's Church on Sunday.Fire crews at the scene of the fire at St. Anne's Church on Sunday.
